# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EMF Equation

### Statement
**Prove that the EMF generated in a DC machine armature is $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$.**

### Given
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole, in Weber
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $P$ = total number of poles
- $N$ = speed of armature in r.p.m.
- $A$ = number of parallel paths in the armature winding

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Flux cut by one conductor per revolution**

As the armature rotates, one conductor passes under all **P** poles in one complete revolution. In doing so, it cuts a total flux of:

$$\text{Flux per rev} = \Phi \times P \quad \text{(Weber)}$$

**Step 2 — Time taken for one revolution**

If the speed is **N revolutions per minute**, the time for one revolution is:

$$T_{rev} = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Average EMF per conductor (Faraday's Law)**

The average rate of change of flux linkage per conductor is:

$$e_{avg} = \frac{\Phi P}{T_{rev}} = \frac{\Phi P}{60/N} = \frac{\Phi P N}{60} \quad \text{Volts}$$

**Step 4 — Number of conductors in series per parallel path**

The **Z** conductors are distributed among **A** parallel paths. Conductors per path:

$$Z_{series} = \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 5 — Total EMF across one path (and hence at the brushes)**

Since all $Z/A$ conductors of one path are in series, the EMFs add directly. The terminal EMF (the EMF between brushes) equals the EMF of any one parallel path:

$$E_g = e_{avg} \times Z_{series}$$

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi P N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

$$\boxed{E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A} \quad \text{Volts}}$$

**Step 6 — Equivalent expression in angular frequency**

Since $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$, the equation can be rewritten as:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z P}{60 A} \cdot N = \frac{\Phi Z P}{60 A} \cdot \frac{60 \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{\Phi Z P\,\omega}{2\pi A}$$

$$E_g = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z \omega \quad \text{Volts}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Torque Equation

### Statement
**Prove that the electromagnetic torque developed in a DC machine is $T_d = \dfrac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a$.**

### Given
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = total conductors
- $P$ = poles
- $A$ = parallel paths
- $I_a$ = total armature current (A)
- $I = I_a/A$ = current per conductor (A, assuming all conductors carry equal current)

### Derivation (Method A — Force × Radius)

**Step 1 — Force on one conductor**

The force on a conductor of active length $l$ carrying current $I$ in flux density $B$ is:

$$f = B\,l\,I = \frac{\Phi}{a}\,I$$

where $a$ is the cross-sectional area of the pole face. We will not need $a$ explicitly — it will cancel with the radius later.

**Step 2 — Total tangential force on all conductors**

There are **Z** conductors, all carrying current $I$ in the armature, and the tangential force per conductor sums:

$$F_{total} = Z \cdot f = Z \cdot \frac{\Phi}{a}\,I$$

**Step 3 — Current per conductor**

$$I = \frac{I_a}{A}$$

So:

$$F_{total} = \frac{Z \Phi I_a}{a\,A}$$

**Step 4 — Torque = Force × Mean Radius**

Let the mean armature radius be **r** (metres). The torque developed is:

$$T_d = F_{total} \times r = \frac{Z \Phi I_a\,r}{a\,A}$$

**Step 5 — Replace $a$ with the area of one pole face**

Each pole face has area $a$. The flux per pole is $\Phi = B \cdot a$, so $a = \Phi/B$. But we can also relate $a$ to the pole pitch and armature geometry. The mean perimeter crossed by the flux is $2\pi r$, and there are **P** poles around it. The width per pole (pole pitch) is:

$$\tau = \frac{2\pi r}{P} \quad \text{(arc length per pole)}$$

The pole-face area is roughly $a = \tau \cdot l$, where $l$ is the axial core length.

Therefore:

$$\frac{r}{a} = \frac{r}{\tau\,l} = \frac{r}{\dfrac{2\pi r}{P}\cdot l} = \frac{P}{2\pi l}$$

Substituting into the torque expression:

$$T_d = \frac{Z \Phi I_a}{A} \cdot \frac{r}{a} = \frac{Z \Phi I_a}{A} \cdot \frac{P}{2\pi l}$$

But $\Phi = B \cdot a = B \cdot \tau l = B \cdot \dfrac{2\pi r}{P}\cdot l$, hence $B\,l = \dfrac{\Phi P}{2\pi r}$. Substituting:

$$T_d = \frac{Z}{A}\cdot I_a \cdot \frac{\Phi P}{2\pi} = \frac{\Phi Z I_a P}{2\pi A}$$

$$\boxed{T_d = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a \quad \text{N·m}}$$

### Derivation (Method B — Energy Conservation, much shorter!)

**Step 1 — Power balance**

In steady state, the **electrical power converted to mechanical form** equals the mechanical power developed:

$$P_{developed} = E_g \cdot I_a = T_d \cdot \omega$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $E_g$ and $\omega$**

$$T_d \cdot \omega = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}\cdot I_a$$

$$T_d \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}\cdot I_a$$

**Step 3 — Cancel N and 60**

$$T_d \cdot 2\pi = \frac{\Phi Z P}{A}\cdot I_a$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T_d$**

$$T_d = \frac{\Phi Z P\,I_a}{2\pi A}$$

$$\boxed{T_d = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a \quad \text{N·m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this method is preferred in KTU valuation:** The energy-conservation derivation is **2 lines shorter**, contains no machine geometry, and is essentially un-mark-down-able. Examiners give full marks if the power-balance identity $E_g I_a = T_d \omega$ is correctly stated.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of the Combined Speed Equation

### Statement
**Show that the speed of a DC motor in steady state is $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K\,\Phi}$, where $K = \dfrac{ZP}{60A}$ is the machine's EMF/torque constant.**

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Substitute the EMF equation into the voltage equation**

For a separately-excited or shunt motor:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

$$V = \frac{\Phi Z P N}{60 A} + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Isolate N**

$$V - I_a R_a = \frac{\Phi Z P N}{60 A}$$

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi} \cdot \frac{60 A}{Z P}$$

**Step 3 — Define the machine constant $K$**

$$K = \frac{Z P}{60 A} \quad \text{(V per Weber per rpm)}$$

Then:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K\,\Phi} \quad \text{r.p.m.}}$$

For a **shunt motor**, $V$ and $\Phi$ are nearly constant; for a **series motor**, $\Phi \propto I_a$ (before saturation), so:

$$N_{series} \propto \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{I_a}$$

which is the inverse-square-root characteristic — the basis of the **series motor's high starting torque**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:**
> A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt motor has 600 armature conductors and runs at 1000 r.p.m. when the armature current is 20 A. The armature resistance is $0.5\ \Omega$ and the field resistance is $250\ \Omega$. Compute: (a) the flux per pole, (b) the back-EMF, (c) the developed torque, (d) the shaft torque, given rotational losses of 500 W, and (e) the efficiency at this load.

### Solution

**Given:** $P=4$, $V=250$ V, $Z=600$, $N=1000$ r.p.m., $I_a=20$ A, $R_a=0.5\ \Omega$, $R_{sh}=250\ \Omega$, rotational loss = 500 W.

Assume **wave winding** (default unless otherwise stated): $A=2$.

**Part (a) — Flux per pole**

Back-EMF:
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - 20(0.5) = 250 - 10 = 240\ \text{V}$$

From EMF equation:
$$\Phi = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 A}{Z P N} = \frac{240 \times 60 \times 2}{600 \times 4 \times 1000}$$

$$\Phi = \frac{28800}{2400000} = 0.012\ \text{Wb} = 12\ \text{mWb}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi = 0.012\ \text{Wb} = 12\ \text{mWb}}$$

**Part (b) — Back-EMF** (already computed)
$$\boxed{E_b = 240\ \text{V}}$$

**Part (c) — Developed Torque**

$$T_d = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a = \frac{4}{2\pi \times 2}\times 0.012 \times 600 \times 20$$

$$T_d = \frac{4}{4\pi}\times 0.012 \times 600 \times 20 = \frac{1}{\pi}\times 144$$

$$T_d = \frac{144}{\pi} \approx 45.84\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_d \approx 45.84\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Part (d) — Shaft Torque**

Torque lost to rotational (friction + windage) losses:
$$T_{loss} = \frac{P_{rot}}{\omega} = \frac{P_{rot}}{2\pi N/60} = \frac{500 \times 60}{2\pi \times 1000} = \frac{30000}{2000\pi} = \frac{15}{\pi} \approx 4.775\ \text{N·m}$$

$$T_{sh} = T_d - T_{loss} = 45.84 - 4.775 = 41.07\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} \approx 41.07\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Part (e) — Efficiency**

Field current:
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1\ \text{A}$$

Line current:
$$I_L = I_a + I_{sh} = 20 + 1 = 21\ \text{A}$$

Input power:
$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 21 = 5250\ \text{W}$$

Output power:
$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N}{60}\cdot T_{sh} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60}\times 41.07 = 104.72 \times 41.07 \approx 4301\ \text{W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{4301}{5250} = 0.8192 \approx 81.92\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 81.92\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check via losses:**
> Armature Cu loss = $20^2 \times 0.5 = 200$ W
> Field Cu loss = $1^2 \times 250 = 250$ W
> Rotational loss = 500 W
> Total losses = $200 + 250 + 500 = 950$ W
> Output = $5250 - 950 = 4300$ W ✓ (matches)
> $\eta = 4300/5250 = 81.9\%$ ✓

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Four Coupled Steady-State Equations

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **causal coupling** between the four steady-state equations, with the two **input variables** (terminal voltage $V$ and mechanical speed $N$ or torque load) and the **two output variables** (armature current $I_a$ and developed torque $T_d$).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["SUPPLY VOLTAGE V"]:::inputnode
    A2["MECHANICAL SPEED N"]:::inputnode
    A3["FLUX PER POLE PHI"]:::inputnode
    A4["MACHINE CONSTANT K = ZP over 60A"]:::paramnode

    B1["EMF EQUATION: Eg = K times PHI times N"]:::eqnnode
    B2["VOLTAGE EQUATION: V = Eb + Ia Ra"]:::eqnnode
    B3["TORQUE EQUATION: Td = K times PHI times Ia times 60 over 2pi"]:::eqnnode
    B4["POWER EQUATION: Pd = Eg times Ia = omega times Td"]:::eqnnode

    C1["ARMATURE CURRENT Ia"]:::outputnode
    C2["DEVELOPED TORQUE Td"]:::outputnode
    C3["DEVELOPED POWER Pd"]:::outputnode
    C4["BACK EMF Eb"]:::outputnode

    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> C4
    A1 --> B2
    C4 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    A3 --> B3
    C1 --> B3
    B3 --> C2
    C4 --> B4
    C1 --> B4
    C2 --> B4
    B4 --> C3

    classDef inputnode fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b58900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputnode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef eqnnode fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef paramnode fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#654ea3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Generator vs. Motor Sign Convention

The same machine rotates in either direction; the steady-state equations are the same algebraically but differ in the **direction of power flow** and the **sign of the $I_a R_a$ drop**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEN["GENERATOR MODE: Mechanical to Electrical"]
        G1["PRIME MOVER"]:::mech
        G2["SHAFT TORQUE T_m"]:::mech
        G3["ARMATURE"]:::machine
        G4["Eg = K Phi N"]:::eqnnode
        G5["V = Eg minus Ia Ra"]:::eqnnode
        G6["LOAD"]:::elec
        G1 --> G2 --> G3
        G3 --> G4 --> G5 --> G6
    end

    subgraph MOT["MOTOR MODE: Electrical to Mechanical"]
        M1["SUPPLY V"]:::elec
        M2["V = Eb plus Ia Ra"]:::eqnnode
        M3["ARMATURE"]:::machine
        M4["Td = K Phi Ia 60 over 2pi"]:::eqnnode
        M5["SHAFT TORQUE T_sh"]:::mech
        M6["LOAD MACHINE"]:::mech
        M1 --> M2 --> M3
        M3 --> M4 --> M5 --> M6
    end

    classDef mech fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef machine fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef eqnnode fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 State Diagram — Generator-Motor Duality

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Motoring
    Motoring --> Generating : Prime mover overdrives the motor beyond no-load speed
    Generating --> Motoring : Reduce prime-mover torque below developed torque
    Motoring --> Motoring : V minus Ia Ra = Eb stable steady state
    Generating --> Generating : Eg minus Ia Ra = V stable steady state

    note right of Motoring
        V equals Eb plus Ia Ra
        Eb opposes V
        Power flows in
    end note

    note right of Generating
        V equals Eg minus Ia Ra
        Eg drives current
        Power flows out
    end note
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the EMF equation of a DC machine and explain the significance of each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The EMF generated in a DC machine armature is given by:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A}$$

where:
- **$\Phi$** = flux per pole, in Weber — determines the magnetic loading.
- **$Z$** = total number of armature conductors — set by the designer.
- **$N$** = speed in r.p.m. — the operating point input.
- **$P$** = number of poles — a design choice affecting frequency and torque.
- **$A$** = number of parallel paths in the winding ($A=P$ for lap, $A=2$ for wave).
- **60** = conversion factor from minutes to seconds (since $E_g$ must be in volts, and flux is in Wb).

The equation tells us that the generated EMF is **directly proportional to flux, speed, and number of conductors**, and **inversely proportional to parallel paths**. It is the cornerstone for sizing, voltage regulation, and speed-control design.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Why is the developed torque in a DC machine independent of speed? Derive or justify the expression.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The developed torque is given by:

$$T_d = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a$$

Notice that the **speed $N$ does not appear** in this expression. Torque depends **only on flux $\Phi$ and armature current $I_a$**, both of which are electrical quantities that do not change with the rotational speed (in steady state).

This can be justified by the energy-conservation identity $P_d = E_g I_a = \omega T_d$. Substituting $E_g \propto N$ and $\omega \propto N$, the speed cancels exactly, leaving $T_d$ as a function of $\Phi$ and $I_a$ only.

The physical origin is that the **force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is independent of its velocity** — only the EMF (and hence the power) scales with speed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Two questions are given; the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each contains two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** *Derive the EMF equation of a DC machine starting from Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. Clearly state the assumptions. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt motor draws 60 A from the supply. The armature resistance is $0.15\ \Omega$ and the shunt field resistance is $100\ \Omega$. The machine has 600 conductors wave-wound. If the rotational losses are 1500 W, find: (i) the back-EMF, (ii) the speed, given that the flux per pole is 25 mWb, and (iii) the shaft torque. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Statement of Faraday's Law**

The average EMF induced in a single conductor is:

$$e_{avg} = \frac{\text{Total flux cut per rev}}{\text{Time per rev}} = \frac{\Phi P}{60/N} = \frac{\Phi P N}{60}\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Conductors in series per parallel path**

$$\text{Conductors per path} = \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Terminal EMF = Sum of conductor EMFs in one path**

$$E_g = \frac{Z}{A}\times \frac{\Phi P N}{60} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}\ \text{V}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The flux is uniformly distributed and constant in space.
2. The armature is star-connected (all conductor EMFs in one path add constructively).
3. The machine is in **steady state** (no commutation transients).
4. The conductors are radial (force always tangential).

**Marking scheme (KTU):**
- 2 marks — Faraday's law correctly stated
- 2 marks — flux-per-revolution calculation
- 2 marks — final assembly with $Z/A$
- 1 mark — assumptions and units

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Given:** $P=4$, $V=250$ V, $I_L = 60$ A, $R_a = 0.15\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 100\ \Omega$, $Z=600$, wave-wound → $A=2$, $P_{rot}=1500$ W, $\Phi = 25$ mWb = $0.025$ Wb.

**Step 1 — Field current**
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Armature current**
$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 60 - 2.5 = 57.5\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 (i) — Back-EMF**
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - 57.5 \times 0.15 = 250 - 8.625 = 241.375\ \text{V}$$
$$\boxed{E_b = 241.375\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 4 (ii) — Speed**

From the EMF equation:
$$E_b = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}\implies N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 A}{\Phi Z P}$$

$$N = \frac{241.375 \times 60 \times 2}{0.025 \times 600 \times 4} = \frac{28965}{60} \approx 482.75\ \text{r.p.m.}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 482.75\ \text{r.p.m.}}$$

**Step 5 (iii) — Developed torque**
$$T_d = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a = \frac{4}{2\pi \times 2}\times 0.025 \times 600 \times 57.5$$

$$T_d = \frac{1}{\pi}\times 862.5 = \frac{862.5}{\pi} \approx 274.55\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Shaft torque**

First compute the developed power:
$$P_d = E_b \cdot I_a = 241.375 \times 57.5 = 13879.06\ \text{W}$$

Shaft power:
$$P_{sh} = P_d - P_{rot} = 13879.06 - 1500 = 12379.06\ \text{W}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 482.75}{60} = 50.54\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$T_{sh} = \frac{P_{sh}}{\omega} = \frac{12379.06}{50.54} \approx 244.93\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} \approx 244.93\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Marking scheme (KTU):**
- (i) 2 marks — $I_{sh}$ and $E_b$ correctly computed
- (ii) 3 marks — Rearranged EMF equation, substituted values, correct $N$
- (iii) 2 marks — Torque formula, $\omega$ conversion, subtraction of rotational loss

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** *Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque developed in a DC machine using the principle of energy conservation. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A 250 V DC series motor runs at 1000 r.p.m. while drawing 40 A. The total series resistance (armature + series field) is $0.5\ \Omega$. The machine is wave-wound with 800 conductors and 4 poles. The flux per pole at this load is 30 mWb. Calculate: (i) the back-EMF, (ii) the developed power and torque, and (iii) the efficiency, given that the rotational losses are 800 W. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Step 1 — Power balance identity**

In steady state, the **electrical power converted to mechanical power** is:

$$P_{developed} = E_g \cdot I_a = T_d \cdot \omega$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the EMF equation**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z P N}{60 A}$$

$$P_d = \frac{\Phi Z P N}{60 A}\cdot I_a$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the angular speed**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

$$T_d \cdot \omega = \frac{\Phi Z P N}{60 A}\cdot I_a$$

$$T_d \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\Phi Z P N}{60 A}\cdot I_a$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $N$ and 60**

$$T_d \cdot 2\pi = \frac{\Phi Z P}{A}\cdot I_a$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T_d$**

$$T_d = \frac{\Phi Z P\,I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a\ \text{N·m}$$

**Marking scheme (KTU):**
- 2 marks — Power balance identity correctly stated
- 2 marks — Substitution of $E_g$ and $\omega$
- 2 marks — Algebraic simplification
- 1 mark — Final expression and units

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:** $V=250$ V, $N=1000$ r.p.m., $I_a = 40$ A (series motor → $I_a = I_L$), $R_a + R_{se} = 0.5\ \Omega$, $Z=800$, $P=4$, $A=2$ (wave), $\Phi = 30$ mWb = $0.030$ Wb, $P_{rot}=800$ W.

**Step 1 (i) — Back-EMF**
$$E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 40 \times 0.5 = 250 - 20 = 230\ \text{V}$$
$$\boxed{E_b = 230\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 2 (ii) — Developed Power and Torque**

$$P_d = E_b \cdot I_a = 230 \times 40 = 9200\ \text{W}$$
$$\boxed{P_d = 9200\ \text{W} = 9.2\ \text{kW}}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = \frac{2000\pi}{60} = \frac{100\pi}{3} \approx 104.72\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$T_d = \frac{P_d}{\omega} = \frac{9200}{104.72} \approx 87.85\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_d \approx 87.85\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Step 3 (iii) — Efficiency**

Total losses = Cu loss + Rotational loss
$$P_{cu} = I_a^2(R_a + R_{se}) = 40^2 \times 0.5 = 800\ \text{W}$$
$$P_{rot} = 800\ \text{W (given)}$$
$$\sum P_{loss} = 800 + 800 = 1600\ \text{W}$$

Input power:
$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 40 = 10000\ \text{W}$$

Shaft output:
$$P_{sh} = P_{in} - P_{loss} = 10000 - 1600 = 8400\ \text{W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{sh}}{P_{in}} = \frac{8400}{10000} = 0.84 = 84\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 84\%}$$

**Marking scheme (KTU):**
- (i) 2 marks — Correct $E_b$ computation with full series resistance
- (ii) 3 marks — $P_d$ from $E_b I_a$, $\omega$ conversion, $T_d$ from $P_d/\omega$
- (iii) 2 marks — Proper loss accounting (note: series motor has no shunt field loss), final efficiency

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of $I_a R_a$** — Mixing generator and motor conventions is the #1 mark-loss reason. Always draw the equivalent circuit first.
> 2. **Wave vs. Lap** — Default to $A=2$ (wave) only if the problem does not specify lap. Read carefully: "wave-wound" or "with 4 parallel paths" are not the same.
> 3. **$\omega$ vs. $N$** — Using $N$ directly in $P = TN$ gives **watts if N is in r.p.s.** For r.p.m., always use $P = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\,T$. Common error: $P = TN$ without the $2\pi/60$ factor.
> 4. **Series motor: $I_L = I_a$** — Unlike a shunt motor, a series motor has no parallel field path. The full line current flows through the armature.
> 5. **Forgetting field copper loss in shunt motor** — A shunt motor has TWO copper losses: armature ($I_a^2 R_a$) and field ($V^2/R_{sh}$). Missing the field loss inflates efficiency by 2–4%.
> 6. **Rotational losses are shaft-side** — They are subtracted from **developed torque** to get **shaft torque**, not from the electrical input.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Steady-State Equations of DC Machines**

- ☐ **EMF Equation:** $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — voltage generated by the rotating armature.
- ☐ **Torque Equation:** $T_d = \dfrac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a$ — mechanical torque developed at the armature surface.
- ☐ **Voltage Equation (Motor):** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ — KVL around the armature loop; current flows in.
- ☐ **Voltage Equation (Generator):** $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ — KVL around the armature loop; current flows out.
- ☐ **Power Equation:** $P_d = E_g I_a = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\,T_d$ — energy conservation between electrical and mechanical ports.
- ☐ **Speed Equation:** $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$ with $K = ZP/(60A)$ — basis of all speed-control laws.
- ☐ **Lap winding:** $A = P$ (many parallel paths, low voltage, high current).
- ☐ **Wave winding:** $A = 2$ (few parallel paths, high voltage, low current).
- ☐ **Angular speed conversion:** $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ rad/s.
- ☐ **Shaft torque:** $T_{sh} = T_d - T_{rot}$ where $T_{rot} = P_{rot}/\omega$.
- ☐ **Efficiency:** $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} = (V I_a - \sum \text{Losses})/P_{in}$ — always verify by summing losses.
- ☐ **Sign rule of thumb:** In a **generator**, the induced EMF is the **source** ($E_g$ is positive and pushes current out). In a **motor**, the back-EMF is the **sink** ($E_b$ is positive and opposes $V$).
- ☐ **Independence principles:** EMF ∝ $N$ (linear); Torque independent of $N$ (depends only on $\Phi$ and $I_a$).
- ☐ **Maximum efficiency condition:** Variable loss (Cu) = Constant loss (iron + friction) — useful for design problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** If a problem gives you flux, conductors, poles, and speed → **always** start by computing $E_g$ or $E_b$ from the EMF equation. If it gives you current and flux → **always** start with the torque equation. If it gives you voltage, resistance, and current → **always** start with the KVL voltage equation. The four equations are your four keys — pick the one that uses the **given** data first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Numerical Problems – Module 1: DC Machines

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> *Numerical Problems* in the context of **DC Machines (Module 1)** refer to quantitative exercises that require the application of the fundamental equations governing the electromechanical energy conversion process in **DC Generators** and **DC Motors**. These problems bridge the gap between theoretical derivations (EMF Equation, Torque Equation) and practical engineering design, involving the calculation of parameters like induced EMF, electromagnetic torque, speed, efficiency, and the verification of machine characteristics under various loading conditions.

## 1.1 Intuitive Overview — Why Do We Solve Numerical Problems in DC Machines?

Imagine you are an electrical engineer tasked with designing a **DC motor** for an electric vehicle's power window. You have a battery rated at **12 V**, but your motor needs to spin a gear at a specific **RPM (speed)** and deliver a particular **torque** to lift the glass. How do you know how many turns of wire to wind on the armature, or how strong the magnetic field needs to be?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pump" Model of a DC Machine:**
> Think of a **DC Generator** as a *water pump pushed by hand*. The harder you push (mechanical input), the more water flows out per second (electrical output). The voltage produced is like the water pressure, and the current is like the flow rate. The **EMF Equation** (`E = kΦN`) tells you the "pressure" you create by pushing coils through the magnetic field. Conversely, a **DC Motor** is like a *turbine driven by water* — the more voltage you apply, the faster the turbine spins, and the more current flows, the more twisting force (torque) it produces.
>
> Numerical problems are essentially **engineering math** that lets you size the "pump" or "turbine" precisely for your application.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Module 1:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Flux (Φ)**: Measured in **Weber (Wb)** — typically between **0.005 Wb** to **0.05 Wb** for small/medium machines.
> - **Speed (N)**: Measured in **Revolutions Per Minute (RPM)**.
> - **Angular Velocity (ω)**: Measured in **radians per second (rad/s)** — conversion: $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$.
> - **EMF (E) / Voltage (V)**: Measured in **Volts (V)**.
> - **Current (Iₐ)**: Armature current in **Amperes (A)**.
> - **Torque (T)**: Measured in **Newton-Meters (N·m)**.
> - **Power (P)**: Measured in **Watts (W)** or **Kilowatts (kW)**.
> - **Efficiency (η)**: Dimensionless ratio, expressed in **percentage (%)**.

---

## 1.2 Categories of Numerical Problems in Module 1

KTU Module 1 numerical problems typically fall into the following categories:

1. **EMF Equation Problems** — Calculating generated EMF or finding unknown parameters (Φ, N, Z, P, A) in a DC Generator.
2. **Torque Equation Problems** — Computing shaft torque, armature torque, and lost torque in a DC Motor.
3. **Power Flow & Efficiency Problems** — Determining input/output power, copper losses, iron losses, and overall efficiency.
4. **Speed Regulation & Characteristics** — Calculating no-load speed, full-load speed, and speed regulation.
5. **Armature Reaction & Commutation Problems** — Computing demagnetizing/total number of conductors.
6. **Combined Problems** — Multi-part questions involving all of the above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:**
> As per the revised Bloom's Taxonomy alignment in NEP 2020, Module 1 numerical problems typically test the cognitive levels **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)**. You are expected to *apply* formulas to compute values and *analyze* machine performance under varying load conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation Philosophy (KTU Examiner's View):**
> Every numerical problem in DC Machines stems from **TWO master equations** — the **EMF Equation** and the **Torque Equation**. If you internalize these two and understand the relationships between electrical, mechanical, and magnetic domains, you can solve over **90% of Module 1 numerical problems**.

## 2.1 The EMF Equation (DC Generator Perspective)

When the armature of a DC machine rotates in a magnetic field, an EMF is induced in the conductors. The average EMF induced is given by:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

Where:
- $E_g$ = Generated EMF (in **Volts**)
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (in **Weber**)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of armature (in **RPM**)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths

> **Why this works:** Each conductor cuts the magnetic flux $\Phi$ a total of $\frac{P}{A}$ times per revolution, and completes $\frac{N}{60}$ revolutions per second. Multiplying by $Z$ gives total EMF per second, and dividing by 1 second gives the average EMF.

### Parallel Paths for Different Winding Types:
- **Lap Winding**: $A = P$
- **Wave Winding**: $A = 2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** For **wave winding**, $A = 2$ regardless of the number of poles. This means wave-wound machines have a *lower induced EMF per conductor* but *higher current capacity*. Lap-wound machines give *higher EMF* and are used for high-current applications.

---

## 2.2 The Torque Equation (DC Motor Perspective)

When a DC machine acts as a motor, current flows through the armature conductors placed in the magnetic field, producing a force and hence a torque:

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

Simplified form:

$$T_a = 0.159 \times \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} \quad (\text{in N·m})$$

Where:
- $T_a$ = Armature Torque (in **N·m**)
- $I_a$ = Armature current (in **Amperes**)

> **Physical Intuition:** Torque is the *mechanical analog of current flow*. Higher flux means stronger magnetic "grip", more conductors mean more force-producing wires, and higher current means more electrons experiencing that force.

---

## 2.3 The Power Equation (Bridge Between Electrical & Mechanical)

The electromagnetic (developed) power is the power converted from electrical to mechanical form (in motor) or vice versa (in generator):

$$P_{conv} = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$$

Where:
- $E_b$ = Back EMF (in motor) or Generated EMF $E_g$ (in generator) (in **V**)
- $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ = Angular velocity (in **rad/s**)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Key Condition |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | EMF Equation | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | $E_g$ in V, $\Phi$ in Wb, $N$ in RPM | All DC machines |
| 2 | Torque Equation | $T_a = 0.159 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $T_a$ in N·m, $I_a$ in A | DC Motor |
| 3 | Power Converted | $P_{m} = E_b \cdot I_a = \dfrac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$ | $P_{m}$ in W, $\omega$ in rad/s | Both modes |
| 4 | Shaft Torque | $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{friction}$ (loss torque) | $T_{sh}$ in N·m | Motor output |
| 5 | Input Power | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ | $V$ in V, $I_L$ in A | Motor input |
| 6 | Output Power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ | $P_{out}$ in W | Motor output |
| 7 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Both modes |
| 8 | Copper Loss | $P_{cu} = I_a^{2} R_a$ | $R_a$ in Ω | Armature |
| 9 | Speed (Motor) | $N = \dfrac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi Z P}$ | From EMF equation | Rearranged |
| 10 | Brush Contact Drop | $V_{brush} = 2 \times 1$ V (typical) | $V_{brush}$ in V | Practical loss |
| 11 | Wave Winding | $A = 2$ | Independent of $P$ | $m$-simplex |
| 12 | Lap Winding | $A = P$ | Equal to number of poles | $P$-simplex |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Unit Consistency is Worth 1 Mark:**
> Always ensure that flux is in **Weber (Wb)**, speed in **RPM** (convert to rev/s by dividing by 60 if needed), and that you use the **correct value of A** (2 for wave, P for lap). Many KTU students lose easy marks by mixing up units or winding types.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important in real engineering?**
> - **Electric Vehicle Design**: Determining torque and speed ratings of DC traction motors.
> - **Renewable Energy**: DC generators are precursors to modern wind turbine generators (AC, but with DC exciters).
> - **Industrial Automation**: Sizing DC servo motors for CNC machines and robotic arms.
> - **Battery-Powered Systems**: Estimating battery drain time using efficiency calculations.
> - **Power Plants**: Backup DC generators for emergency systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Solutions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section Objective:** This section contains **exhaustively detailed** step-by-step solutions to the most important types of numerical problems that appear in KTU University Examinations for Module 1. Every algebraic step and numerical evaluation is shown explicitly — no shortcuts, no skipped steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation (Conceptual Foundation)

**Starting Point:** Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction states that the EMF induced in a conductor is equal to the rate of change of flux linkage.

**Step 1:** Consider one conductor cutting the flux. In one revolution, the conductor cuts a flux equal to:

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = \Phi \times P$$

This is because there are $P$ poles, and as the conductor moves past each pole, it cuts the flux $\Phi$ once.

**Step 2:** For a **simplex wave-wound** machine, the number of parallel paths is $A = 2$, so the number of conductors in series per parallel path is $\frac{Z}{A} = \frac{Z}{2}$.

**Step 3:** Total flux cut per revolution by all conductors in series in one parallel path:

$$\text{Flux cut} = \Phi \times P \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 4:** Time taken for one revolution (in seconds) is:

$$t = \frac{60}{N}$$

**Step 5:** Average EMF per parallel path (and hence the terminal voltage of the machine, ignoring voltage drops):

$$E_g = \frac{\text{Flux cut}}{\text{Time per revolution}} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the master EMF equation. The same equation is used for both DC generators and DC motors, but in a motor, the induced EMF is called **Back EMF ($E_b$)** and it opposes the applied voltage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation (Conceptual Foundation)

**Starting Point:** Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is given by the Lorentz force law:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$

where $B$ is the flux density, $I$ is the current, and $l$ is the length of the conductor.

**Step 1:** The force on a single conductor is:

$$F = B \cdot I_c \cdot l$$

where $I_c = \frac{I_a}{A}$ is the current per conductor.

**Step 2:** Torque produced by one conductor is $F \times r$, where $r$ is the radius of the armature. For a multi-pole machine, the effective radius is $\frac{D}{2}$, but the torque derivation can be simplified by noting that the total torque is proportional to $F \times \frac{D}{2}$ summed over all conductors.

**Step 3:** Summing over all $Z$ conductors and using the relationship between flux density and flux:

$$B = \frac{\Phi}{A_{pole}} = \frac{\Phi P}{2\pi r l}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting and simplifying (after substantial algebra, which we skip in this conceptual view to focus on numericals):

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

**Step 5:** The constant $\frac{1}{2\pi} \approx 0.1591$, giving the practical form:

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{N·m}$$

---

## 3.3 Comprehensive Numerical Solutions (KTU Exam Pattern)

### 📘 Problem 1: EMF Equation (Lap-Wound DC Generator)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**
> *A 4-pole, lap-wound DC generator has 500 armature conductors and a flux per pole of 0.02 Wb. It rotates at 1500 RPM. Calculate the EMF generated. If the machine is rewound as wave-wound with the same conductors, what would be the new EMF at the same speed and flux?*

**Given Data:**
- Number of poles, $P = 4$
- Total armature conductors, $Z = 500$
- Flux per pole, $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb
- Speed, $N = 1500$ RPM

**Step 1: Identify the winding type and parallel paths.**

For lap winding: $A = P = 4$

**Step 2: Apply the EMF equation for lap winding.**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot P} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60}$$

(Notice how $P$ cancels out for lap winding!)

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{0.02 \times 500 \times 1500}{60}$$

**Step 4: Compute numerator first.**

$$0.02 \times 500 = 10$$

$$10 \times 1500 = 15000$$

**Step 5: Divide by 60.**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{15000}{60} = 250 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6: Rewind as wave winding. Now $A = 2$.**

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} = \frac{0.02 \times 500 \times 1500 \times 4}{60 \times 2}$$

**Step 7: Compute numerator.**

$$0.02 \times 500 = 10$$
$$10 \times 1500 = 15000$$
$$15000 \times 4 = 60000$$

**Step 8: Compute denominator.**

$$60 \times 2 = 120$$

**Step 9: Final division.**

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \frac{60000}{120} = 500 \text{ V}$$

**✅ Final Answers:**
- Lap-wound EMF: $E_g = 250$ V
- Wave-wound EMF: $E_g = 500$ V

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Learning:** Wave winding produces **twice the EMF** of lap winding (for a 4-pole machine), because the same conductors are now arranged in fewer parallel paths (2 instead of 4), putting more conductors in series per path.

---

### 📘 Problem 2: Torque and Power in a DC Motor

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> *A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω and a field winding resistance of 250 Ω. The motor draws a total current of 30 A at full load. The armature has 1000 conductors, 4 poles, is wave-wound, and runs at 1000 RPM. Calculate: (a) the flux per pole, (b) the shaft torque, and (c) the efficiency, assuming mechanical and iron losses total 400 W.*

**Given Data:**
- Terminal voltage, $V = 250$ V
- Armature resistance, $R_a = 0.5$ Ω
- Field resistance, $R_{sh} = 250$ Ω
- Line current, $I_L = 30$ A
- Conductors, $Z = 1000$
- Poles, $P = 4$ (wave wound, so $A = 2$)
- Speed, $N = 1000$ RPM
- Mechanical + Iron losses = 400 W

---

**Part (a): Flux per pole**

**Step 1: Calculate shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Calculate armature current using KCL.**

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 30 - 1 = 29 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate back EMF using the motor voltage equation.**

For a DC motor: $V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$

$$E_b = V - I_a \cdot R_a = 250 - (29 \times 0.5) = 250 - 14.5 = 235.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Apply the EMF equation and solve for $\Phi$.**

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$$

Rearranging for $\Phi$:

$$\Phi = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{Z \cdot N \cdot P}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the values.**

$$\Phi = \frac{235.5 \times 60 \times 2}{1000 \times 1000 \times 4}$$

**Step 6: Compute numerator.**

$$235.5 \times 60 = 14130$$
$$14130 \times 2 = 28260$$

**Step 7: Compute denominator.**

$$1000 \times 1000 = 10^6$$
$$10^6 \times 4 = 4 \times 10^6$$

**Step 8: Final division.**

$$\Phi = \frac{28260}{4000000} = 7.065 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi \approx 7.07 \text{ mWb}}$$

---

**Part (b): Shaft Torque**

**Step 1: Calculate the electromagnetic (armature) torque.**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \times 7.065 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 29 \times \frac{4}{2}$$

**Step 3: Simplify $\frac{P}{A}$.**

$$\frac{4}{2} = 2$$

**Step 4: Compute step-by-step.**

$$0.1591 \times 7.065 \times 10^{-3} = 1.124 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$1.124 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 1.124$$
$$1.124 \times 29 = 32.596$$
$$32.596 \times 2 = 65.19 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the loss torque (from mechanical and iron losses).**

$$T_{loss} = \frac{P_{loss}}{\omega} = \frac{P_{loss}}{\frac{2\pi N}{60}} = \frac{60 \cdot P_{loss}}{2\pi N}$$

**Step 6: Substitute and compute.**

$$T_{loss} = \frac{60 \times 400}{2\pi \times 1000} = \frac{24000}{6283.19} = 3.82 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 7: Calculate shaft torque.**

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss} = 65.19 - 3.82 = 61.37 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} \approx 61.37 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

**Part (c): Efficiency**

**Step 1: Calculate input power.**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 30 = 7500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Calculate output (shaft) power.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000 \times 61.37}{60}$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator.**

$$2\pi \times 1000 = 6283.19$$
$$6283.19 \times 61.37 = 385605.3 \text{ (approx.)}$$

**Step 4: Divide by 60.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{385605.3}{60} = 6426.76 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Calculate efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{6426.76}{7500} \times 100 = 85.69\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 85.7\%}$$

---

### 📘 Problem 3: Speed Regulation (DC Shunt Motor)

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern]**
> *A DC shunt motor runs at 1500 RPM on no load. At full load, the armature reaction weakens the flux by 5% and the armature voltage drop is 8 V. If the supply voltage is 220 V and the no-load back EMF is 212 V, calculate the full-load speed.*

**Given Data:**
- No-load speed, $N_0 = 1500$ RPM
- No-load back EMF, $E_{b0} = 212$ V
- Supply voltage, $V = 220$ V
- Armature drop at full load, $I_a R_a = 8$ V
- Flux reduction due to armature reaction, $\Phi_{FL} = 0.95 \cdot \Phi_{NL}$

**Step 1: Calculate full-load back EMF.**

$$E_{b,FL} = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 8 = 212 \text{ V}$$

(Interestingly, in this case the full-load $E_b$ equals the no-load $E_b$ value.)

**Step 2: Apply the speed formula. We know that:**

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P}$$

This means $N \propto \frac{E_b}{\Phi}$.

**Step 3: Form the ratio of full-load to no-load speed.**

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{N_{NL}} = \frac{E_{b,FL} / \Phi_{FL}}{E_{b,NL} / \Phi_{NL}} = \frac{E_{b,FL}}{E_{b,NL}} \cdot \frac{\Phi_{NL}}{\Phi_{FL}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the values.**

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{1500} = \frac{212}{212} \cdot \frac{1}{0.95}$$

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{1500} = 1 \times \frac{1}{0.95} = 1.0526$$

**Step 5: Solve for $N_{FL}$.**

$$N_{FL} = 1500 \times 1.0526 = 1578.9 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 6: Calculate speed regulation.**

$$\text{Regulation} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{1500 - 1578.9}{1578.9} \times 100 = -4.99\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative speed regulation** here means the motor runs *faster* at full load than at no load. This is unusual for a shunt motor and happens because the **armature reaction weakening effect (5%) overcompensates** for the small armature drop.

---

### 📘 Problem 4: Armature Reaction and Demagnetizing Conductors

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Pattern]**
> *A 4-pole, wave-wound DC generator has 800 armature conductors. The brushes are shifted by 4 mechanical degrees from the geometric neutral axis. Calculate the number of demagnetizing and cross-magnetizing armature conductors per pole.*

**Given Data:**
- Poles, $P = 4$
- Conductors, $Z = 800$ (wave-wound, $A = 2$)
- Brush shift, $\theta_{m} = 4°$ (mechanical)

**Step 1: Convert mechanical degrees to electrical degrees.**

$$\theta_{e} = \frac{P}{2} \times \theta_m = \frac{4}{2} \times 4° = 2 \times 4° = 8°$$

**Step 2: Calculate the angular span of demagnetizing zone.**

The demagnetizing zone is the angular range that lies *behind* the new brush position by the same angle $\theta_e$. So the demagnetizing zone spans $2\theta_e$ in electrical degrees:

$$\text{Demag zone} = 2\theta_e = 2 \times 8° = 16°$$

**Step 3: Calculate demagnetizing conductors per pole.**

$$\text{Conductors per pole (electrical)} = \frac{Z}{2P} = \frac{800}{8} = 100 \text{ conductors/pole}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the fraction of demagnetizing zone.**

$$\text{Frac} = \frac{2\theta_e}{180°} = \frac{16°}{180°} = 0.0889$$

**Step 5: Number of demagnetizing conductors per pole.**

$$Z_d = 0.0889 \times 100 = 8.89 \approx 9 \text{ conductors/pole}$$

**Step 6: Total demagnetizing conductors.**

$$Z_{d,\text{total}} = Z_d \times P = 9 \times 4 = 36 \text{ conductors}$$

**Step 7: Cross-magnetizing conductors per pole.**

$$Z_c = 100 - 9 = 91 \text{ conductors/pole}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{d,\text{total}} = 36, \quad Z_c = 91 \text{ per pole}}$$

---

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        VIN[Input Electrical Power Pin = V times IL]
    end

        PSH[Shunt Field Copper Loss Ish squared Rsh]

```

## 4.3 Master Formula Dependency Graph (EMF ↔ Torque ↔ Speed ↔ Power)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EMF[EMF Equation Eg = PhiZN P divided by 60A]
    TORQ[Torque Equation Ta = 0.1591 PhiZIa P divided by A]

    SPEED[Speed Equation N = 60A Eb divided by PhiZP]
    LAPL[Lap: A = P]
    WAVL[Wave: A = 2]
    REG[Regulation = NNL minus NFL divided by NFL times 100]
    EFF[Eff = Pout divided by Pin times 100]
    LAPL --> EMF
    LAPL --> TORQ
    WAVL --> EMF
    WAVL --> TORQ

    POW --> EFF

```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Bank Notice:** All questions in this section follow the **exact pattern of KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations (ESE)**. The Part A questions are 3-mark direct-concept questions, and Part B questions are 14-mark internal-choice problems with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Model solutions with complete step-by-step working are provided.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**State the EMF equation of a DC generator. Explain the significance of the term "number of parallel paths" (A) in the equation.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> The EMF equation of a DC generator is:
>
> $$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$
>
> where $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $Z$ = total armature conductors, $N$ = speed (RPM), $P$ = number of poles, and $A$ = number of parallel paths.
>
> **Significance of A (1 Mark):**
> The number of parallel paths depends on the winding type: $A = P$ for lap winding and $A = 2$ for wave winding. It determines how the armature conductors are interconnected and directly affects both the **generated EMF** and the **current capacity** of the machine. **Wave winding** (A=2) produces higher EMF with lower current rating, while **lap winding** (A=P) produces lower EMF with higher current rating.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define "armature torque" and "shaft torque" of a DC motor. How are they related?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> - **Armature Torque ($T_a$):** The torque developed by the armature conductors due to the interaction of armature current and magnetic flux. It is the gross torque produced inside the machine. (1.5 Marks)
>
> $$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{N·m}$$
>
> - **Shaft Torque ($T_{sh}$):** The net torque available at the motor shaft for doing useful mechanical work, after subtracting the losses due to friction, windage, and iron losses. (1.5 Marks)
>
> **Relationship:** $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss}$, where $T_{loss}$ is the torque lost to mechanical and iron losses.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
> *A 250 V, 4-pole, lap-wound DC shunt motor has 800 armature conductors. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω and the field resistance is 125 Ω. The motor takes a total current of 60 A at full load. The flux per pole is 30 mWb, and the mechanical and iron losses total 800 W.*

**Calculate the following:**
- **(a)** The back EMF, armature current, and speed at full load. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** The shaft torque, output power, and efficiency at full load. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $V = 250$ V, $P = 4$, lap-wound so $A = 4$
- $Z = 800$, $R_a = 0.2$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 125$ Ω
- $I_L = 60$ A, $\Phi = 0.03$ Wb
- Mechanical + Iron losses = 800 W

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{125} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Armature current.**

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 60 - 2 = 58 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Back EMF.**

$$E_b = V - I_a \cdot R_a = 250 - (58 \times 0.2) = 250 - 11.6 = 238.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Speed using EMF equation (rearranged for N).**

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P} = \frac{238.4 \times 60 \times 4}{0.03 \times 800 \times 4}$$

**Step 5: Numerator.**

$$238.4 \times 60 = 14304$$
$$14304 \times 4 = 57216$$

**Step 6: Denominator.**

$$0.03 \times 800 = 24$$
$$24 \times 4 = 96$$

**Step 7: Division.**

$$N = \frac{57216}{96} = 596 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 238.4 \text{ V}, \quad I_a = 58 \text{ A}, \quad N = 596 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Armature torque.**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} = 0.1591 \times 0.03 \times 800 \times 58 \times \frac{4}{4}$$

**Step 2: Simplification (P/A = 1 for lap winding).**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \times 0.03 \times 800 \times 58 \times 1$$

**Step 3: Compute step-by-step.**

$$0.1591 \times 0.03 = 4.773 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$4.773 \times 10^{-3} \times 800 = 3.8184$$
$$3.8184 \times 58 = 221.47 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Loss torque from mechanical and iron losses.**

$$T_{loss} = \frac{60 \times P_{loss}}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 800}{2\pi \times 596} = \frac{48000}{3744.7} = 12.82 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Shaft torque.**

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss} = 221.47 - 12.82 = 208.65 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6: Output power.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 596 \times 208.65}{60}$$

**Step 7: Numerator computation.**

$$2\pi \times 596 = 3744.74$$
$$3744.74 \times 208.65 = 781415.5$$

**Step 8: Division.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{781415.5}{60} = 13023.6 \text{ W} \approx 13.02 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 9: Input power.**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 60 = 15000 \text{ W} = 15 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 10: Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{13023.6}{15000} \times 100 = 86.82\%$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} = 208.65 \text{ N·m}, \quad P_{out} = 13.02 \text{ kW}, \quad \eta = 86.82\%}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**
> *A 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt generator has 600 armature conductors. The armature and field resistances are 0.3 Ω and 200 Ω, respectively. The flux per pole is 0.025 Wb, and the generator delivers a load current of 100 A at 250 V.*

**Calculate the following:**
- **(a)** The generated EMF and the speed of the generator. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** The armature copper loss, total losses, and efficiency of the generator. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $P = 4$, wave-wound, $A = 2$
- $Z = 600$, $R_a = 0.3$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 200$ Ω
- $\Phi = 0.025$ Wb
- Load current $I_L = 100$ A, terminal voltage $V = 250$ V

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{200} = 1.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Calculate armature current (same as load current in a generator when $I_L$ is the line current; here, the load is connected to the terminals, so $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$).**

$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 1.25 = 101.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the voltage drop in the armature.**

$$I_a \cdot R_a = 101.25 \times 0.3 = 30.375 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the generated EMF (DC generator voltage equation: $E_g = V + I_a R_a + V_{brush}$).**

Assuming brush contact drop of 2 V (standard assumption: 1 V per brush):

$$E_g = 250 + 30.375 + 2 = 282.375 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Calculate speed from the EMF equation.**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$$

$$N = \frac{E_g \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P} = \frac{282.375 \times 60 \times 2}{0.025 \times 600 \times 4}$$

**Step 6: Numerator.**

$$282.375 \times 60 = 16942.5$$
$$16942.5 \times 2 = 33885$$

**Step 7: Denominator.**

$$0.025 \times 600 = 15$$
$$15 \times 4 = 60$$

**Step 8: Division.**

$$N = \frac{33885}{60} = 564.75 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{E_g = 282.375 \text{ V}, \quad N = 564.75 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Armature copper loss.**

$$P_{cu,a} = I_a^{2} \cdot R_a = (101.25)^2 \times 0.3$$

**Step 2: Compute $(101.25)^2$.**

$$(101.25)^2 = 10251.5625$$

**Step 3: Multiply by $R_a$.**

$$P_{cu,a} = 10251.5625 \times 0.3 = 3075.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Field copper loss.**

$$P_{cu,sh} = I_{sh}^{2} \cdot R_{sh} = (1.25)^2 \times 200 = 1.5625 \times 200 = 312.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Brush contact loss (assuming 2 V total drop and $I_a$ flowing through brushes).**

$$P_{brush} = V_{brush} \cdot I_a = 2 \times 101.25 = 202.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6: Total electrical losses (assuming no mechanical/iron loss data given, only electrical losses).**

$$P_{loss,total} = P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,sh} + P_{brush} = 3075.47 + 312.5 + 202.5 = 3590.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7: Output power delivered to the load.**

$$P_{out} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 100 = 25000 \text{ W} = 25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8: Input mechanical power.**

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss,total} = 25000 + 3590.47 = 28590.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 9: Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{25000}{28590.47} \times 100 = 87.44\%$$

$$\boxed{P_{cu,a} = 3075.47 \text{ W}, \quad P_{loss,total} = 3590.47 \text{ W}, \quad \eta = 87.44\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
>
> 1. **Confusing A = P (lap) vs A = 2 (wave):** This single mistake can lead to a 50% error in your EMF and Torque calculations. Always re-read the problem statement for the winding type.
> 2. **Forgetting the field current in $I_a$ calculation:** In a **motor**, $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$. In a **generator**, $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the brush contact drop:** Many students assume $E_g = V$ in generators and $E_b = V$ in motors, ignoring the $I_a R_a$ drop. You will lose 1-2 marks if this is missing.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing RPM and rad/s for angular velocity. KTU expects RPM in the EMF equation directly, and rad/s in the power equation $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$.
> 5. **Shaft vs. Armature Torque confusion:** The question may ask for "shaft torque" but students compute "armature torque" and skip the loss torque subtraction.
> 6. **Power equation sign errors:** Output = Input − Losses. Always check the direction of energy flow before writing the equation.
> 7. **No unit on final answer:** A numerical answer without units (V, A, RPM, N·m, W, %) is considered incomplete and will lose 0.5–1 mark per instance.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 Numerical Problems**

- ✅ The **EMF Equation** $E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ is the **master equation** for all DC machine voltage calculations.
- ✅ For **Lap Winding**: $A = P$. For **Wave Winding**: $A = 2$ (independent of poles).
- ✅ The **Torque Equation** $T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$ applies to **motors only**; in generators, this represents the mechanical torque required.
- ✅ The **Voltage Equation**:
   - Motor: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (back EMF opposes applied voltage)
   - Generator: $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ (generated EMF exceeds terminal voltage)
- ✅ The **Current Equation**:
   - Motor: $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$
   - Generator: $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$
- ✅ The **Power Conversion Equation**: $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \omega = \frac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$.
- ✅ **Shaft Torque** = Armature Torque − Loss Torque.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$, always between 0% and 100%.
- ✅ **Speed Regulation** $= \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$. For shunt motors, it's typically +1% to +5% (positive).
- ✅ In **armature reaction** problems: convert mechanical degrees to electrical degrees using $\theta_e = \frac{P}{2} \theta_m$.
- ✅ **Demagnetizing conductors per pole** $= \frac{Z}{2P} \cdot \frac{2\theta_e}{180°}$.
- ✅ **Cross-magnetizing conductors per pole** = (Total conductors per pole) − (Demagnetizing conductors per pole).
- ✅ Always carry **units** through your calculation: Wb, RPM, V, A, Ω, N·m, W, %.
- ✅ **Conversion check**: $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ — if you forget to convert, you'll get errors in power/torque calculations.
- ✅ **Sanity check your answer**: EMF should be in tens to hundreds of volts; torque in single to hundreds of N·m; efficiency typically 75–95%.

---

topics.title: DC Shunt Generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** DC Shunt Generator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **DC Shunt Generator** is a self-excited direct current (DC) generator in which the **field winding** is connected in **parallel (shunt)** with the **armature winding**, and the combination is connected across the load terminals. The term "shunt" (derived from the German/electrical terminology for "parallel branch") indicates that the field circuit provides a parallel path to the armature current. Because the field winding is made of many turns of thin wire with high resistance, it draws only a small fraction of the total current, leaving the bulk of the induced current to be delivered to the external load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "A DC shunt generator is a self-excited DC machine in which the field winding, possessing a high resistance and a large number of turns, is connected in parallel with the armature winding to provide a constant excitation flux that is largely independent of the load current."

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The "Parallel River" Intuition

Imagine a large water reservoir (the **armature** with induced EMF) that feeds water into a pipeline system supplying a city. The reservoir also has a small side-channel (the **shunt field winding**) that draws only a trickle of water to operate a waterwheel regulator, which in turn controls the main water gate. 

- The **trickle of water** through the side channel represents the small **shunt field current (Ish)**.
- The **main water flow** to the city represents the **load current (IL)**.
- The **waterwheel regulator** represents the **field flux (Φ)** which governs the EMF generated.

Because the side channel is narrow and restrictive (high resistance), the small current it carries barely changes whether the city is using 1 liter or 1000 liters of water per second. This is exactly why a DC shunt generator is renowned for its **near-constant terminal voltage** over a wide range of loads.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Rated Speeds** for DC shunt generators: **1000 RPM, 1500 RPM, 3000 RPM** (synchronous-equivalent industrial speeds).
- **Standard Field Winding Resistance (Rsh)**: Typically **50 Ω to 500 Ω** depending on machine rating.
- **Specific Electric Loading (ac)**: Range **10,000 to 50,000 AT/m**.
- **Specific Magnetic Loading (Bav)**: Range **0.4 T to 0.7 T** for standard machines.
- **Voltage Regulation (Shunt)**: Typically **8% to 15%** (relatively poor compared to compound generators).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Shunt" and not "Series"?** In a series generator, the entire load current flows through the field winding, causing the flux to vary wildly with load. In a shunt generator, the field current depends on the terminal voltage (which remains fairly stable), making it an essentially **constant-flux** machine.

### 1.4 Construction Snapshot

| Component | Function | Material |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Yoke** | Outer frame, magnetic path | Cast iron / cast steel |
| **Pole Core & Pole Shoe** | Holds field winding, distributes flux | Laminated silicon steel |
| **Field Winding (Shunt)** | Produces main flux Φ | Many turns of thin enameled copper wire |
| **Armature Core** | Houses armature winding, carries flux | Laminated silicon steel |
| **Armature Winding** | Where EMF is induced | Thick copper conductors in slots |
| **Commutator** | Mechanical rectifier (AC to DC) | Hard-drawn copper segments |
| **Brushes** | Current collection | Carbon / Graphite |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle: The "Why" Behind the Shunt

The DC shunt generator works on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. When the armature is rotated by a prime mover (motor, turbine, engine), the conductors on the armature cut the magnetic flux produced by the shunt field winding, and an EMF is induced.

#### Step-by-Step Logic Flow:

1. **Prime Mover Action**: External mechanical energy (e.g., from a 3-phase induction motor) rotates the armature at a rated speed **N** (in RPM).
2. **Residual Magnetism**: A small residual flux (≈ 2–5% of full flux) exists in the pole cores even without excitation — this is **essential** for self-excitation to begin.
3. **Initial EMF Build-up**: The residual flux induces a small EMF in the armature. A small current **Ish** flows through the shunt field because the field circuit is closed across the armature terminals.
4. **Cumulative Growth**: This small current increases the flux, which increases the EMF, which further increases Ish — a positive feedback loop continues until the magnetization curve and the field circuit resistance line intersect at a stable operating point.
5. **Steady-State Operation**: At equilibrium, the field flux **Φ** is established, and the induced EMF **Eg** is given by the standard machine EMF equation.

### 2.2 The Armature Reaction Phenomenon

When the generator supplies load, the armature conductors carry current. This current produces its own magnetic field (**armature flux, Φa**) which **distorts and weakens** the main field flux produced by the shunt winding. This effect is called **Armature Reaction**.

- **Cross-Magnetizing Effect**: Shifts the resultant flux density waveform (MNA - Magnetic Neutral Axis) in the direction of rotation for a generator.
- **Demagnetizing Effect**: Reduces the net main flux, thereby reducing the induced EMF.
- **Compensating Devices**: **Interpoles (commutating poles)** and **compensating windings** are used to neutralize the effect of armature reaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **shunt generator**, the demagnetizing effect of armature reaction causes a **drop in terminal voltage** with increasing load, even at constant speed. This is the primary reason why shunt generators are considered to have **drooping external characteristics**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$ | **Generated EMF Equation** (fundamental) | $E_g$ in Volts, $\Phi$ in Wb, $N$ in RPM |
| 2 | $E_{g0} = \dfrac{\Phi Z P}{60 \, A}$ | EMF per RPM (constant for a given machine) | Volts per RPM |
| 3 | $V_t = E_g - I_a R_a$ | **Terminal Voltage Equation** (Generator) | $V_t$ in Volts |
| 4 | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | **Armature Current** (sum of load + shunt) | All in Amperes |
| 5 | $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V_t}{R_{sh}}$ | **Shunt Field Current** | Amperes (small, typically 1–5% of $I_a$) |
| 6 | $\text{VR} = \dfrac{E_{g0} - V_{t,\text{full load}}}{V_{t,\text{full load}}} \times 100\%$ | **Voltage Regulation** (in %) | Always positive for shunt gen |
| 7 | $P_{\text{conv}} = E_g \cdot I_a$ | **Electromagnetic / Converted Power** | Watts |
| 8 | $P_{\text{out}} = V_t \cdot I_L$ | **Output Power to Load** | Watts |
| 9 | $P_{\text{copper loss}} = I_a^2 R_a + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$ | Total copper losses | Watts |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_t I_L}{V_t I_L + P_{\text{losses}}} \times 100\%$ | **Efficiency** | % |
| 11 | $E_g = k \Phi \omega$ | **Modern EMF Equation** | $k$ = machine constant, $\omega$ in rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Clarification on $Z$, $P$, $A$ in the EMF Equation:**
> - $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
> - $P$ = Number of poles
> - $A$ = Number of parallel paths in armature
> - For **wave winding**: $A = 2$
> - For **lap winding**: $A = P$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

DC shunt generators, despite being increasingly replaced by AC generators with solid-state rectifiers, are still used in:

1. **Battery Charging Stations** — The nearly constant voltage output is ideal for charging lead-acid batteries without sophisticated regulation.
2. **DC Motor-Generator (MG) Sets** — Historically used as voltage regulators in Ward-Leonard speed control systems for elevators, steel rolling mills, and paper machines.
3. **Electrolysis and Electroplating Plants** — Where stable DC voltage is critical.
4. **Excitation Supply for Synchronous Machines** — Small DC shunt generators (called **exciters**) supply field current to large alternators in power stations.
5. **Laboratory DC Power Supplies** — Educational and research applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Common Pitfall**: Students often forget that in a **shunt generator**, the field winding is connected across the armature terminals (i.e., across the **load**, NOT across an external source). The energy for the field comes from the **generated EMF itself** — this is what makes it a **self-excited** machine.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation ($E_g$)

**Statement to Prove:**
The EMF induced in a DC shunt generator is given by:
$$E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$$

**Derivation:**

Let the following quantities be defined for a DC machine:

- $\Phi$ = Average useful flux per pole, in Webers (Wb).
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors.
- $P$ = Number of magnetic poles.
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature winding.
- $N$ = Speed of the armature in revolutions per minute (RPM).

**Step 1: Flux Cut Per Revolution**

In one complete revolution of the armature, each conductor cuts the flux established under **all** the $P$ poles. Therefore, the flux cut by **one conductor in one revolution** is:

$$\text{Flux per conductor per revolution} = \Phi \times P \quad \text{(in Webers)}$$

**Step 2: Time Required for One Revolution**

If the armature rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the time taken for **one revolution** is:

$$T = \dfrac{60}{N} \quad \text{(in seconds)}$$

**Step 3: Average EMF Induced in One Conductor**

By Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, the average EMF induced in a single conductor is the rate of change of flux linkage:

$$e_{\text{conductor}} = \dfrac{\text{Flux cut per revolution}}{\text{Time for one revolution}} = \dfrac{\Phi P}{\left(\dfrac{60}{N}\right)} = \dfrac{\Phi P N}{60}$$

The unit is in Volts, assuming $\Phi$ is in Wb.

**Step 4: EMF Induced in One Parallel Path**

The total number of armature conductors $Z$ is distributed among $A$ parallel paths. The number of conductors **in series per parallel path** is:

$$\text{Conductors in series per path} = \dfrac{Z}{A}$$

Since these $\dfrac{Z}{A}$ conductors are in series within a single path, the total EMF per parallel path (which is the total generated EMF $E_g$, since all parallel paths have the same voltage) is:

$$E_g = e_{\text{conductor}} \times \dfrac{Z}{A} = \left(\dfrac{\Phi P N}{60}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{Z}{A}\right)$$

**Step 5: Final Formulation**

Rearranging, we obtain the standard KTU EMF equation:

$$E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A} \quad \text{(Volts)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by Dimensional Analysis:**
> $\dfrac{[\text{Wb}] \cdot [\text{dimensionless}] \cdot [\text{RPM}] \cdot [\text{dimensionless}]}{[\text{dimensionless}] \cdot [\text{seconds}]} = \dfrac{\text{Wb}}{\text{s}} = \text{Volts}$ ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Terminal Voltage Equation

**Starting Point: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)**

Apply KVL around the closed loop consisting of the armature EMF source, the armature resistance $R_a$, and the terminal voltage $V_t$:

$$E_g = V_t + I_a R_a$$

Rearranging to express the terminal voltage:

$$V_t = E_g - I_a R_a \quad \text{(Volts)}$$

**Substituting $E_g$ from the EMF equation:**

$$V_t = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A} - I_a R_a$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Armature Current Relation

By **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the top node of the generator (where the armature, shunt field, and load meet):

$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$$

The shunt field current is determined by Ohm's Law applied to the shunt field winding (which is connected across the terminals):

$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V_t}{R_{sh}}$$

Therefore, the armature current becomes:

$$I_a = I_L + \dfrac{V_t}{R_{sh}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Voltage Regulation

**Definition:** Voltage Regulation is the change in terminal voltage from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of full-load voltage.

At **no-load**, $I_L = 0$, so $I_a = I_{sh}$, and the armature drop $I_a R_a$ is negligible. Thus:

$$E_{g0} \approx V_{t,\text{no load}}$$

At **full-load**, the terminal voltage drops to $V_{t,\text{FL}}$ due to armature reaction and $I_a R_a$ drop.

Therefore, Voltage Regulation is:

$$\text{VR} = \dfrac{V_{t,\text{NL}} - V_{t,\text{FL}}}{V_{t,\text{FL}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{VR} = \dfrac{E_{g0} - V_{t,\text{FL}}}{V_{t,\text{FL}}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention**: Always express VR as a **positive percentage** in a generator context. A positive VR indicates drooping characteristics.

---

### 3.5 Power Flow and Efficiency Analysis

The total power flow in a DC shunt generator is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{mechanical input}} &= P_{\text{conv}} + P_{\text{mech losses}} + P_{\text{iron losses}} \\
P_{\text{conv}} &= E_g \cdot I_a \\
P_{\text{out}} &= V_t \cdot I_L \\
P_{\text{copper loss (armature)}} &= I_a^2 R_a \\
P_{\text{copper loss (shunt)}} &= I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = \dfrac{V_t^2}{R_{sh}} \\
P_{\text{stray loss}} &\approx 1\% \text{ of } P_{\text{out}} \text{ (KTU standard assumption)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The overall efficiency is:

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{input}}} = \dfrac{V_t I_L}{V_t I_L + I_a^2 R_a + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} + P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{friction}} + P_{\text{stray}}}$$

---

### 3.6 Worked Example (KTU 2024 Model Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A 4-pole, 500 V, DC shunt generator has an armature winding with 600 conductors wave-connected. The shunt field resistance is 200 Ω, and the armature resistance is 0.1 Ω. The flux per pole is 0.02 Wb. Calculate:
1. The speed of the machine at no-load.
2. The armature current at full-load if the load draws 100 A.
3. The voltage regulation (assume no armature reaction).

**Given Data:**
- $P = 4$, $Z = 600$, $A = 2$ (wave), $V_t = 500$ V
- $R_{sh} = 200 \, \Omega$, $R_a = 0.1 \, \Omega$
- $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb, $I_L = 100$ A

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Shunt Field Current**
$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V_t}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{500}{200} = 2.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Armature Current (Full Load)**
$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 2.5 = 102.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Generated EMF at Full Load**
$$E_{g,\text{FL}} = V_t + I_a R_a = 500 + (102.5)(0.1) = 510.25 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Speed at No-Load (Assume same flux)**

At no-load, $V_t = E_{g0} = 500$ V (approximately, since $I_a = I_{sh} = 2.5$ A, drop is negligible).

$$E_{g0} = \dfrac{\Phi Z N_0 P}{60 \, A}$$

Solving for $N_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
500 &= \dfrac{(0.02)(600)(N_0)(4)}{60 \times 2} \\
500 &= \dfrac{48 \, N_0}{120} \\
500 &= 0.4 \, N_0 \\
N_0 &= \dfrac{500}{0.4} = 1250 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Voltage Regulation**
$$\text{VR} = \dfrac{E_{g0} - V_{t,\text{FL}}}{V_{t,\text{FL}}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{510.25 - 500}{500} \times 100\% = 2.05\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The low VR (≈ 2%) is a hallmark of well-designed shunt generators. Real machines will have higher VR (8–15%) due to armature reaction.

If the field resistance line lies **above** the initial linear part of the OCC, **the machine will fail to self-excite** (a critical KTU viva question).
