## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:**
*"Why is the field winding of a DC shunt generator designed with a large number of turns and high resistance?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The shunt field winding of a DC generator is designed with a large number of turns and high resistance for the following reasons:

1. **(1 mark)** To produce a sufficiently strong **MMF (NI)** for establishing the required magnetic flux, a large number of turns ($N$) is essential since flux $\Phi \propto N I$.
2. **(1 mark)** To restrict the **shunt field current ($I_{sh}$)** to a small value (typically 1–5% of the rated armature current), the resistance $R_{sh}$ must be high, since $I_{sh} = V_t / R_{sh}$.
3. **(1 mark)** High resistance also ensures that the field circuit draws negligible power, preserving the **constant-flux characteristic** that gives the shunt generator its near-constant terminal voltage.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**
*"Define armature reaction in a DC shunt generator and state its two effects."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Armature reaction is the effect produced on the main field flux by the magnetic field created by the current flowing through the armature conductors of a DC generator when it is supplying a load.

**Two Effects (1.5 marks):**
1. **Cross-magnetizing effect**: Distorts the main flux distribution and shifts the Magnetic Neutral Axis (MNA) in the direction of rotation.
2. **Demagnetizing effect**: Reduces the net main flux, leading to a drop in the generated EMF and hence the terminal voltage.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** the EMF equation variable $A$ with armature current — they are different! $A$ = parallel paths; armature current is $I_a$.
> - **Always** include the **shunt field current** when computing $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$. Many students forget $I_{sh}$ and lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Voltage regulation is positive** for generators (drooping characteristic) — never write a negative sign.
> - In derivations, **state Faraday's Law explicitly** before deriving the EMF equation to score full marks.
> - **Do not** use $E$ or $V$ interchangeably — use $E_g$ for generated EMF and $V_t$ for terminal voltage to be precise.
> - When asked to find the **critical field resistance**, explain that it is the **slope of the tangent** to the OCC at the origin.

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a DC generator. Explain why the EMF in a DC shunt generator is approximately constant under varying load conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, wave-connected DC shunt generator has 480 armature conductors. The shunt field resistance and armature resistance are 240 Ω and 0.2 Ω, respectively. The flux per pole is 25 mWb, and the load draws 50 A at 250 V. Calculate:
   1. The speed of the machine.
   2. The generated EMF at full load.
   3. The voltage regulation (assuming no armature reaction). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of EMF Equation (7 marks):**

Refer to the **detailed derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. Key points to include in the exam answer:

1. **(1 mark)** State Faraday's Law: $e = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.
2. **(1 mark)** Compute flux cut per conductor per revolution = $\Phi P$ Wb.
3. **(1 mark)** Compute time for one revolution = $60/N$ seconds.
4. **(1 mark)** EMF per conductor = $\Phi P N / 60$ Volts.
5. **(1 mark)** Number of conductors in series per parallel path = $Z/A$.
6. **(1 mark)** Final equation: $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ Volts.
7. **(1 mark)** Explanation: In a shunt generator, $I_{sh} = V_t / R_{sh}$ is small and nearly constant because $V_t$ is nearly constant. Hence $\Phi$ remains nearly constant, and so does $E_g$ for a given speed.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $P = 4$, $Z = 480$, $A = 2$ (wave), $R_{sh} = 240 \, \Omega$, $R_a = 0.2 \, \Omega$
- $\Phi = 25 \text{ mWb} = 0.025 \text{ Wb}$
- $V_t = 250$ V, $I_L = 50$ A

**Step 1: Shunt Field Current (1 mark)**
$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V_t}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{250}{240} = 1.042 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Armature Current (1 mark)**
$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 50 + 1.042 = 51.042 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Generated EMF at Full Load (1 mark)**
$$E_g = V_t + I_a R_a = 250 + (51.042)(0.2) = 250 + 10.21 = 260.21 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Speed Calculation (2 marks)**
$$E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$
$$260.21 = \dfrac{(0.025)(480)(N)(4)}{60 \times 2}$$
$$260.21 = \dfrac{48 \, N}{120} = 0.4 \, N$$
$$N = \dfrac{260.21}{0.4} = 650.5 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 5: Voltage Regulation (2 marks)**

At no-load, $V_{NL} \approx E_{g0}$ (since $I_a R_a$ is very small with $I_a \approx I_{sh} = 1.042$ A, drop ≈ 0.21 V).
$$V_{NL} \approx 260.21 + 0.21 - I_a R_a \text{ correction} \approx 260.0 \text{ V}$$
$$\text{VR} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{260.0 - 250}{250} \times 100\% = 4\%$$

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the process of **voltage build-up** in a self-excited DC shunt generator. What are the conditions necessary for successful self-excitation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **power flow stages** in a DC shunt generator. A 250 kW, 400 V DC shunt generator has the following losses at full load: Armature copper loss = 4 kW, Shunt field copper loss = 1.5 kW, Iron losses = 3 kW, Mechanical losses = 2.5 kW. Calculate:
   1. The electromagnetic converted power.
   2. The mechanical input power.
   3. The efficiency at full load. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Voltage Build-up Process (7 marks):**

1. **(2 marks) Residual Magnetism**: The pole cores retain a small residual flux ($\Phi_r \approx 2$–5% of full flux) from previous operation, even when no field current is present.
2. **(2 marks) Initial EMF Generation**: When the armature is rotated by a prime mover, the residual flux cuts the armature conductors and induces a small EMF, $E_{g1} = k \Phi_r N$.
3. **(1 mark) Cumulative Build-up**: This small EMF causes a small $I_{sh}$ to flow through the shunt field, increasing the flux to $\Phi_1 = \Phi_r + \Delta \Phi$. This in turn increases the EMF to $E_{g2}$, and the cycle continues.
4. **(1 mark) Equilibrium**: The process continues until the magnetization curve (OCC) intersects the field resistance line $V = I_{sh} R_{sh}$, beyond which no further increase is possible.
5. **(1 mark) Three Conditions for Successful Self-Excitation**:
   - Presence of residual magnetism.
   - Field resistance $R_{sh}$ must be **less than the critical field resistance** (slope of tangent to OCC at origin).
   - Correct **polarity of connection** (positive feedback — if reversed, the EMF will collapse).

**(b) Power Flow and Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Output Power (1 mark)**
$$P_{out} = V_t \times I_L = 400 \times \left(\dfrac{250{,}000}{400}\right) = 250 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Full-Load Current (1 mark)**
$$I_L = \dfrac{250{,}000}{400} = 625 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Electromagnetic Converted Power (1 mark)**

$P_{conv} = P_{out} + \text{Armature Cu Loss} = 250 + 4 = 254 \text{ kW}$

**Step 4: Mechanical Input Power (2 marks)**
$$P_{mech,in} = P_{conv} + \text{Iron Loss} + \text{Mechanical Loss} = 254 + 3 + 2.5 = 259.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Efficiency (2 marks)**
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{mech,in}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{250}{259.5} \times 100\% \approx 96.34\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that the **shunt field copper loss** is supplied from the **armature** (since field is in parallel), so it is **NOT** an additional input from the prime mover. It is already accounted for in the armature current $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EMF Equation**: $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — the most important formula in DC machines.
- **Wave winding** → $A = 2$; **Lap winding** → $A = P$.
- **Self-excitation** requires **residual magnetism** + **field resistance below critical value** + **correct polarity**.
- **Terminal voltage**: $V_t = E_g - I_a R_a$ for generators (always less than $E_g$ at load).
- **Armature current**: $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ (shunt generator), $I_a = I_L$ (series), $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ (compound).
- **Voltage regulation** of shunt generators is **moderate (8–15%)** — better than series but worse than compound or separately excited.
- **Characteristics to draw** in the exam: **OCC (Open Circuit Characteristic)** — $E_g$ vs $I_{sh}$; **External Characteristic** — $V_t$ vs $I_L$; **Internal Characteristic** — $E_g$ vs $I_L$.
- **Drooping characteristics** of shunt generator are due to: (i) Armature resistance drop $I_a R_a$, and (ii) Demagnetizing armature reaction.
- **Applications**: Battery charging, exciters for alternators, electroplating, MG sets, laboratories.
- **Critical field resistance** = slope of the tangent to the linear portion of the OCC at the origin. Excitation fails if $R_{sh}$ exceeds this value.
- **Brush position** is shifted from the geometric neutral axis (GNA) to the magnetic neutral axis (MNA) to improve commutation and reduce sparking under load.
- **Interpoles** (small auxiliary poles between main poles) are connected in series with the armature to neutralize armature reaction at the commutating zone.
- **KTU Viva Tip**: If asked *"What happens if the field circuit of a running shunt generator is suddenly opened?"* — the answer is that the machine will behave like a **separately excited generator** with the flux being only the residual flux, causing a **dangerous drop in terminal voltage**; but if the load is suddenly disconnected, the terminal voltage can shoot up to a **dangerously high value** (due to the residual flux) — this is why **field circuit breakers** with discharge resistors are used.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: No-Load (Open Circuit) Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** No-Load (Open Circuit) Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **No-Load Characteristics (NLC)**, also called the **Open Circuit Characteristics (OCC)** or **Magnetization Characteristic**, of a DC shunt generator is the curve plotted between the **no-load generated EMF at the armature terminals (E₀)** on the Y-axis and the **field winding current (I<sub>f</sub>)** on the X-axis, with the machine driven at its **rated constant speed (N)** and the load terminals kept open (i.e., the load switch is OFF, and only the shunt field winding is energized).

In the strict KTU board-book notation, it is often denoted as **E₀ / V₀ = f(I<sub>f</sub>) at N = N<sub>rated</sub> = constant**. The curve describes how the magnetic flux (and hence the induced EMF) of the machine varies as we progressively strengthen the field excitation from zero to its rated value.

## 1.2 Physical Meaning — Why Does This Curve Matter?

The OCC is essentially a **fingerprint of the magnetic core** of the machine. It tells the engineer three critical pieces of information:

1. **The linearity (or non-linearity) of the magnetic circuit** — whether the iron core is operating in the unsaturated region (linear) or the saturated region (non-linear / curved).
2. **The presence of residual magnetism** (B<sub>r</sub>) — i.e., the small amount of flux that remains in the pole shoes even after the field current is reduced to **zero**.
3. **The condition for self-excitation (voltage buildup)** in a self-excited DC shunt generator, which depends on the relative slopes of the **field resistance line** and the **initial linear portion** of the OCC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** KTU examiners frequently ask students to *explain the shape of the OCC* and *discuss voltage buildup conditions*. Always remember that the OCC is the most important preliminary test conducted on a DC generator before loading it.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Garden Hose & Tap" Model

Imagine the **armature** as a water pump that is being turned at a **constant speed** by a hand crank. The **field winding** is like a **tap (faucet)** that controls how much water (magnetic flux) is pushed through the iron pipes (magnetic core) of the pole shoes.

- When the tap is **almost closed** (small I<sub>f</sub>), the water pressure (E₀) rises **linearly** with how much you open the tap.
- As the tap is opened more, the iron pipes start to **choke** (the iron core begins to **saturate**), and further opening of the tap produces **less and less** increase in water pressure. This is the **knee** of the curve.
- Even with the tap **fully closed** (I<sub>f</sub> = 0), there is a tiny residual trickle of water — this is the **residual EMF (E<sub>r</sub>)** caused by the small amount of **residual magnetism** trapped in the iron.

## 1.4 Experimental Setup (Open Circuit Test on a DC Shunt Generator)

The test is performed using the following arrangement:

- The **DC shunt generator** is coupled to a **prime mover** (usually a 3-phase induction motor or DC motor) and rotated at the **rated speed** (kept strictly constant throughout the test using a tachometer feedback).
- The **armature terminals are kept open** (no-load condition). The shunt field winding is connected to a **separate DC source** (or to the armature terminals themselves for self-excitation study) through a **rheostat (variable resistor)**.
- The field current I<sub>f</sub> is varied from **zero to a maximum value** in steps, and the corresponding open-circuit EMF E₀ (which equals the terminal voltage V under no-load, since the armature current is essentially zero) is recorded.
- A curve of **E₀ (Y-axis) versus I<sub>f</sub> (X-axis)** is plotted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked "What does the OCC represent?", do **not** say "it represents the magnetization curve of the field winding." Correct phrasing: "It represents the magnetization characteristic of the **magnetic circuit of the machine (iron core + air gap + field winding)**, showing how the induced EMF varies with field current at constant speed."

## 1.5 Standard SI / CGS Units and Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| No-load generated EMF | E₀ | **Volts (V)** |
| Field current | I<sub>f</sub> | **Amperes (A)** |
| Speed of rotation | N | **rpm (revolutions per minute)** |
| Magnetic flux per pole | Φ | **Weber (Wb)** |
| Residual flux density | B<sub>r</sub> | **Tesla (T)** |
| Permeability of free space | μ₀ | **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental EMF Equation of a DC Generator

The starting point for understanding the OCC is the **induced EMF equation** of a DC generator. Let us derive it from Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction.

For a DC generator with:
- **P** = number of poles
- **Φ** = useful flux per pole (in Weber, Wb)
- **N** = speed of armature rotation (in rpm, revolutions per minute)
- **Z** = total number of armature conductors
- **A** = number of parallel paths in the armature (= P for lap winding, = 2 for wave winding)
- **P<sub>cut}</sub>** = (ZN/60) = number of conductors cut per second

The flux cut by **one conductor in one revolution** = **PΦ** Wb.
The flux cut by **one conductor per second** = **(PΦN)/60** Wb/sec = (PΦN)/60 V (per conductor).
The **EMF per parallel path** = (flux cut per second per conductor) × (number of conductors in series per parallel path) = (PΦN/60) × (Z/A).

Therefore:

$$
E_{g} \;=\; \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A} \quad \text{(Volts)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **In SI units**, this is the standard textbook equation. Some Indian textbooks multiply by an additional factor — the KTU board accepts the standard form shown above.

## 2.2 Relationship Between E₀, Φ, and I<sub>f</sub> at No-Load

At no-load, the **armature current I<sub>a</sub> ≈ 0** (only the small field current I<sub>f</sub> flows through the armature circuit, but it is diverted into the shunt field winding). Hence, the **armature reaction is negligible**, and the **armature resistance drop (I<sub>a</sub>R<sub>a</sub>) is essentially zero**.

This gives us:

$$
E_{0} \;=\; V_{t} \;+\; I_{a} R_{a} \;\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;\; \boxed{\,E_{0} \,\approx\, V_{t}\,}
$$

where V<sub>t</sub> is the terminal voltage measured by a voltmeter across the open-circuited armature terminals.

The relationship between Φ and I<sub>f</sub> is governed by the **magnetization curve of the magnetic circuit (B-H curve of the iron core)**:

$$
\Phi \;=\; \frac{\mathcal{F}}{\mathcal{R}} \;=\; \frac{N_{f} \, I_{f}}{\mathcal{R}_{\text{core}}} \quad \text{(Ampere-Turns / Reluctance)}
$$

where:
- **N<sub>f</sub> I<sub>f</sub>** = Ampere-Turns (MMF) produced by the field winding
- **ℛ<sub>core</sub>** = total reluctance of the magnetic path (pole core + air gap + armature core + yoke)

## 2.3 The Three Distinct Regions of the OCC

A careful analysis of the OCC reveals **three distinct operating regions**, which correspond to the magnetic state of the iron core:

### Region 1 — Initial Linear Region (Unsaturated Region)
- The pole shoes are operating **well below saturation**.
- The relative permeability μ<sub>r</sub> of the iron is **very high and approximately constant**.
- The **reluctance ℛ is dominated by the air gap**, which is linear.
- Therefore: **Φ ∝ I<sub>f</sub>**, and hence **E₀ ∝ I<sub>f</sub>**.

### Region 2 — The Knee (Commencement of Saturation)
- The pole shoes begin to **saturate** (μ<sub>r</sub> starts to fall rapidly).
- The curve **bends** — additional I<sub>f</sub> produces diminishing returns in Φ and E₀.
- This is the **preferred operating point** of a well-designed generator (≈ 25% bend past the linear region).

### Region 3 — Saturation Region (Top Region)
- The iron is **deeply saturated** (μ<sub>r</sub> ≈ 1, behaving almost like air).
- **Φ increases very slowly** with I<sub>f</sub>, and the curve becomes nearly horizontal.
- Further increases in I<sub>f</sub> **waste copper (I²R losses)** without much gain in EMF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Note:** A common question is: *"Why is the OCC not a perfectly straight line?"* The correct answer is: *"Because the iron core of the pole shoes becomes magnetically saturated as the field current increases, causing the flux Φ to grow non-linearly with I<sub>f</sub>. Saturation causes the reluctance to increase, so further increases in MMF (N<sub>f</sub> I<sub>f</sub>) produce smaller and smaller increases in Φ, and therefore in E₀."*

## 2.4 The Critical Role of Residual Magnetism

Even after the field current is reduced to **zero** (I<sub>f</sub> = 0), the curve does **not** pass through the origin. It intersects the Y-axis at a small positive value known as the **residual EMF (E<sub>r</sub>)** or **residual voltage**. This is due to the **residual magnetism (B<sub>r</sub>)** retained in the iron pole shoes.

Mathematically:

$$
E_{r} \;=\; \frac{P \, \Phi_{r} \, Z \, N}{60 \, A} \quad \text{where} \quad \Phi_{r} \;=\; \text{residual flux per pole}
$$

Typical values of E<sub>r</sub> in a healthy machine: **2% to 5% of the rated terminal voltage**. If E<sub>r</sub> = 0, the machine has **lost its residual magnetism** and **cannot self-excite** — it must be "flashed" (briefly energized from an external DC source to restore residual flux).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **every formula** you need for the KTU board exam on this topic. Memorize it thoroughly.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Induced EMF (general) | $E_g \,=\, \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,N}{60\,A}$ | Valid for **all** DC generators; P = # poles, Z = # conductors, A = # parallel paths |
| 2 | Parallel paths in **lap winding** | $A \,=\, P$ | Most common in DC machines |
| 3 | Parallel paths in **wave winding** | $A \,=\, 2$ | Used for high-voltage machines |
| 4 | No-load EMF equals terminal voltage | $E_0 \;\approx\; V_t$ | Valid only at **no-load** (I<sub>a</sub> ≈ 0) |
| 5 | Magnetic Ohm's Law | $\Phi \,=\, \dfrac{\mathcal{F}}{\mathcal{R}} \,=\, \dfrac{N_f I_f}{\mathcal{R}_{\text{core}}}$ | Foundation of magnetic circuit analysis |
| 6 | Residual EMF | $E_r \,=\, \dfrac{P\,\Phi_r\,Z\,N}{60\,A}$ | $\Phi_r$ is the residual flux per pole |
| 7 | Flux per pole approximation (linear region) | $\Phi \;\approx\; k \cdot I_f$ | Only valid **below the knee** of the OCC |
| 8 | Critical field resistance (R<sub>c</sub>) | Slope of tangent to **linear portion** of OCC: $R_c \,=\, \dfrac{E_0}{I_f}\bigg\vert_{\text{tangent}}$ | Determines **voltage buildup condition** |
| 9 | Voltage buildup condition (DC shunt gen.) | $R_f \,>\, R_c$ for **stable buildup**; if R<sub>f</sub> < R<sub>c</sub>, no buildup | R<sub>f</sub> = total field circuit resistance |
| 10 | Critical speed (N<sub>c</sub>) | $N_c \,=\, N_{\text{rated}} \cdot \dfrac{R_f}{R_c}$ | Below N<sub>c</sub>, machine **fails to build up** voltage |
| 11 | O.C. test field current range | $0 \,\leq\, I_f \,\leq\, 1.2\,I_{f,\text{rated}}$ | Datasheet value; do not exceed due to overheating |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Notice:** All absolute-value bars in the formulas above are written using LaTeX `\,=\,`, not the vertical pipe symbol, to preserve table integrity. Do **not** use plain `|` inside markdown tables.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The OCC is **not merely an academic exercise** — it is the **bedrock of all subsequent DC machine testing and analysis**:

1. **Determination of Voltage Regulation** — By comparing the OCC with the load characteristic, engineers can compute the voltage regulation of a DC generator under various loads.
2. **Selection of Operating Point** — A well-designed DC machine is operated just past the **knee** of the OCC, balancing stability, sensitivity, and copper losses.
3. **Diagnosing Machine Health** — A drop in residual EMF below 2% of rated voltage, or a shift in the knee point, indicates **aging of the iron core**, **loss of residual magnetism**, or **short-circuited field turns**.
4. **Shunt Generator Self-Excitation** — The OCC is essential for verifying that a self-excited shunt generator can build up voltage at the rated speed and field-circuit resistance.
5. **Synchronization in AC Systems** — For synchronous machines, the equivalent test (the open-circuit test of an alternator) serves the same diagnostic role.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Faraday's Law to the EMF Equation

**Starting Premise:** Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction states that the EMF induced in a conductor is equal to the **rate of change of flux linkage**.

**Step 1 — Flux cut by one conductor in one revolution:**

In one complete revolution, a single armature conductor cuts all the flux of **P poles**, i.e., it cuts a total flux equal to **PΦ** Webers.

**Step 2 — Number of revolutions per second:**

If the armature rotates at **N rpm**, then in one second it completes **N/60** revolutions.

**Step 3 — Flux cut by one conductor per second:**

$$
\text{Flux cut per second per conductor} \;=\; P\Phi \cdot \frac{N}{60} \;=\; \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \quad \text{(Wb/sec, i.e., Volts)}
$$

**Step 4 — Average EMF induced in one conductor:**

Since 1 Weber per second = 1 Volt, the EMF per conductor is:

$$
e_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \quad \text{(Volts)}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of conductors in series per parallel path:**

The total armature has **Z** conductors distributed among **A** parallel paths. The conductors in each path are in series, so the number of conductors per path is:

$$
\text{Conductors per path} \;=\; \frac{Z}{A}
$$

**Step 6 — Total EMF per parallel path (= terminal EMF):**

Since the conductors in each path are in series, the EMFs add up:

$$
E_g \;=\; \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \cdot \frac{Z}{A} \;=\; \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A} \quad \text{(Volts)}
$$

**Step 7 — Final canonical form:**

$$
\boxed{\,E_g \;=\; \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A}\,}
$$

This is the **canonical EMF equation** that appears in **every** KTU board exam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative form (using conductors per pole per parallel path):** If we define **Z' = Z/P** as the number of armature conductors per pole, then we can also write $E_g \,=\, \dfrac{\Phi \, Z' \, N}{60} \cdot P / A$. For a lap-wound machine (A = P), this simplifies to $E_g \,=\, \dfrac{\Phi \, Z' \, N}{60}$, which is a useful shortcut.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Critical Field Resistance from the OCC

The **critical field resistance (R<sub>c</sub>)** is defined as the **maximum value of the field circuit resistance** at which a self-excited DC shunt generator can just build up its voltage at a given speed.

**Step 1 — Define the field resistance line:**

The shunt field winding is connected across the armature terminals. By Ohm's Law:

$$
I_f \;=\; \frac{V_t}{R_f} \;\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;\; V_t \;=\; R_f \cdot I_f
$$

This is a **straight line through the origin** with slope = R<sub>f</sub>. In the OCC plot (E₀ on Y-axis, I<sub>f</sub> on X-axis), the **field resistance line** is a straight line from the origin.

**Step 2 — Condition for voltage buildup:**

For self-excitation to occur, the **field resistance line must intersect the OCC**. Moreover, for **stable buildup**, the slope of the field resistance line must be **less steep than the slope of the linear (initial) portion of the OCC**. If the field resistance line is **steeper** than the OCC's initial slope, the two curves intersect only at the residual point (E<sub>r</sub>) and no voltage builds up.

**Step 3 — Mathematical statement:**

The slope of the tangent drawn to the **initial linear portion** of the OCC is:

$$
R_c \;=\; \left.\frac{dE_0}{dI_f}\right\vert_{\text{linear region}} \;=\; \left.\frac{E_0}{I_f}\right\vert_{\text{at any point on the tangent}}
$$

**Step 4 — Voltage buildup criterion:**

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Voltage builds up stably} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; R_f \,<\, R_c\,}
$$

If **R<sub>f</sub> = R<sub>c</sub>**: machine builds up voltage to a point on the **knee** of the OCC, but operation is unstable.
If **R<sub>f</sub> > R<sub>c</sub>**: machine **fails to build up** — this is called the **"false zero" condition** in some textbooks.

**Step 5 — Critical speed derivation:**

If the field circuit resistance R<sub>f</sub> is fixed, but the speed N is varied, then the OCC (which is plotted at rated N) **scales linearly** with N (since E₀ ∝ ΦN at fixed Φ, or more precisely, E₀ ∝ N at fixed I<sub>f</sub> in the linear region).

Therefore, the critical speed at which voltage just begins to build up is:

$$
\boxed{\,N_c \;=\; N_{\text{rated}} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_c}\,}
$$

**Derivation logic:** If we double N, the entire OCC doubles its E₀ values. The field resistance line is unchanged (it depends only on R<sub>f</sub>, not on N). For the tangent condition to be met, the new OCC's slope must equal R<sub>f</sub>. Since E₀ ∝ N, the slope also scales with N. Therefore, N<sub>c</sub>/N<sub>rated</sub> = R<sub>f</sub>/R<sub>c</sub>.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Computing a Point on the OCC

**Given Data:**
- A **4-pole DC shunt generator**, **lap wound** (so A = P = 4)
- Total armature conductors **Z = 600**
- Flux per pole **Φ = 20 mWb = 0.02 Wb**
- Speed **N = 1000 rpm**
- Armature resistance **R<sub>a</sub> = 0.5 Ω**
- Field current **I<sub>f</sub> = 2 A**
- Field winding resistance **R<sub>f</sub> = 100 Ω**

**Find:** The no-load generated EMF E₀, the no-load terminal voltage V<sub>t</sub>, and the loaded terminal voltage if a load current of 50 A is connected.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the no-load generated EMF:**

$$
E_0 \;=\; \frac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,N}{60\,A} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 600 \times 1000}{60 \times 4} \;\;\text{V}
$$

Let us evaluate the numerator and denominator separately:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator:} \quad & 4 \times 0.02 \times 600 \times 1000 \;=\; 48000 \\
\text{Denominator:} \quad & 60 \times 4 \;=\; 240
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_0 \;=\; \frac{48000}{240} \;=\; 200 \;\text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — At no-load (armature current ≈ field current = 2 A):**

Wait — at no-load, the **load switch is open**, so the only current in the armature is the field current diverted through the shunt winding. So I<sub>a</sub> = I<sub>f</sub> = 2 A.

$$
V_{t,\text{no-load}} \;=\; E_0 - I_a R_a \;=\; 200 - (2 \times 0.5) \;=\; 200 - 1 \;=\; 199 \;\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Under load with I<sub>load</sub> = 50 A:**

The armature current now is I<sub>a</sub> = I<sub>load</sub> + I<sub>f</sub> = 50 + 2 = 52 A.

$$
V_{t,\text{loaded}} \;=\; E_0 - I_a R_a \;=\; 200 - (52 \times 0.5) \;=\; 200 - 26 \;=\; 174 \;\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Voltage Regulation:**

$$
\text{VR} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{no-load}} - V_{\text{full-load}}}{V_{\text{full-load}}} \times 100\% \;=\; \frac{199 - 174}{174} \times 100\% \;\approx\; 14.37\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway from the Example:** The OCC (which gives us E₀ = 200 V) is the **starting point** for all load calculations. Without knowing E₀ from the OCC, we cannot compute the terminal voltage under load.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Variations in OCC with Speed and Field Current

The following matrix summarizes **how the OCC changes** when the operating conditions change. This is a common analytical question in KTU exams.

| Condition | Effect on OCC | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Speed **increases** | Entire OCC **shifts upward** (E₀ ∝ N at fixed I<sub>f</sub> in linear region) | At higher speeds, the same I<sub>f</sub> produces higher E₀ |
| Speed **decreases** | Entire OCC **shifts downward** | Below critical speed N<sub>c</sub>, no voltage buildup |
| Field winding turns **increase** (rewound) | OCC **steeper** (more MMF per ampere) | Lower field current needed for same EMF |
| Iron core **ages / overheats** | OCC **flattens** (saturation occurs earlier) | Indicates degraded magnetic material |
| **Residual magnetism lost** | OCC **passes through origin** | Machine fails to self-excite; must be re-flashed |
| Air gap **increases** (worn bearings) | OCC **flattens** (reluctance increases) | Lower induced EMF for same I<sub>f</sub> |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Diagram of the Open-Circuit Test Setup

The following Mermaid diagram shows the complete **functional block architecture** of the open-circuit test setup for a DC shunt generator. (Adapted to block-level functional architecture as per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguard for complex physical drawings.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PM[Prime Mover<br/>3-phase Induction Motor] -->|Mechanical Shaft| GEN[DC Shunt Generator<br/>P, Z, A, R_a, R_f]
    TACH[Tachometer<br/>Speed Feedback] -->|N = constant| CTRL[Speed Controller]
    CTRL --> PM
    SW[Load Switch<br/>OPEN during OCC test] --> LOAD[Load Bank<br/>Resistive Load]
    FWR[Full-Wave Rectifier<br/>or DC Source] --> RHEO[Field Rheostat<br/>R_f variable]
    RHEO -->|I_f| FLD[Shunt Field Winding<br/>N_f turns]
    GEN -->|E_0 across open terminals| VM[Voltmeter<br/>Reads V_t = E_0]
    VM --> DATA[Data Acquisition:<br/>E_0 vs I_f table]
    AMM[Ammeter in Field Circuit] --> DATA
    DATA --> PLOT[Plot: E_0 on Y-axis<br/>I_f on X-axis]
    PLOT --> OCC[OCC Curve<br/>3 Regions:<br/>Linear, Knee, Saturation]
```

**Key functional nodes in the diagram:**

- **PM (Prime Mover):** Provides mechanical input at constant rated speed. Common choice: 3-phase induction motor with V/f control or a calibrated DC motor.
- **GEN (DC Shunt Generator):** The machine under test. The shunt field winding is connected in **parallel** with the armature.
- **SW (Load Switch):** Kept **OPEN** during OCC test — this is the defining feature of the test.
- **FWR + RHEO:** A variable DC excitation source (rectifier + rheostat) used to **sweep** the field current I<sub>f</sub> from 0 to 1.2 × rated value.
- **VM (Voltmeter):** Reads the open-circuit terminal voltage V<sub>t</sub>, which equals E₀ at no-load.
- **AMM (Ammeter):** Measures the field current I<sub>f</sub>.
- **OCC Curve:** The plotted result — see Section 4.2 for its shape.

## 4.2 The Shape of the OCC Curve — Annotated Schematic

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **canonical shape of the OCC** with all its critical points labeled, in a block-level functional architecture style (since the OCC is a curve and not easily drawn in Mermaid natively).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLOT["E_0 (Volts) — Y-Axis"]
        A1["MAX E_0<br/>Saturation Region<br/>Curve nearly horizontal"]
        A2["KNEE POINT<br/>Commencement of Saturation<br/>μ_r begins to fall"]
        A3["LINEAR REGION<br/>Φ proportional to I_f<br/>E_0 proportional to I_f"]
        A4["E_r (Residual EMF)<br/>at I_f = 0<br/>Y-axis intercept"]
    end
    subgraph XAXIS["I_f (Amperes) — X-Axis"]
        X1["Origin O<br/>I_f = 0, E_0 = 0<br/>(Theoretical)"]
        X2["Region 1:<br/>0 to I_f, knee"]
        X3["Region 2:<br/>I_f, knee to I_f, sat"]
        X4["Region 3:<br/>Beyond I_f, sat"]
    end
    A4 -.->|"Residual flux Φ_r<br/>due to B_r"| X1
    A3 -->|"Slope = R_c<br/>Tangent at origin"| X2
    A2 -->|"Bend in curve"| X3
    A1 -->|"Horizontal asymptote<br/>Approaches V_max"| X4
```

**Annotated critical points on the OCC:**

1. **Origin (0, 0):** Theoretical starting point. In practice, the curve intersects the Y-axis at **(0, E<sub>r</sub>)**, where E<sub>r</sub> is the residual EMF.
2. **E<sub>r</sub> (Residual EMF):** Typically **2–5%** of rated voltage. Caused by residual magnetism in the pole shoes.
3. **Linear Region:** From I<sub>f</sub> = 0 up to the **knee point**. The curve is approximately a straight line. The slope of this region defines the **critical field resistance R<sub>c</sub>**.
4. **Knee Point:** The transition zone where the iron core begins to saturate. The curve **bends** sharply. This is the **preferred operating point** of a well-designed machine.
5. **Saturation Region:** Beyond the knee. The curve **flattens**. Additional I<sub>f</sub> produces very little increase in E₀.

## 4.3 Functional Flow: From Faraday's Law to the Plotted OCC

This Mermaid diagram shows the **causal chain** that produces the OCC.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["DC Supply through Rheostat"] -->|"Varies I_f from 0 to 1.2 * I_f,rated"| B["Shunt Field Winding<br/>MMF = N_f * I_f"]
    B -->|"H = MMF / l_m<br/>Magnetic field intensity"| C["Iron Core of Pole Shoes<br/>B-H Curve"]
    C -->|"B increases with H<br/>until saturation"| D["Flux per pole Φ<br/>Φ = B * A_core"]
    D -->|"Φ increases with I_f<br/>(non-linearly post-knee)"| E["Faraday's Law<br/>E_0 = P * Φ * Z * N / 60A"]
    E -->|"Measured by Voltmeter<br/>V_t = E_0 at no-load"| F["Tabulated Pair:<br/>(I_f, E_0)"]
    F -->|"Repeated for many I_f values"| G["PLOT: E_0 vs I_f<br/>= The OCC"]
    C -->|"Below knee: linear<br/>Above knee: saturation<br/>(μ_r decreases)"| H["SHAPE OF OCC<br/>Linear → Knee → Flat"]
    H --> G
```

## 4.4 Comparison: OCC vs. SCC (Short-Circuit Characteristics)

For comprehensive understanding, the OCC is often plotted alongside the **Short-Circuit Characteristics (SCC)** — the curve of I<sub>a</sub> vs. I<sub>f</sub> when the armature terminals are short-circuited.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT["OCC Plot (E_0 vs I_f)"]
        OCC1["Starts at E_r on Y-axis<br/>Rises linearly<br/>Bends at knee<br/>Saturates horizontally"]
    end
    subgraph RIGHT["SCC Plot (I_a vs I_f)"]
        SCC1["Passes through origin<br/>Strictly linear<br/>(since I_a * R_a is small<br/>and core is unsaturated)"]
    end
    LEFT -->|"Used for:"| USE1["Voltage regulation<br/>Self-excitation check<br/>Core diagnosis"]
    RIGHT -->|"Used for:"| USE2["Synchronous impedance<br/>calculation<br/>Short-circuit ratio"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** The SCC is a **straight line through the origin** because during a short-circuit test, the armature current is small enough to keep the core in the **linear (unsaturated) region** of the B-H curve.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A carries 3 marks each. Answers should be concise (typically 4–6 lines) and to the point. The model answers below follow the **valuation key style** expected by KTU examiners.

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "No-Load Characteristics" of a DC shunt generator. What is its other name?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **No-Load Characteristics (NLC)** of a DC shunt generator is the curve plotted between the **no-load generated EMF (E₀)** at the armature terminals and the **shunt field current (I<sub>f</sub>)**, with the machine driven at its **rated constant speed (N)** and the load terminals kept **open** (no-load condition). It is also called the **Open Circuit Characteristics (OCC)** or the **Magnetization Characteristic** of the machine.

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for axis labels, 1 mark for constant-speed condition + alternate names.)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why does the OCC of a DC generator not start from the origin?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The OCC of a DC generator does not start from the origin because of the presence of **residual magnetism (B<sub>r</sub>)** in the iron pole shoes. Even after the field current is reduced to **zero (I<sub>f</sub> = 0)**, a small amount of magnetic flux, called the **residual flux (Φ<sub>r</sub>)**, remains trapped in the pole cores. This residual flux induces a small EMF called the **residual EMF (E<sub>r</sub>)** in the armature when it is rotated at rated speed. Hence, the curve intersects the Y-axis at the point **(0, E<sub>r</sub>)** instead of at the origin.

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark residual magnetism, 1 mark residual EMF E<sub>r</sub>, 1 mark correct Y-axis intercept explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice** (either Question A or Question B must be answered). Each sub-part carries 7 marks. The model solutions below are structured exactly as expected in the **ESE (End Semester Evaluation) answer scripts**.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a DC generator starting from Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction. Define all the symbols used. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neatly labeled diagram, explain the Open-Circuit Characteristics of a DC shunt generator. Discuss the three regions of the OCC and the significance of the **critical field resistance (R<sub>c</sub>)** in the context of voltage buildup in a self-excited shunt generator. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(a) — Derivation of EMF Equation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Faraday's Law (1 mark):**

According to Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, the EMF induced in a conductor is equal to the rate of change of flux linkage. If **dΦ** is the flux cut in time **dt**, then:

$$
e \;=\; -\frac{d\Phi}{dt} \quad \text{(average EMF per conductor)}
$$

**Step 2 — Flux cut by one conductor in one revolution (1 mark):**

In one complete revolution, an armature conductor cuts the flux of **P poles**, i.e., the flux cut is **PΦ** Wb.

**Step 3 — Flux cut per second (1 mark):**

At a speed of **N rpm**, the number of revolutions per second is **N/60**. Hence, the flux cut per conductor per second is:

$$
\text{Flux cut per second} \;=\; P\Phi \cdot \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{(Wb/sec)}
$$

**Step 4 — EMF per conductor (1 mark):**

Since 1 Wb/sec = 1 Volt, the average EMF induced per conductor is:

$$
e \;=\; \frac{P\Phi N}{60} \quad \text{(Volts)}
$$

**Step 5 — Series conductors per parallel path (1 mark):**

The total number of armature conductors is **Z**, and they are distributed among **A** parallel paths. Hence, the number of conductors in series per parallel path is **Z/A**.

**Step 6 — Total EMF per parallel path (1 mark):**

Since the conductors in each parallel path are in series, the total EMF induced is:

$$
E_g \;=\; \frac{P\Phi N}{60} \cdot \frac{Z}{A} \;=\; \frac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,N}{60\,A} \quad \text{(Volts)}
$$

**Step 7 — Definition of symbols (1 mark):**

- **E<sub>g</sub>** = generated EMF (Volts)
- **P** = number of poles
- **Φ** = flux per pole (Wb)
- **Z** = total number of armature conductors
- **N** = speed of armature (rpm)
- **A** = number of parallel paths (= P for lap winding, = 2 for wave winding)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(b) — OCC Explanation and Critical Field Resistance (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition and Experimental Setup (1 mark):**

The **Open Circuit Characteristics (OCC)** of a DC shunt generator is the curve plotted between the **no-load generated EMF (E₀)** on the Y-axis and the **shunt field current (I<sub>f</sub>)** on the X-axis, with the machine running at **constant rated speed** and the **load terminals open**. The test is conducted by varying the field current from zero to about 1.2 times its rated value, using a field rheostat, and recording the corresponding terminal voltage.

**Step 2 — Shape of the OCC and the Three Regions (2 marks):**

The OCC has three distinct regions:
1. **Initial Linear Region:** Below the knee, the iron core is unsaturated. Flux Φ is proportional to I<sub>f</sub>, and hence E₀ ∝ I<sub>f</sub>. The curve is a **straight line**.
2. **Knee Point:** The transition zone where the iron core begins to saturate. The curve **bends** sharply.
3. **Saturation Region:** Beyond the knee, the iron is deeply saturated. The curve **flattens** and approaches a horizontal asymptote.

**Step 3 — Residual Magnetism (1 mark):**

The curve does not pass through the origin; it intersects the Y-axis at the **residual EMF (E<sub>r</sub>)**, which is typically **2–5%** of rated voltage. This is caused by the **residual magnetism** retained in the pole shoes.

**Step 4 — Critical Field Resistance R<sub>c</sub> (1 mark):**

The **critical field resistance (R<sub>c</sub>)** is defined as the **slope of the tangent drawn to the initial linear portion of the OCC** at the origin:

$$
R_c \;=\; \left.\frac{dE_0}{dI_f}\right\vert_{\text{linear region}}
$$

**Step 5 — Voltage Buildup Condition (1 mark):**

For a self-excited DC shunt generator to build up voltage stably, the **field circuit resistance R<sub>f</sub>** must be **less than the critical field resistance R<sub>c</sub>**. If **R<sub>f</sub> > R<sub>c</sub>**, the field resistance line is steeper than the OCC's initial slope, and the machine **fails to build up voltage**. This is the practical significance of the OCC.

**Step 6 — Diagram (1 mark):**

*(Draw a neatly labeled OCC curve with axes E₀ and I<sub>f</sub>, marking the residual EMF E<sub>r</sub>, the linear region, the knee, the saturation region, and the field resistance line tangent to the linear portion — slope = R<sub>c</sub>.)*

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** A **4-pole, lap-wound DC shunt generator** has **500 armature conductors** and a **flux per pole of 0.025 Wb**. It is driven at a speed of **1200 rpm**. The **armature resistance is 0.4 Ω** and the **shunt field resistance is 200 Ω**. Compute: (i) the no-load generated EMF, (ii) the no-load terminal voltage, and (iii) the loaded terminal voltage when the generator supplies a load current of **80 A**. Also, calculate the **voltage regulation**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the concept of **critical field resistance** and **critical speed** of a DC shunt generator. How does the OCC help in determining these quantities? (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(a) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Number of poles, **P = 4**
- Lap wound: **A = P = 4**
- Total armature conductors, **Z = 500**
- Flux per pole, **Φ = 0.025 Wb**
- Speed, **N = 1200 rpm**
- Armature resistance, **R<sub>a</sub> = 0.4 Ω**
- Shunt field resistance, **R<sub>f</sub> = 200 Ω**
- Load current, **I<sub>L</sub> = 80 A**

**Part (i) — No-load generated EMF E₀ (2 marks):**

$$
E_0 \;=\; \frac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,N}{60\,A} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 0.025 \times 500 \times 1200}{60 \times 4} \;\;\text{V}
$$

Evaluating step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator:} \quad & 4 \times 0.025 \;=\; 0.1 \\
& 0.1 \times 500 \;=\; 50 \\
& 50 \times 1200 \;=\; 60000 \\
\text{Denominator:} \quad & 60 \times 4 \;=\; 240 \\
\text{Result:} \quad & E_0 \;=\; \frac{60000}{240} \;=\; 250 \;\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — No-load terminal voltage V<sub>t,NL</sub> (1 mark):**

The field current at rated voltage:

$$
I_f \;=\; \frac{V_t}{R_f} \;\approx\; \frac{250}{200} \;=\; 1.25 \;\text{A}
$$

At no-load, the only armature current is I<sub>f</sub> (since the load switch is open), so I<sub>a</sub> = I<sub>f</sub> = 1.25 A.

$$
V_{t,\text{NL}} \;=\; E_0 - I_a R_a \;=\; 250 - (1.25 \times 0.4) \;=\; 250 - 0.5 \;=\; 249.5 \;\text{V}
$$

**Part (iii) — Loaded terminal voltage V<sub>t,FL</sub> (2 marks):**

Under load, the armature current is:

$$
I_a \;=\; I_L + I_f \;=\; 80 + 1.25 \;=\; 81.25 \;\text{A}
$$

$$
V_{t,\text{FL}} \;=\; E_0 - I_a R_a \;=\; 250 - (81.25 \times 0.4) \;=\; 250 - 32.5 \;=\; 217.5 \;\text{V}
$$

**Voltage Regulation (2 marks):**

$$
\text{VR} \;=\; \frac{V_{t,\text{NL}} - V_{t,\text{FL}}}{V_{t,\text{FL}}} \times 100\% \;=\; \frac{249.5 - 217.5}{217.5} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{VR} \;=\; \frac{32.0}{217.5} \times 100\% \;\approx\; 14.71\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly use **I<sub>L</sub>** (load current) instead of **I<sub>a</sub> = I<sub>L</sub> + I<sub>f</sub>** when computing the armature drop. The **armature current** always equals the **load current plus the field current** in a shunt generator. Forgetting to add I<sub>f</sub> loses **2 marks** in the KTU answer script. Also, the field current must be computed using the **actual terminal voltage** (not the assumed rated voltage) for full accuracy.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(b) — Critical Field Resistance & Critical Speed (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition of Critical Field Resistance (2 marks):**

The **critical field resistance (R<sub>c</sub>)** of a DC shunt generator is defined as the **maximum value of the shunt field circuit resistance** at which the machine can just build up its voltage at the rated speed. It is obtained graphically from the OCC as the **slope of the tangent line drawn to the initial linear (straight-line) portion of the OCC**, passing through the origin. Mathematically:

$$
R_c \;=\; \left.\frac{E_0}{I_f}\right\vert_{\text{at the tangent point on the linear portion}}
$$

**Step 2 — Significance in Voltage Buildup (2 marks):**

The OCC helps in determining the **buildup condition** of a self-excited DC shunt generator:
- If the **field circuit resistance R<sub>f</sub> < R<sub>c</sub>**: the field resistance line is **less steep** than the initial slope of the OCC, so the two curves intersect at a stable point on the OCC. The machine **builds up voltage** successfully.
- If the **field circuit resistance R<sub>f</sub> > R<sub>c</sub>**: the field resistance line is **steeper** than the initial OCC slope, so the two curves intersect only at the residual point. The machine **fails to build up voltage**.

**Step 3 — Definition of Critical Speed (1 mark):**

The **critical speed (N<sub>c</sub>)** is the **minimum speed** at which a self-excited DC shunt generator can build up its voltage when the field circuit resistance R<sub>f</sub> is fixed. It is given by:

$$
N_c \;=\; N_{\text{rated}} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_c}
$$

**Step 4 — How the OCC Helps (1 mark):**

Since the OCC is plotted at the **rated speed**, scaling the OCC to any other speed N is straightforward: the E₀ values scale linearly with N (because E₀ ∝ ΦN at constant Φ, and in the linear region Φ is independent of N). The critical speed is found by finding the speed at which the scaled OCC becomes **tangent** to the field resistance line (i.e., the slope of the scaled OCC at the origin equals R<sub>f</sub>).

**Step 5 — Practical Implication (1 mark):**

In practice, generators are operated at a speed **comfortably above** N<sub>c</sub> to ensure reliable voltage buildup. If a generator fails to excite (e.g., due to loss of residual magnetism), the residual flux must be restored by **"flashing"** the field winding from an external DC source.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes in OCC Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the field resistance line with the load line** — they are different. The field resistance line is V = R<sub>f</sub> · I<sub>f</sub>, while the load line involves V = E₀ − I<sub>a</sub>R<sub>a</sub>.
> 2. **Plotting I<sub>f</sub> on the Y-axis instead of the X-axis** — KTU strictly requires E₀ on Y and I<sub>f</sub> on X. Inverting the axes loses presentation marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the constant-speed condition** — the OCC is meaningful **only at constant speed**. Stating "OCC is the curve between E₀ and I<sub>f</sub>" without mentioning constant N is an incomplete answer.
> 4. **Not specifying "at rated speed"** when discussing R<sub>c</sub> — R<sub>c</sub> is speed-dependent. Always quote the speed alongside R<sub>c</sub>.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section for a 5-minute pre-exam revision.**

- [x] **OCC = E₀ vs I<sub>f</sub> at constant N** (rated speed) with load terminals **open**.
- [x] Also called **Magnetization Characteristic** or **No-Load Saturation Curve**.
- [x] **EMF Equation:** $E_g \,=\, \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,N}{60\,A}$
- [x] **Lap winding:** A = P (most common).
- [x] **Wave winding:** A = 2.
- [x] At no-load: **E₀ ≈ V<sub>t</sub>** (since I<sub>a</sub>R<sub>a</sub> drop is negligible).
- [x] OCC has **three regions**: **Linear → Knee → Saturation**.
- [x] Curve does **not** pass through origin — intersects Y-axis at **E<sub>r</sub>** (residual EMF).
- [x] **Residual EMF (E<sub>r</sub>)** is due to **residual magnetism (B<sub>r</sub>)** in pole shoes; typically **2–5%** of rated voltage.
- [x] **Critical field resistance (R<sub>c</sub>)** = slope of tangent to linear portion of OCC.
- [x] **Voltage buildup condition:** R<sub>f</sub> < R<sub>c</sub> for stable self-excitation.
- [x] If R<sub>f</sub> > R<sub>c</sub>: machine **fails to build up** voltage.
- [x] **Critical speed:** $N_c \,=\, N_{\text{rated}} \cdot \dfrac{R_f}{R_c}$
- [x] **Voltage Regulation:** $\text{VR} \,=\, \dfrac{V_{\text{NL}} - V_{\text{FL}}}{V_{\text{FL}}} \times 100\%$
- [x] Armature current in shunt gen: **I<sub>a</sub> = I<sub>L</sub> + I<sub>f</sub>** (commonly forgotten).
- [x] For lap wound machines, **A = P** (a very common KTU 2-mark question).
- [x] If residual magnetism is lost, **"flash" the field** from external DC to restore it.
- [x] **SCC (Short-Circuit Characteristics)** is a **straight line** through origin — keep this distinct from OCC in exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critical Field Resistance, Critical Speed, Voltage Buildup
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Critical Field Resistance, Critical Speed, Voltage Buildup

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Voltage Buildup (Self-Excitation)

**Voltage Buildup** in a self-excited DC shunt generator is the cumulative process by which a small **residual magnetism** in the field poles is progressively amplified through positive electromagnetic feedback until the machine reaches a stable no-load terminal voltage determined by the saturation of the iron and the field circuit resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"Voltage buildup is the process of self-generation of EMF in a DC shunt generator, in which the initially small EMF due to residual flux drives a small field current, which in turn augments the flux, producing a larger EMF, and this cumulative action continues until equilibrium is reached at the intersection of the Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC) and the Field Resistance Line (FRL)."*

## 1.2 Critical Field Resistance (R_c)

**Critical Field Resistance** is the **maximum allowable value** of the total field circuit resistance of a self-excited DC shunt generator, at a given speed, above which the machine **fails to build up voltage**. It is equal to the **slope of the tangent drawn from the origin to the linear (initial) portion of the OCC**.

$$R_c = \left(\frac{dE_0}{dI_f}\right)_{\text{linear region, at origin}}$$

## 1.3 Critical Speed (N_c)

**Critical Speed** is the **minimum speed** at which a self-excited DC shunt generator, with a given field circuit resistance, will just succeed in building up voltage. Below this speed, the induced EMF is insufficient to sustain the cumulative buildup process.

$$N_c = N_{\text{rated}} \times \frac{R_f}{R_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Ball on a Hill" Picture:**
> Imagine the voltage buildup as a ball rolling up a curved hill (the OCC):
> - **Residual magnetism** is the initial nudge that gives the ball its first push.
> - **Critical Field Resistance (R_c)** is the steepest slope on which the ball can still rest stably. If the hill (FRL) is steeper than this, the ball slides back down — no voltage.
> - **Critical Speed (N_c)** is the minimum speed at which the "pusher" (mechanical rotation) can give the ball enough energy to climb. Drive too slowly, and the ball stalls.
> - **Saturation region** is the flat plateau at the top — the natural resting point of the system.

## 1.4 The Three Essential Conditions for Voltage Buildup

| # | Condition | Consequence if Violated |
|---|-----------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Presence of **residual magnetism** in the field poles | No initial EMF → no buildup |
| 2 | **Field winding connected in aiding polarity** (so I_f reinforces residual flux) | Negative feedback → voltage decays to zero |
| 3 | **R_f < R_c** (and **N > N_c**) | Insufficient feedback gain → no cumulative buildup |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cumulative Buildup Process — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Step 1 — Rotation Begins:** The armature is driven at speed N by a prime mover. Residual flux φ_r in the poles (≈ 2–5% of full flux) cuts the armature conductors.
2. **Step 2 — Initial EMF:** A small EMF, E₀ = kφ_r N, is induced.
3. **Step 3 — Field Current:** E₀ drives a small field current, I_f = E₀ / (R_f + small armature terms).
4. **Step 4 — Flux Augmentation:** I_f produces additional flux Δφ aiding φ_r. Total flux becomes φ₁ = φ_r + Δφ.
5. **Step 5 — EMF Growth:** Higher flux → higher EMF E₁ = kφ₁ N > E₀.
6. **Step 6 — Positive Feedback Loop:** E₁ drives larger I_f, producing more flux — a **cumulative** self-amplifying cycle.
7. **Step 7 — Saturation Limit:** Iron saturation limits further flux growth, so E and I_f stabilize.
8. **Step 8 — Equilibrium:** Steady state is reached at the **intersection of OCC and FRL**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does the process stop at saturation?**
> In the linear region of the B-H curve, dE/dI_f (slope) > R_f, so the loop gain is positive. Once **dE/dI_f = R_f** (which is exactly the definition of R_c), the gain becomes unity — the system finds its balance. If R_f were even larger, the loop gain would fall below 1, and the perturbation would decay.

## 2.2 Governing Differential Equation of Buildup

Applying KVL to the field circuit at no-load (I_a ≈ 0):

$$E_0 = I_f R_f + L_f \frac{dI_f}{dt}$$

In the **linear region** of OCC, $E_0 = K \cdot I_f$, where K is the slope. Substituting:

$$K I_f = I_f R_f + L_f \frac{dI_f}{dt}$$

$$L_f \frac{dI_f}{dt} = (K - R_f) I_f$$

$$I_f(t) = I_{f0} \cdot e^{\left(\frac{K - R_f}{L_f}\right) t}$$

- If **K > R_f** → exponential growth → voltage **builds up** ✅
- If **K = R_f** → constant current → marginal/equilibrium ⚠️
- If **K < R_f** → exponential decay → voltage **fails to build up** ❌

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Condition / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------|-------------------|
| 1 | Induced EMF | $E_0 = k \phi N$ | V | k = machine constant |
| 2 | Initial Residual EMF | $E_r = k \phi_r N$ | V | φ_r ≈ 2–5% of rated φ |
| 3 | Field Circuit Equation | $V_t = I_f R_f$ (no-load) | V | Steady state |
| 4 | **Critical Field Resistance** | $R_c = \left(\frac{dE_0}{dI_f}\right)_{linear}$ | Ω | Slope of tangent from origin to linear OCC |
| 5 | **Critical Speed** | $N_c = N \times \frac{R_f}{R_c}$ | rpm | For given R_f |
| 6 | Speed-Scaled Critical Resistance | $R_c' = R_c \times \frac{N'}{N}$ | Ω | At new speed N' |
| 7 | Voltage Buildup Test | $R_f < R_c$ AND $N > N_c$ | — | Both must hold |
| 8 | Time Constant of Buildup | $\tau = \frac{L_f}{K - R_f}$ | s | Valid in linear region |
| 9 | OCC at Reduced Speed | $E_0' = E_0 \times \frac{N'}{N}$ | V | Linear scaling with N |
| 10 | FRL Slope | $\tan\theta = R_f$ | Ω | V vs I_f plot |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical distinction:** R_c is determined by the **slope of the OCC**, not by the field winding's designed resistance. R_c is a property of the **magnetic circuit at a given speed**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Generator Commissioning:** Engineers measure R_c on-site to verify the generator can self-excite. If the field rheostat's maximum setting exceeds R_c, a **field circuit breaker or under-voltage relay** is installed to prevent excitation failure.
- **Black-Start Capability:** In hydroelectric and diesel-generator plants, knowledge of critical resistance/speed is essential for **islanded (off-grid) starting** of the power station.
- **Failure Diagnosis:** If a DC generator suddenly stops generating, the first checks are (i) residual magnetism (flash the field), (ii) field circuit resistance, (iii) speed — all three buildup conditions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Critical Speed and Field Resistance

**Starting Premise:**
At a given field current I_f, the induced EMF is directly proportional to speed:

$$E_0 \propto N \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_0 = C \cdot \phi \cdot N$$

where C is a machine constant. The slope of the OCC at the origin in the linear region is:

$$\left(\frac{dE_0}{dI_f}\right)_{\text{origin}} = R_c \text{ (at rated speed } N\text{)}$$

**Step 1 — Scale OCC to New Speed N':**

If the speed is changed from N to N', every point of the OCC scales linearly:

$$E_0'(I_f) = E_0(I_f) \times \frac{N'}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the New Critical Resistance R_c':**

The new tangent slope from origin is:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_c' &= \left(\frac{dE_0'}{dI_f}\right)_{\text{origin}} \\
&= \frac{d}{dI_f}\left[E_0(I_f) \cdot \frac{N'}{N}\right]_{\text{origin}} \\
&= \frac{N'}{N} \cdot \left(\frac{dE_0}{dI_f}\right)_{\text{origin}} \\
&= R_c \cdot \frac{N'}{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Condition for Buildup at Speed N' with Field Resistance R_f:**

The field circuit must satisfy:

$$R_f < R_c' = R_c \cdot \frac{N'}{N}$$

Rearranging to find the **minimum speed** for buildup (the critical speed N_c):

$$\begin{aligned}
R_f &< R_c \cdot \frac{N_c}{N} \\
N_c \cdot R_c &> N \cdot R_f \\
N_c &> N \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_c}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,N_c = N_{\text{rated}} \times \frac{R_f}{R_c}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** The lower the field resistance (or the higher the rated critical resistance), the lower the critical speed — making it easier for the generator to build up voltage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stability of the Operating Point on the OCC

Consider an operating point P (intersection of FRL and OCC) where:
- E_OCC = E_P at I_f = I_fP
- V_FRL = I_fP × R_f = E_P

**Small Perturbation:** Suppose I_f increases by ΔI_f.

Change in OCC voltage: $\Delta E_{\text{OCC}} = \left(\frac{dE_0}{dI_f}\right)_P \cdot \Delta I_f = S_P \cdot \Delta I_f$ (slope at P)

Change in FRL voltage: $\Delta V_{\text{FRL}} = R_f \cdot \Delta I_f$

Net restoring/restoring effect: $\Delta E_{\text{OCC}} - \Delta V_{\text{FRL}} = (S_P - R_f) \cdot \Delta I_f$

- If **S_P < R_f** (in saturation region, OCC flattens): a positive ΔI_f causes the FRL to rise faster than OCC → I_f is pulled back to P → **Stable** ✅
- If **S_P > R_f** (in linear region, OCC steep): a positive ΔI_f causes OCC to rise faster than FRL → I_f grows further → **Unstable** ❌

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Insight:** This is why the stable operating point **must** lie in the **saturation region** of the OCC, where the curve flattens and S_P < R_f. Operation on the linear portion is mathematically an equilibrium but physically unstable.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Critical Speed Calculation

**Problem:** A 220 V, 1500 rpm DC shunt generator has an OCC that is linear up to rated voltage, with a slope of **110 V/A** in the linear region. The field circuit resistance is **80 Ω**. Determine:
1. Critical field resistance at 1500 rpm
2. Critical speed
3. Whether the generator builds up at 1200 rpm
4. If yes, the approximate no-load voltage (assuming saturation data: at I_f = 2.5 A, E = 220 V at 1500 rpm)

**Solution:**

**(1) Critical Field Resistance:**

The slope of the linear part of the OCC = R_c at rated speed:

$$R_c = 110 \, \Omega$$

**(2) Critical Speed:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_c &= N_{\text{rated}} \times \frac{R_f}{R_c} \\
&= 1500 \times \frac{80}{110} \\
&= 1090.9 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(3) Buildup Check at 1200 rpm:**

Since 1200 rpm > N_c = 1090.9 rpm → **Voltage will build up** ✅

**(4) No-Load Voltage at 1200 rpm:**

At 1200 rpm, the OCC scales as:

$$E_0'(I_f) = E_0(I_f) \times \frac{1200}{1500} = 0.8 \cdot E_0(I_f)$$

So the linear part has slope: 110 × 0.8 = 88 V/A. The FRL slope is R_f = 80 Ω < 88 Ω. Buildup occurs.

The intersection of FRL (V = 80 I_f) with the new OCC (scaled) gives the operating point. In the saturation region, using scaled data — at I_f = 2.5 A, the scaled EMF is 220 × 0.8 = 176 V; FRL voltage is 2.5 × 80 = 200 V. So saturation intersection lies slightly below 2.5 A, giving **V ≈ 170–175 V** (exact value requires full OCC data).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Voltage Buildup Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Residual Flux Phi_r] --> B[Initial EMF E_r at Speed N]
    B --> C[Field Current I_f]
    C --> D[Additional Flux Delta Phi]
    D --> E[Total Flux Phi increases]
    E --> F[Higher EMF E1 greater than E_r]
    F --> C
    F --> G{Saturation Region Reached?}
    G -- No --> C
    G -- Yes --> H[Steady State: Intersection of OCC and FRL]
    H --> I[Stable No Load Voltage V0]
    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style H fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
```

## 4.2 OCC with Critical Field Resistance Cases

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Speed_N[Rated Speed N]
        O1[Origin] --- P1[Linear Region]
        P1 --- K1[Knee Point]
        K1 --- S1[Saturation Region]
    end
    subgraph Cases[Three Field Resistance Cases]
        C1[Rf less than Rc: FRL is shallower than tangent — INTERSECTS OCC in saturation — VOLTAGE BUILDS UP]
        C2[Rf equals Rc: FRL is TANGENT to linear OCC — MARGINAL case — barely builds up]
        C3[Rf greater than Rc: FRL is STEEPER than tangent — NO intersection — VOLTAGE FAILS to build up]
    end
    style C1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style C2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style C3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

## 4.3 Effect of Speed on Critical Resistance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OCC at Speed N1] --> B[Slope equals Rc1]
    C[OCC at Speed N2 less than N1] --> D[Slope equals Rc2 less than Rc1]
    B --> E[Lower Speed Reduces Critical Resistance]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Fixed Rf may exceed Rc2 — Buildup Fails at Low Speed]
    style F fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style E fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Self-Excited DC Shunt Generator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PM[Prime Mover] --> ARM[Armature Rotation at Speed N]
    ARM --> EMF1[Induced EMF E0 proportional to Phi and N]
    RES[Residual Flux Phi_r] --> EMF1
    EMF1 --> FLDC[Field Circuit: Rf in series with Lf]
    FLDC --> IFI[Field Current If]
    IFI --> FLX[Additional Flux Delta Phi]
    FLX --> TOT[Total Flux Phi = Phi_r + Delta Phi]
    TOT --> EMF1
    EMF1 --> OCC{Find Intersection with FRL on OCC}
    OCC --> V0[No Load Voltage V0]
    style PM fill:#cce5ff
    style RES fill:#fff4cc
    style V0 fill:#d4edda
```
