# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define critical field resistance and critical speed as applied to a self-excited DC shunt generator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Critical Field Resistance (R_c):** It is the maximum value of the field circuit resistance at a given speed, above which the shunt generator fails to build up voltage. Numerically, R_c equals the slope of the tangent drawn from the origin to the linear portion of the Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC). [1.5 marks]
- **Critical Speed (N_c):** It is the minimum speed at which a self-excited DC shunt generator, with a given field resistance R_f, will just succeed in building up voltage. Below N_c, the induced EMF is too small to overcome the IR drop and saturation, and voltage fails to build up. The relation is N_c = N × (R_f / R_c). [1.5 marks]

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the essential conditions required for voltage buildup in a self-excited DC shunt generator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Residual magnetism** must be present in the field poles. [1 mark]
2. The **field winding must be connected in the correct polarity** so that the current due to induced EMF produces flux that **aids** the residual flux (positive feedback). [1 mark]
3. The **field circuit resistance R_f must be less than the critical field resistance R_c**, and the **speed N must be greater than the critical speed N_c**. [1 mark]

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neatly labeled Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC), explain the concept of **critical field resistance** of a DC shunt generator. Discuss what happens to voltage buildup when the field circuit resistance is (i) less than R_c, (ii) equal to R_c, and (iii) greater than R_c.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of OCC:** The OCC is the plot of open-circuit EMF E₀ versus field current I_f at a constant rated speed. It has three regions — linear (initial), knee, and saturation.

**Critical Field Resistance:** It is the slope of the tangent drawn from the origin to the linear portion of the OCC. The line from origin with slope R_c just touches the linear part of the OCC at one point.

**Three Cases:**

- **Case (i): R_f < R_c** — The Field Resistance Line (FRL), with slope R_f, is *less steep* than the tangent. It intersects the OCC in the saturation region. The intersection point gives the **stable no-load voltage**, and voltage buildup occurs successfully.

- **Case (ii): R_f = R_c** — The FRL is *tangent* to the linear portion of the OCC. The system is at marginal equilibrium. Theoretically voltage just builds up, but the operating point lies in the unstable linear region — the voltage is unstable and highly sensitive to speed fluctuations.

- **Case (iii): R_f > R_c** — The FRL is *steeper* than the tangent and does not intersect the OCC. The cumulative buildup process decays, and the machine **fails to build up voltage** (terminal voltage remains at the small residual value).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (–2 marks):** Students often state R_c is "the slope of OCC" — this is **wrong**. R_c is the slope of the **tangent from origin** to the linear part of OCC. In the truly linear region, these coincide, but in general, they are different.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 250 V, 1000 rpm DC shunt generator has the following OCC at rated speed:

| I_f (A) | 0 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 3.0 |
|---------|----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| E₀ (V)  | 5  | 100 | 190 | 230 | 252 | 264 | 272 |

The field circuit resistance is 100 Ω. Find: (i) the critical field resistance, (ii) the voltage to which the machine will build up, and (iii) the critical speed.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Critical Field Resistance:**

From the linear part of the OCC, between I_f = 0.5 A and 1.0 A:

$$R_c = \frac{\Delta E_0}{\Delta I_f} = \frac{190 - 100}{1.0 - 0.5} = \frac{90}{0.5} = 180 \, \Omega$$

**(ii) Voltage Buildup:**

FRL equation: V = 100 · I_f

Test intersection with OCC data:
- I_f = 2.0 A: FRL = 200 V, OCC = 252 V → FRL below OCC
- I_f = 2.5 A: FRL = 250 V, OCC = 264 V → FRL below OCC (close)
- I_f = 3.0 A: FRL = 300 V, OCC = 272 V → FRL above OCC

Intersection between I_f = 2.5 and 3.0 A. Linear interpolation:

At I_f = 2.5 A: Difference = 264 − 250 = 14 V (OCC above FRL)
At I_f = 3.0 A: Difference = 272 − 300 = −28 V (FRL above OCC)

$$I_f = 2.5 + 0.5 \times \frac{14}{14+28} = 2.5 + 0.167 = 2.667 \, A$$

$$V_0 = 2.667 \times 100 \approx 266.7 \, V$$

**The generator builds up to approximately 267 V.** [3.5 marks]

**(iii) Critical Speed:**

$$N_c = N_{\text{rated}} \times \frac{R_f}{R_c} = 1000 \times \frac{100}{180} = 555.6 \, \text{rpm}$$

**The critical speed is approximately 556 rpm.** [2 marks]

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **critical speed** of a DC shunt generator. Derive the relationship between critical speed and field circuit resistance. Explain the consequences if the generator is operated below critical speed.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Critical speed N_c is the minimum speed at which the generator can build up its rated voltage for a given field circuit resistance R_f.

**Derivation:**

The induced EMF at a given I_f is directly proportional to speed: E ∝ N. Hence, when speed changes from N to N', the OCC scales as:

$$E_0'(I_f) = E_0(I_f) \times \frac{N'}{N}$$

The slope of the linear part of OCC at the new speed N' is:

$$R_c' = R_c \times \frac{N'}{N}$$

For voltage to build up at speed N', the field resistance must satisfy:

$$R_f < R_c' = R_c \times \frac{N'}{N}$$

The minimum speed N' for which this holds (equality case) is the critical speed:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_f &= R_c \times \frac{N_c}{N} \\
N_c &= N \times \frac{R_f}{R_c}
\end{aligned}$$

[4 marks for derivation]

**Consequences of Operating Below N_c:**
- The loop gain (K − R_f) becomes negative (since K < R_f in the linear region at reduced speed), causing any small perturbation in field current to **decay exponentially** rather than grow.
- The terminal voltage collapses to a small value determined only by residual flux.
- The generator cannot self-excite and produces no useful output.
- In practical terms, the prime mover must accelerate the generator above N_c for self-excitation to initiate. [3 marks]

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 200 V, 1500 rpm DC shunt generator has an OCC that is linear up to rated voltage. The OCC slope in the linear region is 100 V/A, and the field circuit resistance is 80 Ω. Determine: (i) the no-load voltage at 1500 rpm, (ii) the critical field resistance at 1500 rpm, (iii) the no-load voltage at 1200 rpm, and (iv) the critical speed.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** R_c (slope of linear OCC) = 100 V/A, R_f = 80 Ω, N_rated = 1500 rpm.

**(i) No-load Voltage at 1500 rpm:**

In the linear region, the FRL slope is 80 V/A, and OCC slope is 100 V/A. Since R_f < R_c, voltage builds up. The operating point must lie in the saturation region (stable). With only the linear OCC data given, and assuming the OCC is linear until the rated 200 V:

At I_f where V = 200 V: I_f = 200/100 = 2.0 A (from linear OCC)
FRL voltage at I_f = 2.0 A: V_FRL = 80 × 2.0 = 160 V

Since FRL (160 V) < OCC (200 V) at this point, the actual operating point lies at higher I_f in the saturation knee region. If we assume the OCC saturates exactly at 200 V and remains flat, then equilibrium is at V = 200 V, I_f = 2.5 A (from FRL: 200/80 = 2.5 A).

**Approximate no-load voltage ≈ 200 V** (limited by saturation). [2 marks]

**(ii) Critical Field Resistance at 1500 rpm:**

$$R_c = \left(\frac{dE_0}{dI_f}\right)_{\text{linear}} = 100 \, \Omega$$ [1 mark]

**(iii) No-load Voltage at 1200 rpm:**

OCC slope at 1200 rpm:

$$R_c' = 100 \times \frac{1200}{1500} = 80 \, \Omega$$

Since R_f = 80 Ω = R_c', the FRL is **tangent** to the linear OCC at 1200 rpm. The machine is at the **threshold** of buildup. 

In practice, the voltage will be unstable and limited to a low value on the linear portion. The exact no-load voltage is the **critical EMF** at 1200 rpm:

$$E_{0,\text{critical}} = I_f \times R_f$$

Since the FRL touches the linear OCC tangentially, with both having effective origin intersection, the voltage is indeterminate in linear approximation; in practice, the voltage builds up only to a small value determined by the tangent point. [2 marks]

**(iv) Critical Speed:**

$$N_c = N_{\text{rated}} \times \frac{R_f}{R_c} = 1500 \times \frac{80}{100} = 1200 \, \text{rpm}$$ [2 marks]

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (–2 marks):** A common mistake is using the formula N_c = N × (R_c / R_f) — the **ratio is inverted**. Always remember: higher R_f requires higher N_c, so the formula must have R_f in the numerator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Voltage Buildup**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **Voltage buildup** = cumulative self-excitation of DC shunt generator from residual magnetism.
- [ ] **Critical Field Resistance (R_c)** = slope of tangent from origin to linear OCC at given speed.
- [ ] **Critical Speed (N_c)** = minimum speed for buildup at given R_f.
- [ ] **Buildup requires:** residual flux + correct polarity + R_f < R_c + N > N_c.

### Key Formulas (Memorize)
- [ ] $N_c = N_{\text{rated}} \times \dfrac{R_f}{R_c}$
- [ ] $R_c' = R_c \times \dfrac{N'}{N}$ (scaling with speed)
- [ ] Loop gain condition: $K - R_f > 0$ for buildup
- [ ] Time constant: $\tau = \dfrac{L_f}{K - R_f}$

### Common Numerical Traps
- [ ] **Stable point must be in saturation region** — FRL intersects OCC in saturation, not linear region.
- [ ] When R_f = R_c exactly, the FRL is tangent to the linear OCC — operation is marginal/unstable.
- [ ] OCC scales linearly with speed: E ∝ N at constant I_f.
- [ ] Increasing field resistance → increases critical speed requirement.
- [ ] Decreasing speed → decreases available R_c → may cause R_f to exceed R_c → no buildup.

### Real-World Diagnostic Checks
- [ ] Generator fails to build up? → Check residual magnetism → check field polarity → check R_f vs R_c → check speed.
- [ ] "Flashing the field" = temporarily applying external DC to restore residual magnetism.

### Examiner's Favorite Questions
- [ ] Draw OCC, mark linear/knee/saturation regions, show FRL for 3 cases.
- [ ] Compute R_c from given OCC data (use slope of linear portion).
- [ ] Compute N_c from R_f and R_c.
- [ ] Predict whether voltage will build up at a new speed.
- [ ] Find operating voltage from FRL-OCC intersection (interpolation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Load Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load Characteristics (External Characteristics)** of a DC generator is the curve plotted between the **terminal voltage (V)** at the output terminals and the **load current (I_L)** delivered to the external load, while the machine is driven at its **rated speed (N)** and the **field excitation is kept constant** at its rated value.

In other words, the load characteristic describes how the terminal voltage of a DC generator responds as we progressively increase the electrical load connected across its terminals, from no-load (open circuit) to full-load conditions.

The mathematical relationship governing this behavior for a DC generator is:

$$V = E_g - I_a R_a$$

Where:
- $V$ = Terminal voltage (volts)
- $E_g$ = Generated EMF / Back EMF (volts)
- $I_a$ = Armature current (amperes)
- $R_a$ = Armature circuit resistance (ohms)

## 1.2 The Four Canonical Load Characteristics

Since the generated EMF equation $E_g \propto \Phi \omega$ depends on the flux $\Phi$ which itself depends on the field current $I_f$ (which in turn depends on how the field winding is connected), we get **four distinct load characteristics** based on the type of DC generator:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** Module 1 explicitly mandates the study of **load characteristics of (i) Separately Excited DC Generator, (ii) Self-Excited Shunt DC Generator, (iii) Series DC Generator, and (iv) Compound DC Generator (Cumulative & Differential).**

| Generator Type | Field Winding Connection | Behavior of Flux with Load |
|---|---|---|
| Separately Excited | External DC source | Independent of load |
| Shunt (Self-Excited) | Across armature terminals | Slightly decreases |
| Series (Self-Excited) | In series with armature | Increases linearly |
| Compound (Cumulative) | Both shunt + series aiding | Net slight decrease |
| Compound (Differential) | Both shunt + series opposing | Net strong decrease |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a water pump (your DC generator) pushing water through a pipe (your armature winding) to a tap (the load). When you open the tap more (increase load current), two things happen:
> 1. **Voltage Drop in the Pipe:** Friction in the pipe causes pressure loss → this is analogous to the $I_a R_a$ drop in the armature.
> 2. **Drop in Pump Pressure:** As more water flows, the pump's motor has to work harder and its speed drops slightly, reducing pressure → analogous to **armature reaction** which weakens the main field flux.
>
> The **load characteristic curve** is essentially the "pressure vs. flow rate" plot at the tap outlet!

## 1.4 Voltage Regulation — The Key Performance Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Regulation (VR)** is defined as the percentage change in terminal voltage from no-load to full-load, expressed as a fraction of the full-load voltage. It is the **single most important metric** used to compare load characteristics of different DC generators.

$$\text{VR (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$

A **lower** voltage regulation indicates a "stiffer" or "better" generator (terminal voltage remains nearly constant regardless of load). A **higher** regulation indicates a "softer" machine whose output voltage drops significantly under load.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Governing Equations

### 2.1.1 The Generated EMF Equation (Universal for All DC Machines)

The EMF generated in the armature of a DC machine when rotating at speed $N$ rpm with flux $\Phi$ per pole is:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

Where the variables are summarized below. This is the foundation upon which all load characteristics are built.

### 2.1.2 The Armature Current vs. Load Current Relationship

For a generator, by Kirchhoff's Current Law at the armature node:

$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} \quad \text{(for Shunt/Compound)}$$
$$I_a = I_L \quad \text{(for Separately Excited and Series)}$$

### 2.1.3 Why Does Terminal Voltage Drop Under Load?

Three physical phenomena cause $V_{FL} < V_{NL}$:

1. **Ohmic Drop in Armature Resistance:** $I_a \cdot R_a$ → Direct IR loss
2. **Brush Contact Drop:** Typically $2 \times V_b$ (constant ~2V for carbon brushes)
3. **Armature Reaction (Demagnetizing Effect):** Load current creates a cross-magnetizing MMF that distorts and weakens the main field flux $\Phi$ → reducing $E_g$

For series generators, an **additional saturation effect** causes $E_g$ to increase non-linearly with $I_a$.

## 2.2 Detailed Load Characteristic of Each Type

### 2.2.1 Separately Excited DC Generator
- Flux $\Phi$ = **constant** (independent of $I_a$)
- $E_g$ = constant at fixed speed
- Terminal voltage: $V = E_g - I_a R_a$
- Curve: **Drooping straight line** (linear drop with slope $-R_a$)

### 2.2.2 Shunt (Self-Excited) DC Generator
- $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$, which slightly decreases as $V$ drops
- Combined effect: $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ with $\Phi$ slightly reduced
- Curve: **Slightly drooping** (less steep than separately excited due to compounding of two effects)

### 2.2.3 Series DC Generator
- Field current $I_f = I_a = I_L$ → flux rises with load until saturation
- $V = E_g - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ where $R_{se}$ is series field resistance
- Initial portion: **Rising curve** (magnetization curve dominates)
- After saturation: **Drooping** (IR drop dominates)
- S-curve shape, **never used for constant voltage applications**

### 2.2.4 Compound DC Generator

**Cumulative Compound (Series aiding Shunt):**
- Series flux adds to shunt flux → compensates for armature reaction
- Can be designed as **flat compounded** (no-load = full-load voltage), **over-compounded** (FL > NL voltage), or **under-compounded**

**Differential Compound (Series opposing Shunt):**
- Series flux opposes shunt flux → strong droop
- Rarely used; special applications like arc welding where current limiting is required

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 80% of numerical problems in KTU University Exams on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | Generated EMF equation | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ | Terminal voltage (generator) | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $V = E_g - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | Terminal voltage (series generator) | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | KCL at armature (shunt/compound) | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | $I_a = I_L$ | KCL for separately excited / series | Amperes (A) |
| 6 | $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ | Shunt field current | Amperes (A) |
| 7 | $\text{VR (\%)} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | Voltage regulation | Percentage |
| 8 | $E_g \propto \Phi N$ | Fundamental EMF proportionality | V·s/rev |
| 9 | $P_{dev} = E_g \cdot I_a$ | Power developed in armature | Watts (W) |
| 10 | $R_a(\text{hot}) = R_a(\text{cold}) [1 + \alpha_{0}(T_2 - T_1)]$ | Hot resistance correction | Ohms (Ω) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $Z$ = total armature conductors, $P$ = number of poles, $A$ = number of parallel paths ($A = P$ for lap, $A = 2$ for wave), $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $N$ = speed (rpm).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Voltage regulation** determines whether a generator is suitable for sensitive loads (electronics, motors) — low VR is critical.
- **Cumulative compound generators** are the workhorses of railway traction, elevators, and DC welding (flat-compounded design).
- **Series generators** find application as **boosters** in feeders and as **constant-current sources** for arc lamps and series street lighting (historically).
- **Shunt generators** are used in **battery charging** applications and small DC power supplies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Generated EMF Equation $E_g$

### Step 1 — Flux Cut Per Revolution
Consider one armature conductor. In one revolution, it cuts a flux equal to $P \cdot \Phi$ webers (since it passes under $P$ poles, each of flux $\Phi$).

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = P \Phi \quad \text{(Weber)}$$

### Step 2 — Time for One Revolution
The conductor makes one revolution in $60/N$ seconds (where $N$ is in rpm).

$$\text{Time per revolution} = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

### Step 3 — Average EMF Induced Per Conductor (Faraday's Law)
By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the average EMF induced per conductor:

$$\text{EMF per conductor} = \frac{\text{Flux cut}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{P\Phi}{60/N} = \frac{P\Phi N}{60}$$

### Step 4 — Total EMF in the Armature
There are $Z$ conductors in total. Since they are distributed and connected in $A$ parallel paths, the number of conductors in **series per parallel path** is $Z/A$.

$$\text{Total EMF} = (\text{EMF per conductor}) \times (\text{Conductors in series per path})$$

### Step 5 — Final Equation
Substituting the values:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_g &= \frac{P\Phi N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A} \\
E_g &= \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** This is the **fundamental equation of all DC machines**. The same formula holds for motors, where $E_g$ is called the **back EMF**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Voltage Regulation Expression

### Step 1 — Define No-Load Condition
At no-load ($I_L = 0$), assuming separately excited with constant field current $I_f$:
- For separately excited: $I_a = 0$, so $V_{NL} = E_g$
- For self-excited shunt: $I_a = I_{sh,NL}$ (small), so $V_{NL} \approx E_g - I_{sh}R_a$ (negligible)

### Step 2 — Define Full-Load Condition
At full load, $I_L = I_{FL}$:

$$V_{FL} = E_g - I_{FL} R_a - V_{brushes} - \text{(armature reaction effect)}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Change in Voltage
The difference between no-load and full-load voltage:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{NL} - V_{FL} &= E_g - \left[E_g - I_{FL}R_a - \text{(other drops)}\right] \\
&= I_{FL} R_a + \text{(armature reaction drop in V)} + V_{brushes}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Express as a Percentage of Full-Load Voltage

$$\boxed{\text{VR (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **assumptions** clearly: constant speed, constant field excitation, no armature reaction (unless specified), and cold winding resistance (unless "hot" is specified).

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

> **Problem (Typical KTU 3-mark):** A shunt generator delivers 100 A at 250 V. The armature resistance is $0.1 \, \Omega$ and the shunt field resistance is $250 \, \Omega$. Find the generated EMF and the armature current.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Compute the shunt field current:
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Apply KCL at the armature node:
$$\begin{aligned}
I_a &= I_L + I_{sh} \\
I_a &= 100 + 1 \\
I_a &= 101 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the armature ohmic drop:
$$I_a R_a = 101 \times 0.1 = 10.1 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4:** Apply KVL around the armature loop:
$$\begin{aligned}
E_g &= V + I_a R_a \\
E_g &= 250 + 10.1 \\
E_g &= 260.1 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $I_a = 101$ A and $E_g = 260.1$ V. **Voltage regulation** = $(260.1 - 250)/250 \times 100 = 4.04\%$ — this is a typical "good" regulation value for a shunt generator.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define voltage regulation of a DC generator. Why is low voltage regulation desirable in practical applications?**

**Model Answer:**
Voltage regulation (VR) of a DC generator is defined as the percentage change in terminal voltage from no-load to full-load, with respect to the full-load voltage, while the speed and field excitation remain constant.

$$\text{VR (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$

A low voltage regulation is desirable because it ensures that the terminal voltage remains nearly constant regardless of load variations. This is critical for sensitive equipment like computers, communication systems, electric motors, and lighting circuits, where fluctuating voltage can cause malfunction, reduced lifespan, or poor performance. Generators with low VR are said to have "stiff" voltage characteristics.

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Compare the load characteristics of a shunt and a series DC generator with suitable sketches.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Shunt Generator | Series Generator |
|---|---|---|
| Field connection | Across armature | In series with armature |
| Field current | $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ (nearly const.) | $I_{se} = I_L$ (varies with load) |
| Flux behavior | Almost constant | Increases with load |
| Load characteristic | Slightly drooping | Rising then drooping (S-curve) |
| Voltage regulation | Good (low, ~5-10%) | Poor (very high) |
| Application | DC power supply | Boosters, arc lamps |

In a **shunt generator**, flux remains nearly constant since $I_{sh}$ depends on terminal voltage, which itself does not vary much. Hence $E_g$ stays constant, and $V$ drops only due to $I_a R_a$, producing a slightly drooping curve.

In a **series generator**, flux rises with load (until magnetic saturation), so $E_g$ increases. Initially, $V$ rises; after saturation, the IR drop dominates and $V$ falls, giving a characteristic rising-then-falling S-shape.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the load characteristic of a **cumulative compound DC generator**. Discuss its three classifications: **under-compounded, flat-compounded, and over-compounded**, with the help of characteristic curves.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 250 V, 50 kW shunt generator has an armature resistance of $0.06 \, \Omega$ and a shunt field resistance of $100 \, \Omega$. Calculate: (i) the armature current at full load, (ii) the generated EMF at full load, (iii) the voltage regulation assuming no-load terminal voltage equals rated voltage, and (iv) the power developed in the armature.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **cumulative compound DC generator** has both a shunt field winding (connected across the armature) and a series field winding (in series with the armature) wound on the same pole cores, in such a way that their MMFs **add up** (i.e., the series flux aids the shunt flux).

**Equivalent Circuit:** (Refer to the schematic in Section 4.2 — the series field $R_{se}$ is in series with the armature, and the shunt field $R_{sh}$ is connected in parallel across the output terminals.)

**Operating Principle:**
At no-load, only the shunt field is active, producing flux $\Phi_{sh}$. As load increases, the series field also carries armature current, adding flux $\Phi_{se}$ to the existing flux. The total flux becomes:
$$\Phi_{total} = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se} - \Phi_{ar(\text{demag})}$$

where $\Phi_{ar}$ is the armature reaction demagnetizing component. If $\Phi_{se}$ is properly designed, it can **exactly compensate** for the $I_a R_a$ drop and the armature reaction effect.

**Three Classifications based on Number of Series Turns ($N_{se}$):**

1. **Under-Compounded:** Series turns too few. Net flux decreases slightly with load → $V_{FL} < V_{NL}$ (mild droop).
2. **Flat-Compounded:** Series turns designed precisely. $V_{FL} \approx V_{NL}$ (horizontal curve).
3. **Over-Compounded:** Series turns more than needed. $V_{FL} > V_{NL}$ (rising curve).

**Applications:** DC traction motors (locomotives, trams), elevators, welding sets — wherever "stiff" voltage characteristic is needed.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $V = 250$ V, $P = 50$ kW, $R_a = 0.06 \, \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 100 \, \Omega$

**Step (i): Full-load armature current**
Full-load current delivered to load:
$$I_L = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{50{,}000}{250} = 200 \text{ A}$$

Shunt field current:
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5 \text{ A}$$

Armature current (KCL):
$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 200 + 2.5 = 202.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step (ii): Generated EMF at full load**
Armature ohmic drop:
$$I_a R_a = 202.5 \times 0.06 = 12.15 \text{ V}$$

Generated EMF:
$$\begin{aligned}
E_g &= V + I_a R_a \\
E_g &= 250 + 12.15 \\
E_g &= 262.15 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (iii): Voltage Regulation**
Since $V_{NL} = E_g = 262.15$ V (neglecting $I_{sh}R_a$ at no-load) and $V_{FL} = 250$ V:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{VR (\%)} &= \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{262.15 - 250}{250} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{12.15}{250} \times 100 \\
&= 4.86\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (iv): Power developed in armature**
$$P_{dev} = E_g \times I_a = 262.15 \times 202.5 = 53{,}085.4 \text{ W} \approx 53.09 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mistakes to Avoid):**
> 1. **Forgetting $I_{sh}$** — Many students use $I_a = I_L$ for shunt generators. **Always add $I_{sh}$.**
> 2. **Using $P = V \cdot I_L$** for power developed — That gives output power. Power developed uses $E_g \cdot I_a$.
> 3. **Confusing hot vs cold resistance** — If problem mentions "75°C", apply temperature correction formula.
> 4. **Brush contact drop** — If specified (typically 2V total), add to $I_a R_a$ drop. Don't ignore it!

---

### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **EMF equation of a DC generator** $E_g = \dfrac{P\Phi ZN}{60A}$ starting from Faraday's law. State clearly the meaning of every symbol and its units.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **separately excited DC generator** supplies a load current of 80 A at 220 V. The armature has 600 conductors, 4 poles, and is wave-wound. The flux per pole is 0.05 Wb and the speed is 1000 rpm. The armature resistance is $0.1 \, \Omega$. Calculate: (i) the generated EMF, (ii) the voltage drop in the armature, (iii) the efficiency at this load (neglecting field and mechanical losses), and (iv) comment on the voltage regulation.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation:** (Refer to the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1 above.)

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $E_g$ | Generated EMF | Volts (V) |
| $P$ | Number of poles | Dimensionless |
| $\Phi$ | Flux per pole | Weber (Wb) |
| $Z$ | Total armature conductors | Dimensionless |
| $N$ | Speed of armature | rpm (rev/min) |
| $A$ | Number of parallel paths | $P$ for lap, $2$ for wave |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $I_L = 80$ A, $V = 220$ V, $Z = 600$, $P = 4$, wave-wound ($A = 2$), $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb, $N = 1000$ rpm, $R_a = 0.1 \, \Omega$

**Step (i): Generated EMF**
$$\begin{aligned}
E_g &= \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 0.05 \times 600 \times 1000}{60 \times 2} \\
&= \frac{120{,}000}{120} \\
&= 1000 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The EMF equation gives a hypothetical value **without considering terminal voltage constraints**. In practice, if $E_g > V + I_a R_a$, the machine is operating as a generator.

**Step (ii): Voltage drop in armature**
For separately excited: $I_a = I_L = 80$ A.
$$I_a R_a = 80 \times 0.1 = 8 \text{ V}$$

**Verification (consistency check):**
$$V = E_g - I_a R_a = 1000 - 8 = 992 \text{ V} \neq 220 \text{ V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Observation:** The given values $V = 220$ V and $E_g = 1000$ V are **inconsistent with a 1000 rpm operation**. This suggests the actual operating speed would be much lower, OR the data represents a design problem. For KTU purposes, **report the calculated values as obtained and add a comment.**

Realistic operating speed at $V = 220$ V:
$$N_{actual} = 1000 \times \frac{220 + 8}{1000} = 228 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step (iii): Efficiency (neglecting field & mechanical losses)**
Output power:
$$P_{out} = V \times I_L = 220 \times 80 = 17{,}600 \text{ W} = 17.6 \text{ kW}$$

Copper losses (armature only):
$$P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a = 80^2 \times 0.1 = 640 \text{ W}$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{cu}} = \frac{17{,}600}{17{,}600 + 640} = 96.49\%$$

**Step (iv): Comment on Voltage Regulation**
For separately excited generator:
$$\text{VR (\%)} = \frac{E_g - V}{V} \times 100 = \frac{1000 - 220}{220} \times 100 = 354.5\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This extremely high regulation confirms the inconsistency in the problem data. In a real separately excited machine at constant speed, VR is typically only **3–8%**. The student must recognize this and state the underlying issue in the answer for full marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **General KTU Valuation Pitfalls (All Long-Answer Questions on Load Characteristics):**
> 1. **Neglecting armature reaction** when it should be included (often given as "voltage drops by X volts due to armature reaction").
> 2. **Wrong parallel paths count:** Lap winding → $A = P$; Wave winding → $A = 2$. Confusing these gives **wrong EMF** and **wrong VR**.
> 3. **Sign errors** in $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ vs. $V = E_g + I_a R_a$ (the latter is for motors, not generators).
> 4. **Not specifying units** in final answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 5. **Skipping the circuit diagram** for 7-mark descriptive questions — at least 1–2 marks are reserved for it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam!**

### 📌 Key Definitions
- ✅ **Load characteristic:** Curve of $V$ vs. $I_L$ at rated speed and rated field current.
- ✅ **Voltage regulation:** $\text{VR (\%)} = (V_{NL} - V_{FL})/V_{FL} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Generated EMF:** $E_g = P\Phi ZN / (60A)$.
- ✅ **Armature current (shunt):** $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$.
- ✅ **Armature current (separately excited/series):** $I_a = I_L$.

### 📌 Generator Comparison at a Glance
- ✅ **Best VR (lowest):** Separately Excited > Shunt > Cumulative Compound > Series > Differential Compound.
- ✅ **Best for traction:** Cumulative Compound (especially flat-compounded).
- ✅ **Best for arc lamps / boosters:** Series Generator.
- ✅ **Best for battery charging:** Shunt Generator.
- ✅ **Rising characteristic:** Only Series Generator (initial portion).

### 📌 Critical Numerical Precautions
- ✅ **Lap winding:** $A = P$ | **Wave winding:** $A = 2$.
- ✅ **For shunt generators:** Always compute $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ first.
- ✅ **Power developed in armature:** $P_{dev} = E_g \cdot I_a$ (NOT $V \cdot I_L$).
- ✅ **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = P_{out} / (P_{out} + \text{all losses})$.
- ✅ **Brush drop:** Include if specified (typically 2V total for carbon brushes).

### 📌 Common Curve Shapes (Must Memorize for Sketching)
- ✅ **Separately Excited:** Linear drooping straight line.
- ✅ **Shunt:** Mildly drooping, slightly curved.
- ✅ **Series:** S-shaped (rise, then droop after saturation).
- ✅ **Cumulative Compound:** Flat / over / under-compounded (slight droop or rise).
- ✅ **Differential Compound:** Sharply drooping.

### 📌 One-Line Exam Mantras
- 🧠 *"Separately Excited gives a STRAIGHT LINE drop with slope $-R_a$."*
- 🧠 *"Cumulative Compound is the TRACTION KING."*
- 🧠 *"Series Generator is a CURRENT SOURCE, not a voltage source."*
- 🧠 *"In a generator: $E_g > V$ always. In a motor: $V > E_g$ always."*
- 🧠 *"Lower the VR, stiffer the generator."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
