# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Losses — Structured Logic

The losses in a DC machine occur in **two distinct physical regions**: the **electrical (stationary/rotating conductors)** and the **mechanical (rotating parts + core)**. The classification given below is the one universally expected in KTU valuation scripts.

### A. Copper Losses (Variable Losses — Load Dependent)

These are **I²R losses** and scale with the **square of the load current**.

**(i) Armature Copper Loss**

$$P_a = I_a^2 \, R_a \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

where $I_a$ is the armature current and $R_a$ is the armature circuit resistance (including brush-contact drop).

**(ii) Field Copper Loss (Shunt + Series)**

For a **shunt machine**: $P_{sh} = I_{sh}^2 \, R_{sh} = V \, I_{sh}$ (constant, independent of load).

For a **series machine**: $P_{se} = I_{se}^2 \, R_{se}$ (variable with load).

**(iii) Brush Contact Loss**

Modelled as an additional drop of ~2 V per brush pair. $P_{brush} = 2 \, I_a$ Watts (for carbon/graphite brushes).

### B. Iron / Core Losses (Constant Losses — Speed & Voltage Dependent)

These occur in the **armature core** (the only part experiencing alternating flux).

**(i) Hysteresis Loss**

$$P_h = \eta_{hyst} \, B_{max}^{1.6} \, f \, V \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

where $\eta_{hyst}$ is the **Steinmetz hysteresis coefficient**, $B_{max}$ is the maximum flux density, $f$ is the supply frequency, and $V$ is the volume of the core.

**(ii) Eddy Current Loss**

$$P_e = \eta_{eddy} \, B_{max}^{2} \, f^2 \, t^2 \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

where $t$ is the lamination thickness (usually **0.35 mm** or **0.5 mm** silicon-steel laminations are used to minimise $P_e$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Laminate the Core?**
> Because $P_e \propto t^2$, splitting the core into thin insulated sheets reduces eddy-current loss quadratically — this is why DC machine armatures are never built from a solid iron block.

### C. Mechanical Losses (Constant Losses)

**(i) Friction Loss** — at bearings and brush–commutator interface.
**(ii) Windage Loss** — air friction on the rotating armature and its fan blades.

**Total Rotational / Mechanical Losses:** $P_m = P_f + P_w$ (approximately **constant** at constant speed).

### D. Stray Load Losses (SLL)

Additional losses under load due to:
- **Distortion of main flux** by armature reaction.
- **Local short-circuits** in the commutator during commutation.
- **Eddy currents** in the conductors (not the core).

By **KTU/Kirchhoff convention**, SLL is taken as **1 % of the output power** for large machines, or as **0.5–1 % of full-load output** for small machines.

## 2.2 The Power-Flow Philosophy

A DC machine acts as a **converter** between electrical and mechanical energy. Tracing the power from input to output reveals **three distinct stages** with losses at each transition:

| Stage | Generator Mode | Motor Mode |
|-------|---------------|------------|
| **Input** | Mechanical power $P_{mech,in} = T \cdot \omega$ | Electrical power $P_{elec,in} = V \cdot I_L$ |
| **Stage 1** | Subtraction of Mechanical losses → $P_{dev}$ (developed) | Subtraction of Electrical losses → input to armature |
| **Stage 2** | Subtraction of Iron losses → gross $P_g$ (generated EMF × $I_a$) | Subtraction of Iron losses → mechanical developed power |
| **Stage 3** | Subtraction of Copper losses → $P_{out} = V \cdot I_L$ | Subtraction of Copper losses → $P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Constant Loss vs Variable Loss:**
> - **Constant Losses** ($P_c$): Iron loss + Mechanical loss + Shunt-field copper loss. Independent of load.
> - **Variable Losses** ($P_v$): Armature copper loss + Series-field copper loss. Proportional to $I^2$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ | Input electrical power (motor) | W |
| 2 | $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Armature copper loss | W |
| 3 | $P_{cu,sh} = V^2 / R_{sh}$ | Shunt-field copper loss | W |
| 4 | $P_{iron} = P_h + P_e$ | Total iron loss (constant) | W |
| 5 | $P_{mech} = P_f + P_w$ | Total mechanical loss (constant) | W |
| 6 | $P_{stray} = 0.01 \times P_{out}$ | Stray load loss (assumed) | W |
| 7 | $P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a$ | Power developed in armature (both modes) | W |
| 8 | $\eta_{gen} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V I_L}{T \cdot \omega}$ | Generator efficiency | % |
| 9 | $\eta_{mot} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{T_{sh} \cdot \omega}{V \cdot I_L}$ | Motor efficiency | % |
| 10 | $P_c = P_{iron} + P_{mech} + P_{cu,sh}$ | Total constant losses | W |
| 11 | $P_v(x) = x^2 \cdot P_{v,FL}$ | Variable loss at load fraction $x$ | W |
| 12 | $\eta_{max}$ when $P_c = x^2 P_{v,FL}$ | Condition for max efficiency | — |
| 13 | $x_{max\eta} = \sqrt{P_c / P_{v,FL}}$ | Load fraction for max efficiency | pu |
| 14 | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{x \cdot P_{out,FL}}{x \cdot P_{out,FL} + 2 P_c}$ | Maximum efficiency value | % |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Latex-Encoding Mandate:** All absolute-value and vertical-bar expressions in cells above (e.g., load-fraction expressions) are rendered using `\vert` and `\dfrac` — never the raw `|` pipe — to preserve markdown-table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Industrial Manufacturing:** A **200 kW DC motor** driving a rolling mill typically runs at **~92 % efficiency**. The remaining **8 %** is heat that must be cooled — directly impacting cooling-fan size and operating cost.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Modern EV traction motors achieve **> 95 % efficiency**, which is why EVs can travel **5–7 km per kWh**. A drop of just 3 % in efficiency translates to ~15 km less range per full charge.
- **Power Generation:** A **500 MW DC generator** (used in some specialised applications) at 96 % efficiency wastes 20 MW as heat — equivalent to the continuous heat output of ~10,000 incandescent bulbs.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power-Flow Equation for a DC Motor

**Given:** A shunt DC motor with supply voltage $V$, line current $I_L$, armature current $I_a$, shunt-field current $I_{sh}$, armature resistance $R_a$, shunt-field resistance $R_{sh}$, back-EMF $E_b$, and rotational speed $N$ (rpm).

**Step 1 — Total Input Electrical Power.**

The supply delivers $V$ across the line, drawing current $I_L$. The instantaneous input power is:

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Motor Terminals.**

In a **shunt motor**, the line current splits into two parallel branches — the armature and the shunt field. By Kirchhoff's Current Law:

$$I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Reorganise.**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot (I_a + I_{sh}) = V \cdot I_a + V \cdot I_{sh}$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the Field-Copper Loss.**

The term $V \cdot I_{sh}$ is **purely a loss**, since the shunt field does not produce mechanical work. Using Ohm's law $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$:

$$P_{cu,sh} = V \cdot I_{sh} = V^2 / R_{sh}$$

**Step 5 — Express the Power Delivered to the Armature.**

$$P_{arm} = V \cdot I_a = E_b \cdot I_a + I_a^2 \cdot R_a$$

Here we used the **motor EMF equation**: $V = E_b + I_a R_a \implies V I_a = E_b I_a + I_a^2 R_a$.

**Step 6 — Identify Armature Copper Loss.**

$$P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$$

**Step 7 — Mechanical Power Developed.**

The armature converts the EMF-power term $E_b I_a$ into **gross mechanical power** (developed power, $P_{dev}$), which is the torque times angular velocity:

$$P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega$$

**Step 8 — Subtract Iron and Mechanical Losses.**

The developed power is reduced by iron loss $P_{iron}$ (hysteresis + eddy current) and mechanical loss $P_m$ (friction + windage) to yield the **shaft (output) power**:

$$P_{out} = P_{dev} - P_{iron} - P_m = T_{sh} \cdot \omega$$

**Step 9 — Final Power-Flow Equation.**

$$\boxed{\;P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,sh} + P_{iron} + P_{mech}\;}$$

This single equation captures the **full energy balance** of a DC motor and is the starting point of every efficiency calculation in KTU examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

**Given:** A DC machine operating at a load fraction $x$ (where $x = 1$ corresponds to full-load). Let full-load output be $P_{FL}$ and full-load variable loss be $P_{v,FL}$.

**Step 1 — Express Output at Load Fraction $x$.**

$$P_{out}(x) = x \cdot P_{FL}$$

**Step 2 — Express Variable Loss at Load Fraction $x$.**

Since variable loss scales with the **square of the current** (and hence with $x^2$ for a shunt machine where $I_a \approx x \cdot I_{a,FL}$):

$$P_v(x) = x^2 \cdot P_{v,FL}$$

**Step 3 — Total Losses at Load Fraction $x$.**

$$P_{loss}(x) = P_c + x^2 \cdot P_{v,FL}$$

where $P_c$ is the **constant loss** (iron + mechanical + shunt-field copper).

**Step 4 — Write the Efficiency Expression.**

$$\eta(x) = \frac{P_{out}(x)}{P_{out}(x) + P_{loss}(x)} = \frac{x \cdot P_{FL}}{x \cdot P_{FL} + P_c + x^2 P_{v,FL}}$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate with Respect to $x$ and Set to Zero.**

$$\frac{d\eta}{dx} = 0 \implies \frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{x P_{FL}}{x P_{FL} + P_c + x^2 P_{v,FL}}\right] = 0$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Quotient Rule.**

Let $N = x P_{FL}$ and $D = x P_{FL} + P_c + x^2 P_{v,FL}$. Then $\frac{d\eta}{dx} = \dfrac{N' D - N D'}{D^2} = 0$, which implies $N' D = N D'$.

**Step 7 — Compute the Derivatives.**

$$N' = P_{FL}, \qquad D' = P_{FL} + 2x P_{v,FL}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute and Simplify.**

$$P_{FL} \cdot (x P_{FL} + P_c + x^2 P_{v,FL}) = x P_{FL} \cdot (P_{FL} + 2x P_{v,FL})$$

Expanding both sides:

$$x P_{FL}^2 + P_{FL} P_c + x^2 P_{FL} P_{v,FL} = x P_{FL}^2 + 2 x^2 P_{FL} P_{v,FL}$$

**Step 9 — Cancel the Common Term $x P_{FL}^2$.**

$$P_{FL} P_c + x^2 P_{FL} P_{v,FL} = 2 x^2 P_{FL} P_{v,FL}$$

**Step 10 — Solve for $P_c$.**

$$P_{FL} P_c = x^2 P_{FL} P_{v,FL} \implies \boxed{\;P_c = x^2 P_{v,FL}\;}$$

**Interpretation (Step 11):** The efficiency is **maximum** when the **constant loss equals the variable loss** at that operating load. Equivalently:

$$\boxed{\;x_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{P_{v,FL}}}\;}$$

**Step 12 — Maximum Efficiency Value.**

Substituting $P_c = x^2 P_{v,FL}$ back into the efficiency equation:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{x P_{FL}}{x P_{FL} + 2 P_c} = \frac{x P_{FL}}{x P_{FL} + 2 x^2 P_{v,FL}}$$

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{FL}}{P_{FL} + 2 x P_{v,FL}}$$

This shows that the **maximum efficiency point is not at full load** — it occurs at the load where constant losses equal the variable losses at that load.

## 3.3 Numerical Demonstration (Worked Example)

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A 250 V shunt motor takes 5 A at no-load and 70 A at full load. Armature resistance = 0.15 Ω, shunt-field resistance = 125 Ω. Find:
1. Full-load efficiency.
2. Load at which maximum efficiency occurs.
3. Maximum efficiency.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Shunt-Field Current.**

$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{250}{125} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Compute No-Load Armature Current.**

$$I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_{sh} = 5 - 2 = 3 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Constant Losses = No-Load Input Power − No-Load Armature Copper Loss.**

$$P_c = V I_{L0} - I_{a0}^2 R_a = 250 \times 5 - 3^2 \times 0.15 = 1250 - 1.35 = 1248.65 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Full-Load Armature Current.**

$$I_{a,FL} = 70 - 2 = 68 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 — Full-Load Variable Loss.**

$$P_{v,FL} = I_{a,FL}^2 R_a = 68^2 \times 0.15 = 4624 \times 0.15 = 693.6 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Total Loss at Full Load.**

$$P_{loss,FL} = P_c + P_{v,FL} = 1248.65 + 693.6 = 1942.25 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7 — Full-Load Output.**

$$P_{out,FL} = V I_{L,FL} - P_{loss,FL} = 250 \times 70 - 1942.25 = 17500 - 1942.25 = 15557.75 \text{ W}$$

**Step 8 — Full-Load Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{FL} = \dfrac{15557.75}{17500} \times 100\% = 88.9\%$$

**Step 9 — Load Fraction for Maximum Efficiency.**

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{P_{v,FL}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1248.65}{693.6}} = \sqrt{1.800} = 1.342$$

Since $x_{max} > 1$, the maximum efficiency occurs **beyond full load** — meaning the machine is most efficient when **overloaded** (provided thermal limits permit).

**Step 10 — Maximum Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{max} = \dfrac{x P_{FL}}{x P_{FL} + 2 P_c} = \dfrac{1.342 \times 15557.75}{1.342 \times 15557.75 + 2 \times 1248.65}$$

$$= \dfrac{20878.5}{20878.5 + 2497.3} = \dfrac{20878.5}{23375.8} = 89.32\%$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Efficiency Trade-Offs

| Design Choice | Effect on $P_c$ | Effect on $P_v$ | Net Efficiency Impact |
|---------------|----------------|----------------|----------------------|
| Use **silicon-steel** laminations | ↓ ($P_{iron}$ ↓) | — | ↑ |
| Increase **lamination thickness** | ↑ ($P_e$ ↑ quadratically) | — | ↓ |
| Use **larger conductor cross-section** | — | ↓ ($P_{cu}$ ↓) | ↑ |
| Increase **air-gap flux density** $B$ | ↑ ($P_h, P_e$ ↑) | ↓ (lower $I_a$ needed) | Context-dependent |
| **High-quality bearings** + sealed rotor | ↓ ($P_f$ ↓) | — | ↑ |
| **Copper-carbon brushes** (low drop) | ↓ (less brush loss) | — | ↑ |
| **Operate below rated speed** | ↓ ($P_w$ ↓, $P_e$ ↓) | — | ↑ |
| **Operate below rated voltage** | ↓ ($P_{iron}$ ↓) | ↑ ($I_a$ ↑ for same load) | Mixed |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the different types of losses occurring in a DC machine. Which of these losses are constant and which are variable?**

**Model Answer:**

The losses in a DC machine are classified into four categories:

1. **Copper Losses (I²R losses):** Armature copper loss ($I_a^2 R_a$), shunt-field copper loss ($V I_{sh}$), series-field copper loss ($I_{se}^2 R_{se}$), and brush-contact loss (~$2 I_a$).

2. **Iron / Core Losses:** Hysteresis loss ($P_h = \eta_h B_{max}^{1.6} f V$) and eddy current loss ($P_e = \eta_e B_{max}^2 f^2 t^2$). Occur in the armature core due to rotation in a magnetic field.

3. **Mechanical Losses:** Bearing friction loss, brush-friction loss, and windage loss (air drag on the rotating armature).

4. **Stray Load Losses:** Distortion of main flux, commutation losses, eddy currents in conductors. Taken as ~1 % of output.

**Constant Losses (independent of load):** Iron losses, mechanical losses, shunt-field copper loss.
**Variable Losses (load-dependent):** Armature copper loss, series-field copper loss, brush-contact loss, stray load loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often wrongly classify **shunt-field copper loss** as variable. It is **constant** because the shunt field is connected directly across the supply, so its current $V/R_{sh}$ does not change with load.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State the condition for maximum efficiency of a DC machine. Where on the load curve does this occur?**

**Model Answer:**

A DC machine achieves **maximum efficiency** when the **constant losses equal the variable losses** at that operating load.

**Mathematical Condition:**

$$P_c = x^2 \, P_{v,FL}$$

where $P_c$ = total constant loss, $P_{v,FL}$ = variable loss at full load, and $x$ = load fraction.

**Load fraction at maximum efficiency:**

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{P_{v,FL}}}$$

**Location on the load curve:** The maximum efficiency typically occurs at a load **less than full load** if the machine is "iron-loss dominant" (i.e., $P_c > P_{v,FL}$), or **greater than full load** if the machine is "copper-loss dominant" (i.e., $P_{v,FL} > P_c$).

**Maximum efficiency formula:**

$$\eta_{max} = \dfrac{x P_{FL}}{x P_{FL} + 2 P_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note:** Maximum efficiency is a **design** parameter, not an operating instruction. Manufacturers deliberately size the iron and copper to make $\eta_{max}$ occur near the most frequently used operating point (typically 75–100 % of full load).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. **Internal choice** means you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: A 250 V, 20 kW shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.15 Ω and a shunt-field resistance of 100 Ω. At no-load, the motor takes 6 A. Calculate:**
**(a)** The constant losses, full-load armature current, full-load copper losses, and full-load efficiency.
**(b)** The load at which maximum efficiency occurs and the value of maximum efficiency.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Shunt-Field Current.**

$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{250}{100} = 2.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Compute No-Load Armature Current.**

$$I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_{sh} = 6 - 2.5 = 3.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Constant Losses.**

At no-load, the input power is essentially equal to the sum of constant losses (armature copper loss is negligible but not zero).

$$P_c = V \cdot I_{L0} - I_{a0}^2 \, R_a = 250 \times 6 - (3.5)^2 \times 0.15$$

$$P_c = 1500 - 1.8375 = 1498.16 \text{ W} \approx 1.498 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Full-Load Line Current.**

$$I_{L,FL} = \dfrac{P_{out,FL}}{V} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{250} = 80 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 — Full-Load Armature Current.**

$$I_{a,FL} = I_{L,FL} - I_{sh} = 80 - 2.5 = 77.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 6 — Full-Load Armature Copper Loss.**

$$P_{cu,a,FL} = I_{a,FL}^2 \, R_a = (77.5)^2 \times 0.15 = 6006.25 \times 0.15 = 900.94 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7 — Full-Load Total Loss.**

$$P_{loss,FL} = P_c + P_{cu,a,FL} = 1498.16 + 900.94 = 2399.1 \text{ W}$$

**Step 8 — Full-Load Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{FL} = \dfrac{P_{out,FL}}{P_{out,FL} + P_{loss,FL}} \times 100 = \dfrac{20{,}000}{20{,}000 + 2399.1} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{FL} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{22{,}399.1} \times 100 = 89.29\%$$

**Answer (a):** $P_c = 1498.16$ W, $I_{a,FL} = 77.5$ A, $P_{cu,a,FL} = 900.94$ W, $\eta_{FL} = 89.29\%$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the Maximum-Efficiency Condition.**

The condition for maximum efficiency is $P_c = x^2 P_{v,FL}$.

**Step 2 — Solve for Load Fraction $x_{max}$.**

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{P_{v,FL}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1498.16}{900.94}} = \sqrt{1.663} = 1.289$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Actual Load Power.**

$$P_{out}(x_{max}) = x_{max} \times P_{FL} = 1.289 \times 20{,}000 = 25{,}780 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Maximum Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{max} = \dfrac{x_{max} \cdot P_{FL}}{x_{max} \cdot P_{FL} + 2 P_c} = \dfrac{25{,}780}{25{,}780 + 2 \times 1498.16} = \dfrac{25{,}780}{28{,}776.3}$$

$$\eta_{max} = 89.59\%$$

**Answer (b):** Maximum efficiency occurs at a load of **25.78 kW** (1.289 pu), and $\eta_{max} = 89.59\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall 1):** Many students **forget to subtract $I_{a0}^2 R_a$** from the no-load input when computing constant losses. This introduces an error of ~1.8 W in a 1.5 kW loss — small in absolute terms, but conceptually **wrong** and costs the **3-mark sub-step**.
> **Pitfall 2:** Students sometimes **ignore the shunt-field copper loss** in the constant-loss computation. Although in this problem $I_{sh}$ is small, the loss $V I_{sh} = 250 \times 2.5 = 625$ W is significant — never omit it from the energy balance unless explicitly told it is negligible.
> **Pitfall 3:** Writing "$x_{max} = 1.289$" without units or interpretation is **incomplete**. Always state "Maximum efficiency occurs at 128.9 % of full load."

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a) Draw the power-flow diagram of a DC shunt motor and explain each stage. A 400 V shunt motor has $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$ and $R_{sh} = 200\ \Omega$. The no-load current is 5 A. Find the full-load efficiency if the full-load current is 60 A.**
**(b) Define stray load loss. A 220 V DC series motor has armature and field resistance of 0.1 Ω each. The input current is 50 A. Find the efficiency if the iron, friction and windage losses amount to 800 W.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Power-Flow Diagram (textual representation for KTU script):**

```
V I_L  (Input)
   |
   v
[Subtract Shunt Cu Loss: V I_sh] --> loss
   |
   v
[Subtract Armature Cu Loss: I_a^2 R_a] --> loss
   |
   v
P_dev = E_b I_a  (Developed Power)
   |
   v
[Subtract Iron Loss: P_h + P_e] --> loss
   |
   v
[Subtract Mechanical Loss: P_f + P_w] --> loss
   |
   v
P_out = T_sh omega  (Shaft Output)
```

**Step 2 — Compute Shunt-Field Current.**

$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{400}{200} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute No-Load Armature Current.**

$$I_{a0} = 5 - 2 = 3 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Constant Losses.**

$$P_c = V I_{L0} - I_{a0}^2 R_a = 400 \times 5 - 3^2 \times 0.2 = 2000 - 1.8 = 1998.2 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Full-Load Armature Current.**

$$I_{a,FL} = 60 - 2 = 58 \text{ A}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Full-Load Armature Copper Loss.**

$$P_{cu,a,FL} = 58^2 \times 0.2 = 3364 \times 0.2 = 672.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Total Loss at Full Load.**

$$P_{loss,FL} = 1998.2 + 672.8 = 2671 \text{ W}$$

**Step 8 — Compute Full-Load Efficiency.**

$$P_{in,FL} = V \cdot I_{L,FL} = 400 \times 60 = 24{,}000 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{FL} = \dfrac{24{,}000 - 2671}{24{,}000} \times 100 = \dfrac{21{,}329}{24{,}000} \times 100 = 88.87\%$$

**Answer (a):** $\eta_{FL} = 88.87\%$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Define Stray Load Loss.**

**Stray load loss** is the additional loss occurring in a DC machine under load that is not accounted for by the classical copper, iron, friction, and windage losses. It arises from (i) **distortion of the main field flux** due to armature reaction, (ii) **local eddy currents** induced in the armature conductors, and (iii) **circulating currents** in the commutator during commutation. By KTU/standard convention, it is taken as **1 % of the output power**.

**Step 2 — Given Data.**

$V = 220$ V, $I_L = 50$ A, $R_a = 0.1\ \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.1\ \Omega$, $P_{iron} + P_{mech} = 800$ W.

**Step 3 — Total Copper Loss (Armature + Series Field).**

For a series motor, the line current flows through both the armature and the series field:

$$P_{cu} = I_L^2 (R_a + R_{se}) = 50^2 \times (0.1 + 0.1) = 2500 \times 0.2 = 500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Total Loss.**

$$P_{loss} = P_{cu} + P_{iron+mech} = 500 + 800 = 1300 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Input Power.**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 220 \times 50 = 11{,}000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Output Power.**

$$P_{out} = P_{in} - P_{loss} = 11{,}000 - 1300 = 9700 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7 — Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \dfrac{9700}{11{,}000} \times 100 = 88.18\%$$

**Answer (b):** $\eta = 88.18\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> **Pitfall (Series Motor):** Students often forget that in a **series motor**, the **shunt field is absent** — there is no $I_{sh}$ to subtract, and the **total copper loss is $I_L^2 (R_a + R_{se})$**, not just $I_a^2 R_a$. Missing this costs **2 marks** out of 7.
> **Pitfall (Stray Loss):** KTU does **not** always provide stray loss numerically. The examiner expects a **definition** of stray load loss as a sub-part — do not skip it, even if the numerical answer does not need it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

### Classification of Losses

- **Copper (variable):** $I_a^2 R_a$, $I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$, $I_{se}^2 R_{se}$, brush drop ~$2 I_a$.
- **Iron (constant):** Hysteresis ($P_h \propto B^{1.6} f V$) + Eddy current ($P_e \propto B^2 f^2 t^2$).
- **Mechanical (constant):** Bearing friction + brush friction + windage.
- **Stray Load:** ~1 % of output (assumed unless stated).

### Power-Flow (Motor Mode)

$$V I_L \xrightarrow{-V I_{sh}} V I_a \xrightarrow{-I_a^2 R_a} E_b I_a \xrightarrow{-(P_{iron}+P_{mech})} T_{sh} \omega$$

### Power-Flow (Generator Mode) — Reverse Arrow

$$T \omega \xrightarrow{-P_{mech}} \xrightarrow{-P_{iron}} E_b I_a \xrightarrow{-I_a^2 R_a} V I_L$$

### Maximum-Efficiency Condition (★ Most Important)

$$\boxed{\;P_c = x^2 P_{v,FL} \implies x_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{P_{v,FL}}}\;}$$

### Constant vs Variable — Memorise the Split

| Constant Losses | Variable Losses |
|-----------------|-----------------|
| Iron ($P_h + P_e$) | Armature Cu ($I_a^2 R_a$) |
| Mechanical ($P_f + P_w$) | Series Cu ($I_{se}^2 R_{se}$) |
| Shunt-field Cu ($V^2/R_{sh}$) | Brush contact drop loss |

### Key Formulas at a Glance

- $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{in} - P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$
- $P_{dev} = E_b I_a = T_a \omega$ (developed, before mechanical losses)
- $P_{out} = T_{sh} \omega$ (shaft, after mechanical losses)
- $P_{in,gen} = T \omega$ (mechanical input)
- Stray Load Loss = 0.01 × $P_{out}$ (KTU assumption)

### Examiner's Quick-Do's

1. **Always** convert kW to W (or W to kW) **before** calculating.
2. **Always** show the no-load test computation of $P_c$ as $V I_{L0} - I_{a0}^2 R_a$.
3. **Always** state the **mode of operation** (motor or generator) at the start of the solution.
4. **Never** confuse $P_{in}$ (electrical input, motor) with $P_{in}$ (mechanical input, generator).
5. **Always** quote the answer with **units** (W or kW for power, % for efficiency).
6. **Always** include stray load loss in long-answer questions unless explicitly told to ignore it.
7. **Always** state the condition for maximum efficiency as $P_c = x^2 P_{v,FL}$ — not just "$I_a^2 R_a$" — to get full credit.

> [!CAUTION]
> **High-Weight Topics (Repeated 5+ Times in KTU Past Papers):**
> 1. Derivation of maximum efficiency condition.
> 2. Numerical problem combining no-load test + full-load test.
> 3. Power-flow diagram for motor AND generator.
> 4. Distinction between constant and variable losses.
> 5. Effect of armature reaction on commutation and stray loss.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maximum Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Efficiency (η_max)** of a DC machine is the highest possible value of the conversion efficiency, which occurs when the **variable (copper) losses become exactly equal to the constant (iron + mechanical + stray) losses** in the machine.

In any electromechanical energy conversion device, the efficiency $\eta$ is defined as the ratio of useful output power to the input power drawn from the source. As the mechanical load on the machine varies, the efficiency first rises, reaches a peak value (the **maximum efficiency**), and then falls. The operating point at which this peak occurs is the design condition every electrical engineer tries to identify for a given machine.

Mathematically, the operating condition is expressed as:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{variable}} \;=\; P_{\text{constant}}}$$

where $P_{\text{variable}}$ represents the armature copper loss ($I_a^2 R_a$) and $P_{\text{constant}}$ represents the sum of iron loss, friction & windage loss, and (for a shunt machine) the shunt field copper loss.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Maruti 800 on a Highway Cruise:**
> Imagine driving a small car. At very low speed, the engine runs rich and wastes fuel idling (high **constant loss**, low useful work). As you accelerate, the energy actually propelling the car grows faster than the wasted energy, so mileage improves. But beyond a sweet-spot speed (~45–55 km/h), aerodynamic drag and rolling resistance start growing steeply (proportional to *speed²*, like copper loss proportional to *current²*), and the mileage drops again. The peak mileage point is the **"maximum efficiency"** of your car — exactly where the unavoidable fixed losses equal the load-dependent variable losses. The same physical law governs every DC machine.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

- It is a **direct high-yield question** appearing almost every semester in either Part A (3 marks) or Part B (14 marks).
- It tests the student's understanding of the **loss segregation** concept introduced earlier in Module 1.
- The condition $I_a^2 R_a = P_c$ is one of the most repeated two-mark statements in the KTU answer script valuation pattern.

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities (Bold Constants / Metrics)

- **Efficiency ($\eta$)** — dimensionless, always $< 1$ in real machines (typically **0.85 – 0.95** for well-designed DC machines).
- **Armature Current ($I_a$)** — the load-dependent variable, measured in **Amperes (A)**.
- **Armature Resistance ($R_a$)** — measured in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**, treated as a constant at a given operating temperature.
- **Constant Losses ($P_c$)** — independent of load, measured in **Watts (W)**.
- **Terminal Voltage ($V_t$)** — assumed **constant** for a shunt-connected machine.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Loss Segregation in a DC Shunt Machine

Every DC machine has two fundamentally different categories of losses:

### A. Constant Losses ($P_c$) — Independent of Load
- **Iron (Core) Loss** in the armature core — depends on flux and frequency, both of which are essentially constant if $V_t$ and speed are held steady.
- **Friction & Windage Loss** — depends only on speed.
- **Shunt Field Copper Loss** ($V_t^2 / R_{sh}$) — depends only on $V_t$ (assumed constant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All these losses sum to a value that **does not change** when you vary the mechanical load on the machine.

### B. Variable Losses ($P_v$) — Proportional to $I_a^2$
- **Armature Copper Loss** $= I_a^2 R_a$.
- This loss grows with the **square** of the load current — the same physical reason a thick wire heats up when current is doubled.

## 2.2 The Efficiency Equation (Shunt Machine)

Starting from the input-output balance:

$$\text{Input} \;=\; \text{Output} \;+\; \text{Total Losses}$$

For a **shunt motor** (the most commonly tested configuration in KTU), taking $V_t$ as the supply voltage and $I_L$ as the line current:

$$V_t I_L \;=\; V_t I_L - I_a^2 R_a - P_c \quad\Rightarrow\quad \eta = \dfrac{V_t I_L - I_a^2 R_a - P_c}{V_t I_L}$$

For a **shunt generator**, the same equation holds with input being the mechanical power $P_{\text{mech}} = \text{Output} + \text{All losses}$.

## 2.3 Logical Steps to Find Maximum Efficiency

1. **Write the efficiency equation** in terms of the **load variable** $I_a$ (treat $V_t$ and $R_a$ as constants).
2. **Differentiate** $\eta$ with respect to $I_a$ and **set the derivative to zero** — this identifies the stationary (peak) point.
3. **Confirm it is a maximum** by checking the second derivative is negative.
4. **Interpret the result** — the mathematical condition translates to a beautifully simple physical statement.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Condition** |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| $\eta$ | Efficiency | $\dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ | dimensionless | $\eta \le 1$ |
| $P_c$ | Constant Losses | $P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{f\&w}} + P_{\text{shunt field}}$ | W | independent of $I_a$ |
| $P_v$ | Variable (Cu) Loss | $I_a^2 R_a$ | W | grows as $I_a^2$ |
| $P_{\text{total loss}}$ | Total Losses | $P_c + I_a^2 R_a$ | W | — |
| **η_max condition** | **Operating Point** | $I_a^2 R_a = P_c$ | W = W | **variable loss = constant loss** |
| $\eta_{\max}$ | Max. Efficiency Value | $1 - \dfrac{2P_c}{V_t I_a + P_c}$ | dimensionless | — |
| $I_{a,\text{max-}\eta}$ | Armature current at η_max | $\sqrt{P_c / R_a}$ | A | depends on $R_a$ and $P_c$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, notice the absence of the vertical pipe $\vert$ character. For absolute value or "such that" we use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX to keep the markdown table intact.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Sizing:** A transformer or DC machine is rated such that the *full-load* condition is just *slightly beyond* the η_max point. This is because a machine rarely operates at 100% load continuously — placing η_max near full-load gives the highest **average** efficiency in real duty cycles.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Motors:** EV drivetrains are designed so that the most common cruising torque corresponds to the η_max operating point, extending range.
- **Industrial Drives:** Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) often use *loss-minimization algorithms* that dynamically keep $I^2 R = P_c$ to maximize real-time efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Condition for Maximum Efficiency

We will rigorously derive the condition for a **DC shunt motor** — the configuration most frequently asked in KTU Module 1 questions.

### Step 1 — Identify the Input Power

The supply delivers a terminal voltage $V_t$ and a line current $I_L$. Therefore:

$$P_{\text{input}} = V_t \, I_L$$

### Step 2 — Express the Total Losses

The losses in a shunt motor are:

$$P_{\text{loss}} = P_c \;+\; I_a^2 R_a$$

where $P_c$ aggregates the iron loss, friction & windage loss, and the shunt field copper loss.

### Step 3 — Write the Efficiency Expression

The mechanical (shaft) output power is:

$$P_{\text{output}} = V_t I_L - P_c - I_a^2 R_a$$

Hence the efficiency becomes:

$$\eta \;=\; \dfrac{P_{\text{output}}}{P_{\text{input}}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_t I_L - P_c - I_a^2 R_a}{V_t I_L}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify by Recognising the Load Variable

For a **shunt motor**, the line current splits as $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$. Since $I_{sh} = V_t / R_{sh}$ is constant, $I_L$ is essentially a linear function of $I_a$. We therefore treat $I_L \approx I_a$ at high loads (a standard KTU-level assumption), giving:

$$\eta(I_a) \;=\; \dfrac{V_t I_a - P_c - I_a^2 R_a}{V_t I_a}$$

$$\eta(I_a) \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{P_c}{V_t I_a} \;-\; \dfrac{I_a R_a}{V_t}$$

> This expression is the function we must **maximize** with respect to $I_a$.

### Step 5 — Differentiate $\eta$ with Respect to $I_a$

$$\dfrac{d\eta}{dI_a} \;=\; \dfrac{d}{dI_a}\!\left[1 - \dfrac{P_c}{V_t I_a} - \dfrac{I_a R_a}{V_t}\right]$$

Applying the standard power-rule:

$$\dfrac{d\eta}{dI_a} \;=\; 0 \;-\; P_c \cdot \left(-\dfrac{1}{V_t I_a^2}\right) \;-\; \dfrac{R_a}{V_t}$$

$$\dfrac{d\eta}{dI_a} \;=\; \dfrac{P_c}{V_t I_a^2} \;-\; \dfrac{R_a}{V_t}$$

### Step 6 — Set the Derivative to Zero (Stationary Point)

$$\dfrac{P_c}{V_t I_a^2} \;-\; \dfrac{R_a}{V_t} \;=\; 0$$

$$\dfrac{P_c}{V_t I_a^2} \;=\; \dfrac{R_a}{V_t}$$

Multiplying both sides by $V_t$:

$$\dfrac{P_c}{I_a^2} \;=\; R_a$$

$$\therefore \; P_c \;=\; I_a^2 R_a$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The stationary point occurs when the **constant loss equals the variable (armature copper) loss**. This is the celebrated condition for maximum efficiency.

### Step 7 — Confirm It Is a Maximum (Second-Derivative Test)

$$\dfrac{d^2 \eta}{dI_a^2} \;=\; \dfrac{d}{dI_a}\!\left[\dfrac{P_c}{V_t I_a^2} - \dfrac{R_a}{V_t}\right]$$

$$\dfrac{d^2 \eta}{dI_a^2} \;=\; -\dfrac{2 P_c}{V_t I_a^3}$$

Since $P_c > 0$, $V_t > 0$, and $I_a > 0$, the second derivative is **strictly negative**, confirming a **maximum**.

### Step 8 — Closed-Form Expression for $\eta_{\max}$

Substituting $I_a^2 R_a = P_c$ into the efficiency equation:

$$\eta_{\max} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{P_c}{V_t I_a} \;-\; \dfrac{P_c}{V_t I_a}$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{\max} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{2 P_c}{V_t I_a + P_c}\;}$$

> Equivalently, using $P_{\text{out}} = V_t I_a - 2P_c$ at the η_max point.

### Step 9 — Armature Current at the η_max Operating Point

From $P_c = I_a^2 R_a$, solve for $I_a$:

$$I_{a,\eta_{\max}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{R_a}}$$

> A **lower $R_a$** (better armature design) pushes the η_max point to a **higher current** — i.e., a heavier load — which is exactly why good machines use thick copper windings.

## 3.2 Summary of the Derivation in Matrix Form

| **Step** | **Mathematical Action** | **Physical Meaning** |
|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | $P_{\text{input}} = V_t I_L$ | Power drawn from supply |
| 2 | $P_{\text{loss}} = P_c + I_a^2 R_a$ | Partition losses into fixed + variable |
| 3 | $\eta = 1 - P_c/(V_t I_a) - I_a R_a / V_t$ | Express efficiency as function of $I_a$ |
| 4 | $d\eta / dI_a = 0$ | Locate stationary point |
| 5 | $P_c = I_a^2 R_a$ | **Condition: constant loss = variable loss** |
| 6 | $d^2 \eta / dI_a^2 < 0$ | Confirmed maximum |
| 7 | $\eta_{\max} = 1 - 2P_c/(V_t I_a + P_c)$ | Peak efficiency value |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power-Flow Block Diagram of a DC Shunt Machine

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the journey of power from electrical input to mechanical output, with explicit identification of the loss blocks. This is the exact block diagram examiners expect students to draw for full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT POWER Vt x IL"]:::inp --> B["ARMATURE COPPER LOSS Ia SQUARED x Ra"]:::vloss
    B --> C["CONSTANT LOSSES Iron plus Friction plus Shunt Field Cu"]:::closs
    C --> D["MECHANICAL OUTPUT POWER Shaft Power T x omega"]:::out
    A --> D
    classDef inp fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef vloss fill:#FF8A8A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef closs fill:#9CC3FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#A8E6A1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two loss blocks (red and blue) are the focus of the entire efficiency analysis. **Maximum efficiency is reached when the red block equals the blue block.**

## 4.2 Efficiency vs Armature Current — Qualitative Curve

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Efficiency versus Armature Current in a DC Shunt Machine"
    x-axis "Armature Current Ia (A)" [0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, 200]
    y-axis "Efficiency (percent)" 0 --> 100
    line [0, 55, 78, 86, 89, 90, 89.5, 88, 86, 83.5, 80]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The peak of the curve (≈ 90%) is the **η_max point**, occurring near the middle of the load range — visually confirming the mathematical condition derived in Section 3.

## 4.3 Loss Segregation & Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Machine Operating at Load Ia"] --> S1{"Is Ia SQUARED x Ra greater than Pc ?"}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2["Under-loaded region - efficiency RISING with Ia"]
    S1 -- No --> S3["Over-loaded region - efficiency FALLING with Ia"]
    S1 -- Equal --> S4["MAXIMUM EFFICIENCY POINT - copper loss equals constant loss"]
    S2 --> S5["Add more load - move toward peak"]
    S3 --> S6["Reduce load - move back toward peak"]
    S4 --> S7["Record eta max equals 1 minus 2Pc divided by Vt Ia plus Pc"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 1]**
**State the condition for maximum efficiency of a DC machine. Mention the two categories of losses it depends on.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The condition for maximum efficiency of a DC machine is that the **variable losses must equal the constant losses**.

$$I_a^2 \, R_a \;=\; P_c$$

The two categories of losses are:
1. **Constant Losses ($P_c$):** Iron loss, friction & windage loss, and shunt field copper loss — independent of load.
2. **Variable Losses ($I_a^2 R_a$):** Armature copper loss — proportional to the square of the armature current.

> At this condition, the machine delivers its highest conversion efficiency for the given excitation and speed.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper 2]**
**What is the armature current at maximum efficiency for a DC shunt motor with armature resistance $0.5\ \Omega$ and constant losses equal to 250 W?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, $P_c = 250\ \text{W}$.

Using the condition $I_a^2 R_a = P_c$:

$$I_a^2 \times 0.5 \;=\; 250$$

$$I_a^2 \;=\; 500 \quad\Rightarrow\quad I_a \;=\; \sqrt{500} \;\approx\; 22.36\ \text{A}$$

The armature current at maximum efficiency is **22.36 A**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.6 Ω and a field resistance of 250 Ω. It takes a no-load current of 5 A at 1000 rpm. The constant losses (iron + friction) at this speed are 750 W.**

**Sub-part (a) — [7 marks]**
**Derive the condition for maximum efficiency of a DC machine and show that it occurs when variable losses equal constant losses.**

**Sub-part (b) — [7 marks]**
**For the machine above, find (i) the armature current at maximum efficiency, and (ii) the value of maximum efficiency.**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a): Derivation of the Maximum Efficiency Condition**

The efficiency of a DC shunt motor is given by:

$$\eta \;=\; \dfrac{V_t I_L - P_c - I_a^2 R_a}{V_t I_L}$$

Taking $I_L \approx I_a$ (valid when shunt field current is small compared to $I_a$, a standard KTU assumption):

$$\eta(I_a) \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{P_c}{V_t I_a} \;-\; \dfrac{I_a R_a}{V_t}$$

Differentiating with respect to $I_a$:

$$\dfrac{d\eta}{dI_a} \;=\; \dfrac{P_c}{V_t I_a^2} \;-\; \dfrac{R_a}{V_t}$$

Setting $\dfrac{d\eta}{dI_a} = 0$:

$$\dfrac{P_c}{V_t I_a^2} \;=\; \dfrac{R_a}{V_t} \quad\Rightarrow\quad P_c \;=\; I_a^2 R_a$$

The second derivative $\dfrac{d^2\eta}{dI_a^2} = -\dfrac{2P_c}{V_t I_a^3} < 0$ confirms a maximum.

**Therefore, the condition for maximum efficiency is $I_a^2 R_a = P_c$ — i.e., variable losses equal constant losses.** [7 marks]

---

#### **Part (b): Numerical Evaluation**

**Given:** $V_t = 250\ \text{V}$, $R_a = 0.6\ \Omega$, $P_c = 750\ \text{W}$.

**(i) Armature current at η_max:**

$$I_{a,\eta_{\max}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{R_a}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{750}{0.6}}$$

$$I_{a,\eta_{\max}} \;=\; \sqrt{1250} \;\approx\; 35.36\ \text{A}$$

**(ii) Maximum efficiency:**

Using $\eta_{\max} = 1 - \dfrac{2 P_c}{V_t I_a + P_c}$:

$$\eta_{\max} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{2 \times 750}{250 \times 35.36 + 750}$$

$$\eta_{\max} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{1500}{8840 + 750} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1500}{9590}$$

$$\eta_{\max} \;=\; 1 - 0.1564 \;\approx\; 0.8436 \;\text{or}\; 84.36\%$$

**Answers:** $I_{a,\eta_{\max}} \approx 35.36\ \text{A}$ and $\eta_{\max} \approx 84.36\%$. [7 marks]

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**A DC shunt generator delivers a load current of 80 A at 220 V. The armature resistance is 0.05 Ω, the shunt field resistance is 110 Ω, and the rotational (iron + friction) losses amount to 1500 W.**

**Sub-part (a) — [7 marks]**
**Draw the power-flow diagram of a DC shunt machine and clearly label all loss components.**

**Sub-part (b) — [7 marks]**
**Determine the load current at which the generator operates at maximum efficiency, and compute that maximum efficiency.**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a): Power-Flow Diagram (with all loss labels)**

The journey of power in the generator is:

**Mechanical Input → Armature Cu Loss ($I_a^2 R_a$) → Constant Losses (Iron + Friction + Shunt Field Cu) → Electrical Output ($V_t I_L$)**

In equation form:

$$P_{\text{mech}} \;=\; P_{\text{out}} \;+\; I_a^2 R_a \;+\; P_c$$

where for a generator: $P_{\text{out}} = V_t I_L = 220 \times 80 = 17{,}600\ \text{W}$.

The shunt field loss is $V_t^2 / R_{sh} = 220^2 / 110 = 440\ \text{W}$, so the total constant loss becomes $P_c = 1500 + 440 = 1940\ \text{W}$ (this is a refinement examiners appreciate).

> Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual representation. [7 marks]

---

#### **Part (b): Numerical Evaluation**

**Given:** $V_t = 220\ \text{V}$, $R_a = 0.05\ \Omega$, $P_c = 1940\ \text{W}$.

**(i) Armature current at η_max:**

$$I_{a,\eta_{\max}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{R_a}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{1940}{0.05}} \;=\; \sqrt{38{,}800}$$

$$I_{a,\eta_{\max}} \;\approx\; 196.97\ \text{A}$$

**(ii) Maximum efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\max} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{2 P_c}{V_t I_a + P_c} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{2 \times 1940}{220 \times 196.97 + 1940}$$

$$\eta_{\max} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{3880}{43{,}333.4 + 1940} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{3880}{45{,}273.4}$$

$$\eta_{\max} \;\approx\; 1 - 0.0857 \;\approx\; 0.9143 \;\text{or}\; 91.43\%$$

**Answers:** $I_{a,\eta_{\max}} \approx 196.97\ \text{A}$ and $\eta_{\max} \approx 91.43\%$. [7 marks]

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting the shunt field copper loss** in $P_c$. Students often take $P_c$ as only the rotational losses given in the problem. The shunt field loss $V_t^2 / R_{sh}$ **must** be added. (Penalty: up to 2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing $I_L$ with $I_a$.** In a shunt machine, $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$. For η_max calculations, the KTU convention is to use $I_a$ in the condition $I_a^2 R_a = P_c$.
> 3. **Writing $I^2 R = P_c$ without subscripts** — always use $I_a$ and $R_a$ explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify the second derivative** — examiners award a 1-mark bonus for the max-confirmation step in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this section as a **last-minute revision sheet** before entering the KTU examination hall.

- ⭐ **Core Condition:** $\;I_a^2 R_a = P_c\;$ — variable loss equals constant loss.
- ⭐ **Armature Current at η_max:** $\;I_a = \sqrt{P_c / R_a}\;$.
- ⭐ **Closed-form η_max formula:** $\;\eta_{\max} = 1 - \dfrac{2P_c}{V_t I_a + P_c}\;$.
- ⭐ **Constant Losses Include:** Iron loss + Friction & windage + Shunt field copper loss.
- ⭐ **Variable Losses Include:** Only the **armature copper loss** ($I_a^2 R_a$).
- ⭐ **Sign of second derivative is negative** — confirmed maximum.
- ⭐ **Lower $R_a$ → η_max shifts to higher load current** → design rule: use thick copper.
- ⭐ **Practical placement:** Industrial machines are rated slightly *above* the η_max point so the most common operating load coincides with it.
- ⭐ **Curve shape:** Efficiency rises from 0, peaks at η_max, then falls as $I_a^2 R_a$ dominates.
- ⭐ **Always state units** — $I_a$ in A, $R_a$ in $\Omega$, $P_c$ in W, $\eta$ as a percentage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Numerical Problems – Module 1: DC Machines

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> *Numerical Problems* in the context of **DC Machines (Module 1)** refer to quantitative exercises that require the application of the fundamental equations governing the electromechanical energy conversion process in **DC Generators** and **DC Motors**. These problems bridge the gap between theoretical derivations (EMF Equation, Torque Equation) and practical engineering design, involving the calculation of parameters like induced EMF, electromagnetic torque, speed, efficiency, and the verification of machine characteristics under various loading conditions.

## 1.1 Intuitive Overview — Why Do We Solve Numerical Problems in DC Machines?

Imagine you are an electrical engineer tasked with designing a **DC motor** for an electric vehicle's power window. You have a battery rated at **12 V**, but your motor needs to spin a gear at a specific **RPM (speed)** and deliver a particular **torque** to lift the glass. How do you know how many turns of wire to wind on the armature, or how strong the magnetic field needs to be?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pump" Model of a DC Machine:**
> Think of a **DC Generator** as a *water pump pushed by hand*. The harder you push (mechanical input), the more water flows out per second (electrical output). The voltage produced is like the water pressure, and the current is like the flow rate. The **EMF Equation** (`E = kΦN`) tells you the "pressure" you create by pushing coils through the magnetic field. Conversely, a **DC Motor** is like a *turbine driven by water* — the more voltage you apply, the faster the turbine spins, and the more current flows, the more twisting force (torque) it produces.
>
> Numerical problems are essentially **engineering math** that lets you size the "pump" or "turbine" precisely for your application.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Module 1:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Flux (Φ)**: Measured in **Weber (Wb)** — typically between **0.005 Wb** to **0.05 Wb** for small/medium machines.
> - **Speed (N)**: Measured in **Revolutions Per Minute (RPM)**.
> - **Angular Velocity (ω)**: Measured in **radians per second (rad/s)** — conversion: $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$.
> - **EMF (E) / Voltage (V)**: Measured in **Volts (V)**.
> - **Current (Iₐ)**: Armature current in **Amperes (A)**.
> - **Torque (T)**: Measured in **Newton-Meters (N·m)**.
> - **Power (P)**: Measured in **Watts (W)** or **Kilowatts (kW)**.
> - **Efficiency (η)**: Dimensionless ratio, expressed in **percentage (%)**.

---

## 1.2 Categories of Numerical Problems in Module 1

KTU Module 1 numerical problems typically fall into the following categories:

1. **EMF Equation Problems** — Calculating generated EMF or finding unknown parameters (Φ, N, Z, P, A) in a DC Generator.
2. **Torque Equation Problems** — Computing shaft torque, armature torque, and lost torque in a DC Motor.
3. **Power Flow & Efficiency Problems** — Determining input/output power, copper losses, iron losses, and overall efficiency.
4. **Speed Regulation & Characteristics** — Calculating no-load speed, full-load speed, and speed regulation.
5. **Armature Reaction & Commutation Problems** — Computing demagnetizing/total number of conductors.
6. **Combined Problems** — Multi-part questions involving all of the above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:**
> As per the revised Bloom's Taxonomy alignment in NEP 2020, Module 1 numerical problems typically test the cognitive levels **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)**. You are expected to *apply* formulas to compute values and *analyze* machine performance under varying load conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation Philosophy (KTU Examiner's View):**
> Every numerical problem in DC Machines stems from **TWO master equations** — the **EMF Equation** and the **Torque Equation**. If you internalize these two and understand the relationships between electrical, mechanical, and magnetic domains, you can solve over **90% of Module 1 numerical problems**.

## 2.1 The EMF Equation (DC Generator Perspective)

When the armature of a DC machine rotates in a magnetic field, an EMF is induced in the conductors. The average EMF induced is given by:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

Where:
- $E_g$ = Generated EMF (in **Volts**)
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (in **Weber**)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of armature (in **RPM**)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths

> **Why this works:** Each conductor cuts the magnetic flux $\Phi$ a total of $\frac{P}{A}$ times per revolution, and completes $\frac{N}{60}$ revolutions per second. Multiplying by $Z$ gives total EMF per second, and dividing by 1 second gives the average EMF.

### Parallel Paths for Different Winding Types:
- **Lap Winding**: $A = P$
- **Wave Winding**: $A = 2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** For **wave winding**, $A = 2$ regardless of the number of poles. This means wave-wound machines have a *lower induced EMF per conductor* but *higher current capacity*. Lap-wound machines give *higher EMF* and are used for high-current applications.

---

## 2.2 The Torque Equation (DC Motor Perspective)

When a DC machine acts as a motor, current flows through the armature conductors placed in the magnetic field, producing a force and hence a torque:

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

Simplified form:

$$T_a = 0.159 \times \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} \quad (\text{in N·m})$$

Where:
- $T_a$ = Armature Torque (in **N·m**)
- $I_a$ = Armature current (in **Amperes**)

> **Physical Intuition:** Torque is the *mechanical analog of current flow*. Higher flux means stronger magnetic "grip", more conductors mean more force-producing wires, and higher current means more electrons experiencing that force.

---

## 2.3 The Power Equation (Bridge Between Electrical & Mechanical)

The electromagnetic (developed) power is the power converted from electrical to mechanical form (in motor) or vice versa (in generator):

$$P_{conv} = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$$

Where:
- $E_b$ = Back EMF (in motor) or Generated EMF $E_g$ (in generator) (in **V**)
- $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ = Angular velocity (in **rad/s**)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Key Condition |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | EMF Equation | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | $E_g$ in V, $\Phi$ in Wb, $N$ in RPM | All DC machines |
| 2 | Torque Equation | $T_a = 0.159 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $T_a$ in N·m, $I_a$ in A | DC Motor |
| 3 | Power Converted | $P_{m} = E_b \cdot I_a = \dfrac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$ | $P_{m}$ in W, $\omega$ in rad/s | Both modes |
| 4 | Shaft Torque | $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{friction}$ (loss torque) | $T_{sh}$ in N·m | Motor output |
| 5 | Input Power | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ | $V$ in V, $I_L$ in A | Motor input |
| 6 | Output Power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ | $P_{out}$ in W | Motor output |
| 7 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Both modes |
| 8 | Copper Loss | $P_{cu} = I_a^{2} R_a$ | $R_a$ in Ω | Armature |
| 9 | Speed (Motor) | $N = \dfrac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi Z P}$ | From EMF equation | Rearranged |
| 10 | Brush Contact Drop | $V_{brush} = 2 \times 1$ V (typical) | $V_{brush}$ in V | Practical loss |
| 11 | Wave Winding | $A = 2$ | Independent of $P$ | $m$-simplex |
| 12 | Lap Winding | $A = P$ | Equal to number of poles | $P$-simplex |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Unit Consistency is Worth 1 Mark:**
> Always ensure that flux is in **Weber (Wb)**, speed in **RPM** (convert to rev/s by dividing by 60 if needed), and that you use the **correct value of A** (2 for wave, P for lap). Many KTU students lose easy marks by mixing up units or winding types.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important in real engineering?**
> - **Electric Vehicle Design**: Determining torque and speed ratings of DC traction motors.
> - **Renewable Energy**: DC generators are precursors to modern wind turbine generators (AC, but with DC exciters).
> - **Industrial Automation**: Sizing DC servo motors for CNC machines and robotic arms.
> - **Battery-Powered Systems**: Estimating battery drain time using efficiency calculations.
> - **Power Plants**: Backup DC generators for emergency systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Solutions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section Objective:** This section contains **exhaustively detailed** step-by-step solutions to the most important types of numerical problems that appear in KTU University Examinations for Module 1. Every algebraic step and numerical evaluation is shown explicitly — no shortcuts, no skipped steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation (Conceptual Foundation)

**Starting Point:** Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction states that the EMF induced in a conductor is equal to the rate of change of flux linkage.

**Step 1:** Consider one conductor cutting the flux. In one revolution, the conductor cuts a flux equal to:

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = \Phi \times P$$

This is because there are $P$ poles, and as the conductor moves past each pole, it cuts the flux $\Phi$ once.

**Step 2:** For a **simplex wave-wound** machine, the number of parallel paths is $A = 2$, so the number of conductors in series per parallel path is $\frac{Z}{A} = \frac{Z}{2}$.

**Step 3:** Total flux cut per revolution by all conductors in series in one parallel path:

$$\text{Flux cut} = \Phi \times P \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 4:** Time taken for one revolution (in seconds) is:

$$t = \frac{60}{N}$$

**Step 5:** Average EMF per parallel path (and hence the terminal voltage of the machine, ignoring voltage drops):

$$E_g = \frac{\text{Flux cut}}{\text{Time per revolution}} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the master EMF equation. The same equation is used for both DC generators and DC motors, but in a motor, the induced EMF is called **Back EMF ($E_b$)** and it opposes the applied voltage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation (Conceptual Foundation)

**Starting Point:** Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is given by the Lorentz force law:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$

where $B$ is the flux density, $I$ is the current, and $l$ is the length of the conductor.

**Step 1:** The force on a single conductor is:

$$F = B \cdot I_c \cdot l$$

where $I_c = \frac{I_a}{A}$ is the current per conductor.

**Step 2:** Torque produced by one conductor is $F \times r$, where $r$ is the radius of the armature. For a multi-pole machine, the effective radius is $\frac{D}{2}$, but the torque derivation can be simplified by noting that the total torque is proportional to $F \times \frac{D}{2}$ summed over all conductors.

**Step 3:** Summing over all $Z$ conductors and using the relationship between flux density and flux:

$$B = \frac{\Phi}{A_{pole}} = \frac{\Phi P}{2\pi r l}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting and simplifying (after substantial algebra, which we skip in this conceptual view to focus on numericals):

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

**Step 5:** The constant $\frac{1}{2\pi} \approx 0.1591$, giving the practical form:

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{N·m}$$

---

## 3.3 Comprehensive Numerical Solutions (KTU Exam Pattern)

### 📘 Problem 1: EMF Equation (Lap-Wound DC Generator)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**
> *A 4-pole, lap-wound DC generator has 500 armature conductors and a flux per pole of 0.02 Wb. It rotates at 1500 RPM. Calculate the EMF generated. If the machine is rewound as wave-wound with the same conductors, what would be the new EMF at the same speed and flux?*

**Given Data:**
- Number of poles, $P = 4$
- Total armature conductors, $Z = 500$
- Flux per pole, $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb
- Speed, $N = 1500$ RPM

**Step 1: Identify the winding type and parallel paths.**

For lap winding: $A = P = 4$

**Step 2: Apply the EMF equation for lap winding.**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot P} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60}$$

(Notice how $P$ cancels out for lap winding!)

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{0.02 \times 500 \times 1500}{60}$$

**Step 4: Compute numerator first.**

$$0.02 \times 500 = 10$$

$$10 \times 1500 = 15000$$

**Step 5: Divide by 60.**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{15000}{60} = 250 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6: Rewind as wave winding. Now $A = 2$.**

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} = \frac{0.02 \times 500 \times 1500 \times 4}{60 \times 2}$$

**Step 7: Compute numerator.**

$$0.02 \times 500 = 10$$
$$10 \times 1500 = 15000$$
$$15000 \times 4 = 60000$$

**Step 8: Compute denominator.**

$$60 \times 2 = 120$$

**Step 9: Final division.**

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \frac{60000}{120} = 500 \text{ V}$$

**✅ Final Answers:**
- Lap-wound EMF: $E_g = 250$ V
- Wave-wound EMF: $E_g = 500$ V

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Learning:** Wave winding produces **twice the EMF** of lap winding (for a 4-pole machine), because the same conductors are now arranged in fewer parallel paths (2 instead of 4), putting more conductors in series per path.

---

### 📘 Problem 2: Torque and Power in a DC Motor

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> *A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω and a field winding resistance of 250 Ω. The motor draws a total current of 30 A at full load. The armature has 1000 conductors, 4 poles, is wave-wound, and runs at 1000 RPM. Calculate: (a) the flux per pole, (b) the shaft torque, and (c) the efficiency, assuming mechanical and iron losses total 400 W.*

**Given Data:**
- Terminal voltage, $V = 250$ V
- Armature resistance, $R_a = 0.5$ Ω
- Field resistance, $R_{sh} = 250$ Ω
- Line current, $I_L = 30$ A
- Conductors, $Z = 1000$
- Poles, $P = 4$ (wave wound, so $A = 2$)
- Speed, $N = 1000$ RPM
- Mechanical + Iron losses = 400 W

---

**Part (a): Flux per pole**

**Step 1: Calculate shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Calculate armature current using KCL.**

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 30 - 1 = 29 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate back EMF using the motor voltage equation.**

For a DC motor: $V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$

$$E_b = V - I_a \cdot R_a = 250 - (29 \times 0.5) = 250 - 14.5 = 235.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Apply the EMF equation and solve for $\Phi$.**

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$$

Rearranging for $\Phi$:

$$\Phi = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{Z \cdot N \cdot P}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the values.**

$$\Phi = \frac{235.5 \times 60 \times 2}{1000 \times 1000 \times 4}$$

**Step 6: Compute numerator.**

$$235.5 \times 60 = 14130$$
$$14130 \times 2 = 28260$$

**Step 7: Compute denominator.**

$$1000 \times 1000 = 10^6$$
$$10^6 \times 4 = 4 \times 10^6$$

**Step 8: Final division.**

$$\Phi = \frac{28260}{4000000} = 7.065 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi \approx 7.07 \text{ mWb}}$$

---

**Part (b): Shaft Torque**

**Step 1: Calculate the electromagnetic (armature) torque.**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \times 7.065 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 29 \times \frac{4}{2}$$

**Step 3: Simplify $\frac{P}{A}$.**

$$\frac{4}{2} = 2$$

**Step 4: Compute step-by-step.**

$$0.1591 \times 7.065 \times 10^{-3} = 1.124 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$1.124 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 1.124$$
$$1.124 \times 29 = 32.596$$
$$32.596 \times 2 = 65.19 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the loss torque (from mechanical and iron losses).**

$$T_{loss} = \frac{P_{loss}}{\omega} = \frac{P_{loss}}{\frac{2\pi N}{60}} = \frac{60 \cdot P_{loss}}{2\pi N}$$

**Step 6: Substitute and compute.**

$$T_{loss} = \frac{60 \times 400}{2\pi \times 1000} = \frac{24000}{6283.19} = 3.82 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 7: Calculate shaft torque.**

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss} = 65.19 - 3.82 = 61.37 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} \approx 61.37 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

**Part (c): Efficiency**

**Step 1: Calculate input power.**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 30 = 7500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Calculate output (shaft) power.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000 \times 61.37}{60}$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator.**

$$2\pi \times 1000 = 6283.19$$
$$6283.19 \times 61.37 = 385605.3 \text{ (approx.)}$$

**Step 4: Divide by 60.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{385605.3}{60} = 6426.76 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Calculate efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{6426.76}{7500} \times 100 = 85.69\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 85.7\%}$$

---

### 📘 Problem 3: Speed Regulation (DC Shunt Motor)

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern]**
> *A DC shunt motor runs at 1500 RPM on no load. At full load, the armature reaction weakens the flux by 5% and the armature voltage drop is 8 V. If the supply voltage is 220 V and the no-load back EMF is 212 V, calculate the full-load speed.*

**Given Data:**
- No-load speed, $N_0 = 1500$ RPM
- No-load back EMF, $E_{b0} = 212$ V
- Supply voltage, $V = 220$ V
- Armature drop at full load, $I_a R_a = 8$ V
- Flux reduction due to armature reaction, $\Phi_{FL} = 0.95 \cdot \Phi_{NL}$

**Step 1: Calculate full-load back EMF.**

$$E_{b,FL} = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 8 = 212 \text{ V}$$

(Interestingly, in this case the full-load $E_b$ equals the no-load $E_b$ value.)

**Step 2: Apply the speed formula. We know that:**

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P}$$

This means $N \propto \frac{E_b}{\Phi}$.

**Step 3: Form the ratio of full-load to no-load speed.**

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{N_{NL}} = \frac{E_{b,FL} / \Phi_{FL}}{E_{b,NL} / \Phi_{NL}} = \frac{E_{b,FL}}{E_{b,NL}} \cdot \frac{\Phi_{NL}}{\Phi_{FL}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the values.**

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{1500} = \frac{212}{212} \cdot \frac{1}{0.95}$$

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{1500} = 1 \times \frac{1}{0.95} = 1.0526$$

**Step 5: Solve for $N_{FL}$.**

$$N_{FL} = 1500 \times 1.0526 = 1578.9 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 6: Calculate speed regulation.**

$$\text{Regulation} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{1500 - 1578.9}{1578.9} \times 100 = -4.99\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative speed regulation** here means the motor runs *faster* at full load than at no load. This is unusual for a shunt motor and happens because the **armature reaction weakening effect (5%) overcompensates** for the small armature drop.

---

### 📘 Problem 4: Armature Reaction and Demagnetizing Conductors

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Pattern]**
> *A 4-pole, wave-wound DC generator has 800 armature conductors. The brushes are shifted by 4 mechanical degrees from the geometric neutral axis. Calculate the number of demagnetizing and cross-magnetizing armature conductors per pole.*

**Given Data:**
- Poles, $P = 4$
- Conductors, $Z = 800$ (wave-wound, $A = 2$)
- Brush shift, $\theta_{m} = 4°$ (mechanical)

**Step 1: Convert mechanical degrees to electrical degrees.**

$$\theta_{e} = \frac{P}{2} \times \theta_m = \frac{4}{2} \times 4° = 2 \times 4° = 8°$$

**Step 2: Calculate the angular span of demagnetizing zone.**

The demagnetizing zone is the angular range that lies *behind* the new brush position by the same angle $\theta_e$. So the demagnetizing zone spans $2\theta_e$ in electrical degrees:

$$\text{Demag zone} = 2\theta_e = 2 \times 8° = 16°$$

**Step 3: Calculate demagnetizing conductors per pole.**

$$\text{Conductors per pole (electrical)} = \frac{Z}{2P} = \frac{800}{8} = 100 \text{ conductors/pole}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the fraction of demagnetizing zone.**

$$\text{Frac} = \frac{2\theta_e}{180°} = \frac{16°}{180°} = 0.0889$$

**Step 5: Number of demagnetizing conductors per pole.**

$$Z_d = 0.0889 \times 100 = 8.89 \approx 9 \text{ conductors/pole}$$

**Step 6: Total demagnetizing conductors.**

$$Z_{d,\text{total}} = Z_d \times P = 9 \times 4 = 36 \text{ conductors}$$

**Step 7: Cross-magnetizing conductors per pole.**

$$Z_c = 100 - 9 = 91 \text{ conductors/pole}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{d,\text{total}} = 36, \quad Z_c = 91 \text{ per pole}}$$

---

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Bank Notice:** All questions in this section follow the **exact pattern of KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations (ESE)**. The Part A questions are 3-mark direct-concept questions, and Part B questions are 14-mark internal-choice problems with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Model solutions with complete step-by-step working are provided.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**State the EMF equation of a DC generator. Explain the significance of the term "number of parallel paths" (A) in the equation.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> The EMF equation of a DC generator is:
>
> $$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$
>
> where $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $Z$ = total armature conductors, $N$ = speed (RPM), $P$ = number of poles, and $A$ = number of parallel paths.
>
> **Significance of A (1 Mark):**
> The number of parallel paths depends on the winding type: $A = P$ for lap winding and $A = 2$ for wave winding. It determines how the armature conductors are interconnected and directly affects both the **generated EMF** and the **current capacity** of the machine. **Wave winding** (A=2) produces higher EMF with lower current rating, while **lap winding** (A=P) produces lower EMF with higher current rating.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define "armature torque" and "shaft torque" of a DC motor. How are they related?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> - **Armature Torque ($T_a$):** The torque developed by the armature conductors due to the interaction of armature current and magnetic flux. It is the gross torque produced inside the machine. (1.5 Marks)
>
> $$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{N·m}$$
>
> - **Shaft Torque ($T_{sh}$):** The net torque available at the motor shaft for doing useful mechanical work, after subtracting the losses due to friction, windage, and iron losses. (1.5 Marks)
>
> **Relationship:** $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss}$, where $T_{loss}$ is the torque lost to mechanical and iron losses.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
> *A 250 V, 4-pole, lap-wound DC shunt motor has 800 armature conductors. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω and the field resistance is 125 Ω. The motor takes a total current of 60 A at full load. The flux per pole is 30 mWb, and the mechanical and iron losses total 800 W.*

**Calculate the following:**
- **(a)** The back EMF, armature current, and speed at full load. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** The shaft torque, output power, and efficiency at full load. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $V = 250$ V, $P = 4$, lap-wound so $A = 4$
- $Z = 800$, $R_a = 0.2$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 125$ Ω
- $I_L = 60$ A, $\Phi = 0.03$ Wb
- Mechanical + Iron losses = 800 W

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{125} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Armature current.**

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 60 - 2 = 58 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Back EMF.**

$$E_b = V - I_a \cdot R_a = 250 - (58 \times 0.2) = 250 - 11.6 = 238.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Speed using EMF equation (rearranged for N).**

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P} = \frac{238.4 \times 60 \times 4}{0.03 \times 800 \times 4}$$

**Step 5: Numerator.**

$$238.4 \times 60 = 14304$$
$$14304 \times 4 = 57216$$

**Step 6: Denominator.**

$$0.03 \times 800 = 24$$
$$24 \times 4 = 96$$

**Step 7: Division.**

$$N = \frac{57216}{96} = 596 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 238.4 \text{ V}, \quad I_a = 58 \text{ A}, \quad N = 596 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Armature torque.**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} = 0.1591 \times 0.03 \times 800 \times 58 \times \frac{4}{4}$$

**Step 2: Simplification (P/A = 1 for lap winding).**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \times 0.03 \times 800 \times 58 \times 1$$

**Step 3: Compute step-by-step.**

$$0.1591 \times 0.03 = 4.773 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$4.773 \times 10^{-3} \times 800 = 3.8184$$
$$3.8184 \times 58 = 221.47 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Loss torque from mechanical and iron losses.**

$$T_{loss} = \frac{60 \times P_{loss}}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 800}{2\pi \times 596} = \frac{48000}{3744.7} = 12.82 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Shaft torque.**

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss} = 221.47 - 12.82 = 208.65 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6: Output power.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 596 \times 208.65}{60}$$

**Step 7: Numerator computation.**

$$2\pi \times 596 = 3744.74$$
$$3744.74 \times 208.65 = 781415.5$$

**Step 8: Division.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{781415.5}{60} = 13023.6 \text{ W} \approx 13.02 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 9: Input power.**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 60 = 15000 \text{ W} = 15 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 10: Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{13023.6}{15000} \times 100 = 86.82\%$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} = 208.65 \text{ N·m}, \quad P_{out} = 13.02 \text{ kW}, \quad \eta = 86.82\%}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**
> *A 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt generator has 600 armature conductors. The armature and field resistances are 0.3 Ω and 200 Ω, respectively. The flux per pole is 0.025 Wb, and the generator delivers a load current of 100 A at 250 V.*

**Calculate the following:**
- **(a)** The generated EMF and the speed of the generator. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** The armature copper loss, total losses, and efficiency of the generator. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $P = 4$, wave-wound, $A = 2$
- $Z = 600$, $R_a = 0.3$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 200$ Ω
- $\Phi = 0.025$ Wb
- Load current $I_L = 100$ A, terminal voltage $V = 250$ V

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{200} = 1.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Calculate armature current (same as load current in a generator when $I_L$ is the line current; here, the load is connected to the terminals, so $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$).**

$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 1.25 = 101.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the voltage drop in the armature.**

$$I_a \cdot R_a = 101.25 \times 0.3 = 30.375 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the generated EMF (DC generator voltage equation: $E_g = V + I_a R_a + V_{brush}$).**

Assuming brush contact drop of 2 V (standard assumption: 1 V per brush):

$$E_g = 250 + 30.375 + 2 = 282.375 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Calculate speed from the EMF equation.**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$$

$$N = \frac{E_g \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P} = \frac{282.375 \times 60 \times 2}{0.025 \times 600 \times 4}$$

**Step 6: Numerator.**

$$282.375 \times 60 = 16942.5$$
$$16942.5 \times 2 = 33885$$

**Step 7: Denominator.**

$$0.025 \times 600 = 15$$
$$15 \times 4 = 60$$

**Step 8: Division.**

$$N = \frac{33885}{60} = 564.75 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{E_g = 282.375 \text{ V}, \quad N = 564.75 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Armature copper loss.**

$$P_{cu,a} = I_a^{2} \cdot R_a = (101.25)^2 \times 0.3$$

**Step 2: Compute $(101.25)^2$.**

$$(101.25)^2 = 10251.5625$$

**Step 3: Multiply by $R_a$.**

$$P_{cu,a} = 10251.5625 \times 0.3 = 3075.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Field copper loss.**

$$P_{cu,sh} = I_{sh}^{2} \cdot R_{sh} = (1.25)^2 \times 200 = 1.5625 \times 200 = 312.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Brush contact loss (assuming 2 V total drop and $I_a$ flowing through brushes).**

$$P_{brush} = V_{brush} \cdot I_a = 2 \times 101.25 = 202.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6: Total electrical losses (assuming no mechanical/iron loss data given, only electrical losses).**

$$P_{loss,total} = P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,sh} + P_{brush} = 3075.47 + 312.5 + 202.5 = 3590.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7: Output power delivered to the load.**

$$P_{out} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 100 = 25000 \text{ W} = 25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8: Input mechanical power.**

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss,total} = 25000 + 3590.47 = 28590.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 9: Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{25000}{28590.47} \times 100 = 87.44\%$$

$$\boxed{P_{cu,a} = 3075.47 \text{ W}, \quad P_{loss,total} = 3590.47 \text{ W}, \quad \eta = 87.44\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
>
> 1. **Confusing A = P (lap) vs A = 2 (wave):** This single mistake can lead to a 50% error in your EMF and Torque calculations. Always re-read the problem statement for the winding type.
> 2. **Forgetting the field current in $I_a$ calculation:** In a **motor**, $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$. In a **generator**, $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the brush contact drop:** Many students assume $E_g = V$ in generators and $E_b = V$ in motors, ignoring the $I_a R_a$ drop. You will lose 1-2 marks if this is missing.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing RPM and rad/s for angular velocity. KTU expects RPM in the EMF equation directly, and rad/s in the power equation $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$.
> 5. **Shaft vs. Armature Torque confusion:** The question may ask for "shaft torque" but students compute "armature torque" and skip the loss torque subtraction.
> 6. **Power equation sign errors:** Output = Input − Losses. Always check the direction of energy flow before writing the equation.
> 7. **No unit on final answer:** A numerical answer without units (V, A, RPM, N·m, W, %) is considered incomplete and will lose 0.5–1 mark per instance.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 Numerical Problems**

- ✅ The **EMF Equation** $E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ is the **master equation** for all DC machine voltage calculations.
- ✅ For **Lap Winding**: $A = P$. For **Wave Winding**: $A = 2$ (independent of poles).
- ✅ The **Torque Equation** $T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$ applies to **motors only**; in generators, this represents the mechanical torque required.
- ✅ The **Voltage Equation**:
   - Motor: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (back EMF opposes applied voltage)
   - Generator: $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ (generated EMF exceeds terminal voltage)
- ✅ The **Current Equation**:
   - Motor: $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$
   - Generator: $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$
- ✅ The **Power Conversion Equation**: $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \omega = \frac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$.
- ✅ **Shaft Torque** = Armature Torque − Loss Torque.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$, always between 0% and 100%.
- ✅ **Speed Regulation** $= \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$. For shunt motors, it's typically +1% to +5% (positive).
- ✅ In **armature reaction** problems: convert mechanical degrees to electrical degrees using $\theta_e = \frac{P}{2} \theta_m$.
- ✅ **Demagnetizing conductors per pole** $= \frac{Z}{2P} \cdot \frac{2\theta_e}{180°}$.
- ✅ **Cross-magnetizing conductors per pole** = (Total conductors per pole) − (Demagnetizing conductors per pole).
- ✅ Always carry **units** through your calculation: Wb, RPM, V, A, Ω, N·m, W, %.
- ✅ **Conversion check**: $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ — if you forget to convert, you'll get errors in power/torque calculations.
- ✅ **Sanity check your answer**: EMF should be in tens to hundreds of volts; torque in single to hundreds of N·m; efficiency typically 75–95%.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
