# ⚡ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the commutator and brushes in a DC motor.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
The **commutator** is a cylindrical assembly of copper segments insulated from each other by mica, mounted on the rotor shaft. Its role is to act as a **mechanical rectifier** — it reverses the direction of current in each armature coil every time the coil crosses the **magnetic neutral axis**, ensuring that the torque on the rotor remains **unidirectional** (continuous rotation in one direction).

The **brushes** are carbon/graphite blocks that slide on the commutator surface. They provide a sliding electrical contact between the **stationary external DC supply** and the **rotating armature winding**. Together, the commutator and brushes convert the **AC induced in the armature coils** into **DC at the motor terminals** (in generator) or maintain the correct **current direction in the coils** (in motor). **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is back EMF in a DC motor? Why is it called "back" EMF?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
When the armature of a DC motor rotates, its conductors **cut the main field flux**, and by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an EMF is induced in them. By **Lenz's Law**, this induced EMF **opposes the cause that produces it**, i.e., it opposes the applied supply voltage $V$. Hence it is called **back EMF**, denoted $E_b$.

The back EMF is given by:
$$E_b = \dfrac{\phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

It is also called **counter-EMF** or **regenerative EMF**. The motor equation is $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, meaning the supply voltage must overcome both the back EMF and the armature resistance drop. As speed $N$ increases, $E_b$ increases, drawing less armature current — this is the **self-regulating** property of DC motors. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — *Module Internal Choice*

> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the EMF equation and torque equation of a DC motor. State the relationship between the two.**

**Solution:**

**EMF Equation Derivation:**
Consider a DC motor with $P$ poles, $Z$ total armature conductors, $A$ parallel paths, and speed $N$ RPM.

Flux cut per conductor per revolution = $\phi \cdot P$ Weber.

Time for one revolution = $\dfrac{60}{N}$ seconds.

Average EMF per conductor:
$$e_{cond} = \dfrac{\phi P}{60/N} = \dfrac{\phi P N}{60} \text{ V}$$

Number of conductors in series per parallel path = $\dfrac{Z}{A}$.

Total EMF:
$$\boxed{E_b = \dfrac{\phi Z N P}{60 A}}$$

**Torque Equation Derivation:**
Force on a single conductor of length $L$ carrying current $I_c$ in flux density $B$:
$$F = B \cdot I_c \cdot L = \dfrac{\phi}{a} \cdot I_c \cdot L = \phi \cdot \dfrac{I_c L}{a}$$

With $a = \pi r L$ (pole-face area per conductor), torque per conductor:
$$T_{cond} = F \cdot r = \phi \cdot I_c \cdot \dfrac{Lr}{\pi r L} = \dfrac{\phi I_c}{\pi}$$

With $I_c = I_a / A$, summing over $Z$ conductors and integrating over $P$ poles:
$$\boxed{T_a = \dfrac{\phi Z I_a P}{2 \pi A}}$$

**Relationship:**
Mechanical power developed: $P_m = T_a \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a$

$$\dfrac{\phi Z I_a P}{2\pi A} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{\phi Z N P}{60 A} \cdot I_a \quad ✓$$

Both equations share the term $\phi Z P$ and balance each other through the speed term. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A 440 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.3 Ω and a field resistance of 220 Ω. The motor takes an armature current of 50 A on full load, and the speed is 1000 RPM. Calculate: (i) Back EMF, (ii) Developed torque, (iii) Shaft torque, given that rotational losses are 800 W.**

**Solution:**

**Given:** $V = 440$ V, $R_a = 0.3 \;\Omega$, $R_{sh} = 220 \;\Omega$, $I_a = 50$ A, $N = 1000$ RPM, $P_{rot} = 800$ W.

**(i) Back EMF $E_b$:**
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 440 - (50)(0.3) = 440 - 15 = \boxed{425 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Developed Torque $T_a$:**

Angular speed: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi (1000)}{60} = 104.72$ rad/s

Gross mechanical power:
$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = 425 \times 50 = 21{,}250 \text{ W}$$

$$T_a = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega} = \dfrac{21{,}250}{104.72} = \boxed{202.89 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(iii) Shaft Torque $T_{sh}$:**

Net output power: $P_{out} = P_m - P_{rot} = 21{,}250 - 800 = 20{,}450$ W

$$T_{sh} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\omega} = \dfrac{20{,}450}{104.72} = \boxed{195.28 \text{ N·m}}$$

> ✅ **Check:** $T_a - T_{loss} = 202.89 - \dfrac{800}{104.72} = 202.89 - 7.64 = 195.25$ N·m ✓ **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the construction and working of a DC motor with a neat diagram. Derive the voltage equation.**

**Solution:**

**Construction:** (Refer to SECTION_2 and SECTION_4 for full details)

A DC motor consists of:
- **Yoke** (cast iron/steel frame) — mechanical support + flux path.
- **Pole cores and pole shoes** (laminated silicon steel) — carry field coils and spread flux.
- **Field winding** (copper) — produces main flux $\phi$ when DC-excited.
- **Armature core** (laminated silicon steel) — reduces eddy current loss.
- **Armature winding** (in slots) — carries current $I_a$.
- **Commutator** (copper segments + mica insulation) — reverses current direction.
- **Brushes** (carbon) — sliding electrical contact.

**Working:**
When DC supply is given:
1. Field winding carries $I_f$ → flux $\phi$ established in air gap.
2. Armature carries $I_a$ through brushes + commutator.
3. Conductors in magnetic field experience force $F = B I_a L$ (Fleming's Left-Hand Rule).
4. Net tangential force produces developed torque $T_a$.
5. Rotor rotates, conductors cut flux, generating back EMF $E_b$ (Faraday's + Lenz's Law).

**Voltage Equation Derivation:**
Applying KVL to the armature loop:
$$V - I_a R_a - E_b = 0$$

$$\boxed{V = E_b + I_a R_a}$$

Or in terms of flux and speed:
$$V = \dfrac{\phi Z N P}{60 A} + I_a R_a$$

At starting: $N = 0 \Rightarrow E_b = 0$, so starting current is $I_{a,start} = V/R_a$ (very high — hence the need for a **starter**). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A 250 V DC series motor has an armature + field resistance of 0.5 Ω. When running at 1500 RPM, it draws 40 A. Find: (i) Back EMF, (ii) Power developed, (iii) Developed torque, (iv) Speed at which it will run if the flux is reduced to 70% of the original value, with the same armature current.**

**Solution:**

**Given:** $V = 250$ V, $R_a + R_{se} = 0.5 \;\Omega$, $I_a = 40$ A, $N_1 = 1500$ RPM.

**(i) Back EMF $E_{b1}$:**
$$E_{b1} = V - I_a (R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - (40)(0.5) = 250 - 20 = \boxed{230 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Power Developed $P_m$:**
$$P_m = E_{b1} \cdot I_a = 230 \times 40 = \boxed{9200 \text{ W} = 9.2 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Developed Torque $T_{a1}$:**

$\omega_1 = \dfrac{2\pi (1500)}{60} = 157.08$ rad/s

$$T_{a1} = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega_1} = \dfrac{9200}{157.08} = \boxed{58.57 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(iv) New Speed $N_2$ at $\phi_2 = 0.7 \phi_1$:**

Using $E_b = k \phi N$ and assuming $k$ constant:

$$\dfrac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} = \dfrac{\phi_2 N_2}{\phi_1 N_1}$$

With $I_a$ unchanged, the IR drop is the same, so $E_{b2} = E_{b1} = 230$ V.

$$1 = \dfrac{0.7 \cdot N_2}{1 \cdot 1500}$$

$$N_2 = \dfrac{1500}{0.7} = \boxed{2142.86 \text{ RPM}}$$

> **Note:** In a series motor, $I_a = I_f$, so $\phi \propto I_a$ (ignoring saturation). When $\phi$ is reduced, speed increases — illustrating **field-weakening speed control**. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Unit Errors** — Most common. Forgetting to convert $N$ from RPM to $\omega$ (rad/s) when computing torque. Always: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.
> 2. **Mixing up $E_b$ and $V$** — $E_b \neq V$. They differ by $I_a R_a$. In a shunt motor, this drop is small (~2–5% of $V$).
> 3. **Confusing Developed Torque $T_a$ with Shaft Torque $T_{sh}$** — $T_a = T_{sh} + T_{rot}$ (rotational torque loss). $P_m = T_a \cdot \omega$, but $P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega$.
> 4. **Sign of $E_b$** — In a motor, $E_b$ is *opposing* $V$ (Lenz's Law). In a generator, $E_b$ is *aiding* the current. KTU loves this conceptual question.
> 5. **Wave vs Lap Winding** — Lap: $A = P$ (low voltage, high current). Wave: $A = 2$ (high voltage, low current). Students often mix these up.
> 6. **Series Motor No-Load** — Many students forget to state that series motor must never be started without load. This is a guaranteed 2-mark question in KTU.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this before the exam!)**

- ✅ **DC Motor** converts DC electrical energy into mechanical rotational energy using **Lorentz Force** ($F = BIL$).
- ✅ **Back EMF** $E_b = \dfrac{\phi Z N P}{60 A}$ is generated by rotation; **opposes** applied voltage.
- ✅ **Motor Voltage Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (steady state).
- ✅ **Starting Condition:** $N = 0 \Rightarrow E_b = 0 \Rightarrow I_{a,start} = V/R_a$ (very high → use a **starter**).
- ✅ **Torque Equation:** $T_a = \dfrac{\phi Z I_a P}{2 \pi A} = k_T \phi I_a$ (developed torque, *not* shaft torque).
- ✅ **Speed Equation:** $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \phi}$.
- ✅ **Power Developed:** $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega$.
- ✅ **Shaft Output:** $P_{out} = P_m - P_{rot} = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60}$.
- ✅ **DC Shunt Motor:** $\phi$ = constant → nearly **constant speed** → safe no-load → moderate starting torque.
- ✅ **DC Series Motor:** $\phi \propto I_a$ → high starting torque → **inversely varying speed** → **never run at no-load** (runaway!).
- ✅ **DC Compound Motor:** combination of both — high starting torque + safe no-load behavior.
- ✅ **Commutator** = mechanical rectifier → ensures unidirectional torque.
- ✅ **Brushes** = sliding contact between stationary supply and rotating armature.
- ✅ **Lamination** of armature core reduces **eddy current losses**.
- ✅ **Lap winding:** $A = P$ (high current, low voltage). **Wave winding:** $A = 2$ (high voltage, low current).
- ✅ **Speed control methods:** (1) Armature voltage control, (2) Field flux control, (3) Series resistance in armature.
- ✅ **Mechanical power developed is at the armature periphery**, not at the shaft — subtract rotational losses to get shaft power.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{V I_L - I_a^2 R_a - I_f^2 R_{sh} - \text{rotational + brush losses}}{V I_L}$.

> **🎯 Final Tip:** KTU 2024 examiners love to ask a **2-part question**: (a) derivation of EMF/torque, (b) numerical using the derived formula. Practice both — derivation steps and units carry the marks!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Back EMF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Back EMF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Back EMF (Counter EMF), denoted as $E_b$ or $E_c$:**
> It is the electromotive force induced in the rotating armature conductors of a DC motor when they cut the magnetic flux produced by the field poles. By **Lenz's Law**, this induced EMF always opposes the cause producing it — i.e., the applied supply voltage $V$. Hence it is termed *back* (opposing) EMF.

Mathematically, the magnitude of the average back EMF induced in a DC machine armature is given by the fundamental EMF equation:

$$E_b = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A}$$

where the symbols carry standard meanings listed in the formula sheet below.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a small paddle-wheel boat trying to move upstream against a river current:

- The **engine (supply voltage $V$)** pushes the boat forward.
- As the boat moves, **water resistance (back EMF $E_b$)** builds up, opposing the engine.
- The *net* effort available to move the boat equals **Engine force − Water resistance** — analogous to the net voltage $V - E_b$ that drives current through the armature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Back EMF is the machine's own *electrical signature of rotation*. If the motor spins faster, $E_b$ rises; if it slows down, $E_b$ falls. This single self-regulating property is what makes a DC motor inherently self-stabilizing in speed.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Flux per pole $\Phi$** — measured in **Weber (Wb)**.
- **Speed $N$** — measured in **RPM (revolutions per minute)**.
- **Number of poles $P$** — dimensionless integer (typically 2, 4, 6).
- **Total armature conductors $Z$** — dimensionless integer.
- **Number of parallel paths $A$**:
  - **$A = 2$** for **wave winding**
  - **$A = P$** for **lap winding**
- **Machine constant $K$** — defined later — has units of **V/(Wb·rad/s)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does Back EMF Exist? — The Physical 'Why'

1. The DC motor receives electrical power from the DC supply.
2. Current flows through the **field winding** (producing $\Phi$) and the **armature winding** (placed in slots on the rotor).
3. Interaction of armature current and field flux produces torque — the rotor begins to turn.
4. As soon as the rotor turns, the **armature conductors physically cut the field flux** — by **Faraday's Law**, an EMF is induced in them.
5. By **Lenz's Law**, this induced EMF opposes the very rotation that caused it. Since rotation is caused by supply voltage, the induced EMF opposes the supply voltage.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners love this line:** *"A DC motor is simultaneously a DC generator — it generates a back EMF the instant it starts rotating."*

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Flux linkage:** Each armature conductor links a flux of $\Phi$ Wb twice per pole-pair as it rotates.
- **Step 2 — Rate of change:** For $N$ RPM, the time per revolution is $60/N$ seconds.
- **Step 3 — Induced EMF per conductor:** Average EMF per conductor = (flux cut per revolution) / (time per revolution).
- **Step 4 — Series-parallel grouping:** Conductors are distributed in $A$ parallel paths, each path having $Z/A$ conductors in series.
- **Step 5 — Combine:** Multiply per-conductor EMF by $Z/A$ to get terminal $E_b$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60\,A}$ | Fundamental EMF equation | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, \omega}{2\pi A}$ | EMF in terms of angular speed $\omega$ (rad/s) | V |
| 3 | $E_b = K \, \Phi \, \omega$ | Compact form, $K = \dfrac{Z}{2\pi A}$ = machine constant | V |
| 4 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Voltage equation of a DC motor | V |
| 5 | $V = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_{se})$ | For compound/DC series motor including series-field drop | V |
| 6 | $I_a = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a}$ | Armature current expression | A |
| 7 | $P_d = E_b \, I_a$ | Mechanical power developed in armature | W |
| 8 | $T_a = \dfrac{P_d}{\omega} = K \, \Phi \, I_a$ | Electromagnetic (armature) torque | N·m |
| 9 | $T_a = 9.55 \, \dfrac{E_b \, I_a}{N}$ | Torque in N·m using $N$ in RPM | N·m |
| 10 | $\eta_{elec} = \dfrac{E_b}{V}$ | Electrical efficiency ratio (fraction of input converted to mechanical power) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students forget to convert $N$ from RPM to revolutions per *second* (divide by 60) or to $\omega$ in rad/s. This produces off-by-$2\pi$ errors worth full marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speed Regulation:** $N \propto (V - I_a R_a)/\Phi$ — used in DC drive design.
- **Self-protection:** During sudden overload, rotor slows → $E_b$ drops → $I_a$ rises → protective tripping.
- **Regenerative Braking:** When $E_b > V$, the machine acts as a generator and feeds power back.
- **Servo/Drone BLDC motors:** The same back-EMF principle governs sensorless commutation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Back EMF Equation $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$

Let us derive the back EMF formula from **first principles** using Faraday's Law. This is a KTU-favourite 7-mark derivation.

**Assumptions:**
- Lap winding with $A = P$ parallel paths, or wave winding with $A = 2$.
- The flux cut per pole = $\Phi$ Wb.
- Each conductor cuts flux twice per pole-pair (once under N, once under S).
- Flux per pole-pair = $P \Phi / 2$ (since a pole-pair has one N and one S pole, each carrying $\Phi$).

**Step 1 — Flux cut per revolution by one conductor:**

In one complete revolution, a conductor passes under all $P$ poles. The total flux cut per revolution by a single conductor equals:

$$\text{Flux cut/rev} = P \times \Phi = P \Phi \quad \text{Webers}$$

*Explanation:* Each pole contributes $\Phi$ Wb; the conductor links each pole once per revolution.

**Step 2 — Time taken for one revolution:**

If the armature rotates at $N$ RPM, time per revolution is:

$$T_{rev} = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{seconds}$$

*Explanation:* $N$ revolutions in 60 s → 1 revolution in $60/N$ s.

**Step 3 — Average EMF induced per conductor (Faraday's Law):**

Average EMF per conductor = rate of change of flux linkage:

$$e_{cond} = \frac{\text{Flux cut per rev}}{\text{Time per rev}} = \frac{P \Phi}{60/N} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \quad \text{volts}$$

*Explanation:* Faraday's law: $e = d\lambda/dt$. Here $\lambda = P\Phi$ and $dt = 60/N$.

**Step 4 — Number of conductors in series per parallel path:**

The $Z$ armature conductors are divided into $A$ parallel paths, each containing $Z/A$ conductors connected in **series**. Hence total EMF per path = (EMF per conductor) × (conductors in series per path):

$$E_b = e_{cond} \times \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\,E_b = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A}\,}$$

The back EMF is **directly proportional** to:
- Flux per pole $\Phi$
- Speed $N$
- Total conductors $Z$
- Number of poles $P$

…and **inversely proportional** to the number of parallel paths $A$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Voltage Equation $V = E_b + I_a R_a$

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the armature loop of a DC shunt motor:

**Step 1:** The supply voltage $V$ is the only source.

**Step 2:** Two voltage drops exist in the loop:
- The ohmic drop across armature resistance $R_a$: $I_a R_a$.
- The back EMF $E_b$ (which acts *opposing* $V$, so it is treated as a voltage drop).

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$V = I_a R_a + E_b$$

**Step 4:** Rearranged for armature current:

$$I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This single equation is the *operational heart* of every DC motor. From it, we derive torque, speed, and power relations.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Torque Equation $T_a = K \Phi I_a$

**Step 1 — Mechanical power developed in the armature:**

The power converted from electrical to mechanical form is the power transferred to the back-EMF element:

$$P_d = E_b \, I_a = \left( \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A} \right) I_a$$

**Step 2 — Power in terms of torque and angular speed:**

$$P_d = T_a \cdot \omega = T_a \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Equating the two expressions:**

$$T_a \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A} I_a$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $N/60$ from both sides:**

$$T_a \cdot 2\pi = \frac{P \Phi Z}{A} I_a$$

**Step 5 — Final torque expression:**

$$\boxed{\,T_a = \frac{P \, Z}{2\pi \, A} \, \Phi \, I_a = K \, \Phi \, I_a\,}$$

where the **machine constant** is $K = \dfrac{P Z}{2\pi A}$ and $T_a$ is in **N·m**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook:** $\;E_b = K \Phi \omega\;$ and $\;T_a = K \Phi I_a\;$ — note the beautiful symmetry. Power = $E_b I_a = T_a \omega$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Interrelations

| Quantity | Proportional to | Inverse to |
|----------|------------------|------------|
| $E_b$ | $\Phi,\; N,\; Z,\; P$ | $A$ |
| $I_a$ (motor) | $V - E_b$ | $R_a$ |
| $T_a$ | $\Phi,\; I_a,\; P,\; Z$ | $A$ |
| $N$ (motor) | $V - I_a R_a$ | $\Phi$ |
| $P_d$ (developed) | $E_b,\; I_a$ | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DC Motor Showing Back EMF

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a DC motor, illustrating the relationship between the supply, the armature resistance drop, the back EMF, and the mechanical output. This is the **fallback block architecture** representation (equivalent to the standard equivalent circuit) as required by the protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VS["V Supply Volts"] --> NET["Net Driving Voltage Node V minus Eb"]
    RA["Ra Armature Resistance Ohms"] --> NET
    NET --> IA["Ia Armature Current Amp"]
    IA --> EB["Eb Back EMF Opposes V"]
    EB --> TOR["Ta Armature Torque N m"]
    TOR --> MEC["Mechanical Output Shaft Power Pd"]
    IA --> LOSS["Copper Loss Ia squared Ra"]
    MEC --> SPD["Speed N RPM"]
    SPD -.feeds back.-> EB
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- $V$ supplies electrical power → some is lost as $I_a^2 R_a$ copper loss → the remainder appears across $E_b$ → $E_b$ drives the mechanical output (torque × speed) → the resulting speed $N$ closes the feedback loop by determining $E_b$ itself.

## 4.2 Power Flow Architecture in a DC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PIN["Pin Electrical Input V times Ia"] --> PCU["Pcu Copper Loss Ia squared Ra"]
    PIN --> PMECH["Pd Mechanical Power Developed Eb times Ia"]
    PCU --> HEAT["Heat Dissipation"]
    PMECH --> PBR["Pbrush Friction and Windage Loss"]
    PMECH --> PSHAFT["Pout Shaft Output"]
    PBR --> HEAT
```

**Interpretation:** Input electrical power $P_{in}$ splits at the armature terminals into:
- **Copper loss** $I_a^2 R_a$ (heat).
- **Developed mechanical power** $P_d = E_b I_a$ (rotational form).
- $P_d$ further splits into **rotational losses** (friction + windage + core) and useful **shaft output** $P_{out}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Back EMF is the bridge** between the electrical domain ($V$, $I_a$) and the mechanical domain ($N$, $T_a$). It is the *only* element that appears in both power expressions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — Definition) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define back EMF in a DC motor. Why is it called "back" EMF?**

**Model Answer:**

Back EMF, denoted $E_b$, is the EMF induced in the rotating armature conductors of a DC motor due to the relative motion between the conductors and the main field flux, in accordance with Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction.

$$E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$$

It is called *back* EMF because, by **Lenz's Law**, the direction of this induced EMF is such that it **opposes the applied supply voltage** $V$ that is causing the armature rotation. In the equivalent circuit, $E_b$ therefore appears as a voltage drop opposing the source.

---

### Question 2 (Conceptual — Significance) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is the significance of back EMF in a DC motor? What happens to the back EMF if (i) the flux is doubled, and (ii) the speed is halved?**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance:**
1. Back EMF is the *self-regulating mechanism* of a DC motor — it limits the armature current and provides inherent speed regulation.
2. It governs the operating voltage equation $V = E_b + I_a R_a$.
3. It determines the mechanical power developed: $P_d = E_b I_a$.
4. It forms the basis of regenerative braking.

**Effect of parameter changes (since $E_b \propto \Phi N$):**

(i) If flux $\Phi$ is doubled (with $N$ constant): $E_b$ **doubles** ($2 \times$).

(ii) If speed $N$ is halved (with $\Phi$ constant): $E_b$ is reduced to **half** ($0.5 \times$).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern, students must answer the chosen question in its entirety, including all sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the back EMF induced in the armature of a DC machine. State clearly the role of Lenz's Law in determining its polarity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, 500 V, lap-wound DC shunt motor has 800 armature conductors. The armature resistance is $0.2\ \Omega$ and the flux per pole is $30\ \text{mWb}$. The motor draws an armature current of $100\ \text{A}$ at a certain load. Calculate:
  (i) the back EMF $E_b$,
  (ii) the speed $N$ in RPM,
  (iii) the electromagnetic torque $T_a$ developed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 for full derivation. Summary below:)*

**Step 1:** Flux cut per revolution by one conductor = $P \Phi$ Wb.

**Step 2:** Time per revolution = $60/N$ seconds.

**Step 3:** EMF per conductor = $\dfrac{P \Phi N}{60}$ V.

**Step 4:** Conductors in series per parallel path = $Z/A$.

**Step 5:** 

$$E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A} \quad \text{(Volts)}$$

**Role of Lenz's Law:** The induced EMF always opposes the change that produced it. Here, the rotation is caused by the supply voltage, so the induced EMF opposes the supply. Hence in the equivalent circuit, $E_b$ is treated as a voltage drop in the KVL equation $V = E_b + I_a R_a$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given data:**
- $P = 4$ (lap wound) → $A = P = 4$
- $V = 500\ \text{V}$
- $Z = 800$
- $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$
- $\Phi = 30\ \text{mWb} = 30 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Wb}$
- $I_a = 100\ \text{A}$

**(i) Back EMF $E_b$:**

Using the voltage equation:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 500 - (100)(0.2) = 500 - 20 = 480\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Speed $N$:**

Rearranging the EMF equation:

$$N = \frac{60 \, E_b \, A}{P \, \Phi \, Z}$$

Substituting:

$$N = \frac{60 \times 480 \times 4}{4 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 800}$$

$$N = \frac{60 \times 480 \times 4}{96} = \frac{115200}{96} = 1200\ \text{RPM}$$

**(iii) Electromagnetic torque $T_a$:**

First, mechanical power developed:

$$P_d = E_b \times I_a = 480 \times 100 = 48000\ \text{W}$$

Angular speed:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 40\pi \approx 125.66\ \text{rad/s}$$

Torque:

$$T_a = \frac{P_d}{\omega} = \frac{48000}{125.66} \approx 382.0\ \text{N·m}$$

**Alternative formula check:**

$$T_a = 9.55 \times \frac{E_b I_a}{N} = 9.55 \times \frac{48000}{1200} = 9.55 \times 40 = 382\ \text{N·m} \ \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Starting from the voltage equation of a DC motor, derive the expression for armature torque in terms of flux and armature current. Show that the developed power equals $E_b I_a$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of $0.5\ \Omega$ and runs at 1000 RPM taking an armature current of $20\ \text{A}$ at full load. Calculate:
  (i) the back EMF at full load,
  (ii) the value of additional resistance to be inserted in series with the armature to reduce the speed to 800 RPM, assuming flux remains constant. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Derivation of torque equation:** *(See Section 3.3 for full derivation. Summary below:)*

**Step 1:** Voltage equation: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ → $E_b = V - I_a R_a$.

**Step 2:** Developed mechanical power = $P_d = E_b I_a$ (since $E_b$ represents the energy conversion element).

**Step 3:** Power in mechanical form: $P_d = T_a \omega$, where $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.

**Step 4:** Equating and using $E_b = P \Phi N Z / 60 A$:

$$T_a \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A} \cdot I_a$$

**Step 5:** Solving:

$$\boxed{\,T_a = \frac{P Z}{2\pi A} \, \Phi \, I_a = K \Phi I_a\,}$$

where $K = PZ/(2\pi A)$ is the machine constant. The developed power equals $E_b I_a$ by definition (it is the product of the rotational-EMF and the current through it).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given data:**
- $V = 220\ \text{V}$
- $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$
- $N_1 = 1000\ \text{RPM}$, $I_{a1} = 20\ \text{A}$ (full load)
- $N_2 = 800\ \text{RPM}$ (reduced speed)
- Flux $\Phi$ = constant

**(i) Back EMF at full load:**

$$E_{b1} = V - I_{a1} R_a = 220 - (20)(0.5) = 220 - 10 = 210\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Additional resistance for speed reduction:**

Since $\Phi$ is constant: $E_b \propto N$.

$$E_{b2} = E_{b1} \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 210 \times \frac{800}{1000} = 168\ \text{V}$$

Assuming $I_a$ remains approximately constant at 20 A (shunt motor with constant load torque):

$$E_{b2} = V - I_{a1} (R_a + R_{ext})$$

$$168 = 220 - 20 (0.5 + R_{ext})$$

$$20 (0.5 + R_{ext}) = 220 - 168 = 52$$

$$0.5 + R_{ext} = 2.6$$

$$\boxed{\,R_{ext} = 2.1\ \Omega\,}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Back EMF Problems):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up lap vs wave winding:** Lap wound → $A = P$; wave wound → $A = 2$. A 4-pole machine gives $A = 4$ (lap) or $A = 2$ (wave) — a factor-of-2 error costs **2 to 3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** $\Phi$ is usually given in **mWb** — must convert to **Wb** (multiply by $10^{-3}$). Speed in **RPM** must NOT be plugged directly into $\omega$ formulae.
>
> 3. **Sign of back EMF:** In motor operation, $E_b < V$ always. If a calculation gives $E_b > V$, immediately check direction assumptions — it is now in **generator mode**.
>
> 4. **Torque unit confusion:** Final answer for $T_a$ **must** be in **N·m**. A common error is leaving the answer in some other inconsistent unit.
>
> 5. **Neglecting brush contact drop:** In precise numericals, the brush drop (~1–2 V) is sometimes added to $I_a R_a$ — read the question carefully.
>
> 6. **Skipping derivation steps in 7-mark sub-parts:** Examiners reward *every labeled step*. A bare final formula with no intermediate logic will fetch only 2–3 marks out of 7.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Back EMF** = EMF induced in the rotating armature, opposing applied voltage (Lenz's Law).
- ✅ **Magnitude:** $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$.
- ✅ **Voltage equation (motor):** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$.
- ✅ **Armature current:** $I_a = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a}$.
- ✅ **Torque:** $T_a = K \Phi I_a$, where $K = \dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}$.
- ✅ **Developed power:** $P_d = E_b I_a = T_a \omega$.
- ✅ **Speed relation:** $N \propto \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi}$.
- ✅ **Winding rules:** Lap → $A = P$; Wave → $A = 2$.
- ✅ **Units check:** $\Phi$ in **Wb**, $N$ in **RPM**, $T_a$ in **N·m**, $\omega$ in **rad/s**.
- ✅ **Operating modes:** Motor ⇒ $E_b < V$; Generator ⇒ $E_b > V$.
- ✅ **Symmetry to remember:** $E_b = K \Phi \omega$ and $T_a = K \Phi I_a$ — the same constant $K$ links both.
- ✅ **Use of $9.55$ constant:** $T_a = 9.55 \cdot \dfrac{E_b I_a}{N}$ gives torque in N·m directly.
- ✅ **Speed regulation comes free:** $E_b$ rises with $N$, reducing $I_a$ and hence $T_a$ — natural load-balancing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Torque Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Torque Equation** of a DC machine expresses the electromagnetic torque developed in the armature as a function of machine constructional parameters (number of poles $P$, total armature conductors $Z$, number of parallel paths $A$) and operating quantities (useful flux per pole $\Phi$ and armature current $I_a$).

The general torque equation for any DC machine (motor or generator) is given by:

$$T_a = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2 \pi A} \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

where:
- $T_a$ = Armature (electromagnetic) torque developed, in **N·m**
- $P$ = Total number of magnetic poles
- $\Phi$ = Useful flux per pole, in **Wb**
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $I_a$ = Total armature current, in **A**
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature winding
- $2\pi$ = A constant arising from the circular geometry of the armature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:**
> The torque equation is a *core fundamental* of Module 1 (DC Machines). It directly bridges the electrical input ($V I_a$) with the mechanical output ($T \omega$). A complete derivation from first principles (force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field) is **mandatory** for full marks in any 14-mark Part B question.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **rotating garden sprinkler** in a windstorm. The wind (analogous to magnetic flux $\Phi$) blows on the angled nozzles (analogous to current-carrying armature conductors). Each nozzle experiences a force $F = B I l$ (here, $B$ is "wind strength", $I$ is "water thrust", and $l$ is "nozzle length"). The torque generated by each nozzle is simply $F \times r$ (force $\times$ arm length).

When you **add up the forces from all $Z$ nozzles** spread around the circular ring of radius $r$, and remember that the wind strength $B$ and the circumference $2\pi r$ together determine the total flux $\Phi$ per section, you get a clean expression: $T \propto \Phi \times I_a \times \text{(geometry)}$.

In a DC machine:
- **Flux $\Phi$** = the "wind" (created by the field winding)
- **Armature current $I_a$** = the "water current" flowing through the conductors
- **Number of conductors $Z$** = the more "nozzles", the more turning effort
- **Radius $r$ and pole count $P$** = the "lever arm" and the "number of wind sections"

The machine literally translates electrical energy into mechanical rotation through this beautiful electromagnetic relationship.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Armature Torque vs Shaft Torque:**
> - $T_a$ (Electromagnetic / Developed Torque) — produced inside the armature.
> - $T_{sh}$ (Shaft Torque / Useful Torque) — available at the shaft after subtracting friction, windage, and stray load losses.
> - $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{\text{friction+windage+stray}}$
> This difference is frequently tested in KTU 14-mark problems.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:--------------|
| $T_a$ | Electromagnetic Torque | **N·m** |
| $\Phi$ | Flux per pole | **Wb** (Weber) |
| $I_a$ | Armature current | **A** (Ampere) |
| $N$ | Speed of rotation | **rpm** (revolutions/min) |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity | **rad/s** |
| $P_m$ | Mechanical power developed | **W (Watt)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion reminder (frequently tested):** $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s, and $1 \text{ hp} = 746 \text{ W}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

The torque equation is built on three fundamental physical laws working in unison:

### Step 1 — Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor
When a conductor carrying current $I$ is placed in a magnetic field of flux density $B$ (Tesla), with active length $l$ (meters), the force experienced is:

$$F = B \, I \, l \quad [\text{Newtons}]$$

The direction of this force is given by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule** (in a motor) or **Fleming's Right-Hand Rule** (in a generator).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** This is the most basic electro-mechanical conversion equation. Without force, there is no torque. Every conductor in the armature experiences this force simultaneously.

---

### Step 2 — Torque Produced by a Single Conductor
The torque produced by a single conductor located at a radius $r$ from the axis of rotation is:

$$T_{\text{conductor}} = F \times r = B \, I \, l \, r \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

The radius $r$ is the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation to the conductor slot.

---

### Step 3 — Aggregating All Conductors
The total armature torque is the sum of the torques produced by all conductors that are simultaneously under the influence of the magnetic field. Considering the entire armature with $Z$ total conductors:

- **Current per conductor** $I = \dfrac{I_a}{A}$ (because the total armature current $I_a$ divides into $A$ parallel paths).
- All conductors contribute, but the **average** flux density $B$ is used because flux density varies with position under the pole shoe.

$$T_a = Z \times B \times \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right) \times l \times r$$

---

### Step 4 — Relating Flux Density to Flux per Pole
The average flux density $B$ and the flux per pole $\Phi$ are related through the pole arc area. Consider the cylindrical armature surface beneath one pole. The arc length per pole is $\dfrac{2\pi r}{P}$ (since $P$ poles divide the full circumference $2\pi r$). The axial length is $l$.

Therefore, the average area under one pole is $A_{\text{pole}} = \dfrac{2\pi r l}{P}$.

The flux per pole is the flux density times this area:

$$\Phi = B \times \frac{2\pi r l}{P} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad B = \frac{P \, \Phi}{2\pi r l}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "average"?** The actual flux density distribution under a pole shoe is approximately trapezoidal (fringing at the edges). For analytical simplicity, we use the average value, which gives accurate engineering results.

---

### Step 5 — Final Substitution
Substituting the expression for $B$ into the torque equation from Step 3:

$$T_a = Z \times \left(\frac{P \, \Phi}{2\pi r l}\right) \times \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right) \times l \times r$$

The terms $l$ and $r$ cancel out beautifully:

$$T_a = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi A}$$

This is the **general torque equation** valid for any DC machine — motor or generator — irrespective of winding type (lap or wave).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **must-know formulas** for solving any torque-related KTU exam problem. Memorize them thoroughly.

| # | Formula | Description / Use Case |
|:-:|:--------|:-----------------------|
| 1 | $T_a = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi A}$ | General electromagnetic torque equation (N·m) |
| 2 | $T_a = 0.159 \cdot \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{A}$ | Numerical convenience (since $1/2\pi \approx 0.159$) |
| 3 | $T_a \propto \Phi \cdot I_a$ | Fundamental proportionality (linear machine behavior) |
| 4 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s | Convert rpm $N$ to angular speed $\omega$ |
| 5 | $P_m = T_a \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N \, T_a}{60}$ | Mechanical power developed inside armature (W) |
| 6 | $E_b = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A}$ | Back EMF / Generated EMF equation (V) |
| 7 | $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a$ | Power balance: electrical $\to$ mechanical (in armature) |
| 8 | $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{f+w}$ | Shaft torque (useful) after mechanical losses |
| 9 | $T_{sh} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{\omega}$ | Shaft torque in terms of output power (W) |
| 10 | For **Lap winding**: $A = P \;\Rightarrow\; T_a = \dfrac{Z \, \Phi \, I_a}{2\pi}$ | Simplified form for simplex lap |
| 11 | For **Wave winding**: $A = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; T_a = \dfrac{P \, Z \, \Phi \, I_a}{4\pi}$ | Simplified form for simplex wave |
| 12 | $T_a = \dfrac{E_b \, I_a}{\omega}$ | Alternative torque expression from power balance |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Golden Pair" of DC Machine Equations:**
> $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi ZN}{60A}$ and $T_a = \dfrac{P\Phi ZI_a}{2\pi A}$
> Notice the deep structural symmetry: replace $N$ with $\dfrac{60\omega}{2\pi}$ in $E_b$ and divide by $\omega$ to obtain $T_a = \dfrac{E_b I_a}{\omega}$. This symmetry is a favourite KTU question.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The torque equation is not a textbook abstraction — it is the **workhorse of practical design**:

1. **Motor Selection for Industrial Drives:** When an engineer must choose a motor for a conveyor belt requiring 200 N·m at 1500 rpm, the torque equation directly informs the required $\Phi$ and $I_a$ for the chosen frame size.

2. **Electric Vehicle (EV) Powertrains:** EV traction motors are designed for high starting torque. The torque equation shows that increasing $I_a$ (within thermal limits) increases torque linearly.

3. **Robotic Actuators:** Servo motors in robotics rely on precise $\Phi \times I_a$ control for accurate torque delivery.

4. **Wind Turbine Generators:** In generator mode, the same equation predicts the electromagnetic braking torque resisting rotation, which determines structural loads on the tower.

5. **Hoists and Cranes:** A load-lifting hoist demands constant torque at low speed. The torque equation helps design the shunt field excitation to maintain constant $\Phi$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Design Question Tip:** If a problem asks to "design the machine" for a given torque, you will need to compute $Z$ and $\Phi$ from the torque equation, then verify that the back EMF equation is satisfied — the two equations are *coupled*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the General Torque Equation

### Given Data (Standard DC Machine Configuration)
- Number of poles: $P$
- Total armature conductors: $Z$
- Parallel paths in armature: $A$ (where $A = P$ for lap, $A = 2$ for wave)
- Average flux density in the air gap: $B$ (in Tesla)
- Active length of each armature conductor: $l$ (in meters)
- Mean radius of the armature: $r$ (in meters)
- Total armature current: $I_a$ (in Amperes)
- Useful flux per pole: $\Phi$ (in Weber)

### Derivation — Step 1: Force on a Single Conductor
By the **Biot-Savart / Lorentz force principle**, a straight conductor of length $l$ carrying current $I$, placed perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of flux density $B$, experiences a mechanical force:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$

The direction of this force is perpendicular to both the magnetic field and the current, as given by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**.

---

### Derivation — Step 2: Torque due to One Conductor
The torque generated by this single conductor about the axis of rotation (located at a radial distance $r$ from the conductor) is the force multiplied by the perpendicular distance:

$$T_{\text{one}} = F \times r = B \cdot I \cdot l \cdot r$$

This torque acts in a direction that tends to rotate the armature.

---

### Derivation — Step 3: Total Torque from All Conductors
The armature has $Z$ conductors distributed around its periphery. The current $I$ flowing through each individual conductor is the *branch current*, which is related to the total armature current $I_a$ by:

$$I = \frac{I_a}{A}$$

This is because the total armature current $I_a$ divides equally among $A$ parallel paths. Hence the number of conductors carrying this branch current in series is $Z/A$.

The total electromagnetic torque is the sum of torques from all $Z$ conductors, each producing an average force based on the average flux density $B$:

$$T_a = Z \times \underbrace{B \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} \cdot l}_{\text{Force per conductor}} \times r$$

Therefore:

$$T_a = \frac{Z \cdot B \cdot I_a \cdot l \cdot r}{A} \quad \cdots \cdots \text{(Eq. 1)}$$

---

### Derivation — Step 4: Expressing $B$ in Terms of $\Phi$
The average flux density $B$ under a pole shoe is related to the total flux per pole $\Phi$ through the area subtended by one pole at the armature surface.

The full armature circumference is $2\pi r$. This circumference is divided equally among the $P$ poles, so the **arc length per pole** is:

$$\text{Arc length per pole} = \frac{2\pi r}{P}$$

The **area of the cylindrical surface under one pole** is then:

$$A_{\text{pole}} = \text{Arc length} \times \text{axial length} = \frac{2\pi r}{P} \times l = \frac{2\pi r l}{P}$$

The flux per pole $\Phi$ is the product of the average flux density and this area:

$$\Phi = B \cdot A_{\text{pole}} = B \cdot \frac{2\pi r l}{P}$$

Solving for $B$:

$$B = \frac{P \, \Phi}{2\pi r l} \quad \cdots \cdots \text{(Eq. 2)}$$

---

### Derivation — Step 5: Final Substitution
Substituting Eq. 2 into Eq. 1:

$$T_a = \frac{Z \cdot \left( \dfrac{P \, \Phi}{2\pi r l} \right) \cdot I_a \cdot l \cdot r}{A}$$

Now we observe that $l$ appears in both numerator and denominator, and $r$ likewise cancels:

$$T_a = \frac{Z \cdot P \, \Phi \cdot I_a}{2\pi \cdot A}$$

Reordering for clarity:

$$\boxed{T_a = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi A} \quad [\text{N·m}]}$$

This completes the rigorous derivation from first principles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Validation Point:** This is the *exact* derivation path expected in the KTU answer script. Any shortcut (like directly quoting the result) loses a minimum of 3 to 4 marks out of 7 in a sub-part question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Power Equation (Mechanical Power Developed)

Starting from the fundamental mechanical power relationship:

$$P_m = T_a \cdot \omega$$

With angular velocity $\omega$ expressed in terms of $N$ (rpm):

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad [\text{rad/s}]$$

Substituting the torque expression:

$$P_m = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi A} \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

The $2\pi$ terms cancel, yielding:

$$P_m = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N \cdot I_a}{60 \, A} \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

Now, recall the EMF equation:

$$E_b = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A} \quad [\text{Volts}]$

Therefore, mechanical power developed can be rewritten as:

$$\boxed{P_m = E_b \cdot I_a \quad [\text{Watts}]}$$

This beautiful result is the **energy conversion principle** in its purest form: the electrical power input to the armature ($E_b I_a$, excluding the $I_a^2 R_a$ copper loss) is fully converted to mechanical power in the rotating armature.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Alternative Form $T_a = E_b I_a / \omega$

From the power balance result derived above:

$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega$$

Rearranging for $T_a$:

$$T_a = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{\omega}$$

Substituting $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$ and $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$T_a = \frac{\left( \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} \right) \cdot I_a}{\left( \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \right)} = \frac{P \Phi Z N \cdot I_a \cdot 60}{60 A \cdot 2\pi N}$$

Simplifying:

$$T_a = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi A}$$

This confirms the **consistency** between the two formulations.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A 4-pole, wave-wound DC machine has 600 armature conductors. The flux per pole is $30 \text{ mWb}$ and the armature current is $120 \text{ A}$. Calculate:
1. The electromagnetic torque developed.
2. The mechanical power developed if the speed is $1500 \text{ rpm}$.
3. The shaft torque if rotational losses total $400 \text{ W}$.

**Solution:**

*Given:*
- $P = 4$
- $Z = 600$
- $A = 2$ (wave winding)
- $\Phi = 30 \text{ mWb} = 30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb} = 0.030 \text{ Wb}$
- $I_a = 120 \text{ A}$
- $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$
- Rotational losses $P_{rot} = 400 \text{ W}$

*Part 1: Armature Torque*

$$T_a = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{4 \times 0.030 \times 600 \times 120}{2\pi \times 2}$$

Numerator: $4 \times 0.030 \times 600 \times 120 = 8640$

Denominator: $2\pi \times 2 = 4\pi \approx 12.566$

$$T_a = \frac{8640}{12.566} \approx 687.6 \text{ N·m}$$

*Part 2: Mechanical Power*

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$P_m = T_a \cdot \omega = 687.6 \times 157.08 \approx 1.08 \times 10^5 \text{ W} = 108 \text{ kW}$$

*Part 3: Shaft Torque*

$$P_{\text{out}} = P_m - P_{\text{rot}} = 108{,}000 - 400 = 107{,}600 \text{ W}$$

$$T_{sh} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\omega} = \frac{107{,}600}{157.08} \approx 685.05 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check using $E_b I_a$:**
> $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.030 \times 600 \times 1500}{60 \times 2} = \dfrac{108{,}000}{120} = 900 \text{ V}$
> $P_m = E_b \times I_a = 900 \times 120 = 108{,}000 \text{ W} = 108 \text{ kW}$ ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Power Flow & Torque Relationships in a DC Machine

The diagram below maps the energy flow and torque linkages — the **why and how** behind the torque equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Input V x Ia] --> B[Armature Circuit: Eb and Ia2Ra]
    B --> C[Back EMF x Ia equals Mechanical Power Pm]
    C --> D[Electromagnetic Torque Ta drives Armature]
    D --> E[Armature Rotates at N rpm omega rad per s]
    E --> F[Rotational Losses: Friction, Windage, Stray Load]
    F --> G[Net Mechanical Power at Shaft Pout]
    G --> H[Useful Shaft Torque Tsh]

    A2[Field Excitation: Flux Phi per pole] --> D
    A2 --> C
    B2[Constructional: P, Z, A] --> D
    B2 --> C

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style C fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style D fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style E fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457
    style F fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style G fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00695C
    style H fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825
    style A2 fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F
    style B2 fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Input power** $V I_a$ is split into the armature copper loss $I_a^2 R_a$ and the converted power $E_b I_a$.
- The converted power $E_b I_a$ exists as **mechanical power** $P_m = T_a \omega$ at the armature.
- This $P_m$ is reduced by **rotational losses** (friction, windage, stray) to yield the **shaft power** $P_{out}$.
- The **flux** $\Phi$ (from field winding) and **constructional parameters** $P, Z, A$ together determine the magnitude of $E_b$ and $T_a$ via the two golden equations.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Torque Equation Dependency Tree

This diagram shows the *inputs* required to compute the torque and the *outputs* derived from it.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[P] --> TF[Ta]
    Phi0[Phi] --> TF
    Z0[Z] --> TF
    Ia0[Ia] --> TF
    A0[A] --> TF

    TF --> Pm[Mechanical Power Pm]
    TF --> Tsh[Shaft Torque Tsh]
    Pm --> Eff[Machine Efficiency eta]
    Tsh --> App[Application: Drive Sizing]

    P0 --> Eb[Back EMF Eb]
    Phi0 --> Eb
    Z0 --> Eb
    N0[N rpm] --> Eb
    A0 --> Eb
    Eb --> Pm
    Eb --> Veq[Voltage Equation V]

    style P0 fill:#FFCDD2
    style Phi0 fill:#C8E6C9
    style Z0 fill:#BBDEFB
    style Ia0 fill:#FFF9C4
    style A0 fill:#D1C4E9
    style TF fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:3px
    style Pm fill:#B2DFDB
    style Tsh fill:#DCEDC8
    style Eff fill:#F0F4C3
    style App fill:#FFCCBC
```

**Interpretation:** The torque $T_a$ is a *convergent* quantity — five inputs flow into it. From $T_a$, two distinct engineering outputs arise: mechanical power (used for energy analysis) and shaft torque (used for mechanical design).

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Force on a Conductor (Verbal Schematic)

For the physical intuition that the Mermaid engine cannot directly draw, here is a textual schematic of a single conductor under a pole:

```
              North Pole (N)
         ┌────────────────────────┐
         │                        │
         │   ↗ B (flux density)   │
         │   │                    │
         │   │                    │
   ──────┤   ●━━━━━━●  ← Conductor │
         │   │  I ↑ l             │
         │   ↓                    │
         │  F = B I l   (Fleming's│
         │   Left-Hand Rule: out │
         │   of page)             │
         │                        │
         └────────────────────────┘
              South Pole (S)

   Torque T = F × r  (acting on armature)
```

- The **conductor** (●━━━━━━●) lies in the **slot** of the armature.
- **Current** $I$ flows out of the page (or into the page).
- The **magnetic field** $B$ points from the North pole face to the armature.
- The **force** $F$ is perpendicular to both, found using Fleming's Left-Hand Rule.
- This force acts at radius $r$ from the shaft axis, producing torque $T = F r$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**State and explain the torque equation of a DC machine. Why is the torque proportional to the product of flux and armature current?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The torque developed in a DC machine armature is given by:

$$T_a = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi A} \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

where $P$ is the number of poles, $\Phi$ is the flux per pole, $Z$ is the total number of armature conductors, $I_a$ is the armature current, and $A$ is the number of parallel paths.

The torque is proportional to $\Phi \times I_a$ because the electromagnetic force on each conductor is $F = B I l$, and since $B$ is proportional to $\Phi$, and $I$ is proportional to $I_a / A$, the force—and hence the torque—becomes proportional to $\Phi I_a$ **(1 mark for stating the equation, 1 mark for explanation of proportionality, 1 mark for the physical reason)**.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]`
**Distinguish between armature torque and shaft torque in a DC machine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Armature Torque $T_a$ | Shaft Torque $T_{sh}$ |
|:-------|:----------------------|:----------------------|
| Definition | Torque developed in the armature by electromagnetic action | Torque available at the shaft for useful work |
| Losses Accounted | None | Subtracts friction, windage, and stray load losses |
| Magnitude | Higher | Lower |
| Formula | $T_a = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$ | $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{f+w+s}$ |

The relationship is $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{\text{rotational losses}} / \omega$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry 14 marks and have **internal choice** (Module-level). The structure typically has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]`
**(a)** Derive the torque equation of a DC machine starting from the force on a current-carrying conductor. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-pole, lap-wound DC shunt motor has 720 conductors. The flux per pole is $25 \text{ mWb}$. The armature current is $60 \text{ A}$ and the speed is $1000 \text{ rpm}$. Calculate:
1. The electromagnetic torque developed.
2. The mechanical power developed in the armature.
3. The shaft torque if rotational losses are $500 \text{ W}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks
The derivation follows **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key points to include for full marks:

- **(1 mark)** Define $B$, $I$, $l$, $r$, and write $F = B I l$ with reference to Fleming's Left-Hand Rule.
- **(1 mark)** State $T_{\text{one}} = F \times r = B I l r$.
- **(1 mark)** Aggregate all $Z$ conductors: $I = I_a / A$, leading to $T_a = (Z B I_a l r) / A$.
- **(1 mark)** Derive the relationship $B = P \Phi / (2\pi r l)$ by considering arc length per pole and pole area.
- **(1 mark)** Substitute and simplify to $T_a = P \Phi Z I_a / (2\pi A)$.
- **(1 mark)** State the physical meaning of each term.
- **(1 mark)** State the assumptions: (i) average flux density, (ii) uniform current distribution, (iii) all conductors active at all times (valid for armature reaction effects being neglected initially).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

*Given:*
- $P = 6$, Lap winding $\Rightarrow A = P = 6$
- $Z = 720$
- $\Phi = 25 \text{ mWb} = 0.025 \text{ Wb}$
- $I_a = 60 \text{ A}$
- $N = 1000 \text{ rpm}$
- $P_{rot} = 500 \text{ W}$

**Part 1 — Electromagnetic Torque (3 marks):**

$$T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{6 \times 0.025 \times 720 \times 60}{2\pi \times 6}$$

Simplify $6$ in numerator and denominator:

$$T_a = \frac{0.025 \times 720 \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{1080}{2\pi} = \frac{1080}{6.2832} \approx 171.9 \text{ N·m}$$

**Part 2 — Mechanical Power (2 marks):**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = \frac{100\pi}{3} \approx 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$P_m = T_a \cdot \omega = 171.9 \times 104.72 \approx 18{,}000 \text{ W} = 18 \text{ kW}$$

**Part 3 — Shaft Torque (2 marks):**

$$P_{\text{out}} = P_m - P_{rot} = 18{,}000 - 500 = 17{,}500 \text{ W}$$

$$T_{sh} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\omega} = \frac{17{,}500}{104.72} \approx 167.1 \text{ N·m}$$

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]`
**(a)** Starting from the power balance in a DC machine, show that the mechanical power developed is $P_m = E_b I_a$. Hence derive the torque equation in the alternative form $T_a = E_b I_a / \omega$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, wave-wound DC generator has 500 armature conductors and delivers a current of $80 \text{ A}$ to the load. The flux per pole is $20 \text{ mWb}$ and the machine runs at $1200 \text{ rpm}$. If the armature resistance is $0.2 \, \Omega$ and the rotational losses are $600 \text{ W}$, determine:
1. The back EMF and the electromagnetic torque.
2. The shaft torque.
3. The efficiency of the machine. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks
- **(2 marks)** State that in a DC machine, the electrical input to the armature equals the sum of copper losses and converted (mechanical) power: $V I_a = I_a^2 R_a + E_b I_a$.
- **(1 mark)** Rearrange: $E_b I_a = V I_a - I_a^2 R_a$, where $E_b I_a$ is the **electromagnetic power** converted.
- **(1 mark)** Argue by energy conservation that this converted electrical power must appear as mechanical power: $P_m = E_b I_a$.
- **(2 marks)** Substitute $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ into $P_m = T_a \omega$ and equate: $T_a \omega = E_b I_a$, hence $T_a = E_b I_a / \omega$.
- **(1 mark)** Conclude by noting the structural symmetry between $E_b$ and $T_a$ equations.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

*Given:*
- $P = 4$, Wave $\Rightarrow A = 2$
- $Z = 500$
- $I_a = 80 \text{ A}$ (load current = armature current for generator with shunt field separately excited; for simplicity we treat as series-generator or assume $I_a = I_L$)
- $\Phi = 20 \text{ mWb} = 0.020 \text{ Wb}$
- $N = 1200 \text{ rpm}$
- $R_a = 0.2 \, \Omega$
- $P_{rot} = 600 \text{ W}$

**Part 1 — Back EMF and Electromagnetic Torque (3 marks):**

$$E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} = \frac{4 \times 0.020 \times 500 \times 1200}{60 \times 2}$$

Numerator: $4 \times 0.020 \times 500 \times 1200 = 48{,}000$
Denominator: $120$

$$E_b = \frac{48{,}000}{120} = 400 \text{ V}$$

For torque:

$$T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{4 \times 0.020 \times 500 \times 80}{2\pi \times 2} = \frac{3200}{4\pi} = \frac{3200}{12.566} \approx 254.65 \text{ N·m}$$

**Part 2 — Shaft Torque (2 marks):**

$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = 400 \times 80 = 32{,}000 \text{ W} = 32 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{\text{out}} = P_m - P_{rot} = 32{,}000 - 600 = 31{,}400 \text{ W}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 40\pi \approx 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_{sh} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\omega} = \frac{31{,}400}{125.66} \approx 249.9 \text{ N·m}$$

**Part 3 — Efficiency (2 marks):**

For a generator, output power is $P_{\text{out}} = 31.4 \text{ kW}$.
Input electrical power $P_{in} = V I_a = (E_b + I_a R_a) I_a$ if separately excited with terminal voltage:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a = 400 + 80 \times 0.2 = 400 + 16 = 416 \text{ V}$$

$$P_{in} = V I_a = 416 \times 80 = 33{,}280 \text{ W} = 33.28 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{in}} = \frac{31{,}400}{33{,}280} \approx 0.9435 = 94.35\%$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in Torque Equation problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the value of $A$ (parallel paths):** Many students incorrectly use $A = 2$ for *all* machines. Always remember: Lap winding $\Rightarrow A = P$, Wave winding $\Rightarrow A = 2$. This single error can cost **3 to 4 marks**.
>
> 2. **Unit conversion mistakes:** Forgetting to convert $\Phi$ from mWb to Wb, or mixing rpm with rad/s, or computing $T$ in N·mm instead of N·m. KTU examiners **do deduct** for unit mismatches.
>
> 3. **Confusing $T_a$ with $T_{sh}$:** A common trap in the 7-mark numerical is that the question asks for **shaft torque** but the student calculates **armature torque**. Always read the question twice.
>
> 4. **Omitting the derivation steps:** In a 7-mark sub-part asking to "derive", writing only the final equation fetches a maximum of 1 mark. The KTU valuation key awards marks for: (i) starting from $F = BIl$, (ii) relating $B$ to $\Phi$, (iii) substituting and simplifying. **Show all steps.**
>
> 5. **Writing $2\pi = 6.28$ and rounding prematurely:** Keep the symbol $2\pi$ until the final numerical step, then substitute $\pi \approx 3.1416$. Premature rounding is a frequent silent mark-killer.
>
> 6. **Mixing up motor and generator conventions:** In a motor, $E_b$ opposes $V$ (back-EMF); in a generator, $E_b$ is the source EMF. The **torque equation is the same** in both — but the sign convention in the power flow diagram differs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this section for last-minute exam revision.** Each bullet is a high-yield fact or formula.

### 🔑 Key Formulas
- **Torque Equation:** $T_a = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi A}$ N·m
- **Back EMF Equation:** $E_b = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 A}$ Volts
- **Mechanical Power:** $P_m = T_a \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a$
- **Shaft Torque:** $T_{sh} = T_a - \dfrac{P_{\text{rot}}}{\omega} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{\omega}$

### 🔧 Constructional & Winding Rules
- Lap winding: $A = P$ (used for high-current machines)
- Wave winding: $A = 2$ (used for high-voltage machines)
- Simplex duplex etc. multiply $A$ by an integer factor.

### 📐 Proportionality Rules
- $T_a \propto \Phi \cdot I_a$ (linear in both)
- $E_b \propto \Phi \cdot N$ (linear in both)
- At **constant flux** (shunt motor normal operation): $T_a \propto I_a$
- At **constant supply voltage** (ideal shunt): $N \propto 1/\Phi$

### ⚡ Unit Conversions (Memorize!)
- $1 \text{ mWb} = 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$
- $\omega \text{ (rad/s)} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$
- $1 \text{ hp} = 746 \text{ W}$, $1 \text{ kW} = 1000 \text{ W}$
- $1 \text{ N·m} = 1 \text{ J}$ (in SI units)

### 🧠 Conceptual Pearls
- The torque equation is **identical** in form for DC motor and DC generator — only the *interpretation* of the torque (driving vs resisting) changes.
- The "magic number" $2\pi$ arises from the **circumferential geometry** of the armature (arc length $2\pi r$).
- The "magic number" $60$ in the EMF equation arises from the **time conversion** (rpm to rps).
- The two equations form a **dual pair**: swap $N$ and $I_a$, swap $60$ and $2\pi$, you go from EMF to torque.
- All losses (copper, iron, friction, windage, stray) appear *outside* the core torque equation — the equation itself describes the **ideal** electro-mechanical conversion.

### 🎯 Common Question Patterns in KTU 2024 Scheme
- "Derive the torque equation of a DC machine." → Full derivation, 7 marks.
- "A $P$-pole, [lap/wave]-wound DC machine has $Z$ conductors. Given $\Phi$ and $I_a$, find torque." → Direct application, 3–7 marks.
- "Find the shaft torque given rotational losses." → Two-step problem, 7 marks.
- "Show that $P_m = E_b I_a$." → Energy balance derivation, 5–7 marks.
- "Compare lap and wave winding torque." → Conceptual + numerical, 7 marks.

### ✅ Self-Test Checklist Before the Exam
- [ ] Can I derive $T_a$ from $F = BIl$ without peeking?
- [ ] Do I know when to use $A = P$ vs $A = 2$?
- [ ] Can I convert between $N$ and $\omega$ fluently?
- [ ] Can I compute shaft torque given rotational losses in watts?
- [ ] Can I write the power flow diagram from memory?
- [ ] Do I know the relationship $P_m = E_b I_a$ and how to derive it?

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
