> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of DC Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **DC Motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that converts direct current electrical energy into mechanical energy (rotational motion) based on the principle of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the **Lorentz Force Mechanism**.

**Classification of DC Motors** refers to the systematic categorization of DC motors based on the manner in which the **field winding excitation** is provided to the machine, as well as the **constructional arrangement** of the armature and commutator assembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus**: The classification of DC motors is a *high-weightage* Module 1 topic. The examiner expects students to know the **four standard types** of DC motors with their **exact circuit diagrams**, **torque-speed characteristics**, and **practical applications**.

---

## 1.2 Hierarchical Classification Structure

DC Motors are classified primarily into two broad categories:

| Classification Basis | Categories |
|---|---|
| **Based on Excitation Method** | (a) Separately Excited DC Motor, (b) Self-Excited DC Motor |
| **Based on Construction** | (a) Armature Construction (Lap/Wave wound), (b) Pole Construction (Salient/Non-salient) |
| **Based on Speed Characteristics** | (a) Constant Speed, (b) Variable Speed |

The **Self-Excited DC Motors** are further sub-divided into three sub-types:
- **DC Series Motor**
- **DC Shunt Motor**
- **DC Compound Motor** (Cumulative & Differential)

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Pump & Pipe Model"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy**: Imagine a DC motor as a **water pumping system** that pushes water through different sized pipes.
> - The **armature** = the high-pressure pump (input current flows through it)
> - The **field winding** = the control valve that regulates magnetic strength
> - **Series winding** = a narrow pipe connected in-line with the pump (current passes through both)
> - **Shunt winding** = a wide parallel pipe (current bypasses the pump)
> - **Compound motor** = a hybrid system with both narrow and wide pipes together

The *amount of water (current) that passes through the narrow (series) or wide (shunt) pipe* determines the magnetic strength of the field, which in turn dictates the **speed** and **torque** behavior of the motor — exactly analogous to the way DC motor classification affects its operational characteristics.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Field Winding Resistance ($R_{se}$ or $R_f$)**: Typically ranges from **50 Ω to 200 Ω** (shunt) and **0.1 Ω to 1 Ω** (series).
- **Armature Resistance ($R_a$)**: Typically **0.1 Ω to 2 Ω**.
- **Supply Voltage ($V$)**: Standard values — **220 V DC**, **440 V DC**.
- **Speed Range**: Series motors can reach up to **10,000 RPM**; Shunt motors are designed for **1000–2000 RPM**.
- **Back EMF Constant ($K$)**: Defined as $K = \frac{P \cdot \phi \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$ where $P$ = poles, $\phi$ = flux per pole, $Z$ = conductors, $A$ = parallel paths.

---

## 1.5 Why Classification Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance**: Different industrial applications demand different speed-torque profiles.
> - **Cranes, Hoists, Electric Trains** → Series Motor (high starting torque)
> - **Lathe Machines, Centrifugal Pumps, Fans** → Shunt Motor (constant speed)
> - **Elevators, Rolling Mills, Presses** → Compound Motor (high starting torque + moderate speed regulation)
> - **Servo Control Systems, Laboratory Equipment** → Separately Excited Motor (independent flux control)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles of Each DC Motor Type

### 2.1.1 Separately Excited DC Motor

- The **field winding** is energized by an **independent external DC source** — separate from the armature supply.
- Field current $I_f$ is **independent** of armature current $I_a$.
- Allows **independent control of flux** $\phi$ and armature current.

**Operational Logic Steps**:
1. External supply $V_f$ applied to field winding → produces flux $\phi \propto I_f$.
2. Armature supply $V$ applied to armature circuit.
3. Torque produced: $T = K \cdot \phi \cdot I_a$.
4. Back EMF: $E_b = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$.
5. Voltage equation: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$.

---

### 2.1.2 Self-Excited DC Motor — Shunt Motor

- Field winding connected in **parallel** with the armature.
- Field winding has **high resistance** (hundreds of ohms) with **many turns of thin wire**.
- Field current $I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}}$ remains **approximately constant**.

**Key Characteristics**:
- Flux $\phi$ is **practically constant** (since $I_{sh}$ depends on $V$, not load).
- **No-load speed** is dangerously high (field weakening risk).

---

### 2.1.3 Self-Excited DC Motor — Series Motor

- Field winding connected in **series** with the armature.
- Field winding has **low resistance** (fractions of an ohm) with **few turns of thick wire**.
- Field current $I_{se} = I_a$ (same current flows through both).

**Key Characteristics**:
- Flux $\phi \propto I_a$ (up to magnetic saturation).
- **Cannot be started without load** (danger of infinite speed).
- Produces **very high starting torque**.

---

### 2.1.4 Self-Excited DC Motor — Compound Motor

Has **both** series and shunt field windings on the same pole core.

**Two Sub-Types**:

| Sub-Type | Series Field Polarity | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Cumulative Compound** | Same as shunt | Series flux *adds* to shunt flux → $\phi_{total} = \phi_{sh} + \phi_{se}$ |
| **Differential Compound** | Opposite to shunt | Series flux *subtracts* from shunt flux → $\phi_{total} = \phi_{sh} - \phi_{se}$ |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 12 Must-Know Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Back EMF | $E_b = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$ | Induced voltage opposing applied voltage |
| 2 | Torque Equation | $T = K \cdot \phi \cdot I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque developed |
| 3 | Voltage Equation (Shunt) | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | KVL around armature loop |
| 4 | Voltage Equation (Series) | $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | KVL with series field |
| 5 | Shunt Field Current | $I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}}$ | Constant for fixed supply |
| 6 | Armature Current (Shunt) | $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ | KCL at junction |
| 7 | Armature Current (Series) | $I_a = I_L$ | Same as line current |
| 8 | Speed Equation (Shunt) | $N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K \cdot \phi}$ | Speed in RPM form |
| 9 | Speed Regulation | $\%SR = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$ | Measure of constant-speed property |
| 10 | Power Developed | $P_d = E_b \cdot I_a$ | Converted mechanical power |
| 11 | Input Power | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ | Total electrical power input |
| 12 | Machine Constant | $K = \frac{P \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$ | Construction-dependent constant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Conventions**: $P$ = number of poles, $Z$ = total armature conductors, $A$ = number of parallel paths, $\phi$ = flux per pole (in Weber), $\omega$ = angular speed (rad/s), $N$ = speed in RPM, $I_L$ = line current, $I_a$ = armature current.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Application Map**:
> - **DC Series Motor**: Traction (railways), cranes, vacuum cleaners, hair dryers, sewing machines.
> - **DC Shunt Motor**: Boring machines, printing presses, paper machines, reciprocating pumps, drafting rooms.
> - **Cumulative Compound Motor**: Rolling mills, elevators, concrete mixers, heavy planers.
> - **Differential Compound Motor**: Rarely used (instability issues) — but historically in arc welding generators.
> - **Separately Excited DC Motor**: Ward-Leonard speed control system, servo motors, robotics, CNC machines, electric vehicles (early models).

---

## 2.4 Comparison Matrix of DC Motor Types

| Parameter | Separately Excited | Shunt | Series | Cumulative Compound |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Field Current** | Independent of $I_a$ | Depends on $V$ | $I_f = I_a$ | $I_f$ combined |
| **Flux $\phi$** | Variable (controlled) | Nearly constant | Varies with load | Increases with load |
| **Speed Regulation** | Excellent (5–10%) | Good (10–15%) | Poor (very high) | Moderate (15–25%) |
| **Starting Torque** | Moderate | Low (1.5× FLT) | Very High (5–8× FLT) | High (3–4× FLT) |
| **No-Load Speed** | High but safe | High but limited | Dangerously high | Moderate |
| **Direction of Rotation** | Easily reversed | Reversible | Not easily reversible | Reversible with care |
| **Typical Rating** | Up to 100 kW | 0.5 to 50 kW | 1 to 1500 kW | 5 to 200 kW |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental Back EMF Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal**: Derive the mathematical relationship between the back EMF ($E_b$) and the motor speed ($N$) in a DC motor.

### Step 1: Definition of Average EMF per Conductor

According to **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the average EMF induced in one armature conductor is:

$$e_{avg} = \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

The flux $\phi$ (in Weber) links with each conductor once per revolution. The time taken for one revolution is $\frac{60}{N}$ seconds (where $N$ is speed in RPM).

### Step 2: Express the Time Differential

The flux swept by a conductor per revolution equals $\phi \cdot P$ (since there are $P$ poles, and flux alternates direction under each pole). Therefore:

$$\frac{d\phi}{dt} = \frac{\phi \cdot P}{\frac{60}{N}} = \frac{\phi \cdot P \cdot N}{60}$$

### Step 3: Total Conductors and Parallel Paths

If $Z$ is the total number of armature conductors and $A$ is the number of parallel paths, the number of conductors in series per path is $\frac{Z}{A}$.

### Step 4: Total EMF Generated

The total EMF induced in the armature is the EMF per conductor multiplied by the number of conductors in series:

$$E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \frac{\phi \cdot P \cdot N}{60}$$

### Step 5: Substitute the Machine Constant

Let $K_a = \frac{P \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$ be defined as the **armature constant**. Then:

$$E_b = K_a \cdot \phi \cdot N$$

### Step 6: Convert to Angular Speed Form

Since angular speed $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$, we have $N = \frac{60 \omega}{2\pi}$. Substituting:

$$E_b = K_a \cdot \phi \cdot \frac{60 \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{P \cdot Z}{2\pi A} \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$$

Defining $K = \frac{P \cdot Z}{2\pi A}$:

$$\boxed{E_b = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega}$$

**Engineering Interpretation**: The back EMF is **directly proportional** to both the magnetic flux $\phi$ and the angular velocity $\omega$. As speed increases, back EMF rises, opposing the supply voltage and naturally limiting the current — this is the inherent self-regulating mechanism of all DC motors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Fundamental Torque Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal**: Derive the relationship between electromagnetic torque and the armature current.

### Step 1: Force on a Single Conductor

When a current-carrying conductor of length $l$ is placed in a magnetic field of flux density $B$, the force on it is given by the **Lorentz Force** expression:

$$f = B \cdot I \cdot l$$

The torque produced by this force at radius $r$ from the center is:

$$\tau_{cond} = f \cdot r = B \cdot I \cdot l \cdot r$$

### Step 2: Express Force in Terms of Flux

For a conductor passing under one pole, the average flux density is $B = \frac{\phi}{A_{pole}}$, where $A_{pole}$ is the cross-sectional area of the pole face. The force per conductor is:

$$f = \left(\frac{\phi}{A_{pole}}\right) \cdot I \cdot l = \phi \cdot I \cdot \frac{l}{A_{pole}}$$

The mechanical work done per revolution is force × circumference = $f \cdot 2\pi r$. Setting this equal to electrical energy converted ($E_b \cdot I_a$):

$$T \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{N}{60} = E_b \cdot I_a$$

### Step 3: Combine with the Back EMF Expression

Substituting $E_b = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$:

$$T \cdot \omega = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega \cdot I_a$$

### Step 4: Solve for Torque

Dividing both sides by $\omega$:

$$\boxed{T = K \cdot \phi \cdot I_a}$$

**Engineering Interpretation**: The torque is **directly proportional** to both the flux and the armature current. This is why series motors (where $\phi \propto I_a$) produce such enormous starting torque — $T \propto I_a^2$ in the unsaturated region.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Speed Equation of a DC Shunt Motor

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal**: Express the steady-state speed of a DC shunt motor in terms of supply voltage and load.

### Step 1: Write the Voltage Equation

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the armature loop:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Back EMF Expression

$$V = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega + I_a R_a$$

### Step 3: Solve for Angular Speed

$$K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega = V - I_a R_a$$

$$\omega = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K \cdot \phi}$$

### Step 4: Convert to RPM Form

Using $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$\frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K \cdot \phi}$$

$$N = \frac{60(V - I_a R_a)}{2\pi K \cdot \phi}$$

Substituting $K = \frac{P \cdot Z}{2\pi A}$:

$$N = \frac{60 \cdot A (V - I_a R_a)}{P \cdot Z \cdot \phi}$$

**Final Compact Form**:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K_a \cdot \phi}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation**: For a shunt motor, $\phi$ is nearly constant, so speed depends primarily on the armature drop $I_a R_a$. As load increases, $I_a$ rises, $I_a R_a$ increases, and $N$ drops — giving the gentle **drooping speed-load characteristic** of a shunt motor.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example** (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):
>
> A **220 V DC shunt motor** has the following parameters:
> - $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$
> - $R_{sh} = 220\ \Omega$
> - No-load armature current $I_{a0} = 2\ \text{A}$
> - Armature current at full load $I_{a,FL} = 50\ \text{A}$
>
> **Calculate**: (i) No-load speed, (ii) Full-load speed, (iii) Speed regulation.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute shunt field current (constant)**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{220} = 1\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2: No-load back EMF**

$$E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 220 - (2)(0.5) = 219\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3: Full-load back EMF**

$$E_{b,FL} = V - I_{a,FL} R_a = 220 - (50)(0.5) = 220 - 25 = 195\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4: Speed Ratio**

Since $\phi$ is constant (shunt motor):

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{N_{NL}} = \frac{E_{b,FL}}{E_{b0}} = \frac{195}{219} = 0.8904$$

**Step 5: Speed Regulation**

$$\%SR = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \left(\frac{1}{0.8904} - 1\right) \times 100$$

$$\%SR = (1.1231 - 1) \times 100 = 12.31\%$$

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $I_{sh}$ | 1 | A |
| $E_{b0}$ | 219 | V |
| $E_{b,FL}$ | 195 | V |
| $N_{FL}/N_{NL}$ | 0.8904 | dimensionless |
| Speed Regulation | 12.31 | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification**: A speed regulation of ~12% is **typical for a DC shunt motor** (ideal range: 5–15%). This confirms our calculation aligns with engineering reality.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[DC Motors] --> B1[Based on Excitation]
    A1 --> B2[Based on Construction]
    A1 --> B3[Based on Speed Characteristic]
    B1 --> C1[Separately Excited]
    B1 --> C2[Self Excited]
    B2 --> D1[Armature Wound - Lap Wave]
    B2 --> D2[Pole Type - Salient Non Salient]
    B3 --> E1[Constant Speed - Shunt]
    B3 --> E2[Variable Speed - Series]
    C2 --> F1[DC Series Motor]
    C2 --> F2[DC Shunt Motor]
    C2 --> F3[DC Compound Motor]
    F3 --> G1[Cumulative Compound]
    F3 --> G2[Differential Compound]
    G1 --> H1[Long Shunt Cumulative]
    G1 --> H2[Short Shunt Cumulative]
    G2 --> H3[Long Shunt Differential]
    G2 --> H4[Short Shunt Differential]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note**: The above block architecture shows the **complete hierarchical taxonomy** of DC motors per KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus. Students should memorize this classification tree as a structured framework.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Motor Type Selection Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Industrial Application] --> B1[Required Starting Torque]
    B1 --> C1{High Starting Torque}
    B1 --> C2{Constant Speed Required}
    C1 -->|Yes| D1[DC Series Motor]
    C1 -->|No| E1[Check Speed Regulation]
    E1 -->|Strict| F1[Separately Excited Motor]
    E1 -->|Moderate| F2[Shunt Motor]
    C2 -->|Yes| F2
    C2 -->|No| G1[Compound Motor]
    G1 --> G2[Need Smooth Operation]
    G2 -->|Yes| H1[Cumulative Compound]
    G2 -->|No| H2[Differential Compound]
    D1 --> I1[Application: Cranes, Traction]
    F2 --> I2[Application: Lathes, Fans]
    F1 --> I3[Application: Servo Control]
    H1 --> I4[Application: Rolling Mills, Elevators]
    H2 --> I5[Application: Welding Generators]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Logic**: This functional architecture serves as a **decision-support flowchart** for selecting the appropriate DC motor for any given industrial application. Use this as a quick-reference during KTU 2024 ESE viva and problem-solving.

---

## 4.3 Circuit Schematic Representation (Textual Block Diagram)

### 4.3.1 Separately Excited DC Motor
```
    V_f (+) -----[ R_f ]-----+----(-)
                             |
                          [Shunt Field]
                             |
                            (-) GND

    V (+) ----[ + ]--[ R_a ]--+--[ E_b ]--(-)
              |               |
            [Armature]      [Back EMF]
              |               |
             (-) ------------(+)
```

### 4.3.2 DC Shunt Motor
```
              +---[ R_sh (Field) ]---+
              |                      |
    V (+)-----+                      +-----(-)
              |                      |
              +---[ R_a (Armature)]--+
```

### 4.3.3 DC Series Motor
```
    V (+)-----[ R_a (Armature) ]-----[ R_se (Series Field) ]----(-)
```

### 4.3.4 DC Compound Motor (Cumulative)
```
              +---[ R_sh (Shunt Field) ]---+
              |                            |
    V (+)-----+                            +-----(-)
              |                            |
              +---[ R_a ]--[ R_se (Series) ]-+
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Hint**: In a **long-shunt compound motor**, the shunt field is connected across the line (before the series field). In a **short-shunt compound motor**, the shunt field is connected across the armature only (after the series field). KTU 2024 frequently tests this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Classify DC motors based on the method of excitation. Draw the circuit diagrams of each type.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

DC motors are classified into two main categories based on excitation method:

1. **Separately Excited DC Motor** [1 mark]: The field winding is energized by an *independent external DC source*, separate from the armature. Field current $I_f$ is independent of armature current $I_a$.

2. **Self-Excited DC Motor** [1 mark]: The field winding is supplied from the *same source* as the armature. Sub-types:
   - **Shunt Motor**: Field in parallel with armature ($R_{sh}$ high, $I_{sh}$ constant)
   - **Series Motor**: Field in series with armature ($R_{se}$ low, $I_{se} = I_a$)
   - **Compound Motor**: Both shunt and series fields together (Cumulative / Differential)

3. **Circuit Diagram** [1 mark]: Neat drawing of at least one type with proper labels ($V$, $R_a$, $R_{sh}$, $R_{se}$).

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Compare the starting torque and speed regulation characteristics of DC shunt and DC series motors.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| Feature | DC Shunt Motor | DC Series Motor |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting Torque** | Low (~1.5 × Full-Load Torque) [0.75 marks] | Very High (~5–8 × Full-Load Torque) [0.75 marks] |
| **Speed Regulation** | Good (5–15%) [0.75 marks] | Poor (very high variation) [0.75 marks] |
| **Reason** | Constant flux since $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ | Flux $\phi \propto I_a$, so flux and torque increase sharply at start |

**Conclusion**: Shunt motors are used where constant speed is required; series motors are used where high starting torque is essential (e.g., traction, cranes).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question Set B (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**
> **Module 1: Classification of DC Motors**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**A (a) [7 Marks]**: With neat circuit diagrams, explain the construction and working principle of a **DC compound motor**. Differentiate clearly between **cumulative** and **differential** compound motors with their **torque-speed characteristics**.

**A (b) [7 Marks]**: A **250 V DC shunt motor** has armature resistance of **0.3 Ω** and shunt field resistance of **250 Ω**. At no-load, the armature current is **3 A**, and at full load it is **40 A**. Calculate:
1. No-load speed if full-load speed is **1200 RPM**.
2. Speed regulation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**B (a) [7 Marks]**: Classify DC motors in detail. Explain the **constructional features** and **field winding arrangements** of a **separately excited DC motor** with a neat circuit diagram. List its **industrial applications**.

**B (b) [7 Marks]**: Derive the **fundamental torque equation** of a DC motor starting from the Lorentz force principle. Show that $T = K \cdot \phi \cdot I_a$ and explain the **physical significance** of each term.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A(a) — DC Compound Motor

**Constructional Features** [2 marks]:
- Has **two field windings** on each pole: a **shunt field** (high resistance, many turns) and a **series field** (low resistance, few turns).
- Series field is wound on top of shunt field on the same pole core.
- The two windings can be connected in **long-shunt** (shunt across the line) or **short-shunt** (shunt across the armature).

**Working Principle** [1 mark]:
- Both field windings produce flux simultaneously.
- Total flux $\phi_{total} = \phi_{sh} \pm \phi_{se}$ depending on polarity.

**Cumulative vs Differential** [2 marks]:

| Feature | Cumulative Compound | Differential Compound |
|---|---|---|
| Field Polarity | Series flux **aids** shunt flux | Series flux **opposes** shunt flux |
| Total Flux | $\phi_{sh} + \phi_{se}$ | $\phi_{sh} - \phi_{se}$ |
| Torque-Speed Curve | Drops with load (good regulation) | Rises with load (poor regulation, unstable) |
| Application | Heavy load starting (elevators) | Rarely used (arc welding) |

**Torque-Speed Characteristic** [2 marks]:
- **Cumulative**: Speed drops as load increases (similar to shunt but with a steeper droop), high starting torque.
- **Differential**: Speed rises with load → unstable operation → motor may run away at light loads.

---

### Solution to Question A(b) — Numerical Problem

**Given Data**:
- $V = 250\ \text{V}$, $R_a = 0.3\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 250\ \Omega$
- $I_{a0} = 3\ \text{A}$, $I_{a,FL} = 40\ \text{A}$
- $N_{FL} = 1200\ \text{RPM}$

**Step 1: Compute shunt field current** [1 mark]

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2: Compute back EMF at no-load** [1 mark]

$$E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 250 - (3)(0.3) = 250 - 0.9 = 249.1\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3: Compute back EMF at full load** [1 mark]

$$E_{b,FL} = V - I_{a,FL} R_a = 250 - (40)(0.3) = 250 - 12 = 238\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4: Compute no-load speed** [2 marks]

Since $\phi$ is constant (shunt motor):

$$\frac{N_{NL}}{N_{FL}} = \frac{E_{b0}}{E_{b,FL}}$$

$$N_{NL} = N_{FL} \cdot \frac{E_{b0}}{E_{b,FL}} = 1200 \cdot \frac{249.1}{238}$$

$$N_{NL} = 1200 \times 1.0466 = 1255.97\ \text{RPM} \approx 1256\ \text{RPM}$$

**Step 5: Speed regulation** [2 marks]

$$\%SR = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{1256 - 1200}{1200} \times 100 = \frac{56}{1200} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%SR = 4.67\%}$$

| Result | Value |
|---|---|
| **No-load Speed** | **1256 RPM** |
| **Speed Regulation** | **4.67%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Verification**: A speed regulation of 4.67% is **excellent** for a DC shunt motor (ideal <10%). This confirms the calculation is realistic and the motor has very good constant-speed characteristics.

---

### Solution to Question B(a) — Classification & Separately Excited Motor

**Classification of DC Motors** [3 marks]:

```
DC Motors
├── Separately Excited (field from external source)
└── Self-Excited (field from own armature supply)
    ├── Shunt Motor (R_sh high, field in parallel)
    ├── Series Motor (R_se low, field in series)
    └── Compound Motor
        ├── Cumulative (series flux adds to shunt)
        └── Differential (series flux opposes shunt)
```

**Separately Excited DC motor — Constructional Features** [2 marks]:
- **Armature**: Cylindrical laminated iron core with slots housing the armature winding (closed loop). Commutator with copper segments.
- **Field System**: Salient poles carrying concentrated field windings, fed from a **separate DC source** through slip rings.
- **Brushes & Yoke**: Carbon brushes press against commutator; yoke forms the outer frame.

**Circuit Diagram** [1 mark]:
- Show $V$ supply → $R_a$ + $E_b$ in series; separate $V_f$ → $R_f$ (field) loop.

**Applications** [1 mark]:
- Ward-Leonard speed control systems
- Servo motors, robotics, CNC machines
- Electric vehicle drives, paper mills

---

### Solution to Question B(b) — Torque Equation Derivation

**Step 1: Lorentz Force on a Conductor** [1 mark]

A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force:

$$f = B \cdot I \cdot l \quad \text{(Newtons)}$$

**Step 2: Force per Conductor in Terms of Flux** [1 mark]

If the conductor lies in a magnetic field of flux density $B$ and the area per pole is $A_p$, then $B = \frac{\phi}{A_p}$. The force becomes:

$$f = \phi \cdot I \cdot \frac{l}{A_p}$$

**Step 3: Total Torque from All Conductors** [2 marks]

For $Z$ total conductors, with $A$ parallel paths, $\frac{Z}{A}$ conductors carry current $I_a$ in series. The mechanical work done per revolution must equal the electrical power converted:

$$T \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{N}{60} = E_b \cdot I_a$$

**Step 4: Substitute Back EMF** [1 mark]

Using $E_b = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$ where $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$T \cdot \omega = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega \cdot I_a$$

**Step 5: Final Torque Equation** [1 mark]

$$\boxed{T = K \cdot \phi \cdot I_a}$$

**Physical Significance** [1 mark]:
- $T$ = electromagnetic torque (Nm)
- $K$ = machine constant (depends on geometry: $P, Z, A$)
- $\phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $I_a$ = armature current (A)
- Torque is **linear in flux and current** → independent of speed.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE**:
>
> 1. **Confusing Long-Shunt vs Short-Shunt** (–2 marks): In long-shunt, shunt field is across the line supply. In short-shunt, shunt field is across the armature only. KTU specifically tests this in numerical problems.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s or vice versa** (–1 mark): Speed $N$ is in RPM, but $\omega$ is in rad/s. Use $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$.
>
> 3. **Wrong saturation assumption in series motors** (–2 marks): Below saturation, $\phi \propto I_a$, so $T \propto I_a^2$. Above saturation, $\phi$ is constant, so $T \propto I_a$. Students often forget this.
>
> 4. **Missing units in numerical answers** (–1 mark): Always write **RPM, V, A, Nm, Wb, Ω** explicitly.
>
> 5. **Drawing circuit diagrams without labels** (–2 marks): Label every component ($V$, $R_a$, $R_{sh}$, $R_{se}$, $E_b$, $I_a$, $I_{sh}$, $I_{se}$).
>
> 6. **Writing the differential compound as "safe" or "stable"** (–1 mark): The differential compound is inherently **unstable** and rarely used as a motor. Examiners penalize this confusion.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** (read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall):

- ✅ DC motors are classified into **Separately Excited** and **Self-Excited** types.
- ✅ Self-excited sub-types: **Shunt**, **Series**, **Compound** (Cumulative & Differential).
- ✅ **Shunt field** has high resistance (~hundreds of ohms) and many turns.
- ✅ **Series field** has low resistance (fractions of an ohm) and few turns.
- ✅ Back EMF: $E_b = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$ (in V) or $E_b = K_a \cdot \phi \cdot N$ (in RPM form).
- ✅ Torque: $T = K \cdot \phi \cdot I_a$.
- ✅ **Speed regulation** is best for shunt motors (~5–15%) and worst for series motors (no-load speed infinite theoretically).
- ✅ **Cumulative compound** → high starting torque + moderate speed regulation (most common industrial type).
- ✅ **Differential compound** → unstable, rarely used as a motor.
- ✅ **Separately excited motor** allows independent control of flux → ideal for servo and precision drives.
- ✅ **Long-shunt compound**: shunt field is across the line; **short-shunt compound**: shunt field is across the armature.
- ✅ **Starting torque** highest in series motor ($T \propto I_a^2$ below saturation), lowest in shunt motor.
- ✅ For numerical problems, always start by computing $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ (constant for shunt motors).
- ✅ **Speed equation** for any DC motor: $N = \frac{V - I_a R_a(\text{total})}{K_a \cdot \phi}$ where $R_a(\text{total})$ includes series field if present.
- ✅ KTU 2024 frequently tests: classification, circuit diagrams, characteristics comparison, and numerical problems on speed/back-EMF/speed-regulation.

---

**End of Module 1 — Classification of DC Motors — Premium KTU 2024 Study Notes**
*Total Marks Coverage: A (3) + B (14) = 17 marks typical question patterns | Estimated Study Time: 4–5 hours*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steady-State Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steady-State Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What Are "Steady-State Equations" in DC Machines?

In the context of DC Machines (the primary subject of Module 1 of PCEOT402), **Steady-State Equations** refer to the complete set of time-invariant mathematical relations that govern the **balanced, continuous operation** of a DC generator or DC motor — that is, the operating condition in which all electrical and mechanical quantities (voltage, current, flux, speed, torque) have settled to **constant average values** and the **rate of change of every stored energy element is zero**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Steady-state operation of a DC machine is the operating regime in which the electromagnetic, mechanical, and thermal transients have decayed to zero, leaving the machine governed by a set of algebraic (not differential) equations relating induced EMF, terminal voltage, armature current, field flux, speed, and developed torque.*

Mathematically, the condition of "steadiness" is expressed as:

$$\frac{d\,i_a}{dt} = 0, \quad \frac{d\,\lambda}{dt} = 0, \quad \frac{d\,\omega}{dt} = 0$$

This collapses the dynamic differential-equation model of the machine into **four coupled algebraic relations**:

1. **EMF Equation** (Faraday's Law in rotating form)
2. **Torque Equation** (Biot–Savart / Lorentz force in rotating form)
3. **Voltage Equation** (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature loop)
4. **Power-Flow Equation** (energy conservation between electrical and mechanical ports)

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Water-Pump-Pipe System**
> Imagine a **centrifugal water pump** connected to a network of pipes. Once the pump reaches its rated speed, the flow rate (current), the pressure head (voltage), and the torque required to turn the impeller (mechanical torque) all become **constant with time**. The pump is now in "steady state". The **EMF equation** is like the pressure the impeller can generate per revolution; the **torque equation** is the twisting effort the motor must supply to overcome the water load; the **voltage equation** is the pressure-balance around the pipe loop (pump pressure = load pressure + pipe friction drops); and the **power equation** is the energy bookkeeping between the electrical motor input and the hydraulic output.
> 
> Every fluctuation (a valve opening, a load change) is a **transient** — we are *not* concerned with those here. We are concerned only with the **after-the-fluctuation** algebraic relationships.

---

## 1.3 Why These Equations Matter in KTU Examinations

| Significance | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Foundation of Module 1** | All later topics (characteristics, starters, speed control, losses, efficiency) are *direct consequences* of these four equations. |
| **Direct 7–14 mark problems** | KTU frequently asks derivations of the EMF and torque equations, or numerical problems combining them. |
| **Universal applicability** | The same algebraic skeleton, with sign changes, governs generators, motors, series, shunt, and compound machines. |
| **Engineering design use** | Used to **size** machines, **predict** terminal voltage under load, and **compute** efficiency. |

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Number of poles** → denoted **P** (an even integer, typically 2, 4, 6)
> - **Flux per pole** → denoted **Φ** (in **Weber, Wb**)
> - **Speed** → **N** in revolutions per minute (**r.p.m.**), or **ω** in **rad/s** where $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$
> - **Armature current** → **Iₐ** in **Ampere (A)**
> - **Number of armature conductors** → **Z**
> - **Number of parallel paths** → **A**, where $A = P$ for **wave winding** and $A = 2$ for **lap winding**
> - **Armature resistance** → **Rₐ** in **Ohm (Ω)**
> - **Field/series-field resistance** → **Rₛₑ** in **Ohm (Ω)**
> - **Permeability of free space** → $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ (used in magnetic-circuit derivations)
> - **Standard acceleration of gravity** → $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ (used when computing shaft torque from $P = T\omega$)

---

## 1.5 The Four Governing Equations at a Glance

| # | Equation Name | Generator Form | Motor Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EMF Equation** | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A}$ | $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A}$ |
| 2 | **Torque Equation** | $T_d = \dfrac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\, I_a$ (opposes motion) | $T_d = \dfrac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\, I_a$ (drives motion) |
| 3 | **Armature Voltage Equation** | $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ |
| 4 | **Power Equation** | $P_d = E_g I_a = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\,T_d$ | $P_d = E_b I_a = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\,T_d$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **The sign convention is the only difference** between generator and motor steady-state equations. This is the most common KTU pitfall — forgetting the sign of the $I_a R_a$ drop.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Each Equation

### 2.1.1 The EMF Equation — Origin in Faraday's Law

When the armature rotates at **N r.p.m.**, each conductor cuts the **Φ Weber** of flux per pole. The reasoning proceeds as:

- **One revolution** = the conductor sweeps across **P poles**, so flux cut per revolution = $\Phi \cdot P$.
- **Z conductors** are distributed in **A parallel paths**; the conductors per path = $Z/A$.
- The conductors of one path are in **series**, so the **total EMF per parallel path** is the sum of EMFs of $Z/A$ conductors.
- Per minute, each conductor cuts flux $N \times \Phi P$ times.

The average EMF per conductor is:

$$e_{avg} = \frac{d\Phi}{dt}\bigg|_{per\ rev} = \frac{\Phi P}{\text{time for one rev}} = \frac{\Phi P}{60/N} = \frac{\Phi P N}{60}$$

Multiplying by the **$Z/A$ conductors in series** per path:

$$\boxed{E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** The EMF is **directly proportional** to (a) flux per pole, (b) total conductors, (c) speed, and (d) number of poles. It is **inversely proportional** to the number of parallel paths. This is why a wave-wound machine ($A=2$) produces roughly **$P/2$ times** the EMF of an otherwise identical lap-wound machine ($A=P$).

### 2.1.2 The Torque Equation — Origin in the Lorentz Force

The force on a single conductor of length $l$ carrying current $i$ in a magnetic field of flux density $B$ is:

$$f = B\,l\,i$$

With flux per pole $\Phi = B \cdot A_{pole}$, the **force per pole** becomes $B \cdot l \cdot i_a$ summed over $Z/P$ conductors per pole. The **torque per pole** is this force times the armature radius. Summing over all **P poles** and using the geometric identity that the work done per revolution equals the EMF × charge, we obtain the **canonical torque equation**:

$$\boxed{T_d = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\, I_a = \frac{1}{2\pi}\,\Phi Z I_a \left(\frac{P}{A}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Physical Insight:** Torque is **independent of speed** — it depends only on flux and armature current. This is the foundation of the *flat* $T$–$I_a$ characteristic and the foundation of **armature-current-based torque control** in motors.

### 2.1.3 The Voltage Equation — Kirchhoff Around the Armature Loop

For a **separately excited or shunt motor** (simplest case, no series field):

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

For a **compound motor**:

$$V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se}) \quad \text{(long shunt)}$$
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a + I_{se} R_{se} \quad \text{(short shunt, with } I_{se}=I_a\text{)}$$

For a **generator** (current $I_a$ flows *out* of the positive terminal of the armature):

$$E_g = V + I_a R_a + I_a R_{se} \quad \text{or} \quad V = E_g - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Most common KTU mistake:** Mixing generator and motor sign conventions. Always draw the equivalent circuit and trace current direction **before** writing Kirchhoff's law.

### 2.1.4 The Power Equation — Energy Conservation

Electrical power converted to mechanical form (and *vice versa*):

$$P_d = E_g I_a = E_b I_a = \frac{2\pi N}{60}\,T_d = \omega\, T_d$$

Total **electrical input** to a motor:

$$P_{in} = V\,I_L = V(I_a + I_{sh}) \quad \text{(shunt motor)}$$

**Mechanical output** at the shaft:

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N}{60}\,T_{sh} = \omega\, T_{sh}$$

**Efficiency**:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V I_a - \text{copper loss} - \text{iron loss} - \text{friction}}{V I_a}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All quantities are in SI units unless explicitly stated. Use the table below as your last-minute revision sheet.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Unit | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | $\Phi$ flux, $Z$ conductors, $N$ rpm, $P$ poles, $A$ paths | Volts (V) | All DC machines |
| 2 | $E_g = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,\pi \Phi f\, \dfrac{Z}{A}$ (sinusoidal flux variation) | $f = PN/120$ Hz | V | DC machines with sinusoidal assumption |
| 3 | $T_d = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\,\Phi Z I_a \left(\dfrac{P}{A}\right)$ | $T_d$ developed torque | N·m | All DC machines |
| 4 | $T_d = 0.1591\,\Phi Z I_a \left(\dfrac{P}{A}\right)$ | — | N·m | Numerical substitute (since $1/2\pi \approx 0.1591$) |
| 5 | $T_d = 9.55\,\dfrac{E_g I_a}{N}$ | — | N·m | Computes $T_d$ from electrical quantities |
| 6 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | $V$ supply voltage | V | DC motor (separately excited or shunt, no series) |
| 7 | $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | $R_{se}$ series-field resistance | V | DC series/compound motor (long shunt) |
| 8 | $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ | — | V | DC generator (shunt/separately excited) |
| 9 | $V = E_g - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | — | V | DC series/compound generator (long shunt) |
| 10 | $P_d = E_b I_a = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\,T_d$ | Mechanical developed power | W | All DC machines |
| 11 | $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Armature copper loss | W | All DC machines |
| 12 | $P_{cu,se} = I_{se}^2 R_{se}$ | Series-field copper loss | W | Series/compound machines |
| 13 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V I_a - \sum \text{Losses}}{V I_a}$ | Efficiency | dimensionless | All DC machines |
| 14 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular speed | rad/s | Conversion |
| 15 | $A = P$ (lap), $A = 2$ (wave) | Parallel paths | — | Winding classification |
| 16 | $E_b \propto \Phi N$ | Proportionality | V | Basis of all speed-control laws |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | How the Steady-State Equation Is Used |
|---|---|
| **Design of DC Generators** | Choose $P$, $A$, $Z$ to deliver rated $E_g$ at rated $N$ and $\Phi$. |
| **Design of DC Motors** | Choose $P$, $A$, $Z$ to deliver rated $T_d$ at rated $I_a$ and $\Phi$. |
| **Speed Control (Flux Weakening)** | From $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ and $E_b \propto \Phi N$, we derive $N \propto (V - I_a R_a)/\Phi$ — the basis of Ward–Leonard and chopper control. |
| **Earthing-Fault & Short-Circuit Analysis** | The short-circuit current $I_{sc} = E_g/R_a$ is computed directly from the EMF and voltage equations. |
| **Efficiency Optimisation** | Setting variable copper loss equal to constant iron + friction loss gives the load point of maximum efficiency. |
| **Battery-EV Traction Motors** | Series-motor torque $T \propto I_a^2$ is derived directly from the torque + voltage equations, justifying the series motor's use in traction. |

---

## 2.4 Sign-Convention Master Table (Most Tested!)

| Quantity | Generator | Motor |
|---|---|---|
| $E_g / E_b$ | Induced EMF, source of current | Back-EMF, opposes applied voltage |
| $I_a$ direction | From armature to load | From supply to armature |
| $T_d$ direction | Opposes rotation (Lenz) | Aids rotation |
| Power $E I_a$ | Converted **from** mechanical | Converted **to** mechanical |
| KVL around armature | $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EMF Equation

### Statement
**Prove that the EMF generated in a DC machine armature is $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$.**

### Given
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole, in Weber
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $P$ = total number of poles
- $N$ = speed of armature in r.p.m.
- $A$ = number of parallel paths in the armature winding

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Flux cut by one conductor per revolution**

As the armature rotates, one conductor passes under all **P** poles in one complete revolution. In doing so, it cuts a total flux of:

$$\text{Flux per rev} = \Phi \times P \quad \text{(Weber)}$$

**Step 2 — Time taken for one revolution**

If the speed is **N revolutions per minute**, the time for one revolution is:

$$T_{rev} = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Average EMF per conductor (Faraday's Law)**

The average rate of change of flux linkage per conductor is:

$$e_{avg} = \frac{\Phi P}{T_{rev}} = \frac{\Phi P}{60/N} = \frac{\Phi P N}{60} \quad \text{Volts}$$

**Step 4 — Number of conductors in series per parallel path**

The **Z** conductors are distributed among **A** parallel paths. Conductors per path:

$$Z_{series} = \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 5 — Total EMF across one path (and hence at the brushes)**

Since all $Z/A$ conductors of one path are in series, the EMFs add directly. The terminal EMF (the EMF between brushes) equals the EMF of any one parallel path:

$$E_g = e_{avg} \times Z_{series}$$

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi P N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

$$\boxed{E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A} \quad \text{Volts}}$$

**Step 6 — Equivalent expression in angular frequency**

Since $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$, the equation can be rewritten as:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z P}{60 A} \cdot N = \frac{\Phi Z P}{60 A} \cdot \frac{60 \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{\Phi Z P\,\omega}{2\pi A}$$

$$E_g = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z \omega \quad \text{Volts}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Torque Equation

### Statement
**Prove that the electromagnetic torque developed in a DC machine is $T_d = \dfrac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a$.**

### Given
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = total conductors
- $P$ = poles
- $A$ = parallel paths
- $I_a$ = total armature current (A)
- $I = I_a/A$ = current per conductor (A, assuming all conductors carry equal current)

### Derivation (Method A — Force × Radius)

**Step 1 — Force on one conductor**

The force on a conductor of active length $l$ carrying current $I$ in flux density $B$ is:

$$f = B\,l\,I = \frac{\Phi}{a}\,I$$

where $a$ is the cross-sectional area of the pole face. We will not need $a$ explicitly — it will cancel with the radius later.

**Step 2 — Total tangential force on all conductors**

There are **Z** conductors, all carrying current $I$ in the armature, and the tangential force per conductor sums:

$$F_{total} = Z \cdot f = Z \cdot \frac{\Phi}{a}\,I$$

**Step 3 — Current per conductor**

$$I = \frac{I_a}{A}$$

So:

$$F_{total} = \frac{Z \Phi I_a}{a\,A}$$

**Step 4 — Torque = Force × Mean Radius**

Let the mean armature radius be **r** (metres). The torque developed is:

$$T_d = F_{total} \times r = \frac{Z \Phi I_a\,r}{a\,A}$$

**Step 5 — Replace $a$ with the area of one pole face**

Each pole face has area $a$. The flux per pole is $\Phi = B \cdot a$, so $a = \Phi/B$. But we can also relate $a$ to the pole pitch and armature geometry. The mean perimeter crossed by the flux is $2\pi r$, and there are **P** poles around it. The width per pole (pole pitch) is:

$$\tau = \frac{2\pi r}{P} \quad \text{(arc length per pole)}$$

The pole-face area is roughly $a = \tau \cdot l$, where $l$ is the axial core length.

Therefore:

$$\frac{r}{a} = \frac{r}{\tau\,l} = \frac{r}{\dfrac{2\pi r}{P}\cdot l} = \frac{P}{2\pi l}$$

Substituting into the torque expression:

$$T_d = \frac{Z \Phi I_a}{A} \cdot \frac{r}{a} = \frac{Z \Phi I_a}{A} \cdot \frac{P}{2\pi l}$$

But $\Phi = B \cdot a = B \cdot \tau l = B \cdot \dfrac{2\pi r}{P}\cdot l$, hence $B\,l = \dfrac{\Phi P}{2\pi r}$. Substituting:

$$T_d = \frac{Z}{A}\cdot I_a \cdot \frac{\Phi P}{2\pi} = \frac{\Phi Z I_a P}{2\pi A}$$

$$\boxed{T_d = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a \quad \text{N·m}}$$

### Derivation (Method B — Energy Conservation, much shorter!)

**Step 1 — Power balance**

In steady state, the **electrical power converted to mechanical form** equals the mechanical power developed:

$$P_{developed} = E_g \cdot I_a = T_d \cdot \omega$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $E_g$ and $\omega$**

$$T_d \cdot \omega = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}\cdot I_a$$

$$T_d \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}\cdot I_a$$

**Step 3 — Cancel N and 60**

$$T_d \cdot 2\pi = \frac{\Phi Z P}{A}\cdot I_a$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T_d$**

$$T_d = \frac{\Phi Z P\,I_a}{2\pi A}$$

$$\boxed{T_d = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a \quad \text{N·m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this method is preferred in KTU valuation:** The energy-conservation derivation is **2 lines shorter**, contains no machine geometry, and is essentially un-mark-down-able. Examiners give full marks if the power-balance identity $E_g I_a = T_d \omega$ is correctly stated.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of the Combined Speed Equation

### Statement
**Show that the speed of a DC motor in steady state is $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K\,\Phi}$, where $K = \dfrac{ZP}{60A}$ is the machine's EMF/torque constant.**

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Substitute the EMF equation into the voltage equation**

For a separately-excited or shunt motor:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

$$V = \frac{\Phi Z P N}{60 A} + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Isolate N**

$$V - I_a R_a = \frac{\Phi Z P N}{60 A}$$

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi} \cdot \frac{60 A}{Z P}$$

**Step 3 — Define the machine constant $K$**

$$K = \frac{Z P}{60 A} \quad \text{(V per Weber per rpm)}$$

Then:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K\,\Phi} \quad \text{r.p.m.}}$$

For a **shunt motor**, $V$ and $\Phi$ are nearly constant; for a **series motor**, $\Phi \propto I_a$ (before saturation), so:

$$N_{series} \propto \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{I_a}$$

which is the inverse-square-root characteristic — the basis of the **series motor's high starting torque**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:**
> A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt motor has 600 armature conductors and runs at 1000 r.p.m. when the armature current is 20 A. The armature resistance is $0.5\ \Omega$ and the field resistance is $250\ \Omega$. Compute: (a) the flux per pole, (b) the back-EMF, (c) the developed torque, (d) the shaft torque, given rotational losses of 500 W, and (e) the efficiency at this load.

### Solution

**Given:** $P=4$, $V=250$ V, $Z=600$, $N=1000$ r.p.m., $I_a=20$ A, $R_a=0.5\ \Omega$, $R_{sh}=250\ \Omega$, rotational loss = 500 W.

Assume **wave winding** (default unless otherwise stated): $A=2$.

**Part (a) — Flux per pole**

Back-EMF:
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - 20(0.5) = 250 - 10 = 240\ \text{V}$$

From EMF equation:
$$\Phi = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 A}{Z P N} = \frac{240 \times 60 \times 2}{600 \times 4 \times 1000}$$

$$\Phi = \frac{28800}{2400000} = 0.012\ \text{Wb} = 12\ \text{mWb}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi = 0.012\ \text{Wb} = 12\ \text{mWb}}$$

**Part (b) — Back-EMF** (already computed)
$$\boxed{E_b = 240\ \text{V}}$$

**Part (c) — Developed Torque**

$$T_d = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a = \frac{4}{2\pi \times 2}\times 0.012 \times 600 \times 20$$

$$T_d = \frac{4}{4\pi}\times 0.012 \times 600 \times 20 = \frac{1}{\pi}\times 144$$

$$T_d = \frac{144}{\pi} \approx 45.84\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_d \approx 45.84\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Part (d) — Shaft Torque**

Torque lost to rotational (friction + windage) losses:
$$T_{loss} = \frac{P_{rot}}{\omega} = \frac{P_{rot}}{2\pi N/60} = \frac{500 \times 60}{2\pi \times 1000} = \frac{30000}{2000\pi} = \frac{15}{\pi} \approx 4.775\ \text{N·m}$$

$$T_{sh} = T_d - T_{loss} = 45.84 - 4.775 = 41.07\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} \approx 41.07\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Part (e) — Efficiency**

Field current:
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1\ \text{A}$$

Line current:
$$I_L = I_a + I_{sh} = 20 + 1 = 21\ \text{A}$$

Input power:
$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 21 = 5250\ \text{W}$$

Output power:
$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N}{60}\cdot T_{sh} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60}\times 41.07 = 104.72 \times 41.07 \approx 4301\ \text{W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{4301}{5250} = 0.8192 \approx 81.92\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 81.92\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check via losses:**
> Armature Cu loss = $20^2 \times 0.5 = 200$ W
> Field Cu loss = $1^2 \times 250 = 250$ W
> Rotational loss = 500 W
> Total losses = $200 + 250 + 500 = 950$ W
> Output = $5250 - 950 = 4300$ W ✓ (matches)
> $\eta = 4300/5250 = 81.9\%$ ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Four Coupled Steady-State Equations

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **causal coupling** between the four steady-state equations, with the two **input variables** (terminal voltage $V$ and mechanical speed $N$ or torque load) and the **two output variables** (armature current $I_a$ and developed torque $T_d$).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["SUPPLY VOLTAGE V"]:::inputnode
    A2["MECHANICAL SPEED N"]:::inputnode
    A3["FLUX PER POLE PHI"]:::inputnode
    A4["MACHINE CONSTANT K = ZP over 60A"]:::paramnode

    B1["EMF EQUATION: Eg = K times PHI times N"]:::eqnnode
    B2["VOLTAGE EQUATION: V = Eb + Ia Ra"]:::eqnnode
    B3["TORQUE EQUATION: Td = K times PHI times Ia times 60 over 2pi"]:::eqnnode
    B4["POWER EQUATION: Pd = Eg times Ia = omega times Td"]:::eqnnode

    C1["ARMATURE CURRENT Ia"]:::outputnode
    C2["DEVELOPED TORQUE Td"]:::outputnode
    C3["DEVELOPED POWER Pd"]:::outputnode
    C4["BACK EMF Eb"]:::outputnode

    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> C4
    A1 --> B2
    C4 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    A3 --> B3
    C1 --> B3
    B3 --> C2
    C4 --> B4
    C1 --> B4
    C2 --> B4
    B4 --> C3

    classDef inputnode fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b58900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputnode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef eqnnode fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef paramnode fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#654ea3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Generator vs. Motor Sign Convention

The same machine rotates in either direction; the steady-state equations are the same algebraically but differ in the **direction of power flow** and the **sign of the $I_a R_a$ drop**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEN["GENERATOR MODE: Mechanical to Electrical"]
        G1["PRIME MOVER"]:::mech
        G2["SHAFT TORQUE T_m"]:::mech
        G3["ARMATURE"]:::machine
        G4["Eg = K Phi N"]:::eqnnode
        G5["V = Eg minus Ia Ra"]:::eqnnode
        G6["LOAD"]:::elec
        G1 --> G2 --> G3
        G3 --> G4 --> G5 --> G6
    end

    subgraph MOT["MOTOR MODE: Electrical to Mechanical"]
        M1["SUPPLY V"]:::elec
        M2["V = Eb plus Ia Ra"]:::eqnnode
        M3["ARMATURE"]:::machine
        M4["Td = K Phi Ia 60 over 2pi"]:::eqnnode
        M5["SHAFT TORQUE T_sh"]:::mech
        M6["LOAD MACHINE"]:::mech
        M1 --> M2 --> M3
        M3 --> M4 --> M5 --> M6
    end

    classDef mech fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef machine fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef eqnnode fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Power-Flow Sankey Diagram (Steady State, Motor Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["ELECTRICAL INPUT: V times IL = 5250 W"]:::elec
    P2["FIELD CIRCUIT: Ish squared Rsh = 250 W"]:::lossnode
    P3["ARMATURE CIRCUIT: Ia squared Ra = 200 W"]:::lossnode
    P4["ARMATURE INPUT: V times Ia = 5000 W"]:::elec
    P5["EB times Ia = 4800 W DEVELOPED POWER"]:::mech
    P6["BRUSH CONTACT + ROTATIONAL: 500 W"]:::lossnode
    P7["SHAFT OUTPUT: 4300 W"]:::mech

    P1 -->|250 W| P2
    P1 -->|5000 W| P4
    P4 -->|200 W| P3
    P4 -->|4800 W| P5
    P5 -->|500 W| P6
    P5 -->|4300 W| P7

    classDef elec fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lossnode fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> This Sankey diagram is from the worked numerical in Section 3.4. The exact 81.9% efficiency follows from the power balance shown.

---

## 4.4 State Diagram — Generator-Motor Duality

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Motoring
    Motoring --> Generating : Prime mover overdrives the motor beyond no-load speed
    Generating --> Motoring : Reduce prime-mover torque below developed torque
    Motoring --> Motoring : V minus Ia Ra = Eb stable steady state
    Generating --> Generating : Eg minus Ia Ra = V stable steady state

    note right of Motoring
        V equals Eb plus Ia Ra
        Eb opposes V
        Power flows in
    end note

    note right of Generating
        V equals Eg minus Ia Ra
        Eg drives current
        Power flows out
    end note
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the EMF equation of a DC machine and explain the significance of each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The EMF generated in a DC machine armature is given by:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A}$$

where:
- **$\Phi$** = flux per pole, in Weber — determines the magnetic loading.
- **$Z$** = total number of armature conductors — set by the designer.
- **$N$** = speed in r.p.m. — the operating point input.
- **$P$** = number of poles — a design choice affecting frequency and torque.
- **$A$** = number of parallel paths in the winding ($A=P$ for lap, $A=2$ for wave).
- **60** = conversion factor from minutes to seconds (since $E_g$ must be in volts, and flux is in Wb).

The equation tells us that the generated EMF is **directly proportional to flux, speed, and number of conductors**, and **inversely proportional to parallel paths**. It is the cornerstone for sizing, voltage regulation, and speed-control design.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Why is the developed torque in a DC machine independent of speed? Derive or justify the expression.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The developed torque is given by:

$$T_d = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a$$

Notice that the **speed $N$ does not appear** in this expression. Torque depends **only on flux $\Phi$ and armature current $I_a$**, both of which are electrical quantities that do not change with the rotational speed (in steady state).

This can be justified by the energy-conservation identity $P_d = E_g I_a = \omega T_d$. Substituting $E_g \propto N$ and $\omega \propto N$, the speed cancels exactly, leaving $T_d$ as a function of $\Phi$ and $I_a$ only.

The physical origin is that the **force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is independent of its velocity** — only the EMF (and hence the power) scales with speed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Two questions are given; the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each contains two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** *Derive the EMF equation of a DC machine starting from Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. Clearly state the assumptions. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt motor draws 60 A from the supply. The armature resistance is $0.15\ \Omega$ and the shunt field resistance is $100\ \Omega$. The machine has 600 conductors wave-wound. If the rotational losses are 1500 W, find: (i) the back-EMF, (ii) the speed, given that the flux per pole is 25 mWb, and (iii) the shaft torque. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Statement of Faraday's Law**

The average EMF induced in a single conductor is:

$$e_{avg} = \frac{\text{Total flux cut per rev}}{\text{Time per rev}} = \frac{\Phi P}{60/N} = \frac{\Phi P N}{60}\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Conductors in series per parallel path**

$$\text{Conductors per path} = \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Terminal EMF = Sum of conductor EMFs in one path**

$$E_g = \frac{Z}{A}\times \frac{\Phi P N}{60} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}\ \text{V}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The flux is uniformly distributed and constant in space.
2. The armature is star-connected (all conductor EMFs in one path add constructively).
3. The machine is in **steady state** (no commutation transients).
4. The conductors are radial (force always tangential).

**Marking scheme (KTU):**
- 2 marks — Faraday's law correctly stated
- 2 marks — flux-per-revolution calculation
- 2 marks — final assembly with $Z/A$
- 1 mark — assumptions and units

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Given:** $P=4$, $V=250$ V, $I_L = 60$ A, $R_a = 0.15\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 100\ \Omega$, $Z=600$, wave-wound → $A=2$, $P_{rot}=1500$ W, $\Phi = 25$ mWb = $0.025$ Wb.

**Step 1 — Field current**
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Armature current**
$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 60 - 2.5 = 57.5\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 (i) — Back-EMF**
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - 57.5 \times 0.15 = 250 - 8.625 = 241.375\ \text{V}$$
$$\boxed{E_b = 241.375\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 4 (ii) — Speed**

From the EMF equation:
$$E_b = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}\implies N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 A}{\Phi Z P}$$

$$N = \frac{241.375 \times 60 \times 2}{0.025 \times 600 \times 4} = \frac{28965}{60} \approx 482.75\ \text{r.p.m.}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 482.75\ \text{r.p.m.}}$$

**Step 5 (iii) — Developed torque**
$$T_d = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a = \frac{4}{2\pi \times 2}\times 0.025 \times 600 \times 57.5$$

$$T_d = \frac{1}{\pi}\times 862.5 = \frac{862.5}{\pi} \approx 274.55\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Shaft torque**

First compute the developed power:
$$P_d = E_b \cdot I_a = 241.375 \times 57.5 = 13879.06\ \text{W}$$

Shaft power:
$$P_{sh} = P_d - P_{rot} = 13879.06 - 1500 = 12379.06\ \text{W}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 482.75}{60} = 50.54\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$T_{sh} = \frac{P_{sh}}{\omega} = \frac{12379.06}{50.54} \approx 244.93\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} \approx 244.93\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Marking scheme (KTU):**
- (i) 2 marks — $I_{sh}$ and $E_b$ correctly computed
- (ii) 3 marks — Rearranged EMF equation, substituted values, correct $N$
- (iii) 2 marks — Torque formula, $\omega$ conversion, subtraction of rotational loss

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** *Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque developed in a DC machine using the principle of energy conservation. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A 250 V DC series motor runs at 1000 r.p.m. while drawing 40 A. The total series resistance (armature + series field) is $0.5\ \Omega$. The machine is wave-wound with 800 conductors and 4 poles. The flux per pole at this load is 30 mWb. Calculate: (i) the back-EMF, (ii) the developed power and torque, and (iii) the efficiency, given that the rotational losses are 800 W. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Step 1 — Power balance identity**

In steady state, the **electrical power converted to mechanical power** is:

$$P_{developed} = E_g \cdot I_a = T_d \cdot \omega$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the EMF equation**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z P N}{60 A}$$

$$P_d = \frac{\Phi Z P N}{60 A}\cdot I_a$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the angular speed**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

$$T_d \cdot \omega = \frac{\Phi Z P N}{60 A}\cdot I_a$$

$$T_d \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\Phi Z P N}{60 A}\cdot I_a$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $N$ and 60**

$$T_d \cdot 2\pi = \frac{\Phi Z P}{A}\cdot I_a$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T_d$**

$$T_d = \frac{\Phi Z P\,I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a\ \text{N·m}$$

**Marking scheme (KTU):**
- 2 marks — Power balance identity correctly stated
- 2 marks — Substitution of $E_g$ and $\omega$
- 2 marks — Algebraic simplification
- 1 mark — Final expression and units

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:** $V=250$ V, $N=1000$ r.p.m., $I_a = 40$ A (series motor → $I_a = I_L$), $R_a + R_{se} = 0.5\ \Omega$, $Z=800$, $P=4$, $A=2$ (wave), $\Phi = 30$ mWb = $0.030$ Wb, $P_{rot}=800$ W.

**Step 1 (i) — Back-EMF**
$$E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 40 \times 0.5 = 250 - 20 = 230\ \text{V}$$
$$\boxed{E_b = 230\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 2 (ii) — Developed Power and Torque**

$$P_d = E_b \cdot I_a = 230 \times 40 = 9200\ \text{W}$$
$$\boxed{P_d = 9200\ \text{W} = 9.2\ \text{kW}}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = \frac{2000\pi}{60} = \frac{100\pi}{3} \approx 104.72\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$T_d = \frac{P_d}{\omega} = \frac{9200}{104.72} \approx 87.85\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_d \approx 87.85\ \text{N·m}}$$

**Step 3 (iii) — Efficiency**

Total losses = Cu loss + Rotational loss
$$P_{cu} = I_a^2(R_a + R_{se}) = 40^2 \times 0.5 = 800\ \text{W}$$
$$P_{rot} = 800\ \text{W (given)}$$
$$\sum P_{loss} = 800 + 800 = 1600\ \text{W}$$

Input power:
$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 40 = 10000\ \text{W}$$

Shaft output:
$$P_{sh} = P_{in} - P_{loss} = 10000 - 1600 = 8400\ \text{W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{sh}}{P_{in}} = \frac{8400}{10000} = 0.84 = 84\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 84\%}$$

**Marking scheme (KTU):**
- (i) 2 marks — Correct $E_b$ computation with full series resistance
- (ii) 3 marks — $P_d$ from $E_b I_a$, $\omega$ conversion, $T_d$ from $P_d/\omega$
- (iii) 2 marks — Proper loss accounting (note: series motor has no shunt field loss), final efficiency

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of $I_a R_a$** — Mixing generator and motor conventions is the #1 mark-loss reason. Always draw the equivalent circuit first.
> 2. **Wave vs. Lap** — Default to $A=2$ (wave) only if the problem does not specify lap. Read carefully: "wave-wound" or "with 4 parallel paths" are not the same.
> 3. **$\omega$ vs. $N$** — Using $N$ directly in $P = TN$ gives **watts if N is in r.p.s.** For r.p.m., always use $P = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\,T$. Common error: $P = TN$ without the $2\pi/60$ factor.
> 4. **Series motor: $I_L = I_a$** — Unlike a shunt motor, a series motor has no parallel field path. The full line current flows through the armature.
> 5. **Forgetting field copper loss in shunt motor** — A shunt motor has TWO copper losses: armature ($I_a^2 R_a$) and field ($V^2/R_{sh}$). Missing the field loss inflates efficiency by 2–4%.
> 6. **Rotational losses are shaft-side** — They are subtracted from **developed torque** to get **shaft torque**, not from the electrical input.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Steady-State Equations of DC Machines**

- ☐ **EMF Equation:** $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — voltage generated by the rotating armature.
- ☐ **Torque Equation:** $T_d = \dfrac{P}{2\pi A}\,\Phi Z\,I_a$ — mechanical torque developed at the armature surface.
- ☐ **Voltage Equation (Motor):** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ — KVL around the armature loop; current flows in.
- ☐ **Voltage Equation (Generator):** $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ — KVL around the armature loop; current flows out.
- ☐ **Power Equation:** $P_d = E_g I_a = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\,T_d$ — energy conservation between electrical and mechanical ports.
- ☐ **Speed Equation:** $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$ with $K = ZP/(60A)$ — basis of all speed-control laws.
- ☐ **Lap winding:** $A = P$ (many parallel paths, low voltage, high current).
- ☐ **Wave winding:** $A = 2$ (few parallel paths, high voltage, low current).
- ☐ **Angular speed conversion:** $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ rad/s.
- ☐ **Shaft torque:** $T_{sh} = T_d - T_{rot}$ where $T_{rot} = P_{rot}/\omega$.
- ☐ **Efficiency:** $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} = (V I_a - \sum \text{Losses})/P_{in}$ — always verify by summing losses.
- ☐ **Sign rule of thumb:** In a **generator**, the induced EMF is the **source** ($E_g$ is positive and pushes current out). In a **motor**, the back-EMF is the **sink** ($E_b$ is positive and opposes $V$).
- ☐ **Independence principles:** EMF ∝ $N$ (linear); Torque independent of $N$ (depends only on $\Phi$ and $I_a$).
- ☐ **Maximum efficiency condition:** Variable loss (Cu) = Constant loss (iron + friction) — useful for design problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** If a problem gives you flux, conductors, poles, and speed → **always** start by computing $E_g$ or $E_b$ from the EMF equation. If it gives you current and flux → **always** start with the torque equation. If it gives you voltage, resistance, and current → **always** start with the KVL voltage equation. The four equations are your four keys — pick the one that uses the **given** data first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Numerical Problems – Module 1: DC Machines

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> *Numerical Problems* in the context of **DC Machines (Module 1)** refer to quantitative exercises that require the application of the fundamental equations governing the electromechanical energy conversion process in **DC Generators** and **DC Motors**. These problems bridge the gap between theoretical derivations (EMF Equation, Torque Equation) and practical engineering design, involving the calculation of parameters like induced EMF, electromagnetic torque, speed, efficiency, and the verification of machine characteristics under various loading conditions.

## 1.1 Intuitive Overview — Why Do We Solve Numerical Problems in DC Machines?

Imagine you are an electrical engineer tasked with designing a **DC motor** for an electric vehicle's power window. You have a battery rated at **12 V**, but your motor needs to spin a gear at a specific **RPM (speed)** and deliver a particular **torque** to lift the glass. How do you know how many turns of wire to wind on the armature, or how strong the magnetic field needs to be?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pump" Model of a DC Machine:**
> Think of a **DC Generator** as a *water pump pushed by hand*. The harder you push (mechanical input), the more water flows out per second (electrical output). The voltage produced is like the water pressure, and the current is like the flow rate. The **EMF Equation** (`E = kΦN`) tells you the "pressure" you create by pushing coils through the magnetic field. Conversely, a **DC Motor** is like a *turbine driven by water* — the more voltage you apply, the faster the turbine spins, and the more current flows, the more twisting force (torque) it produces.
>
> Numerical problems are essentially **engineering math** that lets you size the "pump" or "turbine" precisely for your application.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Module 1:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Flux (Φ)**: Measured in **Weber (Wb)** — typically between **0.005 Wb** to **0.05 Wb** for small/medium machines.
> - **Speed (N)**: Measured in **Revolutions Per Minute (RPM)**.
> - **Angular Velocity (ω)**: Measured in **radians per second (rad/s)** — conversion: $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$.
> - **EMF (E) / Voltage (V)**: Measured in **Volts (V)**.
> - **Current (Iₐ)**: Armature current in **Amperes (A)**.
> - **Torque (T)**: Measured in **Newton-Meters (N·m)**.
> - **Power (P)**: Measured in **Watts (W)** or **Kilowatts (kW)**.
> - **Efficiency (η)**: Dimensionless ratio, expressed in **percentage (%)**.

---

## 1.2 Categories of Numerical Problems in Module 1

KTU Module 1 numerical problems typically fall into the following categories:

1. **EMF Equation Problems** — Calculating generated EMF or finding unknown parameters (Φ, N, Z, P, A) in a DC Generator.
2. **Torque Equation Problems** — Computing shaft torque, armature torque, and lost torque in a DC Motor.
3. **Power Flow & Efficiency Problems** — Determining input/output power, copper losses, iron losses, and overall efficiency.
4. **Speed Regulation & Characteristics** — Calculating no-load speed, full-load speed, and speed regulation.
5. **Armature Reaction & Commutation Problems** — Computing demagnetizing/total number of conductors.
6. **Combined Problems** — Multi-part questions involving all of the above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:**
> As per the revised Bloom's Taxonomy alignment in NEP 2020, Module 1 numerical problems typically test the cognitive levels **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)**. You are expected to *apply* formulas to compute values and *analyze* machine performance under varying load conditions.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation Philosophy (KTU Examiner's View):**
> Every numerical problem in DC Machines stems from **TWO master equations** — the **EMF Equation** and the **Torque Equation**. If you internalize these two and understand the relationships between electrical, mechanical, and magnetic domains, you can solve over **90% of Module 1 numerical problems**.

## 2.1 The EMF Equation (DC Generator Perspective)

When the armature of a DC machine rotates in a magnetic field, an EMF is induced in the conductors. The average EMF induced is given by:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

Where:
- $E_g$ = Generated EMF (in **Volts**)
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (in **Weber**)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of armature (in **RPM**)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths

> **Why this works:** Each conductor cuts the magnetic flux $\Phi$ a total of $\frac{P}{A}$ times per revolution, and completes $\frac{N}{60}$ revolutions per second. Multiplying by $Z$ gives total EMF per second, and dividing by 1 second gives the average EMF.

### Parallel Paths for Different Winding Types:
- **Lap Winding**: $A = P$
- **Wave Winding**: $A = 2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** For **wave winding**, $A = 2$ regardless of the number of poles. This means wave-wound machines have a *lower induced EMF per conductor* but *higher current capacity*. Lap-wound machines give *higher EMF* and are used for high-current applications.

---

## 2.2 The Torque Equation (DC Motor Perspective)

When a DC machine acts as a motor, current flows through the armature conductors placed in the magnetic field, producing a force and hence a torque:

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

Simplified form:

$$T_a = 0.159 \times \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} \quad (\text{in N·m})$$

Where:
- $T_a$ = Armature Torque (in **N·m**)
- $I_a$ = Armature current (in **Amperes**)

> **Physical Intuition:** Torque is the *mechanical analog of current flow*. Higher flux means stronger magnetic "grip", more conductors mean more force-producing wires, and higher current means more electrons experiencing that force.

---

## 2.3 The Power Equation (Bridge Between Electrical & Mechanical)

The electromagnetic (developed) power is the power converted from electrical to mechanical form (in motor) or vice versa (in generator):

$$P_{conv} = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$$

Where:
- $E_b$ = Back EMF (in motor) or Generated EMF $E_g$ (in generator) (in **V**)
- $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ = Angular velocity (in **rad/s**)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Key Condition |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | EMF Equation | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | $E_g$ in V, $\Phi$ in Wb, $N$ in RPM | All DC machines |
| 2 | Torque Equation | $T_a = 0.159 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $T_a$ in N·m, $I_a$ in A | DC Motor |
| 3 | Power Converted | $P_{m} = E_b \cdot I_a = \dfrac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$ | $P_{m}$ in W, $\omega$ in rad/s | Both modes |
| 4 | Shaft Torque | $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{friction}$ (loss torque) | $T_{sh}$ in N·m | Motor output |
| 5 | Input Power | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ | $V$ in V, $I_L$ in A | Motor input |
| 6 | Output Power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ | $P_{out}$ in W | Motor output |
| 7 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Both modes |
| 8 | Copper Loss | $P_{cu} = I_a^{2} R_a$ | $R_a$ in Ω | Armature |
| 9 | Speed (Motor) | $N = \dfrac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi Z P}$ | From EMF equation | Rearranged |
| 10 | Brush Contact Drop | $V_{brush} = 2 \times 1$ V (typical) | $V_{brush}$ in V | Practical loss |
| 11 | Wave Winding | $A = 2$ | Independent of $P$ | $m$-simplex |
| 12 | Lap Winding | $A = P$ | Equal to number of poles | $P$-simplex |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Unit Consistency is Worth 1 Mark:**
> Always ensure that flux is in **Weber (Wb)**, speed in **RPM** (convert to rev/s by dividing by 60 if needed), and that you use the **correct value of A** (2 for wave, P for lap). Many KTU students lose easy marks by mixing up units or winding types.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important in real engineering?**
> - **Electric Vehicle Design**: Determining torque and speed ratings of DC traction motors.
> - **Renewable Energy**: DC generators are precursors to modern wind turbine generators (AC, but with DC exciters).
> - **Industrial Automation**: Sizing DC servo motors for CNC machines and robotic arms.
> - **Battery-Powered Systems**: Estimating battery drain time using efficiency calculations.
> - **Power Plants**: Backup DC generators for emergency systems.
