# Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Solutions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section Objective:** This section contains **exhaustively detailed** step-by-step solutions to the most important types of numerical problems that appear in KTU University Examinations for Module 1. Every algebraic step and numerical evaluation is shown explicitly — no shortcuts, no skipped steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation (Conceptual Foundation)

**Starting Point:** Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction states that the EMF induced in a conductor is equal to the rate of change of flux linkage.

**Step 1:** Consider one conductor cutting the flux. In one revolution, the conductor cuts a flux equal to:

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = \Phi \times P$$

This is because there are $P$ poles, and as the conductor moves past each pole, it cuts the flux $\Phi$ once.

**Step 2:** For a **simplex wave-wound** machine, the number of parallel paths is $A = 2$, so the number of conductors in series per parallel path is $\frac{Z}{A} = \frac{Z}{2}$.

**Step 3:** Total flux cut per revolution by all conductors in series in one parallel path:

$$\text{Flux cut} = \Phi \times P \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 4:** Time taken for one revolution (in seconds) is:

$$t = \frac{60}{N}$$

**Step 5:** Average EMF per parallel path (and hence the terminal voltage of the machine, ignoring voltage drops):

$$E_g = \frac{\text{Flux cut}}{\text{Time per revolution}} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the master EMF equation. The same equation is used for both DC generators and DC motors, but in a motor, the induced EMF is called **Back EMF ($E_b$)** and it opposes the applied voltage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation (Conceptual Foundation)

**Starting Point:** Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is given by the Lorentz force law:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$

where $B$ is the flux density, $I$ is the current, and $l$ is the length of the conductor.

**Step 1:** The force on a single conductor is:

$$F = B \cdot I_c \cdot l$$

where $I_c = \frac{I_a}{A}$ is the current per conductor.

**Step 2:** Torque produced by one conductor is $F \times r$, where $r$ is the radius of the armature. For a multi-pole machine, the effective radius is $\frac{D}{2}$, but the torque derivation can be simplified by noting that the total torque is proportional to $F \times \frac{D}{2}$ summed over all conductors.

**Step 3:** Summing over all $Z$ conductors and using the relationship between flux density and flux:

$$B = \frac{\Phi}{A_{pole}} = \frac{\Phi P}{2\pi r l}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting and simplifying (after substantial algebra, which we skip in this conceptual view to focus on numericals):

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

**Step 5:** The constant $\frac{1}{2\pi} \approx 0.1591$, giving the practical form:

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{N·m}$$

---

## 3.3 Comprehensive Numerical Solutions (KTU Exam Pattern)

### 📘 Problem 1: EMF Equation (Lap-Wound DC Generator)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**
> *A 4-pole, lap-wound DC generator has 500 armature conductors and a flux per pole of 0.02 Wb. It rotates at 1500 RPM. Calculate the EMF generated. If the machine is rewound as wave-wound with the same conductors, what would be the new EMF at the same speed and flux?*

**Given Data:**
- Number of poles, $P = 4$
- Total armature conductors, $Z = 500$
- Flux per pole, $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb
- Speed, $N = 1500$ RPM

**Step 1: Identify the winding type and parallel paths.**

For lap winding: $A = P = 4$

**Step 2: Apply the EMF equation for lap winding.**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot P} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60}$$

(Notice how $P$ cancels out for lap winding!)

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{0.02 \times 500 \times 1500}{60}$$

**Step 4: Compute numerator first.**

$$0.02 \times 500 = 10$$

$$10 \times 1500 = 15000$$

**Step 5: Divide by 60.**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{15000}{60} = 250 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6: Rewind as wave winding. Now $A = 2$.**

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} = \frac{0.02 \times 500 \times 1500 \times 4}{60 \times 2}$$

**Step 7: Compute numerator.**

$$0.02 \times 500 = 10$$
$$10 \times 1500 = 15000$$
$$15000 \times 4 = 60000$$

**Step 8: Compute denominator.**

$$60 \times 2 = 120$$

**Step 9: Final division.**

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \frac{60000}{120} = 500 \text{ V}$$

**✅ Final Answers:**
- Lap-wound EMF: $E_g = 250$ V
- Wave-wound EMF: $E_g = 500$ V

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Learning:** Wave winding produces **twice the EMF** of lap winding (for a 4-pole machine), because the same conductors are now arranged in fewer parallel paths (2 instead of 4), putting more conductors in series per path.

---

### 📘 Problem 2: Torque and Power in a DC Motor

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> *A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω and a field winding resistance of 250 Ω. The motor draws a total current of 30 A at full load. The armature has 1000 conductors, 4 poles, is wave-wound, and runs at 1000 RPM. Calculate: (a) the flux per pole, (b) the shaft torque, and (c) the efficiency, assuming mechanical and iron losses total 400 W.*

**Given Data:**
- Terminal voltage, $V = 250$ V
- Armature resistance, $R_a = 0.5$ Ω
- Field resistance, $R_{sh} = 250$ Ω
- Line current, $I_L = 30$ A
- Conductors, $Z = 1000$
- Poles, $P = 4$ (wave wound, so $A = 2$)
- Speed, $N = 1000$ RPM
- Mechanical + Iron losses = 400 W

---

**Part (a): Flux per pole**

**Step 1: Calculate shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Calculate armature current using KCL.**

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 30 - 1 = 29 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate back EMF using the motor voltage equation.**

For a DC motor: $V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$

$$E_b = V - I_a \cdot R_a = 250 - (29 \times 0.5) = 250 - 14.5 = 235.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Apply the EMF equation and solve for $\Phi$.**

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$$

Rearranging for $\Phi$:

$$\Phi = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{Z \cdot N \cdot P}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the values.**

$$\Phi = \frac{235.5 \times 60 \times 2}{1000 \times 1000 \times 4}$$

**Step 6: Compute numerator.**

$$235.5 \times 60 = 14130$$
$$14130 \times 2 = 28260$$

**Step 7: Compute denominator.**

$$1000 \times 1000 = 10^6$$
$$10^6 \times 4 = 4 \times 10^6$$

**Step 8: Final division.**

$$\Phi = \frac{28260}{4000000} = 7.065 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi \approx 7.07 \text{ mWb}}$$

---

**Part (b): Shaft Torque**

**Step 1: Calculate the electromagnetic (armature) torque.**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \times 7.065 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 29 \times \frac{4}{2}$$

**Step 3: Simplify $\frac{P}{A}$.**

$$\frac{4}{2} = 2$$

**Step 4: Compute step-by-step.**

$$0.1591 \times 7.065 \times 10^{-3} = 1.124 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$1.124 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 1.124$$
$$1.124 \times 29 = 32.596$$
$$32.596 \times 2 = 65.19 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the loss torque (from mechanical and iron losses).**

$$T_{loss} = \frac{P_{loss}}{\omega} = \frac{P_{loss}}{\frac{2\pi N}{60}} = \frac{60 \cdot P_{loss}}{2\pi N}$$

**Step 6: Substitute and compute.**

$$T_{loss} = \frac{60 \times 400}{2\pi \times 1000} = \frac{24000}{6283.19} = 3.82 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 7: Calculate shaft torque.**

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss} = 65.19 - 3.82 = 61.37 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} \approx 61.37 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

**Part (c): Efficiency**

**Step 1: Calculate input power.**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 30 = 7500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Calculate output (shaft) power.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000 \times 61.37}{60}$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator.**

$$2\pi \times 1000 = 6283.19$$
$$6283.19 \times 61.37 = 385605.3 \text{ (approx.)}$$

**Step 4: Divide by 60.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{385605.3}{60} = 6426.76 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Calculate efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{6426.76}{7500} \times 100 = 85.69\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 85.7\%}$$

---

### 📘 Problem 3: Speed Regulation (DC Shunt Motor)

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern]**
> *A DC shunt motor runs at 1500 RPM on no load. At full load, the armature reaction weakens the flux by 5% and the armature voltage drop is 8 V. If the supply voltage is 220 V and the no-load back EMF is 212 V, calculate the full-load speed.*

**Given Data:**
- No-load speed, $N_0 = 1500$ RPM
- No-load back EMF, $E_{b0} = 212$ V
- Supply voltage, $V = 220$ V
- Armature drop at full load, $I_a R_a = 8$ V
- Flux reduction due to armature reaction, $\Phi_{FL} = 0.95 \cdot \Phi_{NL}$

**Step 1: Calculate full-load back EMF.**

$$E_{b,FL} = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 8 = 212 \text{ V}$$

(Interestingly, in this case the full-load $E_b$ equals the no-load $E_b$ value.)

**Step 2: Apply the speed formula. We know that:**

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P}$$

This means $N \propto \frac{E_b}{\Phi}$.

**Step 3: Form the ratio of full-load to no-load speed.**

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{N_{NL}} = \frac{E_{b,FL} / \Phi_{FL}}{E_{b,NL} / \Phi_{NL}} = \frac{E_{b,FL}}{E_{b,NL}} \cdot \frac{\Phi_{NL}}{\Phi_{FL}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the values.**

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{1500} = \frac{212}{212} \cdot \frac{1}{0.95}$$

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{1500} = 1 \times \frac{1}{0.95} = 1.0526$$

**Step 5: Solve for $N_{FL}$.**

$$N_{FL} = 1500 \times 1.0526 = 1578.9 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 6: Calculate speed regulation.**

$$\text{Regulation} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{1500 - 1578.9}{1578.9} \times 100 = -4.99\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative speed regulation** here means the motor runs *faster* at full load than at no load. This is unusual for a shunt motor and happens because the **armature reaction weakening effect (5%) overcompensates** for the small armature drop.

---

### 📘 Problem 4: Armature Reaction and Demagnetizing Conductors

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Pattern]**
> *A 4-pole, wave-wound DC generator has 800 armature conductors. The brushes are shifted by 4 mechanical degrees from the geometric neutral axis. Calculate the number of demagnetizing and cross-magnetizing armature conductors per pole.*

**Given Data:**
- Poles, $P = 4$
- Conductors, $Z = 800$ (wave-wound, $A = 2$)
- Brush shift, $\theta_{m} = 4°$ (mechanical)

**Step 1: Convert mechanical degrees to electrical degrees.**

$$\theta_{e} = \frac{P}{2} \times \theta_m = \frac{4}{2} \times 4° = 2 \times 4° = 8°$$

**Step 2: Calculate the angular span of demagnetizing zone.**

The demagnetizing zone is the angular range that lies *behind* the new brush position by the same angle $\theta_e$. So the demagnetizing zone spans $2\theta_e$ in electrical degrees:

$$\text{Demag zone} = 2\theta_e = 2 \times 8° = 16°$$

**Step 3: Calculate demagnetizing conductors per pole.**

$$\text{Conductors per pole (electrical)} = \frac{Z}{2P} = \frac{800}{8} = 100 \text{ conductors/pole}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the fraction of demagnetizing zone.**

$$\text{Frac} = \frac{2\theta_e}{180°} = \frac{16°}{180°} = 0.0889$$

**Step 5: Number of demagnetizing conductors per pole.**

$$Z_d = 0.0889 \times 100 = 8.89 \approx 9 \text{ conductors/pole}$$

**Step 6: Total demagnetizing conductors.**

$$Z_{d,\text{total}} = Z_d \times P = 9 \times 4 = 36 \text{ conductors}$$

**Step 7: Cross-magnetizing conductors per pole.**

$$Z_c = 100 - 9 = 91 \text{ conductors/pole}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{d,\text{total}} = 36, \quad Z_c = 91 \text{ per pole}}$$

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Bank Notice:** All questions in this section follow the **exact pattern of KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations (ESE)**. The Part A questions are 3-mark direct-concept questions, and Part B questions are 14-mark internal-choice problems with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Model solutions with complete step-by-step working are provided.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**State the EMF equation of a DC generator. Explain the significance of the term "number of parallel paths" (A) in the equation.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> The EMF equation of a DC generator is:
>
> $$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$
>
> where $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $Z$ = total armature conductors, $N$ = speed (RPM), $P$ = number of poles, and $A$ = number of parallel paths.
>
> **Significance of A (1 Mark):**
> The number of parallel paths depends on the winding type: $A = P$ for lap winding and $A = 2$ for wave winding. It determines how the armature conductors are interconnected and directly affects both the **generated EMF** and the **current capacity** of the machine. **Wave winding** (A=2) produces higher EMF with lower current rating, while **lap winding** (A=P) produces lower EMF with higher current rating.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define "armature torque" and "shaft torque" of a DC motor. How are they related?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> - **Armature Torque ($T_a$):** The torque developed by the armature conductors due to the interaction of armature current and magnetic flux. It is the gross torque produced inside the machine. (1.5 Marks)
>
> $$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{N·m}$$
>
> - **Shaft Torque ($T_{sh}$):** The net torque available at the motor shaft for doing useful mechanical work, after subtracting the losses due to friction, windage, and iron losses. (1.5 Marks)
>
> **Relationship:** $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss}$, where $T_{loss}$ is the torque lost to mechanical and iron losses.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
> *A 250 V, 4-pole, lap-wound DC shunt motor has 800 armature conductors. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω and the field resistance is 125 Ω. The motor takes a total current of 60 A at full load. The flux per pole is 30 mWb, and the mechanical and iron losses total 800 W.*

**Calculate the following:**
- **(a)** The back EMF, armature current, and speed at full load. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** The shaft torque, output power, and efficiency at full load. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $V = 250$ V, $P = 4$, lap-wound so $A = 4$
- $Z = 800$, $R_a = 0.2$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 125$ Ω
- $I_L = 60$ A, $\Phi = 0.03$ Wb
- Mechanical + Iron losses = 800 W

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{125} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Armature current.**

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 60 - 2 = 58 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Back EMF.**

$$E_b = V - I_a \cdot R_a = 250 - (58 \times 0.2) = 250 - 11.6 = 238.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Speed using EMF equation (rearranged for N).**

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P} = \frac{238.4 \times 60 \times 4}{0.03 \times 800 \times 4}$$

**Step 5: Numerator.**

$$238.4 \times 60 = 14304$$
$$14304 \times 4 = 57216$$

**Step 6: Denominator.**

$$0.03 \times 800 = 24$$
$$24 \times 4 = 96$$

**Step 7: Division.**

$$N = \frac{57216}{96} = 596 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 238.4 \text{ V}, \quad I_a = 58 \text{ A}, \quad N = 596 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Armature torque.**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} = 0.1591 \times 0.03 \times 800 \times 58 \times \frac{4}{4}$$

**Step 2: Simplification (P/A = 1 for lap winding).**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \times 0.03 \times 800 \times 58 \times 1$$

**Step 3: Compute step-by-step.**

$$0.1591 \times 0.03 = 4.773 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$4.773 \times 10^{-3} \times 800 = 3.8184$$
$$3.8184 \times 58 = 221.47 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Loss torque from mechanical and iron losses.**

$$T_{loss} = \frac{60 \times P_{loss}}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 800}{2\pi \times 596} = \frac{48000}{3744.7} = 12.82 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Shaft torque.**

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss} = 221.47 - 12.82 = 208.65 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6: Output power.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 596 \times 208.65}{60}$$

**Step 7: Numerator computation.**

$$2\pi \times 596 = 3744.74$$
$$3744.74 \times 208.65 = 781415.5$$

**Step 8: Division.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{781415.5}{60} = 13023.6 \text{ W} \approx 13.02 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 9: Input power.**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 60 = 15000 \text{ W} = 15 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 10: Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{13023.6}{15000} \times 100 = 86.82\%$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} = 208.65 \text{ N·m}, \quad P_{out} = 13.02 \text{ kW}, \quad \eta = 86.82\%}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**
> *A 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt generator has 600 armature conductors. The armature and field resistances are 0.3 Ω and 200 Ω, respectively. The flux per pole is 0.025 Wb, and the generator delivers a load current of 100 A at 250 V.*

**Calculate the following:**
- **(a)** The generated EMF and the speed of the generator. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** The armature copper loss, total losses, and efficiency of the generator. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $P = 4$, wave-wound, $A = 2$
- $Z = 600$, $R_a = 0.3$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 200$ Ω
- $\Phi = 0.025$ Wb
- Load current $I_L = 100$ A, terminal voltage $V = 250$ V

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{200} = 1.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Calculate armature current (same as load current in a generator when $I_L$ is the line current; here, the load is connected to the terminals, so $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$).**

$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 1.25 = 101.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the voltage drop in the armature.**

$$I_a \cdot R_a = 101.25 \times 0.3 = 30.375 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the generated EMF (DC generator voltage equation: $E_g = V + I_a R_a + V_{brush}$).**

Assuming brush contact drop of 2 V (standard assumption: 1 V per brush):

$$E_g = 250 + 30.375 + 2 = 282.375 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Calculate speed from the EMF equation.**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$$

$$N = \frac{E_g \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P} = \frac{282.375 \times 60 \times 2}{0.025 \times 600 \times 4}$$

**Step 6: Numerator.**

$$282.375 \times 60 = 16942.5$$
$$16942.5 \times 2 = 33885$$

**Step 7: Denominator.**

$$0.025 \times 600 = 15$$
$$15 \times 4 = 60$$

**Step 8: Division.**

$$N = \frac{33885}{60} = 564.75 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{E_g = 282.375 \text{ V}, \quad N = 564.75 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Armature copper loss.**

$$P_{cu,a} = I_a^{2} \cdot R_a = (101.25)^2 \times 0.3$$

**Step 2: Compute $(101.25)^2$.**

$$(101.25)^2 = 10251.5625$$

**Step 3: Multiply by $R_a$.**

$$P_{cu,a} = 10251.5625 \times 0.3 = 3075.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Field copper loss.**

$$P_{cu,sh} = I_{sh}^{2} \cdot R_{sh} = (1.25)^2 \times 200 = 1.5625 \times 200 = 312.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Brush contact loss (assuming 2 V total drop and $I_a$ flowing through brushes).**

$$P_{brush} = V_{brush} \cdot I_a = 2 \times 101.25 = 202.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6: Total electrical losses (assuming no mechanical/iron loss data given, only electrical losses).**

$$P_{loss,total} = P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,sh} + P_{brush} = 3075.47 + 312.5 + 202.5 = 3590.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7: Output power delivered to the load.**

$$P_{out} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 100 = 25000 \text{ W} = 25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8: Input mechanical power.**

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss,total} = 25000 + 3590.47 = 28590.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 9: Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{25000}{28590.47} \times 100 = 87.44\%$$

$$\boxed{P_{cu,a} = 3075.47 \text{ W}, \quad P_{loss,total} = 3590.47 \text{ W}, \quad \eta = 87.44\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
>
> 1. **Confusing A = P (lap) vs A = 2 (wave):** This single mistake can lead to a 50% error in your EMF and Torque calculations. Always re-read the problem statement for the winding type.
> 2. **Forgetting the field current in $I_a$ calculation:** In a **motor**, $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$. In a **generator**, $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the brush contact drop:** Many students assume $E_g = V$ in generators and $E_b = V$ in motors, ignoring the $I_a R_a$ drop. You will lose 1-2 marks if this is missing.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing RPM and rad/s for angular velocity. KTU expects RPM in the EMF equation directly, and rad/s in the power equation $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$.
> 5. **Shaft vs. Armature Torque confusion:** The question may ask for "shaft torque" but students compute "armature torque" and skip the loss torque subtraction.
> 6. **Power equation sign errors:** Output = Input − Losses. Always check the direction of energy flow before writing the equation.
> 7. **No unit on final answer:** A numerical answer without units (V, A, RPM, N·m, W, %) is considered incomplete and will lose 0.5–1 mark per instance.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 Numerical Problems**

- ✅ The **EMF Equation** $E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ is the **master equation** for all DC machine voltage calculations.
- ✅ For **Lap Winding**: $A = P$. For **Wave Winding**: $A = 2$ (independent of poles).
- ✅ The **Torque Equation** $T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$ applies to **motors only**; in generators, this represents the mechanical torque required.
- ✅ The **Voltage Equation**:
   - Motor: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (back EMF opposes applied voltage)
   - Generator: $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ (generated EMF exceeds terminal voltage)
- ✅ The **Current Equation**:
   - Motor: $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$
   - Generator: $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$
- ✅ The **Power Conversion Equation**: $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \omega = \frac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$.
- ✅ **Shaft Torque** = Armature Torque − Loss Torque.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$, always between 0% and 100%.
- ✅ **Speed Regulation** $= \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$. For shunt motors, it's typically +1% to +5% (positive).
- ✅ In **armature reaction** problems: convert mechanical degrees to electrical degrees using $\theta_e = \frac{P}{2} \theta_m$.
- ✅ **Demagnetizing conductors per pole** $= \frac{Z}{2P} \cdot \frac{2\theta_e}{180°}$.
- ✅ **Cross-magnetizing conductors per pole** = (Total conductors per pole) − (Demagnetizing conductors per pole).
- ✅ Always carry **units** through your calculation: Wb, RPM, V, A, Ω, N·m, W, %.
- ✅ **Conversion check**: $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ — if you forget to convert, you'll get errors in power/torque calculations.
- ✅ **Sanity check your answer**: EMF should be in tens to hundreds of volts; torque in single to hundreds of N·m; efficiency typically 75–95%.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Shunt and Series Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Shunt and Series Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In DC machines, the term **"Characteristics"** refers to the predefined functional and graphical relationships between the dependent and independent operating variables of the motor. For DC shunt and series motors, the KTU 2024 syllabus mandates the study of the following three primary curves evaluated under steady-state operating conditions:

1. **Electrical (Torque–Armature Current) Characteristic:** $T$ vs $I_a$
2. **Mechanical (Speed–Armature Current) Characteristic:** $N$ vs $I_a$
3. **Combined (Speed–Torque) Characteristic:** $N$ vs $T$

These curves are obtained experimentally (load test) or analytically using the fundamental DC machine equations. They are critical because they dictate the **load-speed regulation**, **starting behavior**, and ultimately, the **application domain** of the motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The 2024 Scheme explicitly expects students to derive the $T \propto \phi I_a$ relationship, plot the three characteristics for both shunt and series configurations, and justify applications based on these curves. This is a recurring 14-mark question in End Semester Evaluations (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition for First-Time Learners**

Imagine two vehicles climbing a steep hill:

**Shunt Motor ≈ A Car with Cruise Control 🚗**
The field winding of a shunt motor is connected directly across the constant DC supply, just like a cruise control system that constantly monitors and adjusts to maintain a set speed. No matter how steep the hill (load torque), the car (motor) tries to maintain a near-constant speed. The "field flux" ($\phi$) is held virtually constant by the constant supply voltage, so the speed barely drops even when load increases — this is called **good speed regulation**.

**Series Motor ≈ A Tractor with Direct Throttle Linkage 🚜**
In a series motor, the field winding carries the full armature current, just like a tractor whose engine power is directly tied to the accelerator pedal — press harder, more fuel, more pulling force (torque). However, if there is no load (accelerator barely pressed), the engine races dangerously. Thus, a series motor produces **huge starting torque** but **dangerously high no-load speed** — this is why it is **never started without a mechanical load** (e.g., used in cranes, traction, and electric locomotives).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Rated Speed ($N$):** Expressed in revolutions per minute (**rpm**), typically **1500 rpm** or **3000 rpm** for industrial machines.
- **Torque ($T$):** Measured in **Newton-metre (N·m)**.
- **Flux ($\phi$):** Measured in **Weber (Wb)** or per pole.
- **Armature Current ($I_a$):** Measured in **Amperes (A)**.
- **Machine Constants:** $k = \dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}$ — depends on the number of poles ($P$), conductors ($Z$), and parallel paths ($A$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Equations — Step-by-Step Logic

The analytical foundation for plotting characteristics rests on three fundamental equations that every KTU student must memorize and apply:

### Step 1: Torque Equation (Mechanical Output)
The torque developed in a DC motor originates from the interaction between the armature conductors and the magnetic field (Lorentz force):

$$T = k \phi I_a \quad \text{(N·m)}$$

Where:
- $k = \dfrac{PZ}{60A}$ (when speed $N$ is in rpm and Eb in Volts)
- $\phi$ = Flux per pole in **Wb**
- $I_a$ = Armature current in **A**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This single equation tells us that torque depends *jointly* on flux and armature current. In shunt motors $\phi$ is constant, so $T \propto I_a$ (linear). In series motors $\phi$ depends on $I_a$ itself, leading to a **quadratic** $T \propto I_a^2$ relationship before magnetic saturation.

### Step 2: Back EMF Equation (Electrical Side)

$$E_b = \frac{P \phi Z N}{60 A} = k \phi N \quad \text{(Volts)}$$

This is the EMF induced in the rotating armature conductors (Faraday's Law).

### Step 3: Voltage Equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law)
Apply KVL across the armature terminals:

**For Shunt Motor:**

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Therefore:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a$$

**For Series Motor:**

$$V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

Therefore:

$$E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

### Step 4: Speed Equation (Combining 2 and 3)

For **Shunt Motor:**

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \phi}$$

For **Series Motor:**

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \phi}$$

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Shunt Motor Characteristics

Since the shunt field is connected across the constant supply voltage $V$, the shunt field current $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ is **practically constant**. Hence $\phi$ is **assumed constant** (neglecting armature reaction).

### (a) $T$ vs $I_a$ Characteristic
$$T = k \phi I_a \quad \text{with } \phi = \text{constant}$$

- This is a **straight line** passing through the origin.
- Slope $= k\phi$ (constant).
- **Linear rise** in torque with armature current.

### (b) $N$ vs $I_a$ Characteristic
$$N = \frac{V}{k\phi} - \frac{R_a}{k\phi} I_a$$

- This is also a **straight line**, but with a **small negative slope** $= -R_a / k\phi$.
- As $I_a$ increases (more load), $N$ drops slightly due to $I_a R_a$ drop.
- This is called a **"drooping" characteristic** — the drop is typically **3% to 8%** from no-load to full-load (excellent regulation).

### (c) $N$ vs $T$ Characteristic
Since $T \propto I_a$, we can substitute $I_a = T / k\phi$ into the speed equation:

$$N = \frac{V}{k\phi} - \frac{R_a}{(k\phi)^2} T$$

- This is again a **straight line with slight negative slope**.
- Indicates that speed remains **nearly constant** with varying load torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters in Engineering:** The **constant-speed nature** of the shunt motor makes it ideal for applications requiring precise speed regulation, such as **lathes, fans, blowers, printing machines, conveyors, and centrifugal pumps**.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Series Motor Characteristics

In a series motor, the same current $I_a$ flows through both the armature and the series field winding. Thus $I_{se} = I_a$ and $\phi$ depends on $I_a$.

### Case 1: Before Magnetic Saturation (Light Loads)
If the magnetic circuit is unsaturated, flux is proportional to field MMF:

$$\phi \propto I_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = c \cdot I_a \quad \text{where } c \text{ is a constant}$$

Substituting into torque equation:

$$T = k \phi I_a = k (c \cdot I_a) I_a = k c I_a^2$$

$$T \propto I_a^2 \quad \text{(Parabolic / Quadratic)}$$

For speed:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \phi} = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k c I_a}$$

As $I_a$ increases (heavy load), the numerator decreases and denominator increases, so $N$ drops sharply:

$$N \propto \frac{1}{I_a} \quad \text{(Inverse / Hyperbolic)}$$

For the $N$ vs $T$ relationship, since $T \propto I_a^2$, we have $I_a \propto \sqrt{T}$:

$$N \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}} \quad \text{(Inverse square-root)}$$

### Case 2: After Magnetic Saturation (Heavy Loads)
Once the iron saturates, $\phi$ becomes **constant** regardless of further increase in $I_a$:

$$\phi = \text{constant}$$

Then the equations revert to **shunt-motor form**:

$$T \propto I_a \quad \text{(Linear)}$$
$$N \propto \frac{1}{I_a} \quad \text{(Hyperbolic)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Danger Zone:** At very light loads or no-load, $I_a \to 0$, hence $\phi \to 0$. From $N = V / k\phi$, $N$ tends to **infinity**. The motor mechanically self-destructs. Hence a series motor is **never belt-coupled** and **never started without load** (minimum 10–20% load).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters in Engineering:** The **high starting torque** and **high-speed at light load** characteristics make the series motor ideal for **traction (electric trains, trams), cranes, hoists, elevators, and electric vehicles** where the load is always mechanically coupled to the shaft.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| Parameter | Shunt Motor | Series Motor (Unsaturated) | Series Motor (Saturated) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Field Current $I_{sh}$ | $V / R_{sh}$ (constant) | $I_{se} = I_a$ (varies) | $I_{se} = I_a$ (varies) |
| Flux $\phi$ | Constant | $\propto I_a$ | Constant |
| Torque $T$ | $k\phi I_a$ | $k c I_a^2$ | $k\phi I_a$ |
| $T$ vs $I_a$ shape | **Linear (straight line)** | **Parabolic ($T \propto I_a^2$)** | **Linear (straight line)** |
| Speed $N$ | $(V - I_a R_a) / k\phi$ | $\propto 1/I_a$ | $\propto 1/I_a$ |
| $N$ vs $I_a$ shape | Slight negative slope (drooping) | Sharp inverse curve | Inverse curve |
| $N$ vs $T$ shape | Slight negative slope | $\propto 1/\sqrt{T}$ | $\propto 1/T$ |
| Back EMF $E_b$ | $V - I_a R_a$ | $V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | $V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ |
| Speed Regulation | **Good (3–8%)** | **Poor (variable)** | **Poor (variable)** |
| Starting Torque | **Moderate (1.2 to 1.5 × FLT)** | **Very High (2.5 to 4 × FLT)** | **Very High** |
| No-Load Speed | Safe (within limits) | **Dangerously High** | **Dangerously High** |
| Typical Applications | Lathes, fans, pumps | Traction, cranes | Traction, cranes |
| Voltage Build-up Condition | Residual magnetism (same polarity) | Residual magnetism (same polarity) | Residual magnetism (same polarity) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed Regulation** is defined as: $\dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$. Lower percentage = better regulation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Automation:** Shunt motors are the workhorse of conveyor belts and machine tools due to predictable speed.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Modern EVs often use series-excited or separately-excited configurations for high starting torque, with electronic controllers replacing mechanical load coupling.
- **Indian Railways:** The **WAP-5 and WAG-9 locomotives** of Indian Railways use three-phase induction motors but historically, DC series motors powered all EMUs (Electric Multiple Units).
- **Cranes & Hoists:** Series motors handle intermittent, high-torque lifting operations where load may be removed suddenly (e.g., during hook release).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, board-exam-ready derivations. Every algebraic transition is explicitly justified.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Torque Equation ($T = k\phi I_a$)

**Starting Point — Lorentz Force on a Single Conductor:**

A current-carrying conductor of length $l$ metres, carrying current $I$ amperes, placed in a magnetic field of flux density $B$ tesla, experiences a force:

$$F_{single} = B \cdot I \cdot l \quad \text{(Newtons)}$$

**Step 1: Force on All Conductors**

The total armature has $Z$ total conductors arranged in $A$ parallel paths. The number of conductors per parallel path is $Z/A$. The current per conductor is $I_a / A$.

Total tangential force on all conductors:

$$F_{total} = B \cdot l \cdot \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right) = \frac{B \cdot l \cdot Z \cdot I_a}{A}$$

**Step 2: Relate $B$ to Flux $\phi$**

The flux density $B$ is the average flux per unit area of the pole face:

$$B = \frac{\phi}{a} \quad \text{where } a = \text{area per pole}$$

So $B \cdot l = \dfrac{\phi \cdot l}{a}$. However, in DC machine theory, the conductor length and pole area are bundled into a geometric constant. We define the average flux density and armature radius such that:

$$B \cdot l \cdot \frac{1}{a} = \frac{\phi}{(\text{effective area})} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Force} = \phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot (\text{geometric factor})$$

**Step 3: Convert Force to Torque**

Torque $T$ = Force $\times$ radius ($r$) of the armature:

$$T = F_{total} \times r = \frac{\phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot r}{A}$$

**Step 4: Define the Machine Constant**

Since $P$ is the number of poles and the armature radius $r$ is fixed for a given machine, we group all geometric constants into:

$$k = \frac{P \cdot Z}{2\pi A}$$

Therefore, the final torque equation is:

$$\boxed{T = k \phi I_a \quad \text{N·m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant $k$ appears in *both* the EMF and torque equations with subtle differences. In the EMF equation, $k' = \dfrac{PZ}{60A}$ (since $N$ is in rpm). In the torque equation, $k = \dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}$ (SI radians). The ratio $k'/k = 2\pi / 60$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Speed Equation for Shunt Motor

**Step 1:** Start with the back EMF equation:

$$E_b = k' \phi N$$

**Step 2:** Apply KVL across the armature circuit (shunt field is parallel, so we only consider armature loop):

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate $E_b$:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions for $E_b$:

$$k' \phi N = V - I_a R_a$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $N$:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k' \phi}$$

**Step 6:** Observe that for shunt motor, $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ is constant, hence $\phi$ is constant:

$$N = \frac{V}{k' \phi} - \left(\frac{R_a}{k' \phi}\right) I_a$$

This is the equation of a straight line: $N = N_0 - m \cdot I_a$, where:
- **No-load speed:** $N_0 = V / k'\phi$ (when $I_a \approx 0$)
- **Slope:** $m = R_a / k'\phi$ (negative, very small since $R_a$ is small)

**Interpretation:** Speed drops *linearly* but *very slightly* with armature current. This is the hallmark of a shunt motor's good regulation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Speed Equation for Series Motor

**Step 1:** KVL across the series circuit (armature + series field both carry $I_a$):

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a + I_a R_{se} = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

**Step 2:** Equate to back EMF:

$$E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $E_b = k'\phi N$:

$$k'\phi N = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $N$:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k' \phi}$$

**Step 5 (Unsaturated region):** $\phi = c \cdot I_a$:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k' c I_a} = \frac{V}{k' c I_a} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k' c}$$

**Interpretation:** As $I_a \to 0$, the first term $\to \infty$, hence $N \to \infty$ (the dangerous no-load condition). At heavy loads, $I_a$ is large, so the first term becomes small and $N$ drops sharply.

**Step 6 (Saturated region):** $\phi = \phi_{sat}$ (constant):

$$N = \frac{V}{k' \phi_{sat}} - \left(\frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k' \phi_{sat}}\right) I_a$$

This is again a straight line equation, but the slope is much steeper than for a shunt motor due to the larger $(R_a + R_{se})$ term.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the $N$ vs $T$ Relationship for Series Motor (Unsaturated)

**Step 1:** Unsaturated torque: $T = k c I_a^2$, hence:

$$I_a^2 = \frac{T}{k c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_a = \sqrt{\frac{T}{k c}}$$

**Step 2:** Unsaturated speed: $N = \dfrac{V}{k' c I_a} - \dfrac{R_a + R_{se}}{k' c}$

**Step 3:** Substitute $I_a = \sqrt{T / kc}$:

$$N = \frac{V}{k' c \sqrt{T / kc}} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k' c}$$

$$N = \frac{V}{k' c} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{kc}{T}} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k' c}$$

$$N = \frac{V}{k'} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{c \cdot T / k}} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k' c}$$

$$N = \frac{V}{k'} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{cT/k}} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k' c}$$

$$\boxed{N \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}}$$

**Physical Insight:** The speed is inversely proportional to the square root of torque, giving a hyperbolic curve. This is precisely why series motors are **inherently self-regulating** for traction — when the load increases (e.g., a train climbing a gradient), torque rises and speed naturally drops, protecting the motor from mechanical overload.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Comparison at Different Loads

The following matrix illustrates how key variables respond to a load increase from no-load to full-load (FL). Assume a 5 HP, 220 V, 1500 rpm shunt motor and a similar series motor.

| Operating Point | Shunt Motor | Series Motor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| No-load $I_a$ | $\approx 2$ A (small, due to $V/R_a$ only limited by armature reaction) | $\approx 5$ A (minimum due to residual flux) |
| No-load $N$ | $\approx 1500$ rpm (slightly higher than rated) | $\approx 1500$ rpm (with minimum load) |
| Full-load $I_a$ | $\approx 20$ A | $\approx 20$ A |
| Full-load $N$ | $\approx 1450$ rpm (50 rpm drop) | $\approx 1100$ rpm (400 rpm drop) |
| Starting $T$ | $\approx 1.2 \times T_{FL}$ | $\approx 3.0 \times T_{FL}$ |
| Speed Regulation | $\approx 3.3\%$ (good) | $\approx 36\%$ (poor) |
| No-load Risk | Safe | **Dangerous** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For numerical KTU problems**, the typical data given includes $V$, $R_a$, $R_{se}$ (for series), $N_{rated}$, and rated current. The student must compute $\phi$ from no-load condition, then use the torque/speed equations to find $T$ and $N$ at the new load.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Shunt Motor Circuit Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeV["DC Supply V"]:::source
    nodeA["Armature Ra"]:::load
    nodeSh["Shunt Field Rsh"]:::field
    nodeSw["Switch"]:::control
    nodeL["Load Shaft T out"]:::output

    nodeV --> nodeSw
    nodeSw --> nodeA
    nodeSw --> nodeSh
    nodeA --> nodeL
    nodeSh --> nodeL

    classDef source fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef field fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#ffadad,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The armature ($R_a$) and shunt field ($R_{sh}$) are connected in **parallel** across the supply $V$. This is the defining topology of a shunt motor — both branches see the *full supply voltage*, ensuring a nearly constant flux.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Series Motor Circuit Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeV["DC Supply V"]:::source
    nodeSe["Series Field Rse"]:::field
    nodeA["Armature Ra"]:::load
    nodeSw["Switch"]:::control
    nodeL["Load Shaft T out"]:::output

    nodeV --> nodeSw
    nodeSw --> nodeSe
    nodeSe --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeL

    classDef source fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef field fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#ffadad,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The series field ($R_{se}$) and armature ($R_a$) are connected in **series**. The same current $I_a$ flows through both, making flux directly dependent on armature current. This is the defining topology of a series motor.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow — How Characteristics Are Generated

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1["Applied Voltage V"]:::input --> nodeP2["Armature Current Ia"]
    nodeP3["Field Flux phi"]:::variable --> nodeP5["Torque T = k phi Ia"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP5
    nodeP4["Back EMF Eb = V - Ia Ra + Rse"]:::variable --> nodeP6["Speed N = Eb / k phi"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP6
    nodeP2 --> nodeP6
    nodeP5 --> nodeP7["Plot T vs Ia Curve"]:::output
    nodeP2 --> nodeP7
    nodeP6 --> nodeP8["Plot N vs Ia Curve"]:::output
    nodeP2 --> nodeP8
    nodeP6 --> nodeP9["Plot N vs T Curve"]:::output
    nodeP5 --> nodeP9

    classDef input fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef variable fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#ffadad,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 ASCII Characteristic Curves (For Notebook Reproduction)

### Shunt Motor Characteristics

```
   T                                       N
   |       T vs Ia                          |         N vs Ia
   |      /                                 |       _.--.
   |     /                                  |    _.-`
   |    /                                   |  _-`
   |   /                                    |_-`
   |  /                                     |________________ Ia
   | /                                      |
   |/________________ Ia                    |       N vs T
                                            |       _.-`
   N vs Ia (Linear slight droop)            |    _-`
                                            |  _-`
   N0 _.-.                                  |_-`
       `-._.________________________________|________________ T
           `-._
                    Ia
```

### Series Motor Characteristics

```
   T                                          N
   |        T vs Ia                            |        N vs Ia
   |       / (parabolic unsat)                 |     .-
   |      /                       ________     |   .-
   |     /                      /              | .-
   |    /                     /                |-
   |   /                    /                  |________________ Ia
   |  /                  /  (linear sat)
   | /________________/                       |        N vs T
   |_________________________________ Ia      |
                                              |       .-
   N vs Ia (Hyperbolic, inverse)              |    .-
                                              |   -
   Nmax                                     .-|  -  (N prop to 1/sqrt T)
   |\                                       .-| -
   |  \                                  .----|
   |    \_.                          .-------|
   |       \_..________________.------|_____|________________ Ia
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exam sheets:** Students are expected to draw **neatly labeled** graphs with axes ($T$, $N$, $I_a$), origin marking, saturation curve indication, and **shaded regions** showing the unsaturated vs saturated zones for the series motor.

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Motor Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1["Application Requires Constant Speed?"]:::decision
    nodeQ2["Application Requires High Starting Torque?"]:::decision
    nodeR1["Choose SHUNT MOTOR"]:::recommend
    nodeR2["Choose SERIES MOTOR"]:::recommend
    nodeR3["Choose COMPOUND MOTOR"]:::recommend

    nodeQ1 -->|YES| nodeR1
    nodeQ1 -->|NO| nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -->|YES| nodeR2
    nodeQ2 -->|NO| nodeR3

    classDef decision fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef recommend fill:#ffadad,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Design Heuristic:** This flowchart is a standard interview question in campus placements. Memorize it — it directly follows from the characteristics we just studied.
