# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define the term "speed regulation" of a DC motor. Comment on the speed regulation of a DC shunt motor with respect to its no-load and full-load speed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Speed regulation** is defined as the change in motor speed from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of full-load speed:

$$\text{Speed Regulation (\%)} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$$

For a **DC shunt motor**, since the field flux $\phi$ is practically constant (as $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ remains constant), the speed equation $N = (V - I_a R_a) / k\phi$ shows that speed drops only slightly due to the small $I_a R_a$ drop. Typical regulation is **3% to 8%**, which is considered **good regulation**. This is why shunt motors are preferred for constant-speed applications. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Why is a DC series motor never started without a mechanical load? Justify using its characteristic curves.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A DC series motor is never started without load because its speed at no-load tends to infinity (dangerously high), which can mechanically destroy the machine. This is evident from:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k\phi}$$

At no-load, $I_a$ is very small, hence $\phi \propto I_a$ is also very small (unsaturated region). With $\phi \to 0$ in the denominator, $N \to \infty$. Additionally, the $N$ vs $T$ characteristic follows $N \propto 1/\sqrt{T}$, so at $T \to 0$ (no-load), $N$ becomes unbounded. Hence a series motor must always have a minimum mechanical load coupled to its shaft during starting. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU ESE Module 1 Choice — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the three fundamental characteristics (T vs Ia, N vs Ia, N vs T) of a DC **shunt motor**. Sketch the curves and explain why the shunt motor is called a "constant speed motor." **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.3\ \Omega$ and shunt field resistance $R_{sh} = 110\ \Omega$. At no-load, the motor draws an armature current of 5 A and runs at 1500 rpm. At full-load, the armature current is 50 A. Assuming the flux is weakened by 4% due to armature reaction, compute:
- (i) The full-load speed
- (ii) The torque developed at full-load
- (iii) The speed regulation **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation and Curves (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** The shunt field winding is connected across the constant supply voltage $V$, so:

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \text{constant}$$

Hence the field flux $\phi$ is approximately constant (assuming negligible armature reaction).

**Step 2: Torque equation:**

$$T = k\phi I_a$$

Since $\phi$ = constant, **$T$ vs $I_a$ is a straight line** through the origin with slope $k\phi$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3: Speed equation:**

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k\phi} = \frac{V}{k\phi} - \left(\frac{R_a}{k\phi}\right) I_a$$

This is the equation of a straight line with negative slope $R_a/(k\phi)$. **$N$ vs $I_a$ is a slightly drooping straight line.** **(1 mark)**

**Step 4: $N$ vs $T$ relationship:**

Since $T = k\phi I_a \Rightarrow I_a = T/(k\phi)$, substitute into speed equation:

$$N = \frac{V}{k\phi} - \frac{R_a}{(k\phi)^2} T$$

This is again a straight line with very small negative slope $R_a/(k\phi)^2$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5: Sketch** — Draw three graphs on a single sheet with proper labels:
- Y-axis (left): Torque $T$
- Y-axis (right): Speed $N$
- X-axis: Armature current $I_a$ (for two graphs) and Torque $T$ (for the third)
- Mark no-load point, full-load point, and indicate the slight droop.

**Step 6: Why "constant speed motor"?** Since the drop in speed from no-load to full-load is only 3-8% of full-load speed, the speed is nearly constant across the operating range. This property makes the shunt motor ideal for applications like lathes, fans, and centrifugal pumps. **(1 mark for sketch, 3 marks for derivation steps, 1 mark for explanation)**

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $V = 220$ V
- $R_a = 0.3\ \Omega$
- $R_{sh} = 110\ \Omega$
- No-load: $I_{a,NL} = 5$ A, $N_{NL} = 1500$ rpm
- Full-load: $I_{a,FL} = 50$ A
- Flux weakened by 4% at full-load: $\phi_{FL} = 0.96 \phi_{NL}$

**Step 1: Compute No-load back EMF (using KVL):**

$$E_{b,NL} = V - I_{a,NL} R_a = 220 - (5)(0.3) = 220 - 1.5 = 218.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Use back EMF equation to compute $\phi_{NL}$:**

$$E_{b,NL} = k' \phi_{NL} N_{NL}$$

$$218.5 = k' \phi_{NL} (1500)$$

$$k' \phi_{NL} = \frac{218.5}{1500} = 0.14567 \text{ V/rpm}$$

**Step 3: Compute Full-load back EMF:**

$$E_{b,FL} = V - I_{a,FL} R_a = 220 - (50)(0.3) = 220 - 15 = 205 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Compute Full-load flux:**

$$\phi_{FL} = 0.96 \phi_{NL}$$

$$k' \phi_{FL} = 0.96 \times 0.14567 = 0.13984 \text{ V/rpm}$$

**Step 5: Compute Full-load speed:**

$$N_{FL} = \frac{E_{b,FL}}{k' \phi_{FL}} = \frac{205}{0.13984} = 1465.96 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N_{FL} \approx 1466 \text{ rpm}}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 6: Compute Full-load torque:**

$$T_{FL} = k \phi_{FL} I_{a,FL}$$

To find $k\phi$, we use the no-load condition (where torque is minimum but non-zero). For no-load:

$$T_{NL} = k \phi_{NL} I_{a,NL}$$

The constant ratio: 
$$\frac{k\phi_{FL}}{k\phi_{NL}} = 0.96$$

Now, using the no-load torque to find $k\phi$:

We can directly find $T_{FL}$ by noting that:
$$\frac{T_{FL}}{T_{NL}} = \frac{k\phi_{FL} \cdot I_{a,FL}}{k\phi_{NL} \cdot I_{a,NL}} = \frac{0.96 \times 50}{1 \times 5} = 9.6$$

$$T_{NL} = \frac{E_{b,NL} \cdot I_{a,NL}}{2\pi N_{NL} / 60} = \frac{218.5 \times 5}{2\pi \times 1500/60} = \frac{1092.5}{157.08} = 6.955 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T_{FL} = 9.6 \times 6.955 = 66.77 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{FL} \approx 66.77 \text{ N·m}}$$ **(3 marks)**

**Step 7: Compute Speed Regulation:**

$$\text{Speed Regulation} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{1500 - 1466}{1466} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{Speed Regulation} \approx 2.32\%}$$ **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students mistakenly compute speed regulation as $(N_{NL} - N_{FL}) / N_{NL} \times 100$. The correct denominator is **$N_{FL}$** (full-load speed), not $N_{NL}$. This is a 1-mark deduction error if made.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU ESE Module 1 Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the three characteristics (T vs Ia, N vs Ia, N vs T) of a DC **series motor**, distinguishing clearly between the unsaturated and saturated regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V DC series motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$ and series field resistance $R_{se} = 0.3\ \Omega$. At a load current of 20 A, the motor runs at 800 rpm. If the torque demand doubles, calculate the new armature current and new speed. Assume the magnetic circuit is unsaturated and $\phi \propto I_a$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation and Curves (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Field Current Relation:**

In a series motor, the field winding carries the full armature current:

$$I_{se} = I_a$$

**Step 2: Unsaturated Region ($\phi \propto I_a$):**

Assuming $\phi = c I_a$ (where $c$ is constant), substitute into the torque equation:

$$T = k \phi I_a = k c I_a^2$$

$$\boxed{T \propto I_a^2 \quad \text{(Parabolic)}}$$ **(1 mark)**

For speed:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k\phi} = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{kc I_a} = \frac{V}{kc I_a} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{kc}$$

$$\boxed{N \propto \frac{1}{I_a} \quad \text{(Inverse / Hyperbolic)}}$$ **(1 mark)**

For $N$ vs $T$: Since $T \propto I_a^2 \Rightarrow I_a \propto \sqrt{T}$:

$$N \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 3: Saturated Region ($\phi$ = constant):**

Once saturation sets in, $\phi = \phi_{sat}$ becomes constant:

$$T = k \phi_{sat} I_a \propto I_a \quad \text{(Linear)}$$

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \phi_{sat}} = \frac{V}{k \phi_{sat}} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k \phi_{sat}} I_a$$

**$N$ vs $I_a$ becomes a straight line with steep negative slope.** **(1 mark)**

**Step 4: Sketch** — Draw curves showing:
- $T$ vs $I_a$: Parabolic initially, transitioning to linear after the knee-point of saturation.
- $N$ vs $I_a$: Hyperbolic decay in unsaturated region, becoming linear (steep negative slope) in saturated region.
- $N$ vs $T$: Hyperbolic curve ($N \propto 1/\sqrt{T}$) throughout. **(2 marks for sketch)**

**Step 5: Physical Interpretation:**

At no-load, $I_a \to 0$, hence $\phi \to 0$, causing $N \to \infty$. This is the **dangerous overspeed condition**. Hence series motors must always be mechanically loaded before starting. **(1 mark for explanation)**

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $V = 220$ V
- $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$
- $R_{se} = 0.3\ \Omega$
- Initial load: $I_{a1} = 20$ A, $N_1 = 800$ rpm
- Torque doubles: $T_2 = 2 T_1$
- Unsaturated magnetic circuit, so $T \propto I_a^2$

**Step 1: Establish the proportionality:**

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{I_{a2}}{I_{a1}}\right)^2$$

$$2 = \left(\frac{I_{a2}}{20}\right)^2$$

$$\frac{I_{a2}}{20} = \sqrt{2} = 1.4142$$

$$I_{a2} = 20 \times 1.4142 = 28.284 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{a2} \approx 28.28 \text{ A}}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 2: Compute initial back EMF:**

$$E_{b1} = V - I_{a1}(R_a + R_{se}) = 220 - 20 \times (0.5 + 0.3) = 220 - 20 \times 0.8 = 220 - 16 = 204 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Compute initial $k'c$ (machine constant times flux coefficient):**

Using $E_{b1} = k' c I_{a1} N_1$:

$$204 = k' c \times 20 \times 800 = k' c \times 16000$$

$$k' c = \frac{204}{16000} = 0.01275 \text{ V·A/(rpm·A)}$$

**Step 4: Compute final back EMF:**

$$E_{b2} = V - I_{a2}(R_a + R_{se}) = 220 - 28.284 \times 0.8 = 220 - 22.627 = 197.37 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Compute final speed using $E_{b2} = k' c I_{a2} N_2$:**

$$197.37 = 0.01275 \times 28.284 \times N_2$$

$$197.37 = 0.3606 \times N_2$$

$$N_2 = \frac{197.37}{0.3606} = 547.4 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N_2 \approx 547.4 \text{ rpm}}$$ **(3 marks)**

**Step 6: Verify the inverse relationship:**

For unsaturated series motor: $N \propto 1/I_a$

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{I_{a1}}{I_{a2}} = \frac{20}{28.284} = 0.7071$$

$$N_2 = 800 \times 0.7071 = 565.7 \text{ rpm}$$

The slight discrepancy (~3%) is due to the $I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ voltage drop term being neglected in the simple proportionality. The exact value **547.4 rpm** uses the full equation. **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write $N \propto 1/I_a$ and use it directly without considering the $I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ voltage drop. The **correct approach** is to use $N = (V - I_a R_{total}) / k\phi$ with the full voltage equation. Partial mark (½) is awarded if only the proportional method is used.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 1 — DC Motor Characteristics)**

- [x] **Three fundamental characteristics:** $T$-$I_a$, $N$-$I_a$, $N$-$T$ — must be plotted for both motor types.
- [x] **Shunt motor:** Field is parallel to armature; flux $\phi \approx$ constant; **good speed regulation (3-8%)**; **moderate starting torque**.
- [x] **Series motor:** Field is in series with armature; $\phi \propto I_a$ (unsaturated); **dangerous at no-load** ($N \to \infty$); **very high starting torque (2.5 to 4 × FLT)**.
- [x] **Master equation:** $T = k\phi I_a$ — applies universally to all DC motors.
- [x] **KVL equation (Shunt):** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$.
- [x] **KVL equation (Series):** $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$.
- [x] **Speed equation:** $N = (V - I_a R_{total}) / k\phi$ — same form, different $R_{total}$ and $\phi$ dependency.
- [x] **Saturation knee-point:** Above this, $\phi$ stops increasing linearly; curves transition to linear behavior in series motor.
- [x] **Compensation for armature reaction:** Use **interpoles** or **compensating windings** to mitigate flux weakening in shunt motors during numerical problems.
- [x] **Application mapping (Direct exam question):**
  - **Constant speed** → Shunt motor
  - **High starting torque** → Series motor
  - **Both properties moderately** → Cumulative compound motor
- [x] **Speed Regulation formula:** $(N_{NL} - N_{FL}) / N_{FL} \times 100$ — denominator is **$N_{FL}$**, not $N_{NL}$.
- [x] **No-load safety rule:** Series motors are **never started without load**; shunt motors are inherently safe.
- [x] **Why armature reaction matters:** At full-load, $I_a$ is high, the armature MMF distorts the main field, weakening $\phi$ by 3-5%. This causes speed to rise (shunt) or fall less (series) than predicted by simple theory. **Always account for it when given in numericals.**
- [x] **Common KTU numerical trap:** A problem may state "armature reaction weakens the flux by x%." The student must use $\phi_{FL} = (1 - x/100) \cdot \phi_{NL}$ in the full-load back EMF and torque equations.
- [x] **Practical thumb rule:** For a shunt motor, the no-load current is 5-10% of full-load current. For a series motor, the no-load current is defined by the residual flux and mechanical losses.
- [x] **Memory aid for exam:** "Shunt = Stable Speed, Series = Strong Start."

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Always carry forward **at least 5 significant figures** in intermediate calculations to avoid round-off errors in the final answer. Marks are deducted for sloppy numerical hygiene, especially in 14-mark problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Starting of DC Motors (Concepts Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Starting of DC Motors (Concepts Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In DC motors, the process of bringing the armature from rest to its normal operating speed while safely limiting the inrush current drawn from the supply is called **Starting of DC Motors**. Because the armature is initially at rest, there is no counter (back) EMF, and the only opposition to current is the very low armature resistance $R_a$. This causes a dangerously high starting current that must be regulated using an external starting resistor network called a **DC Motor Starter**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Students must be able to *explain the necessity of starters*, *describe the construction and working of 3-point and 4-point starters*, and *understand soft-starting concepts* in DC shunt, series, and compound motors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Tap" Intuition

Imagine you are trying to push a heavy, rusted iron gate open by hand.

- If you **push it suddenly**, the gate barely moves, your full effort is absorbed as a violent jerk, and you may injure yourself.
- If you **push it slowly and gradually**, momentum builds, friction is overcome progressively, and the gate swings open smoothly.

A DC motor at standstill behaves exactly like the rusted gate. The supply voltage $V$ applied suddenly causes a huge "jerk" of current. The starter acts like your *gradual push* — it introduces a controlled external resistance in series with the armature that is **cut out step-by-step** as the motor picks up speed and develops its own back EMF to limit the current naturally.

## 1.3 Why Is Starting Special in DC Motors?

At the moment of switching ON (i.e., at $N = 0$):

- The **back EMF** $E_b = 0$ (because $E_b \propto N$).
- The only impedance to current flow is the tiny armature resistance $R_a$ (typically **0.05 Ω to 1 Ω**).
- The current drawn, called the **starting current** $I_{st}$, becomes enormous — often **10 to 20 times** the full-load current.

$$I_{st} = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{V}{R_a} \quad \text{at } N=0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Worth Remembering:**
> - Typical DC shunt motor full-load current ($I_{fl}$): **5 A to 50 A**
> - Starting current without starter: **$I_{st} \approx 10 \times I_{fl}$ to $20 \times I_{fl}$**
> - Safe permissible starting current: **$1.5 \times I_{fl}$ to $2 \times I_{fl}$**
> - Hence, external starting resistance is **mandatory** for all DC motors above ~0.5 kW.

## 1.4 The Governing Voltage Equation

For any DC motor, the fundamental voltage equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law applied to the armature loop) is:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Where:
- $V$ = Supply voltage applied to armature terminals (**volts, V**)
- $E_b$ = Back EMF (counter EMF) generated by the rotating armature (**volts, V**)
- $I_a$ = Armature current drawn from supply (**amperes, A**)
- $R_a$ = Armature circuit resistance (**ohms, Ω**)

At the **instant of starting** ($N = 0$ implies $E_b = 0$), this simplifies dramatically to:

$$V = I_{st} \cdot R_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{st} = \frac{V}{R_a}$$

This is the equation that explains *why* we need a starter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Starting Current

Let us break down the *why* and *how* of the starting problem:

1. **At rest, speed $N = 0$**: The motor's rotor is stationary. Faraday's law tells us that EMF is generated only when flux cuts conductors — since the conductors are not moving relative to the field, $E_b = 0$.
2. **Full voltage $V$ appears across $R_a$ alone**: With $E_b = 0$, the entire supply voltage is dropped across the tiny armature resistance.
3. **Resulting current is massive**: For a 220 V DC motor with $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, the current is $220 / 0.5 = 440$ A — clearly destructive.
4. **Solution: Insert an external resistor**: Add a starting resistance $R_{se}$ in series with the armature, so the *effective* circuit resistance increases.
5. **Cut out $R_{se}$ progressively**: As the motor speeds up, $E_b$ rises (since $E_b \propto \Phi N$). The starter is operated to gradually cut out $R_{se}$, keeping $I_a$ within safe limits (typically 1.5 to 2 times rated).
6. **At full speed, all $R_{se}$ is removed**: The motor runs on its natural $E_b$ and $R_a$ with the starter now acting merely as a conducting link (with negligible contact resistance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Core Point:**
> The starter is *NOT* a permanent series resistor. It is a **temporarily inserted, gradually removable** resistance whose job is finished the moment the motor reaches rated speed.

## 2.2 Role of Back EMF — The "Natural Current Limiter"

Back EMF $E_b$ is the *natural feedback mechanism* of a DC motor that limits current automatically. It is given by:

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$

Where:
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (**Weber, Wb**)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of armature (**RPM**)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in armature ($A = 2$ for wave, $A = P$ for lap wound)

> As $N$ increases, $E_b$ increases, and from $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, the current $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$ automatically falls. **This is the natural self-regulation that starters exploit.**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature voltage equation (running condition) | V, V, A, Ω |
| 2 | $I_{st} = V / R_a$ | Starting current with no starter (at $N=0$, $E_b=0$) | A |
| 3 | $I_{st,\text{with Rse}} = V / (R_a + R_{se})$ | Starting current with external resistance inserted | A |
| 4 | $E_b = (\Phi Z N P) / (60 A)$ | EMF equation of a DC machine | V (Φ in Wb, N in RPM) |
| 5 | $N \propto (V - I_a R_a) / \Phi$ | Speed equation (derived from EMF eq.) | RPM |
| 6 | Safe $I_{st} \approx 1.5 \, I_{fl}$ to $2 \, I_{fl}$ | Practical safe starting current limit | A |
| 7 | $R_{se,\text{initial}} = (V / I_{st,\text{safe}}) - R_a$ | Required starting resistance calculation | Ω |

> **Engineering Utility:** This entire framework is the foundation of *motor control electronics* — modern DC drives use **PWM choppers** and **IGBT-based soft starters** that electronically vary the effective $R_{se}$ (or rather, vary $V$) to perform exactly the same job as the classical 3-point/4-point starters, but in a contactless, programmable way.

## 2.4 Necessity Summary (For KTU Theory Answers)

A DC motor needs a starter because:

1. **High starting current** would damage the commutator and brushes due to heavy sparking.
2. **Excessive $I^2R$ heating** in the armature winding would burn the insulation.
3. **Very high starting torque** (especially in series motors) produces a sudden mechanical shock, straining the shaft, coupling, and load.
4. **Voltage dip in the supply line** caused by huge $I_a$ affects other connected loads (poor power quality).
5. **Protection of the supply system** (fuses/breakers) from nuisance tripping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exception — Small DC Motors (below ~0.5 kW / fractional kW):**
> For very small motors, the armature resistance itself is relatively high, and the starting current, although several times rated, is still small in absolute value. Such motors can be started **direct-on-line (DOL)** or via a simple series resistance — no elaborate starter is required.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Armature Resistance Alone Cannot Limit Starting Current

**Given:**
- Supply voltage $V = 220$ V (typical for a small DC shunt motor)
- Armature resistance $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$
- Full-load current $I_{fl} = 40$ A

**Step 1: Write the armature voltage equation at the instant of starting.**

At $t = 0^+$ (just after switching ON), the armature speed $N = 0$, so the back EMF $E_b = 0$. Substituting in the general equation $V = E_b + I_a R_a$:

$$V = 0 + I_{st} \cdot R_a$$

**Step 2: Solve for $I_{st}$.**

$$I_{st} = \frac{V}{R_a}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

$$I_{st} = \frac{220 \, \text{V}}{0.5 \, \Omega} = 440 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 4: Compare with full-load current.**

$$\text{Multiple of } I_{fl} = \frac{440}{40} = 11 \text{ times}$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result.**

The starting current is **11 times** the rated current. This is unacceptable because:
- It causes commutator flashover and brush damage.
- The $I^2R$ loss becomes $11^2 = 121$ times the normal armature copper loss, severely overheating the winding.
- The starting torque (which is proportional to $I_a$) becomes ~11 times the rated torque — this would shear the shaft or damage the mechanical load.

> **Conclusion:** A starter is mandatory to keep $I_{st}$ within **1.5 to 2 times** $I_{fl}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Starting Resistance for Safe Starting

**Goal:** Find the value of external starting resistance $R_{se}$ such that the starting current equals the safe limit, say $I_{st,\text{safe}} = 1.5 \, I_{fl}$.

**Step 1: Write the modified armature circuit equation at starting (with $R_{se}$ inserted).**

$$V = I_{st,\text{safe}} \cdot (R_a + R_{se})$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to isolate $R_{se}$.**

$$R_{se} = \frac{V}{I_{st,\text{safe}}} - R_a$$

**Step 3: Substitute the same numerical values.**

$$I_{st,\text{safe}} = 1.5 \times 40 = 60 \, \text{A}$$

$$R_{se} = \frac{220}{60} - 0.5$$

$$R_{se} = 3.667 - 0.5 = 3.167 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4: Verify by computing the starting current with this $R_{se}$.**

$$I_{st} = \frac{V}{R_a + R_{se}} = \frac{220}{0.5 + 3.167} = \frac{220}{3.667} \approx 60 \, \text{A} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** Inserting a starting resistance of about **3.17 Ω** in series with the armature limits the inrush current to a safe 60 A — exactly 1.5 times the rated full-load current.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Speed Build-Up with Starter Stepping

As the starter handle is moved from one stud to the next, $R_{se}$ is reduced in discrete steps. Let us track one such step.

**Step 1: At a particular starter position, total series resistance** $= R_a + R_{se,k}$ **where $R_{se,k}$ is the resistance at step $k$.**

The current in the armature at this instant is:

$$I_{a,k} = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a + R_{se,k}}$$

**Step 2: The torque produced is** $T_k = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_{a,k}$.

If $T_k > T_{load}$, the motor accelerates. The acceleration causes $N$ to rise, which makes $E_b$ rise.

**Step 3: As $E_b$ rises, $I_{a,k}$ falls. When $I_{a,k}$ drops to a lower safe threshold** (say $1.0 \times I_{fl}$), the operator moves to the next stud, removing another chunk of $R_{se}$.

**Step 4: The acceleration repeats until the final stud is reached, where $R_{se} = 0$ and the motor runs on its natural characteristic.**

This stepping process is what gives the classical 3-point/4-point starter its name — the resistance is cut out in **steps** as the motor picks up speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Soft-Starters (Concept Only — as per KTU 2024 Module 1):**
> In modern DC drives, instead of mechanical studs, the resistance is replaced by a **power-electronic chopper** (using IGBT/MOSFET) that rapidly switches the applied voltage on and off. By varying the *duty cycle* (D), the effective average voltage seen by the armature is $V_{avg} = D \cdot V$. This achieves smooth, stepless, programmable starting without any moving contacts.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Starting Current Build-Up & Cut-Out Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Switch ON Supply V] --> B[Armature at rest, N = 0]
    B --> C[Back EMF Eb = 0]
    C --> D[Current Ia = V divided by Ra]
    D --> E{Is Ia within Safe Limit 1.5 to 2 times IFL?}
    E -- No --> F[Insert External Starting Resistance Rse]
    F --> G[Move Starter Handle to Stud 1]
    G --> H[Motor Accelerates, N increases]
    H --> I[Back EMF Eb rises naturally]
    I --> J[Current Ia decreases automatically]
    J --> K{Ia dropped to lower threshold?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Move to Next Stud, Cut out part of Rse]
    L --> H
    K -- No --> H
    E -- Yes --> M[Direct DOL starting, only for fractional kW motors]
    H --> N[Final Stud Reached, Rse = 0]
    N --> O[Motor runs on natural characteristic, Eb balances V minus Ia Ra]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison of Starting Approaches

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Classical_Approach
        A1[DC Supply V] --> A2[Manual Stud Starter 3 point or 4 point]
        A2 --> A3[Armature of DC Motor]
        A3 --> A4[External Rse cut out in steps]
    end
    subgraph Modern_Approach
        B1[DC Supply V] --> B2[PWM Chopper or IGBT Drive]
        B2 --> B3[Variable Effective Voltage Vavg]
        B3 --> A3
    end
    A4 --> C[Motor reaches rated speed]
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Smooth, Stepless, Programmable Starting]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exam:**
> In the KTU 2024 board exam, students may be asked to *draw or explain* the starting arrangement. The expected answer should include: (1) the supply, (2) the starter symbol, (3) the armature, (4) the field winding connection (separately excited or shunt), and (5) the protective devices like No-Volt Coil (NV) and Overload Release (OL).

## 4.3 Conceptual Architecture of a DC Starter (Functional Blocks)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Positive Supply Terminal] --> Q[Starting Resistor Bank Rse with studs 1 to N]
    Q --> R[Armature Terminal A]
    S[Negative Supply Terminal] --> T[Field Winding F]
    T --> U[Armature Terminal A2]
    R --> V[Commutator and Brush Assembly]
    U --> V
    V --> W[Rotating Armature producing back EMF Eb]
    W --> X[Mechanical Shaft Output]
    Y[No Volt Coil NV in parallel with field] --> Z[Hold Starter Handle in ON position magnetically]
    AA[Overload Coil OL in series with armature] --> AB[Trip Starter if current exceeds safe limit]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Q: Why is a starter necessary for a DC motor? List any three reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A starter is necessary for a DC motor because, at the instant of starting, the armature is at rest and hence the back EMF $E_b = 0$. The entire supply voltage is impressed across the very small armature resistance $R_a$, causing a starting current $I_{st} = V/R_a$ that is **10 to 20 times** the rated full-load current. The three main reasons are:
1. **To limit the starting current** to a safe value (1.5 to 2 times $I_{fl}$), preventing damage to the armature winding and commutator brushes.
2. **To protect the motor from excessive starting torque** which would otherwise cause mechanical shock to the shaft, coupling, and driven load.
3. **To prevent voltage dip in the supply line** caused by the huge inrush current, which would affect other loads connected to the same supply.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected]`
**Q: What is the function of the No-Volt Coil (NV Coil) in a DC motor starter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The No-Volt Coil (NV Coil) is an electromagnetic hold-on coil connected across the supply in a 3-point starter. Its functions are:
1. **To hold the starter handle in the ON (running) position** as long as the supply voltage is healthy. The magnetic pull of the NV coil overcomes the spring tension that would otherwise return the handle to the OFF position.
2. **To automatically disconnect the motor from the supply** in the event of a supply failure or voltage dip. When voltage falls, the NV coil loses magnetism, the spring pulls the handle back, and the motor is isolated — preventing a dangerous restart when supply is suddenly restored.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Note:** Part B questions in Module 1 carry 14 marks with internal choice. The structure is typically sub-part (a) for 7 marks and sub-part (b) for 7 marks. Both sub-parts may be long-answer type with derivations, explanations, or numerical problems.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of a **3-point starter** used for a DC shunt motor. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of **0.3 Ω** and a field resistance of **110 Ω**. On full load, the motor draws an armature current of **50 A**. Calculate: *(i) the starting current without a starter, (ii) the value of starting resistance required to limit the starting current to **1.5 times** the full-load armature current, and (iii) the back EMF at full load. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Solution to (a) — 3-Point Starter:**

A 3-point starter is the most commonly used manual starter for DC shunt motors. Its name comes from the fact that it has **three external terminals**:
- **L** (Line terminal) — connected to the positive of the DC supply.
- **A** (Armature terminal) — connected to the armature winding.
- **F** (Field terminal) — connected to the shunt field winding.

**Constructional features:**
1. **A graded starting resistor $R_{se}$** divided into several sections, with tappings brought out to metal **studs** numbered 1, 2, 3, ... N.
2. **A movable brass contact arm (starter handle)** that is gradually moved from stud 1 to stud N to cut out $R_{se}$ step by step.
3. **A No-Volt Coil (NV Coil)** connected between the **F** terminal and the final stud, in series with the field winding — it holds the handle in the ON position magnetically.
4. **A brass arc / overload release (OLR)** connected to the **A** terminal through an electromagnet — it trips the handle to OFF in case of armature overcurrent.
5. **A spring** that returns the handle to the OFF position when NV coil is de-energized or OLR trips.

**Working:**
- The handle is initially at the **OFF** position. Moving it to **stud 1** connects the supply to the armature through the *full* starting resistance. The armature current is limited to a safe value.
- As the motor accelerates, $E_b$ builds up, and $I_a$ naturally falls. The handle is then moved to stud 2, cutting out one section of $R_{se}$.
- This process continues through all studs. At the final stud (stud N), all $R_{se}$ is cut out, the NV coil holds the handle magnetically, and the motor runs on its natural characteristic.
- **Protection:** If the supply fails, the NV coil de-energizes, the spring returns the handle to OFF, and the motor is isolated. If $I_a$ exceeds a set limit, the OLR electromagnet pulls a trip lever, and the handle springs back to OFF.

**Solution to (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:**
- $V = 220$ V
- $R_a = 0.3 \, \Omega$
- $R_{sh} = 110 \, \Omega$
- $I_{a,fl} = 50$ A (full-load armature current)

**(i) Starting current without a starter:**

At $N = 0$, $E_b = 0$. The starting current is determined by $V$ and $R_a$ alone (the field current $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ flows through the field branch, not the armature).

$$I_{st} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{220}{0.3} = 733.33 \, \text{A}$$

**(ii) Required starting resistance to limit $I_{st}$ to $1.5 \times I_{a,fl}$:**

The safe starting current is:

$$I_{st,\text{safe}} = 1.5 \times 50 = 75 \, \text{A}$$

When $R_{se}$ is inserted in series with the armature, the loop equation at $N = 0$ is:

$$V = I_{st,\text{safe}} \cdot (R_a + R_{se})$$

$$R_{se} = \frac{V}{I_{st,\text{safe}}} - R_a = \frac{220}{75} - 0.3 = 2.933 - 0.3 = 2.633 \, \Omega$$

**(iii) Back EMF at full load:**

Using the armature voltage equation at full load:

$$E_b = V - I_{a,fl} \cdot R_a = 220 - (50 \times 0.3) = 220 - 15 = 205 \, \text{V}$$

> **Answer Summary:** (i) $I_{st} = 733.33$ A without starter, (ii) $R_{se} = 2.633$ Ω, (iii) $E_{b,fl} = 205$ V.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected]`

**(a)** Describe the **need for a starter** in DC motors. Derive an expression for the **starting current** of a DC motor and explain how it is limited using an external starting resistance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the **working of a 4-point starter**. State one key advantage of a 4-point starter over a 3-point starter. A 400 V DC series motor has an armature and field combined resistance of **0.5 Ω** and draws a current of **80 A** on full load. Find the starting resistance required to keep the starting current within **1.5 times** the full-load current. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Solution to (a) — Need and Derivation:**

*Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the exhaustive derivation. The key points to write in the exam are:*

1. The armature voltage equation of a DC motor is $V = E_b + I_a R_a$.
2. At the instant of starting, $N = 0$ and hence $E_b = 0$.
3. Substituting $E_b = 0$: $V = I_{st} R_a$, giving $I_{st} = V/R_a$.
4. Since $R_a$ is very small (0.05 to 1 Ω), $I_{st}$ becomes **10 to 20 times** the rated current.
5. This excessive current is limited by inserting an external starting resistance $R_{se}$ in series with the armature, so that $I_{st} = V/(R_a + R_{se})$ falls to a safe value.
6. As the motor speeds up, $E_b$ rises and $R_{se}$ is gradually cut out in steps using a starter.

**Solution to (b) — 4-Point Starter and Numerical:**

**4-Point Starter — Working:**

A 4-point starter has **four external terminals**:
- **L** (Line — positive supply)
- **A** (Armature)
- **F** (Field)
- **N** (Negative — connected to the negative of the supply, completing the field circuit independently of the NV coil)

The key difference from the 3-point starter is that in a 4-point starter, the **NV coil is connected directly across the supply through its own separate circuit** (between L and N), and the field winding is connected between A and N. The NV coil current is thus independent of the field current.

**Advantage over 3-point starter:** In a 3-point starter, the NV coil is in series with the field. If the field current becomes very low (e.g., during weak-field high-speed operation), the NV coil may not get enough current to hold the handle — this can cause a **dangerous false tripping**. The 4-point starter eliminates this problem because the NV coil is on a separate, independent circuit, so it always gets rated voltage.

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 400$ V
- $R_a + R_{se,\text{field}} = 0.5 \, \Omega$ (combined armature + series field resistance, treated as the total series circuit)
- $I_{fl} = 80$ A (full-load current for a series motor = armature current)

**Step 1: Compute the safe starting current.**

$$I_{st,\text{safe}} = 1.5 \times I_{fl} = 1.5 \times 80 = 120 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 2: At starting, $E_b = 0$. The series circuit (armature + series field + external $R_{se}$) carries the starting current.**

$$V = I_{st,\text{safe}} \cdot (R_a + R_{se,\text{field}} + R_{se})$$

**Step 3: Solve for $R_{se}$.**

$$R_{se} = \frac{V}{I_{st,\text{safe}}} - (R_a + R_{se,\text{field}}) = \frac{400}{120} - 0.5 = 3.333 - 0.5 = 2.833 \, \Omega$$

> **Answer:** $R_{se} = 2.833$ Ω.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $R_a$:** Many students write $R_{se} = V / I_{st,\text{safe}}$ and lose 1 mark. The correct formula is $R_{se} = V / I_{st,\text{safe}} - R_a$.
> 2. **Confusing full-load current with armature current for series motors:** In a series motor, the full-load current *is* the armature current (since field is in series), so the formula remains $I_{st} = V/(R_a + R_{se,\text{field}} + R_{se})$.
> 3. **Not drawing the starter diagram:** For 7-mark questions on 3-point/4-point starters, KTU examiners *expect* a labelled circuit diagram. Omitting it typically costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up the 3-point and 4-point NV coil connection:** Remember — 3-point has NV coil in series with the field; 4-point has NV coil on an independent branch.
> 5. **Units and significant figures:** Always write units (V, A, Ω) and present answers to 2–3 decimal places.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ✅ The **armature voltage equation** is $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ — the most fundamental equation in DC machines.
- ✅ At the **instant of starting**, $N = 0 \Rightarrow E_b = 0 \Rightarrow I_{st} = V / R_a$, which is dangerously high.
- ✅ A **starter** is a temporary, stepped, series resistance that limits the starting current to **1.5 to 2 times** the rated current.
- ✅ As the motor accelerates, $E_b$ rises, and the starter resistance is gradually **cut out in steps**.
- ✅ The **3-point starter** has three terminals (L, A, F) and uses an NV coil in series with the field — used for DC shunt motors.
- ✅ The **4-point starter** has four terminals (L, A, F, N) and uses an NV coil on an independent branch — used when field current varies widely (avoids false tripping).
- ✅ For **DC series motors**, starting current is particularly severe because $R_a$ is very small — starters are *absolutely essential*.
- ✅ For **fractional kW DC motors** ($< 0.5$ kW), direct-on-line (DOL) starting may be acceptable.
- ✅ **Modern soft-starters** use **PWM choppers / IGBT drives** to vary the effective voltage, replacing mechanical stepped resistors.
- ✅ The **No-Volt Coil (NV)** protects against supply failure and low voltage.
- ✅ The **Overload Release (OLR)** protects against excessive armature current.
- ✅ The required starting resistance is computed using $R_{se} = (V / I_{st,\text{safe}}) - R_a$.
- ✅ **Back EMF equation**: $E_b = \Phi Z N P / (60 A)$ — recall $A = 2$ for wave winding, $A = P$ for lap winding.
- ✅ Always **state units** in KTU numerical answers: V, A, Ω, RPM, Wb.
- ✅ Always **draw a neat labelled circuit diagram** for starter-related theory questions — it carries 2–3 marks by itself.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Flow Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Power Flow Diagram** is a standardized, single-line graphical and mathematical representation that traces the sequential transformation of energy from its input form to its useful output form in an electromechanical energy conversion device, while explicitly identifying and accounting for every category of power loss along the conversion chain.

In the context of **DC Machines (Module 1 of PCEOT402)**, the Power Flow Diagram is the *conceptual backbone* used to compute **Efficiency ($\eta$)** of both **DC Generators** and **DC Motors** under varying load conditions. It satisfies the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to electromechanical systems:

> **Power In = Power Out + Total Losses**

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{losses}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Power Flow Diagrams are a *mandatory* component of every numerical problem involving efficiency, torque, and loss calculations in DC Machines. The examiner expects students to *draw* the diagram before substituting values into equations.

### 1.2 The Two Mirrored Variants of the Power Flow Diagram

| Variant | Energy Conversion Direction | Input Form | Output Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **DC Motor** | Electrical → Mechanical | Electrical Power from Supply | Mechanical Power at Shaft |
| **DC Generator** | Mechanical → Electrical | Mechanical Power from Prime Mover | Electrical Power at Load Terminals |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Water Wheel Hydropower Plant

Imagine a **hydropower plant** where water stored at a height represents *input energy*:

1. **Input Energy (Potential Energy of Water):** Water flows from a reservoir — this is the gross input.
2. **First Conversion Stage — Turbine:** Water strikes the turbine blades, converting *hydraulic energy* into *mechanical rotation*. Along the way, some water splashes out uselessly (this is a **loss**).
3. **Second Conversion Stage — Generator Coupling:** The rotating shaft turns the generator, converting *mechanical rotation* into *electrical energy*. Heat is generated in the windings due to current flow (**copper loss**), and the iron core heats up due to alternating flux (**iron loss**).
4. **Friction in Bearings:** The shaft rubs against bearings, dissipating energy as heat (**friction and windage loss**) — *before* the useful output is delivered.
5. **Output Energy:** Finally, clean electricity reaches the grid (the consumer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In a *motor*, simply **reverse** the arrows! Water flows the other way. Electrical energy enters, gets converted to mechanical rotation, and shaft work is delivered. The losses are *exactly the same in nature* — they are intrinsic to the machine, not the direction of energy flow.

### 1.4 Categories of Losses Appearing in the Power Flow Diagram

The Power Flow Diagram for a DC Machine categorizes losses into three distinct types based on *where* they occur in the energy chain:

- **Electrical Losses (Copper Losses / $I^2R$ Losses):** Occur in the armature and field winding conductors due to ohmic resistance when current flows.
- **Magnetic Losses (Core / Iron Losses):** Occur in the armature iron core due to **Hysteresis Loss ($P_h$)** and **Eddy Current Loss ($P_e$)** caused by the rotating armature flux.
- **Mechanical Losses:** Comprise **Friction Loss**, **Windage Loss** (air resistance of the rotating armature), and — by KTU convention — are often grouped with the iron losses as *constant (rotational) losses*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant Losses** = Iron Losses + Mechanical Losses (Friction + Windage). These losses depend on **speed and flux**, not on load current. **Variable Losses** = Armature Copper Loss ($I_a^2 R_a$), which depends on the load.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Power Flow Stages — Step-by-Step Logic

The Power Flow Diagram is *not* drawn arbitrarily. It follows a strict **causal logic chain** from input to output, with each arrow representing a **transformation or subtraction step**. Understanding *why* each arrow exists is the key to solving any KTU numerical problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Power Flow:** Every box in the diagram satisfies *local conservation* — what enters a box equals what leaves the box plus the loss *within* that box.

#### 2.1.1 For a **DC Motor** (Electrical → Mechanical)

**Stage 1 — Input Stage:**
- The DC supply delivers **Input Electrical Power** to the motor terminals.
- In a **shunt motor**, this is $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$, where $I_L$ is the line current.
- The line current splits into two parallel branches: the field winding (taking $I_{sh}$) and the armature (taking $I_a$).
- Therefore: $I_L = I_{sh} + I_a \implies I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$.

**Stage 2 — Field Branch Loss:**
- The shunt field winding dissipates **Field Copper Loss**: $P_{sh} = I_{sh}^2 \cdot R_{sh} = V \cdot I_{sh}$.
- This loss occurs *before* the armature, so it is subtracted at this stage.
- Power reaching armature input = $P_{in} - P_{sh}$.

**Stage 3 — Armature Copper Loss:**
- Inside the armature, current $I_a$ flows through armature resistance $R_a$, dissipating **Armature Copper Loss**: $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 \cdot R_a$.
- Subtracting this gives the **Power Developed in the Armature** ($P_d$), also called **Gross Mechanical Power ($P_m$)**.
- This is the power crossing the **air gap** from the electrical side to the mechanical side — it is the *electromechanical conversion* point.

**Stage 4 — Iron (Core) Losses:**
- The rotating armature core experiences alternating flux, causing **Hysteresis Loss** and **Eddy Current Loss**.
- These are subtracted *after* power conversion, on the mechanical side, because KTU convention treats them as **rotational losses**.

**Stage 5 — Mechanical Losses:**
- **Friction Loss** (bearing friction) and **Windage Loss** (air drag on the rotating armature) dissipate mechanical energy as heat and sound.
- These are also **rotational losses**, independent of load.

**Stage 6 — Output Stage:**
- After all rotational losses are subtracted, the remaining power is the **Shaft Power (Output Power)**: $P_{out} = P_{sh,shaft}$.
- This is the **useful mechanical work** delivered to the load.

#### 2.1.2 For a **DC Generator** (Mechanical → Electrical)

The process is **mirrored**. The student simply *reverses the direction of energy flow*:

- **Mechanical Input** from the prime mover → subtract **Mechanical Losses** → subtract **Iron Losses** → arrive at **Power Developed in Armature** ($P_d = E_b \cdot I_a$) → subtract **Armature Copper Loss** ($I_a^2 R_a$) → add **Field Power** from external source (for separately excited / shunt) → arrive at **Output Electrical Power** ($V \cdot I_L$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:** In a **separately excited DC generator**, the field circuit is supplied from an *external* source. Hence, the field copper loss is *not* part of the armature's power flow — it is an *additional* input from a separate supply. In a **shunt generator**, however, the field is in parallel with the load, so the current $I_L = I_a - I_{sh}$ and the field loss is internal to the machine.

### 2.2 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The Power Flow Diagram is **not a mere textbook abstraction**. In professional electrical engineering practice, it is used for:

- **Efficiency Auditing:** Industrial energy auditors use the diagram to pinpoint *which loss dominates* under specific load conditions, enabling targeted retrofitting (e.g., variable frequency drives to lower iron losses at partial loads).
- **Thermal Management Design:** Each loss in the diagram becomes *heat* that must be dissipated. The diagram quantifies heat sources for designing cooling systems (forced air, water jackets).
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Sudden increases in *rotational losses* indicate bearing wear or fan degradation; rises in *copper losses* signal winding degradation or short circuits.
- **Electric Vehicle Design:** Power flow analysis of EV traction motors (often PMSM or induction motors derived from DC machine principles) is critical for range optimization.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $P_{in}$ | Input Power (Motor) | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ | Watts (W) | $V$ = supply voltage; $I_L$ = line current |
| $P_{in}$ | Input Power (Generator) | $P_{in} = T_{in} \cdot \omega$ | Watts | Mechanical input from prime mover |
| $I_{sh}$ | Shunt Field Current | $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ | Amperes (A) | Assumes constant supply voltage $V$ |
| $I_a$ | Armature Current (Motor) | $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ | A | Shunt / Compound motor |
| $I_a$ | Armature Current (Gen) | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | A | Long-shunt compound / Shunt generator |
| $E_b$ | Back EMF / Generated EMF | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ (Motor) | Volts (V) | Also: $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ |
| $E_b$ | Generated EMF (Generator) | $E_b = V + I_a R_a$ | V | Terminal voltage + internal drop |
| $P_{sh}$ | Shunt Field Copper Loss | $P_{sh} = V \cdot I_{sh} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$ | W | Constant for constant $V$ supply |
| $P_{cu,a}$ | Armature Copper Loss | $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$ | W | Variable; depends on $I_a$ |
| $P_{d}$ | Power Developed (Gross Mech.) | $P_{d} = E_b \cdot I_a$ | W | At the air gap — the conversion point |
| $P_{iron}$ | Iron / Core Loss | $P_{iron} = P_h + P_e$ | W | Hysteresis + Eddy current; constant if $N, \Phi$ constant |
| $P_{mech}$ | Mechanical Losses | $P_{mech} = P_{friction} + P_{windage}$ | W | Constant rotational losses |
| $P_{const}$ | Constant Losses | $P_{const} = P_{iron} + P_{mech}$ | W | Independent of load current |
| $P_{out}$ | Output Power (Shaft) | $P_{out} = P_{d} - P_{iron} - P_{mech}$ | W | Final useful power |
| $T_{sh}$ | Shaft Torque | $T_{sh} = P_{out} / \omega$ | N·m | $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ in rad/s |
| $T_{d}$ | Developed (Gross) Torque | $T_{d} = P_{d} / \omega$ | N·m | At the armature surface |
| $\eta$ | Efficiency | $\eta = (P_{out} / P_{in}) \times 100\%$ | % | Always $\eta < 100\%$ for real machines |

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Rule:** Convert RPM to rad/s using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ *before* computing torque from power. KTU examiners routinely test this conversion.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Fundamental Power Equation of a DC Machine (Air-Gap Power)

The Power Flow Diagram is fundamentally built upon the **electromechanical power conversion equation** at the air gap. This derivation is essential for KTU's application-level questions.

**Starting Point:** The armature winding consists of $Z$ conductors, each of resistance $R_a / (Z/2)$ approximately, but the net back EMF is $E_b$ and the current is $I_a$.

> **Step 1: Express the power developed in the armature.**
>
> The armature receives electrical power $V \cdot I_a$ at its terminals. Part of it is dissipated as copper loss in the armature resistance, and the rest is converted to mechanical form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{electrical\,input\,to\,armature} &= V \cdot I_a \\
P_{cu,a} &= I_a^2 R_a \quad \text{(Ohmic dissipation in armature)} \\
P_{d} &= P_{electrical\,input} - P_{cu,a} \\
P_{d} &= V \cdot I_a - I_a^2 R_a \\
P_{d} &= I_a(V - I_a R_a) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 2: Recognize that $V - I_a R_a = E_b$ (the back EMF) for a motor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{d} &= I_a \cdot E_b \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 3: Substitute the EMF equation $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ to get the mechanical power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{d} &= I_a \cdot \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 4: Convert mechanical power to torque using $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ (radians per second).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d &= \frac{P_d}{\omega} = \frac{I_a \cdot \Phi Z N P / 60A}{2\pi N / 60} \\
T_d &= \frac{I_a \cdot \Phi Z P}{2 \pi A} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental torque equation of a DC machine**:

$$\boxed{T_d = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P}{A} \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a}$$

where $k_t = \dfrac{P Z}{2 \pi A}$ is the machine's torque constant. The KTU examiner loves to see this constant derived, not just stated.

### 3.2 Derivation of Efficiency Equation in Terms of Variable Loss

**Efficiency Derivation (Highly Important for KTU 14-Mark Problems):**

Let $P_{out} = $ output power, $P_{const} = $ constant losses (iron + mechanical), and $P_{cu} = $ variable copper loss.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{const} + P_{cu}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Differentiating with respect to load** to find the condition for **maximum efficiency**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\eta}{dP_{out}} &= \frac{(P_{const} + P_{cu}) - P_{out} \cdot \frac{d(P_{cu})}{dP_{out}}}{(P_{const} + P_{cu} + P_{out})^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For maximum efficiency, set numerator to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{const} + P_{cu} - P_{out} \cdot \frac{dP_{cu}}{dP_{out}} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since copper loss $P_{cu} \propto I_a^2 \propto P_{out}^2$ (assuming $P_{out} \propto I_a$ for constant terminal voltage), we have $\dfrac{dP_{cu}}{dP_{out}} = \dfrac{2 P_{cu}}{P_{out}}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{const} + P_{cu} - 2 P_{cu} &= 0 \\
P_{const} - P_{cu} &= 0 \\
P_{const} &= P_{cu} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A DC machine operates at **maximum efficiency when the constant losses equal the variable (copper) losses.**

$$\boxed{\eta_{max}\, \text{occurs when} \quad P_{const} = P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Illustration (Step-by-Step)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme-Style Problem:** A **220 V DC shunt motor** draws a line current of **50 A** at full load. The armature resistance is $R_a = 0.2\,\Omega$, the shunt field resistance is $R_{sh} = 110\,\Omega$, and the rotational (constant) losses total **500 W**. Calculate: (a) Back EMF, (b) Armature Copper Loss, (c) Power Developed, (d) Output Power, (e) Shaft Torque at 1500 RPM, and (f) Efficiency.

**Step 1: Compute shunt field current.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sh} &= \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute armature current.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a &= I_L - I_{sh} = 50 - 2 = 48\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute back EMF $E_b$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= V - I_a R_a = 220 - (48)(0.2) = 220 - 9.6 = 210.4\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Armature Copper Loss.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cu,a} &= I_a^2 \cdot R_a = (48)^2 \times 0.2 = 2304 \times 0.2 = 460.8\,\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Power Developed (Gross Mechanical Power).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= E_b \cdot I_a = 210.4 \times 48 = 10099.2\,\text{W} \approx 10.099\,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute Output (Shaft) Power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= P_d - P_{rotational} = 10099.2 - 500 = 9599.2\,\text{W} \approx 9.599\,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Compute Shaft Torque at N = 1500 RPM.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi = 157.08\,\text{rad/s} \\
T_{sh} &= \frac{P_{out}}{\omega} = \frac{9599.2}{157.08} = 61.11\,\text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Compute Efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{9599.2}{V \cdot I_L} \times 100\% = \frac{9599.2}{220 \times 50} \times 100\% \\
\eta &= \frac{9599.2}{11000} \times 100\% = 87.27\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Loss Breakdown Summary Table

| Loss Category | Computed Value (W) | Percentage of Input |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Field Copper Loss ($P_{sh} = 220 \times 2$) | **440 W** | 4.00% |
| Armature Copper Loss ($P_{cu,a}$) | **460.8 W** | 4.19% |
| Rotational Losses (Iron + Mechanical) | **500 W** | 4.55% |
| **Total Losses** | **1400.8 W** | **12.74%** |
| **Output Power** | **9599.2 W** | **87.27%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** $P_{out} + \text{Total Losses} = 9599.2 + 1400.8 = 11000\,\text{W} = V \cdot I_L$ ✓ Energy balance confirmed.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Power Flow Diagram of a DC Motor (Block Schematic)

The following Mermaid diagram traces the energy flow from the electrical supply to the mechanical shaft, displaying every loss branch as a downward arrow (a "leak" in the energy pipeline).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Electrical Input P_in = V × I_L"]:::inputStyle --> B["Field Branch Loss: P_sh = V × I_sh"]
    B --> C["Armature Input: V × I_a"]
    C --> D["Armature Copper Loss: I_a² × R_a"]
    D --> E["Power Developed at Air Gap: P_d = E_b × I_a"]
    E --> F["Iron / Core Loss: P_h + P_e"]
    E --> G["Friction + Windage Loss"]
    F --> H["Shaft Output Power: P_out = T_sh × ω"]
    G --> H

    classDef inputStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lossStyle fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#FFF
    classDef powerStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A inputStyle
    class B,D,F,G lossStyle
    class C,E powerStyle
    class H outputStyle
```

### 4.2 Power Flow Diagram of a DC Generator (Block Schematic)

The generator diagram is the **mirror image** of the motor diagram — energy flows from mechanical input on the left to electrical output on the right.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["Mechanical Input from Prime Mover: T_in × ω"]:::inputStyle --> M2["Friction + Windage Loss"]
    M2 --> M3["Iron / Core Loss: P_h + P_e"]
    M3 --> M4["Power Developed in Armature: P_d = E_b × I_a"]
    M4 --> M5["Armature Copper Loss: I_a² × R_a"]
    M5 --> M6["Electrical Output: V × I_L"]
    M5 --> M7["Shunt Field Loss: V × I_sh"]

    classDef inputStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lossStyle fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#FFF
    classDef powerStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class M1 inputStyle
    class M2,M3,M5,M7 lossStyle
    class M4 powerStyle
    class M6 outputStyle
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of Power Flow Analysis

This high-level architecture summarizes the **analytical procedure** a KTU student should follow when *given* any DC machine problem involving the Power Flow Diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Step 1: Identify Machine Type (Motor / Generator)"] --> B0["Step 2: Identify Winding Type (Shunt / Series / Compound)"]
    B0 --> C0["Step 3: Extract Given Data: V, I_L, R_a, R_sh, N, Losses"]
    C0 --> D0["Step 4: Compute Branch Currents: I_sh, I_a"]
    D0 --> E0["Step 5: Apply EMF Equation: E_b = V ± I_a × R_a"]
    E0 --> F0["Step 6: Compute Air-Gap Power: P_d = E_b × I_a"]
    F0 --> G0["Step 7: Subtract Rotational Losses: P_out = P_d - P_const"]
    G0 --> H0["Step 8: Compute Efficiency: η = P_out / P_in × 100%"]
    H0 --> I0["Step 9: Compute Torque: T = P / ω"]

    classDef stepStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A0,B0,C0,D0,E0,F0,G0,H0,I0 stepStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Diagrams:** In Mermaid, `flowchart TD` is **top-down** (used for motors) and `flowchart LR` is **left-right** (used for generators). The arrow direction (-->) always indicates the **forward direction of useful power flow**, while the colored boxes indicate the *nature* of the power: **gold (input)**, **green (useful converted power)**, **blue (output)**, **red (losses)**.
