# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Bank Notice:** All questions in this section follow the **exact pattern of KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations (ESE)**. The Part A questions are 3-mark direct-concept questions, and Part B questions are 14-mark internal-choice problems with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Model solutions with complete step-by-step working are provided.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**State the EMF equation of a DC generator. Explain the significance of the term "number of parallel paths" (A) in the equation.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> The EMF equation of a DC generator is:
>
> $$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$
>
> where $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $Z$ = total armature conductors, $N$ = speed (RPM), $P$ = number of poles, and $A$ = number of parallel paths.
>
> **Significance of A (1 Mark):**
> The number of parallel paths depends on the winding type: $A = P$ for lap winding and $A = 2$ for wave winding. It determines how the armature conductors are interconnected and directly affects both the **generated EMF** and the **current capacity** of the machine. **Wave winding** (A=2) produces higher EMF with lower current rating, while **lap winding** (A=P) produces lower EMF with higher current rating.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define "armature torque" and "shaft torque" of a DC motor. How are they related?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> - **Armature Torque ($T_a$):** The torque developed by the armature conductors due to the interaction of armature current and magnetic flux. It is the gross torque produced inside the machine. (1.5 Marks)
>
> $$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{N·m}$$
>
> - **Shaft Torque ($T_{sh}$):** The net torque available at the motor shaft for doing useful mechanical work, after subtracting the losses due to friction, windage, and iron losses. (1.5 Marks)
>
> **Relationship:** $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss}$, where $T_{loss}$ is the torque lost to mechanical and iron losses.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
> *A 250 V, 4-pole, lap-wound DC shunt motor has 800 armature conductors. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω and the field resistance is 125 Ω. The motor takes a total current of 60 A at full load. The flux per pole is 30 mWb, and the mechanical and iron losses total 800 W.*

**Calculate the following:**
- **(a)** The back EMF, armature current, and speed at full load. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** The shaft torque, output power, and efficiency at full load. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $V = 250$ V, $P = 4$, lap-wound so $A = 4$
- $Z = 800$, $R_a = 0.2$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 125$ Ω
- $I_L = 60$ A, $\Phi = 0.03$ Wb
- Mechanical + Iron losses = 800 W

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{125} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Armature current.**

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 60 - 2 = 58 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Back EMF.**

$$E_b = V - I_a \cdot R_a = 250 - (58 \times 0.2) = 250 - 11.6 = 238.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Speed using EMF equation (rearranged for N).**

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P} = \frac{238.4 \times 60 \times 4}{0.03 \times 800 \times 4}$$

**Step 5: Numerator.**

$$238.4 \times 60 = 14304$$
$$14304 \times 4 = 57216$$

**Step 6: Denominator.**

$$0.03 \times 800 = 24$$
$$24 \times 4 = 96$$

**Step 7: Division.**

$$N = \frac{57216}{96} = 596 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 238.4 \text{ V}, \quad I_a = 58 \text{ A}, \quad N = 596 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Armature torque.**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} = 0.1591 \times 0.03 \times 800 \times 58 \times \frac{4}{4}$$

**Step 2: Simplification (P/A = 1 for lap winding).**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \times 0.03 \times 800 \times 58 \times 1$$

**Step 3: Compute step-by-step.**

$$0.1591 \times 0.03 = 4.773 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$4.773 \times 10^{-3} \times 800 = 3.8184$$
$$3.8184 \times 58 = 221.47 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Loss torque from mechanical and iron losses.**

$$T_{loss} = \frac{60 \times P_{loss}}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 800}{2\pi \times 596} = \frac{48000}{3744.7} = 12.82 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Shaft torque.**

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss} = 221.47 - 12.82 = 208.65 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6: Output power.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 596 \times 208.65}{60}$$

**Step 7: Numerator computation.**

$$2\pi \times 596 = 3744.74$$
$$3744.74 \times 208.65 = 781415.5$$

**Step 8: Division.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{781415.5}{60} = 13023.6 \text{ W} \approx 13.02 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 9: Input power.**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 60 = 15000 \text{ W} = 15 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 10: Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{13023.6}{15000} \times 100 = 86.82\%$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} = 208.65 \text{ N·m}, \quad P_{out} = 13.02 \text{ kW}, \quad \eta = 86.82\%}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**
> *A 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt generator has 600 armature conductors. The armature and field resistances are 0.3 Ω and 200 Ω, respectively. The flux per pole is 0.025 Wb, and the generator delivers a load current of 100 A at 250 V.*

**Calculate the following:**
- **(a)** The generated EMF and the speed of the generator. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** The armature copper loss, total losses, and efficiency of the generator. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $P = 4$, wave-wound, $A = 2$
- $Z = 600$, $R_a = 0.3$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 200$ Ω
- $\Phi = 0.025$ Wb
- Load current $I_L = 100$ A, terminal voltage $V = 250$ V

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{200} = 1.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Calculate armature current (same as load current in a generator when $I_L$ is the line current; here, the load is connected to the terminals, so $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$).**

$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 1.25 = 101.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the voltage drop in the armature.**

$$I_a \cdot R_a = 101.25 \times 0.3 = 30.375 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the generated EMF (DC generator voltage equation: $E_g = V + I_a R_a + V_{brush}$).**

Assuming brush contact drop of 2 V (standard assumption: 1 V per brush):

$$E_g = 250 + 30.375 + 2 = 282.375 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Calculate speed from the EMF equation.**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$$

$$N = \frac{E_g \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P} = \frac{282.375 \times 60 \times 2}{0.025 \times 600 \times 4}$$

**Step 6: Numerator.**

$$282.375 \times 60 = 16942.5$$
$$16942.5 \times 2 = 33885$$

**Step 7: Denominator.**

$$0.025 \times 600 = 15$$
$$15 \times 4 = 60$$

**Step 8: Division.**

$$N = \frac{33885}{60} = 564.75 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{E_g = 282.375 \text{ V}, \quad N = 564.75 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Armature copper loss.**

$$P_{cu,a} = I_a^{2} \cdot R_a = (101.25)^2 \times 0.3$$

**Step 2: Compute $(101.25)^2$.**

$$(101.25)^2 = 10251.5625$$

**Step 3: Multiply by $R_a$.**

$$P_{cu,a} = 10251.5625 \times 0.3 = 3075.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Field copper loss.**

$$P_{cu,sh} = I_{sh}^{2} \cdot R_{sh} = (1.25)^2 \times 200 = 1.5625 \times 200 = 312.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Brush contact loss (assuming 2 V total drop and $I_a$ flowing through brushes).**

$$P_{brush} = V_{brush} \cdot I_a = 2 \times 101.25 = 202.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6: Total electrical losses (assuming no mechanical/iron loss data given, only electrical losses).**

$$P_{loss,total} = P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,sh} + P_{brush} = 3075.47 + 312.5 + 202.5 = 3590.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7: Output power delivered to the load.**

$$P_{out} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 100 = 25000 \text{ W} = 25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8: Input mechanical power.**

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss,total} = 25000 + 3590.47 = 28590.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 9: Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{25000}{28590.47} \times 100 = 87.44\%$$

$$\boxed{P_{cu,a} = 3075.47 \text{ W}, \quad P_{loss,total} = 3590.47 \text{ W}, \quad \eta = 87.44\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
>
> 1. **Confusing A = P (lap) vs A = 2 (wave):** This single mistake can lead to a 50% error in your EMF and Torque calculations. Always re-read the problem statement for the winding type.
> 2. **Forgetting the field current in $I_a$ calculation:** In a **motor**, $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$. In a **generator**, $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the brush contact drop:** Many students assume $E_g = V$ in generators and $E_b = V$ in motors, ignoring the $I_a R_a$ drop. You will lose 1-2 marks if this is missing.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing RPM and rad/s for angular velocity. KTU expects RPM in the EMF equation directly, and rad/s in the power equation $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$.
> 5. **Shaft vs. Armature Torque confusion:** The question may ask for "shaft torque" but students compute "armature torque" and skip the loss torque subtraction.
> 6. **Power equation sign errors:** Output = Input − Losses. Always check the direction of energy flow before writing the equation.
> 7. **No unit on final answer:** A numerical answer without units (V, A, RPM, N·m, W, %) is considered incomplete and will lose 0.5–1 mark per instance.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 Numerical Problems**

- ✅ The **EMF Equation** $E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ is the **master equation** for all DC machine voltage calculations.
- ✅ For **Lap Winding**: $A = P$. For **Wave Winding**: $A = 2$ (independent of poles).
- ✅ The **Torque Equation** $T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$ applies to **motors only**; in generators, this represents the mechanical torque required.
- ✅ The **Voltage Equation**:
   - Motor: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (back EMF opposes applied voltage)
   - Generator: $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ (generated EMF exceeds terminal voltage)
- ✅ The **Current Equation**:
   - Motor: $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$
   - Generator: $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$
- ✅ The **Power Conversion Equation**: $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \omega = \frac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$.
- ✅ **Shaft Torque** = Armature Torque − Loss Torque.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$, always between 0% and 100%.
- ✅ **Speed Regulation** $= \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$. For shunt motors, it's typically +1% to +5% (positive).
- ✅ In **armature reaction** problems: convert mechanical degrees to electrical degrees using $\theta_e = \frac{P}{2} \theta_m$.
- ✅ **Demagnetizing conductors per pole** $= \frac{Z}{2P} \cdot \frac{2\theta_e}{180°}$.
- ✅ **Cross-magnetizing conductors per pole** = (Total conductors per pole) − (Demagnetizing conductors per pole).
- ✅ Always carry **units** through your calculation: Wb, RPM, V, A, Ω, N·m, W, %.
- ✅ **Conversion check**: $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ — if you forget to convert, you'll get errors in power/torque calculations.
- ✅ **Sanity check your answer**: EMF should be in tens to hundreds of volts; torque in single to hundreds of N·m; efficiency typically 75–95%.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Testing of DC Machines** refers to a series of standardized experimental procedures performed on DC generators and DC motors to determine their operational characteristics, performance parameters, and efficiency under both no-load and loaded conditions, without necessarily operating them at their full rated load in real-world applications.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, **Testing of DC Machines** falls under the classification of:

1. **Direct (Loading) Tests** — The machine is actually loaded to its rated capacity. Examples: **Brake Test (Mechanical Loading)**, **Resistive Loading Test (Electrical Loading)**.
2. **Indirect (No-Load / Regenerative) Tests** — The machine is not loaded to its rated capacity; instead, efficiency is computed indirectly from measured no-load losses. Examples: **Swinburne's Test**, **Hopkinson's Test (Regenerative / Back-to-Back Test)**, **Field's Test**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check — "The Car Service Center Analogy"**
> Imagine taking your car for service. The mechanic does NOT drive it at 150 km/h on a highway (full load) to check if the brakes work. Instead, he puts it on a **rolling road (dynamometer)** to simulate the load and measures the fuel consumption, brake performance, and engine temperature. Similarly, in electrical machines, fully loading a large DC machine (say, 100 kW) is expensive, requires huge loads (rheostats, water tanks), and wastes enormous energy. **Testing methods** are clever laboratory procedures that determine efficiency and performance *without* the actual full mechanical/electrical loading.

### 1.3 Classification Flowchart of DC Machine Tests

The following broad classification is used in KTU evaluations:

- **No-Load / Open-Circuit Tests**
  - Used to find **magnetic / iron losses** and **rotational losses** (friction + windage).
- **Short-Circuit / Blocked-Rotor Tests**
  - Used in transformers; rarely in DC machines.
- **Loading Tests**
  - **Direct Loading Test (Brake Test)**: Uses mechanical brake.
  - **Indirect Loading Tests**: Swinburne's, Hopkinson's, Field's Test.
- **Regenerative Tests**
  - Power is circulated between two coupled machines (Hopkinson's, Field's).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Mechanical Power Output (P_out)** in Watts or kW
- **Input Power (P_in)** in Watts or kW
- **Efficiency (η)** = (P_out / P_in) × 100%
- **Total Losses (P_loss)** = P_in − P_out
- **Constant Losses (P_c)** = Iron Loss (P_i) + Friction & Windage Loss (P_fw) = approximately constant at all loads (assumed in Swinburne's Test)
- **Stray Load Loss (P_sll)**: small, often lumped with constant losses
- **Armature Copper Loss (P_cu)** = I_a² R_a, varies with the square of load current
- **Brush Contact Drop**: typically **2 V (1 V per brush pair)** — standard constant per KTU board assumptions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Assumption (Syllabus Highlight):**
> The armature reaction and brush contact drop are often **neglected** in Swinburne's Test, and constant losses are assumed to remain **constant from no-load to full load** (this is the fundamental assumption of the test). Stray load losses are usually **lumped with armature copper loss** or considered negligible unless specified.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Swinburne's Test (The Most Frequently Asked Indirect Test)

**Swinburne's Test** is a no-load test performed on a **DC shunt motor** (which can be mechanically coupled to act as a motor in service). The motor is run at **rated voltage** and **rated speed** under **no mechanical load** (no brake, no shaft load). The goal is to find the **constant losses** so that efficiency at *any* load can be computed.

#### 2.1.1 Why "Constant Losses"?
At a given **rated speed** and **field current**, the iron (hysteresis + eddy current) losses depend only on flux density and frequency — both of which are constant. Friction and windage losses also depend only on speed. Hence, these are lumped together as **P_c (constant losses)**.

#### 2.1.2 Circuit Setup Logic
- The shunt motor is supplied with **rated armature voltage (V_L)** and **rated field excitation**.
- The motor runs at no-load, drawing a small **no-load current (I_L0)**.
- Power input is measured by a **wattmeter (W)**.
- Voltmeter reads supply voltage; ammeter reads no-load current.

#### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Logic to Find Constant Losses
1. **Measured no-load input** = V_L × I_L0 = **Wattmeter reading (W_0)**
2. This small input must supply:
   - Iron losses in the armature core
   - Friction and windage losses
   - A tiny armature copper loss (I_a0² R_a)
   - Field copper loss in the shunt winding (V_L × I_sh)
3. **No-load armature current** I_a0 = I_L0 − I_sh
4. **No-load armature copper loss** = I_a0² × R_a (very small, sometimes neglected)
5. **Constant losses (P_c)** = W_0 − I_a0² R_a  *(if armature Cu loss is considered)*
   OR  **P_c = W_0**  *(if armature Cu loss is neglected — KTU standard assumption)*

#### 2.1.4 Efficiency at Any Load (x × Full Load)
Let full load armature current = **I_a(FL)**. Let the load be a fraction **x** of full load.

- **Armature current at x-load** = x · I_a(FL)
- **Armature copper loss at x-load** = (x · I_a(FL))² · R_a
- **Constant losses** = P_c (same as at no-load)
- **Total losses at x-load** = P_c + x² · I_a(FL)² · R_a

- **Input power at x-load** = V_L · [I_a(FL)·x + I_sh]
- **Output power at x-load** = Input − Total losses
- **Efficiency at x-load** = (Output / Input) × 100%

### 2.2 Hopkinson's Test (Regenerative / Back-to-Back Test)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When is Hopkinson's Test preferred over Swinburne's?**
> Hopkinson's Test gives a **more accurate estimate of efficiency**, especially at higher loads, because it accounts for the **stray load losses** which Swinburne's Test cannot determine. It requires **two identical DC machines** mechanically coupled and electrically interconnected.

#### 2.2.1 Setup Logic
- Two **identical DC shunt machines** M1 (motor) and G1 (generator) are mechanically coupled.
- The motor M1 is connected to the supply and drives the generator G1.
- The generator G1 feeds power *back* into the supply (or to a small resistive load), causing **circulating power** between the two machines.
- A small **excitation adjustment** varies the load on the set without an external mechanical load.
- The set essentially **circulates power** — the input from the supply only meets the **total losses** of the system.

#### 2.2.2 Measurement and Calculation Logic
Let:
- V = Supply voltage
- I_L = Line current drawn from supply
- I_1 = Armature current of Motor (M1)
- I_2 = Armature current of Generator (G1)
- I_sh1, I_sh2 = Shunt field currents of M1 and G1
- I = Total current through the armature circuit (I = I_1 + I_2 typically, depending on configuration)

**Power drawn from supply** = V × I_L
**This power supplies ALL losses of the set** (since the generator returns most of the power back).

- **Total losses of the set** = V × I_L
- **Iron losses + Friction losses of each machine** = unknown; assumed equal in both machines.
- **Armature copper losses**:
  - Motor: I_1² × R_a1
  - Generator: I_2² × R_a2
- **Shunt field copper losses** of both machines
- **Stray load losses** of both machines (if accounted for)

**Efficiency of Motor (M1)** is calculated by assuming the **stray load loss (P_sll)** is proportional to the square of armature current:
- P_sll (per machine) ∝ I² (commonly assumed equal to a fraction of armature Cu loss, often taken as a fixed percentage)

**Efficiency of Generator (G1)** is similarly calculated.

### 2.3 Field's Test (For Two Series DC Motors)

**Field's Test** is a back-to-back test used when **two similar series DC motors** are available. The two motors are mechanically coupled. One motor runs as a motor, the other as a generator. A variable resistor (divertor) is used across the series field of the generator to control the field current, and a series-parallel connection is used to vary loading.

- Used in traction applications where series motors are common.
- Allows determination of efficiency **at any load** without actually loading the set.

### 2.4 Brake Test (Direct Loading Test)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Brake Test** is a direct mechanical loading method. A friction brake (rope, belt, or water brake) is applied to a water-cooled pulley mounted on the motor shaft. The mechanical output is given by:
> **P_out = 2π · N · T / 60** where T is the shaft torque in N·m and N is the speed in RPM.

A typical **rope-brake dynamometer** setup uses:
- A rope wound around the pulley
- Spring balances on either side reading S1 and S2 (in kg)
- **Net force (F)** = (S1 − S2) × 9.81 N
- **Torque (T)** = F × R, where R is the radius of the pulley in meters
- **Output power** = (2πNT)/60 watts
- **Input power** = V × I (measured by voltmeter and ammeter)
- **Efficiency η** = (Output / Input) × 100%

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Test Name** | **Machine Type** | **Type** | **Key Formula** | **What it Determines** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Swinburne's Test** | DC Shunt Motor | Indirect, No-Load | P_c = V_L · I_L0 − I_a0² · R_a | Constant losses (P_c) only |
| **Hopkinson's Test** | Two DC Shunt Machines | Indirect, Regenerative | Total Losses = V · I_L | Total losses of the set; efficiencies of both machines |
| **Field's Test** | Two DC Series Motors | Indirect, Regenerative | Similar to Hopkinson's | Efficiency at any load of series motor |
| **Brake Test** | Any DC Motor | Direct, Loaded | P_out = (2πNT)/60 | Output power, torque, efficiency directly |
| **No-Load Test on DC Generator** | DC Shunt Generator | No-Load | P_c = V · I_L0 − (I_L0)² · R_a | Iron + Friction losses |

#### 2.5.1 Critical Formulas for Numerical Problems

**1. Swinburne's — Efficiency at load current I_a:**

$$\eta = \frac{V \cdot I_a - (P_c + I_a^2 \cdot R_a)}{V \cdot I_a} \times 100\%$$

**2. Hopkinson's — Motor efficiency:**

$$\eta_{motor} = \frac{V \cdot I_1 - (I_1^2 R_a + P_c + P_{sll})}{V \cdot I_1} \times 100\%$$

**3. Brake Test — Torque and Output Power:**

$$T = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot 9.81 \cdot R \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

$$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

**4. Output power in kW (compact):**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 1000} \quad [\text{kW}]$$

**5. Relationship between T and P:**

$$P_{out} = \frac{N \cdot T}{k} \quad \text{where } k = 9.5488 \approx 9.55 \text{ (constant)}$$

#### 2.5.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications:**
> - **Swinburne's Test** is the *de-facto standard* in manufacturing industries for routine quality checks of DC shunt motors — fast, cheap, and requires no load.
> - **Hopkinson's Test** is used in research labs and large motor manufacturing units to certify efficiency curves for premium customers.
> - **Brake Test** is used in final acceptance testing of small/medium motors where the actual load can be absorbed (e.g., water-cooled dynamometers in test bays).
> - **Field's Test** was historically used in electric locomotive workshops to test traction motors before installation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Numerical Worked Example — Swinburne's Test

> **Problem Statement:**
> A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of **0.2 Ω** and a shunt field resistance of **250 Ω**. On no-load, it draws a line current of **5 A** at rated voltage. Find:
> (a) The constant losses of the machine.
> (b) The efficiency of the machine at full-load armature current of 50 A.
> (c) The efficiency at half-load (25 A armature current).

#### 3.1.1 Solution — Part (a): Constant Losses

**Step 1:** Identify the given data.
- V = 250 V
- R_a = 0.2 Ω
- R_sh = 250 Ω
- I_L0 (no-load line current) = 5 A

**Step 2:** Calculate the shunt field current.
The shunt field is connected directly across the supply.

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the no-load armature current.
By KCL at the input terminal, the line current splits into the shunt field and the armature:

$$I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_{sh} = 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the no-load input power.
The wattmeter reading (input power at no-load) is:

$$W_0 = V \times I_{L0} = 250 \times 5 = 1250 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the no-load armature copper loss.
Even at no-load, the small armature current causes a copper loss:

$$P_{cu,a0} = I_{a0}^2 \times R_a = 4^2 \times 0.2 = 16 \times 0.2 = 3.2 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the constant losses.
The total input at no-load is consumed by:
- Constant losses (iron + friction + windage) → **P_c**
- Shunt field copper loss → V · I_sh = 250 × 1 = 250 W
- Small armature copper loss at no-load → 3.2 W

Applying power balance:

$$W_0 = P_c + V \cdot I_{sh} + I_{a0}^2 R_a$$

$$1250 = P_c + 250 + 3.2$$

$$P_c = 1250 - 250 - 3.2 = 996.8 \text{ W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In many textbook problems, the no-load armature copper loss (I_a0² R_a) is **neglected** because it is very small. In that simplified case: **P_c = W_0 − V · I_sh = 1000 W**. Always check the problem statement for hints about whether to neglect this term.

#### 3.1.2 Solution — Part (b): Efficiency at Full Load (I_a = 50 A)

**Step 7:** Calculate armature copper loss at full load.

$$P_{cu,a(FL)} = I_{a(FL)}^2 \times R_a = 50^2 \times 0.2 = 2500 \times 0.2 = 500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 8:** Total losses at full load.

$$P_{total(FL)} = P_c + P_{cu,a(FL)} = 996.8 + 500 = 1496.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 9:** Total input current at full load.
The input current at full load is the armature current + shunt field current:

$$I_{L(FL)} = I_{a(FL)} + I_{sh} = 50 + 1 = 51 \text{ A}$$

**Step 10:** Input power at full load.

$$P_{in(FL)} = V \times I_{L(FL)} = 250 \times 51 = 12750 \text{ W}$$

**Step 11:** Output power at full load.

$$P_{out(FL)} = P_{in(FL)} - P_{total(FL)} = 12750 - 1496.8 = 11253.2 \text{ W}$$

**Step 12:** Efficiency at full load.

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{P_{out(FL)}}{P_{in(FL)}} \times 100\% = \frac{11253.2}{12750} \times 100\% \approx 88.26\%$$

#### 3.1.3 Solution — Part (c): Efficiency at Half-Load (I_a = 25 A)

**Step 13:** Armature copper loss at half-load.

$$P_{cu,a(0.5)} = 25^2 \times 0.2 = 625 \times 0.2 = 125 \text{ W}$$

**Step 14:** Total losses at half-load.

$$P_{total(0.5)} = 996.8 + 125 = 1121.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 15:** Input current at half-load.

$$I_{L(0.5)} = 25 + 1 = 26 \text{ A}$$

**Step 16:** Input power at half-load.

$$P_{in(0.5)} = 250 \times 26 = 6500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 17:** Output power at half-load.

$$P_{out(0.5)} = 6500 - 1121.8 = 5378.2 \text{ W}$$

**Step 18:** Efficiency at half-load.

$$\eta_{0.5} = \frac{5378.2}{6500} \times 100\% \approx 82.74\%$$

#### 3.1.4 Efficiency Summary Table

| **Load** | **I_a (A)** | **I_L (A)** | **P_cu,a (W)** | **P_c (W)** | **P_total (W)** | **P_in (W)** | **P_out (W)** | **η (%)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| No-load | 4 | 5 | 3.2 | 996.8 | 1250.0 | 1250.0 | 0.0 | 0.00 |
| Half-load | 25 | 26 | 125.0 | 996.8 | 1121.8 | 6500.0 | 5378.2 | 82.74 |
| Full-load | 50 | 51 | 500.0 | 996.8 | 1496.8 | 12750.0 | 11253.2 | 88.26 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The efficiency is **not** maximum at full load in this case. The maximum efficiency occurs where **variable losses (armature Cu loss) = constant losses (P_c)**. This gives the **most efficient operating point**. The student should always verify this for Swinburne's problems.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Numerical Worked Example — Hopkinson's Test

> **Problem Statement:**
> Two identical 250 V DC shunt machines are tested using Hopkinson's method. The data recorded:
> - V = 250 V
> - Line current from supply, I_L = 5 A
> - I_sh1 (motor field) = 1 A, I_sh2 (generator field) = 1.2 A
> - R_a of each machine = 0.5 Ω
> - Armature current of motor, I_1 = 40 A
> - Armature current of generator, I_2 = 35 A (note: they are different due to circulating current)
> - Stray load loss is 1% of output power for each machine.
>
> Calculate: (a) Total losses of the set, (b) Efficiency of the motor, (c) Efficiency of the generator.

#### 3.2.1 Solution — Part (a): Total Losses of the Set

**Step 1:** Power drawn from supply.
Since the generator returns most power to the supply, the input from the source is essentially the total losses:

$$P_{input,supply} = V \times I_L = 250 \times 5 = 1250 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** This must equal the total losses of the set:

$$P_{total,set} = 1250 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3:** Verify by component calculation.

Armature Cu losses:
$$P_{cu,motor} = I_1^2 R_a = 40^2 \times 0.5 = 1600 \times 0.5 = 800 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{cu,gen} = I_2^2 R_a = 35^2 \times 0.5 = 1225 \times 0.5 = 612.5 \text{ W}$$

Shunt field Cu losses:
$$P_{sh,motor} = V \times I_{sh1} = 250 \times 1 = 250 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{sh,gen} = V \times I_{sh2} = 250 \times 1.2 = 300 \text{ W}$$

Total measurable copper losses:
$$P_{cu,total} = 800 + 612.5 + 250 + 300 = 1962.5 \text{ W}$$

This exceeds the supply input of 1250 W — meaning the assumed values are inconsistent (this is just an illustrative example). In a properly designed Hopkinson's test, the supply input exactly matches total losses. **The point of this exercise is to highlight that the input current I_L is small compared to the armature currents, indicating high circulating power efficiency.**

#### 3.2.2 Solution — Part (b): Motor Efficiency

**For a properly conducted test, let us assume consistent values. The motor efficiency formula is:**

$$\eta_{motor} = \frac{P_{mech,out, motor}}{P_{elec, in, motor}}$$

$$P_{elec,in,motor} = V \times I_1 = 250 \times 40 = 10000 \text{ W}$$

The motor output mechanical power equals the generator input mechanical power (plus rotation losses). For the motor:

$$P_{mech,out,motor} = P_{elec,in,motor} - P_{cu,motor} - P_{stray} - P_{c, motor}$$

Assuming the iron and friction losses are split equally (each machine's constant loss = P_c, with P_c = 500 W say, and stray loss of motor = 1% of motor output = 0.01 × P_mech):

$$P_{mech,out,motor} = 10000 - 800 - 500 - 0.01 \cdot P_{mech,out,motor}$$

$$1.01 \cdot P_{mech,out,motor} = 8700$$

$$P_{mech,out,motor} = 8613.86 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{motor} = \frac{8613.86}{10000} \times 100\% = 86.14\%$$

#### 3.2.3 Solution — Part (c): Generator Efficiency

The generator's mechanical input equals the motor's mechanical output minus rotation losses. In balanced conditions, the generator's electrical output can be calculated as:

$$P_{elec,out,gen} = V \times I_2 = 250 \times 35 = 8750 \text{ W}$$

Assuming similar losses for the generator:
- Armature Cu loss = 612.5 W
- Constant loss = 500 W
- Stray loss = 1% of output

$$\eta_{gen} = \frac{8750}{8750 + 612.5 + 500 + 0.01 \times 8750} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{gen} = \frac{8750}{8750 + 612.5 + 500 + 87.5} \times 100\% = \frac{8750}{9950} \times 100\% = 87.94\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight:** In Hopkinson's test, even though both machines are identical, their efficiencies are **slightly different** because the motor operates at a higher armature current than the generator (the difference goes to the line current I_L). The total losses of the set are essentially the small supply input — proving the elegance of the regenerative method.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Brake Test

> **Problem Statement:**
> A DC shunt motor is tested by a rope-brake dynamometer. The readings are:
> - Spring balance readings: S1 = 12 kg, S2 = 2 kg
> - Pulley diameter: 40 cm (radius R = 0.2 m)
> - Motor speed: 1440 RPM
> - Supply voltage: 220 V
> - Input current: 30 A
>
> Calculate: (a) Shaft torque, (b) Output power, (c) Input power, (d) Efficiency.

#### 3.3.1 Solution

**Step 1:** Net force on the brake.

$$F = (S_1 - S_2) \times 9.81 = (12 - 2) \times 9.81 = 10 \times 9.81 = 98.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Shaft torque.

$$T = F \times R = 98.1 \times 0.2 = 19.62 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3:** Output power.

$$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1440 \times 19.62}{60} = \frac{177,468.6}{60} = 2957.81 \text{ W} \approx 2.958 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Input power.

$$P_{in} = V \times I = 220 \times 30 = 6600 \text{ W} = 6.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5:** Efficiency.

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{2957.81}{6600} \times 100\% = 44.82\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The low efficiency here is because the **rope-brake dynamometer absorbs most of the power as heat**. The brake itself is inefficient for measuring high-power machines; modern test benches use **eddy-current dynamometers** or **regenerative dynamometers**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Swinburne's Test — Circuit Schematic

The following Mermaid block renders the functional architecture of the Swinburne's Test setup, showing all measurement nodes and their logical connections.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[DC Supply 250V] --> nodeB[Voltmeter V]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Shunt Field Winding]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Ammeter A Line]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Ammeter A1 Armature]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Armature Winding Ra]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Shunt Field Resistance Rsh]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Wattmeter W Input]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Return to Supply Negative]
    nodeJ[No Mechanical Load] -.-> nodeF
```

### 4.2 Hopkinson's Test — Block Architecture Flow

This block diagram shows the **circulating power** concept — the motor and generator are mechanically and electrically coupled, with only losses drawn from the supply.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[DC Supply] --> nodeB[Line Switch]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Voltmeter V Across DC Bus]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Ammeter AL Line Current]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Motor Armature M1]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Mechanical Coupling Shaft]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Generator Armature G1]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Return to DC Supply]
    nodeI[Shunt Field M1 Exciter] --> nodeE
    nodeJ[Shunt Field G1 Exciter] --> nodeG
    nodeK[Ammeter A1 Motor Armature] --> nodeE
    nodeL[Ammeter A2 Generator Armature] --> nodeG
    nodeF -. CIRCULATING POWER .-> nodeG
    nodeA -. SMALL LOSS COMPENSATION .-> nodeD
```

### 4.3 Brake Test Setup — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[DC Motor] --> nodeB[Pulley Drum]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Rope Wound on Pulley]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Spring Balance S1 Tight Side]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Spring Balance S2 Slack Side]
    nodeF[Cooling Water Spray] -.-> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeG[Tachometer for Speed N]
    nodeA --> nodeH[Voltmeter V and Ammeter A]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Decision Tree — Which Test to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Want to test a DC Machine] --> nodeB{Available Hardware}
    nodeB -- Single DC Shunt Machine --> nodeC{Is full load feasible to apply}
    nodeB -- Two Identical DC Machines --> nodeD[Hopkinsons Test]
    nodeB -- Two DC Series Motors --> nodeE[Fields Test]
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeF[Brake Test Direct Load]
    nodeC -- No expensive impractical --> nodeG[Swinburnes Test Indirect]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Calculate Stray Load Losses Accurately]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Compute Efficiency at Any Load]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Get Torque and Speed Curves]
```

```mermaid

    nodeD --> nodeK[Kapp Modification of Hopkinson]
```
