<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Model Set]**

---

**Q1. What is Swinburne's Test? Mention one advantage and one limitation.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Swinburne's Test** is an indirect method of testing a DC shunt motor, performed at no-load with rated supply voltage, to determine its constant losses (iron + friction + windage). Once these are known, the efficiency of the motor at any load can be predicted without actually loading it.
> - **Advantage:** It requires very little power to conduct the test (only the no-load input), so it is economical and quick.
> - **Limitation:** It cannot measure stray load losses, which become significant at higher loads, leading to an overestimate of efficiency near full load.

---

**Q2. Why is Hopkinson's Test called a regenerative test?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> Hopkinson's Test is called a **regenerative (or back-to-back) test** because the two coupled DC machines form a closed loop of power circulation — the motor mechanically drives the generator, and the generator's electrical output is fed back into the supply (or to a small portion of the load). The net power drawn from the external supply is only that required to meet the **total losses** of the system. Thus, the major part of the power is **regenerated** within the set, making it highly energy-efficient.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern: Each sub-part carries 7 marks]**

---

### 5.2.1 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / May 2023 Adapted]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Swinburne's Test of a DC shunt motor with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expression for efficiency at any load x times the full load.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A **250 V** DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of **0.15 Ω** and field resistance of **200 Ω**. On no-load, the motor takes a current of **4 A** at rated voltage. Calculate:
> (i) The constant losses of the machine.
> (ii) The efficiency of the motor at half-load and full-load armature current of **60 A**.
> (Assume armature reaction is neglected; neglect no-load armature copper loss for the purpose of this problem.)

#### SOLUTION (a) — Swinburne's Test Explanation and Derivation

**Step 1: Circuit Description**
The shunt motor is connected to a DC supply of rated voltage. A voltmeter V measures the supply voltage, an ammeter A measures the line current I_L, and a wattmeter W measures the total no-load input power. The motor shaft is uncoupled from any mechanical load.

**Step 2: Measured Quantities at No-Load**
- V = rated voltage
- I_L0 = no-load line current
- W_0 = V × I_L0 = no-load input power
- I_sh = V / R_sh (shunt field current, calculated)

**Step 3: Power Balance at No-Load**
The no-load input supplies three categories of power consumption:
- Shunt field copper loss: V · I_sh
- Armature copper loss at no-load: I_a0² · R_a
- Constant losses (iron + friction + windage): P_c

$$W_0 = V \cdot I_{sh} + I_{a0}^2 R_a + P_c$$

**Step 4: Armature Current at No-Load**

$$I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_{sh}$$

**Step 5: Constant Losses**

$$P_c = W_0 - V \cdot I_{sh} - I_{a0}^2 R_a$$

**Step 6: Efficiency at x × Full Load**
Let full-load armature current = I_a(FL). At x-times full load:
- Armature current = x · I_a(FL)
- Armature copper loss = (x · I_a(FL))² · R_a = x² · I_a(FL)² · R_a
- Total losses = P_c + x² · I_a(FL)² · R_a
- Input current = x · I_a(FL) + I_sh
- Input power = V · (x · I_a(FL) + I_sh)
- Output power = Input − Total losses

**Step 7: Final Efficiency Formula**

$$\boxed{\eta_{x} = \frac{V \cdot (x \cdot I_{a(FL)} + I_{sh}) - P_c - x^2 \cdot I_{a(FL)}^2 \cdot R_a}{V \cdot (x \cdot I_{a(FL)} + I_{sh})} \times 100\%}$$

#### SOLUTION (b) — Numerical Calculation

**Given Data:**
- V = 250 V, R_a = 0.15 Ω, R_sh = 200 Ω
- I_L0 = 4 A, I_a(FL) = 60 A
- Neglect no-load armature copper loss

**Step 1: Shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{200} = 1.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: No-load input power (W_0).**

$$W_0 = V \times I_{L0} = 250 \times 4 = 1000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Constant losses (P_c).**
Since no-load armature copper loss is neglected:

$$P_c = W_0 - V \cdot I_{sh} = 1000 - 250 \times 1.25 = 1000 - 312.5 = 687.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Half-load efficiency (x = 0.5).**

Armature current at half load:
$$I_{a(0.5)} = 0.5 \times 60 = 30 \text{ A}$$

Armature copper loss at half load:
$$P_{cu,0.5} = 30^2 \times 0.15 = 900 \times 0.15 = 135 \text{ W}$$

Total losses at half load:
$$P_{total,0.5} = P_c + P_{cu,0.5} = 687.5 + 135 = 822.5 \text{ W}$$

Input current at half load:
$$I_{L,0.5} = 30 + 1.25 = 31.25 \text{ A}$$

Input power at half load:
$$P_{in,0.5} = 250 \times 31.25 = 7812.5 \text{ W}$$

Output power at half load:
$$P_{out,0.5} = 7812.5 - 822.5 = 6990 \text{ W}$$

Efficiency at half load:
$$\eta_{0.5} = \frac{6990}{7812.5} \times 100\% = 89.47\%$$

**Step 5: Full-load efficiency (x = 1).**

Armature copper loss at full load:
$$P_{cu,FL} = 60^2 \times 0.15 = 3600 \times 0.15 = 540 \text{ W}$$

Total losses at full load:
$$P_{total,FL} = 687.5 + 540 = 1227.5 \text{ W}$$

Input current at full load:
$$I_{L,FL} = 60 + 1.25 = 61.25 \text{ A}$$

Input power at full load:
$$P_{in,FL} = 250 \times 61.25 = 15312.5 \text{ W}$$

Output power at full load:
$$P_{out,FL} = 15312.5 - 1227.5 = 14085 \text{ W}$$

Efficiency at full load:
$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{14085}{15312.5} \times 100\% = 91.99\%$$

#### Final Answer Table for Question A (b)

| **Parameter** | **Half-Load** | **Full-Load** |
|---|---|---|
| I_a (A) | 30 | 60 |
| P_cu (W) | 135 | 540 |
| P_c (W) | 687.5 | 687.5 |
| P_total (W) | 822.5 | 1227.5 |
| P_in (W) | 7812.5 | 15312.5 |
| P_out (W) | 6990 | 14085 |
| **η (%)** | **89.47** | **91.99** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Do not forget the shunt field copper loss (V · I_sh)** when computing constant losses. This is the most common mistake — students often equate W_0 to P_c, which is wrong.
> 2. **Use armature current I_a** (not line current) when computing armature copper loss. The line current I_L includes the shunt field current.
> 3. **Constant losses remain constant** at all loads in Swinburne's Test — DO NOT scale them with load current.

---

### 5.2.2 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern: Hopkinson's Test Variant]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe Hopkinson's Test for determining the efficiency of two similar DC shunt machines. Draw a neat connection diagram and explain the principle of power circulation.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** In a Hopkinson's test on two identical **220 V** DC shunt machines, the following data is obtained:
> - Line current from supply: I_L = 8 A
> - Motor armature current: I_1 = 60 A
> - Generator armature current: I_2 = 52 A
> - Shunt field current of motor: I_sh1 = 1.0 A
> - Shunt field current of generator: I_sh2 = 1.2 A
> - Armature resistance of each machine: R_a = 0.1 Ω
> - Stray load loss: 1% of full-load output for each machine.
>
> Calculate the efficiency of:
> (i) The motor
> (ii) The generator

#### SOLUTION (a) — Hopkinson's Test Description

**Step 1: Setup**
Two identical DC shunt machines M (motor) and G (generator) are mechanically coupled on a common shaft. Both are connected to the same DC bus of rated voltage. The motor armature receives current I_1, while the generator armature delivers current I_2 back to the bus (with a small circulating current going out as line current I_L).

**Step 2: Field Excitation**
The motor is excited to rated shunt field current. The generator's field is slightly over-excited (or under-excited) so that the generator's generated EMF exceeds the bus voltage by an amount that drives a current I_2 from the generator to the bus.

**Step 3: Principle of Power Circulation**
- The motor draws power from the bus equal to V × I_1.
- This power is converted to mechanical power and transferred through the shaft to the generator.
- The generator converts it back to electrical power, with V × I_2 going to the bus.
- The **difference** between V × I_1 and V × I_2 is supplied externally as V × I_L.
- This small external supply is the **total losses** of the system, since circulating power is "free" (only losses need to be met).

**Step 4: Advantages**
- Very low external power consumption for testing.
- Allows testing at or near full-load conditions.
- Stray load losses are included in the measurement.

**Step 5: Limitations**
- Requires two identical machines.
- Machines must be carefully matched in characteristics.

#### SOLUTION (b) — Numerical Calculation

**Step 1: Verify total losses = V × I_L.**

$$P_{losses,total} = V \times I_L = 220 \times 8 = 1760 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Armature copper losses.**

$$P_{cu,motor} = I_1^2 R_a = 60^2 \times 0.1 = 360 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{cu,gen} = I_2^2 R_a = 52^2 \times 0.1 = 270.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Shunt field copper losses.**

$$P_{sh,motor} = V \times I_{sh1} = 220 \times 1.0 = 220 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{sh,gen} = V \times I_{sh2} = 220 \times 1.2 = 264 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Total measurable copper losses.**

$$P_{cu,total} = 360 + 270.4 + 220 + 264 = 1114.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Iron + Friction (constant losses of both machines).**
The total losses = sum of all losses, so:

$$P_{iron+fric,total} = P_{losses,total} - P_{cu,total} = 1760 - 1114.4 = 645.6 \text{ W}$$

Assuming equal split between machines:

$$P_{c,motor} = P_{c,gen} = \frac{645.6}{2} = 322.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6: Motor Input Power.**

$$P_{in,motor} = V \times I_1 = 220 \times 60 = 13200 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7: Stray load loss of motor (1% of output).**
For the motor, output is mechanical power developed = P_developed. To find the motor's efficiency, let us denote motor output (mechanical) as P_out,motor.

The motor losses are:
- Armature Cu: 360 W
- Field Cu: 220 W
- Constant loss (Iron + friction): 322.8 W
- Stray load loss: 0.01 × P_out,motor

By definition, P_out,motor = P_in,motor − total losses of motor.

$$P_{out,motor} = 13200 - 360 - 220 - 322.8 - 0.01 \cdot P_{out,motor}$$

$$1.01 \cdot P_{out,motor} = 13200 - 902.8 = 12297.2$$

$$P_{out,motor} = \frac{12297.2}{1.01} = 12175.45 \text{ W}$$

**Step 8: Motor Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{motor} = \frac{P_{out,motor}}{P_{in,motor}} \times 100\% = \frac{12175.45}{13200} \times 100\% = 92.24\%$$

**Step 9: Generator Output Power.**

$$P_{out,gen} = V \times I_2 = 220 \times 52 = 11440 \text{ W}$$

**Step 10: Generator Losses (excluding stray as 1% of input).**
For the generator, the "output" is electrical (V × I_2). The input is mechanical power = P_out,motor transferred through the shaft (approximately, with rotational losses deducted).

Stray load loss of generator = 0.01 × P_out,gen (electrical output):

$$P_{sll,gen} = 0.01 \times 11440 = 114.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 11: Generator Input (Mechanical).**

$$P_{in,gen,mech} = P_{out,gen} + P_{cu,gen} + P_{sh,gen} + P_{c,gen} + P_{sll,gen}$$

$$P_{in,gen,mech} = 11440 + 270.4 + 264 + 322.8 + 114.4 = 12411.6 \text{ W}$$

**Step 12: Generator Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{gen} = \frac{P_{out,gen}}{P_{in,gen,mech}} \times 100\% = \frac{11440}{12411.6} \times 100\% = 92.17\%$$

#### Final Answer for Question B (b)

| **Quantity** | **Motor** | **Generator** |
|---|---|---|
| P_in (W) | 13200 | 12411.6 (mech input) |
| P_out (W) | 12175.45 | 11440 (electrical output) |
| **Efficiency η (%)** | **92.24** | **92.17** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert for Hopkinson's Test:**
> 1. **Stray load loss assumption is critical.** Different problems use different conventions — sometimes stray loss is given as a fixed wattage, sometimes as a percentage of output, sometimes as proportional to (I_a)². Read the problem statement carefully.
> 2. **For the motor**, "output" is mechanical power developed. **For the generator**, "output" is electrical power delivered (V × I_a). Mixing these up is the most common error.
> 3. **Constant losses must be split equally** between the two machines only when they are truly identical and operating at the same speed and flux. Some problems give them as separate quantities.
> 4. The **input current from supply (I_L)** is very small compared to the armature currents (I_1, I_2) — this is the **beauty of the regenerative test**; verify your answer makes physical sense.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- **Testing of DC Machines** is broadly classified into **Direct (Loading)** and **Indirect (No-Load / Regenerative)** methods.
- **Swinburne's Test** is performed on a **DC shunt motor** at **no-load** to find **constant losses (P_c)**, which are then used to predict efficiency at any load.
- **Key Assumption of Swinburne's:** Constant losses remain **constant from no-load to full load** (true at rated voltage and rated speed).
- **Hopkinson's Test** requires **two identical DC shunt machines** and uses **power regeneration** to find total losses with very small external power input.
- **Field's Test** is the series-motor equivalent of Hopkinson's Test.
- **Brake Test** uses mechanical friction (rope, belt, water brake) to apply load directly. It is the most accurate direct method but consumes the output power as heat.
- **Brush contact drop = 2 V total (1 V per brush pair)** is a standard KTU assumption unless stated otherwise.
- **Stray load losses (P_sll)** are NOT determined by Swinburne's Test; they require Hopkinson's Test or empirical assumptions (often 1% of output).
- The **line current** I_L = I_a + I_sh for a DC shunt motor (always true).
- The **armature current** is what determines the variable copper loss: P_cu = I_a² × R_a.
- For Hopkinson's, the **input current I_L is small** because the generator returns most of the motor's drawn power back to the bus.
- The **rope-brake dynamometer** formula: T = (S_1 − S_2) × 9.81 × R, and P_out = (2πNT)/60 W.
- **Maximum efficiency** of a DC machine occurs when **variable losses = constant losses**.
- For a shunt motor: I_a(FL) is given in the problem; for the test, the assumption is that field current I_sh = V / R_sh stays constant.
- **Armature reaction** effects are **neglected** in standard KTU test problems.
- **Always convert** all powers to a **common unit** (Watts) before computing efficiency percentages.
- In **Hopkinson's test efficiency calculation**, distinguish between **electrical input** and **mechanical output** for the motor; the reverse for the generator.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swinburne's Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Swinburne's Test ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Swinburne's Test** is a no-load, indirect method of testing a **DC shunt machine** (or a cumulative compound machine) to predetermine its efficiency at any desired load, without actually loading the machine beyond the no-load condition. The test is formally known as the **"Constant Loss Test"** because it directly yields the sum of constant (stray) losses of the machine — namely the **iron (core) losses** and the **mechanical losses (friction + windage)**. The procedure involves running the machine as a **shunt motor at rated voltage and rated speed under no mechanical load**, and measuring its no-load input current. The armature copper loss at no-load is negligibly small (since no-load armature current is very low), so the entire no-load input to the armature is treated as the **constant loss Wc**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a high-weightage item in Module 1 of PCEOT402. It frequently appears in ESE (End Semester Exam) Part A and Part B. Mastery of the no-load loss segregation logic is essential. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a fuel-efficient car manufacturer who wants to declare the mileage of a new model for **every possible load** — from idling at traffic signals to climbing a steep mountain. Instead of physically driving the car through every road condition (which is impractical and expensive), the engineers run the car on a **dynamometer at idle (no load)** to measure the baseline fuel consumption. They call this the "**idling loss**." Now, knowing the additional fuel required per kg of cargo (the **variable loss**), they mathematically combine both — and presto! — they can confidently publish the mileage for any load condition without ever driving the car uphill. **Swinburne's Test does exactly this for a DC machine:** - **Idling loss** (no-load) = Iron loss + Friction & Windage loss → the **Constant Loss Wc**. - **Variable loss** = Armature copper loss (Ia²·Ra) → depends on load. - The efficiency at any load is calculated by combining these two. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Constant Loss Test"?** The name reflects the fact that for a DC shunt machine operating near rated voltage and speed, the iron loss (∝ V²) and friction/windage loss (∝ N²) remain essentially constant from no-load to full-load. Hence segregating them once at no-load is sufficient for all load conditions. ### 1.3 Applicability Snapshot | Machine Type | Applicable? | Reason | |---|---|---| | DC Shunt Machine | ✅ Yes | Field flux ≈ constant, can safely run on no-load | | Cumulative Compound | ✅ Yes | Behaves similarly to shunt at light loads | | DC Series Machine | ❌ No | Dangerous at no-load (tends to reach dangerously high speeds) | | Separately Excited | ✅ Yes (with care) | Field current held constant by separate source | ### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Conventions - **Rated Voltage (V)** and **Rated Speed (N)** are the two mandatory operating points. - Armature resistance **Ra** and shunt field resistance **Rsh** must be known (measured by a multimeter or by the **voltmeter-ammeter method** with the field disconnected). - The test is purely **electrical input measurement** — no mechanical loading apparatus (like a brake or pony brake) is required. - Output power is **never directly measured**; it is computed from input and losses. - The armature copper loss at no-load is often **neglected in approximate analysis** because Ia₀ is very small (typically 3–6% of full-load current). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Underlying Theory — Why the Test Works A DC shunt machine at rated terminal voltage and rated speed exhibits the following loss segregation: $$ P_{\text{input}} = P_{\text{output}} + P_{\text{losses}} $$ where the **total losses** are classified as: 1. **Constant Losses (Wc)** — independent of load - Iron (core) losses in the armature - Friction loss - Windage loss (air friction on the rotating armature) 2. **Variable Losses** — depend on load - Armature copper loss = $I_a^2 R_a$ - Brush contact loss (sometimes lumped with armature copper loss) 3. **Field Copper Loss** ($V \cdot I_{sh}$ or $I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$) - Approximately constant for a shunt machine (since V is fixed and Rsh is fixed). At **no-load**, the output is zero. The only mechanical output is internal rotational loss (friction + windage), and the input to the armature circuit supplies only the iron loss, mechanical loss, and a tiny armature copper loss. ### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning 1. **Step 1 — No-Load Running:** The machine is run as a motor at rated voltage V and rated speed N with **no mechanical load** on the shaft. 2. **Step 2 — Current Measurement:** Three currents are read: total line current $I_0$, armature current $I_{a0}$, and shunt field current $I_{sh}$. 3. **Step 3 — Field Current Computation:** $$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$$ 4. **Step 4 — Armature Current at No-Load:** By KCL at the armature terminal, $$I_{a0} = I_0 - I_{sh}$$ 5. **Step 5 — No-Load Input to Armature:** $$P_{\text{armature input}} = V \cdot I_{a0}$$ 6. **Step 6 — No-Load Armature Copper Loss:** $$P_{cu,a0} = I_{a0}^2 \cdot R_a$$ 7. **Step 7 — Constant Loss Extraction:** $$W_c = V \cdot I_{a0} - I_{a0}^2 \cdot R_a = \text{Iron Loss} + \text{Friction} + \text{Windage}$$ 8. **Step 8 — Efficiency at Any Load x:** With Wc known, the efficiency at any fractional load x (x = 0 to 1, where 1 = full load) is computed. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master this table** — these formulas are the backbone of every numerical problem in KTU exams on this topic. | # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Shunt field current | $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$ | Constant for shunt machine | | 2 | No-load armature current | $I_{a0} = I_0 - I_{sh}$ | KCL at armature node | | 3 | No-load armature copper loss | $P_{cu,a0} = I_{a0}^2 R_a$ | Usually small, often neglected | | 4 | Constant (stray) loss | $W_c = V \cdot I_{a0} - I_{a0}^2 R_a$ | Iron + Friction + Windage | | 5 | Armature copper loss at load x | $P_{cu,ax} = (x \cdot I_{al})^2 \cdot R_a = x^2 I_{al}^2 R_a$ | Variable with load | | 6 | Total losses at load x | $P_{\text{losses}} = W_c + x^2 I_{al}^2 R_a$ | Sum of constant + variable | | 7 | Motor input at load x | $P_{\text{in,motor}} = V \cdot (x I_{al} + I_{sh})$ | Total current drawn from supply | | 8 | Motor output at load x | $P_{\text{out,motor}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{losses}}$ | | | 9 | Motor efficiency | $\eta_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Expressed as percentage | | 10 | Generator armature current | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | Field is across the load | | 11 | Generator output at load x | $P_{\text{out,gen}} = V \cdot x I_{L,\text{FL}}$ | | | 12 | Generator input at load x | $P_{\text{in,gen}} = P_{\text{out}} + P_{\text{losses}}$ | Mechanical power input | | 13 | Generator efficiency | $\eta_{\text{gen}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | | | 14 | Approximate Wc (when Ia₀ ignored) | $W_c \approx V \cdot I_0$ | Fast estimation | > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU problems, the **load fraction x is often 1 (full load)** unless explicitly stated otherwise. The variable x² term is the key differentiator between full-load and any-load efficiency. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Manufacturer's Acceptance Test:** Swinburne's test is the **cheapest and fastest** way to verify a DC machine's compliance with efficiency guarantees — no loading rig (dynamometer, water brake, or pony brake) is required. - **Loss Segregation:** It cleanly separates **constant losses** from **variable losses**, enabling design engineers to improve specific subsystems (e.g., use better lamination to reduce iron loss, or better bearings to reduce friction). - **Cost-Effective Quality Control:** In production lines of DC motors (e.g., for elevators, electric vehicles, industrial drives), every unit can be tested via Swinburne's method — saving hours of full-load test bench time per machine. - **Predetermination of Efficiency Curve:** Once Wc is known, the efficiency vs. load curve can be plotted mathematically, helping in matching motor rating to application. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Constant Loss Extraction from No-Load Test **Starting Point:** The DC shunt motor runs at no-load (no shaft load, but armature is rotating at rated speed). **Power balance equation at no-load:** $$ P_{\text{in,armature}} = P_{\text{cu,armature}} + P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{friction}} + P_{\text{windage}} $$ **Step 1 — Substitute known quantities:** The input to the armature alone (excluding the shunt field branch) is: $$ V \cdot I_{a0} = I_{a0}^2 R_a + P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{friction}} + P_{\text{windage}} $$ **Step 2 — Group the constant losses:** By definition of the **constant loss Wc**: $$ W_c \triangleq P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{friction}} + P_{\text{windage}} $$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for Wc:** $$ W_c = V \cdot I_{a0} - I_{a0}^2 R_a $$ **Step 4 — Compute the no-load armature current from KCL:** At the armature terminal, the incoming current splits into the armature branch and the shunt field branch. Therefore: $$ I_{a0} = I_0 - I_{sh} = I_0 - \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} $$ **Final expression for constant loss:** $$ \boxed{W_c = V \cdot \left(I_0 - \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}\right) - \left(I_0 - \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}\right)^2 R_a} $$ > [!NOTE] > In the **approximate** version (most KTU problems use this), the term $I_{a0}^2 R_a$ is neglected because $I_{a0}$ is small (no-load). Thus: $W_c \approx V \cdot I_{a0} \approx V \cdot I_0$ (if $I_{sh}$ is also small). --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Efficiency at Fractional Load x (Motor Mode) **Given:** Full-load armature current $I_{al}$, fractional load x. **Step 1 — Armature current at fractional load x:** $$ I_{ax} = x \cdot I_{al} $$ **Step 2 — Variable loss (armature copper loss) at load x:** $$ P_{\text{cu,ax}} = I_{ax}^2 \cdot R_a = (x \cdot I_{al})^2 \cdot R_a = x^2 \cdot I_{al}^2 \cdot R_a $$ **Step 3 — Total losses at load x:** $$ P_{\text{losses},x} = W_c + x^2 \cdot I_{al}^2 \cdot R_a $$ **Step 4 — Motor input current at load x:** The total current drawn from the supply includes both armature and shunt field currents: $$ I_{\text{line},x} = I_{ax} + I_{sh} = x \cdot I_{al} + \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} $$ **Step 5 — Motor input power at load x:** $$ P_{\text{in},x} = V \cdot I_{\text{line},x} = V \cdot \left(x \cdot I_{al} + \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}\right) $$ **Step 6 — Motor output power at load x (by energy balance):** $$ P_{\text{out},x} = P_{\text{in},x} - P_{\text{losses},x} $$ $$ P_{\text{out},x} = V \cdot \left(x \cdot I_{al} + \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}\right) - W_c - x^2 \cdot I_{al}^2 \cdot R_a $$ **Step 7 — Motor efficiency at load x:** $$ \boxed{\eta_{\text{motor},x} = \dfrac{V \cdot \left(x \cdot I_{al} + \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}\right) - W_c - x^2 \cdot I_{al}^2 \cdot R_a}{V \cdot \left(x \cdot I_{al} + \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}\right)} \times 100\%} $$ **At full load (x = 1):** $$ \eta_{\text{motor,FL}} = \dfrac{V \cdot \left(I_{al} + I_{sh}\right) - W_c - I_{al}^2 \cdot R_a}{V \cdot \left(I_{al} + I_{sh}\right)} \times 100\% $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Efficiency at Fractional Load x (Generator Mode) **Step 1 — Load current at fraction x:** $$ I_{Lx} = x \cdot I_{L,\text{FL}} $$ **Step 2 — Generator armature current at load x:** The armature must supply both the load and the shunt field (which is connected across the armature): $$ I_{ax} = I_{Lx} + I_{sh} = x \cdot I_{L,\text{FL}} + \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} $$ **Step 3 — Generator output power at load x:** $$ P_{\text{out},x} = V \cdot I_{Lx} = V \cdot x \cdot I_{L,\text{FL}} $$ **Step 4 — Armature copper loss at load x (variable):** $$ P_{\text{cu},ax} = I_{ax}^2 \cdot R_a = \left(x \cdot I_{L,\text{FL}} + \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}\right)^2 \cdot R_a $$ **Step 5 — Total losses at load x:** $$ P_{\text{losses},x} = W_c + \left(x \cdot I_{L,\text{FL}} + \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}\right)^2 \cdot R_a $$ **Step 6 — Generator input (mechanical) power at load x:** $$ P_{\text{in},x} = P_{\text{out},x} + P_{\text{losses},x} $$ $$ P_{\text{in},x} = V \cdot x \cdot I_{L,\text{FL}} + W_c + \left(x \cdot I_{L,\text{FL}} + \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}\right)^2 \cdot R_a $$ **Step 7 — Generator efficiency at load x:** $$ \boxed{\eta_{\text{gen},x} = \dfrac{V \cdot x \cdot I_{L,\text{FL}}}{V \cdot x \cdot I_{L,\text{FL}} + W_c + \left(x \cdot I_{L,\text{FL}} + \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}\right)^2 \cdot R_a} \times 100\%} $$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Generator Mode — Generator acting as a Motor Comparison) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]** > > A 250 V DC shunt machine has armature resistance $R_a = 0.2 \ \Omega$ and shunt field resistance $R_{sh} = 250 \ \Omega$. At no-load, it draws $I_0 = 5 \ \text{A}$ at rated voltage. Calculate the efficiency at full-load current of $50 \ \text{A}$ when running as **(i) a motor** and **(ii) a generator**. #### Solution **Preliminary Calculations (No-Load Test Data Processing):** **Step 1 — Shunt field current:** $$ I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{250}{250} = 1 \ \text{A} $$ **Step 2 — No-load armature current:** $$ I_{a0} = I_0 - I_{sh} = 5 - 1 = 4 \ \text{A} $$ **Step 3 — No-load armature copper loss:** $$ P_{cu,a0} = I_{a0}^2 \cdot R_a = (4)^2 \times 0.2 = 16 \times 0.2 = 3.2 \ \text{W} $$ **Step 4 — Constant losses:** $$ W_c = V \cdot I_{a0} - I_{a0}^2 \cdot R_a = 250 \times 4 - 3.2 = 1000 - 3.2 = 996.8 \ \text{W} $$ --- #### (i) Running as a Motor (Full Load) **Step 5 — Full-load armature current (given):** $$ I_{al} = 50 \ \text{A} $$ **Step 6 — Full-load armature copper loss:** $$ P_{cu,FL} = I_{al}^2 \cdot R_a = (50)^2 \times 0.2 = 2500 \times 0.2 = 500 \ \text{W} $$ **Step 7 — Total losses at full load:** $$ P_{\text{losses}} = W_c + P_{cu,FL} = 996.8 + 500 = 1496.8 \ \text{W} $$ **Step 8 — Motor input power at full load:** $$ P_{\text{in}} = V \cdot (I_{al} + I_{sh}) = 250 \times (50 + 1) = 250 \times 51 = 12{,}750 \ \text{W} $$ **Step 9 — Motor output power at full load:** $$ P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{losses}} = 12{,}750 - 1496.8 = 11{,}253.2 \ \text{W} $$ **Step 10 — Motor efficiency at full load:** $$ \eta_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{11{,}253.2}{12{,}750} \times 100 = 88.26\% $$ --- #### (ii) Running as a Generator (Full Load) **Step 11 — Generator full-load current (load current):** $$ I_L = 50 \ \text{A} $$ **Step 12 — Generator armature current (load + field):** $$ I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 50 + 1 = 51 \ \text{A} $$ **Step 13 — Generator armature copper loss:** $$ P_{cu} = I_a^2 \cdot R_a = (51)^2 \times 0.2 = 2601 \times 0.2 = 520.2 \ \text{W} $$ **Step 14 — Total losses at full load:** $$ P_{\text{losses}} = W_c + P_{cu} = 996.8 + 520.2 = 1517 \ \text{W} $$ **Step 15 — Generator output power at full load:** $$ P_{\text{out}} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 50 = 12{,}500 \ \text{W} $$ **Step 16 — Generator input (mechanical) power at full load:** $$ P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}} + P_{\text{losses}} = 12{,}500 + 1517 = 14{,}017 \ \text{W} $$ **Step 17 — Generator efficiency at full load:** $$ \eta_{\text{gen}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{12{,}500}{14{,}017} \times 100 = 89.18\% $$ **Final Answer:** $\eta_{\text{motor}} = 88.26\%$, $\eta_{\text{generator}} = 89.18\%$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Observation for KTU exams:** The generator efficiency is **slightly higher** than the motor efficiency because in motor mode, the armature copper loss is based on $I_L$ alone, whereas in generator mode, the armature must carry $I_L + I_{sh}$ — but the output is still $V \cdot I_L$ (load), so the input is larger but the loss is also larger; net effect depends on numerical values. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Add Field Current | Operating Mode | Line Current = ? | Armature Current = ? | Output = ? | |---|---|---|---| | Motor, No-Load | $I_0$ | $I_{a0} = I_0 - I_{sh}$ | 0 | | Motor, Load x | $xI_{al} + I_{sh}$ | $xI_{al}$ | $V \cdot (xI_{al} + I_{sh}) - \text{Losses}$ | | Generator, Load x | $xI_{L,\text{FL}}$ (load) | $xI_{L,\text{FL}} + I_{sh}$ | $V \cdot xI_{L,\text{FL}}$ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Swinburne's Test Circuit ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DCSupply[DC Supply Rated Voltage V] termP((+)) termN((-)) end termP --> sw[Double Pole Switch] sw --> amTotal[Main Ammeter A0] amTotal --> starter[3 Point Starter] starter --> armNode{Armature Terminal} armNode --> arm[Armature Winding
Resistance Ra] arm --> termN volt[V Voltmeter] -.-> armNode volt -.-> termN armNode --> amField[Ammeter Ash] amField --> rheo[Field Rheostat] rheo --> shuntField[Shunt Field Winding
Resistance Rsh] shuntField --> termN note1[No Mechanical Load on Shaft] note2[Field current Ish = V by Rsh] note3[No-load armature current Ia0 = I0 minus Ish] note4[Constant Loss Wc = V times Ia0 minus Ia0 squared Ra] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Flow of Loss Segregation ```mermaid flowchart LR A[No-Load Input
V times I0] --> B[Subtract Ish contribution
V times Ish] B --> C[Armature Input
V times Ia0] C --> D[Subtract No-Load Cu Loss
Ia0 squared Ra] D --> E[Constant Loss Wc
Iron plus Friction plus Windage] E --> F[Add Variable Loss at Load x
x squared times Ial squared Ra] F --> G[Total Losses] G --> H[Subtract from Input or Add to Output] H --> I[Efficiency at Load x] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Tree: When to Use Swinburne's Test ```mermaid flowchart TD start[DCMachine to be tested] --> q1{Is it a series machine?} q1 -- Yes --> reject1[Do NOT use Swinburne
Use Brake Test or other loading method] q1 -- No --> q2{Is it a shunt or
cumulative compound?} q2 -- Yes --> q3[Use Swinburne Test] q2 -- Differentially compounded --> reject2[Avoid Swinburne
Behavior unstable at no-load] q3 --> q4[Run at rated V and N] q4 --> q5[Measure I0, V, Ra, Rsh] q5 --> q6[Compute Wc] q6 --> q7[Efficiency at any load x
without actual loading] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip:** The dotted lines (e.g., `-.->`) in Mermaid indicate measurement connections (voltmeter across the armature), while solid lines indicate power flow. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Swinburne's test. Why is it called a constant loss test?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Swinburne's test is an **indirect, no-load method** of predetermining the efficiency of a DC shunt (or cumulative compound) machine. The machine is run as a motor at rated voltage and rated speed under no mechanical load, and the no-load input power is measured. **Why "Constant Loss Test" (1.5 marks):** The test is called the constant loss test because the **no-load input to the armature (V·Ia₀) approximately equals the sum of iron loss, friction loss, and windage loss** — all of which remain essentially constant from no-load to full-load in a DC shunt machine (since flux and speed vary very little). This constant loss Wc can be computed once and used to determine efficiency at any load without actually loading the machine. --- #### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State any two reasons why Swinburne's test cannot be performed on a DC series machine.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **(1.5 marks)** In a DC series machine, the field winding is connected in **series with the armature**, so the field flux depends on the armature current. At no-load, the armature current is nearly zero, leading to a very weak field and a dangerously **high no-load speed** (theoretically infinite) — which can mechanically destroy the machine. 2. **(1.5 marks)** Because of the extremely weak field at no-load, the **back-EMF Ea = V − IaRa ≈ V** is very large, and the iron loss (which depends on flux²) is not constant at light loads — violating the basic assumption of Swinburne's test that constant losses remain fixed from no-load to full-load. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Solve either **Question A** or **Question B**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1]` **(a)** Explain the procedure of Swinburne's test for a DC shunt machine with the help of a neat circuit diagram. Also state the necessary precautions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 250 V DC shunt machine has armature resistance of 0.2 Ω and shunt field resistance of 250 Ω. At no-load, the input current is 5 A at rated voltage. Calculate the efficiency at full-load current of 50 A when running as (i) a motor and (ii) a generator. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A **(a) Procedure and Circuit Diagram (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Pre-Test Measurements (1 mark):** Measure the **armature resistance Ra** and **shunt field resistance Rsh** using a voltmeter-ammeter method or a Kelvin bridge. Calculate the rated shunt field current as $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$. **Step 2 — Circuit Connections (1.5 marks):** - Connect the armature of the DC shunt machine to a **DC supply of rated voltage V** through a **double-pole switch, ammeter A₀, and a 3-point starter**. - Connect a **voltmeter V across the armature terminals**. - Connect the **shunt field winding in parallel** with the armature, in series with an **ammeter Ash** and a **field rheostat**. - Ensure **no mechanical load** is coupled to the shaft. **Step 3 — Running the Test (1.5 marks):** - Close the switch and gradually increase the supply voltage using a regulator (or use the starter to limit inrush). - **Adjust the field rheostat** to obtain **rated speed** at **rated voltage** on the voltmeter. - Record the readings: no-load input current $I_0$ and supply voltage V. **Step 4 — Computation (1.5 marks):** - Calculate: $I_{a0} = I_0 - I_{sh}$ - Calculate: $W_c = V \cdot I_{a0} - I_{a0}^2 R_a$ (constant loss) - Use Wc to compute efficiency at any desired load. **Step 5 — Precautions (1.5 marks):** - The machine must run at **rated voltage and rated speed** during the test (since constant losses depend on V and N). - The **field rheostat should not be disturbed** after the no-load test, since changing field current changes the constant losses. - The **armature should be at room temperature** when measuring Ra, or the resistance must be corrected to the operating temperature (typically 75 °C) using $R_{hot} = R_{cold} \cdot (235 + T_{hot}) / (235 + T_{cold})$ for copper. - **Brushes must be in good condition**; brush contact loss is included in Wc. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $V = 250 \ \text{V}$, $R_a = 0.2 \ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 250 \ \Omega$, $I_0 = 5 \ \text{A}$ (no-load), $I_{al} = 50 \ \text{A}$ (full-load). **Step 1 (1 mark):** $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh} = 250 / 250 = 1 \ \text{A}$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** $I_{a0} = 5 - 1 = 4 \ \text{A}$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** $W_c = 250 \times 4 - (4)^2 \times 0.2 = 1000 - 3.2 = 996.8 \ \text{W}$ **Step 4 — Motor (1.5 marks):** - Armature Cu loss at FL = $(50)^2 \times 0.2 = 500 \ \text{W}$ - Total losses = $996.8 + 500 = 1496.8 \ \text{W}$ - Input = $250 \times (50 + 1) = 12{,}750 \ \text{W}$ - Output = $12{,}750 - 1496.8 = 11{,}253.2 \ \text{W}$ - $\eta_{\text{motor}} = (11{,}253.2 / 12{,}750) \times 100 = \mathbf{88.26\%}$ **Step 5 — Generator (1.5 marks):** - Generator $I_a = 50 + 1 = 51 \ \text{A}$ - Armature Cu loss = $(51)^2 \times 0.2 = 520.2 \ \text{W}$ - Total losses = $996.8 + 520.2 = 1517 \ \text{W}$ - Output = $250 \times 50 = 12{,}500 \ \text{W}$ - Input = $12{,}500 + 1517 = 14{,}017 \ \text{W}$ - $\eta_{\text{generator}} = (12{,}500 / 14{,}017) \times 100 = \mathbf{89.18\%}$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1]` **(a)** State the assumptions and limitations of Swinburne's test. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 200 V DC shunt motor has armature and field resistances of 0.5 Ω and 200 Ω respectively. On no-load, it draws 4 A from the supply. Estimate the efficiency of the motor when it draws 60 A from the supply. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B **(a) Assumptions and Limitations of Swinburne's Test (7 marks):** **Assumptions (3.5 marks):** 1. **(1 mark)** The **constant losses (iron + friction + windage) remain constant** from no-load to full-load, since flux and speed vary negligibly in a DC shunt machine at rated voltage. 2. **(1 mark)** The **shunt field current $I_{sh}$ is constant** throughout the load range (valid because V and Rsh are essentially constant). 3. **(1 mark)** The **armature reaction effect is neglected** — i.e., flux is assumed unchanged from no-load to full-load. (In reality, armature reaction slightly weakens the flux, slightly reducing iron loss at full load — a second-order correction.) 4. **(0.5 marks)** The **armature resistance $R_a$ does not change significantly** with temperature during the brief test. **Limitations (3.5 marks):** 1. **(1 mark)** **Not applicable to DC series machines** (as no-load speed becomes dangerously high). 2. **(1 mark)** **Stray load losses** (additional iron and copper losses at the commutating zone under load) are **not accounted for**; in reality, they are about 1% of output and are load-dependent. 3. **(1 mark)** Cannot be used for **determining temperature rise or commutation behavior** of the machine under actual load — these require a real load test. 4. **(0.5 marks)** The accuracy degrades for **small machines** where the no-load armature current is not negligible compared to full-load current. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $V = 200 \ \text{V}$, $R_a = 0.5 \ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 200 \ \Omega$, $I_0 = 4 \ \text{A}$ (no-load), $I_L = 60 \ \text{A}$ (at full load). **Step 1 (1 mark):** $I_{sh} = 200 / 200 = 1 \ \text{A}$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** $I_{a0} = 4 - 1 = 3 \ \text{A}$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** $W_c = 200 \times 3 - (3)^2 \times 0.5 = 600 - 4.5 = 595.5 \ \text{W}$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** $I_{al} = I_L - I_{sh} = 60 - 1 = 59 \ \text{A}$ (armature current at load) **Step 5 (1 mark):** Armature Cu loss = $(59)^2 \times 0.5 = 3481 \times 0.5 = 1740.5 \ \text{W}$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Total losses = $595.5 + 1740.5 = 2336 \ \text{W}$; Input = $200 \times 60 = 12{,}000 \ \text{W}$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Output = $12{,}000 - 2336 = 9664 \ \text{W}$ $$ \eta_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{9664}{12{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{80.53\%} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting to subtract $I_{sh}$:** Many students compute $W_c = V \cdot I_0$ without subtracting the shunt field current contribution. This is acceptable in the **approximate** method, but in exact problems, examiners may deduct marks if the difference is significant. > > 2. **Confusing motor and generator armature current:** In **motor mode**, the armature current $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ (line current minus field current). In **generator mode**, $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ (load current plus field current). Mixing these up gives wrong answers. > > 3. **Using the wrong formula for output:** In **motor mode**, output = input − losses. In **generator mode**, input = output + losses. Be careful with the sign convention. > > 4. **Including $I_0^2 R_a$ in full-load losses:** Students sometimes use the no-load current in the variable loss formula. The variable loss **must use the load armature current**, not the no-load current. > > 5. **Forgetting units:** Always show units (W, kW, A, V, Ω). KTU evaluators specifically check for unit consistency. > > 6. **Not stating assumptions:** In Part B theory questions (e.g., "Why is the test not done on series machines?"), failing to state the assumption about constant flux leads to partial mark loss. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before the Exam!** - ✅ Swinburne's Test = **No-Load Test = Constant Loss Test**, applicable to **DC shunt and cumulative compound machines only**. - ✅ Machine is run as a **motor at rated V and rated N**, with **no mechanical load** on the shaft. - ✅ Key quantities measured: $V$, $I_0$, $R_a$, $R_{sh}$. - ✅ **Constant loss:** $W_c = V \cdot I_{a0} - I_{a0}^2 R_a$, where $I_{a0} = I_0 - I_{sh}$. - ✅ **Variable loss** at load x: $x^2 I_{al}^2 R_a$ (motor) or $(xI_L + I_{sh})^2 R_a$ (generator). - ✅ **Motor efficiency** at load x: $\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{V(xI_{al} + I_{sh}) - W_c - x^2 I_{al}^2 R_a}{V(xI_{al} + I_{sh})}$. - ✅ **Generator efficiency** at load x: $\eta_g = \dfrac{V \cdot xI_L}{V \cdot xI_L + W_c + (xI_L + I_{sh})^2 R_a}$. - ✅ **Assumptions:** constant flux, constant $I_{sh}$, constant speed, neglected armature reaction. - ✅ **Limitations:** not for series machines, doesn't capture stray load losses, no thermal info, accuracy drops for small machines. - ✅ **Approximation trick:** $W_c \approx V \cdot I_0$ (used in problems where the no-load armature copper loss is negligible). - ✅ **Distinguish carefully:** Motor armature current = line current − $I_{sh}$; Generator armature current = line current + $I_{sh}$. - ✅ **Test advantages:** cheap, fast, no loading rig needed; **Disadvantages:** no-load test only, doesn't validate thermal or commutation behavior. - ✅ **Always** run at **rated voltage and rated speed** — constant losses are sensitive to both. - ✅ **Remember to correct Ra** to operating temperature (75 °C standard) when high precision is required. - ✅ **For any load x (not just full load), the variable loss scales as $x^2$** — this is a key KTU exam differentiator. - ✅ **Efficiency is highest** at the load where variable losses equal constant losses (i.e., at $x = \sqrt{W_c / I_{al}^2 R_a}$).

# Numerical Problems – Module 1: DC Machines

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> *Numerical Problems* in the context of **DC Machines (Module 1)** refer to quantitative exercises that require the application of the fundamental equations governing the electromechanical energy conversion process in **DC Generators** and **DC Motors**. These problems bridge the gap between theoretical derivations (EMF Equation, Torque Equation) and practical engineering design, involving the calculation of parameters like induced EMF, electromagnetic torque, speed, efficiency, and the verification of machine characteristics under various loading conditions.

## 1.1 Intuitive Overview — Why Do We Solve Numerical Problems in DC Machines?

Imagine you are an electrical engineer tasked with designing a **DC motor** for an electric vehicle's power window. You have a battery rated at **12 V**, but your motor needs to spin a gear at a specific **RPM (speed)** and deliver a particular **torque** to lift the glass. How do you know how many turns of wire to wind on the armature, or how strong the magnetic field needs to be?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pump" Model of a DC Machine:**
> Think of a **DC Generator** as a *water pump pushed by hand*. The harder you push (mechanical input), the more water flows out per second (electrical output). The voltage produced is like the water pressure, and the current is like the flow rate. The **EMF Equation** (`E = kΦN`) tells you the "pressure" you create by pushing coils through the magnetic field. Conversely, a **DC Motor** is like a *turbine driven by water* — the more voltage you apply, the faster the turbine spins, and the more current flows, the more twisting force (torque) it produces.
>
> Numerical problems are essentially **engineering math** that lets you size the "pump" or "turbine" precisely for your application.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Module 1:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Flux (Φ)**: Measured in **Weber (Wb)** — typically between **0.005 Wb** to **0.05 Wb** for small/medium machines.
> - **Speed (N)**: Measured in **Revolutions Per Minute (RPM)**.
> - **Angular Velocity (ω)**: Measured in **radians per second (rad/s)** — conversion: $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$.
> - **EMF (E) / Voltage (V)**: Measured in **Volts (V)**.
> - **Current (Iₐ)**: Armature current in **Amperes (A)**.
> - **Torque (T)**: Measured in **Newton-Meters (N·m)**.
> - **Power (P)**: Measured in **Watts (W)** or **Kilowatts (kW)**.
> - **Efficiency (η)**: Dimensionless ratio, expressed in **percentage (%)**.

---

## 1.2 Categories of Numerical Problems in Module 1

KTU Module 1 numerical problems typically fall into the following categories:

1. **EMF Equation Problems** — Calculating generated EMF or finding unknown parameters (Φ, N, Z, P, A) in a DC Generator.
2. **Torque Equation Problems** — Computing shaft torque, armature torque, and lost torque in a DC Motor.
3. **Power Flow & Efficiency Problems** — Determining input/output power, copper losses, iron losses, and overall efficiency.
4. **Speed Regulation & Characteristics** — Calculating no-load speed, full-load speed, and speed regulation.
5. **Armature Reaction & Commutation Problems** — Computing demagnetizing/total number of conductors.
6. **Combined Problems** — Multi-part questions involving all of the above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:**
> As per the revised Bloom's Taxonomy alignment in NEP 2020, Module 1 numerical problems typically test the cognitive levels **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)**. You are expected to *apply* formulas to compute values and *analyze* machine performance under varying load conditions.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation Philosophy (KTU Examiner's View):**
> Every numerical problem in DC Machines stems from **TWO master equations** — the **EMF Equation** and the **Torque Equation**. If you internalize these two and understand the relationships between electrical, mechanical, and magnetic domains, you can solve over **90% of Module 1 numerical problems**.

## 2.1 The EMF Equation (DC Generator Perspective)

When the armature of a DC machine rotates in a magnetic field, an EMF is induced in the conductors. The average EMF induced is given by:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

Where:
- $E_g$ = Generated EMF (in **Volts**)
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (in **Weber**)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of armature (in **RPM**)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths

> **Why this works:** Each conductor cuts the magnetic flux $\Phi$ a total of $\frac{P}{A}$ times per revolution, and completes $\frac{N}{60}$ revolutions per second. Multiplying by $Z$ gives total EMF per second, and dividing by 1 second gives the average EMF.

### Parallel Paths for Different Winding Types:
- **Lap Winding**: $A = P$
- **Wave Winding**: $A = 2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** For **wave winding**, $A = 2$ regardless of the number of poles. This means wave-wound machines have a *lower induced EMF per conductor* but *higher current capacity*. Lap-wound machines give *higher EMF* and are used for high-current applications.

---

## 2.2 The Torque Equation (DC Motor Perspective)

When a DC machine acts as a motor, current flows through the armature conductors placed in the magnetic field, producing a force and hence a torque:

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

Simplified form:

$$T_a = 0.159 \times \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} \quad (\text{in N·m})$$

Where:
- $T_a$ = Armature Torque (in **N·m**)
- $I_a$ = Armature current (in **Amperes**)

> **Physical Intuition:** Torque is the *mechanical analog of current flow*. Higher flux means stronger magnetic "grip", more conductors mean more force-producing wires, and higher current means more electrons experiencing that force.

---

## 2.3 The Power Equation (Bridge Between Electrical & Mechanical)

The electromagnetic (developed) power is the power converted from electrical to mechanical form (in motor) or vice versa (in generator):

$$P_{conv} = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$$

Where:
- $E_b$ = Back EMF (in motor) or Generated EMF $E_g$ (in generator) (in **V**)
- $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ = Angular velocity (in **rad/s**)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Key Condition |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | EMF Equation | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | $E_g$ in V, $\Phi$ in Wb, $N$ in RPM | All DC machines |
| 2 | Torque Equation | $T_a = 0.159 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $T_a$ in N·m, $I_a$ in A | DC Motor |
| 3 | Power Converted | $P_{m} = E_b \cdot I_a = \dfrac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$ | $P_{m}$ in W, $\omega$ in rad/s | Both modes |
| 4 | Shaft Torque | $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{friction}$ (loss torque) | $T_{sh}$ in N·m | Motor output |
| 5 | Input Power | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ | $V$ in V, $I_L$ in A | Motor input |
| 6 | Output Power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ | $P_{out}$ in W | Motor output |
| 7 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Both modes |
| 8 | Copper Loss | $P_{cu} = I_a^{2} R_a$ | $R_a$ in Ω | Armature |
| 9 | Speed (Motor) | $N = \dfrac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi Z P}$ | From EMF equation | Rearranged |
| 10 | Brush Contact Drop | $V_{brush} = 2 \times 1$ V (typical) | $V_{brush}$ in V | Practical loss |
| 11 | Wave Winding | $A = 2$ | Independent of $P$ | $m$-simplex |
| 12 | Lap Winding | $A = P$ | Equal to number of poles | $P$-simplex |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Unit Consistency is Worth 1 Mark:**
> Always ensure that flux is in **Weber (Wb)**, speed in **RPM** (convert to rev/s by dividing by 60 if needed), and that you use the **correct value of A** (2 for wave, P for lap). Many KTU students lose easy marks by mixing up units or winding types.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important in real engineering?**
> - **Electric Vehicle Design**: Determining torque and speed ratings of DC traction motors.
> - **Renewable Energy**: DC generators are precursors to modern wind turbine generators (AC, but with DC exciters).
> - **Industrial Automation**: Sizing DC servo motors for CNC machines and robotic arms.
> - **Battery-Powered Systems**: Estimating battery drain time using efficiency calculations.
> - **Power Plants**: Backup DC generators for emergency systems.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Solutions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section Objective:** This section contains **exhaustively detailed** step-by-step solutions to the most important types of numerical problems that appear in KTU University Examinations for Module 1. Every algebraic step and numerical evaluation is shown explicitly — no shortcuts, no skipped steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation (Conceptual Foundation)

**Starting Point:** Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction states that the EMF induced in a conductor is equal to the rate of change of flux linkage.

**Step 1:** Consider one conductor cutting the flux. In one revolution, the conductor cuts a flux equal to:

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = \Phi \times P$$

This is because there are $P$ poles, and as the conductor moves past each pole, it cuts the flux $\Phi$ once.

**Step 2:** For a **simplex wave-wound** machine, the number of parallel paths is $A = 2$, so the number of conductors in series per parallel path is $\frac{Z}{A} = \frac{Z}{2}$.

**Step 3:** Total flux cut per revolution by all conductors in series in one parallel path:

$$\text{Flux cut} = \Phi \times P \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 4:** Time taken for one revolution (in seconds) is:

$$t = \frac{60}{N}$$

**Step 5:** Average EMF per parallel path (and hence the terminal voltage of the machine, ignoring voltage drops):

$$E_g = \frac{\text{Flux cut}}{\text{Time per revolution}} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the master EMF equation. The same equation is used for both DC generators and DC motors, but in a motor, the induced EMF is called **Back EMF ($E_b$)** and it opposes the applied voltage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation (Conceptual Foundation)

**Starting Point:** Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is given by the Lorentz force law:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$

where $B$ is the flux density, $I$ is the current, and $l$ is the length of the conductor.

**Step 1:** The force on a single conductor is:

$$F = B \cdot I_c \cdot l$$

where $I_c = \frac{I_a}{A}$ is the current per conductor.

**Step 2:** Torque produced by one conductor is $F \times r$, where $r$ is the radius of the armature. For a multi-pole machine, the effective radius is $\frac{D}{2}$, but the torque derivation can be simplified by noting that the total torque is proportional to $F \times \frac{D}{2}$ summed over all conductors.

**Step 3:** Summing over all $Z$ conductors and using the relationship between flux density and flux:

$$B = \frac{\Phi}{A_{pole}} = \frac{\Phi P}{2\pi r l}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting and simplifying (after substantial algebra, which we skip in this conceptual view to focus on numericals):

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

**Step 5:** The constant $\frac{1}{2\pi} \approx 0.1591$, giving the practical form:

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{N·m}$$

---

## 3.3 Comprehensive Numerical Solutions (KTU Exam Pattern)

### 📘 Problem 1: EMF Equation (Lap-Wound DC Generator)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**
> *A 4-pole, lap-wound DC generator has 500 armature conductors and a flux per pole of 0.02 Wb. It rotates at 1500 RPM. Calculate the EMF generated. If the machine is rewound as wave-wound with the same conductors, what would be the new EMF at the same speed and flux?*

**Given Data:**
- Number of poles, $P = 4$
- Total armature conductors, $Z = 500$
- Flux per pole, $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb
- Speed, $N = 1500$ RPM

**Step 1: Identify the winding type and parallel paths.**

For lap winding: $A = P = 4$

**Step 2: Apply the EMF equation for lap winding.**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot P} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60}$$

(Notice how $P$ cancels out for lap winding!)

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{0.02 \times 500 \times 1500}{60}$$

**Step 4: Compute numerator first.**

$$0.02 \times 500 = 10$$

$$10 \times 1500 = 15000$$

**Step 5: Divide by 60.**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{15000}{60} = 250 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6: Rewind as wave winding. Now $A = 2$.**

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} = \frac{0.02 \times 500 \times 1500 \times 4}{60 \times 2}$$

**Step 7: Compute numerator.**

$$0.02 \times 500 = 10$$
$$10 \times 1500 = 15000$$
$$15000 \times 4 = 60000$$

**Step 8: Compute denominator.**

$$60 \times 2 = 120$$

**Step 9: Final division.**

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \frac{60000}{120} = 500 \text{ V}$$

**✅ Final Answers:**
- Lap-wound EMF: $E_g = 250$ V
- Wave-wound EMF: $E_g = 500$ V

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Learning:** Wave winding produces **twice the EMF** of lap winding (for a 4-pole machine), because the same conductors are now arranged in fewer parallel paths (2 instead of 4), putting more conductors in series per path.

---

### 📘 Problem 2: Torque and Power in a DC Motor

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> *A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω and a field winding resistance of 250 Ω. The motor draws a total current of 30 A at full load. The armature has 1000 conductors, 4 poles, is wave-wound, and runs at 1000 RPM. Calculate: (a) the flux per pole, (b) the shaft torque, and (c) the efficiency, assuming mechanical and iron losses total 400 W.*

**Given Data:**
- Terminal voltage, $V = 250$ V
- Armature resistance, $R_a = 0.5$ Ω
- Field resistance, $R_{sh} = 250$ Ω
- Line current, $I_L = 30$ A
- Conductors, $Z = 1000$
- Poles, $P = 4$ (wave wound, so $A = 2$)
- Speed, $N = 1000$ RPM
- Mechanical + Iron losses = 400 W

---

**Part (a): Flux per pole**

**Step 1: Calculate shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Calculate armature current using KCL.**

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 30 - 1 = 29 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate back EMF using the motor voltage equation.**

For a DC motor: $V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$

$$E_b = V - I_a \cdot R_a = 250 - (29 \times 0.5) = 250 - 14.5 = 235.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Apply the EMF equation and solve for $\Phi$.**

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$$

Rearranging for $\Phi$:

$$\Phi = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{Z \cdot N \cdot P}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the values.**

$$\Phi = \frac{235.5 \times 60 \times 2}{1000 \times 1000 \times 4}$$

**Step 6: Compute numerator.**

$$235.5 \times 60 = 14130$$
$$14130 \times 2 = 28260$$

**Step 7: Compute denominator.**

$$1000 \times 1000 = 10^6$$
$$10^6 \times 4 = 4 \times 10^6$$

**Step 8: Final division.**

$$\Phi = \frac{28260}{4000000} = 7.065 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi \approx 7.07 \text{ mWb}}$$

---

**Part (b): Shaft Torque**

**Step 1: Calculate the electromagnetic (armature) torque.**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \times 7.065 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 29 \times \frac{4}{2}$$

**Step 3: Simplify $\frac{P}{A}$.**

$$\frac{4}{2} = 2$$

**Step 4: Compute step-by-step.**

$$0.1591 \times 7.065 \times 10^{-3} = 1.124 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$1.124 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 1.124$$
$$1.124 \times 29 = 32.596$$
$$32.596 \times 2 = 65.19 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the loss torque (from mechanical and iron losses).**

$$T_{loss} = \frac{P_{loss}}{\omega} = \frac{P_{loss}}{\frac{2\pi N}{60}} = \frac{60 \cdot P_{loss}}{2\pi N}$$

**Step 6: Substitute and compute.**

$$T_{loss} = \frac{60 \times 400}{2\pi \times 1000} = \frac{24000}{6283.19} = 3.82 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 7: Calculate shaft torque.**

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss} = 65.19 - 3.82 = 61.37 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} \approx 61.37 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

**Part (c): Efficiency**

**Step 1: Calculate input power.**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 30 = 7500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Calculate output (shaft) power.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000 \times 61.37}{60}$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator.**

$$2\pi \times 1000 = 6283.19$$
$$6283.19 \times 61.37 = 385605.3 \text{ (approx.)}$$

**Step 4: Divide by 60.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{385605.3}{60} = 6426.76 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Calculate efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{6426.76}{7500} \times 100 = 85.69\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 85.7\%}$$

---

### 📘 Problem 3: Speed Regulation (DC Shunt Motor)

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern]**
> *A DC shunt motor runs at 1500 RPM on no load. At full load, the armature reaction weakens the flux by 5% and the armature voltage drop is 8 V. If the supply voltage is 220 V and the no-load back EMF is 212 V, calculate the full-load speed.*

**Given Data:**
- No-load speed, $N_0 = 1500$ RPM
- No-load back EMF, $E_{b0} = 212$ V
- Supply voltage, $V = 220$ V
- Armature drop at full load, $I_a R_a = 8$ V
- Flux reduction due to armature reaction, $\Phi_{FL} = 0.95 \cdot \Phi_{NL}$

**Step 1: Calculate full-load back EMF.**

$$E_{b,FL} = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 8 = 212 \text{ V}$$

(Interestingly, in this case the full-load $E_b$ equals the no-load $E_b$ value.)

**Step 2: Apply the speed formula. We know that:**

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P}$$

This means $N \propto \frac{E_b}{\Phi}$.

**Step 3: Form the ratio of full-load to no-load speed.**

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{N_{NL}} = \frac{E_{b,FL} / \Phi_{FL}}{E_{b,NL} / \Phi_{NL}} = \frac{E_{b,FL}}{E_{b,NL}} \cdot \frac{\Phi_{NL}}{\Phi_{FL}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the values.**

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{1500} = \frac{212}{212} \cdot \frac{1}{0.95}$$

$$\frac{N_{FL}}{1500} = 1 \times \frac{1}{0.95} = 1.0526$$

**Step 5: Solve for $N_{FL}$.**

$$N_{FL} = 1500 \times 1.0526 = 1578.9 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 6: Calculate speed regulation.**

$$\text{Regulation} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{1500 - 1578.9}{1578.9} \times 100 = -4.99\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative speed regulation** here means the motor runs *faster* at full load than at no load. This is unusual for a shunt motor and happens because the **armature reaction weakening effect (5%) overcompensates** for the small armature drop.

---

### 📘 Problem 4: Armature Reaction and Demagnetizing Conductors

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Pattern]**
> *A 4-pole, wave-wound DC generator has 800 armature conductors. The brushes are shifted by 4 mechanical degrees from the geometric neutral axis. Calculate the number of demagnetizing and cross-magnetizing armature conductors per pole.*

**Given Data:**
- Poles, $P = 4$
- Conductors, $Z = 800$ (wave-wound, $A = 2$)
- Brush shift, $\theta_{m} = 4°$ (mechanical)

**Step 1: Convert mechanical degrees to electrical degrees.**

$$\theta_{e} = \frac{P}{2} \times \theta_m = \frac{4}{2} \times 4° = 2 \times 4° = 8°$$

**Step 2: Calculate the angular span of demagnetizing zone.**

The demagnetizing zone is the angular range that lies *behind* the new brush position by the same angle $\theta_e$. So the demagnetizing zone spans $2\theta_e$ in electrical degrees:

$$\text{Demag zone} = 2\theta_e = 2 \times 8° = 16°$$

**Step 3: Calculate demagnetizing conductors per pole.**

$$\text{Conductors per pole (electrical)} = \frac{Z}{2P} = \frac{800}{8} = 100 \text{ conductors/pole}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the fraction of demagnetizing zone.**

$$\text{Frac} = \frac{2\theta_e}{180°} = \frac{16°}{180°} = 0.0889$$

**Step 5: Number of demagnetizing conductors per pole.**

$$Z_d = 0.0889 \times 100 = 8.89 \approx 9 \text{ conductors/pole}$$

**Step 6: Total demagnetizing conductors.**

$$Z_{d,\text{total}} = Z_d \times P = 9 \times 4 = 36 \text{ conductors}$$

**Step 7: Cross-magnetizing conductors per pole.**

$$Z_c = 100 - 9 = 91 \text{ conductors/pole}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{d,\text{total}} = 36, \quad Z_c = 91 \text{ per pole}}$$

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Bank Notice:** All questions in this section follow the **exact pattern of KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations (ESE)**. The Part A questions are 3-mark direct-concept questions, and Part B questions are 14-mark internal-choice problems with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Model solutions with complete step-by-step working are provided.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**State the EMF equation of a DC generator. Explain the significance of the term "number of parallel paths" (A) in the equation.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> The EMF equation of a DC generator is:
>
> $$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$
>
> where $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $Z$ = total armature conductors, $N$ = speed (RPM), $P$ = number of poles, and $A$ = number of parallel paths.
>
> **Significance of A (1 Mark):**
> The number of parallel paths depends on the winding type: $A = P$ for lap winding and $A = 2$ for wave winding. It determines how the armature conductors are interconnected and directly affects both the **generated EMF** and the **current capacity** of the machine. **Wave winding** (A=2) produces higher EMF with lower current rating, while **lap winding** (A=P) produces lower EMF with higher current rating.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define "armature torque" and "shaft torque" of a DC motor. How are they related?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> - **Armature Torque ($T_a$):** The torque developed by the armature conductors due to the interaction of armature current and magnetic flux. It is the gross torque produced inside the machine. (1.5 Marks)
>
> $$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{N·m}$$
>
> - **Shaft Torque ($T_{sh}$):** The net torque available at the motor shaft for doing useful mechanical work, after subtracting the losses due to friction, windage, and iron losses. (1.5 Marks)
>
> **Relationship:** $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss}$, where $T_{loss}$ is the torque lost to mechanical and iron losses.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
> *A 250 V, 4-pole, lap-wound DC shunt motor has 800 armature conductors. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω and the field resistance is 125 Ω. The motor takes a total current of 60 A at full load. The flux per pole is 30 mWb, and the mechanical and iron losses total 800 W.*

**Calculate the following:**
- **(a)** The back EMF, armature current, and speed at full load. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** The shaft torque, output power, and efficiency at full load. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $V = 250$ V, $P = 4$, lap-wound so $A = 4$
- $Z = 800$, $R_a = 0.2$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 125$ Ω
- $I_L = 60$ A, $\Phi = 0.03$ Wb
- Mechanical + Iron losses = 800 W

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{125} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Armature current.**

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 60 - 2 = 58 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Back EMF.**

$$E_b = V - I_a \cdot R_a = 250 - (58 \times 0.2) = 250 - 11.6 = 238.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Speed using EMF equation (rearranged for N).**

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P} = \frac{238.4 \times 60 \times 4}{0.03 \times 800 \times 4}$$

**Step 5: Numerator.**

$$238.4 \times 60 = 14304$$
$$14304 \times 4 = 57216$$

**Step 6: Denominator.**

$$0.03 \times 800 = 24$$
$$24 \times 4 = 96$$

**Step 7: Division.**

$$N = \frac{57216}{96} = 596 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 238.4 \text{ V}, \quad I_a = 58 \text{ A}, \quad N = 596 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Armature torque.**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A} = 0.1591 \times 0.03 \times 800 \times 58 \times \frac{4}{4}$$

**Step 2: Simplification (P/A = 1 for lap winding).**

$$T_a = 0.1591 \times 0.03 \times 800 \times 58 \times 1$$

**Step 3: Compute step-by-step.**

$$0.1591 \times 0.03 = 4.773 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$4.773 \times 10^{-3} \times 800 = 3.8184$$
$$3.8184 \times 58 = 221.47 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Loss torque from mechanical and iron losses.**

$$T_{loss} = \frac{60 \times P_{loss}}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 800}{2\pi \times 596} = \frac{48000}{3744.7} = 12.82 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Shaft torque.**

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_{loss} = 221.47 - 12.82 = 208.65 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6: Output power.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 596 \times 208.65}{60}$$

**Step 7: Numerator computation.**

$$2\pi \times 596 = 3744.74$$
$$3744.74 \times 208.65 = 781415.5$$

**Step 8: Division.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{781415.5}{60} = 13023.6 \text{ W} \approx 13.02 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 9: Input power.**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 60 = 15000 \text{ W} = 15 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 10: Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{13023.6}{15000} \times 100 = 86.82\%$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} = 208.65 \text{ N·m}, \quad P_{out} = 13.02 \text{ kW}, \quad \eta = 86.82\%}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**
> *A 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt generator has 600 armature conductors. The armature and field resistances are 0.3 Ω and 200 Ω, respectively. The flux per pole is 0.025 Wb, and the generator delivers a load current of 100 A at 250 V.*

**Calculate the following:**
- **(a)** The generated EMF and the speed of the generator. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** The armature copper loss, total losses, and efficiency of the generator. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $P = 4$, wave-wound, $A = 2$
- $Z = 600$, $R_a = 0.3$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 200$ Ω
- $\Phi = 0.025$ Wb
- Load current $I_L = 100$ A, terminal voltage $V = 250$ V

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate shunt field current.**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{200} = 1.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Calculate armature current (same as load current in a generator when $I_L$ is the line current; here, the load is connected to the terminals, so $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$).**

$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 1.25 = 101.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the voltage drop in the armature.**

$$I_a \cdot R_a = 101.25 \times 0.3 = 30.375 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the generated EMF (DC generator voltage equation: $E_g = V + I_a R_a + V_{brush}$).**

Assuming brush contact drop of 2 V (standard assumption: 1 V per brush):

$$E_g = 250 + 30.375 + 2 = 282.375 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Calculate speed from the EMF equation.**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$$

$$N = \frac{E_g \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P} = \frac{282.375 \times 60 \times 2}{0.025 \times 600 \times 4}$$

**Step 6: Numerator.**

$$282.375 \times 60 = 16942.5$$
$$16942.5 \times 2 = 33885$$

**Step 7: Denominator.**

$$0.025 \times 600 = 15$$
$$15 \times 4 = 60$$

**Step 8: Division.**

$$N = \frac{33885}{60} = 564.75 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{E_g = 282.375 \text{ V}, \quad N = 564.75 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Armature copper loss.**

$$P_{cu,a} = I_a^{2} \cdot R_a = (101.25)^2 \times 0.3$$

**Step 2: Compute $(101.25)^2$.**

$$(101.25)^2 = 10251.5625$$

**Step 3: Multiply by $R_a$.**

$$P_{cu,a} = 10251.5625 \times 0.3 = 3075.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Field copper loss.**

$$P_{cu,sh} = I_{sh}^{2} \cdot R_{sh} = (1.25)^2 \times 200 = 1.5625 \times 200 = 312.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Brush contact loss (assuming 2 V total drop and $I_a$ flowing through brushes).**

$$P_{brush} = V_{brush} \cdot I_a = 2 \times 101.25 = 202.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6: Total electrical losses (assuming no mechanical/iron loss data given, only electrical losses).**

$$P_{loss,total} = P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,sh} + P_{brush} = 3075.47 + 312.5 + 202.5 = 3590.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7: Output power delivered to the load.**

$$P_{out} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 100 = 25000 \text{ W} = 25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8: Input mechanical power.**

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss,total} = 25000 + 3590.47 = 28590.47 \text{ W}$$

**Step 9: Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{25000}{28590.47} \times 100 = 87.44\%$$

$$\boxed{P_{cu,a} = 3075.47 \text{ W}, \quad P_{loss,total} = 3590.47 \text{ W}, \quad \eta = 87.44\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
>
> 1. **Confusing A = P (lap) vs A = 2 (wave):** This single mistake can lead to a 50% error in your EMF and Torque calculations. Always re-read the problem statement for the winding type.
> 2. **Forgetting the field current in $I_a$ calculation:** In a **motor**, $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$. In a **generator**, $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the brush contact drop:** Many students assume $E_g = V$ in generators and $E_b = V$ in motors, ignoring the $I_a R_a$ drop. You will lose 1-2 marks if this is missing.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing RPM and rad/s for angular velocity. KTU expects RPM in the EMF equation directly, and rad/s in the power equation $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$.
> 5. **Shaft vs. Armature Torque confusion:** The question may ask for "shaft torque" but students compute "armature torque" and skip the loss torque subtraction.
> 6. **Power equation sign errors:** Output = Input − Losses. Always check the direction of energy flow before writing the equation.
> 7. **No unit on final answer:** A numerical answer without units (V, A, RPM, N·m, W, %) is considered incomplete and will lose 0.5–1 mark per instance.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 Numerical Problems**

- ✅ The **EMF Equation** $E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ is the **master equation** for all DC machine voltage calculations.
- ✅ For **Lap Winding**: $A = P$. For **Wave Winding**: $A = 2$ (independent of poles).
- ✅ The **Torque Equation** $T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$ applies to **motors only**; in generators, this represents the mechanical torque required.
- ✅ The **Voltage Equation**:
   - Motor: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (back EMF opposes applied voltage)
   - Generator: $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ (generated EMF exceeds terminal voltage)
- ✅ The **Current Equation**:
   - Motor: $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$
   - Generator: $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$
- ✅ The **Power Conversion Equation**: $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \omega = \frac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$.
- ✅ **Shaft Torque** = Armature Torque − Loss Torque.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$, always between 0% and 100%.
- ✅ **Speed Regulation** $= \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$. For shunt motors, it's typically +1% to +5% (positive).
- ✅ In **armature reaction** problems: convert mechanical degrees to electrical degrees using $\theta_e = \frac{P}{2} \theta_m$.
- ✅ **Demagnetizing conductors per pole** $= \frac{Z}{2P} \cdot \frac{2\theta_e}{180°}$.
- ✅ **Cross-magnetizing conductors per pole** = (Total conductors per pole) − (Demagnetizing conductors per pole).
- ✅ Always carry **units** through your calculation: Wb, RPM, V, A, Ω, N·m, W, %.
- ✅ **Conversion check**: $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ — if you forget to convert, you'll get errors in power/torque calculations.
- ✅ **Sanity check your answer**: EMF should be in tens to hundreds of volts; torque in single to hundreds of N·m; efficiency typically 75–95%.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Phase Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Single Phase Transformers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Phase Transformer:** A single phase transformer is a **static electromagnetic energy conversion device** that transfers electrical energy from one alternating current (AC) circuit to another through **mutual electromagnetic induction**, without any change in frequency, while stepping up or stepping down the voltage (and consequently the current) based on the turns ratio between its primary and secondary windings.

The transformer operates on the fundamental principle of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, discovered by **Michael Faraday in 1831**, and the physical structure comprises two or more magnetically coupled coils wound on a common **laminated ferromagnetic core** (typically **CRGO - Cold Rolled Grain Oriented silicon steel**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The module covers single-phase transformer construction, working principle, EMF equation, ideal & practical transformer concepts, equivalent circuit, phasor diagram on no-load and on-load, voltage regulation, losses, efficiency, and open-circuit (OC) and short-circuit (SC) tests.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have a fast-flowing, narrow stream of water. You want to deliver that same water but with **less pressure (voltage)** so it can be safely used in a small garden sprinkler. A **transformer** is essentially an "electrical pressure converter" — it is the electrical equivalent of a **gear system** or a **hydraulic press**.

- **Input Side (Primary):** Receives high voltage, low current (like a thin fast stream).
- **Output Side (Secondary):** Delivers low voltage, high current (like a wide slow stream).
- **The Magnetic Core (Iron Heart):** Acts as the common pathway that "carries" the energy between the two sides, but no actual electrical wire connects them.

The key magical rule is: **Voltage ratio = Turns ratio**. If the primary has 1000 turns and the secondary has 100 turns, the output voltage will be exactly **1/10th** of the input voltage. The energy is conserved (minus small losses), so the current goes up by 10 times.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Static?** Unlike motors and generators, a transformer has **no moving parts**. It is one of the most efficient electrical machines ever built, often reaching **98% to 99% efficiency** in large power transformers.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|-----------|----------------------|
| Supply frequency (f) | **50 Hz** (India), **60 Hz** (US) |
| Operating flux density (B) | **1.1 to 1.4 Tesla** for CRGO steel |
| Lamination thickness | **0.35 mm** or **0.5 mm** |
| Standard kVA ratings | 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500, 1000 kVA |
| Efficiency range | **95% to 99%** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
