**A long-shunt compound DC generator delivers a load current of 100 A at 250 V. It has armature resistance of 0.05 Ω, series field resistance of 0.04 Ω, and shunt field resistance of 125 Ω. The brush contact drop is 1 V per brush (assume 2 brushes). The armature reaction weakens the flux by 5%.**

**(a)** Calculate the EMF generated by the machine at full load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the machine is run as a motor on the same supply voltage with the same armature current, calculate its speed, armature torque, and shaft power, assuming rotational losses are 1500 W. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — EMF at Full Load:**

**Step 1:** Shunt field current:
$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{250}{125} = 2 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 2:** Armature current (long-shunt):
$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 2 = 102 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 3:** Total voltage drops in the armature circuit:
$$\text{Drop} = I_a R_a + I_{se} R_{se} + \text{Brush drop} = (102 \times 0.05) + (100 \times 0.04) + (2 \times 1)$$

$$\text{Drop} = 5.1 + 4.0 + 2.0 = 11.1 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 4:** Generated EMF:
$$E_g = V + \text{Drop} = 250 + 11.1 = 261.1 \ \text{V}$$

> ✅ **Answer (a):** $E_g = 261.1$ V **(7 marks: 1M for $I_{sh}$, 1M for $I_a$, 2M for drops, 1M for equation, 2M for final answer)**

---

**Part (b) — Operating as a Motor:**

**Step 1:** When operated as a motor, the back-EMF:
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a - \text{Brush drop} = 250 - 5.1 - 2.0 = 242.9 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Since armature reaction reduces the flux by 5% in the generator mode, in the motor mode (at the same armature current), the flux is similarly reduced. Let $\Phi_0$ be the no-load flux. The flux at full load:
$$\Phi_{FL} = 0.95 \, \Phi_0$$

**Step 3:** EMF equation: $E \propto \Phi N$ (for fixed $P, Z, A$). Since the generator and motor operate at the *same armature current* and the *same physical machine*, the flux is the same in both modes (assuming the same magnetisation characteristic). Thus, the EMF is proportional to speed:

$$\dfrac{E_b}{E_g} = \dfrac{N_m}{N_g}$$

Given that the generator speed $N_g$ is the rated speed (let's call it $N_g$ for the no-load condition; the full-load speed of a compound generator is typically within 2–5% of no-load for a long-shunt cumulative). Assuming $N_g = 1500$ rpm (standard for this class of machine):

$$N_m = N_g \times \dfrac{E_b}{E_g} = 1500 \times \dfrac{242.9}{261.1} = 1500 \times 0.9303 = 1395.4 \ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 4:** Armature torque (using the relation $T_a = 60 \times E_b \times I_a / (2\pi N_m)$):

$$T_a = \dfrac{60 \times 242.9 \times 102}{2\pi \times 1395.4}$$

$$T_a = \dfrac{1{,}486{,}548}{8767.4} = 169.55 \ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 5:** Shaft power:
$$\omega_m = \dfrac{2\pi N_m}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1395.4}{60} = 146.13 \ \text{rad/s}$$

$$P_{sh} = T_{sh} \times \omega_m$$

Shaft torque (subtracting rotational losses):
$$T_{sh} = T_a - \dfrac{P_{\text{rot}}}{\omega_m} = 169.55 - \dfrac{1500}{146.13} = 169.55 - 10.27 = 159.28 \ \text{N·m}$$

$$P_{sh} = 159.28 \times 146.13 = 23{,}273 \ \text{W} \approx 23.27 \ \text{kW}$$

> ✅ **Answer (b):** $N_m \approx 1395$ rpm, $T_a \approx 169.55$ N·m, $P_{sh} \approx 23.27$ kW **(7 marks: 2M for $E_b$, 2M for $N_m$, 2M for $T_a$, 1M for $P_{sh}$)**

---

### **🟢 Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]

**A 250 V DC shunt motor draws a current of 5 A at no load and runs at 1200 rpm. The armature resistance is 0.3 Ω and the shunt field resistance is 250 Ω.**

**(a)** Estimate the efficiency of the motor at **full-load** (line current = 50 A) and at **1.25 times full-load** using Swinburne's test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Hopkinson's test** is to be performed on two identical machines. Calculate the **circulating current** and the **excitation currents** required when the test is conducted to determine the efficiency at a **load equivalent to 50 A output current**. Armature resistance of each machine = 0.2 Ω, total iron + friction loss = 1000 W, supply voltage = 250 V. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Swinburne's Test Efficiency:**

**Step 1:** Shunt field current (constant):
$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{250}{250} = 1 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 2:** No-load armature current:
$$I_{a0} = 5 - 1 = 4 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 3:** No-load armature copper loss:
$$P_{cu,a,0} = 4^2 \times 0.3 = 16 \times 0.3 = 4.8 \ \text{W}$$

**Step 4:** Constant losses:
$$P_{\text{const}} = V I_{NL} - I_{a0}^2 R_a = 250 \times 5 - 4.8 = 1250 - 4.8 = 1245.2 \ \text{W}$$

(Alternative formulation: $P_{\text{const}} = 1250 - 4.8 - 1^2 \times 250 = 1245.2 - 250 = 995.2$ W if we want to exclude shunt Cu loss. The first formulation is standard for KTU.)

**Step 5 — At Full Load ($I_L = 50$ A):**

$$I_{a,FL} = 50 - 1 = 49 \ \text{A}$$
$$P_{cu,a,FL} = 49^2 \times 0.3 = 2401 \times 0.3 = 720.3 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{in,FL}} = 250 \times 50 = 12{,}500 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{out,FL}} = 12{,}500 - 720.3 - 1^2 \times 250 - 1245.2 = 12{,}500 - 720.3 - 250 - 1245.2 = 10{,}284.5 \ \text{W}$$

Wait — there's a double-counting issue. The standard Swinburne approach is:

$$P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{cu,a}} - P_{\text{cu,sh}} - P_{\text{const}}$$

where $P_{\text{const}}$ already includes the rotational losses AND the shunt copper loss. Let me redo:

**Corrected Swinburne calculation:**

The total no-load input = 1250 W. At no load, this entire input goes to cover: armature Cu loss (4.8 W), shunt Cu loss (250 W), and rotational losses (which is the rest).

Rotational losses = $1250 - 4.8 - 250 = 995.2$ W (this is the *true* constant loss assumed independent of load, since it represents friction, windage, and core losses at constant speed and flux).

So the **total losses at any load** = $I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + P_{\text{rot}}$

**At Full Load:**
$$I_a = 49 \ \text{A}, \quad P_{cu,a} = 720.3 \ \text{W}, \quad P_{cu,sh} = 250 \ \text{W}, \quad P_{\text{rot}} = 995.2 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{in}} = 12{,}500 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{out}} = 12{,}500 - 720.3 - 250 - 995.2 = 10{,}534.5 \ \text{W}$$
$$\eta_{FL} = \dfrac{10{,}534.5}{12{,}500} \times 100\% = 84.28\%$$

**At 1.25 × Full Load ($I_L = 62.5$ A):**
$$I_a = 61.5 \ \text{A}, \quad P_{cu,a} = 61.5^2 \times 0.3 = 3782.25 \times 0.3 = 1134.7 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{in}} = 250 \times 62.5 = 15{,}625 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{out}} = 15{,}625 - 1134.7 - 250 - 995.2 = 13{,}245.1 \ \text{W}$$
$$\eta_{1.25} = \dfrac{13{,}245.1}{15{,}625} \times 100\% = 84.77\%$$

> ✅ **Answer (a):** $\eta_{FL} = 84.28\%$, $\eta_{1.25 \times FL} = 84.77\%$ **(7 marks: 1M for $P_{\text{const}}$, 2M for FL calc, 2M for 1.25×FL calc, 2M for efficiency values)**

---

**Part (b) — Hopkinson's Test:**

**Setup:** Two identical machines — one acts as a **motor (M)** and the other as a **generator (G)**, mechanically coupled. The generator supplies power back to the motor, so the supply only needs to provide the *losses*.

**Step 1:** Total losses at the test condition = Iron + friction losses = 1000 W (for both machines combined; assume split 50:50 → 500 W each).

The supply voltage is 250 V. The total input from the supply is:

$$P_{\text{supply}} = 1000 \ \text{W (total losses)}$$

$$I_{\text{supply}} = \dfrac{1000}{250} = 4 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 2:** The "load equivalent to 50 A" means the *output* of the generator is 50 A. In a Hopkinson test, the **armature current of the generator** = load current = 50 A, and the armature current of the motor is slightly more (to cover the losses).

**Step 3:** Let the armature current of the motor = $I_{am}$ and of the generator = $I_{ag} = 50$ A.

The circulating current equation (for machines connected in parallel on the same supply):
$$I_{am} = I_{ag} + I_{\text{supply}} = 50 + 4 = 54 \ \text{A}$$

(Wait — this assumes the supply provides the differential current directly. In some Hopkinson configurations, the supply current is shared between the two field windings.)

Standard Hopkinson formulation:
$$I_{\text{circulating}} = I_a = 50 \ \text{A (in each armature)}$$
$$I_{\text{line from supply}} = \dfrac{\text{Total losses}}{V} = \dfrac{1000}{250} = 4 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 4:** Excitation currents:

For the **motor**: $I_{am} = 50 + 4/2 = 52$ A (or simplified $50 + 2 = 52$ A if the supply current splits equally)

For the **generator**: $I_{ag} = 50 - 2 = 48$ A

Field currents are typically small (1–2% of rated). The excitation current $I_f$ for each machine:

If the field winding resistance is, say, $R_{sh} = 250 \ \Omega$ (assumed standard for this rating):
$$I_f = \dfrac{250}{250} = 1 \ \text{A per machine}$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency at this load:**

Power developed by the motor:
$$P_{\text{motor,dev}} = E_b \times I_{am} \approx V \times I_{am} = 250 \times 52 = 13{,}000 \ \text{W}$$

Power developed by the generator:
$$P_{\text{gen,dev}} = E_g \times I_{ag} = 250 \times 48 = 12{,}000 \ \text{W}$$

Power output of the generator (to load):
$$P_{\text{out,gen}} = V \times I_{load} = 250 \times 50 = 12{,}500 \ \text{W}$$

Total losses in the system (both machines) = $P_{\text{cu,armatures}} + P_{\text{iron,fric}} = 2 \times I_a^2 R_a + 1000$

$$P_{\text{losses}} = 2 \times 50^2 \times 0.2 + 1000 = 1000 + 1000 = 2000 \ \text{W}$$

Efficiency of the generator:
$$\eta_{\text{gen}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{out}} + \text{losses}_{\text{gen}}} = \dfrac{12{,}500}{12{,}500 + (50^2 \times 0.2 + 500)}$$

$$\eta_{\text{gen}} = \dfrac{12{,}500}{12{,}500 + 1000} = \dfrac{12{,}500}{13{,}500} = 92.59\%$$

> ✅ **Answer (b):** $I_{\text{circulating}} = 50$ A, $I_{\text{motor,arm}} = 52$ A, $I_{\text{gen,arm}} = 48$ A, $I_{\text{field}} = 1$ A, $\eta_{\text{gen}} = 92.59\%$

> **Mark Split:** Setup diagram (1M), Circulating current (2M), Excitation calculations (2M), Efficiency (2M)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in DC Machine Numericals:**
> 
> **1. Wrong Sign of Voltage Equation (–2 to –3 marks):**
> Students write $E_g = V - I_a R_a$ for a generator. This is **WRONG**. The correct form is $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ because the armature current flows *from* the machine to the load, causing a voltage *drop* in the opposite direction.
> 
> **2. Forgetting to Convert rpm to rad/s (–1 mark):**
> In torque-power problems, $P = T \omega$ requires $\omega$ in rad/s. The conversion is $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. Forgetting this gives an answer off by a factor of ~9.55.
> 
> **3. Confusion Between Lap and Wave Winding (–2 marks):**
> - **Lap:** $A = P$ (more current, less voltage)
> - **Wave:** $A = 2$ (less current, more voltage)
> 
> **4. Ignoring Brush Contact Drop (–1 to –2 marks):**
> Brush drop is typically **1 V per brush** (or 2 V total). KTU problems often include this — skipping it is an easy mark loss.
> 
> **5. Armature Reaction Sign Convention:**
> Armature reaction *weakens* the main field flux in a generator (demagnetizing effect) and *strengthens* it in a motor (cross-magnetizing + demagnetizing net effect). The 4–6% flux reduction must be applied to the *main field flux* before computing the EMF.
> 
> **6. Swinburne Double-Counting:**
> Don't subtract the shunt field Cu loss *twice* — once as a "constant loss" and again explicitly. The $V I_{NL}$ input already accounts for it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — DC Machine Numericals (Module 2)**
> 
> ✅ **EMF Equation:** $E = P \Phi NZ / 60A$ — Memorize the variable meanings.
> 
> ✅ **Torque Equation:** $T_a = 0.159 \times (PZ/A) \times \Phi \times I_a$ — Note: $N$ is **NOT** in this equation.
> 
> ✅ **Lap vs Wave:** $A = P$ (lap) / $A = 2$ (wave) — Choose correctly based on the problem.
> 
> ✅ **Generator Equation:** $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ (+ brush drop).
> 
> ✅ **Motor Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (+ brush drop).
> 
> ✅ **Shunt Field Current:** $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ — Always computed first in shunt/compound problems.
> 
> ✅ **Armature Current:**
> - **Shunt generator:** $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$
> - **Shunt motor:** $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$
> - **Long-shunt compound:** Same as shunt (but $V$ drop includes $R_{se}$).
> 
> ✅ **Power Conversions:**
> - $P_{\text{developed}} = E_g \times I_a = T_a \times \omega$
> - $P_{\text{output}} = T_{sh} \times \omega = P_{\text{dev}} - P_{\text{rot}}$
> - $P_{\text{input}} = V \times I_L$ (motor) / $P_{\text{output of gen}}$
> 
> ✅ **Efficiency:**
> $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Total losses}}{P_{\text{in}}}$$
> 
> ✅ **Swinburne's Test:**
> - $P_{\text{const}} = V I_{NL} - I_{a0}^2 R_a$ (or equivalent)
> - Apply to **any load** without actually loading the machine.
> 
> ✅ **Brake Test:**
> - $T_{sh} = (W_1 - W_2) \times r$ in N·m
> - Suitable for small/medium motors only.
> 
> ✅ **Hopkinson's Test (Regenerative):**
> - Requires two **identical** machines mechanically coupled.
> - Supply provides only the *total losses*.
> - Can compute efficiency at any load.
> 
> ✅ **Speed Regulation:**
> $$\text{Reg} = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$$
> 
> ✅ **Unit Conversions:**
> - $1 \ \text{kW} = 1000 \ \text{W}$
> - $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ (rad/s)
> - $1 \ \text{N·m} \approx 10.197 \ \text{kgf·cm}$ (rarely needed)
> 
> ✅ **Final Sanity Checks:**
> - Generator: $E_g > V$ always
> - Motor: $E_b < V$ always
> - Efficiency $\leq 100\%$ always
> - Torque sign: positive for motoring, negative for generating
> - In Swinburne/Hopkinson, **rotational losses are constant** (assumed independent of load).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operation on No Load and on Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operation on No Load and on Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Module 2: Operation on No Load and on Load

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):**
> A **transformer** is a static electrical machine that transfers electrical energy between two or more circuits through **electromagnetic induction** without any change in frequency. The terms "**No-Load Operation**" and "**On-Load Operation**" describe the two extreme working conditions of a transformer: when the secondary is open (no current drawn) and when the secondary delivers rated current to a connected load, respectively.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water pressure booster system** in a building:

- **No-Load Condition** = You turn on the booster pump, but no taps are open. The pump still draws a small amount of current to keep the motor idling and maintain magnetic "pressure" (flux) in the system. This idling current is small (typically **2–5%** of rated current) but essential to create the magnetic field.

- **On-Load Condition** = Now you open the taps. Water (current) flows. The pump motor works harder, draws more current, and there is a slight **drop in pressure** at the outlet (voltage drop) because of internal friction (resistance) and water-hammer effects (leakage reactance). The pump has to compensate for this drop.

In a transformer:
- The **magnetizing current** is like the no-load pump current (creates flux).
- The **load current** causes voltage drops across the winding resistance and leakage reactance, which is why the secondary terminal voltage changes with load.
- The **primary draws more current** from the source to maintain energy balance (Ampere-turn balance).

### 📌 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Frequency** ($f$): Standard power frequencies are **50 Hz** (India, Europe) and **60 Hz** (USA, Japan).
- **No-Load Current ($I_0$)**: Typically **2% to 5%** of full-load current for power transformers; can be up to **20-30%** for small distribution transformers.
- **Power Factor at No-Load ($\cos \phi_0$)**: Very low, typically **0.1 to 0.2 lagging**, because the current is dominated by the magnetizing component.
- **Turns Ratio ($a$)**: $a = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$ (ideal case).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** The most common mistake students make is confusing **magnetizing current ($I_m$)** with **no-load current ($I_0$)**. The no-load current has TWO components: the magnetizing component $I_m$ (creates flux) and the core-loss component $I_w$ (supplies hysteresis + eddy current losses). $I_0 = \sqrt{I_w^2 + I_m^2}$.

### ⚙️ Why "No-Load" and "On-Load" Studies Matter

- **No-load test** gives us the **core (iron) losses** and the **magnetizing branch parameters** ($R_c$, $X_m$).
- **On-load analysis** reveals the **voltage regulation**, **efficiency**, and the **equivalent circuit parameters** ($R_{01}, X_{01}$).
- Together, these two operating points define the **complete performance** of a transformer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 No-Load Operation: Step-by-Step Physics

When the **secondary winding is open-circuited** and the **rated primary voltage $V_1$ is applied**:

1. A small alternating flux $\phi$ is set up in the core, alternating at the supply frequency $f$.
2. The flux induces an EMF $E_1$ in the primary (opposing $V_1$, by Lenz's Law) and an EMF $E_2$ in the secondary.
3. The primary draws a small no-load current $I_0$ from the source.
4. $I_0$ has two orthogonal components:
   - **$I_w$ (Working / Core-Loss Component)**: In phase with $V_1$; supplies iron losses.
   - **$I_m$ (Magnetizing Component)**: Lags $V_1$ by 90°; creates the working flux.
5. The no-load power factor angle $\phi_0$ is small (typically **70° to 85°**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **E-Balance at No-Load:**
> $$V_1 = -E_1 + I_0 Z_{primary}$$
> Since $I_0$ is very small, the primary resistance drop $I_0 R_1$ and leakage reactance drop $I_0 X_1$ are negligible. Therefore, $V_1 \approx E_1$. This is the foundation of the **EMF equation of a transformer**.

### 🔑 The EMF Equation

For a transformer winding with $N$ turns, maximum flux $\Phi_m$ Weber, and supply frequency $f$ Hz:

$$E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$$

- The constant **4.44** comes from the RMS conversion of a sinusoidal flux: $E_{rms} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \Phi_m$.
- For the primary: $E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_1$
- For the secondary: $E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_2$

> [!NOTE]
> The ratio $\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = a$ is the **transformation ratio** (or turns ratio). When $a > 1$, it's a **step-down** transformer; when $a < 1$, it's a **step-up** transformer.

## 2.2 On-Load Operation: Step-by-Step Physics

When a **load is connected** to the secondary:

1. Secondary current $I_2$ flows through the load (and the secondary winding).
2. $I_2$ creates its own flux $\phi_2$ that **opposes** the main flux (Lenz's Law).
3. To maintain the original flux $\phi$ (so that $E_1$ can balance $V_1$), the primary draws **additional current** $I_2'$ from the source.
4. This new current $I_2'$ creates a flux $\phi_2'$ that **cancels** $\phi_2$ — this is **Ampere-Turn Balance**: $N_1 I_2' = N_2 I_2$.
5. The total primary current becomes: $I_1 = I_0 + I_2'$.
6. The secondary terminal voltage $V_2$ falls below $E_2$ due to internal drops:
   $$V_2 = E_2 - I_2(R_2 + jX_2)$$

### ⚖️ Ampere-Turn (MMF) Balance Equation

The fundamental equation that keeps the core flux constant:

$$N_1 I_1 = N_1 I_0 + N_2 I_2$$

Or, after dividing by $N_1$:

$$I_1 = I_0 + \dfrac{I_2}{a}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **why** the primary current increases when load is connected — the transformer is essentially a **flux-maintaining device**, and any change in secondary MMF is counteracted by an equal and opposite primary MMF.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$ | EMF per winding | $f$ in Hz, $\Phi_m$ in Wb, $N$ is turns |
| $\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = a$ | Turns ratio | Dimensionless |
| $I_0 = \sqrt{I_w^2 + I_m^2}$ | No-load current | $I_0, I_w, I_m$ in Amperes |
| $I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0$ | Core-loss component | Amperes |
| $I_m = I_0 \sin\phi_0$ | Magnetizing component | Amperes |
| $P_i = V_1 I_0 \cos\phi_0$ | Iron (core) loss | $V_1$ in V, $I_0$ in A, Watts |
| $N_1 I_1 = N_1 I_0 + N_2 I_2$ | MMF balance | Ampere-turns |
| $I_2' = \dfrac{I_2}{a}$ | Load current referred to primary | Amperes |
| $\% \text{Reg} = \dfrac{V_{2,NL} - V_{2,FL}}{V_{2,FL}} \times 100$ | Voltage regulation | Percentage |
| $\eta = \dfrac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi + P_i + P_{cu}}$ | Efficiency | Dimensionless (×100 for %) |
| $P_{cu} = I_1^2 R_{01} = I_2^2 R_{02}$ | Copper loss | Watts |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Alert:** In the regulation formula, do not confuse $V_{2,NL}$ (no-load secondary voltage = $E_2$ when referred) with $V_{2,FL}$ (full-load terminal voltage). Using $V_{2,NL}$ as the denominator is a very common error in KTU exams.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Transformer Design**: No-load loss and load loss are the two primary constraints. Modern amorphous-core transformers reduce $P_i$ by **70-80%**.
- **Distribution Networks**: Voltage regulation directly impacts the **sag** experienced by end consumers. Indian DISCOMs typically specify a regulation of less than **5%** at full load.
- **Audio Transformers**: The on-load behavior (impedance matching) is critical for coupling amplifier stages to loudspeakers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: EMF Equation $E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$

**Starting Point:** A sinusoidal alternating flux is established in the core.

$$\phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 1:** The flux links each of the $N$ turns. By Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, the instantaneous EMF induced in a single turn is the negative rate of change of flux:

$$e_{1\text{-turn}} = -\dfrac{d\phi}{dt} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}[\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)]$$

$$e_{1\text{-turn}} = -\Phi_m \, \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** The total instantaneous EMF across $N$ turns in series is $N$ times the single-turn EMF:

$$e_N = -N \Phi_m \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3:** The maximum value (peak) of this EMF occurs when $\cos(\omega t) = -1$:

$$E_{max} = N \Phi_m \omega$$

**Step 4:** Convert angular frequency $\omega$ to supply frequency $f$ using $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$E_{max} = N \Phi_m (2\pi f) = 2\pi f N \Phi_m$$

**Step 5:** Convert peak value to RMS value by dividing by $\sqrt{2}$:

$$E_{rms} = \dfrac{E_{max}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2\pi f N \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the constant $\dfrac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2\pi}{1.4142} = 4.442 \approx 4.44$:

$$E_{rms} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$$

$$\boxed{E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Ampere-Turn Balance from Lenz's Law

**Assumption:** Core flux $\Phi_m$ remains essentially **constant** from no-load to full-load (this is what makes transformers efficient).

**Step 1:** At no-load, the only MMF acting on the core is $N_1 I_0$:

$$\text{MMF}_{no\text{-}load} = N_1 I_0$$

**Step 2:** When load is connected, secondary current $I_2$ creates a demagnetizing MMF $N_2 I_2$:

$$\text{MMF}_{secondary} = N_2 I_2 \quad \text{(opposes main flux)}$$

**Step 3:** To keep $\Phi_m$ constant, the primary must draw an additional current $I_2'$ such that its MMF exactly cancels the secondary MMF:

$$N_1 I_2' = N_2 I_2$$

$$I_2' = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} I_2 = \dfrac{I_2}{a}$$

**Step 4:** Total primary current is the phasor sum (not arithmetic, because $I_0$ and $I_2'$ are not in phase):

$$\vec{I_1} = \vec{I_0} + \vec{I_2'}$$

**Step 5:** Multiplying both sides by $N_1$ gives the MMF balance:

$$N_1 \vec{I_1} = N_1 \vec{I_0} + N_2 \vec{I_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This balance holds **phasorially**, not just arithmetically. In KTU problems, when drawing the phasor diagram, $I_0$ and $I_2'$ are added as vectors, not scalars.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: No-Load vs On-Load Comparison

| **Parameter** | **No-Load Condition** | **On-Load Condition** |
|---|---|---|
| Secondary Current ($I_2$) | **0** (open circuit) | $I_2 = \dfrac{V_2}{Z_L}$ |
| Primary Current ($I_1$) | $I_0$ (small, 2-5% of FL) | $I_1 = I_0 + I_2/a$ (high) |
| Core Flux ($\Phi_m$) | Set up by $I_0$ alone | Maintained by combined MMF |
| Primary Voltage Equation | $V_1 \approx E_1$ | $V_1 = E_1 + I_1(R_1 + jX_1)$ |
| Secondary Voltage Equation | $V_2 = E_2$ | $V_2 = E_2 - I_2(R_2 + jX_2)$ |
| Power Input | Iron loss $P_i$ only | $P_i$ + Copper loss $P_{cu}$ |
| Power Output | 0 (no load) | $V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2$ |
| Power Factor | Very low (0.1 - 0.2) | Determined by load |
| KTU Practical Test | **OC Test** (gives $R_c, X_m$) | **SC Test** (gives $R_{eq}, X_{eq}$) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 No-Load Phasor Diagram (Schematic)

The no-load phasor diagram is the **first phasor diagram** every KTU student must master. Below is the Mermaid block-diagram representation of the construction sequence:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Step 1: Draw Flux Phi as Reference"] --> B["Step 2: Draw E1 and E2 Lagging Phi by 90 degrees"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Draw I_m in Phase with Phi"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Draw V1 equal and opposite to E1 since I0 is tiny"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Draw I_w in Phase with V1"]
    E --> F["Step 6: I0 is Phasor Sum of I_w and I_m"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Mark phi0 as Angle between V1 and I0"]
```

**Construction Steps in Detail:**

1. Take the **mutual flux $\phi$** as the reference (horizontal, along positive x-axis).
2. The induced EMFs $E_1$ and $E_2$ **lag** the flux by **90°** (negative y-axis).
3. The magnetizing component $I_m$ is **in phase** with $\phi$ (along positive x-axis).
4. The applied voltage $V_1 \approx -E_1$ (along positive y-axis, opposite to $E_1$).
5. The working (core-loss) component $I_w$ is **in phase** with $V_1$ (along positive y-axis).
6. $I_0$ is the phasor sum: $\vec{I_0} = \vec{I_w} + \vec{I_m}$.
7. $\phi_0$ is the no-load power factor angle (between $V_1$ and $I_0$).

## 4.2 On-Load Phasor Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Step 1: Draw Phi as Reference"] --> B["Step 2: Draw E2 Lagging Phi by 90 deg"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Mark V2 = E2 minus I2 R2 minus I2 X2 drop"]
    C --> D["Step 4: I2 is Drawn at Power Factor Angle phi2 from V2"]
    D --> E["Step 5: E1 = V1 minus I1 R1 minus I1 X1 primary drop"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Draw I0 and I2 dash, I1 = I0 plus I2 dash"]
```

**Construction Steps in Detail:**

1. Draw flux $\phi$ as reference.
2. Draw $E_2$ lagging by 90°.
3. From the tip of $E_2$, subtract the resistance drop $I_2 R_2$ (parallel to $I_2$) and leakage reactance drop $I_2 X_2$ (perpendicular, leading $I_2$ by 90°). The result is $V_2$.
4. Draw $I_2$ at the load power factor angle $\phi_2$ from $V_2$ (lagging for inductive load).
5. To construct $E_1$: start from $-V_1$ (or equivalently, mark $V_1$ as input), add the primary drops.
6. Draw $I_0$ at angle $\phi_0$ from $V_1$ (small, lagging).
7. Reverse the direction of $I_2$ (since secondary flux opposes primary), then $I_2' = -I_2 / a$.
8. $I_1 = I_0 + I_2'$ (phasor sum).

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit on Load (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["V1 Applied"] --> B["R1 + jX1 Primary Leakage"]
    B --> C["Magnetizing Branch: Rc parallel Xm"]
    C --> D["R2 prime + jX2 prime Secondary Leakage Referred"]
    D --> E["V2 prime Output"]
```

**Description of the Equivalent Circuit:**

- **$R_1, X_1$**: Primary winding resistance and leakage reactance.
- **$R_c$** (Core Loss Resistance): Models iron losses; in parallel with $X_m$.
- **$X_m$** (Magnetizing Reactance): Models the magnetizing current that creates $\phi$.
- **$R_2', X_2'$**: Secondary winding resistance and leakage reactance, **referred to primary** (i.e., multiplied by $a^2$).
- **$V_2'$**: Secondary terminal voltage referred to primary ($= a V_2$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Practice:** Always refer quantities to **one side** (primary or secondary) before solving. The choice depends on which side has the most data given in the problem. The standard "approximate equivalent" is when we shift the shunt branch ($R_c \parallel X_m$) to either the input or output terminals to simplify calculations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define "No-Load Operation" of a transformer. What is the typical range of no-load current as a percentage of full-load current?**

**Model Answer:**

> **No-load operation** of a transformer refers to the operating condition when the **secondary winding is open-circuited** (no load is connected) and **rated primary voltage is applied** to the primary winding.
>
> Under this condition, the primary draws a small current $I_0$ from the source to:
> (i) Create the working magnetic flux in the core (magnetizing component $I_m$).
> (ii) Supply the core (iron) losses (working component $I_w$).
>
> The no-load current is typically **2% to 5%** of the full-load current for power transformers, and may be higher (up to 20-30%) for small distribution transformers.
>
> The no-load power factor $\cos\phi_0$ is very poor (0.1 to 0.2 lagging) because $I_m \gg I_w$.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the EMF equation of a transformer and explain the significance of the constant 4.44.**

**Model Answer:**

> The EMF equation of a transformer is:
>
> $$E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$$
>
> where:
> - $E$ = RMS value of induced EMF (Volts)
> - $f$ = supply frequency (Hz)
> - $\Phi_m$ = maximum value of core flux (Wb)
> - $N$ = number of turns
>
> **Significance of 4.44:** The constant is derived from $\dfrac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}}$.
> - $2\pi$ arises from the sinusoidal nature of the flux ($\omega = 2\pi f$).
> - $\sqrt{2}$ arises from the conversion of peak EMF to RMS EMF.
> - This constant is what makes the EMF equation specifically applicable to **sinusoidal** AC supplies.

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 2 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a single-phase transformer. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 5 kVA, 200 V/400 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has 80 turns on the secondary winding. Calculate:
   (i) The number of primary turns.
   (ii) The approximate value of primary and secondary full-load currents.
   (iii) The maximum value of flux in the core. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Derivation of EMF Equation

**Step 1:** Let the alternating flux in the core be sinusoidal:

$$\phi = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) \text{ Weber}$$

**Step 2:** By Faraday's Law, the EMF induced per turn is:

$$e = -\dfrac{d\phi}{dt} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}(\Phi_m \sin\omega t) = -\omega \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3:** The peak value of this EMF is:

$$E_{max} = \omega \Phi_m = 2\pi f \Phi_m$$

**Step 4:** For $N$ turns, the total peak EMF is:

$$E_{max,total} = 2\pi f \Phi_m N$$

**Step 5:** The RMS value is obtained by dividing by $\sqrt{2}$:

$$E_{rms} = \dfrac{2\pi f \Phi_m N}{\sqrt{2}} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$$

$$\boxed{E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N}$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given Data:**

- Rating: $S = 5$ kVA
- Primary Voltage: $V_1 = 200$ V (LV side, since fewer turns)
- Secondary Voltage: $V_2 = 400$ V
- Frequency: $f = 50$ Hz
- Secondary turns: $N_2 = 80$

**Step 1 — Find the Turns Ratio:**

$$a = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{200}{400} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

So $V_1 < V_2$ — this is a **step-up transformer**.

**Step 2 — Number of Primary Turns:**

$$N_1 = a \times N_2 = 0.5 \times 80 = \boxed{40 \text{ turns}}$$

**Step 3 — Full-Load Primary Current ($I_{1,FL}$):**

$$I_{1,FL} = \dfrac{S}{V_1} = \dfrac{5000}{200} = \boxed{25 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 4 — Full-Load Secondary Current ($I_{2,FL}$):**

$$I_{2,FL} = \dfrac{S}{V_2} = \dfrac{5000}{400} = \boxed{12.5 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Flux in the Core:**

Using the secondary EMF equation (at no-load, $V_2 = E_2$):

$$E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_2$$

$$400 = 4.44 \times 50 \times \Phi_m \times 80$$

$$400 = 4.44 \times 50 \times 80 \times \Phi_m$$

$$400 = 17760 \times \Phi_m$$

$$\Phi_m = \dfrac{400}{17760} = 0.02252 \text{ Wb} = \boxed{22.52 \text{ mWb}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the operation of a transformer on no-load with a neat phasor diagram. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 10 kVA, 2000 V/200 V, 50 Hz, single-phase transformer has its secondary delivering rated current to a load at 0.8 power factor lagging. If the total resistance and leakage reactance referred to the primary are $R_{01} = 5\,\Omega$ and $X_{01} = 8\,\Omega$ respectively, find:
   (i) The voltage regulation.
   (ii) The primary applied voltage $V_1$ (approximate, neglecting no-load branch).
   (iii) The copper loss at full load. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — No-Load Operation

**Theory:**

When the secondary is open-circuited and rated primary voltage $V_1$ is applied, a small no-load current $I_0$ flows in the primary. $I_0$ has two components:
- $I_m$: in phase with flux, creates the working flux.
- $I_w$: in phase with $V_1$, supplies core (iron) losses.

The induced EMFs $E_1$ and $E_2$ lag the flux by 90°. Since $I_0$ is very small, the voltage drops $I_0 R_1$ and $I_0 X_1$ are negligible, so $V_1 \approx -E_1$.

**Phasor Diagram (Descriptive, since Mermaid cannot draw vectors):**

```
                          I_w ↑    V1 ↑
                              \    /
                               \  /
                                \/  φ0
                  I_0 _________/‾‾________________→
                              /\
                             /  \
                       I_m ↗      E1, E2
                                  (downward)
```

**Construction sequence:**
1. $\phi$ → reference (horizontal).
2. $E_1, E_2$ → 90° lagging (downward).
3. $I_m$ → in phase with $\phi$ (right).
4. $V_1$ → 180° from $E_1$ (upward).
5. $I_w$ → in phase with $V_1$ (upward).
6. $I_0$ → phasor sum of $I_w$ and $I_m$.
7. $\phi_0$ → angle between $V_1$ and $I_0$ (large, lagging).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given Data:**

- $S = 10$ kVA, $V_1 = 2000$ V, $V_2 = 200$ V, $f = 50$ Hz
- Power factor $\cos\phi_2 = 0.8$ lagging
- $R_{01} = 5\,\Omega$, $X_{01} = 8\,\Omega$ (referred to primary)

**Step 1 — Turns Ratio:**

$$a = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{2000}{200} = 10$$

**Step 2 — Full-Load Secondary Current:**

$$I_{2,FL} = \dfrac{S}{V_2} = \dfrac{10000}{200} = 50 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent Full-Load Primary Current (Referred):**

$$I_1 = \dfrac{I_2}{a} = \dfrac{50}{10} = 5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage Regulation (Approximate Formula):**

For lagging power factor:

$$\%\text{Reg} = \dfrac{I_1(R_{01}\cos\phi + X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V_1} \times 100$$

With $\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\sin\phi = 0.6$:

$$\%\text{Reg} = \dfrac{5 \times (5 \times 0.8 + 8 \times 0.6)}{2000} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Reg} = \dfrac{5 \times (4 + 4.8)}{2000} \times 100 = \dfrac{5 \times 8.8}{2000} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Reg} = \dfrac{44}{2000} \times 100 = \boxed{2.2\%}$$

**Step 5 — Copper Loss at Full Load:**

$$P_{cu} = I_1^2 \cdot R_{01} = (5)^2 \times 5 = 25 \times 5 = \boxed{125 \text{ W}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of consistency:** $P_{cu} = I_2^2 R_{02}$ where $R_{02} = R_{01}/a^2 = 5/100 = 0.05\,\Omega$. Then $P_{cu} = 50^2 \times 0.05 = 2500 \times 0.05 = 125$ W. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in $V_1 \approx -E_1$:** At no-load, $V_1$ and $E_1$ are 180° out of phase. Drawing them in the same direction loses **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$ factor:** Writing the EMF equation as $E = 4.44 f N \Phi$ is correct, but if a student derives it as $E = 2\pi f N \Phi_m$, they are giving peak value, not RMS — partial credit only.
> 3. **Using wrong side for current:** In part (b), students often use $I_{2,FL} = 50$ A directly with $R_{01}$ to compute $P_{cu}$, getting $50^2 \times 5 = 12500$ W (a 100× error!). Always refer the current to the same side as the resistance.
> 4. **Phasor vs Scalar Addition:** Writing $I_1 = I_0 + I_2'$ as an arithmetic sum is a **3-mark loss**. It is a phasor sum.
> 5. **Units in $\Phi_m$:** Always express flux in **Weber (Wb)** or **milliWeber (mWb)**, not just "Weber number". Consistency earns full marks.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **EMF equation**: $E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$ — derived from Faraday's Law for sinusoidal flux.
- ✅ **No-load current** = magnetizing ($I_m$) + core-loss ($I_w$) components, **phasorially summed**.
- ✅ **No-load power factor** is very poor (0.1–0.2 lagging) — the current is almost entirely magnetizing.
- ✅ **At no-load**: $V_1 \approx E_1$ (because $I_0$ is tiny and internal drops are negligible).
- ✅ **On-load principle**: The transformer maintains core flux **constant** by **Ampere-Turn balance**: $N_1 I_1 = N_1 I_0 + N_2 I_2$.
- ✅ **Voltage regulation** = (No-load voltage − Full-load voltage) / Full-load voltage × 100.
- ✅ **Referred values**: $R_2' = a^2 R_2$, $X_2' = a^2 X_2$, $V_2' = a V_2$, $I_2' = I_2 / a$ — always refer to one side before solving.
- ✅ **Practical KTU tip**: For voltage regulation with lagging PF, use $\% \text{Reg} = \dfrac{I_1(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)}{V_1} \times 100$.
- ✅ **OC test** (no-load) gives **iron loss** $P_i$ and **magnetizing branch parameters** ($R_c, X_m$).
- ✅ **SC test** (on-load equivalent) gives **copper loss** $P_{cu}$ and **leakage parameters** ($R_{eq}, X_{eq}$).
- ✅ **Phasor diagram sequence**: Always start with $\phi$ as reference; then EMFs lag by 90°; voltages are drawn after subtracting internal drops.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phasor Diagram at Different Load Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Phasor Diagram at Different Load Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phasor Diagram of a Transformer at Different Load Conditions:** A vectorial (phasor) representation that graphically depicts the magnitude and phase relationships between primary and secondary voltages, induced EMFs, magnetizing current, load current, and various voltage drops (resistive and reactive) in a single-phase (or three-phase) transformer operating under varying load power factors — namely **no-load, lagging power factor, leading power factor, and unity power factor** conditions.

The phasor diagram is constructed on the common reference of the mutual flux **Φ**, which serves as the universal reference phasor because it links the primary and secondary windings electromagnetically.

## 1.2 Key Phasors in a Transformer

| Phasor Symbol | Meaning | Phase Relationship |
|---------------|---------|--------------------|
| $\vec{\Phi}$ | Mutual/Core flux | Reference (0°) |
| $E_1$ | Primary induced EMF | Lags $\vec{\Phi}$ by **90°** |
| $E_2$ | Secondary induced EMF | Lags $\vec{\Phi}$ by **90°** (in phase with $E_1$) |
| $V_1$ | Primary applied voltage | Leads $\vec{\Phi}$ by an angle $\theta_0$ (slightly < 90°) |
| $V_2$ | Secondary terminal voltage | Lags $\vec{\Phi}$ by ~90° (but reduced by drops) |
| $I_0$ | No-load current | Leads $\vec{\Phi}$ by magnetizing angle |
| $I_\mu$ | Magnetizing component of $I_0$ | In phase with $\vec{\Phi}$ |
| $I_w$ | Core-loss (working) component of $I_0$ | In phase with $V_1$ |
| $I_2$ | Secondary load current | Depends on load impedance & power factor |
| $I_1$ | Primary current | Vector sum of $I_0$ and reflected $I_2$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tug-of-War" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a transformer as a **water pump system** with two pipes connected by a flexible membrane (the core).
> - The **membrane (flux Φ)** is the only common element.
> - One pipe has high-pressure water flowing in (primary side), the other pipe supplies the load (secondary side).
> - The **pressure (voltage)** in each pipe and the **flow rate (current)** change depending on what's connected at the load end (a heavy load, light load, or no load).
> - The phasor diagram is essentially a **dashboard showing pressures, flows, and friction losses** in both pipes at the same instant — making it easy to see *why* the output pressure drops when the load increases.

## 1.4 Why "Different Load Conditions"?

A transformer is rarely operated at a single fixed load. Industrial loads include:
- **Induction motors** → lagging pf (inductive)
- **Synchronous motors (over-excited)** → leading pf (capacitive)
- **Heaters / Resistors** → unity pf (resistive)
- **Lightly loaded systems** → near no-load

The phasor diagram shifts **subtly but significantly** under each case, affecting:
- Voltage regulation
- Power factor on the primary side
- Core saturation behavior
- Real and reactive power flow direction

This makes the phasor diagram the **single most important analytical tool** for transformer performance evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Principles (The "Why" Behind Each Step)

The phasor diagram is built on **four fundamental laws**:

1. **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction:**
   Both $E_1$ and $E_2$ are induced by the same flux $\Phi$ → they are **in phase** with each other, both lagging $\Phi$ by 90°.

2. **Lenz's Law:**
   The induced EMFs **oppose** the cause that produces them → convention taken as EMF phasor lagging flux phasor.

3. **KVL on Primary Side:**
   $$V_1 = -E_1 + I_1 R_1 + j I_1 X_1$$
   (The applied voltage must overcome the back-EMF and internal drops.)

4. **KVL on Secondary Side:**
   $$E_2 = V_2 + I_2 R_2 + j I_2 X_2$$
   (The induced EMF supplies the terminal voltage and internal drops.)

5. **Magnetizing Current Decomposition:**
   $$I_0 = I_w + j I_\mu$$
   - $I_w$ → in phase with $V_1$ (represents core loss)
   - $I_\mu$ → lags $V_1$ by 90°, in phase with $\Phi$ (magnetization)

## 2.2 Construction Logic — Step by Step (Universal Method)

The phasor diagram is drawn in the same **fixed sequence** for every load condition. The only thing that changes is the **direction/length** of $I_2$:

**Step 1:** Take $\Phi$ as horizontal reference (drawn along positive x-axis).
**Step 2:** Draw $E_1$ and $E_2$ lagging $\Phi$ by 90° (along negative y-axis).
**Step 3:** Draw $I_0$ leading $\Phi$ by magnetizing angle $\alpha_0$ (typically 70°–80°).
**Step 4:** Decompose $I_0$ into $I_w$ (along $V_1$) and $I_\mu$ (along $\Phi$).
**Step 5:** Determine $I_2$ direction based on load pf.
**Step 6:** Compute $I_1 = I_0 + I_2'$ where $I_2' = I_2/K$ (referred to primary).
**Step 7:** Apply primary KVL: draw $-E_1$ leading $\Phi$ by 90°, then add $I_1R_1$ (parallel to $I_1$) and $jI_1X_1$ (perpendicular to $I_1$, leading) to obtain $V_1$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m$ | Primary induced EMF (RMS) | Volts |
| 2 | $E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_m$ | Secondary induced EMF (RMS) | Volts |
| 3 | $K = N_1/N_2 = E_1/E_2$ | Transformation ratio | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $I_2' = I_2 / K$ | Secondary current referred to primary | Amperes |
| 5 | $V_1 = -E_1 + I_1 R_1 + j I_1 X_1$ | Primary KVL equation | Volts |
| 6 | $E_2 = V_2 + I_2 R_2 + j I_2 X_2$ | Secondary KVL equation | Volts |
| 7 | $I_0 = I_w + j I_\mu$ | No-load current decomposition | Amperes |
| 8 | $I_1 = I_0 + I_2'$ | Primary current (MMF balance) | Amperes |
| 9 | $\cos\phi_0 = I_w / I_0$ | No-load power factor | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\cos\phi_2 = \text{load pf}$ | Secondary load power factor | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $VR = (V_{2,NL} - V_{2,FL})/V_{2,FL} \times 100$ | Voltage regulation (ideal approx.) | % |
| 12 | $\theta_1 = \angle V_1 - \angle I_1$ | Primary power factor angle | Radians |
| 13 | $\tan\alpha_0 = I_\mu/I_w$ | Magnetizing angle computation | Dimensionless |
| 14 | $P_{in} = V_1 I_1 \cos\phi_1$ | Input real power | Watts |
| 15 | $P_{out} = V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2$ | Output real power | Watts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - At **no-load:** $I_2 = 0$ → $I_1 = I_0$ (very small, lagging)
> - At **lagging pf:** $I_2$ lags $V_2$; $V_2 < E_2$; regulation is **positive**
> - At **leading pf:** $I_2$ leads $V_2$; $V_2$ can exceed $E_2$; regulation is **negative**
> - At **unity pf:** $I_2$ in phase with $V_2$; intermediate behavior

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | How Phasor Diagram Helps |
|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Power System Design** | Determines voltage drop and regulation for transformer selection |
| **Power Quality Analysis** | Reveals leading/lagging current trends for pf correction |
| **Protection Coordination** | Shows short-circuit current magnitudes and angles |
| **Loss Estimation** | Computes copper losses ($I_1^2 R_1 + I_2^2 R_2$) accurately |
| **Capacitor Bank Sizing** | Leading-pf operation helps in choosing compensation ratings |
| **Tap Changer Design** | Quantifies voltage variation across load spectrum |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Primary Current from MMF Balance

The MMF produced by the primary current $I_1$ must balance the counter MMF of the secondary current plus the no-load excitation MMF:

$$N_1 I_1 = N_0 I_0 + N_2 I_2$$

Dividing throughout by $N_1$:

$$I_1 = I_0 + \frac{N_2}{N_1} I_2 = I_0 + \frac{I_2}{K}$$

This justifies Step 6 of the construction. The two components $I_0$ and $I_2/K$ are **vectorially added** (not algebraically) because they are phasors with different phase angles.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Voltage Regulation Expression (Approximate)

Starting from the secondary KVL:

$$E_2 = V_2 + I_2 R_2 + j I_2 X_2$$

Taking magnitudes and applying the small-angle approximation (since $I_2 R_2$ and $I_2 X_2$ are typically much smaller than $V_2$):

$$E_2 \approx V_2 + I_2 (R_2 \cos\phi_2 + X_2 \sin\phi_2)$$

Therefore:

$$V_{2,NL} - V_{2,FL} = I_2 (R_2 \cos\phi_2 + X_2 \sin\phi_2)$$

The **per-unit voltage regulation** becomes:

$$VR = \frac{I_2 (R_2 \cos\phi_2 + X_2 \sin\phi_2)}{V_{2,FL}}$$

**Crucial observation:** The sign of $\sin\phi_2$ determines whether regulation is positive (lagging pf, $\phi_2 > 0$) or negative (leading pf, $\phi_2 < 0$).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behavior Under Each Load Condition

| Parameter | No-Load | Unity PF | Lagging PF | Leading PF |
|-----------|---------|----------|------------|------------|
| $I_2$ magnitude | 0 | $I_2$ | $I_2$ | $I_2$ |
| $I_2$ phase vs $V_2$ | — | In phase | Lags | Leads |
| $I_1$ magnitude | $I_0$ (tiny) | Moderate | Largest (worst case) | Moderate |
| $V_2$ vs $E_2$ | $V_2 \approx E_2$ | $V_2 < E_2$ | $V_2 < E_2$ (max drop) | $V_2 > E_2$ (rises) |
| Voltage regulation | 0% | Small positive | Positive (highest) | Negative |
| Primary pf $\cos\phi_1$ | Very low (lag) | Slightly lagging | Lagging (low) | May become leading |
| Reactive power flow | $Q$ absorbed by $X_1$ | $Q$ absorbed by $X_1 + X_2$ | High $Q$ absorbed | $Q$ supplied back |

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Typical Numerical)

> **Given:** A 5 kVA, 200 V/400 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has the following parameters referred to HV side: $R_{01} = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $X_{01} = 1.2 \, \Omega$. Find the voltage regulation at full load with 0.8 pf lagging.

**Step 1 — Compute Full-Load Current (HV side):**

$$I_{2,FL} = \frac{S}{V_{HV}} = \frac{5000}{400} = 12.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Resolve $I_2$ into components relative to $V_2$:**

$$I_2 \cos\phi_2 = 12.5 \times 0.8 = 10.0 \text{ A (in phase with } V_2)$$

$$I_2 \sin\phi_2 = 12.5 \times 0.6 = 7.5 \text{ A (lagging } V_2 \text{ by 90°)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Voltage Drop Components:**

$$\text{Resistive drop} = I_2 R_{01} \cos\phi_2 = 12.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.8 = 5.0 \text{ V}$$

$$\text{Reactive drop} = I_2 X_{01} \sin\phi_2 = 12.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.6 = 9.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Total Approximate Voltage Drop:**

$$\Delta V \approx I_2 (R_{01} \cos\phi_2 + X_{01} \sin\phi_2) = 5.0 + 9.0 = 14.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Voltage Regulation:**

$$VR = \frac{\Delta V}{V_{2,FL}} \times 100 = \frac{14.0}{400} \times 100 = 3.5\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** For 0.8 pf **lagging** operation, regulation is **positive** (3.5%), which matches our analytical matrix — lagging loads cause the largest voltage drops.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Construction Pipeline

The phasor diagram is built in a **strict ordered pipeline**. The Mermaid flowchart below maps the construction logic so you can never forget a step during the exam:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Reference Phasor"] --> B["Step 1: Draw Mutual Flux Phi along +X axis"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Draw E1 and E2 lagging Phi by 90 deg along -Y"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Draw I0 leading Phi by alpha_0 angle"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Decompose I0 into Iw along V1 direction and Imu along Phi"]
    E --> F{"Step 5: Load Condition?"}
    F -->|No Load| G["I2 = 0; I1 = I0; V2 = E2"]
    F -->|Unity PF| H["I2 in phase with V2; I1 = I0 + I2/K"]
    F -->|Lagging PF| I["I2 lags V2 by phi_2; I1 is largest"]
    F -->|Leading PF| J["I2 leads V2 by phi_2; V2 may exceed E2"]
    G --> K["Step 6: Apply Secondary KVL: E2 = V2 + I2R2 + jI2X2"]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L["Step 7: Apply Primary KVL: V1 = -E1 + I1R1 + jI1X1"]
    L --> M["Step 8: Measure angles for power factor calculation"]
    M --> N["End: Complete Phasor Diagram"]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Phasor Layout — ASCII Vectorial Map

The following block diagram shows the **relative orientation of key phasors** across all four operating conditions. This is how the phasor diagram "rotates" or "stretches" with load.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph NO_LOAD["NO-LOAD CONDITION"]
        NL1["Phi: along +X axis"]
        NL2["E1, E2: along -Y axis (lagging 90 deg)"]
        NL3["I0: small phasor, leading Phi by ~75 deg"]
        NL4["V2 = E2 (no drop)"]
    end
    subgraph UNITY_PF["UNITY POWER FACTOR"]
        U1["I2: along V2 direction (in phase)"]
        U2["I1 = I0 + I2 prime vectorially"]
        U3["V2 slightly less than E2"]
    end
    subgraph LAG_PF["LAGGING POWER FACTOR"]
        L1["I2: lags V2 by phi_2"]
        L2["I1: largest magnitude (worst case)"]
        L3["V2 much less than E2 (largest drop)"]
    end
    subgraph LEAD_PF["LEADING POWER FACTOR"]
        LD1["I2: leads V2 by phi_2"]
        LD2["V2 may exceed E2 (negative regulation)"]
        LD3["I1 may be smaller than unity PF case"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Reference Map

The phasor diagram is a **graphical equivalent of the T-model** of a transformer. Each branch of the T-model maps directly onto the phasor diagram as shown:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TMODEL["T-EQUIVALENT CIRCUIT"]
        V1["V1"] --> R1["R1 + jX1"]
        R1 --> E1["-E1 (Primary induced EMF)"]
        E1 --> BR["Branch: jX0 with Iw and Imu"]
        BR --> E2["E2 (Secondary induced EMF)"]
        E2 --> R2["R2 + jX2"]
        R2 --> V2["V2 (Terminal)"]
    end
    V1 -.represents.-> P1["Phasor: V1"]
    E1 -.represents.-> P2["Phasor: -E1 (leads Phi by 90)"]
    BR -.represents.-> P3["Phasor: I0 with Iw, Imu"]
    E2 -.represents.-> P4["Phasor: E2 (lags Phi by 90)"]
    V2 -.represents.-> P5["Phasor: V2"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** The phasor diagram is *not* drawn arbitrarily. It must reproduce the T-model's KVL and KCL relationships. If your phasor diagram violates KVL/KCL, the construction is wrong.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"State the conditions under which the no-load phasor diagram of a transformer is drawn. What is the phase relationship between the induced EMF and the mutual flux?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** At no-load, the secondary current $I_2 = 0$ and the primary current equals the no-load current $I_1 = I_0$, which is very small (typically 2–5% of full-load current).
- **(1 mark)** The applied voltage $V_1$ has a small phase lead over $E_1$ due to the impedance drop $I_0(R_1 + jX_1)$, and $V_2 \approx E_2$.
- **(1 mark)** The induced EMFs $E_1$ and $E_2$ both **lag the mutual flux $\Phi$ by 90°** (Faraday's law: $e = N \, d\Phi/dt$, so peak EMF corresponds to zero flux crossing).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Compare the voltage regulation of a transformer at (i) unity power factor, (ii) 0.8 lagging pf, and (iii) 0.8 leading pf loads. Justify with the help of phasor diagram reasoning."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** At **unity pf**, $\sin\phi_2 = 0$, so $VR \approx (I_2 R_2 \cos\phi_2)/V_2 \times 100$ → small positive value.
- **(1 mark)** At **0.8 lagging pf**, both $\cos\phi_2$ and $\sin\phi_2$ are positive → regulation is **largest and positive** (e.g., 3–5%).
- **(1 mark)** At **0.8 leading pf**, $\cos\phi_2$ is positive but $\sin\phi_2$ is negative → regulation becomes **negative** (e.g., −1% to −3%), meaning $V_{2,FL} > V_{2,NL}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Lagging & Unity PF Emphasis**
`[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Draw the phasor diagram of a single-phase transformer supplying a load at **(i) no-load, (ii) unity power factor, (iii) 0.8 lagging power factor**. State clearly the steps of construction and mark all angles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10 kVA, 2000 V/200 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has the following parameters referred to the HV side: $R_{01} = 8 \, \Omega$, $X_{01} = 15 \, \Omega$. Calculate the **voltage regulation** and **secondary terminal voltage** at full load with 0.8 pf lagging. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

> **Construction Steps (3 marks):**
> 1. Take $\Phi$ as horizontal reference (positive x-axis). Draw $E_1$ and $E_2$ lagging $\Phi$ by 90° along the negative y-axis (same phase for ideal transformer).
> 2. Draw $I_0$ leading $\Phi$ by angle $\alpha_0 = \tan^{-1}(I_\mu/I_w) \approx 70°$–$80°$.
> 3. Decompose $I_0$ into $I_w$ (along future $V_1$ direction) and $I_\mu$ (along $\Phi$).
> 4. Apply secondary KVL: $E_2 = V_2 + I_2 R_2 + jI_2 X_2$ → $V_2$ phasor ends slightly before $E_2$ (shorter, slightly shifted).
> 5. Determine $I_2$ direction: at unity pf → along $V_2$; at lagging pf → lagging $V_2$ by $\phi_2$.
> 6. $I_1 = I_0 + I_2/K$ (vector addition).
> 7. Apply primary KVL: $V_1 = -E_1 + I_1 R_1 + jI_1 X_1$ → adds drops parallel and perpendicular to $I_1$.

> **Phasor Relationships (2 marks):**
> - $\Phi$ is the universal reference.
> - $E_1$, $E_2$ lag $\Phi$ by 90°.
> - $I_0$ leads $\Phi$ by magnetizing angle.
> - At no-load: $I_2 = 0$, $I_1 = I_0$, $V_2 = E_2$.
> - At unity pf: $I_2$ in phase with $V_2$.
> - At lagging pf: $I_2$ lags $V_2$ by $\phi_2$.

> **Marked Angles (2 marks):**
> - Angle between $V_1$ and $I_1$ = $\phi_1$ (primary pf angle).
> - Angle between $V_2$ and $I_2$ = $\phi_2$ (load pf angle).
> - Angle of $I_0$ from $\Phi$ = $\alpha_0$ (magnetizing angle).
> - All drops perpendicular/parallel to currents as per KVL.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute full-load secondary current (referred to HV side):**

$$I_{2,FL} = \frac{S}{V_{HV}} = \frac{10000}{2000} = 5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Identify pf and reactive components:**

$$\cos\phi_2 = 0.8 \Rightarrow \sin\phi_2 = 0.6$$

$$I_2 \cos\phi_2 = 5 \times 0.8 = 4 \text{ A (in phase with } V_2)$$

$$I_2 \sin\phi_2 = 5 \times 0.6 = 3 \text{ A (lagging } V_2 \text{ by 90°)}$$

**Step 3: Compute voltage drop components using approximate formula:**

$$\text{Resistive drop} = I_2 R_{01} \cos\phi_2 = 5 \times 8 \times 0.8 = 32 \text{ V}$$

$$\text{Reactive drop} = I_2 X_{01} \sin\phi_2 = 5 \times 15 \times 0.6 = 45 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Total approximate voltage drop:**

$$\Delta V = 32 + 45 = 77 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Compute voltage regulation:**

$$VR = \frac{\Delta V}{V_{2,FL}} \times 100 = \frac{77}{2000} \times 100 = 3.85\%$$

**Step 6: Compute secondary terminal voltage at full load:**

$$V_{2,FL} = V_{2,NL} - \Delta V = 2000 - 77 = 1923 \text{ V (approx.)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to refer all parameters to the **same side** (HV or LV) before applying the voltage regulation formula. If $R_2$ and $X_2$ are given on the LV side, you **must** refer them up by $K^2$ before using $R_{01}, X_{01}$ formula. **Loss of 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** For lagging pf, $\sin\phi_2$ is **positive** (not negative). A sign error in the drop equation can make the answer negative for a lagging load. **Loss of 1 mark.**
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing the approximate and exact regulation formulas. KTU usually accepts the approximate one, but for higher accuracy, the exact formula $E_2 = \sqrt{(V_2 \cos\phi_2 + I_2 R_2)^2 + (V_2 \sin\phi_2 + I_2 X_2)^2}$ is required.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Not labeling $I_w$ and $I_\mu$ components in the phasor diagram. KTU examiners award marks for **complete labelling**, not just the shape. **Loss of up to 2 marks.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Leading PF & Advanced Emphasis (Internal Choice)**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the phasor diagram of a transformer operating at **0.8 leading power factor**. Show how the secondary terminal voltage can rise above the no-load voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 kVA, 4400 V/220 V, 50 Hz distribution transformer is tested and has $R_{01} = 9.6 \, \Omega$ and $X_{01} = 19.2 \, \Omega$ (HV side). Find the **percentage voltage regulation** at full load with **(i) 0.8 pf lagging** and **(ii) 0.8 pf leading**. Comment on the practical significance of the negative regulation case. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

> **Phasor Diagram Construction (3 marks):**
> - Draw $\Phi$ along +X axis.
> - Draw $E_1$ and $E_2$ lagging $\Phi$ by 90°.
> - At 0.8 leading pf, $I_2$ **leads** $V_2$ by angle $\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87°$.
> - Apply secondary KVL: $E_2 = V_2 + I_2 R_2 + jI_2 X_2$.
> - Because $I_2$ leads $V_2$, the reactive drop $jI_2 X_2$ now **opposes** the natural drop direction; the resultant $E_2$ may be shorter than $V_2$, meaning $V_2 > E_2$ in magnitude.

> **Explanation of Voltage Rise (2 marks):**
> - For a **leading pf** load, the load current flowing through $X_2$ produces a reactive drop that **partially cancels** the EMF-phase component of $E_2$.
> - The phasor $V_2$ therefore **extends beyond** $E_2$, i.e., $V_{2,FL} > V_{2,NL}$.
> - Voltage regulation becomes **negative** (e.g., −2% to −5%).

> **Key Marked Angles (2 marks):**
> - $\phi_2 = 36.87°$ (leading angle marked **above** $V_2$).
> - The angle between $E_2$ and $V_2$ is no longer purely a drop but a **rotation** with possible magnitude increase.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Common Setup (1 mark):**

$$I_{2,FL} = \frac{S}{V_{HV}} = \frac{50000}{4400} = 11.36 \text{ A}$$

$$\cos\phi_2 = 0.8, \quad \sin\phi_2 = 0.6$$

---

**Part (i) — 0.8 pf Lagging (3 marks):**

$$VR_{lag} = \frac{I_2 (R_{01} \cos\phi_2 + X_{01} \sin\phi_2)}{V_{2,FL}} \times 100$$

$$VR_{lag} = \frac{11.36 \times (9.6 \times 0.8 + 19.2 \times 0.6)}{4400} \times 100$$

$$VR_{lag} = \frac{11.36 \times (7.68 + 11.52)}{4400} \times 100 = \frac{11.36 \times 19.20}{4400} \times 100$$

$$VR_{lag} = \frac{218.11}{4400} \times 100 = 4.96\%$$

---

**Part (ii) — 0.8 pf Leading (3 marks):**

For leading pf, $\sin\phi_2$ enters with a **negative sign**:

$$VR_{lead} = \frac{I_2 (R_{01} \cos\phi_2 - X_{01} \sin\phi_2)}{V_{2,FL}} \times 100$$

$$VR_{lead} = \frac{11.36 \times (7.68 - 11.52)}{4400} \times 100 = \frac{11.36 \times (-3.84)}{4400} \times 100$$

$$VR_{lead} = \frac{-43.62}{4400} \times 100 = -0.99\%$$

> **Practical Significance Comment (in answer, 0 marks required but recommended):**
> - Negative regulation means $V_{2,FL} > V_{2,NL}$ → useful in **underground cable systems** with high capacitance (Ferranti effect).
> - In practice, leading-pf loads are rare in distribution; this scenario often arises during **light-load conditions** with cable capacitance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B Specific):**
> 1. **Sign Error in Sin Term:** For leading pf, $\sin\phi_2$ must be **subtracted**, not added. This is the single most common error in voltage regulation problems. **Loss of 2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert kVA to kW Units:** When $I_2$ is calculated, ensure VA rating is divided by the correct voltage (HV side here, since parameters are on HV). **Loss of 1 mark.**
> 3. **Phasor Diagram Label Incomplete:** Marking $I_2$ as just an arrow without specifying the **leading angle from $V_2$** loses marks. KTU expects $I_2$ drawn distinctly **above** $V_2$ for leading pf. **Loss of 1–2 marks.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ The **mutual flux $\Phi$ is ALWAYS the reference phasor** — never choose $V_1$ as reference in transformer phasor diagrams.
- ✅ $E_1$ and $E_2$ **lag $\Phi$ by 90°** — they are in **phase** with each other.
- ✅ $I_0$ **leads $\Phi$ by $\alpha_0$** (magnetizing angle, typically 70°–85°).
- ✅ $I_0$ has **two components**: $I_w$ (core loss, in phase with $V_1$) and $I_\mu$ (magnetizing, in phase with $\Phi$).
- ✅ $I_1 = I_0 + I_2/K$ is a **vector sum**, not algebraic.
- ✅ **Phasor diagram sequence is fixed**: $\Phi \rightarrow E_1, E_2 \rightarrow I_0 \rightarrow I_2 \rightarrow I_1 \rightarrow V_2 \rightarrow V_1$.
- ✅ **Voltage regulation signs**:
  - **Lagging pf:** $VR > 0$ (positive, $V_{2,FL} < V_{2,NL}$)
  - **Unity pf:** $VR > 0$ (small positive)
  - **Leading pf:** $VR < 0$ (negative, $V_{2,FL} > V_{2,NL}$)
- ✅ **Approximate VR formula:** $VR \approx \frac{I_2}{V_2} (R \cos\phi_2 \pm X \sin\phi_2) \times 100$; the **±** is the most-tested aspect.
- ✅ The **largest voltage drop** occurs at **0.8 lagging pf** for typical X/R ratios of transformers.
- ✅ **Capacitive loads** (leading pf) cause $V_2$ to **rise** — this is the principle behind **Ferranti effect** in long transmission lines.
- ✅ In KTU exams, always **refer all parameters to ONE side** before numerical computation (usually HV side).
- ✅ The phasor diagram is **not just a drawing** — it encodes KVL, KCL, and MMF balance simultaneously. If any one is violated, the diagram is wrong.
- ✅ **No-load losses** ($P_i$) are read from the wattmeter in OC test; they equal $V_1 I_0 \cos\phi_0$.
- ✅ **Approximate equivalent circuit** is preferred for phasor diagram construction in KTU (since $R_0$ and $X_0$ are moved to the primary side).
- ✅ **For 3-phase transformers**, the phasor diagram is per-phase — multiply by $\sqrt{3}$ for line quantities in star connections.
- ✅ KTU often asks: *"Why does $V_2$ rise for leading pf loads?"* — answer: because the reactive drop $I_2 X_2$ partially cancels the EMF, and the resultant terminal voltage ends up **ahead of** $E_2$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalent Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equivalent Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalent Circuit of a Transformer:** A lumped-parameter electrical network that mathematically models the operational behavior of an actual transformer by representing its distributed electromagnetic phenomena (copper losses, iron losses, leakage fluxes, and magnetizing effects) as discrete, measurable circuit elements (resistors, inductors, and dependent sources) connected between two ideal terminals.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the equivalent circuit is the **single most important analytical tool** for analyzing transformer performance under any loading condition. It allows us to replace the complex, distributed magnetic coupling between primary and secondary windings with a familiar circuit network, enabling direct application of Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) and Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water Pipe" Analogy:** Imagine a complex plumbing system where water (electrical energy) flows from a large reservoir (primary) through narrow, rough pipes (winding resistances), past partially closed valves (leakage reactances), into a smaller pipe system (secondary). Some water is lost to leaky joints (core losses), and a small trickle is always diverted to spin a turbine that powers the local controls (magnetizing current). The **equivalent circuit** is essentially a *scaled-down blueprint* of all these losses and energy transfers drawn as a simple electrical schematic, letting us predict flow rates, pressures (voltages), and losses using basic circuit laws.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standards

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R_1$ | Primary winding resistance | **Ohm (Ω)** | Models copper loss in primary |
| $R_2$ | Secondary winding resistance | **Ohm (Ω)** | Models copper loss in secondary |
| $X_1$ | Primary leakage reactance | **Ohm (Ω)** | Models primary flux that does not link secondary |
| $X_2$ | Secondary leakage reactance | **Ohm (Ω)** | Models secondary flux that does not link primary |
| $R_c$ | Core loss resistance (shunt) | **Ohm (Ω)** | Represents iron + hysteresis losses |
| $X_m$ | Magnetizing reactance (shunt) | **Ohm (Ω)** | Represents the no-load magnetizing current path |
| $a$ | Turns ratio ($N_1 / N_2$) | Dimensionless | Transformation ratio |

### 1.4 Why We Need the Equivalent Circuit

The equivalent circuit bridges the gap between **theory** (ideal transformer equations) and **practice** (real, lossy transformers). It enables:
- **Voltage regulation** calculation under any load
- **Efficiency** determination at any power factor
- **Prediction** of primary/secondary currents and voltages
- **Design validation** through short-circuit and open-circuit tests
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building the Equivalent Circuit — Step-by-Step Logic

The construction of the equivalent circuit proceeds from a rigorous physical argument to a compact circuit model:

1. **Step 1 — Ideal Transformer Core:** Start with an ideal transformer (no losses, perfect coupling). Represented as a perfect transformer with turns ratio $a = N_1 / N_2$, where $V_1 / V_2 = N_1 / N_2$ and $I_1 / I_2 = N_2 / N_1$.

2. **Step 2 — Add Primary Imperfections:** A real primary winding has resistance $R_1$ (causing $I^2R$ copper loss) and leakage reactance $X_1 = 2\pi f L_1$ (causing voltage drop due to flux that fails to reach the secondary). These are placed **in series** with the primary terminal.

3. **Step 3 — Add Secondary Imperfections:** Similarly, the secondary winding has resistance $R_2$ and leakage reactance $X_2$, placed in series with the secondary terminal.

4. **Step 4 — Account for Magnetization & Core Loss:** The iron core is continuously magnetized by an alternating current (magnetizing current $I_m$ flowing through $X_m$) and also dissipates energy as heat (hysteresis + eddy current losses represented by $R_c$). These are placed in a **shunt (parallel) branch** across the induced EMF.

5. **Step 5 — Refer to a Common Side:** To enable KVL/KCL analysis, all secondary quantities must be "referred" (scaled) to the primary side (or vice versa) using the turns ratio squared for impedance.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind the Shunt Branch Position:** The shunt branch is placed at the input terminals of the equivalent circuit (after $R_1, X_1$) because the magnetizing current and core loss current are *drawn by the core*, which physically sits at the primary side of the magnetic coupling.

### 2.2 The Two Canonical Models

**Model A — Exact Equivalent Circuit (T-Model):**
The T-model is the literal translation of the physical description. It has the shunt branch in the middle of two series arms.

**Model B — Approximate Equivalent Circuit (L-Model):**
Since the no-load current is only **2–5%** of the full-load current, the voltage drop across $R_1$ and $X_1$ caused by the shunt branch current is negligible. Therefore, the shunt branch is shifted to the input terminals, forming an inverted 'L' shape. **This is the model used in ~95% of KTU numerical problems.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $a = N_1 / N_2$ | Turns ratio (Primary : Secondary) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $R_2' = a^2 R_2$ | Secondary resistance referred to primary | Ω |
| 3 | $X_2' = a^2 X_2$ | Secondary leakage reactance referred to primary | Ω |
| 4 | $V_2' = a \, V_2$ | Secondary voltage referred to primary | V |
| 5 | $I_2' = I_2 / a$ | Secondary current referred to primary | A |
| 6 | $Z_2' = a^2 Z_2$ | Total secondary impedance referred to primary | Ω |
| 7 | $R_{01} = R_1 + a^2 R_2$ | Equivalent resistance referred to primary | Ω |
| 8 | $X_{01} = X_1 + a^2 X_2$ | Equivalent leakage reactance referred to primary | Ω |
| 9 | $Z_{01} = \sqrt{R_{01}^2 + X_{01}^2}$ | Equivalent impedance magnitude | Ω |
| 10 | $\cos\phi_0 = P_0 / (V_0 \cdot I_0)$ | No-load power factor (OC test) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\cos\phi_{sc} = P_{sc} / (V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc})$ | Short-circuit power factor (SC test) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{VR} = \dfrac{V_1/a - V_2}{V_2} \times 100\%$ | Voltage Regulation formula | % |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Applications:** The equivalent circuit is used in **power transformer design** (ABB, Siemens, Schneider Electric), **distribution transformer rating** calculations, **fault current analysis** in power systems, **transformer protection relay** coordination, and **renewable energy** grid-tie inverter interface design. The parameters $R_{01}, X_{01}, R_c, X_m$ are stamped on every transformer's nameplate as the "transformer test certificate data."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Referring Secondary Impedance to Primary Side

Consider a transformer with turns ratio $a = N_1/N_2$. Let a load impedance $Z_L$ be connected across the secondary terminals.

**Step 1 — Power Invariance Principle**
The power delivered to the load must remain identical whether we view the load on the secondary side or refer it to the primary side.

$$P_{\text{secondary}} = P_{\text{primary}}$$

$$I_2^2 \cdot Z_L = I_1^2 \cdot Z_L'$$

**Step 2 — Current Relationship from Ideal Transformer**
For an ideal transformer, ampere-turn balance requires:

$$N_1 \cdot I_1 = N_2 \cdot I_2$$

$$\therefore \quad I_1 = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot I_2 = \frac{I_2}{a}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Power Invariance Equation**

$$\left(\frac{I_2}{a}\right)^2 \cdot Z_L' = I_2^2 \cdot Z_L$$

$$\frac{Z_L'}{a^2} = Z_L$$

**Step 4 — Final Referred Impedance Expression**

$$Z_L' = a^2 \cdot Z_L$$

This is the **fundamental transformation rule** for referring any secondary-side impedance to the primary side. The same rule applies to resistances and reactances individually:

$$R_2' = a^2 R_2 \qquad \text{and} \qquad X_2' = a^2 X_2$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Regulation from the Phasor Diagram

**Step 1 — Define the No-Load and Full-Load Secondary Voltages**
At no load, $V_2 = V_{20}$. Under load, secondary terminal voltage drops to $V_2$. Voltage regulation is defined as:

$$\text{VR} = \frac{V_{20} - V_2}{V_2} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Approximate Expression Using Equivalent Impedance**
For lagging power factor $\cos\phi_2$ (typical inductive load), KVL on the secondary side yields:

$$V_{20} = V_2 + I_2(R_2 \cos\phi_2 + X_2 \sin\phi_2)$$

**Step 3 — Referring to Primary for Simplified Calculation**
Using the turns ratio and the approximate equivalent circuit, this becomes:

$$\text{VR} = \frac{I_1(R_{01}\cos\phi_2 + X_{01}\sin\phi_2)}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

For a **leading** power factor load, the $\sin\phi_2$ term changes sign:

$$\text{VR}_{\text{leading}} = \frac{I_1(R_{01}\cos\phi_2 - X_{01}\sin\phi_2)}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

**Step 1 — Express Efficiency**
Efficiency is output power divided by input power:

$$\eta = \frac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2 + P_i + P_c(x)}$$

where $x$ is the **loading fraction** (fraction of full load), $P_i$ is the iron loss (constant), and $P_c$ is the full-load copper loss.

**Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to Loading Fraction**

$$\frac{d\eta}{dx} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Result**
The condition for maximum efficiency is:

$$\boxed{P_i = x^2 P_c}$$

At **full load** ($x = 1$), this gives the design optimum $P_i = P_c$ — i.e., iron loss equals full-load copper loss.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Approximate vs. Exact Equivalent Circuit

| Feature | Exact (T-Model) | Approximate (L-Model) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Shunt branch position | Middle of series arms | At input terminals |
| Accuracy | Highest | ~2-3% error at full load |
| Computational complexity | Higher (two loops) | Lower (single loop) |
| Use in KTU problems | Conceptual analysis | Numerical problems |
| Valid when | Always | $I_0 \ll I_1$ (true for all practical transformers) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Approximate Equivalent Circuit (L-Model) — Functional Architecture

The following block diagram maps the energy flow and circuit topology of the approximate equivalent circuit, which is the workhorse model for KTU numerical problems.

```mermaid
graph LR
    input1["PRIMARY TERMINAL V1"] --> r1["R1 - Primary Resistance"]
    r1 --> x1["X1 - Primary Leakage Reactance"]
    x1 --> nodeA["NODE A - Core Entry Point"]
    nodeA --> rc["Rc - Core Loss Branch SHUNT"]
    nodeA --> xm["Xm - Magnetizing Reactance SHUNT"]
    nodeA --> r2p["R2 referred to Primary equal to a squared R2"]
    r2p --> x2p["X2 referred to Primary equal to a squared X2"]
    x2p --> load1["LOAD ZL referred to Primary equal to a squared ZL"]
    load1 --> return1["PRIMARY RETURN PATH"]
    nodeA --> coreflux["IDEAL TRANSFORMER a turns ratio"]
    coreflux --> secondaryScaled["SCALED SECONDARY TERMINAL V2 prime"]
    style input1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style load1 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style nodeA fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style coreflux fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Exact Equivalent Circuit (T-Model) — Functional Architecture

The T-model places the shunt branch in the middle, which is its defining topological feature.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["PRIMARY V1"] --> S1["R1 + jX1 Primary Series Impedance"]
    S1 --> M1["NODE M - Shunt Branch Junction"]
    M1 --> SHUNT["SHUNT BRANCH Rc parallel Xm"]
    M1 --> S2["R2 prime + jX2 prime Referred Secondary Impedance"]
    S2 --> P2["LOAD ZL prime Referred Load"]
    P2 --> RT1["RETURN PATH"]
    M1 -.MAGNETIC COUPLING.-> IDEAL["IDEAL TRANSFORMER a"]
    IDEAL --> ACTUAL["ACTUAL SECONDARY V2 I2"]
    style P1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style SHUNT fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style IDEAL fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    style ACTUAL fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Test Data Extraction Procedure

This diagram illustrates how real transformer nameplate data is processed into equivalent circuit parameters — a routine procedure in KTU lab examinations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Transformer's Nameplate Data V1 V2 S rating f"] --> B["Open Circuit Test on LV Side"]
    A --> C["Short Circuit Test on HV Side"]
    B --> D["Measure V0 I0 P0 watts"]
    C --> E["Measure Vsc Isc Psc watts"]
    D --> F["Compute cos phi zero and Xm and Rc from P0 I0 V0"]
    E --> G["Compute R01 eq and X01 eq from Psc Isc Vsc"]
    F --> H["Build Equivalent Circuit"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Use for VR and Efficiency Calculations"]
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "equivalent circuit" of a transformer. List the parameters used to represent it and state which parameter represents core loss.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The equivalent circuit of a transformer is a representation of a real transformer by an electrical circuit comprising resistances, reactances, and an ideal transformer. The parameters are: $R_1, R_2$ (winding resistances), $X_1, X_2$ (leakage reactances), $R_c$ (core loss resistance), and $X_m$ (magnetizing reactance). **The parameter $R_c$ represents the core (iron) loss**, comprising hysteresis and eddy current losses in the iron core. (3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for parameters list, 1 for identifying $R_c$)

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the no-load branch (shunt branch) shifted to the primary side in the approximate equivalent circuit? State the assumption made.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The no-load branch consists of $R_c$ and $X_m$ in parallel and is shifted to the primary terminals to simplify the circuit into an L-shape, which makes voltage and current calculations easier. **The assumption made is that the no-load current $I_0$ is very small (typically 2-5% of full-load current), and therefore the voltage drop across $R_1$ and $X_1$ due to the no-load current is negligible.** (3 marks — 1 for shifting reason, 2 for the assumption)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE — 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the exact equivalent circuit of a single-phase transformer referred to the primary side. Explain the significance of each parameter. Derive the expressions for $R_2'$ and $X_2'$ in terms of the turns ratio $a$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 10 kVA, 2000 V / 200 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has the following test data:
- **OC Test (on LV side):** 200 V, 1.2 A, 60 W
- **SC Test (on HV side):** 60 V, 5 A, 80 W

Calculate: (i) Equivalent resistance and reactance referred to the HV side, (ii) The efficiency at full load and 0.8 power factor lagging, (iii) The voltage regulation at full load and 0.8 power factor leading.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Solution:**
- Draw the T-model with $R_1, X_1$ in series on primary, $R_c \parallel X_m$ in shunt, and $R_2', X_2'$ in series on the secondary-referred side. *(1 mark)*
- **Significance of each parameter:** $R_1, R_2$ → copper losses; $X_1, X_2$ → leakage flux effects; $R_c$ → core loss; $X_m$ → magnetizing current. *(2 marks)*
