# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

State and explain the **EMF equation of a DC generator**. What is the significance of the term "number of parallel paths" in this equation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The EMF equation of a DC generator is:

$$E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$$

Where $P$ = number of poles, $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $N$ = speed (rpm), $Z$ = total armature conductors, and $A$ = number of parallel paths.

The **number of parallel paths** $A$ depends on the winding type:
- **Lap winding:** $A = P$
- **Wave winding:** $A = 2$

The parallel paths determine how the armature conductors are grouped into series-connected sets. A wave-wound machine generates higher EMF (lower $A$) at lower currents, while a lap-wound machine generates lower EMF at higher currents, making it suitable for high-current applications.

> **Mark Split (KTU pattern):** Definition (1M) + Formula explanation (1M) + Significance of $A$ (1M)

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

Differentiate between **Swinburne's test** and **Brake test** for DC motors. State one advantage and one limitation of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Swinburne's Test | Brake Test |
|---|---|---|
| Loading | No-load only | Actual mechanical loading |
| Power consumption during test | Very low | High |
| Types of motors tested | Shunt and compound | Shunt, series, compound |
| Efficiency computation | Indirect (predicted) | Direct (measured) |
| Advantage | Economical and fast | Gives actual efficiency at any load |
| Limitation | Cannot measure stray loss variation with load | Difficult to arrange for large motors; brake drum heats up |

> **Mark Split (KTU pattern):** 3 differences × 1 mark each = 3 marks

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **🔵 Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]

**A long-shunt compound DC generator delivers a load current of 100 A at 250 V. It has armature resistance of 0.05 Ω, series field resistance of 0.04 Ω, and shunt field resistance of 125 Ω. The brush contact drop is 1 V per brush (assume 2 brushes). The armature reaction weakens the flux by 5%.**

**(a)** Calculate the EMF generated by the machine at full load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the machine is run as a motor on the same supply voltage with the same armature current, calculate its speed, armature torque, and shaft power, assuming rotational losses are 1500 W. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — EMF at Full Load:**

**Step 1:** Shunt field current:
$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{250}{125} = 2 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 2:** Armature current (long-shunt):
$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 2 = 102 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 3:** Total voltage drops in the armature circuit:
$$\text{Drop} = I_a R_a + I_{se} R_{se} + \text{Brush drop} = (102 \times 0.05) + (100 \times 0.04) + (2 \times 1)$$

$$\text{Drop} = 5.1 + 4.0 + 2.0 = 11.1 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 4:** Generated EMF:
$$E_g = V + \text{Drop} = 250 + 11.1 = 261.1 \ \text{V}$$

> ✅ **Answer (a):** $E_g = 261.1$ V **(7 marks: 1M for $I_{sh}$, 1M for $I_a$, 2M for drops, 1M for equation, 2M for final answer)**

---

**Part (b) — Operating as a Motor:**

**Step 1:** When operated as a motor, the back-EMF:
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a - \text{Brush drop} = 250 - 5.1 - 2.0 = 242.9 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Since armature reaction reduces the flux by 5% in the generator mode, in the motor mode (at the same armature current), the flux is similarly reduced. Let $\Phi_0$ be the no-load flux. The flux at full load:
$$\Phi_{FL} = 0.95 \, \Phi_0$$

**Step 3:** EMF equation: $E \propto \Phi N$ (for fixed $P, Z, A$). Since the generator and motor operate at the *same armature current* and the *same physical machine*, the flux is the same in both modes (assuming the same magnetisation characteristic). Thus, the EMF is proportional to speed:

$$\dfrac{E_b}{E_g} = \dfrac{N_m}{N_g}$$

Given that the generator speed $N_g$ is the rated speed (let's call it $N_g$ for the no-load condition; the full-load speed of a compound generator is typically within 2–5% of no-load for a long-shunt cumulative). Assuming $N_g = 1500$ rpm (standard for this class of machine):

$$N_m = N_g \times \dfrac{E_b}{E_g} = 1500 \times \dfrac{242.9}{261.1} = 1500 \times 0.9303 = 1395.4 \ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 4:** Armature torque (using the relation $T_a = 60 \times E_b \times I_a / (2\pi N_m)$):

$$T_a = \dfrac{60 \times 242.9 \times 102}{2\pi \times 1395.4}$$

$$T_a = \dfrac{1{,}486{,}548}{8767.4} = 169.55 \ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 5:** Shaft power:
$$\omega_m = \dfrac{2\pi N_m}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1395.4}{60} = 146.13 \ \text{rad/s}$$

$$P_{sh} = T_{sh} \times \omega_m$$

Shaft torque (subtracting rotational losses):
$$T_{sh} = T_a - \dfrac{P_{\text{rot}}}{\omega_m} = 169.55 - \dfrac{1500}{146.13} = 169.55 - 10.27 = 159.28 \ \text{N·m}$$

$$P_{sh} = 159.28 \times 146.13 = 23{,}273 \ \text{W} \approx 23.27 \ \text{kW}$$

> ✅ **Answer (b):** $N_m \approx 1395$ rpm, $T_a \approx 169.55$ N·m, $P_{sh} \approx 23.27$ kW **(7 marks: 2M for $E_b$, 2M for $N_m$, 2M for $T_a$, 1M for $P_{sh}$)**

---

### **🟢 Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]

**A 250 V DC shunt motor draws a current of 5 A at no load and runs at 1200 rpm. The armature resistance is 0.3 Ω and the shunt field resistance is 250 Ω.**

**(a)** Estimate the efficiency of the motor at **full-load** (line current = 50 A) and at **1.25 times full-load** using Swinburne's test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Hopkinson's test** is to be performed on two identical machines. Calculate the **circulating current** and the **excitation currents** required when the test is conducted to determine the efficiency at a **load equivalent to 50 A output current**. Armature resistance of each machine = 0.2 Ω, total iron + friction loss = 1000 W, supply voltage = 250 V. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Swinburne's Test Efficiency:**

**Step 1:** Shunt field current (constant):
$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{250}{250} = 1 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 2:** No-load armature current:
$$I_{a0} = 5 - 1 = 4 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 3:** No-load armature copper loss:
$$P_{cu,a,0} = 4^2 \times 0.3 = 16 \times 0.3 = 4.8 \ \text{W}$$

**Step 4:** Constant losses:
$$P_{\text{const}} = V I_{NL} - I_{a0}^2 R_a = 250 \times 5 - 4.8 = 1250 - 4.8 = 1245.2 \ \text{W}$$

(Alternative formulation: $P_{\text{const}} = 1250 - 4.8 - 1^2 \times 250 = 1245.2 - 250 = 995.2$ W if we want to exclude shunt Cu loss. The first formulation is standard for KTU.)

**Step 5 — At Full Load ($I_L = 50$ A):**

$$I_{a,FL} = 50 - 1 = 49 \ \text{A}$$
$$P_{cu,a,FL} = 49^2 \times 0.3 = 2401 \times 0.3 = 720.3 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{in,FL}} = 250 \times 50 = 12{,}500 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{out,FL}} = 12{,}500 - 720.3 - 1^2 \times 250 - 1245.2 = 12{,}500 - 720.3 - 250 - 1245.2 = 10{,}284.5 \ \text{W}$$

Wait — there's a double-counting issue. The standard Swinburne approach is:

$$P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{cu,a}} - P_{\text{cu,sh}} - P_{\text{const}}$$

where $P_{\text{const}}$ already includes the rotational losses AND the shunt copper loss. Let me redo:

**Corrected Swinburne calculation:**

The total no-load input = 1250 W. At no load, this entire input goes to cover: armature Cu loss (4.8 W), shunt Cu loss (250 W), and rotational losses (which is the rest).

Rotational losses = $1250 - 4.8 - 250 = 995.2$ W (this is the *true* constant loss assumed independent of load, since it represents friction, windage, and core losses at constant speed and flux).

So the **total losses at any load** = $I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + P_{\text{rot}}$

**At Full Load:**
$$I_a = 49 \ \text{A}, \quad P_{cu,a} = 720.3 \ \text{W}, \quad P_{cu,sh} = 250 \ \text{W}, \quad P_{\text{rot}} = 995.2 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{in}} = 12{,}500 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{out}} = 12{,}500 - 720.3 - 250 - 995.2 = 10{,}534.5 \ \text{W}$$
$$\eta_{FL} = \dfrac{10{,}534.5}{12{,}500} \times 100\% = 84.28\%$$

**At 1.25 × Full Load ($I_L = 62.5$ A):**
$$I_a = 61.5 \ \text{A}, \quad P_{cu,a} = 61.5^2 \times 0.3 = 3782.25 \times 0.3 = 1134.7 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{in}} = 250 \times 62.5 = 15{,}625 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{out}} = 15{,}625 - 1134.7 - 250 - 995.2 = 13{,}245.1 \ \text{W}$$
$$\eta_{1.25} = \dfrac{13{,}245.1}{15{,}625} \times 100\% = 84.77\%$$

> ✅ **Answer (a):** $\eta_{FL} = 84.28\%$, $\eta_{1.25 \times FL} = 84.77\%$ **(7 marks: 1M for $P_{\text{const}}$, 2M for FL calc, 2M for 1.25×FL calc, 2M for efficiency values)**

---

**Part (b) — Hopkinson's Test:**

**Setup:** Two identical machines — one acts as a **motor (M)** and the other as a **generator (G)**, mechanically coupled. The generator supplies power back to the motor, so the supply only needs to provide the *losses*.

**Step 1:** Total losses at the test condition = Iron + friction losses = 1000 W (for both machines combined; assume split 50:50 → 500 W each).

The supply voltage is 250 V. The total input from the supply is:

$$P_{\text{supply}} = 1000 \ \text{W (total losses)}$$

$$I_{\text{supply}} = \dfrac{1000}{250} = 4 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 2:** The "load equivalent to 50 A" means the *output* of the generator is 50 A. In a Hopkinson test, the **armature current of the generator** = load current = 50 A, and the armature current of the motor is slightly more (to cover the losses).

**Step 3:** Let the armature current of the motor = $I_{am}$ and of the generator = $I_{ag} = 50$ A.

The circulating current equation (for machines connected in parallel on the same supply):
$$I_{am} = I_{ag} + I_{\text{supply}} = 50 + 4 = 54 \ \text{A}$$

(Wait — this assumes the supply provides the differential current directly. In some Hopkinson configurations, the supply current is shared between the two field windings.)

Standard Hopkinson formulation:
$$I_{\text{circulating}} = I_a = 50 \ \text{A (in each armature)}$$
$$I_{\text{line from supply}} = \dfrac{\text{Total losses}}{V} = \dfrac{1000}{250} = 4 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 4:** Excitation currents:

For the **motor**: $I_{am} = 50 + 4/2 = 52$ A (or simplified $50 + 2 = 52$ A if the supply current splits equally)

For the **generator**: $I_{ag} = 50 - 2 = 48$ A

Field currents are typically small (1–2% of rated). The excitation current $I_f$ for each machine:

If the field winding resistance is, say, $R_{sh} = 250 \ \Omega$ (assumed standard for this rating):
$$I_f = \dfrac{250}{250} = 1 \ \text{A per machine}$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency at this load:**

Power developed by the motor:
$$P_{\text{motor,dev}} = E_b \times I_{am} \approx V \times I_{am} = 250 \times 52 = 13{,}000 \ \text{W}$$

Power developed by the generator:
$$P_{\text{gen,dev}} = E_g \times I_{ag} = 250 \times 48 = 12{,}000 \ \text{W}$$

Power output of the generator (to load):
$$P_{\text{out,gen}} = V \times I_{load} = 250 \times 50 = 12{,}500 \ \text{W}$$

Total losses in the system (both machines) = $P_{\text{cu,armatures}} + P_{\text{iron,fric}} = 2 \times I_a^2 R_a + 1000$

$$P_{\text{losses}} = 2 \times 50^2 \times 0.2 + 1000 = 1000 + 1000 = 2000 \ \text{W}$$

Efficiency of the generator:
$$\eta_{\text{gen}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{out}} + \text{losses}_{\text{gen}}} = \dfrac{12{,}500}{12{,}500 + (50^2 \times 0.2 + 500)}$$

$$\eta_{\text{gen}} = \dfrac{12{,}500}{12{,}500 + 1000} = \dfrac{12{,}500}{13{,}500} = 92.59\%$$

> ✅ **Answer (b):** $I_{\text{circulating}} = 50$ A, $I_{\text{motor,arm}} = 52$ A, $I_{\text{gen,arm}} = 48$ A, $I_{\text{field}} = 1$ A, $\eta_{\text{gen}} = 92.59\%$

> **Mark Split:** Setup diagram (1M), Circulating current (2M), Excitation calculations (2M), Efficiency (2M)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in DC Machine Numericals:**
> 
> **1. Wrong Sign of Voltage Equation (–2 to –3 marks):**
> Students write $E_g = V - I_a R_a$ for a generator. This is **WRONG**. The correct form is $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ because the armature current flows *from* the machine to the load, causing a voltage *drop* in the opposite direction.
> 
> **2. Forgetting to Convert rpm to rad/s (–1 mark):**
> In torque-power problems, $P = T \omega$ requires $\omega$ in rad/s. The conversion is $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. Forgetting this gives an answer off by a factor of ~9.55.
> 
> **3. Confusion Between Lap and Wave Winding (–2 marks):**
> - **Lap:** $A = P$ (more current, less voltage)
> - **Wave:** $A = 2$ (less current, more voltage)
> 
> **4. Ignoring Brush Contact Drop (–1 to –2 marks):**
> Brush drop is typically **1 V per brush** (or 2 V total). KTU problems often include this — skipping it is an easy mark loss.
> 
> **5. Armature Reaction Sign Convention:**
> Armature reaction *weakens* the main field flux in a generator (demagnetizing effect) and *strengthens* it in a motor (cross-magnetizing + demagnetizing net effect). The 4–6% flux reduction must be applied to the *main field flux* before computing the EMF.
> 
> **6. Swinburne Double-Counting:**
> Don't subtract the shunt field Cu loss *twice* — once as a "constant loss" and again explicitly. The $V I_{NL}$ input already accounts for it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — DC Machine Numericals (Module 2)**
> 
> ✅ **EMF Equation:** $E = P \Phi NZ / 60A$ — Memorize the variable meanings.
> 
> ✅ **Torque Equation:** $T_a = 0.159 \times (PZ/A) \times \Phi \times I_a$ — Note: $N$ is **NOT** in this equation.
> 
> ✅ **Lap vs Wave:** $A = P$ (lap) / $A = 2$ (wave) — Choose correctly based on the problem.
> 
> ✅ **Generator Equation:** $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ (+ brush drop).
> 
> ✅ **Motor Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (+ brush drop).
> 
> ✅ **Shunt Field Current:** $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ — Always computed first in shunt/compound problems.
> 
> ✅ **Armature Current:**
> - **Shunt generator:** $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$
> - **Shunt motor:** $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$
> - **Long-shunt compound:** Same as shunt (but $V$ drop includes $R_{se}$).
> 
> ✅ **Power Conversions:**
> - $P_{\text{developed}} = E_g \times I_a = T_a \times \omega$
> - $P_{\text{output}} = T_{sh} \times \omega = P_{\text{dev}} - P_{\text{rot}}$
> - $P_{\text{input}} = V \times I_L$ (motor) / $P_{\text{output of gen}}$
> 
> ✅ **Efficiency:**
> $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Total losses}}{P_{\text{in}}}$$
> 
> ✅ **Swinburne's Test:**
> - $P_{\text{const}} = V I_{NL} - I_{a0}^2 R_a$ (or equivalent)
> - Apply to **any load** without actually loading the machine.
> 
> ✅ **Brake Test:**
> - $T_{sh} = (W_1 - W_2) \times r$ in N·m
> - Suitable for small/medium motors only.
> 
> ✅ **Hopkinson's Test (Regenerative):**
> - Requires two **identical** machines mechanically coupled.
> - Supply provides only the *total losses*.
> - Can compute efficiency at any load.
> 
> ✅ **Speed Regulation:**
> $$\text{Reg} = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$$
> 
> ✅ **Unit Conversions:**
> - $1 \ \text{kW} = 1000 \ \text{W}$
> - $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ (rad/s)
> - $1 \ \text{N·m} \approx 10.197 \ \text{kgf·cm}$ (rarely needed)
> 
> ✅ **Final Sanity Checks:**
> - Generator: $E_g > V$ always
> - Motor: $E_b < V$ always
> - Efficiency $\leq 100\%$ always
> - Torque sign: positive for motoring, negative for generating
> - In Swinburne/Hopkinson, **rotational losses are constant** (assumed independent of load).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Losses and Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Losses and Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a **DC Machine**, the input electrical power (or mechanical power, depending on whether it is operating as a motor or generator) is never converted entirely into the desired output form. A portion of this power is inevitably dissipated as **losses**, manifesting primarily as heat, sound, and mechanical vibration. The ratio of useful output power to the total input power defines the **Efficiency (η)** of the machine.

Mathematically, the efficiency is expressed as:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Input Power}} = \frac{\text{Input Power} - \text{Losses}}{\text{Input Power}} = 1 - \frac{\text{Losses}}{\text{Input Power}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must master the classification of losses into **constant losses** (independent of load) and **variable losses** (load-dependent). The ability to compute efficiency under varying load conditions (e.g., fractional loading) is a **high-frequency exam topic**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are filling a large water tank using a long hose connected to a municipal pump. 

- The water entering the tank is your **useful output**.
- Some water leaks out through small holes in the hose (**copper losses** as heat in windings).
- Some water seeps out from the tank joints (**iron/core losses** in the magnetic core).
- The pump itself makes noise and vibrates, wasting energy as sound and heat (**mechanical losses** like friction and windage).

Just as you would never get 100% of the pump's input water into the tank, an electrical machine never converts 100% of input electrical power to mechanical output (or vice versa). The "leaks" in an electrical machine are called **losses**, and minimizing them is the central goal of every electrical machine designer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Efficiency is a **dimensionless quantity** typically expressed as a **percentage (%)**. Well-designed modern DC machines achieve efficiencies in the range of **85% to 95%**, depending on their rating (fractional kW to MW class).

## 1.3 Classification of Losses at a Glance

The losses in a DC machine are broadly classified into **four major categories**:

| Loss Category | Nature | Dependency on Load |
|---------------|--------|---------------------|
| **Copper Losses (I²R losses)** | Electrical (heat in conductors) | Variable (∝ I²) |
| **Iron / Core Losses** | Magnetic (heat in core) | Approximately Constant |
| **Mechanical Losses** | Mechanical (friction, windage) | Approximately Constant |
| **Stray Load Losses** | Miscellaneous | Variable (∝ I²) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Loss Component

### 2.1.1 Copper Losses (Electrical / I²R Losses)

Copper losses occur due to the **resistance of the windings** when current flows through them. By Joule's Law of Heating, the power dissipated is **I²R**.

**a) Armature Copper Loss (P_cu,a)**
- Occurs in the **armature winding** resistance ($R_a$).
- Formula: $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$
- It is the **largest contributor** to variable losses and varies with the **square of armature current**.

**b) Field Copper Loss (P_cu,f)**
- Occurs in the **field winding** (shunt, series, or compound) resistance ($R_f$ or $R_{se}$).
- For a **shunt machine**: $P_{cu,f} = I_f^2 R_f = V \cdot I_f$ (since $I_f = V / R_f$ is approximately constant).
- For a **series machine**: $P_{cu,se} = I_{se}^2 R_{se} = I_a^2 R_{se}$ (variable).
- Shunt field loss is treated as a **constant loss** because field current is nearly independent of load.

**c) Brush Contact Loss**
- Power lost at the brush-commutator interface due to contact resistance.
- Approximated as: $P_{brush} = V_{brush} \cdot I_a$, where $V_{brush} \approx 2V$ (drop per brush pair).
- Often lumped with armature copper loss for simplicity.

### 2.1.2 Iron / Core Losses (P_i)

These occur in the **armature core** (made of laminated silicon steel) due to the rotating magnetic field. They consist of two sub-components:

**a) Hysteresis Loss (P_h)**
- Energy lost per cycle to **realign magnetic domains** in the core material as it undergoes magnetization cycles.
- **Steinmetz Equation**: $P_h = \eta_h \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V$
- Where: $\eta_h$ = Steinmetz hysteresis coefficient, $B_m$ = maximum flux density, $f$ = frequency, $V$ = volume of core.
- Proportional to $f$ and $B_m^{1.6}$.

**b) Eddy Current Loss (P_e)**
- Energy lost due to **circulating currents** induced in the core by the changing flux.
- Formula: $P_e = k_e \cdot B_m^2 \cdot f^2 \cdot t^2 \cdot V$
- Where: $k_e$ = eddy current constant, $t$ = thickness of lamination.
- Proportional to $f^2$ and $B_m^2$.
- **Reduced by lamination** (using thin sheets insulated from each other) and using high-resistivity silicon steel.

> [!NOTE]
> In a **DC machine**, since the field flux is constant (for shunt/separately excited), iron losses occur only in the **armature core** (where flux alternates due to rotation). The field pole cores do not experience significant iron losses.

### 2.1.3 Mechanical Losses (P_m)

These are independent (or weakly dependent) on load and consist of:

**a) Friction Loss**
- Bearing friction at the shaft.
- Brush friction against the commutator.

**b) Windage Loss**
- Air friction caused by the rotating armature and the cooling fan attached to the shaft.
- Proportional to the cube of the rotational speed: $P_{windage} \propto N^3$.

### 2.1.4 Stray Load Losses (P_stray)

These are the **"miscellaneous" losses** that cannot be precisely classified into the above categories. They include:
- Minor eddy currents in conductors.
- Harmonic losses due to non-sinusoidal flux distribution.
- Losses due to short-circuit currents in coils undergoing commutation.
- **Empirical estimate**: $P_{stray} \approx 1\% \text{ of output power}$ (as per IEEE/IEC standards).

### 2.1.5 Total Losses

$$P_{total} = P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,f} + P_i + P_m + P_{stray}$$

It is convenient to group these as:
- **Constant Losses** ($P_c$): $P_{cu,f}$ (shunt) + $P_i$ + $P_m$
- **Variable Losses** ($P_v$): $P_{cu,a}$ (and series field loss for series machines) + $P_{stray}$

$$P_{total} = P_c + P_v = P_c + I_a^2 R_a \text{ (approximately)}$$

## 2.2 The Concept of Efficiency

### 2.2.1 Efficiency for a DC Shunt Motor (Most Common in KTU Exams)

For a **shunt motor**, the input is electrical power from the supply:

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = V \cdot (I_a + I_f)$$

The output is mechanical power developed at the shaft:

$$P_{out} = P_{in} - P_{total} = P_{in} - (P_c + I_a^2 R_a)$$

$$\eta_{motor} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \frac{P_c + I_a^2 R_a}{V \cdot I_L}$$

### 2.2.2 Efficiency for a DC Generator

For a **shunt generator**, the output is electrical power delivered to the load:

$$P_{out} = V \cdot I_L$$

The input is mechanical power supplied by the prime mover:

$$P_{in,m} = P_{out} + P_{total}$$

$$\eta_{gen} = \frac{V \cdot I_L}{V \cdot I_L + P_c + I_a^2 R_a}$$

### 2.2.3 Efficiency at Fractional Load

If a machine is designed for a **full-load current** $I_{fl}$ but operates at a fraction $x$ of the full load, the new armature current is $x \cdot I_{fl}$. The variable losses scale as $x^2$:

$$\eta_x = \frac{x \cdot P_{out,fl}}{x \cdot P_{out,fl} + P_c + x^2 \cdot P_{v,fl}}$$

## 2.3 Condition for Maximum Efficiency

The **efficiency of a DC machine is maximum** when the **variable losses equal the constant losses**. This is a fundamental result derived from calculus.

$$\boxed{\text{Condition: } P_c = P_v \implies P_{cu,a} = P_{constant} \text{ at max efficiency}}$$

**Proof of the condition (intuitive):**
- At low loads: Constant losses dominate → efficiency is low.
- At high loads: Variable (I²R) losses dominate → efficiency drops again.
- Efficiency peaks at an **intermediate load** where the two loss types balance.

The armature current at which maximum efficiency occurs is:

$$I_{a,max\eta} = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{R_a}}$$

The corresponding output power at maximum efficiency is:

$$P_{out,max\eta} = V \cdot I_L - P_c - P_c = V \cdot I_L - 2P_c$$

(Since at max efficiency, $P_v = P_c$, so total losses = $2P_c$).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Armature Copper Loss | $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$ | W | Variable loss |
| 2 | Shunt Field Copper Loss | $P_{cu,f} = V \cdot I_f$ | W | Constant loss |
| 3 | Series Field Copper Loss | $P_{cu,se} = I_{se}^2 R_{se}$ | W | Variable loss |
| 4 | Hysteresis Loss | $P_h = \eta_h B_m^{1.6} f V$ | W | $\propto f, B_m^{1.6}$ |
| 5 | Eddy Current Loss | $P_e = k_e B_m^2 f^2 t^2 V$ | W | $\propto f^2, B_m^2$ |
| 6 | Iron Loss | $P_i = P_h + P_e$ | W | Constant for fixed speed |
| 7 | Stray Load Loss | $P_{stray} \approx 0.01 \cdot P_{out}$ | W | Empirical 1% rule |
| 8 | Total Constant Loss | $P_c = P_{cu,f} + P_i + P_m$ | W | Independent of load |
| 9 | Total Variable Loss | $P_v = I_a^2 R_a + P_{stray}$ | W | $\propto I^2$ |
| 10 | Motor Efficiency | $\eta_m = 1 - \frac{P_{total}}{V I_L}$ | p.u. or % | $I_L = I_a + I_f$ |
| 11 | Generator Efficiency | $\eta_g = 1 - \frac{P_{total}}{P_{out} + P_{total}}$ | p.u. or % | Output is electrical |
| 12 | Condition for Max η | $P_c = P_v$ | — | Design optimization point |
| 13 | Armature Current at Max η | $I_{a,max\eta} = \sqrt{P_c / R_a}$ | A | For shunt machine |
| 14 | Efficiency at Fractional Load $x$ | $\eta_x = \frac{x P_{out,fl}}{x P_{out,fl} + P_c + x^2 P_{v,fl}}$ | p.u. or % | Common KTU problem type |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of **maximum efficiency condition ($P_c = P_v$)** has profound practical implications:

- **Machine Sizing & Selection:** A machine that operates at full load 100% of the time should be designed so that the full-load point coincides with the maximum efficiency point. This is why industrial machines are **rated for continuous full-load operation**.

- **Loss Minimization Techniques:** 
  - Use **laminated silicon steel** cores to reduce eddy current losses.
  - Use **low-resistance copper** (or aluminum for low-cost) windings.
  - Use **high-grade insulation** to allow higher operating temperatures.
  - Use **sealed bearings** and aerodynamic rotor designs to reduce mechanical losses.

- **Energy Economics:** Even a 1% efficiency improvement in a 1000 MW machine saves **10 MW** of continuous power → millions of rupees in electricity bills annually. This is why efficiency standards (e.g., IE3, IE4 motors) are mandated globally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Condition for Maximum Efficiency

We will derive the condition for maximum efficiency of a **DC shunt motor** in rigorous step-by-step fashion. The same logic applies to DC generators with appropriate sign conventions.

### Setup:

For a DC shunt motor, the input power is:
$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = V(I_a + I_f)$$

Since $I_f$ is small and approximately constant, we can consider $P_{in} \propto I_a$ for simplification.

The total losses are:
$$P_{loss} = P_c + I_a^2 R_a$$

where $P_c$ = constant losses (field copper + iron + mechanical), and $I_a^2 R_a$ = variable (armature copper) loss.

The output power is:
$$P_{out} = P_{in} - P_{loss}$$

The efficiency is defined as:
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{in} - P_{loss}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \frac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Substitute the expressions:

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{P_c + I_a^2 R_a}{V \cdot I_L}$$

**Step 2:** To find the maximum, differentiate $\eta$ with respect to $I_a$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d\eta}{dI_a} = -\frac{d}{dI_a}\left[\frac{P_c + I_a^2 R_a}{V \cdot I_L}\right] = 0$$

**Step 3:** Using the quotient rule (or simplifying, since $V \cdot I_L \approx V \cdot I_a$ for analysis):

$$\frac{d}{dI_a}\left[\frac{P_c + I_a^2 R_a}{V \cdot I_a}\right] = \frac{(V \cdot I_a)(2 I_a R_a) - (P_c + I_a^2 R_a)(V)}{(V \cdot I_a)^2} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Numerator must equal zero:

$$2 V I_a^2 R_a - V(P_c + I_a^2 R_a) = 0$$

**Step 5:** Expand and simplify:

$$2 V I_a^2 R_a - V P_c - V I_a^2 R_a = 0$$

$$V I_a^2 R_a - V P_c = 0$$

$$I_a^2 R_a = P_c$$

**Step 6:** Final Condition:

$$\boxed{P_c = I_a^2 R_a \implies P_{constant} = P_{variable}}$$

**Step 7:** The armature current at maximum efficiency is:

$$I_{a,max\eta} = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{R_a}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** At maximum efficiency, every additional watt of constant loss is "matched" by an additional watt of variable loss. The total losses at this point are exactly $2 P_c$, so the efficiency at maximum efficiency is:
> $$\eta_{max} = 1 - \frac{2P_c}{V \cdot I_L}$$

## 3.2 Complete Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical Problem Type)

### Problem Statement:
> A 220 V DC shunt motor draws an armature current of 50 A at full load. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω and the shunt field resistance is 110 Ω. The iron losses are 400 W, friction and windage losses are 250 W. Calculate:
> (a) Total losses
> (b) Output power and efficiency at full load
> (c) The load at which efficiency is maximum
> (d) Maximum efficiency value

### Given Data:
- $V = 220$ V
- $I_{a,fl} = 50$ A
- $R_a = 0.2$ Ω
- $R_{sh} = 110$ Ω
- $P_i = 400$ W (iron loss)
- $P_{fw} = 250$ W (friction & windage)

### Part (a): Total Losses at Full Load

**Step 1: Shunt field current**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Line current at full load**

$$I_L = I_a + I_{sh} = 50 + 2 = 52 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Shunt field copper loss (constant)**

$$P_{cu,f} = V \cdot I_{sh} = 220 \times 2 = 440 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Armature copper loss (variable)**

$$P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a = (50)^2 \times 0.2 = 2500 \times 0.2 = 500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Iron loss (constant, given)**

$$P_i = 400 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6: Mechanical loss (constant, given)**

$$P_{fw} = 250 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7: Total constant losses**

$$P_c = P_{cu,f} + P_i + P_{fw} = 440 + 400 + 250 = 1090 \text{ W}$$

**Step 8: Total losses at full load**

$$P_{total,fl} = P_c + P_{cu,a} = 1090 + 500 = 1590 \text{ W}$$

### Part (b): Output Power and Efficiency at Full Load

**Step 1: Input power at full load**

$$P_{in,fl} = V \cdot I_L = 220 \times 52 = 11440 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Output power at full load**

$$P_{out,fl} = P_{in,fl} - P_{total,fl} = 11440 - 1590 = 9850 \text{ W} = 9.85 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Efficiency at full load**

$$\eta_{fl} = \frac{P_{out,fl}}{P_{in,fl}} = \frac{9850}{11440} = 0.861 = 86.1\%$$

### Part (c): Load at Which Efficiency is Maximum

**Step 1: Apply the maximum efficiency condition**

$$I_a^2 R_a = P_c$$

**Step 2: Solve for armature current at max efficiency**

$$I_{a,max\eta}^2 = \frac{P_c}{R_a} = \frac{1090}{0.2} = 5450$$

$$I_{a,max\eta} = \sqrt{5450} \approx 73.82 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Express as a fraction of full load**

$$x = \frac{I_{a,max\eta}}{I_{a,fl}} = \frac{73.82}{50} \approx 1.476$$

**Interpretation:** The maximum efficiency occurs at **147.6% of full load** (i.e., above rated load). This is acceptable because the design margin is considered safe, but typically designers aim for $I_{a,max\eta}$ close to $I_{a,fl}$.

### Part (d): Maximum Efficiency Value

**Step 1: Armature copper loss at max efficiency = constant losses**

$$P_{cu,a,max\eta} = P_c = 1090 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Total losses at max efficiency**

$$P_{total,max\eta} = P_c + P_{cu,a,max\eta} = 2 P_c = 2 \times 1090 = 2180 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Input power at max efficiency**

$$I_{L,max\eta} = I_{a,max\eta} + I_{sh} = 73.82 + 2 = 75.82 \text{ A}$$

$$P_{in,max\eta} = V \cdot I_{L,max\eta} = 220 \times 75.82 = 16680.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Output power at max efficiency**

$$P_{out,max\eta} = P_{in,max\eta} - P_{total,max\eta} = 16680.4 - 2180 = 14500.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Maximum efficiency**

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{14500.4}{16680.4} = 0.8693 = 86.93\%$$

## 3.3 Efficiency at Fractional Load — General Formula Derivation

Let the machine be designed for full-load armature current $I_{a,fl}$ with corresponding variable loss $P_{v,fl} = I_{a,fl}^2 R_a$. At fractional load $x$ (i.e., $I_a = x \cdot I_{a,fl}$), the variable loss becomes $x^2 \cdot P_{v,fl}$, and the output power becomes $x \cdot P_{out,fl}$.

$$\eta_x = \frac{x \cdot P_{out,fl}}{x \cdot P_{out,fl} + P_c + x^2 \cdot P_{v,fl}}$$

This equation is **invaluable for KTU problems** that ask for efficiency at "half load", "75% load", etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Many students compute "half load efficiency" by simply halving the full-load losses. This is **WRONG** — only the **variable losses** scale with the square of the load fraction; **constant losses remain the same** at all loads.

## 3.4 Power Flow Diagram for a DC Shunt Motor (Verbal Walkthrough)

The **power flow** in a DC shunt motor follows this sequence (each arrow represents a power transition with losses subtracted):

1. **Input Electrical Power** ($V \cdot I_L$) → enters the machine from the supply.
2. **Subtract Shunt Field Copper Loss** ($V \cdot I_{sh}$) → remaining power goes to armature circuit.
3. **Subtract Armature Copper Loss** ($I_a^2 R_a$) → remaining power is the **Gross Mechanical Power Developed** ($P_g = E_b \cdot I_a$).
4. **Subtract Iron Losses** ($P_i$) and **Mechanical Losses** ($P_{fw}$) → remaining power is the **Net Output Shaft Power** ($P_{out}$).

The same logic, in reverse, applies to a **DC Generator** (mechanical input → electrical output).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Flow Diagram — DC Shunt Motor (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **flow of power** through a DC shunt motor, clearly identifying where each loss is subtracted.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SupplyAC[DC Supply V x IL] -->|Electrical Input Pin| SubField
    SubField["Subtract: Shunt Field Copper Loss V x Ish"] --> ArmPath
    ArmPath["Armature Power V x Ia"] --> SubArm
    SubArm["Subtract: Armature Copper Loss Ia^2 x Ra"] --> GrossMech
    GrossMech["Gross Mechanical Power Developed Eb x Ia"] --> SubIron
    SubIron["Subtract: Iron Core Loss Pi"] --> MidMech
    MidMech["Subtract: Friction and Windage Loss Pfw"] --> OutShaft
    OutShaft["Output Shaft Power Pout"]

    LossStray["Stray Load Loss Pstray"] -.Deducts.-> OutShaft

    style SupplyAC fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style SubField fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ArmPath fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style SubArm fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style GrossMech fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style SubIron fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style MidMech fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OutShaft fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style LossStray fill:#fecaca,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Classification of Losses — Hierarchical Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TotalLoss["Total Losses in DC Machine"] --> Class1[Constant Losses]
    TotalLoss --> Class2[Variable Losses]
    TotalLoss --> Class3[Stray Losses]

    Class1 --> C1a[Shunt Field Cu Loss]
    Class1 --> C1b[Iron Core Loss]
    Class1 --> C1c[Mechanical Friction]
    Class1 --> C1d[Windage Loss]

    C1b --> C1b1[Hysteresis Loss Ph]
    C1b --> C1b2[Eddy Current Loss Pe]

    Class2 --> C2a[Armature Cu Loss Ia^2 Ra]
    Class2 --> C2b[Series Field Cu Loss if applicable]
    Class2 --> C2c[Brush Contact Loss]

    Class3 --> C3a[Harness Harmonic Losses]
    Class3 --> C3b[Commutation Losses]
    Class3 --> C3c[Minor Eddy Currents]

    style TotalLoss fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Class1 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Class2 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Class3 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Efficiency vs Load Curve — Conceptual Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start["Load 0%"] --> BuildUp["Efficiency rises as load increases"]
    BuildUp --> Peak["Maximum Efficiency at Pc equals Pv"]
    Peak --> Decline["Efficiency declines at high loads due to I^2R"]
    Decline --> End["Overload region"]

    Note["Fixed Point: Constant losses always present"] -.Annotates.-> BuildUp

    style Start fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000
    style BuildUp fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#000
    style Peak fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Decline fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#000
    style End fill:#fecaca,stroke:#000
    style Note fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Loss Reduction Strategies (Block Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Goal["Maximize DC Machine Efficiency"] --> A1[Reduce Copper Loss]
    Goal --> A2[Reduce Iron Loss]
    Goal --> A3[Reduce Mechanical Loss]

    A1 --> A1a[Use thick low resistance conductors]
    A1 --> A1b[Use high conductivity copper or aluminum]
    A1 --> A1c[Reduce brush contact drop with carbon graphite]

    A2 --> A2a[Use laminated silicon steel core]
    A2 --> A2b[Increase core resistivity of material]
    A2 --> A2c[Reduce flux density by design margin]

    A3 --> A3a[Use sealed or low friction bearings]
    A3 --> A3b[Streamline rotor fan blade profile]
    A3 --> A3c[Use precision balanced rotor assembly]

    style Goal fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A1 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#000
    style A2 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#000
    style A3 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#000
```
