# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

State and explain the **EMF equation of a DC generator**. What is the significance of the term "number of parallel paths" in this equation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The EMF equation of a DC generator is:

$$E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$$

Where $P$ = number of poles, $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $N$ = speed (rpm), $Z$ = total armature conductors, and $A$ = number of parallel paths.

The **number of parallel paths** $A$ depends on the winding type:
- **Lap winding:** $A = P$
- **Wave winding:** $A = 2$

The parallel paths determine how the armature conductors are grouped into series-connected sets. A wave-wound machine generates higher EMF (lower $A$) at lower currents, while a lap-wound machine generates lower EMF at higher currents, making it suitable for high-current applications.

> **Mark Split (KTU pattern):** Definition (1M) + Formula explanation (1M) + Significance of $A$ (1M)

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

Differentiate between **Swinburne's test** and **Brake test** for DC motors. State one advantage and one limitation of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Swinburne's Test | Brake Test |
|---|---|---|
| Loading | No-load only | Actual mechanical loading |
| Power consumption during test | Very low | High |
| Types of motors tested | Shunt and compound | Shunt, series, compound |
| Efficiency computation | Indirect (predicted) | Direct (measured) |
| Advantage | Economical and fast | Gives actual efficiency at any load |
| Limitation | Cannot measure stray loss variation with load | Difficult to arrange for large motors; brake drum heats up |

> **Mark Split (KTU pattern):** 3 differences × 1 mark each = 3 marks

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **🔵 Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]

**A long-shunt compound DC generator delivers a load current of 100 A at 250 V. It has armature resistance of 0.05 Ω, series field resistance of 0.04 Ω, and shunt field resistance of 125 Ω. The brush contact drop is 1 V per brush (assume 2 brushes). The armature reaction weakens the flux by 5%.**

**(a)** Calculate the EMF generated by the machine at full load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the machine is run as a motor on the same supply voltage with the same armature current, calculate its speed, armature torque, and shaft power, assuming rotational losses are 1500 W. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — EMF at Full Load:**

**Step 1:** Shunt field current:
$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{250}{125} = 2 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 2:** Armature current (long-shunt):
$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 2 = 102 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 3:** Total voltage drops in the armature circuit:
$$\text{Drop} = I_a R_a + I_{se} R_{se} + \text{Brush drop} = (102 \times 0.05) + (100 \times 0.04) + (2 \times 1)$$

$$\text{Drop} = 5.1 + 4.0 + 2.0 = 11.1 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 4:** Generated EMF:
$$E_g = V + \text{Drop} = 250 + 11.1 = 261.1 \ \text{V}$$

> ✅ **Answer (a):** $E_g = 261.1$ V **(7 marks: 1M for $I_{sh}$, 1M for $I_a$, 2M for drops, 1M for equation, 2M for final answer)**

---

**Part (b) — Operating as a Motor:**

**Step 1:** When operated as a motor, the back-EMF:
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a - \text{Brush drop} = 250 - 5.1 - 2.0 = 242.9 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Since armature reaction reduces the flux by 5% in the generator mode, in the motor mode (at the same armature current), the flux is similarly reduced. Let $\Phi_0$ be the no-load flux. The flux at full load:
$$\Phi_{FL} = 0.95 \, \Phi_0$$

**Step 3:** EMF equation: $E \propto \Phi N$ (for fixed $P, Z, A$). Since the generator and motor operate at the *same armature current* and the *same physical machine*, the flux is the same in both modes (assuming the same magnetisation characteristic). Thus, the EMF is proportional to speed:

$$\dfrac{E_b}{E_g} = \dfrac{N_m}{N_g}$$

Given that the generator speed $N_g$ is the rated speed (let's call it $N_g$ for the no-load condition; the full-load speed of a compound generator is typically within 2–5% of no-load for a long-shunt cumulative). Assuming $N_g = 1500$ rpm (standard for this class of machine):

$$N_m = N_g \times \dfrac{E_b}{E_g} = 1500 \times \dfrac{242.9}{261.1} = 1500 \times 0.9303 = 1395.4 \ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 4:** Armature torque (using the relation $T_a = 60 \times E_b \times I_a / (2\pi N_m)$):

$$T_a = \dfrac{60 \times 242.9 \times 102}{2\pi \times 1395.4}$$

$$T_a = \dfrac{1{,}486{,}548}{8767.4} = 169.55 \ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 5:** Shaft power:
$$\omega_m = \dfrac{2\pi N_m}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1395.4}{60} = 146.13 \ \text{rad/s}$$

$$P_{sh} = T_{sh} \times \omega_m$$

Shaft torque (subtracting rotational losses):
$$T_{sh} = T_a - \dfrac{P_{\text{rot}}}{\omega_m} = 169.55 - \dfrac{1500}{146.13} = 169.55 - 10.27 = 159.28 \ \text{N·m}$$

$$P_{sh} = 159.28 \times 146.13 = 23{,}273 \ \text{W} \approx 23.27 \ \text{kW}$$

> ✅ **Answer (b):** $N_m \approx 1395$ rpm, $T_a \approx 169.55$ N·m, $P_{sh} \approx 23.27$ kW **(7 marks: 2M for $E_b$, 2M for $N_m$, 2M for $T_a$, 1M for $P_{sh}$)**

---

### **🟢 Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]

**A 250 V DC shunt motor draws a current of 5 A at no load and runs at 1200 rpm. The armature resistance is 0.3 Ω and the shunt field resistance is 250 Ω.**

**(a)** Estimate the efficiency of the motor at **full-load** (line current = 50 A) and at **1.25 times full-load** using Swinburne's test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Hopkinson's test** is to be performed on two identical machines. Calculate the **circulating current** and the **excitation currents** required when the test is conducted to determine the efficiency at a **load equivalent to 50 A output current**. Armature resistance of each machine = 0.2 Ω, total iron + friction loss = 1000 W, supply voltage = 250 V. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Swinburne's Test Efficiency:**

**Step 1:** Shunt field current (constant):
$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{250}{250} = 1 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 2:** No-load armature current:
$$I_{a0} = 5 - 1 = 4 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 3:** No-load armature copper loss:
$$P_{cu,a,0} = 4^2 \times 0.3 = 16 \times 0.3 = 4.8 \ \text{W}$$

**Step 4:** Constant losses:
$$P_{\text{const}} = V I_{NL} - I_{a0}^2 R_a = 250 \times 5 - 4.8 = 1250 - 4.8 = 1245.2 \ \text{W}$$

(Alternative formulation: $P_{\text{const}} = 1250 - 4.8 - 1^2 \times 250 = 1245.2 - 250 = 995.2$ W if we want to exclude shunt Cu loss. The first formulation is standard for KTU.)

**Step 5 — At Full Load ($I_L = 50$ A):**

$$I_{a,FL} = 50 - 1 = 49 \ \text{A}$$
$$P_{cu,a,FL} = 49^2 \times 0.3 = 2401 \times 0.3 = 720.3 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{in,FL}} = 250 \times 50 = 12{,}500 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{out,FL}} = 12{,}500 - 720.3 - 1^2 \times 250 - 1245.2 = 12{,}500 - 720.3 - 250 - 1245.2 = 10{,}284.5 \ \text{W}$$

Wait — there's a double-counting issue. The standard Swinburne approach is:

$$P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{cu,a}} - P_{\text{cu,sh}} - P_{\text{const}}$$

where $P_{\text{const}}$ already includes the rotational losses AND the shunt copper loss. Let me redo:

**Corrected Swinburne calculation:**

The total no-load input = 1250 W. At no load, this entire input goes to cover: armature Cu loss (4.8 W), shunt Cu loss (250 W), and rotational losses (which is the rest).

Rotational losses = $1250 - 4.8 - 250 = 995.2$ W (this is the *true* constant loss assumed independent of load, since it represents friction, windage, and core losses at constant speed and flux).

So the **total losses at any load** = $I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + P_{\text{rot}}$

**At Full Load:**
$$I_a = 49 \ \text{A}, \quad P_{cu,a} = 720.3 \ \text{W}, \quad P_{cu,sh} = 250 \ \text{W}, \quad P_{\text{rot}} = 995.2 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{in}} = 12{,}500 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{out}} = 12{,}500 - 720.3 - 250 - 995.2 = 10{,}534.5 \ \text{W}$$
$$\eta_{FL} = \dfrac{10{,}534.5}{12{,}500} \times 100\% = 84.28\%$$

**At 1.25 × Full Load ($I_L = 62.5$ A):**
$$I_a = 61.5 \ \text{A}, \quad P_{cu,a} = 61.5^2 \times 0.3 = 3782.25 \times 0.3 = 1134.7 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{in}} = 250 \times 62.5 = 15{,}625 \ \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{out}} = 15{,}625 - 1134.7 - 250 - 995.2 = 13{,}245.1 \ \text{W}$$
$$\eta_{1.25} = \dfrac{13{,}245.1}{15{,}625} \times 100\% = 84.77\%$$

> ✅ **Answer (a):** $\eta_{FL} = 84.28\%$, $\eta_{1.25 \times FL} = 84.77\%$ **(7 marks: 1M for $P_{\text{const}}$, 2M for FL calc, 2M for 1.25×FL calc, 2M for efficiency values)**

---

**Part (b) — Hopkinson's Test:**

**Setup:** Two identical machines — one acts as a **motor (M)** and the other as a **generator (G)**, mechanically coupled. The generator supplies power back to the motor, so the supply only needs to provide the *losses*.

**Step 1:** Total losses at the test condition = Iron + friction losses = 1000 W (for both machines combined; assume split 50:50 → 500 W each).

The supply voltage is 250 V. The total input from the supply is:

$$P_{\text{supply}} = 1000 \ \text{W (total losses)}$$

$$I_{\text{supply}} = \dfrac{1000}{250} = 4 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 2:** The "load equivalent to 50 A" means the *output* of the generator is 50 A. In a Hopkinson test, the **armature current of the generator** = load current = 50 A, and the armature current of the motor is slightly more (to cover the losses).

**Step 3:** Let the armature current of the motor = $I_{am}$ and of the generator = $I_{ag} = 50$ A.

The circulating current equation (for machines connected in parallel on the same supply):
$$I_{am} = I_{ag} + I_{\text{supply}} = 50 + 4 = 54 \ \text{A}$$

(Wait — this assumes the supply provides the differential current directly. In some Hopkinson configurations, the supply current is shared between the two field windings.)

Standard Hopkinson formulation:
$$I_{\text{circulating}} = I_a = 50 \ \text{A (in each armature)}$$
$$I_{\text{line from supply}} = \dfrac{\text{Total losses}}{V} = \dfrac{1000}{250} = 4 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 4:** Excitation currents:

For the **motor**: $I_{am} = 50 + 4/2 = 52$ A (or simplified $50 + 2 = 52$ A if the supply current splits equally)

For the **generator**: $I_{ag} = 50 - 2 = 48$ A

Field currents are typically small (1–2% of rated). The excitation current $I_f$ for each machine:

If the field winding resistance is, say, $R_{sh} = 250 \ \Omega$ (assumed standard for this rating):
$$I_f = \dfrac{250}{250} = 1 \ \text{A per machine}$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency at this load:**

Power developed by the motor:
$$P_{\text{motor,dev}} = E_b \times I_{am} \approx V \times I_{am} = 250 \times 52 = 13{,}000 \ \text{W}$$

Power developed by the generator:
$$P_{\text{gen,dev}} = E_g \times I_{ag} = 250 \times 48 = 12{,}000 \ \text{W}$$

Power output of the generator (to load):
$$P_{\text{out,gen}} = V \times I_{load} = 250 \times 50 = 12{,}500 \ \text{W}$$

Total losses in the system (both machines) = $P_{\text{cu,armatures}} + P_{\text{iron,fric}} = 2 \times I_a^2 R_a + 1000$

$$P_{\text{losses}} = 2 \times 50^2 \times 0.2 + 1000 = 1000 + 1000 = 2000 \ \text{W}$$

Efficiency of the generator:
$$\eta_{\text{gen}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{out}} + \text{losses}_{\text{gen}}} = \dfrac{12{,}500}{12{,}500 + (50^2 \times 0.2 + 500)}$$

$$\eta_{\text{gen}} = \dfrac{12{,}500}{12{,}500 + 1000} = \dfrac{12{,}500}{13{,}500} = 92.59\%$$

> ✅ **Answer (b):** $I_{\text{circulating}} = 50$ A, $I_{\text{motor,arm}} = 52$ A, $I_{\text{gen,arm}} = 48$ A, $I_{\text{field}} = 1$ A, $\eta_{\text{gen}} = 92.59\%$

> **Mark Split:** Setup diagram (1M), Circulating current (2M), Excitation calculations (2M), Efficiency (2M)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in DC Machine Numericals:**
> 
> **1. Wrong Sign of Voltage Equation (–2 to –3 marks):**
> Students write $E_g = V - I_a R_a$ for a generator. This is **WRONG**. The correct form is $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ because the armature current flows *from* the machine to the load, causing a voltage *drop* in the opposite direction.
> 
> **2. Forgetting to Convert rpm to rad/s (–1 mark):**
> In torque-power problems, $P = T \omega$ requires $\omega$ in rad/s. The conversion is $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. Forgetting this gives an answer off by a factor of ~9.55.
> 
> **3. Confusion Between Lap and Wave Winding (–2 marks):**
> - **Lap:** $A = P$ (more current, less voltage)
> - **Wave:** $A = 2$ (less current, more voltage)
> 
> **4. Ignoring Brush Contact Drop (–1 to –2 marks):**
> Brush drop is typically **1 V per brush** (or 2 V total). KTU problems often include this — skipping it is an easy mark loss.
> 
> **5. Armature Reaction Sign Convention:**
> Armature reaction *weakens* the main field flux in a generator (demagnetizing effect) and *strengthens* it in a motor (cross-magnetizing + demagnetizing net effect). The 4–6% flux reduction must be applied to the *main field flux* before computing the EMF.
> 
> **6. Swinburne Double-Counting:**
> Don't subtract the shunt field Cu loss *twice* — once as a "constant loss" and again explicitly. The $V I_{NL}$ input already accounts for it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — DC Machine Numericals (Module 2)**
> 
> ✅ **EMF Equation:** $E = P \Phi NZ / 60A$ — Memorize the variable meanings.
> 
> ✅ **Torque Equation:** $T_a = 0.159 \times (PZ/A) \times \Phi \times I_a$ — Note: $N$ is **NOT** in this equation.
> 
> ✅ **Lap vs Wave:** $A = P$ (lap) / $A = 2$ (wave) — Choose correctly based on the problem.
> 
> ✅ **Generator Equation:** $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ (+ brush drop).
> 
> ✅ **Motor Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (+ brush drop).
> 
> ✅ **Shunt Field Current:** $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ — Always computed first in shunt/compound problems.
> 
> ✅ **Armature Current:**
> - **Shunt generator:** $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$
> - **Shunt motor:** $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$
> - **Long-shunt compound:** Same as shunt (but $V$ drop includes $R_{se}$).
> 
> ✅ **Power Conversions:**
> - $P_{\text{developed}} = E_g \times I_a = T_a \times \omega$
> - $P_{\text{output}} = T_{sh} \times \omega = P_{\text{dev}} - P_{\text{rot}}$
> - $P_{\text{input}} = V \times I_L$ (motor) / $P_{\text{output of gen}}$
> 
> ✅ **Efficiency:**
> $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Total losses}}{P_{\text{in}}}$$
> 
> ✅ **Swinburne's Test:**
> - $P_{\text{const}} = V I_{NL} - I_{a0}^2 R_a$ (or equivalent)
> - Apply to **any load** without actually loading the machine.
> 
> ✅ **Brake Test:**
> - $T_{sh} = (W_1 - W_2) \times r$ in N·m
> - Suitable for small/medium motors only.
> 
> ✅ **Hopkinson's Test (Regenerative):**
> - Requires two **identical** machines mechanically coupled.
> - Supply provides only the *total losses*.
> - Can compute efficiency at any load.
> 
> ✅ **Speed Regulation:**
> $$\text{Reg} = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$$
> 
> ✅ **Unit Conversions:**
> - $1 \ \text{kW} = 1000 \ \text{W}$
> - $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ (rad/s)
> - $1 \ \text{N·m} \approx 10.197 \ \text{kgf·cm}$ (rarely needed)
> 
> ✅ **Final Sanity Checks:**
> - Generator: $E_g > V$ always
> - Motor: $E_b < V$ always
> - Efficiency $\leq 100\%$ always
> - Torque sign: positive for motoring, negative for generating
> - In Swinburne/Hopkinson, **rotational losses are constant** (assumed independent of load).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3-Phase Transformer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 3-Phase Transformer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Phase Transformer:** A static electromagnetic device that transfers electrical energy between two or more three-phase circuits through electromagnetic induction, operating on the principle of mutual induction between three sets of primary and secondary windings displaced in space by **120° electrical** on a common magnetic core or on three separate cores forming a three-phase bank.

A 3-phase transformer may be realized in **two ways**:

1. **Bank of Three Identical Single-Phase Transformers** — windings electrically connected in standard 3-phase configurations (Y or Δ) but magnetically independent.
2. **An Integrated Three-Phase Unit** — three primary and three secondary windings mounted on a common magnetic core structure (3-limb, 5-limb, or shell type).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Frequency:** **50 Hz** in India (KTU-relevant context), with design flux density around **1.1 to 1.4 Tesla** in CRGO silicon steel laminations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **massive three-lane highway (the magnetic core)** that carries traffic in the form of magnetic flux. Instead of three independent single-lane roads (three single-phase transformers), a 3-phase transformer is like a **unified express corridor** where three alternating flux waves travel together, each separated by a time gap of 1/50 × (1/3) = **6.67 ms** in a 50 Hz system.

**Real-world analogy — Apartment Water Supply:**
Think of a residential complex. Instead of installing three separate water tanks and three separate pumps (bank of single-phase units), you install **one large interconnected tank system** (a single 3-phase transformer). The water (magnetic flux) flows in three rhythmic pulses (phases A, B, C), 120° apart, and every apartment (load) gets balanced water (power). The integrated unit is cheaper, lighter, and more efficient — exactly why utilities prefer 3-limb core-type 3-phase transformers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Single-Phase:**
> - A single-phase transformer has **two windings** on a **single magnetic circuit**.
> - A 3-phase transformer has **six windings** (3 primary + 3 secondary) coupled through a **shared 3-phase magnetic circuit**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of 3-Phase Transformers

| Category | Type | Description | KTU Relevance |
|----------|------|-------------|---------------|
| **By Construction** | Bank of 3 single-phase units | Three independent transformers connected in Y/Δ | Common in EHV substations for spares |
| | 3-limb core type | Most common, fluxes sum to zero in common limb | Power distribution transformers |
| | 5-limb core type | Used for very high voltage (EHV) | Reduces core height |
| | Shell type | Sandwich windings, robust to short circuits | Large generator transformers |
| **By Connection** | Star-Star (Yy) | Both HV & LV in star | Distribution transformers |
| | Delta-Delta (Dd) | Both sides in delta | Industrial, low voltage |
| | Star-Delta (Yd) | HV star, LV delta | Step-down at substation |
| | Delta-Star (Dy) | HV delta, LV star | Step-down distribution |
| | Open Delta (V-V) | Two transformers serve 3-phase load | Emergency/standby |
| | Scott (T-T) | Two single-phase for 3-phase | Phase conversion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details

### 2.1.1 Three-Limb Core Type
- Three vertical limbs of **identical cross-section** carry windings of phases A, B, and C.
- The top and bottom yokes return the flux.
- At any instant, the **phasor sum of three fluxes is zero**:
  $$\Phi_A + \Phi_B + \Phi_C = 0$$
- Because the fluxes sum to zero, the **return yoke carries no net flux** in the ideal symmetric case, but in practice it carries the **3rd harmonic and zero-sequence components**.
- **Why this matters:** For balanced sinusoidal excitation, only the fundamental flux is balanced. The **3rd harmonic flux** has zero sequence nature — all three phases have it in-phase — so it must find a return path. In a 3-limb core, the only return path is through the air/oil, leading to **3rd harmonic voltages**.

### 2.1.2 Five-Limb Core Type
- Two additional outer limbs (no windings) provide a **low-reluctance return path** for zero-sequence and 3rd harmonic fluxes.
- **Eliminates 3rd harmonic voltage problems.**
- Used for **EHV transformers** (≥ 220 kV) and very high MVA ratings.

### 2.1.3 Shell Type
- Each phase has its own flux path — they do not share a common limb.
- **Three independent magnetic circuits** are present within a single mechanical structure.
- Excellent short-circuit strength; used for generator step-up transformers.

---

## 2.2 Standard Three-Phase Connections & Vector Groups

A **vector group** is the IEC standard notation that captures the **phasing and phase displacement** between primary and secondary line voltages.

**Notation format:** `HV-Connection − LV-Connection − Phase-displacement (clock number)`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clock Notation Rule:** The high-voltage (HV) side line voltage $V_{AB}$ is taken as the minute hand pointing at **12 o'clock**. The low-voltage (LV) side line voltage $V_{ab}$ indicates the hour, where each hour = **30°** of phase lag.

**Phase displacement (in degrees) = Clock number × 30°**

### Common Vector Groups:

| Vector Group | Phase Shift | Primary Connection | Secondary Connection | KTU Typical Use |
|--------------|-------------|-------------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| **Yy0** | 0° | Star | Star | Distribution, autotransformers |
| **Yy6** | 180° | Star | Star | Specific paralleling |
| **Dd0** | 0° | Star | Star | Industrial, low voltage |
| **Dd6** | 180° | Delta | Delta | Rectifier transformers |
| **Dy1** | -30° (lag) | Delta | Star | Step-down distribution (most common) |
| **Dy11** | +30° (lead) | Delta | Star | KTU — most common step-down |
| **Yd1** | -30° | Star | Delta | Step-up (generator) |
| **Yd11** | +30° | Star | Delta | Step-up (generator) |
| **Yz5** | -150° | Star | Zigzag | Earthing transformer |

---

## 2.3 Voltage, Current & Turns Ratio Relationships

For a 3-phase transformer, define the **per-phase turns ratio**:
$$a = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}}$$

The **line voltage and line current transformations** depend on connection type.

### Key Relationship Table (KTU Formula Sheet)

| Quantity | Star Primary | Delta Primary | Star Secondary | Delta Secondary |
|----------|--------------|---------------|----------------|-----------------|
| $V_{line}$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ | $V_{ph}$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ | $V_{ph}$ |
| $I_{line}$ | $I_{ph}$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$ | $I_{ph}$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$ |

### 2.3.1 Star-Star (Yy) Connection
$$\frac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = a$$
$$\frac{I_{L1}}{I_{L2}} = a$$
Line voltages on both sides are in phase; this is **Yy0**.

### 2.3.2 Delta-Delta (Dd) Connection
$$\frac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = a$$
$$\frac{I_{L1}}{I_{L2}} = a$$
Line currents on both sides are in phase; this is **Dd0**.

### 2.3.3 Star-Delta (Yd11) Connection — Step-up
$$\frac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot a$$
$$\frac{I_{L1}}{I_{L2}} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}$$
The secondary line voltage leads the primary line voltage by **30°**.

### 2.3.4 Delta-Star (Dy11) Connection — Step-down
$$\frac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}$$
$$\frac{I_{L1}}{I_{L2}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot a$$
The secondary line voltage lags the primary line voltage by **30°** (or leads by 30° depending on convention).

---

## 2.4 Conditions for Parallel Operation of Three-Phase Transformers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Two 3-phase transformers can be connected in parallel only when **all** of the following conditions are satisfied. KTU frequently tests this topic.

1. **Same Voltage Ratio** (same turns ratio on a per-phase basis).
2. **Same Polarity / Phase Sequence** (ABC must match ABC).
3. **Same Vector Group** (must be identical or compatible; e.g., Dy11 parallel with Dy11 only — Yy0 is NOT parallel with Dy11 because of the 30° phase shift).
4. **Same Short-Circuit Impedance** (or impedance ratio must be identical, with X/R ratio matching for proper load sharing).
5. **Same Phase Sequence** (especially when considering cyclic connections).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Vector Group Compatibility Matters:** A circulating current flows between the two transformers if their secondary EMFs are not in phase. The magnitude of this circulating current is:
> $$I_{circ} = \frac{\vert E_{21} - E_{22} \vert}{Z_{s1} + Z_{s2}}$$
> This circulating current can be dangerously high — even 5° mismatch can cause damaging currents.

---

## 2.5 Open Delta (V-V) Connection

When one of three transformers in a Δ-Δ bank fails, the remaining **two transformers** can still feed a 3-phase load — this is the **V-V or open-delta connection**.

**Available capacity:**
$$S_{V-V} = \frac{S_{\Delta-\Delta}}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.577 \cdot S_{\Delta-\Delta}$$

**Utilization factor:**
$$UF = \frac{S_{V-V}}{2 \cdot S_{each}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.577$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "open delta capacity" with "1/3 of full capacity." It is actually **57.7%** of full Δ capacity — i.e., 0.577 × S_full, not 0.667 × S_full.

---

## 2.6 Scott Connection (T-T Connection)

A Scott connection uses **two single-phase transformers** to convert between **3-phase and 2-phase** systems (or to create an artificial neutral).

- **Main (Teaser) Transformer:** has a center-tapped primary; the **teaser (T) transformer** has an 86.6% tap on the main transformer's primary.
- The teaser transformer primary has **$\sqrt{3}/2 = 0.866$** times the turns of the main.
- The secondaries produce two-phase outputs **displaced by 90°**, which is then combined for 3-phase.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$ | Per-phase EMF (same as single-phase) |
| 2 | $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ (star) | Line-to-line voltage (star) |
| 3 | $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$ (delta) | Line current (delta) |
| 4 | $a = N_1 / N_2$ | Turns ratio (per phase) |
| 5 | $\frac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = a$ (Yy, Dd) | Line ratio for same connections |
| 6 | $\frac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \sqrt{3} a$ (Yd) | Line ratio for Yd |
| 7 | $\frac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}$ (Dy) | Line ratio for Dy |
| 8 | $S_{3-\phi} = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L$ | Three-phase apparent power |
| 9 | $S_{V-V} = S_{\Delta-\Delta} / \sqrt{3}$ | V-V capacity |
| 10 | $UF = 1/\sqrt{3} = 0.577$ | Open-delta utilization |
| 11 | $\text{Phase shift} = N_{clock} \times 30°$ | Clock notation |
| 12 | $I_{circ} = \frac{\vert \Delta E \vert}{Z_{s1} + Z_{s2}}$ | Parallel circulating current |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dy11 transformers** (the workhorse) — used in **11 kV / 433 V distribution** in Kerala, where the delta primary stabilizes the neutral and provides a path for 3rd harmonic currents.
- **Yy0 transformers** — used at **generator terminals** as **GSU (Generator Step-Up)** units connecting to 220 kV / 400 kV EHV grid.
- **Scott connections** — used in **electric locomotives** to convert 3-phase supply from overhead lines to 2-phase for traction motors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Per-Phase EMF Equation for a 3-Phase Transformer

The EMF equation for a 3-phase transformer is **identical on a per-phase basis** to a single-phase transformer because each phase winding behaves as an independent single-phase transformer (magnetically linked through the core).

**Starting from Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction:**

The instantaneous flux in phase A varies sinusoidally:
$$\phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

By Faraday's law, the induced EMF in a single turn is:
$$e_{turn} = -N \cdot \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

**Step 1:** For a winding with $N$ turns, the total induced EMF magnitude (RMS) is:
$$E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$$

**Step 2:** For 3-phase, each phase winding sees the same flux magnitude $\Phi_m$ but displaced by $120°$ in time:
$$\phi_A(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$\phi_B(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$
$$\phi_C(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$

**Step 3:** The induced EMFs are also displaced by $120°$:
$$e_A(t) = E_m \sin(\omega t - 90°)$$
$$e_B(t) = E_m \sin(\omega t - 210°)$$
$$e_C(t) = E_m \sin(\omega t - 330°)$$

**Result:** The per-phase RMS EMF equation is:
$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **identical** to the single-phase transformer equation. The "3-phase" nature comes from the spatial/temporal displacement of three such windings, not from a different EMF equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Ratio for Star-Delta (Yd11) Connection

This is a **classic KTU problem**.

**Given:** A 3-phase transformer with HV side in **Star** and LV side in **Delta**.
- HV line voltage: $V_{L1}$
- LV line voltage: $V_{L2}$
- Per-phase turns ratio: $a = N_1 / N_2$

**Step 1:** HV side phase voltage:
$$V_{ph1} = \frac{V_{L1}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 2:** LV side phase voltage (in delta, $V_{ph2} = V_{L2}$):
$$V_{ph2} = V_{L2}$$

**Step 3:** By transformer action, the per-phase voltage ratio equals the turns ratio:
$$\frac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}} = a$$

**Step 4:** Substituting:
$$\frac{V_{L1}/\sqrt{3}}{V_{L2}} = a$$

**Step 5:** Solving for the line-voltage ratio:
$$\frac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \sqrt{3} \, a$$

**Step 6:** Phase shift — In Yd11, the secondary line voltage **leads** the primary line voltage by **30°** (Dy11 convention) — i.e., clock position 11 means $11 \times 30° = 330°$ lag, or equivalently 30° lead.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Current Ratio for Delta-Star (Dy11) Connection

**Given:** HV in Delta, LV in Star, per-phase turns ratio $a$, balanced load.

**Step 1:** For balanced load, $I_{L1} = I_{ph1}$ on delta primary (line current = phase current).

**Step 2:** For star secondary, $I_{L2} = I_{ph2}$ (line current = phase current).

**Step 3:** Per-phase ampere-turn balance (ideal transformer):
$$N_1 \, I_{ph1} = N_2 \, I_{ph2}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:
$$\frac{I_{ph1}}{I_{ph2}} = \frac{1}{a}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting:
$$\frac{I_{L1}}{I_{L2}} = \frac{1}{a}$$

**Step 6:** Now express in terms of delta-primary current relationship. For delta primary, $I_{L1} = \sqrt{3} \, I_{ph1}$, so $I_{ph1} = I_{L1}/\sqrt{3}$:

$$\frac{I_{L1}/\sqrt{3}}{I_{L2}} = \frac{1}{a}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for the line-current ratio:
$$\frac{I_{L1}}{I_{L2}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{a}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Open-Delta (V-V) Capacity

**Given:** A Δ-Δ bank rated $S_{\Delta-\Delta}$ total. One transformer is removed. Find remaining capacity.

**Step 1:** In a full Δ-Δ bank with three identical transformers, each rated $S_1$:
$$S_{\Delta-\Delta} = 3 \, S_1$$

**Step 2:** In V-V (open delta), only two transformers remain. Each has the same line voltage across it, and the same phase current $I_{ph}$ that it was rated for.

**Step 3:** Total 3-phase power in V-V:
$$S_{V-V} = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L$$

But in V-V, the line current equals the phase current flowing through the transformer winding (since one transformer is in series between two line terminals):
$$I_L = I_{ph}$$

**Step 4:** So:
$$S_{V-V} = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_{ph}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with full Δ where $I_L = \sqrt{3} \, I_{ph}$:
$$S_{\Delta-\Delta} = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \cdot \sqrt{3} \, I_{ph} = 3 \, V_L \, I_{ph}$$

**Step 6:** Take the ratio:
$$\frac{S_{V-V}}{S_{\Delta-\Delta}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_{ph}}{3 \, V_L \, I_{ph}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore:
$$S_{V-V} = \frac{S_{\Delta-\Delta}}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577 \, S_{\Delta-\Delta}$$

**Step 8:** Utilization Factor:
$$UF = \frac{S_{V-V}}{2 \cdot S_1} = \frac{0.577 \cdot 3 \, S_1}{2 \, S_1} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Circulating Current During Parallel Operation

**Given:** Two transformers T1 and T2 in parallel, with secondary open-circuit EMFs $E_{21}$ and $E_{22}$ that are **not perfectly matched** (due to vector group mismatch, ratio mismatch, or impedance mismatch).

**Step 1:** Apply KVL around the loop formed by the two secondary windings (in series opposition):
$$E_{21} - E_{22} = I_{circ} (Z_{s1} + Z_{s2})$$

**Step 2:** Solving for the circulating current:
$$I_{circ} = \frac{\vert E_{21} - E_{22} \vert}{\vert Z_{s1} + Z_{s2} \vert}$$

**Step 3:** Express the EMF difference in terms of phase-angle mismatch $\delta$ (assuming equal magnitude EMFs $E$):
$$\vert E_{21} - E_{22} \vert = 2 E \sin\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** For small $\delta$ (in radians), $\sin(\delta/2) \approx \delta/2$:
$$I_{circ} \approx \frac{E \cdot \delta}{Z_{s1} + Z_{s2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Forgetting that even a **5° phase shift mismatch** between two transformers of the same rating can produce circulating currents exceeding full-load current, because the impedance $Z_s$ is small (typically 5-10%).

---

## 3.6 Derivation: 3rd Harmonic in 3-Phase Transformers

**Step 1:** In a Y-connected primary with isolated neutral, **3rd harmonic currents cannot flow** in the lines (because the three 3rd harmonic currents are co-phasal: $i_{3A} = i_{3B} = i_{3C}$ — they sum to $3 i_3$ which has no return path through the line).

**Step 2:** Therefore, the magnetizing current is forced to be **sinusoidal** (assuming balanced supply) only in terms of fundamental, and the flux must contain a 3rd harmonic component.

**Step 3:** The 3rd harmonic flux $\Phi_3$ induces a 3rd harmonic EMF $E_3 = 4.44 \cdot 3f \cdot \Phi_3 \cdot N$ in each phase winding.

**Step 4:** In a 3-limb core, this 3rd harmonic flux has no closed low-reluctance path — it must return through air/oil, causing:
- Excessive core loss
- **3rd harmonic voltages** appearing in the line-to-neutral voltage
- Possible resonance with line capacitance

**Step 5:** In a 5-limb core, the outer unwound limbs provide the return path for $\Phi_3$, eliminating the problem.

**Step 6:** In a **delta-connected primary**, the 3rd harmonic currents can circulate **within the delta** (since $i_{3A} + i_{3B} + i_{3C} = 3 i_3$ is the circulating delta current), and the flux remains sinusoidal — **no 3rd harmonic flux problem**. This is why **Delta-primary** is preferred for high-voltage distribution transformers.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Standard Connections

| Property | Yy0 | Dd0 | Yd11 | Dy11 | Yz5 |
|----------|-----|-----|------|------|-----|
| **Phase Shift (LV side)** | 0° | 0° | +30° (lead) | -30° (lag) | -150° |
| **Neutral Available** | Yes (both sides) | No | Yes (LV only) | Yes (LV only) | Yes (LV only) |
| **3rd Harmonic Issue** | Severe (3-limb) | None | None | None | None |
| **Neutral Stability** | Poor (3rd harmonic) | N/A | Good | Good | Excellent |
| **Insulation Level (HV)** | $\frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ phase stress | $V_L$ phase stress | $\frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $V_L$ | $\frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ |
| **Use Case** | Autotransformer | Industrial | Step-up | Step-down (KTU common) | Earthing |
| **Parallel OK With** | Yy0, Yy6, Dd0, Dd6, Yz11, Dzi0 | Dd0, Dd6, Yy0, Yy6 | Yd11 only | Dy11 only | Yz5 only |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above is a **block-level functional architecture flow** showing the magnetic circuit interconnections of a 3-limb core. For physical cross-section drawings, refer to your prescribed KTU textbook.

---

## 4.2 Standard 3-Phase Connection Diagrams

### 4.2.1 Star-Star (Yy0) Connection

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["HV Phase A"] --- AX["Primary Winding A"]
    B["HV Phase B"] --- BY["Primary Winding B"]
    C["HV Phase C"] --- CZ["Primary Winding C"]
    AX --- N1["HV Neutral N"]
    BY --- N1
    CZ --- N1
    AX2["Secondary Winding a"] --- A2["LV Phase a"]
    BY2["Secondary Winding b"] --- B2["LV Phase b"]
    CZ2["Secondary Winding c"] --- C2["LV Phase c"]
    AX2 --- N2["LV Neutral n"]
    BY2 --- N2
    CZ2 --- N2
```

### 4.2.2 Delta-Delta (Dd0) Connection

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["HV Line A"] --- AB["Winding A to B"]
    B1["HV Line B"] --- BC["Winding B to C"]
    C1["HV Line C"] --- CA["Winding C to A"]
    AB --- B1
    BC --- C1
    CA --- A1
    a1["LV Line a"] --- ab["Winding a to b"]
    b1["LV Line b"] --- bc["Winding b to c"]
    c1["LV Line c"] --- ca["Winding c to a"]
    ab --- b1
    bc --- c1
    ca --- a1
```

### 4.2.3 Star-Delta (Yd11) Connection — Step-Up

```mermaid
graph TB
    HV["HV SIDE STAR"] --- L1["Line A"]
    HV --- L2["Line B"]
    HV --- L3["Line C"]
    HV --- Nstar["Neutral Point"]
    L1 --- PA["Phase A Winding"]
    L2 --- PB["Phase B Winding"]
    L3 --- PC["Phase C Winding"]
    PA --- Nstar
    PB --- Nstar
    PC --- Nstar
    SA["Phase A Secondary a"] --- SB["Phase B Secondary b"]
    SB --- SC["Phase C Secondary c"]
    SC --- SA
    SA --- L1L["LV Line a"]
    SB --- L2L["LV Line b"]
    SC --- L3L["LV Line c"]
```

---

## 4.3 Scott (T-T) Connection Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    P3["3-Phase Primary Supply R Y B"] --- M["Main Transformer Primary Center Tapped"]
    M --- T["Teaser Transformer Primary at 86.6 percent tap"]
    M1["Main Secondary M1 M2"] --- S2["Two-Phase Output Phase 1"]
    T1["Teaser Secondary T1 T2"] --- S3["Two-Phase Output Phase 2"]
    S2 --- S3
    S2 --- LOAD["Balanced 2-Phase Load or 3-Phase after recombination"]
    S3 --- LOAD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 86.6% tap?** The teaser primary must have $N_t = N_m \cdot \cos(30°) = 0.866 \, N_m$ turns to produce the correct flux magnitudes in the magnetic core that satisfy 3-phase balance.

---

## 4.4 Vector Group Clock Notation Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    CLK12["12 O Clock 0 Degree Reference HV VAB"]
    CLK1["1 O Clock Minus 30 Degree"]
    CLK11["11 O Clock Plus 30 Degree"]
    CLK6["6 O Clock 180 Degree"]
    CLK5["5 O Clock Minus 150 Degree"]
    CLK12 --- CLK1
    CLK12 --- CLK11
    CLK12 --- CLK6
    CLK12 --- CLK5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Rule:** If the LV side is at **clock 11** (e.g., Dy11), the LV line voltage $V_{ab}$ **lags** the HV line voltage $V_{AB}$ by $11 \times 30° = 330°$, which is equivalent to **leading by 30°**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "vector group" of a three-phase transformer. With an example, explain the meaning of the notation "Dy11".**

**Model Answer:**

A **vector group** of a 3-phase transformer is the IEC standard code that identifies:
1. The **connection type** of the HV windings (first letter: Y, D, or Z).
2. The **connection type** of the LV windings (second letter: y, d, or z).
3. The **phase displacement** between HV and LV line voltages (third element: a clock number from 0 to 11).

**For Dy11:**
- **D** → HV primary is connected in **Delta**.
- **y** → LV secondary is connected in **Star** (with neutral brought out).
- **11** → The LV line voltage $V_{ab}$ **lags** the HV line voltage $V_{AB}$ by $11 \times 30° = 330°$, which is equivalent to a **30° lead** (since $-30° \equiv +330°$).

**Practical example:** A Dy11 transformer is the standard for 11 kV/433 V distribution in Kerala — delta primary provides a closed path for 3rd harmonic currents, and the star secondary supplies the 3-phase 4-wire distribution with neutral.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List any four conditions that must be satisfied for the successful parallel operation of two three-phase transformers.**

**Model Answer:**

The four mandatory conditions are:
1. **Same voltage ratio** (per-phase turns ratio must be identical).
2. **Same polarity** (the relative polarity markings of the windings must match).
3. **Same vector group** (the phase displacement and connection types must be identical; e.g., Dy11 with Dy11, NOT Dy11 with Yy0).
4. **Same per-unit impedance** (or impedance ratio identical), with matching X/R ratio for proper load sharing.

A fifth condition often listed is **same phase sequence** (A-B-C on both transformers).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Full Descriptive with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with neat phasor diagrams the **Star-Delta (Yd11)** connection of a three-phase transformer. Derive the relationship between line voltages on the two sides in terms of the turns ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A three-phase transformer has a turns ratio of 11:1 (phase). The primary is connected in **delta** and the secondary in **star**. The primary is supplied at **11 kV, 50 Hz**. Find:
  (i) The secondary line voltage
  (ii) The secondary line current when the secondary delivers **500 kVA**
  (iii) The vector group, assuming standard phase convention. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Connection Description:** In Yd11, the HV (primary) windings are connected in star, with the neutral point optionally available, and the LV (secondary) windings are connected in delta (closed mesh). This is a **step-up** connection typically used at generating stations.

**Phasor Diagram (Description):**
- HV side: $V_{AN}, V_{BN}, V_{CN}$ are 120° apart, with line voltages $V_{AB}, V_{BC}, V_{CA}$ leading their respective phase voltages by 30°.
- LV side: The delta windings are wound such that the secondary line voltage $V_{ab}$ **leads** the primary line voltage $V_{AB}$ by **30°** (hence clock number 11 → 330° lag = 30° lead).

**Derivation:**
Let $a = N_1 / N_2$ (per phase).

HV phase voltage: $V_{ph1} = V_{L1} / \sqrt{3}$

LV phase voltage: $V_{ph2} = V_{L2}$ (since delta: line = phase)

Per-phase transformation:
$$\frac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}} = a \implies \frac{V_{L1}/\sqrt{3}}{V_{L2}} = a$$

$$\boxed{\frac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \sqrt{3} \, a}$$

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $a = N_1/N_2 = 11$ (per phase)
- Primary: **Delta**, Secondary: **Star**
- $V_{L1} = 11 \text{ kV}$, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$
- $S = 500 \text{ kVA}$

**Note:** Even though the problem says "primary delta" and "secondary star," we will use the corresponding **Dy11** formula.

**(i) Secondary line voltage:**

For Dy11:
$$\frac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}$$

$$V_{L2} = \frac{V_{L1} \cdot \sqrt{3}}{a} = \frac{11000 \times \sqrt{3}}{11} = 1000 \times \sqrt{3} \approx 1732.05 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{L2} \approx 1732 \text{ V} = 1.732 \text{ kV}}$$

**(ii) Secondary line current:**

$$S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L2} \cdot I_{L2}$$

$$I_{L2} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L2}} = \frac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 1732.05}$$

$$I_{L2} = \frac{500000}{3000} \approx 166.67 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{L2} \approx 166.67 \text{ A}}$$

**(iii) Vector Group:**

Primary delta, secondary star, with standard phase shift → **Dy11** (assuming the secondary line voltage $V_{ab}$ leads $V_{AB}$ by 30°, equivalent to clock position 11).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** What is meant by the **Scott connection**? Explain its working with a neat diagram, and state one important application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A three-phase **5 kVA, 200 V/100 V** transformer is connected in **V-V (open delta)** configuration. Find:
  (i) The maximum 3-phase load that can be delivered in V-V operation.
  (ii) The utilization factor of each transformer.
  (iii) The line currents at full V-V load, assuming balanced resistive load. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Definition:** The Scott connection is a method of interconnecting **two single-phase transformers** to transform **3-phase power to 2-phase power** (or vice versa). The two transformers are designated as the **Main transformer** and the **Teaser transformer**.

**Construction:**
- The **Main transformer** has a center-tapped primary winding, with the two halves producing equal voltages.
- The **Teaser transformer** has its primary connected between one end of the main primary and the **86.6% tap** (i.e., the $\sqrt{3}/2$ point) of the main primary.
- This 86.6% tap ensures that the teaser primary voltage is **$\sqrt{3}/2$ times** the main primary voltage, and is **90° displaced in phase** from it.
- The two secondaries deliver voltages that are **90° apart in phase**, which is the defining property of a 2-phase system.

**Working (Phasor Logic):**
Let $V_R, V_Y, V_B$ be the 3-phase primary line voltages (120° apart). Connect the main primary between R and B (across 200 V, say). The teaser primary is connected between Y and the **mid-point** of the main primary. By geometry, the teaser primary voltage equals $(√3/2) \times V_{RB}$ and is **displaced by 90°** in phase from $V_{RB}$.

On the secondary side, the main and teaser secondaries produce voltages $V_m$ and $V_t$ that are 90° apart — a balanced 2-phase system.

**Application:**
- **Electric locomotives** (e.g., Indian Railways WAP and WAG class locomotives) use Scott-connected transformers to convert 3-phase 25 kV OHE supply to 2-phase for feeding AC series traction motors.
- Conversion of single-phase to 3-phase supply (e.g., rural electrification).

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** 3-phase rating: **5 kVA, 200 V / 100 V** (delta-delta originally, but operated in V-V).

**Per-transformer rating in original Δ-Δ bank:**
Each transformer: $S_1 = S_{\Delta-\Delta}/3 = 5000/3 \approx 1666.67$ VA

Each transformer's phase voltage rating = line voltage rating = **200 V** (primary) and **100 V** (secondary).
Each transformer's rated current:
- Primary: $I_{1,rated} = S_1/V_{1,phase} = 1666.67/200 \approx 8.33$ A
- Secondary: $I_{2,rated} = S_1/V_{2,phase} = 1666.67/100 \approx 16.67$ A

**(i) Maximum 3-phase load in V-V:**

$$S_{V-V} = \frac{S_{\Delta-\Delta}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{5000}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 2886.75 \text{ VA} \approx 2.887 \text{ kVA}$$

**(ii) Utilization factor:**

$$UF = \frac{S_{V-V}}{2 \cdot S_1} = \frac{2886.75}{2 \times 1666.67} = \frac{2886.75}{3333.33} = 0.866$$

Wait — let me correct this. The proper definition of UF for V-V is:

$$UF = \frac{\text{Output power}}{\text{Sum of installed kVA}} = \frac{S_{V-V}}{2 S_1} = \frac{0.577 \cdot 3 S_1}{2 S_1} = \frac{0.577 \times 3}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 0.866$$

Actually, the standard V-V UF = $1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577$ (with respect to the *total* nameplate capacity of the **three-transformer** bank). With respect to the **two installed** units, it is $\sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$.

**KTU convention:** $UF = 1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577$ (with respect to the three-transformer bank capacity).

**(iii) Line currents at full V-V load:**

Secondary line voltage: $V_{L2} = 100$ V
$$I_{L2} = \frac{S_{V-V}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L2}} = \frac{2886.75}{\sqrt{3} \times 100} = \frac{2886.75}{173.21} \approx 16.67 \text{ A}$$

This matches the rated current of each individual transformer winding — confirming V-V operation is at **full rated current** of each remaining transformer.

Primary line current:
$$I_{L1} = \frac{S_{V-V}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L1}} = \frac{2886.75}{\sqrt{3} \times 200} \approx 8.33 \text{ A}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the clock notation logic:** Many students write "Dy11 means 110° phase shift" — WRONG. It's **30° lead** (or 330° lag).
> 2. **Mixing up V-V capacity:** A common error is writing $S_{V-V} = S_{\Delta-\Delta} \times 2/3$. The correct factor is $1/\sqrt{3}$, not $2/3$.
> 3. **Treating Yy, Dd, and Yd identically:** The line-voltage transformation ratio is $a$ for Yy and Dd, but $\sqrt{3} a$ for Yd and $a/\sqrt{3}$ for Dy. This is a high-weightage error.
> 4. **Ignoring vector group compatibility for parallel operation:** Students often say "Dy11 can be paralleled with Yd11" — WRONG. Vector groups must be **identical** (including clock number).
> 5. **Confusing Scott connection with Open Delta:** Scott is for 3-phase ↔ 2-phase conversion; Open Delta is for 3-phase ↔ 3-phase using only two units.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember High-Yield Points (KTU 2024):**

- **Vector group notation:** HV-connection − LV-connection − Clock number (each hour = 30°).
- **Most common KTU distribution transformer:** **Dy11** (11 kV Δ / 433 V Y).
- **Line-to-phase voltage relations:** $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$ for star, $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph}$ for delta.
- **Per-phase EMF equation:** $E_{ph} = 4.44 f \Phi_m N$ — **same as single-phase**.
- **Voltage transformation ratios:**
  - Yy, Dd: $V_{L1}/V_{L2} = a$
  - Yd11: $V_{L1}/V_{L2} = \sqrt{3} a$
  - Dy11: $V_{L1}/V_{L2} = a/\sqrt{3}$
- **Open-delta (V-V) capacity:** $S_{V-V} = S_{\Delta-\Delta}/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577 \, S_{\Delta-\Delta}$.
- **Utilization factor (V-V):** $UF = 1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577$ (with respect to 3-transformer nameplate).
- **Parallel operation conditions:** Same ratio, polarity, vector group, impedance, phase sequence.
- **Scott (T-T) connection:** Teaser primary has $0.866 \times$ turns of main; produces 90° phase shift → 2-phase from 3-phase.
- **3rd harmonic issue:** Y-connected primary with isolated neutral has 3rd harmonic voltage problem; **delta primary eliminates it** (3rd harmonic currents circulate in delta).
- **5-limb core:** Provides low-reluctance path for 3rd harmonic flux → used in EHV transformers.
- **3-limb core:** Most common; fluxes sum to zero for fundamental, but 3rd harmonic flux has no return path through the iron.
- **Clock convention:** $V_{ab}$ position relative to $V_{AB}$ (12 o'clock reference); 11 o'clock = 30° lead (or 330° lag).
- **Kerala-specific application:** KSEB uses Dy11 for 11 kV/433 V distribution; Yy0 (autotransformer) for EHV interconnect.
- **Yd11 vs Dy11 distinction:** Yd11 has HV in star, LV in delta (used for step-up at generators); Dy11 has HV in delta, LV in star (used for step-down at substations).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to DC Machine Construction

A **DC Machine** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy (as a motor) or mechanical energy into electrical energy (as a generator). The construction of a DC machine is the foundational framework upon which its entire operating principle, commutation, and characteristic behavior depend.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Constructional details of DC machines — Yoke, Pole Core & Pole Shoes, Field Coils, Armature Core, Armature Windings, Commutator, and Brushes. Comparison of lap and wave windings is also a high-weightage area in subsequent topics of this module.

## 1.2 Main Structural Parts of a DC Machine

A DC machine is broadly classified into two major assemblies:

1. **The Stator (Stationary Part)** — Yoke, Pole Core, Pole Shoes, and Field Windings
2. **The Rotor (Rotating Part)** — Armature Core, Armature Winding, Commutator, and Shaft

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The *stator* of a DC machine houses the field system which produces the main working flux, while the *rotor* (armature) carries the conductors in which the EMF is induced. These two are separated by a small mechanical clearance called the **air gap**, typically **0.5 mm to 3 mm**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Wheel + Magnet" Picture

Imagine a giant water wheel (the **armature**) spinning between two enormous horseshoe magnets (the **field poles**). As the wheel spins, copper pipes attached to its rim cut through the magnetic field lines (just like a bicycle cutting through the wind). This "cutting" generates a force on the water (electrons) inside the pipes, causing flow (current).

In a DC machine:
- The **magnets** = Field poles (with field windings that act as electromagnets)
- The **water wheel** = Armature with copper conductors
- The **pipes** = Armature windings (closed loops)
- The **slip rings that reverse connection every half-turn** = Commutator
- The **brushes** = The contacts that tap off the unidirectional current

> [!TIP]
> **Why does the commutator exist?** A simple rotating loop in a magnetic field produces **AC** voltage. The commutator acts as a mechanical rectifier — it reverses the external connection every half cycle so that the output seen at the brushes is **DC**.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Materials Used

| Component | Material | Function |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Yoke | Cast Iron or Cast Steel (later, Silicon Steel) | Mechanical support + flux return path |
| Pole Core | Annealed Steel Laminations | Carries field flux; reduces eddy current loss |
| Pole Shoe | Laminated Steel (curved shape) | Spreads flux uniformly; supports field coils |
| Field Coils | Insulated Copper Wire | Carries DC excitation current → produces flux |
| Armature Core | Silicon Steel Laminations (0.35–0.5 mm) | Houses armature conductors; reduces hysteresis & eddy current loss |
| Armature Winding | Enameled Copper Conductors | Carries induced current; forms closed loops |
| Commutator | Hard-drawn Copper Bars (insulated by Mica) | Mechanical rectifier; collects current from armature |
| Brushes | Carbon / Graphite / Carbon-Copper | Maintains sliding contact with commutator |
| Shaft | Mild Steel | Transmits mechanical torque; supports rotor |
| Bearings | Ball / Roller Type | Reduces friction; allows smooth rotation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Gap (δ):** The clearance between the pole shoe and the armature surface. For small machines: **1–3 mm**; for medium machines: **3–5 mm**; for large machines: **5–10 mm**. A smaller air gap gives better magnetic coupling but increases manufacturing precision requirements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Component

### 2.1.1 The Yoke (Frame)

The **yoke** is the outer cylindrical frame of the DC machine. It performs two critical roles:

1. **Mechanical Support:** Holds the pole cores, bearings, and brush gear assembly in place.
2. **Magnetic Flux Return Path:** Provides a low-reluctance path for the magnetic flux produced by the field poles back to the adjacent pole.

**Material Selection Logic:**
- **Cast Iron** → Used in older, smaller machines. High permeability but brittle.
- **Cast Steel** → Used in modern machines. Higher flux density capacity (**≈ 1.5 T**).
- **Rolled Steel** → Used in large machines (better mechanical strength).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point:** The yoke thickness is designed such that the flux density in it does not exceed **1.2 to 1.5 Wb/m²** to avoid magnetic saturation.

---

### 2.1.2 Pole Core and Pole Shoes

The **pole core** is the rectangular cross-sectioned iron part bolted to the yoke. The **pole shoe** is the wider, curved portion that faces the armature.

**Functions of the Pole Shoe:**
- **Flux Distribution:** Spreads the flux uniformly over a larger armature surface area, ensuring sinusoidal flux distribution.
- **Mechanical Support:** Holds the field coils in place.
- **Reduction of Air Gap Reluctance:** Larger surface area = lower reluctance.

**Why Laminated?**
The pole core is built from **0.5 mm to 0.8 mm thick annealed steel laminations** insulated from each other by paper or varnish. This **drastically reduces eddy current losses** since the pole is also subjected to AC flux due to armature reaction.

---

### 2.1.3 Field Windings (Field Coils)

The field windings are coils of insulated copper wire wound around the pole cores. They are connected in **series** to form a continuous winding around all poles.

**Types (Critical for KTU 2024):**

| Type | Connection to Armature | Used In |
|------|------------------------|---------|
| **Shunt Winding** | Parallel to armature (high turns, thin wire) | DC Generators supplying constant voltage, DC Motors with good speed regulation |
| **Series Winding** | Series with armature (few turns, thick wire) | DC Series Motors, Compound machines |
| **Compound Winding** | Both shunt + series | Compound DC Generators/Motors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Formula:** Field Ampere-Turns (AT) per pole:
>
> $$\text{AT/pole} = \frac{\Phi \times \mathcal{R}}{\text{Number of poles}}$$
>
> where $\Phi$ is the flux per pole and $\mathcal{R}$ is the total magnetic circuit reluctance.

---

### 2.1.4 Armature Core

The armature core is a **cylindrical drum** mounted on the shaft, made of high-grade **silicon steel laminations (0.35 mm to 0.5 mm thick)**.

**Why Laminate?**
The armature core rotates in a magnetic field, so it experiences:
- **Eddy Current Losses** → Reduced by laminations
- **Hysteresis Losses** → Reduced by silicon content (4% Si in steel)

**Key Feature — Ventilating Ducts:**
Radial ducts (~10 mm wide) are provided at regular intervals along the core length to allow **air circulation** for cooling.

---

### 2.1.5 Armature Winding

The armature winding consists of **insulated copper conductors** placed in slots on the outer periphery of the armature core. Each conductor carries a fraction of the total armature current.

**Two Main Winding Types (High KTU Weightage):**

| Feature | Lap Winding | Wave Winding |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| Parallel Paths (A) | A = P | A = 2 |
| Conductors in Parallel | Many | Few |
| Current Capacity | High | Low |
| Voltage Capacity | Low | High |
| Number of Brushes | = P (number of poles) | 2 (always) |
| Applications | Heavy current DC machines (e.g., traction motors) | High voltage DC machines |

Where **P** = Number of poles.

---

### 2.1.6 Commutator

The commutator is the most distinctive part of a DC machine. It is a **cylindrical structure** built from hard-drawn **copper bars** (typically trapezoidal cross-section) insulated from each other by thin layers of **mica** (about 0.5–0.8 mm thick).

**Functions:**
1. **Mechanical Rectification:** Collects current from armature coils and delivers unidirectional (DC) current to the external circuit.
2. **Facilitates Connection:** Acts as the junction between the rotating armature and the stationary external circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** Mica is used as insulation between commutator segments because it has nearly the same wear rate as copper, ensuring the commutator surface remains smooth over time.

---

### 2.1.7 Brushes and Brush Gear

Brushes are made of **carbon, graphite, or a carbon-copper mixture**. They are held against the commutator surface by **brush holders** with adjustable spring pressure.

**Brush Pressure:** Typically **1.5 to 2.5 N/cm²** of brush contact area.

**Function:** Maintains a sliding electrical contact between the rotating commutator and the stationary external circuit.

**Why Carbon?**
- Self-lubricating
- Soft → doesn't wear out the commutator
- High contact resistance → helps in commutation

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Total Conductors (Z) | $Z = \text{Number of slots} \times \text{Conductors per slot}$ | — |
| 2 | Parallel Paths (Lap) | $A = P$ | P = number of poles |
| 3 | Parallel Paths (Wave) | $A = 2$ | Independent of P |
| 4 | EMF per Conductor | $E_c = \dfrac{\Phi \, P \, N}{60 \, A}$ | Volts, for a single conductor |
| 5 | Generated EMF (E_g) | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$ | Volts |
| 6 | Speed (N) | $N = \dfrac{60 \, A \, E_g}{\Phi \, Z \, P}$ | RPM |
| 7 | Flux per Pole (Φ) | $\Phi = \dfrac{60 \, A \, E_g}{Z \, N \, P}$ | Weber (Wb) |
| 8 | Commutator Segments | = Number of coils | — |
| 9 | Pole Pitch | $\tau_p = \dfrac{\pi \, D}{P}$ | Slots per pole |
| 10 | Coil Span | Should be ≈ Pole Pitch | — |
| 11 | Number of Brushes (Lap) | = P | — |
| 12 | Number of Brushes (Wave) | Always 2 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Application:** DC machine construction principles directly influence the design of modern **brushless DC (BLDC) motors**, where the electronic commutator replaces the mechanical one. Understanding the physical construction is essential for troubleshooting, rewinding, and performance prediction in industrial applications.

---

## 2.3 Why the Construction Matters in Operation

The geometry of the **air gap**, the **shape of the pole shoe**, and the **distribution of armature windings** all determine:

- The **waveform of the generated EMF** (closer to DC if distributed winding is used)
- The **magnitude of armature reaction**
- The **effectiveness of commutation**
- The **mechanical balance and vibration characteristics**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** In subsequent modules, when deriving EMF equation and torque equation, students must remember that the **number of parallel paths (A)** depends on the winding type. This is a frequent source of confusion and a guaranteed question in Part A.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation of a DC Generator

> **KTU 2024 Foundation Step:** This derivation is the cornerstone of Module 2 and links directly to the "Construction" topic — specifically the role of conductors, poles, and parallel paths.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions and Given Quantities

Let:
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (in Weber, Wb)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of the armature (in RPM)
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature winding
- $E_g$ = Generated EMF (in Volts)

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Convert rotational speed from RPM to revolutions per second.**

$$\text{Revolutions per second} = \frac{N}{60}$$

**Step 2: Time taken for one complete revolution.**

$$T = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{seconds per revolution}$$

**Step 3: EMF induced in one conductor (Faraday's Law).**

According to Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, the average EMF induced in a single conductor cutting $\Phi$ Webers of flux per pole, passing under $P$ poles in one revolution, is:

$$E_c = \frac{\text{Flux cut by conductor per second}}{1}$$

$$E_c = \frac{\Phi \times P}{T}$$

**Step 4: Substitute T.**

$$E_c = \frac{\Phi \times P}{\frac{60}{N}} = \frac{\Phi \, P \, N}{60}$$

**Step 5: EMF per parallel path.**

Since $A$ conductors are connected in series per parallel path, the total EMF per parallel path is:

$$E_{\text{per path}} = E_c \times \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right) = \frac{\Phi \, P \, N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 6: Since all parallel paths have the same EMF, the generated EMF E_g is:**

$$\boxed{E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}}$$

This is the **fundamental EMF equation of a DC machine** — a high-yield KTU 2024 formula.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Verification:** $\Phi$ [Wb] × $Z$ [dimensionless] × $N$ [rev/min] × $P$ [dimensionless] ÷ ($A$ [dimensionless] × 60 [s/min]) = [Wb/s] = [V] ✓

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Construction on Performance

The following table summarizes how each **constructional feature** influences the **electromagnetic and mechanical performance** of a DC machine.

| Constructional Feature | Influences | How It Works | Trade-off |
|------------------------|------------|--------------|-----------|
| **Yoke Material (Cast Steel vs Cast Iron)** | Mechanical strength, Flux density | Higher saturation flux in steel | Increased cost |
| **Lamination Thickness (0.35 mm vs 0.5 mm)** | Eddy current loss | Thinner → lower losses | Higher manufacturing cost |
| **Pole Shoe Curvature** | Flux distribution uniformity | Larger shoe area → uniform flux | Larger overall size |
| **Number of Poles (P)** | Frequency of flux reversal, speed | More poles → lower speed for same frequency | Larger machine size |
| **Air Gap Length (δ)** | Magnetic reluctance, Armature reaction | Smaller gap → stronger flux | Manufacturing tolerance issues |
| **Winding Type (Lap vs Wave)** | Current/Voltage rating, Number of brushes | Lap = High current, Wave = High voltage | Application-specific |
| **Commutator Material** | Wear rate, Contact resistance | Hard-drawn copper = good conductivity | Requires regular maintenance |
| **Brush Material (Carbon/Graphite)** | Commutation, Friction | Soft carbon = good commutation | Higher contact drop |
| **Number of Slots** | EMF waveform quality | More slots → smoother EMF | Complex winding layout |
| **Distributed vs Concentrated Winding** | EMF ripple, Harmonics | Distributed → reduced harmonics | Higher copper usage |

---

## 3.3 Comparison: Lap Winding vs Wave Winding (Analytical Proof)

For a given machine with:
- $P = 4$ poles
- $Z = 600$ conductors
- $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb
- $N = 1000$ RPM

**Case 1: Lap Wound (A = P = 4)**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A} = \frac{0.05 \times 600 \times 1000 \times 4}{60 \times 4} = 500 \, \text{V}$$

Current per path: $I_a / A = I_a / 4$ (current capacity is high)

**Case 2: Wave Wound (A = 2)**

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \frac{0.05 \times 600 \times 1000 \times 4}{60 \times 2} = 1000 \, \text{V}$$

Current per path: $I_a / A = I_a / 2$ (current capacity is lower, but voltage is doubled)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Wave winding always produces **double the EMF** of lap winding for the same machine, but only carries **half the current per path**. This is why wave windings are preferred for **high-voltage, low-current** applications like HT DC generators.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem — Comprehensive KTU Style

**Problem:** A 4-pole DC generator with 600 armature conductors generates 250 V at 1500 RPM. Calculate:
(i) The flux per pole.
(ii) The EMF generated if speed is increased to 1800 RPM (with flux unchanged).
(iii) The EMF if the same machine is wave-wound instead of lap-wound at 1500 RPM.

**Given:**
- $P = 4$
- $Z = 600$
- $E_{g1} = 250$ V
- $N_1 = 1500$ RPM
- Lap wound → $A = P = 4$

**Solution (i): Flux per pole**

$$\Phi = \frac{60 \, A \, E_{g1}}{Z \, N_1 \, P} = \frac{60 \times 4 \times 250}{600 \times 1500 \times 4} = 0.01667 \, \text{Wb}$$

$$\Phi = 16.67 \, \text{mWb}$$

**Solution (ii): EMF at N₂ = 1800 RPM (Φ unchanged)**

Since $E_g \propto N$ for constant Φ:

$$E_{g2} = E_{g1} \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 250 \times \frac{1800}{1500} = 300 \, \text{V}$$

**Solution (iii): EMF if wave-wound (A = 2)**

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A} = \frac{0.01667 \times 600 \times 1500 \times 4}{60 \times 2} = 500 \, \text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Wave EMF = 2 × Lap EMF (since A is halved, EMF doubles). 500 = 2 × 250 ✓

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[STATOR ASSEMBLY] --> nodeB[Yoke Frame]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Pole Core]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Pole Shoe]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Field Winding Coils]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Air Gap Zone]

    nodeG[ROTOR ASSEMBLY] --> nodeH[Shaft]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Armature Core Laminations]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Armature Slots with Conductors]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Armature Winding Closed Loops]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Commutator Segments]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Carbon Brushes]
    nodeM --> nodeN[External DC Load Circuit]

    nodeF -.-> nodeJ

    nodeO[Cooling System] --> nodeP[Radial Ventilating Ducts]
    nodeP --> nodeI

    nodeQ[Brush Gear Assembly] --> nodeM

    nodeR[End Shields and Bearings] --> nodeH
    nodeR --> nodeB
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Magnetic Flux Path in a DC Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[North Pole Shoe] -->|Flux leaves| nodeB[Air Gap]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Armature Core Teeth and Core]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Air Gap]
    nodeD --> nodeE[South Pole Shoe]
    nodeE --> nodeF[South Pole Core]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Yoke Frame]
    nodeG --> nodeH[North Pole Core]
    nodeH --> nodeA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Interpretation:** The flux completes a closed loop — N → Air Gap → Armature → Air Gap → S → Yoke → N. This is called the **magnetic circuit** of the DC machine. Each pole pair contributes one complete loop.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: List any six main parts of a DC machine and state the function of the yoke.**

**Model Answer:**

The six main parts of a DC machine are:

1. **Yoke** — Provides mechanical support and acts as a flux return path.
2. **Pole Core and Pole Shoe** — Carry and distribute the field flux.
3. **Field Windings** — Produce the main working flux when energized.
4. **Armature Core** — Houses the armature conductors; built of laminations to reduce eddy current loss.
5. **Armature Winding** — Carries the induced current; arranged in closed loops.
6. **Commutator** — Acts as a mechanical rectifier to convert the internally induced AC into DC at the brushes.

**Function of Yoke:** The yoke performs two functions: (i) it provides **mechanical support** for the pole cores and the entire machine, and (ii) it provides a **low-reluctance return path** for the magnetic flux from one pole to the adjacent pole, completing the magnetic circuit.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: Why is the armature core of a DC machine laminated? What material is used for commutator segments?**

**Model Answer:**

**Lamination of Armature Core:**
The armature core rotates in a magnetic field, which induces EMF not only in the conductors but also in the iron body of the core itself. This induced EMF circulates **eddy currents** in the core, causing significant **I²R losses (eddy current losses)** and unwanted heating.

To minimize these losses, the armature core is constructed using **thin laminations (0.35–0.5 mm)** of **silicon steel** insulated from each other by a thin coat of varnish or paper. The laminations restrict the eddy current paths to within each thin layer, drastically reducing their magnitude and the associated losses.

Additionally, the **silicon content (≈ 4%)** in the steel reduces **hysteresis loss**.

**Commutator Material:** Commutator segments are made of **hard-drawn high-conductivity copper**, insulated from each other by thin layers of **mica** (about 0.5–0.8 mm thick). Mica is chosen because it has nearly the same wear rate as copper, ensuring the commutator surface remains smooth and cylindrical over the machine's lifetime.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the constructional details of a DC machine. Explain the functions of any five main parts in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, lap-wound DC generator has 600 armature conductors and runs at 1500 RPM. The flux per pole is 20 mWb. Calculate:
  (i) The generated EMF.
  (ii) The EMF if the speed is reduced to 1200 RPM (with flux unchanged).
  (iii) The EMF if the machine is rewound as wave-wound, running at 1500 RPM with the same flux. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

A DC machine consists of two main assemblies: the **stator** (stationary) and the **rotor** (rotating), separated by an air gap.

**(Sketch):** A labeled cross-sectional diagram should be drawn showing all major components. (Refer to the schematic in Section 4.5.)

**Functions of Five Main Parts:**

1. **Yoke:** Outer frame of the machine. Provides mechanical support to the entire machine and serves as a low-reluctance return path for the magnetic flux.

2. **Pole Core and Pole Shoe:** Made of laminated annealed steel. The pole core carries the field flux, and the curved pole shoe distributes the flux uniformly over the armature surface and supports the field coils.

3. **Field Winding:** Insulated copper coils wound around the pole cores. When DC current is passed, it produces the main working magnetic flux in the air gap.

4. **Armature Core:** A cylindrical drum of laminated silicon steel mounted on the shaft. It houses the armature conductors in slots on its periphery. Laminations reduce eddy current and hysteresis losses.

5. **Commutator and Brushes:** The commutator (made of copper segments insulated by mica) acts as a mechanical rectifier. The carbon brushes maintain sliding electrical contact, delivering unidirectional (DC) current to the external circuit.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:**
- $P = 4$ (poles)
- $Z = 600$ (conductors)
- $N_1 = 1500$ RPM
- $\Phi = 20 \text{ mWb} = 0.02 \text{ Wb}$
- Lap wound: $A = P = 4$

**(i) Generated EMF (Lap Wound at 1500 RPM):**

$$E_{g1} = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N_1 \, P}{60 \, A} = \frac{0.02 \times 600 \times 1500 \times 4}{60 \times 4}$$

$$E_{g1} = \frac{72000}{240} = 300 \, \text{V}$$

**(ii) EMF at N₂ = 1200 RPM (Lap, Φ unchanged):**

Since $E_g \propto N$:

$$E_{g2} = E_{g1} \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 300 \times \frac{1200}{1500} = 240 \, \text{V}$$

**(iii) EMF for Wave Winding (A = 2) at 1500 RPM:**

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \frac{0.02 \times 600 \times 1500 \times 4}{60 \times 2} = \frac{72000}{120} = 600 \, \text{V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part A):** Students frequently lose marks in the construction sketch by:
> - Forgetting to indicate the **air gap** (it's worth 1 mark on its own).
> - Not labeling both **N and S poles**.
> - Confusing the position of **brushes** (should be on the commutator, not on the slip rings).
> - Failing to mention the **material** of each part (e.g., "silicon steel laminations for armature core").

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):** Common mistakes in numerical problems:
> - Using $A = P$ for wave winding (should always be $A = 2$).
> - Forgetting to convert mWb to Wb (multiply by $10^{-3}$).
> - Substituting RPM directly without dividing by 60.
> - Writing units inconsistently in the final answer.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Compare lap winding and wave winding in a DC machine. State at least six points of comparison. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of the commutator and brushes in a DC machine with a neat diagram. A 6-pole, wave-wound DC generator has 720 conductors and delivers 60 A to the load. The flux per pole is 30 mWb. If the machine is driven at 1000 RPM, calculate:
  (i) The generated EMF.
  (ii) The current per parallel path.
  (iii) The power developed in the armature. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

| S.No | Parameter | Lap Winding | Wave Winding |
|------|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | Parallel Paths (A) | A = P | A = 2 |
| 2 | Number of Brushes | = P | Always 2 |
| 3 | Conductors in Series per Path | Z/A = Z/P | Z/2 |
| 4 | EMF Generated | Lower | Higher (≈ 2× Lap) |
| 5 | Current Capacity | Higher | Lower |
| 6 | Applications | Low-voltage, high-current DC machines (e.g., traction) | High-voltage, low-current DC machines |
| 7 | Winding Complexity | More complex (more commutator segments connected) | Simpler |
| 8 | Equalizer Rings | Required | Not required |
| 9 | Armature reaction effects | More prominent | Less prominent |

**Conclusion:** Lap winding is preferred for **high-current applications** (e.g., electric locomotives), while wave winding is preferred for **high-voltage applications** (e.g., HT DC generators in power stations).

#### Model Solution (b):

**Role of Commutator and Brushes:**

The commutator is a cylindrical assembly of hard-drawn copper segments insulated from each other by mica, mounted on the shaft. Its function is to **rectify the alternating EMF induced in the armature windings** into a unidirectional (DC) EMF at the external terminals. The **brushes** (made of carbon or graphite) press against the rotating commutator and provide a sliding electrical contact, transferring the DC current to the external load circuit.

In a DC motor, the commutator and brushes perform the **reverse function** — they reverse the direction of current in the armature conductors at the right moment to ensure **continuous unidirectional torque** production.

**Given:**
- $P = 6$ (poles)
- Wave wound: $A = 2$
- $Z = 720$ conductors
- $I_L = 60$ A (assume no field current, so $I_a = I_L = 60$ A)
- $\Phi = 30 \text{ mWb} = 0.03 \text{ Wb}$
- $N = 1000$ RPM

**(i) Generated EMF:**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A} = \frac{0.03 \times 720 \times 1000 \times 6}{60 \times 2}$$

$$E_g = \frac{129600}{120} = 1080 \, \text{V}$$

**(ii) Current per Parallel Path:**

$$I_{\text{per path}} = \frac{I_a}{A} = \frac{60}{2} = 30 \, \text{A}$$

**(iii) Power Developed in the Armature:**

$$P_{\text{developed}} = E_g \times I_a = 1080 \times 60 = 64800 \, \text{W} = 64.8 \, \text{kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):** For wave winding, students often mistakenly use $A = P = 6$ instead of $A = 2$. This single error will lead to a **completely wrong answer** (EMF would be $1080/3 = 360$ V, which is incorrect). Also, the power developed formula $E_g \times I_a$ is the **electromagnetic power converted**, not the output power at the shaft.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DC Machine Construction**

- [x] **Yoke** = Outer frame; Cast Steel; flux return path + mechanical support.
- [x] **Pole Core** = Laminated; carries field flux; bolted to yoke.
- [x] **Pole Shoe** = Curved; spreads flux uniformly; supports field coils.
- [x] **Field Winding** = Insulated copper; excited by DC; produces main flux.
- [x] **Armature Core** = Laminated Silicon Steel (0.35–0.5 mm); houses conductors.
- [x] **Armature Winding** = Lap (A = P) for high current; Wave (A = 2) for high voltage.
- [x] **Commutator** = Copper bars + Mica insulation; mechanical rectifier.
- [x] **Brushes** = Carbon/Graphite; sliding contact; ~1.5–2.5 N/cm² pressure.
- [x] **Air Gap** = 0.5–3 mm (small machines), 5–10 mm (large machines).
- [x] **EMF Equation:** $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$ — must memorize!
- [x] **EMF ∝ N** (linear relationship at constant flux).
- [x] **EMF ∝ Φ** (linear relationship at constant speed).
- [x] **Lap → High current, low voltage, P brushes, A = P.**
- [x] **Wave → Low current, high voltage, 2 brushes, A = 2.**
- [x] **Pole Pitch** $\tau_p = \dfrac{\pi D}{P}$ (in slots per pole).
- [x] **Lamination Purpose** → Reduce eddy current losses (and hysteresis via Si addition).
- [x] **Materials:** Cast Iron (old, brittle), Cast Steel (modern), Silicon Steel (armature).
- [x] **Mica thickness in commutator** ≈ 0.5–0.8 mm; same wear rate as copper.
- [x] **Brush material** = Carbon (low current density machines), Carbon-Copper (high current density).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Advice:** In the exam, always:
> 1. **Draw a labeled diagram** for any construction question (worth 2–3 marks by itself).
> 2. **Mention materials explicitly** — examiners look for this.
> 3. **Highlight the air gap** in your sketches.
> 4. **Show unit conversions** in numerical problems (e.g., mWb → Wb).
> 5. **State the winding type assumption** clearly (Lap → A = P, Wave → A = 2).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Connections of 3-Phase Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Different Connections of 3-Phase Transformers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **3-phase transformer** is a static electromagnetic device that transfers electrical energy between three-phase circuits by electromagnetic induction. The three single-phase windings (or one 3-phase core-type/coil assembly) can be connected in several standardized configurations depending on the application, voltage level, and grounding requirements.

The **standard 3-phase transformer connections** recognized under IEC 60076 and studied in the KTU 2024 Electrical Machines syllabus are:

| Connection | Primary-Secondary | KTU Vector Group Tag |
|------------|-------------------|----------------------|
