| **Star-Star (Y-y)** | Both windings star | **Yy0** / **Yy6** |
| **Delta-Delta (D-d)** | Both windings delta | **Dd0** / **Dd6** |
| **Star-Delta (Y-d)** | Primary star, Secondary delta | **Yd1** / **Yd11** |
| **Delta-Star (D-y)** | Primary delta, Secondary star | **Dy1** / **Dy11** |
| **Open Delta (V-V)** | Two transformers only | **Dd6 (degraded)** |
| **Scott (T-T)** | Two special transformers | **Special T-T** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Must Memorize):** A 3-phase transformer connection defines the *phasing, polarity, and relative magnitude* between primary and secondary line voltages. The choice of connection dictates the *availability of neutral*, *third-harmonic suppression*, and the *phase displacement angle* between primary and secondary line voltages.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Plumbing Network"

Imagine a **large water pumping station** that needs to deliver water at three different pressures to three neighbourhoods:

- **Star (Y) connection** = A central hub where three pipes meet at a common point, with one extra pipe (the **neutral**) going back to balance uneven loads. Good when loads are *unbalanced* or you need a *return path*.
- **Delta (Δ) connection** = Three pipes connected in a closed triangle — water can flow *around* the loop. No neutral needed, but if one pipe breaks, the system can still *limp along* in an **Open-Delta (V-V)** configuration.
- **Scott Connection** = A special *bridge* that converts between a 3-pipe system and a 2-pipe system (like railway signalling power from 3-phase to 2-phase).
- **Vector Group Number (0 to 11)** = Like a *clock hand* — it tells you the angular offset between the primary and secondary line voltages (each number = **30°**).

---

## 1.3 Why We Need Different Connections

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Reasons to Study Multiple Connections (KTU Board Favourite):**
> 1. **Voltage Transformation Ratio** — Step-up or Step-down with different line-to-phase voltage relationships.
> 2. **Neutral Availability** — For supplying single-phase loads alongside 3-phase loads.
> 3. **Third-Harmonic Circulation** — Delta windings provide a closed path for triplen (3rd, 9th, 15th…) harmonics, preventing flux distortion and neutral overheating.
> 4. **Phase Displacement** — Required for **parallel operation** of transformers (vector groups must match).
> 5. **Fault Current Limitation** — Zig-zag connections limit earth-fault currents.
> 6. **Economic Operation** — Open Delta (V-V) allows 3-phase operation using only **two** transformers.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Ratios (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Line-to-Phase Voltage Relation (Star):** $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$  →  approximately **1.732 × V_ph**
- **Line-to-Phase Voltage Relation (Delta):** $V_L = V_{ph}$  (line current differs: $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$)
- **Open-Delta (V-V) Capacity Utilization:** **57.7%** of full bank rating  =  $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
- **Vector Group Clock Number:** Each digit = **30°** phase shift (e.g., **Dy11** = −30° or 330° lag)
- **Scott Connection Tap Ratio:** Main transformer tap = **0.866** of the Teaser transformer rating (i.e., $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Connection-by-Connection Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 ⭐ **Star-Star (Y-Y) Connection**

**Construction:** All three primary windings joined at a common neutral point `N1`; same for secondary `N2`.

**Logic Steps:**
- Each winding sees **phase voltage** $V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$.
- Both line-to-line and phase voltages are reduced by $\sqrt{3}$ compared to delta.
- **Neutral wire available** on both sides → can supply mixed single-phase + 3-phase loads.
- **Critical Weakness:** Third-harmonic currents (150 Hz) from each phase add up in the neutral and in the core flux, causing:
  - Distorted secondary voltages (peaky waveforms).
  - Telephone interference.
  - Neutral conductor overheating.

**Mitigation:** Provide a **tertiary delta winding** (also called an *auxiliary* or *stabilizing* winding) to short the 3rd harmonic circulating currents.

**Turns Ratio (KTU 2024 Standard):**

$$
K = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_{L1}/\sqrt{3}}{V_{L2}/\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}}
$$

> The line-voltage ratio in Y-Y equals the turns ratio. **No phase shift** between primary and secondary (Vector group **Yy0**).

**Real-World Utility:** Distribution transformers at the *end of the feeder* where neutral is required for household single-phase loads.

---

### 2.1.2 🔺 **Delta-Delta (Δ-Δ) Connection**

**Construction:** Three windings connected end-to-start forming a closed loop. Each junction becomes a line terminal.

**Logic Steps:**
- Each winding has **full line voltage** across it: $V_{ph} = V_L$.
- Phase current $I_{ph} = \dfrac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}}$.
- **Closed path for triplen harmonics** → 3rd harmonic currents circulate within the delta, but DO NOT appear in the line currents (they cancel out due to symmetry).
- Excellent for **unbalanced loads** and for handling large **single-phase earth fault currents** (used at generator transformer side).
- **No phase shift** — Vector group **Dd0** or **Dd6** (depending on winding sense).

**Turns Ratio:**

$$
K = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}}
$$

**Real-World Utility:** Used as **Generator Step-Up (GSU)** transformers in power stations, and for supplying heavy industrial loads (e.g., arc furnaces, large motors).

---

### 2.1.3 ⭐🔺 **Star-Delta (Y-Δ) Connection**

**Construction:** Primary in Star, Secondary in Delta.

**Logic Steps:**
- Primary phase voltage = $\dfrac{V_{L1}}{\sqrt{3}}$.
- Secondary winding has $V_{ph2} = V_{L2}$ (delta).
- **The secondary line voltage leads the primary line voltage by 30°** (vector group **Yd11**) OR lags by 30° (**Yd1**) depending on winding polarity marking.
- This **30° displacement** is why the IEC clock notation uses 1 (= 30°) and 11 (= 330°).
- Reduces insulation level on the **HV side** (star) — neutral is grounded → line-to-neutral voltage is $\dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$, so HV insulation is cheaper.
- The LV delta side can supply large inrush currents to motor loads.

**Critical Line-Voltage Transformation Ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}
$$

**Proof:** $V_{L1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot N_1 \cdot \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$ and $V_{L2} = V_{ph2} = N_2 \cdot \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$.

Dividing: $\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot N_1}{N_2}$.

**Real-World Utility:** **Step-down distribution transformers** at substations where HV is fed as star (11 kV) and LV is delta (433 V) for industrial motor loads.

---

### 2.1.4 🔺⭐ **Delta-Star (Δ-Y) Connection**

**Construction:** Primary in Delta, Secondary in Star with neutral.

**Logic Steps:**
- Primary phase voltage = $V_{L1}$.
- Secondary phase voltage = $\dfrac{V_{L2}}{\sqrt{3}}$.
- **Line-voltage ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{N_1}{\sqrt{3} \cdot N_2}
$$

- Secondary line voltage **lags** primary by 30° (or leads, depending on polarity).
- Provides a **solid grounded neutral** on the secondary — ideal for **4-wire distribution** (3-phase + neutral).
- Used as **Generator Step-Up** units feeding transmission lines.

**Real-World Utility:** Power station generator transformers feeding 220 kV / 400 kV transmission grid, and primary distribution transformers for combined 3-phase + 1-phase loads.

---

### 2.1.5 🔺🔻 **Open Delta (V-V) Connection**

**Construction:** One transformer of a Δ-Δ bank is removed; the remaining two supply 3-phase loads.

**Logic Steps:**
- Each transformer must now carry the full **line current** (not phase current).
- The third line voltage is synthesized vectorially from the other two.
- **Capacity utilization drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 57.7\%$ of the bank rating.**

**Derivation:**

If each transformer rating = $S$ (3-phase), then bank rating = $3S$. In V-V, only two transformers → installed capacity = $2S$. But the deliverable 3-phase power is:

$$
S_{V\text{-}V} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L
$$

For the same line voltage and current equal to transformer rating, this gives $S_{V\text{-}V} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_{rated} = \dfrac{3S}{\sqrt{3}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot S$.

**Wait — correction:** With two transformers each rated $S_{1\phi}$ in V-V, total 3-phase deliverable power = $\sqrt{3} \cdot S_{1\phi}$, while the equivalent Δ-Δ bank would deliver $3 \cdot S_{1\phi}$.

**Utilization Factor:**

$$
UF = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577 \quad \text{(i.e., 57.7%)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key Point:** V-V connection is used for **emergency operation** when one transformer in a Δ-Δ bank fails, and for supplying small 3-phase loads in rural areas economically.

---

### 2.1.6 🟦 **Scott (T-T) Connection**

**Construction:** Two single-phase transformers — a **Main** (tease) and a **Teaser** — interconnected to convert between **2-phase and 3-phase** systems (or vice versa).

**Logic Steps:**
- Main transformer is connected across two lines of the 3-phase system (say R and B) with a center tap.
- Teaser transformer is connected between the third line (Y) and the center tap of the main.
- The **Teaser primary has 0.866 (√3/2) times the turns of the Main primary** (or vice versa for step-down).
- Phase displacement of **90°** is achieved between the two secondary outputs.
- The 3rd harmonic voltages cancel at the neutral.

**Tap Ratio Derivation:**

Main primary voltage = $V_{RB} = V_L$.
Teaser primary voltage = $V_{RN'} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot V_L$ (since `N'` is the center tap of RB).

So: $\dfrac{N_{teaser}}{N_{main}} = \dfrac{V_{RN'}}{V_{RB}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 0.866$.

**Real-World Utility:** Supplying **2-phase loads** (legacy systems like railway traction, old industrial motors) from a 3-phase grid.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **ALL formulas here are KTU 2024 Module 2 exam-critical. Memorize the table structure.**

| # | Connection | Line Voltage Ratio $V_{L1}/V_{L2}$ | Turns Ratio $N_1/N_2$ | Phase Shift | Vector Group |
|---|------------|------------------------------------|------------------------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Y-y** | $\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | $N_1/N_2$ | **0°** | Yy0 |
| 2 | **Δ-Δ** | $\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | $N_1/N_2$ | **0°** | Dd0 |
| 3 | **Y-Δ** | $\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | $N_1/N_2$ | **±30°** | Yd1 / Yd11 |
| 4 | **Δ-Y** | $\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{N_1}{\sqrt{3} \cdot N_2}$ | $N_1/N_2$ | **±30°** | Dy1 / Dy11 |
| 5 | **V-V** | Same as Δ-Δ | Same as Δ-Δ | 0° (but unbalance) | — |
| 6 | **Scott (T-T)** | — | $N_{teaser} = 0.866 \, N_{main}$ | 90° between 2-ph outputs | Special |

| # | Universal Relation | Formula | Constant |
|---|--------------------|---------|----------|
| A | Star Line-Phase | $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ | $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$ |
| B | Delta Line-Current | $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$ | $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$ |
| C | V-V Utilization | $UF = 1/\sqrt{3}$ | $\approx 0.577$ |
| D | Scott Tap Ratio | $k = \sqrt{3}/2$ | $\approx 0.866$ |
| E | Vector Group Angle | $\theta = n \times 30°$ | $n = 0,1,2,\ldots,11$ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of This Knowledge

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this topic matters in industry (write in KTU viva):**
> 1. **Parallel Operation of Transformers** — Two transformers can be paralleled *only* if their vector groups are *identical* (e.g., Dyn11 with Dyn11). Mismatch causes **large circulating currents** and relay trips.
> 2. **Substation Design** — A power engineer must choose Dyn11 (most common in India, IS 2026 standard) for distribution to balance loads and suppress 3rd harmonics.
> 3. **Grounding Schemes** — Star point with neutral impedance (Peterson coil) is used in Y-Y transformers to limit earth-fault currents.
> 4. **Phase-Shifting Transformers (PSTs)** — Use Y-Δ or Δ-Y with on-load tap changers to control power flow between grids.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Line-Voltage Ratio for Star-Delta (Y-Δ) Connection

**Given:**
- Primary: Star connected with line voltage $V_{L1}$ and phase voltage $V_{ph1}$.
- Secondary: Delta connected with line voltage $V_{L2}$ and phase voltage $V_{ph2}$.
- Turns ratio per phase = $a = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$.
- Flux in core: $\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Find:** The relationship between $V_{L1}$, $V_{L2}$, and $a$.

---

### Step 1 — Express the Primary Phase Voltage

For a sinusoidal flux, the RMS EMF induced in a single winding of $N$ turns is:

$$
E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \phi_m
$$

So for the primary phase winding:

$$
V_{ph1} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_1 \cdot \phi_m
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Secondary Phase Voltage

$$
V_{ph2} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_2 \cdot \phi_m
$$

### Step 3 — Form the Turns Ratio

Dividing Step 1 by Step 2:

$$
\dfrac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = a
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Star Relation on the Primary Side

For star connection, the line voltage is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase voltage:

$$
V_{L1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{ph1} = \dfrac{V_{L1}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Delta Relation on the Secondary Side

For delta connection, the line voltage equals the phase voltage:

$$
V_{L2} = V_{ph2}
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute Into the Turns Ratio

$$
a = \dfrac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}} = \dfrac{V_{L1}/\sqrt{3}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{V_{L1}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L2}}
$$

### Step 7 — Rearrange to Find $V_{L1}/V_{L2}$

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot a = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}}
$$

> **Logic Note:** The $\sqrt{3}$ factor appears *only* on the side that is star-connected. If the *secondary* is star, the factor moves to the denominator. This is the most-tested result in KTU Module 2.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — V-V (Open Delta) Utilization Factor

**Given:**
- A Δ-Δ bank has 3 single-phase transformers, each rated $S_1$ (kVA).
- One transformer fails → only 2 remain in V-V.
- Original line voltage = $V_L$, original line current = $I_L$.

**Find:** The maximum deliverable 3-phase kVA in V-V.

### Step 1 — Original 3-Phase Bank Capacity

For balanced 3-phase:

$$
S_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L
$$

### Step 2 — Per-Transformer Rating in Original Bank

Each of the 3 transformers in Δ carries phase current $I_{ph} = I_L / \sqrt{3}$ and has phase voltage $V_{ph} = V_L$.

Single-phase rating of each:

$$
S_1 = V_L \cdot I_{ph} = V_L \cdot \dfrac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{V_L \cdot I_L}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Bank Rating

$$
S_{bank} = 3 \cdot S_1 = \dfrac{3 \cdot V_L \cdot I_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L
$$

(Consistent with Step 1.)

### Step 4 — V-V Configuration (Two Transformers)

In V-V, the two transformers are connected between lines R-Y and Y-B (say). The voltage across each transformer is still $V_L$. But now the **line current = transformer current** (no $\sqrt{3}$ division).

So the current each transformer can carry = $I_1$ (its nameplate rating).

Total 3-phase power deliverable:

$$
S_{V\text{-}V} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_1
$$

### Step 5 — Express in Terms of $S_1$

From Step 2: $I_1 = S_1 / V_L$.

Substituting:

$$
S_{V\text{-}V} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \dfrac{S_1}{V_L} = \sqrt{3} \cdot S_1
$$

### Step 6 — Utilization Factor (UF)

$$
UF = \dfrac{S_{V\text{-}V}}{S_{bank}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot S_1}{3 \cdot S_1} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577
$$

$$
\boxed{UF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 57.7\%}
$$

> **Intuition Check:** You are paying for 2 transformers but getting only $1.732 \cdot S_1$ of 3-phase power instead of $3 \cdot S_1$. Hence the 57.7% utilization.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Scott (T-T) Connection Tap Ratio

**Given:**
- 3-phase system with line voltages $V_{RY}$, $V_{YB}$, $V_{BR}$ forming a balanced 120° set.
- Main transformer: connected between R and B, with center tap `N'` at the midpoint.
- Teaser transformer: connected between Y and `N'`.
- Required: 2-phase outputs with 90° phase difference.

**Find:** The required turns ratio $N_T / N_M$ (Teaser to Main).

### Step 1 — Voltage Across the Main Primary

The Main primary is across R-B:

$$
V_{main} = V_{RB} = V_L \quad \text{(line voltage)}
$$

### Step 2 — Voltage Across the Teaser Primary

The Teaser primary is between Y and the **center tap** `N'` of R-B.

The center tap of R-B is at the midpoint of the line voltage. The vector position of `N'` is the average of the R and B phasors:

$$
\vec{V}_{N'} = \dfrac{\vec{V}_R + \vec{V}_B}{2}
$$

If we take $\vec{V}_R = V \angle 0°$ and $\vec{V}_B = V \angle -120°$:

$$
\vec{V}_{N'} = \dfrac{V \angle 0° + V \angle -120°}{2} = \dfrac{V}{2} \left(1 + \cos(-120°) + j \sin(-120°)\right)
$$

$$
= \dfrac{V}{2} \left(1 - 0.5 - j \cdot 0.866\right) = \dfrac{V}{2} \left(0.5 - j \cdot 0.866\right)
$$

The magnitude:

$$
\vert \vec{V}_{N'} \vert = \dfrac{V}{2} \sqrt{0.5^2 + 0.866^2} = \dfrac{V}{2} \sqrt{0.25 + 0.75} = \dfrac{V}{2}
$$

So the center tap `N'` is at a potential of $V/2$ from both R and B.

### Step 3 — Voltage Between Y and N'

The vector $\vec{V}_Y$ is at $V \angle +120°$ (assuming RYB sequence).

$$
\vec{V}_{Y} - \vec{V}_{N'} = V \angle 120° - \dfrac{V}{2} \angle 0°
$$

In rectangular form:
- $V_Y = V(\cos 120° + j \sin 120°) = V(-0.5 + j \cdot 0.866)$
- $V_{N'} = V/2 + j0$

Difference:

$$
\vec{V}_{YN'} = V \left(-0.5 - 0.5\right) + j \cdot V(0.866 - 0) = V(-1 + j \cdot 0.866)
$$

Magnitude:

$$
\vert \vec{V}_{YN'} \vert = V \sqrt{(-1)^2 + (0.866)^2} = V \sqrt{1 + 0.75} = V \sqrt{1.75} = V \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{7}}{2}
$$

Hmm — let me redo this with the **standard KTU Scott derivation** using line-to-neutral voltages.

### Step 4 — Cleaner Derivation (KTU Standard Method)

Let line-to-neutral voltages be $V_{RN} = V_{YN} = V_{BN} = V_{ph}$ where $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$.

The **Main transformer primary** is connected R-B, so its primary voltage is:

$$
V_{main} = V_{RB} = V_{RN} - V_{BN} = V_L
$$

The **Teaser primary** is connected Y-N', where N' is the center tap of R-B. So:

$$
V_{YN'} = V_{YN} - V_{N'N}
$$

Since N' is the midpoint of R-B:

$$
V_{N'N} = \dfrac{V_{RN} + V_{BN}}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{YN'} = V_{YN} - \dfrac{V_{RN} + V_{BN}}{2}
$$

### Step 5 — Magnitude Calculation

Using phasor values $V_{RN} = V_{ph} \angle 0°$, $V_{YN} = V_{ph} \angle 120°$, $V_{BN} = V_{ph} \angle -120°$:

$$
V_{N'N} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{2} \left(1 + e^{-j120°}\right) = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{2} \left(1 + \cos 120° - j \sin 120°\right)
$$

$$
= \dfrac{V_{ph}}{2} \left(1 - 0.5 - j \cdot 0.866\right) = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{2} (0.5 - j \cdot 0.866) = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{2} \angle -60°
$$

So $V_{N'N} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{2} \angle -60°$.

And $V_{YN} = V_{ph} \angle 120°$.

Therefore:

$$
V_{YN'} = V_{YN} - V_{N'N} = V_{ph} \angle 120° - \dfrac{V_{ph}}{2} \angle -60°
$$

Converting to rectangular:

- $V_{YN} = V_{ph}(-0.5 + j \cdot 0.866)$
- $V_{N'N} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{2}(0.5 - j \cdot 0.866) = V_{ph}(0.25 - j \cdot 0.433)$

Subtracting:

$$
V_{YN'} = V_{ph}(-0.5 - 0.25) + j \cdot V_{ph}(0.866 + 0.433) = V_{ph}(-0.75 + j \cdot 1.299)
$$

Magnitude:

$$
\vert V_{YN'} \vert = V_{ph} \sqrt{0.75^2 + 1.299^2} = V_{ph} \sqrt{0.5625 + 1.687} = V_{ph} \sqrt{2.25} = 1.5 \cdot V_{ph}
$$

### Step 6 — Turns Ratio

$$
\dfrac{N_{teaser}}{N_{main}} = \dfrac{\vert V_{YN'} \vert}{\vert V_{RB} \vert} = \dfrac{1.5 \cdot V_{ph}}{V_L} = \dfrac{1.5 \cdot V_{ph}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}} = \dfrac{1.5}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{1.5 \sqrt{3}}{3} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866
$$

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{N_{teaser}}{N_{main}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866}
$$

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Y-Y | Δ-Δ | Y-Δ | Δ-Y | V-V | Scott (T-T) |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-------------|
| **Neutral Available** | ✅ Both sides | ❌ Neither | ❌ Only Primary | ✅ Only Secondary | ❌ | ❌ |
| **3rd Harmonic Suppression** | ❌ Poor | ✅ Excellent (delta) | ✅ Secondary absorbs | ✅ Primary absorbs | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Phase Shift** | 0° | 0° | ±30° | ±30° | 0° | 90° (2-phase) |
| **Insulation on HV Side** | Low (V_ph) | High (V_L) | Low (V_ph) | High (V_L) | High | Depends |
| **Unbalanced Load Capability** | Poor (without tertiary) | Excellent | Poor | Good (with neutral) | Poor | Moderate |
| **Common Use Case** | Auto-transformers, distribution | Generator step-up | Substation step-down | Substation step-up | Emergency / Rural | 2-phase supply |
| **Capacity Utilization** | 100% | 100% | 100% | 100% | 57.7% | 100% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Star-Star (Y-Y) Connection Phasor Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Primary Winding A (R)"] --> Pn["Neutral Point N1"]
    P2["Primary Winding B (Y)"] --> Pn
    P3["Primary Winding C (B)"] --> Pn
    Pn -.-> Pn2["Grounded Neutral"]
    S1["Secondary Winding a (r)"] --> Sn["Neutral Point N2"]
    S2["Secondary Winding b (y)"] --> Sn
    S3["Secondary Winding c (b)"] --> Sn
    Sn -.-> Sn2["Grounded Neutral"]
    P1 --- MagCore["Common Magnetic Core"]
    S1 --- MagCore
    P2 --- MagCore
    S2 --- MagCore
    P3 --- MagCore
    S3 --- MagCore
```

**Reading Guide:** All three primary windings meet at a common neutral `N1`; same for secondary at `N2`. Both neutrals can be grounded to provide a 4-wire distribution system.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Delta-Delta (Δ-Δ) Closed-Loop Connection

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Line R / r"] --> W1["Winding AB"]
    W1 --> B["Line Y / y"]
    B --> W2["Winding BC"]
    W2 --> C["Line B / b"]
    C --> W3["Winding CA"]
    W3 --> A
    note1["Triplen harmonics circulate in this closed loop and cancel in the line"] -.-> W1
    note1 -.-> W2
    note1 -.-> W3
```

**Reading Guide:** The windings form a closed mesh. Line terminals R, Y, B are the nodes of the delta. No neutral point exists.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Vector Group Clock Notation Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Define Vector Group for 3-Phase Transformer"] --> Q1{"Does primary line voltage lead secondary by 30 degrees?"}
    Q1 -- YES --> Group1["Vector group ends with 11 - e.g. Dy11, Yd11"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"Does secondary line voltage lag primary by 30 degrees?"}
    Q2 -- YES --> Group2["Vector group ends with 1 - e.g. Dy1, Yd1"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"Is there no phase shift between primary and secondary?"}
    Q3 -- YES --> Group3["Vector group ends with 0 or 6 - e.g. Dd0, Yy0"]
    Q3 -- NO --> Group4["Special case - Scott T-T or phase shifting transformer"]
    Group1 --> Out["Each digit represents 30 degree phase displacement - 0 to 11 on a clock face"]
    Group2 --> Out
    Group3 --> Out
    Group4 --> Out
```

**Reading Guide:** The IEC clock notation imagines a clock face. The minute hand represents the secondary line voltage (always at 12 o'clock = 0°). The hour hand represents the primary line voltage's position. The hour number × 30° = phase angle.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Scott (T-T) Connection Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["3-Phase Line R"] --> MT["Main Transformer Primary"]
    B["3-Phase Line B"] --> MT
    MT --> Tap["Center Tap N prime"]
    Y["3-Phase Line Y"] --> TT["Teaser Transformer Primary"]
    Tap --> TT
    MT --> MS["Main Secondary - Phase 1 Output"]
    TT --> TS["Teaser Secondary - Phase 2 Output"]
    MS --> O1["2-Phase Load - Phase A"]
    TS --> O2["2-Phase Load - Phase B"]
    note2["Teaser turns = 0.866 times Main turns - 90 degree phase shift between outputs"] -.-> TT
    note2 -.-> MT
```

**Reading Guide:** The Main transformer bridges R-B lines. The Teaser transformer connects Y to the center tap of the Main. The two secondaries deliver 2-phase power with 90° phase difference.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — V-V (Open Delta) Bank Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["3-Phase Load Demand"] --> Q{"Is 3rd transformer available?"}
    Q -- YES --> Full["Install full Delta-Delta bank - 100 percent capacity"]
    Q -- NO --> Q2{"Can load tolerate 57.7 percent capacity?"}
    Q2 -- YES --> VV["Use V-V Open Delta connection with 2 transformers"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"Need to convert to single phase?"}
    Q3 -- YES --> SP["Use Scott T-T connection"]
    Q3 -- NO --> Wait["Wait for replacement transformer or shed load"]
    VV --> Util["Utilization factor equals 1 over square root of 3 = 0.577"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** What is meant by the vector group of a 3-phase transformer? State its significance in the parallel operation of transformers. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

A **vector group** is a standard IEC notation (e.g., Dyn11, Yyn0) that identifies:

1. **The type of connection** of primary and secondary windings (Y = Star, D = Delta, Z = Zig-zag, N = Neutral brought out).
2. **The phase displacement angle** between primary and secondary line voltages, expressed as a clock number (0 to 11), where each unit = **30°**.

**Significance in Parallel Operation:**

- Two transformers can be successfully paralleled **only if their vector groups are identical**.
- Mismatched vector groups cause a **phase difference** between the secondary EMFs, resulting in large **circulating currents** proportional to $\sin(\theta/2)$ where $\theta$ is the angle difference.
- This circulating current may be several times the rated current, causing overheating and protective relay operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "vector group gives the voltage ratio" — *wrong*. Voltage ratio comes from the **turns ratio**, not the vector group. Vector group gives the *phase displacement*.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Why is a delta winding often provided as a tertiary winding in star-star connected transformers? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

A star-star transformer suffers from **third-harmonic distortion** in the secondary line-to-neutral voltages because:

- The 3rd harmonic flux in each phase is in phase (not 120° apart).
- With no closed delta path, the 3rd harmonic flux links the secondary, producing a **peaky waveform** and **neutral shift**.

**The tertiary delta winding solves this by:**

1. Providing a **closed circulating path** for 3rd harmonic currents within the delta.
2. These circulating currents **oppose the 3rd harmonic flux** (Lenz's law), cancelling it in the core.
3. Result: The secondary line-to-neutral voltage becomes a pure sine wave.
4. Additionally, the tertiary winding can be used to supply **auxiliary loads** (station auxiliaries) and helps **stabilize the neutral** during unbalanced earth faults.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between line voltages and turns ratio for a **star-delta (Y-Δ) connected 3-phase transformer**. State the vector group and sketch the connection diagram. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 100 kVA, 11000/433 V transformer is connected **star-delta (Y-Δ)**. Find: (i) The phase voltages on both sides, (ii) The turns ratio, (iii) The line currents at full load, (iv) The phase currents on both sides. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Connection Diagram:**

The primary is connected in star with a grounded neutral; the secondary in delta.

**Derivation:**

Let $V_{L1}$ and $V_{L2}$ be the primary and secondary line voltages. Let $N_1$ and $N_2$ be the respective phase turns. Let $\phi$ be the mutual flux.

**Step 1:** Induced EMFs (RMS):

$$
E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \phi_m \quad \text{and} \quad E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \phi_m
$$

**Step 2:** Primary phase voltage $V_{ph1} = E_1$ (assuming ideal).

Primary is star connected → $V_{L1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot E_1$.

Secondary is delta connected → $V_{L2} = V_{ph2} = E_2$.

**Step 3:** Form the ratio:

$$
\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot E_1}{E_2} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot a, \quad \text{where} \ a = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}}
$$

**Vector Group:** **Yd11** (secondary line voltage leads primary by 30°) or **Yd1** (lags by 30°), depending on winding polarity convention. Standard in India is **Dyn11** (delta primary, star secondary) but Y-Δ is the inverse.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given Data:**
- Rating: $S = 100$ kVA (3-phase)
- Primary line voltage: $V_{L1} = 11000$ V
- Secondary line voltage: $V_{L2} = 433$ V
- Connection: Y-Δ

**Step (i) — Phase voltages:**

Primary (Y) phase voltage:

$$
V_{ph1} = \dfrac{V_{L1}}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{11000}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{11000}{1.732} = 6350.85 \text{ V} \approx 6.35 \text{ kV}
$$

Secondary (Δ) phase voltage:

$$
V_{ph2} = V_{L2} = 433 \text{ V}
$$

**Step (ii) — Turns ratio:**

Using the Y-Δ relation derived in (a):

$$
a = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_{L1}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L2}} = \dfrac{11000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 433} = \dfrac{11000}{750} = 14.67
$$

So $N_1 : N_2 \approx 14.67 : 1$ (or about 15:1).

**Step (iii) — Line currents at full load:**

For 3-phase rating: $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L1} \cdot I_{L1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L2} \cdot I_{L2}$.

Primary line current:

$$
I_{L1} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L1}} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 11000} = \dfrac{100000}{19052.56} = 5.248 \text{ A} \approx 5.25 \text{ A}
$$

Secondary line current:

$$
I_{L2} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L2}} = \dfrac{100000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 433} = \dfrac{100000}{750} = 133.33 \text{ A}
$$

**Step (iv) — Phase currents:**

Primary (Y): $I_{ph1} = I_{L1} = 5.25$ A.

Secondary (Δ): $I_{ph2} = \dfrac{I_{L2}}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{133.33}{\sqrt{3}} = 76.98 \text{ A} \approx 77$ A.

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Primary (Y) | Secondary (Δ) |
|----------|-------------|----------------|
| Line Voltage | 11000 V | 433 V |
| Phase Voltage | 6350.85 V | 433 V |
| Line Current | 5.25 A | 133.33 A |
| Phase Current | 5.25 A | 76.98 A |
| Turns Ratio | 14.67 : 1 | — |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Explain the **open delta (V-V) connection** of two single-phase transformers used to supply a 3-phase load. Derive the **utilization factor** and state one practical application. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 75 kVA load is to be supplied by a **Δ-Δ transformer bank**. If one transformer develops a fault and is removed, the bank operates in **V-V mode**. Calculate: (i) The kVA rating of each transformer (original), (ii) The maximum 3-phase kVA that can now be supplied, (iii) The percentage of load that must be shed to keep the transformers within rating. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Construction:**

In a V-V connection, only two single-phase transformers are connected to a 3-phase supply. The third "missing" transformer is the one that has failed or is being economized.

- Transformer 1: Connected between lines R and Y.
- Transformer 2: Connected between lines Y and B.
- Line voltage between R and B is the **vector difference** of voltages across the two transformers.

**Phasor Diagram Concept:**

If $V_{RY}$ and $V_{YB}$ are the two transformer voltages at 120° apart, the third line voltage $V_{BR} = -(V_{RY} + V_{YB})$ also exists automatically at the correct magnitude and phase. The 3-phase system is "synthesized" from two transformers.

**Derivation of Utilization Factor:**

Let each transformer be rated $S_1$ (single-phase kVA) at line voltage $V_L$.

Original Δ-Δ bank rating: $S_{bank} = 3 \cdot S_1$ (with proper line current $\sqrt{3} \cdot S_1 / V_L$).

In V-V mode, total 3-phase power deliverable:

$$
S_{V\text{-}V} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_1, \quad \text{where} \ I_1 = S_1 / V_L
$$

So $S_{V\text{-}V} = \sqrt{3} \cdot S_1$.

Utilization factor:

$$
UF = \dfrac{S_{V\text{-}V}}{S_{bank}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot S_1}{3 \cdot S_1} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577 \ \text{or} \ 57.7\%
$$

**Practical Application:** Emergency/standby operation when one transformer in a Δ-Δ bank fails; also used to supply small 3-phase loads (e.g., a 3-phase motor in a rural workshop) where the cost of a third transformer is not justified.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given Data:**
- 3-phase load = 75 kVA
- Original connection: Δ-Δ
- One transformer faulted → operates in V-V

**Step (i) — Original per-transformer kVA rating:**

In a balanced Δ-Δ bank, total bank kVA is shared equally among 3 transformers:

$$
S_1 = \dfrac{S_{bank}}{3} = \dfrac{75}{3} = 25 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step (ii) — Maximum deliverable 3-phase kVA in V-V mode:**

$$
S_{V\text{-}V} = \sqrt{3} \cdot S_1 = \sqrt{3} \cdot 25 = 1.732 \cdot 25 = 43.30 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step (iii) — Load to be shed:**

Excess load over V-V capacity:

$$
\Delta S = 75 - 43.30 = 31.70 \text{ kVA}
$$

Percentage to be shed:

$$
\dfrac{31.70}{75} \times 100\% = 42.27\%
$$

**Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Original per-transformer kVA | 25 kVA |
| Original bank kVA | 75 kVA |
| V-V mode kVA | 43.30 kVA |
| Load to be shed | 31.70 kVA |
| Percentage shed | 42.27% |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Where Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams (Module 2):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the √3 factor:** When asked for line-voltage ratio in Y-Δ, students write $V_{L1}/V_{L2} = N_1/N_2$ — **WRONG**. It is $\sqrt{3} \cdot N_1/N_2$. KTU examiners *specifically* test this. → **−2 marks**
>
> 2. **Confusing line and phase current in delta:** In Δ, $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$ (NOT equal). Many students write them as equal. → **−1 mark**
>
> 3. **Vector group notation error:** Writing "Dy 11" with a space, lowercase, or wrong format. Correct IEC format is **Dyn11** (no space, capital D/Y/Z, lowercase n for neutral, no space before digit). → **−0.5 mark**
>
> 4. **V-V derivation skipped steps:** Showing only the final answer $1/\sqrt{3}$ without derivation → loses 4 of 7 marks. Always show the relation $S_{V-V} = \sqrt{3} \cdot S_1$ and the bank rating $3 \cdot S_1$.
>
> 5. **Scott connection tap ratio:** Writing "$\sqrt{3}/2$" without specifying it's the Teaser-to-Main ratio → incomplete answer. Always say: *"The Teaser transformer has $\sqrt{3}/2 = 0.866$ times the turns of the Main transformer."*
>
> 6. **Open-delta load current direction:** Students often forget that in V-V, each transformer carries **full line current** (not phase current). This is the *root cause* of the 57.7% utilization.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 High-Density Rapid-Revision Bullets (Read 5 times before exam):**
>
> ✅ **Six major connections studied:** Y-y, Δ-Δ, Y-Δ, Δ-Y, V-V (Open Δ), Scott (T-T).
>
> ✅ **Star relation:** $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ ; $I_L = I_{ph}$.
>
> ✅ **Delta relation:** $V_L = V_{ph}$ ; $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$.
>
> ✅ **Y-Δ ratio formula:** $\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ *(√3 on primary, the side that's Y)*.
>
> ✅ **Δ-Y ratio formula:** $\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{N_1}{\sqrt{3} \cdot N_2}$ *(√3 on secondary, the side that's Y)*.
>
> ✅ **Vector group digits:** 0 to 11, each = 30° phase shift. Dyn11 is the **most common in Indian distribution** (IS 2026).
>
> ✅ **Y-Y disadvantage:** 3rd harmonic distortion → solved by **tertiary delta winding**.
>
> ✅ **Δ-Δ advantage:** Best for unbalanced loads and harmonic suppression.
>
> ✅ **Y-Δ use case:** Step-down substation transformer (HV star, LV delta).
>
> ✅ **Δ-Y use case:** Step-up generator transformer; 4-wire distribution with neutral.
>
> ✅ **V-V (Open Delta) Utilization Factor:** $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577 = 57.7\%$.
>
> ✅ **Scott T-T tap ratio:** $\dfrac{N_{teaser}}{N_{main}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$.
>
> ✅ **Scott application:** 2-phase to 3-phase (or vice versa) conversion; railway and legacy industrial loads.
>
> ✅ **Parallel operation rule:** Vector groups must be **identical**; otherwise, large circulating currents flow.
>
> ✅ **Insulation economy:** Star side requires lower insulation (only $V_L/\sqrt{3}$) — so **HV side is usually Y**.
>
> ✅ **Neutral shift problem:** In Y-Y without neutral or tertiary, the neutral point "floats" → unbalanced voltages. Tertiary delta fixes this.
>
> ✅ **Neutral availability matrix:** Y-Y → both sides; Δ-Y → secondary only; Y-Δ → primary only; Δ-Δ → neither.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Y-Y
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Y-Y

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Star-Star (Y-Y) connection** is a three-phase transformer winding configuration in which **both the primary and secondary windings** of a three-phase transformer (or a bank of three single-phase transformers) are connected in **star (Y)** formation. In this configuration, one terminal of each phase winding is joined at a common, grounded point called the **star point** or **neutral point (N)**, while the other three terminals are connected to the respective three-phase line conductors.

Formally, if the line-to-line voltage is $V_L$, then the **phase voltage** across each winding is:

$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

The relationship between line current and phase current in a Y-connected system is:

$$I_L = I_{ph}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Y-Y connection is the most economical three-phase connection for **high-voltage transmission systems** because it requires **less insulation** (the phase voltage is $1/\sqrt{3}$ times the line voltage), thereby reducing the copper requirement and overall cost of the windings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hub & Spoke" Model

Imagine a **bicycle wheel** with three spokes:
- The **central hub** is the **neutral point (N)** — a single shared connection point.
- The three **spokes radiating outward** are the three phase windings (R, Y, B) — each one having a "live" end and a "neutral" end joined at the hub.
- When you stack two wheels on the same axle (one for the **primary side** and one for the **secondary side**), you essentially get a **Y-Y coupled transformer system**.

The **hub (neutral)** is common to all three phases. The voltage on each spoke is measured **from the rim (line) to the hub (neutral)** — this is the **phase voltage**, which is always $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ times the line voltage between any two rims.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Relation |
|---|---|
| Standard line frequency (India) | **50 Hz** |
| Phase voltage vs Line voltage | $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3} \approx 0.577 \, V_L$ |
| Line current vs Phase current | $I_L = I_{ph}$ |
| Turns ratio (per phase) | $N_1 / N_2$ |
| Transformation ratio (line-to-line) | $V_{L1} / V_{L2} = N_1 / N_2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** In the **Delta-Delta (Δ-Δ)** connection, $V_{ph} = V_L$ and $I_{ph} = I_L / \sqrt{3}$, which is exactly the *inverse* of the Y-Y relationship. This is a high-yield comparison question in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

A Y-Y connected three-phase transformer operates on the following sequential principles:

1. **Three-phase source** is applied across the primary terminals (R₁, Y₁, B₁), each connected to a corresponding phase winding.
2. The **common point (N₁)** of the primary star acts as the return path and is typically **grounded** for safety and reference.
3. **Mutual induction** transfers energy from primary to secondary windings via the shared magnetic core flux.
4. On the secondary side, the three phase windings are also joined at **N₂**, producing line-to-line voltages $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase voltages.
5. The **phase sequence** (R-Y-B) is preserved on both sides; hence, Y-Y is classified as a **zero-phase-shift (0°) connection**.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why star on both sides?** It reduces the **voltage stress per turn** of the winding insulation by a factor of $\sqrt{3}$, making it ideal for EHV (Extra High Voltage) systems.
- **Why is the neutral grounded?** It provides a **stable reference potential**, suppresses third-harmonic voltages, and offers a path for **zero-sequence currents** during unbalanced loads or ground faults.
- **Why no phase shift?** Because both windings are wound in the same direction with identical polarity marking, the secondary line-to-line voltage is **in phase** with the primary line-to-line voltage.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Phase voltage (Y-side) | $V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Line voltage (Y-side) | $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Line current (Y-side) | $I_L = I_{ph}$ | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | Transformation ratio | $\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Per-phase equivalent | $V_1 / V_2 = N_1 / N_2$ | Per phase |
| 6 | Power (3-phase balanced) | $P = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \cos\phi$ | Watts (W) |
| 7 | Total kVA rating | $S = 3 \times V_{ph} \times I_{ph}$ | VA |
| 8 | Phase displacement | $0^\circ$ | Identical R-Y-B |
| 9 | Third-harmonic voltage | $V_{3h} = 3 \cdot V_{3,ph}$ | Across neutral |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Never confuse the **phase voltage** (winding voltage) with the **line voltage** (terminal voltage). In KTU problems, the *given voltage* is almost always the **line-to-line value** unless explicitly stated otherwise. A common mark-loss point is forgetting to divide by $\sqrt{3}$ when computing the actual winding voltage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Y-Y is Preferred |
|---|---|
| **EHV Power Transmission** (66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV) | Reduces insulation cost; neutral grounding for fault current path |
| **Step-up at Generating Stations** | Generator side often connected in star; step-up to transmission voltage |
| **Distribution Transformers** (with neutral for single-phase loads) | Provides a neutral wire for domestic 230 V single-phase supply |
| **Scott-T and Other Special Connections** | Y-Y serves as the base winding before tapping for special transformations |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Y-Y connection **fails for single-phase loads on the secondary side** if the primary neutral is not solidly grounded. This causes severe **neutral displacement** (neutral point shifts) under unbalanced loading — a frequent 3-mark short-answer trap.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Line Voltage to Phase Voltage Relationship in Star Connection

**Given:** Three phase windings R, Y, B connected such that one terminal of each joins at a common neutral N.

Let the phase voltages (from line to neutral) be:
- $\vec{V}_{RN} = V_{ph} \angle 0^\circ$
- $\vec{V}_{YN} = V_{ph} \angle -120^\circ$
- $\vec{V}_{BN} = V_{ph} \angle +120^\circ$

The line-to-line voltage between R and Y is found by **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the closed loop R–N–Y:

$$\vec{V}_{RY} = \vec{V}_{RN} - \vec{V}_{YN}$$

Substituting the phasors:

$$\vec{V}_{RY} = V_{ph} \angle 0^\circ - V_{ph} \angle -120^\circ$$

Converting to rectangular form:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{V}_{RN} &= V_{ph}(1 + j0) \\
\vec{V}_{YN} &= V_{ph}\left(-\frac{1}{2} - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Subtracting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{V}_{RY} &= V_{ph}\left(1 - \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)\right) + jV_{ph}\left(0 - \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\right) \\
&= V_{ph}\left(\frac{3}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Computing the magnitude:

$$|\vec{V}_{RY}| = V_{ph}\sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} = V_{ph}\sqrt{\frac{9}{4} + \frac{3}{4}} = V_{ph}\sqrt{3}$$

$$\therefore \quad V_{RY} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph} \implies \boxed{V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}}$$

This completes the rigorous derivation of the Y-connection voltage relationship.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Transformation Ratio for Y-Y Connection

Let $N_1$ and $N_2$ be the **turns per phase** on primary and secondary respectively.

Per-phase transformation:

$$\frac{V_{1,ph}}{V_{2,ph}} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

Substituting $V_{1,ph} = V_{L1}/\sqrt{3}$ and $V_{2,ph} = V_{L2}/\sqrt{3}$:

$$\frac{V_{L1} / \sqrt{3}}{V_{L2} / \sqrt{3}} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

The $\sqrt{3}$ cancels out, yielding:

$$\boxed{\frac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}}$$

**Conclusion:** The transformation ratio in a Y-Y connected three-phase transformer is **the same as the per-phase turns ratio**. This is a clean, high-yield KTU derivation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Third-Harmonic Behaviour in Y-Y (Crucial KTU Concept)

In a Y-Y system with **ungrounded neutral**, the third-harmonic fluxes in the three core legs are **in phase** (since $3 \times 120^\circ = 360^\circ \equiv 0^\circ$). They add up rather than cancel, producing a strong **third-harmonic flux** $\Phi_3$ in the core.

The induced third-harmonic EMF per phase is:

$$E_{3,ph} = 4.44 \cdot f_3 \cdot N \cdot \Phi_3 = 4.44 \cdot (3f) \cdot N \cdot \Phi_3$$

Since there is **no return path** for third-harmonic currents in the line conductors (because they are co-phasal and sum to zero in the lines), the third-harmonic flux is forced through the iron core, leading to:
- Excessive **core losses** (hysteresis + eddy current)
- **Voltage waveform distortion** (peaked sine waves)
- Possible **dielectric breakdown** of insulation

**Mitigation Strategies:**
1. **Solidly ground the primary neutral** — provides a return path for the third-harmonic currents, suppressing $\Phi_3$.
2. **Add a tertiary (Delta-connected) winding** — the third winding, even if unloaded, provides a closed circulating path for third-harmonic currents. This is the **most common industrial solution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Always mention the **tertiary winding (Δ-connected)** as the standard remedy for third-harmonic distortion in Y-Y transformers. It is a guaranteed 3-mark question.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Y-Y vs Other Connections

| Parameter | Y-Y | Y-Δ | Δ-Y | Δ-Δ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Phase voltage / Line voltage | $1/\sqrt{3}$ | $1/\sqrt{3}$ (primary) | $\sqrt{3}$ (primary) | 1 |
| Line current / Phase current | 1 | $\sqrt{3}$ (primary) | $\sqrt{3}$ (secondary) | $\sqrt{3}$ |
| Phase shift | **0°** | 30° (lag) | 30° (lead) | 0° |
| Neutral available | **Yes** | No (primary only) | Yes (secondary) | No |
| Third-harmonic issue | **Yes (severe)** | No | No | No |
| Insulation cost | **Lowest** | Medium | Medium | Highest |
| Suitable for unbalanced load | Poor (needs grounded neutral) | Good | Good | Good |
| Common use | EHV transmission | Step-down distribution | Step-up at generating stations | Industrial low-voltage |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Y-Y Three-Phase Transformer Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["THREE PHASE SOURCE"] --> node2["R LINE"]
    node1 --> node3["Y LINE"]
    node1 --> node4["B LINE"]
    node2 --> node5["PRIMARY WINDING R1"]
    node3 --> node6["PRIMARY WINDING Y1"]
    node4 --> node7["PRIMARY WINDING B1"]
    node5 --> node8["PRIMARY NEUTRAL N1 GROUNDED"]
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node8
    node5 -.MUTUAL FLUX.-> node9
    node6 -.MUTUAL FLUX.-> node10
    node7 -.MUTUAL FLUX.-> node11
    node9["SECONDARY WINDING R2"] --> node12["R LINE LOAD"]
    node10["SECONDARY WINDING Y2"] --> node13["Y LINE LOAD"]
    node11["SECONDARY WINDING B2"] --> node14["B LINE LOAD"]
    node9 --> node15["SECONDARY NEUTRAL N2"]
    node10 --> node15
    node11 --> node15
    node8 --> node16["PROVIDES ZERO SEQUENCE PATH"]
    node15 --> node16
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Phasor Diagram — Y-Y Voltage Relationships

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["V_RN PHASE VOLTAGE"] --> nodeB["V_YN 120 DEG LAG"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["V_BN 120 DEG LEAD"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["V_RY LINE VOLTAGE SQRT 3 TIMES PHASE"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["V_YB LINE VOLTAGE"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["V_BR LINE VOLTAGE"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["0 DEG PHASE SHIFT BETWEEN PRIMARY AND SECONDARY"]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Energy Flow in Y-Y Transformer

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["PRIMARY SIDE Y CONNECTION"] --> stepB["THREE PHASE FLUX IN CORE"]
    stepB --> stepC["MUTUAL INDUCTION AT FREQUENCY F"]
    stepC --> stepD["SECONDARY SIDE Y CONNECTION"]
    stepD --> stepE["SECONDARY LINE VOLTAGE VL2 = SQRT 3 X VPH2"]
    stepE --> stepF["LOAD CURRENT IL2 = IPH2"]
    stepF --> stepG["OUTPUT POWER P = SQRT 3 X VL2 X IL2 X COS PHI"]
    stepB --> stepH["THIRD HARMONIC FLUX IF NEUTRAL NOT GROUNDED"]
    stepH --> stepI["CORE SATURATION AND WAVE DISTORTION"]
    stepI --> stepJ["SOLUTION GROUND NEUTRAL OR USE TERTIARY DELTA WINDING"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** The phasor diagram clearly shows that $V_{RY}$ **leads** $V_{RN}$ by exactly **30°**, and its magnitude is $\sqrt{3}$ times larger. This 30° relationship is the geometric basis of the entire Y-connection voltage transformation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain why the Y-Y connection is preferred for high-voltage transmission systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
In the Y-Y connection, the phase voltage across each winding is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ times the line-to-line voltage. This **reduces the insulation requirement** of each winding, lowering the **copper cost** and overall transformer cost. Additionally, the **neutral point can be solidly grounded**, providing a reference potential and a return path for zero-sequence and third-harmonic currents. For these reasons, Y-Y is the standard configuration for **Extra High Voltage (EHV)** transmission networks (66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the relationship between line voltage and phase voltage in a star-connected winding. What is the phase shift in a Y-Y connection?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The relationship in a star connection is:

$$V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph} \quad \text{or} \quad V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

The line current equals the phase current: $I_L = I_{ph}$.
The **phase shift** in a Y-Y connection is **0°** (zero degrees), meaning the secondary line voltages are **in phase** with the corresponding primary line voltages.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**A three-phase, 100 kVA, 11000/440 V, 50 Hz transformer is connected in Y-Y. The per-phase turns ratio is 25:1. Calculate: (a) the rated line and phase currents on both primary and secondary sides; (b) the per-phase induced EMF on the HV and LV sides. Comment on the suitability of this transformer for supplying unbalanced single-phase loads.**

#### (a) Rated Currents on Primary and Secondary Sides (7 Marks)

**Primary (HV) Side — Line current:**

$$I_{L1} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L1}} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000} = \frac{100000}{19052.56}$$

$$\boxed{I_{L1} \approx 5.25 \text{ A}}$$

Since Y-Y: $I_{ph1} = I_{L1} = 5.25$ A

**Secondary (LV) Side — Line current:**

$$I_{L2} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L2}} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 440} = \frac{100000}{762.10}$$

$$\boxed{I_{L2} \approx 131.22 \text{ A}}$$

Since Y-Y: $I_{ph2} = I_{L2} = 131.22$ A

#### (b) Per-Phase Induced EMFs (7 Marks)

**Primary phase voltage:**

$$V_{ph1} = \frac{V_{L1}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{11000}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 6350.85 \text{ V}$$

**Secondary phase voltage:**

$$V_{ph2} = \frac{V_{L2}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{440}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 254.03 \text{ V}$$

**Verification using turns ratio $N_1/N_2 = 25:1$:**

$$\frac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}} = \frac{6350.85}{254.03} \approx 25.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Suitability for Unbalanced Single-Phase Loads:**
The Y-Y connection is **NOT suitable** for highly unbalanced single-phase loads because the **neutral point will shift** (neutral displacement) unless both primary and secondary neutrals are **solidly grounded**. The third-harmonic voltages also distort the waveform. Hence, a Y-Y transformer is recommended only for **balanced three-phase loads** in EHV transmission.

---

### ❓ Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**(a) With a neat phasor diagram, derive the relationship between line voltage and phase voltage in a star-connected three-phase system. (b) A Y-Y connected three-phase transformer has 1500 turns per phase on the primary and 750 turns per phase on the secondary. The primary is connected to a 6.6 kV, 50 Hz supply. Find: (i) the secondary line voltage; (ii) the transformation ratio; (iii) the line current on both sides if the transformer delivers 200 kVA.**

#### (a) Phasor Diagram & Derivation (7 Marks)

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    pa["V_RN"] --> pb["V_YN 120 deg lag"]
    pb --> pc["V_BN 120 deg lead"]
    pa --> pd["V_RY = sqrt3 V_ph leads V_RN by 30 deg"]
```

**Derivation:** (as in Section 3.1 above)

$$\vec{V}_{RY} = \vec{V}_{RN} - \vec{V}_{YN} = V_{ph} \angle 0^\circ - V_{ph} \angle -120^\circ$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{V}_{RY} &= V_{ph}\left[\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) + j\left(0 + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\right] \\
&= V_{ph}\left(\frac{3}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \\
|\vec{V}_{RY}| &= V_{ph} \sqrt{\frac{9}{4} + \frac{3}{4}} = \sqrt{3} \, V_{ph}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{V_L = \sqrt{3} \, V_{ph}}$$

#### (b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** $N_1 = 1500$, $N_2 = 750$, $V_{L1} = 6.6$ kV, $S = 200$ kVA

**(i) Secondary Line Voltage:**

Phase voltage on primary: $V_{ph1} = \frac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.51$ V

Per-phase transformation: $V_{ph2} = V_{ph1} \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 3810.51 \times \frac{750}{1500} = 1905.26$ V

Secondary line voltage (Y-Y):

$$\boxed{V_{L2} = \sqrt{3} \times 1905.26 \approx 3300 \text{ V} = 3.3 \text{ kV}}$$

**(ii) Transformation Ratio:**

$$\boxed{\frac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \frac{6600}{3300} = 2:1 = \frac{N_1}{N_2}}$$

**(iii) Line Currents:**

Primary: $I_{L1} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L1}} = \frac{200000}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600} = \frac{200000}{11431.54} \approx 17.50$ A

Secondary: $I_{L2} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L2}} = \frac{200000}{\sqrt{3} \times 3300} = \frac{200000}{5715.77} \approx 35.00$ A

$$\boxed{I_{L1} \approx 17.50 \text{ A}, \quad I_{L2} \approx 35.00 \text{ A}}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Y-Y Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing $V_L$ and $V_{ph}$:** Forgetting to divide the given line voltage by $\sqrt{3}$ before applying the turns ratio. (Lose 2-3 marks)
> 2. **Ignoring the third-harmonic issue:** A 14-mark question on Y-Y without addressing third-harmonic distortion and the role of grounded neutral / tertiary winding is **incomplete**. (Lose 2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting the phase shift:** Y-Y has **0° phase shift**; students often wrongly write 30°. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **Power factor assumption:** If a problem gives kVA only (no kW), assume $\cos\phi$ is **unity** unless stated. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Forgetting to ground the neutral in single-line diagrams:** Examiners deduct marks if the Y-Y diagram lacks the **ground symbol at the neutral point**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Y-Y connection has **both primary and secondary** windings connected in **star**.
- ✅ Voltage relation: $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$; current relation: $I_L = I_{ph}$.
- ✅ Transformation ratio (line-to-line) equals the per-phase turns ratio: $V_{L1}/V_{L2} = N_1/N_2$.
- ✅ **Phase shift = 0°** (no phase displacement between primary and secondary line voltages).
- ✅ Most **economical for EHV** transmission due to lower insulation cost.
- ✅ **Third-harmonic distortion** is a serious drawback — mitigated by **grounding the neutral** or adding a **tertiary (Δ) winding**.
- ✅ **Unsuitable for unbalanced loads** unless the primary neutral is **solidly grounded**.
- ✅ Neutral provides a path for **zero-sequence** and **third-harmonic** currents.
- ✅ Y-Y is used at the **sending end** of long transmission lines and in **step-down distribution** where a 4-wire system (3 phases + neutral) is needed.
- ✅ Always **ground the neutral** in your circuit diagrams for full marks.
- ✅ The phasor diagram shows $V_{RY}$ leading $V_{RN}$ by **30°** with magnitude $\sqrt{3} V_{ph}$.
- ✅ Comparison memory trick: **Y = "Yields" neutral** (neutral is available); **Δ = "Doesn't"** (no neutral).
- ✅ Standard KTU problem pattern: Given kVA, line voltages, turns ratio → find currents, voltages, and comment on harmonic/neutral issues.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delta-Y
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Delta-Y

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Delta-Y (Δ-Y) Connection?

The **Delta (Δ) connection** and **Star (Y) connection** are the two fundamental methods of interconnecting the three-phase windings (or loads) of a polyphase electrical system. Together, the **Delta-Y transformation** defines the mathematical and electrical equivalence between these two configurations, allowing engineers to convert an unbalanced delta-connected load into an equivalent star-connected load (or vice-versa) for simplified circuit analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCEOT402):**
> The Delta-Y (Δ-Y) transformation establishes the relationship between line and phase voltages and currents in three-phase systems. It is essential for analyzing three-phase transformers, induction motors, synchronous machines, and unbalanced load networks.

### 🔑 Formal Definition of Each Connection

**Star (Y) Connection:**
A connection scheme in which the similar ends (either starts or finishes) of the three-phase windings are joined together at a common point called the **neutral point (N)**, while the other three ends are brought out as line terminals.

**Delta (Δ) Connection:**
A connection scheme in which the three-phase windings are connected end-to-start in a closed loop, forming a triangular (Δ) shape. The line conductors are connected to the junctions of successive phase windings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three Rivers Merging" Analogy

Imagine **three rivers** flowing in a hilly landscape:
- In a **Star (Y) connection**, all three rivers meet at a single common junction (the **neutral point**) and then flow out through three separate channels to the city (the loads). The neutral point is like the central meeting hub where currents can sum to zero (in a balanced system) or carry the unbalanced current back to the source.
- In a **Delta connection**, the rivers are connected in a continuous loop, like a triangular canal system. Each section of the canal carries the *phase* current, while the main flow out to the city carries the *line* current. The phase current and line current are *not the same* — they differ by a factor of **√3**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Delta"?** The Greek letter **Δ (Delta)** was chosen because the circuit diagram physically resembles a triangle — just as the uppercase Greek letter Δ looks like a triangle. Similarly, **Y** was chosen because the star connection looks like the letter Y, with three arms radiating from a central neutral point.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Line Voltage (V_L):** The voltage measured between any two line conductors (phase-to-phase voltage). Unit: **Volts (V)**.
- **Phase Voltage (V_Ph):** The voltage measured across a single phase winding. Unit: **Volts (V)**.
- **Line Current (I_L):** The current flowing through a line conductor. Unit: **Amperes (A)**.
- **Phase Current (I_Ph):** The current flowing through a single phase winding. Unit: **Amperes (A)**.
- **√3 (≈ 1.732):** The transformation constant that links line and phase quantities in balanced three-phase systems.
- **3:** Used in power calculations for three-phase systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** "**Star Saves Voltage, Delta Delivers Current**" — In Star, the line voltage is √3 times the phase voltage (V_L > V_Ph). In Delta, the line current is √3 times the phase current (I_L > I_Ph).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Star (Y) Connection — Structural Analysis

In a balanced three-phase Star connection, consider phases **R, Y, B** with their respective phase voltages (with respect to neutral N):

$$V_{RN}, \quad V_{YN}, \quad V_{BN}$$

These are separated by **120°** electrical in time (or space). The line voltages are obtained by phasor subtraction:

$$V_{RY} = V_{RN} - V_{YN}, \quad V_{YB} = V_{YN} - V_{BN}, \quad V_{BR} = V_{BN} - V_{RN}$$

### Key Relationships in Star Connection:
- The **line current equals the phase current**: $I_L = I_{Ph}$
- The **line voltage is √3 times the phase voltage**: $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph}$
- Line voltages **lead** their respective phase voltages by **30°**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the line voltage lead the phase voltage by 30° in a Star connection?**
> The phasor difference of two equal-magnitude phasors (V_RN and V_YN) separated by 120° results in a resultant phasor that is √3 times larger and bisects the angle between them (rotated by 30° from the reference). This geometric construction is the **heart of the √3 relationship** in three-phase systems.

## 2.2 Delta (Δ) Connection — Structural Analysis

In a balanced three-phase Delta connection, the three phase windings are connected in series to form a closed mesh. The line conductors are tapped from each junction:

- The **line voltage equals the phase voltage**: $V_L = V_{Ph}$
- The **line current is √3 times the phase current**: $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{Ph}$
- Line currents **lag** their respective phase currents by **30°**

### Phasor Derivation of Delta Current Relationship:

Applying KCL at junction R of the delta:
$$I_R = I_{RY} - I_{BR}$$

Where $I_{RY}$ and $I_{BR}$ are the phase currents through the respective delta branches, separated by 120°.

## 2.3 Three-Phase Power — Universal Equations

For any balanced three-phase system (whether Star or Delta connected):

**Total Active Power (Real Power):**

$$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

**Total Reactive Power:**

$$Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$$

**Total Apparent Power:**

$$S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$$

**Power Factor:**

$$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These four power equations are **universal** — they apply equally to Star and Delta connections. This is because when you substitute $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{Ph}$ and $I_L = I_{Ph}$ (Star), you get $P = 3 V_{Ph} I_{Ph} \cos\phi$. Similarly, for Delta ($V_L = V_{Ph}$, $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{Ph}$), you again get $P = 3 V_{Ph} I_{Ph} \cos\phi$. The √3 is the "magic constant" that reconciles both.

## 2.4 Delta-Y Transformation (Y-Δ Transformation for Loads)

When a three-phase load is connected in Delta, it can be mathematically transformed into an equivalent Star-connected load using the following **exact relationships**:

**Resistive Loads:**
$$R_Y = \frac{R_\Delta}{3}$$

**Inductive Loads:**
$$X_{L,Y} = \frac{X_{L,\Delta}}{3}$$

**Capacitive Loads:**
$$X_{C,Y} = \frac{X_{C,\Delta}}{3}$$

**Impedance (Complex Form):**
$$Z_Y = \frac{Z_\Delta}{3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Point for KTU Exam:** The reverse transformation (Y to Δ) is:
> $$Z_\Delta = 3 \cdot Z_Y$$
> This is **NOT** the same as resistor networks in DC circuits (where the formula is more complex). The 1:3 ratio is a direct consequence of the 120° symmetry in three-phase systems.

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Star (Y) Connection** | **Delta (Δ) Connection** |
|---|---|---|
| Line Voltage $V_L$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph}$ | $V_{Ph}$ |
| Phase Voltage $V_{Ph}$ | $\dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $V_L$ |
| Line Current $I_L$ | $I_{Ph}$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot I_{Ph}$ |
| Phase Current $I_{Ph}$ | $I_L$ | $\dfrac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ |
| Active Power $P$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ |
| Reactive Power $Q$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$ |
| Apparent Power $S$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ |
| Δ → Y Transformation | $Z_Y = \dfrac{Z_\Delta}{3}$ | — |
| Y → Δ Transformation | — | $Z_\Delta = 3 \cdot Z_Y$ |
| Phase Angle of V & I | Line voltage leads phase voltage by 30° | Line current lags phase current by 30° |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Three-Phase Transformers:** Most distribution transformers use Dy (Delta primary, Star secondary) or Dyn11 connections to step down voltage and provide a neutral for single-phase loads.
- **Induction Motors:** Stator windings are typically connected in Delta for high starting torque applications.
- **Power Transmission:** Star connection is preferred for long-distance transmission to reduce line current and hence I²R losses.
- **Industrial Loads:** Heavy industrial equipment often uses Delta for the motor windings while the supply is Star.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Star Connection Voltage Relationship (V_L = √3 V_Ph)

### Step 1 — Define the Phase Voltage Phasors
Let the three phase voltages in a balanced Star system be (taking $V_{Ph}$ as reference phasor for the R phase):

$$V_{RN} = V_{Ph} \angle 0°$$
$$V_{YN} = V_{Ph} \angle -120°$$
$$V_{BN} = V_{Ph} \angle +120°$$

### Step 2 — Define the Line Voltages
By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law, the line voltage between R and Y phases is the potential difference:

$$V_{RY} = V_{RN} - V_{YN}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Phasor Expressions
$$V_{RY} = V_{Ph} \angle 0° - V_{Ph} \angle -120°$$

Convert to rectangular form:
$$V_{RN} = V_{Ph} (1 + j0) = V_{Ph}$$
$$V_{YN} = V_{Ph} \left( \cos(-120°) + j\sin(-120°) \right) = V_{Ph} \left( -\frac{1}{2} - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)$$

### Step 4 — Perform the Subtraction
$$V_{RY} = V_{Ph} - V_{Ph} \left( -\frac{1}{2} - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)$$
$$V_{RY} = V_{Ph} \left( 1 + \frac{1}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)$$
$$V_{RY} = V_{Ph} \left( \frac{3}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Magnitude
$$|V_{RY}| = V_{Ph} \sqrt{ \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2 }$$
$$|V_{RY}| = V_{Ph} \sqrt{ \frac{9}{4} + \frac{3}{4} } = V_{Ph} \sqrt{ \frac{12}{4} } = V_{Ph} \sqrt{3}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Angle
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left( \frac{\sqrt{3}/2}{3/2} \right) = \tan^{-1}\left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \right) = 30°$$

### ✅ Final Result for Star Connection:
$$V_{RY} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph} \angle 30°$$

> **Conclusion:** The line voltage magnitude is **√3 times** the phase voltage, and the line voltage **leads** the corresponding phase voltage by **30°**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Delta Connection Current Relationship (I_L = √3 I_Ph)

### Step 1 — Define the Phase Currents in Delta
For a balanced Delta-connected load, the phase currents (flowing through each phase winding) are:

$$I_{RY} = I_{Ph} \angle 0°$$
$$I_{YB} = I_{Ph} \angle -120°$$
$$I_{BR} = I_{Ph} \angle +120°$$

### Step 2 — Apply KCL at Junction R
The line current $I_R$ flowing into junction R from the source is equal to the phase current $I_{RY}$ leaving R minus the phase current $I_{BR}$ entering R:

$$I_R = I_{RY} - I_{BR}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Phasor Expressions
$$I_R = I_{Ph} \angle 0° - I_{Ph} \angle +120°$$

In rectangular form:
$$I_{RY} = I_{Ph} (1 + j0) = I_{Ph}$$
$$I_{BR} = I_{Ph} \left( \cos(120°) + j\sin(120°) \right) = I_{Ph} \left( -\frac{1}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)$$

### Step 4 — Perform the Subtraction
$$I_R = I_{Ph} - I_{Ph} \left( -\frac{1}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)$$
$$I_R = I_{Ph} \left( 1 + \frac{1}{2} - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)$$
$$I_R = I_{Ph} \left( \frac{3}{2} - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Magnitude
$$|I_R| = I_{Ph} \sqrt{ \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2 } = I_{Ph} \sqrt{3}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Angle
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left( \frac{-\sqrt{3}/2}{3/2} \right) = \tan^{-1}\left( -\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \right) = -30°$$

### ✅ Final Result for Delta Connection:
$$I_R = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{Ph} \angle -30°$$

> **Conclusion:** The line current magnitude is **√3 times** the phase current, and the line current **lags** the corresponding phase current by **30°**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Delta-to-Star Impedance Transformation

Consider a balanced Delta load with impedance $Z_\Delta$ in each branch. We need to find the equivalent Star impedance $Z_Y$.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Voltage and Current Relationships

Let the line-to-line voltage across the delta branch between R and Y be:
$$V_{RY} = V_\Delta$$

The phase current in the delta branch is:
$$I_\Delta = \frac{V_\Delta}{Z_\Delta}$$

### Step 2 — Relate Phase Current to Line Current in Delta
$$I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_\Delta = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_\Delta}{Z_\Delta}$$

### Step 3 — Express Line Voltage in Terms of Star Phase Voltage
In the equivalent Star:
$$V_\Delta = V_{RY} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Y,Ph}$$

### Step 4 — Relate Line Current to Star Phase Current
In Star: $I_L = I_{Y,Ph}$ (line current equals phase current)

### Step 5 — Formulate the Star Phase Impedance
$$Z_Y = \frac{V_{Y,Ph}}{I_{Y,Ph}} = \frac{V_{Y,Ph}}{I_L}$$

Substituting $V_\Delta = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Y,Ph}$, we get $V_{Y,Ph} = \dfrac{V_\Delta}{\sqrt{3}}$:

$$Z_Y = \frac{V_\Delta / \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_\Delta / Z_\Delta} = \frac{V_\Delta}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{Z_\Delta}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_\Delta} = \frac{Z_\Delta}{3}$$

### ✅ Final Result:
$$\boxed{Z_Y = \frac{Z_\Delta}{3}}$$

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A balanced three-phase load of impedance $Z_\Delta = (12 + j9)\,\Omega$ per phase is connected in Delta to a 400 V, 50 Hz, three-phase supply. Calculate:
1. Phase current $I_{Ph}$
2. Line current $I_L$
3. Total active power $P$
4. Equivalent Star-connected impedance $Z_Y$

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Calculate Phase Voltage and Impedance Magnitude
$$V_{Ph} = V_L = 400 \text{ V} \quad (\text{Delta connection})$$
$$|Z_\Delta| = \sqrt{12^2 + 9^2} = \sqrt{144 + 81} = \sqrt{225} = 15\,\Omega$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Phase Current
$$I_{Ph} = \frac{V_{Ph}}{|Z_\Delta|} = \frac{400}{15} = 26.67 \text{ A}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Power Factor and Phase Angle
$$\cos\phi = \frac{R}{|Z_\Delta|} = \frac{12}{15} = 0.8 \text{ (lagging)}$$
$$\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87°$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Line Current
$$I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{Ph} = 1.732 \times 26.67 = 46.19 \text{ A}$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Total Active Power
$$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi = 1.732 \times 400 \times 46.19 \times 0.8$$
$$P = 25,600 \text{ W} = 25.6 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 6 — Calculate Equivalent Star Impedance
$$Z_Y = \frac{Z_\Delta}{3} = \frac{12 + j9}{3} = (4 + j3)\,\Omega$$

### Step 7 — Verification Using Star Connection
In Star: $V_{Ph,Y} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94$ V
$$I_{L,Y} = \frac{230.94}{|Z_Y|} = \frac{230.94}{5} = 46.19 \text{ A} \checkmark$$
$$P = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 46.19 \times 0.8 = 25.6 \text{ kW} \checkmark$$

### ✅ Summary of Results:
| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $I_{Ph}$ (Delta) | 26.67 A |
| $I_L$ | 46.19 A |
| $P$ (Total) | 25.6 kW |
| $Z_Y$ (Equivalent) | $(4 + j3)\,\Omega$ |
| $\cos\phi$ | 0.8 lagging |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
