# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] 
**"Define the no-load condition of a synchronous generator. Why does the terminal voltage equal the induced EMF under this condition?"**

**Model Answer:**

A synchronous generator is said to be on **no-load** when its armature terminals are **open-circuited** and no current is delivered to any external load. Under this condition, the armature current $I_a = 0$.

Since there is no armature current, the following consequences arise:

1. The **armature reaction** is **zero** (no flux produced by the armature to distort the main field).
2. The **voltage drops** across armature resistance ($I_a R_a = 0$) and synchronous reactance ($I_a X_s = 0$) are both zero.
3. Applying KVL to the armature circuit: $V_t = E_{ph} - I_a R_a - j I_a X_s = E_{ph} - 0 - 0 = E_{ph}$.

Hence, the terminal voltage equals the induced EMF per phase on no-load.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] 
**"What is the winding factor of a synchronous generator? Why is it always less than unity for distributed windings?"**

**Model Answer:**

The **winding factor** $K_w$ is the ratio of the actual EMF induced in a distributed, short-pitched winding to the EMF that would be induced in a full-pitched, concentrated winding having the same number of turns.

$$K_w = K_p \times K_d = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{ideal}}$$

For distributed windings, the **distribution factor $K_d < 1$** because coils placed in different slots have EMFs that are phase-displaced from each other; their vector sum is less than their arithmetic sum. Similarly, for short-pitched coils, the **pitch factor $K_p < 1$** because the two coil sides are not $180^\circ$ electrical apart, reducing the net EMF per coil.

Since both $K_p < 1$ and $K_d < 1$, the winding factor $K_w < 1$ for practical synchronous generator windings.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** 
> - **Do NOT forget to convert** turns per coil into turns per phase — this is where 30% of students lose marks in EMF problems.
> - **Do NOT confuse** line EMF and phase EMF — write the connection clearly (star/delta).
> - **Always state** $V_t = E_{ph}$ explicitly at the start of any no-load problem.
> - **Show units** at every intermediate step — examiners deduct 1 mark for unit-less final answers.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a synchronous generator, $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, N_{ph} \, K_p \, K_d$, starting from Faraday's Law. State clearly the meaning of pitch factor and distribution factor.

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, star-connected synchronous generator has a single-layer, full-pitch winding with 36 slots and 10 conductors per slot. The flux per pole is 0.1 Wb. Calculate:
   (i) The induced EMF per phase.
   (ii) The line EMF.

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** State Faraday's Law. EMF is induced in a conductor when it cuts magnetic flux. Average EMF per conductor:

$$E_{avg} = \dfrac{d\lambda}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Total flux cut by one conductor in one revolution = $P \phi$. Time for one revolution = $60/N_s$ seconds. Time for one electrical cycle = $120/(P N_s) = 1/f$ seconds.

**Step 3:** Average EMF per conductor = $\dfrac{P \phi f}{1} = 2 f \phi$ (using $P = 2$ for one electrical cycle's worth of poles).

**Step 4:** RMS value using form factor 1.11:

$$E_{cond,rms} = 1.11 \times 2 f \phi = 2.22 f \phi$$

**Step 5:** For $N_{ph}$ turns per phase:

$$E_{ideal} = 2.22 f \phi N_{ph}$$

**Step 6:** Apply **pitch factor** $K_p = \cos(\alpha/2)$ — the ratio of EMF induced in a short-pitched coil to that in a full-pitched coil. For full-pitch coils, $\alpha = 0$ and $K_p = 1$; for short-pitched coils, $\alpha > 0$ and $K_p < 1$.

**Step 7:** Apply **distribution factor** $K_d = \dfrac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \sin(\beta/2)}$ — the ratio of EMF induced in a distributed winding to that in a concentrated winding. Since EMFs in different slots are phase-displaced, $K_d < 1$.

**Step 8:** Final equation:

$$\boxed{E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, N_{ph} \, K_p \, K_d}$$

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz, $S = 36$, $Z_{slot} = 10$ conductors, $\phi = 0.1$ Wb, full-pitch, single-layer, star-connected.

**Step 1 — Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 — Slot angle:**

$$\beta = \dfrac{180^\circ \times 4}{36} = 20^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Slots per pole per phase:**

$$m = \dfrac{36}{4 \times 3} = 3$$

**Step 4 — Distribution factor:**

$$K_d = \dfrac{\sin(3 \times 20^\circ/2)}{3 \sin(20^\circ/2)} = \dfrac{\sin 30^\circ}{3 \sin 10^\circ} = \dfrac{0.500}{3 \times 0.1736} = 0.9598$$

**Step 5 — Pitch factor (full-pitch, $\alpha = 0$):**

$$K_p = \cos(0/2) = 1.0$$

**Step 6 — Turns per phase:**

Total conductors = $S \times Z_{slot} = 36 \times 10 = 360$. Conductors per phase = $360/3 = 120$. Turns per phase = $120/2 = 60$.

$$N_{ph} = 60$$

**Step 7 — EMF per phase:**

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.1 \times 60 \times 1.0 \times 0.9598$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.1 \times 60 \times 0.9598$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 = 222$$

$$222 \times 0.1 = 22.2$$

$$22.2 \times 60 = 1332$$

$$E_{ph} = 1332 \times 0.9598 \approx 1278.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 8 — Line EMF (star-connected):**

$$E_L = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph} = 1.732 \times 1278.5 \approx 2214.4 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{ph} \approx 1278.5 \text{ V}, \quad E_L \approx 2214.4 \text{ V}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the open-circuit characteristic (OCC) of a synchronous generator with a neat sketch. Why does the curve saturate at higher field currents?

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz synchronous generator has 54 slots. The winding is short-pitched by 2 slots. Each coil has 5 turns. The flux per pole is 0.08 Wb. Calculate:
   (i) The pitch factor and distribution factor.
   (ii) The induced EMF per phase and the line EMF (assume star connection).

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition:**
The **Open-Circuit Characteristic (OCC)** is the plot of the **open-circuit armature EMF per phase** $E_{ph}$ versus the **field current $I_f$**, taken at **rated synchronous speed** with the **armature terminals open**.

**Procedure:**
1. Drive the rotor at rated synchronous speed $N_s$ using a prime mover.
2. Keep all three armature terminals open.
3. Vary the DC field current $I_f$ from zero to its maximum value in steps.
4. At each step, record $E_{ph}$ from a voltmeter connected across the armature.
5. Plot $E_{ph}$ (y-axis) versus $I_f$ (x-axis).

**Shape of the curve:**
- The initial portion is a **straight line** (air-gap line). In this region, the iron core is unsaturated, and the flux $\phi$ is directly proportional to $I_f$.
- Beyond the "knee" of the curve, the slope decreases. This is because the **iron core begins to saturate** — additional field current produces progressively less flux.
- The curve asymptotically approaches a saturation limit.

**Why saturation occurs:**
At higher field currents, the iron teeth and yoke approach the **maximum flux density** of the steel (typically 1.5–1.8 T for CRGO silicon steel). Once the magnetic material saturates, increments in MMF produce only small increments in flux, and hence the EMF rises slowly.

**Sketch (Textual Representation):**

```
  E_ph
   │                              ___________
   │                        _____/
   │                    ___/
   │                 __/  ← Knee (saturation starts)
   │              __/
   │          ___/
   │       __/   ← Air-gap line (linear region)
   │     _/
   │   _/
   │ _/
   │/____________________________________ I_f
   O
```

**Engineering use:** The OCC is essential for the **synchronous impedance method**, **MMF method**, and **Potier triangle method** of voltage regulation calculation.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $P = 6$, $f = 50$ Hz, $S = 54$, short-pitch $y_c = 2$ slots, $T_c = 5$ turns/coil, $\phi = 0.08$ Wb, star-connected.

**Step 1 — Slot angle:**

$$\beta = \dfrac{180^\circ \times 6}{54} = 20^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Chording angle:**

$$\alpha = y_c \times \beta = 2 \times 20^\circ = 40^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Pitch factor:**

$$K_p = \cos(\alpha/2) = \cos(20^\circ) = 0.9397$$

**Step 4 — Slots per pole per phase:**

$$m = \dfrac{54}{6 \times 3} = 3$$

**Step 5 — Distribution factor:**

$$K_d = \dfrac{\sin(3 \times 20^\circ/2)}{3 \sin(20^\circ/2)} = \dfrac{\sin 30^\circ}{3 \sin 10^\circ} = \dfrac{0.500}{0.5209} = 0.9598$$

**Step 6 — Winding factor:**

$$K_w = 0.9397 \times 0.9598 = 0.9020$$

**Step 7 — Turns per phase:**

Coils per phase = $S/3 = 18$. Turns per phase:

$$N_{ph} = T_c \times 18 = 5 \times 18 = 90$$

**Step 8 — EMF per phase:**

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.08 \times 90 \times 0.9020$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 = 222$$

$$222 \times 0.08 = 17.76$$

$$17.76 \times 90 = 1598.4$$

$$E_{ph} = 1598.4 \times 0.9020 \approx 1441.8 \text{ V}$$

**Step 9 — Line EMF (star-connected):**

$$E_L = \sqrt{3} \times 1441.8 \approx 2497.2 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{K_p = 0.9397, \quad K_d = 0.9598, \quad E_{ph} \approx 1441.8 \text{ V}, \quad E_L \approx 2497.2 \text{ V}}$$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Synchronous Generator on No-Load**

- **No-load condition** means open-circuited armature terminals: $I_a = 0$, no armature reaction, no internal voltage drop.
- **Terminal voltage equals induced EMF on no-load:** $V_t = E_{ph}$.
- **Fundamental EMF equation:** $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, N_{ph} \, K_p \, K_d$. Memorize this in the order: form factor × frequency × flux × turns × pitch factor × distribution factor.
- **Frequency relation:** $f = P N_s / 120$. For 50 Hz, 4-pole machines run at 1500 RPM; 2-pole at 3000 RPM; 6-pole at 1000 RPM.
- **Pitch factor** $K_p = \cos(\alpha/2)$ is always $\leq 1$; equals 1 for full-pitch coils.
- **Distribution factor** $K_d = \sin(m\beta/2) / [m \sin(\beta/2)]$ is always $\leq 1$; equals 1 for a concentrated winding ($m = 1$).
- **Winding factor** $K_w = K_p \times K_d$ is always $\leq 1$.
- **OCC** is plotted with armature open and field current varied; it is **linear initially** and **saturates** at higher $I_f$.
- **OCC is the basis** of all voltage regulation methods (EMF, MMF, ZPF, Potier).
- **Slot angle** in electrical degrees: $\beta = 180^\circ P / S$.
- **Slots per pole per phase:** $m = S / (3P)$ for 3-phase machines.
- **Line EMF:** $\sqrt{3} \times$ phase EMF for star connection; equal to phase EMF for delta connection.
- **Typical KTU 14-mark question pattern:** derive the EMF equation (7 marks) + numerical computation of $E_{ph}$ given machine parameters (7 marks).
- **Common pitfall:** forgetting to divide conductors by 2 to get turns, or forgetting to multiply coils-per-phase by turns-per-coil to get $N_{ph}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Circuit Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Open Circuit Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC)** of a synchronous generator (alternator) is the curve showing the relationship between the **open-circuit terminal voltage (E₀)** at the armature terminals and the **DC field current (I_f)** when the machine is driven at its rated **synchronous speed (N_s)** with the armature winding kept **open-circuited** (no load connected).

Since the armature current $I_a = 0$, there is no armature reaction and no armature leakage reactance drop. Therefore, the terminal voltage $V_t$ equals the internally induced EMF $E_0$ (also written as $E_{ph}$ per phase or $E_L$ line-to-line).

The OCC is also called:
- **No-Load Saturation Curve**
- **Magnetization Characteristic (MCC)** — *Magnetizing Curve Characteristic*
- **Open-Circuit Saturation Curve**

The plot is drawn between:
- **X-axis** → Field current $I_f$ in **Amperes**
- **Y-axis** → Open-circuit line-to-line voltage $V_{oc}$ (or per-phase EMF $E_0$) in **Volts (V)** or **per-unit (p.u.)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a water pump that pushes water only when you turn a small "primer knob" harder. At first, when you barely turn the knob, water trickles out weakly (linear zone). As you turn the knob more vigorously, water flow shoots up rapidly — but eventually, the pipe becomes "saturated" and turning the knob harder gives only a little extra flow (knee and saturation zone).

In the same way:
- The **field current $I_f$** is like your hand turning the knob — it produces magnetic flux $\phi$ inside the iron core.
- The **induced voltage $E_0$** at the armature is like the water flow coming out.
- In the **linear region** (small $I_f$), flux grows in direct proportion to current because the iron's permeability is constant (dominated by air gap).
- Once the iron saturates, increasing $I_f$ gives **diminishing returns** on voltage — the curve bends and flattens.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> - **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ rpm, where $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ in India and $P$ = number of poles.
> - **Rated Voltage Testing Limit:** Field current is increased until $E_0 \approx 1.25\,V_{rated}$ (125 % of rated voltage) to capture the knee point and full saturation.
> - **Slip Rings & Brushes:** Used to feed DC excitation to the rotor (field winding).
> - **Prime Mover:** A DC motor or turbine that mechanically drives the alternator at synchronous speed during the test.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps to Conduct the OCC Test

1. The synchronous machine is mechanically coupled to a **prime mover** (e.g., DC shunt motor).
2. The prime mover is run at the **rated synchronous speed** $N_s$ and the speed is kept **constant** throughout the test.
3. The armature terminals of the stator are kept **open** (no external load).
4. A **DC voltmeter** is connected across the armature terminals (line-to-line) and a **DC ammeter** is connected in series with the rotor field winding.
5. The field current is gradually increased from **zero** in steps, using a **rheostat** in the field circuit.
6. At each step, readings of $I_f$ and $V_{oc}$ (open-circuit voltage) are tabulated.
7. Field current is increased until the open-circuit voltage reaches about **125 % of rated voltage** to clearly capture the saturation region.
8. The readings are plotted — $I_f$ along X-axis and $V_{oc}$ along Y-axis — to obtain the **OCC**.

## 2.2 Regions of the OCC Curve

The OCC has three distinct regions:

- **Region I — Linear / Initial Portion (Air-Gap Line):** For small values of $I_f$, the iron is unsaturated. The flux $\phi$ is proportional to mmf ($N_f I_f$). Hence, $E_0 \propto \phi \propto I_f$. This region follows the **air-gap line** (a straight line through origin).
- **Region II — Knee Point:** A transition where the iron begins to saturate. The curve starts bending.
- **Region III — Saturation Region:** Further increase in $I_f$ produces very little extra $\phi$ and hence little extra $E_0$. The curve flattens.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air-Gap Line (AGL):** The straight-line extension of the linear portion of the OCC. It represents the characteristic the machine would have if there were no iron saturation — only the air-gap reluctance. It is used to compute the **Potier reactance** $X_p$ and **Potier triangle**.

## 2.3 Why OCC is Non-Linear — The Magnetic Saturation Effect

The magnetic circuit of the synchronous machine consists of:
- **Iron parts** (stator core, rotor pole core) — non-linear $B$–$H$ relationship
- **Air gap** — linear, constant reluctance

Total mmf required: $\text{mmf}_{total} = \text{mmf}_{iron} + \text{mmf}_{air-gap}$

$$E_0 = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, K_w \, K_p$$

- $f$ = frequency (Hz)
- $N$ = turns per phase
- $\phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $K_w$ = winding factor
- $K_p$ = pitch factor

Since $\phi$ saturates with $I_f$ (due to iron saturation), $E_0$ does **not** remain linear with $I_f$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the OCC

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Determination of Synchronous Impedance $Z_s$** — combined with SC test.
> 2. **Voltage Regulation Calculation** — using EMF method (synchronous impedance method), MMF method, Potier triangle method, ASA method, etc.
> 3. **Determination of Short-Circuit Ratio (SCR):**
> $$\text{SCR} = \frac{I_f \text{ corresponding to rated voltage on OCC}}{I_f \text{ corresponding to rated current on SCC}}$$
> 4. **Setting the operating point** of the machine for a given terminal voltage.
> 5. **Predicting excitation requirement** for various load conditions.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm |
| Induced EMF (RMS, per phase) | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi \, K_w$ | Volts |
| Line voltage (star) | $E_L = \sqrt{3} \, E_{ph}$ | Volts |
| Voltage regulation | $\%\text{VR} = \dfrac{\vert E_0 - V \vert}{V} \times 100$ | % |
| Air-gap EMF (from AGL) | $E_{ag} \propto I_f$ (linear) | Volts |
| Short-circuit ratio | $\text{SCR} = \dfrac{I_{f(\text{rated V, OCC})}}{I_{f(\text{rated I, SCC})}}$ | dimensionless |
| Synchronous impedance | $Z_s = \dfrac{E_{oc}}{I_{sc}}\,\bigg\vert_{\text{same } I_f}$ | $\Omega$ |
| Synchronous reactance | $X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2}$ | $\Omega$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipes inside tables replaced by $\vert$ or `\mid`** to avoid breaking markdown rendering. Example: $\vert E_0 - V \vert$ instead of $\vert E_0 - V \vert$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation (Foundation of OCC)

Consider one conductor of a single-phase armature winding rotating in a magnetic field at synchronous speed. The average EMF induced in one conductor is given by Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.

### Step 1 — Flux Cut Per Revolution
The flux cut by one conductor in one revolution = $P \cdot \phi$ webers, where $P$ = number of poles and $\phi$ = flux per pole.

### Step 2 — Time for One Revolution
Time taken for one revolution at $N_s$ rpm = $\dfrac{60}{N_s}$ seconds.

### Step 3 — Average EMF Per Conductor (Faraday's Law)
By Faraday's law, average EMF per conductor = rate of change of flux linkage.

$$E_{avg,cond} = \frac{P \, \phi}{\dfrac{60}{N_s}} = \frac{P \, N_s \, \phi}{60}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Synchronous Speed
Substituting $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{avg,cond} &= \frac{P \cdot \dfrac{120 f}{P} \cdot \phi}{60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{120 f \, \phi}{60} \\[6pt]
&= 2 f \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Account for Winding Distribution and Pitch
The actual RMS EMF per phase is obtained by applying a **form factor** of 1.11 (ratio of RMS to average for a sine wave), the **distribution factor $K_d$**, and the **pitch (chording) factor $K_p$**.

> **Winding factor:** $K_w = K_d \cdot K_p$

### Step 6 — Final EMF Equation (Per Phase, RMS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi \, K_w \\[6pt]
\text{where: } & \\
f &= \text{frequency in Hz} \\
N_{ph} &= \text{turns per phase} \\
\phi &= \text{flux per pole in Wb} \\
K_w &= K_d \cdot K_p \quad \text{(winding factor)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **fundamental EMF equation** that governs the OCC. Since $E_0 \propto \phi$ and $\phi$ is a **non-linear function of $I_f$**, the OCC is non-linear.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of OCC Regions

| **Region** | **Field Current Range** | **Magnetic State** | **Slope $dE/dI_f$** | **Governing Equation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Linear | $0$ to $\approx 0.5\,I_{f,\text{rated}}$ | Iron unsaturated | Constant (steep) | $E_0 \approx k \cdot I_f$ |
| Knee Point | $\approx 0.5\,I_{f,\text{rated}}$ | Beginning of saturation | Decreasing | Transition zone |
| Saturation | $> I_{f,\text{rated}}$ | Iron saturated | Very small | $E_0$ flattens |

## 3.3 Voltage Regulation — Using OCC

For a lagging power-factor load at rated terminal voltage $V$ and rated armature current $I_a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_0 &= \sqrt{(V \cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V \sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2} \\[6pt]
\% \text{VR} &= \frac{\vert E_0 - V \vert}{V} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $R_a$ = armature resistance per phase (measured by voltmeter–ammeter or bridge method)
- $X_s$ = synchronous reactance per phase (from OCC + SCC)
- $Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$ = synchronous impedance

> **KTU Examiner Note:** The EMF method **over-estimates** voltage regulation because it ignores the demagnetizing effect of armature reaction in a saturating machine. Hence OCC-based regulation values are conservative.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OCC Test — Experimental Setup (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the functional architecture of the OCC test setup, with safe alphanumeric node IDs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PM[Prime Mover DC Motor] -- Mechanical Shaft Coupling --> ALT[Synchronous Alternator]
    ALT -- Rotor Field Winding --> SR[Slip Rings and Brushes]
    SR -- DC Supply --> FVR[Field Variable Rheostat]
    FVR -- Controlled DC --> FW[Field Winding on Rotor]
    ALT -- Armature Terminals --> OC[Open Circuited Stator Terminals]
    OC -- Voltmeter V --> VM[Digital Voltmeter Line to Line]
    OC -- Ammeter --> AM[Ammeter in Field Circuit]
    ALT -- Speed Sensor --> T[Tachometer Reads Ns]
    FVR -- Adjusts --> CTRL[Operator Adjusts If in Steps]
    VM -- Reads E0 --> PLOT[Plot OCC: E0 vs If]
    AM -- Reads If --> PLOT
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **prime mover** rotates the alternator at constant synchronous speed $N_s$.
- A **variable DC source** (via rheostat) feeds the **rotor field winding** through **slip rings**.
- The **armature terminals are open** — no current flows.
- A **voltmeter** reads the line-to-line induced EMF $E_0$.
- An **ammeter** reads the field current $I_f$.
- The plot $E_0$ vs $I_f$ is the OCC.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the OCC Plot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OCC[OCC Plot Coordinates]
        XAXIS["X Axis: Field Current If in Amps"]
        YAXIS["Y Axis: Open Circuit Voltage E0 in Volts"]
    end
    ORIX["Origin O 0 0"] --> LIN["Linear Region Air Gap Line AGL"]
    LIN --> KNEE["Knee Point Saturation Onset"]
    KNEE --> SAT["Saturation Region Flattening Curve"]
    AGLD["Air Gap Line Straight Line Extension"] -.Through Origin.-> LIN
    RAT["Rated Voltage Point on OCC"] --> VR["Used for Voltage Regulation Calculation"]
    SAT --> SCR["Used to Find SCR with SCC"]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The OCC starts linear (air-gap line), bends at the **knee point**, and flattens in saturation.
- The **air-gap line** is the linear extrapolation through the initial portion.
- The **rated voltage point** on OCC is used for voltage regulation.
- The **ratio** of field currents (OCC at rated voltage : SCC at rated current) gives the **SCR**.

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit View at Open-Circuit Condition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EF["Induced EMF E0"] --> RA["Armature Resistance Ra"]
    RA --> OPEN["Open Terminals Ia equals 0"]
    OPEN --> XS["No Voltage Drop across Xs since Ia 0"]
    XS --> VT["Terminal Voltage Vt equals E0"]
```

> **Why $V_t = E_0$ at no-load?** Because $I_a = 0$, the drops $I_a R_a$ and $I_a X_s$ are both zero. So the entire induced EMF appears across the open terminals.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Open Circuit Characteristics of a synchronous generator. Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC) of a synchronous generator is the curve plotted between the open-circuit terminal voltage $E_0$ (on the Y-axis) and the field current $I_f$ (on the X-axis), with the machine running at rated synchronous speed and the armature winding kept open-circuited.

**Significance:**
- Determines the **magnetization behavior** of the machine (linear and saturation regions).
- Used in the determination of **synchronous impedance $Z_s$** in combination with the SCC.
- Used for calculating **voltage regulation** by EMF, MMF, Potier triangle, and ASA methods.
- Helps determine the **Short-Circuit Ratio (SCR)**.
- Aids in choosing the **operating point of excitation** for a desired terminal voltage under load.

> **Examiner's Note:** Writing only the definition without significance will fetch only **2 out of 3 marks**. Always mention at least 2–3 applications.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**What is the air-gap line? How is it obtained from the OCC?**

**Model Answer:**
The **air-gap line (AGL)** is a straight line drawn through the origin as an extension of the initial linear portion of the OCC. It represents the relationship between $E_0$ and $I_f$ that would exist if there were no iron saturation — i.e., the magnetic circuit consisted only of the air gap.

**How it is obtained:**
1. Plot the OCC experimentally.
2. Identify the **initial straight-line portion** (small $I_f$, before saturation).
3. Extend this straight line backward / forward through the origin using a ruler.
4. This straight line is the air-gap line.

**Utility:** It is used in the **Potier triangle method** to determine Potier reactance $X_p$ and the armature reaction MMF $F_{ar}$, leading to accurate voltage regulation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the procedure to obtain the **Open Circuit Characteristics (OCC)** of a synchronous generator. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the EMF equation of a synchronous machine. Show how the EMF depends on field current. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Procedure to Obtain OCC

**Apparatus Required:**
- Synchronous alternator (machine under test)
- DC shunt motor (prime mover)
- Tachometer
- DC voltmeter (0–500 V range), DC ammeter (field circuit)
- Variable DC source with field rheostat

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to the diagram in SECTION 4.1)

**Procedure:**
1. The synchronous machine is coupled to a **prime mover** (DC shunt motor).
2. Start the prime mover and adjust its speed so that the alternator runs at the **rated synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$.
3. Keep the **stator (armature) terminals open** — no load is connected.
4. Ensure the field winding is initially **unexcited** ($I_f = 0$).
5. Gradually increase the field current $I_f$ in small steps using a **field rheostat**.
6. At each step, note the corresponding **open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$** (line-to-line) on the voltmeter.
7. Continue increasing $I_f$ until $V_{oc}$ reaches about **125 % of rated voltage**.
8. Slowly reduce $I_f$ back to zero, noting readings to check hysteresis effect.
9. Plot a graph of $V_{oc}$ (Y-axis) versus $I_f$ (X-axis) — this curve is the **OCC**.

**Observations:**
- For small $I_f$, the OCC is a **straight line** (air-gap line region).
- After the knee point, the curve **flattens** due to iron saturation.
- The slope $\dfrac{dE_0}{dI_f}$ decreases progressively.

**Precautions:**
- Speed must be maintained constant at $N_s$.
- Voltmeter should have proper range (preferably 1.5× rated voltage).
- Field current should be increased slowly to avoid overshoot.

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation of EMF Equation

(Refer to the exhaustive derivation in SECTION 3.1. Key final form to be reproduced here:)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi \, K_w \\
\text{where} \quad K_w &= K_d \cdot K_p
\end{aligned}
$$

**Dependence of EMF on Field Current:**
- From $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi \, K_w$, since $f$, $N_{ph}$, $K_w$ are constants for a given machine, $E_{ph} \propto \phi$.
- Now $\phi = \dfrac{\text{mmf}}{\mathcal{R}_{total}}$ where $\mathcal{R}_{total} = \mathcal{R}_{iron} + \mathcal{R}_{air}$.
- For unsaturated iron, $\mathcal{R}_{iron}$ is small and constant, so $\phi \propto I_f$ → **linear region**.
- Once iron saturates, $\mathcal{R}_{iron}$ becomes very large (since $\mu_r \to$ small), and the slope $\dfrac{d\phi}{dI_f}$ decreases rapidly → **non-linear / saturation region**.
- Thus, the **OCC has a non-linear shape** with a clearly identifiable knee point.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the significance of the **air-gap line**. How is it used in finding the **synchronous impedance**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz, star-connected synchronous generator has 200 turns per phase and a winding factor of 0.96. The field current of 5 A produces a flux of 0.05 Wb per pole. Calculate the **induced EMF per phase and the line voltage**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Air-Gap Line & Synchronous Impedance

**Significance of Air-Gap Line:**
- Represents the linear (unsaturated) behavior of the magnetic circuit.
- Used in **Potier triangle method** to find $X_p$ and armature reaction MMF.
- Used to calculate the **unsaturated synchronous reactance**.

**Procedure to Find Synchronous Impedance using OCC and SCC:**

1. Conduct the **OCC test** — plot $E_0$ vs $I_f$.
2. Conduct the **SC test** — short the armature terminals through an ammeter, drive at $N_s$, vary $I_f$ and note $I_{sc}$. Plot $I_{sc}$ vs $I_f$ (the SCC is a **straight line** since the armature reaction is strongly demagnetizing and the machine stays unsaturated).
3. For any given field current $I_f$:
   - Read $E_{oc}$ from OCC.
   - Read $I_{sc}$ from SCC.
4. Compute synchronous impedance:

$$
Z_s = \frac{E_{oc}}{I_{sc}} \quad \text{(at the same } I_f \text{)}
$$

5. Synchronous reactance:
$$
X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2}
$$

Where $R_a$ is measured by the voltmeter–ammeter method (DC resistance per phase) and then multiplied by 1.6 to get the effective AC resistance.

**Typical Pattern:** $Z_s$ is **not constant** with $I_f$ due to saturation — for accurate regulation, $Z_s$ should be computed at the field current that corresponds to the desired operating point.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem Solution

**Given:**
- $P = 6$ poles, $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$, star-connected
- $N_{ph} = 200$ turns/phase
- $K_w = 0.96$, $\phi = 0.05\,\text{Wb}$, $I_f = 5\,\text{A}$

**Step 1 — EMF per Phase:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi \, K_w \\[6pt]
&= 4.44 \times 50 \times 200 \times 0.05 \times 0.96 \\[6pt]
&= 4.44 \times 50 \times 200 \times 0.048 \\[6pt]
&= 4.44 \times 50 \times 9.6 \\[6pt]
&= 4.44 \times 480 \\[6pt]
&= 2131.2 \,\text{V per phase}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Line Voltage (Star Connection):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_L &= \sqrt{3} \, E_{ph} \\[6pt]
&= 1.732 \times 2131.2 \\[6pt]
&\approx 3691.2 \,\text{V (line-to-line)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **$E_{ph} \approx 2131.2\,\text{V}$ per phase**
- **$E_L \approx 3691.2\,\text{V}$ (line-to-line)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the winding factor** $K_w$ — results in ~4 % higher EMF.
> 2. **Using line turns instead of phase turns** in star connection — common error.
> 3. **Confusing line and phase EMF** — always convert $E_{ph}$ to $E_L$ using $\sqrt{3}$ for star.
> 4. **Wrong units of $\phi$** — must be in Webers, not milliWebers.
> 5. **Not stating the connection type** — star vs delta changes the conversion factor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — OCC of Synchronous Generator**

- **Definition:** Plot of $E_0$ vs $I_f$ at synchronous speed with armature open.
- **Test Conditions:** $N = N_s$ (constant), armature terminals open, $I_a = 0$.
- **Curve Shape:** Initially linear (air-gap line region), bends at knee point, flattens in saturation.
- **Air-Gap Line (AGL):** Linear extrapolation through origin of the linear portion of OCC. Represents behaviour without iron saturation.
- **EMF Equation (foundation):** $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi \, K_w$.
- **Winding Factor:** $K_w = K_d \cdot K_p$.
- **Why non-linear?** Iron saturation → $\phi$ vs $I_f$ becomes non-linear → $E_0$ vs $I_f$ becomes non-linear.
- **At no-load:** $V_t = E_0$ since $I_a R_a = I_a X_s = 0$.
- **Significance:** Determines $Z_s$ (with SCC), voltage regulation, SCR, and excitation requirements.
- **Test limit:** Field current is increased until $E_0 \approx 1.25\,V_{rated}$.
- **Tools used:** Voltmeter (across armature), Ammeter (in field), Tachometer (to maintain $N_s$).
- **SCR Formula:** $\text{SCR} = \dfrac{I_{f, \text{rated V on OCC}}}{I_{f, \text{rated I on SCC}}}$.
- **EMF Method of Regulation:** Uses OCC + SCC; gives **over-estimation** due to neglect of saturation effects on armature reaction.
- **Star vs Delta:** For star, $E_L = \sqrt{3} \, E_{ph}$; for delta, $E_L = E_{ph}$.
- **Air-gap line use:** Potier triangle method → Potier reactance $X_p$ → more accurate voltage regulation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronous Generator on Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synchronous Generator on Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Synchronous Generator (Alternator)** operating **on load** refers to the steady-state condition where the machine's armature winding delivers current to an external load. Unlike the no-load condition, the armature now carries a balanced three-phase current, which produces its own **rotating magnetic field** (the *armature reaction flux*). This armature flux interacts with the main **field flux** produced by the rotor, distorting the resultant air-gap flux and changing the terminal voltage relative to the internally induced EMF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Behaviour of synchronous generator when supplying isolated loads — Armature reaction, Synchronous impedance, Phasor diagram for lagging, leading, and unity power factor loads, and the concept of voltage regulation.

The two key EMFs involved are:
- **E₀ (or E_ph)** — Induced EMF per phase at no-load (depends only on excitation and speed).
- **V (or V_ph)** — Terminal voltage per phase at load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Armature Reaction (AR):**
> The effect of the magnetic field produced by the armature current on the main field flux of a synchronous generator is called *armature reaction*. It is the **most critical** topic in Module 3 because it directly determines the terminal voltage and regulation of the machine.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Two-Person Rowing Analogy:**
> Imagine a large boat being rowed across a river.
> - The **rotor field** is the rower pulling the oars straight ahead (main driving force).
> - The **armature current** is the sideways current of the river pushing against the boat.
>
> At **no-load**, the boat moves straight. The moment load is connected, the "river current" (armature flux) pushes the boat sideways, altering its course. The rower must compensate (more excitation) to keep the boat on track. This sideways push is the **armature reaction**, and the extra effort needed is the **synchronous reactance drop**.

For a synchronous generator, the armature current creates a magnetic field that:
- **Cranks (rotates) the main field flux** by a certain angle depending on the power factor.
- **Either weakens (demagnetising)** or **strengthens (magnetising)** the main field depending on the load power factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU 2024):**
> - **Synchronous Speed (N_s)** = **120 f / P** (in rpm), where f = supply frequency (**50 Hz** in India), P = number of poles.
> - **Frequency (f)** = P·N_s / 120 — fixed by rotor speed and pole count.
> - All per-phase quantities are denoted in lowercase: **V_ph, I_a, E_ph, R_a, X_s**.

## 1.3 Load Power Factor — The Controlling Variable

The **power factor of the load** completely determines the *nature* of the armature reaction. This is the single most important idea in this module.

| Load Power Factor | Nature of Armature Reaction | Effect on Main Field |
|---|---|---|
| **Zero Lagging (pure inductive)** | Purely **Demagnetising** | Weakens main field |
| **Zero Leading (pure capacitive)** | Purely **Magnetising** | Strengthens main field |
| **Unity (resistive)** | Purely **Cross-Magnetising** | Distorts main field (no weakening) |
| **Lagging (general)** | Partly demagnetising + partly cross-magnetising | Weakens + distorts |
| **Leading (general)** | Partly magnetising + partly cross-magnetising | Strengthens + distorts |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

When a synchronous generator delivers load, three physical events occur **simultaneously**:

**Step 1: EMF Induction**
- The rotor's DC-excited field rotates at synchronous speed, cutting the stationary stator (armature) conductors.
- A balanced 3-phase EMF is induced: **E_ph = 4.44 · f · φ · T_ph · K_w**
- K_w = winding factor; T_ph = turns per phase.

**Step 2: Armature Current Flow**
- The induced EMF drives current **I_a** through the closed external load.
- The current is determined by Ohm's law applied to the external load and the internal impedance of the machine.

**Step 3: Armature Reaction**
- The 3-phase armature currents themselves produce a **rotating magnetic field** at the same synchronous speed as the rotor.
- This field is **stationary relative to the rotor** (because both rotate at N_s).
- It is, however, **displaced in space** from the main field axis by an angle **ψ = (90° − φ)**, where φ is the load power factor angle.
- The two fluxes combine vectorially, producing a **resultant air-gap flux** that is reduced, increased, or distorted.

## 2.2 The Synchronous Impedance Concept

The combined effect of:
- Armature winding resistance (**R_a**)
- Armature reaction effect (**X_AR**)

is replaced by a single equivalent **synchronous reactance (X_s)**:

$$X_s \;=\; X_a \;+\; X_{AR}$$

where:
- **X_a** = leakage reactance of armature winding (constant).
- **X_AR** = reactance representing armature reaction effect (a fictitious quantity).

The **synchronous impedance (Z_s)** is then:

$$Z_s \;=\; \sqrt{R_a^{\,2} \;+\; X_s^{\,2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU 2024):**
> Although R_a and X_a are physical quantities, **X_AR is a fictitious construct** — it is *defined* such that the voltage drop **I_a · X_AR** equals the EMF consumed in opposing armature reaction. This is purely an analytical convenience that allows the generator to be treated as a simple series circuit.

## 2.3 Equivalent Circuit & Voltage Equation

The per-phase equivalent circuit of a loaded synchronous generator is:

```
     E_ph ──[ R_a ]──[ jX_s ]──●───── V_ph
                                │
                                I_a → (to load)
```

Applying KVL (taking **V_ph** as reference and phasor sum of internal drops):

$$\boxed{\;E_{ph} \;=\; V_{ph} \;+\; I_a R_a \;+\; j\,I_a X_s\;}$$

This is the **fundamental generator voltage equation** — the most tested formula in Module 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phasor Sign Convention (KTU Board Standard):**
> - **E_ph leads V_ph** for a generator supplying a lagging power factor load.
> - The drop **I_a R_a** is parallel to **I_a**.
> - The drop **j I_a X_s** leads **I_a** by **90°**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Induced EMF per phase | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot \phi \cdot T_{ph} \cdot K_w$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}$ | rpm |
| 3 | Frequency | $f = \dfrac{P \cdot N_s}{120}$ | Hz (**50 Hz** in India) |
| 4 | Synchronous reactance | $X_s = X_a + X_{AR}$ | Ω (ohms) |
| 5 | Synchronous impedance | $Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$ | Ω |
| 6 | Generator EMF equation | $E_{ph} = V_{ph} + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s$ | per-phase vector |
| 7 | Phasor magnitude | $E_{ph} = \sqrt{(V_{ph}\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V_{ph}\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$ | for lagging pf |
| 8 | Voltage Regulation | $\% \text{VR} = \dfrac{\vert E_{ph} - V_{ph}\vert}{V_{ph}} \times 100$ | always positive % |
| 9 | Power factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Power angle (δ) | $\delta = \angle E_{ph} - \angle V_{ph}$ | degrees / radians |

> [!NOTE]
> **VTU/KTU Convention — Use `\mid` or `\vert` for absolute value** (to avoid breaking markdown tables). For instance, $\mid E_{ph} - V_{ph}\mid$ not $\vert E_{ph} - V_{ph} \vert$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Synchronous generators on load are the **backbone of power generation worldwide**. Understanding the on-load behaviour enables engineers to:
- **Predict terminal voltage** under varying load conditions — critical for **excitation control system design** (AVR — Automatic Voltage Regulator).
- **Determine the correct kVAr rating** of generator switchgear and bus-bars.
- **Calculate transformer tap settings** and parallel operation compatibility.
- **Design power system stabilisers (PSS)** and prevent **steady-state instability** by ensuring the power angle δ stays below the stability limit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Magnitude of E_ph for a Lagging Power Factor Load

This is the single most important derivation for KTU Module 3 problems.

**Step 1 — Reference axis selection**
Take **V_ph** as the reference phasor along the real axis:

$$V_{ph} = V_{ph}\angle 0°$$

**Step 2 — Express armature current**
For a **lagging** power factor load, the current **I_a** lags **V_ph** by angle φ:

$$I_a = I_a\angle(-\phi) = I_a(\cos\phi - j\sin\phi)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the resistance drop (I_a R_a)**
This drop is parallel to I_a (i.e., at angle −φ):

$$I_a R_a = I_a R_a(\cos\phi - j\sin\phi)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the synchronous reactance drop (j I_a X_s)**
Multiplying by **j** rotates any complex number by **+90° counter-clockwise**:

$$j I_a X_s = I_a X_s \angle (90° - \phi) = I_a X_s(\sin\phi + j\cos\phi)$$

**Step 5 — Apply KVL: E_ph = V_ph + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s**
Adding the real parts (x-components) and imaginary parts (y-components) separately:

Real part:

$$E_{\text{real}} = V_{ph} + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi$$

Imaginary part:

$$E_{\text{imag}} = -I_a R_a \sin\phi + I_a X_s \cos\phi$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude of E_ph**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= \sqrt{E_{\text{real}}^{\,2} + E_{\text{imag}}^{\,2}} \\
&= \sqrt{(V_{ph} + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi)^2 \;+\; (-I_a R_a \sin\phi + I_a X_s \cos\phi)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Common engineering simplification**
Because **R_a ≪ X_s** in most alternators (R_a is typically < 0.1 · X_s), the resistance drop is often neglected:

$$\boxed{\;E_{ph} \;\approx\; \sqrt{(V_{ph}\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 \;+\; (V_{ph}\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}\;}$$

When R_a is fully neglected:

$$\boxed{\;E_{ph} \;\approx\; \sqrt{V_{ph}^{\,2} \;+\; (I_a X_s)^2 \;+\; 2 V_{ph} I_a X_s \sin\phi}\;}$$

## 3.2 Special-Case Derivations (All Three Power Factors)

### Case A: Unity Power Factor (φ = 0°, cos φ = 1, sin φ = 0)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= \sqrt{(V_{ph} + I_a R_a)^2 + (I_a X_s)^2} \\
\text{or} \quad E_{ph} &\approx \sqrt{V_{ph}^{\,2} + (I_a X_s)^2} \quad \text{(neglecting } R_a\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Effect:** E_ph > V_ph, so voltage *rises* on load (negative regulation). Armature reaction is purely **cross-magnetising** — it distorts the main field but does not weaken it.

### Case B: Zero Lagging Power Factor (φ = 90°, cos φ = 0, sin φ = 1) — Pure Inductive Load

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= \sqrt{(I_a R_a)^2 + (V_{ph} + I_a X_s)^2} \\
\text{or} \quad E_{ph} &\approx V_{ph} + I_a X_s \quad \text{(neglecting } R_a\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Effect:** Armature reaction is **purely demagnetising**. E_ph increases the most; the field is weakened maximally. This is the **worst-case** for voltage regulation.

### Case C: Zero Leading Power Factor (φ = −90°, cos φ = 0, sin φ = −1) — Pure Capacitive Load

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= \sqrt{(I_a R_a)^2 + (V_{ph} - I_a X_s)^2} \\
\text{or} \quad E_{ph} &\approx V_{ph} - I_a X_s \quad \text{(neglecting } R_a\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Effect:** Armature reaction is **purely magnetising**. E_ph < V_ph; the field is strengthened. This is the **best-case** for regulation (largest *negative* regulation).

### Case D: General Lagging Power Factor (e.g., 0.8 lagging)

$$\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87°,\quad \sin\phi = 0.6$$

$$E_{ph} = \sqrt{(V_{ph}\cdot 0.8 + I_a R_a)^2 + (V_{ph}\cdot 0.6 + I_a X_s)^2}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Voltage Regulation (VR)

The voltage regulation is the **change in terminal voltage** when the load is removed (full-load → no-load), keeping excitation and speed constant.

**Step 1 — At full load**, terminal voltage is V_ph (rated). Internal EMF is E_ph (computed above).

**Step 2 — At no-load**, terminal voltage = induced EMF = E_ph (since I_a = 0).

**Step 3 — Define regulation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\, \text{VR} &= \frac{\mid E_{ph} - V_{ph}\mid}{V_{ph}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{E_{ph(\text{no-load})} - V_{ph(\text{full-load})}}{V_{ph(\text{full-load})}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign of VR (KTU Convention):**
> - **Lagging & Unity pf** → E_ph > V_ph → **VR is positive**.
> - **Leading pf** (sufficiently leading) → E_ph < V_ph → **VR is negative**.
> - This **negative regulation** at leading pf is why synchronous generators used with long transmission lines (which look capacitive due to the **Ferranti effect**) may experience rising terminal voltage.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Phasor Diagram Position of E_ph

| Load Power Factor | Real Drop Component | Imaginary Drop Component | Position of E_ph relative to V_ph |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unity (R-load)** | $V_{ph} + I_a R_a$ | $I_a X_s$ | Slightly above V_ph, leading by δ |
| **Lagging (R-L load)** | $V_{ph}\cos\phi + I_a R_a$ | $V_{ph}\sin\phi + I_a X_s$ | Above and ahead of V_ph |
| **Leading (R-C load)** | $V_{ph}\cos\phi + I_a R_a$ | $-V_{ph}\sin\phi + I_a X_s$ | Below V_ph (may even lag V_ph) |
| **Zero lagging (pure L)** | $I_a R_a$ | $V_{ph} + I_a X_s$ | Far above V_ph |
| **Zero leading (pure C)** | $I_a R_a$ | $V_{ph} - I_a X_s$ | Far below V_ph |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Mnemonic — "LAG = LIFT, LEAD = LOWER":**
> For a generator, **Lagging pf lifts E_ph above V_ph**, and **Leading pf lowers E_ph below V_ph**. This is the most repeated KTU exam fact for phasor diagram questions.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Armature Reaction. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Armature Reaction (AR)** is defined as the effect produced on the **main field flux** of a synchronous generator due to the magnetic field created by the **armature current** flowing through the stator winding when the machine is on load.

**Key points (for full marks):**
1. AR is caused by the 3-phase armature currents producing a rotating magnetic field at synchronous speed.
2. This armature flux is **stationary relative to the rotor** (both rotate at N_s).
3. The nature of AR depends on the **power factor of the load**:
   - **Lagging pf** → Demagnetising + Cross-magnetising
   - **Unity pf** → Purely Cross-magnetising
   - **Leading pf** → Magnetising + Cross-magnetising
4. AR changes both the **magnitude** and **waveform** of the resultant air-gap flux.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always state that AR is *produced by the load current*, not the field current. Many students lose 1 mark for saying "AR is the effect of armature flux on itself."

---

### Question 2: What is Synchronous Reactance? [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Synchronous Reactance (X_s)** is the sum of the **leakage reactance (X_a)** of the armature winding and the **fictitious reactance (X_AR)** representing the effect of armature reaction.

$$X_s = X_a + X_{AR}$$

**Key points:**
1. **X_a** is a real, physical quantity due to armature winding leakage flux.
2. **X_AR** is a fictitious quantity, defined such that the voltage drop **I_a · X_AR** equals the EMF that would be required to overcome the back-effect of armature reaction.
3. X_s is treated as a constant for analytical purposes (in simple analysis, neglecting saturation).
4. It allows the generator to be modelled by a simple series circuit: **E_ph = V_ph + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module Question (14 marks) carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice). Solutions must include the phasor diagram and step-by-step derivations.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**A (a)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz synchronous generator has a star-connected armature winding with 200 turns per phase and a winding factor of 0.96. The flux per pole is 0.05 Wb. The armature resistance per phase is 0.4 Ω and synchronous reactance per phase is 6 Ω. Calculate the open-circuit line voltage at no-load. **(7 marks)**

**A (b)** If the above machine delivers a full-load current of 50 A at 0.8 power factor lagging, calculate:
- (i) The induced EMF per phase **E_ph** (using full formula, with R_a).
- (ii) The line-to-line induced EMF on open-circuit.
- (iii) The voltage regulation in percent. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ⭐ SOLUTION — Question A

### Part A(a) Solution — No-Load EMF (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Phases: 3 (star-connected)
- Poles: P = 4
- Frequency: f = 50 Hz
- Turns per phase: T_ph = 200
- Winding factor: K_w = 0.96
- Flux per pole: φ = 0.05 Wb

**Step 1 — Compute Synchronous Speed (for understanding):**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply EMF Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= 4.44 \cdot f \cdot \phi \cdot T_{ph} \cdot K_w \\
&= 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.05 \times 200 \times 0.96
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing step-by-step:
- 4.44 × 50 = 222
- 222 × 0.05 = 11.1
- 11.1 × 200 = 2220
- 2220 × 0.96 = **2131.2 V**

$$\boxed{E_{ph(\text{no-load})} = 2131.2 \text{ V per phase}}$$

**Step 3 — Star-connected line voltage:**

$$E_{L(\text{no-load})} = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph} = 1.732 \times 2131.2 = 3691.24 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{L(\text{no-load})} \approx 3691.24 \text{ V} \;(\text{or } \approx 3.69 \text{ kV})}$$

---

### Part A(b) Solution — Full-Load EMF and Regulation (7 marks)

**Given (additional data):**
- I_a = 50 A
- pf = 0.8 lagging → cos φ = 0.8 → φ = 36.87° → sin φ = 0.6
- R_a = 0.4 Ω, X_s = 6 Ω
- Rated terminal voltage V_ph (assume full-load V_ph = open-circuit V_ph per phase = 2131.2 V — this is the standard KTU assumption for "synchronous generator" problems where the rated voltage equals the no-load voltage).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** In on-load problems, the **rated terminal voltage V_ph** is given or equals the no-load phase voltage. The full-load current and power factor are the new given quantities.

**Step 1 — Compute Real and Imaginary Components of the EMF Equation**

For lagging pf, the formula is:

$$E_{ph} = \sqrt{(V_{ph}\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V_{ph}\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term:**

$V_{ph}\cos\phi = 2131.2 \times 0.8 = 1704.96 \text{ V}$

$I_a R_a = 50 \times 0.4 = 20 \text{ V}$

Sum (real part) = $1704.96 + 20 = 1724.96 \text{ V}$

$V_{ph}\sin\phi = 2131.2 \times 0.6 = 1278.72 \text{ V}$

$I_a X_s = 50 \times 6 = 300 \text{ V}$

Sum (imaginary part) = $1278.72 + 300 = 1578.72 \text{ V}$

**Step 3 — Compute E_ph:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= \sqrt{(1724.96)^2 + (1578.72)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{2\,975\,487.6 + 2\,492\,597.0} \\
&= \sqrt{5\,468\,084.6} \\
&\approx 2338.4 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{E_{ph(\text{full-load})} \approx 2338.4 \text{ V per phase}}$$

**Step 4 — Line-to-line EMF (open-circuit, i.e., the induced EMF itself):**

$$E_{L(\text{full-load})} = \sqrt{3} \times 2338.4 = 4050.21 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{L(\text{full-load})} \approx 4050.2 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 5 — Voltage Regulation:**

$$\% \text{VR} = \frac{E_{ph(\text{no-load})} - V_{ph(\text{full-load})}}{V_{ph(\text{full-load})}} \times 100$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{VR} &= \frac{2338.4 - 2131.2}{2131.2} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{207.2}{2131.2} \times 100 \\
&\approx 9.72\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{VR} \approx 9.72\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing V_ph and V_L** — Star-connected terminal voltage V_L = √3 V_ph. Always work in per-phase first.
> 2. **Sign error in phasor formula** — For leading pf, the term (V_ph sin φ + I_a X_s) becomes (V_ph sin φ − I_a X_s) since φ is negative.
> 3. **Forgetting the cos φ and sin φ terms** in the bracketed expressions — this is the most common 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Computing regulation with line voltages directly** without per-phase reduction.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**B (a)** Define voltage regulation. A 3-phase, 1500 kVA, 6600 V, star-connected synchronous generator has R_a = 0.4 Ω and X_s = 5 Ω per phase. Find the percentage voltage regulation at:
- (i) Unity power factor full-load.
- (ii) 0.8 power factor lagging full-load. **(7 marks)**

**B (b)** A 3-phase synchronous generator has an induced EMF of 320 V per phase at no-load. It delivers a current of 60 A at 0.7 power factor leading. The synchronous impedance per phase is (0.5 + j5) Ω. Find the terminal voltage per phase and the voltage regulation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ⭐ SOLUTION — Question B

### Part B(a) Solution — Regulation at Unity and 0.8 Lagging pf (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Apparent power S = 1500 kVA
- Line voltage V_L = 6600 V
- Star-connected → V_ph = 6600 / √3 = 3810.5 V
- R_a = 0.4 Ω, X_s = 5 Ω

**Step 1 — Compute Full-Load Current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= \frac{S}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L} = \frac{1\,500\,000}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600} \\
&= \frac{1\,500\,000}{11\,431.5} \\
&\approx 131.22 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{I_a = 131.22 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 2 — Case (i): Unity Power Factor**

cos φ = 1, sin φ = 0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ph}\cos\phi + I_a R_a &= 3810.5 \times 1 + 131.22 \times 0.4 \\
&= 3810.5 + 52.49 = 3863.0 \text{ V} \\
V_{ph}\sin\phi + I_a X_s &= 0 + 131.22 \times 5 = 656.1 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= \sqrt{(3863.0)^2 + (656.1)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{14\,922\,769 + 430\,467.2} \\
&= \sqrt{15\,353\,236.2} \\
&\approx 3918.3 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{VR}_{\text{unity}} &= \frac{3918.3 - 3810.5}{3810.5} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{107.8}{3810.5} \times 100 \\
&\approx 2.83\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{VR at unity pf} \approx 2.83\%}$$

**Step 3 — Case (ii): 0.8 Lagging Power Factor**

cos φ = 0.8, sin φ = 0.6, φ = 36.87°.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ph}\cos\phi + I_a R_a &= 3810.5 \times 0.8 + 52.49 \\
&= 3048.4 + 52.49 = 3100.89 \text{ V} \\
V_{ph}\sin\phi + I_a X_s &= 3810.5 \times 0.6 + 656.1 \\
&= 2286.3 + 656.1 = 2942.4 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= \sqrt{(3100.89)^2 + (2942.4)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{9\,615\,518.5 + 8\,657\,717.8} \\
&= \sqrt{18\,273\,236.3} \\
&\approx 4274.7 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{VR}_{0.8\,\text{lag}} &= \frac{4274.7 - 3810.5}{3810.5} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{464.2}{3810.5} \times 100 \\
&\approx 12.18\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{VR at 0.8 pf lagging} \approx 12.18\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Notice how lagging pf causes the regulation to increase from 2.83% (unity) to 12.18% (0.8 lag). This is the *demagnetising armature reaction* at work.

---

### Part B(b) Solution — Leading Power Factor and Terminal Voltage (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- E_ph (open-circuit) = 320 V per phase
- I_a = 60 A
- pf = 0.7 leading → φ = −45.57° (negative because leading)
- cos φ = 0.7, sin φ = 0.714
- Synchronous impedance Z_s = (0.5 + j5) Ω
- → R_a = 0.5 Ω, X_s = 5 Ω

**Step 1 — Choose Reference and Apply KVL**

The generator EMF equation is:

$$E_{ph} = V_{ph} + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s$$

Solving for V_ph:

$$V_{ph} = E_{ph} - I_a R_a - j I_a X_s$$

**Step 2 — Express I_a (leading pf means current leads V_ph; for now, take V_ph as reference — but E_ph is given, so we need a different approach)**

**Alternative Approach:** Take E_ph as the reference along the real axis:

$$E_{ph} = 320 + j0 \text{ V}$$

For leading pf, current **I_a** leads terminal voltage V_ph. However, since we don't know V_ph yet, we use the standard approach: **take V_ph as reference** and write I_a in terms of leading pf.

For leading pf, the current leads V_ph by angle φ (where φ is the magnitude of the pf angle):

$$I_a = I_a\angle(+\phi) = 60(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi) = 60(0.7 + j0.714) = 42 + j42.84 \text{ A}$$

Wait — careful: in the generator convention, for **leading** pf, the current I_a leads V_ph. The phasor drop equation E_ph = V_ph + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s is preserved; we just plug in I_a with positive angle.

**Step 3 — Compute the voltage drops**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a R_a &= (42 + j42.84)(0.5) = 21 + j21.42 \\
j I_a X_s &= j(42 + j42.84)(5) = j(210 + j214.2) = -214.2 + j210
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the drops**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a R_a + j I_a X_s &= (21 + j21.42) + (-214.2 + j210) \\
&= (21 - 214.2) + j(21.42 + 210) \\
&= -193.2 + j231.42 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for V_ph**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ph} &= E_{ph} - (I_a R_a + j I_a X_s) \\
&= (320 + j0) - (-193.2 + j231.42) \\
&= 320 + 193.2 - j231.42 \\
&= 513.2 - j231.42 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude of V_ph**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mid V_{ph}\mid &= \sqrt{(513.2)^2 + (-231.42)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{263\,374.2 + 53\,555.2} \\
&= \sqrt{316\,929.4} \\
&\approx 563.0 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{V_{ph} \approx 563.0 \text{ V per phase}}$$

**Step 7 — Voltage Regulation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{VR} &= \frac{E_{ph(\text{no-load})} - V_{ph(\text{full-load})}}{V_{ph(\text{full-load})}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{320 - 563.0}{563.0} \times 100 \\
&\approx -43.16\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{VR} \approx -43.16\% \text{ (negative regulation)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Leading PF Subtleties:**
> 1. **Sign of sin φ term in E_ph magnitude formula:** For leading pf, the bracketed expression becomes $(V_{ph}\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V_{ph}\sin\phi - I_a X_s)^2$. Here sin φ is **subtracted**, not added.
> 2. **Negative regulation is valid** — do not panic; it just means E_ph < V_ph (the capacitive load magnetised the field, raising terminal voltage).
> 3. **Power angle δ is negative** for leading pf loads — E_ph lags V_ph.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU Module 3 — Rapid Revision Flash Card: Synchronous Generator on Load**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- [x] **Armature reaction** is the effect of the armature's own magnetic field on the main field flux.
- [x] It is **caused by the load current**, not the field current.
- [x] It causes both **magnitude change** and **waveform distortion** of the air-gap flux.

### ⚡ Power Factor → Armature Reaction Mapping (MUST MEMORISE)
- [x] **Pure Lagging (0 pf lag)** → **Purely Demagnetising** → worst-case voltage rise on removal of load.
- [x] **Pure Leading (0 pf lead)** → **Purely Magnetising** → voltage drops on load removal.
- [x] **Unity pf** → **Purely Cross-Magnetising** → no demagnetisation, only distortion.
- [x] **Lagging (general)** → Demagnetising + Cross-magnetising.
- [x] **Leading (general)** → Magnetising + Cross-magnetising.

### 📐 Key Equations (Always Per-Phase)
- [x] $E_{ph} = V_{ph} + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s$ (the master equation)
- [x] $X_s = X_a + X_{AR}$ (synchronous reactance)
- [x] $Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$ (synchronous impedance)
- [x] $\%\text{VR} = \dfrac{\vert E_{ph} - V_{ph}\vert}{V_{ph}} \times 100$
- [x] EMF magnitude (lagging): $E_{ph} = \sqrt{(V_{ph}\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V_{ph}\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$

### 🎨 Phasor Diagram Quick Rules
- [x] **Lagging pf** → E_ph is drawn **above** V_ph, leading by power angle δ.
- [x] **Leading pf** → E_ph is drawn **below** V_ph (and may lag V_ph).
- [x] **I_a R_a** drop is parallel to **I_a**; **j I_a X_s** drop is 90° **ahead** of **I_a**.

### 🚫 Common Mistakes to Avoid
- [x] Don't mix up **line** and **phase** values in star connections.
- [x] Don't forget the **cos φ** and **sin φ** factors in the bracketed expressions.
- [x] Don't drop the **I_a R_a** term without verifying if the problem expects it.
- [x] Don't assume E_ph > V_ph always — leading pf gives E_ph < V_ph.
- [x] Don't forget the √3 factor when computing line values from phase values (or vice versa).

### 🧠 Numerical Pattern for KTU Problems
- [x] Always start with **per-phase values**.
- [x] For star: $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$, $I_{ph} = I_L$.
- [x] For delta: $V_{ph} = V_L$, $I_{ph} = I_L / \sqrt{3}$.
- [x] Always include the **phasor diagram** for 1–2 extra marks.
- [x] Mention the **nature of armature reaction** explicitly — KTU examiners reward this.

### 💡 Real-World Engineering Tie-In
- [x] Negative VR at leading pf → relates to **Ferranti effect** in long transmission lines.
- [x] Demagnetising AR at lagging pf → why **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator)** must increase excitation under load.
- [x] Synchronous impedance is a **simplified model** — exact analysis uses **Potier triangle** and **MMF method** (typically Module 4/5 topics).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Armature Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Armature Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Armature Reaction** is defined as the effect of the magnetic field produced by the current flowing in the armature conductors on the distribution of flux in the main field of a DC machine. In other words, it is the *distortion of the main field flux* caused by the armature's own MMF (Magneto-Motive Force) when the machine operates under load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"The armature reaction is the effect of armature flux on the main field flux, which results in the distortion of the main field flux and a reduction in the net effective flux per pole."*

Mathematically, the resultant air-gap flux distribution is given by:

$$\phi_{resultant} = \phi_{main} \pm \phi_{armature}$$

Where the **+** sign applies when armature MMF *aids* main MMF (over-pole tips), and the **−** sign applies when it *opposes* main MMF (under-pole tips).

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 Context:** This topic belongs to the section dealing with DC Machine Commutation, Performance Characteristics, and Voltage Regulation. The concept is **prerequisite** to understanding interpoles, compensating windings, and commutation problems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a heavy door forward (this is your **main field flux**, produced by the field winding). Now, suppose a friend starts pushing the door sideways from the right (this is the **armature flux**, produced by armature current). 

- Your forward push still exists, but the door's net movement is no longer straight ahead — it is **distorted** sideways.
- Worse, your friend is partly pushing against you on the left side, **reducing your effective forward push**.

This is exactly what happens inside a DC machine:
- **Main field flux** = the "forward push" (set up by field winding).
- **Armature flux** = the "sideways push" (set up by armature conductors carrying current).
- **Result:** The main field is **distorted** and **weakened** in the net forward direction.

> [!TIP]
> Think of **Armature Reaction** as a *two-faced villain*:
> 1. **Distortion** → shifts the MNA (Magnetic Neutral Axis).
> 2. **Demagnetization** → reduces the total flux per pole (Φ ↓ → E ↓ → reduced terminal voltage).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Brush shift angle** (θ_b): typically 0° to 30° in practical machines.
- **Cross-magnetizing ampere-turns** are measured per pole in units of **At/pole** (Ampere-turns per pole).
- **Effective flux reduction factor** ranges between **2% to 5%** in well-designed machines.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask the direct definition for **3 marks**. Write the textbook definition word-for-word and add a small diagram — this guarantees full marks.

---

## 1.4 Two Distinct Effects of Armature Reaction

| # | Effect | Physical Meaning | Consequence |
|---|--------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Demagnetizing Effect** | Armature MMF directly opposes main MMF | Reduces net flux Φ per pole |
| 2 | **Cross-Magnetizing Effect** | Armature MMF is perpendicular to main MMF | Distorts main field, shifts MNA |

Both effects occur **simultaneously** when brushes are shifted from GNA (Geometrical Neutral Axis) to compensate for armature reaction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Setup: GNA vs MNA vs BNA

To understand armature reaction, three axes must be clearly distinguished:

- **GNA (Geometrical Neutral Axis):** The axis *perpendicular* to the main field axis. It is fixed by construction.
- **MNA (Magnetic Neutral Axis):** The axis *perpendicular* to the *resultant* air-gap flux axis. With no load, MNA coincides with GNA. Under load, **MNA shifts** from GNA due to armature reaction.
- **BNA (Brush Neutral Axis):** The axis along which the brushes are placed. Normally, brushes are kept at MNA to ensure **sparkless commutation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The direction of brush shift depends on the **direction of rotation**:
> - **Generator:** Brushes shift **forward** (in the direction of rotation).
> - **Motor:** Brushes shift **backward** (against the direction of rotation).

---

## 2.2 Distribution of Armature MMF (Step-by-Step Logic)

The armature winding carries current. Consider a 2-pole DC machine. Let:
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors.
- $I_a$ = armature current (A).
- $P$ = number of poles.

**Step 1: Total ampere-conductors**
$$A = Z \cdot I_a \;\; \text{[ampere-conductors]}$$

**Step 2: Total ampere-conductors per pole**
$$A/pole = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{P}$$

**Step 3: Ampere-turns per pole** (since each turn has 2 conductors in simplex lap/wave)
$$AT_a/pole = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **armature MMF per pole**, the foundational quantity for all armature reaction calculations.

---

## 2.3 Cross-Magnetizing and Demagnetizing Components

When brushes are shifted by an angle $\theta_b$ (mechanical or electrical) from GNA, the armature MMF splits into two components:

### (a) Cross-Magnetizing Component (per pole)
$$AT_c = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \cdot \left(\frac{2\theta_b}{180°}\right) = \frac{Z \cdot I_a \cdot \theta_b}{180° \cdot P}$$

Where $\theta_b$ is in electrical degrees.

### (b) Demagnetizing Component (per pole)
$$AT_d = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2\theta_b}{180°}\right)$$

### (c) Net Effective Demagnetizing (with compensating winding or interpole)
$$AT_{d,net} = AT_d - AT_{interpole} - AT_{comp}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Armature ampere-conductors | $A = Z \cdot I_a$ | A-conductors | Total for the machine |
| 2 | AT per pole (armature) | $AT_a = \dfrac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}$ | At/pole | Foundation formula |
| 3 | Cross-magnetizing AT/pole | $AT_c = \dfrac{Z \cdot I_a \cdot 2\theta_b}{360° \cdot P}$ | At/pole | When brushes shifted by $\theta_b$ |
| 4 | Demagnetizing AT/pole | $AT_d = \dfrac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}\left(1 - \dfrac{2\theta_b}{180°}\right)$ | At/pole | Acts opposite to main field |
| 5 | Net flux per pole | $\Phi_{net} = \Phi_{main} - \Phi_{AR(equivalent)}$ | Wb | Reduced by demagnetizing AT |
| 6 | Generated EMF | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$ | V | Standard DC machine EMF equation |
| 7 | Voltage drop (armature) | $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ | V | Generator convention |
| 8 | Back-EMF (motor) | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | V | Motor convention |
| 9 | AT per pole (field) | $AT_f = N_f \cdot I_f$ | At/pole | Main field MMF |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical KTU Hint:** Whenever a problem says "brushes are shifted by $x$° from GNA", it is **always** a demagnetization + cross-magnetization problem. When it says "brushes in GNA", only **cross-magnetization** exists (no demagnetization).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Armature reaction is **not just a textbook concept** — it has critical practical implications:

1. **Voltage Regulation of Generators:** Demagnetization causes terminal voltage to drop more than predicted, hurting regulation.
2. **Speed Regulation of Motors:** Reduced flux (due to demagnetization) increases speed, which can damage machinery.
3. **Commutation Failure:** Distortion of MNA causes coils under commutation to have non-zero induced EMF, leading to **sparking at brushes** — a major failure mode.
4. **Compensating Windings & Interpoles:** Modern DC machines use these to **nullify** armature reaction — a direct engineering solution to the problem.

> [!NOTE]
> In **synchronous machines** (Module 4/5 of this course), the *same* mathematical framework applies, but there, armature reaction can be **magnetizing, demagnetizing, or cross-magnetizing** depending on the load power factor — this is the next step in your study.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Armature MMF is Cross-Magnetizing at GNA

### Setup
Consider a 2-pole DC machine with brushes placed on the **Geometrical Neutral Axis (GNA)**. Armature current $I_a$ flows through the winding.

**Step 1: Conductor layout**

In a 2-pole machine, the armature has $Z$ conductors distributed uniformly around the periphery. The conductors under the North Pole carry current *outward* (say, dot convention) and conductors under the South Pole carry current *inward* (cross convention), because the commutator reverses direction every half rotation.

**Step 2: Direction of MMF**

Applying the right-hand grip rule, the current-carrying armature conductors produce an MMF whose axis lies along the **GNA itself** (i.e., horizontal in our 2-pole model).

**Step 3: Direction of Main Field MMF**

The field winding produces MMF along the **direct axis (d-axis)**, which is perpendicular to GNA.

**Step 4: Perpendicularity check**

Since the main field MMF axis and armature MMF axis are at 90° to each other, the armature MMF is **purely cross-magnetizing** when brushes are on GNA.

$$\theta_b = 0° \implies AT_d = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}\left(1 - \frac{2 \cdot 0°}{180°}\right) = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \cdot 1 = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}$$

$$\theta_b = 0° \implies AT_c = \frac{Z \cdot I_a \cdot 2 \cdot 0°}{360° \cdot P} = 0$$

Wait — this seems contradictory. Let me re-examine the standard convention:

> The standard KTU convention is:
> - When brushes are on **GNA** → armature MMF is **purely cross-magnetizing**, **zero demagnetizing**.
> - When brushes are **shifted from GNA** → a **demagnetizing** component appears.

**Corrected Step 4 (with correct sign convention):**

The angle $\theta_b$ is the **brush shift from GNA in electrical degrees**. The armature conductors within the brush shift angle ($2\theta_b$ total) produce demagnetizing MMF, and the rest produce cross-magnetizing MMF.

**Step 5: Ampere-conductors in the demagnetizing zone**

In a simplex lap winding, the conductors in the demagnetizing zone form a belt of width $2\theta_b$ electrical degrees on each side of GNA (in total, $2\theta_b$ wide per pole-pair).

Number of demagnetizing conductors per pole:
$$Z_{d} = \frac{2\theta_b}{360°} \cdot Z \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{Z \cdot \theta_b}{360°}$$

Wait, let me redo this cleanly:

**Step 5 (Corrected and Clean):**

The number of conductors in the demagnetizing zone per pole:
$$Z_d = \frac{2\theta_b}{360°} \cdot Z = \frac{Z \cdot \theta_b}{180°}$$

The number of demagnetizing ampere-conductors per pole:
$$A_d = Z_d \cdot I_a / (\text{number of parallel paths per pole})$$

For a simplex lap winding, $A = P$, and current per conductor is $I_a/A = I_a/P$.

**Step 6: Demagnetizing AT per pole**

Each turn has 2 conductors, so demagnetizing turns per pole:
$$T_d = \frac{Z_d}{2} = \frac{Z \cdot \theta_b}{360°}$$

Demagnetizing AT per pole (with current $I_a/P$ per conductor... but in AT we use total AT in series):

The correct and standard formula used in KTU textbooks is:

$$\boxed{AT_d/pole = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \cdot \left(\frac{2\theta_b}{180°}\right) = \frac{Z \cdot I_a \cdot \theta_b}{180° \cdot P}}$$

This is the **demagnetizing** component (used to oppose main field).

**Step 7: Cross-magnetizing AT per pole**

The remaining conductors (outside the demagnetizing zone) are:
$$Z_c = Z - Z_d = Z - \frac{Z \cdot \theta_b}{180°} = Z\left(1 - \frac{\theta_b}{180°}\right)$$

Cross-magnetizing AT per pole:
$$\boxed{AT_c/pole = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2\theta_b}{180°}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the convention swap:** Different textbooks swap which component is "demagnetizing" and which is "cross-magnetizing" in terms of the angle factor. The KTU convention used here is:
> - **Demagnetizing component** has factor $\dfrac{2\theta_b}{180°}$ (proportional to brush shift).
> - **Cross-magnetizing component** has factor $\left(1 - \dfrac{2\theta_b}{180°}\right)$ (decreases as brushes shift).

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** A 4-pole, wave-wound DC machine has 600 armature conductors. The armature current is 120 A. The brushes are shifted by 6° mechanical from GNA. Calculate:
(a) Demagnetizing AT/pole
(b) Cross-magnetizing AT/pole
(c) Net flux per pole if main field AT/pole = 5500 At/pole, and the demagnetization reduces flux by 1 AT of armature MMF per 5 AT of main field.

### Given Data
$$P = 4, \quad A = 2 \text{ (wave)}, \quad Z = 600, \quad I_a = 120 \text{ A}, \quad \theta_b = 6° \text{ (mechanical)}$$

**Step 1: Convert mechanical angle to electrical angle**

$$\theta_{b,electrical} = \theta_{b,mech} \times \frac{P}{2} = 6° \times \frac{4}{2} = 12°$$

**Step 2: Demagnetizing AT per pole**

$$AT_d = \frac{Z \cdot I_a \cdot \theta_{b,elec}}{180° \cdot P}$$

$$AT_d = \frac{600 \times 120 \times 12°}{180° \times 4} = \frac{864000}{720} = 1200 \text{ At/pole}$$

**Step 3: Cross-magnetizing AT per pole**

$$AT_c = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2\theta_{b,elec}}{180°}\right)$$

$$AT_c = \frac{600 \times 120}{2 \times 4} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2 \times 12°}{180°}\right)$$

$$AT_c = \frac{72000}{8} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{24}{180}\right)$$

$$AT_c = 9000 \cdot \left(1 - 0.1333\right) = 9000 \times 0.8667 = 7800 \text{ At/pole}$$

**Step 4: Net flux calculation**

Reduction in AT/pole due to demagnetization = 1200 At/pole

Given: 1 AT of armature reduces flux equivalent to 5 AT of main field.

Effective AT reduction = $1200 \times 5 = 6000$ At/pole

Net AT/pole = $5500 - 6000 = -500$ At/pole

Since the net AT is **negative**, this means the field is **over-demagnetized** — flux reverses. In practice, this is why we use **interpoles** and **compensating windings**.

For a realistic design, the demagnetization should be < 5% of main AT. Here, $1200/5500 = 21.8\%$, which is far too high. **The machine design is flawed.**

If the demagnetization is acceptable (say 5% of main AT = 275 At/pole), then:
$$\Phi_{net} = \Phi_{main} \times \left(1 - \frac{AT_{d,equivalent}}{AT_{main}}\right)$$

$$\Phi_{net} = \Phi_{main} \times \left(1 - \frac{275}{5500}\right) = 0.95 \cdot \Phi_{main}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison: Lap vs Wave Winding

| Parameter | Lap Winding | Wave Winding |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Number of parallel paths ($A$) | $A = P$ | $A = 2$ |
| Conductors per path | $Z/P$ | $Z/2$ |
| Current per conductor | $I_a/P$ | $I_a/2$ |
| Demagnetizing AT/pole | $\dfrac{Z I_a \theta_b}{180° P}$ | Same formula, but $I_a$ is total, so equivalent effect is different |
| Cross-magnetizing AT/pole | $\dfrac{Z I_a}{2P}\left(1 - \dfrac{2\theta_b}{180°}\right)$ | Same |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** In wave winding, the demagnetizing effect per pole is **less severe** for the same total armature current because $A = 2$ regardless of $P$, distributing the MMF differently.

---

## 3.4 Final Analytical Summary Table

| Condition | Demagnetizing AT | Cross-Magnetizing AT |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Brushes on GNA ($\theta_b = 0°$) | 0 At/pole | $\dfrac{Z I_a}{2P}$ At/pole |
| Brushes shifted by $\theta_b$ | $\dfrac{Z I_a \theta_b}{180° P}$ At/pole | $\dfrac{Z I_a}{2P}\left(1 - \dfrac{2\theta_b}{180°}\right)$ At/pole |
| Brushes shifted by 90° (theoretical max) | $\dfrac{Z I_a}{2P}$ At/pole | 0 At/pole |
| With compensating winding (ideal) | 0 | 0 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Flux Distribution in a 2-Pole DC Generator Under Load

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Main Field Winding Energized] --> B[Main Flux Phi_m Along Direct Axis D Axis]
    C[Armature Carries Load Current I_a] --> D[Armature MMF Along GNA]
    D --> E[Armature Flux Phi_a Cross Axis]
    B --> F[Vector Superposition at Air Gap]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Resultant Flux Phi_r Distorted and Shifted]
    G --> H[MNA Shifts From GNA by Angle Theta]
    H --> I[Brushes Must Be Shifted to New MNA for Sparkless Commutation]
    I --> J[Brush Shift Introduces Demagnetizing Component]
    J --> K[Net Flux Per Pole Reduced Phi_net Less Than Phi_m]
    K --> L[Terminal Voltage Drops Under Load]

    style A fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
