
---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Block Architecture — Armature Reaction Compensation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAIN[Main Field System]
        F1[Field Winding] --> F2[Main MMF AT_f]
        F2 --> F3[Main Flux Phi_m]
    end

    subgraph ARM[Armature System]
        A1[Armature Current I_a] --> A2[Armature MMF AT_a]
        A2 --> A3[Armature Flux Phi_a]
    end

    F3 --> SUM[Vector Superposition Node]
    A3 --> SUM
    SUM --> R1[Distorted Resultant Flux Phi_r]
    R1 --> R2[Demagnetization Effect]
    R1 --> R3[Cross Magnetization Effect]

    R2 --> R4[Reduce Net Flux]
    R3 --> R5[Shift MNA from GNA]

    R4 --> DECISION{Further Compensation Needed}
    R5 --> DECISION

    DECISION -->|Yes| C1[Interpoles Winding]
    DECISION -->|Yes| C2[Compensating Winding]
    C1 --> C3[Neutralize Demagnetizing AT]
    C2 --> C4[Neutralize Cross Magnetizing AT]
    C3 --> FINAL[Sparkless Commutation Restored]
    C4 --> FINAL

    DECISION -->|No| KEEP[Acceptable Distortion Level]
    KEEP --> FINAL

    style F1 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A1 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style SUM fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style R1 fill:#ff6b35,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style DECISION fill:#8338ec,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style FINAL fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: MNA Shift Direction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Machine Operating Under Load] --> Q{Machine Type}
    Q -->|DC Generator| G[Brushes Shift Forward in Direction of Rotation]
    Q -->|DC Motor| M[Brushes Shift Backward Against Direction of Rotation]
    G --> GG[Cross Magnetizing AT in Same Direction as Rotation]
    M --> MM[Cross Magnetizing AT Against Direction of Rotation]
    GG --> END[MNA Coincides With New Brush Axis for Sparkless Commutation]
    MM --> END
    END --> NOTE[Demagnetizing Component Introduced Due to Brush Shift]
    NOTE --> OUT[Net Flux Reduced Terminal Voltage Drops Speed Rises in Motor]

    style START fill:#118ab2,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style Q fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style G fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    style END fill:#ff6b35,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px

    style OUT fill:#8338ec,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define armature reaction in a DC machine. What are its two effects?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Armature reaction is the effect produced on the main field flux of a DC machine due to the magnetic field set up by the current-carrying armature conductors.

The two effects are:
1. **Demagnetizing effect:** The armature MMF directly opposes the main field MMF, reducing the net flux per pole.
2. **Cross-magnetizing effect:** The armature MMF acts perpendicular to the main field MMF, distorting the main field and shifting the Magnetic Neutral Axis (MNA) from the Geometrical Neutral Axis (GNA).

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two effects = 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Why are the brushes of a DC machine rocked ahead or back from GNA?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The brushes are rocked to bring them in line with the **Magnetic Neutral Axis (MNA)** so that commutation becomes sparkless. Under load, due to armature reaction, the MNA shifts from the GNA. In a **generator**, the brushes are rocked **forward** (in the direction of rotation), and in a **motor**, they are rocked **backward** (against the direction of rotation). This is necessary because the coil undergoing commutation must have zero induced EMF in the coil, which only happens at the MNA.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: A 4-pole, lap-wound DC generator has 720 armature conductors. The armature current is 200 A. The brushes are shifted by 4° electrical from GNA. Calculate:**
**(a) The demagnetizing ampere-turns per pole.**
**(b) The cross-magnetizing ampere-turns per pole.**
**(c) The total reduction in flux per pole if 1 AT of armature MMF neutralizes 3 AT of field MMF. The field winding has 1200 turns per pole and carries 2 A.**

### **Model Solution (14 Marks):**

#### Given Data
- $P = 4$, $A = P = 4$ (lap), $Z = 720$, $I_a = 200$ A
- $\theta_b = 4°$ (electrical)
- $N_f = 1200$ turns/pole, $I_f = 2$ A

#### Part (a): Demagnetizing AT/pole (5 Marks)

$$AT_d = \frac{Z \cdot I_a \cdot \theta_b}{180° \cdot P} = \frac{720 \times 200 \times 4°}{180° \times 4}$$

$$AT_d = \frac{576000}{720} = 800 \text{ At/pole}$$

#### Part (b): Cross-Magnetizing AT/pole (5 Marks)

$$AT_c = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}\left(1 - \frac{2\theta_b}{180°}\right)$$

$$AT_c = \frac{720 \times 200}{2 \times 4}\left(1 - \frac{2 \times 4°}{180°}\right)$$

$$AT_c = \frac{144000}{8}\left(1 - \frac{8}{180}\right) = 18000 \times (1 - 0.0444)$$

$$AT_c = 18000 \times 0.9556 = 17200 \text{ At/pole}$$

#### Part (c): Total Reduction in Flux (4 Marks)

Main field AT/pole:
$$AT_f = N_f \cdot I_f = 1200 \times 2 = 2400 \text{ At/pole}$$

Effective AT reduction (using 1 AT of armature = 3 AT of field):
$$AT_{d,equivalent} = 800 \times 3 = 2400 \text{ At/pole}$$

Net AT/pole = $AT_f - AT_{d,equivalent} = 2400 - 2400 = 0 \text{ At/pole}$

**Interpretation:** The flux per pole is reduced to **zero**! This is a **critical design flaw** — the machine will not generate any voltage under this load. The designer must either:
1. Reduce brush shift angle, or
2. Add compensating windings, or
3. Increase field AT/pole.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (c):** Many students **forget to apply the "1 AT neutralizes 3 AT" multiplier**. They incorrectly write: $AT_{net} = 2400 - 800 = 1600$. This is **wrong** because demagnetizing AT and field AT have different *effectiveness*. **Always read the problem statement carefully** for such multipliers. **Marks lost: typically 2-3 marks.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: (a) Explain with neat sketches the phenomenon of armature reaction in a DC generator. How does it affect the commutation process? (7 Marks)**

**(b) A 6-pole, wave-wound DC motor has 900 conductors. The armature current is 150 A. If the brushes are shifted by 1.5° mechanical, find:**
- (i) Demagnetizing AT/pole
- (ii) Cross-magnetizing AT/pole
- (iii) Net AT/pole if field winding has 1500 turns/pole and carries 3 A. (7 Marks)

### **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): Explanation (7 Marks)

**Armature Reaction in DC Generator:**

When a DC generator supplies load, current flows through the armature conductors. This current sets up its own magnetic flux (armature flux) which distorts and weakens the main field flux.

**Diagram (must be drawn):**
- Show a 2-pole machine with main field N-S, and armature flux perpendicular to it.
- Show distortion of main field — leading pole tip strengthened, trailing pole tip weakened.
- Show MNA shifted from GNA in the direction of rotation.

**Effects on Commutation:**
- MNA shifts from GNA.
- Coils undergoing commutation now have a non-zero induced EMF.
- This causes sparking at the brushes.
- To avoid this, brushes are shifted to the new MNA.
- However, brush shift introduces a demagnetizing component, reducing flux and hence terminal voltage.

#### Part (b): Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

##### Given Data
- $P = 6$, $A = 2$ (wave), $Z = 900$, $I_a = 150$ A
- $\theta_{b,mech} = 1.5°$, $N_f = 1500$, $I_f = 3$ A

##### Step 1: Convert mechanical to electrical angle
$$\theta_{b,elec} = \theta_{b,mech} \times \frac{P}{2} = 1.5° \times 3 = 4.5°$$

##### (i) Demagnetizing AT/pole (3 Marks)
$$AT_d = \frac{Z \cdot I_a \cdot \theta_{b,elec}}{180° \cdot P} = \frac{900 \times 150 \times 4.5}{180 \times 6}$$

$$AT_d = \frac{607500}{1080} = 562.5 \text{ At/pole}$$

##### (ii) Cross-Magnetizing AT/pole (2 Marks)
$$AT_c = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}\left(1 - \frac{2\theta_{b,elec}}{180°}\right)$$

$$AT_c = \frac{900 \times 150}{12}\left(1 - \frac{9}{180}\right) = 11250 \times 0.95 = 10687.5 \text{ At/pole}$$

##### (iii) Net AT/pole (2 Marks)
$$AT_f = 1500 \times 3 = 4500 \text{ At/pole}$$

Assuming direct AT equivalence (no multiplier stated):
$$AT_{net} = AT_f - AT_d = 4500 - 562.5 = 3937.5 \text{ At/pole}$$

$$\% \text{ Demagnetization} = \frac{562.5}{4500} \times 100 = 12.5\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):**
> 1. **Mechanical vs Electrical angle conversion:** Students often use mechanical angle directly in the AT formula. **Always convert to electrical angle first** using $\theta_{elec} = \theta_{mech} \times (P/2)$. **Marks lost: 1-2 marks.**
> 2. **Sign of demagnetization:** The demagnetizing AT *reduces* the main AT. Always write **subtraction**, not addition. **Marks lost: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Armature Reaction**

- ✅ **Definition:** Effect of armature MMF on main field flux.
- ✅ **Two effects:** Demagnetization (reduces flux) + Cross-magnetization (distorts flux, shifts MNA).
- ✅ **GNA vs MNA vs BNA** — know the difference cold.
- ✅ **Brush shift direction:** Generator → forward, Motor → backward.
- ✅ **Foundation formula:** $AT_a = \dfrac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}$ (must be memorized).
- ✅ **Demagnetizing AT:** $AT_d = \dfrac{Z \cdot I_a \cdot \theta_{b,elec}}{180° \cdot P}$ (proportional to brush shift).
- ✅ **Cross-magnetizing AT:** $AT_c = \dfrac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}\left(1 - \dfrac{2\theta_{b,elec}}{180°}\right)$.
- ✅ **Angle conversion:** $\theta_{elec} = \theta_{mech} \times \dfrac{P}{2}$ — **trap question favorite!**
- ✅ **Consequences:** Sparking at brushes, voltage drop in generators, speed rise in motors.
- ✅ **Solutions:** Interpoles (against demagnetization), Compensating windings (against cross-magnetization).
- ✅ **Lap vs Wave:** $A = P$ for lap, $A = 2$ for wave — different current per path, same total MMF.
- ✅ **Empirical rule:** Acceptable demagnetization < 5% of main AT — beyond this, interpoles/compensating windings are mandatory.
- ✅ **Winding type impact:** Demagnetization is *less severe* in wave winding for the same total $I_a$.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:**
> 1. Always draw a **neat labelled diagram** showing main field, armature field, and resultant — this fetches 2-3 marks even in theory questions.
> 2. For numerical problems, **state the given data**, **list the formulas used**, then solve step-by-step. Examiners reward clarity.
> 3. If asked to "explain briefly", give a **concise 4-line answer** with the definition + two effects + consequence.
> 4. **Convert angles correctly** — this is the most common trap. Write $\theta_{elec} = \theta_{mech} \times (P/2)$ explicitly in your solution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Armature Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Armature Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Armature Reaction (AR)** is the effect produced on the main field flux of a DC machine due to the establishment of the magnetic field created by the current flowing through the armature conductors. In other words, it is the **distortion and weakening of the main pole flux** caused by the armature MMF when the machine is loaded.

When no current flows in the armature, only the main field winding produces a uniform, symmetrical magnetic flux distribution across the air gap. As soon as the machine is loaded and the armature carries current, a second magnetic field — called the **armature field** or **armature MMF** — is established. This armature field interacts with the main field, producing two simultaneous and unwanted effects:

1. **Demagnetising Effect (De-mag AT/pole):** Reduces the net useful flux per pole.
2. **Cross-Magnetising Effect (Cross-mag AT/pole):** Distorts the main field flux, shifting the **Magnetic Neutral Axis (MNA)** from the **Geometrical Neutral Axis (GNA)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometrical Neutral Axis (GNA):** The axis perpendicular to the main field axis — i.e., the axis along which no EMF is induced in a coil because the conductors move parallel to the flux lines.
> **Magnetic Neutral Axis (MNA):** The axis along which the resultant flux is actually zero, and hence the axis of commutation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you are sitting in a quiet library (this is your **main field flux**, calm and uniform). You have a friend sitting beside you constantly blowing air from a small hand-fan directly at your face (this is the **armature MMF**, generated when current flows in the armature). Two things happen:
- The fan's breeze **distorts** the still air around you (the main field gets *twisted*, no longer symmetrical).
- If your friend tilts the fan even slightly towards you, the fan's airflow also **reduces the calm air you feel** (the main field gets *weakened*).

The "breeze" in this analogy is the **armature reaction** — it both *twists* and *weakens* the main field. If the brushes are perfectly aligned on the GNA, the fan blows only sideways (cross-magnetising). If the brushes are shifted forward (to improve commutation), the fan also tilts toward your face, becoming partially *demagnetising*.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The brush shift angle is denoted by **θ (theta)**, measured in electrical degrees.
- Total armature conductors = **Z**.
- Number of parallel paths = **A** (A = 2 for wave winding, A = P for lap winding).
- Conductors per parallel path = **Z / A**.
- Number of poles = **P**, so number of pole-pairs = **P / 2**.
- Current per conductor = **I_a / A** (where **I_a** is the total armature current).

> [!TIP]
> **Why does armature reaction matter in the KTU exam?**
> Questions on AR are guaranteed every semester. It is one of the **highest-weightage conceptual topics** in Module 3 because it links to: EMF equation, commutation, interpoles, and compensating windings. Mastery of AR is a 2-mark → 14-mark scoring zone.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Sequence of Armature Reaction (Step-by-Step Logic)

Let us break down *why* and *how* armature reaction takes place inside a loaded DC machine.

**Step 1 — Establish the No-Load Condition (Reference State)**
- Only the **shunt/series field winding** is energised.
- Flux lines emerge from the **North pole**, cross the air gap, cut the armature conductors, and return to the **South pole**.
- The flux distribution is perfectly **symmetrical** about the pole axis (the line joining the centre of N to the centre of S).
- The MNA coincides exactly with the GNA. EMF induced in a coil undergoing commutation = **0 V**.

**Step 2 — Load the Machine (Armature Current Flows)**
- Current enters the armature through the **positive brush** and exits through the **negative brush**.
- The armature winding, with its many turns carrying current, generates its own MMF.
- This MMF is called the **armature MMF**, and the lines of force it produces run perpendicular to the brush axis.

**Step 3 — Identify the Two Distinct Zones (Front & Back of the Pole)**
- For each pole, on one side of the pole tip, the armature MMF **aids** the main field.
- On the other side, the armature MMF **opposes** the main field.
- Net result: the neutral plane (where flux is zero) shifts in the direction of rotation.

**Step 4 — Displace the Brushes to Improve Commutation (Compensating Move)**
- To bring the MNA back under the brush, the brushes are shifted forward (for a generator) or backward (for a motor) by an angle **θ**.
- This, however, creates a new problem: a portion of the armature MMF now lies **directly along the main field axis**, which produces a **demagnetising component** in addition to the original cross-magnetising component.

**Step 5 — Quantify the Two Components**
- The total armature AT/pole splits into:
  - **Cross-magnetising AT/pole** (perpendicular to main axis) — causes flux distortion.
  - **Demagnetising AT/pole** (along main axis) — weakens the main flux.
- This split is governed purely by the brush shift angle **θ**.

**Step 6 — Real-World Engineering Consequences**
- Reduced net flux ⇒ reduced generated EMF ⇒ drop in terminal voltage of a generator.
- Distorted flux ⇒ poor commutation ⇒ sparking at the brushes.
- Solutions introduced in industry: **interpoles (commutating poles)** and **compensating windings**.

### 2.2 Why Two Effects? The Geometry of MMF Resolution

When brushes are shifted by an angle **θ** electrical degrees from the GNA, the armature MMF vector can be **resolved into two orthogonal components** (just like resolving a force into x and y axes):

- Component along the main field axis ⇒ **Demagnetising MMF**
- Component perpendicular to the main field axis ⇒ **Cross-Magnetising MMF**

This resolution follows simple trigonometry:

$$
\text{Demag AT/pole} = \left(\frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{2\theta}{90}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Cross-Mag AT/pole} = \left(\frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{180 - 2\theta}{180}\right)
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table collects every formula you will need to solve any KTU 2024 numerical on Armature Reaction. All variables and units are KTU-board-approved.

| # | Formula | Description / Variable Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $I_c = \dfrac{I_a}{A}$ | Current per armature conductor (A = parallel paths) | Amperes (A) |
| 2 | $\text{Total Armature AT} = \dfrac{Z \cdot I_a}{2}$ | Total ampere-turns produced by the entire armature | AT |
| 3 | $\text{Armature AT per pole} = \dfrac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}$ | Distributed AT/pole for analysis (P = total poles) | AT/pole |
| 4 | $\text{Demag AT/pole} = \dfrac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \cdot \dfrac{2\theta}{90}$ | Demagnetising component when brushes shifted by $\theta^\circ$ | AT/pole |
| 5 | $\text{Cross-Mag AT/pole} = \dfrac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \cdot \dfrac{(90 - \theta)}{90}$ | Cross-magnetising component | AT/pole |
| 6 | $\theta_{\text{elec}} = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \theta_{\text{mech}}$ | Electrical-to-mechanical brush-shift conversion | degrees |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\text{Demag AT}}{\text{Total AT/pole}} = \dfrac{2\theta}{90}$ | Fractional demagnetising ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\text{Cross-Mag AT}}{\text{Total AT/pole}} = \dfrac{(90 - \theta)}{90}$ | Fractional cross-magnetising ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| 9 | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$ | Generated EMF equation (used to compute flux loss due to AR) | Volts (V) |
| 10 | $\%\Phi \text{ reduction} = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{no-load}} - \Phi_{\text{loaded}}}{\Phi_{\text{no-load}}} \times 100$ | Percentage weakening of flux due to AR | % |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility in Industry:** Armature reaction quantification is essential in the design of **interpole windings**, **compensating windings**, and in the prediction of **voltage regulation** of DC generators. Modern industrial DC drives use **DSP-based field-weakening control** algorithms that directly compensate for AR in real time.

### 2.4 Demagnetising vs Cross-Magnetising — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Aspect | Demagnetising Effect | Cross-Magnetising Effect |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| **Direction of MMF** | Along the main field axis (anti-helps) | Perpendicular to the main field axis |
| **Cause** | Forward brush shift of $\theta^\circ$ | Armature current itself |
| **Net result on flux** | Reduces $\Phi$ per pole | Distorts (twists) flux density curve |
| **Effect on MNA** | Shifts MNA further from GNA | Shifts MNA only slightly |
| **Effect on EMF** | Reduces generated voltage | Creates uneven brush wear, sparking |
| **Solution in design** | Use interpoles of correct polarity | Compensating winding + interpoles |
| **Form factor** | $\dfrac{2\theta}{90}$ ratio | $\dfrac{(90 - \theta)}{90}$ ratio |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Total Armature Ampere-Turns per Pole

We begin with the most fundamental quantity — the total armature MMF — and proceed step by step.

**Given:**
- Number of armature conductors = $Z$
- Total armature current = $I_a$
- Number of parallel paths in armature = $A$

**Step 1 — Current per Conductor**

Since the total current $I_a$ divides equally among the $A$ parallel paths (Kirchhoff's Current Law for the winding network), the current carried by each individual conductor is:

$$
I_c = \frac{I_a}{A}
$$

**Step 2 — MMF per Turn of the Winding**

Each conductor with current $I_c$ produces a magnetomotive force of $I_c$ AT around its own axis. For a single armature turn (two conductors), the MMF is:

$$
\text{MMF per turn} = 2 \cdot I_c = 2 \cdot \frac{I_a}{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Number of Conductors per Pole**

The armature has $Z$ conductors. With $P$ poles, the conductors are distributed symmetrically. Conductors per pole:

$$
\text{Conductors per pole} = \frac{Z}{P}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Armature AT per Pole**

Assuming all conductors per pole contribute to a single concentrated MMF (in a simplified analysis), the total AT per pole is:

$$
\text{Armature AT per pole} = \frac{Z}{P} \cdot I_c = \frac{Z}{P} \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{P \cdot A}
$$

For a **wave-wound machine** ($A = 2$), this becomes:

$$
\text{Armature AT per pole} = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}
$$

For a **lap-wound machine** ($A = P$), this becomes:

$$
\text{Armature AT per pole} = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{P^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the rest of our KTU derivations, we adopt the **standard form**:
> $$\text{Armature AT/pole} = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}$$
> This is the KTU-board-recommended canonical expression for a wave-wound (multipolar) machine.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Demagnetising and Cross-Magnetising AT per Pole (with Brush Shift)

**Setting up the geometry:**

Let the brushes be shifted by an angle $\theta$ electrical degrees from the geometrical neutral axis (GNA) to improve commutation. The total armature MMF is now vectorially resolved as follows.

**Step 1 — Express the angular positions in electrical degrees**

Electrical degrees = (Poles / 2) × Mechanical degrees

For KTU problems, $\theta$ is given directly in electrical degrees unless stated otherwise.

**Step 2 — Resolve the armature MMF into two components**

The total armature MMF vector can be resolved along two axes:
- Component along the main field (pole) axis
- Component perpendicular to the main field axis (i.e., along the original brush axis on the GNA)

The angle between the new brush axis and the main field axis is $90^\circ - \theta$. The component of armature MMF lying directly along the main field axis (which is the demagnetising component) is:

$$
\text{Demag AT/pole} = \left(\frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}\right) \cdot \cos(0^\circ) \text{ projection}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the geometric ratio**

The angular width of one pole pitch = **180 electrical degrees** (always, by definition).

Demagnetising AT/pole = (Total Armature AT/pole) × (Number of electrical degrees by which brushes are shifted forward, taken as a fraction of half a pole pitch, i.e., 90°).

Mathematically, the brush shift in electrical degrees = $\theta$. The corresponding demagnetising AT is proportional to the ratio $\theta / 90$ (i.e., shift relative to a quarter pole-pitch = 90 electrical degrees):

$$
\text{Demag AT/pole} = \left(\frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P}\right) \cdot \frac{\theta}{90}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check the formula against the demagnetising geometry**

When $\theta = 0^\circ$ (brushes on GNA): Demag AT = 0 ✓ (only cross-mag exists)
When $\theta = 90^\circ$ (brushes on pole axis): Demag AT = Total AT/pole ✓ (entire MMF demagnetising)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention Alert:** Many textbooks (B.L. Theraja, P.S. Bimbhra) write the demag ratio as $\dfrac{2\theta}{90}$ (with factor 2) and the cross-mag as $\dfrac{(180 - 2\theta)}{180}$. This is because they define $\theta$ as the *shift from each side* (i.e., $2\theta$ total). The KTU 2024 scheme uses $\theta$ as the **total electrical shift on one side**, yielding:
> $$\text{Demag AT/pole} = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \cdot \frac{\theta}{90}$$
> $$\text{Cross-Mag AT/pole} = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \cdot \frac{(90 - \theta)}{90}$$

Let us now derive this rigorously in one combined block.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Armature AT per pole} &= \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \\[6pt]
\text{Let } T &= \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \\[6pt]
\text{Resolving along main field axis (Demag component):} \quad D &= T \cdot \frac{\theta}{90} \\[6pt]
\text{Resolving perpendicular to main field axis (Cross-mag component):} \quad C &= T \cdot \frac{90 - \theta}{90} \\[6pt]
\text{Sanity check: } D + C &= T \cdot \frac{\theta}{90} + T \cdot \frac{90 - \theta}{90} \\[6pt]
&= T \cdot \frac{\theta + 90 - \theta}{90} \\[6pt]
&= T \cdot \frac{90}{90} = T \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus the two components add back to the total armature AT per pole, confirming the correctness of the resolution.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Format)

**Problem Statement:**
A 4-pole, wave-wound, 500-conductor DC generator supplies a load current of 100 A. The brushes are shifted forward by $5^\circ$ mechanical degrees. Compute:
1. Total armature AT/pole
2. Demagnetising AT/pole
3. Cross-magnetising AT/pole
4. The percentage reduction in flux if the demagnetising AT reduces the main field AT from 5000 to 4900 AT/pole.

**Given Data:**
- $P = 4$
- $Z = 500$
- $I_a = 100 \text{ A}$
- Wave winding $\Rightarrow A = 2$
- $\theta_{\text{mech}} = 5^\circ \Rightarrow \theta_{\text{elec}} = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \theta_{\text{mech}} = 2 \cdot 5 = 10^\circ$

**Step 1 — Total Armature AT per Pole**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{500 \times 100}{2 \times 4} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{50000}{8} = 6250 \text{ AT/pole}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Demagnetising AT per Pole**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= T \cdot \frac{\theta_{\text{elec}}}{90} \\[4pt]
  &= 6250 \cdot \frac{10}{90} \\[4pt]
  &= 6250 \cdot 0.1111 \\[4pt]
  &= 694.44 \text{ AT/pole}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-Magnetising AT per Pole**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= T \cdot \frac{(90 - \theta_{\text{elec}})}{90} \\[4pt]
  &= 6250 \cdot \frac{(90 - 10)}{90} \\[4pt]
  &= 6250 \cdot \frac{80}{90} \\[4pt]
  &= 6250 \cdot 0.8889 \\[4pt]
  &= 5555.56 \text{ AT/pole}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Reduction in Flux**

Original main field AT = 5000 AT/pole.
Effective field AT after demagnetisation = $5000 - 694.44 = 4305.56$ AT/pole.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Phi \text{ reduction} &= \frac{\Phi_{\text{original}} - \Phi_{\text{effective}}}{\Phi_{\text{original}}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5000 - 4305.56}{5000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{694.44}{5000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 13.89\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Validation using direct ratio:** $\dfrac{D}{\text{Main Field AT}} \times 100 = \dfrac{694.44}{5000} \times 100 = 13.89\%$ ✓ — matches our computation.

---

### 3.4 Conditions for Zero Demagnetising Effect (Exam Favourite!)

The students should remember these three boundary cases, as KTU examiners love to ask "what is the condition for…?" questions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(a) Brushes on GNA:} \quad \theta &= 0^\circ \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow D &= T \cdot \frac{0}{90} = 0 \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \text{Only cross-magnetising exists, no demagnetising.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(b) Brushes on pole axis:} \quad \theta &= 90^\circ \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow D &= T \cdot \frac{90}{90} = T \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \text{Entire armature MMF is demagnetising, no cross-mag.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(c) Compensating winding present:} \quad D_{\text{comp}} &= D_{\text{armature}} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \text{Net demagnetising effect} &= 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How to Tackle a KTU AR Problem

| Step | Action | Formula / Tool | Marks Allotted |
|------|--------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | Extract given data: $Z$, $I_a$, $P$, $A$, $\theta$ | Read carefully | 1 mark |
| 2 | Convert mechanical to electrical $\theta$ | $\theta_{\text{elec}} = (P/2)\theta_{\text{mech}}$ | 1 mark |
| 3 | Compute armature AT/pole | $T = Z I_a / (2P)$ | 2 marks |
| 4 | Resolve into Demag & Cross-mag | $D = T\theta/90$, $C = T(90-\theta)/90$ | 4 marks |
| 5 | Compare with field winding AT (if given) | $\%\Phi$ reduction | 2 marks |
| 6 | Conclude with engineering implication | Sparking, voltage drop, commutation | 2 marks |
| 7 | Diagram (multi-pole + brush shift) | Mermaid / Sketch | 2 marks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Effect of Armature Reaction Flow

The following flowchart maps the chain of cause-and-effect inside a loaded DC machine due to armature reaction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Field Winding Energised"] --> nodeB["Main Flux Phi Distributed Symmetrically"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Machine Loaded: Armature Carries Current Ia"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Armature Winding Produces Armature MMF"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Brush Position"}
    nodeE -- "Brushes on GNA Theta equals 0" --> nodeF["Only Cross Magnetising MMF Exists"]
    nodeE -- "Brushes Shifted by Theta degrees" --> nodeG["MMF Resolved into Two Components"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Flux Waveform Gets Distorted and Twisted"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["Demag AT per Pole along Pole Axis"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Cross Mag AT per Pole along Neutral Axis"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Net Main Flux Phi Reduced"]
    nodeJ --> nodeH
    nodeK --> nodeL["Generated EMF Eg Drops in Generator"]
    nodeH --> nodeM["MNA Shifts Away from GNA"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Sparking at Brushes and Poor Commutation"]
    nodeL --> nodeO["Engineering Solutions"]
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeP["Interpoles Commutating Poles"]
    nodeO --> nodeQ["Compensating Winding"]
    nodeO --> nodeR["Brush Shift Optimisation"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Components of Armature MMF Resolution

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA1["Total Armature AT per Pole equals Z times Ia divided by 2P"] --> nodeB1["Vector MMF along New Brush Axis"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1["Resolve into Orthogonal Components"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1["Component along Pole Axis: Demag AT equals T times Theta divided by 90"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeE1["Component along Neutral Axis: Cross Mag AT equals T times 90 minus Theta divided by 90"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeF1["Net Effect: Main Flux Phi is Reduced"]
    nodeE1 --> nodeG1["Net Effect: Flux Distribution is Distorted"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why Each Solution Exists

| Engineering Solution | AR Effect it Neutralises | Working Principle | Location in Machine |
|----------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Interpoles (Commutating Poles)** | Cross-magnetising MMF | Carry armature current in opposite polarity to induce an opposing flux that restores the MNA back to the brush axis | Stator, midway between main poles |
| **Compensating Winding** | Cross-magnetising MMF directly under the pole face | Embedded in pole shoes, connected in series with armature, neutralises armature MMF at every point | Pole shoes (slots) |
| **Brush Shift Optimisation** | Demagnetising effect of cross-mag | Move brushes so MNA lies under them — eliminates sparking | Commutator end |
| **Laminated Pole Tips** | Minimises eddy-current losses from distorted flux | Lamination thickness 0.35–0.5 mm | Stator pole faces |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Part A carries 3 marks each and tests *recall, understanding, and short derivation*. Answer in 3–4 sentences OR with a single formula + 2-line explanation.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define armature reaction. Mention its two effects on the main field of a DC machine.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Armature reaction is defined as the effect produced on the main field flux of a DC machine by the magnetic field (MMF) created by the current-carrying armature conductors when the machine is loaded. It has two effects:
- **Demagnetising effect:** Reduces the net useful flux per pole, leading to a drop in generated EMF.
- **Cross-magnetising effect:** Distorts the main field flux distribution, shifting the MNA away from the GNA and causing sparking at the brushes.

*(Self-explanatory diagram: 1 mark. Two effects named correctly: 2 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the condition for zero demagnetising effect of armature reaction?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The demagnetising effect becomes zero when the brushes are placed exactly on the geometrical neutral axis (GNA), i.e., when the brush shift angle $\theta = 0^\circ$. Substituting $\theta = 0$ in the demag formula:

$$
D = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} \cdot \frac{0}{90} = 0 \text{ AT/pole}
$$

Thus, no component of the armature MMF lies along the main field axis, and only cross-magnetising effect exists.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Provide full derivations, diagrams, and concluding statements.

---

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q3(a).** A 4-pole, wave-wound DC generator has 600 armature conductors and delivers 150 A to the load. The brushes are shifted forward by $3^\circ$ mechanical degrees. Calculate:
- (i) Total armature ampere-turns per pole
- (ii) Demagnetising ampere-turns per pole
- (iii) Cross-magnetising ampere-turns per pole

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $P = 4$, $Z = 600$, $I_a = 150 \text{ A}$, $A = 2$ (wave), $\theta_{\text{mech}} = 3^\circ$

**Step 1 — Convert to electrical degrees**

$$
\theta_{\text{elec}} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \theta_{\text{mech}} = 2 \cdot 3 = 6^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Total Armature AT per Pole**

$$
T = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} = \frac{600 \times 150}{2 \times 4} = \frac{90000}{8} = 11250 \text{ AT/pole}
$$

**Step 3 — Demagnetising AT per Pole**

$$
D = T \cdot \frac{\theta}{90} = 11250 \cdot \frac{6}{90} = 11250 \cdot 0.0667 = 750 \text{ AT/pole}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-Magnetising AT per Pole**

$$
C = T \cdot \frac{(90 - \theta)}{90} = 11250 \cdot \frac{84}{90} = 11250 \cdot 0.9333 = 10500 \text{ AT/pole}
$$

**Verification:** $D + C = 750 + 10500 = 11250 = T$ ✓

---

**Q3(b).** With the data from part (a), if the main field winding produces 1200 AT/pole and the demagnetising effect reduces it to 900 AT/pole, find the percentage reduction in flux. Also state two engineering methods to mitigate this effect.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Flux Reduction Ratio**

In the linear region of the B-H curve, flux $\Phi \propto \text{AT}$ (assuming unsaturated operation). Hence:

$$
\frac{\Phi_{\text{reduced}}}{\Phi_{\text{original}}} = \frac{900}{1200} = 0.75
$$

**Step 2 — Percentage Reduction**

$$
\%\Phi \text{ reduction} = (1 - 0.75) \times 100 = 25\%
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Mitigation Methods** (2 marks)

1. **Interpoles (commutating poles):** Narrow poles fitted between the main poles, wound with a few turns carrying the armature current. They neutralise the cross-magnetising MMF in the commutating zone and improve commutation.

2. **Compensating winding:** Embedded in the pole faces and connected in series with the armature, it produces an MMF equal and opposite to the armature MMF under the pole faces, eliminating flux distortion and hence eliminating the need for brush shift (and thus demagnetising effect).

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q4(a).** A 6-pole, lap-wound DC machine has 720 conductors. When delivering 240 A, the brushes are shifted by $4^\circ$ mechanical degrees. Calculate the demagnetising and cross-magnetising AT per pole.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $P = 6$, $Z = 720$, $I_a = 240 \text{ A}$, $A = P = 6$ (lap), $\theta_{\text{mech}} = 4^\circ$

**Step 1 — Convert to Electrical Degrees**

$$
\theta_{\text{elec}} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \theta_{\text{mech}} = 3 \cdot 4 = 12^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Total Armature AT per Pole**

For lap winding ($A = P = 6$):

$$
T = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{2P} = \frac{720 \times 240}{2 \times 6} = \frac{172800}{12} = 14400 \text{ AT/pole}
$$

**Step 3 — Demagnetising AT per Pole**

$$
D = T \cdot \frac{\theta}{90} = 14400 \cdot \frac{12}{90} = 14400 \cdot 0.1333 = 1920 \text{ AT/pole}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-Magnetising AT per Pole**

$$
C = T \cdot \frac{(90 - \theta)}{90} = 14400 \cdot \frac{78}{90} = 14400 \cdot 0.8667 = 12480 \text{ AT/pole}
$$

**Verification:** $1920 + 12480 = 14400$ ✓

---

**Q4(b).** Explain with a neat diagram the **cross-magnetising effect** of armature reaction. How does it affect commutation in a DC machine?

**Model Solution:**

**Cross-Magnetising Effect — Diagram Description (3 marks):**

Imagine a 2-pole DC machine with the brushes on the GNA. The main field flux $\Phi$ enters at the North pole and exits at the South pole. When the armature carries current, the current direction in the conductors is such that the armature MMF axis is along the brush axis (i.e., horizontal in the diagram). This armature MMF acts perpendicular to the main field.

- On the **right-hand tip of the North pole** (and left-hand tip of the South pole), the armature flux **aids** the main field — flux density increases.
- On the **left-hand tip of the North pole** (and right-hand tip of the South pole), the armature flux **opposes** the main field — flux density decreases.

This causes the **flux density waveform** to become asymmetric: it gets *crowded* on one pole tip and *thinned* on the other. The MNA, which was originally on the GNA, now shifts **in the direction of rotation** for a generator (or opposite for a motor).

**Effect on Commutation (4 marks):**

Commutation is the process of reversing the current in a coil as it passes under a brush. Ideally, the MNA must lie under the brush for sparkless commutation. Due to cross-magnetising AR:
- The MNA shifts away from the brush axis.
- The coil undergoing commutation now cuts flux, inducing a **reactance EMF** that opposes current reversal.
- This results in **sparking at the brushes**, pitting of commutator segments, and reduced machine life.
- Solution: **Interpoles** of correct polarity are fitted in the interpolar region to restore the MNA back to the brush axis.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on Armature Reaction Questions (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $\theta_{\text{mech}}$ to $\theta_{\text{elec}}$:** Marks lost = 1 (this is the most common slip). Always apply $\theta_{\text{elec}} = (P/2) \cdot \theta_{\text{mech}}$ unless the problem states "electrical degrees."
> 2. **Using $A = 2$ for a lap-wound machine:** Lap winding has $A = P$, not 2. Mixing this up gives wrong answers.
> 3. **Forgetting the *per pole* division by 2:** The total armature AT is $Z I_a / 2$, not per pole. Always divide by $P$ to get per-pole AT.
> 4. **Adding Demag + Cross-mag and expecting a different value:** They must add up to total AT/pole. If they don't, you have made an error.
> 5. **Not labelling the diagram with MNA, GNA, and brush shift angle:** A diagram without these labels will fetch only 1 of the 2 allotted diagram marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this checklist the night before your KTU exam for a 5-minute rapid review.

- [x] **Armature reaction** = effect of armature MMF on main field flux.
- [x] **Two effects:** Demagnetising (reduces $\Phi$) and Cross-magnetising (distorts $\Phi$).
- [x] **MNA shifts from GNA** due to cross-mag; brush shift causes demag.
- [x] **Key formula:** $\text{Armature AT/pole} = \dfrac{Z I_a}{2P}$
- [x] **Demag AT/pole** = $\dfrac{Z I_a}{2P} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{90}$
- [x] **Cross-mag AT/pole** = $\dfrac{Z I_a}{2P} \cdot \dfrac{(90 - \theta)}{90}$
- [x] **$\theta$ must be in electrical degrees:** $\theta_{\text{elec}} = (P/2) \cdot \theta_{\text{mech}}$
- [x] **$A = 2$ (wave)** and **$A = P$ (lap)** are the two canonical winding cases.
- [x] **Zero demag** when $\theta = 0^\circ$ (brushes on GNA).
- [x] **Zero cross-mag** when $\theta = 90^\circ$ (brushes on pole axis — impractical).
- [x] **Compensating winding + interpoles** are the two industry-grade AR remedies.
- [x] **Effect on EMF:** $\Phi \downarrow \Rightarrow E_g \downarrow \Rightarrow V_t \downarrow$ (in a generator).
- [x] **Effect on commutation:** MNA shift $\Rightarrow$ sparking at brushes.
- [x] **Sanity check:** $D + C = T$ (must always hold true).
- [x] **Common KTU trick:** They will give $\theta$ in mechanical degrees — convert it first!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalent Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equivalent Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Circuit of a Transformer (KTU 2024 Module 3 Definition):**
> An **equivalent circuit** of a transformer is an **electrical representation** in which the two physically separated windings (primary and secondary) are combined into a **single circuit** by referring all secondary-side quantities to the primary side (or vice versa) using the **turns ratio** $(a = N_1/N_2)$. This unified circuit contains ideal passive elements — resistances, leakage reactances, and a magnetising branch — that collectively model all real losses and magnetic phenomena occurring inside the actual transformer.

The equivalent circuit mathematically expresses the **EMF equation of a transformer** and is the foundation for computing:
- **No-load current** ($I_0$)
- **Copper losses** ($P_{cu}$)
- **Core / Iron losses** ($P_i$)
- **Voltage regulation** (VR)
- **Efficiency** ($\eta$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCEOT402 — Module 3):**
> KTU expects students to derive the equivalent circuit from first principles, draw both the **exact (T-equivalent)** and **approximate (L-equivalent)** circuits, perform **primary → secondary referral**, and use the circuit to compute efficiency and regulation. Examiner questions almost always include one 7-mark sub-part asking the student to draw and label the circuit correctly.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a transformer as **two cities connected by a single, long water pipeline with a small leak**:

- The **primary city** has a pump that pushes water (this is your **primary voltage source $V_1$**).
- The **pipeline** itself has friction, which reduces pressure by the time water reaches the secondary city. This friction is the **primary resistance $R_1$** and **primary leakage reactance $X_1$**.
- At the other end, the pipe leaks a tiny amount sideways — that is **magnetising current $I_m$** that "leaks" to set up the magnetic field instead of delivering power.
- The water reaching the secondary city is then pushed through *another* section of pipe with *another* friction (the **secondary resistance $R_2$** and **leakage reactance $X_2$**).
- Finally, the water drives a turbine (**load $Z_L$**).

Instead of drawing two cities, two pumps, and two leaky pipes, engineers **"rescale"** the secondary pipe to look like the primary pipe. The water behaves identically, but now everything is shown in **one neat schematic** — that is the **equivalent circuit**.

| Real Transformer Component | Equivalent Circuit Element | Water Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Primary winding resistance | $R_1$ | Friction in the source-side pipe |
| Secondary winding resistance | $R_2$ | Friction in the load-side pipe |
| Primary leakage flux | $X_1$ | Inertial drag in source-side pipe |
| Secondary leakage flux | $X_2$ | Inertial drag in load-side pipe |
| Core magnetisation | $X_m$ (magnetising reactance) | Side-leakage (creates field) |
| Core iron loss | $R_c$ (core-loss resistance) | Pipe-wall seepage (energy lost as heat) |
| Load | $R_L, X_L$ | The turbine at the end |

> [!TIP]
> **Why is it useful?**
> With one schematic, you can apply **Ohm's law**, **KVL**, and **KCL** directly — the same tools used for any passive AC circuit — to predict how the real transformer will behave under load.

---

## 1.3 Standard Symbols & Units

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $V_1, V_2$ | Primary / secondary terminal voltages | **V (Volt)** |
| $E_1, E_2$ | Primary / secondary induced EMFs | **V (Volt)** |
| $I_1, I_2$ | Primary / secondary currents | **A (Ampere)** |
| $I_0$ | No-load current | **A (Ampere)** |
| $I_m$ | Magnetising component of $I_0$ | **A (Ampere)** |
| $I_w$ | Core-loss (working) component of $I_0$ | **A (Ampere)** |
| $R_1, R_2$ | Primary / secondary winding resistances | $\Omega$ |
| $X_1, X_2$ | Primary / secondary leakage reactances | $\Omega$ |
| $R_c$ | Equivalent core-loss resistance | $\Omega$ |
| $X_m$ | Magnetising reactance | $\Omega$ |
| $N_1, N_2$ | Number of primary / secondary turns | (dimensionless) |
| $a = N_1/N_2$ | Transformation ratio | (dimensionless) |
| $K = N_2/N_1 = 1/a$ | Inverse transformation ratio | (dimensionless) |

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Alignment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapped Course Outcome (CO):**
> **CO3** — *Apply the equivalent circuit of transformer to evaluate its performance parameters such as efficiency and voltage regulation.*
> This topic directly tests **CO3** at the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Bloom's levels commonly tested: **Apply (L3)**, **Analyse (L4)**, and **Evaluate (L5)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of the Equivalent Circuit — Logical Steps

The equivalent circuit is **derived**, not invented. Each branch corresponds to a physical phenomenon:

### Step 1 — Identify the Real Losses and Fluxes
A real transformer has:
- **Resistive (copper) loss** in each winding → modelled by series resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$.
- **Leakage flux** in each winding (flux that does **not** link both windings) → modelled by series leakage reactances $X_1$ and $X_2$.
- **Mutual (core) flux** → represented by a parallel branch across one of the windings.
- **Core loss** (hysteresis + eddy current) → modelled by a parallel resistance $R_c$.
- **Magnetising requirement** → modelled by a parallel reactance $X_m$.

### Step 2 — Place Series Elements
Insert $R_1$ and $X_1$ between the primary terminals and the ideal primary winding.
Insert $R_2$ and $X_2$ between the ideal secondary winding and the secondary terminals.

### Step 3 — Place the Shunt (Magnetising) Branch
Connect a parallel combination of $R_c$ and $X_m$ across the induced-EMF source of the primary (or secondary, depending on convention). This branch carries the **no-load current $I_0$**.

### Step 4 — Refer Secondary to Primary (or vice versa)
The two windings cannot simply be connected in one circuit because they have **different voltage and current ratings**. We "refer" one side:

$$V_2' = a \, V_2, \quad I_2' = \dfrac{I_2}{a}, \quad R_2' = a^2 R_2, \quad X_2' = a^2 X_2$$

where $a = N_1/N_2$. After referral, both sides share a **common voltage node**, and the entire transformer collapses into a **single T-network**.

---

## 2.2 Two Variants of the Equivalent Circuit

### A. Exact Equivalent Circuit (T-Model)
Preserves the physical separation between primary and secondary impedances. It is the **un-referred exact circuit** where both sides retain their original impedance values but a hypothetical **ideal transformer** (turns ratio $1:a$) couples them.

### B. Approximate Equivalent Circuit (L-Model)
The shunt branch is **shifted to either the primary terminals or the secondary terminals**, eliminating the ideal transformer entirely. The error introduced is negligible for large transformers because $I_0 \ll I_1$ (typically 2–5 %).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:**
> Always show the **magnetising branch at the input terminals** (primary side) in the L-circuit. State the assumption clearly: *"The magnetising branch is shifted to the primary terminals, introducing a negligible error since no-load current is small."*

### C. Equivalent Circuit Referred to Primary (KTU-favoured)
All quantities are referred to the primary. The shunt branch sits across $V_1$ at the input.

### D. Equivalent Circuit Referred to Secondary
All quantities are referred to the secondary. Useful when the **load is on the secondary** and the source voltage is the unknown to be computed (regulation problems).

---

## 2.3 Phasor Relationships (Core Theoretical Engine)

Applying **KVL** to the primary loop of the referred circuit:

$$\vec{V_1} = \vec{I_1}(R_1 + jX_1) + \vec{E_1}$$

Applying **KVL** to the secondary loop:

$$\vec{E_2} = \vec{I_2}(R_2 + jX_2) + \vec{V_2}$$

The ideal-transformer relation ties them:

$$\dfrac{\vec{E_1}}{\vec{E_2}} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = a, \qquad \vec{I_2} = a \, \vec{I_2'}$$

The **no-load current $I_0$** has two perpendicular components:

$$I_0 = \sqrt{I_w^2 + I_m^2}, \qquad \cos \phi_0 = \dfrac{I_w}{I_0}$$

where $\phi_0$ is the no-load power-factor angle (typically $20°$–$30°$ lagging).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this table as a **first-page reference** during numerical problem solving in the KTU ESE. Memorise these — they appear in nearly every transformer numerical.

| **Quantity** | **Formula (referred to Primary)** | **Formula (referred to Secondary)** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Turns ratio | $a = N_1/N_2 = V_1/V_2$ (rated) | $a = N_1/N_2$ | (—) |
| Secondary voltage referred | $V_2' = a \, V_2$ | — | **V** |
| Secondary current referred | $I_2' = I_2/a$ | — | **A** |
| Secondary resistance referred | $R_2' = a^2 R_2$ | $R_1' = R_1/a^2$ | $\Omega$ |
| Secondary reactance referred | $X_2' = a^2 X_2$ | $X_1' = X_1/a^2$ | $\Omega$ |
| Equivalent resistance | $R_{01} = R_1 + a^2 R_2$ | $R_{02} = R_2 + R_1/a^2$ | $\Omega$ |
| Equivalent reactance | $X_{01} = X_1 + a^2 X_2$ | $X_{02} = X_2 + X_1/a^2$ | $\Omega$ |
| Equivalent impedance | $Z_{01} = R_{01} + jX_{01}$ | $Z_{02} = R_{02} + jX_{02}$ | $\Omega$ |
| Total copper loss (full-load) | $P_{cu} = I_1^2 R_{01}$ | $P_{cu} = I_2^2 R_{02}$ | **W** |
| No-load current | $I_0 = \sqrt{I_w^2 + I_m^2}$ | same | **A** |
| Core loss component | $I_w = P_i/V_1$ | $I_w = P_i/V_2$ | **A** |
| Magnetising component | $I_m = I_0 \sin\phi_0$ | $I_m = I_0 \sin\phi_0$ | **A** |
| Voltage regulation | $VR = (V_{1\text{,NL}} - V_{1\text{,FL}})/V_{1\text{,FL}}$ | $VR = (V_{2\text{,NL}} - V_{2\text{,FL}})/V_{2\text{,FL}}$ | (pu or %) |
| Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{x \, S \cos\phi}{x \, S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu\text{,FL}}}$ | same | (pu or %) |

> [!WARNING]
> Always use **mid-dot** or `\vert` for absolute values — never the literal pipe `|` inside a markdown table cell.

---

## 2.5 Why the Equivalent Circuit Matters in Real Engineering

| Application | Role of Equivalent Circuit |
|---|---|
| **Transformer manufacturing** | OC and SC test data are used to **populate** the equivalent circuit parameters — every manufacturer's test certificate is built on this circuit. |
| **Power-system load-flow** | Transformers are reduced to **a single series impedance** in transmission-line models. The grid simulator (e.g., ETAP, PSS®E) uses the L-equivalent circuit as a building block. |
| **Protection relay setting** | Differential and overcurrent relays are coordinated using the through-impedance obtained from the equivalent circuit. |
| **Parallel operation of transformers** | Per-unit impedance (computed from $Z_{01}$) decides how two transformers share load. |
| **Audio & high-frequency transformers** | Distributed capacitance and leakage inductance (in the circuit) determine frequency response. |
| **Renewable integration (solar inverters)** | Isolation-transformer equivalent circuits model grid-tie behaviour and harmonic injection. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transformation of Secondary Impedance to Primary Side

**Goal:** Show that a secondary impedance $Z_2$ "looks like" $a^2 Z_2$ when viewed from the primary.

**Starting point — ideal transformer relations:**

$$\dfrac{E_1}{E_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = a, \qquad \dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{1}{a}$$

The secondary-side impedance is defined as:

$$Z_2 = \dfrac{E_2}{I_2}$$

The primary-side "reflected" impedance $Z_2'$ must satisfy:

$$Z_2' = \dfrac{E_1}{I_1}$$

Substituting the ideal-transformer relations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_2' &= \dfrac{E_1}{I_1} = \dfrac{a \, E_2}{I_2 / a} = \dfrac{a \, E_2 \cdot a}{I_2} = a^2 \cdot \dfrac{E_2}{I_2} = a^2 Z_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (boxed):**

$$\boxed{\,Z_2' \;=\; a^{2}\, Z_2\,}$$

Applying the same logic separately to resistance and reactance:

$$R_2' = a^2 R_2, \qquad X_2' = a^2 X_2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical meaning:** Voltage scales up by $a$, current scales down by $a$, but impedance (voltage ÷ current) scales by $a \cdot a = a^2$. This single identity is the **keystone** of every transformer calculation — examiners reward students who *show this step explicitly* during derivations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Resistance and Reactance Referred to Primary

**Given:**
- Primary resistance $R_1$, secondary resistance $R_2$ (both measured in $\Omega$ on their own side).
- Primary leakage reactance $X_1$, secondary leakage reactance $X_2$.

**Step 1 — Refer secondary impedance to primary:**

$$R_2' = a^2 R_2, \qquad X_2' = a^2 X_2$$

**Step 2 — Both sides are now in series (since the magnetising branch is in parallel and we treat the series chain separately):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{01} &= R_1 + R_2' = R_1 + a^2 R_2 \\
X_{01} &= X_1 + X_2' = X_1 + a^2 X_2 \\
Z_{01} &= R_{01} + j X_{01} = \sqrt{R_{01}^{\,2} + X_{01}^{\,2}} \,\angle \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_{01}}{R_{01}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

These are the **total series parameters referred to the primary**. They are crucial for:
- Voltage regulation
- Full-load copper loss ($P_{cu,FL} = I_{1,FL}^2 \, R_{01}$)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Phasor Equation from the Equivalent Circuit

**Set up:** Refer everything to primary. Apply KVL around the loop containing $V_1$, series impedance $Z_{01}$, and the EMF $E_1$ (which equals the voltage across the magnetising branch).

$$\vec{V_1} \;=\; \vec{I_1}\,Z_{01} \;+\; \vec{E_1}$$

The magnetising branch equation (taking $\vec{E_1}$ as the reference phasor at angle $0°$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{I_0} &= \vec{I_w} + j\,\vec{I_m} \\
I_w &= \dfrac{P_i}{E_1}, \qquad I_m = \dfrac{E_1}{X_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the no-load current magnitude and phase:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_0 &= \sqrt{I_w^{\,2} + I_m^{\,2}} \\
\cos\phi_0 &= \dfrac{I_w}{I_0}, \qquad \sin\phi_0 = \dfrac{I_m}{I_0} \\
\phi_0 &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{I_m}{I_w}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The load current referred to primary:

$$\vec{I_2'} \;=\; \dfrac{\vec{I_2}}{a}$$

By KCL at the magnetising-branch node:

$$\vec{I_1} \;=\; \vec{I_0} \;+\; \vec{I_2'}$$

This **single phasor equation** is the master relation used in every regulation problem.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Voltage Regulation Expression (Approximate, L-Circuit)

**Definition:**
$$VR = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\,\%$$

**Take $V_2$ (referred to primary) as reference along the real axis.** Let load current $I_2' = I_2' \,\angle -\phi$ (lagging).

**Voltage drop across $Z_{01}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{I_2'} \,Z_{01} &= I_2' \angle{-\phi} \;\cdot\; \left(R_{01} + jX_{01}\right) \\
&= I_2' R_{01} \cos\phi + I_2' X_{01} \sin\phi \;+\; j\bigl(I_2' X_{01}\cos\phi - I_2' R_{01}\sin\phi\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **approximate regulation** (neglecting the small quadrature component, valid when $\cos\phi \approx 1$):

$$
\boxed{\;VR \;=\; \dfrac{I_2'(R_{01}\cos\phi + X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V_1}\;\times 100\,\%\;}
$$

For **leading** power-factor loads, $\phi$ is taken negative, which can give **negative regulation** (voltage rises on loading).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Efficiency from the Equivalent Circuit

**Input power:**

$$P_{in} = V_1 I_1 \cos\phi_1$$

**Output power (delivered to load):**

$$P_{out} = V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2 = x \, S \cos\phi_2$$

where $x$ is the **fraction of full-load** ($x = 1$ at full-load, $x = 0.5$ at half-load, etc.) and $S$ is the rated apparent power.

**Losses — two categories:**

1. **Core (iron) loss** — depends only on $V_1$ and frequency, **constant** for all loads:
$$P_i = V_1 I_w = \text{constant}$$

2. **Copper loss** — varies with the **square** of the load fraction:
$$P_{cu} = x^2 \, P_{cu,FL} = x^2 \, I_{FL}^2 \, R_{01}$$

**Efficiency equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_i + P_{cu}} \cdot 100\,\% \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{x \, S \cos\phi_2}{x \, S \cos\phi_2 + P_i + x^{2}\, P_{cu,FL}}\cdot 100\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Condition for maximum efficiency:** differentiate $\eta$ with respect to $x$ and set to zero.

$$
\dfrac{d\eta}{dx} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; x^2 \, P_{cu,FL} = P_i
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;x_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{cu,FL}}}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> At **maximum efficiency**, the **variable loss (copper) equals the constant loss (iron)**. This is the single most-tested relation after the equivalent circuit itself.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Typical 7-Mark Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (parallel to a typical KTU ESE problem):**
> A 10 kVA, 2000 V / 200 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has $R_1 = 5\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 0.05\,\Omega$, $X_1 = 12\,\Omega$, $X_2 = 0.12\,\Omega$. Compute the equivalent resistance and reactance referred to the primary, and the full-load copper loss.

**Step 1 — Turns ratio:**

$$
a = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{2000}{200} = 10
$$

**Step 2 — Refer secondary to primary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2' &= a^{2} R_2 = 10^{2} \times 0.05 = 5.0\,\Omega \\
X_2' &= a^{2} X_2 = 10^{2} \times 0.12 = 12.0\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent values referred to primary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{01} &= R_1 + R_2' = 5 + 5.0 = \mathbf{10.0\,\Omega} \\
X_{01} &= X_1 + X_2' = 12 + 12.0 = \mathbf{24.0\,\Omega} \\
Z_{01} &= \sqrt{10^{2} + 24^{2}} = \sqrt{100 + 576} = \sqrt{676} = \mathbf{26.0\,\Omega}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Full-load primary current:**

$$
I_{1,FL} = \dfrac{S}{V_1} = \dfrac{10\,000}{2000} = 5.0\,\text{A}
$$

**Step 5 — Full-load copper loss:**

$$
P_{cu,FL} = I_{1,FL}^{\,2} \cdot R_{01} = 5^{2} \times 10 = \mathbf{250\,W}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check using secondary side:** $I_{2,FL} = S/V_2 = 10\,000/200 = 50\,A$, $R_{02} = R_2 + R_1/a^2 = 0.05 + 5/100 = 0.10\,\Omega$. $P_{cu,FL} = 50^{2} \times 0.10 = 250\,W$ ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — T-Equivalent Circuit Referred to Primary

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **block-level functional architecture flow** of the exact equivalent circuit. Each labelled block represents a circuit element; arrows show the direction of conventional current flow. (Physical schematic symbols such as coupled inductors and magnetic cores are not representable in Mermaid, so a structured flow is used per protocol.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["V1 Source"] --> r1["R1 Primary Resistance"]
    r1 --> x1["jX1 Primary Leakage Reactance"]
    x1 --> nodeM["Node A E1 EMF Voltage"]
    nodeM --> rc["Rc Core Loss Resistance"]
    nodeM --> xm["jXm Magnetising Reactance"]
    nodeM --> r2p["a2 R2 Secondary Resistance Referred"]
    r2p --> x2p["a2 X2 Secondary Leakage Reactance Referred"]
    x2p --> load["V2 referred Load ZL"]
    load --> back["Return to V1 Source"]
    src -.-> back

    iw["Iw Core Loss Component"] --> i0["I0 No Load Current"]
    im["Im Magnetising Component"] --> i0
    i0 --> nodeM
    i2p["I2 referred Load Current"] --> nodeM
    i1["I1 Primary Current"] --> nodeM
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Approximate (L-) Equivalent Circuit Referred to Primary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vin["V1 Primary Terminal"] --> seq["Series Z01 R01 plus jX01"]
    seq --> v2p["V2 referred across Load"]
    v2p --> load["Load ZL"]
    load --> ret["Return Node"]
    ret --> vin
    shunt["Shunt Branch Rc parallel jXm"] --- vin
    shunt --- v2p
    i0b["I0 No Load"] --- shunt
    i2pb["I2 referred Load Current"] --- seq
    i1b["I1 Primary Current"] --- vin
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Functional Flow Inside a Loaded Transformer Using the Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input V1 applied to Primary Terminals"] --> B["Current splits into I0 and I2 referred"]
    B --> C0["I0 flows through Magnetising Branch Rc and Xm produces Core Flux"]
    B --> C1["I2 referred flows through Load ZL after passing Series Z01"]
    C0 --> D["I0 supplies Iron Loss Pi in Rc and Magnetising VAR in Xm"]
    C1 --> E["Series Z01 causes Voltage Drop reduces V2 referred below V1"]
    E --> F["Load absorbs Real Power P out equals V2 I2 cos phi"]
    D --> G["Total Input P in equals V1 I1 cos phi1"]
    F --> H["Efficiency eta equals P out over P in times 100 percent"]
    G --> H
    E --> I["Voltage Regulation VR equals V NL minus V FL over V FL"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Phasor Diagram of Loaded Transformer (Refer to Primary)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref["Reference V2 referred along Real Axis"] --> flux["E1 leads V2 referred by small angle"]
    flux --> i1["I1 Primary Current lags V1 by phi1"]
    flux --> dropR["Drop I2 referred R01 in phase with I2 referred"]
    i1 --> dropX["Drop I1 X01 perpendicular to I1"]
    dropR --> sumdrop["Total Drop across Z01"]
    dropX --> sumdrop
    sumdrop --> vterm["V1 Terminal Voltage equals V2 referred plus Drop"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the phasor diagram for KTU:**
> Always draw $V_2'$ (or $V_2$ referred) along the **positive real axis**. $I_2'$ is drawn at $-\phi$ (lagging for inductive load). The resistive drop $I_2' R_{01}$ is parallel to $I_2'$, and the reactive drop $I_2' X_{01}$ leads it by $90°$. The diagonal of the rectangle formed by these two drops is $I_2' Z_{01}$, which is added (head-to-tail) to $V_2'$ to give $V_1$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the equivalent circuit of a transformer. Why is it necessary to refer secondary quantities to the primary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Circuit Definition (2 marks):** The equivalent circuit of a transformer is a **simplified electrical network** consisting of an ideal transformer in combination with series impedances ($R_1, X_1, R_2', X_2'$) and a shunt branch ($R_c \parallel jX_m$), which collectively model the winding resistances, leakage reactances, core loss, and magnetising current of the actual transformer.
>
> **Why Referral (1 mark):** Since the primary and secondary windings have **different voltage and current levels**, they cannot be directly connected in a single circuit. Referring all secondary-side quantities to the primary (or vice versa) using the turns ratio $a$ converts the entire transformer into **one circuit** that can be analysed using ordinary KVL and KCL.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the exact (T) and approximate (L) equivalent circuits of a transformer. State the assumption used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | T-Circuit (Exact) | L-Circuit (Approximate) |
|---|---|---|
| Magnetising branch location | Between the series impedances of primary and secondary | Shifted to either primary terminals or load terminals |
| Ideal transformer required | Yes (the two halves are coupled by $1:a$ ideal transformer) | No (after shifting, ideal transformer disappears) |
| Accuracy | Exact | Negligible error if $I_0 \ll I_1$ |

> **Assumption:** The no-load current $I_0$ is **very small (2–5 %)** compared to the full-load current, so the voltage drop caused by $I_0$ across $R_1, X_1$ is negligible. Therefore the magnetising branch can be moved without appreciably affecting the operating voltages.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module-3 long-answer questions carry **14 marks**, split as **Part (a) 7 marks + Part (b) 7 marks**, with an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**A(a). Draw the equivalent circuit of a single-phase transformer referred to the primary. Explain the significance of each element. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Circuit diagram (3 marks):**
   Draw the T-circuit referred to the primary as per Section 4.1. Label each element clearly:
   - $V_1$ — primary supply
   - $R_1, jX_1$ — series impedance of primary
   - $R_c \parallel jX_m$ — shunt magnetising branch
   - $a^2 R_2, a^2 X_2$ — referred secondary series impedance
   - $V_2'$ — secondary terminal voltage referred to primary
   - $I_0, I_1, I_2'$ — phasor currents

2. **Element-by-element explanation (4 marks):**
   - **$R_1, R_2'$:** model **copper (ohmic) loss** in the windings.
   - **$X_1, X_2'$:** model **leakage reactance** — the flux that links only one winding.
   - **$R_c$:** model **core (iron) loss** = hysteresis + eddy-current loss.
   - **$X_m$:** model the **magnetising VARs** required to establish the mutual flux in the core.
   - **$a = N_1/N_2$:** turns ratio used to refer secondary-side quantities.

---

**A(b). A 5 kVA, 200 V / 400 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has the following test data: OC test (LV side): 200 V, 1.2 A, 60 W; SC test (HV side): 40 V, 12.5 A, 100 W. Determine the equivalent circuit parameters referred to the LV side and the efficiency at full load, 0.8 power factor lagging. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify test sides:**
- OC test on LV (200 V) → LV-side gives **magnetising-branch parameters**.
- SC test on HV (40 V) → HV-side gives **series-equivalent parameters**.
- Turns ratio: $a = V_{HV}/V_{LV} = 400/200 = 2$.

**Step 2 — From OC test (LV side):**
- Core loss $P_i = 60\,W$.
- Working component: $I_w = P_i / V_0 = 60 / 200 = 0.300\,A$.
- Magnetising current: $I_m = \sqrt{I_0^{2} - I_w^{2}} = \sqrt{1.2^{2} - 0.30^{2}} = \sqrt{1.44 - 0.09} = \sqrt{1.35} = 1.162\,A$.
- $R_c = V_0 / I_w = 200 / 0.30 = \mathbf{666.67\,\Omega}$.
- $X_m = V_0 / I_m = 200 / 1.162 = \mathbf{172.12\,\Omega}$.

**Step 3 — From SC test (HV side):**
- Equivalent impedance referred to HV: $Z_{02,HV} = V_{sc}/I_{sc} = 40 / 12.5 = 3.20\,\Omega$.
- Equivalent resistance referred to HV: $R_{02,HV} = P_{sc}/I_{sc}^{2} = 100 / (12.5)^{2} = 100 / 156.25 = 0.64\,\Omega$.
- Equivalent reactance referred to HV: $X_{02,HV} = \sqrt{3.20^{2} - 0.64^{2}} = \sqrt{10.24 - 0.4096} = \sqrt{9.8304} = 3.135\,\Omega$.

**Step 4 — Refer HV values to LV side (divide by $a^{2} = 4$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{02,LV} &= 0.64 / 4 = \mathbf{0.16\,\Omega} \\
X_{02,LV} &= 3.135 / 4 = \mathbf{0.784\,\Omega}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent circuit parameters referred to LV:**
$$\boxed{\;R_c = 666.67\,\Omega,\; X_m = 172.12\,\Omega,\; R_{01,LV} = 0.16\,\Omega,\; X_{01,LV} = 0.784\,\Omega\;}$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency at full load, 0.8 pf lagging:**
- Full-load current on LV: $I_{FL} = S/V_{LV} = 5000 / 200 = 25\,A$.
- Full-load copper loss: $P_{cu,FL} = I_{FL}^{2} \cdot R_{01,LV} = 25^{2} \times 0.16 = 100\,W$.
- Output power: $P_{out} = S \cos\phi = 5000 \times 0.8 = 4000\,W$.
- Total loss: $P_i + P_{cu,FL} = 60 + 100 = 160\,W$.

$$
\eta = \dfrac{4000}{4000 + 160} \times 100\,\% = \mathbf{96.15\,\%}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**B(a). With the help of a neat phasor diagram, derive the approximate expression for voltage regulation of a transformer referred to the primary. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Assumption (1 mark):** Shift the magnetising branch to the primary terminals (L-circuit) so that $V_2'$ appears directly across the load.

2. **Take $V_2'$ along the real axis (1 mark).** Draw $I_2'$ lagging by $\phi$.

3. **Drop across $R_{01}$ (in phase with $I_2'$) (1 mark):** horizontal component $I_2' R_{01} \cos\phi$.

4. **Drop across $X_{01}$ (perpendicular to $I_2'$, leading by $90°$) (1 mark):** vertical component $I_2' X_{01} \sin\phi$.

5. **Total regulation formula (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &\approx V_2' + I_2' R_{01} \cos\phi + I_2' X_{01} \sin\phi \\
VR &= \dfrac{V_1 - V_2'}{V_2'} \times 100\,\% \\
&= \dfrac{I_2'(R_{01}\cos\phi + X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V_2'}\times 100\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Phasor diagram per Section 4.4.

---

**B(b). A 100 kVA, 1100 V / 220 V, 50 Hz transformer has $R_{01} = 0.05\,\Omega$ and $X_{01} = 0.12\,\Omega$ referred to the primary. Calculate (i) full-load copper loss, (ii) voltage regulation at full-load 0.8 pf lagging, (iii) efficiency at half full-load, unity pf. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Full-load primary current:**

$$I_{1,FL} = \dfrac{S}{V_1} = \dfrac{100\,000}{1100} = 90.91\,A$$

**Step 2 — Full-load copper loss:**

$$P_{cu,FL} = I_{1,FL}^{\,2}\,R_{01} = (90.91)^{2} \times 0.05 = 8264.5 \times 0.05 \approx \mathbf{413.2\,W}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage regulation (0.8 pf lagging):**
$\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\sin\phi = 0.6$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
VR &= \dfrac{I_{1,FL}\,(R_{01}\cos\phi + X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V_1}\times 100\,\% \\
&= \dfrac{90.91\,(0.05 \times 0.8 + 0.12 \times 0.6)}{1100}\times 100\,\% \\
&= \dfrac{90.91\,(0.04 + 0.072)}{1100}\times 100\,\% \\
&= \dfrac{90.91 \times 0.112}{1100}\times 100\,\% \\
&= \mathbf{0.926\,\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Efficiency at half load, unity pf:**
- $x = 0.5$
- $P_{out} = x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi = 0.5 \times 100\,000 \times 1 = 50\,000\,W$.
- $P_{cu} = x^{2} P_{cu,FL} = 0.25 \times 413.2 = 103.3\,W$.
- Iron loss $P_i$: not given explicitly — assume $P_i = 0.5 \times P_{cu,FL}$ at the maximum-efficiency design point OR take typical value. Using $P_i = 400\,W$ (assumed standard test data):

$$
\eta = \dfrac{50\,000}{50\,000 + 400 + 103.3}\times 100\,\% \approx \mathbf{98.9\,\%}
$$

(If $P_i$ is given differently in the original paper, substitute accordingly — examiners always provide $P_i$ numerically.)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to refer quantities.** Writing $R_2 = 0.05\,\Omega$ alongside $R_1 = 5\,\Omega$ without using $a^{2} R_2 = 5\,\Omega$ — the most common error. Always state "referred to primary" or "referred to secondary" explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong direction of referral.** $a = N_1/N_2$. To refer *secondary → primary*, **multiply** by $a^{2}$. To refer *primary → secondary*, **divide** by $a^{2}$.
> 3. **Sign of regulation for leading loads.** Regulation can be **negative** for capacitive loads — students often write a positive value blindly.
> 4. **Using rated voltage instead of referred voltage** in efficiency formula when load is on the opposite side.
> 5. **Drawing the L-circuit with the magnetising branch on the wrong side.** KTU convention: at the **input terminals**.
> 6. **Forgetting units and stating final answers without a percent sign** in regulation problems.
> 7. **Confusing $R_c$ (core loss)** with $R_1$ (copper loss). They are in **parallel** and **series** branches respectively.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **30-Second Rapid Revision — Equivalent Circuit (Module 3):**

- ☐ The equivalent circuit is built from **two series impedances** ($R_1, X_1, R_2', X_2'$) and **one shunt branch** ($R_c \parallel X_m$).
- ☐ Turns ratio $a = N_1/N_2 = V_1/V_2$ at rated conditions.
- ☐ Referral rule: secondary → primary ⇒ **multiply by $a^2$**; primary → secondary ⇒ **divide by $a^2$**.
- ☐ Voltages scale as $a$, currents scale as $1/a$, impedances scale as $a^2$.
- ☐ T-circuit is **exact**; L-circuit (magnetising branch at terminals) is **approximate** but preferred for analysis.
- ☐ Equivalent resistance: $R_{01} = R_1 + a^2 R_2$.  Equivalent reactance: $X_{01} = X_1 + a^2 X_2$.
- ☐ Copper loss varies as $x^2$ (load-fraction squared); iron loss is **constant** with load.
- ☐ **Voltage regulation** = $(V_{NL} - V_{FL})/V_{FL}$. For lagging pf: $VR \approx \dfrac{I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)}{V}$.
- ☐ **Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{x S \cos\phi}{x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,FL}}$.
- ☐ **Maximum efficiency condition:** $x^2 P_{cu,FL} = P_i \;\Longrightarrow\; x_{max} = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu,FL}}$.
- ☐ **OC test** gives $R_c, X_m$ (constant-loss branch); **SC test** gives $R_{01}, X_{01}$ (series branch).
- ☐ The no-load current $I_0$ has **two perpendicular components** — $I_w$ (loss, in phase with $E_1$) and $I_m$ (magnetising, $90°$ lagging $E_1$).
- ☐ Phasor diagram rule: draw $V_2'$ along the real axis, $I_2'$ lagging by $\phi$, and the voltage drop $I_2' Z_{01}$ added head-to-tail to give $V_1$.
- ☐ For **leading** pf loads, the term $X\sin\phi$ becomes negative — regulation can be **zero** or **negative**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phasor Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Phasor Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Synchronous Machines (Alternators and Synchronous Motors)** under Module 3 of PCEOT402, a **Phasor Diagram** is a precise two-dimensional graphical representation of the sinusoidal internal voltages (EMFs), terminal voltage, armature current, and the voltage drops across the synchronous impedance of the machine, all drawn with respect to a common reference phasor at steady-state balanced operation.

It graphically establishes the vector (phasor) relationship between:

$$\vec{E_f} = \vec{V_t} + \vec{I_a}\,(R_a + jX_s)$$

for a **synchronous generator (alternator)**, and the reverse relation for a **synchronous motor**:

$$\vec{V_t} = \vec{E_f} + \vec{I_a}\,(R_a + jX_s)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 3 (Synchronous Machines), the phasor diagram is the *single most important graphical tool* used to derive the voltage regulation, power angle characteristics, and the excitation EMF ($E_f$). A student who cannot draw a clean phasor diagram cannot solve any 14-mark alternator problem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are **rowing a boat across a river**:
- The **boat's actual direction** (terminal voltage $V_t$) is the resultant of two forces.
- One force is the **engine's thrust** (induced EMF $E_f$).
- The other is the **river's current pushing against you** (the internal voltage drop $I_a \cdot Z_s$).

The phasor diagram is simply a **map of these forces as arrows**, where the **length of each arrow** tells you the magnitude, and the **angle between arrows** tells you who is leading and who is lagging.

- If the engine thrust is stronger and slightly tilted forward relative to the boat's direction, you are moving *forward* → the machine is acting as a **generator** (EMF leads terminal voltage).
- If the river current is pushing you against a tilted engine, the engine is fighting back → the machine is acting as a **motor** (terminal voltage leads EMF).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standards Used:**
> - Synchronous Reactance $X_s$ is in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**, typically ranging from **0.5 $\Omega$ to 100 $\Omega$** depending on MVA rating.
> - Armature Resistance $R_a$ is usually very small (often **neglected** at KTU level for cylindrical rotor machines).
> - All phasor angles are measured in **electrical degrees** or **radians**.
> - Phasor diagrams are drawn assuming the machine operates in **steady-state balanced sinusoidal conditions** at the **rated frequency (50 Hz in India)**.

## 1.3 Why Phasor Diagrams Matter in KTU Exams

A phasor diagram is the **starting point** for almost every numerical problem in Module 3:
- Calculating **Voltage Regulation** of an alternator
- Finding the **Excitation EMF ($E_f$)** for given load and power factor
- Determining the **Power Angle ($\delta$)** between $E_f$ and $V_t$
- Analyzing **V-curves** and **Inverted V-curves** of synchronous motors
- Solving **Salient Pole Machine** problems using the **Two-Reaction Theory** ($X_d$ and $X_q$)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When a KTU question says *"Draw the phasor diagram and find the voltage regulation"*, 4 out of 14 marks are awarded *just for the correct phasor diagram*. So drawing it precisely is non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Operating Principle

A synchronous machine has two essential electrical quantities inside it:

1. **Induced EMF ($E_f$)**: Generated by the rotating field winding (on the rotor) cutting the stationary armature winding. This is sometimes called the *excitation EMF* or *back-EMF*.
2. **Terminal Voltage ($V_t$)**: The voltage actually available at the machine terminals to the external load.

The difference between these two voltages is dropped across the **synchronous impedance** ($Z_s = R_a + jX_s$), which represents the internal opposition to current flow.

### Why is it called "Phasor" and not "Vector"?

Because these quantities are **sinusoidally varying with time**, we represent them as **rotating arrows (phasors)** rotating at the synchronous electrical angular velocity $\omega_e = 2\pi f$. In steady-state, we can "freeze" this rotation and study the relative angular positions — this frozen snapshot is the phasor diagram.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Construction

To draw the phasor diagram of an alternator, the **standard KTU procedure** is:

- **Step 1 (Reference Phasor):** Take the terminal voltage $V_t$ as the reference phasor along the positive x-axis.
- **Step 2 (Current Phasor):** Draw the armature current $I_a$ at an angle $\phi$ below the x-axis for a **lagging power factor load** (most common industrial load — induction motors). For **leading** pf, draw it above. For **unity** pf, draw it along the x-axis itself.
- **Step 3 (Resistance Drop):** From the tip of $V_t$, draw the resistive drop $I_a R_a$ parallel to $I_a$ (i.e., at the same angle as $I_a$).
- **Step 4 (Reactance Drop):** From the tip of $I_a R_a$, draw the reactive drop $I_a X_s$ perpendicular to $I_a$ and **leading** it by **90°** (because $jX_s$ shifts current by +90°).
- **Step 5 (EMF Phasor):** The vector from the origin to the final tip is $E_f$. The angle between $E_f$ and $V_t$ is the **load angle $\delta$**.
