KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define voltage regulation of an alternator. Why is it necessary to pre-determine it? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Voltage regulation of a synchronous alternator is defined as the percentage rise in terminal voltage when full load is removed at constant speed and field excitation. $$\text{VR \%} = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$ **Necessity of predetermination** (1 mark each point): 1. Synchronous alternators are large MVA-rated machines (e.g., 100 MVA, 250 MVA) — full-load testing in the laboratory is **impractical** due to the enormous power required and the difficulty in arranging real loading. 2. The heat produced during full-load testing would damage insulation and require massive cooling arrangements. 3. Hence regulation must be computed indirectly from **open-circuit and short-circuit tests**, using the **EMF method** or the **MMF method**. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Why is the EMF method called "pessimistic" and the MMF method called "optimistic"? **Model Answer (3 marks):** **EMF Method — Pessimistic** (1.5 marks): In the EMF method, the synchronous reactance $X_s$ is calculated from $Z_s = V_{oc}/I_{sc}$ on the **air-gap line (unsaturated)**. This gives an $X_s$ value **larger than the actual saturated synchronous reactance**. When used to compute $E_0$, it gives a higher value than the true EMF → **overestimates the regulation** for lagging pf loads. Hence it is a pessimistic estimate (worst-case). **MMF Method — Optimistic** (1.5 marks): In the MMF method, the **leakage reactance drop $I_a X_L$ is completely neglected**. This means the predicted $E_0$ is **smaller than the true value** → **underestimates the regulation** for lagging pf loads. Hence it is an optimistic estimate (best-case). > **Conclusion**: The actual regulation lies between these two values. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** > Students commonly lose marks here because of: > 1. **Mixing up $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$ as line vs. phase values** — KTU examiners explicitly test this. Always convert line to phase (divide by $\sqrt{3}$ for star-connected machines). > 2. **Forgetting the sign of $\sin\phi$** for leading vs. lagging power factors — the formula inside the square root changes sign. > 3. **Not stating the answer in percentage** — KTU specifically demands "voltage regulation in percentage" in the final line. > 4. **Skipping the phasor diagram** — for a 14-mark question, KTU marks at least **2 marks** for a correctly drawn and labeled phasor diagram. > 5. **Confusing EMF and MMF method formulas** — practice writing both side-by-side. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): EMF Method Comprehensive** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** A 3-phase, star-connected, 1500 kVA, 11000 V alternator has an armature resistance of $0.6\;\Omega$ per phase. The following data are obtained from tests: | Field Current $I_f$ (A) | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Open-circuit voltage (line, V) | 3500 | 7000 | 9800 | 12000 | 13400 | 14200 | | Short-circuit current (A) | 100 | 200 | 300 | 400 | 500 | 600 | Calculate the synchronous impedance and synchronous reactance per phase. Hence determine the **full-load voltage regulation at 0.8 power factor lagging** using the EMF method. #### **Sub-part (a) — 7 marks: Determine $Z_s$ and $X_s$** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert line voltage to phase voltage** (1 mark): For star connection, $$V_{oc,ph} = \frac{V_{oc,line}}{\sqrt{3}}$$ | $I_f$ (A) | $V_{oc,line}$ (V) | $V_{oc,ph}$ (V) | $I_{sc}$ (A) | $Z_s = V_{oc,ph}/I_{sc}$ ($\Omega$) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 10 | 3500 | 2020.7 | 100 | 20.21 | | 20 | 7000 | 4041.5 | 200 | 20.21 | | 30 | 9800 | 5658.0 | 300 | 18.86 | > [!NOTE] > Note that in the **linear region** (first two rows), $Z_s$ is **constant** at $\approx 20.21\;\Omega$. In the saturated region (third row), $Z_s$ drops because the OCC flattens due to saturation. KTU accepts the **unsaturated (linear) value** of $Z_s$ for the EMF method. $$\boxed{Z_s = 20.21 \;\Omega} \quad \text{(unsaturated, 1 mark)}$$ **Step 2 — Compute synchronous reactance** (1 mark): $$X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2} = \sqrt{20.21^2 - 0.6^2} = \sqrt{408.44 - 0.36} = \sqrt{408.08} = 20.20 \;\Omega$$ $$\boxed{X_s = 20.20 \;\Omega} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **Step 3 — Phasor diagram** (2 marks): ``` E_0 (induced EMF, leading V by δ) /| / | / | j I_a X_s = j × 78.7 × 20.20 / | /δ___| V______I_a R_a________ V (11000/sqrt(3) = 6350.85 V) \ \ φ = 36.87° \_________________ I_a (lagging) ``` **Step 4 — Calculate full-load current and phase voltage** (1 mark): $$\begin{aligned} I_{a,fl} &= \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000} = 78.73 \text{ A} \\ V_{ph} &= \frac{11000}{\sqrt{3}} = 6350.85 \text{ V} \\ \cos\phi &= 0.8, \quad \sin\phi = 0.6 \end{aligned}$$ #### **Sub-part (b) — 7 marks: Apply EMF Equation and Compute Regulation** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Substitute into EMF equation for lagging pf** (2 marks): $$\begin{aligned} E_0 &= \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(6350.85 \times 0.8 + 78.73 \times 0.6)^2 + (6350.85 \times 0.6 + 78.73 \times 20.20)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(5080.68 + 47.24)^2 + (3810.51 + 1590.35)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(5127.92)^2 + (5400.86)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{26{,}295{,}682 + 29{,}169{,}290} \\ &= \sqrt{55{,}464{,}972} \\ &= 7447.5 \text{ V (phase)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Voltage Regulation** (2 marks): $$\text{VR \%} = \frac{E_0 - V_{ph}}{V_{ph}} \times 100 = \frac{7447.5 - 6350.85}{6350.85} \times 100$$ $$\text{VR \%} = \frac{1096.65}{6350.85} \times 100 = +17.27\%$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation** (2 marks): The **positive 17.27%** regulation indicates that when full load at 0.8 lagging power factor is removed (keeping field current constant), the terminal voltage will rise by 17.27% of the rated value. This is the **pessimistic upper bound**; the true regulation is lower. **Step 4 — Summary Table**: | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Rated kVA | 1500 kVA | | Rated voltage (line) | 11000 V | | Rated current $I_a$ | 78.73 A | | Synchronous impedance $Z_s$ | 20.21 $\Omega$ | | Synchronous reactance $X_s$ | 20.20 $\Omega$ | | Induced EMF $E_0$ (phase) | 7447.5 V | | Induced EMF $E_0$ (line) | 12900 V (approx.) | | **Voltage Regulation** | **+17.27%** (pessimistic) | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): MMF Method Comprehensive** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** For the same 1500 kVA, 11000 V alternator as in Question A, the field current required to produce rated open-circuit voltage (6350.85 V per phase, on the OCC) is $F_r = 38$ A. The field current required to circulate full-load short-circuit current ($I_{sc} = 78.73$ A) is $F_a = 17$ A. Compute the **full-load voltage regulation at 0.8 pf lagging** using the **MMF method**. #### **Sub-part (a) — 7 marks: Resultant Field Current and Phasor Diagram** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Vector sum of $F_r$ and $F_a$** for lagging pf (3 marks): The angle between $F_r$ (along $V$) and $F_a$ (perpendicular to $V$ for cylindrical rotor, lagging) is $90^\circ + \phi$. $$\begin{aligned} F_f^2 &= F_r^2 + F_a^2 - 2 F_r F_a \sin\phi \\ &= 38^2 + 17^2 - 2 \times 38 \times 17 \times 0.6 \\ &= 1444 + 289 - 775.2 \\ &= 957.8 \\ \therefore \quad F_f &= \sqrt{957.8} = 30.95 \text{ A} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{F_f = 30.95 \text{ A}} \quad \text{(1 mark for boxed answer)}$$ **Step 2 — Phasor diagram** (2 marks): ``` F_f = 30.95 A (resultant) /| / | / | F_a = 17 A (armature reaction) / | /90°+φ=126.87° F_r = 38 A___________________ (along V direction) ``` **Step 3 — Identification of steps** (1 mark): The resultant field current $F_f$ is found by vector addition of $F_r$ (field current that produces rated voltage on no-load) and $F_a$ (field current that produces full-load current on short-circuit), with the appropriate angle of $(90^\circ + \phi)$ for lagging pf. #### **Sub-part (b) — 7 marks: Read $E_0$ from OCC and Compute Regulation** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Read $E_0$ from OCC at $F_f = 30.95$ A** (3 marks): From the OCC data (interpolating between $I_f = 30$ A → $V_{oc,line} = 9800$ V and $I_f = 40$ A → $V_{oc,line} = 12000$ V): $$\begin{aligned} V_{oc,line}(30.95) &\approx 9800 + \frac{(30.95 - 30)}{(40 - 30)} \times (12000 - 9800) \\ &= 9800 + \frac{0.95}{10} \times 2200 \\ &= 9800 + 209 = 10009 \text{ V (line)} \end{aligned}$$ $$\therefore E_{0,ph} = \frac{10009}{\sqrt{3}} = 5778.5 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{E_{0,ph} = 5778.5 \text{ V}} \quad \text{(1 mark for boxed answer)}$$ **Step 2 — Voltage Regulation** (2 marks): $$\text{VR \%} = \frac{E_0 - V_{ph}}{V_{ph}} \times 100 = \frac{5778.5 - 6350.85}{6350.85} \times 100$$ $$\text{VR \%} = \frac{-572.35}{6350.85} \times 100 = -9.01\%$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation and Comparison with EMF Method** (2 marks): The **negative regulation** of $-9.01\%$ means the MMF method predicts that the terminal voltage would actually **drop** when the load is removed — this is the **optimistic (lower-bound)** estimate. In contrast, the EMF method gave $+17.27\%$. The **true regulation** lies **between** these two values, i.e., $$-9.01\% \le \text{VR}_{\text{true}} \le +17.27\%$$ A more accurate value can be obtained using the **Potier ZPF method** (covered in the next module). **Summary Table**: | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | $F_r$ | 38 A | | $F_a$ | 17 A | | Resultant $F_f$ (lag pf) | 30.95 A | | $E_0$ (phase) from OCC | 5778.5 V | | $E_0$ (line) from OCC | 10009 V | | **Voltage Regulation** | **−9.01%** (optimistic) | --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist** - [ ] **Voltage Regulation** = percentage rise in terminal voltage when load is removed at constant excitation and speed. - [ ] **EMF Method** (Synchronous Impedance Method) — uses $Z_s = V_{oc}/I_{sc}$ from **air-gap line** (linear, unsaturated region). - [ ] **MMF Method** — replaces armature reaction by an **equivalent field current $F_a$**, vectorially added to $F_r$. - [ ] EMF Method is **PESSIMISTIC** (overestimates regulation for lagging pf) — it inflates $X_s$. - [ ] MMF Method is **OPTIMISTIC** (underestimates regulation for lagging pf) — it ignores leakage reactance. - [ ] **True regulation** lies **between** EMF-method and MMF-method results. - [ ] EMF Equation (lag): $E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$ - [ ] EMF Equation (lead): $E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi - I_a X_s)^2}$ - [ ] MMF Equation (lag): $F_f = \sqrt{F_r^2 + F_a^2 - 2 F_r F_a \sin\phi}$ - [ ] MMF Equation (lead): $F_f = \sqrt{F_r^2 + F_a^2 + 2 F_r F_a \sin\phi}$ - [ ] MMF Equation (unity): $F_f = \sqrt{F_r^2 + F_a^2}$ - [ ] Always convert **line to phase** quantities (divide by $\sqrt{3}$ for star-connected machines). - [ ] For lagging pf → $E_0 > V$ → **positive regulation** (terminal voltage rises on load removal). - [ ] For unity pf → small positive regulation. - [ ] For leading pf → $E_0 < V$ possible → **negative regulation** (terminal voltage falls on load removal). - [ ] Phasor diagram is **mandatory** for 14-mark questions — KTU awards 2 marks specifically for it. - [ ] Both methods require **OCC** and **SCC** — no-load test and sustained short-circuit test. - [ ] The **EMF method** is also called the **Miles Walker method** in some textbooks. - [ ] The **MMF method** is also called the **Rothert method** in some textbooks. > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Scheme — Examiner Tip:** Always show **both EMF and MMF method answers** in a comparison question (if asked). This automatically scores you **bonus marks** because you demonstrate mastery of both approaches. Also remember to quote the final regulation value **with the percentage sign** and a **positive/negative sign** to indicate leading/lagging nature. ---

topics.title: Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fundamental Governing Equations of DC Machines

The DC machine, whether operating as a **generator** or a **motor**, is governed by three foundational mathematical relations that form the bedrock of all numerical analysis under the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 syllabus. These equations interconnect the magnetic flux, electrical energy conversion, and mechanical energy output.

### 🔑 Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EMF Equation (Faraday's Law for Rotating Machines):** The average EMF induced in a DC machine armature winding is given by
> $$E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$
> where **Φ** is the useful flux per pole (in Weber), **Z** is the total number of armature conductors, **N** is the speed in RPM, **P** is the number of poles, and **A** is the number of parallel paths in the armature.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Brigade on a Ferris Wheel"

Imagine a **Ferris wheel of buckets**. Each bucket carries a small quantity of water (representing flux linkage). As the wheel rotates, every bucket passes a fixed collection point. The amount of water collected per second depends on:
- **How much water each bucket carries** → equivalent to **Φ × Z** (flux × conductors)
- **How fast the wheel turns** → equivalent to **N** (speed)
- **How many arms of the wheel deliver water to the spout** → equivalent to **P/A** (pole-to-path ratio)
- **Fixed time constant (60 seconds)** → normalization factor

This is exactly how conductors cutting magnetic flux generate **EMF** in a DC machine.

### 🛠 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Number of parallel paths**: Lap winding → **A = P**, Wave winding → **A = 2**
> - **SI Unit of Φ**: Weber (Wb)
> - **SI Unit of E_g**: Volts (V)
> - **SI Unit of N**: Revolutions per minute (RPM)

---

## 1.2 Mechanical Output & Electromagnetic Torque

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torque Equation:** The electromagnetic torque developed in a DC machine armature is
> $$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P}{A} \cdot \Phi \, Z \, I_a = 0.1591 \, \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{A}$$
> This torque is measured in **Newton-meters (N·m)** and represents the rotational force available at the shaft (motor) or required to drive the armature (generator).

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — The "Torque See-Saw"

Think of torque as the **rotational push** produced when a current-carrying conductor sits in a magnetic field. The total "push" on the shaft is the sum of the push of every active conductor at any instant. More poles (P) create more simultaneous pushing zones; fewer parallel paths (A) channel more current per conductor — hence the **P/A** multiplier in the formula.

### ⚡ Power Conversion Chain

> [!NOTE]
> **The Power Triangle for DC Machines:**
> - **Electrical Power Input (Motor):** $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$
> - **Mechanical Power Developed:** $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega_s$
> - **Shaft Power Output (Motor):** $P_{sh} = P_m - \text{mechanical losses}$
> - **Electrical Power Output (Generator):** $P_{out} = V \cdot I_L$

where the angular velocity in rad/s is $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.

---

## 1.3 Speed Regulation & Armature Reaction — Why They Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Back EMF:** In a motor, the EMF induced in the armature opposes the applied voltage, hence called **back EMF** ($E_b$). At steady state:
> $$V = E_b + I_a R_a \quad \text{(Motor)}$$
> $$E_g = V + I_a R_a \quad \text{( Generator)}$$

The **speed of a DC motor** at any operating point is therefore:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}, \quad \text{where } k = \frac{P Z}{60 A} = \text{constant}$$

This single equation is the **mother of all DC motor numerical problems** in the KTU board examination.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of DC Machine Numericals

Every numerical problem in this module can be broken into three logical pillars:

### Pillar 1 — EMF Generation (Generator & Motor alike)
1. Conductors cut magnetic flux → EMF is induced.
2. The induced EMF is **proportional** to flux (Φ), speed (N), and number of conductors (Z).
3. Winding configuration determines **A** (parallel paths) — Lap: A = P, Wave: A = 2.
4. Total EMF appears at the **brushes** as the sum of all series-connected paths.

### Pillar 2 — Torque Production (Interaction)
1. Current-carrying conductors in a magnetic field experience a force (**F = BIL**).
2. The collective force, multiplied by the armature radius, yields torque.
3. Torque is **proportional** to flux and armature current.
4. Armature reaction **distorts** the main field, requiring either a compensating winding or interpoles for large machines.

### Pillar 3 — Power Flow (Energy Conversion)
1. **Generator**: Mechanical → Electrical via $P_m = E_g \cdot I_a = T \cdot \omega_s$.
2. **Motor**: Electrical → Mechanical via $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = T \cdot \omega_s$.
3. **Losses** are subtracted from input power to get the **shaft power** or **output power**.

---

## 2.2 📜 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is your **rapid-revision arsenal** for all Module 3 numericals. Memorize the units and remember the **sign convention** for motor vs. generator.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|:-:|:-------------|:---------|:-----------------|:------------|
| 1 | **EMF Equation** | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | $\Phi$: Wb, Z: integer, N: RPM, P: integer, A: integer | All DC machines |
| 2 | **Back EMF (Motor)** | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | V: Volts, $I_a$: A, $R_a$: Ω | Motor running |
| 3 | **Generated EMF (Gen)** | $E_g = V + I_a R_a + I_a R_{se}$ | Includes series field drop | Generator loaded |
| 4 | **Electromagnetic Torque** | $T_a = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi A}$ | $T_a$: N·m, $I_a$: A | Both modes |
| 5 | **Mechanical Power** | $P_m = E_b I_a = \dfrac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$ | $P_m$: Watts | Energy conversion |
| 6 | **Shaft Power (Motor)** | $P_{sh} = P_m - P_{mech\,losses}$ | Brush + friction + windage | Motor output |
| 7 | **Output Power (Gen)** | $P_{out} = V \cdot I_L$ | V: terminal V, $I_L$: line A | Generator output |
| 8 | **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\,\%$ | Always in % | Both modes |
| 9 | **Speed (Motor)** | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi Z P / (60 A)} = \dfrac{E_b}{k \Phi}$ | N in RPM | Motor speed calc |
| 10 | **Speed Regulation** | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\,\%$ | Percentage | Motor behavior |
| 11 | **Armature Current (Shunt)** | $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ | Currents in A | KCL at terminal |
| 12 | **Shunt Field Current** | $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ | Ohm's law | Field circuit |
| 13 | **Lap Winding Paths** | A = P | — | Winding type |
| 14 | **Wave Winding Paths** | A = 2 | — | Winding type |
| 15 | **Series Motor Torque** | $T \propto \Phi \cdot I_a \propto I_a^2$ (before saturation) | Proportionality | DC series motor |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL SYNTAX RULE (Anti-Truncation Protocol):** All table cells use LaTeX math mode (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value) to avoid breaking the markdown table.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The numerical mastery of these equations is not academic — it is the **language of design** used by:

- 🔧 **ISRO & DRDO**: Sizing aerospace DC actuators where every gram of conductor counts.
- 🏭 **BHEL & L&T**: Designing 500 MW DC motor-generator sets for rolling mills.
- 🚗 **Tesla, Tata Motors, Ola Electric**: Calibrating the brushless DC (BLDC) controllers in EV drivetrains.
- ⚙️ **KSIDC Industrial Plants (Kerala)**: Choosing DC series motors for cranes and hoists in Cochin Shipyard.

> The **P/A ratio** in the EMF and torque equations is the **designer's tuning knob** — lap windings give higher current capacity (more paths), while wave windings give higher voltage capacity (only 2 paths) for the same flux and speed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EMF Equation

### Step 1: Consider one conductor moving through one pole pair
- Flux per pole = $\Phi$ Weber.
- Time for one revolution = $60/N$ seconds.
- The number of poles crossed per revolution = $P$.
- Average flux cut per conductor per revolution = $\Phi \cdot P$.
- Average rate of flux cutting = $\dfrac{\Phi P}{60/N} = \dfrac{\Phi P N}{60}$ Wb/sec.

### Step 2: According to Faraday's Law, average EMF per conductor
$$e_{avg} = \frac{\Phi P N}{60} \text{ volts}$$

### Step 3: The Z conductors are distributed over A parallel paths
- Conductors in **series per path** = $Z/A$.
- EMF per path (which equals terminal EMF) = $\dfrac{Z}{A} \cdot e_{avg}$.

### Step 4: Substituting and simplifying

$$
E_g = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \frac{\Phi P N}{60}
$$

$$
E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \text{ volts}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU 2024 Module 3 formula**. Every numerical you will see is a permutation of this equation with known/unknown rearrangement.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Torque Equation

### Step 1: Force on one armature conductor
$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L \quad \text{Newtons}$$

where B = flux density = $\Phi / (\text{area per pole})$, I = conductor current = $I_a / A$, and L = active conductor length.

### Step 2: Torque of one conductor
$$T_{one} = F \cdot r = B \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} \cdot L \cdot r$$

where r is the mean armature radius. Note that $2 \pi r L = \text{armature surface} = \dfrac{\pi D L}{}$ and the flux per pole is $\Phi = B \cdot A_{pole}$.

### Step 3: Total armature torque (summing all Z conductors)

$$
T_a = Z \cdot B \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} \cdot L \cdot r
$$

After substituting $B \cdot 2\pi r L = P \Phi$ and simplifying:

$$
T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P}{A} \cdot \Phi Z I_a \quad \text{N·m}
$$

This is the **electromagnetic torque** developed at the armature surface.

---

## 3.3 🧮 Comprehensive Numerical Problem Bank (KTU 2024 Pattern)

Each problem is solved **exhaustively** with KTU board-style step-by-step layout.

---

### 🔢 Problem 1 — EMF Equation (Lap Wound DC Generator)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Pattern]**

> An 8-pole, lap-wound DC generator has 960 conductors and runs at 1000 RPM. The flux per pole is 0.02 Wb. Calculate the EMF generated. If the generator is rewound as a wave winding, what will be the new EMF at the same speed and flux?

#### ✅ Solution

**Given Data:**
- P = 8 poles
- Z = 960 conductors
- N = 1000 RPM
- Φ = 0.02 Wb

**Case (a) — Lap Winding:**

Step 1: For lap winding, the number of parallel paths is
$$A = P = 8$$

Step 2: Apply the EMF equation
$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

Step 3: Substitute the values
$$E_g = \frac{0.02 \times 960 \times 1000 \times 8}{60 \times 8}$$

Step 4: Cancel the 8 in numerator and denominator
$$E_g = \frac{0.02 \times 960 \times 1000}{60}$$

Step 5: Compute the numerator
$$0.02 \times 960 = 19.2$$
$$19.2 \times 1000 = 19{,}200$$

Step 6: Divide by 60
$$E_g = \frac{19{,}200}{60} = 320 \text{ V}$$

**Case (b) — Wave Winding:**

Step 1: For wave winding, the number of parallel paths is
$$A = 2$$

Step 2: Apply the EMF equation
$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} = \frac{0.02 \times 960 \times 1000 \times 8}{60 \times 2}$$

Step 3: Compute the numerator
$$0.02 \times 960 \times 1000 \times 8 = 153{,}600$$

Step 4: Divide by denominator (120)
$$E_g = \frac{153{,}600}{120} = 1280 \text{ V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Wave winding produces **4× the voltage** of lap winding for the same machine — because A drops from 8 to 2 (factor 4). This is the **fundamental trade-off** between voltage and current capacity.

---

### 🔢 Problem 2 — Torque, Power, and Back EMF
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]**

> A 4-pole, 500 V DC shunt motor has 800 conductors lap wound. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω, field resistance 250 Ω, and flux per pole is 0.03 Wb. The motor draws an armature current of 50 A. Calculate: (a) Back EMF, (b) Speed, (c) Electromagnetic torque, (d) Mechanical power developed, (e) Shaft power if rotational losses are 600 W.

#### ✅ Solution

**Given Data:**
- P = 4, V = 500 V, Z = 800, Lap wound → A = P = 4
- $R_a = 0.2$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 250$ Ω
- Φ = 0.03 Wb, $I_a = 50$ A

**Part (a) — Back EMF:**

Step 1: For a motor, the back EMF opposes the applied voltage:
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a$$

Step 2: Substitute
$$E_b = 500 - (50 \times 0.2)$$
$$E_b = 500 - 10 = 490 \text{ V}$$

**Part (b) — Speed:**

Step 1: Use the EMF equation, rearranged for N
$$E_b = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \implies N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi Z P}$$

Step 2: Substitute
$$N = \frac{490 \times 60 \times 4}{0.03 \times 800 \times 4}$$

Step 3: Cancel the 4 in numerator and denominator
$$N = \frac{490 \times 60}{0.03 \times 800}$$

Step 4: Compute numerator
$$490 \times 60 = 29{,}400$$

Step 5: Compute denominator
$$0.03 \times 800 = 24$$

Step 6: Final division
$$N = \frac{29{,}400}{24} = 1225 \text{ RPM}$$

**Part (c) — Electromagnetic Torque:**

Step 1: Apply the torque equation
$$T_a = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi A}$$

Step 2: Substitute
$$T_a = \frac{4 \times 0.03 \times 800 \times 50}{2\pi \times 4}$$

Step 3: Cancel the 4 in numerator and denominator
$$T_a = \frac{0.03 \times 800 \times 50}{2\pi}$$

Step 4: Compute numerator
$$0.03 \times 800 = 24$$
$$24 \times 50 = 1200$$

Step 5: Compute denominator
$$2\pi = 6.2832$$

Step 6: Final division
$$T_a = \frac{1200}{6.2832} \approx 190.99 \text{ N·m}$$

**Part (d) — Mechanical Power Developed:**

Step 1: Mechanical power = back EMF × armature current
$$P_m = E_b \times I_a$$

Step 2: Substitute
$$P_m = 490 \times 50 = 24{,}500 \text{ W} = 24.5 \text{ kW}$$

Step 3: Verification using torque and speed
$$P_m = \frac{2\pi N T_a}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1225 \times 190.99}{60} \approx 24{,}500 \text{ W} \checkmark$$

**Part (e) — Shaft Power:**

Step 1: Subtract rotational losses from mechanical power
$$P_{sh} = P_m - P_{rotational}$$
$$P_{sh} = 24{,}500 - 600 = 23{,}900 \text{ W} = 23.9 \text{ kW}$$

---

### 🔢 Problem 3 — DC Series Motor (High-Torque Application)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

> A 250 V DC series motor has armature and field resistances of 0.3 Ω and 0.2 Ω respectively. At a certain load, it draws 40 A and runs at 1500 RPM. If the load is increased such that the armature current rises to 60 A, calculate the new speed. Assume flux is proportional to armature current (linear region).

#### ✅ Solution

**Given Data:**
- V = 250 V, $R_a = 0.3$ Ω, $R_{se} = 0.2$ Ω
- Initial: $I_{a1} = 40$ A, $N_1 = 1500$ RPM
- Final: $I_{a2} = 60$ A
- Assumption: $\Phi \propto I_a$ (unsaturated series motor)

**Step 1: Find the constant $k \Phi$ at the first operating point**

Back EMF at first point:
$$E_{b1} = V - I_{a1}(R_a + R_{se})$$
$$E_{b1} = 250 - 40 \times (0.3 + 0.2) = 250 - 40 \times 0.5 = 250 - 20 = 230 \text{ V}$$

The constant relating EMF, speed, and flux:
$$k\Phi_1 = \frac{E_{b1}}{N_1} = \frac{230}{1500} = 0.1533 \text{ V·min/rev}$$

**Step 2: Since Φ ∝ Ia, the flux ratio is**
$$\frac{\Phi_2}{\Phi_1} = \frac{I_{a2}}{I_{a1}} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5$$

Therefore: $k\Phi_2 = 1.5 \times 0.1533 = 0.2300$ V·min/rev

**Step 3: Find back EMF at the second operating point**
$$E_{b2} = V - I_{a2}(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 60 \times 0.5 = 250 - 30 = 220 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Compute new speed**
$$N_2 = \frac{E_{b2}}{k\Phi_2} = \frac{220}{0.2300} \approx 956.5 \text{ RPM}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** In a series motor, **load increase → current increase → flux increase → speed decrease**. This is the **inverse speed-load characteristic** that makes series motors ideal for cranes, electric traction (Indian Railways), and hoists.

---

### 🔢 Problem 4 — DC Generator Efficiency and Power Flow
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

> A 50 kW, 250 V DC shunt generator has armature and shunt field resistances of 0.05 Ω and 100 Ω respectively. It runs at 1000 RPM. The rotational losses amount to 2 kW. Calculate: (a) Line current, (b) Generated EMF, (c) Armature copper loss, (d) Efficiency.

#### ✅ Solution

**Given Data:**
- Output rating: $P_{out} = 50{,}000$ W, V = 250 V
- $R_a = 0.05$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 100$ Ω
- N = 1000 RPM
- Rotational (mechanical) losses: $P_{rot} = 2000$ W

**Part (a) — Line Current:**

Step 1: Output power equals terminal voltage × line current
$$P_{out} = V \cdot I_L$$

Step 2: Rearrange for $I_L$
$$I_L = \frac{P_{out}}{V} = \frac{50{,}000}{250} = 200 \text{ A}$$

**Part (b) — Generated EMF:**

Step 1: Compute shunt field current
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5 \text{ A}$$

Step 2: Apply KCL at the armature terminal — armature current supplies both load and shunt field
$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 200 + 2.5 = 202.5 \text{ A}$$

Step 3: Generated EMF (for a generator):
$$E_g = V + I_a R_a = 250 + 202.5 \times 0.05 = 250 + 10.125 = 260.125 \text{ V}$$

**Part (c) — Armature Copper Loss:**

Step 1: Armature copper loss
$$P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 \cdot R_a = (202.5)^2 \times 0.05$$
$$P_{cu,a} = 41{,}006.25 \times 0.05 = 2050.31 \text{ W}$$

**Part (d) — Efficiency:**

Step 1: Compute all losses
- Shunt field copper loss: $P_{cu,sh} = V \cdot I_{sh} = 250 \times 2.5 = 625$ W
- Armature copper loss: $P_{cu,a} = 2050.31$ W
- Rotational losses: $P_{rot} = 2000$ W
- Total losses: $P_{loss} = 625 + 2050.31 + 2000 = 4675.31$ W

Step 2: Input power
$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss} = 50{,}000 + 4675.31 = 54{,}675.31 \text{ W}$$

Step 3: Efficiency
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\,\% = \frac{50{,}000}{54{,}675.31} \times 100\,\%$$
$$\eta \approx 91.45\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification via direct efficiency formula:**
> $\eta = \dfrac{V \cdot I_L}{V \cdot I_L + P_{loss}} \times 100 = \dfrac{250 \times 200}{250 \times 200 + 4675.31} \times 100 = \dfrac{50{,}000}{54{,}675.31} \times 100 \approx 91.45\%$ ✓

---

### 🔢 Problem 5 — Speed Regulation & Armature Reaction
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

> A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω and runs at 1000 RPM at no-load with an armature current of 5 A. At full load, the armature current is 50 A. Assuming flux is weakened by 5% due to armature reaction at full load, calculate the full-load speed and the speed regulation.

#### ✅ Solution

**Given Data:**
- V = 220 V, $R_a = 0.5$ Ω
- No-load: $N_1 = 1000$ RPM, $I_{a1} = 5$ A
- Full-load: $I_{a2} = 50$ A
- Armature reaction: flux reduced by 5%, i.e., $\Phi_2 = 0.95 \cdot \Phi_1$

**Step 1: Back EMF at no-load**
$$E_{b1} = V - I_{a1} R_a = 220 - 5 \times 0.5 = 220 - 2.5 = 217.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Back EMF at full-load**
$$E_{b2} = V - I_{a2} R_a = 220 - 50 \times 0.5 = 220 - 25 = 195 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Apply the speed ratio equation**

The general relationship: $\dfrac{E_b}{N} \propto \Phi$, so
$$\frac{E_{b1}}{N_1 \cdot \Phi_1} = \frac{E_{b2}}{N_2 \cdot \Phi_2}$$

Step 4: Rearrange for $N_2$
$$N_2 = \frac{E_{b2} \cdot N_1 \cdot \Phi_1}{E_{b1} \cdot \Phi_2}$$

Step 5: Substitute $\Phi_2 = 0.95 \Phi_1$
$$N_2 = \frac{195 \times 1000 \times \Phi_1}{217.5 \times 0.95 \cdot \Phi_1}$$

Step 6: Cancel $\Phi_1$
$$N_2 = \frac{195 \times 1000}{217.5 \times 0.95}$$

Step 7: Compute denominator
$$217.5 \times 0.95 = 206.625$$

Step 8: Final division
$$N_2 = \frac{195{,}000}{206.625} \approx 943.7 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 9: Speed Regulation**
$$\text{SR} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\,\% = \frac{1000 - 943.7}{943.7} \times 100\,\%$$
$$\text{SR} = \frac{56.3}{943.7} \times 100\,\% \approx 5.97\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Shunt motors exhibit **very good speed regulation** (typically 5–10%) because flux remains nearly constant. This is why shunt motors are used for **lathes, fans, centrifugal pumps, and printing presses**.

---

## 3.4 📊 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Right Formula

> [!NOTE]
> Use this decision matrix the moment you read a problem statement to **instantly identify** which formula block to deploy.

| Problem Mentions | Use This Formula | Watch Out For |
|:-----------------|:-----------------|:--------------|
| "Generated EMF", "induced EMF" | $E_g = \Phi Z N P / (60 A)$ | Identify lap vs. wave first |
| "Motor speed", "speed regulation" | $N = E_b / (k \Phi)$ | Armature reaction → flux change |
| "Torque developed", "shaft torque" | $T_a = P \Phi Z I_a / (2\pi A)$ | Convert RPM to rad/s for power |
| "Efficiency", "losses" | $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in} \times 100$ | Include rotational + copper losses |
| "Series motor at different load" | Use $\Phi \propto I_a$ (linear) | Saturation effect absent |
| "Shunt motor", "constant flux" | $\Phi = \text{const}$, only $E_b$ varies | $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Flow Block Diagram (DC Generator vs. DC Motor)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **energy conversion chain** for both modes of operation, which is essential for solving efficiency and power-related numericals.

    ARMATURE_G -->|Subtract| CU_G
    ARMATURE_G -->|Subtract| ROT_G
    ARMATURE_G --> ELEC_G

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *(3 Marks)*

**Q: A 4-pole DC machine has 600 conductors and a flux per pole of 0.05 Wb. Calculate the EMF generated when running at 1200 RPM with a lap winding.**

#### ✅ Model Answer

Step 1: For lap winding, A = P = 4.

Step 2: Apply the EMF equation:
$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} = \frac{0.05 \times 600 \times 1200 \times 4}{60 \times 4}$$

Step 3: Cancel 4 and compute:
$$E_g = \frac{0.05 \times 600 \times 1200}{60} = \frac{36{,}000}{60} = 600 \text{ V}$$

**Final Answer:** $E_g = \mathbf{600\,V}$

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** *(3 Marks)*

**Q: State the EMF equation of a DC generator. Define each term with its SI unit.**

#### ✅ Model Answer

The EMF equation of a DC generator is:
$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | SI Unit |
|:------:|:--------|:--------|
| $\Phi$ | Flux per pole | Weber (Wb) |
| $Z$ | Total number of armature conductors | — |
| $N$ | Speed of armature | RPM (rev/min) |
| $P$ | Number of poles | — |
| $A$ | Number of parallel paths | — |
| 60 | Time constant (seconds per minute) | — |

For **lap winding**: A = P. For **wave winding**: A = 2.

**Final Answer:** EMF is directly proportional to flux, conductors, speed, and poles; inversely proportional to parallel paths.

---

## 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### 🎯 Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> A 220 V, 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt motor has 800 conductors, an armature resistance of 0.25 Ω, and a field resistance of 110 Ω. The motor takes 40 A from the supply. Calculate: (i) Back EMF, (ii) Speed, (iii) Torque developed, (iv) Shaft power output if mechanical losses are 500 W.

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Given:**
- V = 220 V, P = 4, Wave wound → A = 2
- Z = 800, $R_a = 0.25$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 110$ Ω
- $I_L = 40$ A, Mechanical losses = 500 W

**Step 1: Compute shunt field current**
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Compute armature current (KCL)**
$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 40 - 2 = 38 \text{ A}$$

**(i) Back EMF:**
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 38 \times 0.25 = 220 - 9.5 = 210.5 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Speed:**
$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi Z P} \quad \text{(we need Φ from rating — assume Φ = 0.025 Wb)}$$
$$N = \frac{210.5 \times 60 \times 2}{0.025 \times 800 \times 4} = \frac{25{,}260}{80} = 315.75 \text{ RPM}$$

**(iii) Torque developed:**
$$T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{4 \times 0.025 \times 800 \times 38}{2\pi \times 2}$$
$$T_a = \frac{3040}{12.566} = 241.9 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iv) Shaft power:**
$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = 210.5 \times 38 = 7999 \text{ W}$$
$$P_{sh} = P_m - P_{mech} = 7999 - 500 = 7499 \text{ W} \approx 7.5 \text{ kW}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> If the same motor is rewound as a lap winding keeping flux and current unchanged, what would be the new speed? Comment on the relative merits of lap and wave windings.

#### ✅ Model Solution

For lap winding: A = P = 4

$$N_{new} = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi Z P} = \frac{210.5 \times 60 \times 4}{0.025 \times 800 \times 4} = \frac{50{,}520}{80} = 631.5 \text{ RPM}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Lap Winding (A = P) | Wave Winding (A = 2) |
|:--------|:-------------------:|:--------------------:|
| Voltage per path | Lower | Higher (4× for P=4) |
| Current capacity | Higher (more paths) | Lower |
| Generated EMF | Lower | Higher (4× for P=4) |
| Commutator segments | Many | Fewer |
| Use case | High-current DC machines | High-voltage DC machines |

> **Conclusion:** Lap winding gives higher speed (more current capacity, lower voltage per path) while wave winding yields higher voltage but lower speed.

---

### 🎯 Question B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> A 250 V DC shunt generator delivers a load current of 100 A. The armature resistance is 0.1 Ω, the shunt field resistance is 250 Ω, and the rotational losses are 1500 W. Find: (i) Generated EMF, (ii) Armature copper loss, (iii) Mechanical power input, (iv) Electrical efficiency and overall efficiency.

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Given:** V = 250 V, $I_L = 100$ A, $R_a = 0.1$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 250$ Ω, $P_{rot} = 1500$ W

**Step 1: Shunt field current**
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Armature current (KCL for generator)**
$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 1 = 101 \text{ A}$$

**(i) Generated EMF:**
$$E_g = V + I_a R_a = 250 + 101 \times 0.1 = 250 + 10.1 = 260.1 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Armature copper loss:**
$$P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a = (101)^2 \times 0.1 = 10{,}201 \times 0.1 = 1020.1 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Mechanical power input:**

First, electrical power developed in armature:
$$P_{e,arm} = E_g \cdot I_a = 260.1 \times 101 = 26{,}270.1 \text{ W}$$

This equals the mechanical power converted (in a generator):
$$P_m = P_{e,arm} = 26{,}270.1 \text{ W}$$

Total mechanical power input = mechanical power converted + rotational losses:
$$P_{mech,in} = P_m + P_{rot} = 26{,}270.1 + 1500 = 27{,}770.1 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Efficiencies:**

- Output power: $P_{out} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 100 = 25{,}000$ W
- Input power: $P_{in} = P_{mech,in} = 27{,}770.1$ W

- **Overall (Commercial) Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{25{,}000}{27{,}770.1} \times 100 \approx 90.03\%$$

- **Electrical Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{elec} = \frac{V \cdot I_L}{E_g \cdot I_a} \times 100 = \frac{25{,}000}{26{,}270.1} \times 100 \approx 95.17\%$$

- **Mechanical Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_{e,arm}}{P_{mech,in}} \times 100 = \frac{26{,}270.1}{27{,}770.1} \times 100 \approx 94.60\%$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> A 250 V DC series motor takes 50 A and runs at 800 RPM. The total resistance of the armature and field is 0.4 Ω. If the load is increased such that the motor draws 80 A, calculate the new speed assuming flux is proportional to current.

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Step 1: Back EMF at first condition**
$$E_{b1} = V - I_{a1}(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 50 \times 0.4 = 250 - 20 = 230 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Speed-flux constant at first point**
$$k\Phi_1 = \frac{E_{b1}}{N_1} = \frac{230}{800} = 0.2875 \text{ V·min/rev}$$

**Step 3: Flux ratio (linear region)**
$$\Phi_2 = \Phi_1 \cdot \frac{I_{a2}}{I_{a1}} = \Phi_1 \cdot \frac{80}{50} = 1.6 \, \Phi_1$$

**Step 4: Back EMF at second condition**
$$E_{b2} = V - I_{a2}(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 80 \times 0.4 = 250 - 32 = 218 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: New speed constant**
$$k\Phi_2 = 1.6 \times 0.2875 = 0.460 \text{ V·min/rev}$$

**Step 6: New speed**
$$N_2 = \frac{E_{b2}}{k\Phi_2} = \frac{218}{0.460} \approx 473.9 \text{ RPM}$$

**Speed drop:** $\Delta N = 800 - 473.9 = 326.1$ RPM (about 40.8% reduction)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Traps in DC Machine Numerical Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to update A** when the problem says "rewound as wave" — students keep A = P from lap. This alone can cost **2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign convention confusion** — In a generator, $E_g = V + I_a R_a$; in a motor, $E_b = V - I_a R_a$. Mixing these up is a **3-mark killer**.
> 3. **Forgetting shunt field current** in a shunt machine — $I_a \neq I_L$. Always compute $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ first. Common cause of **1–2 mark loss**.
> 4. **Units mismatch** — Flux must be in Weber, speed in RPM (not rad/s) for the EMF equation. The 60 in the denominator handles the conversion. **1-mark deduction** if missed.
> 5. **Power equation mix-up** — Remember $P_m = E_b I_a$ (electromagnetic) vs. $P_{sh} = P_m - \text{mechanical losses}$ (shaft). And $P_{out} = V \cdot I_L$ (terminal). KTU examiners **specifically look** for this distinction.
>
> **Bonus Tip:** Always verify your answer using the **power consistency check** — if $P_m = E_b I_a$ and $P_m = T_a \cdot (2\pi N/60)$ disagree, you have made a unit or formula error.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 DC Machine Numericals**

- ✅ **EMF Equation:** $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — the foundational formula. Knowing this alone solves 60% of numericals.
- ✅ **Lap Winding:** A = P → high current, low voltage. **Wave Winding:** A = 2 → low current, high voltage.
- ✅ **Motor Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ → Back EMF = $V - I_a R_a$.
- ✅ **Generator Equation:** $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ → Generated EMF > Terminal V.
- ✅ **Shunt Motor:** $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$, where $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$.
- ✅ **Torque:** $T_a = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} = \dfrac{60 P_m}{2\pi N}$. Conversion: 1 N·m × (2π/60) RPM = 1 W.
- ✅ **Speed:** $N = \dfrac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi Z P}$. For DC shunt motor with constant flux, $N \approx \dfrac{E_b}{k\Phi}$.
- ✅ **Series Motor:** $\Phi \propto I_a$ (unsaturated) → $T \propto I_a^2$ → high starting torque, inverse speed-load curve.
- ✅ **Armature Reaction:** Reduces main flux at high loads → weakens back EMF → causes **speed drop** in motors, **voltage drop** in generators. Counter-measure: interpoles, compensating windings.
- ✅ **Power Chain (Generator):** $P_{mech,in} \to P_m = E_g I_a \to P_{out} = V I_L$ (subtracting Cu and rotational losses).
- ✅ **Power Chain (Motor):** $P_{in} = V I_L \to P_m = E_b I_a \to P_{sh}$ (subtracting rotational losses).
- ✅ **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{V I_L}{V I_L + \text{losses}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Speed Regulation:** $\text{SR} = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$. Shunt motor SR ≈ 5–10%, Series motor SR = negative (always).
- ✅ **Mechanical Power:** $P_m = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_a}{60}$ where N is in RPM and T in N·m. Equivalently $P_m = E_b I_a$.
- ✅ **Armature Current Formulas:**
  - **Shunt motor:** $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$
  - **Series motor:** $I_a = I_L$ (same loop)
  - **Compound motor:** $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$, with field carrying both shunt and series components.
- ✅ **Always** check if a problem says "wound" (lap or wave) — it controls A directly.
- ✅ **Unit conversion trap:** 1 HP = 746 W. 1 N·m = 1/9.81 kgf·m. 1 rad/s = 60/(2π) RPM.
- ✅ **KTU Board Pattern:** Almost every problem asks **3–4 sub-parts** combining (i) EMF, (ii) Speed, (iii) Torque, (iv) Power/Efficiency. Practice linking them via $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \omega_s$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Parallel Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Parallel Operation of Transformers** is defined as the connection of two or more transformers having the same polarity, voltage ratio, and phase sequence, on both their primary and secondary sides, to a common bus-bar so that they jointly supply a common load.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, the topic of **Parallel Operation of Single-Phase Transformers** falls under **Module 3: Transformers (Single Phase)** and is treated as a high-weightage analytical topic, often carrying 7 to 14 marks in End Semester Examinations.

The term **"parallel"** strictly refers to electrical paralleling of secondary windings, while the primaries may be fed either from a common source or from independent but synchronously operating sources.

### Formal Conditions (KTU Verbatim)

For **satisfactory parallel operation** without circulating currents or unequal load sharing, the following conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Identical Voltage Ratio (Turns Ratio)** — $N_1/N_2$ must be the same for all transformers.
2. **Identical Polarity** — Instantaneous polarities of all windings must be the same.
3. **Zero Phase Displacement** — For three-phase units, the phase shift between primary and secondary must be identical (e.g., all Dy11 or Yd1).
4. **Same Phase Sequence** — Only relevant for polyphase transformers.
5. **Similar Percentage Impedance ($Z\%$)** — To ensure load is shared in proportion to their kVA ratings.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: Two Water Pumps Feeding a Common Overhead Tank**

Imagine **two water pumps (Pump A and Pump B)** of different capacities connected to the **same overhead tank** through separate pipes. The tank represents the **common load bus-bar**.

- If both pumps produce **exactly the same pressure (voltage)**, water (current) flows in proportion to each pump's pipe diameter (impedance). This is the **ideal parallel condition**.
- If one pump produces a slightly higher pressure due to manufacturing tolerance (slightly different turns ratio), water will continuously circulate between the two pumps even when the tank is closed. This wasted flow is analogous to the **circulating current** in transformers.
- If the pipe diameters (impedances) are mismatched, the smaller pipe carries less water even though both pumps are equally rated, leading to **unequal load sharing**.

**Intuitive takeaway:** Perfect paralleling is impossible without identical specifications; hence engineers quantify the *circulating currents* and *unequal sharing* that result from small mismatches.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Rated Apparent Power | $S$ | kVA or MVA |
| Percentage Impedance | $Z\%$ | % |
| Turns Ratio | $a = N_1/N_2$ | dimensionless |
| No-Load Voltage | $V_1, V_2$ | Volts (V) |
| Frequency (Indian Standard) | $f = \mathbf{50}$ **Hz** | Hertz |
| Power Factor of Load | $\cos\phi$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):** *"Parallel operation of single-phase transformers — conditions, load sharing with equal and unequal voltage ratios."* This wording guarantees that numerical problems on **load sharing** and **circulating current** are mandatory in ESE questions.

---

## 1.4 Why Engineers Use Parallel Operation

- **Reliability / Redundancy:** If one transformer faults, the others continue to supply the load.
- **Economy:** Small transformers operating near full load are more efficient than one oversized unit lightly loaded.
- **Flexibility:** Load growth can be met by adding another transformer in parallel rather than replacing the existing one.
- **Transport Limitation:** Large MVA transformers exceed road/rail transport weight limits; several smaller units paralleled solve this.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions for Satisfactory Parallel Operation — Logical Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> The following five conditions are **independent constraints**, but they are mathematically interrelated. Engineers must verify ALL of them before energising transformers in parallel.

### Condition 1: Same Voltage (Turns) Ratio
- **Why:** If ratios differ, even with identical primary voltage, the secondary EMFs differ.
- **How it fails:** A circulating current $I_c$ flows between secondaries at no-load:
  $$I_c = \frac{V_{A2} - V_{B2}}{Z_{A2} + Z_{B2}}$$
- This circulating current adds to the load current of one transformer and subtracts from the other, causing unnecessary copper losses and possible saturation.

### Condition 2: Same Polarity
- **Why:** Opposite polarity causes a dead short-circuit across the secondaries (EMFs in series opposition).
- **How:** A polarity test using a voltmeter on adjacent secondaries must show **zero voltage** when the transformers are fed in parallel on the primary side with proper polarity markings (dot convention).

### Condition 3: Zero Relative Phase Displacement (Three-Phase Only)
- **Why:** For three-phase units, identical phase sequence AND identical vector group are mandatory.
- **How it fails:** Connecting Dy11 with Yd1 in parallel causes a 30° phase mismatch, leading to severe circulating currents up to 6× rated current.

### Condition 4: Similar Percentage Impedances ($Z\%_A \approx Z\%_B$)
- **Why:** Load shared by each transformer is inversely proportional to its impedance:
  $$S_A : S_B = \frac{1}{Z_A} : \frac{1}{Z_B}$$
- If $Z\%_A = 5\%$ and $Z\%_B = 10\%$, transformer A (lower impedance) carries twice the share relative to its rating, and may overload while transformer B remains under-loaded.

### Condition 5: Same Phase Sequence (Polyphase Only)
- RYB must match RYB; otherwise severe circulating currents and rotation reversal of connected motors occur.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every numerical question on parallel operation.**

| # | Scenario | Formula | Variables |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Load shared by Transformer A (equal ratios) | $S_A = \dfrac{Z_B}{Z_A + Z_B} \cdot S_{total}$ | $Z$ in ohms, $S$ in kVA |
| 2 | Load shared by Transformer B (equal ratios) | $S_B = \dfrac{Z_A}{Z_A + Z_B} \cdot S_{total}$ | $Z$ in ohms, $S$ in kVA |
| 3 | Equal ratios form (using $Z\%$) | $S_A = \dfrac{S_{A,rated}}{Z\%_A \left(\dfrac{S_{A,rated}}{Z\%_A} + \dfrac{S_{B,rated}}{Z\%_B}\right)} \cdot S_{total}$ | $Z\%$ on own kVA base |
| 4 | Circulating current (unequal ratios) | $I_c = \dfrac{V_{A2} - V_{B2}}{Z_{A2} + Z_{B2}}$ | $V$ in V, $Z$ in ohms |
| 5 | EMF difference due to turns ratio | $\Delta V = V_1 \left(\dfrac{1}{a_A} - \dfrac{1}{a_B}\right)$ | $a$ = turns ratio |
| 6 | Total secondary current (A) | $I_{A,total} = I_{load,A} + I_c$ | vector sum |
| 7 | kVA loading of each unit (combined) | $S_{A,actual} = \sqrt{3}\,V\,I_{A,total}$ | Polyphase |
| 8 | Per-unit impedance conversion | $Z_{pu} = \dfrac{Z_{\Omega} \cdot S_{base}}{V_{base}^2}$ | For common-base calculation |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Parallel Operation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Power Grid Substations** | Multiple 100 MVA units run in parallel; one is taken out for maintenance without load shedding. |
| **Industrial Plants** | Two identical transformers are paralleled; one acts as a hot standby. |
| **Renewable Energy** | Multiple wind/pole-mounted distribution transformers paralleled to feed a microgrid. |
| **Hospitals / Data Centers** | N+1 redundancy configuration — required for guaranteed uptime. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Trick:** Always convert percentage impedance to a **common kVA base** before adding them. The most common mistake is adding $Z\%$ values directly from different rated bases, which yields wrong answers.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Load Sharing with **Equal Voltage Ratios** but **Different kVA Ratings and Different $Z\%$**

### Setup
Two single-phase transformers A and B are connected in parallel on both primary and secondary sides. They share a common load $S$ at power factor $\cos\phi$. Both have the **same turns ratio**, so the no-load secondary EMFs are equal ($E_{A2} = E_{B2} = V_2$).

### Circuit Representation

Let $Z_{A}$ and $Z_{B}$ be the equivalent series impedances of transformers A and B referred to the secondary side. The two secondaries can be replaced by their Thevenin equivalents in parallel:

- Transformer A: EMF $= V_2$, impedance $= Z_A$
- Transformer B: EMF $= V_2$, impedance $= Z_B$
- Load: Impedance $= Z_L$ at terminal voltage $V_T$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Apply **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the common secondary node. The currents from each transformer sum to the load current:

$$I_A + I_B = I_L$$

**Step 2:** Apply **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around each transformer's loop to the load terminal:

$$V_2 = I_A Z_A + V_T$$

$$V_2 = I_B Z_B + V_T$$

**Step 3:** Subtract the two equations to eliminate $V_2$ and $V_T$:

$$I_A Z_A = I_B Z_B$$

**Step 4:** Hence, the currents are inversely proportional to the impedances:

$$I_A : I_B = \dfrac{1}{Z_A} : \dfrac{1}{Z_B}$$

**Step 5:** Express $I_A$ and $I_B$ in terms of total load current $I_L$. From $I_A + I_B = I_L$ and $I_A Z_A = I_B Z_B$:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_B &= \dfrac{Z_A}{Z_A + Z_B} \cdot I_L \\[6pt]
I_A &= \dfrac{Z_B}{Z_A + Z_B} \cdot I_L
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Convert currents to apparent power using $S = V \cdot I$ (with the same terminal voltage $V_T$ for both):

$$\begin{aligned}
S_A &= V_T \cdot I_A = \dfrac{Z_B}{Z_A + Z_B} \cdot S_{total} \\[6pt]
S_B &= V_T \cdot I_B = \dfrac{Z_A}{Z_A + Z_B} \cdot S_{total}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** The transformer with the **higher impedance** carries **less load**, and the transformer with the **lower impedance** carries **more load** than its rating would suggest. This is the fundamental rule.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** Two transformers A (500 kVA, 5% impedance) and B (250 kVA, 4% impedance) are in parallel supplying a total load of 600 kVA at 0.8 PF lagging. Both have the same turns ratio. Calculate the load shared by each.

**Step 1:** Convert $Z\%$ to a common base. Choose common base $S_{base} = 500$ kVA.

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_A\,\% \text{ on common base} &= 5\% \quad (\text{already on base}) \\[6pt]
Z_B\,\% \text{ on common base} &= 4 \times \dfrac{500}{250} = 8\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Since $Z \propto Z\%$ on a common base, the load share is:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_A &= \dfrac{Z_B}{Z_A + Z_B} \cdot S_{total} = \dfrac{8}{5 + 8} \times 600 \\[6pt]
S_A &= \dfrac{8}{13} \times 600 \approx 369.23 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S_B &= \dfrac{5}{5 + 8} \cdot S_{total} = \dfrac{5}{13} \times 600 \\[6pt]
S_B &= \dfrac{5}{13} \times 600 \approx 230.77 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Verify loadings as percentages of rating:

$$\text{Loading of A} = \dfrac{369.23}{500} \times 100 = 73.85\%$$

$$\text{Loading of B} = \dfrac{230.77}{250} \times 100 = 92.31\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Transformer B is the limiting unit. The maximum load that can be supplied without overloading B is when B is at 100%, i.e., $S_{max} = 250 \times (13/5) = 650$ kVA. Beyond this, B overloads.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Circulating Current with **Unequal Voltage Ratios** but **Equal $Z\%$**

### Setup
Two transformers A and B have turns ratios $a_A$ and $a_B$ respectively, with $a_A \neq a_B$. Both primaries are supplied by the same primary voltage $V_1$. No external load is connected (no-load condition). Determine the circulating current.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Express the secondary induced EMFs in terms of $V_1$:

$$E_{A2} = \dfrac{V_1}{a_A}, \quad E_{B2} = \dfrac{V_1}{a_B}$$

**Step 2:** Because both secondaries are connected in parallel, the voltage across the common secondary bus must be a single value. Therefore, a circulating current flows internally between the two secondaries. Let $I_c$ be this circulating current.

**Step 3:** Apply KVL around the loop formed by the two secondaries (going through one transformer, back through the other):

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{A2} - E_{B2} &= I_c \cdot Z_A + I_c \cdot Z_B \\[6pt]
V_1\left(\dfrac{1}{a_A} - \dfrac{1}{a_B}\right) &= I_c (Z_A + Z_B)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the circulating current:

$$I_c = \dfrac{V_1 \left(\dfrac{1}{a_A} - \dfrac{1}{a_B}\right)}{Z_A + Z_B} = \dfrac{E_{A2} - E_{B2}}{Z_A + Z_B}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The circulating current flows **continuously** even at no-load. It causes:
> - Additional copper losses (I²R heating)
> - Reduced available kVA capacity for the actual load
> - Possible saturation if impedance is very low

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Combined Effect — **Both Unequal Ratios AND Unequal $Z\%$**

This is the most general KTU question. The actual current in each transformer is the **phasor sum** of:
- Its share of the load current
- The circulating current

**Step 1:** Compute load currents using the equal-ratio formula (treating the load as if distributed by impedance ratio alone):

$$I_{LA} = \dfrac{Z_B}{Z_A + Z_B} \cdot I_L, \quad I_{LB} = \dfrac{Z_A}{Z_A + Z_B} \cdot I_L$$

**Step 2:** Compute the circulating current due to EMF mismatch:

$$I_c = \dfrac{V_1 \left(\dfrac{1}{a_A} - \dfrac{1}{a_B}\right)}{Z_A + Z_B}$$

**Step 3:** Phasor addition. The actual current in each transformer is:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_A &= I_{LA} + I_c \\[6pt]
I_B &= I_{LB} - I_c
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Adding $I_{LA}$ and $I_c$ as scalars. Since $Z_A$ and $Z_B$ have different $X/R$ ratios, the angles must be tracked using phasor arithmetic. In KTU examinations, if both transformers are assumed to have the **same $X/R$ ratio**, scalar addition is acceptable; otherwise, phasor addition is mandatory.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Condition | Equal Ratios | Equal $Z\%$ | Loading Effect | Circulating Current |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Ideal Case | ✓ | ✓ | Proportional to kVA rating | Zero |
| Unequal $Z\%$ only | ✓ | ✗ | Inversely proportional to $Z\%$ | Zero |
| Unequal Ratios only | ✗ | ✓ | Distorted by circulating current | Non-zero |
| Both unequal | ✗ | ✗ | Complex phasor sum | Non-zero |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Parallel Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SourceA[Common Primary Source V1] --> PrimA[Transformer A Primary]
    SourceB[Common Primary Source V1] --> PrimB[Transformer B Primary]
    PrimA --> SecA[Transformer A Secondary EMF E_A2]
    PrimB --> SecB[Transformer B Secondary EMF E_B2]
    SecA --> BusTop[Common Secondary Bus Bar]
    SecB --> BusTop
    BusTop --> Load[Common Load S_L at Power Factor cos phi]
    BusTop --> Return[Neutral / Return Path]
    Return --> SecA
    Return --> SecB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This flowchart captures the power flow path. Both primaries share the same voltage $V_1$, both secondaries tie onto a common bus, and the bus supplies a single unified load.

---

## 4.2 Decision Flow: Conditions Check Before Paralleling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Begin Paralleling Check]) --> C1{1. Same Turns Ratio?}
    C1 -- No --> Fail1[STOP: Unequal Ratios Cause Circulating Current]
    C1 -- Yes --> C2{2. Same Polarity?}
    C2 -- No --> Fail2[STOP: Risk of Short Circuit]
    C2 -- Yes --> C3{3. Same Phase Sequence?}
    C3 -- No --> Fail3[STOP: 3-phase Mismatch]
    C3 -- Yes --> C4{4. Similar Z percent?}
    C4 -- No --> Warn1[WARNING: Load Shared Unevenly - Verify Ratings]
    C4 -- Yes --> C5{5. Zero Phase Displacement?}
    C5 -- No --> Fail4[STOP: Vector Group Mismatch]
    C5 -- Yes --> Approve[SAFE TO PARALLEL]
    Warn1 --> Approve
```

---

## 4.3 Vector Diagram Showing Circulating Current Effect

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VA[Transformer A Phasors]
        IL_A[Load Current I_LA]
        IC_A[Circulating Current I_c]
        I_A[Actual Current I_A]
    end
    subgraph VB[Transformer B Phasors]
        IL_B[Load Current I_LB]
        IC_B[Circulating Current Minus I_c]
        I_B[Actual Current I_B]
    end
    IL_A --> I_A
    IC_A --> I_A
    IL_B --> I_B
    IC_B --> I_B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Skill:** Always represent currents as vectors from a common origin. The phasor sum $\vec{I}_A = \vec{I}_{LA} + \vec{I}_c$ shows why the actual kVA loading differs from the simple scalar load share.

---

## 4.4 Phasor Geometry Showing Induced EMF Mismatch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1[Primary Voltage V1] --> Div1{Divide by Turns Ratio a_A}
    Div1 --> EA2[Induced EMF E_A2 = V1 slash a_A]
    V1 --> Div2{Divide by Turns Ratio a_B}
    Div2 --> EB2[Induced EMF E_B2 = V1 slash a_B]
    EA2 --> Diff[Phasor Difference Delta E = E_A2 minus E_B2]
    EB2 --> Diff
    Diff --> Circ[Drives Circulating Current I_c through Z_A plus Z_B]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**State any four conditions that must be satisfied for the satisfactory parallel operation of single-phase transformers.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The following conditions must be satisfied:

1. **Same voltage ratio (turns ratio):** Otherwise, circulating currents flow between the secondaries at no-load.
2. **Same polarity:** Otherwise, the induced EMFs oppose each other, causing a short circuit.
3. **Similar percentage impedance:** To ensure load is shared in proportion to the kVA ratings of the transformers.
4. **Same phase sequence and zero relative phase displacement:** Mandatory for three-phase transformers to avoid circulating currents.

*(For 3 marks, any 4 of the 5 conditions explained in one line each suffice.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Define the term "circulating current" in the context of parallel operation of transformers. Under what condition does it arise?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Circulating current** is the current that flows between the secondary windings of two transformers connected in parallel, **even when no external load is connected**.

It arises when the **turns ratios (voltage ratios) of the two transformers are not exactly identical**, causing a difference in their induced secondary EMFs. The phasor difference of these EMFs drives a continuous current through the series combination of both transformer impedances:

$$I_c = \dfrac{V_1 \left(\dfrac{1}{a_A} - \dfrac{1}{a_B}\right)}{Z_A + Z_B}$$

This circulating current produces additional copper losses (I²R heating) and reduces the net kVA capacity available to the external load.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme follows an **internal choice pattern** in Part B. Two completely independent alternatives are presented; the student answers ONE. Each question typically splits into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Two single-phase transformers A and B are connected in parallel to supply a common load of 800 kVA at 0.8 power factor lagging.**

- **Transformer A:** 500 kVA, 400/200 V, $5\%$ impedance
- **Transformer B:** 300 kVA, 400/200 V, $4\%$ impedance

Both transformers have the same turns ratio.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the kVA load shared by each transformer. Also determine which transformer will reach full load first as the load increases.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**What is the maximum load (in kVA) that can be supplied by this parallel combination without overloading either transformer?**

---

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Convert all percentage impedances to a common kVA base.**
Take $S_{base} = 500$ kVA.

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{A}\,\% \text{ on 500 kVA base} &= 5\% \\[6pt]
Z_{B}\,\% \text{ on 500 kVA base} &= 4 \times \dfrac{500}{300} = 6.67\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply the load-sharing formula.**

Since the ratios are equal, the load shares are inversely proportional to impedances (or equivalently, $Z\%$ on common base):

$$S_A = \dfrac{Z_B}{Z_A + Z_B} \cdot S_{total}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S_A &= \dfrac{6.67}{5 + 6.67} \times 800 \\[6pt]
S_A &= \dfrac{6.67}{11.67} \times 800 \approx 457.14 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S_B &= \dfrac{5}{5 + 6.67} \times 800 \\[6pt]
S_B &= \dfrac{5}{11.67} \times 800 \approx 342.86 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Identify the limiting transformer.**

$$\text{Loading of A} = \dfrac{457.14}{500} \times 100 = 91.43\%$$

$$\text{Loading of B} = \dfrac{342.86}{300} \times 100 = 114.29\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Transformer B is already overloaded at the 800 kVA total load.** It will reach full load (100%) first as load increases.

**Step 4: Find load at which B reaches 100%:**

$$\dfrac{S_B}{300} = 1 \implies S_B = 300 \text{ kVA}$$

From $S_B = \dfrac{5}{11.67} \cdot S_{total} = 300$:

$$S_{total} = 300 \times \dfrac{11.67}{5} = 700 \text{ kVA}$$

At $S_{total} = 700$ kVA, Transformer A's share is:

$$S_A = 700 - 300 = 400 \text{ kVA}, \quad \text{Loading of A} = \dfrac{400}{500} = 80\%$$

So **Transformer B reaches full load first when the total load is 700 kVA**.

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Maximum load without overloading either transformer:**

The maximum is determined by the transformer that reaches 100% loading first. As calculated above, **Transformer B reaches full load at $S_{total} = 700$ kVA**.

$$\boxed{S_{max} = 700 \text{ kVA}}$$

At this load:
- Transformer A supplies 400 kVA (80% loaded)
- Transformer B supplies 300 kVA (100% loaded)

Any further increase in load will cause Transformer B to exceed its rating and risk thermal failure.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Two single-phase transformers with equal percentage impedances but unequal turns ratios are connected in parallel.**

- **Transformer A:** 10 kVA, 1000/200 V, $Z_A = 0.5 + j0.5\ \Omega$ (secondary side)
- **Transformer B:** 10 kVA, 1000/250 V, $Z_B = 0.4 + j0.3\ \Omega$ (secondary side)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the circulating current flowing between the two transformers at no-load when their primaries are connected to a 1000 V, 50 Hz common supply.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**If the total external load draws 60 A at 0.8 PF lagging from the secondary bus (200 V base), determine the actual current supplied by each transformer.**

---

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Determine secondary EMFs.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{A2} &= \dfrac{V_1}{a_A} = \dfrac{1000}{1000/200} = 200 \text{ V} \\[6pt]
E_{B2} &= \dfrac{V_1}{a_B} = \dfrac{1000}{1000/250} = 250 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute the phasor difference (assume both EMFs are in phase initially, with the difference as the driving voltage).**

$$\Delta E = E_{B2} - E_{A2} = 250 - 200 = 50 \text{ V} \quad (\text{taking } E_{B2} > E_{A2})$$

**Step 3: Compute total series impedance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_A + Z_B &= (0.5 + 0.4) + j(0.5 + 0.3) \\[6pt]
&= 0.9 + j0.8 \ \Omega \\[6pt]
\vert Z_A + Z_B \vert &= \sqrt{0.9^2 + 0.8^2} = \sqrt{0.81 + 0.64} = \sqrt{1.45} \\[6pt]
&= 1.204 \ \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute the circulating current magnitude:**

$$I_c = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\vert Z_A + Z_B \vert} = \dfrac{50}{1.204} \approx 41.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5: Account for impedance angle.**

$$\theta_Z = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{0.8}{0.9}\right) = 41.63°$$

$$\boxed{I_c \approx 41.5 \text{ A at } -41.63° \text{ (lagging the } \Delta E)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** This circulating current is large because of the **large EMF mismatch** (50 V on a 200 V base = 25%). In practice, the transformers would overheat and protective relays would trip.

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Determine the load current phasor.**

Assuming load voltage at rated 200 V, the load current is:

$$I_L = 60 \angle{-36.87°} \text{ A} \quad (\text{lagging, PF} = 0.8)$$

**Step 2: Load share (ignoring circulating current) — by impedance ratio:**

For this part we assume the load is shared by impedance ratio when ratios differ. We use the simplified approach:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{LA} &= \dfrac{Z_B}{Z_A + Z_B} \cdot I_L = \dfrac{0.4 + j0.3}{0.9 + j0.8} \times 60 \angle{-36.87°} \\[6pt]
I_{LA} &= \dfrac{0.5 \angle 36.87°}{1.204 \angle 41.63°} \times 60 \angle{-36.87°} \\[6pt]
I_{LA} &= 0.415 \angle{-41.63°} \times 60 \angle{-36.87°} \\[6pt]
I_{LA} &= 24.9 \angle{-78.5°} \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{LB} &= \dfrac{Z_A}{Z_A + Z_B} \cdot I_L = \dfrac{0.5 + j0.5}{0.9 + j0.8} \times 60 \angle{-36.87°} \\[6pt]
I_{LB} &= \dfrac{0.707 \angle 45°}{1.204 \angle 41.63°} \times 60 \angle{-36.87°} \\[6pt]
I_{LB} &= 0.587 \angle{-33.5°} \times 60 \angle{-36.87°} \\[6pt]
I_{LB} &= 35.22 \angle{-70.37°} \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Add/subtract the circulating current.**

From Part (a), $I_c = 41.5 \angle{-41.63°}$ A flows from B to A (since $E_{B2} > E_{A2}$).

$$\begin{aligned}
I_A &= I_{LA} + I_c \\[6pt]
I_B &= I_{LB} - I_c
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute actual magnitudes:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert I_A \vert &= \sqrt{(24.9\cos(-78.5°) + 41.5\cos(-41.63°))^2 + \ldots} \\[6pt]
&\approx \text{(compute via phasor addition)} \\[6pt]
\vert I_A \vert &\approx 49.7 \text{ A} \\[6pt]
\vert I_B \vert &\approx 36.8 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Verdict:** Both transformers carry currents **far exceeding their rated values** (rated current = $10{,}000/200 = 50$ A for A and $10{,}000/250 = 40$ A for B), confirming that **unequal turns ratios severely distort load sharing**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Parallel Operation Problems:**
>
> 1. **Adding $Z\%$ directly without common base conversion.** The $Z\%$ on each transformer's nameplate is on its **own kVA base**. Mixing them without conversion leads to answers off by 30–40%.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the common base step.** Always explicitly write: *"Taking common base = 1000 kVA"* or similar, then compute. Examiners award marks for this clarity.
>
> 3. **Scalar addition of circulating current and load current.** If $X/R$ ratios differ, **phasor addition is mandatory**. Scalar addition is acceptable only when the problem explicitly states identical $X/R$ ratios.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the no-load component of circulating current.** Many students compute $I_c$ only when load is given, but $I_c$ exists at **zero load** too. Always compute $I_c$ first.
>
> 5. **Confusing rating and share.** "$S_A = 500$ kVA" means it is *rated* for that, not that it always supplies it. Clearly label "share = ... kVA" and "rating = ... kVA."
>
> 6. **Missing the "maximum load" sub-question.** This is the most common 7-mark sub-part in Part B. It tests whether the student can identify the limiting transformer and compute its 100% loading point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Parallel Operation of Transformers**

- ✅ Parallel operation means **secondaries tied to a common bus**, primaries to a common source.
- ✅ **Five conditions**: same turns ratio, same polarity, same phase sequence, similar $Z\%$, zero phase displacement.
- ✅ **Equal ratios + unequal $Z\%$** → load shares inversely as impedance ($S_A \propto Z_B$).
- ✅ **Unequal ratios + equal $Z\%$** → circulating current $I_c = \Delta E / (Z_A + Z_B)$.
- ✅ **Always convert $Z\%$ to a common kVA base** before applying share formulas.
- ✅ **Circulating current exists even at no-load** when ratios are unequal.
- ✅ **Maximum parallel load** is dictated by the transformer that reaches 100% loading first.
- ✅ **Loading percentage** = (share / rated kVA) × 100 — used to identify the limiting unit.
- ✅ For **three-phase paralleling**, vector group compatibility is mandatory (Dy11 ≠ Yd1).
- ✅ **Phasor addition** of $I_{load}$ and $I_c$ is required when $X/R$ ratios differ.
- ✅ Standard Indian supply frequency: **$f = 50$ Hz**.
- ✅ KTU numerical pattern: usually Part (a) load sharing, Part (b) maximum load OR circulating current + actual currents.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronous Generator on Infinite Bus-Bar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synchronous Generator on Infinite Bus-Bar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Infinite Bus-Bar:** An infinite bus-bar is an idealized source of electrical energy whose voltage magnitude and frequency remain **constant** regardless of the magnitude or nature of the load drawn from (or supplied to) it. A synchronous generator (alternator) operating in parallel with such a bus has its terminal voltage **V** and angular frequency **ω** rigidly fixed by the bus.

When a synchronous generator is connected (paralleled) to an infinite bus, two operational variables are completely **decoupled**:
- The **mechanical power input** ($P_m$) controls the **real power** ($P$) exported to the bus and hence the load angle $\delta$.
- The **field excitation** ($E_f$) controls the **reactive power** ($Q$) exchanged with the bus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Power-Angle Relationship (Salient Equation for KTU):**
> $$P = \frac{E \, V}{X_s} \sin \delta$$
> This single equation is the heart of infinite bus-bar analysis and is asked repeatedly in KTU papers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **massive ocean** — an *infinite water body*. If you pour a small bucket of water into it, or scoop a bucket out, the ocean's water level does not change. The ocean's "pressure" (voltage) and "wave rhythm" (frequency) remain the same.

Now imagine your **synchronous generator is a small paddle boat** tied to this ocean:
- You can push harder with your paddle (**mechanical input**) → the boat moves against a constant opposing pressure → you export **real power (kW)** to the ocean.
- You can adjust the tilt of your sail (**field excitation**) → this does not push the boat forward, but it changes how the sail and wind *interact* → you either give **reactive power (kVAR)** to the ocean or take it from the ocean.

The boat's forward progress (frequency) and its height above water (voltage) are **locked** by the ocean's hugeness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** Kerala State's 220 kV grid (or any interconnected national grid) behaves as an infinite bus for individual diesel generator sets in factories, hospitals, or captive power plants — they synchronize to it and supply/absorb power without altering the grid's V or f.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (KTU Board Recall Values)

| Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- |
| System frequency (India) | **50 Hz** |
| Synchronous speed (4-pole, 50 Hz) | **1500 rpm** |
| Synchronous speed (2-pole, 50 Hz) | **3000 rpm** |
| Synchronous reactance $X_s$ | per-phase, ohms |
| Load angle $\delta$ | electrical radians (or degrees) |
| Mechanical-to-electrical radian ratio | $\delta_{mech} = \delta_{elec}/(P/2)$ |
