# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-Conditions: Synchronization (Paralleling Procedure)

Before an alternator can be safely connected to an infinite bus, **four mandatory conditions** (often tested for 3 marks) must be satisfied:

1. **Equal RMS terminal voltages:** $|E_{gen}| = |V_{bus}|$
2. **Identical frequency:** $f_{gen} = f_{bus}$
3. **Same phase sequence** (RYB on both sides — not RBY).
4. **Zero phase displacement** at the instant of switching — corresponding phasors must be in phase (i.e., $\delta = 0$).

> [!WARNING]
> A single mismatch in any of the above can produce **enormous circulating currents** (limited only by $X_s$), mechanical shock, and possible damage to the machine windings. This is a favourite KTU short-answer question.

---

## 2.2 Synchronizing Methods (Lamp Methods)

| Method | Lamp Connection | Observation When Correct |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dark Lamp Method** | Each lamp across one corresponding phase | All three lamps **dark simultaneously** (since phase difference across each is zero) |
| **Bright Lamp Method** | Each lamp connected between *similar* phases cross-wise | All three lamps **glow bright equally** in unison |
| **Synchroscope Method** | Synchroscope instrument | Pointer remains **stationary at "in-phase" mark** |

---

## 2.3 The Power-Angle Equation (Centre of KTU Module 3)

When a cylindrical-rotor synchronous generator feeds an infinite bus of voltage $V$ through a synchronous reactance $X_s$, with internal EMF $E$ induced at load angle $\delta$ behind $V$:

**Real Power Exported:**
$$P = \frac{E \, V}{X_s} \sin \delta$$

This is derived by resolving current phasor $\bar{I}$ along and perpendicular to $\bar{V}$, and computing $P = 3 V I \cos\phi$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Observation:** Since $V$ and $\omega$ are now **constants** imposed by the infinite bus, $P$ depends ONLY on $E$ (excitation) and $\delta$ (mechanical input indirectly).

---

## 2.4 Synchronizing Power & Synchronizing Torque

The *slope* of the power-angle curve at the operating point defines the **stability** of operation.

**Synchronizing Power (per electrical radian):**
$$P_{sy} = \frac{dP}{d\delta} = \frac{E \, V}{X_s} \cos \delta$$

> [!NOTE]
> Positive $P_{sy}$ implies **steady-state stability** — a small perturbation in $\delta$ creates a restoring power that pushes the machine back to its operating point.

**Synchronizing Torque (3-phase machine, in mechanical rad/s):**
$$T_{sy} = \frac{P_{sy}}{\omega_s / P_{pairs}} \quad \text{or in per-electrical-radian form:} \quad T_{sy} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E \, V}{X_s} \cos \delta$$

where $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ is the synchronous angular frequency (electrical rad/s).

---

## 2.5 Effect of Changing Excitation (Field Current $I_f$)

With $P_m$ constant, an increase in $I_f$ increases $E$. Since $V$ is fixed and $P = \frac{EV}{X_s}\sin\delta$ must remain constant, $\delta$ must **decrease**. The new phasor diagram shows:

- **Over-excited** ($E > V$ component along V-axis): Machine delivers **lagging reactive power** to bus — acts like a *capacitor*.
- **Normally excited** ($E$ in phase with $V$): Unity power factor operation at the machine terminals.
- **Under-excited** ($E < V$ component): Machine **absorbs** reactive power — acts like an *inductor*.

---

## 2.6 Effect of Changing Mechanical Input

With $I_f$ (and hence $E$) constant, increasing $P_m$ increases $\delta$ such that $\sin\delta$ rises, exporting more real power. The frequency remains locked at $f_{bus}$ by the infinite bus.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{E V}{X_s} \sin\delta$ | Real power exported to infinite bus | KTU favourite — power-angle relation |
| 2 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{E V}{X_s}$ | Maximum transferable power at $\delta = 90^\circ$ | Stability limit |
| 3 | $P_{sy} = \dfrac{E V}{X_s} \cos\delta$ | Synchronizing power per electrical rad | Steady-state stability check |
| 4 | $T_{sy} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s}\dfrac{E V}{X_s}\cos\delta$ | Synchronizing torque (3-$\phi$) | Mechanical design question |
| 5 | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{E \cos\delta}{V}$ | Power factor at machine terminals | Determining p.f. after excitation change |
| 6 | $Q = \dfrac{E V}{X_s}\cos\delta - \dfrac{V^2}{X_s}$ | Reactive power supplied by generator | Reactive power V-curve analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize that for a generator on infinite bus, $V$ and $f$ are **given constants**, never unknowns. Solve problems by fixing the appropriate two variables based on the question stem.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Grid Code Compliance:** Synchronizing large thermal/hydro generators to the Indian grid (POWERGRID) requires all four conditions — a routine KTU viva and lab topic.
- **Load-Sharing:** Hydro and thermal units share real load by adjusting $P_m$ and share reactive load by adjusting $I_f$ — the *infinite bus assumption* simplifies this dispatch problem.
- **Reactor-less VAR Compensation:** Over-excited synchronous generators in industries (e.g., as *synchronous condensers*) supply lagging kVAR to improve plant power factor — again relying on infinite bus behaviour.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Power-Angle Equation

**Setup:** A 3-phase, Y-connected cylindrical rotor alternator with internal EMF per phase $\bar{E}$ at load angle $\delta$ behind terminal voltage $\bar{V}$, connected through synchronous reactance $X_s$ to an infinite bus of voltage $\bar{V}$.

Let us set $\bar{V}$ as the reference phasor:
$$\bar{V} = V \angle 0^\circ \quad ; \quad \bar{E} = E \angle \delta$$

The armature current per phase (neglecting $R_a$ as is standard practice):
$$\bar{I}_a = \frac{\bar{E} - \bar{V}}{j X_s}$$

Resolve $\bar{E}$ into rectangular components:
$$\bar{E} = E \cos\delta + j \, E \sin\delta$$

Therefore:
$$\bar{E} - \bar{V} = (E \cos\delta - V) + j \, (E \sin\delta)$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $-j$:
$$\bar{I}_a = \frac{(E \cos\delta - V) + j (E \sin\delta)}{j X_s} = \frac{E \sin\delta - j(E \cos\delta - V)}{X_s}$$

So the complex current:
$$\bar{I}_a = \frac{E \sin\delta}{X_s} - j \cdot \frac{(E \cos\delta - V)}{X_s}$$

The complex power per phase:
$$\bar{S}_{ph} = \bar{V} \, \bar{I}_a^{*} = V \angle 0^\circ \cdot \left[ \frac{E \sin\delta}{X_s} + j \cdot \frac{(E \cos\delta - V)}{X_s} \right]$$

Separating real and imaginary:
$$\bar{S}_{ph} = \frac{E V \sin\delta}{X_s} + j \, \frac{V(E \cos\delta - V)}{X_s}$$

Total three-phase complex power:
$$\bar{S}_{3\phi} = 3 \bar{S}_{ph} = \frac{3 E V \sin\delta}{X_s} + j \, \frac{3V(E \cos\delta - V)}{X_s}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{P = \frac{3 E V}{X_s} \sin\delta \quad \text{and} \quad Q = \frac{3V(E \cos\delta - V)}{X_s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a per-phase equivalent (often used in KTU), the 3 is dropped:
> $$P = \frac{E V}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Synchronizing Power

Synchronizing power is defined as the small change in electrical power $P$ per unit small change in load angle $\delta$, **evaluated at the operating point**:

$$P_{sy} = \left.\frac{\partial P}{\partial \delta}\right|_{\delta = \delta_0}$$

Starting from:
$$P = \frac{E V}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

Differentiating with respect to $\delta$:
$$\frac{dP}{d\delta} = \frac{E V}{X_s} \cos\delta$$

At the operating load angle $\delta_0$:
$$\boxed{P_{sy} = \frac{E V}{X_s} \cos\delta_0}$$

The condition for steady-state stability:
$$P_{sy} > 0 \implies \cos\delta_0 > 0 \implies \delta_0 < 90^\circ$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Limit:** The maximum real power transfer occurs at $\delta = 90^\circ$, giving $P_{max} = EV/X_s$. Operating beyond this leads to **pole-slipping** and loss of synchronism. KTU has asked this directly multiple times.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Synchronizing Torque (3-Phase)

Mechanical synchronous speed:
$$\omega_s = 2\pi f_s \text{ (electrical rad/s)}$$

For a $P$-pole machine, mechanical synchronous speed is:
$$\omega_m = \frac{2\omega_s}{P}$$

Synchronizing torque is the synchronizing power per mechanical radian:
$$T_{sy} = \frac{P_{sy}}{\omega_s} \quad \text{(for per-phase form with 3-}\phi\text{ power)}$$

Substituting:
$$\boxed{T_{sy} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E V}{X_s} \cos\delta_0}$$

If $P_{sy}$ is to be expressed per mechanical radian (KTU occasional question):
$$T_{sy,\,mech} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E V}{X_s} \cos\delta_0 \quad \text{for a } P\text{-pole machine}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Excitation Change Effects (at Constant $P_m$)

Assume generator operating at $P = P_0$ (constant) on infinite bus with $V$, $X_s$ fixed. Let $\delta_1$, $E_1$ be initial values, $\delta_2$, $E_2$ be final values.

| Condition | $\delta$ | $\cos\delta$ | $E \cos\delta$ | Power Factor $\cos\phi$ | Reactive Power |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Under-excited** ($E_2 < E_1$) | $\delta_2 > \delta_1$ | smaller | smaller | Leading | Machine **absorbs** Q |
| **Normally excited** | $\delta = \delta_{normal}$ | — | $= V$ | Unity | $Q = 0$ |
| **Over-excited** ($E_2 > E_1$) | $\delta_2 < \delta_1$ | larger | larger | Lagging | Machine **delivers** Q |

Derivation of $\cos\phi$:
$$\cos\phi = \frac{E \cos\delta}{V} \quad \text{(per-phase, neglecting } R_a\text{)}$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> A 3-phase, 6.6 kV, Y-connected, 50 Hz, 4-pole cylindrical-rotor synchronous generator has $X_s = 10\ \Omega$/phase. It is synchronized to an infinite bus of 6.6 kV (line). If excitation is adjusted so that $E = 5$ kV/phase and load angle is $30^\circ$ electrical, find:
> (i) Real power exported
> (ii) Synchronizing power at this operating point
> (iii) Maximum power transferable at this excitation
> (iv) Synchronizing torque (mechanical) for the 4-pole machine

**Given (per-phase):**
$$V_{ph} = \frac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5 \text{ V}; \quad E = 5000 \text{ V}; \quad X_s = 10\ \Omega; \quad \delta = 30^\circ$$

**(i) Real power per phase:**
$$P_{ph} = \frac{E V}{X_s}\sin\delta = \frac{5000 \times 3810.5}{10} \times \sin 30^\circ$$
$$P_{ph} = \frac{19{,}052{,}500}{10} \times 0.5 = 952{,}625 \text{ W} \approx 952.6 \text{ kW per phase}$$

**Total 3-phase real power:**
$$P_{3\phi} = 3 \times 952.6 = 2857.9 \text{ kW} \approx 2.86 \text{ MW}$$

**(ii) Synchronizing power per electrical radian (3-$\phi$):**
$$P_{sy} = \frac{3 E V}{X_s} \cos\delta = \frac{3 \times 5000 \times 3810.5}{10} \times \cos 30^\circ$$
$$P_{sy} = 5{,}715{,}750 \times 0.8660 = 4{,}949{,}839.5 \text{ W} \approx 4.95 \text{ MW/rad}$$

**(iii) Maximum transferable power:**
$$P_{max} = \frac{3 E V}{X_s} = \frac{3 \times 5000 \times 3810.5}{10} = 5{,}715{,}750 \text{ W} \approx 5.72 \text{ MW (at } \delta = 90^\circ\text{)}$$

**(iv) Synchronizing torque:**
$$\omega_s = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.159 \text{ rad/s (electrical)}$$
$$T_{sy} = \frac{P_{sy}}{\omega_s} = \frac{4{,}949{,}839.5}{314.159} = 15{,}754.7 \text{ N·m} \approx 15.75 \text{ kN·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Because the machine has $P = 4$ poles, mechanical $\omega_m = \omega_s / 2 = 157.08$ rad/s. The torque is the same whether computed per electrical or mechanical radian because the *real power* is the physical quantity.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Synchronization Lamp Connection (Dark Lamp Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GAA[GEN PHASE R] --- LA[L1]
    GAB[GEN PHASE Y] --- LB[L2]
    GAC[GEN PHASE B] --- LC[L3]
    BBA[BUS PHASE R] --- LA
    BBB[BUS PHASE Y] --- LB
    BBC[BUS PHASE B] --- LC
    LA -.WHEN IN SYNC.-> D1[DARK]
    LB -.WHEN IN SYNC.-> D2[DARK]
    LC -.WHEN IN SYNC.-> D3[DARK]
    style GAA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style GAB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style GAC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style BBA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style BBB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style BBC fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
```

> Each lamp $L_1$, $L_2$, $L_3$ is connected across corresponding phases (R-R, Y-Y, B-B). When phase, voltage, frequency and sequence match, the voltage across each lamp is zero — all lamps glow **dark simultaneously**.

---

## 4.2 Phasor Diagram — Generator on Infinite Bus (Over-excited vs. Under-excited)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PD[PHASOR SCENARIOS] --> A1[OVER EXCITED]
    PD --> A2[NORMALLY EXCITED]
    PD --> A3[UNDER EXCITED]
    A1 --> R1[E LEADS V BY DELTA]
    R1 --> O1[MACHINE DELIVERS LAGGING REACTIVE POWER Q]
    A2 --> R2[E AND V IN PHASE UNITY PF]
    R2 --> O2[Q EQUALS ZERO]
    A3 --> R3[E LAGS V BY DELTA]
    R3 --> O3[MACHINE ABSORBS LEADING REACTIVE POWER]
    style PD fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style A1 fill:#FFCCCB,stroke:#333
    style A2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style A3 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333
    style O1 fill:#FFEEEE,stroke:#333
    style O2 fill:#EEFFEE,stroke:#333
    style O3 fill:#EEEEFF,stroke:#333
```

> In all three cases, $\bar{V}$ is the reference (fixed by infinite bus). The locus of $\bar{E}$ for varying excitation is a circle of radius $E$ centred at the tip of $\bar{V}$, with $\delta$ varying accordingly to maintain $P$ constant.

---

## 4.3 Power-Angle Curve (KTU Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ORIGIN[ORIGIN] --> PAXIS[P WATTS]
    ORIGIN --> DAXIS[DELTA DEGREES]
    PAXIS --> PMAX[P MAX AT DELTA 90 DEGREES]
    DAXIS --> D30[30 DEG NORMAL OPERATION]
    DAXIS --> D90[90 DEG STABILITY LIMIT]
    D30 --> OP[OPERATING POINT]
    OP --> SLOPE[SYNCHRONIZING POWER SLOPE P_SY]
    style ORIGIN fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style PMAX fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
    style OP fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333
    style SLOPE fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333
```

> The curve $P = (EV/X_s)\sin\delta$ rises sinusoidally. Operating point on the **rising slope** gives positive $P_{sy}$ (stable). Operating past $90^\circ$ gives negative $P_{sy}$ (unstable) — the machine loses synchronism.

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Infinite Bus Operation Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[INFINITE BUS GRID] --> FIX[FIXED V AND FIXED F]
    FIX --> GEN[CONNECTED SYNCHRONOUS GENERATOR]
    MECH[MECHANICAL INPUT P_M] -->|REAL POWER P| GEN
    EXC[FIELD EXCITATION I_F] -->|REACTIVE POWER Q| GEN
    GEN --> OUT1[P EQUALS EV BY X_S SIN DELTA]
    GEN --> OUT2[Q EQUALS 3V BY X_S E COS DELTA MINUS V]
    GEN --> SYN[SYNCHRONIZING POWER P_SY EQUALS EV BY X_S COS DELTA]
    SYN --> STAB[STEADY STATE STABILITY CHECK]
    style IN fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,color:#FFFFFF
    style FIX fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333
    style GEN fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333
    style MECH fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style EXC fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
    style STAB fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
```

---

## 4.5 Synchronization Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START ALTERNATOR AND BRING TO NEAR RATED SPEED] --> S2[ADJUST EXCITATION TO MATCH BUS VOLTAGE]
    S2 --> S3[ADJUST PRIME MOVER TO MATCH BUS FREQUENCY]
    S3 --> S4[CHECK PHASE SEQUENCE USING PHASE SEQUENCE INDICATOR]
    S4 --> S5[CONNECT SYNCHRONIZING LAMPS OR SYNCHROSCOPE]
    S5 --> S6{OBSERVE LAMPS}
    S6 -->|ALL DARK BRIGHT OR SLOWLY DARK BRIGHT| S7[FINE TUNE SPEED TO SLOW DOWN LAMP FLICKER]
    S7 --> S8[CLOSE CIRCUIT BREAKER AT INSTANT LAMPS GO DARK]
    S8 --> S9[GENERATOR NOW PARALLELED TO INFINITE BUS]
    S6 -->|FAST FLICKERING OR UNSTEADY| S10[STOP AND RECHECK SEQUENCE OR VOLTAGE]
    style S1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style S8 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
    style S9 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333

```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State any four essential conditions required for synchronizing a 3-phase alternator to an infinite bus-bar.**

**Model Answer:**

The four essential conditions are:
1. **Equal RMS voltages:** The magnitude of the generated EMF must equal the infinite bus voltage ($|E_{gen}| = |V_{bus}|$).
2. **Same frequency:** The frequency of the generated voltage must equal the bus frequency ($f_{gen} = f_{bus} = 50$ Hz).
3. **Same phase sequence:** The phase sequence (e.g., RYB) of the generator must match that of the bus.
4. **Zero phase angle difference:** At the instant of switching, corresponding phase voltages must be in phase (load angle $\delta = 0$).

[3 Marks]

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define "infinite bus-bar" and explain why terminal voltage of a generator connected to it remains constant.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> An **infinite bus-bar** is an idealized electrical source whose voltage and frequency remain constant irrespective of the load connected to it, owing to its very large capacity compared to any single connected machine.

Since the bus capacity is theoretically infinite, the addition or removal of a finite load (the synchronizing generator) does not appreciably change the bus voltage magnitude or frequency. Therefore, for the connected generator, **V** and **f** are treated as rigid constants.

[3 Marks]

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ QUESTION A — Full Model Solution (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, Y-connected synchronous generator with synchronous reactance of $10\ \Omega$/phase is connected to an infinite bus of 6.6 kV (line). The generator excitation gives an EMF of 5 kV/phase.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

Draw the phasor diagram when the generator supplies rated load at 0.8 p.f. lagging and determine the load angle $\delta$.

**Solution:**

Per-phase bus voltage:
$$V_{ph} = \frac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5 \text{ V}$$

For a generator supplying load at power factor $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging, the current $\bar{I}_a$ lags $\bar{V}$ by $\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87^\circ$.

The voltage equation (per phase, neglecting $R_a$):
$$\bar{E} = \bar{V} + j X_s \bar{I}_a$$

Compute $jX_s\bar{I}_a$: it leads $\bar{I}_a$ by $90^\circ$, so it leads $\bar{V}$ by $(90^\circ - 36.87^\circ) = 53.13^\circ$.

The real and imaginary components of $\bar{E}$ with $\bar{V} = 3810.5 \angle 0^\circ$ V:

Real axis:
$$E \cos\delta = V_{ph} + X_s I_a \sin\phi$$

Imaginary axis:
$$E \sin\delta = X_s I_a \cos\phi$$

We need $I_a$. Without specified load, we use the rated EMF magnitude relation. From $E^2 = (V_{ph} + X_s I_a \sin\phi)^2 + (X_s I_a \cos\phi)^2$:

$$E^2 = V_{ph}^2 + 2 V_{ph} X_s I_a \sin\phi + X_s^2 I_a^2 (\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi)$$
$$E^2 = V_{ph}^2 + 2 V_{ph} X_s I_a \sin\phi + X_s^2 I_a^2$$

Given $E = 5000$ V, $V_{ph} = 3810.5$ V, $X_s = 10\ \Omega$, $\sin\phi = 0.6$:

$$25{,}000{,}000 = 14{,}519{,}900 + 2 \times 3810.5 \times 10 \times I_a \times 0.6 + 100 I_a^2$$

$$25{,}000{,}000 = 14{,}519{,}900 + 45{,}726 I_a + 100 I_a^2$$

$$100 I_a^2 + 45{,}726 I_a - 10{,}480{,}100 = 0$$

Solving the quadratic:
$$I_a = \frac{-45{,}726 + \sqrt{(45{,}726)^2 + 4 \times 100 \times 10{,}480{,}100}}{200}$$
$$I_a = \frac{-45{,}726 + \sqrt{2.0909 \times 10^9 + 4.192 \times 10^9}}{200}$$
$$I_a = \frac{-45{,}726 + \sqrt{6.283 \times 10^9}}{200} = \frac{-45{,}726 + 79{,}266.7}{200} \approx 167.7 \text{ A}$$

Now:
$$E \sin\delta = X_s I_a \cos\phi = 10 \times 167.7 \times 0.8 = 1341.6 \text{ V}$$
$$\sin\delta = \frac{1341.6}{5000} = 0.2683 \implies \boxed{\delta \approx 15.57^\circ}$$

**Phasor Diagram Description:**
- $\bar{V}$ drawn horizontally (reference), magnitude 3810.5 V.
- $\bar{I}_a$ lagging $\bar{V}$ by $36.87^\circ$.
- $jX_s\bar{I}_a$ perpendicular to $\bar{I}_a$, leading it by $90^\circ$.
- $\bar{E} = \bar{V} + jX_s\bar{I}_a$ — leads $\bar{V}$ by $\delta = 15.57^\circ$, magnitude 5000 V.

[7 Marks: 4 for numerical calculation + 3 for phasor description]

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

If the mechanical input is increased by 20% with excitation unchanged, find the new load angle and the new power factor.

**Solution:**

Since $V$, $X_s$, and $E$ remain unchanged (infinite bus + constant excitation), the new $P_2$ satisfies:
$$P_2 = 1.20 \times P_1$$

But the real power-angle relation gives:
$$P = \frac{E V}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

So:
$$\sin\delta_2 = 1.20 \times \sin\delta_1 = 1.20 \times 0.2683 = 0.3220$$
$$\boxed{\delta_2 \approx 18.77^\circ}$$

The new $E \cos\delta_2$:
$$E \cos\delta_2 = 5000 \times \cos(18.77^\circ) = 5000 \times 0.9466 = 4733.0 \text{ V}$$

New power factor:
$$\cos\phi_2 = \frac{E \cos\delta_2}{V_{ph}} = \frac{4733.0}{3810.5} = 1.242$$

> [!WARNING]
> This value exceeds 1 — a physical impossibility. **Conclusion:** At this rated excitation and a 20% overload, the machine **cannot** maintain synchronism with the infinite bus at the given $V_{ph}$. The maximum real power at this excitation is $P_{max} = EV/X_s \approx 1.905$ MW (per phase ~635 kW). KTU students often miss the feasibility check!

[7 Marks: 3 for $\delta_2$, 4 for analysis & p.f. discussion]

---

### ✅ QUESTION B — Alternative Full Model Solution (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz synchronous generator with $X_s = 8\ \Omega$/phase is synchronized to an infinite bus-bar of 3.3 kV (line). Operating data: $E = 2.2$ kV/phase, $\delta = 25^\circ$ electrical.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

Calculate the real power, reactive power, and power factor at the machine terminals.

**Solution:**

Per-phase voltage:
$$V_{ph} = \frac{3300}{\sqrt{3}} = 1905.3 \text{ V}; \quad E = 2200 \text{ V}; \quad \delta = 25^\circ$$

**Real Power (3-phase):**
$$P = \frac{3 E V_{ph}}{X_s} \sin\delta = \frac{3 \times 2200 \times 1905.3}{8} \times \sin 25^\circ$$
$$P = 1{,}571{,}873 \times 0.4226 = \boxed{664.4 \text{ kW}}$$

**Reactive Power (3-phase):**
$$Q = \frac{3 V_{ph}}{X_s}(E \cos\delta - V_{ph})$$
$$Q = \frac{3 \times 1905.3}{8} \times (2200 \times \cos 25^\circ - 1905.3)$$
$$Q = 714.5 \times (1994.4 - 1905.3) = 714.5 \times 89.1 = \boxed{63{,}651 \text{ VAR} \approx 63.65 \text{ kVAR (lagging)}}$$

**Power Factor:**
$$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{\sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}} = \frac{664{,}400}{\sqrt{664{,}400^2 + 63{,}651^2}}$$
$$\cos\phi = \frac{664{,}400}{\sqrt{4.414 \times 10^{11} + 4.052 \times 10^{9}}} = \frac{664{,}400}{\sqrt{4.454 \times 10^{11}}}$$
$$\cos\phi = \frac{664{,}400}{667{,}383} = \boxed{0.9955 \text{ (lagging)}}$$

[7 Marks: 3 for P, 2 for Q, 2 for p.f.]

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

If the excitation is increased by 15% while keeping the same real power output, determine the new load angle and the new reactive power supplied.

**Solution:**

New EMF: $E_2 = 1.15 \times 2200 = 2530$ V/phase.

Since real power $P$ is constant (same mechanical input):
$$\frac{E_1 V_{ph}}{X_s} \sin\delta_1 = \frac{E_2 V_{ph}}{X_s} \sin\delta_2$$
$$E_1 \sin\delta_1 = E_2 \sin\delta_2$$
$$2200 \times \sin 25^\circ = 2530 \times \sin\delta_2$$
$$\sin\delta_2 = \frac{2200 \times 0.4226}{2530} = \frac{929.7}{2530} = 0.3675$$
$$\boxed{\delta_2 = \sin^{-1}(0.3675) \approx 21.55^\circ}$$

**New Reactive Power:**
$$Q_2 = \frac{3 V_{ph}}{X_s}(E_2 \cos\delta_2 - V_{ph})$$
$$Q_2 = 714.5 \times (2530 \times \cos 21.55^\circ - 1905.3)$$
$$Q_2 = 714.5 \times (2530 \times 0.9297 - 1905.3)$$
$$Q_2 = 714.5 \times (2352.0 - 1905.3) = 714.5 \times 446.7$$
$$\boxed{Q_2 \approx 319{,}190 \text{ VAR} \approx 319.2 \text{ kVAR (lagging)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation (for viva):** Increasing excitation by 15% raised the lagging reactive power output by approximately **5×** (from 63.65 kVAR to 319.2 kVAR), while keeping real power nearly the same. This is the fundamental principle of **reactive power control by field excitation** on an infinite bus — heavily tested in KTU.

[7 Marks: 3 for $\delta_2$, 4 for new Q]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP REASONS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN THIS TOPIC:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert line voltage to phase voltage** when using the per-phase formula. KTU papers always specify line kV — you must divide by $\sqrt{3}$.
>
> 2. **Mixing 3-phase and per-phase formulas.** The equation $P = EV/X_s \sin\delta$ is per-phase. Multiplying by 3 gives total 3-phase power.
>
> 3. **Treating $V$ and $f$ as variables** when the question explicitly says "infinite bus-bar" — they are **constants**. This error can wreck an entire 14-mark answer.
>
> 4. **Not converting $\delta$ units** when computing $P_{sy}$ in W/degree vs W/radian: $P_{sy}$ (per degree) $= P_{sy}(\text{per rad}) \times \pi/180$.
>
> 5. **Ignoring feasibility checks** — students compute $\sin\delta > 1$ and don't recognize that the operating point is impossible (loss of synchronism).
>
> 6. **Confusing leading/lagging reactive power direction** when the generator is over- vs under-excited. Always draw the phasor diagram first — that resolves 90% of confusion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Infinite bus-bar** ⇒ terminal voltage **V** and frequency **f** are **constants**.
- [x] The four synchronization conditions: equal voltage, equal frequency, same phase sequence, in-phase.
- [x] Dark-lamp method: lamps dark in unison at synchronization instant.
- [x] Bright-lamp (cross-wise) method: lamps glow bright equally.
- [x] **Real power** $P = \dfrac{EV}{X_s}\sin\delta$ — controlled by **mechanical input**.
- [x] **Reactive power** $Q = \dfrac{3V}{X_s}(E\cos\delta - V)$ — controlled by **excitation**.
- [x] **Maximum power** $P_{max} = \dfrac{EV}{X_s}$ at $\delta = 90^\circ$ — **stability limit**.
- [x] **Synchronizing power** $P_{sy} = \dfrac{EV}{X_s}\cos\delta$ — must be **positive** for stability.
- [x] **Synchronizing torque** $T_{sy} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s}\dfrac{EV}{X_s}\cos\delta$.
- [x] **Over-excited** generator delivers **lagging** reactive power; **under-excited** absorbs reactive power.
- [x] Load angle $\delta$ **decreases** when excitation increases (at constant $P$); $\delta$ **increases** when $P_m$ increases (at constant $E$).
- [x] Operating beyond $\delta = 90^\circ$ causes **pole-slipping** and **loss of synchronism**.
- [x] Always draw the phasor diagram first to avoid sign/direction confusion.
- [x] For KTU 14-mark problems, **show every algebraic step** — partial credit is awarded for methodology.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of **DC Machines (Generators & Motors)** covered under Module 3 of PCEOT402, the term **"Conditions"** refers to a set of critical, mathematically derivable operating criteria that the machine must satisfy to achieve specific desired performance objectives. The principal conditions studied are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 – "Conditions" Core Scope:**
> 1. **Condition for Maximum Efficiency** of a DC Shunt Motor (Variable vs Constant losses criterion).
> 2. **Condition for Voltage Build-up** in a Self-Excited DC Shunt Generator (Critical Resistance & Critical Speed).
> 3. **Conditions for Successful Parallel Operation** of DC Shunt Generators (Equal Voltage, Same Polarity, Identical Load Sharing Characteristics).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Definition (Board Examiner Wording):**
> *"A condition in a DC machine is a mathematically expressible relationship between machine parameters (field current, armature current, resistance, speed) that must be satisfied to obtain a pre-defined operational goal — typically peak efficiency, voltage self-excitation, or stable load sharing when connected in parallel with another DC source."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Building the Student's Intuition

Imagine a **water tap connected to a kitchen sink**.

- When you open the tap a little, water trickles out — most of the energy is wasted in *friction* (constant loss) and a tiny bit carries the water (useful work). Efficiency is **poor**.
- When you open it fully, the water rushes out, but the pipe is too small to handle the flow — most of the energy is wasted in *turbulence inside the pipe* (variable loss, i.e., I²R). Efficiency is **again poor**.
- Somewhere **in the middle**, the friction and turbulence losses balance each other — and the water does the **maximum useful work per unit of energy supplied**. That sweet spot is the **"Condition for Maximum Efficiency"** of a DC motor.

For **parallel operation** of generators, think of **two water pumps feeding a common tank**:

- Both pumps must push water at the **same pressure (voltage)**.
- Their pressure (EMF) must rise in the **same direction (polarity)** — otherwise, one pump would be pushing against the other.
- They should share the load **fairly** based on their capacity, not let one pump do all the work while the other idles.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Expected Values)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Armature Resistance | $R_a$ | **0.05 Ω to 1.0 Ω** (typical) |
| Shunt Field Resistance | $R_{sh}$ | **50 Ω to 250 Ω** (typical) |
| Armature Reaction Constant | $K$ | Depends on machine design |
| Back EMF Constant | $K_a \phi$ | V/(rad/s) |
| Speed of DC Machine | $N$ | **rpm** (revolution per minute) |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | Dimensionless (expressed as **%**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The condition for maximum efficiency is purely a **loss-balancing condition**: the *copper (variable) loss must equal the constant (iron + friction + windage) loss*. This is a universal principle in energy-conversion devices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — The Three Core Conditions

### 2.1.1 Condition for Maximum Efficiency of a DC Shunt Motor

**Step 1 — Classify the losses in a DC Shunt Motor:**
- **Constant Losses ($P_c$):** Iron (core) loss + Friction & Windage loss + Field copper loss.
  - These are *practically independent* of armature current (load).
- **Variable Losses ($P_v$):** Armature copper loss = $I_a^2 \, R_a$.
  - These vary as the **square** of the armature current.

**Step 2 — Express efficiency mathematically:**

For a DC shunt motor, input power = $V \, I_L$ (where $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$), and output power = mechanical developed power at the shaft.

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}} = \frac{V I_L - P_c - I_a^2 R_a}{V I_L}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the calculus condition for maximum:**

Differentiate $\eta$ with respect to armature current $I_a$ and set $\dfrac{d\eta}{dI_a} = 0$.

**Step 4 — Arrive at the loss-balancing condition:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CONDITION FOR MAXIMUM EFFICIENCY:**
> $$\boxed{\text{Variable Copper Loss} \;=\; \text{Constant Loss}}$$
> $$\boxed{I_a^2 R_a \;=\; P_c}$$
> The armature current at maximum efficiency is:
> $$\boxed{I_{a,\, \text{max}\,\eta} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{R_a}}}$$

### 2.1.2 Condition for Voltage Build-up in a Self-Excited DC Shunt Generator

A self-excited generator must "wake itself up" using its own residual magnetism. The voltage build-up is governed by two boundary conditions:

**Step 1 — Initial Condition:** Some **residual flux** ($\phi_{\text{res}}$) must exist in the field poles.

**Step 2 — Field Circuit Excitation:**
$$
I_f = \dfrac{E_g}{R_f + R_{\text{series}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Two Critical Conditions:**

| Condition | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Critical (Critical) Field Resistance** | $\dfrac{dE_g}{dI_f} = R_f$ at the operating point | The slope of the OCC equals the field circuit resistance line. |
| **Critical Speed** | $N_{\text{crit}} = N \cdot \dfrac{R_f}{R_{f,\,\text{actual}}}$ | Below this speed, voltage will **not** build up. |

**Step 4 — Voltage Build-up Equation:**
$$
V = \dfrac{I_f R_f}{1 - \dfrac{R_f \cdot dI_f}{dE_g}}
$$

### 2.1.3 Conditions for Successful Parallel Operation of DC Shunt Generators

For two (or more) DC shunt generators to operate safely and stably in parallel:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Mandatory Conditions (Sequential Order):**
> 1. **Same Polarity** of terminal voltages (after connecting the switch, the voltmeter must read **zero**).
> 2. **Same Terminal Voltage** magnitude (EMF₁ ≈ EMF₂).
> 3. **Similar External Characteristics** (load-voltage curves) for proper load sharing.
> 4. **Drooping Characteristic** of the prime mover (speed must decrease as load increases) — this ensures **stable load sharing** without "hunting".

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{a,\text{max}\,\eta} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{R_a}}$ | Armature current at max efficiency | A |
| 2 | $P_{\text{out,\,max}\,\eta} = V I_a - 2 I_a^2 R_a$ | Output at max efficiency | W |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{V I_a - 2 I_a^2 R_a}{V I_a}$ | Maximum efficiency (with $I_{sh}$ neglected) | — |
| 4 | $P_c = I_a^2 R_a$ (at max $\eta$) | Loss-balancing condition | W |
| 5 | $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ (Generator) | Generator EMF equation | V |
| 6 | $E_b = V + I_a R_a$ (Motor) | Motor back-EMF equation | V |
| 7 | $R_f^{\text{crit}} = \left.\dfrac{dE_g}{dI_f}\right\vert_{\text{initial}}$ | Critical field resistance (slope of OCC) | Ω |
| 8 | $N_{\text{crit}} = N_{\text{actual}} \cdot \dfrac{R_{f,\,\text{actual}}}{R_{f}^{\text{crit}}}$ | Critical speed for build-up | rpm |
| 9 | $I_{L1} + I_{L2} = I_{\text{total}}$ (parallel) | KCL at common busbar | A |
| 10 | $E_{g1} - I_{a1}R_{a1} = E_{g2} - I_{a2}R_{a2}$ | Voltage equality for paralleling | V |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These conditions are foundational in **designing industrial DC drives**, **battery charging systems**, and **DC power stations** where multiple shunt generators operate in parallel to ensure redundancy and uninterrupted supply.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Condition for Maximum Efficiency of a DC Shunt Motor

### Assumptions
- Neglect shunt field current $I_{sh}$ compared to armature current $I_a$ (i.e., $I_L \approx I_a$).
- Supply voltage $V$ is constant.
- Constant losses $P_c$ are independent of load.

### Step 1: Write the Input and Output Power Expressions

For a DC shunt motor, the line current drawn from the supply is:

$$
I_L = I_a + I_{sh}
$$

Since $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ is a small constant, and for simplification (and as done in standard KTU solutions):

$$
I_L \approx I_a
$$

**Input Power:**

$$
P_{\text{in}} = V \cdot I_a
$$

**Total Losses in the motor** consist of constant losses and variable copper losses:

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = P_c + I_a^2 R_a
$$

**Output Power (at the shaft):**

$$
P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{loss}} = V I_a - P_c - I_a^2 R_a
$$

### Step 2: Formulate the Efficiency Expression

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{V I_a - P_c - I_a^2 R_a}{V I_a}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\eta = 1 - \dfrac{P_c + I_a^2 R_a}{V I_a}
$$

$$
\eta = 1 - \dfrac{P_c}{V I_a} - \dfrac{I_a R_a}{V}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Maximum Condition Using Calculus

To find the maximum efficiency, differentiate $\eta$ with respect to $I_a$ and equate to zero.

$$
\dfrac{d\eta}{dI_a} = 0 - \left( -\dfrac{P_c}{V I_a^2} \right) - \dfrac{R_a}{V} = 0
$$

$$
\dfrac{P_c}{V I_a^2} - \dfrac{R_a}{V} = 0
$$

Multiplying both sides by $V$:

$$
\dfrac{P_c}{I_a^2} - R_a = 0
$$

### Step 4: Solve for the Armature Current

$$
\dfrac{P_c}{I_a^2} = R_a
$$

$$
I_a^2 = \dfrac{P_c}{R_a}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{a,\,\text{max}\,\eta} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{R_a}}}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute Back to Get the Loss-Balancing Condition

Multiplying both sides of the above equation by $R_a$:

$$
I_a^2 R_a = P_c
$$

This proves that at maximum efficiency, the **armature copper loss equals the constant (iron + friction + windage) loss**.

### Step 6: Compute the Maximum Efficiency Value

Substituting $P_c = I_a^2 R_a$ into the output power expression:

$$
P_{\text{out,\,max}} = V I_a - I_a^2 R_a - I_a^2 R_a = V I_a - 2 I_a^2 R_a
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{V I_a - 2 I_a^2 R_a}{V I_a} = 1 - \dfrac{2 I_a R_a}{V}
$$

### Step 7: Final Compact Form (KTU Board Solution Format)

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{max}} = 1 - \dfrac{2 \, I_{a,\,\text{max}\,\eta} \, R_a}{V}}
$$

> **Verification by Second Derivative Test:** Differentiating $\dfrac{d\eta}{dI_a}$ once more gives $\dfrac{2 P_c}{V I_a^3} > 0$, confirming a **minimum** for $\dfrac{d\eta}{dI_a}$ at the critical point, which corresponds to a **maximum** of $\eta$. (Standard KTU examiner verification.)

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Field Resistance Condition for Voltage Build-up

The generated EMF in a self-excited DC shunt generator at no-load is:

$$
E_g = f(I_f)
$$

This is the **Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC)** — a non-linear curve.

The field circuit equation is:

$$
V = I_f \cdot R_f
$$

In the linear plot of $E_g$ vs $I_f$, this is a straight line through the origin with slope $R_f$.

For voltage to build up, this line must intersect the OCC **above the residual voltage point**.

The **critical condition** is when the field-resistance line is **tangent to the initial linear portion of the OCC**:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{dE_g}{dI_f}\bigg\vert_{\text{at residual point}} = R_f^{\text{crit}}}
$$

If the actual $R_f > R_f^{\text{crit}}$, the line is flatter than the OCC tangent and voltage **builds up**.
If $R_f < R_f^{\text{crit}}$, the line is steeper and voltage **collapses**.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Parallel Operation

| Condition | Mathematical Check | Practical Test | Consequence of Violation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polarity Match | $V_{\text{gen1}} = +V_{\text{gen2}}$ | Voltmeter across open switch reads 0 V | Massive short-circuit current; switch explosion |
| Voltage Match | $\vert E_{g1} - E_{g2} \vert \to 0$ | Adjust $R_f$ of incoming generator | Circulating current; unequal load sharing |
| Similar Characteristics | $E_{g1}(I_{L1}) \approx E_{g2}(I_{L2})$ | Plot external characteristics | Unstable load sharing; "fighting" between machines |
| Prime Mover Droop | $\dfrac{dN}{dP_L} < 0$ | Governor characteristic test | Hunting, oscillation, instability |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Loss Partitioning in a DC Shunt Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Supply Voltage V]:::inp --> B[Input Power P_in = V * I_L]:::inp
    B --> C[Armature Current I_a]:::inp
    B --> D[Shunt Field Current I_sh]:::inp
    C --> E[Variable Loss: I_a^2 R_a]:::var
    C --> F[Mech Power developed: E_b * I_a]:::mech
    D --> G[Field Copper Loss: I_sh^2 R_sh = constant]:::const
    F --> H[Constant Losses: P_c - Iron + Friction + Windage]:::const
    H --> I[Shaft Output Power P_out]:::out
    F --> I
    E --> J[Total Losses]:::var
    G --> J
    H --> J
    J --> K[Efficiency eta = P_out / P_in]:::eta
    I --> K
    K --> L{Condition Check}:::cond
    L -- E_loss = P_c --> M[Maximum Efficiency Achieved]:::max
    L -- E_loss not equal P_c --> N[Sub-optimal Efficiency]:::sub

    classDef inp fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef var fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef mech fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef const fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef out fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef eta fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef cond fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef max fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef sub fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Voltage Build-up in a Self-Excited DC Shunt Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Residual Flux phi_res]:::res --> B[Induced EMF E_g]:::emf
    B --> C[Field Current I_f = E_g / R_f]:::fc
    C --> D[Increased Flux phi]:::flux
    D --> E[Higher EMF E_g_new]:::emf
    E --> F{Build-up Condition Met?}:::cond
    F -- Yes --> G[Stable Voltage V_0]:::stab
    F -- No --> H[Voltage Collapses]:::fail
    F -- Tangent --> I[Critical Field Resistance R_f_crit]:::crit

    classDef res fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef emf fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef fc fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef flux fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef cond fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef stab fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef fail fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef crit fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Parallel Operation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GEN1[DC Shunt Generator 1]
        G1E[EMF E_g1]:::gen1
        G1A[Armature R_a1]:::gen1
        G1F[Field R_f1]:::gen1
    end
    subgraph GEN2[DC Shunt Generator 2]
        G2E[EMF E_g2]:::gen2
        G2A[Armature R_a2]:::gen2
        G2F[Field R_f2]:::gen2
    end
    SW[Equalizer Switch SW]:::sw
    BUS[Common Bus-bar V]:::bus
    LOAD[Common Load I_L]:::load

    G1E --> G1A --> SW
    G2E --> G2A --> SW
    SW --> BUS
    G1F --> BUS
    G2F --> BUS
    BUS --> LOAD
    G1F -.->|V_f1 = V_f2| G2F

    classDef gen1 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef gen2 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef sw fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef bus fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef load fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q1. State and prove the condition for maximum efficiency of a DC shunt motor. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

**Condition:** A DC shunt motor operates at maximum efficiency when the **variable loss (armature copper loss) equals the constant loss (iron + friction + windage losses).**

**Proof:** For a DC shunt motor, assuming $I_L \approx I_a$,

$$
\eta = 1 - \dfrac{P_c + I_a^2 R_a}{V I_a} = 1 - \dfrac{P_c}{V I_a} - \dfrac{I_a R_a}{V}
$$

Differentiating with respect to $I_a$ and equating to zero:

$$
\dfrac{d\eta}{dI_a} = \dfrac{P_c}{V I_a^2} - \dfrac{R_a}{V} = 0
$$

This yields $I_a^2 R_a = P_c$, i.e., **variable loss = constant loss**. ∎

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. List any three conditions that must be satisfied before connecting two DC shunt generators in parallel. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

The three essential conditions are:

1. **Same Polarity:** The terminal voltages of the two generators must have the **same polarity** (verified by a voltmeter reading **zero** across the open switch before closing it).
2. **Equal Terminal Voltage:** The generated EMFs must be **approximately equal** in magnitude ($E_{g1} \approx E_{g2}$).
3. **Similar External Characteristics:** The two generators should have **similar load-voltage curves** to ensure stable and proportional load sharing.

*(Other accepted conditions: drooping prime-mover characteristic, proper field rheostat adjustment.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**A(a). Derive the condition for maximum efficiency of a DC shunt motor. A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω and a field resistance of 250 Ω. The constant losses (iron + friction) amount to 500 W. Find: (i) the armature current at maximum efficiency, and (ii) the maximum efficiency. Take $I_L \approx I_a$.  [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:** As shown in Section 3.1 above,

$$
I_{a,\text{max}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{R_a}}
$$

**Given Data:**
- $V = 250$ V, $R_a = 0.5$ Ω, $P_c = 500$ W.

**(i) Armature current at maximum efficiency:**

$$
I_{a,\text{max}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{500}{0.5}} = \sqrt{1000} \approx 31.62 \text{ A}
$$

**(ii) Maximum efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{max}} = 1 - \dfrac{2 \, I_{a,\text{max}} \, R_a}{V} = 1 - \dfrac{2 \times 31.62 \times 0.5}{250}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{max}} = 1 - \dfrac{31.62}{250} = 1 - 0.1265 = 0.8735
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{max}} \approx 87.35\%}
$$

---

**A(b). Explain with a neat sketch the conditions for voltage build-up in a self-excited DC shunt generator. Define critical field resistance and critical speed.  [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (Examiner's Key Points):**

**Necessary Conditions for Voltage Build-up:**

1. **Presence of residual magnetism** in the field poles.
2. The **field winding connection** must be such that the field current produced by the induced EMF **aids** the residual flux (proper field polarity).
3. The **field circuit resistance** must be **less than the critical field resistance**.
4. The **speed of rotation** must be **above the critical speed**.

**Definitions:**

- **Critical Field Resistance ($R_f^{\text{crit}}$):** It is the slope of the tangent drawn to the **initial linear portion of the Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC)** at the residual flux point. Mathematically:
$$
R_f^{\text{crit}} = \left.\dfrac{dE_g}{dI_f}\right\vert_{\text{at residual}}
$$
- **Critical Speed ($N_{\text{crit}}$):** It is the minimum speed at which the shunt generator will just **fail to build up voltage** for a given field resistance. Below this speed, voltage build-up is not possible.

**Sketch (ASCII representation accepted in answer scripts):**

```
E_g |
    |            ___--- OCC
    |        _-/
    |     _-/  ←——— Critical Resistance line
    |   _/        (tangent to OCC at residual)
    | _/  
    |/ ← Residual voltage
    +—————————————————————> I_f
            Slope = R_f
```

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**B(a). Two DC shunt generators each rated at 100 kW, 250 V are to operate in parallel. The external characteristics of the two machines are approximately linear with voltage drops of 5% and 7% of rated voltage from no-load to full-load. Determine: (i) the load shared by each machine when the total load is 600 A, and (ii) the bus-bar voltage at this load.  [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Rated: 100 kW, 250 V → Rated current per machine = 100,000/250 = 400 A.
- Voltage drops: 5% and 7% of 250 V → $\Delta V_1 = 12.5$ V, $\Delta V_2 = 17.5$ V.

**Step 1: Slope of External Characteristics**

$$
m_1 = \dfrac{\Delta V_1}{I_{L1,\text{full}}} = \dfrac{12.5}{400} = 0.03125 \text{ V/A}
$$

$$
m_2 = \dfrac{\Delta V_2}{I_{L2,\text{full}}} = \dfrac{17.5}{400} = 0.04375 \text{ V/A}
$$

**Step 2: Setting up the equations**

For parallel operation, the **common terminal voltage** is $V$:

$$
V = 250 - 0.03125 \, I_1 = 250 - 0.04375 \, I_2
$$

$$
I_1 + I_2 = 600 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $I_2 = 600 - I_1$**

$$
0.03125 \, I_1 = 0.04375 \, (600 - I_1)
$$

$$
0.03125 \, I_1 = 26.25 - 0.04375 \, I_1
$$

$$
(0.03125 + 0.04375) I_1 = 26.25
$$

$$
0.075 \, I_1 = 26.25
$$

$$
\boxed{I_1 = 350 \text{ A}, \quad I_2 = 250 \text{ A}}
$$

**Step 4: Bus-bar voltage**

$$
V = 250 - 0.03125 \times 350 = 250 - 10.9375 = 239.0625 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{bus}} \approx 239.06 \text{ V}}
$$

---

**B(b). A 200 V DC shunt motor takes a no-load current of 4 A. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω and the field resistance is 200 Ω. Calculate: (i) the constant losses, (ii) the armature current at maximum efficiency, and (iii) the maximum efficiency.  [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $V = 200$ V, $I_{L0} = 4$ A (no-load), $R_a = 0.2$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 200$ Ω.

**Step 1: Compute shunt field current**

$$
I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{200}{200} = 1 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2: No-load armature current**

$$
I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_{sh} = 4 - 1 = 3 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3: Constant losses (input at no-load = sum of all losses)**

No-load input power:

$$
P_{\text{in,0}} = V \times I_{L0} = 200 \times 4 = 800 \text{ W}
$$

No-load armature copper loss:

$$
P_{cu,a0} = I_{a0}^2 R_a = 3^2 \times 0.2 = 1.8 \text{ W}
$$

Field copper loss (constant):

$$
P_{cu,sh} = V \cdot I_{sh} = 200 \times 1 = 200 \text{ W}
$$

Therefore, constant losses (iron + friction + windage):

$$
P_c = P_{\text{in,0}} - P_{cu,a0} - P_{cu,sh} = 800 - 1.8 - 200 = 598.2 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_c \approx 598.2 \text{ W}}
$$

**Step 4: Armature current at maximum efficiency**

$$
I_{a,\text{max}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{R_a}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{598.2}{0.2}} = \sqrt{2991} \approx 54.69 \text{ A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{a,\text{max}} \approx 54.69 \text{ A}}
$$

**Step 5: Maximum efficiency**

$$
\eta_{\text{max}} = 1 - \dfrac{2 \, I_{a,\text{max}} R_a}{V} = 1 - \dfrac{2 \times 54.69 \times 0.2}{200}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{max}} = 1 - \dfrac{21.876}{200} = 1 - 0.1094 = 0.8906
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{max}} \approx 89.06\%}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_a$:** Many students forget to subtract $I_{sh}$ from the line current. In Part B numericals, **always state the assumption** clearly: "$I_L \approx I_a$ for simplicity" or compute the exact value.
> 2. **Ignoring the residual flux condition:** A common error in voltage build-up questions is stating only the critical resistance/speed condition but **forgetting the residual magnetism** requirement (which is the *first* and most fundamental condition).
> 3. **Sign Convention Errors:** In generator equations, students often write $V = E_g + I_a R_a$ instead of $V = E_g - I_a R_a$. Remember: **drop is always in the direction of current flow.**
> 4. **Parallel Operation Sequence:** The conditions must be checked in the **correct order** — polarity first, then voltage magnitude, then characteristics. Random ordering costs marks for **methodology**.
> 5. **Units & Significant Figures:** Always quote final answers with proper units (A, V, %) and avoid premature rounding during intermediate steps.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Condition for Maximum Efficiency** = Variable copper loss = Constant loss ($I_a^2 R_a = P_c$).
- [x] **Current at max efficiency:** $I_{a,\text{max}} = \sqrt{P_c / R_a}$ (amperes).
- [x] **Max efficiency formula:** $\eta_{\text{max}} = 1 - \dfrac{2 I_{a,\text{max}} R_a}{V}$ (dimensionless).
- [x] **Voltage Build-up needs:** Residual flux + Proper field polarity + $R_f < R_f^{\text{crit}}$ + $N > N_{\text{crit}}$.
- [x] **Critical Field Resistance** = slope of tangent to initial linear portion of OCC.
- [x] **Critical Speed** = minimum speed below which voltage build-up is impossible.
- [x] **Parallel Operation Conditions:** (1) Same polarity, (2) Equal voltage, (3) Similar characteristics, (4) Drooping prime-mover.
- [x] **KTU Favourite Numerical:** Two shunt generators with linear droops → solve using simultaneous equations $V = E_{g1} - m_1 I_1 = E_{g2} - m_2 I_2$ and $I_1 + I_2 = I_{\text{total}}$.
- [x] **Always** quote the no-load input power when finding constant losses from no-load test data.
- [x] **Remember:** In a DC motor, $E_b = V - I_a R_a$; in a DC generator, $V = E_g - I_a R_a$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Synchronisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methods of Synchronisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Synchronisation** of an alternator is the precise procedural operation of connecting a 3-phase synchronous generator (alternator) to a live **infinite bus bar** (an already-energised, large power system whose voltage and frequency remain constant irrespective of the load on the incoming machine) such that the generator seamlessly shares the load without any transient inrush current, mechanical shock, or voltage disturbance.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, it is classified as a *Pre-paralleling Procedure* governed by the **Synchronising Conditions** (also called the *Four Conditions of Parallel Operation*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Methods of Synchronisation of alternators include:
> 1. Three-Dark Lamp Method (Dark Lamp Method)
> 2. Two-Bright and One-Dark Lamp Method (Rotating Lamp Method / Lamp Synchroniser)
> 3. Synchroscope Method
> 4. Modern Automatic / Static Synchronisers

## 1.2 The Four Mandatory Conditions of Synchronisation

For a *perfect* synchronisation without any circulating current or torque冲击, the following four conditions must be satisfied **simultaneously** at the instant of closing the circuit breaker:

| # | Condition | Physical Meaning |
|---|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Equal RMS Voltage Magnitude** | $E_1 = E_2$ (incoming generator EMF = bus bar voltage) |
| 2 | **Equal Frequency** | $f_1 = f_2$ (incoming machine frequency = bus bar frequency, typically **50 Hz** in India) |
| 3 | **Identical Phase Sequence** | R-Y-B sequence of the incoming machine must match the bus bar sequence (R-Y-B) |
| 4 | **Zero Phase Angle Difference** | Phase angle between the two voltages must be **zero** ($\delta = 0^\circ$) at the moment of switching |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Merging Traffic Lanes" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Two Rivers Merging into One:**
> Imagine two streams of water flowing toward a junction. For the merger to be smooth, *no splash, no whirlpool*:
> - **Voltage match** = The water levels (heights) of both streams must be **equal** (otherwise a "head" pushes water back).
> - **Frequency match** = The flow speed of water in both streams must be **identical** (otherwise turbulence).
> - **Phase match** = The wave crests must arrive at the junction **at the exact same instant** (otherwise they crash into each other).
> - **Phase sequence** = The *direction of swirl* (clockwise vs counter-clockwise) must be the same in both streams.
>
> The **synchronising process** is essentially the engineer's "valve control" — adjusting the incoming alternator's **field excitation** (voltage) and **prime-mover speed** (frequency) until all four conditions are simultaneously met.

## 1.4 Why Synchronisation is Necessary

If synchronisation is performed incorrectly:
- A large **circulating current** flows between the machines (given by $I_{circ} = (E_1 - E_2)/Z_s$, where $Z_s$ is the synchronous impedance).
- This causes **severe mechanical shock** to the shaft and windings.
- The **protective relays** of the system will trip the breaker instantaneously, causing synchronisation failure.
- In the worst case, the alternator can be permanently damaged.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common student error is forgetting the **phase sequence** condition. The lamps will go dark simultaneously *only if* the phase sequence is correct. If the sequence is reversed, the lamps will light up in a *rotating sequence* even when voltage and frequency match.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Electrical Principle

Consider two 3-phase voltage sources — the **incoming alternator** with induced EMF $E_1$ at frequency $f_1$, and the **bus bar** with voltage $V$ at frequency $f_2$. At the moment *just before* closing the paralleling switch, the phasor difference between $E_1$ and $V$ is:

$$\Delta V = E_1 \angle{\delta} - V \angle{0}$$

If the switch is closed when $\Delta V \neq 0$, a **transient circulating current** $I_{circ}$ flows between the two sources, given by:

$$I_{circ} = \frac{E_1 \angle{\delta} - V \angle{0}}{Z_s}$$

where $Z_s = R_a + jX_s$ is the **synchronous impedance** of the incoming machine.

### 2.1.1 Why Frequency Must Match (The "Beating" Phenomenon)

When the frequencies differ ($f_1 \neq f_2$), the relative phase angle $\delta$ between $E_1$ and $V$ continuously changes at a rate equal to the **slip frequency**:

$$f_s = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert$$

This is the principle by which the *lamp brightness* oscillates — the lamps "breathe" (go from bright to dark) at exactly this slip frequency $f_s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Power Grid Connection:** Every thermal, hydro, and nuclear power plant in Kerala (KSEB grid) uses these synchronisation procedures before feeding power into the **SRPC (Southern Regional Power Committee)** grid.
> - **Diesel Generators:** DG sets in hospitals/data centres use *automatic synchronisers* to connect to the standby bus.
> - **Ship & Aircraft Power Systems:** Where multiple alternators run in parallel for redundancy.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield items — memorise these verbatim.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|----------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_1 = V$ (RMS magnitude) | Voltage match condition | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $f_1 = f_2 = 50$ Hz (India) | Frequency match condition | Hertz (Hz) |
| 3 | $\delta = 0^\circ$ | Phase angle match | Degrees or rad |
| 4 | $f_s = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert$ | Slip frequency (rate of lamp flickering) | Hz |
| 5 | $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ | Slip angular frequency | rad/s |
| 6 | $I_{circ} = \dfrac{E_1 \angle\delta - V \angle 0}{Z_s}$ | Circulating current at improper sync | Amperes (A) |
| 7 | $Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$ | Synchronous impedance | $\Omega$ (Ohms) |
| 8 | $\delta = (f_1 - f_2) \cdot 360^\circ \cdot t$ | Instantaneous phase angle between sources | Degrees |
| 9 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed of alternator | RPM |
| 10 | Period of lamp flicker: $T = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | Time between successive dark instants | Seconds (s) |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Synchronisation Procedure (KTU Standard Operating Protocol)

1. **Start the prime mover** (diesel engine / turbine) and bring the alternator up to its **rated speed** (approximately 1500 RPM for a 4-pole, 50 Hz machine).
2. **Adjust the field excitation** (DC field current) using the **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator)** or field rheostat until the generated EMF $E_1$ exactly equals the bus bar voltage $V$.
3. **Fine-tune the prime-mover speed** so that $f_1$ is *very close* to but slightly above $f_2$ (typically $f_1 = f_2 + 0.5$ Hz) so that the incoming machine acts as a **generator** (not a motor) after paralleling.
4. **Verify phase sequence** using a **phase sequence indicator** (or by observing the rotating lamp pattern — see Section 3).
5. **Wait for the moment of synchronism** — the instant when all three lamps go dark (in 3-dark method) or when the synchroscope pointer is at the 12 o'clock red mark.
6. **Close the circuit breaker** at this exact instant.
7. **Transfer the load** from the prime mover governor to the desired power output.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Mathematical Basis of Lamp Flickering

Consider the voltage across one of the synchronising lamps. If the bus bar phase is $V_m \sin(\omega_1 t)$ and the corresponding incoming alternator phase is $E_m \sin(\omega_2 t + \phi)$, the instantaneous voltage across the lamp is:

$$v_L(t) = E_m \sin(\omega_2 t + \phi) - V_m \sin(\omega_1 t)$$

Using the trigonometric identity for difference of sines:

$$\sin A - \sin B = 2 \cos\!\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin\!\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$$

Let $A = \omega_2 t + \phi$ and $B = \omega_1 t$. Then:

$$\frac{A+B}{2} = \frac{(\omega_2 + \omega_1)t + \phi}{2}$$

$$\frac{A-B}{2} = \frac{(\omega_2 - \omega_1)t + \phi}{2}$$

So the lamp voltage becomes:

$$v_L(t) = 2 \cos\!\left(\frac{(\omega_1 + \omega_2)t + \phi}{2}\right) \sin\!\left(\frac{(\omega_2 - \omega_1)t + \phi}{2}\right)$$

This can be interpreted as a **high-frequency carrier** (at the average frequency $\frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}$) whose **amplitude is modulated** at the slip frequency $\omega_s = \omega_2 - \omega_1$.

### 3.1.1 Key Conclusions from the Derivation

- The **lamp voltage amplitude** is modulated by a sine wave of frequency $f_s = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert$.
- The lamp reaches **maximum brightness** twice per slip cycle (when $\sin(\omega_s t / 2) = \pm 1$).
- The lamp is **fully dark** when $\sin(\omega_s t / 2) = 0$, i.e., at instants where the two voltages are in **exact phase opposition** *or* **exact phase coincidence** across the specific lamp's connection.

## 3.2 Detailed Analysis: Why Three-Dark Lamp Method Works

In the **3-Dark Lamp Method**, lamps are connected as follows:

- **Lamp 1 (L1)**: Between phase R of incoming and phase R of bus bar.
- **Lamp 2 (L2)**: Between phase Y of incoming and phase Y of bus bar.
- **Lamp 3 (L3)**: Between phase B of incoming and phase B of bus bar.

The voltage across each lamp is the **phase voltage difference** of the corresponding phases. For all three lamps to be dark simultaneously:

$$v_{L1}(t) = v_{L2}(t) = v_{L3}(t) = 0$$

This requires the three phasors $E_1 \angle\delta, E_1 \angle(\delta - 120^\circ), E_1 \angle(\delta + 120^\circ)$ to coincide *exactly* with $V \angle 0^\circ, V \angle(-120^\circ), V \angle(+120^\circ)$ respectively. This is only possible if:

1. $\delta = 0^\circ$ (phase coincidence), AND
2. $E_1 = V$ (magnitude match), AND
3. $f_1 = f_2$ (frequency match), AND
4. The phase sequence is identical (R-Y-B matches R-Y-B).

> [!NOTE]
> **Disadvantage of 3-Dark Method:** It is impossible to determine the *direction of approach* to synchronism (whether the incoming machine is running *fast* or *slow*). This is overcome by the **2-bright, 1-dark method**.

## 3.3 Detailed Analysis: Two-Bright and One-Dark Lamp Method

In this method, the lamp connections are *crossed*:

- **Lamp 1 (L1)**: Between R of incoming and R of bus bar → goes **DARK** at synchronism.
- **Lamp 2 (L2)**: Between Y of incoming and **B** of bus bar → goes **BRIGHT** at synchronism.
- **Lamp 3 (L3)**: Between B of incoming and **Y** of bus bar → goes **BRIGHT** at synchronism.

### 3.3.1 Voltage Across the "Bright" Lamps

For Lamp L2 connected between Y (incoming) and B (bus bar):

$$v_{L2}(t) = E_m \sin(\omega_2 t - 120^\circ) - V_m \sin(\omega_1 t + 120^\circ)$$

At exact synchronism ($f_1 = f_2$, $E_m = V_m$, and $\delta = 0$ between like phases):

$$v_{L2} = V_m [\sin(\omega t - 120^\circ) - \sin(\omega t + 120^\circ)]$$

Using the identity $\sin(A) - \sin(B) = 2 \cos\!\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin\!\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$:

$$v_{L2} = V_m \cdot 2 \cos(\omega t) \sin(-120^\circ)$$

$$v_{L2} = -2 \sqrt{3} \cdot V_m \cos(\omega t)$$

This is a sinusoid with **peak amplitude $2\sqrt{3} \cdot V_m$** — meaning the bright lamps receive **line-to-line** voltage even at synchronism.

The **RMS** value of voltage across the bright lamps is $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$, i.e., the **full line voltage** — so they glow brightly.

> [!TIP]
> **Direction of Rotation of Brightness Pattern:**
> - If the incoming alternator runs **SLOW** ($f_1 < f_2$), the bright lamps darken in the order L2 → L3 → L2 → L3 (a particular direction).
> - If **FAST** ($f_1 > f_2$), the order reverses.
> - The engineer adjusts the prime-mover throttle so that the dark period is *longer* (slower beating) and synchronises at the dark moment.

## 3.4 Synchroscope — The Modern Precision Instrument

The synchroscope is essentially a **split-phase induction motor** with:

- One winding (the **polarising winding**) connected across one phase of the bus bar and the corresponding phase of the incoming machine.
- A second winding (the **rotating field winding**) connected through a **phase-splitting network** (typically a resistance in one branch and a reactance in the other, producing a 90° phase shift) across two of the bus bar phases.

### 3.4.1 Working Principle

The pointer of the synchroscope rotates at the **slip frequency** $f_s$. The direction of rotation indicates whether the incoming machine is running **fast** or **slow**:

- **Pointer rotating in the "FAST" direction** (clockwise typically) → incoming frequency is too high → *reduce* prime-mover throttle.
- **Pointer rotating in the "SLOW" direction** → incoming frequency is too low → *increase* throttle.
- **Pointer stationary at the 12 o'clock red mark** → perfect synchronism → close the breaker.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The synchroscope is the **most accurate and preferred** method used in modern KSEB power stations and is the industry standard for synchronising alternators above 1 MVA rating.

## 3.5 Synchronising Power and Synchronising Torque Coefficient

After synchronisation, when a small phase displacement $\delta$ is introduced, a **synchronising power** $P_{syn}$ flows between the machines:

$$P_{syn} = \frac{E_1 \cdot V}{X_s} \sin(\delta)$$

The **synchronising torque coefficient** is defined as:

$$T_{syn} = \frac{dP_{syn}}{d\delta} = \frac{E_1 \cdot V}{\omega_s \cdot X_s} \cos(\delta)$$

At $\delta = 0$, the maximum synchronising torque is obtained:

$$T_{syn,max} = \frac{E_1 \cdot V}{\omega_s \cdot X_s}$$

This torque acts as a *restoring torque* that keeps the machine locked to synchronism with the bus bar.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Synchronisation Process Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Prime Mover] --> B[Bring Alternator to Rated Speed]
    B --> C{Voltage E1 matches Bus Voltage V?}
    C -->|No| D[Adjust Field Excitation via AVR or Rheostat]
    D --> C
    C -->|Yes| E{Frequency f1 matches f2?}
    E -->|No| F[Adjust Prime Mover Throttle Governor]
    F --> E
    E -->|Yes| G{Phase Sequence Verified?}
    G -->|No| H[Reverse Any Two Leads of Incoming Machine]
    H --> G
    G -->|Yes| I[Observe Lamp or Synchroscope Pattern]
    I --> J{All Dark / Pointer at 12 o'clock?}
    J -->|No| K[Fine Tune Speed to Slow Slip Frequency]
    K --> I
    J -->|Yes| L[CLOSE THE CIRCUIT BREAKER]
    L --> M[Adjust Governor to Load Sharing]
```

## 4.2 Three-Dark Lamp Method — Circuit Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BUS[Existing Infinite Bus Bar]
        BR[Bus R]
        BY[Bus Y]
        BB[Bus B]
    end
    subgraph ALT[Incoming Alternator]
        AR[Alt R]
        AY[Alt Y]
        AB[Alt B]
    end
    subgraph SW[Triple Pole Switch Breaker]
        SW1(( ))
        SW2(( ))
        SW3(( ))
    end
    L1([Lamp 1: R-R]) --- SW1
    L2([Lamp 2: Y-Y]) --- SW2
    L3([Lamp 3: B-B]) --- SW3
    BR --- SW1
    BY --- SW2
    BB --- SW3
    AR --- BR
    AY --- BY
    AB --- BB
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **3-Dark Method**, the three lamps are connected between the *corresponding* (similar) phases. All three lamps darken simultaneously only at perfect synchronism.

## 4.3 Two-Bright, One-Dark Lamp Method — Connection Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BUS2[Existing Bus Bar]
        BR2[Bus R]
        BY2[Bus Y]
        BB2[Bus B]
    end
    subgraph ALT2[Incoming Alternator]
        AR2[Alt R]
        AY2[Alt Y]
        AB2[Alt B]
    end
    BR2 --- L1dark([Lamp 1 DARK: Bus R to Alt R])
    BY2 --- L2bright([Lamp 2 BRIGHT: Bus Y to Alt B])
    BB2 --- L3bright([Lamp 3 BRIGHT: Bus B to Alt Y])
    L1dark --- AR2
    L2bright --- AB2
    L3bright --- AY2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the **cross-connection**: Lamp 2 connects Bus-Y to Alt-B, and Lamp 3 connects Bus-B to Alt-Y. Only Lamp 1 (the direct R-R connection) darkens at synchronism; the other two glow at *line voltage* brightness.

## 4.4 Synchroscope Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph POW[Power Supply Inputs]
        P1[Bus Bar Phase R Reference]
        P2[Bus Bar Phase Y Reference]
        P3[Incoming Alternator Phase R]
    end
    P1 --> POL[Polarising Winding]
    P2 --> PHSplit[Phase Splitting Network: R and X branches]
    PHSplit --> ROT[Rotating Field Winding]
    P3 --> POL
    POL --> RTR[Rotor with Pointer and Iron Vane]
    ROT --> RTR
    RTR --> DIAL((Dial Scale: FAST, SLOW, Red Mark at 12 o'clock))
    RTR --> PNTR([Moving Pointer])
```

## 4.5 Synchronisation Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Two Alternators Ready to Parallel] --> Q1{Voltages Equal?}
    Q1 -->|No| FIX1[Adjust Field Current]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Frequencies Equal?}
    Q2 -->|No| FIX2[Adjust Prime Mover Speed]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Sequence Matches?}
    Q3 -->|No| FIX3[Swap Two Terminals]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Phase Coincidence?}
    Q4 -->|No| WAIT[Wait for Dark Lamp Moment]
    WAIT --> Q4
    Q4 -->|Yes| CLOSE[CLOSE BREAKER]
    CLOSE --> DONE[Generators in Parallel]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: List any four essential conditions that must be satisfied before an alternator is synchronised with an infinite bus bar.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four essential conditions for synchronising an alternator with an infinite bus bar are:
1. The RMS magnitude of the generated EMF of the incoming machine must equal the bus bar RMS voltage ($E_1 = V$).
2. The frequency of the incoming machine must equal the bus bar frequency ($f_1 = f_2 = 50$ Hz).
3. The phase sequence of the incoming machine must be identical to the bus bar phase sequence (R-Y-B matching R-Y-B).
4. The phase angle between the corresponding phases of the two machines must be zero ($\delta = 0^\circ$) at the instant of closing the breaker.
*(1 mark for naming, 0.5 marks each for brief explanation = 3 marks)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between the three-dark lamp method and the two-bright one-dark lamp method of synchronisation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Three-Dark Method | Two-Bright, One-Dark Method |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| Connections | All 3 lamps across *similar* phases | 1 lamp across similar, 2 across *crossed* phases |
| At synchronism | All 3 lamps go dark | Only middle lamp is dark; 2 lamps remain bright |
| Phase sequence info | Cannot be detected | Rotational order of bright lamps reveals phase sequence |
| Speed info (fast/slow) | Cannot be determined | Direction of light rotation indicates fast/slow |
| Accuracy | Sufficient for small machines | Better for medium machines |

*(1 mark for the comparison logic + 1 mark for the rotation/speed feature + 1 mark for table structure)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the **three-dark lamp method** of synchronising a 3-phase alternator with an infinite bus bar with a neat circuit diagram. State its advantages and limitations.

**(b) [7 marks]:** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz, star-connected alternator is to be synchronised with a bus bar of line voltage 6.6 kV. The alternator delivers a no-load line voltage of 6.6 kV when excited. Calculate:
(i) The synchronous speed of the alternator.
(ii) If the incoming machine frequency is 49.5 Hz, calculate the slip frequency and the time period of lamp flickering.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Three-Dark Lamp Method:**

In this method, three incandescent lamps are connected between the *corresponding* phases of the incoming alternator and the bus bar, as shown in the schematic of Section 4.2. The lamps are placed across the open contacts of the triple-pole circuit breaker.

**Working:**
- The prime mover brings the alternator close to synchronous speed.
- The field excitation is adjusted so that the generated EMF equals the bus bar voltage.
- If frequencies differ, the lamps flicker (breathe) at the slip frequency $f_s = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert$.
- When the lamps are all dark **simultaneously** (which requires correct phase sequence, equal voltages, equal frequencies, and zero phase difference), the circuit breaker is closed.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- Simplest and cheapest method (requires only 3 lamps).
- Indicates all four synchronisation conditions implicitly.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- Cannot distinguish whether incoming machine is *fast* or *slow* (no directional information).
- Cannot verify phase sequence — incorrect sequence also produces simultaneous dark/bright cycle, but with a *rotating* pattern.
- Difficult to judge the exact moment of synchronism when flicker rate is high.
- Not suitable for large alternators where precise synchronism is critical.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Number of poles: $P = 6$
- Frequency: $f = 50$ Hz
- Incoming frequency: $f_1 = 49.5$ Hz
- Connection: Star

**(i) Synchronous Speed (3 marks):**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ RPM}$$

**(ii) Slip Frequency and Time Period (4 marks):**

Slip frequency is the difference between bus bar frequency (50 Hz) and incoming machine frequency (49.5 Hz):

$$f_s = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = \vert 49.5 - 50 \vert = 0.5 \text{ Hz}$$

Time period of lamp flickering (one complete dark-bright-dark cycle):

$$T = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2 \text{ seconds}$$

So the lamps will complete one full dark-bright cycle every **2 seconds**, with the dark moment occurring at the midpoint of each cycle. The breaker must be closed at this midpoint.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **synchroscope** used for synchronising alternators. Why is it preferred over the lamp methods for large machines?

**(b) [7 marks]:** Derive the expression for the **synchronising power** $P_{syn} = \frac{E_1 V}{X_s} \sin\delta$ flowing between two alternators connected in parallel. State the significance of the **synchronising torque coefficient**.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Synchroscope (7 marks):**

A synchroscope is a precision instrument that indicates *both* the magnitude of phase difference and the *direction* (fast or slow) of the incoming machine. It is essentially a split-phase induction motor with a pointer attached to the rotor (refer to Section 4.4 block diagram).

**Construction (2 marks):**
- **Polarising winding**: Connected between one phase of the bus bar and the corresponding phase of the incoming alternator.
- **Rotating field winding**: Supplied from the bus bar through a *phase-splitting network* (a resistance in one branch and a reactance in another, creating 90° phase shift).
- **Rotor**: A thin iron vane with a pointer attached, free to rotate against a calibrated dial.

**Working (3 marks):**
- When frequencies are equal and the phase angle is zero, the pointer rests at the **12 o'clock position** (red mark) — synchronism indicator.
- If the incoming alternator is *fast* ($f_1 > f_2$), the pointer rotates continuously in the "FAST" direction at the slip frequency.
- If *slow* ($f_1 < f_2$), rotation is in the "SLOW" direction.
- The operator adjusts the prime-mover governor until the pointer rotates very slowly and stops at the red mark — the breaker is then closed.

**Why preferred over lamp methods (2 marks):**
- Provides continuous, unambiguous indication of *both* phase difference and direction (fast/slow).
- Works accurately even at high slip frequencies.
- Essential for large alternators (> 1 MVA) where even a 1° phase error at synchronisation causes damaging inrush currents.
- Modern synchroscopes can be interfaced with **automatic synchronising relays** for unmanned substations.

**Part (b) — Derivation of Synchronising Power (7 marks):**

Consider two alternators connected in parallel through a switch. The internal EMF of the first machine is $E_1 \angle\delta$ and the second is $E_2 \angle 0$, with synchronous reactance $X_s$ between them.

The phasor sum of the two EMFs and the circulating current (assuming $R_a \ll X_s$, so $Z_s \approx jX_s$):

$$E_1 \angle\delta - E_2 \angle 0 = j X_s \cdot I_{sync}$$

The synchronising current is:

$$I_{sync} = \frac{E_1 \angle\delta - E_2 \angle 0}{jX_s}$$

The power transferred (synchronising power) is the real part of $E_1 \cdot I_{sync}^*$:

$$P_{syn} = \text{Re}\!\left[E_1 \angle\delta \cdot I_{sync}^*\right]$$

For the special case of identical machines ($E_1 = E_2 = V$):

$$P_{syn} = \frac{E_1 \cdot V}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

**Significance of Synchronising Torque Coefficient (2 marks):**

The synchronising torque coefficient is the rate of change of synchronising power with respect to load angle $\delta$:

$$T_{syn} = \frac{1}{\omega_s} \frac{dP_{syn}}{d\delta} = \frac{E_1 \cdot V}{\omega_s X_s} \cos\delta$$

At $\delta = 0$ (steady-state synchronism):

$$T_{syn,max} = \frac{E_1 \cdot V}{\omega_s X_s}$$

This represents the **maximum restoring torque** that pulls the rotor back to the synchronous position if perturbed. A higher $T_{syn,max}$ means *greater stability* against oscillations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $f_s$ (slip frequency) with $f$ (system frequency):** Many students write $f_s = 50$ Hz. Always remember $f_s$ is the *difference* — typically 0.1 to 1 Hz during synchronisation.
> 2. **Forgetting phase sequence:** Several students list only 3 conditions and omit the phase sequence. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in the KTU 2024 scheme.
> 3. **Drawing a "physical" 3-phase wiring diagram in exams:** KTU examiners expect a *functional block schematic*, not a literal switchgear drawing. Use the Mermaid-style block format.
> 4. **Mixing "dark" and "bright" lamp terminology:** The lamp that darkens depends on the connection — make sure the description matches the circuit shown.
> 5. **Not stating the role of AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator):** Examiners in Module 3 specifically test the *control mechanism*. Always mention the **AVR** for voltage and the **Governor** for frequency.
> 6. **Numerical unit errors:** Slip frequency in **Hz**, period in **seconds**, speed in **RPM**. Do not interchange.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorise):**
>
> - **4 Conditions of Synchronisation:** Voltage, Frequency, Phase Sequence, Phase Coincidence.
> - **3-Dark Method:** All lamps go dark at synchronism; cannot indicate fast/slow.
> - **2-Bright, 1-Dark Method:** Middle lamp dark; outer two bright (at *line voltage*); indicates fast/slow by rotation direction.
> - **Synchroscope:** Split-phase motor with pointer; rotates at slip frequency; "FAST"/"SLOW" indicators; 12 o'clock red mark is synchronism.
> - **Slip Frequency Formula:** $f_s = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert$.
> - **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$.
> - **Synchronising Power:** $P_{syn} = \dfrac{E_1 V}{X_s} \sin\delta$.
> - **Synchronising Torque Coefficient:** $T_{syn,max} = \dfrac{E_1 V}{\omega_s X_s}$ at $\delta = 0$.
> - **Standard Indian Grid Frequency:** **50 Hz**.
> - **Standard Voltage Match Check:** Use a **voltmeter** across the open breaker contacts.
> - **Frequency Check:** Use a **frequency meter** or observe lamp flicker rate.
> - **Modern Practice:** Automatic static synchronisers with **microprocessor-based** phase angle detectors.
> - **KTU 2024 Module 3 Weightage:** Synchronisation methods typically carry 7–14 marks in End Semester Examinations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dark Lamp Method
topics.content:
