> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Dark Lamp Method # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Dark Lamp Method** (also known as the **Three Dark Lamp Method** or **Siemens Method**) is a classical, low-cost, manual technique used to synchronize (parallel) an incoming **3-phase alternator** with a live **infinite bus bar** or with an already-running alternator. It uses a set of three incandescent lamps connected across the main switch (circuit breaker) that separates the incoming machine from the bus. The lamps glow, brighten, and dim cyclically as the phase voltages of the incoming machine slip against the bus. When **all three lamps become completely dark (extinguished) simultaneously**, it indicates the instant of perfect synchronism, at which point the circuit breaker is closed to connect the machines in parallel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Dark Lamp Method falls under *Module 3 – Synchronization of Alternators* and is a guaranteed short-answer question in every End Semester Evaluation (ESE). Students must memorize the lamp connection logic, phase sequence, and the exact instant of closing the breaker. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are trying to merge two rows of marching soldiers into a single row. You have to make sure their steps fall at exactly the same time and in the same direction before they can join. The **lamps act like your "eyes"** watching the two rows. When a lamp is bright, the two soldiers are "out of step" (voltages are far apart). When the lamp is dark, they are "in step" (voltages match perfectly). When **all three lamps go dark at the same time**, you know every single foot of the two rows is aligned — that is the exact moment to merge them in parallel (close the breaker). ## 1.3 Physical Setup Overview Three identical incandescent lamps (typically rated for **line voltage**, e.g., 415 V for a 3-phase system) are connected: - **Lamp 1**: Between Phase R of bus and Phase R of incoming alternator - **Lamp 2**: Between Phase Y of bus and Phase Y of incoming alternator - **Lamp 3**: Between Phase B of bus and Phase B of incoming alternator The common point of the lamps is essentially across the poles of the **Triple Pole Double Throw (TPDT)** switch / circuit breaker. ## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities (KTU Standard) - **Rated Voltage per Lamp** = Line Voltage of the system (e.g., **415 V**) - **Synchronizing Frequency Difference** $\Delta f = f_{bus} - f_{incoming} \approx$ **0.5 Hz to 1 Hz** (for safe observation) - **Phase Sequence**: Must be **identical (R-Y-B)** on both sides - **Brightness Cycle Frequency** = **2 × slip frequency** ($2f_s$) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Pre-Conditions for Synchronization (Pre-Sync Checklist) Before the dark lamp method can be applied, **four mandatory conditions** must be verified. KTU examiners frequently test these: 1. **Equal RMS Terminal Voltages**: $V_{incoming} = V_{bus}$ 2. **Equal Frequencies**: $f_{incoming} = f_{bus}$ 3. **Identical Phase Sequence**: Both machines must have the same R-Y-B rotation. 4. **Zero Phase Displacement at the instant of closing**: Phase angles must coincide. ## 2.2 Operational Logic (How the Dark Lamp Method Works) The voltage across each lamp is the **phasor difference** between the corresponding phase voltages of the bus and the incoming alternator. - Let $V_{bus} = V \angle 0°$ and $V_{incoming} = V \angle \delta$, where $\delta = (\omega_{bus} - \omega_{incoming})t = 2\pi f_s t$ is the **slip angle** and $f_s$ is the slip frequency. - The voltage across Lamp 1: $V_{L1} = V_{bus,R} - V_{incoming,R}$ - The voltage across Lamp 2: $V_{L2} = V_{bus,Y} - V_{incoming,Y}$ - The voltage across Lamp 3: $V_{L3} = V_{bus,B} - V_{incoming,B}$ ### Step-by-Step Logic of Lamp Behavior - **When $\delta = 0°$**: The two voltages are equal in magnitude and in phase. Their phasor difference is **zero** → **All three lamps are DARK**. ✅ **THIS IS THE INSTANT TO CLOSE THE BREAKER.** - **When $\delta = 180°$**: The two voltages are equal in magnitude but **opposite** in phase. Their phasor difference is **$2V$** → **All three lamps are BRIGHTEST (Maximum Glow)**. - **When $\delta = 60°, 180°, 300°$** (intermediate): Lamps glow at intermediate brightness. ## 2.3 Why "Three Dark Lamps" is the True Synchronism Indicator > [!TIP] > **Critical Insight:** A **single dark lamp** could be misleading because it might occur at $V_L = 0$ due to phase opposition being balanced by magnitude differences. However, when **all three lamps** darken **simultaneously**, it is a mathematically rigorous proof that all three phase differences are zero — i.e., perfect synchronism. ## 2.4 Phase Sequence Verification (Bonus Use of the Method) If the phase sequences of the two machines are **opposite (R-Y-B vs R-B-Y)**, the lamp behavior pattern changes: - **Correct sequence (R-Y-B)**: All 3 lamps darken and brighten **together** (simultaneous). - **Opposite sequence (R-B-Y)**: The lamps darken and brighten in a **rotational/circular pattern** (lamp 1 → lamp 2 → lamp 3, like a chasing light). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** If the lamps show a "rotating" glow pattern, the phase sequence is **wrong**. Do NOT close the breaker. Interchange any two leads of the incoming machine and re-check. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units / Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Slip Frequency | $f_s = f_{bus} - f_{incoming}$ | Hz (typically 0.5–1 Hz) | | 2 | Slip Angle | $\delta = 2\pi f_s t$ | radians or degrees | | 3 | Voltage across Lamp (correct seq.) | $V_L = 2V \sin(\delta/2)$ | Volts (line-to-line) | | 4 | Voltage across Lamp (wrong seq.) | $V_L = \sqrt{3}\,V \cos(\delta/2)$ | Volts | | 5 | Sync Condition (closing instant) | $V_L = 0 \implies \delta = 0°$ | All three lamps dark | | 6 | Max Voltage across Lamp | $V_{L,max} = 2V_{phase}$ | At $\delta = 180°$ | | 7 | Lamp Glow Cycle | 2 cycles per slip cycle | Bright–Dark–Bright–Dark per second = $2f_s$ | | 8 | Lamp Voltage Rating | = Line Voltage of system | Volts | | 9 | Phase Coincidence (Correct) | All lamps dark simultaneously | Closing instant | | 10 | Phase Coincidence (Wrong) | Rotating pattern (chasing lights) | Do NOT close | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power stations** use this method as a **backup** when a precision **Synchroscope** fails. - **Small diesel generator sets** in hospitals, ships, and remote telecom towers use the dark lamp method because it requires no expensive instruments — only three lamps and a switch. - **Laboratory training**: Every electrical machines lab in KTU-affiliated colleges uses the dark lamp method to teach students the fundamental concept of synchronization. - **Industrial cogeneration plants** rely on this method for emergency paralleling of captive generators with the grid. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Voltage Across the Lamps (Correct Phase Sequence) ### Given Data - Bus phase voltages (Reference, RYB sequence): - $V_{R,b} = V \angle 0°$ - $V_{Y,b} = V \angle -120°$ - $V_{B,b} = V \angle +120°$ - Incoming alternator phase voltages (RYB sequence, with slip angle $\delta$): - $V_{R,i} = V \angle \delta$ - $V_{Y,i} = V \angle (\delta - 120°)$ - $V_{B,i} = V \angle (\delta + 120°)$ ### Step 1: Voltage Across Lamp Connected Between R-phase of Bus and R-phase of Incoming $$ V_{LR} = V_{R,b} - V_{R,i} $$ Convert both to rectangular form: $$ V_{R,b} = V(\cos 0° + j\sin 0°) = V + j0 $$ $$ V_{R,i} = V(\cos\delta + j\sin\delta) $$ Subtracting the phasors: $$ V_{LR} = V - V\cos\delta - jV\sin\delta $$ Factor out $V$: $$ V_{LR} = V[(1 - \cos\delta) - j\sin\delta] $$ ### Step 2: Apply Trigonometric Half-Angle Identities Recall the standard half-angle identities: $$ 1 - \cos\delta = 2\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) $$ $$ \sin\delta = 2\sin\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) $$ Substituting these identities: $$ V_{LR} = V\left[2\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) - j \cdot 2\sin\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)\right] $$ Factor out $2\sin(\delta/2)$: $$ V_{LR} = 2V\sin\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)\left[\sin\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) - j\cos\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)\right] $$ ### Step 3: Convert the Bracket to Polar Form The bracket $\left[\sin(\delta/2) - j\cos(\delta/2)\right]$ has magnitude $1$ and angle $-(90° - \delta/2) = -(\delta/2 - 90°)$. Equivalently, the angle is $-(90° - \delta/2)$. Simplifying using Euler's identity, the magnitude of the bracket is: $$ \left\vert \sin\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) - j\cos\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) \right\vert = \sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) + \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)} = 1 $$ Thus the magnitude of $V_{LR}$ reduces to: $$ \left\vert V_{LR} \right\vert = 2V\sin\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) $$ This is the **RMS voltage across lamp 1** (and by symmetry, identical analysis applies to lamps 2 and 3 in the correct-sequence case). ### Step 4: Verify Boundary Conditions **Case 1 — Synchronism ($\delta = 0°$):** $$ \left\vert V_{LR} \right\vert = 2V\sin(0°) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Lamp is DARK ✅} $$ **Case 2 — Maximum Glow ($\delta = 180°$):** $$ \left\vert V_{LR} \right\vert = 2V\sin(90°) = 2V \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Lamp is BRIGHTEST} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Confirmation:** At $\delta = 0$, all three lamps have **zero voltage** across them simultaneously because in the RYB-RYB connection, $\sin(\delta/2)$ is identical for all three phases. Hence "all dark" is a **unique and unambiguous** indicator. ## 3.2 Derivation for Opposite Phase Sequence (R-Y-B vs R-B-Y) If the phase sequence is **wrong**, the incoming voltages become $V \angle \delta$, $V \angle (\delta + 120°)$, $V \angle (\delta - 120°)$. Repeating the same phasor subtraction for the R-phase lamp: $$ V_{LR} = V_{R,b} - V_{R,i} = V\angle 0° - V\angle\delta $$ Using the cosine rule for phasor subtraction: $$ \left\vert V_{LR} \right\vert = \sqrt{V^2 + V^2 - 2V^2\cos\delta} = V\sqrt{2(1 - \cos\delta)} $$ Applying the identity $1 - \cos\delta = 2\sin^2(\delta/2)$: $$ \left\vert V_{LR} \right\vert = V\sqrt{4\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)} = 2V\sin\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wait — this is the same expression!** The above result is for the **R-phase** in a wrong-sequence connection. However, the *other two lamps* will have a different angular displacement, leading to a $\sqrt{3}V\cos(\delta/2)$ form for the others. The key takeaway is: in wrong sequence, the three lamps do **not** darken simultaneously — instead, they exhibit a **rotating / chasing glow pattern**. ## 3.3 Lamp Glow Frequency (Analytical Proof) In one complete slip cycle, $\delta$ varies from $0° \to 360°$. - The lamp voltage $V_L = 2V\sin(\delta/2)$ becomes zero at $\delta = 0°$ and at $\delta = 360°$ — i.e., **twice per slip cycle**. - Therefore, the **lamp glow frequency** = **$2 \times$ slip frequency** = $2f_s$. This is why the lamps flicker on and off at a rate faster than the slip frequency. ## 3.4 Synchronizing Power Surge Analysis (Conceptual) At the instant the breaker is closed (ideally $\delta = 0°$), no synchronizing current flows. If a small error $\delta_{error}$ exists: $$ I_{sync} = \frac{V_{bus} \angle 0° - V_{incoming} \angle \delta_{error}}{Z_{sync}} $$ If $\delta_{error}$ is small, $|V_{bus} - V_{incoming}| \approx 0$, so $I_{sync} \approx 0$ — a smooth paralleling. > [!WARNING] > If the breaker is closed at $\delta = 180°$, $V_L = 2V$, leading to a **catastrophic short-circuit-like current** that will damage both machines and trip the breaker violently. This is precisely why the dark lamp method is used — it gives a clear, observable cue to close at $\delta = 0°$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Physical Connection Diagram (Adapted to Mermaid Block Diagram) > [!NOTE] > Since a true 3-phase physical wiring diagram is best drawn on paper, the following Mermaid block diagram captures the **functional signal flow** between the bus, lamps, incoming alternator, and the synchronizing switch — which is what KTU examiners typically expect in a theory question. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["BUS BAR
3-Phase R-Y-B
Voltage = V, Freq = f1"] A --> A1["Phase R Terminal"] A --> A2["Phase Y Terminal"] A --> A3["Phase B Terminal"] A1 --> LMP1["LAMP 1
Rated V"] A2 --> LMP2["LAMP 2
Rated V"] A3 --> LMP3["LAMP 3
Rated V"] LMP1 --> SW1["SWITCH POLE 1
Triple Pole Switch"] LMP2 --> SW2["SWITCH POLE 2
Triple Pole Switch"] LMP3 --> SW3["SWITCH POLE 3
Triple Pole Switch"] SW1 --> GEN1["Incoming Alternator
Phase R"] SW2 --> GEN2["Incoming Alternator
Phase Y"] SW3 --> GEN3["Incoming Alternator
Phase B"] GEN4["Incoming Alternator
3-Phase Prime Mover Driven"] --> GEN1 GEN4 --> GEN2 GEN4 --> GEN3 SW_OBS["OBSERVATION
All 3 Lamps Dark
CLOSE BREAKER NOW"] -.-> SW1 SW_OBS -.-> SW2 SW_OBS -.-> SW3 ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flow Diagram (Lamp Behavior Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD ST["Start: Adjust Speed of
Prime Mover of Incoming
Alternator"] --> CHK1{"Voltage Equal?
V_in = V_bus"} CHK1 -->|No| ADJ1["Adjust Excitation
of Incoming Alternator"] --> CHK1 CHK1 -->|Yes| CHK2{"All 3 Lamps
Dark Simultaneously?"} CHK2 -->|No, Rotating Pattern| WRONG["Phase Sequence
WRONG
Interchange 2 Leads"] --> CHK2 CHK2 -->|No, Glowing| FINE["Frequencies Slightly
Different - Acceptable
Fine Tune Speed"] --> CHK2 CHK2 -->|Yes| CLOSE["CLOSE THE
CIRCUIT BREAKER
IMMEDIATELY"] CLOSE --> DONE["Alternators Now
Connected in Parallel"] WRONG --> ST ``` ## 4.3 Lamp Brightness vs Slip Angle Graph (Conceptual Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR G1["Lamp Voltage V_L = 2V sin(δ/2)"] --> P1["δ = 0°: V_L = 0 → DARK"] G1 --> P2["δ = 60°: V_L = V → HALF GLOW"] G1 --> P3["δ = 180°: V_L = 2V → MAX BRIGHT"] G1 --> P4["δ = 360°: V_L = 0 → DARK AGAIN"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 ``` ## 4.4 Component Summary Block ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1["Equipment Required"] E1["3 Incandescent Lamps (Line Voltage Rated)"] E2["1 Triple Pole Double Throw (TPDT) Switch / Breaker"] E3["Voltmeter (0 - 500V AC)"] E4["Frequency Meter (or Lamps as Indicator)"] end subgraph S2["Procedure Steps"] P1["Step 1: Bring Incoming Alternator to rated speed"] P2["Step 2: Adjust Excitation to match Bus Voltage"] P3["Step 3: Observe Lamps for Dark-Bright cycle"] P4["Step 4: At all-dark instant, close breaker"] end subgraph S3["Outcomes"] O1["Correct: All lamps dark simultaneously → CLOSE"] O2["Wrong: Rotating pattern → CHANGE Phase Sequence"] O3["Wrong: Lamps not going dark → ADJUST Frequency"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **State the conditions to be satisfied for synchronizing a 3-phase alternator with an infinite bus bar. Mention how these conditions are achieved using the dark lamp method.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four conditions for synchronization are: 1. **Equal terminal voltages** of the incoming alternator and the bus ($V_i = V_b$). Achieved by adjusting the **field excitation** of the incoming alternator. 2. **Equal frequencies** of the incoming alternator and the bus ($f_i = f_b$). Achieved by adjusting the **speed of the prime mover** driving the alternator. 3. **Identical phase sequence**. Verified by observing the lamp pattern — if lamps darken together, sequence is correct; if rotating, interchange any two leads. 4. **Zero phase angle difference at the instant of closing the breaker**. Indicated by the moment when **all three lamps are dark simultaneously**. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **What is meant by the dark lamp method of synchronization? Why are all three lamps required to darken simultaneously, instead of just one?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **dark lamp method** is a manual technique for synchronizing an incoming alternator with a live bus by observing three incandescent lamps connected across the main switch. When all three lamps are **simultaneously dark**, it indicates zero voltage across every lamp, meaning the corresponding phase voltages on both sides are equal in magnitude and phase — this is the exact instant to close the circuit breaker. A single lamp going dark is not a reliable indicator because the voltage across it could be zero due to a magnitude difference coincidentally balancing a phase difference. Only when **all three lamps** darken at the same instant is it mathematically guaranteed that all three phases of the two systems are perfectly matched. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a)** Explain with a neat connection diagram the **dark lamp method** of synchronizing a 3-phase alternator with an infinite bus bar. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two alternators are to be synchronized using the dark lamp method. Describe how you would verify the **correct phase sequence** from the lamp behavior. What action would you take if the phase sequence were incorrect? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.A(a): **1. Circuit Description (3 marks):** Three identical incandescent lamps, each rated for the system **line voltage** (e.g., 415 V), are connected as follows: - Lamp L1 between **Phase R of the bus** and **Phase R of the incoming alternator**. - Lamp L2 between **Phase Y of the bus** and **Phase Y of the incoming alternator**. - Lamp L3 between **Phase B of the bus** and **Phase B of the incoming alternator**. The common terminals of the lamps are routed to a **Triple Pole Double Throw (TPDT) switch** that connects/disconnects the incoming alternator from the bus. **2. Pre-Synchronization Procedure (2 marks):** - The incoming alternator is brought to **rated speed** using its prime mover. - Its **excitation** is adjusted so that the terminal voltage equals the bus voltage. - The lamps begin to glow, dim, and brighten in a cyclic pattern as the incoming machine's frequency slips against the bus. **3. Synchronization Instant (2 marks):** - The frequency is finely adjusted so that the lamp glow cycle slows down to about **1 Hz** (i.e., 1 dark-bright cycle per second). - At the instant **all three lamps become dark simultaneously**, the **circuit breaker is closed**, paralleling the two machines. #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): **1. Lamp Behavior with Correct Phase Sequence (3 marks):** When both machines have the **same phase sequence (R-Y-B)**, the three lamp voltages are equal in magnitude at every instant because of the symmetry of the 3-phase system. Therefore, **all three lamps darken and brighten together (simultaneously)**. This simultaneous darkening is the cue to close the breaker. **2. Lamp Behavior with Wrong Phase Sequence (2 marks):** If the phase sequences are opposite (one is R-Y-B and the other is R-B-Y), the symmetry is broken. The lamps no longer darken together; instead, their dark instants occur in a **rotational, chasing-light pattern** (e.g., L1 dark → L2 dark → L3 dark → L1 dark, in a cyclic sequence). The lamps will never be all dark at the same time. **3. Corrective Action (2 marks):** If a rotating pattern is observed, the phase sequence of the incoming alternator must be reversed. The standard method is to **interchange any two of the three line leads** of the incoming machine connected to the switch (e.g., swap the Y and B leads). After this, the lamp pattern should revert to simultaneous darkening, confirming the correct sequence. The breaker can then be closed at the next all-dark instant. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a)** Derive an expression for the **voltage across the lamps** in the dark lamp method when the phase sequences are **correct**. Show that the voltage becomes zero at the instant of synchronism. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The voltage across each lamp in a dark lamp method synchronization varies as $V_L = 2V\sin(\delta/2)$. If the bus line voltage is **3.3 kV** and the slip frequency is **0.5 Hz**, calculate: (i) The **maximum voltage** across any lamp. (ii) The **lamp glow frequency** (number of dark-bright cycles per second). (iii) The **time interval** between two successive dark instants. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): **Given:** - Bus phase voltages: $V_{R,b} = V\angle 0°$, $V_{Y,b} = V\angle -120°$, $V_{B,b} = V\angle +120°$ - Incoming alternator phase voltages (correct RYB sequence with slip angle $\delta$): $V_{R,i} = V\angle\delta$, $V_{Y,i} = V\angle(\delta-120°)$, $V_{B,i} = V\angle(\delta+120°)$ **Step 1: Voltage across Lamp 1 (R-phase):** $$ V_{LR} = V_{R,b} - V_{R,i} = V\angle 0° - V\angle\delta $$ **Step 2: Convert to rectangular form:** $$ V_{LR} = V(1 - \cos\delta) - jV\sin\delta $$ **Step 3: Apply half-angle identities** $1 - \cos\delta = 2\sin^2(\delta/2)$ and $\sin\delta = 2\sin(\delta/2)\cos(\delta/2)$: $$ V_{LR} = 2V\sin\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)\left[\sin\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) - j\cos\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)\right] $$ **Step 4: Magnitude of the bracketed term is 1**, so: $$ \left\vert V_{LR} \right\vert = 2V\sin\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) $$ **Step 5: By symmetry**, identical expressions hold for $V_{LY}$ and $V_{LB}$. **Step 6: At synchronism, $\delta = 0°$:** $$ \left\vert V_L \right\vert = 2V\sin(0°) = 0 $$ Hence, the voltage across every lamp is zero, and all three lamps darken simultaneously at the exact instant of synchronism. **(7 marks — full derivation)** #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Given:** - Bus line voltage $V_L$ (line) = **3.3 kV** = 3300 V - Slip frequency $f_s$ = **0.5 Hz** **Step 1: Phase voltage (V):** $$ V = \frac{V_{line}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{3300}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1905.26 \text{ V} $$ **(i) Maximum voltage across any lamp (at $\delta = 180°$):** $$ V_{L,max} = 2V\sin(90°) = 2 \times 1905.26 = 3810.52 \text{ V} $$ > [!NOTE] > The lamp must be rated for **at least 3810 V** to survive the maximum glow condition — which is why lamps are usually chosen for **system line voltage** (3300 V) and the operator must close the breaker quickly when lamps darken, never leaving the system in the $\delta = 180°$ state. **(ii) Lamp glow frequency:** In one complete slip cycle ($\delta$: $0° \to 360°$), the lamp voltage $V_L = 2V\sin(\delta/2)$ passes through zero **twice** (at $\delta = 0°$ and $\delta = 360°$). $$ \text{Lamp glow frequency} = 2f_s = 2 \times 0.5 = 1 \text{ Hz} $$ So the lamps complete **1 full dark-bright cycle per second**. **(iii) Time interval between two successive dark instants:** Since there are 2 dark instants per slip cycle: $$ T_{slip} = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2 \text{ s} $$ $$ T_{dark\,interval} = \frac{T_{slip}}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \text{ s} $$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $V_{L,max} \approx 3810.52$ V - (ii) Lamp glow frequency = **1 Hz** - (iii) Time between successive dark instants = **1 second** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:** > 1. **Forgetting to mention lamp voltage rating**: Examiners deduct 1 mark if you don't state the lamp rating equals the system line voltage. > 2. **Conflating line and phase voltage in calculations**: In Q.B(b)(i), use phase voltage in $V_L = 2V\sin(\delta/2)$, not line voltage. > 3. **Missing the phase sequence check**: Many students describe the dark lamp method but fail to explain how the rotating-pattern observation detects wrong phase sequence. This is a 2-mark item in KTU evaluations. > 4. **Closing the breaker at "any dark lamp"**: A common wrong answer. The breaker must close at the instant **all three lamps** are dark simultaneously, not when just one is dark. > 5. **Confusing lamp glow frequency with slip frequency**: Glow frequency is $2f_s$, not $f_s$. This is a frequently tested distinction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - The **Dark Lamp Method** is the simplest, lamp-based manual technique for synchronizing a 3-phase alternator with a live bus bar. - **Three incandescent lamps** are connected across the triple-pole switch, one per phase (R, Y, B). - **All four synchronizing conditions** must be met: equal voltage, equal frequency, identical phase sequence, zero phase angle. - The **voltage across each lamp** is given by $V_L = 2V\sin(\delta/2)$, where $\delta$ is the slip angle. - The lamps glow brightest when $\delta = 180°$ (voltage = $2V$) and become **dark when $\delta = 0°$** (voltage = 0). - The **breaker must be closed at the instant all three lamps are dark simultaneously**. - **Lamp glow frequency** = $2 \times$ slip frequency (i.e., 2 dark instants per slip cycle). - **Correct phase sequence** → all lamps darken/brighten together (simultaneous pattern). - **Wrong phase sequence** → lamps darken in a rotating, chasing-light pattern; fix by interchanging any two leads of the incoming machine. - **Typical lamp rating** = system line voltage (e.g., 415 V, 3.3 kV, 6.6 kV, 11 kV). - **Practical slip frequency** for safe manual operation = 0.5 to 1 Hz (so the operator has time to react). - **Disadvantages**: Slow, requires skilled operator, cannot indicate the **sign** of slip (i.e., whether the incoming machine is fast or slow) — the **synchroscope** overcomes this. - **Industrial use**: Backup method when synchroscope fails; used in small captive power plants, ships, hospitals. - **Mathematical confirmation**: $V_L = 0$ when $\delta = 0°$ → all three lamps dark → perfect synchronism — the unique and unambiguous cue. - **KTU weightage**: Guaranteed 3-mark question in Part A; a 7-mark sub-part of a 14-mark Part B question every ESE.

topics.title: Bright Lamp Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bright Lamp Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bright Lamp Method** (also called the **Lamp Polarity Test**) is a classical, qualitative electrical test used to determine the **relative instantaneous polarity** between the primary and secondary windings of a single-phase transformer. It identifies whether the windings are **additively polar** (voltages add when windings are connected in series) or **subtractively polar** (voltages oppose each other).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Bright Lamp Method is the *only* method prescribed at the undergraduate level to identify the dot-polarity convention of transformer windings **without using an instrument-based (voltmeter/oscilloscope) approach**. It is a direct application of Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to mutually-coupled coils.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine **two battery cells** lying on a table:
- **Cell-A** is unmarked; you don't know which end is positive.
- **Cell-B** is also unmarked.

You want to know if connecting them **end-to-end in the same direction** will *boost* the voltage (additive) or *cancel* the voltage (subtractive).

**The Bright Lamp Method is the "brightness-vision" of a tester**:
- You connect the cells in series, attach a small lamp, and **see the glow**.
- **Bright glow** → cells are in the *same direction* → additive.
- **Dim glow / No glow** → cells are *opposing each other* → subtractive.

In a transformer, the AC supply replaces the battery, but the principle is identical: the lamp acts as a **visual integrator of the resulting series voltage** between the two un-marked coils.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why a Lamp, not a Voltmeter?**  
> A lamp provides a *binary visual cue* (bright or dim) — making it the simplest field instrument. A voltmeter requires reading, scaling, and the polarity sign convention is ambiguous if the meter leads are also un-marked.

## 1.3 Standard Test Terminals (KTU Convention)

| Winding | Standard Terminals | Role in Test |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Primary (HV or LV) | **A₁** and **A₂** | Energized from AC mains |
| Secondary (the other) | **a₁** and **a₂** | Lamp connected across it |
| Linking wire | **A₂ ↔ a₁** (assumed) | Series interconnection |
| Indicator | **Filament Lamp** (rated ≈ V₂) | Connected between A₁ and a₂ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The test is governed by the **Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and **Mutual Coupling** between two coils sharing a common magnetic core. The stepwise logic is:

1. **Pre-mark the terminals** arbitrarily as A₁–A₂ (primary) and a₁–a₂ (secondary). These are *guesses*; the test validates them.
2. **Energize the primary** winding with a **sinusoidal AC supply** of rated voltage V₁ at the standard frequency (50 Hz in India).
3. **Short A₂ to a₁** using a small jumper wire. This places the two windings in a *series-aiding* or *series-opposing* configuration depending on the actual polarity.
4. **Connect a filament lamp** between the *remaining* free terminals A₁ and a₂.
5. **Observe** the lamp:
   - **Filament glows brightly** → V_lamp ≈ V₁ + V₂ → **Additive Polarity** (assumed markings are correct).
   - **Filament glows dimly** → V_lamp ≈ V₁ − V₂ → **Subtractive Polarity** (assumed markings are wrong; swap a₁ ↔ a₂).
6. **Mark the verified terminals permanently** for future parallel operation.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Glow

- A transformer's primary and secondary voltages are **mutually induced** and follow the dot convention.
- If the dotted terminals (A₁ and a₁) are at the **same instantaneous polarity**, the two EMFs **add up** along the series loop → A₁ and a₂ become the *extreme* potentials → lamp sees the **sum** → bright.
- If the dotted terminals are at **opposite instantaneous polarity**, the EMFs **oppose** each other in the loop → lamp sees the **difference** → dim.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> All formulas below assume a **sinusoidal steady-state** AC excitation and an **ideal** (lossless) transformer model. V₁ = RMS primary voltage, V₂ = RMS secondary voltage, a = N₁/N₂ = turns ratio.

| # | Parameter / Case | Governing Equation | Lamp Condition | Engineering Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Ideal secondary EMF | $V_2 = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot V_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{a}$ | Reference | Always induced |
| 2 | **Additive** Polarity (Lamp voltage) | $V_{\text{lamp}} = V_1 + V_2 = V_1 \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{a} \right)$ | **Bright glow** | A₁ and a₁ are *same* polarity |
| 3 | **Subtractive** Polarity (Lamp voltage) | $V_{\text{lamp}} = \vert V_1 - V_2 \vert = \left\vert V_1 - \dfrac{V_1}{a} \right\vert$ | **Dim glow** | A₁ and a₁ are *opposite* polarity |
| 4 | 1:1 Transformer (Subtractive) | $V_{\text{lamp}} = V_1 - V_1 = 0$ | **No glow** | EMFs perfectly cancel |
| 5 | Step-up (a < 1) Subtractive | $V_{\text{lamp}} = V_2 - V_1$ | **No glow** if $V_2 < V_1$ | Inversion possible |
| 6 | Dot Convention Rule | $V_{A_1a_2} = V_1 + V_2$ (additive) | Marks dot on A₁ & a₂ side | Standard IEEE/KTU |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse the **dot convention** with the **physical direction of winding**. Dots are *assigned* after the test, not before. The Bright Lamp Method is the *experiment* that *assigns* the dots.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why the Bright Lamp Test Matters |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Parallel Operation of Transformers** | Two transformers can only be paralleled if their H.V. and L.V. sides have the *same* polarity. The Bright Lamp test is the pre-paralleling check. |
| **Three-Phase Transformer Bank** (Yy, Dd, Yd, Dy) | Identifying the start-finish of each limb before forming a bank. Wrong polarity → circulating currents → catastrophic failure. |
| **Autotransformer Polarity** | Determines the *aiding* or *opposing* connection for buck/boost operation. |
| **CT (Current Transformer) Installation** | Polarity of CTs is critical for protective relay coordination (directional overcurrent, differential protection). |
| **Field Commissioning** | Site engineers use the lamp test to verify the manufacturer's markings in the field. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Phasor Setup (Analytical Foundation)

Let us represent the primary voltage as the reference phasor:

$$\tilde{V}_1 = V_1 \angle 0^\circ = V_1 \quad \text{(RMS, taken along real axis)}$$

By the **transformer EMF equation**, the secondary induced EMF is:

$$\tilde{V}_2 = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot \tilde{V}_1 = \frac{V_1}{a}$$

The **mutual phase relationship** between $\tilde{V}_1$ and $\tilde{V}_2$ depends on the dot convention:

- **Additive (Dots on A₁ and a₁):** $\tilde{V}_2$ is *in phase* with $\tilde{V}_1$ → $\tilde{V}_2 = +\dfrac{V_1}{a}$
- **Subtractive (Dots on A₁ and a₂):** $\tilde{V}_2$ is *180° out of phase* with $\tilde{V}_1$ → $\tilde{V}_2 = -\dfrac{V_1}{a}$

## 3.2 Derivation of Lamp Voltage — Additive Polarity

**Assumed Configuration:** A₂ is shorted to a₁; lamp is between A₁ and a₂.

Define potential at A₁ (the live primary terminal) as $V_{A_1}$.

Define potential at A₂ (the other primary terminal) as the *neutral reference* of the supply, so $V_{A_2} = 0$ (in isolation).

Now apply the **series short** $V_{A_2} = V_{a_1}$:

$$V_{a_1} = V_{A_2} = 0$$

In **additive polarity**, a₁ has the *same instantaneous sign* as A₁, so its induced potential relative to a₂ is:

$$V_{a_1} - V_{a_2} = +\frac{V_1}{a}$$

Substituting $V_{a_1} = 0$:

$$0 - V_{a_2} = +\frac{V_1}{a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{a_2} = -\frac{V_1}{a}$$

The lamp is connected between A₁ and a₂. The **lamp voltage** (potential difference) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{lamp}} &= V_{A_1} - V_{a_2} \\
&= V_1 - \left( -\frac{V_1}{a} \right) \\
&= V_1 + \frac{V_1}{a} \\
&= V_1 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{a} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{V_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{add}} = V_1 \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{a} \right)}$

Since $V_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{add}} > V_1 > 0$, the lamp receives a voltage **greater than the supply** and glows with **maximum brightness** (filament over-driven, may saturate visually). → **Additive Polarity confirmed.**

## 3.3 Derivation of Lamp Voltage — Subtractive Polarity

**Same physical connection:** A₂ shorted to a₁; lamp between A₁ and a₂.

Define $V_{A_2} = 0$ and $V_{A_1} = V_1$ (from supply).

Apply the short:

$$V_{a_1} = V_{A_2} = 0$$

In **subtractive polarity**, a₁ has the *opposite instantaneous sign* to A₁, so:

$$V_{a_1} - V_{a_2} = -\frac{V_1}{a}$$

Substituting $V_{a_1} = 0$:

$$0 - V_{a_2} = -\frac{V_1}{a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{a_2} = +\frac{V_1}{a}$$

The lamp voltage becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{lamp}} &= V_{A_1} - V_{a_2} \\
&= V_1 - \left( +\frac{V_1}{a} \right) \\
&= V_1 \left( 1 - \frac{1}{a} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{V_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{sub}} = V_1 \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{a} \right)}$

Since $\dfrac{1}{a} \leq 1$ for step-down or 1:1 transformers, $V_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{sub}} \leq V_1$. The lamp glows **dimly** (or not at all for 1:1). → **Subtractive Polarity confirmed.**

## 3.4 Special Case Analysis Matrix

| Transformer Type | Turns Ratio a = N₁/N₂ | V_lamp (Additive) | V_lamp (Subtractive) | Visual Cue (Additive) | Visual Cue (Subtractive) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Step-Down (e.g., 230/115 V) | a = 2 | $V_1(1 + 0.5) = 1.5\,V_1$ | $V_1(1 - 0.5) = 0.5\,V_1$ | Very Bright | Dim |
| 1:1 Isolation | a = 1 | $2\,V_1$ | $0$ | Extremely Bright | Lamp OFF |
| Step-Up (e.g., 115/230 V) | a = 0.5 | $V_1(1 + 2) = 3\,V_1$ | $V_1(1 - 2) = -V_1$ (i.e., $\vert V_1 \vert$) | Extremely Bright | Dim (inverted phase) |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A 4 kVA, 200 V / 100 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer is tested for polarity by the Bright Lamp Method. The primary is connected to a 200 V supply, and a 100 V lamp is used. Calculate the lamp voltage in both cases and predict the observation.

**Given:**
- V₁ = 200 V, V₂ (rated) = 100 V
- Turns ratio: a = N₁/N₂ = V₁/V₂ = 200/100 = **2**
- Lamp rating: 100 V

**Solution (Additive):**

$$V_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{add}} = 200 \left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) = 200 \times 1.5 = 300 \text{ V}$$

The lamp is over-voltaged (3× rated) → **Bright glow (filament saturates)**. *Caution: prolonged test may damage the lamp.*

**Solution (Subtractive):**

$$V_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{sub}} = 200 \left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right) = 200 \times 0.5 = 100 \text{ V}$$

The lamp is exactly at rated voltage → **Normal brightness (steady glow).**

> [!NOTE]  
> **Board Exam Trick:** In KTU valuation, examiners often set numericals where the *subtractive* voltage equals the lamp's rated voltage — making the test a clean "rated glow" vs "over-glow" comparison. Memorize the formula $V_{\text{lamp}} = V_1(1 \pm 1/a)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Test Setup (Additive Polarity)

The Mermaid block diagram below depicts the **signal/power flow** during the Bright Lamp test. The transformer is abstracted as a two-port magnetic coupling block, and the lamp is the visible indicator.

```mermaid
graph LR
    SUP[AC SUPPLY V1 50Hz]:::source
    P1[PRIMARY TERMINAL A1]:::node
    P2[PRIMARY TERMINAL A2]:::node
    T1[TRANSFORMER CORE MUTUAL COUPLING]:::device
    S1[SECONDARY TERMINAL a1]:::node
    S2[SECONDARY TERMINAL a2]:::node
    LMP[FILAMENT LAMP INDICATOR]:::lamp
    JW[JUMPER WIRE A2 to a1]:::wire

    SUP --- P1
    P1 --- T1
    T1 --- P2
    P2 --- JW
    JW --- S1
    S1 --- T1
    T1 --- S2
    P1 --- LMP
    LMP --- S2

    classDef source fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef device fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lamp fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wire fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#7e22ce,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Polarity Identification Procedure

```mermaid
graph TB
    START([START TEST]):::startend
    S1[STEP 1: Mark terminals A1 A2 a1 a2 arbitrarily]:::step
    S2[STEP 2: Connect A2 to a1 via jumper]:::step
    S3[STEP 3: Connect lamp between A1 and a2]:::step
    S4[STEP 4: Energize primary with V1]:::step
    OBS{OBSERVE LAMP}:::decision
    BR[BRIGHT GLOW]:::positive
    DM[DIM GLOW or NO GLOW]:::negative
    C1[ADDITIVE POLARITY CONFIRMED Mark A1 and a1 as dots]:::result
    C2[SUBTRACTIVE POLARITY Swap markings a1 is now a2]:::result
    FIN([TEST COMPLETE]):::startend

    START --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> OBS
    OBS -- Vlamp approx V1 plus V2 --> BR --> C1 --> FIN
    OBS -- Vlamp approx V1 minus V2 --> DM --> C2 --> FIN

    classDef startend fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef step fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef positive fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef negative fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Schematic Mapping Table (KTU Board Diagram Convention)

| Element | Standard Board Symbol | Labelling |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Primary Winding | Two overlapping humps | A₁ — A₂ (with dot on A₁ if additive) |
| Secondary Winding | Two overlapping humps (right side) | a₁ — a₂ (with dot on a₁ if additive) |
| Magnetic Core | Two parallel vertical lines | E-I laminations |
| AC Source | Circle with "~" inside | V₁ = 200 V, 50 Hz |
| Lamp | Circle with "X" inside | Rated V₂ |
| Jumper | Straight line | A₂ to a₁ |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Drawing Tip for KTU Board Exam:** Always draw the **dots** explicitly on the terminals *after* stating the test result. The dots are the *conclusion* of the test, not the assumption.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define the Bright Lamp Method. What information does it provide about a transformer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Bright Lamp Method is a polarity test used to determine the relative instantaneous polarity between the primary and secondary windings of a single-phase transformer.

**(1 mark for definition)** It involves connecting one terminal of the primary (A₂) to one terminal of the secondary (a₁) and placing a filament lamp across the remaining terminals (A₁ and a₂). **(1 mark for procedure)**

When the primary is energized, the lamp either glows brightly (indicating *additive polarity* — windings aid each other) or dimly (indicating *subtractive polarity* — windings oppose each other). **(1 mark for inference)** This test identifies the dot convention for safe parallel operation of transformers.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Distinguish between additive and subtractive polarity in transformers. How are they identified using the Bright Lamp Method?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Additive Polarity | Subtractive Polarity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **EMF relationship** (1 mark) | Voltages *add* in the series loop: $V_{\text{lamp}} = V_1 + V_2$ | Voltages *oppose*: $V_{\text{lamp}} = V_1 - V_2$ |
| **Dot convention** (1 mark) | Dots on A₁ and a₁ (same-side terminals) | Dots on A₁ and a₂ (opposite-side terminals) |
| **Lamp observation** (1 mark) | **Bright glow** (filament saturates) | **Dim glow** or no glow |

The Bright Lamp Method distinguishes them by the *brightness* of the lamp — bright for additive, dim for subtractive.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two alternatives are provided; the student answers **ONE** complete alternative.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) Explain with a neat circuit diagram the procedure of the Bright Lamp Method to determine the polarity of a single-phase transformer. State the inference drawn from bright and dim lamp conditions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Circuit Setup (3 marks):**
- The primary winding terminals are assumed and marked **A₁ and A₂**; secondary as **a₁ and a₂**.
- The primary winding is connected to a single-phase AC supply of rated voltage V₁ and frequency 50 Hz.
- A small jumper wire is used to **short terminal A₂ to terminal a₁**.
- A **filament lamp** (rated approximately equal to V₂) is connected between the remaining free terminals **A₁ and a₂**.

**2. Observation and Inference (2 marks):**
- If the lamp **glows brightly**, the assumed polarity is **additive** (A₁ and a₁ are of the same polarity). Mark these as the dotted terminals.
- If the lamp **glows dimly** (or not at all), the polarity is **subtractive** (A₁ and a₁ are of opposite polarity). Swap the markings of a₁ and a₂ and re-verify.

**3. Governing Equation (2 marks):**

$$\text{Bright (Additive):} \quad V_{\text{lamp}} = V_1 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{a} \right)$$

$$\text{Dim (Subtractive):} \quad V_{\text{lamp}} = V_1 \left( 1 - \frac{1}{a} \right)$$

where $a = N_1/N_2$ is the turns ratio.

---

**(b) A 5 kVA, 400 V / 200 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer is polarity-tested using the Bright Lamp Method. The primary is energized at 400 V. A 200 V, 100 W filament lamp is used as the indicator. Calculate the voltage across the lamp and the current through it for both additive and subtractive polarities. Comment on the lamp's brightness. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- V₁ = 400 V (primary voltage)
- V₂ = 200 V (rated secondary voltage)
- Turns ratio: $a = V_1/V_2 = 400/200 = 2$
- Lamp rating: 200 V, 100 W → Lamp resistance $R_L = V^2/P = (200)^2/100 = 400 \; \Omega$ **(0.5 mark)**

**Additive Polarity — Case 1 (3 marks):**

$$V_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{add}} = V_1 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{a} \right) = 400 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{2} \right) = 400 \times 1.5 = \mathbf{600 \; V}$$

$$I_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{add}} = \frac{V_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{add}}}{R_L} = \frac{600}{400} = \mathbf{1.5 \; A}$$

$$P_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{add}} = \frac{V^2}{R_L} = \frac{(600)^2}{400} = \mathbf{900 \; W}$$

**Comment:** Lamp receives **3× its rated voltage** (600 V vs 200 V). The filament will glow at **extreme brightness (white-hot)** and will likely **burn out within seconds**. → Additive Polarity confirmed.

**Subtractive Polarity — Case 2 (3 marks):**

$$V_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{sub}} = V_1 \left( 1 - \frac{1}{a} \right) = 400 \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2} \right) = 400 \times 0.5 = \mathbf{200 \; V}$$

$$I_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{sub}} = \frac{V_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{sub}}}{R_L} = \frac{200}{400} = \mathbf{0.5 \; A}$$

$$P_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{sub}} = \frac{V^2}{R_L} = \frac{(200)^2}{400} = \mathbf{100 \; W}$$

**Comment:** Lamp operates at **exactly its rated values** (200 V, 100 W) → **Normal warm glow**. → Subtractive Polarity confirmed.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the voltage across the lamp in the Bright Lamp Method for (i) additive polarity and (ii) subtractive polarity. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Setup (1 mark):**
- Primary: A₁–A₂ energized with $V_1$ (RMS).
- Secondary: a₁–a₂ with induced EMF $V_2 = V_1/a$, where $a = N_1/N_2$.
- Jumper: A₂ connected to a₁.
- Lamp: Connected between A₁ and a₂.
- Define $V_{A_2} = 0$ (reference) and $V_{A_1} = V_1$ (from supply).

**(i) Additive Polarity Derivation (3 marks):**
In additive polarity, terminals A₁ and a₁ have the *same instantaneous sign*. Hence:

$$V_{a_1} - V_{a_2} = +\frac{V_1}{a}$$

Since $V_{a_1} = V_{A_2} = 0$ (jumper connection):

$$0 - V_{a_2} = +\frac{V_1}{a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{a_2} = -\frac{V_1}{a}$$

Lamp voltage:

$$V_{\text{lamp}} = V_{A_1} - V_{a_2} = V_1 - \left(-\frac{V_1}{a}\right) = V_1 \left(1 + \frac{1}{a}\right) \quad \text{(BRIGHT)}$$

**(ii) Subtractive Polarity Derivation (3 marks):**
In subtractive polarity, terminals A₁ and a₁ have *opposite instantaneous signs*. Hence:

$$V_{a_1} - V_{a_2} = -\frac{V_1}{a}$$

Since $V_{a_1} = 0$:

$$0 - V_{a_2} = -\frac{V_1}{a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{a_2} = +\frac{V_1}{a}$$

Lamp voltage:

$$V_{\text{lamp}} = V_{A_1} - V_{a_2} = V_1 - \frac{V_1}{a} = V_1 \left(1 - \frac{1}{a}\right) \quad \text{(DIM)}$$

**Conclusion:** The factor $(1 \pm 1/a)$ is the *hallmark* of the Bright Lamp Method. Additive gives sum, subtractive gives difference.

---

**(b) A 1:1 isolation transformer (230 V / 230 V) is tested using the Bright Lamp Method. The primary is connected to a 230 V AC supply. What will be the observation in (i) additive and (ii) subtractive cases? Justify your answer with calculations. Why is this test particularly important for 1:1 transformers? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- V₁ = 230 V
- Turns ratio: a = 1
- V₂ = 230/1 = 230 V

**(i) Additive Case (2.5 marks):**

$$V_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{add}} = V_1 \left(1 + \frac{1}{a}\right) = 230 \times (1 + 1) = \mathbf{460 \; V}$$

The lamp receives **double the rated voltage** → extremely bright, possible burn-out.

**(ii) Subtractive Case (2.5 marks):**

$$V_{\text{lamp}}^{\text{sub}} = V_1 \left(1 - \frac{1}{a}\right) = 230 \times (1 - 1) = \mathbf{0 \; V}$$

The lamp receives **zero voltage** → **lamp does NOT glow at all** (complete darkness).

**Justification of Importance (1 mark):**
For a 1:1 transformer, the *complete absence of glow* in the subtractive case is the **most unambiguous visual cue** in the entire polarity test. Unlike step-down transformers (where the lamp merely dims), the 1:1 case gives a binary "ON/OFF" result — making it the *gold standard* for laboratory demonstrations and for verifying the dot convention in isolation transformers used in medical equipment, instrument transformers, and noise-sensitive measurement systems.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — Read Carefully:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder (–2 marks):** Students often write $V_{\text{lamp}} = V_1 - V_2$ for *additive* polarity. The correct formula is **$V_1 + V_2$** for additive. Memorize: **B**right = Su**M** (BMS trick).
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to State the Lamp Rating (–1 mark):** If the question gives a lamp rating, your answer MUST compare the calculated $V_{\text{lamp}}$ to the rating. "Bright glow" without this comparison is incomplete.
> 
> 3. **Confusing Dot Convention with Winding Direction (–1 mark):** The dots are *assigned* AFTER the test, not *assumed* before. Writing "assuming dots on A₁ and a₁" before the test is a conceptual error.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the Jumper Connection (–1 mark):** A common omission. The test is *impossible* without the A₂-to-a₁ jumper — the lamp voltage formula is derived assuming this connection.
> 
> 5. **No Circuit Diagram in Part (a) (–2 marks):** KTU examiners *expect* a circuit diagram for any 7-mark descriptive question. Drawing it neatly with proper dot markings is worth at least 2 marks by itself.
> 
> 6. **Unit Errors in Numerical Answers (–0.5 mark):** Always write units (V, A, W) — partial credit is reduced by half for missing units in numerical sub-parts.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The Bright Lamp Method is a *qualitative* test for identifying the **relative polarity (additive vs subtractive)** of transformer windings using a filament lamp as a visual indicator.
- **Core Setup:** Primary energized → A₂ shorted to a₁ → lamp across A₁ and a₂.
- **Bright Lamp ⇒ Additive Polarity** (terminals A₁ and a₁ are of *same* instantaneous polarity; dot on these).
- **Dim/No Lamp ⇒ Subtractive Polarity** (terminals A₁ and a₁ are of *opposite* instantaneous polarity; swap a₁ ↔ a₂).
- **Master Formulas:** $V_{\text{lamp}} = V_1(1 \pm 1/a)$ where the **+** sign is additive, **−** is subtractive.
- **1:1 Special Case:** Subtractive gives $V_{\text{lamp}} = 0$ → lamp completely OFF (the most unambiguous cue).
- **Step-Down Typical Case (a = 2):** Additive gives $1.5\,V_1$, subtractive gives $0.5\,V_1$.
- **Step-Up Typical Case (a = 0.5):** Additive gives $3\,V_1$, subtractive gives $V_1$ (lamp glows normally — counter-intuitive!).
- **Governing Principle:** Kirchhoff's Voltage Law applied to a magnetically-coupled series loop.
- **Real-World Use:** Pre-paralleling check for transformers, three-phase bank formation, CT polarity verification, autotransformer buck/boost mode identification.
- **Lamp Rating:** Should be approximately equal to V₂ (rated secondary voltage) to give a safe "rated glow" in the subtractive case and a *visibly brighter* glow in the additive case.
- **Caution:** In additive mode, lamp is over-driven; keep test duration short (≤ 5 seconds) to avoid filament burn-out.
- **Difference from Voltmeter Test:** Voltmeter test gives numerical reading; Lamp test gives *visual* binary cue — no instrument calibration needed, ideal for field work.
- **Related Concepts to Revise:** Dot convention, Mutual inductance, EMF equation of transformer, Polarity test using voltmeter, Parallel operation conditions.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** The Bright Lamp Method is most frequently asked as a *short derivation* in Part A (3 marks) or as a *numerical sub-question* in Part B (7 marks). Memorize the two formulas $V_1(1 + 1/a)$ and $V_1(1 - 1/a)$ — they are the single most important equations in this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronous Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synchronous Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronous Motor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *synchronous motor* is a doubly-excited AC machine whose rotor rotates at a *constant speed* (the **synchronous speed**, $N_s$) that is independent of the load, provided the supply frequency is held constant. The rotor is either a **salient-pole** rotor (with projecting field poles) or a **cylindrical (non-salient) rotor** (with uniformly distributed field winding), and is excited by a separate DC source through slip rings.

Mathematically, the rotor speed is rigidly tied to the supply frequency by:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$

where:
- $N_s$ = synchronous speed in **rpm** (revolutions per minute)
- $f$ = supply frequency in **Hz** (Hertz) — typically **50 Hz** in India
- $P$ = total number of magnetic poles on the machine

> [!NOTE]
> Because the rotor "locks in step" (synchronizes) with the rotating magnetic field of the stator, there is **no slip** between the rotor and the stator field — the slip is identically **zero** at steady state. This is the defining physical property that distinguishes a synchronous machine from an induction machine.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marching Soldiers" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a circular roundabout (the **stator** rotating magnetic field) moving at a constant angular speed. Soldiers standing on the rim (the **stator winding** producing N–S poles) clap their hands in time (the **3-phase AC supply**). Now imagine a child sitting on a small wheel at the center (the **rotor**) holding two bar magnets (the **field poles**). If the child spins the small wheel by hand and tries to match the *exact* rotation rate of the marching claps, the magnets will "lock" to the field of the clapping soldiers. Once locked, the small wheel rotates at **exactly** the same speed as the roundabout — neither faster nor slower. Push on the small wheel (apply load) and it *leans* a bit (the **load angle $\delta$** increases) but still rotates at the same speed.

This "leaning" is precisely the **torque angle** $\delta$ — the angular displacement between the rotor pole axis and the resultant stator flux axis. As long as the load keeps $\delta$ below a critical value (the **pull-out torque**), the motor runs synchronously. If the load exceeds pull-out torque, the rotor **falls out of step** and comes to a halt (the "marching soldiers outpace the child").

### 1.3 Why Synchronous Motors? (Engineering Relevance)

> [!NOTE]
> Synchronous motors are the **workhorses of constant-speed industrial drives** — compressors, rolling mills, cement mills, large pumps, and power-factor correction banks. Their unique ability to operate at **leading, unity, or lagging power factor** (by adjusting field current) makes them *dual-purpose* devices: they drive mechanical loads **and** correct the power factor of the plant simultaneously. This dual role is heavily tested in KTU University Examinations.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Ratings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard Indian supply: $f = \mathbf{50\ Hz}$
> Standard pole counts in industry: $P = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, \ldots$ (always even)
> Standard synchronous speeds (at **50 Hz**): $3000, 1500, 1000, 750, 600, \ldots\ \text{rpm}$
> Mechanical synchronous angular speed: $\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{4\pi f}{P}$ **rad/s**
> Electrical synchronous angular speed: $\omega_e = 2\pi f$ **rad/s** (always the supply angular frequency)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction — Two Rotor Topologies

A synchronous motor has two mechanically different rotor constructions, each with distinct electrical behaviour.

#### A. Salient-Pole (Projecting-Pole) Rotor
- Field poles **project inward** toward the air gap.
- Field winding is concentrated around each pole shoe.
- Used for **low-speed** machines (typically $N_s \le 1500\ \text{rpm}$) — e.g., hydro-generators, large compressors.
- Exhibits **direct-axis (d-axis)** and **quadrature-axis (q-axis)** reactances, with $X_d \ne X_q$. This produces the **reluctance torque** (used in *reluctance motors*, a subclass).

#### B. Cylindrical (Non-Salient) Rotor
- Rotor is a **smooth, uniform cylinder** of forged steel with slots milled axially to house the field winding.
- Air-gap **reluctance is uniform** in all angular positions.
- Used for **high-speed** machines (typically $N_s = 1500$ or $3000\ \text{rpm}$) — e.g., turbo-alternators, high-speed synchronous motors.
- Has a single synchronous reactance $X_s = X_d = X_q$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** When a problem says "uniform air gap" or doesn't mention pole projections, treat it as a **cylindrical rotor** with $X_s$ only. When it says "salient pole," you **must** split the analysis into d-axis and q-axis using the **two-reaction theory** (Blondel's theorem).

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step by Step

1. The **3-phase stator winding** is supplied with balanced 3-phase AC voltages.
2. The resulting 3-phase currents produce a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** in the air gap, rotating at synchronous speed $N_s$.
3. The **rotor field winding** is excited with DC current, producing alternate N–S poles on the rotor.
4. At standstill, the rotor is stationary while the stator RMF rotates past it at $N_s$ — the rotor experiences a rapidly reversing torque that **averages to zero** (the stator RMF is too fast to "catch" the rotor). Therefore, **a synchronous motor has no inherent starting torque**.
5. Once the rotor is brought to near-synchronous speed (by an external means), the rotor poles **lock** with the opposite-polarity stator RMF poles. From this instant, the rotor rotates at $N_s$ — *synchronous operation is established*.
6. The mechanical load determines the **torque angle** $\delta$: more load $\Rightarrow$ rotor poles lag further behind the stator flux $\Rightarrow$ $\delta$ increases, up to a maximum (pull-out).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **average torque** developed by a synchronous motor at synchronous speed is:
>
> $$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$$
>
> This shows torque is **maximum when $\delta = 90^\circ$** — this is the **pull-out torque**. For stable operation, $\delta$ is held between **0° and ~30°** typically.

### 2.3 Methods of Starting

Since a synchronous motor has no inherent starting torque, one of the following auxiliary methods is used:

| Method | Principle | Typical Application |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Pony-motor starting** | A small auxiliary motor drives the rotor to near-$N_s$ before DC excitation is applied | Large salient-pole machines |
| **Damper-winding starting** | Cage-like copper bars in the rotor pole faces act as an induction-motor cage during start-up; once near $N_s$, DC is applied and the rotor pulls into synchronism | Most modern synchronous motors (standard method) |
| **Variable-frequency (VFD) starting** | Stator supply frequency is ramped up from near-zero, with rotor following; DC is applied after the rotor catches up | Modern industrial drives |
| **Reduced-voltage starting** | Auto-transformer or reactor starting to limit inrush current | Medium-size machines |

> [!TIP]
> The **damper winding** has a dual purpose: (1) it provides **starting torque as an induction motor**, and (2) it **damps out mechanical oscillations (hunting)** once the motor is in synchronism.

### 2.4 Equivalent Circuit and Phasor Diagram (Cylindrical Rotor)

The per-phase equivalent circuit of a cylindrical-rotor synchronous motor (armature side referred to the stator) consists of:
- Armature resistance $R_a$
- Synchronous reactance $X_s$ (combination of leakage reactance $X_l$ and armature reaction reactance $X_{ar}$)

$$V = E_f + I_a(R_a + j X_s)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention for motor:** $E_f$ (back-EMF) and $V$ (terminal voltage) are on **opposite sides** of $I_a$ in the phasor diagram. The angle between $E_f$ and $V$ is the **torque angle** $\delta$, with $E_f$ **lagging** $V$ for a motor (rotor flux lags resultant flux).

### 2.5 Power-Factor Behaviour — The "Over-Excited, Under-Excited" Story

By varying the field current $I_f$ while keeping the load (and hence $P$) constant, the motor's armature current and power factor change dramatically. This is summarized in the **V-curve** relationship (see §3.4 for derivation and plot).

| Excitation State | Field Current $I_f$ | $E_f$ Magnitude | Power Factor |
|------------------|---------------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Normal (under-excited)** | Below rated | $E_f < V$ | **Lagging** |
| **Unity excitation** | At rated level | $E_f \approx V$ | **Unity** |
| **Over-excited** | Above rated | $E_f > V$ | **Leading** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **over-excited synchronous motor behaves like a capacitor** — it draws leading current. This is the basis of using synchronous motors as **synchronous condensers** for power-factor correction in industrial plants.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / KTU Context |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | **Most fundamental equation** of synchronous machines |
| 2 | Mechanical angular speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | Use in mechanical power equations |
| 3 | EMF equation (per phase, RMS) | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, k_w$ | V | $\phi$ = flux per pole in **Wb**, $T_{ph}$ = turns/phase, $k_w$ = winding factor |
| 4 | Back-EMF (motor) | $E_f$ | V | Induced EMF in the armature due to rotor field; **lags** $V$ in a motor |
| 5 | Synchronous impedance | $\bar{Z}_s = R_a + j X_s$ | $\Omega$ | Per-phase, complex form |
| 6 | Synchronous reactance | $X_s = X_l + X_{ar}$ | $\Omega$ | $X_l$ = leakage, $X_{ar}$ = armature reaction |
| 7 | Power developed (mech., per phase) | $P_d = \dfrac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$ (with $R_a \approx 0$) | W | For cylindrical rotor, $R_a$ neglected |
| 8 | Total mechanical power (3-phase) | $P_m = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$ | W | Most-used power expression in KTU |
| 9 | Torque developed | $T_e = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$ | N·m | Pull-out torque at $\delta = 90^\circ$ |
| 10 | Power factor (motor) | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L}$ | — | Sign convention: lagging is +ve for motoring load |
| 11 | Salient-pole power (2-reaction) | $P = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{X_d}\sin\delta + \dfrac{3 V^2}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{X_q} - \dfrac{1}{X_d}\right)\!\sin 2\delta$ | W | KTU 14-mark favourite for salient-pole problems |
| 12 | Reluctance power term | $P_r = \dfrac{3 V^2}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{X_d - X_q}{X_d X_q}\right)\!\sin 2\delta$ | W | Appears only when $X_d \ne X_q$ |
| 13 | Condition for unity p.f. | $E_f \cos\delta = V$ | V | For a cylindrical motor with $R_a = 0$ |
| 14 | Condition for zero p.f. (max $I_a$) | $E_f \cos\delta = \dfrac{V}{2}$ | V | Used in deriving V-curve minimum current point |
| 15 | Synchronous condenser rating | $Q_c = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L$ | VAR | Reactive power supplied as leading current |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pipe-Symbol Warning:** In handwritten or LaTeX-free contexts, $\vert Z_s \vert$ is written as the magnitude. In the table above, $\dfrac{1}{X_q} - \dfrac{1}{X_d}$ is **not** a vertical bar — it is a difference. Do not confuse with $\vert\cdot\vert$ magnitude.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: EMF Equation of a Synchronous Machine (Per Phase, RMS)

Let the flux per pole be $\Phi$ **Wb**, frequency be $f$ **Hz**, total turns per phase be $T_{ph}$, and winding factor be $k_w$.

> **Step 1 — Flux variation in one cycle.** Since the rotor flux $\Phi$ links and unlinks a stator conductor twice per mechanical rotation per pair of poles, the flux variation per cycle (per electrical revolution) follows a sinusoidal law.

> **Step 2 — Average EMF per conductor.** From Faraday's law, the average EMF induced in one conductor as it cuts the flux $\Phi$ once is:
> $$\text{EMF}_{\text{avg,cond}} = 4 f \Phi \quad [\text{volts}]$$

> **Step 3 — Convert average to RMS.** Assuming sinusoidal flux variation, the form factor is $1.11$ (i.e., $k_f = 1.11$):
> $$\text{EMF}_{\text{rms,cond}} = 1.11 \times 4 f \Phi = 4.44 \, f \Phi$$

> **Step 4 — Account for all turns in a coil and the winding factor.** The total RMS EMF per phase is the EMF per conductor times the total turns per phase times the winding factor (which accounts for distribution and pitch):
> $$\boxed{\,E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi \, T_{ph} \, k_w\,} \quad [\text{volts, RMS, per phase}]$$

> **Step 5 — Multi-line alignment for clarity.** The complete derivation step block:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{avg,cond}} &= 4 \, f \, \Phi \\[4pt]
E_{\text{rms,cond}} &= k_f \cdot E_{\text{avg,cond}} = 1.11 \times 4 \, f \, \Phi = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi \\[4pt]
E_{ph} &= 4.44 \, f \, \Phi \cdot T_{ph} \cdot k_w
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **4.44** is the product $4 \times 1.11$. KTU examiners expect students to **memorize the form factor 1.11** for sine waves and to clearly distinguish between **average** ($4 f \Phi$) and **RMS** ($4.44 f \Phi$) values.

### 3.2 Derivation: Power Developed by a Cylindrical-Rotor Synchronous Motor

**Assumption:** $R_a \ll X_s$ and is neglected. Per-phase terminal voltage is $V$, back-EMF is $E_f$, and the angle between $V$ and $E_f$ (rotor side) is $\delta$.

> **Step 1 — Phasor equation.** From the per-phase equivalent circuit:
> $$V = E_f + j I_a X_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_a = \dfrac{V - E_f}{j X_s} = \dfrac{E_f - V}{j X_s}$$

> **Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator to eliminate $j$.** Multiplying top and bottom by $j$ rotates the numerator by $+90^\circ$ and makes the denominator real:
> $$I_a = \dfrac{j(E_f - V)}{X_s}$$

> **Step 3 — Resolve into components.** Take $V$ as the reference phasor (along real axis): $V = V \angle 0^\circ$, and $E_f = E_f \angle{-\delta}$ (lagging $V$ in a motor by $\delta$).
> $$I_a = \dfrac{j E_f \angle{-\delta} - j V}{X_s} = \dfrac{j (E_f \cos\delta - j E_f \sin\delta) - j V}{X_s}$$

> **Step 4 — Expand and collect real/imaginary parts.**
> $$I_a = \dfrac{E_f \sin\delta}{X_s} + j \cdot \dfrac{E_f \cos\delta - V}{X_s}$$

> **Step 5 — Identify the in-phase (real) component.** The real part of $I_a$ is in phase with $V$, so the per-phase real power input from the stator is:
> $$P_{\text{in,per phase}} = V \cdot I_a \cos\phi = V \cdot \dfrac{E_f \sin\delta}{X_s}$$

> **Step 6 — Multiply by 3 for three phases.** The total power input to all three phases (and, neglecting $R_a$, equal to the mechanical power developed) is:
> $$\boxed{\,P_m = \dfrac{3 \, V \, E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta\,} \quad [\text{watts}]$$

> **Step 7 — Convert to torque.** Divide by mechanical synchronous angular speed $\omega_s$:
> $$T_e = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

> **Step 8 — Alignment block summary.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= E_f + j I_a X_s \\[4pt]
I_a &= \dfrac{E_f - V}{j X_s} = \dfrac{j(E_f - V)}{X_s} \\[4pt]
I_a &= \dfrac{E_f \sin\delta}{X_s} + j\dfrac{E_f \cos\delta - V}{X_s} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{per phase}} &= V \cdot \dfrac{E_f \sin\delta}{X_s} \\[4pt]
P_m &= \dfrac{3 V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta \quad \text{(total 3-phase)} \\[4pt]
T_e &= \dfrac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum power condition:** Since $\sin\delta$ is maximum (= 1) at $\delta = 90^\circ$, the **maximum (pull-out) power** of a cylindrical-rotor synchronous motor is $P_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{X_s}$. Stable operation requires $\delta$ in the range $0^\circ < \delta < 90^\circ$ (typically $\delta \le 30^\circ$ for safe margin).

### 3.3 Derivation: Salient-Pole Power (Two-Reaction Theory)

When the rotor has salient (projecting) poles, the air-gap reluctance depends on the angular position of the rotor. The armature MMF $F_a$ is split into two orthogonal components using **Blondel's two-reaction theory**:

- **Direct-axis component $F_d = F_a \sin\psi$** — aligned with the rotor pole axis (field axis)
- **Quadrature-axis component $F_q = F_a \cos\psi$** — aligned perpendicular to the pole axis

where $\psi$ is the angle between $E_f$ and $I_a$. Each component produces its own EMF, with respective reactances $X_d$ and $X_q$:

> **Step 1 — Two orthogonal voltages.**
> $$V = E_f + j I_d X_d + j I_q X_q \quad \text{(neglecting } R_a\text{)}$$

> **Step 2 — Resolve $I_a$ into d- and q-axis components** using the angle $\psi$:
> $$I_d = I_a \sin\psi, \qquad I_q = I_a \cos\psi$$

> **Step 3 — Resolve $V$ into components along $d$ and $q$ axes.** Taking $E_f$ along the q-axis (perpendicular convention is common; here we adopt the standard textbook derivation with $E_f$ on the d-axis, but the final form is convention-independent). With $E_f$ along d-axis and $V$ leading $E_f$ by $\delta$:
> $$V \sin\delta = I_q X_q \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_q = \dfrac{V \sin\delta}{X_q}$$
> $$V \cos\delta = E_f + I_d X_d \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_d = \dfrac{V \cos\delta - E_f}{X_d}$$

> **Step 4 — Compute per-phase real power** as $V \cdot I_a \cos\phi$ with $\cos\phi$ resolved:
> $$P_{\text{per phase}} = V \cos\delta \cdot I_q + V \sin\delta \cdot I_d$$

> **Step 5 — Substitute $I_d$ and $I_q$.**
> $$P_{\text{per phase}} = V \cos\delta \cdot \dfrac{V \sin\delta}{X_q} + V \sin\delta \cdot \dfrac{V \cos\delta - E_f}{X_d}$$

> **Step 6 — Simplify and multiply by 3.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{per phase}} &= \dfrac{V^2 \sin\delta \cos\delta}{X_q} + \dfrac{V \sin\delta \, V \cos\delta - V E_f \sin\delta}{X_d} \\[6pt]
&= V \sin\delta \cos\delta \left(\dfrac{V}{X_q} + \dfrac{V}{X_d}\right) - \dfrac{V E_f}{X_d} \sin\delta \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{V^2 \sin\delta \cos\delta}{2} \left(\dfrac{1}{X_q} + \dfrac{1}{X_d}\right) - \dfrac{V E_f}{X_d} \sin\delta
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This intermediate form is **not** the standard textbook form. To get the clean form, note that $\dfrac{V^2}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{X_q} - \dfrac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin 2\delta$ can be added and subtracted. After algebraic manipulation (see below), the final form is:

> **Step 7 — Final simplified result for total 3-phase power:**

$$
\boxed{\,P = \underbrace{\dfrac{3 V E_f}{X_d} \sin\delta}_{\text{Excitation power}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{3 V^2}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{X_q} - \dfrac{1}{X_d}\right)\!\sin 2\delta}_{\text{Reluctance power}}\,}
$$

> **Step 8 — Numerical evaluation check (KTU-style).** Suppose $V = 400/\sqrt{3} \approx 230.9\ \text{V}$ (phase), $E_f = 300\ \text{V}$, $X_d = 2\ \Omega$, $X_q = 1.2\ \Omega$, $\delta = 20^\circ$. Then:
> - Excitation power = $\dfrac{3 \times 230.9 \times 300}{2} \sin 20^\circ = \dfrac{3 \times 230.9 \times 300 \times 0.342}{2} \approx 35{,}540\ \text{W}$
> - Reluctance power = $\dfrac{3 \times (230.9)^2}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{1.2} - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) \sin 40^\circ = \dfrac{3 \times 53{,}315}{2} \times 0.5 \times 0.6428 \approx 25{,}705\ \text{W}$
> - Total $P \approx 35{,}540 + 25{,}705 = 61{,}245\ \text{W} \approx 61.2\ \text{kW}$

### 3.4 Derivation: V-Curve Relation (Field Current vs Armature Current)

> **Step 1 — Constant mechanical power assumption.** At a fixed load, the real power $P = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ is constant.

> **Step 2 — Vary excitation $E_f$ (i.e., field current $I_f$).** As $E_f$ changes, both $I_L$ and $\cos\phi$ adjust to keep $P$ constant. The relation (from the phasor diagram with $R_a \approx 0$) is:
> $$I_a \cos\phi = \text{constant} = \dfrac{P}{3 V_{ph}}$$

> **Step 3 — The locus of $E_f$ tip is a circle.** With $V_{ph}$ as a fixed reference and $I_a \cos\phi$ constant, the tip of $E_f$ traces a horizontal line (or, equivalently, a circle when $E_f$ varies with both magnitude and angle). The phasor diagram shows $E_f$ moving along a vertical line through a fixed real-axis point.

> **Step 4 — Minimum armature current condition.** $|I_a|$ is minimum when $I_a$ is in phase with $V$, i.e., $\cos\phi = 1$. This happens at the **unity-power-factor** excitation. Solving:
> $$E_f \cos\delta = V \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_f = V / \cos\delta$$

> **Step 5 — Substituting back into the $I_a$ phasor expression.** When $E_f = V / \cos\delta$, the perpendicular (reactive) component of $I_a$ vanishes, and the minimum current is:
> $$I_{a,\min} = \dfrac{E_f \sin\delta}{X_s} = \dfrac{V \tan\delta}{X_s}$$

> **Step 6 — Final V-curve shape.** Plotting $I_a$ on the y-axis and $I_f$ on the x-axis for various constant mechanical loads yields a family of **V-shaped curves** — the "V-curves." The locus of the unity-power-factor points (the bottoms of the Vs) is the **inverted V-curve** (or "compound characteristic").

> **Step 7 — Key conclusion.**
> - For any load, there is **one value of $I_f$** that gives unity p.f. (minimum $I_a$).
> - For $I_f$ below this, p.f. is **lagging** (machine draws reactive power from the bus).
> - For $I_f$ above this, p.f. is **leading** (machine supplies reactive power to the bus — acts as a synchronous condenser).

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical example (KTU-style):** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole synchronous motor has $X_s = 4\ \Omega$ per phase. It draws 20 A at 0.8 p.f. lagging. Find $E_f$ and $\delta$. Solution outline: $V_{ph} = 400/\sqrt{3} = 230.9\ \text{V}$, $I_a = 20 \angle -36.87^\circ\ \text{A}$, then $E_f = V - j I_a X_s = 230.9 - j(20 \angle -36.87^\circ)(4)$. Compute step by step in the model answer below (§5).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — KTU 14-Mark Sub-Question Templates

| Sub-Question Theme | Required Inputs | Required Outputs | Key Equations to Apply |
|--------------------|-----------------|------------------|------------------------|
| Find $E_f$ and $\delta$ at given load | $V, I_a, \cos\phi, X_s$ | $E_f = \vert V - j I_a X_s \vert$, $\delta = \angle(E_f, V)$ | $E_f = \sqrt{V^2 + (I_a X_s)^2 - 2 V I_a X_s \sin\phi}$ |
| Pull-out torque | $V, E_f, X_s, f, P$ | $T_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s}$ | $T_{\text{max}} = T_e \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sin\delta_{\text{op}}}$ |
| V-curve analysis | $P, V, X_s$, vary $E_f$ | $I_a$ vs $E_f$ or $I_f$ | $I_a^2 = \left(\dfrac{E_f \sin\delta}{X_s}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{V - E_f \cos\delta}{X_s}\right)^2$ |
| Synchronous condenser rating | Required leading kVAR | Field current, $E_f$, $\tan\delta = \dfrac{I_a X_s \pm V\sin\phi}{V \cos\phi}$ | $Q_c = 3 V_{ph} I_a \sin\phi$ |
| Salient-pole power | $V, E_f, X_d, X_q, \delta$ | $P_{\text{exc}}$, $P_{\text{rel}}$, $P_{\text{total}}$ | $P = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{X_d} \sin\delta + \dfrac{3 V^2}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{X_q} - \dfrac{1}{X_d}\right)\!\sin 2\delta$ |
| Hunting frequency | $P, f, J$ | Natural frequency $f_n$ | $f_n = \dfrac{P}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{p_{\text{sync}}}{J}}$ (where $J$ is inertia) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Synchronous Motor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC3P["3-Phase AC Supply (50 Hz)"] --> VFD["VFD / Starter / DOL"]
    VFD --> STATOR["Stator Winding (Armature)"]
    DC["DC Excitation Source"] --> SLIPRING["Slip Rings & Brushes"]
    SLIPRING --> ROTOR["Rotor Field Winding"]
    DAMPER["Damper Winding (Cage Bars)"] -.-> ROTOR
    STATOR --> AIRGAP["Air-Gap Flux Linkage"]
    ROTOR --> AIRGAP
    AIRGAP --> MECH["Mechanical Shaft Output"]
    LOAD["Mechanical Load"] --> MECH
    CTRL["Governor / Load Sensor"] --> DC

    classDef power fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:1px
    classDef control fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:1px
    classDef mech fill:#e8fce8,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:1px
    class AC3P,VFD,STATOR,DC,SLIPRING,ROTOR,AIRGAP power
    class CTRL control
    class MECH,LOAD,DAMPER mech
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Electrical power flows from the **3-phase supply** through a starter/VFD to the **stator**. A separate **DC source** feeds the **rotor field winding** through **slip rings**. The mechanical coupling is established through the **air-gap flux** linking the two windings. The **damper winding** provides starting torque and oscillation damping. The **controller** adjusts DC excitation to control power factor.

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Power-Factor Correction Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BUS["Plant AC Bus (e.g., 11 kV)"] --> INDLOAD["Inductive Loads (Motors, Transformers)"]
    INDLOAD -->|Demands lagging kVAR| BUS
    BUS --> SYNC_MOTOR["Over-Excited Synchronous Motor"]
    SYNC_MOTOR -->|Supplies leading kVAR| BUS
    SYNC_MOTOR --> MECHLOAD2["Mechanical Load (Pump / Mill)"]
    SYNC_MOTOR -.->|Excitation increased| EXC_CTRL["AVR / Exciter"]
    EXC_CTRL -->|Field current| SYNC_MOTOR

    classDef elec fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:1px
    classDef mech fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ctrl fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:1px
    class BUS,INDLOAD,SYNC_MOTOR elec
    class MECHLOAD2 mech
    class EXC_CTRL ctrl
```

> **Reading the diagram:** When the **synchronous motor is over-excited**, it draws a **leading current** from the bus. This leading kVAR is delivered **to the bus** to compensate for the **lagging kVAR** demanded by inductive loads. The result: the bus's overall power factor improves toward unity. The motor simultaneously drives its mechanical load.

### 4.3 Phasor Diagram Block (Cylindrical Rotor, Motor Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V_REF["V (Reference, along +x axis)"] --> I_TIP["Tip of I_a Phasor"]
    V_REF --> E_TIP["E_f Lags V by δ"]
    V_REF --> DROP["j·I_a·X_s Drop"]
    I_TIP -->|Lagging by φ| DROP
    DROP -->|Perpendicular to I_a| E_TIP
    E_TIP -->|Vector sum: V = E_f + j·I_a·X_s| V_REF

    classDef ref fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1px
    classDef lag fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:1px
    classDef drop fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:1px
    class V_REF ref
    class I_TIP,E_TIP lag
    class DROP drop
```

> **Reading the diagram:** For a motor at **lagging power factor**, take $V$ as the reference. The armature current $I_a$ lags $V$ by $\phi$. The voltage drop $j I_a X_s$ is **perpendicular to $I_a$** (rotated $+90^\circ$ anticlockwise). The back-EMF $E_f$ is found by vector subtraction: $E_f = V - j I_a X_s$. In a motor, $E_f$ **lags** $V$ by the torque angle $\delta$.

### 4.4 Excitation State Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Motor at constant load P"] --> Q1{"Compare I_f to rated?"}
    Q1 -->|Below rated| Q2{"Is E_f < V?"}
    Q1 -->|Above rated| Q3["Over-excited"]
    Q1 -->|Equal to rated| Q4["Normal (unity or near-unity p.f.)"]
    Q2 -->|Yes| UE["Under-excited: Lagging p.f., draws reactive Q from bus"]
    Q2 -->|No / borderline| Q5["Near unity p.f., minimum I_a"]
    Q3 --> OE["Over-excited: Leading p.f., supplies reactive Q to bus (Synchronous Condenser Mode)"]
    UE --> APPL["Used rarely; can cause instability at high loads"]
    OE --> APPL2["Preferred for industrial p.f. correction"]
    Q5 --> APPL3["Most efficient operation point"]
    Q4 --> APPL3

    classDef ok fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:1px
    classDef warn fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:1px
    classDef neutral fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:1px
    class Q5,APPL2,APPL3,OE ok
    class UE,APPL warn
    class Q1,Q2,Q4,START neutral
```

flowchart TB

    POLE_BODY --> DAMPER_W["Damper Winding (Copper Bars in Pole Faces)"]
