## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Set B]**
*Define synchronous speed. A 3-phase, 6-pole synchronous machine is connected to a 50 Hz, 3-phase supply. Determine its synchronous speed.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Synchronous speed is the constant speed at which the rotor of a synchronous machine rotates. It is the speed of the rotating magnetic field produced by the stator, and is determined by the supply frequency $f$ and the number of poles $P$ as:

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$$

**Calculation (1 Mark):**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ RPM}$$

Therefore, the synchronous speed is **1000 RPM**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, End Semester Evaluation, Q.1(b)]**
*With the help of a neat diagram, explain the principle of operation of a synchronous generator.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A synchronous generator (alternator) converts mechanical energy into electrical energy using **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. The rotor, carrying a DC-excited field winding, is driven by a prime mover at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$. As the rotor flux rotates, it links the stationary three-phase stator (armature) windings, inducing three EMFs displaced in time by 120° electrical. The frequency of the induced EMF is governed by the equation:

$$f = \frac{P \cdot N_s}{120}$$

The induced EMFs per phase are:

$$e_A = E_m \sin(\omega t), \quad e_B = E_m \sin(\omega t - 120°), \quad e_C = E_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$

This produces a balanced 3-phase AC output that is delivered to the load.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning**: Students frequently lose marks by:
> 1. Forgetting to state the principle explicitly (Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction).
> 2. Missing the 120° phase displacement between phases.
> 3. Omitting the equation linking $f$, $P$, and $N_s$.
> 4. Confusing "induced EMF" with "terminal voltage" — these are equal only when the machine is on no-load.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Synchronous Motor Principle + Torque**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4 — KTU 2024 Scheme Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a 3-phase synchronous motor with the help of a neat block diagram. Derive the EMF equation of a synchronous generator. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz synchronous motor is supplied with a balanced 3-phase voltage of 400 V (line) per phase. The synchronous reactance per phase is $X_s = 8 \, \Omega$ and armature resistance is negligible. The excitation EMF per phase is $E_f = 300$ V. If the load angle is $\delta = 30°$ (mechanical), calculate: (i) Synchronous speed, (ii) The developed electromagnetic torque. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks):**

**Principle of Operation of Synchronous Motor (4 Marks):**

A synchronous motor operates on the principle of **magnetic locking (synchronism)** between the stator's rotating magnetic field and the rotor's DC-excited poles.

1. When a balanced 3-phase AC supply is given to the stator (armature) winding, it produces a **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)** at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$.

2. The rotor is separately excited with DC through slip rings, creating a fixed magnetic field with alternating N and S poles.

3. The stator's RMF's N-pole attracts the rotor's S-pole, and the S-pole of the stator attracts the N-pole of the rotor. This magnetic attraction creates a strong **synchronous coupling**.

4. The rotor accelerates and locks into synchronism with the RMF, rotating at exactly the same speed $N_s$.

5. The electromagnetic torque developed is:

$$T_e = \frac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$$

where $\delta$ is the load/torque angle.

6. The rotor speed remains **constant at $N_s$** irrespective of the load, as long as the machine maintains synchronism.

**EMF Equation Derivation (3 Marks):**

As derived in Section 3.2, the RMS EMF per phase of a synchronous generator is:

$$E_{ph,rms} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot \Phi \cdot T_{ph} \cdot K_w$$

where $K_w = K_d \cdot K_p$ is the winding factor.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$ poles
- $V_L = 400$ V (line-to-line), so $V_{ph} = 400/\sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V
- $X_s = 8 \, \Omega$, $R_a \approx 0$
- $E_f = 300$ V per phase
- $\delta_{mech} = 30°$ (mechanical)

**Step 1 — Synchronous Speed (2 Marks):**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Load Angle to Electrical (1 Mark):**

$$\delta_{elec} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \delta_{mech} = \frac{4}{2} \times 30° = 60° \text{ electrical}$$

**Step 3 — Electromagnetic Torque (4 Marks):**

$$T_e = \frac{3 V_{ph} E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta_{elec}$$

$$T_e = \frac{3 \times 230.94 \times 300}{157.08 \times 8} \times \sin(60°)$$

$$T_e = \frac{207846}{1256.64} \times 0.866$$

$$T_e = 165.39 \times 0.866 = 143.23 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_e \approx 143.2 \text{ N·m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**:
> 1. **Mechanical vs Electrical Angle**: The load angle $\delta$ in the torque equation is the *electrical* angle. If the problem states a mechanical angle, multiply by $P/2$ first. Forgetting this conversion is the most common error.
> 2. **Per-Phase vs Line Voltage**: $V$ in the torque equation is per phase, not line-to-line. Always divide line voltage by $\sqrt{3}$ for star-connected machines.
> 3. **Unit of $\omega_s$**: Use rad/s, not RPM. Multiply RPM by $2\pi/60$ to convert.
> 4. **Sign of $\delta$**: $\sin\delta$ is positive for motoring (positive torque). The convention is $E_f$ leads $V$ by $\delta$ in motoring mode.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternating Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 4 Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the constructional features of (i) salient pole and (ii) cylindrical pole rotors of a synchronous machine. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, star-connected synchronous generator has the following data: 1500 kVA, 6.6 kV, 50 Hz, 4 poles. The armature winding has 180 turns per phase with a winding factor $K_w = 0.96$. The flux per pole is 0.1 Wb. Calculate: (i) The synchronous speed, (ii) The RMS EMF per phase (line-to-neutral), (iii) The line-to-line generated EMF. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks):**

**Salient Pole Rotor (3.5 Marks):**

- **Construction**: Consists of a large number of *projecting poles* made of laminated steel, bolted or dovetailed to a heavy cast-iron or fabricated steel yoke (spider).
- **Pole Shoes**: Laminated pole shoes are mounted on the pole core. The field winding is wound around the pole core.
- **Air Gap**: **Non-uniform** air gap — minimum under the pole center (small reluctance) and maximum in the inter-polar region (large reluctance).
- **Rotational Speed**: Used in **low-speed machines** (100–1500 RPM) such as hydro-electric generators and synchronous motors.
- **Features**: Large diameter, short axial length, high inertia, requires heavy and robust mechanical design.
- **Damping Winding**: Often equipped with damper/amortisseur winding in the pole faces for motor starting and damping oscillations.

**Cylindrical (Non-Salient) Pole Rotor (3.5 Marks):**

- **Construction**: A **smooth, solid rotor** machined from a high-grade forged steel alloy. Slots are cut on the surface to accommodate the DC field winding. The slots are typically 2/3 of the pole pitch, with end-windings retained by non-magnetic metallic wedges.
- **Air Gap**: **Uniform** air gap over the entire periphery, giving equal reluctance in all directions.
- **Rotational Speed**: Used in **high-speed machines** (1500–3000 RPM) such as turbo-alternators in thermal, gas, and nuclear power stations.
- **Features**: Small diameter, long axial length, mechanically robust, excellent dynamic balancing, lower windage losses.
- **Maintenance**: Field winding is more accessible, but construction requires precision machining.

**Comparison Table (Implicit in answer):**

| Feature | Salient Pole | Cylindrical Pole |
|---------|--------------|------------------|
| Air gap | Non-uniform | Uniform |
| Speed | Low (< 1500 RPM) | High (1500–3000 RPM) |
| Diameter | Large | Small |
| Axial length | Short | Long |
| Application | Hydro, motors | Turbo, thermal |

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Rating: 1500 kVA, 6.6 kV (line), 50 Hz, 4 poles, star-connected
- $T_{ph} = 180$ turns/phase, $K_w = 0.96$, $\Phi = 0.1$ Wb

**Step 1 — Synchronous Speed (2 Marks):**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — RMS EMF per Phase (3 Marks):**

Using the EMF equation:

$$E_{ph,rms} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot \Phi \cdot T_{ph} \cdot K_w$$

$$E_{ph,rms} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.1 \times 180 \times 0.96$$

$$E_{ph,rms} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.1 \times 180 \times 0.96$$

Computing step by step:
- $4.44 \times 50 = 222$
- $222 \times 0.1 = 22.2$
- $22.2 \times 180 = 3996$
- $3996 \times 0.96 = 3836.16$ V

$$E_{ph,rms} \approx 3836.2 \text{ V (line-to-neutral)}$$

**Step 3 — Line-to-Line EMF (2 Marks):**

For a star-connected machine:

$$E_{L,rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot E_{ph,rms} = 1.732 \times 3836.16$$

$$E_{L,rms} = 6644.23 \text{ V} \approx 6.64 \text{ kV}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning for Question B**:
> 1. **Forgetting $K_w$**: Many students write $E = 4.44 f \Phi T$ only, ignoring the winding factor. This loses 1 mark. Always state $K_w$ explicitly.
> 2. **Star vs Delta Connection**: For star, $E_L = \sqrt{3} E_{ph}$; for delta, $E_L = E_{ph}$. Read the problem carefully.
> 3. **Units of Flux**: Flux $\Phi$ must be in Webers (Wb), not milliwebers or maxwells. $1$ Wb $= 10^8$ lines.
> 4. **No-Load vs Loaded EMF**: The EMF equation gives the *internally generated* EMF, which equals terminal voltage only at no-load. For loaded conditions, use $\underline{E_f} = \underline{V} + \underline{I_a}(R_a + jX_s)$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **synchronous machine** operates at a constant speed $N_s = 120f/P$ determined solely by supply frequency and pole count.
- [x] The **principle of generator action** is Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction — a rotating magnetic field cuts stationary conductors.
- [x] The **principle of motor action** is the magnetic locking between the stator's RMF and the rotor's DC-excited poles, producing synchronous coupling torque.
- [x] In a synchronous motor, rotor speed = synchronous speed (always), unlike an induction motor where rotor speed is less than synchronous speed.
- [x] **Construction**: Stator = 3-phase winding on laminated core; Rotor = either salient pole (low speed) or cylindrical (high speed).
- [x] **EMF Equation (KTU Gold Standard)**: $E_{ph,rms} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot \Phi \cdot T_{ph} \cdot K_w$, where $K_w = K_d \cdot K_p$.
- [x] **EMF equation step derivation**: Average EMF per conductor $= P \Phi N_s / 60$ → Multiply by $T_{ph}$ → Apply form factor $1.11$ → Apply $K_w$.
- [x] **Synchronous speed formula**: $N_s = 120f/P$ (RPM); $\omega_s = 4\pi f / P$ (rad/s).
- [x] **Electromagnetic torque**: $T_e = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$; maximum at $\delta = 90°$ (pull-out).
- [x] **Mechanical vs Electrical angle**: $\theta_{elec} = (P/2) \cdot \theta_{mech}$.
- [x] **Phasor equation**: $\underline{E_f} = \underline{V} + \underline{I_a}(R_a + jX_s)$ — basis of voltage regulation problems.
- [x] **Frequency of generated EMF**: $f = P N / 120$ — same equation as synchronous speed; this is the *synchronous constraint*.
- [x] **Winding factor $K_w$**: Always $\le 1$. $K_d$ accounts for distributed winding; $K_p = \cos(\alpha/2)$ for short-pitched coils.
- [x] **Pull-out torque**: The maximum torque a synchronous motor can deliver before losing synchronism. Always specify at $\delta = 90°$.
- [x] **Loss of synchronism**: If load torque exceeds pull-out torque, the rotor stalls — a critical safety issue.
- [x] **KTU 2024 must-knows**: EMF equation derivation, synchronous speed, constructional features (salient vs cylindrical), EMF and torque formulas, mechanical-electrical angle conversion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy**:
> 1. Memorize $N_s = 120f/P$ and the EMF equation $E = 4.44 f \Phi T_{ph} K_w$ — these appear in nearly every University Exam.
> 2. Practice drawing the phasor diagram for synchronous generator at lagging, unity, and leading power factors.
> 3. Always specify per-phase vs line values clearly. Use $V_{ph}$ and $E_{ph}$ in formulas unless otherwise stated.
> 4. For numerical problems, present step-by-step substitution with units. Examiners award partial marks for each correct step.
> 5. Be ready to compare salient pole and cylindrical pole rotors in tabular form — a high-probability 7-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification and Constructional Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification and Constructional Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **transformer** is a static electromagnetic device that transfers electrical energy from one alternating-current circuit to one or more other circuits without any rotating parts, based on the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction** between two (or more) magnetically coupled coils. The energy transfer occurs at a constant frequency, with a possible change in voltage (step-up or step-down) and/or current level, while the apparent power remains approximately constant (neglecting losses).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** Classification and Constructional Features of Single-Phase Transformers, Three-Phase Transformers, Auto-Transformers, and Special (Instrument) Transformers.

The term "**Classification**" refers to the systematic categorization of transformers based on **construction**, **phase**, **voltage level**, **application**, and **method of cooling**. "**Constructional Features**" refers to the physical components that constitute a transformer — namely, the **magnetic core**, the **windings**, the **tank**, the **insulating oil**, the **bushings**, the **conservator**, the **breather**, the **cooling system**, and the **protective devices** like the **Buchholz relay**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two buckets of water connected at the bottom by a thin pipe.
- One bucket is shallow but wide (low pressure, high volume flow) — this is the **low-voltage winding (LV)** with many turns of thick wire carrying high current at low voltage.
- The other bucket is deep but narrow (high pressure, low volume flow) — this is the **high-voltage winding (HV)** with many turns of thin wire carrying small current at high voltage.
- The water (magnetic flux) sloshes back and forth through the connecting pipe (the magnetic core), transferring energy without the buckets ever touching.

> The "sloshing" alternating flux in the core links both windings, just as the oscillating water level in one bucket influences the other. **No physical contact, no rotating parts, just shared magnetic linkage.**

> [!NOTE]
> A transformer does **not generate** electrical energy; it merely **transforms** voltage/current levels. The input power (minus losses) equals the output power — this is the heart of transformer action.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Materials

| Component | Standard Material | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| Core Laminations | **CRGO Steel (Cold-Rolled Grain-Oriented)**, Si-steel | High permeability, low hysteresis loss |
| LV Winding | **Copper (annealed electrolytic)** | High conductivity, ductility |
| HV Winding | **Copper (enamelled)** or Aluminium | High insulation strength |
| Insulating Oil | **Mineral oil (Transformer oil)** | High dielectric strength (**≈ 30 kV / 2.5 mm gap**), good cooling |
| Bushings | **Porcelain / Epoxy resin** | Outdoor weather resistance, insulation |
| Tap Changer | Copper contacts with brass/phosphor-bronze | Handles **±5% / ±10%** voltage variation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Operating frequency: **50 Hz** (India). Core flux density limit: **1.1 to 1.4 Tesla** for CRGO steel. Maximum permissible oil temperature: **90 °C** (top oil).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Transformers (Step-by-Step Logic)

### (A) Based on Construction (Core Geometry)

- **Core-Type Transformer**:
  - The windings **surround a considerable part of the core**.
  - The core is built up of **L-shaped or U+I laminations**.
  - Used for **high-voltage** applications.
  - Magnetic flux has a **single path** through one limb.

- **Shell-Type Transformer**:
  - The core **surrounds a considerable part of the windings**.
  - Built using **E-I or F-F laminations**.
  - Used for **low-voltage, high-current** applications.
  - Magnetic flux has **two return paths** — provides better mechanical protection and natural short-circuit strength.

### (B) Based on Number of Phases

- **Single-Phase Transformer** — used in distribution transformers, instrument transformers.
- **Three-Phase Transformer** — either a **single three-phase unit** (3 limbs) or **three single-phase units** connected together (bank of three single-phase transformers).

### (C) Based on Voltage Transformation

- **Step-Up Transformer** — $N_2 > N_1$, raises voltage (used at generating stations).
- **Step-Down Transformer** — $N_2 < N_1$, reduces voltage (used in distribution).

### (D) Based on Application

- **Power Transformer** — large rating (**> 200 kVA**), used in generating stations and substations. High efficiency (**≈ 98–99%**).
- **Distribution Transformer** — small rating (**≤ 200 kVA**), used at load centers. Lower efficiency but operates **24 hours at near-full load**.
- **Instrument Transformer**:
  - **Current Transformer (CT)** — measures high current, secondary rated at **5 A** (or 1 A).
  - **Potential Transformer (PT)** — measures high voltage, secondary rated at **110 V** (or 100/√3 V).
- **Auto-Transformer** — single winding with taps, used for small voltage variation and as a starter.
- **Special Transformers** — furnace transformer, rectifier transformer, traction transformer, etc.

### (E) Based on Method of Cooling

| Type | Cooling Medium | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| ONAN (Oil Natural Air Natural) | Oil + Air | Distribution transformers (≤ 2.5 MVA) |
| ONAF (Oil Natural Air Forced) | Oil + Air (fans) | Power transformers |
| OFAF (Oil Forced Air Forced) | Oil (pumped) + Air (fans) | Large power transformers |
| OFWF (Oil Forced Water Forced) | Oil (pumped) + Water | Very large transformers |
| AN (Air Natural) / AF (Air Forced) | Air only | Dry-type transformers (indoor) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is oil used?** Transformer oil serves **two purposes simultaneously**: (1) **Insulation** — provides high dielectric strength between live parts and the tank, and (2) **Cooling** — circulates by natural convection, carrying heat from the core and windings to the tank walls.

## 2.2 Constructional Features — Detailed Component Analysis

### (A) Magnetic Core

- The core provides a **low-reluctance path** for the magnetic flux.
- It is laminated (sheets of thickness **0.35 mm to 0.5 mm**) to reduce **eddy current losses**.
- Each lamination is coated with **varnish or paper insulation (0.025 mm)** to electrically isolate them.
- **CRGO steel (M4, M5, M6 grades)** offers low hysteresis loss and high permeability.

### (B) Windings

- **Primary (LV)** and **Secondary (HV)** windings are wound on the core limbs.
- Two main types of winding geometry:
  - **Concentric (Cylindrical) Winding** — LV placed near the core, HV placed over LV (with insulation in between). Used in core-type transformers.
  - **Sandwich (Pancake) Winding** — LV and HV sections alternate; used in shell-type transformers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is LV placed near the core?** Because the LV winding is easier to insulate from the grounded core (lower voltage stress), and HV is placed on the outside where insulation to the tank is naturally enhanced by the oil and the HV-to-core distance is greater.

### (C) Tank, Oil & Cooling

- The **tank** is made of mild steel and holds the oil, windings, and core.
- **Conservator** — an expansion tank mounted on top of the main tank, partially filled with oil, allowing for oil expansion due to temperature rise.
- **Breather** — contains **silica gel** (blue when dry, pink when saturated) to absorb moisture from the air entering the conservator.
- **Cooling tubes / Radiators** — increase the surface area for heat dissipation.
- **Buchholz Relay** — gas-operated protective relay installed in the pipe between the main tank and the conservator; detects **incipient faults** (slow gas generation) and **major faults** (sudden oil surge).

### (D) Bushings

- Porcelain or epoxy insulators through which the **line conductors** enter or leave the tank.
- They isolate the live conductors from the grounded tank.

### (E) Tap Changer

- **Off-Load Tap Changer (OLTC)** — manually operated when transformer is de-energized; provides **±5%** or **±10%** voltage variation in steps of **2.5%** or **1.25%**.
- **On-Load Tap Changer (OLTC)** — operated while the transformer is energized; allows continuous voltage regulation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EMF per turn | $E_t = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m$ | Volts; $f$ in Hz, $\phi_m$ in Wb |
| 2 | Primary EMF | $E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \phi_m$ | Volts |
| 3 | Secondary EMF | $E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \phi_m$ | Volts |
| 4 | Transformation Ratio (K) | $K = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = \dfrac{I_1}{I_2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Step-up condition | $K > 1$ ($N_2 > N_1$) | — |
| 6 | Step-down condition | $K < 1$ ($N_2 < N_1$) | — |
| 7 | Max. flux density | $B_m = \dfrac{\phi_m}{A_i}$ | Tesla; $A_i$ = iron area (m²) |
| 8 | Voltage ratio (ideal) | $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$ | KCL/KVL of ideal transformer |
| 9 | Power relation (ideal) | $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$ | Apparent power conserved |
| 10 | Core window area (approx) | $A_w = \dfrac{kVA \times 10^3}{3.33 \, f \, B_m \, J \, K_w}$ | $J$ = current density, $K_w$ = window factor |

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **4.44** arises from the integration of a sinusoidal flux waveform: $E_{rms} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_{max}$. KTU examiners frequently test the origin of this constant.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation of a Transformer

Consider a transformer winding with **$N$** turns, linked by an alternating sinusoidal flux:

$$\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

where $\phi_m$ is the maximum flux in Weber (Wb).

**Step 1** — Instantaneous induced EMF (per turn) from Faraday's Law:
$$e = -\dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$$

**Step 2** — Differentiate the flux with respect to time:
$$e = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\big[\phi_m \sin(\omega t)\big] = -\phi_m \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3** — Rewrite using the maximum value (which occurs when $\cos(\omega t) = 1$):
$$e_{max} = \phi_m \omega = 2 \pi f \, \phi_m$$

**Step 4** — For a sinusoidal waveform, RMS value = $\dfrac{\text{Peak value}}{\sqrt{2}}$:
$$E_{rms} \,(\text{per turn}) = \dfrac{2 \pi f \, \phi_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \, \pi \, f \, \phi_m$$

**Step 5** — Substitute $\sqrt{2} \, \pi \approx 4.44$:
$$E_{rms} \,(\text{per turn}) = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m$$

**Step 6** — For a coil of $N$ turns (assuming all turns are identically linked):
$$E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_m \quad \text{volts}$$

**Step 7** — Apply to primary and secondary:
$$\boxed{E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \phi_m \quad \text{and} \quad E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \phi_m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** A common derivation question asks: *"Why is the form factor 4.44 and not 1.11?"* The 4.44 accounts for both the sinusoidal RMS-to-peak conversion (× √2) and the integration of the flux waveform over a half-cycle (× π/2 ≈ 1.57). Multiplying: $1.57 \times 2.83 \approx 4.44$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Core Type vs Shell Type

| Feature | Core Type | Shell Type |
|---|---|---|
| Core geometry | L-shaped / U-I laminations | E-I or F-F laminations |
| Winding placement | Surrounds the core | Core surrounds the windings |
| Number of limbs carrying flux | 2 (one with windings, one as return) | 3 (centre with windings, two as return paths) |
| Magnetic circuit length | Longer | Shorter |
| Mechanical strength | Lower | Higher |
| Cooling of windings | Better (more oil contact) | Slightly inferior |
| Repair convenience | Easier | Harder |
| Typical application | HV power transformers | LV, high-current transformers |
| Output rating | Generally higher per unit | Generally lower |
| Flux distribution | Uniform per limb | Non-uniform — needs tapered windings |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Power vs Distribution Transformer

| Parameter | Power Transformer | Distribution Transformer |
|---|---|---|
| Rated kVA | **> 200 kVA** (typically several MVA) | **≤ 200 kVA** |
| Location | Generating / Sub-stations | Load centres / consumer premises |
| Loading cycle | Loaded at rated load during peak hours | Loaded for **24 hours** at near full load |
| All-day efficiency | Less critical | **Most important** (uses all-day efficiency) |
| Iron losses | High (operates near full flux) | Lower |
| Copper losses | Lower | Higher (long-duration load) |
| Flux density | Near 1.4 T | Near 1.2 T (to reduce iron loss) |
| Tap changer | OLTC common | Usually OLTC or NLTC |
| Design priority | Maximum efficiency at full load | **All-day efficiency** |

## 3.4 Numerical Example — EMF Equation Application

> **Problem:** A 50 Hz, single-phase transformer has a core area of **200 cm²**. The maximum flux density is **1.2 T**. The primary has **300 turns** and the secondary has **900 turns**. Find (a) the primary voltage, (b) the secondary voltage, (c) the transformation ratio.

**Given:**

$$f = 50 \text{ Hz}, \quad A_i = 200 \text{ cm}^2 = 200 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2, \quad B_m = 1.2 \text{ T}$$
$$N_1 = 300, \quad N_2 = 900$$

**Step (a) — Maximum flux:**
$$\phi_m = B_m \times A_i = 1.2 \times 200 \times 10^{-4} = 0.024 \text{ Wb}$$

**Step (b) — Primary EMF:**
$$E_1 = 4.44 \times f \times N_1 \times \phi_m$$
$$E_1 = 4.44 \times 50 \times 300 \times 0.024$$
$$E_1 = 1598.4 \text{ V} \approx 1.598 \text{ kV}$$

**Step (c) — Secondary EMF:**
$$E_2 = 4.44 \times f \times N_2 \times \phi_m = 4.44 \times 50 \times 900 \times 0.024$$
$$E_2 = 4795.2 \text{ V} \approx 4.795 \text{ kV}$$

**Step (d) — Transformation ratio:**
$$K = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{900}{300} = 3$$

This is a **step-up transformer** with $K > 1$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Transformer Constructional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Three Phase AC Supply 11kV] --> B[Bushing HV side]
    B --> C[Tap Changer OLTC]
    C --> D[HV Winding 900 turns thin Cu]
    D --> E[Magnetic Core CRGO Laminations 0.35mm]
    E --> F[LV Winding 300 turns thick Cu]
    F --> G[Bushing LV side]
    G --> H[Load 415V]
    I[Core Winding Assembly] --> J[Insulating Oil Mineral]
    J --> K[Main Tank MS sheet 6mm]
    K --> L[Cooling Tubes Radiators]
    K --> M[Conservator Expansion tank]
    M --> N[Silica Gel Breather]
    K --> O[Buchholz Relay Gas operated]
    O --> P[Protection Trip Circuit]
    K --> Q[Oil Temperature Indicator]
    K --> R[Explosion Vent Diaphragm]
    E --- I
    D --- I
    F --- I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The top half (A → H) traces the **electrical path** of energy; the bottom half (I → R) traces the **auxiliary systems** for cooling, oil expansion, moisture control, and protection.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    T[Transformer Classification]
    T --> A1[By Construction]
    T --> A2[By Phase]
    T --> A3[By Voltage]
    T --> A4[By Application]
    T --> A5[By Cooling]

    A1 --> A1a[Core Type]
    A1 --> A1b[Shell Type]
    A1 --> A1c[Berry Type]

    A2 --> A2a[Single Phase]
    A2 --> A2b[Three Phase]

    A3 --> A3a[Step Up]
    A3 --> A3b[Step Down]
    A3 --> A3c[1:1 Isolation]

    A4 --> A4a[Power Transformer]
    A4 --> A4b[Distribution Transformer]
    A4 --> A4c[Instrument Transformer]
    A4 --> A4d[Auto Transformer]
    A4 --> A4e[Special Welding Furnace Traction]

    A5 --> A5a[ONAN]
    A5 --> A5b[ONAF]
    A5 --> A5c[OFAF]
    A5 --> A5d[OFWF]
    A5 --> A5e[AN Dry Type]
    A5 --> A5f[AF Dry Type]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Core Type vs Shell Type (Magnetic Flux Path)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CoreType[Core Type Transformer]
        C1[Left Limb] --> C2[Top Yoke]
        C2 --> C3[Right Limb with Windings]
        C3 --> C4[Bottom Yoke]
        C4 --> C1
    end
    subgraph ShellType[Shell Type Transformer]
        S1[Centre Limb with Windings] --> S2[Top Outer Yoke]
        S2 --> S3[Left Side Limb]
        S3 --> S4[Bottom Outer Yoke]
        S4 --> S5[Right Side Limb]
        S5 --> S2
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Functional Flow Inside a Transformer (Energy + Auxiliary)

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[AC Input V1] --> CORE[Mutual Magnetic Core Phi]
    CORE --> OUT[AC Output V2]
    CORE --> CL[Core Loss Pi Fe]
    W1[Primary Winding Cu Loss Pc1] --> HEAT[Heat Generation]
    W2[Secondary Winding Cu Loss Pc2] --> HEAT
    CL --> HEAT
    HEAT --> COOL[Oil Circulation Natural Convection]
    COOL --> RAD[Radiator Tubes Air Cooling]
    RAD --> AMB[Ambient Air]
    BUC[Buchholz Relay] --> PROT[Protective Trip]
    BR[Silica Gel Breather] --> OILQ[Oil Quality Maintained]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The functional flow clearly separates the **power path** (Input → Core → Output) from the **loss path** (Core + Cu losses → Heat → Oil → Radiator → Air). It also highlights the **protection path** (Buchholz → Trip) and the **maintenance path** (Breather → Oil quality).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]**

**Q1.** Distinguish between **core-type** and **shell-type** transformers with neat diagrams.

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- **Core-type:** Windings surround the core; core has two limbs (one carrying flux with windings, one as return path). Uses L-shaped laminations. Has a longer magnetic circuit.
- **Shell-type:** Core surrounds the windings; three limbs (centre carries flux with windings, two as return paths). Uses E-I laminations. Shorter magnetic path, better mechanical protection.
- **Application:** Core-type for HV transmission; Shell-type for LV high-current applications.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** List the **functions of transformer oil** and explain why **silica gel breather** is used.

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- **Functions of oil:** (1) Insulation — high dielectric strength ≈ 30 kV / 2.5 mm, (2) Cooling — circulates by natural convection carrying heat to tank walls/radiators.
- **Silica gel breather:** The conservator is connected to atmosphere through a breather containing silica gel. The gel **absorbs moisture** from the incoming air (blue when dry → pink when saturated), preventing moisture entry into the oil which would otherwise reduce its dielectric strength.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Module 4 Choice]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **constructional features of a single-phase core-type transformer**. List at least **eight** major components. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A single-phase core-type transformer consists of the following components:

1. **Core** — Built from laminated CRGO steel sheets (0.35 mm thick), coated with varnish. Provides low-reluctance path. The cross-section is stepped (circumscribing circle) to approximate a circular shape for better winding utilization.

2. **LV Winding** — Placed near the core, made of thick insulated copper conductors. Carries higher current at lower voltage.

3. **HV Winding** — Placed concentrically over the LV winding with proper insulation in between. Made of thinner conductors with more turns. Carries lower current at higher voltage.

4. **Insulating Oil** — Fills the tank; provides both insulation and cooling.

5. **Tank** — Mild steel enclosure (6–12 mm thick for large units) holding core, windings, and oil.

6. **Conservator** — Mounted above the main tank, partially filled with oil; allows oil expansion during heating.

7. **Breather** — Contains silica gel to absorb atmospheric moisture.

8. **Buchholz Relay** — Gas-operated protective device mounted between tank and conservator; detects incipient faults.

9. **Tap Changer** — Adjusts turns ratio to maintain secondary voltage; can be off-load (NLTC) or on-load (OLTC).

10. **Bushings** — Porcelain insulators for input/output conductor entry into the tank.

11. **Cooling Tubes / Radiators** — Increase surface area for heat dissipation.

12. **Explosion Vent** — Safety diaphragm that ruptures under sudden overpressure.

**Neat diagram (text representation):**
- Two vertical limbs (one with concentric LV and HV windings) connected by top and bottom yokes.
- Whole assembly immersed in oil inside a sealed tank.
- Bushings emerging from the top of the tank for HV and LV terminals.

**(b)** A single-phase, 50 Hz transformer has 400 primary turns and 1000 secondary turns. The net cross-sectional area of the core is **250 cm²**. If the primary is connected to a **2.4 kV, 50 Hz** supply, calculate the **(i)** maximum flux in the core, **(ii)** secondary EMF, and **(iii)** transformation ratio. **(7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
$$f = 50 \text{ Hz}, \quad N_1 = 400, \quad N_2 = 1000, \quad A_i = 250 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.025 \text{ m}^2, \quad V_1 = 2400 \text{ V}$$

**Step (i) — Maximum Flux:**

Using the EMF equation:
$$E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \phi_m$$

Assuming $E_1 \approx V_1$ (no-load condition):
$$\phi_m = \dfrac{E_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_1} = \dfrac{2400}{4.44 \times 50 \times 400} = \dfrac{2400}{88800}$$

$$\phi_m = 0.02703 \text{ Wb} \approx 27.03 \text{ mWb}$$

**Step (ii) — Secondary EMF:**

$$E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \phi_m = 4.44 \times 50 \times 1000 \times 0.02703$$
$$E_2 = 5999.07 \text{ V} \approx 6.0 \text{ kV}$$

**Step (iii) — Transformation Ratio:**

$$K = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{1000}{400} = 2.5$$

> This is a **step-up transformer** (K > 1).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of transformers** based on **(i) construction, (ii) phase, (iii) application, and (iv) cooling**. Give one example for each category. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Based on Construction:**
- **Core-Type:** Windings surround the core. Example: Most power transformers at EHT substations.
- **Shell-Type:** Core surrounds the windings. Example: Distribution transformers for low-voltage industrial loads.

**(ii) Based on Phase:**
- **Single-Phase:** Used in residential distribution (e.g., 11 kV / 230 V pole-mounted transformer).
- **Three-Phase:** Used in industrial power distribution (e.g., 11 kV / 433 V substation transformer).

**(iii) Based on Application:**
- **Power Transformer** (e.g., 100 MVA, 220/66 kV at substation).
- **Distribution Transformer** (e.g., 250 kVA, 11 kV / 433 V).
- **Instrument Transformer**:
  - **CT** (Current Transformer) — primary carries line current; secondary rated at 5 A.
  - **PT** (Potential Transformer) — primary connected across line; secondary rated at 110 V.
- **Auto-Transformer** (e.g., 3-phase variac in laboratories).
- **Special Transformers** — welding, furnace, traction.

**(iv) Based on Cooling (per IS/IEC standards):**
- **ONAN** — Distribution transformers ≤ 2.5 MVA.
- **ONAF** — Power transformers up to ~10 MVA.
- **OFAF / OFWF** — Large EHV transformers.
- **AN/AF (Dry Type)** — Indoor use, fire-safe locations (e.g., hospitals, high-rises).

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the **construction and working of a Buchholz relay**. Why is it provided only on **oil-immersed transformers**? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
- The Buchholz relay is a **gas-operated** protective relay installed in the **oil pipe** connecting the main tank to the conservator.
- It has a **cast iron housing** with two internal floats:
  - **Upper Float (Mercury Switch)** — for **minor (incipient) faults**.
  - **Lower Float (Mercury Switch)** — for **major (catastrophic) faults**.

**Working:**

1. **Incipient Fault (slow):** A small fault (e.g., partial discharge) decomposes the oil, producing **gas bubbles** that rise and collect in the upper chamber of the Buchholz relay. As oil level drops, the **upper float tilts**, closing the mercury switch and triggering an **alarm** — not a trip.

2. **Severe Fault (sudden):** A major internal fault (e.g., short circuit between windings) causes a **sudden surge of oil** from the main tank to the conservator. The surge pushes the **lower float**, closing the mercury switch and **tripping the circuit breaker** — disconnecting the transformer instantly.

**Why only oil-immersed transformers?**
- The relay works by **detecting gas accumulation** in oil and **oil surges** — phenomena that occur only in oil-filled transformers.
- Dry-type transformers have no oil, so Buchholz protection is **not applicable**. They use temperature sensors and pressure sensors instead.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Writing $E_1 = 4.44 f N_1 \phi_m$ and forgetting to convert **cm² to m²** (factor of $10^{-4}$). This leads to answers off by a factor of 10,000.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **CT secondary rated 5 A** with **PT secondary rated 110 V**. CT is connected in **series** with the line; PT is connected in **parallel** (across the line).
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Writing the Buchholz relay is for **fire detection** — it is for **internal electrical faults**, not external fire.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Assuming all transformers are three-phase — many KTU questions test the **single-phase core-type** specifically.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Classification & Constructional Features)**

- **Transformer Definition:** Static device, mutual induction, 50 Hz in India, no moving parts.
- **EMF Equation:** $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_m$ — the **4.44** comes from $\sqrt{2} \times \pi$.
- **Transformation Ratio:** $K = N_2 / N_1 = E_2 / E_1 = I_1 / I_2$.
- **Step-up vs Step-down:** $K > 1$ → step-up; $K < 1$ → step-down.
- **Core-Type:** 2 limbs, windings surround core, LV inside HV outside, used for HV.
- **Shell-Type:** 3 limbs, core surrounds windings, better mechanical strength, used for LV.
- **Laminations:** CRGO, 0.35 mm thick, varnish-coated → reduces eddy current loss.
- **Why LV inside?** Easier insulation to grounded core; HV outside for better dielectric strength to tank.
- **Transformer Oil:** Two functions — **insulation** (dielectric strength ≈ 30 kV / 2.5 mm) and **cooling** (natural convection).
- **Conservator:** Expansion tank, partially filled, allows oil to expand/contract.
- **Breather:** Contains silica gel — **blue (dry) → pink (saturated with moisture)**.
- **Buchholz Relay:** Gas-operated; upper float = alarm (incipient), lower float = trip (severe).
- **Tap Changer:** NLTC (±5%/±10% in 2.5% steps); OLTC = on-load.
- **Cooling Codes:** ONAN, ONAF, OFAF, OFWF (oil-immersed); AN, AF (dry-type).
- **Power Transformer:** > 200 kVA, substation, max efficiency at full load.
- **Distribution Transformer:** ≤ 200 kVA, load centre, **all-day efficiency** matters.
- **CT:** Series connection, secondary = 5 A or 1 A; **must never be open-circuited on load**.
- **PT:** Parallel connection, secondary = 110 V or 100/√3 V; **must never be short-circuited**.
- **Auto-Transformer:** Single tapped winding, used for small ratio variation and motor starting.
- **Core Material Grade:** M4 (best, lowest loss) → M5 → M6 (lower grade).
- **Flux Density:** $B_m = 1.1$ to $1.4$ Tesla typical; never exceed saturation (≈ 1.6 T for CRGO).
- **Tank Material:** Mild steel, thickness 6–12 mm (larger units use corrugated or radiator-finned walls).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Torque Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In electromechanical energy conversion, the **Torque Equation** establishes the fundamental relationship between the mechanical torque developed in the rotating member (rotor) of an electrical machine and the underlying electrical, magnetic, and geometric parameters of the machine. Mathematically, it expresses the **electromagnetic torque (Tₑ)** as the product of a machine constant, the flux per pole, and the armature/rotor current — capturing the conversion of electrical power into mechanical power (motor action) or vice-versa (generator action).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner Definition (Memorize This):**
> *"The electromagnetic torque developed in a DC machine is directly proportional to the flux per pole (Φ) and the armature current (Iₐ), and is given by the constant **kT** such that **T = kT · Φ · Iₐ** where kT = (P·Z)/(2π·A) is the machine's torque construction constant."*

For a Three-Phase Induction Motor, the torque equation describes the **relative angular velocity (slip, s)** between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor, along with rotor circuit parameters (R₂, X₂), and is expressed as:

$$
T_{e} \;=\; \frac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \frac{s \, E_{2}^{2} \, R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + (s \, X_{2})^{2}}
$$

where **ωs = 2π·f / P** is the synchronous angular velocity in **rad/s**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Wheel of Magnetism" 🌀

Imagine a giant water wheel connected to a paddle system:

| Real-World Object | Electrical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Force of water pushing paddles | Magnetic force on current-carrying conductors |
| Flow rate of water (litres/sec) | Magnitude of armature current (Iₐ) |
| Strength of the magnetic field pulling paddles | Flux per pole (Φ) |
| Diameter of the wheel | Machine construction constant (P·Z/A) |
| Twisting force on the shaft | Electromagnetic Torque (Tₑ) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** The harder the magnetic field "pushes" the conductors (more flux) AND the more current flowing through them (more Iₐ), the larger the twisting force (torque) on the shaft. This is a *double multiplication* — torque grows as the product of Φ and Iₐ, not their sum.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard quantities are **bolded** for emphasis and form the backbone of the torque equation:

- **Φ** (Phi) — Average flux per pole, measured in **Weber (Wb)**
- **Iₐ** — Armature current in **Ampere (A)**
- **P** — Total number of poles (always an **even integer**)
- **Z** — Total number of armature conductors
- **A** — Number of parallel paths in the armature winding (A = P for **lap winding**, A = 2 for **wave winding**)
- **s** — Per-unit slip, dimensionless (range **0 to 1** for motoring)
- **ωs** — Synchronous speed in **rad/s**
- **Tₑ** — Electromagnetic torque in **Newton-metre (N·m)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Torque — Lorentz Force Principle

Every torque equation in rotating machines traces back to a single physical law: the **Lorentz Force Law** experienced by a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field.

> **Lorentz Force on a Single Conductor:**
> $$F \;=\; B \, I \, L \, \sin\theta$$

For conductors lying radially in a uniform radial magnetic field, **θ = 90°**, so sin θ = 1, giving the maximum force.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Build-Up of the Torque Equation (DC Machine)

To understand *why* the torque equation takes the form it does, we proceed in structured logical layers:

**Layer 1 — Force on a Single Conductor**
A single conductor carrying current I, of active length L, placed in flux density B, experiences:
$$F_{\text{single}} \;=\; B \, I \, L$$

**Layer 2 — Total Force Around the Armature**
Since the armature has Z total conductors arranged in A parallel paths, the number of conductors *in series per parallel path* is Z/A. The current per conductor is Iₐ/A. The total peripheral force (tangential) becomes:
$$F_{\text{total}} \;=\; \frac{\Phi \, Z \, I_{a}}{2\pi}$$

**Layer 3 — Converting Force to Torque**
The armature radius is **D/2**, where D is the armature diameter. The flux density B = Φ / A_pole, and the pole area relates to the mean armature circumference πD/P. After substitution, the torque is:
$$T_{e} \;=\; F_{\text{total}} \times \frac{D}{2} \;=\; \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_{a}}{2\pi \, A}$$

**Layer 4 — Grouping the Constants**
Define the **machine torque constant** as:
$$k_{T} \;=\; \frac{P \, Z}{2\pi \, A}$$

This gives the **canonical DC machine torque equation**:
$$T_{e} \;=\; k_{T} \, \Phi \, I_{a}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The factor **2π** appears because we are converting from one full mechanical revolution (circumference = πD) to the *work done per radian* (torque). A common error is writing 2π in the numerator or forgetting it entirely.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The torque equation is not a textbook abstraction — it is the **design cornerstone** for:

- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Traction Motors:** Determines acceleration profile (T = J·α) and hill-climbing ability.
- **Industrial Drives & CNC Spindles:** Torque-speed curves are derived directly from this equation.
- **Power Generation:** The opposing electromagnetic torque (counter-torque) determines the mechanical power input to generators.
- **HVAC Compressors & Pumps:** Match load torque to motor torque for energy-efficient operation.
- **Robotics & Servo Systems:** Required for precision position control.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_{e} = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_{a}}{2\pi \, A}$ | **General DC machine torque** (full form) | N·m |
| 2 | $T_{e} = k_{T} \, \Phi \, I_{a}$ | **Compact DC torque equation** (kT = PZ/2πA) | N·m |
| 3 | $T_{a} = k_{T} \, \Phi \, I_{a} - T_{f}$ | **Net (shaft) torque** after subtracting friction | N·m |
| 4 | $T_{\text{sh}} = 9.55 \, \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{N}$ | **Shaft torque from output power & speed** | N·m |
| 5 | $T_{a} = 9.55 \, \dfrac{E_{b} \, I_{a}}{N}$ | **Armature torque from EMF and current** | N·m |
| 6 | $T_{e} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{s \, E_{2}^{2} \, R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}$ | **Induction motor torque equation** | N·m |
| 7 | $T_{\max} = \dfrac{3 \, E_{2}^{2}}{2 \, \omega_{s} \, X_{2}}$ | **Maximum (breakdown) torque** at $s_{m} = R_{2}/X_{2}$ | N·m |
| 8 | $T_{\text{start}} = \dfrac{3 \, E_{2}^{2} \, R_{2}}{\omega_{s} \, X_{2}^{2}}$ | **Starting torque** (at s = 1) | N·m |
| 9 | $\omega_{s} = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P}$ | **Synchronous angular speed** | rad/s |
| 10 | $N_{s} = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | **Synchronous speed** | RPM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - For **Lap winding**: A = P (so A is *large* → kT is *small* → low-voltage, high-current machines).
> - For **Wave winding**: A = 2 (so A is *small* → kT is *large* → high-voltage, low-current machines).
> - **Slip s = 0** → Synchronous speed → No relative motion → Torque = 0 (ideal no-load).
> - **Slip s = 1** → Standstill → Starting condition.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: DC Machine Torque Equation

**Starting Premise:** The electromagnetic torque in a DC machine arises from the interaction between the armature current-carrying conductors and the main field flux.

**Step 1 — Establish the flux density at the armature surface.**

The average flux density B (in Tesla) is the average flux per pole divided by the average pole arc area:
$$B_{av} \;=\; \frac{\Phi}{\text{Pole Arc Area}} \;=\; \frac{\Phi}{(\pi D / P) \cdot L}$$

where **D** is the armature diameter and **L** is the core length. Since the *pole arc* subtends a fraction of the pole pitch, we use the mean circumference πD/P.

**Step 2 — Force on a single armature conductor.**

Using the Lorentz force law for a conductor of active length L carrying current I_conductor in a radial magnetic field (so the force is purely tangential):
$$F_{\text{per conductor}} \;=\; B_{av} \cdot I_{\text{conductor}} \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Express the conductor current in terms of armature current Iₐ.**

The armature has **A** parallel paths. The total armature current Iₐ is shared equally among these paths, so:
$$I_{\text{conductor}} \;=\; \frac{I_{a}}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Express B_av in terms of Φ.**

Substituting the pole arc area expression:
$$F_{\text{per conductor}} \;=\; \left(\frac{\Phi \cdot P}{\pi D L}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{I_{a}}{A}\right) \cdot L \;=\; \frac{\Phi \, P \, I_{a}}{\pi \, D \, A}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by the total number of conductors Z.**

The total tangential force around the entire armature periphery is:
$$F_{\text{total}} \;=\; Z \cdot F_{\text{per conductor}} \;=\; \frac{Z \, \Phi \, P \, I_{a}}{\pi \, D \, A}$$

**Step 6 — Convert peripheral force to torque using the moment arm D/2.**

Torque = Force × perpendicular distance from the shaft axis. The radius of the armature is D/2:
$$T_{e} \;=\; F_{\text{total}} \cdot \frac{D}{2} \;=\; \frac{Z \, \Phi \, P \, I_{a}}{\pi \, D \, A} \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Cancel the diameter D and simplify.**

$$T_{e} \;=\; \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_{a}}{2\pi \, A}$$

**Step 8 — Group the machine geometry into kT.**

$$T_{e} \;=\; \underbrace{\left(\frac{P \, Z}{2\pi \, A}\right)}_{k_{T}} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_{a}$$

**Step 9 — Cross-verification using power balance.**

Mechanical power developed P_m = Tₑ · ω_m. Electrical power converted P_conv = E_b · Iₐ. Setting them equal:
$$T_{e} \cdot \omega_{m} \;=\; E_{b} \cdot I_{a}$$

Since $E_{b} = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A}$ and $\omega_{m} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$, we get:
$$T_{e} \;=\; \frac{E_{b} \, I_{a}}{\omega_{m}} \;=\; \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_{a}}{2\pi \, A} \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms the derivation via two independent paths (force-based and power-based).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Induction Motor Torque Equation

**Starting Premise:** The rotor of an induction motor experiences a torque proportional to the power crossing the air gap and inversely proportional to synchronous speed.

**Step 1 — Rotor induced EMF at slip s.**

The slip frequency is f_r = s · f. The rotor EMF at slip is:
$$E_{2s} \;=\; s \cdot E_{2}$$

where E₂ is the rotor EMF at standstill (s = 1).

**Step 2 — Rotor circuit impedance at slip s.**

The rotor inductance reactance scales with frequency, so at slip s:
$$Z_{2} \;=\; R_{2} + j \, s \, X_{2}$$
$$\vert Z_{2} \vert \;=\; \sqrt{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Rotor current per phase.**

$$I_{2} \;=\; \frac{E_{2s}}{\vert Z_{2} \vert} \;=\; \frac{s \, E_{2}}{\sqrt{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Power factor of the rotor circuit.**

$$\cos\phi_{2} \;=\; \frac{R_{2}}{\sqrt{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}}$$

**Step 5 — Rotor copper loss per phase.**

$$P_{\text{cu,phase}} \;=\; I_{2}^{2} \, R_{2} \;=\; \frac{s^{2} \, E_{2}^{2} \, R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Total mechanical power developed (air-gap power minus rotor copper loss).**

For a 3-phase machine, total air-gap power:
$$P_{g} \;=\; 3 \cdot \frac{s \, E_{2}^{2}}{\sqrt{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}} \cdot \frac{R_{2}}{\sqrt{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}}$$

Wait — let us re-derive carefully. Air-gap power per phase = E₂s · I₂ · cos φ₂:
$$P_{g,\text{phase}} \;=\; \frac{s \, E_{2}^{2} \, R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}$$

Total air-gap power (3-phase):
$$P_{g} \;=\; 3 \cdot \frac{s \, E_{2}^{2} \, R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}$$

Mechanical power developed = (1 − s) × air-gap power:
$$P_{m} \;=\; (1 - s) \cdot P_{g}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to torque using T = P / ωs.**

Since the rotating field is at synchronous speed ωs, the torque is given by the air-gap power divided by synchronous angular velocity:
$$T_{e} \;=\; \frac{P_{g}}{\omega_{s}} \;=\; \frac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \frac{s \, E_{2}^{2} \, R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}$$

This is the **complete induction motor torque-slip equation**.

**Step 8 — Derive slip at maximum torque.**

Differentiate Tₑ with respect to s and set dT/ds = 0. After algebraic simplification:
$$s_{m} \;=\; \frac{R_{2}}{X_{2}}$$

**Step 9 — Substitute s_m back into Tₑ to get T_max.**

$$T_{\max} \;=\; \frac{3}{2 \omega_{s}} \cdot \frac{E_{2}^{2}}{X_{2}}$$

Note that T_max is **independent of rotor resistance R₂**, but the slip at which it occurs **is directly proportional to R₂**. This is the principle behind rotor resistance control for soft-starting.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | DC Machine | Induction Motor |
|-----------|------------|-----------------|
| Flux location | Stator (field poles) | Stator (distributed winding) |
| Current input | Armature (rotor) | Stator (rotor induced) |
| Speed type | Variable (V, Φ control) | Near-constant (slightly below Ns) |
| Torque ∝ | Φ · Iₐ (linear) | Complex function of slip s |
| Maximum torque location | Saturation-limited | At slip $s_m = R_2/X_2$ |
| Control variable | Armature voltage, field flux | Voltage, frequency, R₂ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Torque Generation Architecture in a DC Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Supply Input] --> B[Field Winding - Stator]
    A --> C[Armature Winding - Rotor]
    B --> B1[Excites Main Flux Phi per pole]
    B1 --> B2[Radial Magnetic Field in Air Gap]
    C --> C1[Armature Current Ia flows]
    C1 --> C2[Lorentz Force on each conductor]
    B2 --> D[Interaction Zone Air Gap]
    C2 --> D
    D --> D1[Tangential Force F at radius D/2]
    D1 --> D2[Mechanical Torque Te = F x D/2]
    D2 --> D3[Constant kT x Phi x Ia]
    D3 --> E[Shaft Torque T_sh = Te - T_friction]
    E --> F[Mechanical Load]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Power Flow Showing Torque Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Electrical Input]
        V[V x Ia - Electrical Power Input]
    end
    IN --> P1[Armature Copper Loss Ia^2 x Ra]
    IN --> P2[Air Gap Power Pg = Eb x Ia]
    P1 --> P3[Heat Loss]
    P2 --> P4[Mechanical Power Pm = Te x omega_m]
    P4 --> P5[Friction Windage Loss]
    P4 --> P6[Shaft Output Pout = T_sh x omega_m]
```

## 4.3 Torque vs Slip Characteristic (Induction Motor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Slip s = 0 - Synchronous] -->|Te = 0| B[Region 1 - Low Slip - Motoring]
    B -->|Te increases| C[Slip s_m - Maximum Torque T_max]
    C -->|Te decreases| D[Slip s = 1 - Starting Torque T_start]
    D -->|Operating Region| E[Slip between 0 and s_m - Stable]
    D -->|Operating Region| F[Slip between s_m and 1 - Unstable]
    style A fill:#e1f5e1
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#e1e8ff
    style F fill:#ffe1f1
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow — From Flux to Mechanical Output

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phi - Flux per pole Wb] --> C[Multiplication]
    B[Ia - Armature Current A] --> C
    C --> D[Product Phi x Ia]
    D --> E[Multiply by kT = PZ/2piA]
    E --> F[Electromagnetic Torque Te N.m]
    F --> G[Subtract Friction Torque Tf]
    G --> H[Shaft Torque T_sh]
    H --> I[Multiply by Angular Speed omega_m]
    I --> J[Mechanical Power Output W]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the torque equation of a DC machine. Why is the torque proportional to flux and armature current?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The electromagnetic torque developed by a DC machine is given by:
$$T_{e} = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_{a}}{2\pi \, A} = k_{T} \, \Phi \, I_{a}$$

where **P** = number of poles, **Φ** = flux per pole, **Z** = total armature conductors, **A** = number of parallel paths, **Iₐ** = armature current, and **kT = PZ/(2πA)** is the machine constant.

**Why proportional to Φ:** Flux Φ represents the strength of the magnetic field in the air gap. A stronger field exerts greater Lorentz force (F = BIL) on each armature conductor, producing a larger tangential force and hence more torque.

**Why proportional to Iₐ:** The current in each conductor (Iₐ/A) is directly responsible for the electromagnetic force via F = BIL. Higher current → more force per conductor → more torque.

Since both Φ and Iₐ are independent design variables and contribute multiplicatively (not additively) to torque, the relationship is a product.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define slip in an induction motor. What is its significance in the torque equation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Slip (s)** is defined as the fractional difference between synchronous speed (Ns) and the actual rotor speed (N), expressed as a per-unit (or percentage) value:
$$s = \frac{N_{s} - N}{N_{s}}$$

**Significance in the torque equation:**
- The induction motor torque equation $T_{e} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{s \, E_{2}^{2} \, R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}$ is a direct function of slip s.
- At s = 0 (synchronous speed), torque is **zero** because there is no relative motion between the rotating field and rotor, so no EMF is induced, no current flows, and no torque is produced.
- At s = 1 (standstill), the motor develops its **starting torque**.
- At slip $s_{m} = R_2 / X_2$, the motor develops **maximum (breakdown) torque**.

Slip is therefore the fundamental operating parameter that determines the torque output of an induction motor.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the torque equation of a DC machine starting from the basic force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Force on a single conductor in a magnetic field (Lorentz force):
$$F = B \, I \, L$$

**Step 2:** Average flux density at the armature surface:
$$B_{av} = \frac{\Phi \, P}{\pi D L}$$

**Step 3:** Conductor current (with A parallel paths):
$$I_{\text{conductor}} = \frac{I_{a}}{A}$$

**Step 4:** Force per conductor:
$$F_{\text{single}} = B_{av} \cdot I_{\text{conductor}} \cdot L = \frac{\Phi \, P \, I_{a}}{\pi D A}$$

**Step 5:** Total tangential force on Z conductors:
$$F_{\text{total}} = \frac{Z \, \Phi \, P \, I_{a}}{\pi D A}$$

**Step 6:** Torque = Force × radius (D/2):
$$T_{e} = F_{\text{total}} \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_{a}}{2\pi A}$$

**Step 7:** Grouping the machine constant:
$$\boxed{T_{e} = k_{T} \, \Phi \, I_{a} \quad \text{where} \quad k_{T} = \frac{P Z}{2\pi A}}$$

**(b) A 4-pole, 500 V DC shunt motor has 720 armature conductors wave-wound. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω, field resistance is 200 Ω, and the flux per pole is 30 mWb. The motor draws an armature current of 50 A at a speed of 1000 rpm. Calculate: (i) the back EMF, (ii) the electromagnetic torque developed, and (iii) the shaft torque if rotational losses are 500 W. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- P = 4, V = 500 V, Z = 720
- Winding: Wave → A = 2
- Rₐ = 0.2 Ω, R_sh = 200 Ω
- Φ = 30 mWb = 30 × 10⁻³ Wb
- Iₐ = 50 A, N = 1000 rpm
- Rotational losses P_rot = 500 W

**(i) Back EMF:**
$$E_{b} = V - I_{a} R_{a} = 500 - 50 \times 0.2 = 500 - 10 = 490 \, \text{V}$$

**(ii) Electromagnetic Torque:**

Using the EMF equation to cross-verify:
$$E_{b} = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A} = \frac{4 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 720 \times 1000}{60 \times 2} = \frac{86400}{120} = 720 \, \text{V}$$

*Note: The given 490 V does not match the EMF equation — this implies the operating flux or condition varies. We will use the formula T = E_b·I_a/ω for consistency with the given operating values.*

Angular speed:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \, \text{rad/s}$$

Electromagnetic torque (using power balance):
$$T_{e} = \frac{E_{b} \cdot I_{a}}{\omega} = \frac{490 \times 50}{104.72} = \frac{24500}{104.72} \approx 233.96 \, \text{N·m}$$

Alternatively using direct formula:
$$T_{e} = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_{a}}{2\pi A} = \frac{4 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 720 \times 50}{2\pi \times 2} = \frac{4320}{12.566} \approx 343.77 \, \text{N·m}$$

*Note to students: There is a small numerical inconsistency in the textbook problem (likely the value of flux under load differs from no-load). For the exam, write both methods and state the assumption.*

**(iii) Shaft Torque:**

First, compute mechanical power developed:
$$P_{m} = E_{b} \times I_{a} = 490 \times 50 = 24500 \, \text{W} = 24.5 \, \text{kW}$$

Output power:
$$P_{\text{out}} = P_{m} - P_{\text{rot}} = 24500 - 500 = 24000 \, \text{W}$$

Shaft torque:
$$T_{\text{sh}} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\omega} = \frac{24000}{104.72} \approx 229.18 \, \text{N·m}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the electromagnetic torque of a three-phase induction motor in terms of slip. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Define slip s and slip frequency:
$$s = \frac{N_{s} - N}{N_{s}}, \quad f_{r} = s f$$

**Step 2:** Rotor EMF at slip s:
$$E_{2s} = s \, E_{2}$$

**Step 3:** Rotor impedance at slip s:
$$Z_{2} = R_{2} + j \, s \, X_{2}, \quad \vert Z_{2} \vert = \sqrt{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Rotor current per phase:
$$I_{2} = \frac{s \, E_{2}}{\sqrt{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}}$$

**Step 5:** Power factor:
$$\cos\phi_{2} = \frac{R_{2}}{\sqrt{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}}$$

**Step 6:** Air-gap power per phase = E₂s · I₂ · cos φ₂:
$$P_{g,\text{phase}} = s \, E_{2} \cdot \frac{s \, E_{2}}{\sqrt{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}} \cdot \frac{R_{2}}{\sqrt{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}}$$

$$P_{g,\text{phase}} = \frac{s^{2} \, E_{2}^{2} \, R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}$$

**Step 7:** Total 3-phase air-gap power:
$$P_{g} = \frac{3 \, s^{2} \, E_{2}^{2} \, R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}$$

**Step 8:** Convert to torque using T = P_g / ω_s (since torque is delivered at synchronous speed):
$$\boxed{T_{e} = \frac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \frac{s \, E_{2}^{2} \, R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}}$$

Note the s² in numerator cancels with one factor of s after rewriting — careful students write s² explicitly first.

**(b) A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor has rotor resistance R₂ = 0.4 Ω and standstill rotor reactance X₂ = 2 Ω. The ratio of standstill rotor EMF to rotor reactance is E₂/X₂ = 50. Compute: (i) the slip at maximum torque, (ii) the maximum torque, and (iii) the starting torque. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- f = 50 Hz, P = 4, R₂ = 0.4 Ω, X₂ = 2 Ω
- E₂/X₂ = 50 → E₂ = 50 × 2 = 100 V (per phase, assumed)

**Step 1 — Synchronous speed:**
$$N_{s} = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \, \text{RPM}$$
$$\omega_{s} = \frac{2\pi N_{s}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**(i) Slip at maximum torque:**
$$s_{m} = \frac{R_{2}}{X_{2}} = \frac{0.4}{2} = 0.2 \;(\text{or } 20\%)$$

**(ii) Maximum torque:**
$$T_{\max} = \frac{3}{2 \, \omega_{s}} \cdot \frac{E_{2}^{2}}{X_{2}} = \frac{3 \times 100^{2}}{2 \times 157.08 \times 2} = \frac{30000}{628.32} \approx 47.75 \, \text{N·m}$$

**(iii) Starting torque (at s = 1):**
$$T_{\text{start}} = \frac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \frac{E_{2}^{2} \, R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + X_{2}^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 100^{2} \times 0.4}{157.08 \times (0.4^{2} + 2^{2})}$$

$$T_{\text{start}} = \frac{3 \times 10000 \times 0.4}{157.08 \times (0.16 + 4)} = \frac{12000}{157.08 \times 4.16} = \frac{12000}{653.45} \approx 18.36 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Verification:** Starting torque / Max torque ratio = 2(R₂/X₂) / [1 + (R₂/X₂)²]:
$$\frac{T_{\text{start}}}{T_{\max}} = \frac{2 \times 0.2}{1 + 0.2^{2}} = \frac{0.4}{1.04} \approx 0.385$$
$$T_{\text{start}} = 0.385 \times 47.75 \approx 18.36 \, \text{N·m} \quad \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgot the 2π factor:** Writing $T = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{A}$ (missing 2π) is the **single most common error**. Always double-check the 2π in the denominator.
> 2. **Confusing A = P (lap) with A = 2 (wave):** Many students write A = P for wave-wound machines. **Memorize:** Wave → A = 2, always.
> 3. **Forgetting the s² cancellation step:** In the induction motor derivation, students often write s² in the numerator and *leave it there* when writing the final torque equation. The correct form has only **one** factor of s in the numerator.
> 4. **Mixing ωs with ωr:** Torque = P_gap / ω_s (synchronous), NOT P_mech / ω_m (mechanical). Using the wrong denominator is a 2-mark penalty.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency:** Φ must be in Weber (not mWb), I in Ampere, N in m/s (linear) or RPM converted to rad/s for angular speed. Inductances must be in Henry.
> 6. **Numerical substitution mistakes:** Forgetting to square E₂ or R₂ when using the (R₂² + (sX₂)²) denominator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

- ⚡ **DC Machine Torque:** $T_{e} = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_{a}}{2\pi \, A} = k_{T} \Phi I_{a}$ — Torque is **linear** in Φ and Iₐ.
- ⚡ **Induction Motor Torque:** $T_{e} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{s \, E_{2}^{2} \, R_{2}}{R_{2}^{2} + (s X_{2})^{2}}$ — Torque is a **non-linear** function of slip s.
- ⚡ **Slip at max torque:** $s_{m} = R_{2} / X_{2}$ — directly proportional to rotor resistance.
- ⚡ **Maximum torque:** $T_{\max} = \dfrac{3 E_{2}^{2}}{2 \omega_{s} X_{2}}$ — **independent** of rotor resistance R₂.
- ⚡ **At s = 0:** Torque = 0 (synchronous speed, no relative motion).
- ⚡ **At s = 1:** Starting torque, $T_{st} = \dfrac{3 E_{2}^{2} R_{2}}{\omega_{s} X_{2}^{2}}$.
- ⚡ **Wave winding:** A = 2; **Lap winding:** A = P.
- ⚡ **Power balance:** $T_{e} \cdot \omega_{m} = E_{b} \cdot I_{a}$ (DC machine) and $T_{e} \cdot \omega_{s} = P_{g}$ (induction machine).
- ⚡ **Shaft torque:** $T_{\text{sh}} = T_{e} - T_{f} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{\omega_{m}}$.
- ⚡ **Conversion factor:** $9.55 = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}$, so $T = 9.55 \cdot \dfrac{P}{N}$ when P in watts and N in RPM.
- ⚡ **For DC motors:** Above base speed → field weakening (constant power region); Below base speed → armature control (constant torque region).
- ⚡ **The torque equation is a *design equation* — it dictates motor sizing for every EV, robot, and industrial drive in existence.**
