<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — TORQUE-SLIP CHARACTERISTICS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torque-Slip Characteristic** of a three-phase induction motor is the graphical (and analytical) relationship between the **electromagnetic torque developed by the rotor (T)** and the **per-unit slip (s)** of the motor, evaluated across the entire operating range from standstill (s = 1) to synchronous speed (s = 0).

The **slip (s)** is defined as the relative difference between the synchronous speed ($N_s$) of the rotating magnetic field and the actual rotor speed ($N_r$):

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

The torque-slip curve is one of the most important performance plots in AC machine analysis, as it determines the **starting behaviour**, **acceleration capability**, **steady-state operating point**, and **pull-out (breakdown) limit** of the induction motor.

### 1.2 Key Operating Speeds (Reference Values)

- **Synchronous Speed** ($N_s$): $\frac{120 f}{P}$ rpm, where **f** is supply frequency and **P** is the number of poles.
- **Full-Load Speed** ($N_{fl}$): Typically 2–5% below $N_s$.
- **No-Load Speed**: Very close to $N_s$ (slip < 0.1%).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Cyclist on a Hilly Track 🚴

Imagine a cyclist on a circular track:

- The **cyclist's pedalling force** is analogous to the **motor torque (T)**.
- The **gap between the cyclist's speed and the track's apparent "wind speed" (synchronous field)** is the **slip (s)**.
- When the cyclist is just starting, they need a **huge push (high starting torque)** — that is, slip is at its maximum (**s = 1**).
- Once the cyclist is cruising at near-track speed, only a small pedalling force is needed to overcome wind drag — this is the **low-slip running region**.
- If the track suddenly tilts upward (a sudden mechanical overload), the cyclist slows down (slip increases) and must apply more force. If the slope is too steep, the cyclist may stall (motor pulls out).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The induction motor is *self-protecting* — if mechanical load increases beyond the breakdown torque, the motor cannot accelerate further, slip increases rapidly, and the motor either stalls or draws huge current (and trips on overcurrent protection).

### 1.4 Why is this Topic Critical for KTU 2024?

- It is the **foundation for all subsequent induction motor problems** (Module 4 onward).
- Numerical questions on **maximum torque, starting torque, slip at maximum torque, and the effect of rotor resistance** are **high-frequency items** in KTU End Semester Examinations.
- It is directly mapped to **CO2 / CO3** of PCEOT402 (Analyse the performance characteristics of AC electrical machines).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Torque Equation (First Principles)

The electromagnetic torque developed by a three-phase induction motor (per-phase equivalent circuit model) is given by:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^{2} \, R_2}{R_2^{2} + (sX_2)^{2}} \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

where:
- **T** = Electromagnetic torque developed (N·m)
- **$E_2$** = Rotor induced EMF per phase at standstill (V)
- **$R_2$** = Rotor resistance per phase (Ω)
- **$X_2$** = Rotor reactance per phase at standstill (Ω)
- **s** = Per-unit slip
- **$\omega_s$** = Synchronous angular speed in rad/s = $\frac{2\pi N_s}{60}$

### 2.2 Logical Steps to Reach the Torque Equation

1. **The rotating magnetic field** (produced by the 3-phase stator winding) cuts the stationary rotor conductors at synchronous speed.
2. A **rotor EMF ($E_{2s} = sE_2$)** is induced at slip frequency $sf$.
3. Rotor current flows: $I_2 = \frac{sE_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$.
4. **Gross mechanical power** developed: $P_m = 3 I_2^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}(1-s)$.
5. Since $P_m = T \cdot \omega_r = T \cdot (1-s)\omega_s$, dividing yields the torque equation above.

## 2.3 Critical Torque Quantities (Extremum Analysis)

### (a) **Starting Torque ($T_{st}$)** — at $s = 1$

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + X_2^{2}}$$

### (b) **Slip for Maximum Torque ($s_{mT}$)** — derived by setting $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$$

### (c) **Maximum (Pull-Out / Breakdown) Torque ($T_{max}$)** — independent of $R_2$!

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2}$$

### (d) **Condition for Maximum Torque at Starting (i.e., $s_{mT} = 1$)**

$$R_2 = X_2$$

> [!TIP]
> **This is the principle behind slip-ring (wound rotor) induction motors** — external rotor resistance is added so that $R_2 = X_2$, making $T_{st} = T_{max}$. The motor then starts with **maximum possible torque**!

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Slip $s$ | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | $0 \le s \le 1$ for motoring |
| 2 | Synchronous speed $N_s$ | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | In rpm |
| 3 | Angular sync. speed $\omega_s$ | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | In rad/s |
| 4 | Torque $T$ | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + (s X_2)^{2}}$ | General expression |
| 5 | Starting torque $T_{st}$ | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + X_2^{2}}$ | At $s = 1$ |
| 6 | Slip for max. torque $s_{mT}$ | $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | From $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$ |
| 7 | Maximum torque $T_{max}$ | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2}$ | **Independent of $R_2$** |
| 8 | Condition $T_{st} = T_{max}$ | $R_2 = X_2$ | Used in slip-ring motors |
| 9 | Torque ratio $\dfrac{T}{T_{max}}$ | $\dfrac{T}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{s}{s_{mT}} + \dfrac{s_{mT}}{s}}$ | Universal torque ratio |
| 10 | Starting-to-max ratio $\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{max}}$ | $\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2 \, s_{mT}}{1 + s_{mT}^{2}}$ | Useful for design checks |
| 11 | Full-load slip (typical) | $s_{fl} \approx 0.02$ to $0.05$ | For standard cage motors |
| 12 | Mechanical power $P_m$ | $P_m = 3 I_2^{2} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{s}(1-s)$ | Gross developed power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These equations are used daily in industries for:
> - **Motor selection** (matching starting torque to load).
> - **Soft-starter / VFD design** (controlling starting current and torque).
> - **Hoist and elevator applications** (requiring high $T_{st}$).
> - **Slip-ring motor control** (varying external rotor resistance to alter $s_{mT}$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Full Torque Equation of a Three-Phase Induction Motor

We will derive this from the **per-phase equivalent circuit** of the induction motor referred to the stator.

### Step 1 — Define the equivalent circuit rotor branch

The rotor branch (referred to stator) has:
- Resistance: $\dfrac{R_2}{s}$
- Reactance: $X_2$ (constant, evaluated at standstill frequency)

The total rotor impedance is:

$$Z_2 = \frac{R_2}{s} + jX_2$$

### Step 2 — Rotor current per phase

$$I_2 = \frac{E_2}{Z_2} = \frac{E_2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}}}$$

The magnitude is:

$$\vert I_2 \vert = \frac{E_2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}}}$$

### Step 3 — Power input to the rotor (air-gap power) per phase

$$P_{g,1\phi} = I_2^{2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

Total air-gap power for 3 phases:

$$P_g = 3 \, I_2^{2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

### Step 4 — Gross mechanical power developed

The fraction $(1-s)$ of the air-gap power is converted to mechanical form:

$$P_m = (1 - s) \, P_g = 3 \, I_2^{2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} \cdot (1 - s)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $I_2$ expression

$$P_m = 3 \cdot \frac{E_2^{2}}{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} \cdot (1 - s)$$

### Step 6 — Express torque as $P_m / \omega_r$

Since the actual rotor angular speed is $\omega_r = (1 - s) \omega_s$:

$$T = \frac{P_m}{\omega_r} = \frac{P_m}{(1 - s) \, \omega_s}$$

The $(1-s)$ terms cancel:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^{2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}}{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}}$$

### Step 7 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $s^{2}$ to simplify

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^{2} \, R_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}}$$

> ✅ **This is the canonical KTU-board torque equation.**

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Condition for Maximum Torque

Starting from the torque equation:

$$T = \frac{3 E_2^{2}}{\omega_s} \cdot \underbrace{\left[\frac{s \, R_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}}\right]}_{f(s)}$$

Since $\dfrac{3 E_2^{2}}{\omega_s}$ is a positive constant (independent of $s$), the maximum of $T$ coincides with the maximum of $f(s)$.

### Step 1 — Differentiate $f(s)$ with respect to $s$ and set to zero

Let $f(s) = \dfrac{s R_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}}$. Applying the quotient rule:

$$\frac{df}{ds} = \frac{R_2 \left( R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2} \right) - s R_2 \left( 2 s X_2^{2} \right)}{\left( R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2} \right)^{2}}$$

### Step 2 — Numerator simplification

$$\text{Numerator} = R_2 \left[ R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2} - 2 s^{2} X_2^{2} \right] = R_2 \left[ R_2^{2} - s^{2} X_2^{2} \right]$$

### Step 3 — Equate numerator to zero

$$R_2^{2} - s^{2} X_2^{2} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s^{2} X_2^{2} = R_2^{2}$$

$$\boxed{s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}}$$

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Maximum Torque Value

Substitute $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ back into the torque equation:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{\left(\frac{R_2}{X_2}\right) E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + \left(\frac{R_2}{X_2}\right)^{2} X_2^{2}}$$

### Step 1 — Simplify the denominator

$$R_2^{2} + \left(\frac{R_2}{X_2}\right)^{2} X_2^{2} = R_2^{2} + R_2^{2} = 2 R_2^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Simplify the numerator

$$\left(\frac{R_2}{X_2}\right) \cdot E_2^{2} \cdot R_2 = \frac{R_2^{2} E_2^{2}}{X_2}$$

### Step 3 — Combine

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{R_2^{2} E_2^{2}}{X_2}}{2 R_2^{2}} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^{2}}{2 X_2}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $R_2$ terms cancel out** — proving that **maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance**, although the slip at which it occurs is directly proportional to $R_2$.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Universal Torque Ratio

We derive the expression $\dfrac{T}{T_{max}}$ which is useful when the slip is varied:

$$\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{ \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}} }{ \dfrac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2} }$$

### Step 1 — Cancel common factors $\dfrac{3 E_2^{2}}{\omega_s}$

$$\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{ \dfrac{2 s R_2 X_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}} }{ 1 } = \frac{2 s R_2 X_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}}$$

### Step 2 — Divide numerator and denominator by $R_2 X_2$ and introduce $s_{mT} = R_2/X_2$

Divide top and bottom by $R_2 X_2$:

$$\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{2 s}{\dfrac{R_2}{X_2} + \dfrac{s^{2} X_2}{R_2}} = \frac{2 s}{s_{mT} + \dfrac{s^{2}}{s_{mT}}}$$

### Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $s_{mT}$

$$\boxed{\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{2}{\dfrac{s}{s_{mT}} + \dfrac{s_{mT}}{s}}}$$

> **Engineering use:** This is the *master equation* used in numerical problems to compute torque at *any* slip, given only $T_{max}$ and $s_{mT}$.

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 5 — Ratio of Starting Torque to Maximum Torque

At $s = 1$:

$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \frac{2}{\dfrac{1}{s_{mT}} + \dfrac{s_{mT}}{1}} = \frac{2 \, s_{mT}}{1 + s_{mT}^{2}}$$

This ratio is always $\le 1$, with equality only when $s_{mT} = 1$ (i.e., $R_2 = X_2$).

---

## 3.6 Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor has rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.05\;\Omega$ and standstill reactance $X_2 = 0.5\;\Omega$ per phase. At a slip of 0.04, the rotor EMF is 100 V per phase. Compute the developed torque.

### Step 1 — Synchronous speed

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 2 — Plug into torque equation with $s = 0.04$

$$T = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{0.04 \times 100^{2} \times 0.05}{0.05^{2} + (0.04 \times 0.5)^{2}}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate denominator

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.0025 + (0.02)^{2} = 0.0025 + 0.0004 = 0.0029\;\Omega^{2}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate numerator

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.04 \times 10000 \times 0.05 = 20$$

### Step 5 — Final calculation

$$T = \frac{3 \times 20}{157.08 \times 0.0029} = \frac{60}{0.4555} \approx 131.7 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 131.7 \text{ N·m}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Logic Tree of Torque Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stator 3-phase Supply] --> B[Rotating Magnetic Field at Ns]
    B --> C[Rotor EMF E2s = sE2 induced]
    C --> D[Rotor Current I2 computed from Z2 = R2/s + jX2]
    D --> E[Air-Gap Power Pg = 3 I2² R2/s]
    E --> F[Gross Mechanical Power Pm = 1-s times Pg]
    F --> G[Torque T = Pm divided by omega r = 1-s times omega s]
    G --> H[Final Equation T = 3 by omega s times s E2² R2 over R2² + sX2²]
    H --> I{Analyse Extremum}
    I --> J[dT by ds = 0]
    J --> K[Slip at Max Torque smT = R2 by X2]
    J --> L[Max Torque Tmax = 3 E2² by 2 omega s X2]
    H --> M[At s = 1]
    M --> N[Starting Torque Tst = 3 E2² R2 by omega s R2² + X2²]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Operating Regions of Torque-Slip Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REGION_LOW_SLIP[s near 0 — Normal Running Region]
        RS1[Slip small, s less than 0.05] --> RT1[Stable operation] --> RP1[Torque approximately proportional to s]
    end

    subgraph REGION_MID[s around smT — Pull-out Region]
        RM1[Slip equals R2 by X2] --> RMT[Maximum Torque Tmax occurs] --> RCOM[Beyond Tmax, motor cannot sustain load]
    end

    subgraph REGION_HIGH[s near 1 — Starting Region]
        RH1[Slip equals 1, rotor at standstill] --> RST[Starting Torque Tst] --> RNOTE[Stable only if Tst greater than load torque at start]
    end

    REGION_LOW_SLIP --> REGION_MID
    REGION_MID --> REGION_HIGH
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Effect of Rotor Resistance on Torque-Slip Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RES[Increase Rotor Resistance R2] --> EFF1[SmT = R2 by X2 increases, curve shifts right]
    RES --> EFF2[Tmax unchanged because Tmax depends only on E2 and X2]
    RES --> EFF3[Tst increases until R2 equals X2, then decreases]
    RES --> APP[Application: Slip-ring motors use external R2 to start heavy loads]

    subgraph CASE_A[R2 small — Cage Motor]
        CA1[SmT less than 1] --> CA2[Low starting torque] --> CA3[High starting current]
    end

    subgraph CASE_B[R2 equal X2 — Ideal Start]
        CB1[SmT equals 1] --> CB2[Tst equals Tmax] --> CB3[Best starting performance]
    end

    subgraph CASE_C[R2 very large]
        CC1[SmT much greater than 1] --> CC2[Tst falls below Tmax] --> CC3[Poor starting, useful only for low-load soft start]
    end

    CASE_A --> CASE_B
    CASE_B --> CASE_C
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Process Flow — Numerical Problem Solving Pattern (KTU Exam Trick)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Read problem: Given R2, X2, E2, f, P, s] --> P2[Compute Ns = 120f by P]
    P2 --> P3[Compute omega s = 2 pi Ns by 60]
    P3 --> P4{What is asked?}
    P4 -- Torque at slip s --> Q1[Use general T equation]
    P4 -- Max torque --> Q2[Compute Tmax = 3 E2² by 2 omega s X2]
    P4 -- Slip for max torque --> Q3[Compute smT = R2 by X2]
    P4 -- Starting torque --> Q4[Put s = 1 in T equation]
    Q1 --> OUT[Show full step-by-step substitution]
    Q2 --> OUT
    Q3 --> OUT
    Q4 --> OUT
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this flowchart for KTU exams** — examiners expect students to write the formula, substitute values in the *same order* as the formula, and then simplify step-by-step. Skipping the substitution step is the most common reason for mark deduction.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the term "slip" in a three-phase induction motor. Why is it impossible for an induction motor to run at synchronous speed?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Slip (s)** is defined as the difference between the synchronous speed ($N_s$) of the rotating magnetic field and the actual rotor speed ($N_r$), expressed as a fraction (or per-unit) of $N_s$:
>
> $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$
>
> The induction motor **cannot run at synchronous speed** because rotor torque is produced *only* due to the **relative motion** between the rotating field and the rotor conductors. If $N_r = N_s$ (i.e., $s = 0$), the rotor conductors would be **stationary with respect to the field**, no EMF would be induced, no rotor current would flow, and hence **no torque** would be developed. The motor would decelerate due to load torque and slip would re-establish. (3 Marks)

---

### **Q2. State the condition for maximum torque in a 3-phase induction motor. What is the value of slip at maximum torque?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> The condition for maximum torque is obtained by setting $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$ in the torque-slip equation, which yields:
>
> $$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$$
>
> At this slip, the rotor circuit impedance angle equals $45°$, i.e., $\tan\phi = \dfrac{s_{mT} X_2}{R_2} = 1$.
>
> The corresponding maximum torque is:
>
> $$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2}$$
>
> Notably, **$T_{max}$ is independent of rotor resistance $R_2$**, although the slip at which it occurs is directly proportional to $R_2$. (3 Marks)

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 End Semester Examination pattern, Part B questions of Module 4 offer internal choice. Below are **Question A** and its alternative **Question B**. Attempt ANY ONE.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the electromagnetic torque developed by a three-phase induction motor. Hence, deduce the condition for maximum torque and the value of maximum torque.
> **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor has rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.3\;\Omega$ and standstill rotor reactance $X_2 = 1.5\;\Omega$ per phase. The rotor induced EMF at standstill is 110 V per phase. Compute:
> - (i) Starting torque
> - (ii) Slip at maximum torque
> - (iii) Maximum torque
> - (iv) Full-load torque at slip $s = 0.04$
> **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Per-phase rotor branch impedance:**

$$Z_2 = \frac{R_2}{s} + jX_2$$

**Step 2 — Rotor current magnitude:**

$$\vert I_2 \vert = \frac{E_2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}}}$$

**Step 3 — Air-gap power (3-phase):**

$$P_g = 3 \, I_2^{2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} = \frac{3 E_2^{2} \cdot R_2 / s}{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical power developed:**

$$P_m = (1 - s) P_g$$

**Step 5 — Torque:**

$$T = \frac{P_m}{(1 - s) \omega_s} = \frac{3 E_2^{2} \cdot R_2 / s}{\omega_s \left[\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}\right]}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s^{2}$:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum torque condition:**

Differentiating $T$ w.r.t. $s$ and equating to zero gives:

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum torque value:**

Substituting $s_{mT}$ in equation and simplifying:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** $T_{max}$ is independent of $R_2$. Higher $R_2$ shifts $s_{mT}$ to the right but does not change the peak torque value.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 6$, $R_2 = 0.3\;\Omega$, $X_2 = 1.5\;\Omega$, $E_2 = 110$ V.

**Preliminary Calculations:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = \frac{100\pi}{3} \approx 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(i) Starting Torque ($s = 1$):**

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{1 \times 110^{2} \times 0.3}{0.3^{2} + 1.5^{2}}$$

Denominator: $0.09 + 2.25 = 2.34$

Numerator: $1 \times 12100 \times 0.3 = 3630$

$$T_{st} = \frac{3 \times 3630}{104.72 \times 2.34} = \frac{10890}{245.05} \approx 44.44 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{st} \approx 44.4 \text{ N·m}} \quad \text{(1.5 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Slip at Maximum Torque:**

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.3}{1.5} = 0.2 \; (20\%)$$

$$\boxed{s_{mT} = 0.2} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**(iii) Maximum Torque:**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 \times 110^{2}}{2 \times 104.72 \times 1.5} = \frac{36300}{314.16} \approx 115.55 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} \approx 115.5 \text{ N·m}} \quad \text{(1.5 Marks)}$$

**(iv) Full-Load Torque ($s = 0.04$):**

$$T_{fl} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{0.04 \times 12100 \times 0.3}{0.3^{2} + (0.04 \times 1.5)^{2}}$$

Denominator: $0.09 + 0.0036 = 0.0936$

Numerator: $0.04 \times 12100 \times 0.3 = 145.2$

$$T_{fl} = \frac{3 \times 145.2}{104.72 \times 0.0936} = \frac{435.6}{9.802} \approx 44.44 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{fl} \approx 44.4 \text{ N·m}} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

> **Observation (0.5 Marks):** In this motor, starting torque equals full-load torque (both ~44.4 N·m). The motor is suitable for light starting loads but **not** for heavy starting loads such as hoists.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **torque-slip characteristics** of a three-phase induction motor. Identify and justify the **stable** and **unstable** operating regions.
> **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A 3-phase induction motor has rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.04\;\Omega$ and standstill reactance $X_2 = 0.4\;\Omega$ per phase. The slip at maximum torque is **0.1**. A new rotor design requires the starting torque to be **equal to the maximum torque**. Determine:
> - (i) The new rotor resistance required.
> - (ii) The new slip at maximum torque.
> - (iii) Comment on the effect of this change on the full-load speed of the motor.
> **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 Marks]

**Sketch of Torque-Slip Curve (Descriptive):**

A typical torque-slip curve is shown below. The x-axis represents slip $s$ (0 to 1.2) and the y-axis represents torque $T$. The curve rises from a value $T_{st}$ at $s = 1$, reaches a peak $T_{max}$ at $s = s_{mT}$, and then falls back towards zero as $s \to 0$.

```
T │            ★ Tmax
  │           /│\
  │          / │ \
  │   Tst   /  │  \
  │────────/   │   \─────────── (stable)
  │       /    │    \
  │      / UNSTABLE\   \  STABLE
  │     /     │     \     \
  │____/______│______\_____\____ s
  0    smT    │      1.0
              │
```

**Key Features of the Curve (1.5 Marks each):**

1. **At $s = 0$ (Synchronous speed):** Torque $T = 0$ because there is no relative motion.
2. **At $s = s_{mT}$ (Maximum torque point):** $T = T_{max}$ — this is the *breakdown torque*. Beyond this point, the motor cannot sustain a higher load.
3. **At $s = 1$ (Standstill):** $T = T_{st}$ — the starting torque.
4. **Normal operating region:** $0 < s < s_{mT}$ — the **stable region**.
5. **Pull-out region:** $s_{mT} < s < 1$ — the **unstable region** (in practice, motor won't settle here).

**Stable vs Unstable Region Justification (3.5 Marks):**

- In the **stable region** ($0 < s < s_{mT}$), the slope $\dfrac{dT}{ds} > 0$. This means: if load suddenly increases, the motor slows down (slip increases) and the motor torque *also increases* to match — equilibrium is restored.
- In the **unstable region** ($s_{mT} < s < 1$), the slope $\dfrac{dT}{ds} < 0$. If load increases, slip increases, but motor torque *decreases* further — the motor decelerates rapidly and **stalls**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $R_2 = 0.04\;\Omega$, $X_2 = 0.4\;\Omega$, $s_{mT} = 0.1$ (original).

**Step 1 — Verify original $s_{mT}$:**

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.04}{0.4} = 0.1 \;\; \checkmark$$

**(i) New rotor resistance for $T_{st} = T_{max}$:**

For $T_{st} = T_{max}$, we need $s_{mT} = 1$, which gives:

$$R_2' = X_2 = 0.4\;\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_2' = 0.4\;\Omega} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**(ii) New slip at maximum torque:**

$$\boxed{s_{mT}' = \frac{R_2'}{X_2} = \frac{0.4}{0.4} = 1.0} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**(iii) Effect on Full-Load Speed (3 Marks):**

- The maximum torque value is **unchanged** (since $T_{max}$ is independent of $R_2$).
- The new slip at maximum torque is now at $s = 1$ (standstill). This means in the new design, the motor can deliver its **peak torque right from starting**, which is ideal for **high-inertia, heavy-starting loads** (crushers, conveyors, hoists).
- However, the **full-load speed drops** because, for the same load torque, the operating slip $s_{fl}$ is now **larger**. A typical full-load slip might increase from $\sim 4\%$ to $\sim 6$–$8\%$, reducing the full-load speed.
- This is the trade-off — **better starting performance** but **poorer speed regulation** under load.

> This is precisely why **slip-ring induction motors with external rotor resistance controllers** are used in industrial applications requiring both high starting torque and reasonable running efficiency.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong use of $\omega_s$ vs $N_s$:** Students frequently substitute $N_s$ (in rpm) directly into the formula instead of converting to rad/s using $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60}$. This yields wrong units (N·m vs N·m/min). **Always convert to rad/s.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 3:** For a three-phase motor, the total torque is **three times** the per-phase value. Omitting the factor of 3 is a **2-mark deduction** trap.
>
> 3. **Confusing rotor EMF with supply voltage:** The induced rotor EMF $E_2$ is **not** equal to the stator supply voltage $V$. Use the transformer relation $\dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ (or stator/rotor turns ratio) if the problem gives $V$ and not $E_2$.
>
> 4. **Sign error in $s_{mT}$ derivation:** The slip at maximum torque is always **positive** for motoring mode. Taking $s_{mT} = -\dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ is a critical sign error — check direction of motion.
>
> 5. **Showing final answer without intermediate steps:** KTU examiners award marks for **substitution and simplification steps**, not just the final numerical value. A 7-mark problem with only the answer gets at most **2 marks**.
>
> 6. **Mixing up starting torque formula with maximum torque formula:** $T_{st} \ne T_{max}$ in general. $T_{st} = T_{max}$ only when $R_2 = X_2$.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Slip** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$; always between **0 and 1** for motoring.
- ✅ **Synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ rpm.
- ✅ **Torque equation:** $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}}$ — this is the *single most important* formula of Module 4.
- ✅ **Slip at maximum torque:** $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ — directly proportional to rotor resistance.
- ✅ **Maximum torque:** $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2}$ — **independent of $R_2$**.
- ✅ **Starting torque:** $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + X_2^{2}}$ — evaluated at $s = 1$.
- ✅ **Condition for $T_{st} = T_{max}$:** $R_2 = X_2$ — used in **slip-ring (wound rotor) motors**.
- ✅ **Stable operating region:** $0 < s < s_{mT}$ (positive slope of T-s curve).
- ✅ **Unstable operating region:** $s_{mT} < s < 1$ (negative slope — motor will stall).
- ✅ **Effect of increasing rotor resistance:** $s_{mT}$ shifts right, $T_{max}$ unchanged, $T_{st}$ rises initially then falls, speed regulation worsens.
- ✅ **Universal torque ratio:** $\dfrac{T}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{s}{s_{mT}} + \dfrac{s_{mT}}{s}}$ — use this when only $T_{max}$ and $s_{mT}$ are given.
- ✅ **Always convert $N_s$ to $\omega_s$** in rad/s before substituting in torque formula.
- ✅ **Three-phase factor of 3** must always appear in the numerator for total torque.
- ✅ **Power input to rotor** = air-gap power $P_g = \dfrac{3 I_2^{2} R_2}{s}$.
- ✅ **Mechanical power developed** $P_m = (1 - s) P_g$.
- ✅ **Rotor copper loss** $= s \cdot P_g$ — the **fundamental energy loss mechanism** in induction motors.

# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🔷 1.1 What is a Three-Phase Induction Motor?

A **Three-Phase Induction Motor (TIM)** is a self-starting, robust, and maintenance-free AC machine that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy based on the principle of **electromagnetic induction** between a stationary stator and a rotating rotor. It is the **workhorse of modern industry**, driving pumps, fans, compressors, conveyors, and machine tools in approximately **80% of all industrial applications**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A three-phase induction motor is a singly-excited AC machine in which the stator winding is connected to a 3-phase supply and the rotor derives its power through electromagnetic induction from the stator field at a speed less than the synchronous speed."*

### 🔹 1.2 The "Why" Behind Module 4 Numerical Problems

In Module 4, you are required to **analyze the steady-state performance** of a three-phase induction motor using:
- The **exact per-phase equivalent circuit**
- The **approximate equivalent circuit** (with magnetizing branch shifted to stator terminals)
- The **power flow stages** (Stator Input → Air-Gap Power → Mechanical Power Developed → Shaft Output)
- The **torque-slip characteristic**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight (KTU Examiner Focus):**
> Every numerical problem in this module ultimately traces back to **two master equations**:
> 1. **Power Division:** $P_{gap} : P_{cu,rotor} : P_{m,dev} = 1 : s : (1-s)$
> 2. **Torque Equation:** $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$
>
> Master these two, and you master Module 4 numericals.

## 🔷 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Slip Conveyor Belt"

Imagine a **rotating magnetic field** as a giant **circular conveyor belt** moving at synchronous speed $N_s$. The **rotor conductors** are like **fitness enthusiasts running on this belt**.

- If the runner matches the belt's speed perfectly → **No relative motion** → **No induced EMF** → **No torque** (useless!)
- If the runner stops completely → **Maximum relative motion** → **High induced EMF, but rotor is inefficient** (high losses)
- The **sweet spot** is somewhere in between, where the runner falls slightly behind the belt by a small percentage called the **slip ($s$)** — this is exactly when **maximum useful mechanical work** is done.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world reference:** A ceiling fan runs at ~1440 rpm when the supply is 50 Hz, 4-pole ($N_s = 1500$ rpm). The slip is 4%, which is the **typical full-load operating zone** for industrial motors.

## 🔷 1.4 Standard Assumptions in KTU Numerical Problems

| Assumption | Why It Is Made |
|------------|----------------|
| Stator and rotor windings are **symmetrical and balanced** | Permits use of a **single per-phase equivalent circuit** |
| Magnetizing current is **purely reactive** | Core loss lumped into a separate shunt resistance $R_c$ (when given) |
| Rotor EMF $E_2$ and reactance $X_2$ are **referred to the stator side** ($E_2'$, $X_2'$) | Enables single-loop circuit analysis |
| Mechanical losses = Friction + Windage only (if not specified) | Simplifies the power flow diagram |
| Synchronous speed is **constant** for a given supply | Decouples electrical and mechanical sub-problems |

# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔷 2.1 The Power Flow — Stage by Stage Logic

A three-phase induction motor's power flow follows a strict **cascaded deduction chain**. The key is that **rotor copper loss is the only "tax" paid between air-gap power and mechanical power developed**.

$$\boxed{P_{input} \to P_{stator\,losses} \to P_{gap} \to P_{cu,rotor} \to P_{m,dev} \to P_{mech\,losses} \to P_{shaft}}$$

Where:
- $P_{stator\,losses} = P_{cu,stator} + P_{core}$ (stator copper + iron loss)
- $P_{gap} = $ Power crossing the air-gap from stator to rotor
- $P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{gap}$ (rotor copper loss is the fraction $s$ of air-gap power)
- $P_{m,dev} = (1-s) \cdot P_{gap}$ (mechanical power developed)
- $P_{shaft} = P_{m,dev} - P_{mech\,losses}$ (after friction, windage, stray load loss)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Induction Motor Power Stages:**
> The **air-gap power** is the parent quantity, and it splits between the **rotor copper loss** ($s$ share) and the **developed mechanical power** ($(1-s)$ share). The ratio $P_{cu,rotor} : P_{m,dev} = s : (1-s)$ is the **single most useful fact** in any KTU power flow problem.

## 🔷 2.2 The Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit (Thevenin & Exact)

In the **exact equivalent circuit**, the rotor branch is modelled as $R_2'/s$, which is split for analysis convenience as:
- $R_2'$ → actual rotor resistance (carries $P_{cu,rotor}$)
- $R_2'(1-s)/s$ → mechanical load equivalent resistance (carries $P_{m,dev}$)

### 🔹 Thevenin Equivalent (Used for Torque Problems)

To simplify torque calculations, the circuit is reduced to a **single-series Thevenin equivalent** seen from the rotor terminals:

$$V_{th} = V_1 \cdot \frac{jX_m}{(R_1 + jX_1) + jX_m}, \quad Z_{th} = \frac{(R_1 + jX_1)(jX_m)}{R_1 + jX_1 + jX_m}$$

The Thevenin form for **total rotor-side impedance at slip $s$**:
$$Z_{rotor} = \frac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2'$$

## 🔷 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **The table below is the ONLY reference you need during exam preparation for Module 4 numericals. Print it. Memorize it.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | $P$ = number of poles (even integer) |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | pu (0 to 1) | $s = 0$ at synchronous, $s = 1$ at standstill |
| 3 | Slip in rpm | $N_{slip} = N_s - N_r$ | rpm | The "missing rpm" relative to field |
| 4 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s f$ | Hz | At standstill: $f_r = f$ |
| 5 | Rotor EMF at slip | $E_{2s} = s E_{2(\text{standstill})}$ | V/phase | Linear with slip |
| 6 | Rotor reactance at slip | $X_{2s} = s X_{2(\text{standstill})}$ | Ω/phase | Linear with slip |
| 7 | Synchronous angular velocity | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | For torque equations |
| 8 | Gross (developed) torque | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | N·m | Uses **referred** $E_2$, $R_2$, $X_2$ if $V_{th}$ used |
| 9 | Slip at max torque | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | pu | Independent of load! |
| 10 | Maximum torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2}{X_2}$ | N·m | **Independent of $R_2$** (a remarkable result) |
| 11 | Starting torque ($s=1$) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ | N·m | Often less than full-load torque in cage motors |
| 12 | Air-gap power | $P_{gap} = \dfrac{P_{cu,rotor}}{s} = \dfrac{P_{m,dev}}{1-s}$ | W | The "parent" power |
| 13 | Developed mechanical power | $P_{m,dev} = (1-s) P_{gap} = \dfrac{3 I_2'^2 R_2' (1-s)}{s}$ | W | Across the air gap |
| 14 | Shaft output power | $P_{shaft} = T_{shaft} \cdot \omega_r$ | W | Where $\omega_r = (1-s)\omega_s$ |
| 15 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{input}} \times 100\%$ | % | Total efficiency |
| 16 | Power factor | $\cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{input}}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_L}$ | pu | For 3-phase line values |
| 17 | Torque ratio to max | $\dfrac{T}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2 a s}{a^2 + s^2}$ where $a = \dfrac{R_2}{s_{max} X_2}$ | pu | Useful for torque-speed curve plotting |

## 🔷 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry / Equipment | Why Induction Motor is the Only Choice |
|----------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Pumps & Compressors** (water treatment, HVAC, petrochemical) | Constant-speed, high reliability, no sparking (explosion-safe) |
| **Conveyor Belts & Mixers** (food, mining, cement) | High starting torque, rugged construction |
| **Elevators & Cranes** (with VFD control) | V/f control gives variable speed with constant torque |
| **Electric Vehicles (Tesla, Tata, Hyundai EVs)** | Squirrel-cage induction motor with vector control (Field-Oriented Control) |
| **Domestic Appliances** (washing machines, refrigerators) | Single-phase capacitor-start or shaded-pole variants |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection:** Modern Tesla Model S/X uses a **3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor** with a copper rotor bar design, controlled by a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** that adjusts the stator frequency from near-DC (during start) to ~200 Hz at high speed. The KTU module gives you the **theoretical foundation** for this very real engineering system.

## 📘 PROBLEM 1: Slip, Rotor Frequency, and Rotor EMF (Basic, 5-Mark Type)

### **Question:**
A 3-phase, **50 Hz, 6-pole** induction motor runs at a full-load speed of **940 rpm**. If the standstill rotor EMF per phase is **120 V**, calculate:
1. Synchronous speed $N_s$
2. Slip at full load $s$
3. Rotor frequency $f_r$
4. Rotor EMF per phase at full load

### **Solution:**

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 6$, $N_r = 940$ rpm, $E_{2(standstill)} = 120$ V/phase

#### 🔸 Step 1: Synchronous Speed
$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

**Explanation:** For any 6-pole machine at 50 Hz, the field rotates at exactly 1000 rpm. A 6-pole machine cannot run at any other synchronous speed for a 50 Hz supply.

#### 🔸 Step 2: Slip
$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1000 - 940}{1000} = \frac{60}{1000} = 0.06 \text{ pu} = 6\%$$

**Explanation:** The rotor lags the field by 6%. This is a healthy full-load slip (typical range: 3–8%).

#### 🔸 Step 3: Rotor Frequency
$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.06 \times 50 = 3 \text{ Hz}$$

**Explanation:** The relative motion between the rotor and the field is small, so the frequency "seen" by the rotor conductors is only 3 Hz, even though the stator supply is 50 Hz.

#### 🔸 Step 4: Rotor EMF at Full Load
$$E_{2(s)} = s \cdot E_{2(standstill)} = 0.06 \times 120 = 7.2 \text{ V/phase}$$

**Explanation:** EMF is directly proportional to slip. At full load, only 6% of the standstill EMF is induced.

### ✅ **Final Answers:**
$$\boxed{N_s = 1000 \text{ rpm}, \quad s = 0.06, \quad f_r = 3 \text{ Hz}, \quad E_{2(s)} = 7.2 \text{ V}}$$

---

## 📘 PROBLEM 2: Power Flow Diagram and Output Torque (Standard 7-Mark Type)

### **Question:**
A 3-phase, **400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole** induction motor runs at **1440 rpm** with the following data:
- Total stator copper loss: **1500 W**
- Rotor copper loss: **1000 W**
- Iron (core) loss: **1200 W**
- Mechanical (friction + windage) loss: **500 W**

Calculate:
1. Air-gap power $P_{gap}$
2. Mechanical power developed $P_{m,dev}$
3. Shaft output power $P_{shaft}$
4. Output torque $T_{out}$ in N·m
5. Input power and overall efficiency

### **Solution:**

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$ poles, $N_r = 1440$ rpm

#### 🔸 Step 1: Synchronous Speed and Slip
$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$
$$s = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04$$

#### 🔸 Step 2: Air-Gap Power
Using the **golden rule** — rotor copper loss is $s$ times the air-gap power:
$$P_{gap} = \frac{P_{cu,rotor}}{s} = \frac{1000}{0.04} = 25000 \text{ W} = 25 \text{ kW}$$

**Explanation:** Even though only 1 kW is lost in the rotor, the parent air-gap power is 25 kW because the rotor "sees" the entire 25 kW — the copper loss is just the $s = 0.04$ portion.

#### 🔸 Step 3: Mechanical Power Developed
$$P_{m,dev} = (1 - s) P_{gap} = (1 - 0.04) \times 25000 = 0.96 \times 25000 = 24000 \text{ W} = 24 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification check:** $P_{cu,rotor} + P_{m,dev} = 1000 + 24000 = 25000$ W ✓ (matches $P_{gap}$)

#### 🔸 Step 4: Shaft Output Power
$$P_{shaft} = P_{m,dev} - P_{mech\,losses} = 24000 - 500 = 23500 \text{ W} = 23.5 \text{ kW}$$

#### 🔸 Step 5: Output Torque
The output torque is computed using the **actual rotor speed**:
$$\omega_r = \frac{2 \pi N_r}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1440}{60} = 48 \pi = 150.796 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_{out} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\omega_r} = \frac{23500}{150.796} = 155.85 \text{ N·m}$$

#### 🔸 Step 6: Input Power and Efficiency
Total stator losses = $P_{cu,stator} + P_{core} = 1500 + 1200 = 2700$ W
$$P_{input} = P_{gap} + P_{stator\,losses} = 25000 + 2700 = 27700 \text{ W} = 27.7 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{input}} \times 100\% = \frac{23500}{27700} \times 100\% = 84.84\%$$

### ✅ **Final Answers:**
$$\boxed{P_{gap} = 25 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{m,dev} = 24 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{shaft} = 23.5 \text{ kW}, \quad T_{out} = 155.85 \text{ N·m}, \quad \eta = 84.84\%}$$

---

## 📘 PROBLEM 3: Slip at Maximum Torque and Torque Calculations (7-Mark Type)

### **Question:**
A 3-phase, **6-pole, 50 Hz** induction motor has the following referred-to-stator parameters per phase:
- Rotor resistance $R_2' = 0.4\ \Omega$
- Rotor reactance at standstill $X_2' = 2\ \Omega$
- Rotor EMF (referred) at standstill $E_2' = 200\ \text{V/phase}$

Calculate:
1. Synchronous speed in rad/s
2. Slip at which maximum torque occurs
3. Maximum torque developed
4. Full-load torque at slip $s = 0.04$
5. Starting torque at $s = 1$
6. Ratio of starting to maximum torque

### **Solution:**

**Given:** $P = 6$, $f = 50$ Hz, $R_2' = 0.4\ \Omega$, $X_2' = 2\ \Omega$, $E_2' = 200$ V

#### 🔸 Step 1: Synchronous Angular Velocity
$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$
$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

#### 🔸 Step 2: Slip at Maximum Torque
$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{X_2'} = \frac{0.4}{2} = 0.2$$

**Explanation:** $s_{max}$ is a property of the rotor design. It does NOT depend on the load. A high $s_{max}$ implies high starting torque (good for elevators).

#### 🔸 Step 3: Maximum Torque
$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2'^2}{X_2'} = \frac{3}{2 \times 104.72} \cdot \frac{200^2}{2}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{209.44} \cdot \frac{40000}{2} = 0.01432 \times 20000 = 286.48 \text{ N·m}$$

**Note the powerful result:** $T_{max}$ does NOT contain $R_2'$. Changing $R_2'$ shifts the slip at which max torque occurs but does NOT change the value of $T_{max}$.

#### 🔸 Step 4: Full-Load Torque at s = 0.04
Using the general torque equation:
$$T_{fl} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + (s X_2')^2}$$

Numerator: $s E_2'^2 R_2' = 0.04 \times 200^2 \times 0.4 = 0.04 \times 40000 \times 0.4 = 640$
Denominator: $R_2'^2 + (s X_2')^2 = 0.4^2 + (0.04 \times 2)^2 = 0.16 + 0.0064 = 0.1664$

$$T_{fl} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{640}{0.1664} = 0.02865 \times 3846.15 = 110.20 \text{ N·m}$$

#### 🔸 Step 5: Starting Torque (s = 1)
$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + X_2'^2} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{200^2 \times 0.4}{0.4^2 + 2^2}$$

$$T_{st} = 0.02865 \cdot \frac{16000}{0.16 + 4} = 0.02865 \cdot \frac{16000}{4.16} = 0.02865 \times 3846.15 = 110.20 \text{ N·m}$$

**Interesting observation:** For this motor, $T_{st} \approx T_{fl}$ because $s_{max} = 0.2$ is far from operating region $s = 0.04$.

#### 🔸 Step 6: Ratio of Starting to Maximum Torque
$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \frac{110.20}{286.48} = 0.3847 = 38.47\%$$

**Verification using the shortcut formula:** $T_{st}/T_{max} = 2a/(1+a^2)$ where $a = R_2'/(s_{max} X_2') = 0.4/(0.2 \times 2) = 1$
$$T_{st}/T_{max} = 2(1)/(1+1^2) = 2/2 = 1.0$$

**WAIT — Discrepancy Alert!** Let me recompute. When $a = R_2/s_{max} X_2 = 1$, this means the operating slip equals $s_{max}$ at $s = s_{max} = 0.2$, NOT at $s = 0.04$.

Re-evaluating $a$ for **starting** ($s=1$): $a_{st} = R_2'/(1 \cdot X_2') = 0.4/2 = 0.2$
$$T_{st}/T_{max} = \frac{2 a_{st}}{a_{st}^2 + 1} = \frac{2(0.2)}{0.04 + 1} = \frac{0.4}{1.04} = 0.3846 \text{ ✓}$$

### ✅ **Final Answers:**
$$\boxed{\omega_s = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}, \quad s_{max} = 0.2, \quad T_{max} = 286.48 \text{ N·m}, \quad T_{fl} = 110.20 \text{ N·m}, \quad T_{st} = 110.20 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

## 📘 PROBLEM 4: Shaft Power and Input Current from Equivalent Circuit (14-Mark Type)

### **Question:**
A 3-phase, **50 Hz, 4-pole, 400 V** delta-connected cage induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator:
- $R_1 = 0.5\ \Omega$, $X_1 = 1.5\ \Omega$
- $R_2' = 0.6\ \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.5\ \Omega$
- $X_m = 50\ \Omega$ (magnetizing reactance, core loss neglected)

At a slip of **5%**, calculate:
1. The synchronous speed and rotor speed
2. The Thevenin voltage and Thevenin impedance as seen from the rotor branch
3. The rotor current referred to the stator $I_2'$
4. The air-gap power, mechanical power developed, and rotor copper loss
5. The shaft power (assuming 1.5% of $P_{m,dev}$ as mechanical losses)
6. The overall efficiency (assuming stator iron loss = 600 W)

### **Solution:**

**Given:** $V_{L} = 400$ V, delta → $V_{ph} = 400$ V, $R_1 = 0.5\ \Omega$, $X_1 = 1.5\ \Omega$, $R_2' = 0.6\ \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.5\ \Omega$, $X_m = 50\ \Omega$, $s = 0.05$

#### 🔸 Step 1: Synchronous and Rotor Speed
$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$
$$N_r = (1 - s) N_s = 0.95 \times 1500 = 1425 \text{ rpm}$$

#### 🔸 Step 2: Thevenin Equivalent

**Thevenin voltage** (voltage divider across $jX_m$):
$$V_{th} = V_{ph} \cdot \frac{jX_m}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)} = 400 \cdot \frac{j50}{0.5 + j(1.5 + 50)} = 400 \cdot \frac{j50}{0.5 + j51.5}$$

**Magnitude calculation:**
$$\vert V_{th} \vert = 400 \cdot \frac{50}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + 51.5^2}} = 400 \cdot \frac{50}{\sqrt{0.25 + 2652.25}} = 400 \cdot \frac{50}{51.5024}$$
$$\boxed{\vert V_{th} \vert = 400 \cdot 0.97084 = 388.34 \text{ V}}$$

**Angle of $V_{th}$:**
$$\angle V_{th} = 90° - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{51.5}{0.5}\right) = 90° - 89.444° = 0.556°$$

**Thevenin impedance:**
$$Z_{th} = \frac{(R_1 + jX_1)(jX_m)}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)} = \frac{(0.5 + j1.5)(j50)}{0.5 + j51.5}$$

Numerator: $(0.5 + j1.5)(j50) = j25 + j^2 75 = -75 + j25$
$$\vert \text{Num} \vert = \sqrt{75^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{5625 + 625} = \sqrt{6250} = 79.057\ \Omega \text{ at } 180° - 18.43° = 161.57°$$
$$\vert \text{Den} \vert = 51.5024\ \Omega \text{ at } 89.444°$$
$$\vert Z_{th} \vert = \frac{79.057}{51.5024} = 1.535\ \Omega, \quad \angle Z_{th} = 161.57° - 89.444° = 72.13°$$

Converting to rectangular:
$$R_{th} = 1.535 \cos(72.13°) = 1.535 \times 0.3072 = 0.4715\ \Omega$$
$$X_{th} = 1.535 \sin(72.13°) = 1.535 \times 0.9516 = 1.4606\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{Z_{th} = 0.4715 + j1.4606\ \Omega}$$

#### 🔸 Step 3: Rotor Current

**Rotor branch impedance at slip $s = 0.05$:**
$$\frac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2' = \frac{0.6}{0.05} + j1.5 = 12 + j1.5\ \Omega$$

**Total impedance seen by $V_{th}$:**
$$Z_{total} = Z_{th} + \frac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2' = (0.4715 + 12) + j(1.4606 + 1.5) = 12.4715 + j2.9606\ \Omega$$

$$\vert Z_{total} \vert = \sqrt{12.4715^2 + 2.9606^2} = \sqrt{155.54 + 8.765} = \sqrt{164.31} = 12.818\ \Omega$$

**Rotor current:**
$$I_2' = \frac{\vert V_{th} \vert}{\vert Z_{total} \vert} = \frac{388.34}{12.818} = 30.30 \text{ A}$$

#### 🔸 Step 4: Air-Gap Power, Mechanical Power, Rotor Copper Loss

**Rotor copper loss** (across the $R_2'$ resistor):
$$P_{cu,rotor} = 3 I_2'^2 R_2' = 3 \times (30.30)^2 \times 0.6 = 3 \times 918.09 \times 0.6 = 1652.56 \text{ W}$$

**Air-gap power** (using the golden rule $P_{cu,rotor} = s P_{gap}$):
$$P_{gap} = \frac{P_{cu,rotor}}{s} = \frac{1652.56}{0.05} = 33051.2 \text{ W} \approx 33.05 \text{ kW}$$

**Mechanical power developed:**
$$P_{m,dev} = (1-s) P_{gap} = 0.95 \times 33051.2 = 31398.6 \text{ W} \approx 31.40 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification:** $P_{m,dev} = 3 I_2'^2 \cdot R_2' \cdot \frac{1-s}{s} = 1652.56 \times 0.95/0.05 = 1652.56 \times 19 = 31398.6$ W ✓

#### 🔸 Step 5: Shaft Power
Mechanical loss = $0.015 \times P_{m,dev} = 0.015 \times 31398.6 = 470.98$ W
$$P_{shaft} = P_{m,dev} - P_{mech} = 31398.6 - 470.98 = 30927.6 \text{ W} \approx 30.93 \text{ kW}$$

#### 🔸 Step 6: Overall Efficiency

**Stator copper loss** (using $I_1 \approx I_2' + I_m$ — for this problem, we approximate using $I_2' = 30.30$ A as the dominant current):
$$P_{cu,stator} = 3 I_2'^2 R_1 = 3 \times 918.09 \times 0.5 = 1377.13 \text{ W}$$

**Input power:**
$$P_{input} = P_{shaft} + P_{cu,stator} + P_{core} + P_{cu,rotor} + P_{mech}$$
$$P_{input} = 30927.6 + 1377.13 + 600 + 1652.56 + 470.98 = 35028.3 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{input}} = \frac{30927.6}{35028.3} = 88.30\%$$

### ✅ **Final Consolidated Answer:**
$$\boxed{N_r = 1425 \text{ rpm}, \quad V_{th} = 388.34 \angle 0.556° \text{ V}, \quad I_2' = 30.30 \text{ A}, \quad P_{shaft} = 30.93 \text{ kW}, \quad \eta = 88.30\%}$$

---

## 📘 PROBLEM 5: Power-Flow Stage Deduction from Limited Data (Short, 5-Mark Type)

### **Question:**
A 3-phase induction motor running at slip **0.05** has an air-gap power of **40 kW**. Find:
1. Rotor copper loss
2. Mechanical power developed
3. Rotor speed if supply is **50 Hz, 4-pole**
4. Shaft torque (assume mechanical losses = 800 W)

### **Solution:**

#### 🔸 Step 1: Rotor Copper Loss
$$P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{gap} = 0.05 \times 40000 = 2000 \text{ W} = 2 \text{ kW}$$

#### 🔸 Step 2: Mechanical Power Developed
$$P_{m,dev} = (1-s) P_{gap} = 0.95 \times 40000 = 38000 \text{ W} = 38 \text{ kW}$$

#### 🔸 Step 3: Rotor Speed
$$N_s = 1500 \text{ rpm}, \quad N_r = (1-s) N_s = 0.95 \times 1500 = 1425 \text{ rpm}$$

#### 🔸 Step 4: Shaft Torque
$$P_{shaft} = P_{m,dev} - P_{mech} = 38000 - 800 = 37200 \text{ W}$$
$$\omega_r = \frac{2\pi \times 1425}{60} = 149.23 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$T_{shaft} = \frac{37200}{149.23} = 249.28 \text{ N·m}$$

### ✅ **Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{P_{cu,rotor} = 2 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{m,dev} = 38 \text{ kW}, \quad N_r = 1425 \text{ rpm}, \quad T_{shaft} = 249.28 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🔷 4.1 Power Flow Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

This is the **single most important diagram** for Module 4 numerical problems. Memorize the sequence of deductions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["P_in: Stator Input Power (3V_L I_L cos φ)"] -->|"Loss: P_cu,stator"| B["P_stator: Stator Output Power"]
    A -->|"Loss: P_core"| B
    B -->|"Air Gap Crossing"| C["P_gap: Air-Gap Power"]
    C -->|"Loss: P_cu,rotor = s·P_gap"| D["P_m,dev: Mechanical Power Developed"]
    D -->|"Loss: P_mech = P_friction + P_windage"| E["P_shaft: Shaft Output Power"]
    
    C -.->|"(1-s)·P_gap"| D
    D -.->|"T_shaft = P_shaft / ω_r"| E

```

> [!NOTE]
> **The two key "taxes" are coloured in yellow-orange. Everything to the right of P_gap is governed by the simple ratio $s : (1-s)$.**

## 🔷 4.2 Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1["V1 (Stator Phase Voltage)"] --> R1["R1: Stator Resistance"]
    R1 --> X1["jX1: Stator Leakage Reactance"]
    X1 --> node1{"Node A"}
    node1 -->|"jXm: Magnetizing Branch"| GND1["(GND)"]
    node1 --> X2["jX2': Rotor Leakage Reactance"]
    X2 --> node2{"Node B"}
    node2 -->|"R2'/s: Active Rotor Branch"| GND2["(GND)"]
    
    GND2 -.->|"R2' alone: P_cu,rotor"| NOTATION1["Carries rotor copper loss"]
    GND2 -.->|"R2'(1-s)/s split": P_m,dev"| NOTATION2["Carries developed mechanical power"]
    
    style V1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style X1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style X2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style node1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style node2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style NOTATION1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style NOTATION2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 🔷 4.3 Torque-Slip Characteristic (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Operating Region"] --> B["s = 0: Synchronous"]
    A --> C["0 < s < s_max: Stable Operating Zone"]
    A --> D["s = s_max = R2/X2: Maximum Torque"]
    A --> E["s_max < s < 1: Unstable Region"]
    A --> F["s = 1: Standstill (Starting Torque)"]
    
    B --> B1["T = 0, No relative motion"]
    C --> C1["T rises as (s/R2) for small s"]
    D --> D1["T_peak = 3E2²/(2ωs X2)"]
    E --> E1["T falls as 1/s for large s"]
    F --> F1["T_st = 3E2²R2/(ωs R2²+X2²)"]
    
    style A fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Point Insight:** An induction motor **always** operates in region **C (stable)**, between $s = 0$ and $s = s_{max}$. If a load pushes the slip beyond $s_{max}$ (region E), the motor will stall — this is called **pull-out** or **breakdown**.

## 🔷 4.4 Thevenin Equivalent Reduction (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original Circuit: V1 — R1 — jX1 — jXm shunt — jX2' — R2'/s"] --> B["Step 1: Reduce Stator to V_th, Z_th"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Rotor side becomes R2'/s + jX2'"]
    C --> D["Step 3: I2' = V_th / Z_th + R2'/s + jX2'"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Use I2' to find all powers and torques"]
    
    style A fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 🔷 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model 1]**
**Define slip of an induction motor. A 6-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor runs at 940 rpm. Calculate the slip and rotor frequency.**

**Model Answer (Concise):**
**Slip** is the fractional difference between synchronous speed and actual rotor speed:
$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

**Computation:**
- $N_s = (120 \times 50)/6 = 1000$ rpm
- $s = (1000 - 940)/1000 = 0.06$ pu
- $f_r = s f = 0.06 \times 50 = 3$ Hz

> **Marks Allocation:** Definition (1 mark) + Calculation (2 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model 1]**
**State the relationship between air-gap power, rotor copper loss, and mechanical power developed in a 3-phase induction motor.**

**Model Answer:**
If $P_{gap}$ is the air-gap power transferred to the rotor, then:
$$P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{gap}$$
$$P_{m,dev} = (1 - s) \cdot P_{gap}$$

These two quantities sum back to the air-gap power: $P_{cu,rotor} + P_{m,dev} = P_{gap}$ ✓

> **Marks Allocation:** Two equations (2 marks) + Verification statement (1 mark)

---

## 🔷 5.2 PART B Questions — 14 Marks (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from each module in the University Exam. Each carries **14 marks** with sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **📌 QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Pattern Model, Module 4]**

**(a)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has standstill rotor parameters (referred to stator) as: $R_2' = 0.2\ \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.0\ \Omega$, $E_2' = 100$ V/phase. Compute: **(7 Marks)**
   1. Slip for maximum torque
   2. Maximum torque developed
   3. Starting torque
   4. Ratio of starting torque to maximum torque

**(b)** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor draws **50 A at 0.85 p.f. lagging** from the supply. The stator losses total **3 kW**, and the rotor copper loss is **1.2 kW**. Friction and windage loss = **1 kW**. Calculate: **(7 Marks)**
   1. Air-gap power
   2. Mechanical power developed
   3. Shaft power
   4. Efficiency
   5. Rotor speed

#### **Model Solution for Q. A(a):**

**Step 1: Synchronous angular velocity**
$$N_s = (120 \times 50)/6 = 1000 \text{ rpm}, \quad \omega_s = (2\pi \times 1000)/60 = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Slip for maximum torque**
$$s_{max} = R_2'/X_2' = 0.2/1.0 = 0.2$$

**Step 3: Maximum torque**
$$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2'^2}{2 \omega_s X_2'} = \frac{3 \times 100^2}{2 \times 104.72 \times 1.0} = \frac{30000}{209.44} = 143.24 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Starting torque (s = 1)**
$$T_{st} = \frac{3 E_2'^2 R_2'}{\omega_s (R_2'^2 + X_2'^2)} = \frac{3 \times 100^2 \times 0.2}{104.72 \times (0.04 + 1)} = \frac{6000}{108.83} = 55.13 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Ratio**
$$T_{st}/T_{max} = 55.13/143.24 = 0.385 = 38.5\%$$

#### **Model Solution for Q. A(b):**

**Given:** $P_{input} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos \phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 50 \times 0.85 = 29,445.9$ W ≈ 29.45 kW

**Step 1: Air-gap power**
$P_{gap} = P_{input} - P_{stator\,losses} = 29,445.9 - 3000 = 26,445.9$ W

**Step 2: Slip** (from rotor copper loss)
$P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{gap} \Rightarrow s = P_{cu,rotor}/P_{gap} = 1200/26,445.9 = 0.0454$

**Step 3: Mechanical power developed**
$P_{m,dev} = (1-s) P_{gap} = (1 - 0.0454) \times 26,445.9 = 25,245.9$ W

**Step 4: Shaft power**
$P_{shaft} = P_{m,dev} - P_{mech} = 25,245.9 - 1000 = 24,245.9$ W ≈ 24.25 kW

**Step 5: Rotor speed**
$N_r = (1 - s) N_s = (1 - 0.0454) \times 1500 = 0.9546 \times 1500 = 1431.9$ rpm

**Step 6: Efficiency**
$\eta = P_{shaft}/P_{input} = 24,245.9/29,445.9 = 82.34\%$

---

### **📌 QUESTION B — Alternative Choice (Same 14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **torque developed** by a 3-phase induction motor, starting from the power crossing the air gap. Hence, show that the slip at maximum torque is $s_{max} = R_2/X_2$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz induction motor runs at **720 rpm** at full load. The rotor copper loss is **5% of the mechanical power developed**. Calculate: **(7 Marks)**
   1. Number of poles
   2. Slip
   3. Rotor copper loss as a percentage of input to the rotor
   4. If the mechanical losses are 1.5 kW, find the efficiency if input is 30 kW

#### **Model Solution for Q. B(a):**

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Air-gap power = 3 × (rotor current²) × (R₂'/s)
$$P_{gap} = 3 I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}$$

**Step 2:** Mechanical power developed = (1-s) × Air-gap power
$$P_{m,dev} = (1-s) P_{gap} = 3 I_2'^2 R_2' \cdot \frac{1-s}{s}$$

**Step 3:** Rotor current from the equivalent circuit:
$$I_2' = \frac{s E_2'}{\sqrt{R_2'^2 + (s X_2')^2}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute in torque equation $T = P_{m,dev}/\omega_s$:
$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + (s X_2')^2}$$

**Step 5:** To find $s_{max}$, differentiate $T$ with respect to $s$ and set to zero:

Let $k = (3 E_2'^2)/\omega_s$. Then $T = k \cdot s R_2' / (R_2'^2 + s^2 X_2'^2)$.

$$\frac{dT}{ds} = 0 \Rightarrow R_2'^2 + s^2 X_2'^2 = s \cdot 2 s X_2'^2 \Rightarrow R_2'^2 = s^2 X_2'^2$$

$$\therefore s_{max} = R_2'/X_2' \quad \blacksquare$$

#### **Model Solution for Q. B(b):**

**Step 1: Number of poles** — for 50 Hz and 720 rpm, poles must be such that $N_s$ slightly exceeds 720.
Trying P = 8: $N_s = 120 \times 50 / 8 = 750$ rpm. So $P = 8$ ✓

**Step 2: Slip**
$$s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s = (750 - 720)/750 = 0.04$$

**Step 3: Rotor copper loss as % of $P_{gap}$**
Given $P_{cu,rotor} = 0.05 \times P_{m,dev}$ and $P_{cu,rotor} = s P_{gap}$:
$$s P_{gap} = 0.05 (1-s) P_{gap} \Rightarrow s = 0.05(1-s) \Rightarrow s(1 + 0.05) = 0.05 \Rightarrow s = 0.05/1.05 = 0.0476$$

Since our computed slip is 0.04, this is **inconsistent**, meaning the data is approximate. For exam credit, use the **given slip = 0.04** for downstream calculations:
$$P_{cu,rotor}/P_{gap} = s = 0.04 = 4\%$$

**Step 4: Efficiency** — Using $s = 0.04$:
$P_{gap} = P_{input} - P_{stator\,losses}$. Since stator loss is not given, approximate $P_{gap} \approx P_{input} = 30$ kW (typical KTU assumption when not specified).

$P_{m,dev} = (1-s) P_{gap} = 0.96 \times 30,000 = 28,800$ W
$P_{shaft} = 28,800 - 1,500 = 27,300$ W
$\eta = 27,300/30,000 = 91\%$

---

## 🔷 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Numericals**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $N_s$ from rpm to rad/s** in torque equations → lose **2 marks** per such instance. Always write $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$ explicitly.
> 2. **Using line voltage in single-phase equivalent circuit** of a star-connected motor → Should be $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$. For delta, $V_{ph} = V_L$. This mistake costs **3 marks**.
> 3. **Computing $T_{shaft}$ using $\omega_s$ instead of $\omega_r$** → Output torque is at rotor speed, not synchronous. Subtract mechanical losses from $P_{m,dev}$ before dividing.
> 4. **Mixing up the "developed torque" and "shaft torque"** → $T_{dev} = P_{m,dev}/\omega_s$ (note: $P_{m,dev}/\omega_r$ also works), $T_{shaft} = P_{shaft}/\omega_r$.
> 5. **Not stating units in the final answer** → KTU examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for "N·m" missing.
> 6. **Conflating per-phase and total 3-phase power** → Air-gap, rotor copper, etc. are **3-phase total** powers; multiply per-phase values by 3.
> 7. **Calculating efficiency incorrectly when stator losses are given** → Use the full power balance, not just the rotor side.

---

## 🔷 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save this for exam day)**

- ✅ **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$. Always between 0 and 1.
- ✅ **Rotor frequency** $f_r = s f$. Never equal to $f$ except at standstill.
- ✅ **Rotor EMF** $E_{2s} = s E_{2(standstill)}$. Linear in slip.
- ✅ **The Air-Gap Power Theorem:** $P_{cu,rotor} : P_{m,dev} = s : (1-s)$. This is the **single most powerful identity** in Module 4.
- ✅ **Maximum torque slip** $s_{max} = R_2/X_2$. Independent of load and supply voltage.
- ✅ **Maximum torque** $T_{max} = 3 E_2^2 / (2 \omega_s X_2)$. Independent of $R_2$.
- ✅ **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f/P$. For 50 Hz: 2-pole→3000, 4-pole→1500, 6-pole→1000, 8-pole→750, 10-pole→600, 12-pole→500 rpm.
- ✅ **For torque calculation:** always use $\omega_s$, not $\omega_r$.
- ✅ **For shaft power:** always use $\omega_r$ in denominator, and subtract mechanical losses.
- ✅ **3-phase power** = $\sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos \phi$ (line values) or $3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos \phi$ (per-phase values).
- ✅ **Thevenin simplification** is mandatory for problems asking for $T_{max}$, $T_{st}$, or torque at arbitrary $s$ when $R_1$, $X_1$, and $X_m$ are given.
- ✅ **Two equivalent forms** of rotor branch: $R_2'/s$ (compact) or $R_2' + R_2'(1-s)/s$ (split, for power identification).
- ✅ **Thevenin resistance and reactance are typically small** ($R_{th} < 1\ \Omega$, $X_{th} \approx X_1$ when $X_m \gg X_1$).
- ✅ **Practical slip range:** 2–8% at full load for standard cage motors. If given $s > 0.1$ in a problem, treat it as a low-load or starting condition.
- ✅ **Number of poles** must be an even integer (2, 4, 6, 8, ...). For 50 Hz, synchronous speeds are restricted to the sequence 3000, 1500, 1000, 750, 600, 500, ...
- ✅ **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked for "rotor copper loss as a percentage of input power," remember it is $s \times P_{gap}/P_{input}$, NOT just $s$ — this distinction is often tested.

---

**🎯 END OF MODULE 4 — NUMERICAL PROBLEMS | KTU 2024 SCHEME | PCEOT402**
