topics.title: Phasor Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Phasor Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Phasor Diagram (KTU 2024 Definition):** A phasor diagram is a **two-dimensional vector representation** of sinusoidal alternating quantities (voltages and currents) in an AC electrical machine, drawn in the complex plane using rotating vectors of **fixed magnitude** rotating at the **synchronous angular velocity** $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ rad/s. At any instant, the projection of each rotating phasor on the vertical axis gives the instantaneous value of that quantity.

In the context of **Synchronous Machines** (Module 4 focus), the phasor diagram consolidates the **Internal Generated EMF ($E_f$)**, the **Terminal Voltage ($V$)**, the **Armature Current ($I_a$)**, and the synchronous reactance drops into a single closed vector polygon governed by the machine's internal voltage equation.

### 1.2 The Governing Voltage Equation

The complete steady-state voltage equation for a synchronous machine is:

$$E_f = V + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s$$

Where every quantity is a **phasor** (complex number) and not a simple magnitude. The relative angular displacement between $E_f$ and $V$ is called the **power angle** or **load angle**, denoted by $\delta$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tug-of-War" Picture

Imagine two teams pulling a rope:
- **Team A (Internal EMF $E_f$)** represents the magnetic field created by the **rotor** (field winding excited by DC).
- **Team B (Terminal Voltage $V$)** represents the **external load** the machine is supplying.
- **The rope's tension and angle** represent the **phasor relationship** between $E_f$ and $V$.
- The **angle the rope makes with the horizontal** is the **load angle $\delta$** — the bigger the load, the more the rope tilts.

> [!NOTE]  
> If the two teams pull with equal force in the **same direction** ($\delta = 0°$), the machine is at **no-load**. As the load increases, Team A (rotor field) must tilt backward relative to the load, creating $\delta$ to deliver more power. The phasor diagram captures this "tilt" geometrically.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Conventions

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Convention:** The terminal voltage $V$ is **always taken as the reference phasor** (along the positive real axis). All other phasors ($E_f$, $I_a$, and reactance drops) are drawn **relative to $V$** based on the operating power factor.

- **Power Factor Angle $\phi$:** Angle between $V$ and $I_a$.
  - **Lagging pf** → $I_a$ drawn **clockwise** from $V$ (inductive load).
  - **Leading pf** → $I_a$ drawn **counter-clockwise** from $V$ (capacitive load).
- **Load Angle $\delta$:** Angle between $V$ and $E_f$ (always positive for generator action, $E_f$ leads $V$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why We Need a Phasor Diagram

The phasor diagram is **not optional** in KTU examinations — it is the **primary visual proof** of your understanding of machine behavior. It is used to:

1. **Determine the excitation voltage** $E_f$ for a given load and power factor.
2. **Compute the voltage regulation** of a synchronous generator.
3. **Analyze the V-curve and inverted V-curve** behavior of synchronous machines.
4. **Visualize the power angle** $\delta$ for stability and power flow studies.
5. **Apply the two-reaction theory** for salient-pole machines.

## 2.2 The Two Machine Models

### A. Cylindrical Rotor (Round Rotor) Model
Used for **turbo-alternators** (high-speed, 2-pole or 4-pole). The air gap is uniform, so the synchronous reactance is a **single scalar value** $X_s$.

$$E_f = V + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s$$

### B. Salient Pole Rotor Model
Used for **hydro-generators** (low-speed, multi-pole). The air gap is non-uniform, so we use the **Blondel's Two-Reaction Theory** with **direct-axis reactance $X_d$** and **quadrature-axis reactance $X_q$**.

$$E_f = V + I_a R_a + j I_d X_d + j I_q X_q$$

Where:
- $I_d = I_a \sin(\psi)$ — direct-axis component
- $I_q = I_a \cos(\psi)$ — quadrature-axis component
- $\psi = \delta + \phi$ — the internal angle between $E_f$ and $I_a$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Use of Phasor Diagram |
|-------------|----------------------|
| Power Grid Operation | Real-time dispatch of reactive power (VAr) from generators |
| Excitation System Design | Setting the automatic voltage regulator (AVR) setpoint |
| Stability Analysis | Computing the steady-state stability limit $P_{max} = \frac{E_f V}{X_s}$ |
| Transformer Vector Group | Identifying phase shift between primary and secondary |
| Protection Relay Coordination | Determining directional overcurrent settings |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Module 4)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Applicable Machine |
|---|---------|----------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $E_f = V + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s$ | Cylindrical rotor EMF equation | Round rotor Gen/Motor |
| 2 | $E_f^2 = (V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi \pm I_a X_s)^2$ | Magnitude of $E_f$ from terminal quantities | Round rotor (lag −, lead +) |
| 3 | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{V\sin\phi \pm I_a X_s}{V\cos\phi + I_a R_a}$ | Load angle from triangle geometry | Round rotor |
| 4 | $E_f = V + I_a R_a + j I_d X_d + j I_q X_q$ | Salient pole EMF equation | Salient pole Gen/Motor |
| 5 | $I_d = I_a \sin\psi,\quad I_q = I_a \cos\psi$ | Two-reaction decomposition | Salient pole |
| 6 | $\psi = \delta + \phi$ | Internal angle relation | Salient pole |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{E_f V}{X_s}\sin\delta$ | Power-angle equation (cylindrical) | Cylindrical rotor |
| 8 | $P = \dfrac{E_f V}{X_d}\sin\delta + \dfrac{V^2}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{X_q} - \dfrac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin 2\delta$ | Power-angle equation (salient) | Salient pole |
| 9 | $\text{VR}\% = \dfrac{\vert E_0\vert - \vert V_{fl}\vert}{\vert V_{fl}\vert} \times 100$ | Voltage Regulation | Synchronous Generator |
| 10 | $V = E_f - I_a(R_a + j X_s)$ | Synchronous Motor EMF equation | Synchronous Motor |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Sign Convention Rule for Reactive Drop:** In the magnitude formula (Row 2), use the **MINUS sign** for **lagging power factor** (current lags voltage, inductive load) and the **PLUS sign** for **leading power factor** (current leads voltage, capacitive load).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cylindrical Rotor Generator EMF Equation

We start from **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** applied to the synchronous machine stator winding:

$$e = N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt} = -\omega_s N \phi_m \sin(\omega_s t)$$

The RMS phasor representation of this internally induced EMF is $E_f$. Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** to the stator armature circuit, going from the generated EMF source through the armature impedance to the terminals:

**Step 1: Write the per-phase equivalent circuit equation**

The synchronous machine is modeled as an EMF source $E_f$ in series with $R_a$ and $X_s$, connected to terminal voltage $V$.

$$E_f = V + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s$$

**Step 2: Resolve into rectangular components**

Let $V = V\angle 0°$ (reference) and $I_a = I_a\angle -\phi$ (lagging pf).

The resistive drop $I_a R_a$ is **parallel** to $I_a$:
$$I_a R_a = I_a R_a (\cos\phi - j\sin\phi)$$

The reactive drop $jI_a X_s$ **leads** $I_a$ by exactly $90°$:
$$j I_a X_s = I_a X_s (\cos(\phi - 90°) + j\sin(\phi - 90°))$$
$$j I_a X_s = I_a X_s (\sin\phi + j\cos\phi)$$

**Step 3: Sum the components**

$$E_f = V + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi + j(I_a X_s \cos\phi - I_a R_a \sin\phi)$$

**Step 4: Separate real and imaginary parts**

$$\text{Re}(E_f) = V + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi$$
$$\text{Im}(E_f) = I_a X_s \cos\phi - I_a R_a \sin\phi$$

**Step 5: Compute the magnitude of $E_f$**

Using $\vert E_f \vert^2 = [\text{Re}(E_f)]^2 + [\text{Im}(E_f)]^2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_f^2 &= (V\cos 0° + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi)^2 \\
&\quad + (I_a X_s \cos\phi - I_a R_a \sin\phi)^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Expand using $(a+b+c)^2 + (d-e)^2$ identity**

Recognizing that $V\cos 0° = V$, after algebraic expansion (shown grouped):

$$\begin{aligned}
E_f^2 &= (V + I_a R_a \cos\phi)^2 + (I_a X_s \sin\phi)^2 + 2(V + I_a R_a \cos\phi)(I_a X_s \sin\phi) \\
&\quad + (I_a X_s \cos\phi)^2 + (I_a R_a \sin\phi)^2 - 2(I_a X_s \cos\phi)(I_a R_a \sin\phi)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Cancel cross-product terms using $\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi = 1$**

The mixed terms $+2V \cdot I_a X_s \sin\phi$ and $-2 V \cdot I_a R_a \sin\phi$ combine, but after regrouping by collecting $V$ terms:

$$E_f^2 = (V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2$$

For **lagging pf** ($\sin\phi > 0$): $\quad E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$

For **leading pf** ($\sin\phi < 0$, drop the negative sign): $\quad E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi - I_a X_s)^2}$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Final Boxed Result (Lagging Power Factor):**
> $$\boxed{E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}}$$

**Step 8: Compute the load angle $\delta$**

$$\tan\delta = \dfrac{V\sin\phi + I_a X_s}{V\cos\phi + I_a R_a}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Two-Reaction Theory (Salient Pole)

**Step 1: Set up the coordinate system**

Place the **direct axis (d-axis)** along the rotor pole axis (along $E_f$). The **quadrature axis (q-axis)** is $90°$ ahead in the direction of rotation.

**Step 2: Decompose the armature current**

Let $\psi$ be the angle between $E_f$ and $I_a$. Then:

$$I_d = I_a \sin\psi \quad \text{(component along d-axis, magnetizing/demagnetizing)}$$
$$I_q = I_a \cos\psi \quad \text{(component along q-axis, perpendicular to d-axis)}$$

**Step 3: Determine the angle $\psi$**

From the geometry of the phasor diagram (where $E_f$ leads $V$ by $\delta$, and $I_a$ lags $V$ by $\phi$):

$$\psi = \delta + \phi$$

**Step 4: Apply the voltage equation along each axis**

Along d-axis: $\quad V_d = E_f - I_d X_d - I_a R_a$ (approximately, neglecting $R_a$ drop along d-axis initially)

The complete KVL gives:

$$E_f = V + I_a R_a + j I_d X_d + j I_q X_q$$

**Step 5: Write the power equation by taking the dot product**

Multiplying both sides by the conjugate of $I_a$ and taking the real part:

$$P = \text{Re}(V \cdot I_a^*) = \text{Re}(E_f I_a^*) - I_a^2 R_a$$

Neglecting $R_a$ and substituting $I_d, I_q$ in terms of $\delta$ and $\psi = \delta + \phi$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{E_f V}{X_d}\sin\delta + \frac{V^2}{2}\left(\frac{X_d - X_q}{X_d X_q}\right)\sin 2\delta
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Final Boxed Salient Pole Power Equation:**
> $$\boxed{P = \frac{E_f V}{X_d}\sin\delta + \frac{V^2 (X_d - X_q)}{2 X_d X_q}\sin 2\delta}$$

The **first term** is the **electromagnetic power** (same as cylindrical rotor). The **second term** is the **reluctance power**, which exists **even without field excitation** ($E_f = 0$) due to the non-uniform air gap.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A **3-phase, 5 kVA, 220 V, 50 Hz**, Y-connected synchronous generator has $R_a = 0.4 \, \Omega$ and $X_s = 6 \, \Omega$ per phase. Find $E_f$ and $\delta$ when delivering rated load at **0.8 lagging power factor**.

**Given Data**

$$V_{ph} = \frac{220}{\sqrt{3}} = 127.02 \text{ V}, \quad I_a = \frac{5000}{\sqrt{3} \times 220} = 13.12 \text{ A}, \quad \cos\phi = 0.8 \Rightarrow \sin\phi = 0.6$$

**Step A: Compute the horizontal component (real axis)**

$$V\cos\phi + I_a R_a = 127.02 \times 0.8 + 13.12 \times 0.4 = 101.62 + 5.25 = 106.87 \text{ V}$$

**Step B: Compute the vertical component (imaginary axis)**

$$V\sin\phi + I_a X_s = 127.02 \times 0.6 + 13.12 \times 6 = 76.21 + 78.72 = 154.93 \text{ V}$$

**Step C: Compute $E_f$**

$$E_f = \sqrt{106.87^2 + 154.93^2} = \sqrt{11421 + 24003} = \sqrt{35424} = 188.21 \text{ V (per phase)}$$

**Step D: Compute the load angle $\delta$**

$$\tan\delta = \frac{154.93}{106.87} = 1.4496 \Rightarrow \delta = \tan^{-1}(1.4496) = 55.4°$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Interpretation:** The internal EMF $E_f$ (**188.21 V**) is significantly higher than the terminal voltage $V$ (**127.02 V**) because the synchronous reactance causes a large voltage drop. The load angle is **55.4°**, which is **extremely high** — typical operation requires $\delta < 30°$ for stable operation. This indicates the machine is heavily loaded or has a poorly designed $X_s$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Construction Sequence of a Cylindrical Rotor Phasor Diagram (Lagging pf)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: V as Reference Phasor] --> stepB[Draw V along positive real axis at angle 0]
    stepB --> stepC{Determine Power Factor}
    stepC -->|Lagging Inductive| stepD[Draw Ia at angle phi CLOCKWISE from V]
    stepC -->|Leading Capacitive| stepE[Draw Ia at angle phi COUNTERCLOCKWISE from V]
    stepD --> stepF[Draw IaRa vector PARALLEL to Ia from origin of V]
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG[Draw jIaXs vector PERPENDICULAR LEADING Ia by 90 deg]
    stepG --> stepH[Add jIaXs tip-to-tail from IaRa end point]
    stepH --> stepI[Draw Ef from origin V to final tip of jIaXs]
    stepI --> stepJ[Mark load angle delta between V and Ef]
    stepJ --> endK[Completed Phasor Diagram]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Power Flow Through a Synchronous Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mechA[Mechanical Power Input Pmech] --> shaftB[Shaft Torque Tm]
    shaftB --> couplingC[Rotating Rotor Field]
    couplingC --> fieldD[Field Winding Excited by DC Current If]
    fieldD --> airgapE[Air Gap Flux Phi]
    airgapE --> statorF[Stator Armature Winding]
    statorF --> emfG[Induced EMF Ef at angle delta to V]
    emfG --> terminalH[Terminal Voltage V delivered to Load]
    terminalH --> loadI[Electrical Load at Power Factor cos phi]
    loadI --> currentJ[Armature Current Ia flows]
    currentJ --> reactdropK[Voltage Drop IaRa plus jIaXs]
    reactdropK --> completeL[Closed Vector Loop KVL Satisfied]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Two-Reaction Theory Decomposition for Salient Pole

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startX[Input: Ia phasor at angle phi from V] --> angleY[Compute internal angle psi equals delta plus phi]
    angleY --> decompZ[Decompose Ia into orthogonal components]
    decompZ --> compId[Id equals Ia sin psi ALONG d axis]
    decompZ --> compIq[Iq equals Ia cos psi ALONG q axis]
    compId --> xdrop1[Voltage drop jIdXd along d axis]
    compIq --> xdrop2[Voltage drop jIqXq along q axis]
    xdrop1 --> sumup[Vector Sum with V and IaRa]
    xdrop2 --> sumup
    sumup --> resultEf[Output Ef phasor with two reactance contributions]
```

## 4.4 Equivalent Circuit Representation (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Ef Internal EMF Source] --> ra1[Ra Armature Resistance]
    ra1 --> xs1[jXs Synchronous Reactance]
    xs1 --> term1[V Terminal Voltage]
    term1 --> load1[External Load ZL at Power Factor cos phi]
    load1 --> src1
    note1[For Salient Pole: Replace jXs with jXd in d branch and jXq in q branch in parallel]
    xs1 -.-> note1
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU Exam):** Always label every phasor with its symbol, **angle**, and **magnitude**. Examiners award partial credit for correctly drawn arrows even if numerical values are slightly off.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define a phasor diagram. Why is the terminal voltage $V$ taken as the reference phasor in synchronous machine analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **phasor diagram** is a graphical representation of sinusoidal AC quantities (voltages and currents) in the complex plane as vectors of fixed magnitude rotating at synchronous angular velocity $\omega_s = 2\pi f$. The instantaneous value of any quantity is the projection of its phasor on the vertical axis.

The terminal voltage $V$ is taken as the reference phasor (angle = $0°$) because:

1. **Practical Measurability:** $V$ can be directly measured at the machine terminals using a voltmeter, making it the most accessible reference.
2. **Load Specification:** The load is characterized by its power factor angle $\phi$ relative to $V$, so the current $I_a$ is naturally drawn at angle $\phi$ from $V$.
3. **Standard Convention:** KTU, IEEE, and IEC standards all prescribe $V$ as the reference to ensure uniformity in phasor diagram interpretation across all textbooks and references.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between the phasor diagrams of a cylindrical rotor and salient pole synchronous generator. What is the role of the direct-axis and quadrature-axis reactances?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Cylindrical Rotor | Salient Pole Rotor |
|---------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Air Gap | Uniform | Non-uniform (small along d-axis, large along q-axis) |
| Reactance | Single value $X_s$ | Two values: $X_d$ (direct-axis) and $X_q$ (quadrature-axis) |
| EMF Equation | $E_f = V + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s$ | $E_f = V + I_a R_a + j I_d X_d + j I_q X_q$ |
| Theory Used | Simple KVL | Blondel's Two-Reaction Theory |
| Application | Turbo-alternators | Hydro-generators |

**Role of $X_d$ and $X_q$:**
- $X_d$ is the reactance offered to the **direct-axis component** $I_d$ of armature current, which flows along the **pole axis** (path of minimum reluctance).
- $X_q$ is the reactance offered to the **quadrature-axis component** $I_q$, which flows **perpendicular** to the pole axis (path of maximum reluctance).
- Since $X_d > X_q$, the salient pole machine produces **reluctance power** in addition to electromagnetic power.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** A 3-phase, **10 kVA, 400 V, 50 Hz**, star-connected synchronous generator has synchronous reactance $X_s = 12 \, \Omega$ and negligible armature resistance. Determine the induced EMF $E_f$ and the load angle $\delta$ when the generator delivers full-load current at:
- **(i)** Unity power factor
- **(ii)** 0.8 lagging power factor

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$V_L = 400 \text{ V} \Rightarrow V_{ph} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V}$$
$$I_a = \frac{10000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400} = 14.43 \text{ A}, \quad R_a \approx 0$$

**Part (i) Unity Power Factor ($\phi = 0°$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_f &= \sqrt{(V\cos 0° + 0)^2 + (V\sin 0° + I_a X_s)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(230.94)^2 + (0 + 14.43 \times 12)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{53333 + 30013} = \sqrt{83346} = 288.7 \text{ V (per phase)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\tan\delta = \frac{I_a X_s}{V} = \frac{173.16}{230.94} = 0.75 \Rightarrow \delta = 36.87°$$

**Part (ii) 0.8 Lagging Power Factor ($\cos\phi = 0.8, \sin\phi = 0.6$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_f &= \sqrt{(V\cos\phi)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(230.94 \times 0.8)^2 + (230.94 \times 0.6 + 14.43 \times 12)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(184.75)^2 + (138.56 + 173.16)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{34133 + (311.72)^2} = \sqrt{34133 + 97169} = \sqrt{131302} = 362.4 \text{ V (per phase)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\tan\delta = \frac{V\sin\phi + I_a X_s}{V\cos\phi} = \frac{311.72}{184.75} = 1.687 \Rightarrow \delta = 59.3°$$

**Q (b) [7 marks]:** Draw the phasor diagram for the **0.8 lagging pf** case and explain how the load angle $\delta$ varies with the power factor of the load.

**Model Solution (Phasor Diagram Description):**

```
                                    E_f (362.4 V, δ = 59.3°)
                                   /|
                                  / |
                                 /  | jIaXs (173.16 V)
                                /   |  (perpendicular to Ia)
                               /    |
                              /  ___|__________
                             /  |   |         
                            /   |   |IaRa ≈ 0
                           /    |   |         
                          /     |   |         (Ia at 14.43 A, lagging V by 36.87°)
                         /δ     |   |________Ia
                        /_______|____________________________
                                  V (230.94 V, reference)
```

**Explanation of load angle variation with pf:**

1. **Unity pf:** $\delta$ is small because the reactive component of the current is zero. Only the perpendicular drop $I_a X_s$ exists, giving a moderate $\delta$.
2. **Lagging pf:** $\delta$ **increases significantly** because the reactive component $I_a \sin\phi$ adds constructively to the $I_a X_s$ drop. The vertical leg becomes much larger, tilting $E_f$ further ahead.
3. **Leading pf:** $\delta$ **decreases** (and may even become negative, making $E_f$ lag $V$ — underexcited operation) because $I_a \sin\phi$ subtracts from the $I_a X_s$ drop, reducing the vertical leg.
4. **At zero pf leading (purely capacitive):** $E_f$ may fall below $V$, indicating the machine acts as a **synchronous condenser** absorbing reactive power.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** A salient pole synchronous generator has $X_d = 0.9$ pu and $X_q = 0.6$ pu. It supplies a load of $1.0$ pu at $0.8$ power factor lagging to an infinite bus of $1.0$ pu voltage. Compute the excitation EMF $E_f$ and the load angle $\delta$. Neglect armature resistance.

**Model Solution:**

**Given (per unit):**
$$V = 1.0, \quad I_a = 1.0, \quad \cos\phi = 0.8, \quad \sin\phi = 0.6$$
$$X_d = 0.9, \quad X_q = 0.6, \quad R_a = 0$$

**Step 1: Take $V$ as reference and find the power angle relationship**

$$P = V I_a \cos\phi = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.8 = 0.8 \text{ pu}$$
$$Q = V I_a \sin\phi = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.6 = 0.6 \text{ pu (lagging)}$$

**Step 2: Apply the two-reaction EMF equation**

The complex power relation gives:
$$E_f = V + j I_d X_d + j I_q X_q$$

For small $\delta$ approximation (or solving iteratively):

**Step 3: Use the direct and quadrature axis decomposition**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_f \sin\delta &= I_q X_q \\
E_f \cos\delta &= V + I_d X_d
\end{aligned}$$

where $I_d = I_a \sin(\delta + \phi)$ and $I_q = I_a \cos(\delta + \phi)$.

**Step 4: Solve iteratively**

Assume $\delta = 18°$ as first guess:
$$\psi = 18° + 36.87° = 54.87°$$
$$I_d = 1.0 \times \sin(54.87°) = 0.818$$
$$I_q = 1.0 \times \cos(54.87°) = 0.575$$

$$E_f \sin\delta = 0.575 \times 0.6 = 0.345$$
$$E_f \cos\delta = 1.0 + 0.818 \times 0.9 = 1.736$$

$$E_f = \sqrt{0.345^2 + 1.736^2} = \sqrt{0.119 + 3.014} = 1.77 \text{ pu}$$
$$\delta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.345}{1.736}\right) = 11.25°$$

Re-iterating with $\delta = 11.25°$:
$$\psi = 11.25° + 36.87° = 48.12°$$
$$I_d = \sin(48.12°) = 0.745, \quad I_q = \cos(48.12°) = 0.667$$

$$E_f \sin\delta = 0.667 \times 0.6 = 0.400$$
$$E_f \cos\delta = 1.0 + 0.745 \times 0.9 = 1.671$$

$$E_f = \sqrt{0.400^2 + 1.671^2} = \sqrt{0.16 + 2.79} = 1.718 \text{ pu}$$
$$\delta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.400}{1.671}\right) = 13.46°$$

**Converged Answer:**
$$\boxed{E_f = 1.72 \text{ pu}, \quad \delta = 13.5°}$$

**Q (b) [7 marks]:** Explain the concept of **reluctance power** in a salient pole machine. Under what conditions does the reluctance power become zero?

**Model Answer:**

**Reluctance Power Definition:**
The reluctance power is the **second harmonic power term** in the salient pole power equation:

$$P_{rel} = \frac{V^2 (X_d - X_q)}{2 X_d X_q} \sin 2\delta$$

It arises because the **non-uniform air gap** of the salient pole machine creates a **variable reluctance** path for the magnetic flux. As the rotor rotates, the stator flux linkage changes even with a **constant amplitude rotating MMF**, producing an additional torque component.

**Key Properties:**

1. **Exists without field excitation:** $P_{rel}$ is independent of $E_f$, so it is present even when the field current is **zero** ($I_f = 0$).
2. **Vanishes in cylindrical rotor machines:** Since $X_d = X_q$ for a uniform air gap, $P_{rel} = 0$.
3. **Has double frequency variation:** $P_{rel} \propto \sin 2\delta$ — it has a periodicity of $180°$ instead of $360°$.
4. **Maximum at $\delta = 45°$:** The $\sin 2\delta$ term peaks at $\delta = 45°$.

**Conditions for Reluctance Power = 0:**

1. **Cylindrical rotor construction** (uniform air gap, $X_d = X_q$).
2. **Load angle $\delta = 0°$** or $\delta = 90°$ (i.e., no relative angular displacement between stator MMF and rotor pole).
3. **No terminal voltage** ($V = 0$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Error in the EMF Magnitude Formula (≈ 3 marks lost per question):** Students often write $V\sin\phi - I_a X_s$ for **lagging pf**, but the correct form is $V\sin\phi + I_a X_s$. The minus sign is only for **leading pf**. Always re-verify with a phasor diagram.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert Line Values to Phase Values (≈ 2 marks):** For a star-connected machine, $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$ and $I_{ph} = I_L$. Using line voltage directly in a per-phase phasor equation is the **#1 mistake** in KTU scripts.
> 
> 3. **Wrong Angle Reference (≈ 2 marks):** The load angle $\delta$ is between $E_f$ and $V$, **not** between $I_a$ and $V$. The latter is $\phi$. Confusing these two angles is a frequent error.
> 
> 4. **Drawing the Phasor Diagram Without Arrows (≈ 1 mark):** KTU examiners expect **directional arrowheads** on every phasor and **angle markings** with arc symbols. A bare triangle without arrows gets partial credit only.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the Salient Pole Two-Reaction Theory (≈ 4 marks):** For a **hydro-generator**, applying the cylindrical rotor formula $E_f = V + jI_a X_s$ is **structurally wrong**. Always check whether $X_d \ne X_q$ is given.
> 
> 6. **No Units in Final Answer (≈ 1 mark):** Always write $E_f$ in **volts (per phase)** or **line-to-line kV**, with the unit clearly stated. A bare number is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Phasor Diagram):**
> 
> - **Reference Convention:** $V$ is the reference phasor, drawn along the positive real axis. This is **non-negotiable** in KTU exams.
> - **Lagging pf →** $I_a$ is drawn **clockwise** from $V$ by angle $\phi$. $I_a X_s$ drop leads $I_a$ by $90°$ and adds to the imaginary component.
> - **Leading pf →** $I_a$ is drawn **counter-clockwise** from $V$ by angle $\phi$. The vertical component uses a **minus sign**: $V\sin\phi - I_a X_s$.
> - **Cylindrical Rotor Equation:** $E_f = V + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s$ (single reactance).
> - **Salient Pole Equation:** $E_f = V + I_a R_a + j I_d X_d + j I_q X_q$ (two reactances, two-reaction theory).
> - **Magnitude Formula (Lagging):** $E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$
> - **Magnitude Formula (Leading):** $E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi - I_a X_s)^2}$
> - **Load Angle:** $\delta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{V\sin\phi \pm I_a X_s}{V\cos\phi + I_a R_a}\right)$ — this is the angle between $E_f$ and $V$, **not** between $I_a$ and $V$.
> - **Power-Angle Equation (Cylindrical):** $P = \dfrac{E_f V}{X_s}\sin\delta$ — pure sinusoidal, max at $\delta = 90°$.
> - **Power-Angle Equation (Salient):** $P = \dfrac{E_f V}{X_d}\sin\delta + \dfrac{V^2 (X_d - X_q)}{2 X_d X_q}\sin 2\delta$ — has the **reluctance power** term.
> - **Two-Reaction Decomposition:** $I_d = I_a \sin\psi$, $I_q = I_a \cos\psi$, where $\psi = \delta + \phi$.
> - **Phase vs Line Conversion:** Star: $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$, $I_{ph} = I_L$. Delta: $V_{ph} = V_L$, $I_{ph} = I_L/\sqrt{3}$.
> - **Synchronous Motor Phasor Diagram:** The equation is **reversed** — $V = E_f + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s$ (motor convention), so $E_f$ is **smaller** than $V$ for normal operation.
> - **Voltage Regulation:** $\text{VR}\% = \dfrac{\vert E_0 \vert - \vert V_{fl} \vert}{\vert V_{fl} \vert} \times 100$ — found by comparing no-load and full-load phasor diagrams.
> - **Stability Limit:** Maximum power transfer occurs at $\delta = 90°$ (cylindrical) or slightly less (salient, due to the $\sin 2\delta$ term adding then subtracting).
> - **Drawing Checklist for Exams:** Use a **sharp pencil and ruler**, label **every phasor** with its symbol, **mark angles** with arc symbols ($\phi$, $\delta$), and add **arrowheads** to show direction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalent Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Equivalent Circuit # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Equivalent Circuit of a Three-Phase Induction Motor** is an *electrical network analogue* that represents the electromechanical energy conversion process of the machine on a *per-phase* basis. It maps the distributed magnetic coupling between the stator and the rotor windings into a single-phase lumped-parameter circuit, in which all rotor quantities (resistance, leakage reactance, and induced EMF) are mathematically **referred to the stator side** through the effective turns ratio. The circuit faithfully reproduces the stator copper loss, core (iron) loss, rotor copper loss, mechanical (friction & windage) loss, and the developed mechanical power output. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The rotor resistance appearing in the equivalent circuit is **NOT** a fixed value — it becomes $R_2' / s$, where $s$ is the **slip**. This slip-dependence is what makes the circuit model the conversion of *electrical* energy into *mechanical* energy. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **water wheel connected to a water pump** through a pipe with a constriction (representing the air gap). | Real Machine Part | Water Analogy | |---|---| | Stator supply (3-phase, 50 Hz) | Water pump pushing water at a constant rate | | Air gap (magnetic coupling) | Pipe with constriction | | Rotor (short-circuited conductors) | Water wheel with fins | | Rotor resistance $R_2$ | Friction on the wheel axle | | Slip $s$ | How much the wheel *slips* relative to the water flow | | Mechanical load | A bucket being lifted by the wheel | When the rotor is **stationary** ($s=1$), all the water energy is dissipated as heat (rotor copper loss) — the wheel can't lift anything. When the rotor **runs at synchronous speed** ($s=0$), the wheel spins as fast as the water flow — no relative motion, so no current, no torque, but also no power transfer. Somewhere *between* these extremes, the wheel *slips* slightly, and the friction (resistance) is "seen" by the water as a *converted mechanical load* equivalent to $R_2 (1-s)/s$. This slipping-friction-to-mechanical-power conversion is *exactly* what the slip-dependent resistance $R_2'/s$ does in the equivalent circuit. > [!NOTE] > **The "magic" of $R_2'/s$:** Mathematically, $R_2'/s = R_2' + R_2'(1-s)/s$. The first term represents true rotor copper loss (heat). The second term is a *fictitious* resistance that represents the *mechanical power developed* per phase. The motor "tricks" the circuit into believing it is dissipating power, but that "dissipation" is actually useful mechanical work. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following constants and standard values are essential for all KTU numerical problems on this topic: - **Supply frequency in India:** $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ - **Synchronous speed formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ (where $P$ = number of poles) - **Slip range for normal operation:** $0 < s < 1$ (motor mode) - **Standard per-phase analysis basis** in KTU problems # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why We Need an Equivalent Circuit The induction motor has *no physical electrical connection* between stator and rotor. The only link is the **rotating magnetic flux** in the air gap. To apply standard AC circuit analysis (Kirchhoff's laws, phasor diagrams, Thevenin's theorem), we need a *lumped* circuit that: 1. Refers the rotor's electrical parameters to the stator's reference frame. 2. Captures the frequency difference (rotor frequency = $sf$) inside the circuit itself. 3. Models the *slip-dependent* power conversion elegantly. ## 2.2 Building the Equivalent Circuit — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — Start with the physical stator winding** Apply per-phase stator voltage $V_1$. Current $I_1$ flows through stator resistance $R_1$ and leakage reactance $X_1$, producing the air-gap EMF $E_1$. $$V_1 = E_1 + I_1 (R_1 + jX_1)$$ **Step 2 — Add the magnetizing branch** The rotating flux $\Phi$ is set up by the *magnetizing current* $I_\phi$, which is supplied through the magnetizing reactance $X_m$ in parallel with the core-loss resistance $R_c$. $$I_0 = I_1 - I_2' \quad \text{and} \quad I_0 = I_c + I_\phi = \dfrac{E_1}{R_c} + \dfrac{E_1}{jX_m}$$ **Step 3 — Model the rotor side** The rotor EMF $E_2$ has frequency $sf$ and rotor leakage reactance is $X_2 = 2 \pi (sf) L_2$. To bring everything to stator frequency $f$, we *transform* rotor quantities to the stator side. **Step 4 — Refer rotor quantities to stator (turns ratio $a = N_1/N_2$)** $$E_2' = a E_2, \quad R_2' = a^2 R_2, \quad X_2' = a^2 X_2, \quad I_2' = \dfrac{I_2}{a}$$ **Step 5 — Introduce the slip-dependent resistance** Since the rotor frequency is $sf$, the rotor reactance at stator frequency is $a^2 \cdot 2\pi f L_2 = X_2'$ (already at stator frequency). However, the *effective resistance* must account for slip. The rotor's KVL gives: $$E_2 = I_2 \left( \dfrac{R_2}{s} + jX_2 \right) \quad \text{at rotor frequency } sf$$ When referred to stator: $$E_2' = I_2' \left( \dfrac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2' \right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the $1/s$ factor?** Because the rotor impedance at slip $s$ is $Z_2 = R_2 + j sX_2$, and dividing by $s$ gives the *equivalent* form at stator frequency while preserving the *power balance*. The factor $(1-s)/s$ within this term is the mechanical power analog. **Step 6 — Assemble the full circuit** The complete per-phase equivalent circuit is shown in Section 4 (Mermaid diagram). ## 2.3 Power Flow Correspondence The circuit's electrical "power dissipation" maps one-to-one to the motor's actual power flow: | Circuit Quantity | Real Machine Quantity | |---|---| | $3 I_1^2 R_1$ | Stator copper loss ($P_{sc}$) | | $3 I_c^2 R_c = 3 E_1^2 / R_c$ | Core/iron loss ($P_{core}$) | | $3 I_2'^2 R_2'$ | Rotor copper loss ($P_{rc}$) | | $3 I_2'^2 \cdot R_2' (1-s)/s$ | Mechanical power developed ($P_m$) | **Air-gap power** $P_{ag} = 3 I_2'^2 R_2' / s$ is the total power crossing the air gap. **Mechanical power developed** $P_m = (1-s) P_{ag}$. **Rotor copper loss** $P_{rc} = s P_{ag}$. This gives the elegant KTU result: $P_m / P_{rc} = (1-s)/s$, and **gross torque** $T_g = P_{ag}/\omega_s$ where $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s/60$. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The table below is your exam-day lifeline.** Memorize the *form*, not just the *symbol*. KTU often tests the *relationship* between quantities. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = 120 f / P$ | rpm | $P$ = poles | | 2 | Actual rotor speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | rpm | $s$ = slip | | 3 | Slip | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | per unit (p.u.) | $0 < s < 1$ in motor | | 4 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s f$ | Hz | Frequency of rotor currents | | 5 | Transformation ratio | $a = N_1 / N_2 \approx E_1 / E_2$ | — | For wound rotor; $\approx 1$ for cage | | 6 | Referred rotor EMF | $E_2' = a E_2$ | V/ph | | | 7 | Referred rotor R | $R_2' = a^2 R_2$ | $\Omega$/ph | | | 8 | Referred rotor X | $X_2' = a^2 X_2$ | $\Omega$/ph | At stator frequency $f$ | | 9 | Rotor current (ref.) | $I_2' = E_2' / \sqrt{(R_2'/s)^2 + X_2'^2}$ | A | KTU-most-tested formula | | 10 | Stator current | $I_1 = \bar{I}_0 + \bar{I}_2'$ | A | Phasor sum | | 11 | No-load current | $I_0 = E_1/R_c + E_1/(jX_m)$ | A | | | 12 | Stator copper loss | $P_{sc} = 3 I_1^2 R_1$ | W | | | 13 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{rc} = 3 I_2'^2 R_2' = s P_{ag}$ | W | | | 14 | Air-gap power | $P_{ag} = 3 I_2'^2 R_2' / s$ | W | | | 15 | Mech. power developed | $P_m = 3 I_2'^2 R_2' (1-s)/s$ | W | | | 16 | Gross torque | $T_g = P_{ag}/\omega_s$ | N·m | $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s/60$ | | 17 | Shaft torque | $T_{sh} = P_{out}/\omega_r$ | N·m | $\omega_r = 2\pi N_r/60$ | | 18 | Power factor angle | $\cos\phi = \text{Re}(V_1 I_1^*) / (\vert V_1\vert \vert I_1\vert)$ | p.u. | Per phase | | 19 | Thevenin voltage | $V_{th} = V_1 \cdot jX_m/(R_1 + j(X_1+X_m))$ | V | See Section 3 | | 20 | Thevenin impedance | $Z_{th} = (R_1 + jX_1)(jX_m)/Z_{total}$ | $\Omega$ | | | 21 | Thevenin resistance | $R_{th} \approx R_1 (X_m/(X_1+X_m))^2$ | $\Omega$ | Approximation | | 22 | Thevenin reactance | $X_{th} \approx X_1$ | $\Omega$ | Approximation if $X_m \gg X_1$ | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The equivalent circuit is not a textbook toy. It is the **backbone of every induction motor design tool, soft-starter, VFD controller, and protection relay**. Practicing engineers use it to: - **Predict starting current** (at $s=1$): $I_{st} \approx V_1 / \sqrt{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$ — typically 5–7 times full-load. - **Design DOL starters, star-delta starters, autotransformer starters**. - **Compute efficiency maps** for IE3/IE4 premium-efficiency motors. - **Simulate broken-bar faults** in condition-monitoring systems. - **Size capacitors for power-factor correction** in industrial plants. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Power Flow Breakdown from $R_2'/s$ This is the most important derivation for KTU — it is the *foundation* of every torque/power problem. ### Statement Show that the air-gap power $P_{ag}$ in a 3-phase induction motor splits into rotor copper loss and mechanical power developed in the ratio $s : (1-s)$. ### Proof The rotor referred EMF drives the rotor branch current: $$I_2' = \dfrac{E_2'}{\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{R_2'}{s}\right)^2 + X_2'^2}}$$ The total power consumed in the rotor branch (per phase) is: $$P_{2, \text{total/ph}} = I_2'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s}$$ Multiplying by 3 for all three phases: $$P_{ag} = 3 I_2'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s} \quad \text{...(i)}$$ Now split $R_2'/s$ using algebraic decomposition: $$\dfrac{R_2'}{s} = R_2' + R_2' \cdot \dfrac{(1-s)}{s}$$ Substitute back into (i): $$P_{ag} = 3 I_2'^2 \left[ R_2' + R_2' \cdot \dfrac{(1-s)}{s} \right]$$ $$\therefore \quad P_{ag} = \underbrace{3 I_2'^2 R_2'}_{\text{Rotor Cu Loss } P_{rc}} + \underbrace{3 I_2'^2 R_2' \cdot \dfrac{(1-s)}{s}}_{\text{Mech. Power } P_m}$$ Thus: $$P_{rc} = s P_{ag} \quad \text{and} \quad P_m = (1-s) P_{ag}$$ Dividing: $$\dfrac{P_{rc}}{P_m} = \dfrac{s}{1-s}$$ **Hence proved.** $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering meaning:** A small slip (e.g., $s = 0.04$) means rotor copper loss is only **4%** of mechanical power — this is why induction motors are highly efficient (typically 85–95%). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Thevenin Equivalent Seen From Rotor To simplify the calculation of $I_2'$ (and hence torque, power), we replace everything to the *left* of the air gap with a Thevenin equivalent. ### Step 1 — Find $V_{th}$ The Thevenin voltage is the open-circuit voltage at the air-gap terminals, i.e., the voltage across the magnetizing branch when the rotor is *disconnected*. Apply voltage divider: $$V_{th} = V_1 \cdot \dfrac{Z_m}{Z_1 + Z_m}$$ where $Z_1 = R_1 + jX_1$ and $Z_m = \dfrac{R_c \cdot jX_m}{R_c + jX_m}$ (full magnetizing branch). If we neglect $R_c$ (a common KTU simplification for high-efficiency approximations), $Z_m \approx jX_m$: $$\begin{aligned} V_{th} &\approx V_1 \cdot \dfrac{jX_m}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)} \\ \\ &= \dfrac{V_1 \cdot X_m}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_m)^2}} \angle \left[ 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_1 + X_m}{R_1}\right) \right] \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Find $Z_{th}$ With $V_1$ short-circuited, the stator impedance $Z_1$ appears in parallel with $Z_m$: $$Z_{th} = \dfrac{Z_1 \cdot Z_m}{Z_1 + Z_m} = \dfrac{(R_1 + jX_1)(jX_m)}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)}$$ Rationalize by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator. Write $Z_{th} = R_{th} + jX_{th}$. **Approximation (very commonly used and accepted by KTU):** Since $X_m \gg X_1$ and $X_m \gg R_1$: $$R_{th} \approx R_1 \left(\dfrac{X_m}{X_1 + X_m}\right)^2 \quad ; \quad X_{th} \approx X_1$$ ### Step 3 — Reduced Equivalent Circuit The rotor current in the simplified (Thevenin) form is: $$I_2' = \dfrac{V_{th}}{(R_{th} + R_2'/s) + j(X_{th} + X_2')}$$ And the magnitude: $$\vert I_2' \vert = \dfrac{V_{th}}{\sqrt{(R_{th} + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}}$$ ### Step 4 — Torque Expression Gross electromagnetic torque (in synchronous watts per rad/s): $$\begin{aligned} T_g &= \dfrac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^2}{(R_{th} + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2} \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s} \end{aligned}$$ **Starting torque** (at $s = 1$): $$T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^2 \cdot R_2'}{(R_{th} + R_2')^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}$$ **Maximum torque** (occurs at slip $s_m$): $$s_m = \dfrac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}}$$ $$T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2}{2 \omega_s (R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2})}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance $R_2'$**, but the slip $s_m$ at which it occurs is *directly proportional* to $R_2'$. This is the principle behind **wound-rotor / slip-ring motors** used for high starting torque (e.g., cranes, elevators). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Quick Decision Tree for KTU Problems When you read a KTU question, ask these questions in order: | If the question asks for… | Use the formula… | Common pitfall | |---|---|---| | Synchronous speed | $N_s = 120f/P$ | Forgetting $P$ is total poles (4-pole, 6-pole, etc.) | | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s f$ | Using line voltage instead of phase voltage | | Rotor current magnitude | $I_2' = E_2'/\sqrt{(R_2'/s)^2 + X_2'^2}$ | Forgetting to **refer** rotor values first | | Air-gap power | $P_{ag} = 3 I_2'^2 R_2'/s$ | Using $R_2'$ without dividing by $s$ | | Rotor copper loss | $P_{rc} = s P_{ag}$ | Confusing $P_m$ with $P_{rc}$ | | Mechanical power | $P_m = (1-s) P_{ag}$ | Confusing gross and shaft power | | Output (shaft) power | $P_{out} = P_m - P_{mech losses}$ | Forgetting friction & windage loss | | Efficiency | $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} \times 100\%$ | Not subtracting all losses | | Torque | $T_g = P_{ag}/\omega_s$ | Using $\omega_r$ instead of $\omega_s$ | | Power factor | $\cos\phi = P_{in}/(\sqrt{3} V_L I_L)$ | Using phase values when line is given | | Starting current | $I_{st} = V_1/\sqrt{(R_1+R_2')^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2}$ | Forgetting per-phase basis | --- ## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor has per-phase parameters (referred to stator): $R_1 = 0.5\ \Omega$, $X_1 = 1\ \Omega$, $R_2' = 0.5\ \Omega$, $X_2' = 1\ \Omega$, $R_c = 300\ \Omega$, $X_m = 50\ \Omega$. The motor runs at $N_r = 1440$ rpm. Find (a) slip, (b) rotor current, (c) air-gap power, (d) mechanical power developed, (e) rotor copper loss, (f) starting torque. **Solution:** Synchronous speed: $$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ **(a) Slip:** $$s = \dfrac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \dfrac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \text{ p.u.}$$ **(b) Rotor current:** Take $E_1 \approx V_1/\sqrt{3}$ if supply is 415 V line-to-line (typical). Assume $V_1 = 240$ V/ph for simplicity. With exact circuit, $E_1 = V_1 - I_1(R_1 + jX_1)$ requires iteration. For KTU, often $E_1 \approx V_1$ is used when no-load losses are small. Let $E_2' \approx 240$ V. $$I_2' = \dfrac{240}{\sqrt{(0.5/0.04)^2 + 1^2}} = \dfrac{240}{\sqrt{12.5^2 + 1}} = \dfrac{240}{12.54} \approx 19.14 \text{ A}$$ **(c) Air-gap power:** $$P_{ag} = 3 \times (19.14)^2 \times \dfrac{0.5}{0.04} = 3 \times 366.34 \times 12.5 = 13737.75 \text{ W} \approx 13.74 \text{ kW}$$ **(d) Mechanical power:** $$P_m = (1 - 0.04) \times 13737.75 = 0.96 \times 13737.75 = 13188.24 \text{ W} \approx 13.19 \text{ kW}$$ **(e) Rotor copper loss:** $$P_{rc} = s \times P_{ag} = 0.04 \times 13737.75 = 549.51 \text{ W}$$ **Check:** $P_m + P_{rc} = 13188.24 + 549.51 = 13737.75 = P_{ag}$ ✓ **(f) Starting torque ($s=1$):** $$T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2 \cdot R_2'}{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$ With $\omega_s = 2\pi \times 1500/60 = 157.08$ rad/s and $V_1 = 240$ V: $$T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{157.08} \cdot \dfrac{240^2 \times 0.5}{(0.5+0.5)^2 + (1+1)^2} = \dfrac{3}{157.08} \cdot \dfrac{28800}{8} = 68.74 \text{ N·m}$$ # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is meant by referring the rotor quantities to the stator side in the equivalent circuit of a three-phase induction motor? Why is it necessary?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Referring the rotor quantities to the stator side means mathematically transforming the rotor's EMF ($E_2$), resistance ($R_2$), leakage reactance ($X_2$), and current ($I_2$) to equivalent values as seen from the stator terminals, using the effective turns ratio $a = N_1/N_2$. The transformed quantities are: - $E_2' = aE_2$ - $R_2' = a^2 R_2$ - $X_2' = a^2 X_2$ - $I_2' = I_2/a$ **It is necessary** because (i) the stator and rotor are magnetically coupled (not electrically), so a common reference frame is required for circuit analysis; (ii) the rotor frequency is $sf$ while the stator is at $f$, and referring allows us to analyze everything at a single (stator) frequency; and (iii) it enables the use of Thevenin's theorem and standard AC network theorems on the entire motor. **(3 marks)** ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why does the equivalent circuit of an induction motor contain a resistance equal to $R_2(1-s)/s$? What does this resistance physically represent?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The resistance $R_2(1-s)/s$ (referred to stator as $R_2'(1-s)/s$) appears because the rotor's effective resistance in the equivalent circuit is $R_2/s$ (or $R_2'/s$). Algebraically splitting this: $$\dfrac{R_2'}{s} = R_2' + R_2' \cdot \dfrac{(1-s)}{s}$$ The first term $R_2'$ represents the *actual* rotor copper loss (heat dissipated). The second term $R_2'(1-s)/s$ is a *fictitious* (equivalent) resistance that represents the **gross mechanical power developed** by the motor. Although no current physically flows in a mechanical shaft, mathematically, the power $3 I_2'^2 \cdot R_2'(1-s)/s$ is *exactly* equal to the mechanical power converted from electrical energy at the air gap. This is the genius of the equivalent circuit — it allows mechanical power to be computed as if it were an electrical dissipation. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. Always state the **assumptions** (e.g., $R_c$ neglected, $X_m \gg X_1$, $V_1 \approx E_1$) at the start of a long answer. Students who skip this lose **1 mark** consistently. > 2. **Sign convention** for slip: $s > 0$ for motor, $s < 0$ for generator, $s > 1$ for braking — write it explicitly. > 3. **Units** in the final numerical answer: N·m for torque, kW for power, rpm for speed, p.u. for slip. Marks are deducted if units are missing. > 4. When computing efficiency, remember to subtract **all** losses (stator Cu + core + rotor Cu + friction & windage + stray). Forgetting even one loss = **-1 mark**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** With a neat per-phase equivalent circuit of a 3-phase induction motor, derive the expression for the air-gap power and show that rotor copper loss is $s$ times the air-gap power. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to stator: $R_1 = 0.3\ \Omega$, $R_2' = 0.25\ \Omega$, $X_1 = X_2' = 0.6\ \Omega$, magnetizing reactance $X_m = 30\ \Omega$, core-loss resistance $R_c$ is negligible. The motor runs at a slip of 4%. Calculate: (i) synchronous speed, (ii) rotor speed, (iii) rotor current referred to stator, (iv) air-gap power, (v) mechanical power developed, and (vi) starting torque. Assume $V_1 = 230$ V (phase). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) The per-phase equivalent circuit is drawn (full marks for clear diagram with $R_1, X_1$, magnetizing branch $R_c \parallel X_m$, and rotor branch $R_2'/s + jX_2'$). The stator applied voltage is $V_1$ per phase. Applying KVL to the rotor loop: $$E_2' = I_2' \left( \dfrac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2' \right)$$ Magnitude of rotor current (referred): $$I_2' = \dfrac{E_2'}{\sqrt{(R_2'/s)^2 + X_2'^2}}$$ Per-phase power consumed in the rotor branch (the *air-gap* power per phase): $$P_{ag,\text{ph}} = I_2'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s}$$ Total air-gap power (3 phases): $$P_{ag} = 3 I_2'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s} \quad \text{...(1)}$$ Now split $R_2'/s$ using the identity $R_2'/s = R_2' + R_2'(1-s)/s$: $$P_{ag} = 3 I_2'^2 \left[ R_2' + R_2' \cdot \dfrac{(1-s)}{s} \right]$$ The first bracket term is the rotor copper loss (heat): $$P_{rc} = 3 I_2'^2 R_2' = s \cdot P_{ag} \quad \text{(from equation 1)}$$ The second bracket term is the mechanical power developed: $$P_m = 3 I_2'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'(1-s)}{s} = (1-s) P_{ag}$$ Therefore, $\boxed{P_{rc} = s P_{ag}}$ and $\boxed{P_m = (1-s) P_{ag}}$. **QED (7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 6$ poles, $R_1 = 0.3\ \Omega$, $R_2' = 0.25\ \Omega$, $X_1 = X_2' = 0.6\ \Omega$, $X_m = 30\ \Omega$, $s = 0.04$, $V_1 = 230$ V/ph. **Step 1 — Thevenin Equivalent:** $$\begin{aligned} V_{th} &= V_1 \cdot \dfrac{jX_m}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)} \\ \\ &= 230 \cdot \dfrac{j30}{0.3 + j(0.6 + 30)} \\ \\ &= 230 \cdot \dfrac{30 \angle 90^\circ}{\sqrt{0.3^2 + 30.6^2} \angle \tan^{-1}(30.6/0.3)} \\ \\ &\approx 230 \cdot \dfrac{30 \angle 90^\circ}{30.6 \angle 89.44^\circ} \\ \\ &\approx 225.49 \angle 0.56^\circ \text{ V} \end{aligned}$$ $$Z_{th} = \dfrac{(0.3 + j0.6)(j30)}{0.3 + j30.6} = \dfrac{18 + j9}{0.3 + j30.6} \approx 0.295 + j0.598\ \Omega$$ So $R_{th} \approx 0.295\ \Omega$ and $X_{th} \approx 0.598\ \Omega$. **Step 2 — Rotor current at $s = 0.04$:** $$I_2' = \dfrac{V_{th}}{\sqrt{(R_{th} + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} R_{th} + R_2'/s &= 0.295 + 0.25/0.04 = 0.295 + 6.25 = 6.545\ \Omega \\ X_{th} + X_2' &= 0.598 + 0.6 = 1.198\ \Omega \\ I_2' &= \dfrac{225.49}{\sqrt{6.545^2 + 1.198^2}} = \dfrac{225.49}{\sqrt{42.84 + 1.435}} \\ &= \dfrac{225.49}{\sqrt{44.27}} = \dfrac{225.49}{6.654} \approx 33.89 \text{ A} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Air-gap power:** $$P_{ag} = 3 \times (33.89)^2 \times \dfrac{0.25}{0.04} = 3 \times 1148.54 \times 6.25 = 21535.13 \text{ W} \approx 21.54 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4 — Mechanical power developed:** $$P_m = (1 - 0.04) \times 21535.13 = 0.96 \times 21535.13 = 20673.7 \text{ W} \approx 20.67 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5 — Starting torque at $s = 1$:** Synchronous angular speed: $\omega_s = 2\pi \times N_s/60 = 2\pi \times 1000/60 = 104.72$ rad/s (where $N_s = 1000$ rpm) $$I_{2,st}' = \dfrac{225.49}{\sqrt{(0.295 + 0.25)^2 + 1.198^2}} = \dfrac{225.49}{\sqrt{0.297 + 1.435}} = \dfrac{225.49}{1.317} \approx 171.2 \text{ A}$$ $$T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot I_{2,st}'^2 \cdot R_2' = \dfrac{3}{104.72} \times (171.2)^2 \times 0.25 = 0.02865 \times 7326.86 \approx 209.9 \text{ N·m}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $N_s = 1000$ rpm - (ii) $N_r = 960$ rpm - (iii) $I_2' \approx 33.89$ A - (iv) $P_{ag} \approx 21.54$ kW - (v) $P_m \approx 20.67$ kW - (vi) $T_{st} \approx 209.9$ N·m **Total = 7 marks** (each part ~1.16 marks; partial credit for showing work even if numerical error) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Derive the Thevenin equivalent circuit of a 3-phase induction motor as seen from the rotor terminals. Using the Thevenin equivalent, derive expressions for (i) the slip at maximum torque, and (ii) the maximum torque. Show that the maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to stator: $R_1 = 0.4\ \Omega$, $X_1 = 1.2\ \Omega$, $R_2' = 0.3\ \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.2\ \Omega$. The magnetizing branch may be assumed to be a pure reactance $X_m = 40\ \Omega$. The motor draws rated current at a slip of 5%. If the supply voltage is 400 V (line), compute: (i) the input power factor, (ii) the developed torque, and (iii) the maximum torque and the slip at which it occurs. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Thevenin equivalent derivation** (as in Section 3.2 of these notes): - $V_{th} = V_1 \cdot jX_m / (R_1 + j(X_1+X_m))$ - $Z_{th} = (R_1 + jX_1) \parallel jX_m \approx R_{th} + jX_{th}$ - Rotor current: $I_2' = V_{th} / \sqrt{(R_{th} + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}$ **Torque expression:** $$T_g = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_2'/s)}{(R_{th} + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}$$ **Condition for maximum torque:** $\dfrac{dT_g}{ds} = 0$. Let $A = R_{th}$, $B = X_{th} + X_2'$, $r = R_2'$. Then $T_g \propto \dfrac{r/s}{(A + r/s)^2 + B^2}$. Set $x = r/s$. Then $T_g \propto \dfrac{x}{(A+x)^2 + B^2}$. Differentiate w.r.t. $x$ and set to 0: $$\dfrac{dT_g}{dx} = 0 \Rightarrow (A+x)^2 + B^2 - 2x(A+x) = 0 \Rightarrow A^2 + B^2 = x^2$$ $$x = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{R_2'}{s_m} = \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}$$ $$\boxed{s_m = \dfrac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}}}$$ Substituting back: $$T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2}{2 \omega_s \left[ R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2} \right]}$$ Since $R_2'$ does **not** appear in $T_{max}$, the maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance. **QED (7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Given:** $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz, $R_1 = 0.4\ \Omega$, $X_1 = 1.2\ \Omega$, $R_2' = 0.3\ \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.2\ \Omega$, $X_m = 40\ \Omega$, $s = 0.05$, $V_L = 400$ V. Phase voltage: $V_1 = 400/\sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V/ph. Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120 \times 50/4 = 1500$ rpm, $\omega_s = 2\pi \times 1500/60 = 157.08$ rad/s. **Step 1 — Thevenin:** $$V_{th} = 230.94 \times \dfrac{40}{\sqrt{0.4^2 + (1.2+40)^2}} = 230.94 \times \dfrac{40}{\sqrt{0.16 + 1697.44}} = 230.94 \times \dfrac{40}{41.20} \approx 224.22 \text{ V}$$ $$Z_{th} = \dfrac{(0.4 + j1.2)(j40)}{0.4 + j41.2} \approx 0.397 + j1.197\ \Omega$$ So $R_{th} = 0.397\ \Omega$, $X_{th} = 1.197\ \Omega$. **Step 2 — Rotor current at $s = 0.05$:** $$I_2' = \dfrac{224.22}{\sqrt{(0.397 + 0.3/0.05)^2 + (1.197 + 1.2)^2}} = \dfrac{224.22}{\sqrt{6.397^2 + 2.397^2}} = \dfrac{224.22}{\sqrt{40.92 + 5.75}} = \dfrac{224.22}{6.83} \approx 32.83 \text{ A}$$ **Step 3 — Magnetizing current:** $$I_0 = \dfrac{V_1}{jX_m} \approx \dfrac{230.94}{40} \angle -90^\circ \approx 5.77 \angle -90^\circ \text{ A} = -j5.77 \text{ A}$$ Rotor current phasor (approximately in phase with $V_{th}$, which is nearly in phase with $V_1$): $$I_2' \approx 32.83 \angle 0^\circ \text{ A (taking } V_1 \text{ as reference)}$$ **Step 4 — Stator current:** $$I_1 = I_0 + I_2' = 32.83 - j5.77 \text{ A}$$ Magnitude: $\vert I_1 \vert = \sqrt{32.83^2 + 5.77^2} = \sqrt{1077.81 + 33.29} = \sqrt{1111.1} = 33.33$ A **Step 5 — Power factor:** $$\cos\phi = \dfrac{\text{Re}(I_1)}{\vert I_1 \vert} = \dfrac{32.83}{33.33} = 0.985 \text{ lagging}$$ **Step 6 — Developed torque:** $$T_g = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s} = \dfrac{3}{157.08} \times (32.83)^2 \times \dfrac{0.3}{0.05} = 0.01910 \times 1077.81 \times 6 = 123.5 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 7 — Maximum torque and slip:** $$s_m = \dfrac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}} = \dfrac{0.3}{\sqrt{0.397^2 + (1.197 + 1.2)^2}} = \dfrac{0.3}{\sqrt{0.158 + 5.747}} = \dfrac{0.3}{2.432} = 0.1234$$ $$T_{max} = \dfrac{3 \times (224.22)^2}{2 \times 157.08 \times (0.397 + 2.432)} = \dfrac{3 \times 50274.6}{2 \times 157.08 \times 2.829} = \dfrac{150823.8}{888.7} \approx 169.7 \text{ N·m}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) Power factor = **0.985 lagging** - (ii) Developed torque = **123.5 N·m** - (iii) Maximum torque = **169.7 N·m** at slip = **0.1234** (≈12.34%) **Total = 7 marks** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.** ### A. Core Concepts (must be crystal clear) - ☐ **Three-phase induction motor** is a *single-excited, double-cage* machine — only stator is energized; rotor gets induced EMF. - ☐ **Slip** $s$ is the fractional difference between synchronous and rotor speed: $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$. - ☐ **Rotor frequency** $f_r = s f$ — rotor currents have a *lower* frequency than stator. - ☐ The **equivalent circuit is per phase**, referred to stator. Magnetizing branch is in *parallel* with rotor branch. - ☐ The **rotor resistance in the equivalent circuit is $R_2'/s$**, NOT $R_2'$. This slip-dependence is what makes the motor *work*. ### B. Power Flow Chain (memorize the cascade) $$\boxed{P_{in} \to P_{sc} \to P_{ag} \to P_m \to P_{out}}$$ where $P_{ag} = P_{scu} / s$ (split into $P_{rcu} = sP_{ag}$ and $P_m = (1-s)P_{ag}$). ### C. The "Trio of Torques" | Torque | Symbol | Formula | Occurs at | |---|---|---|---| | Full-load torque | $T_{fl}$ | $P_{out}/\omega_r$ | $s \approx 0.03$–$0.05$ | | Starting torque | $T_{st}$ | $3V_{th}^2 R_2'/\omega_s[(R_{th}+R_2')^2 + (X_{th}+X_2')^2]$ | $s = 1$ | | Maximum (pull-out) torque | $T_{max}$ | $3V_{th}^2/[2\omega_s(R_{th}+\sqrt{R_{th}^2+(X_{th}+X_2')^2})]$ | $s = s_m$ | ### D. Critical Numerical Tricks - ☐ **Synchronous speed** is *always* a clean number: 3000, 1500, 1000, 750, 600 … (for 50 Hz). - ☐ **Approximation** when $X_m \gg X_1$: $V_{th} \approx V_1 \cdot X_m/(X_1 + X_m)$ and $X_{th} \approx X_1$. Use this for sanity-checking long calculations. - ☐ **Power in a 3-phase system** is $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$. - ☐ **Torque unit** is N·m. To convert from gross to shaft torque, subtract friction & windage. ### E. Common Mistakes to Avoid - ❌ Forgetting to convert *line* voltage to *phase* voltage (or vice versa). - ❌ Using $N_r$ instead of $N_s$ in $\omega$ when computing gross torque. - ❌ Mixing up *gross* torque ($T_g$, developed) and *shaft* torque ($T_{sh}$, output). - ❌ Treating $R_2'$ as a fixed value in $P_{rc} = sP_{ag}$ — yes, the *resistance* is fixed; the *power* varies with $s$ because $I_2'$ varies with $s$. - ❌ Claiming $T_{max}$ depends on $R_2'$ — it does **NOT**. ### F. KTU-Preferred Answer Format for 14-Mark Questions 1. **Assumptions** (1 mark): state all idealizations. 2. **Diagram** (1 mark): neat per-phase equivalent circuit with all labels. 3. **Formula derivations** (4–5 marks): clear steps, identities highlighted. 4. **Numerical substitution** (5–6 marks): show units throughout. 5. **Final boxed answer** (1 mark): with units and **correct sign** (lagging/leading). > [!WARNING] > **The single biggest reason KTU students lose marks on this topic:** They write $I_2' = E_2'/(R_2' + jX_2')$ — **forgetting the $1/s$ factor in the resistance**. Always remember: rotor resistance in the equivalent circuit is **$R_2'/s$**, not $R_2'$. This one mistake costs ~3 marks per question.

topics.title: Power Flow Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Flow Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Power Flow Diagram in Electrical Machines

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

A **Power Flow Diagram** (also called a **Power Balance Diagram** or **Energy Conversion Diagram**) is a graphical representation that traces the path of power from the input terminals of an electrical machine, through its internal conversion stages (involving various losses), to the final output terminals. It is an **accounting tool** that enforces the **Law of Conservation of Energy** applied to electromechanical energy conversion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A Power Flow Diagram is a block-level schematic that quantitatively shows the input power, segregates it into useful output power and individual loss components (copper, iron, mechanical, stray), and visually demonstrates that:
> $$\boxed{P_{\text{input}} = P_{\text{output}} + P_{\text{total losses}}}$$

In all rotating electrical machines, power conversion follows a universal three-stage cascade:
1. **Electrical Input Stage** — Power is drawn from the source in electrical form.
2. **Intermediate Conversion Stage** — Power undergoes conversion (electrical → mechanical in motors; mechanical → electrical in generators), with internal losses being subtracted at each node.
3. **Mechanical/Electrical Output Stage** — Useful power is delivered to the load, with the remainder having been dissipated as heat, sound, or radiation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water Pipeline with Leaks" Analogy:**
> Imagine a water pipeline that pumps water from a reservoir (input) to a town (output). Along the pipeline, water leaks out at three points: near the pump (mechanical friction in bearings), through the pipe walls (iron/core losses due to hysteresis and eddy currents), and due to turbulence in the flow (copper losses from resistance of the windings). The Power Flow Diagram is exactly like this — it shows the **gross inflow**, the **leakages at every stage**, and the **net useful outflow**. The efficiency of the machine is simply the ratio of water reaching the town to water pumped from the reservoir.

**Key Insight:** A Power Flow Diagram is essentially an *energy bank statement* for the machine. Every watt that enters must either leave as useful work or be accounted for as a loss — never destroyed, never created.

## 1.3 The Four Universal Loss Categories

Every loss in an electrical machine falls into one of these four fundamental buckets, and these are the *nodes* you will see in any power flow diagram:

| # | Loss Category | Physical Origin | Where It Appears | Magnitude Indicator |
|---|---------------|-----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Copper Loss (I²R)** | Ohmic heating in windings | Armature, field, rotor bars | Proportional to current squared |
| 2 | **Iron/Core Loss** | Hysteresis + Eddy currents in steel | Stator/rotor core laminations | Proportional to $V^2$ and frequency |
| 3 | **Mechanical Loss** | Friction + windage | Bearings, brush contact, air resistance | Roughly constant at given speed |
| 4 | **Stray Load Loss** | Leakage flux, harmonics, pulse loading | Non-uniform air-gap distribution | $\approx 1\%$ of output for DC machines |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Hot Tip:** When asked to draw a Power Flow Diagram, *always* label every block with the loss equation — not just the loss name. A diagram with numerical values gets **2 extra marks** even if the structure is the same as the textbook.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power Flow Stages — The Generic Template

The power flow in **any** electrical machine can be decomposed into **five canonical stages**. Master this template, and you can draw a power flow diagram for *any* machine in the syllabus.

**Stage 1 — Input Power Entry**
- The machine receives input from an external source.
- For motors: Electrical input $P_{\text{in,elec}} = V_{\text{terminal}} \times I_{\text{line}} \times \cos\phi$.
- For generators: Mechanical input $P_{\text{in,mech}} = T_{\text{prime}} \times \omega_m$.

**Stage 2 — Field/Copper Loss Subtraction**
- Power consumed in exciting the magnetic field is the first loss drawn.
- For shunt field: $P_{\text{sh}} = V^2 / R_{\text{sh}}$.
- For series field: $P_{\text{se}} = I_{\text{se}}^2 R_{\text{se}}$.
- Remaining power is now the **power developed in the armature** (gross mechanical power for motors).

**Stage 3 — Armature Copper Loss Subtraction**
- $P_{\text{cu,a}} = I_a^2 R_a$ is subtracted.
- For motors: Power after this subtraction = **Gross Mechanical Power Developed** $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a$.
- For generators: Power before armature copper loss = **Gross Electrical Power Generated** $E_g \cdot I_a$.

**Stage 4 — Iron + Mechanical Loss Subtraction**
- $P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{friction}} + P_{\text{windage}}$ are subtracted together.
- For motors: Remaining = **Shaft (Output) Power** $P_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{sh}} \cdot \omega$.
- For generators: Remaining = **Net Mechanical Input Required** from prime mover.

**Stage 5 — Stray Load Loss Subtraction**
- $P_{\text{stray}}$ is the final subtraction before output.
- Often grouped with iron + mechanical losses in KTU problems.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear in ~80% of numerical problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Applies To |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------|
| 1 | Input Electrical Power | $P_{\text{in}} = V_t I_L$ | W (DC) | DC Machines |
| 2 | Input AC Power | $P_{\text{in}} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | W | 3-φ Induction, Synchronous |
| 3 | Armature Copper Loss | $P_{\text{cu,a}} = I_a^2 R_a$ | W | All rotating machines |
| 4 | Shunt Field Copper Loss | $P_{\text{sh}} = V^2 / R_{\text{sh}}$ | W | DC Shunt/Compound |
| 5 | Series Field Copper Loss | $P_{\text{se}} = I_{\text{se}}^2 R_{\text{se}}$ | W | DC Series/Compound |
| 6 | Gross Mechanical Power (Motor) | $P_m = E_b I_a$ | W | DC Motor |
| 7 | Gross Electrical Power (Generator) | $P_g = E_g I_a$ | W | DC Generator |
| 8 | Shaft Output Power | $P_{\text{sh}} = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{\text{sh}}}{60}$ | W | All rotating machines |
| 9 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | All rotating machines |
| 10 | Mechanical Losses (Total) | $P_{\text{mech}} = P_{\text{fric}} + P_{\text{wind}}$ | W | All rotating machines |
| 11 | Constant Losses | $P_{\text{const}} = P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{mech}}$ | W | DC Machines |
| 12 | Stray Load Loss | $P_{\text{stray}} \approx 0.01 \, P_{\text{out}}$ | W | DC Machines (approx) |
| 13 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | % | All Machines |
| 14 | Condition for Max Efficiency | Variable loss = Constant loss | — | All Machines |
| 15 | Back EMF (DC Motor) | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | V | DC Motor |
| 16 | Generated EMF (DC Gen) | $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ | V | DC Generator |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Power Flow Diagrams are not academic curiosities — they are the **backbone of industrial machine design and audit**. Their applications include:

- **Thermal Management Design:** Knowing exactly where each watt of loss is dissipated allows engineers to place cooling fins, fans, and oil-cooling jackets at the right physical locations.
- **Efficiency Improvement Programs:** Industrial audits use power flow diagrams to identify the *dominant loss* (e.g., if copper loss is 60% of total loss, the design needs thicker conductors).
- **Cost-of-Loss Calculations:** Electricity tariffs make losses expensive. Power flow diagrams allow plant engineers to estimate annual operating cost: $\text{Annual Cost} = P_{\text{loss}} \times \text{Tariff} \times \text{Operating Hours}$.
- **Condition Monitoring:** Deviation from the expected power flow (e.g., a sudden increase in iron loss) is a diagnostic indicator of bearing failure, insulation degradation, or partial short circuits.
- **Standards Compliance:** IE (International Efficiency) class ratings for motors (IE1, IE2, IE3, IE4) are defined strictly by the loss budget visible in the power flow diagram.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — DC Shunt Motor Power Flow

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently tested** power flow in KTU Module 4. Master this derivation.

**Given:** A DC shunt motor with terminal voltage $V$, armature current $I_a$, shunt field current $I_{\text{sh}}$, armature resistance $R_a$, and shunt field resistance $R_{\text{sh}}$, running at speed $N$ with shaft torque $T_{\text{sh}}$.

**Step 1 — Total Input Power Drawn from Supply**

The supply feeds *two* parallel branches: the armature and the shunt field. Therefore:

$$P_{\text{in}} = V \cdot I_L = V \cdot (I_a + I_{\text{sh}})$$

**Step 2 — Subtract Shunt Field Copper Loss**

The shunt field draws a small current $I_{\text{sh}} = V / R_{\text{sh}}$ regardless of load. The power consumed in the field winding is:

$$P_{\text{sh}} = V \cdot I_{\text{sh}} = \frac{V^2}{R_{\text{sh}}}$$

The remaining power flows into the armature circuit:

$$P_{\text{armature input}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{sh}} = V \cdot I_a$$

**Step 3 — Subtract Armature Copper Loss**

Current $I_a$ flowing through armature resistance $R_a$ dissipates:

$$P_{\text{cu,a}} = I_a^2 R_a$$

The remaining power is the **Gross Mechanical Power Developed** by the electromagnetic torque:

$$P_m = V \cdot I_a - I_a^2 R_a = I_a (V - I_a R_a) = E_b \cdot I_a$$

where $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ is the back-EMF (a direct application of Kirchhoff's Voltage Law across the armature loop).

**Step 4 — Subtract Iron + Mechanical Losses**

The rotating armature experiences core losses (hysteresis + eddy current) in its iron core and mechanical losses (friction at bearings, windage, brush friction):

$$P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{mech}} = \text{constant losses (roughly speed-dependent)}$$

The remaining power is the **Net Shaft Output Power** delivered to the mechanical load:

$$P_{\text{sh}} = P_m - (P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{mech}})$$

**Step 5 — Express Shaft Power via Torque and Speed**

The mechanical output is also expressible through the shaft torque:

$$P_{\text{sh}} = T_{\text{sh}} \cdot \omega = \frac{2 \pi N T_{\text{sh}}}{60}$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the Final Power Flow Equation**

Combining all five stages into a single accounting identity:

$$P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}} + P_{\text{sh,field}} + P_{\text{cu,a}} + P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{mech}} + P_{\text{stray}}$$

In compact notation:

$$P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}} + \sum P_{\text{losses}}$$

**Step 7 — Derivation of the Efficiency Equation**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{\sum P_{\text{losses}}}{P_{\text{in}}}$$

**Step 8 — Condition for Maximum Efficiency**

Total loss = Variable loss (depends on load) + Constant loss (independent of load).

$$P_{\text{loss,total}} = I_a^2 R_a + P_{\text{constant}}$$

Differentiating $P_{\text{loss}}$ with respect to $I_a$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{d(P_{\text{loss}})}{dI_a} = 2 I_a R_a = 0$$

This gives the minimum at $I_a = 0$ (no load), but the *maximum efficiency* occurs where:

$$\boxed{\text{Variable Loss} = \text{Constant Loss}}$$

$$I_a^2 R_a = P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{mech}}$$

At this operating point, the machine is most thermodynamically efficient.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DC Generator Power Flow (Reverse Direction)

> [!NOTE]
> For generators, the power flow runs **right-to-left**: mechanical input on the right, electrical output on the left.

**Step 1 — Mechanical Input from Prime Mover**

$$P_{\text{in,mech}} = T_{\text{prime}} \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N T_{\text{prime}}}{60}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract Mechanical + Iron Losses**

The prime mover must first overcome rotational losses before electromagnetic conversion can occur:

$$P_{\text{em}} = P_{\text{in,mech}} - (P_{\text{fric}} + P_{\text{wind}} + P_{\text{iron}})$$

This is the **electromagnetic power** that crosses the air-gap.

**Step 3 — Equate to Electrical Power Generated**

By the principle of conservation, the mechanical power developed equals the electrical power generated in the armature winding:

$$P_{\text{em}} = E_g \cdot I_a$$

**Step 4 — Subtract Armature Copper Loss**

$$P_{\text{cu,a}} = I_a^2 R_a$$

$$P_{\text{terminal}} = E_g I_a - I_a^2 R_a = I_a (E_g - I_a R_a) = V_t \cdot I_a$$

**Step 5 — Subtract Shunt Field Copper Loss**

For a shunt generator, the terminal voltage also powers the field:

$$P_{\text{out,elec}} = V_t I_L = V_t (I_a - I_{\text{sh}})$$

Final power flow:

$$P_{\text{in,mech}} = P_{\text{out,elec}} + P_{\text{mech}} + P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{cu,a}} + P_{\text{sh,field}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transformer Power Flow

**Step 1 — Input Power to Primary**

$$P_1 = V_1 I_1 \cos\phi_1$$

**Step 2 — Subtract Primary Copper Loss**

$$P_{\text{cu1}} = I_1^2 R_1$$

$$P' = P_1 - I_1^2 R_1 \quad \text{(power entering the iron core)}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract Core Loss (Iron Loss)**

$$P_{\text{iron}} = P_{\text{hyst}} + P_{\text{eddy}}$$

$$P'' = P' - P_{\text{iron}} \quad \text{(power transferred across the core to secondary)}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract Secondary Copper Loss**

$$P_{\text{cu2}} = I_2^2 R_2$$

$$P_{\text{out}} = P'' - I_2^2 R_2 = V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Numerical Application

**Problem:** A 220 V DC shunt motor draws a line current of 50 A. The shunt field resistance is $110\ \Omega$ and armature resistance is $0.2\ \Omega$. The iron loss is 500 W, friction + windage loss is 300 W. Calculate: (a) Back EMF, (b) Gross mechanical power developed, (c) Shaft output power, (d) Efficiency.

**Step (a) — Find Field Current and Armature Current**

$$I_{\text{sh}} = \frac{V}{R_{\text{sh}}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2\ \text{A}$$

$$I_a = I_L - I_{\text{sh}} = 50 - 2 = 48\ \text{A}$$

**Step (b) — Back EMF**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (48 \times 0.2) = 220 - 9.6 = 210.4\ \text{V}$$

**Step (c) — Gross Mechanical Power Developed**

$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = 210.4 \times 48 = 10099.2\ \text{W}$$

**Step (d) — Shaft Output Power**

$$P_{\text{sh}} = P_m - (P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{mech}}) = 10099.2 - (500 + 300) = 9299.2\ \text{W}$$

**Step (e) — Input Power**

$$P_{\text{in}} = V \cdot I_L = 220 \times 50 = 11000\ \text{W}$$

**Step (f) — Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{9299.2}{11000} \times 100\% = 84.54\%$$

**Cross-Verification via Loss Accounting:**

$$P_{\text{losses}} = P_{\text{sh,field}} + P_{\text{cu,a}} + P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{mech}}$$

$$P_{\text{losses}} = (220 \times 2) + (48^2 \times 0.2) + 500 + 300$$

$$P_{\text{losses}} = 440 + 460.8 + 500 + 300 = 1700.8\ \text{W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{11000 - 1700.8}{11000} \times 100\% = 84.54\% \quad \checkmark$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📊 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — DC Shunt Motor Power Flow

The diagram below is a **block-level functional architecture** mapping every power transformation stage in a DC shunt motor. Each block represents a physical location in the machine where a power transition occurs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUP["DC SUPPLY 220V"]:::input
    B1["SHUNT FIELD WINDING"]:::field
    B2["ARMATURE WINDING"]:::arm
    B3["AIR GAP COUPLING"]:::conv
    B4["MECHANICAL SHAFT"]:::mech
    B5["MECHANICAL LOAD"]:::output
    L1["L_sh = V*I_sh = 440W"]:::loss
    L2["L_cu_a = I_a^2 R_a = 460.8W"]:::loss
    L3["L_iron = 500W"]:::loss
    L4["L_fric + L_wind = 300W"]:::loss
    SUP -- "P_in = 11000W" --> B1
    SUP -- "P_in = 11000W" --> B2
    B1 -. "P_sh" .-> L1
    B2 -- "E_b * I_a = 10099W" --> B3
    B2 -. "P_cu_a" .-> L2
    B3 -- "P_m = 10099W" --> B4
    B3 -. "P_iron" .-> L3
    B4 -- "P_sh = 9299W" --> B5
    B4 -. "P_fric + P_wind" .-> L4

    classDef input fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef field fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px

    classDef output fill:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — DC Generator Power Flow (Reverse Cascade)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PM["PRIME MOVER DIESEL/TURBINE"]:::input
    SH["MECHANICAL SHAFT"]:::mech
    AG["AIR GAP COUPLING"]:::conv

    OUT["ELECTRICAL LOAD"]:::output

    L4["L_sh = V*I_sh = 440W"]:::loss
    PM -- "P_in_mech = 11700W" --> SH
    SH -- "P_em = 10900W" --> AG
    SH -. "P_fric + P_wind" .-> L1
    AG -- "E_g * I_a = 10900W" --> AW
    AG -. "P_iron" .-> L2
    AW -- "V_t * I_L = 9999W" --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Single-Phase Transformer Power Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["AC PRIMARY SOURCE"]:::input
    P1["PRIMARY WINDING"]:::arm
    CORE["MAGNETIC CORE"]:::conv
    P2["SECONDARY WINDING"]:::arm

    P1 -. "I1^2 R1" .-> LL1
    P1 -- "Power into Core" --> CORE
    CORE -. "Hysteresis + Eddy" .-> LL2
    CORE -- "Transferred Power" --> P2
    P2 -. "I2^2 R2" .-> LL3
```

```mermaid
    CUS["I_se^2 R_se SERIES COPPER"]:::loss

    MECH["P_mech = P_fric + P_wind"]:::const
    STR["P_stray approx 0.01 P_out"]:::loss

    TL --> CL

    VL --> CUS
    CL --> IR
    CL --> MECH
```
