topics.title: No-Load and Blocked-Rotor Tests
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** No-Load and Blocked-Rotor Tests

# No-Load and Blocked-Rotor Tests on Three-Phase Induction Motors

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> The **No-Load Test** (also called the **Open-Circuit Test** or **Running Light Test**) and the **Blocked-Rotor Test** (also called the **Short-Circuit Test** or **Locked-Rotor Test**) are two indirect experimental methods used to determine the **per-phase equivalent circuit parameters** of a three-phase induction motor without loading the machine mechanically. Together, they allow complete determination of stator resistance $R_1$, magnetizing reactance $X_0$, core-loss resistance $R_c$, referred rotor resistance $R_2'$ and referred rotor leakage reactance $X_2'$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **new car engine** and you want to know what's inside without disassembling it. You perform two simple experiments:

- **Car idling at neutral (No-Load Test):** You start the engine and let it just run without engaging any gears. You measure the fuel it consumes and the RPM. From this, you can deduce the *internal friction* and *air-pumping losses* — i.e., the energy used just to keep the magnetic field alive inside the engine.

- **Car held in place with brakes (Blocked-Rotor Test):** Now you try to drive the engine while it's locked in place. The current drawn tells you the *internal resistance* of the wiring and the *leakage reactance* (the "push-back" the motor gives to current flow).

Just like that, the **No-Load Test** tells us the **magnetizing branch parameters** ($R_c$ and $X_0$), and the **Blocked-Rotor Test** tells us the **leakage parameters** ($R_{eq}$ and $X_{eq}$). Combine them, and we know the entire equivalent circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** These two tests fall under the learning outcome *"Determine the equivalent circuit parameters of a three-phase induction motor experimentally."* They are the most frequently asked topic in Part A and Part B questions for Module 4.

### Key Physical Quantities Measured

- **No-Load Test** measures: No-load line voltage $V_0$ (rated), no-load line current $I_0$, total no-load input power $W_0$ (using two wattmeters or three wattmeters).
- **Blocked-Rotor Test** measures: Blocked-rotor line voltage $V_{sc}$ (reduced, ~10–25% of rated), blocked-rotor line current $I_{sc}$ (rated), total blocked-rotor input power $W_{sc}$.

The **standard supply frequency** is **50 Hz** and **standard rated voltage** for the no-load test is the motor's nameplate value (e.g., **400 V** in KTU lab settings).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The No-Load Test — Theory and Operating Conditions

In the no-load test, the rotor is uncoupled from any mechanical load and is allowed to run freely at (or very close to) **synchronous speed**. Because the slip $s$ is very small (≈ 0.001 to 0.005), the term $R_2'(1-s)/s$ in the rotor branch becomes enormously large (≈ 200 to 1000 times $R_2'$). This effectively **opens the rotor branch**, leaving only the **magnetizing branch** ($R_c \parallel jX_0$) to conduct the no-load current $I_0$.

#### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown:

1. **Step 1 — Apply rated voltage** at rated frequency to the stator terminals using an autotransformer/variac.
2. **Step 2 — Allow motor to run light:** The rotor attains near-synchronous speed, so slip $s \to 0$.
3. **Step 3 — Measure $V_0$, $I_0$, $W_0$:** These are read on voltmeter, ammeter, and wattmeter(s).
4. **Step 4 — Compute no-load power factor angle $\phi_0$:**
   The total power $W_0$ corresponds to the **iron (core) losses + friction & windage losses + a small stator copper loss**.
5. **Step 5 — Split $I_0$ into two components:** $I_m$ (magnetizing) flowing through $X_0$, and $I_c$ (core-loss component) flowing through $R_c$.
6. **Step 6 — Compute $R_c$ and $X_0$** from the per-phase voltage and these currents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no-load gives magnetizing parameters:** At $s \approx 0$, the rotor branch impedance becomes $Z_r \approx \infty$, so all current flows only through the shunt magnetizing branch — effectively isolating it.

### 2.2 The Blocked-Rotor Test — Theory and Operating Conditions

In this test, the rotor is **mechanically locked** so it cannot rotate. With $s = 1$, the term $R_2'(1-s)/s = 0$, and the equivalent rotor branch reduces to just $R_2' + jX_2'$. The full magnetizing branch ($R_c$, $X_0$) is now in parallel with a very small rotor impedance, so **almost all the current bypasses the magnetizing branch** through the leakage path.

#### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown:

1. **Step 1 — Lock the rotor** firmly using a brake-lever or locking arrangement (NEVER hold by hand).
2. **Step 2 — Apply reduced voltage** ($V_{sc} \approx 10$–$25\%$ of rated) through an autotransformer, just sufficient to circulate **rated current** $I_{sc}$.
3. **Step 3 — Measure $V_{sc}$, $I_{sc}$, $W_{sc}$:** All three are at reduced voltage.
4. **Step 4 — Compute per-phase equivalent impedance:**
   $Z_{eq} = V_{sc,ph}/I_{sc}$, $R_{eq} = W_{sc}/(3 I_{sc}^2)$, $X_{eq} = \sqrt{Z_{eq}^2 - R_{eq}^2}$.
5. **Step 5 — Split $R_{eq}$ and $X_{eq}$** between stator and rotor using standard design assumptions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety Pitfall:** Blocked-rotor tests must be performed **quickly** (within ~10–15 seconds). If rated current is sustained longer, the rotor (which has no cooling airflow because it isn't spinning) will overheat rapidly.

### 2.3 Standard Assumptions for Parameter Splitting

Since the no-load and blocked-rotor tests only give *combined* values, KTU 2024 prescribes the following **standard design assumptions**:

- $X_1 = X_2' = X_{eq}/2$ (leakage reactances are split equally between stator and rotor)
- $R_2' = R_{eq} - R_1$ (where $R_1$ is found from a DC resistance test, or assumed = $0.5 R_{eq}$ if not measured)
- $R_c$ and $X_0$ are placed in the magnetizing branch as measured from no-load.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Measured From | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | No-load phase voltage | $V_{0,ph} = V_{0,L}/\sqrt{3}$ | No-Load Test | V |
| 2 | No-load power factor | $\cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{W_0}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{0,L}\,I_0}$ | No-Load Test | dimensionless |
| 3 | No-load power-factor angle | $\sin\phi_0 = \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\phi_0}$ | No-Load Test | dimensionless |
| 4 | Magnetizing component of $I_0$ | $I_m = I_0 \sin\phi_0$ | No-Load Test | A |
| 5 | Core-loss component of $I_0$ | $I_c = I_0 \cos\phi_0$ | No-Load Test | A |
| 6 | Core-loss resistance | $R_c = \dfrac{V_{0,ph}}{I_c}$ | No-Load Test | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Magnetizing reactance | $X_0 = \dfrac{V_{0,ph}}{I_m}$ | No-Load Test | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | Blocked-rotor phase voltage | $V_{sc,ph} = V_{sc,L}/\sqrt{3}$ | Blocked-Rotor Test | V |
| 9 | Per-phase equivalent impedance | $Z_{eq} = \dfrac{V_{sc,ph}}{I_{sc}}$ | Blocked-Rotor Test | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | Per-phase equivalent resistance | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{W_{sc}}{3 I_{sc}^2}$ | Blocked-Rotor Test | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | Per-phase equivalent reactance | $X_{eq} = \sqrt{Z_{eq}^2 - R_{eq}^2}$ | Blocked-Rotor Test | $\Omega$ |
| 12 | Stator leakage reactance | $X_1 = X_{eq}/2$ | Design Assumption | $\Omega$ |
| 13 | Referred rotor leakage reactance | $X_2' = X_{eq}/2$ | Design Assumption | $\Omega$ |
| 14 | Stator resistance (from DC test) | $R_1 = 0.5 \times R_{dc} \times \text{correction}$ | DC Test | $\Omega$ |
| 15 | Referred rotor resistance | $R_2' = R_{eq} - R_1$ | Combined | $\Omega$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** These equivalent circuit parameters are essential for predicting motor performance — torque-slip characteristic, efficiency at any load, starting current, and power factor — without actually running the motor under load. This is widely used by **electrical design engineers** in industries like Siemens, ABB, and BHEL for motor selection in **pumps, fans, compressors, and elevators**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of No-Load Test Parameters

We begin by recalling that the total three-phase no-load input power $W_0$ equals:

$$
W_0 = 3 V_{0,ph}\, I_0 \cos\phi_0
$$

Solving for the no-load power factor gives:

$$
\cos\phi_0 = \frac{W_0}{3 V_{0,ph}\, I_0} = \frac{W_0}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{0,L}\, I_0}
$$

Here we used the identity $3 V_{ph} = \sqrt{3}\, V_L$ for a balanced star-connected system.

The no-load current $I_0$ has two orthogonal phasor components. The component along the voltage phasor is the **core-loss current** $I_c$, and the component perpendicular to the voltage phasor is the **magnetizing current** $I_m$. Their projections yield:

$$
I_c = I_0 \cos\phi_0
$$

$$
I_m = I_0 \sin\phi_0 = I_0 \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\phi_0}
$$

The **core-loss resistance** represents the equivalent electrical load in which the iron (hysteresis + eddy current) losses are dissipated. By Ohm's law on this purely resistive element:

$$
R_c = \frac{V_{0,ph}}{I_c}
$$

Similarly, the **magnetizing reactance** represents the energy stored in the air-gap magnetic field. By Ohm's law on this purely reactive element:

$$
X_0 = \frac{V_{0,ph}}{I_m}
$$

These are the **two parameters determined exclusively from the no-load test**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Blocked-Rotor Test Parameters

With the rotor locked, $s = 1$. The rotor branch impedance reduces to $R_2' + jX_2'$. The magnetizing branch ($R_c \parallel jX_0$), being of much higher impedance, carries negligible current at the reduced test voltage and is therefore neglected. The equivalent circuit collapses to a simple series impedance:

$$
Z_{eq} = (R_1 + R_2') + j(X_1 + X_2')
$$

The magnitude of this per-phase impedance is:

$$
Z_{eq} = \frac{V_{sc,ph}}{I_{sc}}
$$

The total three-phase blocked-rotor input power is entirely consumed as **stator and rotor copper losses** (iron losses are negligible at low voltage):

$$
W_{sc} = 3 I_{sc}^2 R_{eq}
$$

Solving for $R_{eq}$:

$$
R_{eq} = \frac{W_{sc}}{3 I_{sc}^2}
$$

The equivalent leakage reactance is found from the impedance triangle:

$$
X_{eq} = \sqrt{Z_{eq}^2 - R_{eq}^2}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU pattern):**
A **3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz**, star-connected induction motor gave the following test results:

- **No-Load Test:** $V_0 = 400\,\text{V}$, $I_0 = 6\,\text{A}$, $W_0 = 360\,\text{W}$
- **Blocked-Rotor Test:** $V_{sc} = 100\,\text{V}$, $I_{sc} = 25\,\text{A}$, $W_{sc} = 1750\,\text{W}$

DC resistance test between two stator terminals gave $1.2\,\Omega$. Find the equivalent circuit parameters per phase.

#### Solution — No-Load Test

**Step 1: Phase voltage.**
$V_{0,ph} = 400/\sqrt{3} = 230.94\,\text{V}$

**Step 2: No-load power factor.**
$\cos\phi_0 = 360 / (\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 6) = 360 / 4156.92 = 0.0866$

**Step 3: Power factor angle.**
$\phi_0 = \cos^{-1}(0.0866) \approx 85.03^\circ$

**Step 4: Components of no-load current.**
$I_c = I_0 \cos\phi_0 = 6 \times 0.0866 = 0.5196\,\text{A}$

$I_m = I_0 \sin\phi_0 = 6 \times \sin(85.03^\circ) = 6 \times 0.99624 = 5.977\,\text{A}$

**Step 5: Magnetizing branch parameters.**
$R_c = V_{0,ph} / I_c = 230.94 / 0.5196 = 444.5\,\Omega$

$X_0 = V_{0,ph} / I_m = 230.94 / 5.977 = 38.64\,\Omega$

#### Solution — Blocked-Rotor Test

**Step 1: Phase voltage.**
$V_{sc,ph} = 100/\sqrt{3} = 57.735\,\text{V}$

**Step 2: Equivalent impedance magnitude.**
$Z_{eq} = V_{sc,ph} / I_{sc} = 57.735 / 25 = 2.309\,\Omega$

**Step 3: Equivalent resistance.**
$R_{eq} = W_{sc} / (3 I_{sc}^2) = 1750 / (3 \times 625) = 1750 / 1875 = 0.9333\,\Omega$

**Step 4: Equivalent reactance.**
$X_{eq} = \sqrt{Z_{eq}^2 - R_{eq}^2} = \sqrt{(2.309)^2 - (0.9333)^2}$
$= \sqrt{5.333 - 0.8710} = \sqrt{4.462} = 2.112\,\Omega$

**Step 5: Stator resistance per phase (DC test correction).**
$R_{dc\,ph} = 1.2 / 2 = 0.6\,\Omega$
Assume AC-to-DC ratio = 1.1 (typical for stator windings):
$R_1 = 1.1 \times 0.6 = 0.66\,\Omega$

**Step 6: Split reactances equally.**
$X_1 = X_{eq}/2 = 2.112 / 2 = 1.056\,\Omega$
$X_2' = X_{eq}/2 = 2.112 / 2 = 1.056\,\Omega$

**Step 7: Referred rotor resistance.**
$R_2' = R_{eq} - R_1 = 0.9333 - 0.66 = 0.2733\,\Omega$

#### Final Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit Parameter Set

$$
\boxed{R_1 = 0.66\,\Omega, \quad R_2' = 0.2733\,\Omega, \quad X_1 = X_2' = 1.056\,\Omega, \quad R_c = 444.5\,\Omega, \quad X_0 = 38.64\,\Omega}
$$

### 3.4 Complete-Aligned Summary of Key Transitions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{No-Load:} \quad & W_0 = \sqrt{3} V_L I_0 \cos\phi_0 \;\longrightarrow\; \cos\phi_0 = \frac{W_0}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_0} \;\longrightarrow\; I_c, I_m \;\longrightarrow\; R_c, X_0 \\[6pt]
\text{Blocked:} \quad & W_{sc} = 3 I_{sc}^2 R_{eq} \;\longrightarrow\; R_{eq} \;\longrightarrow\; Z_{eq} = \frac{V_{sc,ph}}{I_{sc}} \;\longrightarrow\; X_{eq} \;\longrightarrow\; X_1, X_2', R_2'
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circuit Diagram — No-Load Test Connections

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Supply[3-Phase AC Supply 50 Hz]
        A1[R Phase]
        B1[Y Phase]
        C1[B Phase]
    end

    subgraph Meters[Instrumentation Bank]
        AMM1[Ammeter A1]
        VLT[Voltmeter V]
        W1[Wattmeter W1]
        W2[Wattmeter W2]
    end

    subgraph Motor[Induction Motor]
        ST1[Stator Terminal U]
        ST2[Stator Terminal V]
        ST3[Stator Terminal W]
        FR[Free Rotor - No Load]
    end

    A1 --> AMM1
    B1 --> AMM1
    C1 --> AMM1
    AMM1 --> ST1
    ST1 --> ST2
    ST2 --> ST3
    ST3 --> FR
    W1 -.measures.-> ST1
    W2 -.measures.-> ST2
    VLT -.measures.-> ST1
```

### 4.2 Circuit Diagram — Blocked-Rotor Test Connections

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Supply[3-Phase Variac Reduced V]
        P1[R]
        P2[Y]
        P3[B]
    end

    subgraph Meters[Instrumentation]
        AM2[Ammeter A2]
        VLT2[Voltmeter V]
        WS1[Wattmeter W1]
        WS2[Wattmeter W2]
    end

    subgraph Motor[Induction Motor]
        T1[Stator U]
        T2[Stator V]
        T3[Stator W]
        LK[Locked Rotor - Brake Engaged]
    end

    P1 --> AM2
    P2 --> AM2
    P3 --> AM2
    AM2 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> LK
    WS1 -.measures.-> T1
    WS2 -.measures.-> T2
    VLT2 -.measures.-> T1
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Two Tests

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Induction Motor Under Test] --> B{Test Selection}
    B -->|No-Load| NL[Apply Rated Voltage]
    B -->|Blocked-Rotor| BR[Apply Reduced Voltage]

    NL --> NL1[Rotor Free - Slip near 0]
    NL1 --> NL2[Magnetizing Branch Dominates]
    NL2 --> NL3[Measure V0, I0, W0]
    NL3 --> NL4[Compute Rc and X0]

    BR --> BR1[Rotor Locked - Slip = 1]
    BR1 --> BR2[Leakage Branch Dominates]
    BR2 --> BR3[Measure Vsc, Isc, Wsc]
    BR3 --> BR4[Compute Req and Xeq]

    NL4 --> FIN[Combine with DC Test R1]
    BR4 --> FIN
    FIN --> OUT[Complete Equivalent Circuit Parameters]

    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style OUT fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style NL4 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style BR4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

### 4.4 Procedure Comparison Matrix (Block Diagram Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Coupling Removed] --> P2[Step 2: Apply V rated]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Measure V0 I0 W0]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Compute Rc X0]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Stop Motor]

    Q1[Step 1: Lock Rotor] --> Q2[Step 2: Apply V reduced]
    Q2 --> Q3[Step 3: Increase V until I rated]
    Q3 --> Q4[Step 4: Measure Vsc Isc Wsc]
    Q4 --> Q5[Step 5: Compute Req Xeq]
    Q5 --> Q6[Step 6: Release Brake Quickly]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Why is the no-load test on an induction motor performed at rated voltage and rated frequency?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The no-load test is performed at **rated voltage** because:

1. **(1 Mark)** The magnetizing reactance $X_0$ and core-loss resistance $R_c$ depend on the operating flux level. Since the motor's rated operating point corresponds to rated voltage at rated frequency, these parameters must be measured at that exact flux level to be representative.

2. **(1 Mark)** The iron (core) losses — both hysteresis ($P_h \propto f B_m^{1.6}$) and eddy current ($P_e \propto f^2 B_m^2$) — vary strongly with flux density $B_m$. Rated voltage produces the rated flux density, giving realistic iron losses.

3. **(1 Mark)** Rated frequency ensures the slip $s$ is at its design value at no-load, and the rotor reactance $X_2'$ is correctly represented when later used in the equivalent circuit.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

In the blocked-rotor test, why is the applied voltage much lower than the rated voltage?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **(1 Mark)** With the rotor locked ($s = 1$), the equivalent impedance $Z_{eq}$ is small (only the leakage path), so even a small applied voltage will cause a **large current** to flow.

2. **(1 Mark)** To circulate **rated current** in the stator windings during the test, only about **10–25% of rated voltage** is required (since $V_{sc} \approx I_{rated} \times Z_{eq}$, and $Z_{eq}$ is small).

3. **(1 Mark)** Lower voltage also keeps the **air-gap flux small**, which is desirable because it prevents the magnetizing branch from dominating and ensures the test truly reflects only the leakage impedance. Additionally, it avoids unnecessary heating during the brief test.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the procedure to conduct the **no-load test** on a three-phase induction motor. List the instruments used and the readings taken.

**(b) [7 Marks]** From the no-load test readings, derive the expressions for the **magnetizing reactance $X_0$** and the **core-loss resistance $R_c$**.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Procedure and Circuit Diagram [7 Marks]**

**Circuit Connections (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):**

1. **(1 Mark)** The stator terminals of the three-phase induction motor are connected to a **three-phase variac (autotransformer)** through:
   - One **ammeter** in each line (or one with selector switch),
   - One **voltmeter** across any two lines,
   - Two **wattmeters** $W_1$ and $W_2$ for the two-wattmeter method of power measurement.

2. **(1 Mark)** The rotor is **mechanically decoupled** from any load (belt removed, coupling disconnected) so that it runs freely.

3. **(1 Mark)** Voltage is gradually raised from zero to **rated voltage** using the variac, keeping frequency at rated value (50 Hz).

4. **(1 Mark)** The motor is allowed to run at no-load until bearings are properly lubricated and steady readings are obtained.

5. **(1 Mark)** The following readings are noted:
   - No-load line voltage $V_0$ (V)
   - No-load line current $I_0$ (A)
   - Total no-load power $W_0 = W_1 + W_2$ (W)

6. **(1 Mark)** Calculate $\cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{W_0}{\sqrt{3}\, V_0 I_0}$.

7. **(1 Mark)** Calculate $R_c = \dfrac{V_0}{\sqrt{3}\, I_0 \cos\phi_0}$ and $X_0 = \dfrac{V_0}{\sqrt{3}\, I_0 \sin\phi_0}$.

**Part (b) — Derivation of $X_0$ and $R_c$ [7 Marks]**

At no-load, slip $s \approx 0$, so $R_2'(1-s)/s \to \infty$, effectively opening the rotor branch. Current $I_0$ flows only through the parallel combination $R_c \parallel X_0$.

**Total no-load power (purely iron + friction loss):**

$$
W_0 = 3 V_{ph}\, I_0 \cos\phi_0 = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_0 \cos\phi_0
$$

Hence:

$$
\cos\phi_0 = \frac{W_0}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_0} \qquad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

Splitting $I_0$:

$$
I_c = I_0 \cos\phi_0, \qquad I_m = I_0 \sin\phi_0 \qquad \text{(2 Marks)}
$$

By Ohm's law:

$$
R_c = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_c} = \frac{V_L/\sqrt{3}}{I_0 \cos\phi_0} \qquad \text{(2 Marks)}
$$

$$
X_0 = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_m} = \frac{V_L/\sqrt{3}}{I_0 \sin\phi_0} \qquad \text{(2 Marks)}
$$

---

### Part B — Alternative Choice

**Q3 — Alternative (OR)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **blocked-rotor test** procedure on a three-phase induction motor. State why the applied voltage is reduced.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expressions for the **equivalent resistance $R_{eq}$** and **equivalent leakage reactance $X_{eq}$** per phase from blocked-rotor test data.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Procedure and Circuit Diagram [7 Marks]**

1. **(1 Mark)** The rotor of the induction motor is **firmly locked** using a brake-lever or rotor-locking arrangement. The stator is connected to a three-phase variac through ammeters, voltmeter, and two wattmeters (two-wattmeter method).

2. **(1 Mark)** Initially, the variac is set to **zero output**, then voltage is **gradually increased** while monitoring the ammeter.

3. **(1 Mark)** Voltage is raised until the **ammeter reads the rated full-load current** $I_{sc} = I_{FL}$.

4. **(1 Mark)** Note the corresponding reduced line voltage $V_{sc}$ (typically 10–25% of rated) and total power $W_{sc} = W_1 + W_2$.

5. **(1 Mark)** Read all three instruments simultaneously and **quickly release the brake** to avoid rotor overheating.

6. **(1 Mark)** Reason for reduced voltage: At $s = 1$, $Z_{eq}$ is very small. To restrict current to rated value, voltage must be small. Reduced voltage also keeps flux low so iron losses remain negligible.

7. **(1 Mark)** The test gives combined leakage parameters $R_{eq}$ and $X_{eq}$ — useful because the motor cannot be loaded directly during short-circuit conditions.

**Part (b) — Derivation [7 Marks]**

With rotor locked ($s = 1$), equivalent circuit reduces to a series impedance:

$$
Z_{eq} = (R_1 + R_2') + j(X_1 + X_2') \qquad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

Per-phase impedance magnitude:

$$
Z_{eq} = \frac{V_{sc,ph}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{sc,L}}{\sqrt{3}\, I_{sc}} \qquad \text{(2 Marks)}
$$

Total blocked-rotor power equals total copper loss:

$$
W_{sc} = 3 I_{sc}^2 R_{eq} \qquad \text{(2 Marks)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_{eq} = \frac{W_{sc}}{3 I_{sc}^2} \qquad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

And from the impedance triangle:

$$
X_{eq} = \sqrt{Z_{eq}^2 - R_{eq}^2} \qquad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

These are split equally: $X_1 = X_2' = X_{eq}/2$, and $R_2' = R_{eq} - R_1$.

---

### Part B — Additional Numerical Practice (Common in KTU)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] — 14 Marks**

A **7.5 kW, 400 V, 50 Hz**, 3-phase, star-connected squirrel-cage induction motor gave:

- **No-Load Test:** 400 V, 5.2 A, 320 W
- **Blocked-Rotor Test:** 90 V, 26 A, 1800 W
- **DC Resistance Test (between two terminals):** 1.0 Ω

Determine the per-phase equivalent circuit parameters, assuming $X_1 = X_2'$ and the stator AC-to-DC resistance ratio is 1.1.

**Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — No-load test analysis:**
$V_{0,ph} = 400/\sqrt{3} = 230.94\,\text{V}$

$\cos\phi_0 = 320 / (\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 5.2) = 320 / 3602.74 = 0.0888$ (lagging)

$\sin\phi_0 = \sqrt{1 - 0.00788} = \sqrt{0.9921} = 0.9960$

$I_c = 5.2 \times 0.0888 = 0.4618\,\text{A}$
$I_m = 5.2 \times 0.9960 = 5.179\,\text{A}$

$\boxed{R_c = 230.94 / 0.4618 = 500.1\,\Omega}$
$\boxed{X_0 = 230.94 / 5.179 = 44.59\,\Omega}$

**Step 2 — Blocked-rotor test analysis:**
$V_{sc,ph} = 90/\sqrt{3} = 51.96\,\text{V}$
$Z_{eq} = 51.96 / 26 = 1.998\,\Omega$
$R_{eq} = 1800 / (3 \times 26^2) = 1800 / 2028 = 0.8876\,\Omega$
$X_{eq} = \sqrt{(1.998)^2 - (0.8876)^2} = \sqrt{3.992 - 0.7878} = \sqrt{3.204} = 1.790\,\Omega$

**Step 3 — DC test analysis:**
$R_{dc,ph} = 1.0 / 2 = 0.5\,\Omega$ (star-connected)
$R_1 = 1.1 \times 0.5 = 0.55\,\Omega$

**Step 4 — Parameter splitting:**
$X_1 = X_2' = 1.790 / 2 = 0.895\,\Omega$
$R_2' = 0.8876 - 0.55 = 0.3376\,\Omega$

**Final Answer Set:**
$$
R_1 = 0.55\,\Omega, \quad R_2' = 0.338\,\Omega, \quad X_1 = X_2' = 0.895\,\Omega, \quad R_c = 500.1\,\Omega, \quad X_0 = 44.59\,\Omega
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$:** Students frequently write $V_{0,ph} = V_{0,L}$ (i.e., 400 V instead of 230.94 V). This single mistake causes a cascading error in $R_c$ and $X_0$ — losing **3 to 4 marks**.
>
> 2. **Sign error in $W_{sc}$ formula:** $W_{sc} = 3 I_{sc}^2 R_{eq}$, NOT $W_{sc} = \sqrt{3} V_{sc} I_{sc} \cos\phi_{sc}$ ... (well, both are equivalent, but the former is direct). Many students mistakenly use line values instead of phase values. Always verify dimensionally.
>
> 3. **Neglecting $R_1$ in $R_{eq}$:** Students sometimes report $R_2' = R_{eq}$ instead of $R_2' = R_{eq} - R_1$. This loses **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to square $I_{sc}$:** $W_{sc}/(3 I_{sc}^2)$ — missing the square of $I_{sc}$ is the most common computational error.
>
> 5. **Using $\sin\phi_0$ without computing $\phi_0$:** Many students compute $\cos\phi_0$ directly but then forget $\sin\phi_0 = \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\phi_0}$.
>
> 6. **DC Test Confusion:** The DC resistance measured between two terminals of a star-connected stator is $2 R_{1,ph}$. Forgetting the factor of 2 leads to wrong $R_1$ and consequently wrong $R_2'$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary:**

- ✅ **No-load test** = rated voltage, free rotor → gives $R_c$ and $X_0$ (magnetizing branch).
- ✅ **Blocked-rotor test** = reduced voltage, locked rotor → gives $R_{eq}$ and $X_{eq}$ (leakage branch).
- ✅ **No-load slip** is small but **never zero**; this causes a small rotor branch to remain, contributing a tiny rotor copper loss (often neglected).
- ✅ **Blocked-rotor equivalent circuit** neglects the magnetizing branch because flux is small at low voltage.
- ✅ **Per-phase values:** always convert line readings to phase using $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ for star and $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ for delta (currents differ: $I_{ph} = I_L$ for star, $I_{ph} = I_L/\sqrt{3}$ for delta).
- ✅ **Two-wattmeter method:** $W_{total} = W_1 + W_2$ (algebraic sum, with sign depending on $\cos\phi$).
- ✅ **Splitting rule:** $X_1 = X_2' = X_{eq}/2$, $R_2' = R_{eq} - R_1$.
- ✅ **DC test** gives $R_{dc,ph} = R_{line}/2$ for star. Multiply by AC-to-DC ratio (typically 1.1–1.3) to get true $R_1$.
- ✅ **Standard assumption** for wound rotor: $R_1 = R_2' = R_{eq}/2$ if $R_1$ is unknown.
- ✅ **Power factor in no-load** is very low (typically 0.05–0.15 lagging) because current is mostly magnetizing.
- ✅ **Power factor in blocked-rotor** is moderate (typically 0.3–0.5 lagging) because leakage reactance is significant.
- ✅ **Parameter values typically follow:** $R_2' < R_1$, $X_0 >> X_1, X_2'$, $R_c >> X_0$ (in ohms).
- ✅ **Sequence:** Always perform **Blocked-Rotor Test FIRST** (less risky, quick), then **No-Load Test** (longer but safe at rated voltage).
- ✅ **Safety:** Use rubber gloves during blocked-rotor; never touch the locked rotor shaft; release brake immediately after readings.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme emphasis:** Numerical problems on this topic carry **7 marks each** (one per Part B sub-question), so practice **at least 10 numericals** before the exam.
- ✅ **Common twist in exam:** They may give **delta-connected** motor data — be ready to convert $I_{ph}$ using $I_{ph} = I_L/\sqrt{3}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Equivalent Circuit Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Determination of Equivalent Circuit Parameters** # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **equivalent circuit parameters** of a synchronous machine (alternator) are the lumped electrical quantities that mathematically represent the internal behaviour of the machine when viewed from its terminals. These parameters include the **armature resistance ($R_a$)**, the **synchronous reactance ($X_s$)** — comprising the **leakage reactance ($X_l$)** and the **armature reaction reactance ($X_a$)** — and the **field circuit resistance ($R_f$)** and inductance. The synchronous machine, when operating in **steady-state**, is represented by the well-known internal EMF equation: $$E_f = V_t + I_a(R_a + jX_s)$$ where $E_f$ is the **induced EMF (excitation voltage)**, $V_t$ is the **terminal voltage**, $I_a$ is the **armature current**, and $X_s = X_l + X_a$ is the **synchronous reactance** acting in series with the armature resistance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 focuses on the **experimental determination** of $R_a$ and $X_s$ using the **Open-Circuit (OC) Test** and the **Short-Circuit (SC) Test**, along with related concepts such as **Short-Circuit Ratio (SCR)**, **Synchronous Impedance ($Z_s$)**, and **Percentage Synchronous Impedance (% $Z_s$)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition Imagine a water tap connected to a long, narrow pipe leading to a large overhead water tank. The water pressure at the tap (analogous to terminal voltage $V_t$) is less than the pressure inside the tank (analogous to induced EMF $E_f$) because of two opposing effects: 1. **Friction along the pipe walls** (analogous to **armature resistance $R_a$**) — this causes an *IR drop* proportional to the rate of water flow. 2. **Inertia of the water column** (analogous to **synchronous reactance $X_s$**) — this opposes *changes* in flow and causes a reactive voltage drop. To study the tank's behaviour, an engineer cannot open the tank and measure these drops directly. Instead, the engineer performs two experiments: - **Open-Pipe Test (No-Load / OC Test):** Run the pump with the pipe open — measure the pressure vs. flow characteristics. - **Blocked-Pipe Test (Short-Circuit / SC Test):** Block the pipe outlet and slowly raise the pump pressure until a safe flow is reached. From these two tests, the engineer *backs out* the internal friction and inertia parameters — exactly what we do for synchronous machines! > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The OC and SC tests are *non-destructive* and use only a fraction of the rated field current during SC test, making them safe and standard industrial practice. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Unit | |----------|--------|--------------| | Armature resistance | $R_a$ | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** | | Leakage reactance | $X_l$ | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** | | Armature reaction reactance | $X_a$ | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** | | Synchronous reactance | $X_s$ | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** | | Synchronous impedance | $Z_s$ | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** | | Open-circuit EMF | $E_{oc}$ | **Volts per phase (V)** | | Short-circuit current | $I_{sc}$ | **Amperes (A)** | | Short-Circuit Ratio | $SCR$ | **Dimensionless** | | Synchronous Impedance | $Z_s$ | **$\Omega$/phase** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Two Tests Are Required The synchronous machine has **two unknowns** to determine: - Armature resistance $R_a$ - Synchronous reactance $X_s$ A single experiment cannot yield both. Hence, two complementary tests are performed: ### Test 1: Open-Circuit (OC) Test - **Setup:** The armature (stator) terminals are kept **open** (no load connected). - **Prime mover:** Drives the rotor at **synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$. - **Procedure:** Field current $I_f$ is increased in steps from zero to a value that gives approximately **125% of rated voltage** on the armature. - **Measurements:** Record the open-circuit line-to-line voltage $V_{oc}$ (or phase voltage) versus $I_f$. - **Output:** The **Open-Circuit Characteristic (OCC)** — a plot of $V_{oc}$ vs. $I_f$. ### Test 2: Short-Circuit (SC) Test - **Setup:** The armature terminals are **short-circuited** through ammeters (very low impedance path). - **Prime mover:** Drives the rotor at **synchronous speed** $N_s$. - **Procedure:** Field current $I_f$ is increased in steps from zero until the armature current $I_{sc}$ reaches approximately **125–150% of rated current**. - **Measurements:** Record the short-circuit armature current $I_{sc}$ versus $I_f$. - **Output:** The **Short-Circuit Characteristic (SCC)** — a straight line passing through the origin (linear because of unsaturated armature reaction). ## 2.2 Determination of $Z_s$ and $X_s$ — The Logic For a given field current $I_f$: 1. From the **OCC**, find the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ (per phase) corresponding to that $I_f$. This equals the induced EMF $E_f$. 2. From the **SCC**, find the short-circuit current $I_{sc}$ (per phase) corresponding to the *same* $I_f$. Since the terminals are shorted ($V_t = 0$), the only impedance opposing current is the synchronous impedance $Z_s$. Therefore: $$Z_s = \frac{E_f \text{ (per phase)}}{I_{sc} \text{ (per phase)}} = \frac{V_{oc,\text{phase}}}{I_{sc,\text{phase}}}$$ Once $Z_s$ is known, the **synchronous reactance** is obtained by subtracting the known (or separately measured) armature resistance: $$X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Armature resistance $R_a$** is usually measured directly by a **DC resistance test** (or volt-ampere method) on the stator winding, since during operation it is AC-resistance and slightly different. KTU expects the *DC value* for calculations unless otherwise stated. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------------------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | **rpm** | $P$ = number of poles | | 2 | Induced EMF per phase | $E_f = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$ | **V** | EMF equation of alternator | | 3 | Synchronous impedance | $Z_s = \dfrac{V_{oc,\text{phase}}}{I_{sc,\text{phase}}}$ | $\Omega$/phase | Direct from OC and SC tests | | 4 | Synchronous reactance | $X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2}$ | $\Omega$/phase | Subtraction method | | 5 | % Synchronous impedance | $\% Z_s = \dfrac{I_{rated} \cdot Z_s}{V_{rated,\text{phase}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Indicates voltage regulation quality | | 6 | Short-Circuit Ratio | $SCR = \dfrac{I_f \text{ for rated OC voltage}}{I_f \text{ for rated SC current}}$ | **dimensionless** | Inverse of PU $Z_s$ | | 7 | SCR in terms of $Z_s$ | $SCR = \dfrac{1}{Z_{s,\text{pu}}}$ | **dimensionless** | For cylindrical rotor: 0.5–0.7 | | 8 | Voltage Regulation (EMF method) | $\% \text{Reg} = \dfrac{\vert E_0 \vert - \vert V_t \vert}{\vert V_t \vert} \times 100$ | **%** | Negative for leading PF | | 9 | Per-phase OCC voltage | $V_{oc,\text{phase}} = \dfrac{V_{oc,\text{line}}}{\sqrt{3}}$ | **V** | For star-connected armature | | 10 | Per-phase SC current | $I_{sc,\text{phase}} = I_{sc,\text{line}}$ | **A** | For star-connected armature | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** The **SCR** dictates the *operating performance* of a synchronous machine. A **low SCR** (0.5–0.7) indicates a *stiff machine* (cylindrical rotor) with poor voltage regulation but high stability margin. A **high SCR** (1.0–1.5) indicates a *salient-pole machine* with good regulation but lower steady-state stability limit. ## 2.4 Concept of Short-Circuit Ratio (SCR) The SCR is defined as the **ratio of the field current required to produce rated open-circuit voltage** to the **field current required to produce rated short-circuit current** (with armature shorted): $$SCR = \frac{I_{f,\text{OCC @ rated }V_{oc}}}{I_{f,\text{SCC @ rated }I_{sc}}}$$ Geometrically, this is the ratio of the *OCC height* to the *SCC height*, both at their respective rated values. **Key relationships:** - $SCR \propto \dfrac{1}{Z_s}$ (in per unit) - $SCR$ is **inversely proportional** to the *synchronous reactance* - A high $X_s$ → low SCR → poor voltage regulation, better fault current limit # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of $Z_s$ from OC and SC Test Data ### Step 1: Set up the equivalent circuit A synchronous machine in steady state can be represented by an EMF source $E_f$ in series with an impedance $Z_s = R_a + jX_s$, with terminal voltage $V_t$ across the load. ### Step 2: Apply the test conditions **Open-Circuit Test:** $$V_t = V_{oc} = E_f \quad \text{(since } I_a = 0 \text{ and internal drop is zero)}$$ **Short-Circuit Test:** $$V_t = 0 \implies E_f = I_{sc} \cdot Z_s$$ ### Step 3: Express $Z_s$ directly From the SC test condition: $$Z_s = \frac{E_f}{I_{sc}}$$ But $E_f$ is the same as the open-circuit voltage produced by the *same* $I_f$. Therefore, choosing a particular value of field current $I_f$: $$Z_s = \frac{V_{oc} \text{ (at that } I_f \text{)}}{I_{sc} \text{ (at that } I_f \text{)}}$$ ### Step 4: Per-phase conversion For a star-connected armature: $$V_{oc,\text{phase}} = \frac{V_{oc,\text{line}}}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad I_{sc,\text{phase}} = I_{sc,\text{line}}$$ So the final expression for synchronous impedance per phase becomes: $$Z_s = \frac{V_{oc,\text{line}} / \sqrt{3}}{I_{sc,\text{line}}}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Pattern) **Problem Statement:** A 3-phase, star-connected, 50 Hz, 4-pole synchronous generator has a rating of **10 kVA, 400 V**. On the open-circuit test at rated speed, the field current of **2.5 A** produces a line voltage of **400 V**. On the short-circuit test, the same field current of **2.5 A** produces an armature current of **rated value**. The armature resistance per phase is **0.6 $\Omega$**. Calculate: 1. Synchronous impedance $Z_s$ per phase 2. Synchronous reactance $X_s$ per phase 3. Short-Circuit Ratio (SCR) 4. % Synchronous Impedance ### Step 1: Identify the ratings Rated line voltage $V_L = 400$ V, star-connected → $V_{ph} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94$ V. Rated line current: $$I_L = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \frac{10{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400} = 14.43 \text{ A}$$ ### Step 2: Calculate $Z_s$ Given: At $I_f = 2.5$ A, $V_{oc,\text{line}} = 400$ V and $I_{sc,\text{line}} = 14.43$ A. $$V_{oc,\text{phase}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V}$$ $$Z_s = \frac{V_{oc,\text{phase}}}{I_{sc,\text{phase}}} = \frac{230.94}{14.43} = 16.0 \, \Omega/\text{phase}$$ ### Step 3: Calculate $X_s$ $$X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2} = \sqrt{16.0^2 - 0.6^2} = \sqrt{256 - 0.36} = \sqrt{255.64}$$ $$X_s = 15.99 \approx 16.0 \, \Omega/\text{phase}$$ (Note: $R_a$ is often so small that $X_s \approx Z_s$ for cylindrical rotor machines.) ### Step 4: Calculate % $Z_s$ $$\% Z_s = \frac{I_{rated} \cdot Z_s}{V_{rated,\text{phase}}} \times 100 = \frac{14.43 \times 16.0}{230.94} \times 100$$ $$\% Z_s = \frac{230.88}{230.94} \times 100 \approx 99.97\% \approx 100\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Such a high %$Z_s$ implies a very poor voltage regulation, which is unrealistic for a healthy machine. The data has been chosen for pedagogical clarity. Real machines have %$Z_s$ in the range of **8%–20%** for cylindrical rotor and **15%–30%** for salient-pole machines. ### Step 5: Calculate SCR For a star-connected machine, the rated OCC voltage occurs at $I_f = 2.5$ A (given directly). The rated SCC current (14.43 A) also occurs at $I_f = 2.5$ A. Therefore: $$SCR = \frac{I_{f,\text{OCC @ rated}}}{I_{f,\text{SCC @ rated}}} = \frac{2.5}{2.5} = 1.0$$ > [!NOTE] > A SCR of 1.0 indicates the machine has $Z_{s,\text{pu}} = 1.0$, which is consistent with the calculated % $Z_s \approx 100\%$. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Test When? | Condition | Test Used | What Is Determined | |-----------|-----------|-------------------| | Need to find $E_f$ vs. $I_f$ relation | **OC Test** | OCC curve, used to find generated EMF | | Need to find $I_{sc}$ vs. $I_f$ relation | **SC Test** | SCC curve, used for stability analysis | | Need to find $Z_s$ | **Both** (combined) | $Z_s = V_{oc} / I_{sc}$ at same $I_f$ | | Need to find $X_s$ | **Both + DC test** | $X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2}$ | | Need to find regulation | **Both + DC test** | EMF method, MMF method, or ASA method | | Need to find $R_a$ | **DC resistance test** (or ammeter-voltmeter) | $R_a = V_{DC} / I_{DC}$, then AC = 1.6 × DC (approx) | ## 3.4 Why SCC is Linear and OCC is Non-Linear - **OCC** is curved at higher $I_f$ due to **magnetic saturation** of the iron core. The relation $E_f \propto \phi$ becomes non-linear as $\phi$ saturates. - **SCC** is essentially a straight line because the armature current is small (limited by $Z_s$), the armature's MMF is small, and the machine operates in the **unsaturated linear region** of the magnetic circuit. The total flux is small, so $I_{sc} \propto I_f$ linearly. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: OC and SC Test Setup The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the physical test setup connections for both the Open-Circuit and Short-Circuit tests, showing the prime mover, the synchronous machine, the field supply, and the measurement instruments. ```mermaid flowchart TB PM[Prime Mover
DC Motor or Engine] -->|Mechanical Shaft| SM[Synchronous Machine
3-Phase Stator + Rotor] SM -->|Stator Terminals U V W| SW[Selector Switch
OC or SC Position] SW -->|Position A: OC| OCBox[Open-Circuited Terminals
Voltmeter V across UV VW WU] SW -->|Position B: SC| SCBox[Short-Circuited Terminals
Ammeter A in series] FLD[DC Field Excitation Supply
Variable Voltage Source] -->|Slip Rings| SM FLD -->|Field Ammeter Af| FLDMeas[Measure If] OCBox --> MOC[Record VOC vs If
Plot OCC Curve] SCBox --> MSC[Record ISC vs If
Plot SCC Curve] MOC --> COMB[Combine OCC and SCC
at same If] MSC --> COMB COMB --> CALC[Calculate Zs, Xs, SCR
and Voltage Regulation] ``` ## 4.2 Step-by-Step Procedure Flow Chart ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Start: Disconnect Load] --> B[Run Prime Mover at Ns] B --> C{Test Type} C -->|OC Test| D[Open Stator Terminals] C -->|SC Test| E[Short Stator Terminals
via Ammeter] D --> F[Increase If in Steps
up to 125% of rated V] E --> G[Increase If in Steps
up to 125% of rated I] F --> H[Record VOC at each If] G --> I[Record ISC at each If] H --> J[Plot OCC: VOC vs If] I --> K[Plot SCC: ISC vs If] J --> L[Read VOC at If for rated V] K --> M[Read ISC at If for rated I] L --> N[Zs = VOC phase / ISC phase] M --> N N --> O[Measure Ra by DC Test] O --> P[Compute Xs = sqrt Zs^2 - Ra^2] P --> Q[Compute SCR and %Zs] ``` ## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Representation ```mermaid flowchart LR Ef[Ef
Induced EMF] -->|Series| Ra[Ra
Armature Resistance] Ra -->|Series| Xl[Xl
Leakage Reactance] Xl -->|Series| Xa[Xa
Armature Reaction Reactance] Xa --> Vt[Vt
Terminal Voltage] Vt --> Load[Load
Z Load] Load -->|Return Path| Ef Xl -.->|Combined as| Xs[Xs = Xl + Xa
Synchronous Reactance] Xa -.-> Xs ``` ## 4.4 Phasor Diagram Block — EMF Method (for context) ```mermaid flowchart LR V[Vt
Reference Phasor] --> AddOA[Add IaRa drop
in phase with Ia] AddOA --> AddOX[Add IaXs drop
90 deg ahead of Ia] AddOX --> E[Ef
Resultant Phasor] subgraph Lagging_PF Ia1[Ia lags Vt by theta] end Ia1 --> AddOA ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Adaptation Rationale:** Detailed physical drawings of machine windings and the laboratory wiring are impractical in text form. The block diagrams above map the **functional interactions** of all test components in a way that directly supports KTU's emphasis on **understanding the test procedure and data flow**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]` **Define the following terms with reference to a synchronous machine:** **(a)** Synchronous impedance **(b)** Short-Circuit Ratio **(c)** Percentage synchronous impedance **Model Answer:** **(a) Synchronous Impedance ($Z_s$):** It is the ratio of the open-circuit phase voltage to the short-circuit phase current at the same field current and synchronous speed. $$Z_s = \frac{V_{oc,\text{phase}}}{I_{sc,\text{phase}}} \quad [\Omega/\text{phase}]$$ **(b) Short-Circuit Ratio (SCR):** It is the ratio of the field current required to produce rated open-circuit voltage on the OCC to the field current required to produce rated short-circuit current on the SCC. $$SCR = \frac{I_{f,\text{rated VOC}}}{I_{f,\text{rated ISC}}}$$ **(c) Percentage Synchronous Impedance (% $Z_s$):** It is the ratio of the voltage drop across $Z_s$ at rated current to the rated phase voltage, expressed as a percentage. $$\% Z_s = \frac{I_{rated} \cdot Z_s}{V_{rated,\text{phase}}} \times 100$$ ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]` **Why is the short-circuit characteristic (SCC) of a synchronous machine a straight line, while the open-circuit characteristic (OCC) is curved at higher field currents?** **Model Answer:** - The **OCC** is curved at higher field currents due to **magnetic saturation** of the iron core. As the field current increases, the magnetic flux $\phi$ in the iron path saturates, so the EMF $E_f \propto \phi$ no longer increases linearly with $I_f$. - The **SCC** is a straight line because during the short-circuit test, the armature current is small (limited by the synchronous impedance), keeping the operating flux low. The machine operates in the **linear (unsaturated) region** of the magnetic circuit, so $I_{sc} \propto I_f$ throughout. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Main Exam, Q. Module 4(b)]` **(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the **Open-Circuit (OC) test** and **Short-Circuit (SC) test** performed on a synchronous generator. State the purpose of each test. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A 3-phase, star-connected, 50 Hz, 6-pole synchronous generator rated **500 kVA, 3.3 kV** has an armature resistance of **0.2 $\Omega$/phase**. On the OC test at rated speed, the field current of **20 A** gives rated open-circuit voltage. The SC test at the same field current gives a line current of **87.5 A**. Calculate: - (i) Synchronous impedance $Z_s$ per phase - (ii) Synchronous reactance $X_s$ per phase - (iii) Short-Circuit Ratio (SCR) - (iv) % Synchronous impedance **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — OC and SC Test Description **Open-Circuit Test:** 1. The armature (stator) terminals are kept **open** (disconnected from any load). 2. The rotor is driven by a prime mover (DC motor or engine) at **synchronous speed** $N_s = 120f/P$. 3. The field winding is excited by a DC source through slip rings, and the field current $I_f$ is varied in steps from zero. 4. At each step, the **open-circuit line voltage** $V_{oc}$ is measured. 5. $I_f$ is increased until the line voltage reaches approximately **125% of rated voltage**. 6. A plot of $V_{oc}$ (or $E_{ph}$) vs. $I_f$ gives the **Open-Circuit Characteristic (OCC)**, which resembles the **magnetisation curve** of the machine. 7. **Purpose:** To determine the **no-load saturation curve** and find the **generated EMF** $E_f$ for any given field current. **Short-Circuit Test:** 1. The armature terminals are **short-circuited** through three ammeters (one in each phase), with very low-impedance connections. 2. The rotor is driven at synchronous speed. 3. The field current $I_f$ is gradually increased in steps from zero. 4. At each step, the **armature current** $I_{sc}$ is measured. 5. $I_f$ is increased until $I_{sc}$ reaches approximately **125% of rated armature current**. 6. A plot of $I_{sc}$ vs. $I_f$ gives the **Short-Circuit Characteristic (SCC)**, which is a **straight line passing through the origin**. 7. **Purpose:** Combined with the OCC, it allows determination of $Z_s$ and $X_s$. #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given data:** - $S = 500$ kVA, $V_L = 3.3$ kV, star-connected, $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 6$ - $R_a = 0.2 \, \Omega$/phase - At $I_f = 20$ A: $V_{oc,\text{line}} = 3.3$ kV and $I_{sc,\text{line}} = 87.5$ A **Step 1: Compute rated values** - Phase voltage: $V_{ph} = \dfrac{3300}{\sqrt{3}} = 1905.26$ V - Rated line current: $I_L = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \dfrac{500{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 3300} = 87.47 \approx 87.5$ A **Step 2: Synchronous Impedance** $$Z_s = \frac{V_{oc,\text{phase}}}{I_{sc,\text{phase}}} = \frac{1905.26}{87.5} = 21.77 \, \Omega/\text{phase}$$ **Step 3: Synchronous Reactance** $$X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2} = \sqrt{(21.77)^2 - (0.2)^2} = \sqrt{473.93 - 0.04} = \sqrt{473.89}$$ $$X_s = 21.77 \, \Omega/\text{phase}$$ (since $R_a \ll Z_s$, $X_s \approx Z_s$) **Step 4: Short-Circuit Ratio** Both rated VOC and rated ISC occur at $I_f = 20$ A, hence: $$SCR = \frac{I_{f,\text{OCC @ rated}}}{I_{f,\text{SCC @ rated}}} = \frac{20}{20} = 1.0$$ **Step 5: % Synchronous Impedance** $$\% Z_s = \frac{I_{rated} \cdot Z_s}{V_{rated,\text{phase}}} \times 100 = \frac{87.5 \times 21.77}{1905.26} \times 100$$ $$\% Z_s = \frac{1904.88}{1905.26} \times 100 \approx 99.98\% \approx 100\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common student error is forgetting the **$\sqrt{3}$** factor when converting line voltage to phase voltage for a star-connected machine. This single mistake can cascade and yield wrong values for $Z_s$, $X_s$, SCR, and %$Z_s$ — losing 2–3 marks. Always state whether the machine is **star or delta connected**, and convert consistently. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary Exam, Q. Module 4(b)] — Alternative Choice]` **(a)** Define **percentage synchronous impedance** and **short-circuit ratio**. Derive the relationship between SCR and $Z_s$ in per unit. **[7 marks]** **(b)** The OCC of a 3-phase, star-connected, 1500 kVA, 6.6 kV alternator gave the following readings at rated speed: | Field current $I_f$ (A) | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 | 70 | 80 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | OCV (line) (V) | 1500 | 3000 | 4200 | 5200 | 5800 | 6300 | 6700 | 7000 | | SC current (A) | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | 130 | The SCC is a straight line passing through the origin and giving 130 A at $I_f = 80$ A. The armature resistance is **0.5 $\Omega$/phase**. Calculate the **synchronous reactance** at $I_f = 80$ A, and the **SCR** of the machine. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Definitions and Derivation **Definition of % Synchronous Impedance:** The percentage synchronous impedance is defined as the ratio of the voltage drop across the synchronous impedance at rated armature current to the rated phase voltage, multiplied by 100. $$\% Z_s = \frac{I_{rated} \cdot Z_s}{V_{rated,\text{phase}}} \times 100$$ **Definition of SCR:** The Short-Circuit Ratio is the ratio of the field current required to generate rated open-circuit voltage on the OCC to the field current required to circulate rated short-circuit current on the SCC. $$SCR = \frac{I_{f, \text{OCC @ rated VOC}}}{I_{f, \text{SCC @ rated ISC}}}$$ **Derivation of $SCR = \dfrac{1}{Z_{s,\text{pu}}}$:** Consider a field current $I_f$ that produces rated short-circuit current $I_{sc,\text{rated}}$ when the armature is shorted. The corresponding open-circuit EMF would be $E_f = I_{sc,\text{rated}} \cdot Z_s$. The field current $I_f'$ required to produce the rated open-circuit voltage $V_{rated}$ on the OCC is generally *different* from $I_f$ (since OCC and SCC have different slopes). In the **unsaturated linear region** (low $I_f$), the OCC and SCC have proportional relationships, and the ratio of field currents equals the inverse ratio of corresponding flux (and hence EMFs): $$SCR = \frac{I_{f, \text{OCC @ rated VOC}}}{I_{f, \text{SCC @ rated ISC}}} = \frac{V_{rated} / K}{I_{sc,\text{rated}} / K} = \frac{V_{rated}}{I_{sc,\text{rated}} \cdot Z_s} = \frac{V_{rated}}{I_{rated} \cdot Z_s}$$ But $\dfrac{V_{rated}}{I_{rated} \cdot Z_s} = \dfrac{1}{Z_{s,\text{pu}}}$ (since $Z_{s,\text{pu}} = \dfrac{Z_s \cdot I_{rated}}{V_{rated}}$). Therefore: $$\boxed{SCR = \frac{1}{Z_{s,\text{pu}}}}$$ #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given data:** - $S = 1500$ kVA, $V_L = 6.6$ kV, star-connected - $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$/phase - At $I_f = 80$ A: OCV (line) = 7000 V and SC current (line) = 130 A **Step 1: Compute rated phase voltage and current** - $V_{ph} = \dfrac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5$ V - $I_L = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600} = 131.2$ A **Step 2: Synchronous Impedance at $I_f = 80$ A** Open-circuit phase voltage at $I_f = 80$ A: $V_{oc,\text{phase}} = \dfrac{7000}{\sqrt{3}} = 4041.5$ V $$Z_s = \frac{V_{oc,\text{phase}}}{I_{sc,\text{phase}}} = \frac{4041.5}{130} = 31.09 \, \Omega/\text{phase}$$ **Step 3: Synchronous Reactance at $I_f = 80$ A** $$X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2} = \sqrt{(31.09)^2 - (0.5)^2} = \sqrt{966.6 - 0.25} = \sqrt{966.35}$$ $$X_s = 31.08 \, \Omega/\text{phase}$$ **Step 4: Short-Circuit Ratio** From the OCC table, the rated OCV of **6600 V (line)** occurs at approximately $I_f \approx 65$ A (interpolating: between 60 A → 6300 V and 70 A → 6700 V). Using linear interpolation: $$I_{f,\text{OCC @ 6600V}} = 60 + \frac{6600 - 6300}{6700 - 6300} \times (70 - 60) = 60 + \frac{300}{400} \times 10 = 60 + 7.5 = 67.5 \text{ A}$$ From the SCC (straight line), the rated SC current of 131.2 A corresponds to: $$I_{f,\text{SCC @ 131.2 A}} = \frac{131.2}{130} \times 80 = 80.7 \text{ A}$$ Therefore: $$SCR = \frac{I_{f,\text{OCC @ rated}}}{I_{f,\text{SCC @ rated}}} = \frac{67.5}{80.7} = 0.836$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (SCC/Interpolation):** Many students make the mistake of using the *same* $I_f$ value for both numerator and denominator in the SCR formula. **The SCR requires two different field currents** — one that produces rated VOC, and another (generally different) that produces rated ISC. Forgetting the interpolation step or using $I_f = 80$ A for both will cost at least **2 marks**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] The **synchronous impedance** $Z_s = R_a + jX_s$ is found by combining the OC test and SC test data at the *same* field current. - [ ] The **OC test** gives $E_f$ (open-circuit voltage) vs. $I_f$ — a *non-linear curve* due to **magnetic saturation**. - [ ] The **SC test** gives $I_{sc}$ (short-circuit current) vs. $I_f$ — a *straight line* because the machine operates in the **unsaturated region** with low flux. - [ ] The formula $Z_s = \dfrac{V_{oc,\text{phase}}}{I_{sc,\text{phase}}}$ uses **per-phase** quantities — always divide line voltage by $\sqrt{3}$ for star-connected machines. - [ ] $X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2}$ where $R_a$ is measured by the **DC resistance test** (voltmeter-ammeter method). - [ ] The **Short-Circuit Ratio (SCR)** is the ratio of the field current needed to produce rated VOC to the field current needed to produce rated ISC. - [ ] In per-unit, $SCR = \dfrac{1}{Z_{s,\text{pu}}}$. - [ ] **Cylindrical rotor machines** have $SCR \approx 0.5$–$0.7$ (high $X_s$, stiff). - [ ] **Salient-pole machines** have $SCR \approx 1.0$–$1.5$ (low $X_s$, better regulation). - [ ] A **low SCR** means poor voltage regulation but higher transient stability limit. - [ ] A **high SCR** means better voltage regulation but lower fault current contribution. - [ ] The **% synchronous impedance** is numerically equal to the percentage voltage regulation *approximation* for the EMF method at unity power factor. - [ ] During the SC test, the field current is kept *small* (≈ 25% of rated) to avoid drawing dangerously high currents — this is a **safety-critical** experimental detail often asked as a 2-mark question. - [ ] Both OC and SC tests must be performed at **rated synchronous speed** for the results to be valid. - [ ] The phasor diagram for the EMF method: $E_0 = V_t + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s$ — draw the **resistive drop in phase with $I_a$** and the **reactive drop 90° ahead of $I_a$**.

topics.title: Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🔷 1.1 What is a Three-Phase Induction Motor?

A **Three-Phase Induction Motor (TIM)** is a self-starting, robust, and maintenance-free AC machine that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy based on the principle of **electromagnetic induction** between a stationary stator and a rotating rotor. It is the **workhorse of modern industry**, driving pumps, fans, compressors, conveyors, and machine tools in approximately **80% of all industrial applications**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A three-phase induction motor is a singly-excited AC machine in which the stator winding is connected to a 3-phase supply and the rotor derives its power through electromagnetic induction from the stator field at a speed less than the synchronous speed."*

### 🔹 1.2 The "Why" Behind Module 4 Numerical Problems

In Module 4, you are required to **analyze the steady-state performance** of a three-phase induction motor using:
- The **exact per-phase equivalent circuit**
- The **approximate equivalent circuit** (with magnetizing branch shifted to stator terminals)
- The **power flow stages** (Stator Input → Air-Gap Power → Mechanical Power Developed → Shaft Output)
- The **torque-slip characteristic**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight (KTU Examiner Focus):**
> Every numerical problem in this module ultimately traces back to **two master equations**:
> 1. **Power Division:** $P_{gap} : P_{cu,rotor} : P_{m,dev} = 1 : s : (1-s)$
> 2. **Torque Equation:** $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$
>
> Master these two, and you master Module 4 numericals.

## 🔷 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Slip Conveyor Belt"

Imagine a **rotating magnetic field** as a giant **circular conveyor belt** moving at synchronous speed $N_s$. The **rotor conductors** are like **fitness enthusiasts running on this belt**.

- If the runner matches the belt's speed perfectly → **No relative motion** → **No induced EMF** → **No torque** (useless!)
- If the runner stops completely → **Maximum relative motion** → **High induced EMF, but rotor is inefficient** (high losses)
- The **sweet spot** is somewhere in between, where the runner falls slightly behind the belt by a small percentage called the **slip ($s$)** — this is exactly when **maximum useful mechanical work** is done.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world reference:** A ceiling fan runs at ~1440 rpm when the supply is 50 Hz, 4-pole ($N_s = 1500$ rpm). The slip is 4%, which is the **typical full-load operating zone** for industrial motors.

## 🔷 1.4 Standard Assumptions in KTU Numerical Problems

| Assumption | Why It Is Made |
|------------|----------------|
| Stator and rotor windings are **symmetrical and balanced** | Permits use of a **single per-phase equivalent circuit** |
| Magnetizing current is **purely reactive** | Core loss lumped into a separate shunt resistance $R_c$ (when given) |
| Rotor EMF $E_2$ and reactance $X_2$ are **referred to the stator side** ($E_2'$, $X_2'$) | Enables single-loop circuit analysis |
| Mechanical losses = Friction + Windage only (if not specified) | Simplifies the power flow diagram |
| Synchronous speed is **constant** for a given supply | Decouples electrical and mechanical sub-problems |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔷 2.1 The Power Flow — Stage by Stage Logic

A three-phase induction motor's power flow follows a strict **cascaded deduction chain**. The key is that **rotor copper loss is the only "tax" paid between air-gap power and mechanical power developed**.

$$\boxed{P_{input} \to P_{stator\,losses} \to P_{gap} \to P_{cu,rotor} \to P_{m,dev} \to P_{mech\,losses} \to P_{shaft}}$$

Where:
- $P_{stator\,losses} = P_{cu,stator} + P_{core}$ (stator copper + iron loss)
- $P_{gap} = $ Power crossing the air-gap from stator to rotor
- $P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{gap}$ (rotor copper loss is the fraction $s$ of air-gap power)
- $P_{m,dev} = (1-s) \cdot P_{gap}$ (mechanical power developed)
- $P_{shaft} = P_{m,dev} - P_{mech\,losses}$ (after friction, windage, stray load loss)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Induction Motor Power Stages:**
> The **air-gap power** is the parent quantity, and it splits between the **rotor copper loss** ($s$ share) and the **developed mechanical power** ($(1-s)$ share). The ratio $P_{cu,rotor} : P_{m,dev} = s : (1-s)$ is the **single most useful fact** in any KTU power flow problem.

## 🔷 2.2 The Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit (Thevenin & Exact)

In the **exact equivalent circuit**, the rotor branch is modelled as $R_2'/s$, which is split for analysis convenience as:
- $R_2'$ → actual rotor resistance (carries $P_{cu,rotor}$)
- $R_2'(1-s)/s$ → mechanical load equivalent resistance (carries $P_{m,dev}$)

### 🔹 Thevenin Equivalent (Used for Torque Problems)

To simplify torque calculations, the circuit is reduced to a **single-series Thevenin equivalent** seen from the rotor terminals:

$$V_{th} = V_1 \cdot \frac{jX_m}{(R_1 + jX_1) + jX_m}, \quad Z_{th} = \frac{(R_1 + jX_1)(jX_m)}{R_1 + jX_1 + jX_m}$$

The Thevenin form for **total rotor-side impedance at slip $s$**:
$$Z_{rotor} = \frac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2'$$

## 🔷 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **The table below is the ONLY reference you need during exam preparation for Module 4 numericals. Print it. Memorize it.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | $P$ = number of poles (even integer) |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | pu (0 to 1) | $s = 0$ at synchronous, $s = 1$ at standstill |
| 3 | Slip in rpm | $N_{slip} = N_s - N_r$ | rpm | The "missing rpm" relative to field |
| 4 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s f$ | Hz | At standstill: $f_r = f$ |
| 5 | Rotor EMF at slip | $E_{2s} = s E_{2(\text{standstill})}$ | V/phase | Linear with slip |
| 6 | Rotor reactance at slip | $X_{2s} = s X_{2(\text{standstill})}$ | Ω/phase | Linear with slip |
| 7 | Synchronous angular velocity | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | For torque equations |
| 8 | Gross (developed) torque | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | N·m | Uses **referred** $E_2$, $R_2$, $X_2$ if $V_{th}$ used |
| 9 | Slip at max torque | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | pu | Independent of load! |
| 10 | Maximum torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2}{X_2}$ | N·m | **Independent of $R_2$** (a remarkable result) |
| 11 | Starting torque ($s=1$) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ | N·m | Often less than full-load torque in cage motors |
| 12 | Air-gap power | $P_{gap} = \dfrac{P_{cu,rotor}}{s} = \dfrac{P_{m,dev}}{1-s}$ | W | The "parent" power |
| 13 | Developed mechanical power | $P_{m,dev} = (1-s) P_{gap} = \dfrac{3 I_2'^2 R_2' (1-s)}{s}$ | W | Across the air gap |
| 14 | Shaft output power | $P_{shaft} = T_{shaft} \cdot \omega_r$ | W | Where $\omega_r = (1-s)\omega_s$ |
| 15 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{input}} \times 100\%$ | % | Total efficiency |
| 16 | Power factor | $\cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{input}}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_L}$ | pu | For 3-phase line values |
| 17 | Torque ratio to max | $\dfrac{T}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2 a s}{a^2 + s^2}$ where $a = \dfrac{R_2}{s_{max} X_2}$ | pu | Useful for torque-speed curve plotting |

## 🔷 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry / Equipment | Why Induction Motor is the Only Choice |
|----------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Pumps & Compressors** (water treatment, HVAC, petrochemical) | Constant-speed, high reliability, no sparking (explosion-safe) |
| **Conveyor Belts & Mixers** (food, mining, cement) | High starting torque, rugged construction |
| **Elevators & Cranes** (with VFD control) | V/f control gives variable speed with constant torque |
| **Electric Vehicles (Tesla, Tata, Hyundai EVs)** | Squirrel-cage induction motor with vector control (Field-Oriented Control) |
| **Domestic Appliances** (washing machines, refrigerators) | Single-phase capacitor-start or shaded-pole variants |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection:** Modern Tesla Model S/X uses a **3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor** with a copper rotor bar design, controlled by a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** that adjusts the stator frequency from near-DC (during start) to ~200 Hz at high speed. The KTU module gives you the **theoretical foundation** for this very real engineering system.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Numerical Solutions (Solved Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following problems are **modeled on the KTU 2024 Scheme question patterns**, including 2-mark sub-questions, 7-mark derivations, and the classic 14-mark two-part questions. **Each step is fully written out** so you can follow the logic in real-time during exam preparation.

---

## 📘 PROBLEM 1: Slip, Rotor Frequency, and Rotor EMF (Basic, 5-Mark Type)

### **Question:**
A 3-phase, **50 Hz, 6-pole** induction motor runs at a full-load speed of **940 rpm**. If the standstill rotor EMF per phase is **120 V**, calculate:
1. Synchronous speed $N_s$
2. Slip at full load $s$
3. Rotor frequency $f_r$
4. Rotor EMF per phase at full load

### **Solution:**

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 6$, $N_r = 940$ rpm, $E_{2(standstill)} = 120$ V/phase

#### 🔸 Step 1: Synchronous Speed
$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

**Explanation:** For any 6-pole machine at 50 Hz, the field rotates at exactly 1000 rpm. A 6-pole machine cannot run at any other synchronous speed for a 50 Hz supply.

#### 🔸 Step 2: Slip
$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1000 - 940}{1000} = \frac{60}{1000} = 0.06 \text{ pu} = 6\%$$

**Explanation:** The rotor lags the field by 6%. This is a healthy full-load slip (typical range: 3–8%).

#### 🔸 Step 3: Rotor Frequency
$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.06 \times 50 = 3 \text{ Hz}$$

**Explanation:** The relative motion between the rotor and the field is small, so the frequency "seen" by the rotor conductors is only 3 Hz, even though the stator supply is 50 Hz.

#### 🔸 Step 4: Rotor EMF at Full Load
$$E_{2(s)} = s \cdot E_{2(standstill)} = 0.06 \times 120 = 7.2 \text{ V/phase}$$

**Explanation:** EMF is directly proportional to slip. At full load, only 6% of the standstill EMF is induced.

### ✅ **Final Answers:**
$$\boxed{N_s = 1000 \text{ rpm}, \quad s = 0.06, \quad f_r = 3 \text{ Hz}, \quad E_{2(s)} = 7.2 \text{ V}}$$

---

## 📘 PROBLEM 2: Power Flow Diagram and Output Torque (Standard 7-Mark Type)

### **Question:**
A 3-phase, **400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole** induction motor runs at **1440 rpm** with the following data:
- Total stator copper loss: **1500 W**
- Rotor copper loss: **1000 W**
- Iron (core) loss: **1200 W**
- Mechanical (friction + windage) loss: **500 W**

Calculate:
1. Air-gap power $P_{gap}$
2. Mechanical power developed $P_{m,dev}$
3. Shaft output power $P_{shaft}$
4. Output torque $T_{out}$ in N·m
5. Input power and overall efficiency

### **Solution:**

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$ poles, $N_r = 1440$ rpm

#### 🔸 Step 1: Synchronous Speed and Slip
$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$
$$s = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04$$

#### 🔸 Step 2: Air-Gap Power
Using the **golden rule** — rotor copper loss is $s$ times the air-gap power:
$$P_{gap} = \frac{P_{cu,rotor}}{s} = \frac{1000}{0.04} = 25000 \text{ W} = 25 \text{ kW}$$

**Explanation:** Even though only 1 kW is lost in the rotor, the parent air-gap power is 25 kW because the rotor "sees" the entire 25 kW — the copper loss is just the $s = 0.04$ portion.

#### 🔸 Step 3: Mechanical Power Developed
$$P_{m,dev} = (1 - s) P_{gap} = (1 - 0.04) \times 25000 = 0.96 \times 25000 = 24000 \text{ W} = 24 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification check:** $P_{cu,rotor} + P_{m,dev} = 1000 + 24000 = 25000$ W ✓ (matches $P_{gap}$)

#### 🔸 Step 4: Shaft Output Power
$$P_{shaft} = P_{m,dev} - P_{mech\,losses} = 24000 - 500 = 23500 \text{ W} = 23.5 \text{ kW}$$

#### 🔸 Step 5: Output Torque
The output torque is computed using the **actual rotor speed**:
$$\omega_r = \frac{2 \pi N_r}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1440}{60} = 48 \pi = 150.796 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_{out} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\omega_r} = \frac{23500}{150.796} = 155.85 \text{ N·m}$$

#### 🔸 Step 6: Input Power and Efficiency
Total stator losses = $P_{cu,stator} + P_{core} = 1500 + 1200 = 2700$ W
$$P_{input} = P_{gap} + P_{stator\,losses} = 25000 + 2700 = 27700 \text{ W} = 27.7 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{input}} \times 100\% = \frac{23500}{27700} \times 100\% = 84.84\%$$

### ✅ **Final Answers:**
$$\boxed{P_{gap} = 25 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{m,dev} = 24 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{shaft} = 23.5 \text{ kW}, \quad T_{out} = 155.85 \text{ N·m}, \quad \eta = 84.84\%}$$

---

## 📘 PROBLEM 3: Slip at Maximum Torque and Torque Calculations (7-Mark Type)

### **Question:**
A 3-phase, **6-pole, 50 Hz** induction motor has the following referred-to-stator parameters per phase:
- Rotor resistance $R_2' = 0.4\ \Omega$
- Rotor reactance at standstill $X_2' = 2\ \Omega$
- Rotor EMF (referred) at standstill $E_2' = 200\ \text{V/phase}$

Calculate:
1. Synchronous speed in rad/s
2. Slip at which maximum torque occurs
3. Maximum torque developed
4. Full-load torque at slip $s = 0.04$
5. Starting torque at $s = 1$
6. Ratio of starting to maximum torque

### **Solution:**

**Given:** $P = 6$, $f = 50$ Hz, $R_2' = 0.4\ \Omega$, $X_2' = 2\ \Omega$, $E_2' = 200$ V

#### 🔸 Step 1: Synchronous Angular Velocity
$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$
$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

#### 🔸 Step 2: Slip at Maximum Torque
$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{X_2'} = \frac{0.4}{2} = 0.2$$

**Explanation:** $s_{max}$ is a property of the rotor design. It does NOT depend on the load. A high $s_{max}$ implies high starting torque (good for elevators).

#### 🔸 Step 3: Maximum Torque
$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2'^2}{X_2'} = \frac{3}{2 \times 104.72} \cdot \frac{200^2}{2}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{209.44} \cdot \frac{40000}{2} = 0.01432 \times 20000 = 286.48 \text{ N·m}$$

**Note the powerful result:** $T_{max}$ does NOT contain $R_2'$. Changing $R_2'$ shifts the slip at which max torque occurs but does NOT change the value of $T_{max}$.

#### 🔸 Step 4: Full-Load Torque at s = 0.04
Using the general torque equation:
$$T_{fl} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + (s X_2')^2}$$

Numerator: $s E_2'^2 R_2' = 0.04 \times 200^2 \times 0.4 = 0.04 \times 40000 \times 0.4 = 640$
Denominator: $R_2'^2 + (s X_2')^2 = 0.4^2 + (0.04 \times 2)^2 = 0.16 + 0.0064 = 0.1664$

$$T_{fl} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{640}{0.1664} = 0.02865 \times 3846.15 = 110.20 \text{ N·m}$$

#### 🔸 Step 5: Starting Torque (s = 1)
$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + X_2'^2} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{200^2 \times 0.4}{0.4^2 + 2^2}$$

$$T_{st} = 0.02865 \cdot \frac{16000}{0.16 + 4} = 0.02865 \cdot \frac{16000}{4.16} = 0.02865 \times 3846.15 = 110.20 \text{ N·m}$$

**Interesting observation:** For this motor, $T_{st} \approx T_{fl}$ because $s_{max} = 0.2$ is far from operating region $s = 0.04$.

#### 🔸 Step 6: Ratio of Starting to Maximum Torque
$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \frac{110.20}{286.48} = 0.3847 = 38.47\%$$

**Verification using the shortcut formula:** $T_{st}/T_{max} = 2a/(1+a^2)$ where $a = R_2'/(s_{max} X_2') = 0.4/(0.2 \times 2) = 1$
$$T_{st}/T_{max} = 2(1)/(1+1^2) = 2/2 = 1.0$$

**WAIT — Discrepancy Alert!** Let me recompute. When $a = R_2/s_{max} X_2 = 1$, this means the operating slip equals $s_{max}$ at $s = s_{max} = 0.2$, NOT at $s = 0.04$.

Re-evaluating $a$ for **starting** ($s=1$): $a_{st} = R_2'/(1 \cdot X_2') = 0.4/2 = 0.2$
$$T_{st}/T_{max} = \frac{2 a_{st}}{a_{st}^2 + 1} = \frac{2(0.2)}{0.04 + 1} = \frac{0.4}{1.04} = 0.3846 \text{ ✓}$$

### ✅ **Final Answers:**
$$\boxed{\omega_s = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}, \quad s_{max} = 0.2, \quad T_{max} = 286.48 \text{ N·m}, \quad T_{fl} = 110.20 \text{ N·m}, \quad T_{st} = 110.20 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

## 📘 PROBLEM 4: Shaft Power and Input Current from Equivalent Circuit (14-Mark Type)

### **Question:**
A 3-phase, **50 Hz, 4-pole, 400 V** delta-connected cage induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator:
- $R_1 = 0.5\ \Omega$, $X_1 = 1.5\ \Omega$
- $R_2' = 0.6\ \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.5\ \Omega$
- $X_m = 50\ \Omega$ (magnetizing reactance, core loss neglected)

At a slip of **5%**, calculate:
1. The synchronous speed and rotor speed
2. The Thevenin voltage and Thevenin impedance as seen from the rotor branch
3. The rotor current referred to the stator $I_2'$
4. The air-gap power, mechanical power developed, and rotor copper loss
5. The shaft power (assuming 1.5% of $P_{m,dev}$ as mechanical losses)
6. The overall efficiency (assuming stator iron loss = 600 W)

### **Solution:**

**Given:** $V_{L} = 400$ V, delta → $V_{ph} = 400$ V, $R_1 = 0.5\ \Omega$, $X_1 = 1.5\ \Omega$, $R_2' = 0.6\ \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.5\ \Omega$, $X_m = 50\ \Omega$, $s = 0.05$

#### 🔸 Step 1: Synchronous and Rotor Speed
$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$
$$N_r = (1 - s) N_s = 0.95 \times 1500 = 1425 \text{ rpm}$$

#### 🔸 Step 2: Thevenin Equivalent

**Thevenin voltage** (voltage divider across $jX_m$):
$$V_{th} = V_{ph} \cdot \frac{jX_m}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)} = 400 \cdot \frac{j50}{0.5 + j(1.5 + 50)} = 400 \cdot \frac{j50}{0.5 + j51.5}$$

**Magnitude calculation:**
$$\vert V_{th} \vert = 400 \cdot \frac{50}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + 51.5^2}} = 400 \cdot \frac{50}{\sqrt{0.25 + 2652.25}} = 400 \cdot \frac{50}{51.5024}$$
$$\boxed{\vert V_{th} \vert = 400 \cdot 0.97084 = 388.34 \text{ V}}$$

**Angle of $V_{th}$:**
$$\angle V_{th} = 90° - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{51.5}{0.5}\right) = 90° - 89.444° = 0.556°$$

**Thevenin impedance:**
$$Z_{th} = \frac{(R_1 + jX_1)(jX_m)}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)} = \frac{(0.5 + j1.5)(j50)}{0.5 + j51.5}$$

Numerator: $(0.5 + j1.5)(j50) = j25 + j^2 75 = -75 + j25$
$$\vert \text{Num} \vert = \sqrt{75^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{5625 + 625} = \sqrt{6250} = 79.057\ \Omega \text{ at } 180° - 18.43° = 161.57°$$
$$\vert \text{Den} \vert = 51.5024\ \Omega \text{ at } 89.444°$$
$$\vert Z_{th} \vert = \frac{79.057}{51.5024} = 1.535\ \Omega, \quad \angle Z_{th} = 161.57° - 89.444° = 72.13°$$

Converting to rectangular:
$$R_{th} = 1.535 \cos(72.13°) = 1.535 \times 0.3072 = 0.4715\ \Omega$$
$$X_{th} = 1.535 \sin(72.13°) = 1.535 \times 0.9516 = 1.4606\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{Z_{th} = 0.4715 + j1.4606\ \Omega}$$

#### 🔸 Step 3: Rotor Current

**Rotor branch impedance at slip $s = 0.05$:**
$$\frac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2' = \frac{0.6}{0.05} + j1.5 = 12 + j1.5\ \Omega$$

**Total impedance seen by $V_{th}$:**
$$Z_{total} = Z_{th} + \frac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2' = (0.4715 + 12) + j(1.4606 + 1.5) = 12.4715 + j2.9606\ \Omega$$

$$\vert Z_{total} \vert = \sqrt{12.4715^2 + 2.9606^2} = \sqrt{155.54 + 8.765} = \sqrt{164.31} = 12.818\ \Omega$$

**Rotor current:**
$$I_2' = \frac{\vert V_{th} \vert}{\vert Z_{total} \vert} = \frac{388.34}{12.818} = 30.30 \text{ A}$$

#### 🔸 Step 4: Air-Gap Power, Mechanical Power, Rotor Copper Loss

**Rotor copper loss** (across the $R_2'$ resistor):
$$P_{cu,rotor} = 3 I_2'^2 R_2' = 3 \times (30.30)^2 \times 0.6 = 3 \times 918.09 \times 0.6 = 1652.56 \text{ W}$$

**Air-gap power** (using the golden rule $P_{cu,rotor} = s P_{gap}$):
$$P_{gap} = \frac{P_{cu,rotor}}{s} = \frac{1652.56}{0.05} = 33051.2 \text{ W} \approx 33.05 \text{ kW}$$

**Mechanical power developed:**
$$P_{m,dev} = (1-s) P_{gap} = 0.95 \times 33051.2 = 31398.6 \text{ W} \approx 31.40 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification:** $P_{m,dev} = 3 I_2'^2 \cdot R_2' \cdot \frac{1-s}{s} = 1652.56 \times 0.95/0.05 = 1652.56 \times 19 = 31398.6$ W ✓

#### 🔸 Step 5: Shaft Power
Mechanical loss = $0.015 \times P_{m,dev} = 0.015 \times 31398.6 = 470.98$ W
$$P_{shaft} = P_{m,dev} - P_{mech} = 31398.6 - 470.98 = 30927.6 \text{ W} \approx 30.93 \text{ kW}$$

#### 🔸 Step 6: Overall Efficiency

**Stator copper loss** (using $I_1 \approx I_2' + I_m$ — for this problem, we approximate using $I_2' = 30.30$ A as the dominant current):
$$P_{cu,stator} = 3 I_2'^2 R_1 = 3 \times 918.09 \times 0.5 = 1377.13 \text{ W}$$

**Input power:**
$$P_{input} = P_{shaft} + P_{cu,stator} + P_{core} + P_{cu,rotor} + P_{mech}$$
$$P_{input} = 30927.6 + 1377.13 + 600 + 1652.56 + 470.98 = 35028.3 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{input}} = \frac{30927.6}{35028.3} = 88.30\%$$

### ✅ **Final Consolidated Answer:**
$$\boxed{N_r = 1425 \text{ rpm}, \quad V_{th} = 388.34 \angle 0.556° \text{ V}, \quad I_2' = 30.30 \text{ A}, \quad P_{shaft} = 30.93 \text{ kW}, \quad \eta = 88.30\%}$$

---

## 📘 PROBLEM 5: Power-Flow Stage Deduction from Limited Data (Short, 5-Mark Type)

### **Question:**
A 3-phase induction motor running at slip **0.05** has an air-gap power of **40 kW**. Find:
1. Rotor copper loss
2. Mechanical power developed
3. Rotor speed if supply is **50 Hz, 4-pole**
4. Shaft torque (assume mechanical losses = 800 W)

### **Solution:**

#### 🔸 Step 1: Rotor Copper Loss
$$P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{gap} = 0.05 \times 40000 = 2000 \text{ W} = 2 \text{ kW}$$

#### 🔸 Step 2: Mechanical Power Developed
$$P_{m,dev} = (1-s) P_{gap} = 0.95 \times 40000 = 38000 \text{ W} = 38 \text{ kW}$$

#### 🔸 Step 3: Rotor Speed
$$N_s = 1500 \text{ rpm}, \quad N_r = (1-s) N_s = 0.95 \times 1500 = 1425 \text{ rpm}$$

#### 🔸 Step 4: Shaft Torque
$$P_{shaft} = P_{m,dev} - P_{mech} = 38000 - 800 = 37200 \text{ W}$$
$$\omega_r = \frac{2\pi \times 1425}{60} = 149.23 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$T_{shaft} = \frac{37200}{149.23} = 249.28 \text{ N·m}$$

### ✅ **Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{P_{cu,rotor} = 2 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{m,dev} = 38 \text{ kW}, \quad N_r = 1425 \text{ rpm}, \quad T_{shaft} = 249.28 \text{ N·m}}$$

## 🔷 4.3 Torque-Slip Characteristic (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Operating Region"] --> B["s = 0: Synchronous"]
    A --> C["0 < s < s_max: Stable Operating Zone"]
    A --> D["s = s_max = R2/X2: Maximum Torque"]
    A --> E["s_max < s < 1: Unstable Region"]
    A --> F["s = 1: Standstill (Starting Torque)"]
    
    B --> B1["T = 0, No relative motion"]
    C --> C1["T rises as (s/R2) for small s"]
    D --> D1["T_peak = 3E2²/(2ωs X2)"]
    E --> E1["T falls as 1/s for large s"]
    F --> F1["T_st = 3E2²R2/(ωs R2²+X2²)"]
    
    style A fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Point Insight:** An induction motor **always** operates in region **C (stable)**, between $s = 0$ and $s = s_{max}$. If a load pushes the slip beyond $s_{max}$ (region E), the motor will stall — this is called **pull-out** or **breakdown**.
