# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 🔷 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model 1]**
**Define slip of an induction motor. A 6-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor runs at 940 rpm. Calculate the slip and rotor frequency.**

**Model Answer (Concise):**
**Slip** is the fractional difference between synchronous speed and actual rotor speed:
$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

**Computation:**
- $N_s = (120 \times 50)/6 = 1000$ rpm
- $s = (1000 - 940)/1000 = 0.06$ pu
- $f_r = s f = 0.06 \times 50 = 3$ Hz

> **Marks Allocation:** Definition (1 mark) + Calculation (2 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model 1]**
**State the relationship between air-gap power, rotor copper loss, and mechanical power developed in a 3-phase induction motor.**

**Model Answer:**
If $P_{gap}$ is the air-gap power transferred to the rotor, then:
$$P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{gap}$$
$$P_{m,dev} = (1 - s) \cdot P_{gap}$$

These two quantities sum back to the air-gap power: $P_{cu,rotor} + P_{m,dev} = P_{gap}$ ✓

> **Marks Allocation:** Two equations (2 marks) + Verification statement (1 mark)

---

## 🔷 5.2 PART B Questions — 14 Marks (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from each module in the University Exam. Each carries **14 marks** with sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **📌 QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Pattern Model, Module 4]**

**(a)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has standstill rotor parameters (referred to stator) as: $R_2' = 0.2\ \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.0\ \Omega$, $E_2' = 100$ V/phase. Compute: **(7 Marks)**
   1. Slip for maximum torque
   2. Maximum torque developed
   3. Starting torque
   4. Ratio of starting torque to maximum torque

**(b)** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor draws **50 A at 0.85 p.f. lagging** from the supply. The stator losses total **3 kW**, and the rotor copper loss is **1.2 kW**. Friction and windage loss = **1 kW**. Calculate: **(7 Marks)**
   1. Air-gap power
   2. Mechanical power developed
   3. Shaft power
   4. Efficiency
   5. Rotor speed

#### **Model Solution for Q. A(a):**

**Step 1: Synchronous angular velocity**
$$N_s = (120 \times 50)/6 = 1000 \text{ rpm}, \quad \omega_s = (2\pi \times 1000)/60 = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Slip for maximum torque**
$$s_{max} = R_2'/X_2' = 0.2/1.0 = 0.2$$

**Step 3: Maximum torque**
$$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2'^2}{2 \omega_s X_2'} = \frac{3 \times 100^2}{2 \times 104.72 \times 1.0} = \frac{30000}{209.44} = 143.24 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Starting torque (s = 1)**
$$T_{st} = \frac{3 E_2'^2 R_2'}{\omega_s (R_2'^2 + X_2'^2)} = \frac{3 \times 100^2 \times 0.2}{104.72 \times (0.04 + 1)} = \frac{6000}{108.83} = 55.13 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Ratio**
$$T_{st}/T_{max} = 55.13/143.24 = 0.385 = 38.5\%$$

#### **Model Solution for Q. A(b):**

**Given:** $P_{input} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos \phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 50 \times 0.85 = 29,445.9$ W ≈ 29.45 kW

**Step 1: Air-gap power**
$P_{gap} = P_{input} - P_{stator\,losses} = 29,445.9 - 3000 = 26,445.9$ W

**Step 2: Slip** (from rotor copper loss)
$P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{gap} \Rightarrow s = P_{cu,rotor}/P_{gap} = 1200/26,445.9 = 0.0454$

**Step 3: Mechanical power developed**
$P_{m,dev} = (1-s) P_{gap} = (1 - 0.0454) \times 26,445.9 = 25,245.9$ W

**Step 4: Shaft power**
$P_{shaft} = P_{m,dev} - P_{mech} = 25,245.9 - 1000 = 24,245.9$ W ≈ 24.25 kW

**Step 5: Rotor speed**
$N_r = (1 - s) N_s = (1 - 0.0454) \times 1500 = 0.9546 \times 1500 = 1431.9$ rpm

**Step 6: Efficiency**
$\eta = P_{shaft}/P_{input} = 24,245.9/29,445.9 = 82.34\%$

---

### **📌 QUESTION B — Alternative Choice (Same 14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **torque developed** by a 3-phase induction motor, starting from the power crossing the air gap. Hence, show that the slip at maximum torque is $s_{max} = R_2/X_2$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz induction motor runs at **720 rpm** at full load. The rotor copper loss is **5% of the mechanical power developed**. Calculate: **(7 Marks)**
   1. Number of poles
   2. Slip
   3. Rotor copper loss as a percentage of input to the rotor
   4. If the mechanical losses are 1.5 kW, find the efficiency if input is 30 kW

#### **Model Solution for Q. B(a):**

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Air-gap power = 3 × (rotor current²) × (R₂'/s)
$$P_{gap} = 3 I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}$$

**Step 2:** Mechanical power developed = (1-s) × Air-gap power
$$P_{m,dev} = (1-s) P_{gap} = 3 I_2'^2 R_2' \cdot \frac{1-s}{s}$$

**Step 3:** Rotor current from the equivalent circuit:
$$I_2' = \frac{s E_2'}{\sqrt{R_2'^2 + (s X_2')^2}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute in torque equation $T = P_{m,dev}/\omega_s$:
$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + (s X_2')^2}$$

**Step 5:** To find $s_{max}$, differentiate $T$ with respect to $s$ and set to zero:

Let $k = (3 E_2'^2)/\omega_s$. Then $T = k \cdot s R_2' / (R_2'^2 + s^2 X_2'^2)$.

$$\frac{dT}{ds} = 0 \Rightarrow R_2'^2 + s^2 X_2'^2 = s \cdot 2 s X_2'^2 \Rightarrow R_2'^2 = s^2 X_2'^2$$

$$\therefore s_{max} = R_2'/X_2' \quad \blacksquare$$

#### **Model Solution for Q. B(b):**

**Step 1: Number of poles** — for 50 Hz and 720 rpm, poles must be such that $N_s$ slightly exceeds 720.
Trying P = 8: $N_s = 120 \times 50 / 8 = 750$ rpm. So $P = 8$ ✓

**Step 2: Slip**
$$s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s = (750 - 720)/750 = 0.04$$

**Step 3: Rotor copper loss as % of $P_{gap}$**
Given $P_{cu,rotor} = 0.05 \times P_{m,dev}$ and $P_{cu,rotor} = s P_{gap}$:
$$s P_{gap} = 0.05 (1-s) P_{gap} \Rightarrow s = 0.05(1-s) \Rightarrow s(1 + 0.05) = 0.05 \Rightarrow s = 0.05/1.05 = 0.0476$$

Since our computed slip is 0.04, this is **inconsistent**, meaning the data is approximate. For exam credit, use the **given slip = 0.04** for downstream calculations:
$$P_{cu,rotor}/P_{gap} = s = 0.04 = 4\%$$

**Step 4: Efficiency** — Using $s = 0.04$:
$P_{gap} = P_{input} - P_{stator\,losses}$. Since stator loss is not given, approximate $P_{gap} \approx P_{input} = 30$ kW (typical KTU assumption when not specified).

$P_{m,dev} = (1-s) P_{gap} = 0.96 \times 30,000 = 28,800$ W
$P_{shaft} = 28,800 - 1,500 = 27,300$ W
$\eta = 27,300/30,000 = 91\%$

---

## 🔷 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Numericals**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $N_s$ from rpm to rad/s** in torque equations → lose **2 marks** per such instance. Always write $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$ explicitly.
> 2. **Using line voltage in single-phase equivalent circuit** of a star-connected motor → Should be $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$. For delta, $V_{ph} = V_L$. This mistake costs **3 marks**.
> 3. **Computing $T_{shaft}$ using $\omega_s$ instead of $\omega_r$** → Output torque is at rotor speed, not synchronous. Subtract mechanical losses from $P_{m,dev}$ before dividing.
> 4. **Mixing up the "developed torque" and "shaft torque"** → $T_{dev} = P_{m,dev}/\omega_s$ (note: $P_{m,dev}/\omega_r$ also works), $T_{shaft} = P_{shaft}/\omega_r$.
> 5. **Not stating units in the final answer** → KTU examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for "N·m" missing.
> 6. **Conflating per-phase and total 3-phase power** → Air-gap, rotor copper, etc. are **3-phase total** powers; multiply per-phase values by 3.
> 7. **Calculating efficiency incorrectly when stator losses are given** → Use the full power balance, not just the rotor side.

---

## 🔷 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save this for exam day)**

- ✅ **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$. Always between 0 and 1.
- ✅ **Rotor frequency** $f_r = s f$. Never equal to $f$ except at standstill.
- ✅ **Rotor EMF** $E_{2s} = s E_{2(standstill)}$. Linear in slip.
- ✅ **The Air-Gap Power Theorem:** $P_{cu,rotor} : P_{m,dev} = s : (1-s)$. This is the **single most powerful identity** in Module 4.
- ✅ **Maximum torque slip** $s_{max} = R_2/X_2$. Independent of load and supply voltage.
- ✅ **Maximum torque** $T_{max} = 3 E_2^2 / (2 \omega_s X_2)$. Independent of $R_2$.
- ✅ **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f/P$. For 50 Hz: 2-pole→3000, 4-pole→1500, 6-pole→1000, 8-pole→750, 10-pole→600, 12-pole→500 rpm.
- ✅ **For torque calculation:** always use $\omega_s$, not $\omega_r$.
- ✅ **For shaft power:** always use $\omega_r$ in denominator, and subtract mechanical losses.
- ✅ **3-phase power** = $\sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos \phi$ (line values) or $3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos \phi$ (per-phase values).
- ✅ **Thevenin simplification** is mandatory for problems asking for $T_{max}$, $T_{st}$, or torque at arbitrary $s$ when $R_1$, $X_1$, and $X_m$ are given.
- ✅ **Two equivalent forms** of rotor branch: $R_2'/s$ (compact) or $R_2' + R_2'(1-s)/s$ (split, for power identification).
- ✅ **Thevenin resistance and reactance are typically small** ($R_{th} < 1\ \Omega$, $X_{th} \approx X_1$ when $X_m \gg X_1$).
- ✅ **Practical slip range:** 2–8% at full load for standard cage motors. If given $s > 0.1$ in a problem, treat it as a low-load or starting condition.
- ✅ **Number of poles** must be an even integer (2, 4, 6, 8, ...). For 50 Hz, synchronous speeds are restricted to the sequence 3000, 1500, 1000, 750, 600, 500, ...
- ✅ **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked for "rotor copper loss as a percentage of input power," remember it is $s \times P_{gap}/P_{input}$, NOT just $s$ — this distinction is often tested.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Starting of Induction Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ STARTING OF INDUCTION MOTORS — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting of an Induction Motor** is the process of bringing the stationary rotor of a three-phase (or single-phase) induction motor from rest (slip $s = 1$) up to its rated operating speed (slip typically $s = 0.02$ to $0.05$) in a controlled manner, while limiting the **inrush starting current** to safe values as prescribed by IS/IEC standards and ensuring adequate **starting torque** to overcome load friction, load inertia, and load torque.

Mathematically, at the instant of starting:
$$N_r = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = 1$$

The rotor conductors, being stationary, are cut by the synchronous rotating field at the **maximum possible rate**, inducing the largest possible rotor EMF and hence drawing a very large stator current from the supply.

## 1.2 Why Is Starting a Critical Topic?

A cage induction motor, when started direct-on-line, behaves like a **transformer with a short-circuited secondary**. The rotor impedance at $s = 1$ is very small, so the starting current surges to **5 to 7 times** the full-load current. The two major problems encountered are:

| Problem | Magnitude | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **High Starting Current** | 5–7 × Full Load Current (FLC) | Voltage dip in supply, nuisance tripping, heating |
| **Poor Starting Torque** | 0.75 – 1.5 × Full Load Torque (FLT) | Motor may stall under heavy loads |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Starting methods are categorized for **squirrel-cage** and **slip-ring (wound-rotor)** induction motors, with the trade-off between starting current and starting torque being the core analytical theme.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy 🚗

Imagine pushing a heavy **stationary lorry** (the rotor) up a hill. To get it rolling, you must apply a **large horizontal push** (starting current & torque), but as it starts moving, less effort is needed to keep it going.

Now imagine the same lorry is parked inside a **narrow tunnel connected to a small generator station** (the weak power supply). A huge initial push from the motor would cause the generator voltage to **collapse** (voltage dip), affecting other users.

The motor starter is therefore like a **gearbox of mechanical advantage** — it reduces the initial "push" (current) to a safe level, while still producing enough force (torque) to move the load.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ (in **rpm**), where $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ in India
- **Per-phase rated voltage:** $415\,\text{V}$ (line-to-line, three-phase)
- **Permissible starting current (IS 325):** Generally limited to **1.5 to 2.0 × FLC** for motors above **5 kW**
- **Typical pull-out torque:** **1.6 to 2.5 × FLT**
- **Rotor copper loss at start ($s = 1$):** Equals the air-gap power, i.e., the **entire** air-gap power is dissipated as heat in the rotor

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Poor Starting Performance

The per-phase equivalent circuit referred to the stator gives the rotor current at any slip $s$ as:

$$I_2' = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

At $s = 1$, $\frac{R_2'}{s} = R_2'$, which is **small** compared to $(X_1 + X_2')$. Hence the impedance is dominated by leakage reactance, and the rotor draws a **large lagging current at poor power factor**.

The starting torque is given by:

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot R_2'}{\left(R_1 + R_2'\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

### Key Observations (Why DOL Fails for Large Motors):

- $T_{st} \propto V_1^2$ — A small dip in $V_1$ drastically reduces torque
- $T_{st} \propto R_2'$ for small $R_2'$ — Cage rotors have fixed, low $R_2'$, so torque cannot be boosted internally
- The starting current is **not in phase** with the supply voltage (poor pf), hence it draws excessive reactive power
- **Rotor copper loss at start** $= s \cdot P_g = 1 \cdot P_g = P_g$ (entire air-gap power turns into heat)

## 2.2 Classification of Starting Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we cannot simply "reduce the voltage" to limit current?**
> Because $T_{st} \propto V^2$, a $50\%$ voltage reduction cuts the starting torque to **$25\%$** of its DOL value — often insufficient to drive the load. This trade-off is at the heart of every starting method.

### A. Squirrel-Cage Induction Motors

1. **Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter** — used for motors up to **5 kW**
2. **Stator Resistance (Reactor) Starter** — older method, high losses
3. **Autotransformer Starter** — preferred for **5 kW to 25 kW** motors
4. **Star-Delta Starter** — most common for motors above **5 kW**
5. **Soft Starter (Thyristor-based)** — modern, smooth, expensive
6. **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** — best performance, highest cost

### B. Slip-Ring (Wound-Rotor) Induction Motors

7. **Rotor Resistance Starting** — adds external resistance via slip rings; gives **high starting torque** with **low starting current**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet 📋

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm, $f$ in Hz |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless, $0 < s < 1$ |
| 3 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s f$ | Hz |
| 4 | Per-phase starting current (DOL) | $I_{st} = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$ | A/phase |
| 5 | Starting torque | $T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 R_2'}{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$ | N·m |
| 6 | Autotransformer tap ratio | $x = \frac{V_{\text{reduced}}}{V_{\text{rated}}}$, $0 < x < 1$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Autotransformer: line starting current | $I_{L,\text{auto}} = x \cdot I_{st,\text{DOL}}$ | A |
| 8 | Autotransformer: starting torque | $T_{st,\text{auto}} = x^2 \cdot T_{st,\text{DOL}}$ | N·m |
| 9 | Star-Delta: line starting current | $I_{L,\text{Y-}\Delta} = \frac{1}{3} I_{st,\text{DOL}}$ | A |
| 10 | Star-Delta: starting torque | $T_{st,\text{Y-}\Delta} = \frac{1}{3} T_{st,\text{DOL}}$ | N·m |
| 11 | Rotor resistance: max starting torque condition | $R_2' + R_{\text{ext}} = X_1 + X_2'$ | ohms |
| 12 | Slip at max torque | $s_{mT} = \frac{R_2' + R_{\text{ext}}'}{X_1 + X_2'}$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Condition for max torque at start ($s_{mT}=1$) | $R_2' + R_{\text{ext}}' = \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$ | ohms |
| 14 | Air-gap power | $P_g = \frac{3 I_2'^2 R_2'}{s}$ | W |
| 15 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{\text{cu,rotor}} = s \cdot P_g$ | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule to Memorize:**
> - Voltage reduction by factor $x$ → **Line current reduced by $x$** → **Torque reduced by $x^2$**
> - Star-Delta = a special case of voltage reduction where $x = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$, giving $\frac{1}{3}$ reduction in both current AND torque

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Starting methods are essential in:

- **Industrial drives**: Pumps, compressors, conveyors, fans in process industries
- **HVAC systems**: Large chiller and blower motors in commercial buildings
- **Cement and steel plants**: Slip-ring motors in crushers and mills needing high starting torque
- **Electric traction**: Controlled soft-start is critical for trains and EVs
- **Renewable energy**: Wind turbine generators use variable frequency soft-starts

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Starting Torque in Terms of Full-Load Torque

**Given:** A 3-phase, $P$-pole induction motor with per-phase parameters $R_1, X_1, R_2', X_2'$, supply voltage $V_1$, synchronous speed $\omega_s$.

**Step 1 — General Torque Equation from Equivalent Circuit:**

The mechanical power developed per phase is:
$$P_m = I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s} \cdot (1 - s)$$

The total air-gap power (per-phase) is:
$$P_g = I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}$$

Total electromagnetic torque:
$$T_e = \frac{3 \cdot P_g}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $I_2'$ from the equivalent circuit:**

$$I_2' = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Torque Expression:**

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}}{\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s^2$:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot s \cdot R_2'}{\left(R_1 s + R_2'\right)^2 + \left[(X_1 + X_2') s\right]^2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Starting Condition $s = 1$:**

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot R_2'}{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

**Step 5 — Ratio of Starting Torque to Full-Load Torque:**

Let the full-load slip be $s_f$. The full-load torque is:
$$T_{fl} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot s_f R_2'}{(R_1 s_f + R_2')^2 + [(X_1 + X_2') s_f]^2}$$

Therefore the ratio is:
$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{fl}} = \frac{R_2'}{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2} \cdot \frac{(R_1 s_f + R_2')^2 + [(X_1 + X_2') s_f]^2}{s_f R_2'}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a typical cage motor, $R_1 \ll X_1 + X_2'$ and $R_2' \ll X_1 + X_2'$, so the denominator is dominated by $(X_1 + X_2')^2$. Hence $T_{st}$ is small.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Star-Delta Starting Current and Torque

**Step 1 — DOL Starting Phase Current:**

In direct-on-line starting, the stator phase voltage equals the line voltage $V_L$ (delta connection):
$$V_{\text{ph,DOL}} = V_L$$

$$I_{\text{ph,DOL}} = \frac{V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$$

Line current in delta: $I_{L,\text{DOL}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{\text{ph,DOL}}$
$$I_{L,\text{DOL}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$$

**Step 2 — Star Starting Phase Current:**

In star connection, the same line voltage $V_L$ appears across two phases in series. The phase voltage becomes:
$$V_{\text{ph,Y}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

The phase current in star:
$$I_{\text{ph,Y}} = \frac{V_L / \sqrt{3}}{Z_{\text{ph}}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{\text{ph}}}$$

In star, line current = phase current:
$$I_{L,\text{Y}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{\text{ph}}}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio of Line Currents:**

$$\frac{I_{L,\text{Y}}}{I_{L,\text{DOL}}} = \frac{V_L / (\sqrt{3} Z_{\text{ph}})}{\sqrt{3} V_L / Z_{\text{ph}}} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\boxed{I_{L,\text{Y-}\Delta} = \frac{1}{3} I_{L,\text{DOL}}}$$

**Step 4 — Torque Ratio:**

Since $T \propto V_{\text{ph}}^2$:

$$\frac{T_{\text{Y}}}{T_{\Delta}} = \left(\frac{V_L/\sqrt{3}}{V_L}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\boxed{T_{st,\text{Y-}\Delta} = \frac{1}{3} T_{st,\text{DOL}}}$$

**Conclusion:** Star-Delta starting reduces both starting current and starting torque to **one-third** of their DOL values.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Autotransformer Starting

**Step 1 — Reduced Voltage Applied to Motor:**

If the autotransformer has a tapping ratio $x = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$, then the voltage applied to the motor stator is:
$$V_m = x \cdot V_L$$

**Step 2 — Motor Phase Current at Reduced Voltage:**

The motor draws phase current:
$$I_m = \frac{x V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$$

**Step 3 — Line Current from Supply:**

In an autotransformer, the primary (line) current relates to the secondary (motor) current by:
$$I_L = x \cdot I_m = x \cdot \frac{x V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}} = x^2 \cdot \frac{V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$$

The DOL starting current is $I_{L,\text{DOL}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$ (per phase in delta). The **line current ratio** is:

$$\frac{I_{L,\text{auto}}}{I_{L,\text{DOL}}} = x$$

$$\boxed{I_{L,\text{auto}} = x \cdot I_{L,\text{DOL}}}$$

**Step 4 — Starting Torque:**

$$\frac{T_{\text{auto}}}{T_{\text{DOL}}} = \left(\frac{x V_L}{V_L}\right)^2 = x^2$$

$$\boxed{T_{st,\text{auto}} = x^2 \cdot T_{st,\text{DOL}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Tapping Selection Rule:** If a load requires at least $T_{\text{req}}$ starting torque while limiting line current to $I_{\text{max}}$, select the **highest possible** $x$ such that $x^2 T_{st,\text{DOL}} \geq T_{\text{req}}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Rotor Resistance Starting (Slip-Ring Motor)

**Step 1 — Slip for Maximum Torque:**

The slip at which maximum torque occurs is:
$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2' + R_{\text{ext}}'}{X_1 + X_2'}$$

**Step 2 — To obtain maximum torque at start, we require $s_{mT} = 1$:**

$$1 = \frac{R_2' + R_{\text{ext}}'}{X_1 + X_2'}$$

$$R_{\text{ext}}' = (X_1 + X_2') - R_2'$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Torque Itself (independent of $R_2'$):**

$$T_{\max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{2(R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2})}$$

This shows that by adding external rotor resistance, we can **achieve maximum torque at starting** without changing the supply voltage — a unique advantage of slip-ring motors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Rotor resistance starting is the **only method** that simultaneously reduces starting current AND increases starting torque.

## 3.5 Numerical Verification Example

> A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor has $R_1 = 0.5\,\Omega$, $X_1 = 1.0\,\Omega$, $R_2' = 0.6\,\Omega$, $X_2' = 1.0\,\Omega$. Calculate: (a) DOL starting current per phase, (b) DOL starting torque, (c) Star-Delta starting current and torque.

**Given:** $V_1 = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 231\,\text{V}$, $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$, $P = 4$
$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\,\text{rpm}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Part (a) — DOL Starting Current Per Phase:**

$$Z_{\text{start}} = \sqrt{(0.5 + 0.6)^2 + (1.0 + 1.0)^2} = \sqrt{1.21 + 4.00} = \sqrt{5.21} = 2.282\,\Omega$$

$$I_{st} = \frac{231}{2.282} = 101.2\,\text{A/phase}$$

**Part (b) — DOL Starting Torque:**

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{231^2 \times 0.6}{5.21}$$

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{32013.6}{5.21} = 0.01910 \times 6144.6 = 117.4\,\text{N·m}$$

**Part (c) — Star-Delta:**

$$I_{L,\text{Y-}\Delta} = \frac{101.2}{3} = 33.7\,\text{A/phase (line)}$$

$$T_{st,\text{Y-}\Delta} = \frac{117.4}{3} = 39.1\,\text{N·m}$$

## 4.2 Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["3-Phase Supply 400V 50Hz"]:::supply --> B["Triple Pole Switch S1"]
    B --> C["Overload Relay OLR"]
    C --> D["Contactor Coil K1"]
    D --> E["Stator Windings U V W"]
    E --> F["Squirrel-Cage Rotor"]

    G["Start Push Button NO"] --> D
    H["Stop Push Button NC"] --> D

```

## 4.3 Star-Delta Starter Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Supply L1 L2 L3"]:::main --> B["Main Contactor KM1"]
    B --> C["Stator Terminals U1 V1 W1"]
    A --> D["Star Contactor KM2"]
    D --> E["Shorts W2 U2 V2 Together"]
    A --> F["Delta Contactor KM3"]
    F --> G["Connects W2 to V1, U2 to W1, V2 to U1"]

    H["Timer Relay"] --> D
    H --> F
    H -.->|Time delay 3-10s| I["KM2 OFF KM3 ON"]

    classDef main fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#006,color:#000
```

**Operating Sequence:**
1. **Start**: KM1 + KM2 close → motor runs in **Star** (reduced voltage)
2. **After preset time**: Timer triggers KM2 to open
3. **Transition**: KM3 closes → motor runs in **Delta** (full voltage)
4. KM1 remains closed throughout; mechanical interlock prevents KM2 and KM3 being closed simultaneously

    B -->|Tap xV| C["Motor Stator"]
    B -->|Tap 1 Full V| D["Bypass Contactor"]

    E["Control Circuit"] --> B
    E --> D
    E -.->|After 5-15s| F["Switch to Full Voltage"]

    classDef input fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#006,color:#000
```

```

## 4.6 Comparison Matrix: Starting Methods

| Method | Starting Current | Starting Torque | Cost | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DOL | 5–7 × FLC | 1.5–2 × FLT | Low | ≤ 5 kW |
| Star-Delta | 1.7–2.3 × FLC | 0.5–0.7 × FLT | Medium | Pumps, fans |
| Autotransformer | Adjustable | Adjustable | Medium-High | Compressors |
| Rotor Resistance | 1.5–2 × FLC | 2–2.5 × FLT | High | Cranes, mills |
| Soft Starter (SCR) | Smooth | Adjustable | High | HVAC, conveyors |
| VFD | Adjustable | Up to 1.5 × FLT | Very High | Precision drives |

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

**Q1. Why is a direct-on-line starter not preferred for large induction motors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A direct-on-line starter applies full rated voltage to the motor at start. This causes:
1. **Excessive starting current** (5–7 times FLC) leading to voltage dips in the supply, overheating, and nuisance tripping of protective devices.
2. **High starting torque** may damage the shaft coupling or driven load mechanically.
3. **Mechanical shock** to the windings due to sudden high inrush.

Hence DOL is restricted to motors of rating up to **5 kW**.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Q2. Why does a squirrel-cage induction motor have a low starting torque despite drawing high starting current?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
At start, $s = 1$ and the rotor leakage reactance $X_2'$ is much larger than $R_2'$. The rotor current $I_2'$ lags the rotor EMF by a large angle, giving a **poor power factor** ($\cos\phi_2 \approx 0.2$–$0.3$). Although the magnitude of $I_2'$ is large, its in-phase component (which produces torque) is small. Since $T \propto \phi \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi_2$, the starting torque is low. The current is largely **magnetizing in nature** rather than power-producing.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **star-delta starting method** for a 3-phase induction motor. Derive the expressions for the ratio of starting current and starting torque to their corresponding DOL values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator: $R_1 = 0.3\,\Omega$, $X_1 = 0.6\,\Omega$, $R_2' = 0.4\,\Omega$, $X_2' = 0.6\,\Omega$. The motor is started using a star-delta starter. Calculate: (i) DOL starting current per phase, (ii) DOL starting torque, (iii) Starting line current with star-delta starter, (iv) Starting torque with star-delta starter. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Sketch:** Already provided in Section 4.3 above.

**Derivation:**

In DOL starting (delta connection), phase voltage = line voltage:
$$V_{\text{ph},\Delta} = V_L, \quad I_{\text{ph},\Delta} = \frac{V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}, \quad I_{L,\Delta} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$$

In star connection, phase voltage = $V_L / \sqrt{3}$:
$$I_{\text{ph},Y} = \frac{V_L / \sqrt{3}}{Z_{\text{ph}}}, \quad I_{L,Y} = I_{\text{ph},Y} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} Z_{\text{ph}}}$$

Ratio of line currents:
$$\frac{I_{L,Y}}{I_{L,\Delta}} = \frac{V_L / (\sqrt{3} Z_{\text{ph}})}{\sqrt{3} V_L / Z_{\text{ph}}} = \frac{1}{3}$$

Since $T \propto V_{\text{ph}}^2$:
$$\frac{T_{st,Y}}{T_{st,\Delta}} = \left(\frac{V_L/\sqrt{3}}{V_L}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{3}$$

> **[Step 1 of marks]** Circuit description: 1 mark
> **[Step 2]** Star connection phase voltage relation: 1 mark
> **[Step 3]** Delta connection phase voltage relation: 1 mark
> **[Step 4]** Current ratio derivation: 2 marks
> **[Step 5]** Torque ratio derivation: 2 marks

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:** $V_L = 400\,\text{V}$, $V_1 = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 231\,\text{V}$, $P = 6$
$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000\,\text{rpm}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Impedance at start ($s = 1$):**
$$Z_{\text{start}} = \sqrt{(0.3 + 0.4)^2 + (0.6 + 0.6)^2} = \sqrt{0.49 + 1.44} = \sqrt{1.93} = 1.389\,\Omega$$

**(i) DOL Starting Current Per Phase:**
$$I_{st,\Delta} = \frac{231}{1.389} = 166.3\,\text{A/phase}$$

**(ii) DOL Starting Torque:**
$$T_{st,\Delta} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{231^2 \times 0.4}{1.93}$$
$$T_{st,\Delta} = 0.02865 \cdot \frac{21336.4}{1.93} = 0.02865 \times 11055.1 = 316.7\,\text{N·m}$$

**(iii) Star-Delta Starting Line Current:**
$$I_{L,Y} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot I_{L,\Delta} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot 166.3 = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 288.0 = 96.0\,\text{A}$$

**(iv) Star-Delta Starting Torque:**
$$T_{st,Y} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 316.7 = 105.6\,\text{N·m}$$

> **[Step 1 of marks]** Identify $N_s$ and $\omega_s$: 1 mark
> **[Step 2]** Calculate $Z_{\text{start}}$: 1 mark
> **[Step 3]** DOL current calculation: 1 mark
> **[Step 4]** DOL torque calculation: 2 marks
> **[Step 5]** Star-Delta results with 1/3 factor: 2 marks

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a)** Explain with a circuit diagram the **autotransformer starting method** for a 3-phase induction motor. Derive the expressions for starting current and starting torque in terms of DOL values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase induction motor draws 6 times its full-load current at start with DOL and develops 1.5 times the full-load torque. Determine the tapping of the autotransformer to limit the starting current to 1.5 times FLC. Also, find the starting torque at this tap. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Circuit Diagram:** Already shown in Section 4.4.

**Construction:** A 3-phase autotransformer with adjustable tappings (typically 50%, 65%, 80% of full voltage) is connected between the supply and the motor. At start, the motor is connected to the reduced-voltage tap. After a preset time (5–15 s), the changeover contactor transfers the motor to the full-voltage line.

**Derivation:**

Let tapping ratio $x = \frac{V_{\text{motor}}}{V_{\text{line}}}$.

Motor terminal voltage: $V_m = x \cdot V_L$

Motor draws phase current: $I_m = \frac{x V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$

The line current from supply (autotransformer action):
$$I_L = x \cdot I_m = x^2 \cdot \frac{V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$$

In DOL: $I_{L,\text{DOL}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$

Ratio:
$$\frac{I_L}{I_{L,\text{DOL}}} = x \quad \Longrightarrow \quad I_{L,\text{auto}} = x \cdot I_{L,\text{DOL}}$$

Torque ratio:
$$\frac{T_{\text{auto}}}{T_{\text{DOL}}} = \left(\frac{x V_L}{V_L}\right)^2 = x^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_{\text{auto}} = x^2 \cdot T_{\text{DOL}}$$

> **[Step 1 of marks]** Diagram and description: 2 marks
> **[Step 2]** Voltage relation: 1 mark
> **[Step 3]** Current derivation: 2 marks
> **[Step 4]** Torque derivation: 2 marks

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:** $I_{st,\text{DOL}} = 6 \cdot I_{\text{FL}}$, $T_{st,\text{DOL}} = 1.5 \cdot T_{\text{FL}}$, $I_{L,\text{auto}} = 1.5 \cdot I_{\text{FL}}$

**Step 1:** Use the current reduction formula:
$$I_{L,\text{auto}} = x \cdot I_{st,\text{DOL}}$$

$$1.5 \cdot I_{\text{FL}} = x \cdot 6 \cdot I_{\text{FL}}$$

$$x = \frac{1.5}{6} = 0.25 \quad \text{or} \quad 25\%$$

**Step 2:** Calculate starting torque at this tap:
$$T_{st,\text{auto}} = x^2 \cdot T_{st,\text{DOL}} = (0.25)^2 \cdot 1.5 \cdot T_{\text{FL}}$$

$$T_{st,\text{auto}} = 0.0625 \times 1.5 \cdot T_{\text{FL}} = 0.09375 \cdot T_{\text{FL}}$$

$$\boxed{T_{st,\text{auto}} \approx 0.094 \cdot T_{\text{FL}}}$$

> **[Step 1 of marks]** Substituting the relation: 2 marks
> **[Step 2]** Solving for $x$: 2 marks
> **[Step 3]** Torque calculation: 2 marks
> **[Step 4]** Final answer with units: 1 mark

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor** in DOL current — line current is $\sqrt{3}$ times phase current in delta; in star they are equal.
> 2. **Confusing $x$ and $x^2$** — autotransformer reduces current by $x$ but torque by $x^2$ (this is the most-failed concept).
> 3. **Using $V_L$ instead of $V_1$** in per-phase equivalent circuit formulas. Always convert: $V_1 = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ for star, $V_1 = V_L$ for delta.
> 4. **Wrong slip at start** — must explicitly state $s = 1$ before applying the starting formula.
> 5. **Mixing up Full-Load Torque and Starting Torque** — ratios are taken with DOL starting values, not full-load.
> 6. **Not showing unit conversions** — $N_s$ in rpm must be converted to $\omega_s$ in rad/s in torque calculations.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **At start** $s = 1$, rotor frequency = supply frequency, rotor sees **maximum induced EMF**.
- ✅ **DOL starting current** = 5–7 × FLC, **starting torque** = 0.75–1.5 × FLT.
- ✅ **No-load slip** ≈ 0, **Full-load slip** = 0.02–0.05.
- ✅ **Star-Delta**: current ↓ by $1/3$, torque ↓ by $1/3$. Most common for **> 5 kW** cage motors.
- ✅ **Autotransformer**: current ↓ by $x$, torque ↓ by $x^2$. Common tappings: **50%, 65%, 80%**.
- ✅ **Slip-ring (rotor resistance) starting**: only method giving **high starting torque** with **low current**.
- ✅ Condition for **maximum starting torque** in slip-ring motor: $R_2' + R_{\text{ext}}' = X_1 + X_2'$.
- ✅ **Soft starters** use back-to-back thyristors (SCRs) for phase-angle control.
- ✅ **VFD** provides the best soft-start by varying both voltage and frequency simultaneously.
- ✅ **Rotor copper loss at start** equals the **entire air-gap power** (since $s = 1$).
- ✅ **Knee-point voltage**: voltage at which maximum torque occurs at start.
- ✅ **IS 325** limits starting current to ~1.5 × FLC for motors above 5 kW.
- ✅ **Pull-out torque** = maximum torque, typically 1.6–2.5 × FLT.
- ✅ **Star connection gives phase voltage** = $V_L / \sqrt{3}$, which is the key to the $1/3$ reduction in star-delta starting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Starters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Starters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Core Problem — Why Do We Even Need a Starter?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** *Starting methods and speed control of DC and AC machines. Necessity of starter, types of starters for DC shunt motors (3-point and 4-point starters), and starters for 3-phase induction motors.*

When a DC motor is switched ON directly across the rated supply voltage, the **back EMF ($E_b$) is zero** because the armature is initially at rest. This means the only opposition to current flow is the **very low armature resistance ($R_a$)**, which is typically less than **0.5 Ω** for medium-sized machines.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket of Water" Intuition

Imagine trying to drink an entire **bucket of water** in one gulp versus sipping it through a **straw** slowly. The bucket represents **direct-on-line starting** — it floods the motor with destructive inrush current. The straw represents a **starter** — it carefully limits the gulp (current) until the motor "warms up" and develops its own internal resistance (back EMF).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Insight (Armature Current at Starting):**
> $$I_{st} = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{V - 0}{R_a} = \frac{V}{R_a}$$
> Since $R_a$ is extremely small, $I_{st}$ becomes **10 to 20 times** the full-load current, which would:
> - Burn the armature windings
> - Damage the commutator and brushes
> - Cause severe voltage drop in the supply
> - Produce a huge mechanical shock to the load

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Starter

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> A **starter** is an electrical control device used to limit the armature current of a DC motor (or starting current of an AC motor) to a safe predetermined value during the acceleration period, by introducing a **variable external resistance** in series with the armature circuit that is gradually cut out as the motor picks up speed and develops back EMF.

## 1.3 Classification of Starters (KTU Module 4 Scope)

```
TYPES OF STARTERS
│
├── 1. DC MOTOR STARTERS
│       ├── (a) 3-Point Starter
│       └── (b) 4-Point Starter
│
└── 2. 3-φ INDUCTION MOTOR STARTERS
        ├── (a) Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter
        ├── (b) Star-Delta Starter
        ├── (c) Auto-Transformer Starter
        └── (d) Rotor Resistance Starter (Slip-Ring IM only)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** For **5-mark short questions**, students often forget to mention the *necessity* of the starter before describing its construction. Always start with the **"Why"** — the starting current problem — then move to **"How"** the starter solves it.

## 1.4 Why Starting Current is a Problem — Quantitative Picture

| Machine Type | Starting Current (without starter) | Safe Limit | Multiplier |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC Shunt Motor | $V / R_a$ | 1.5 × Full Load | **8–12 × FLC** |
| DC Series Motor | $V / (R_a + R_{se})$ | 1.5 × Full Load | **5–10 × FLC** |
| 3-φ Squirrel Cage IM | Direct switching | 1.5 × Full Load | **5–7 × FLC** |
| 3-φ Slip-Ring IM | Rotor resistance added | 1.5 × Full Load | **1.5–2 × FLC** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle Behind Every Starter

The fundamental equation governing starter design is the **armature circuit KVL**:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a + I_a R_{ext}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Supply voltage
- $E_b = k\phi\omega$ = Back EMF (proportional to speed)
- $I_a$ = Armature current
- $R_a$ = Armature resistance
- $R_{ext}$ = External starter resistance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core "Why" Behind Starter Design:**
> At $t = 0$ (start), $\omega = 0$, so $E_b = 0$. The starter inserts a large $R_{ext}$ to keep $I_a$ within safe limits. As the motor accelerates, $E_b$ grows, and the starter progressively removes $R_{ext}$ in discrete steps until $R_{ext} = 0$ at rated speed.

## 2.2 The 3-Point Starter — Construction & Operation

### Constructional Features

The **3-point starter** has three terminals (hence the name):

1. **L (Line)** — Connected to the positive supply.
2. **A (Armature)** — Connected to the armature terminal.
3. **F (Field)** — Connected to the shunt field winding.

It also contains:
- A **rheostat (resistance steps)** — usually **6 to 8 studs** marked on an iron plate (the "face plate").
- A **movable brass arm (handle)** — pivoted at the L terminal, which slides across the resistance studs.
- A **soft iron piece** attached to the arm, held in place by a **Hold-On (No-Voltage) Coil (NVC)** electromagnet.
- An **Overload Release (OLR)** mechanism — trips the starter if current exceeds a safe value.

### Detailed Working Logic

1. The arm is moved to the **first stud**. The **full starting resistance** is in series. Current is limited to a safe value, and the motor begins to rotate slowly.
2. As the motor gains speed, $E_b$ increases, and armature current tries to drop. The arm is moved to the **next stud**, cutting out one resistance section.
3. This process continues until the arm reaches the **final stud** (marked RUN), where all external resistance is excluded, and the motor runs at full speed.
4. The **NVC** holds the arm in the RUN position as long as supply voltage is healthy.

### The Critical Role of the NVC (No-Voltage Coil)

> [!NOTE]
> **The NVC is connected in series with the shunt field winding.** It serves **three** functions:
> 1. **No-Volt Protection (NVP):** If supply fails, the NVC de-energizes → spring pulls the arm back to OFF position → motor does not restart on its own when supply returns.
> 2. **Overload Protection (OLP):** A trip coil / overload electromagnet pulls the arm back if armature current exceeds the set limit.
> 3. **Field Rheostat (FR):** Often integrated — moving the arm in the RUN position also varies the field current for **speed control above base speed**.

### ⚠️ Drawback of the 3-Point Starter

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Hot-Spot — "The Field Weakening Trap":**
> If the **field rheostat is used too aggressively** to increase speed (i.e., field current is reduced too much), the NVC — being in series with the field — carries very little current and **loses its holding power**. The arm springs back, and the motor **suddenly shuts down**. This is the famous **"weak-field problem" of the 3-point starter**.

## 2.3 The 4-Point Starter — Elimination of the Weak-Field Problem

### Key Constructional Difference

In a **4-point starter**, the NVC is **NOT connected in series with the shunt field**. Instead, it is connected across the supply in a **separate fourth point**, in series with a high-value protective resistor. This means the NVC current is **independent of the field current**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Terminals of a 4-Point Starter:**
> 1. **L** — Line (positive supply)
> 2. **A** — Armature
> 3. **F** — Field
> 4. **NVC** — Independent connection to the No-Voltage Coil (4th point, hence the name)

### Comparative Analysis

| Feature | 3-Point Starter | 4-Point Starter |
|---|---|---|
| NVC Connection | Series with shunt field | Parallel (with series resistor) |
| NVC Current | Depends on field current | **Independent of field current** |
| Weak-field problem | **Yes — exists** | **No — eliminated** |
| Speed control via field | Possible | **Not possible** (separate rheostat needed) |
| Cost | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Use case | Constant speed applications | Where wide speed range is needed |

## 2.4 Starter Resistance Calculation — Analytical Foundation

The design of a starter involves calculating the **resistance per step** such that armature current oscillates between two limits:
- **Upper limit:** $I_{max} \approx 1.5 \times I_{FL}$ (safe maximum)
- **Lower limit:** $I_{min} \approx I_{FL}$ (or slightly above)

The **Geometric Progression Rule** governs step design:

$$\frac{R_{ext(n)}}{R_{ext(n-1)}} = k = \text{constant ratio} = \frac{I_{min}}{I_{max}}$$

Where:
- $R_{ext(n)}$ = Total circuit resistance during the $n$-th step
- $k$ = **Starter ratio** (less than unity)

### Master Formula Table (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — Total Starting Resistance:**
> $$R_1 = \frac{V}{I_{max}}$$
> This is the total circuit resistance at the **first stud** (when $E_b = 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — Starter Ratio:**
> $$k = \left(\frac{I_{min}}{I_{max}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}}$$
> Where $n$ = number of starter sections (resistance steps).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — Number of Sections:**
> $$n = \frac{\log(I_{min}/I_{max})}{\log(k)}$$

**Resistance per section table:**

| Section | Total Circuit Resistance | Resistance in THIS Section |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (First stud) | $R_1 = V/I_{max}$ | $R_{step,1} = R_1 - kR_1 = R_1(1-k)$ |
| 2 | $R_2 = kR_1$ | $R_{step,2} = R_2(1-k)$ |
| 3 | $R_3 = kR_2 = k^2 R_1$ | $R_{step,3} = R_3(1-k)$ |
| … | … | … |
| $n$ (Final stud) | $R_n = k^{n-1} R_1 = R_a$ | $R_{step,n} = R_n - R_a$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In the last section, the total resistance must equal $R_a$ (only armature resistance remains), because the starter's purpose is done — the motor is at full speed and has developed sufficient back EMF to limit current naturally.

## 2.5 Induction Motor Starters — Comparative Analysis

### (a) Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter

- Simplest, cheapest, used for **motors up to 5 HP (≈ 3.7 kW)**.
- Full voltage applied directly. Starting current is **5–7 × FLC**.
- Includes **overload relay (OLR)** and **contactor** for protection.
- **High starting torque** — suitable for applications like pumps, compressors where the load can take the mechanical shock.

### (b) Star-Delta Starter

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Principle:**
> 1. **Starting:** Stator winding connected in **STAR** → phase voltage = $V_L / \sqrt{3}$ → starting current reduced to **1/3** of DOL current.
> 2. **Running:** After motor reaches ~80% speed, winding is reconfigured to **DELTA** → full line voltage across each phase.

| Quantity | Star Connection | Delta Connection | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase Voltage | $V_L/\sqrt{3}$ | $V_L$ | $1/\sqrt{3}$ |
| Phase Current | $I_{ph}$ | $I_{ph}$ | 1 |
| **Line Current** | $I_{ph}$ | $\sqrt{3} I_{ph}$ | **1/3** |
| **Starting Torque** | $T_{st}/3$ | $T_{st}$ | **1/3** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — The Torque Trap:**
> Star-delta reduces **both** starting current **AND** starting torque to 1/3. This starter is **only suitable for light/medium loads** (fans, centrifugal pumps). It is **NOT** suitable for high-inertia loads or loads requiring high starting torque.

### (c) Auto-Transformer Starter

- Uses a **3-phase tapped autotransformer** to apply a reduced voltage (typically **50%, 60%, 80% taps**) during starting.
- After acceleration, full voltage is switched in via a contactor.
- **Advantage:** Can apply 50% or 80% voltage (unlike star-delta which only gives 57.7% i.e., $1/\sqrt{3}$).
- **Starting current drawn from supply** = $x \times I_{st,DOL}$, where $x$ is the tap fraction.
- **Starting torque** = $x^2 \times T_{st,DOL}$ (square-law relationship — critical formula).

### (d) Rotor Resistance Starter (Slip-Ring IM only)

- External 3-phase resistance is added to the **rotor circuit** via slip rings.
- Unlike stator-voltage-based starters, this directly controls the **rotor current**.
- **High starting torque** can be obtained — suitable for **heavy loads** (hoists, cranes, elevators).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DOL starters** dominate small industrial applications.
- **Star-delta starters** are the workhorse of HVAC and water-pumping industries.
- **Auto-transformer starters** are used in textile mills and paper industries where adjustable voltage is needed.
- **Rotor resistance starters** are critical in mining, elevators, and crane applications where high starting torque is non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Starter Ratio Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the expression for starter ratio $k$ in terms of armature current limits and number of steps.

### Given Conditions
- Motor: DC Shunt, rated voltage $V$, armature resistance $R_a$, full load current $I_{FL}$
- Upper current limit: $I_{max}$ (when handle is on a stud)
- Lower current limit: $I_{min}$ (when handle is between studs, just before moving to next stud)
- Number of resistance sections: $n$

### Step 1: Condition at the First Stud

The arm is on the **first stud**. The motor is at rest, $E_b = 0$. Total circuit resistance = $R_1$.

$$I_{max} = \frac{V - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V}{R_1}$$

$$\therefore R_1 = \frac{V}{I_{max}}$$

### Step 2: Condition Just Before Moving to the Second Stud

The arm is about to leave the first stud. Motor has accelerated, back EMF is now $E_{b1}$. Current has dropped to $I_{min}$.

$$I_{min} = \frac{V - E_{b1}}{R_1}$$

### Step 3: Condition Just After Moving to the Second Stud

The arm moves to the second stud. Total resistance becomes $R_2$ (smaller). Current jumps back to $I_{max}$ momentarily.

$$I_{max} = \frac{V - E_{b1}}{R_2}$$

### Step 4: Eliminating $E_{b1}$

From Step 2: $V - E_{b1} = I_{min} \cdot R_1$

Substituting in Step 3:

$$I_{max} = \frac{I_{min} \cdot R_1}{R_2}$$

$$\therefore R_2 = \frac{I_{min}}{I_{max}} \cdot R_1$$

### Step 5: Defining the Ratio

Let $k = \dfrac{I_{min}}{I_{max}}$. Then:

$$\boxed{R_2 = k \cdot R_1}$$

### Step 6: Extending the Pattern

By the same logic applied to each successive step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3 &= k \cdot R_2 = k^2 \cdot R_1 \\
R_4 &= k \cdot R_3 = k^3 \cdot R_1 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
R_n &= k^{n-1} \cdot R_1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Final Boundary Condition

At the **last stud**, all starter resistance is cut out. Only $R_a$ remains:

$$R_n = R_a$$

$$\therefore k^{n-1} \cdot R_1 = R_a$$

### Step 8: Final Master Expression for $k$

Substituting $R_1 = V/I_{max}$:

$$k^{n-1} \cdot \frac{V}{I_{max}} = R_a$$

$$k^{n-1} = \frac{I_{max} \cdot R_a}{V}$$

Taking the $(n-1)$-th root:

$$\boxed{k = \left(\frac{I_{max} \cdot R_a}{V}\right)^{\frac{1}{n-1}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative form using $I_{min}/I_{max}$ ratio:**
> Since $k = I_{min}/I_{max}$, we can also write:
> $$k = \left(\frac{I_{min}}{I_{max}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n-1}}$$
> Both expressions are equivalent. The first uses machine parameters; the second uses current limits.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Question]**
> A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω. The full-load armature current is 40 A. Design a starter such that the armature current during starting is limited to a maximum of 80 A. Calculate:
> (a) Total starting resistance required
> (b) The number of starter sections if the lower current limit is 45 A
> (c) The resistance of the first section
> (d) Total resistance to be cut out by the starter

### Part (a) — Total Starting Resistance

By Ohm's law at the first stud (motor at rest):

$$R_1 = \frac{V}{I_{max}} = \frac{220}{80} = 2.75\;\Omega$$

### Part (b) — Number of Sections

Using the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{I_{min}}{I_{max}} = \frac{45}{80} = 0.5625 \\
n - 1 &= \frac{\log(k)}{\log\left(\frac{I_{max} \cdot R_a}{V}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

First, compute the internal reference:

$$\frac{I_{max} \cdot R_a}{V} = \frac{80 \times 0.5}{220} = \frac{40}{220} = 0.1818$$

Now:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n - 1 &= \frac{\log(0.5625)}{\log(0.1818)} \\
\log(0.5625) &= -0.2497 \\
\log(0.1818) &= -0.7404 \\
n - 1 &= \frac{-0.2497}{-0.7404} = 0.337
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $n-1$ must be an integer, we round **up** to $n-1 = 1$, giving $\mathbf{n = 2}$ sections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** In actual design, $n$ is always rounded up to the next integer to ensure $R_n \geq R_a$. We then **recompute** the actual $k$ and current limits using $n = 2$.

### Part (c) — Resistance of the First Section

With $n = 2$ sections, recompute $k$ using the alternative form:

$$k = \left(\frac{I_{min}}{I_{max}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n-1}} = \left(\frac{45}{80}\right)^{\frac{1}{1}} = 0.5625$$

Total resistance at stud 2:

$$R_2 = k \cdot R_1 = 0.5625 \times 2.75 = 1.547\;\Omega$$

Resistance of the **first section** (between stud 1 and stud 2):

$$R_{step,1} = R_1 - R_2 = 2.75 - 1.547 = 1.203\;\Omega$$

Resistance of the **second section** (which must bring total down to $R_a$):

$$R_{step,2} = R_2 - R_a = 1.547 - 0.5 = 1.047\;\Omega$$

### Part (d) — Total Resistance Cut Out

$$R_{total\,cut} = R_{step,1} + R_{step,2} = 1.203 + 1.047 = 2.25\;\Omega$$

**Verification:** $R_1 = R_a + R_{total\,cut} = 0.5 + 2.25 = 2.75\;\Omega\;\checkmark$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Starter Types

| Parameter | DOL | Star-Delta | Auto-Transformer | Rotor Resistance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage applied at start | $V_L$ (full) | $V_L/\sqrt{3}$ | $xV_L$ (tap) | Full + rotor R |
| Starting current (supply side) | $I_{sc}$ | $I_{sc}/3$ | $x \cdot I_{sc}$ | $I_{sc} \cdot \text{(controlled)}$ |
| Starting torque | $T_{st}$ | $T_{st}/3$ | $x^2 T_{st}$ | **High (up to 2.5×FLC torque)** |
| Cost | Lowest | Low | Medium-High | Medium |
| Motor type suitability | All 3-φ IM | Squirrel cage | Squirrel cage | **Slip-ring only** |
| Power range (typical) | < 5 HP | 5–25 HP | 10–200 HP | Any HP |
| Starting current peak | Highest | Low | Adjustable | Adjustable |
| Smoothness of start | Poor (single step) | Moderate (1 step) | Good (tap selection) | **Best (continuous)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule (KTU Practical Note):**
> - **Light load, no torque demand** → Star-Delta
> - **Adjustable start** → Auto-Transformer
> - **High starting torque** → Rotor Resistance Starter
> - **Small motors, simplicity** → DOL

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Note:** Notice in the 4-point starter, the NVC branch (yellow path) is **electrically independent** of the field winding branch (red path). This independence is the entire reason the 4-point starter exists.
