# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Why is a starter necessary for a DC shunt motor? Why is a 3-point starter not suitable for a wide range of speed control?*

**Model Answer:**

A starter is necessary because at the instant of starting, the back EMF $E_b$ is zero, and the armature current is limited only by the very small armature resistance $R_a$. This causes a starting current of $I_{st} = V/R_a$, which is 8 to 12 times the full-load current, leading to insulation damage and commutator problems. A starter inserts a variable external resistance to limit this current.

A 3-point starter is unsuitable for wide speed control because the no-volt coil (NVC) is connected in series with the shunt field winding. When the field rheostat is used to weaken the field for higher speeds, the NVC current reduces below its holding value, releasing the handle and disconnecting the motor — this is the "weak-field problem."

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write only one of the two parts. Both are required. Marks split: 1.5 for necessity, 1.5 for the weak-field issue.

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Compare star-delta and auto-transformer starters in terms of starting current and starting torque.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Star-Delta | Auto-Transformer |
|---|---|---|
| Starting current (from supply) | $I_{sc}/3$ | $x \cdot I_{sc}$ (where $x$ = tap fraction) |
| Starting torque | $T_{st}/3$ | $x^2 \cdot T_{st}$ |

For example, with a 60% tap, auto-transformer gives starting current = 0.6 × $I_{sc}$ and starting torque = 0.36 × $T_{st}$, which is **better than star-delta's 1/3 for current and matches for torque at 57.7%**. The key advantage is **flexibility** — the auto-transformer allows tap selection for fine tuning.

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. The structure is sub-part (a) for **7 marks** and sub-part (b) for **7 marks**.

### Question A (Choice 1) — Starter Design Calculation

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

A 400 V, 1500 RPM DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.4 Ω and a field winding resistance of 200 Ω. The full-load armature current is 50 A. The motor is to be started with a face-plate starter such that the current during starting does not exceed 1.5 times the full-load current. Calculate:

**(a)** The total starting resistance and the number of starter sections required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The resistance of each section and the back EMF at the instant the starter is on the third stud. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1: Identify given data**
- $V = 400$ V
- $R_a = 0.4$ Ω
- $I_{FL} = 50$ A
- $I_{max} = 1.5 \times 50 = 75$ A (given as upper limit)
- Lower limit $I_{min}$: Standard practice is to take $I_{min} \geq I_{FL}$. We take $I_{min} = I_{FL} = 50$ A (otherwise the motor will not develop rated torque between studs).

**Step 2: Total starting resistance at stud 1**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= \frac{V}{I_{max}} = \frac{400}{75} \\
R_1 &= 5.333\;\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the starter ratio $k$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{I_{min}}{I_{max}} = \frac{50}{75} = 0.6667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Find number of sections using the terminal condition $R_n = R_a$**

Using $R_n = k^{n-1} \cdot R_1 = R_a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.6667^{n-1} \times 5.333 &= 0.4 \\
0.6667^{n-1} &= \frac{0.4}{5.333} = 0.075 \\
(n-1)\log(0.6667) &= \log(0.075) \\
(n-1)(-0.1761) &= -1.1249 \\
n - 1 &= 6.39
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounding **up**: $n - 1 = 7$, so $\mathbf{n = 8}$ sections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification with $n = 8$:**
> $$R_8 = 0.6667^7 \times 5.333 = 0.0587 \times 5.333 = 0.313\;\Omega$$
> This is less than $R_a = 0.4$ Ω, which means by stud 8, more resistance has been cut out than required. So the **actual ratio** will be slightly different, but the design is safe.

**Final Answer Part (a):** $R_1 = 5.333$ Ω, $n = 8$ sections.

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1: Recompute actual $k$ using $n = 8$**

For accurate per-section resistance, the actual $k$ should satisfy $R_8 = R_a$ exactly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{actual} &= \left(\frac{R_a}{R_1}\right)^{\frac{1}{n-1}} = \left(\frac{0.4}{5.333}\right)^{\frac{1}{7}} \\
k_{actual} &= (0.075)^{0.1429} \\
\log(k_{actual}) &= 0.1429 \times \log(0.075) = 0.1429 \times (-1.1249) = -0.1608 \\
k_{actual} &= 10^{-0.1608} = 0.6904
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Choice:** We can either (a) keep the original $k = 0.6667$ and use $n = 8$ (which gives $R_8 < R_a$ — safe), or (b) recompute $k$ so that the boundary condition is exactly met. For KTU problems, **method (a)** is more common and acceptable.

**Step 2: Total resistance at each stud (using $k = 0.6667$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 5.333\;\Omega \\
R_2 &= k R_1 = 0.6667 \times 5.333 = 3.555\;\Omega \\
R_3 &= k R_2 = 0.6667 \times 3.555 = 2.370\;\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Resistance of each section**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{step,1} &= R_1 - R_2 = 5.333 - 3.555 = 1.778\;\Omega \\
R_{step,2} &= R_2 - R_3 = 3.555 - 2.370 = 1.185\;\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Back EMF at stud 3**

At stud 3, total circuit resistance = $R_3 = 2.370$ Ω. The current at this moment is $I_{max} = 75$ A.

By KVL: $V = E_{b,3} + I_{max} \cdot R_3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{b,3} &= V - I_{max} \cdot R_3 \\
E_{b,3} &= 400 - 75 \times 2.370 \\
E_{b,3} &= 400 - 177.75 \\
\boxed{E_{b,3} &= 222.25\;\text{V}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Part (b):** $R_{step,1} \approx 1.78$ Ω, $R_{step,2} \approx 1.19$ Ω, $E_{b,3} = 222.25$ V.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Starter Types & Comparison

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a 4-point starter for a DC shunt motor. What is its advantage over a 3-point starter? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a star-delta starter for a 3-phase induction motor. Derive the relationship between starting current and starting torque in star mode with respect to direct-on-line starting. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

**Construction (4 points, key points to mention):**

A 4-point starter has **four terminals**:
1. **L (Line)** — connected to the positive of the DC supply.
2. **A (Armature)** — connected to the armature terminal.
3. **F (Field)** — connected to the shunt field winding.
4. **The 4th point (NVC terminal)** — connected to the No-Volt Coil (NVC) through a high-value protective resistor.

The starter contains:
- A set of **resistance studs** mounted on an insulated plate.
- A **movable handle/arm** that slides over the studs.
- A **No-Volt Coil (NVC)** connected between the 4th point and the negative supply via a protective resistor (typically 200–500 Ω).
- An **Overload Release (OLR)** coil.
- A **spring** that returns the handle to the OFF position when the NVC de-energizes.

**Working:**

1. Handle is moved to stud 1. Full starting resistance is in series with the armature. Motor accelerates slowly.
2. As speed picks up, $E_b$ increases, current decreases. Handle is moved stud-by-stud to progressively cut out resistance.
3. At the final stud, all external resistance is excluded; motor runs at rated speed.
4. NVC is **energized independently** of the field current (it draws its own small current from the supply via the protective resistor). It holds the handle in the RUN position.

**Advantage over 3-point starter:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Critical Advantage:**
> In the 3-point starter, the NVC is in series with the field. When the field rheostat is used for speed control (weakening the field), the NVC current drops below the holding value, and the handle springs back, shutting down the motor.
>
> In the 4-point starter, the NVC current is **independent of field current** because it is connected directly across the supply through a protective resistor. Hence, **the weak-field problem is eliminated**, and the 4-point starter can be used for wide-range speed control applications.

#### Part (b) Solution

**Star-Delta Working:**

A star-delta starter has **three contactors**:
- **Main Contactor (MC)** — connects the stator terminals to the supply.
- **Star Contactor (YC)** — short-circuits the three stator terminals together (forming the star point) during starting.
- **Delta Contactor (KC)** — connects the stator terminals in delta configuration for running.

**Timing sequence:**
1. **Starting mode (0–5 seconds typical):** MC and YC are ON. Stator windings are in **star**. Phase voltage across each winding = $V_L/\sqrt{3}$.
2. **Transition:** YC is opened, KC is closed after a small time delay (to prevent short-circuit).
3. **Running mode:** MC and KC are ON. Stator windings are in **delta**. Phase voltage = $V_L$ (full line voltage).

**Derivation of Starting Current Ratio:**

Let $I_{ph,DO}$ = phase current in DOL starting, $Z$ = phase impedance.

DOL starting (delta):
- Phase voltage = $V_L$
- Phase current = $V_L / Z$
- Line current = $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L / Z$

Star starting:
- Phase voltage = $V_L/\sqrt{3}$
- Phase current = $(V_L/\sqrt{3}) / Z = V_L / (\sqrt{3} Z)$
- Line current = Phase current = $V_L / (\sqrt{3} Z)$

**Ratio of line currents:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_{L,star}}{I_{L,delta}} &= \frac{V_L / (\sqrt{3} Z)}{\sqrt{3} V_L / Z} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} Z} \cdot \frac{Z}{\sqrt{3} V_L} = \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{I_{L,star} = \frac{I_{L,DO}}{3}}$$

**Derivation of Starting Torque Ratio:**

Starting torque is proportional to the **square of the phase voltage** (since $T \propto V^2$ for induction motors at a given slip):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_{st,star}}{T_{st,delta}} &= \left(\frac{V_{ph,star}}{V_{ph,delta}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{V_L/\sqrt{3}}{V_L}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{T_{st,star} = \frac{T_{st,DO}}{3}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — The Common Mistake:**
> Many students write $I_{star} = I_{DO} / \sqrt{3}$ (which is **WRONG**). The correct ratio is **1/3** because line current and phase current have **different** relationships in star and delta. Memorize the **1/3 — 1/3 rule**: both starting current and starting torque reduce to one-third in star-delta starting.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save This Section for Last-Minute Revision):**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ A starter is required because starting current = $V/R_a$ is 8–12× the full-load value.
- ☐ As motor accelerates, $E_b$ grows and naturally limits current; starter cuts out resistance progressively.
- ☐ Three terminals in a 3-point starter: **L, A, F**. Four in a 4-point starter: **L, A, F, NVC** (separate).

### The Famous Formulas
- ☐ $R_1 = V/I_{max}$ — total starting resistance at first stud.
- ☐ $k = I_{min}/I_{max}$ — starter ratio (always less than 1).
- ☐ $k = (I_{max} \cdot R_a / V)^{1/(n-1)}$ — actual ratio formula.
- ☐ $R_n = k^{n-1} \cdot R_1 = R_a$ — boundary condition.
- ☐ Resistance of $n$-th section: $R_{step,n} = R_n (1-k)$.

### Key Differences (3-point vs 4-point)
- ☐ 3-point: NVC in series with field → **weak-field problem exists**.
- ☐ 4-point: NVC independent of field → **no weak-field problem**, but speed control via starter is not possible.

### Induction Motor Starter Rules
- ☐ DOL: 5–7× inrush, high torque, small motors only.
- ☐ Star-Delta: current and torque both 1/3 of DOL, light loads only.
- ☐ Auto-Transformer: current = $x \cdot I_{sc}$, torque = $x^2 \cdot T_{sc}$.
- ☐ Rotor Resistance: only for slip-ring IM, gives high starting torque.

### Numerical Problem Solving Sequence
- ☐ Step 1: Identify $V$, $R_a$, $I_{FL}$, $I_{max}$, $I_{min}$.
- ☐ Step 2: Compute $R_1 = V/I_{max}$.
- ☐ Step 3: Compute $k = I_{min}/I_{max}$.
- ☐ Step 4: Solve for $n$ using log formula; round up.
- ☐ Step 5: Compute $R_{step}$ for each section.
- ☐ Step 6: Verify with $E_b = V - I \cdot R_{total}$.

### Must-Remember Facts
- ☐ Auto-transformer current ratio is **linear** in $x$; torque ratio is **square** in $x$.
- ☐ Star-delta gives $V/\sqrt{3}$ per phase = 0.577 $V_L$ (this is the reason the tap is 57.7%).
- ☐ Rotor resistance starter is the **only** starter that gives high starting torque AND low starting current simultaneously.
- ☐ The starter is a **temporary** device — once motor reaches rated speed, it is fully cut out.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DOL Starter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** DOL Starter # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter** is the simplest and most widely used reduced-cost method for starting a three-phase squirrel-cage **induction motor**. In this method, the motor terminals are connected directly to the full-rated supply voltage by means of a main contactor, causing the motor to draw its **natural locked-rotor (starting) current** during the starting transient. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEOT402 – Module 4):** > The Direct-On-Line starter is treated as the *baseline reference starter*. All other starters (Star-Delta, Auto-Transformer, Rotor Resistance starters) are introduced as modifications designed to overcome the inherent drawbacks of the DOL method. **Key Standard Metrics (as per IS/IEC 60947-4-1):** - **Starting current** $I_{st} = (5 \text{ to } 7) \times I_{fl}$ for standard induction motors. - **Starting torque** $T_{st} = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5) \times T_{fl}$ for standard cage motors. - Typical permissible DOL starts per hour: **6 to 15 starts/hr** (depending on motor kW rating). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are pushing a heavy, loaded shopping cart that has been stationary for hours. If you **shove it suddenly with full force**, the wheels jerk forward, the cart shakes, and there is a high chance items will fall off or the cart may even tip. However, if you apply force **slowly and gradually** (ramping up), the cart moves smoothly with controlled acceleration. The induction motor behaves similarly. When the rotor is stationary, the relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor is at its **maximum** ($N_s - 0 = N_s$). By Faraday's law, this maximum slip induces a **very large EMF** in the rotor bars, which in turn draws a **very large current** from the supply (often 5–7 times the rated current). The DOL starter, by switching the motor directly to the line, subjects the supply to this enormous inrush — analogous to the "sudden shove" on the cart. > [!NOTE] > **Why not always use reduced-voltage starters then?** > Because the DOL starter is **simple, cheap, rugged, and provides maximum starting torque**. For small motors (typically up to **5 HP / 3.7 kW**), the supply system can easily absorb the inrush without significant voltage dip, making DOL the **economically optimal** choice. ## 1.3 Components of a DOL Starter A DOL starter is not just a switch; it is a carefully engineered assembly consisting of: | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Molded Case Circuit Breaker (MCCB) / Isolator** | Provides short-circuit protection and isolation for maintenance. | | **Main Contactor (3-pole electromagnetic contactor)** | Makes/breaks the three-phase power supply to the motor stator. | | **Overload Relay (OLR)** | Provides sustained overcurrent protection (e.g., 115% of FLC for > 1 hour); typically bimetallic or electronic. | | **Start Push-Button (NO contact)** | Energizes the contactor coil to start the motor. | | **Stop Push-Button (NC contact)** | De-energizes the contactor coil to stop the motor. | | **Holding/Auxiliary Contact (NO)** | Bypasses the Start push-button to keep the contactor latched. | | **Indicating Lamps (R, Y, G)** | Display power-on, running, and trip status. | | **Control Transformer / MCB** | Steps down and protects the control circuit. | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic – Step by Step The DOL starter is governed by a **two-circuit architecture**: a **Power Circuit** (carrying motor current) and a **Control Circuit** (carrying low-current switching signals). The interaction between them follows a strict, deterministic sequence. ### Step 1: Initial State - Three-phase **415 V, 50 Hz** AC supply is available at the input terminals. - The **Stop** push-button (NC) is in its normal-closed position. - The **Start** push-button (NO) is in its normal-open position. - The contactor coil `C` is **de-energized**, so the three main power contacts `1-2`, `3-4`, `5-6` are **open**, isolating the motor. - The auxiliary NO contact `13-14` is **open**. ### Step 2: Pressing the Start Push-Button When the Start button is momentarily pressed: - Control current flows: `Phase → Stop(NC) → Start(NO, now closed) → Contactor Coil C → Neutral`. - Contactor coil `C` energizes, producing an electromagnetic field. - The three main contacts (`1-2`, `3-4`, `5-6`) **close simultaneously**, applying full line voltage to the motor terminals (U, V, W). - The auxiliary contact `13-14` (NO) **closes** in parallel with the Start push-button. This is called the **holding (latching) contact** — it maintains the coil-energizing path even after the Start button is released. > [!NOTE] > **Why is the auxiliary contact needed?** > The Start button is **momentary**; releasing it would open the circuit and de-energize the coil. The auxiliary NO contact provides a **seal-in** path, so the contactor remains latched as long as the Stop button is not pressed or the OLR does not trip. ### Step 3: Motor Running State - The motor accelerates from standstill toward synchronous speed $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$. - During acceleration, slip $s$ decreases from 1 to rated slip $s_{fl}$ (typically 0.02–0.05). - The motor current decays from $I_{st}$ to full-load current $I_{fl}$. ### Step 4: Stopping the Motor When the Stop button is pressed: - The NC contact of the Stop button **opens**, breaking the holding path. - Coil `C` de-energizes. - All main contacts and the auxiliary holding contact **open by spring action**. - The motor is disconnected from the supply and decelerates to rest due to load + friction torque. ### Step 5: Overload Trip If motor current exceeds the OLR setting for a preset time (inverse-time characteristic): - The bimetallic strips in the OLR **bend and mechanically actuate** the NC contact in the control circuit. - The control circuit is broken → coil de-energizes → motor trips. - A **trip indicator** (often red) confirms the fault. ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Design | Design Element | Engineering Reason | |---|---| | Contactor instead of a switch | Remote operation, frequent switching, arc-quenching | | Separate control & power circuits | Operator safety (low-voltage control), ease of interlocks | | OLR in series with motor (not in control) | OLR must sense *motor* current, not coil current | | Holding contact | Avoids the need for the operator to keep button pressed | | Stop button in series (not in parallel) | **Fail-safe** design: any wire break stops the motor | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Master the following table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU numerical on induction motor starters. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | rpm | | 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | p.u. (0 to 1) | | 3 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz | | 4 | Stator-to-rotor turns ratio effect on induced EMF | $E_2' = s \cdot E_2$ | V (rotor EMF referred to stator at slip $s$) | | 5 | Rotor copper loss (mechanical form) | $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_{g2}$ | W | | 6 | Mechanical power developed | $P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_{g2}$ | W | | 7 | Starting / Full-load current ratio (cage motor) | $I_{st} = k \cdot I_{fl}$, where $k \in [5, 7]$ | p.u. | | 8 | Starting / Full-load torque ratio (cage motor) | $T_{st} = k^2 \cdot s_{fl} \cdot T_{fl}$ | p.u. | | 9 | Approximate starting torque formula | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{R_2' / s}$ | Nm (at $s = 1$) | | 10 | DOL Starting current (line) | $I_{L,st} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph,st}$ | A (3-φ) | **Boundary Conditions to Remember:** - At starting (DOL): $s = 1$, $N_r = 0$, rotor reactance at standstill = $X_2$ (maximum). - At running (steady state): $s = s_{fl} \approx 0.02$ to $0.05$, $N_r \approx N_s$. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility DOL starters are the **industry default** for: - **Pumps** (centrifugal, submersible) — up to ~5 HP. - **Fans and blowers** — light starting duty. - **Compressors** (small reciprocating) — moderate starting torque. - **Conveyor belts** (short, lightly loaded) — high starting torque available. - **Machine tools** (lathes, drills, milling machines) — frequent start/stop cycles. > [!NOTE] > In **KTU lab examinations**, students are commonly asked to *wire a DOL starter on a test bench* and verify the holding contact logic using a multimeter. Memorize the **latching sequence** — it is a guaranteed question. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why Starting Current is 5–7 times Full-Load Current We begin from the per-phase approximate equivalent circuit of an induction motor referred to the stator. $$ I_2' = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{\left( \dfrac{R_2'}{s} \right)^2 + (X_2')^2}} $$ This is the standard rotor-current equation (referred to stator) for an induction motor, derived by neglecting the magnetizing branch (a common KTU-level approximation for cage-motor analysis). **At starting:** $s = 1$ $$ I_{2,st}' = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{(R_2')^2 + (X_2')^2}} $$ **At full load (typical):** $s = s_{fl} \approx 0.03$ $$ I_{2,fl}' = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{\left( \dfrac{R_2'}{0.03} \right)^2 + (X_2')^2}} = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{(33.3 \, R_2')^2 + (X_2')^2}} $$ **Take the ratio:** $$ \dfrac{I_{2,st}'}{I_{2,fl}'} = \dfrac{\sqrt{(33.3 \, R_2')^2 + (X_2')^2}}{\sqrt{(R_2')^2 + (X_2')^2}} $$ **Typical cage motor design data:** $R_2' \approx 0.4 \, X_2'$. Substituting: $$ \dfrac{I_{2,st}'}{I_{2,fl}'} = \dfrac{\sqrt{(13.32)^2 + (1)^2}}{\sqrt{(0.4)^2 + (1)^2}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{177.4 + 1}}{\sqrt{0.16 + 1}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{178.4}}{\sqrt{1.16}} = \dfrac{13.36}{1.077} $$ $$ \dfrac{I_{2,st}'}{I_{2,fl}'} \approx 12.4 $$ > [!NOTE] > The theoretical ratio is often higher than the **practical 5–7** range because the **magnetizing branch** (which we neglected) diverts a portion of the stator current. The practical locked-rotor current is therefore somewhat lower than the simple $V_1 / Z_2'$ estimate. ## 3.2 Derivation: Starting Torque for DOL Starting The air-gap power per phase is: $$ P_{g2,\text{phase}} = (I_2')^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s} $$ Total air-gap power (3 phases): $$ P_{g2} = 3 (I_2')^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s} $$ The mechanical power developed (shaft + rotor copper loss): $$ P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_{g2} $$ Synchronous mechanical angular speed: $$ \omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P} $$ Torque developed: $$ T = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3 (I_2')^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s} \cdot (1-s)}{\omega_s} $$ At starting, $s = 1$, so $(1-s) = 0$ in the mechanical power term — *but* we use the air-gap power divided by synchronous speed to get the **electromagnetic torque at standstill**: $$ T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2}{(R_2')^2 + (X_2')^2} \cdot R_2' $$ This is the **DOL starting torque** equation. Rearranging: $$ T_{st} = \dfrac{3 \, V_1^2 \, R_2'}{\omega_s \left[ (R_2')^2 + (X_2')^2 \right]} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice: **$T_{st} \propto V_1^2$**. This is the foundation of all reduced-voltage starters. Halving the voltage reduces starting torque to **one-fourth**. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style, 14-Mark Standard) > **Problem:** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole squirrel-cage induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator: $R_1 = 0.6 \, \Omega$, $X_1 = 1.0 \, \Omega$, $R_2' = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.0 \, \Omega$. The motor is started using a **DOL starter**. > > **Calculate:** > (a) The starting current drawn by the motor. > (b) The starting torque developed. > (c) The full-load current and full-load torque, assuming full-load slip $s_{fl} = 0.04$. > (d) Comment on the suitability of DOL starting if the supply transformer is rated **100 kVA** with **5% impedance**. --- ### Part (a): Starting Current At starting, $s = 1$. Using the approximate equivalent circuit (neglecting magnetizing branch for high-current case): $$ I_{st} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{\sqrt{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}} $$ Per-phase voltage: $$ V_{ph} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V} $$ Substituting: $$ I_{st} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{(0.6 + 0.5)^2 + (1.0 + 1.0)^2}} $$ $$ I_{st} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{(1.1)^2 + (2.0)^2}} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{1.21 + 4.0}} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{5.21}} $$ $$ I_{st} = \dfrac{230.94}{2.2825} = 101.18 \text{ A} $$ **Line starting current** (3-φ, star-connected assumed inside delta-equivalent): $$ I_{L,st} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{st} = 175.26 \text{ A} $$ > [!NOTE] > If the motor is delta-connected (as most cage motors are), the line current equals the per-phase current in the simplified model — be sure to clarify the connection in your answer. ### Part (b): Starting Torque Synchronous speed: $$ N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm} $$ Synchronous angular speed: $$ \omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s} $$ Starting torque: $$ T_{st} = \dfrac{3 \cdot I_{st}^2 \cdot R_2'}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3 \times (101.18)^2 \times 0.5}{157.08} $$ $$ T_{st} = \dfrac{3 \times 10237.4 \times 0.5}{157.08} = \dfrac{15356.1}{157.08} = 97.75 \text{ Nm} $$ ### Part (c): Full-Load Current and Torque At $s_{fl} = 0.04$: $$ I_{fl} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{\sqrt{\left(R_1 + \dfrac{R_2'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}} $$ $$ \dfrac{R_2'}{s_{fl}} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.04} = 12.5 \, \Omega $$ $$ I_{fl} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{(0.6 + 12.5)^2 + (2.0)^2}} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{(13.1)^2 + 4.0}} $$ $$ I_{fl} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{171.61 + 4.0}} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{175.61}} = \dfrac{230.94}{13.252} $$ $$ I_{fl} = 17.43 \text{ A (per phase)} $$ Full-load torque: $$ T_{fl} = \dfrac{3 \cdot I_{fl}^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s_{fl}} \cdot (1-s_{fl})}{\omega_s} $$ $$ T_{fl} = \dfrac{3 \times (17.43)^2 \times 12.5 \times 0.96}{157.08} $$ $$ T_{fl} = \dfrac{3 \times 303.80 \times 12.5 \times 0.96}{157.08} = \dfrac{10936.8}{157.08} = 69.62 \text{ Nm} $$ **Verification of current ratio:** $$ \dfrac{I_{st}}{I_{fl}} = \dfrac{101.18}{17.43} \approx 5.81 $$ This is within the typical 5–7 range. ✓ **Verification of torque ratio:** $$ \dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{fl}} = \dfrac{97.75}{69.62} \approx 1.40 $$ This is within the typical 1.5–2.5 range. ✓ ### Part (d): Suitability of DOL Starting Transformer full-load current: $$ I_{TR,fl} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400} = 144.34 \text{ A} $$ Voltage dip during starting (approximate, using per-unit method): $$ \% \text{ Dip} = \dfrac{I_{st,pu}}{1 + \text{Source p.u. impedance}} $$ Assuming the motor starting current is about $5.81 \times 17.43 = 101.18$ A, the starting kVA: $$ S_{st} = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 101.18 = 70.10 \text{ kVA} $$ As a fraction of transformer rating: $$ \dfrac{S_{st}}{S_{TR}} = \dfrac{70.10}{100} = 0.701 \text{ p.u. (70.1\%)} $$ This is a **substantial demand** (~70% of transformer capacity) — likely to cause a noticeable voltage dip on a shared supply and may affect other connected loads. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to include the source (transformer) impedance in the calculation. A transformer rated 100 kVA with 5% impedance means it can deliver up to **2000 A** into a short circuit, but during motor starting, the dip on the bus is still significant. Always compute **per-unit starting kVA** vs transformer kVA for a complete suitability analysis. **Verdict:** DOL starting is **marginally acceptable** here, but if other sensitive loads share the bus, a reduced-voltage starter (e.g., Star-Delta) is recommended. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DOL vs Other Starters | Feature | DOL | Star-Delta | Auto-Transformer | Soft Starter | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Starting voltage** | 100% $V_L$ | 57.7% $V_L$ ($1/\sqrt{3}$) | 50–80% $V_L$ (taps) | 30–100% $V_L$ (ramp) | | **Starting current** | 100% $I_{st}$ | 33.3% $I_{st}$ | $k^2 \times I_{st}$ | Adjustable | | **Starting torque** | 100% $T_{st}$ | 33.3% $T_{st}$ | $k^2 \times T_{st}$ | Adjustable | | **Cost** | **Lowest** | Low | Medium | High | | **Typical motor size** | ≤ 5 HP | 5–25 HP | 10–100+ HP | All sizes | | **Starting transients** | Severe | Moderate (2-step) | Moderate (tapped) | Smooth (ramp) | | **Maintenance** | Minimal | Moderate | Moderate | Higher (electronics) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > **KTU Practical Tip:** In the lab exam, you will be expected to draw the *control circuit* on paper, identify each component by its BIS/IS symbol, and trace the current path verbally. Practice the latching logic diagram below until you can reproduce it from memory. ## 4.1 Power Circuit Block Diagram The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **power flow path** from the 3-phase supply to the induction motor through the DOL starter assembly. ```mermaid graph LR SUP["3-Phase 415V 50Hz Supply"] --> MCCB["MCCB / Isolator

(3-Pole, Electromagnetic)"] CONT --> OLR["Overload Relay
(Bimetallic, Inverse-Time)"] OLR --> M["3-Phase Squirrel-Cage

(If Star Connected)"] classDef power fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a1a1a classDef protect fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a1a1a classDef load fill:#d4edda,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a1a1a class SUP,CONT,RTN power class MCCB,OLR protect class M load ``` ## 4.2 Control Circuit Latching Logic The following Mermaid state diagram captures the **latching (seal-in) behavior** of the DOL starter, which is the most frequently examined concept in KTU theory papers. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle: Supply ON Idle: Coil De-Energized
Motor Stopped Idle --> Starting: Press START Button Starting: Coil Energized

(Auxiliary NO holds coil) Running: Holding Contact Closed

(Sustained Overcurrent) Stopping: Coil De-Energized

Motor Decelerates Tripped: Coil De-Energized
Red Indicator ON Stopping --> Idle: Motor at Rest Tripped --> Idle: Reset OLR Manually Idle --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow (Detailed Block View) For students preparing for the *design / viva* portion of the KTU lab exam, the following block-level functional architecture summarizes **how signals and power interact** in a complete DOL starter panel. ```mermaid graph TB subgraph PWR["POWER CIRCUIT (3-Phase, 415V)"] P1["L1 / R-Phase Input"] --> P2["MCCB Pole 1"] P3["L2 / Y-Phase Input"] --> P4["MCCB Pole 2"] P5["L3 / B-Phase Input"] --> P6["MCCB Pole 3"] P2 --> P7["Contactor Main Contact 1-2"] P4 --> P8["Contactor Main Contact 3-4"] P6 --> P9["Contactor Main Contact 5-6"] P7 --> P10["OLR Phase 1"] P8 --> P11["OLR Phase 2"] P9 --> P12["OLR Phase 3"] P10 --> P13["Motor Terminal U"] P11 --> P14["Motor Terminal V"] P12 --> P15["Motor Terminal W"] end subgraph CTL["CONTROL CIRCUIT (1-Phase, 230V)"] C1["Phase Supply"] --> C2["Control MCB"] C2 --> C3["STOP Push-Button NC"] C3 --> C4["START Push-Button NO"] C3 --> C5["OLR NC Contact 95-96"] C4 --> C6["Node X
(Coil Input)"] C5 --> C6 C6 --> C7["Contactor Coil C"] C7 --> C8["Neutral"] C6 --> C9["Auxiliary NO Contact 13-14
(Holding/Seal-In)"] C9 --> C6 end subgraph IND["INDICATORS"] I1["RED Lamp: Power ON"] I2["GREEN Lamp: Motor Running"] I3["AMBER Lamp: OLR Tripped"] end C9 -.latches coil.-> C7 P13 -.drives.-> MTR["3-Phase Induction Motor"] P14 -.drives.-> MTR P15 -.drives.-> MTR ``` ## 4.4 Conventional Wiring Diagram (Schematic Representation) The schematic below represents the **standard IS/IEC symbology** used in KTU answer scripts and industrial wiring diagrams. ``` POWER CIRCUIT CONTROL CIRCUIT R ──[MCCB]──┬──/(C1)──┬──[OLR-1]─── U │ │ Y ──[MCCB]──┼──/(C2)──┼──[OLR-2]─── V R ──[MCB]──[STOP]──┬──[START NO]──┬──(C)── N │ │ │ │ B ──[MCCB]──┴──/(C3)──┴──[OLR-3]─── W │ [AUX NO 13-14] │ │ └──[OLR-NC 95-96]──┘ LEGEND: /(Cn) = Main contactor power contacts (normally open, closed when coil C is energized) [OLR] = Overload relay heater coils (one per phase, in power circuit) [C] = Contactor coil (in control circuit) [AUX] = Auxiliary normally-open contact (in control circuit, mechanically linked to C) [STOP] = Normally-closed push-button (in series — fail-safe) [START] = Normally-open push-button (momentary, in parallel with AUX) [OLR-NC]= OLR auxiliary contact 95-96 (in series with coil, opens on trip) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** When drawing the schematic in the exam, always: > 1. Show the **power circuit on top** and the **control circuit on the bottom** (or side-by-side, clearly separated). > 2. Number the terminals (1–2, 3–4, 5–6 for power; A1–A2 for coil; 13–14 for auxiliary NO; 95–96 for OLR NC) — examiners award marks for this **standardization**. > 3. Mark the **mechanical linkage** between the coil `C` and all its contacts with a **dashed line**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define a DOL starter. Why is it preferred for motors up to 5 HP?** **Model Answer:** A **Direct-On-Line (DOL) starter** is a motor-starting device that connects a three-phase induction motor directly to the full-rated supply voltage through a main contactor, with no impedance inserted in the stator circuit during starting. It is preferred for motors up to **5 HP (≈ 3.7 kW)** for the following reasons: 1. The starting inrush current (5–7 times FLC) is small in absolute magnitude (typically < 30 A), so it does not cause a significant voltage dip on the supply. 2. It provides the **maximum possible starting torque**, which is essential for loads that require high breakaway torque. 3. It is the **simplest, most rugged, and least expensive** starter — minimum components, minimum failure points. 4. No reduced-voltage transitions mean **no torque pulsations** during start-up. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for current rationale + 1 for torque/cost rationale)* --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is the function of the auxiliary contact (13-14) in a DOL starter control circuit?** **Model Answer:** The auxiliary contact labeled **13-14** is a **normally-open (NO) contact** that is mechanically linked to the main contactor's armature. Its function is to act as a **holding (or seal-in) contact**. **Operating sequence:** 1. When the operator presses the **START** push-button, the contactor coil energizes and the main power contacts close, starting the motor. 2. Simultaneously, the auxiliary contact **13-14 closes** in parallel with the START push-button. 3. When the operator **releases the START** button, current continues to flow through the closed 13-14 contact, keeping the coil energized. 4. This **latches** the contactor in the energized state until the **STOP** button is pressed (which breaks the seal-in path) or the **OLR trips**. Without this contact, the operator would have to **hold the START button continuously**, which is impractical and unsafe. *(3 marks: 1 for identification + 1 for latching mechanism + 1 for safety/practicality)* --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** For every 14-mark question, the paper provides **internal choice** between two questions (Or option). Below, both alternatives are fully solved so you can practice both paths. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **power and control circuits** of a DOL starter used for a three-phase induction motor. Label all components and current paths. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-phase, **400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole**, squirrel-cage induction motor has per-phase parameters (referred to stator): $R_1 = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $X_1 = 1.2 \, \Omega$, $R_2' = 0.4 \, \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.2 \, \Omega$. Using a **DOL starter**, calculate: **(i)** the starting current, **(ii)** the starting torque, and **(iii)** the ratio of starting current to full-load current if the full-load slip is **0.04**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to A(a): **Components in the power circuit:** - Three-phase **MCCB** (or isolator + fuses) for short-circuit protection. - **Main contactor** (3-pole electromagnetic) with power contacts `1-2, 3-4, 5-6`. - **Overload relay (OLR)** with three heater coils (one per phase) for sustained overcurrent protection. - The motor terminals **U, V, W**. **Components in the control circuit (single-phase, 230 V derived via control transformer or directly):** - **Control MCB** for protection. - **STOP push-button** (NC) in series — fail-safe placement. - **OLR auxiliary contact 95-96** (NC) in series — opens on trip. - **START push-button** (NO) in parallel with the auxiliary holding contact. - **Holding contact 13-14** (NO) of the contactor. - **Contactor coil** `C` (terminals A1, A2). **Operating sequence (already covered in Section 2.1):** Press START → coil energizes → main contacts close → motor starts → auxiliary 13-14 closes → release START → coil remains latched. Press STOP or OLR trips → coil de-energizes → all contacts open. **Schematic:** Refer to Section 4.4 above for the standard diagram. *(7 marks: 2 for power circuit + 2 for control circuit + 2 for operating sequence + 1 for neat labeled schematic)* --- #### Solution to A(b): **Given:** - $V_L = 400$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$ poles - $R_1 = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $X_1 = 1.2 \, \Omega$ - $R_2' = 0.4 \, \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.2 \, \Omega$ - $s_{fl} = 0.04$ **Per-phase voltage:** $$ V_{ph} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V} $$ **(i) Starting current** (at $s = 1$): $$ I_{st} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{\sqrt{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}} $$ $$ I_{st} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{(0.5 + 0.4)^2 + (1.2 + 1.2)^2}} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{(0.9)^2 + (2.4)^2}} $$ $$ I_{st} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{0.81 + 5.76}} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{6.57}} = \dfrac{230.94}{2.563} $$ $$ \boxed{I_{st} = 90.10 \text{ A (per phase)}} $$ **Synchronous speed and angular speed:** $$ N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm} $$ $$ \omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s} $$ **(ii) Starting torque:** $$ T_{st} = \dfrac{3 \cdot I_{st}^2 \cdot R_2'}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3 \times (90.10)^2 \times 0.4}{157.08} $$ $$ T_{st} = \dfrac{3 \times 8118.01 \times 0.4}{157.08} = \dfrac{9741.6}{157.08} $$ $$ \boxed{T_{st} = 62.02 \text{ Nm}} $$ **(iii) Full-load current** (at $s_{fl} = 0.04$): $$ \dfrac{R_2'}{s_{fl}} = \dfrac{0.4}{0.04} = 10.0 \, \Omega $$ $$ I_{fl} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{(0.5 + 10.0)^2 + (1.2 + 1.2)^2}} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{(10.5)^2 + (2.4)^2}} $$ $$ I_{fl} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{110.25 + 5.76}} = \dfrac{230.94}{\sqrt{116.01}} = \dfrac{230.94}{10.770} $$ $$ I_{fl} = 21.44 \text{ A (per phase)} $$ **Current ratio:** $$ \dfrac{I_{st}}{I_{fl}} = \dfrac{90.10}{21.44} \approx \boxed{4.20} $$ > [!NOTE] > A current ratio of **4.2** is **at the lower end** of the typical 5–7 range, which makes sense because the rotor resistance $R_2' = 0.4 \, \Omega$ is **relatively large** compared to its reactance (ratio 0.4/1.2 = 0.33). Motors designed for **high starting torque** often have a higher $R_2'/X_2'$ ratio — this slightly suppresses the starting current while boosting starting torque. *(7 marks: 2 for starting current + 2 for starting torque + 2 for full-load current + 1 for current ratio and comment)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]` **(a)** Explain, with phasor reasoning, why the **starting current** of an induction motor is **5 to 7 times** the full-load current when started by a **DOL starter**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** List and explain **any four advantages** and **any four disadvantages** of the DOL starter. Under what conditions would you recommend a DOL starter over a Star-Delta starter? **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to B(a): **Phasor Reasoning for High Starting Current:** At the instant of starting, the rotor is **stationary** ($N_r = 0$), so the slip $s = 1$. The relative velocity between the rotating magnetic field (synchronous speed $N_s$) and the rotor is at its **maximum** value of $N_s$. **Step 1 — Frequency in rotor:** The frequency of the induced EMF in the rotor circuit is $f_r = s \cdot f$. At start, $f_r = f$ (i.e., 50 Hz, equal to stator frequency). **Step 2 — Rotor reactance at standstill:** The rotor leakage reactance referred to the stator is: $$ X_2' = 2 \pi f L_2' $$ This is the **maximum possible** rotor reactance, because the frequency is at its rated value. **Step 3 — Rotor impedance at standstill:** $$ Z_2' = \sqrt{(R_2')^2 + (X_2')^2} $$ Since $R_2'$ is small (typically 0.3–0.5 $\Omega$) and $X_2'$ is at its maximum, the impedance is **dominated by reactance** and the **power factor is very low** (typically 0.3–0.4 lagging). **Step 4 — Stator current phasor:** Neglecting the magnetizing branch for this high-current approximation: $$ \vec{I}_{st} = \dfrac{\vec{V}_1}{\vec{Z}_1 + \vec{Z}_2'} $$ where $\vec{Z}_1 = R_1 + jX_1$ and $\vec{Z}_2' = R_2' + jX_2'$ (at $s = 1$). The denominator magnitude is **small** (e.g., 2.5 $\Omega$ in our example), and the phase angle is **large** (60–70° lagging), so: - The current magnitude is **5–7×** the rated value. - The power factor is **poor** (0.3–0.4), which means much of the current is reactive. **Step 5 — Contrast with running condition:** At full load, $s \approx 0.04$, so the effective rotor resistance $R_2'/s \approx 25 \cdot R_2'$, which is now much larger than the reactance. The impedance is **large** and **mostly resistive** (high power factor ~0.85). The current is therefore small and in phase with the voltage. **Conclusion:** The high starting current arises from the **low rotor impedance at standstill** (low $R_2'/s$ ratio, dominant $X_2'$), which is a direct consequence of **Faraday's law applied to a stationary conductor in a rotating field**. *(7 marks: 2 for slip/rotor frequency analysis + 2 for impedance evaluation at start + 2 for phasor and PF argument + 1 for the contrast with running condition)* --- #### Solution to B(b): **Four Advantages of DOL Starter:** 1. **Simple and inexpensive construction:** It uses only one contactor, one OLR, and two push-buttons. The total component count is the lowest among all starters, reducing capital cost and maintenance overhead. 2. **Highest starting torque:** Since full line voltage is applied, the starting torque is at its **maximum** ($T_{st} \approx 1.5$ to $2.5 \times T_{fl}$). This is critical for high-inertia loads or loads with high breakaway friction (e.g., loaded conveyors, positive-displacement pumps). 3. **Smooth acceleration (single-step):** There are no voltage transitions or torque dips during starting. The motor accelerates **monotonically** from zero to rated speed in a single smooth ramp. 4. **Compact size and easy maintenance:** The entire assembly fits in a small enclosure. Spare parts (contact kits, OLR elements) are universally available and easy to replace. **Four Disadvantages of DOL Starter:** 1. **Very high starting current (5–7× FLC):** This causes a significant **voltage dip** on the supply, which can disturb other connected loads (lights flickering, contactors dropping out, electronic equipment malfunctioning). 2. **Severe mechanical stress on the motor and driven load:** The abrupt torque application produces **mechanical shock** on couplings, belts, gears, and foundations. Belt slippage, shaft fatigue, and bearing wear are accelerated. 3. **Not suitable for large motors:** For motors above **5–10 HP**, the inrush current is so large that it can trip upstream protection devices (MCCBs, fuses) unless they are oversized — defeating the purpose of protection. 4. **High thermal stress on motor windings:** The $I^2R$ heating during the starting transient is severe (proportional to $I_{st}^2 \approx 25$ to $49 \times I_{fl}^2$). Frequent starts (e.g., more than 15 per hour) can cause cumulative insulation damage. **When to prefer DOL over Star-Delta:** A DOL starter should be chosen over a Star-Delta starter under the following conditions: - **Motor rating ≤ 5 HP (3.7 kW):** The absolute starting current is small enough not to disturb the supply. - **The load requires high starting torque** (typically > 50% of $T_{fl}$): Examples include loaded conveyors, positive-displacement pumps, and compressors. Star-Delta provides only **33.3%** of DOL starting torque, which may be insufficient to break away the load. - **The starting duration is short** and starts are infrequent. - **Cost is a primary constraint** and the supply system is robust (e.g., dedicated industrial feeder with high short-circuit capacity). - **Simplicity and reliability** are paramount (e.g., remote agricultural pump sets, emergency fans). *(7 marks: 1.5 per advantage × 4 = 6 marks + 0.5 per disadvantage × 4 = 2 marks + 1 mark for the recommendation summary. Total = 9 marks → rebalanced: 1 per advantage = 4, 0.75 per disadvantage = 3, total 7 marks for the comparison portion)* **Balanced marking (as per KTU pattern):** - Advantages: 4 × 1 = 4 marks - Disadvantages: 3 × 1 = 3 marks *(any 3 out of 4)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Confusing rotor and stator impedances:** Many students write the starting current equation using $R_2' / s$ instead of $R_2'$ (i.e., forgetting that at starting, $s = 1$). This gives a **higher** $I_{st}$ than reality and leads to inconsistent answers. > 2. **Forgetting the per-phase to line conversion:** Always state whether the answer is per-phase or line current. If the motor is delta-connected, the line current is $\sqrt{3} \times$ the per-phase (stator) current. > 3. **Mixing up `13-14` and `95-96`:** `13-14` is the **contactor auxiliary NO** (holding); `95-96` is the **OLR NC** (trip). Swapping these will lose 1–2 marks instantly on the schematic. > 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers:** KTU examiners **strictly deduct** 0.5 marks for missing units (V, A, Nm, rpm, rad/s). > 5. **Drawing the schematic without numbering terminals:** Always use the standard IS/IEC terminal numbers — this signals *professional competence* and earns full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:** - ☐ **DOL = Direct-On-Line** = motor terminals connected to full line voltage via a contactor, no impedance in the stator circuit. - ☐ Suitable for **motors up to ~5 HP** (3.7 kW); for larger motors, the inrush causes unacceptable voltage dip. - ☐ Starting current = **5–7 × FLC**; Starting torque = **1.5–2.5 × FLT** for standard cage motors. - ☐ **Power circuit** components: MCCB → Contactor → OLR → Motor (in that order). - ☐ **Control circuit** components: Control MCB → STOP (NC) → OLR-NC (95-96) → parallel combination of START (NO) and AUX-NO (13-14) → Coil (A1-A2) → Neutral. - ☐ **13-14 = holding contact** (NO, latches the coil); **95-96 = OLR trip contact** (NC, opens on overload). - ☐ STOP button is placed in **series** (fail-safe); START button is in **parallel** with the holding contact. - ☐ Starting current formula: $I_{st} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{\sqrt{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$ at $s = 1$. - ☐ Starting torque formula: $T_{st} = \dfrac{3 \, I_{st}^2 \, R_2'}{\omega_s}$ at $s = 1$. - ☐ $T_{st} \propto V^2$ — this is the basis of all reduced-voltage starters. - ☐ **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ — memorize this; it appears in **every** numerical. - ☐ **$\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$** — be careful with units (rad/s, not rpm!). - ☐ DOL is **cheapest, simplest, and highest starting torque** among all starters, but it draws the **highest starting current** and is the **most stressful** on the supply and the motor. - ☐ The **auxiliary holding contact** is the heart of the control circuit — without it, the START button would have to be held continuously. - ☐ The OLR provides **overload protection** (sustained moderate overcurrent), **not short-circuit protection** (which is the MCCB's job). --- **End of Module 4 — DOL Starter Notes** *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework.*

topics.title: Autotransformer Starter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Autotransformer Starter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An **Autotransformer Starter** is a reduced-voltage starting method used for three-phase squirrel-cage induction motors in which a step-down **autotransformer** is connected between the supply and the motor terminals during the starting period. The reduced voltage is obtained by selecting an appropriate **fixed tap** on the autotransformer winding (typically **50%, 65%, or 80%** of the line voltage), thereby limiting the inrush current and the starting torque to safe mechanical and electrical values.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are trying to drink a very thick milkshake through a straw. If you try to suck with full force right away, the straw collapses, the liquid splashes, and you choke. But if you **pre-thin the milkshake with milk** (reducing its "viscosity resistance") for the first few sips, you can establish a smooth flow, and *then* you go for the full thick milkshake.

In the same way, a squirrel-cage induction motor, when switched directly on a **415 V** line, draws **5 to 7 times** its rated current (the "thick milkshake"). This causes:
- Voltage dip in the supply network
- Mechanical shock to the driven load
- Overheating of the winding

The autotransformer acts like the **"thinning milk"** — it temporarily **reduces the applied voltage** so that the motor draws less current and produces less torque for the first few seconds. Once the motor accelerates to ~80% speed, the autotransformer is **bypassed**, and the motor is connected directly to the full mains supply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Autotransformer and not a Regular Two-Winding Transformer?**
> - **Lower cost** (single winding, less copper, less core material).
> - **Smaller size** for the same kVA handling.
> - **Higher efficiency** because power transfer is mostly by *conduction* (direct connection) and only a small fraction flows *transformer-wise* through magnetic coupling.
> - **Tap changing** is simpler — just a contactor selecting the desired tap.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- Standard tap settings: **50%, 65%, 80%** of line voltage.
- Typical DOL starting current: **5 to 7 × Full Load Current (FLC)**.
- Starting torque with autotransformer: **25%, 42%, 64%** of DOL torque (for the 50%, 65%, 80% taps respectively, since torque ∝ V²).
- Power factor during starting: **0.3 to 0.5** (lagging).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operating Principle — Structured Logic

The autotransformer starter operates in **three distinct stages**:

1. **Stage 1 — Starting (Reduced Voltage Mode)**
   - Contactor **S** (Start) is closed; contactor **R** (Run) is open.
   - The motor is fed through the autotransformer at a fraction `a` of the line voltage (`a = 0.5, 0.65, or 0.8`).
   - Motor terminal voltage: $V_m = a \cdot V_L$
   - Motor starting current: $I_m = a \cdot I_{DOL}$
   - Line current drawn from supply: $I_L = a^2 \cdot I_{DOL}$
   - Motor accelerates under reduced torque $T_{st} = a^2 \cdot T_{DOL}$.

2. **Stage 2 — Transition (Open-Circuit Transition)**
   - Contactor **S** is opened (motor momentarily disconnected from autotransformer).
   - The motor coasts; its speed does not collapse instantly due to inertia.
   - This transition is **brief** (~50–100 ms) to avoid excessive speed drop.

3. **Stage 3 — Running (Full Voltage Mode)**
   - Contactor **R** is closed; the motor is now directly across the line.
   - Motor runs at full voltage and develops rated torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The line current reduction is **proportional to the SQUARE of the tap ratio**, but the torque reduction is also proportional to the square of the tap ratio. This is why autotransformer starting is preferred over star-delta for **high-inertia loads** — at the 80% tap, you still get **64% of DOL torque**, which is often enough to break the load loose.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Tap ratio | $a = V_{\text{tap}} / V_L$ | per unit (pu) | a = 0.5, 0.65, 0.8 |
| 2 | Motor voltage during start | $V_m = a \cdot V_L$ | Volts (V) | Reduced applied voltage |
| 3 | Motor starting current | $I_m = a \cdot I_{DOL}$ | Amperes (A) | Direct DOL current reduced by `a` |
| 4 | **Line current from supply** | $I_L = a^2 \cdot I_{DOL}$ | Amperes (A) | **Most important for KTU** |
| 5 | Starting torque | $T_{st} = a^2 \cdot T_{DOL}$ | N·m | Because $T \propto V^2$ |
| 6 | Motor input kVA (starter) | $S_m = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_m \cdot I_m$ | kVA | 3-phase apparent power |
| 7 | Supply kVA | $S_L = a \cdot S_m$ | kVA | Reduced by factor `a` |
| 8 | Autotransformer rating | $S_{AT} = (1 - a) \cdot S_m$ | kVA | The "transformed" portion only |
| 9 | Torque reduction factor | $T_{st}/T_{DOL} = a^2$ | pu | 0.25, 0.4225, 0.64 for the 3 taps |
| 10 | Current reduction factor | $I_L/I_{DOL} = a^2$ | pu | Same as torque reduction factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Autotransformer starters are extensively used in **centrifugal pumps, large fans, blowers, compressors, and conveyor belts** where the starting torque requirement is moderate (not extremely high) and the supply network is sensitive to voltage dips. They are also used as **Korndorfer starters** (closed-transition variant) in industrial plants.

### Why the Formula $I_L = a^2 \cdot I_{DOL}$ Matters

This is the **central insight** that KTU examiners love to test. Consider:
- If we just inserted a series reactor (reactor starting), the line current equals the motor current — only a factor of `a` reduction.
- But with an autotransformer, the **current transformation** in the transformer step-down action causes an *additional* factor of `a` reduction, giving $a \times a = a^2$.

This **double reduction** is what makes the autotransformer starter **superior to reactor starting** for the same voltage tap.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Line Current vs. Motor Current Relationship

**Given:**
- A three-phase step-down autotransformer with primary (line) winding turns $N_1$ and secondary (motor) winding turns $N_2$.
- Tap ratio: $a = N_2 / N_1 = V_m / V_L$ (where $V_m$ is the motor terminal voltage and $V_L$ is the line voltage).

**Step 1 — Voltage relation across the autotransformer:**

The autotransformer is a step-down transformer, so the voltage across the motor terminals is a fraction `a` of the line voltage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_m &= a \cdot V_L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Current relation inside the autotransformer:**

For an ideal transformer (ignoring losses and magnetizing current), the ampere-turns must balance between the primary and secondary windings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 \cdot I_1 &= N_2 \cdot I_2 \\
\frac{I_1}{I_2} &= \frac{N_2}{N_1} = a
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the current **leaving the autotransformer secondary** (i.e., entering the motor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_m &= I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

And the current **entering the autotransformer primary** (i.e., drawn from the line):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= I_1 = a \cdot I_2 = a \cdot I_m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the motor starting current:**

The motor's own starting characteristic (at reduced voltage $V_m = a V_L$) gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_m &= a \cdot I_{DOL}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $I_{DOL}$ is the current the motor would have drawn under Direct-On-Line starting (at full voltage $V_L$). This is because the motor's impedance is approximately constant, so current is proportional to voltage.

**Step 4 — Final expression for line current:**

Substituting the expression for $I_m$ into the line current equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= a \cdot I_m \\
&= a \cdot (a \cdot I_{DOL}) \\
&= a^2 \cdot I_{DOL}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The line current drawn from the supply is reduced by a factor of $a^2$ compared to DOL starting, while the motor terminal voltage is reduced by a factor of $a$. This is the **defining feature** of autotransformer starting.

---

### Derivation 2: Starting Torque Reduction

**Given:**
- Induction motor torque equation: $T = \dfrac{3}{2\pi n_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2 / s}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}$
- At starting slip $s = 1$, the torque is proportional to the square of the applied voltage.

**Step 1 — General torque-voltage proportionality:**

For a squirrel-cage induction motor, at any given slip (and in particular at $s = 1$, i.e., starting), the air-gap torque is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \propto V^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Torque under DOL starting:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{DOL} &= k \cdot V_L^2
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k$ is a constant depending on motor parameters.

**Step 3 — Torque under autotransformer starting:**

The motor sees voltage $V_m = a V_L$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{st} &= k \cdot V_m^2 = k \cdot (a V_L)^2 = a^2 \cdot k V_L^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Ratio of starting torques:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_{st}}{T_{DOL}} &= a^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical illustration for the three standard taps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a = 0.50 &\Rightarrow T_{st}/T_{DOL} = 0.25 \quad (25\% \text{ of DOL torque}) \\
a = 0.65 &\Rightarrow T_{st}/T_{DOL} = 0.4225 \quad (42.25\% \text{ of DOL torque}) \\
a = 0.80 &\Rightarrow T_{st}/T_{DOL} = 0.64 \quad (64\% \text{ of DOL torque})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Derivation 3: Autotransformer kVA Rating (Why It Is Smaller Than Motor kVA)

**Step 1 — Total power delivered to the motor:**

The motor input apparent power (3-phase) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_m &= \sqrt{3} \cdot V_m \cdot I_m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Power drawn from the supply:**

The supply apparent power is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_L &= \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $V_m = a V_L$ and $I_L = a \cdot I_m$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_L &= \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot (a \cdot I_m) \\
&= a \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_m \\
&= a \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{V_m}{a} \cdot I_m \\
&= \sqrt{3} \cdot V_m \cdot I_m = S_m
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this is the apparent power at the line. To get the apparent power **rating of the autotransformer winding itself** (the part that is "transformed" through magnetic action, not conducted directly), we use the principle of autotransformer action:

**Step 3 — Portion of power transferred by transformation:**

In an autotransformer, only the power corresponding to the tapped portion is transferred through the magnetic core. The remainder flows by direct conduction.

The "transformed" kVA rating of the autotransformer is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{AT} &= (1 - a) \cdot S_m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check at 65% tap:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{AT} &= (1 - 0.65) \cdot S_m = 0.35 \cdot S_m
\end{aligned}
$$

So the autotransformer only needs to be rated at **35% of the motor kVA** — this is why autotransformers are physically small and cheap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The autotransformer is rated for $(1-a)$ of the motor kVA. The remaining `a` portion is conducted directly from line to load through the common winding, which is essentially just a wire.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Parameter | DOL Starting | Autotransformer (tap `a`) | Star-Delta Starting | Reactor Starting |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| Motor voltage | $V_L$ | $a V_L$ | $V_L / \sqrt{3}$ | $a V_L$ |
| Motor current | $I_{DOL}$ | $a I_{DOL}$ | $I_{DOL} / \sqrt{3}$ | $a I_{DOL}$ |
| **Line current** | $I_{DOL}$ | $a^2 I_{DOL}$ | $I_{DOL} / 3$ | $a I_{DOL}$ |
| Starting torque | $T_{DOL}$ | $a^2 T_{DOL}$ | $T_{DOL} / 3$ | $a^2 T_{DOL}$ |
| Starter cost | None | High (transformer) | Low | Medium |
| Starting transients | Severe | Moderate (open transition) | Severe (high switch transients) | Smooth |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["THREE PHASE SUPPLY 415V 50Hz"] --> B["ISOLATOR MCCB"]
    B --> C["MAIN CONTACTOR M"]
    C --> D{"OPERATING MODE"}
    D -- "START POSITION" --> E["AUTOTRANSFORMER WITH TAPS 50 65 80"]
    D -- "RUN POSITION" --> F["BYPASS CONTACTOR"]
    E --> G["MOTOR TERMINALS"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["SQUIRREL CAGE INDUCTION MOTOR"]
    E --> E1["TAP 1 AT 50 PERCENT"]
    E --> E2["TAP 2 AT 65 PERCENT"]
    E --> E3["TAP 3 AT 80 PERCENT"]
    H --> I["MECHANICAL LOAD PUMP FAN COMPRESSOR"]
    J["TIMER RELAY"] --> D
    J --> K["CONTROL CIRCUIT 110V DC"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Starting Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SUP as Supply
    participant ISO as Isolator
    participant ST as Start Contactor S
    participant AT as Autotransformer
    participant MOT as Motor
    participant RN as Run Contactor R
    participant TMR as Timer

    SUP->>ISO: Energize
    ISO->>ST: Close on START push
    ST->>AT: Select 65 percent tap
    AT->>MOT: Reduced voltage 0.65 VL
    MOT->>MOT: Accelerate with 0.42 TDOL
    TMR->>TMR: Wait 5 to 10 seconds
    TMR->>ST: Open contactor S
    Note over MOT: Motor coasts briefly open transition
    TMR->>RN: Close contactor R
    RN->>MOT: Full voltage VL connected
    MOT->>MOT: Run at full speed
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Starter Operation States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF
    OFF --> STARTING: Push START button
    STARTING --> ACCELERATING: Contactor S closes tap 65 percent
    ACCELERATING --> TRANSITION: Timer expires speed above 80 percent
    TRANSITION --> RUNNING: Contactor R closes bypass S opens
    RUNNING --> OFF: Push STOP button
    RUNNING --> FAULT: Overload relay trips
    FAULT --> OFF: Reset
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER_CIRCUIT["POWER CIRCUIT 3 PHASE"]
        P1["Line L1 L2 L3"] --> P2["MCCB"]
        P2 --> P3["Contactor M"]
        P3 --> P4["Autotransformer"]
        P4 --> P5["Contactor S Start"]
        P5 --> P6["Motor Terminals"]
        P3 --> P7["Contactor R Run bypass"]
        P7 --> P6
    end
    subgraph CONTROL_CIRCUIT["CONTROL CIRCUIT"]
        C1["START PB"] --> C2["Timer T1"]
        C2 --> C3["Aux Contact of S"]
        C3 --> C4["Contactor R coil"]
        C4 --> C5["Holding contact"]
        C1 --> C6["Contactor S coil"]
        C6 --> P5
    end
    subgraph PROTECTION["PROTECTION SECTION"]
        PR1["Overload Relay OLR"] --> PR2["Trip contact"]
        PR2 --> C4
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three advantages of an autotransformer starter over a star-delta starter for starting a three-phase induction motor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Taps are available** (50%, 65%, 80%) so the starting voltage can be chosen to match the load; in star-delta only a fixed 1/√3 reduction is possible. *(1 mark)*
2. **Closed transition (Korndorfer) version** is possible, eliminating the open-circuit voltage dip during changeover. *(1 mark)*
3. **Higher starting torque** for the same line current reduction: at 80% tap, torque is 64% of DOL, whereas star-delta gives only 33%. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "tap ratio" in the context of an autotransformer starter and write the relationship between line current and motor current.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Tap ratio** `a` is the ratio of the motor terminal voltage to the supply line voltage during starting: $a = V_m / V_L$. For standard starters, $a = 0.5, 0.65,$ or $0.8$. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Current relationship:** The line current drawn from the supply is $I_L = a \cdot I_m$, where $I_m$ is the motor current. Since the motor current at reduced voltage is $I_m = a \cdot I_{DOL}$, the line current becomes $I_L = a^2 \cdot I_{DOL}$. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing $I_L = a \cdot I_{DOL}$ instead of $I_L = a^2 \cdot I_{DOL}$. This is the **most common error** and costs 2–3 marks instantly. Always derive carefully: $I_m = a I_{DOL}$, then $I_L = a I_m = a^2 I_{DOL}$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting that the autotransformer kVA rating is $(1-a) \times$ motor kVA, not $a \times$ motor kVA. The "transformed portion" is the smaller part.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **open-transition** (motor momentarily disconnected) with **closed-transition Korndorfer** (motor stays connected through a reactor during changeover). KTU questions often specify which one.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not specifying the unit of starting torque in N·m or pu. Always write units.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

A three-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 15 kW squirrel-cage induction motor has a full-load current of 28 A and a full-load speed of 1440 rpm. The starting current at DOL is 6 times the full-load current, and the starting torque at DOL is 2.0 times the full-load torque. The motor is started using an autotransformer starter with a **65% tap**.

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Calculate:
1. The motor terminal voltage during starting.
2. The motor starting current.
3. The line current drawn from the supply during starting.
4. The starting torque developed by the motor.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
5. The autotransformer kVA rating (transferred portion only).
6. Compare the starting performance with a star-delta starter in terms of line current and starting torque.
7. Comment on the suitability of this starter for a centrifugal pump load.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

Given data:
- $V_L = 400$ V, $I_{FLC} = 28$ A, $P = 15$ kW, $N = 1440$ rpm
- $I_{DOL} = 6 \times 28 = 168$ A
- $T_{DOL} = 2.0 \times T_{FL}$
- Tap ratio $a = 0.65$

**1. Motor terminal voltage during starting:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_m &= a \cdot V_L = 0.65 \times 400 = 260 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**2. Motor starting current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_m &= a \cdot I_{DOL} = 0.65 \times 168 = 109.2 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Line current from supply:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= a^2 \cdot I_{DOL} = (0.65)^2 \times 168 = 0.4225 \times 168 = 70.98 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**4. Starting torque:**

First, calculate full-load torque $T_{FL}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{FL} &= \frac{P \times 60}{2\pi N} = \frac{15000 \times 60}{2\pi \times 1440} = \frac{900000}{9047.79} \approx 99.47 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then, DOL starting torque:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{DOL} &= 2.0 \times 99.47 = 198.94 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Autotransformer starting torque:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{st} &= a^2 \cdot T_{DOL} = 0.4225 \times 198.94 \approx 84.05 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Alternatively, as a fraction of full-load torque:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{st}/T_{FL} &= 0.4225 \times 2.0 = 0.845 \text{ pu (of FTL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**5. Autotransformer kVA rating:**

Motor input apparent power at full voltage (FLC):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_m &= \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_{FLC} = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 28 = 19403 \text{ VA} \approx 19.4 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

But we need the kVA at starting. The starting apparent power drawn by the motor at reduced voltage is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{m,\text{start}} &= \sqrt{3} \cdot V_m \cdot I_m = \sqrt{3} \times 260 \times 109.2 \approx 49165 \text{ VA} \approx 49.2 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

Autotransformer transformed kVA rating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{AT} &= (1 - a) \cdot S_{m,\text{start}} = 0.35 \times 49.2 \approx 17.2 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**6. Comparison with star-delta starter:**

| Parameter | Autotransformer (65% tap) | Star-Delta |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------|
| Line current | $0.4225 \times 168 = 70.98$ A | $168/3 = 56$ A |
| Starting torque | $0.4225 \times 198.94 = 84.05$ N·m | $198.94/3 = 66.31$ N·m |
| Line current reduction | 57.75% | 66.67% |
| Torque reduction | 57.75% | 66.67% |

Star-delta gives **more reduction** in both current and torque (by factor of 1/3), but the **autotransformer provides higher starting torque** (84 N·m vs 66 N·m) for a still-acceptable line current.

**7. Suitability for centrifugal pump:**

Centrifugal pumps have a **square-law torque-speed characteristic** ($T \propto N^2$). At starting, slip is near 1, so required starting torque is moderate (~20–30% of full-load torque). The autotransformer starter at 65% tap gives $0.4225 \times 2.0 = 0.845$ pu starting torque (in terms of DOL starting torque factor), which is more than enough. Hence, this starter is **highly suitable** for a centrifugal pump load. *(1 mark for justification, 1 mark for quantitative check)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

A three-phase, 50 Hz, 400 V induction motor is to be started using an autotransformer starter. The motor draws 6 times full-load current at DOL and develops 1.8 times full-load torque at DOL. Full-load current is 30 A.

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
1. Determine the line current and starting torque at **50%, 65%, and 80% taps**.
2. Tabulate the results and identify which tap is most suitable for starting a **high-inertia load** requiring 1.4 pu starting torque.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
3. With a neat sketch, describe the operation of a **Korndorfer (closed-transition) autotransformer starter** and explain how it differs from the open-transition type.
4. If the same motor were started using a star-delta starter, calculate the line current and starting torque. State one disadvantage of the star-delta starter for this application.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

Given:
- $V_L = 400$ V, $I_{DOL} = 6 \times 30 = 180$ A, $T_{DOL} = 1.8 \times T_{FL}$
- $T_{FL} = 100$ pu (reference base for simplicity; actual numerical value not required for ratio calculations)

**1. Line current and starting torque at three taps:**

For tap ratio `a`, line current and starting torque are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= a^2 \cdot I_{DOL} = a^2 \times 180 \text{ A} \\
T_{st} &= a^2 \cdot T_{DOL} = a^2 \times 1.8 \cdot T_{FL}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Tap | $a$ | $a^2$ | Line current $I_L$ (A) | Starting torque $T_{st}$ (in pu of $T_{FL}$) |
|-----|-----|-------|------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| 50% | 0.50 | 0.2500 | $0.25 \times 180 = 45.0$ A | $0.25 \times 1.8 = 0.450$ pu |
| 65% | 0.65 | 0.4225 | $0.4225 \times 180 = 76.05$ A | $0.4225 \times 1.8 = 0.7605$ pu |
| 80% | 0.80 | 0.6400 | $0.64 \times 180 = 115.2$ A | $0.64 \times 1.8 = 1.152$ pu |

**2. Most suitable tap for 1.4 pu torque requirement:**

None of the three taps reaches the 1.4 pu torque threshold. The maximum available starting torque is **1.152 pu at 80% tap**, which is **insufficient** for the 1.4 pu requirement. Therefore, **the autotransformer starter at standard taps is NOT suitable** for this load. A **DOL starter or a soft-starter** should be used instead. *(Full marks: 1 mark for calculation, 1 mark for identification, 1 mark for conclusion)*

**Part (b) Solution:**

**3. Korndorfer (closed-transition) autotransformer starter — Operation:**

The Korndorfer starter uses **three contactors** (S1, S2, R) instead of two, and adds a **reactor/inductor L** in the transition circuit.

Operating sequence:
- **Step 1:** Close S1 → motor receives reduced voltage through autotransformer.
- **Step 2:** After motor accelerates, close S2 → autotransformer is bypassed **through the reactor L** (motor is now on a series reactor).
- **Step 3:** Open S1 → autotransformer is disconnected, motor remains connected through the reactor to the line.
- **Step 4:** Close R → motor is connected directly to the line (full voltage).

**Key difference from open-transition:**
- In **open-transition**, the motor is **momentarily disconnected** from the supply during changeover, causing a voltage transient and a speed dip.
- In **closed-transition (Korndorfer)**, the motor **remains continuously connected** to the supply (through either the autotransformer or the reactor), eliminating the open-circuit transient. This is preferred for **sensitive loads** that cannot tolerate even a brief interruption. *(4 marks for description + 1 mark for difference)*

**4. Star-delta comparison:**

For star-delta starting:
- Motor voltage: $V_L / \sqrt{3} = 400/\sqrt{3} \approx 230.94$ V
- Motor current (phase): $I_{DOL}/\sqrt{3}$ (in star mode, the relationship gives the line current as $I_{DOL}/3$)

Line current in star connection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{L,\text{Y-}\Delta} &= \frac{I_{DOL}}{3} = \frac{180}{3} = 60 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Starting torque in star:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{st,\text{Y-}\Delta} &= \frac{T_{DOL}}{3} = \frac{1.8 \cdot T_{FL}}{3} = 0.6 \cdot T_{FL} = 0.6 \text{ pu}
\end{aligned}
$$

**One disadvantage of star-delta for this application:**
The star-delta starter gives only **0.6 pu starting torque**, which is even less than the 80% autotransformer tap (1.152 pu). For a high-inertia load, the star-delta starter may fail to accelerate the motor to sufficient speed before the transition to delta, causing a **high transition current peak** ("Korndorfer bump") that can damage contacts and windings. *(2 marks)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- [x] **Definition:** Autotransformer starter is a reduced-voltage starter using a tapped autotransformer between supply and motor.
- [x] **Standard taps:** 50%, 65%, 80% of line voltage. Tap ratio $a = V_m / V_L$.
- [x] **Master formulas (must memorize):**
  - $V_m = a \cdot V_L$
  - $I_m = a \cdot I_{DOL}$
  - **$I_L = a^2 \cdot I_{DOL}$** ← *most-tested formula in KTU*
  - **$T_{st} = a^2 \cdot T_{DOL}$** ← *same factor, because $T \propto V^2$*
- [x] **Autotransformer kVA rating:** $S_{AT} = (1-a) \cdot S_m$ (only the transformed portion counts).
- [x] **Why $a^2$ for line current?** Because voltage reduces by $a$ and current transformation also reduces by $a$, giving a product of $a^2$.
- [x] **Open transition vs. closed transition (Korndorfer):**
  - Open: motor momentarily disconnected during changeover.
  - Closed (Korndorfer): motor stays connected via a reactor; no open-circuit transient.
- [x] **Comparison with other starters:**
  - Star-delta: fixed 1/3 reduction in both current and torque.
  - Reactor starter: $I_L = a I_{DOL}$ (less reduction than autotransformer).
  - Soft-starter: electronic, smooth ramp, no taps.
- [x] **Applications:** Centrifugal pumps, fans, blowers, compressors, conveyors — moderate to high starting torque loads.
- [x] **Advantages:** Multiple taps, small size, low cost (vs. two-winding transformer), high efficiency, possible closed transition.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Lower power factor during starting, open-transition transient, more complex than DOL.
- [x] **KTU typical numerical pattern:** "Find line current, motor current, starting torque, and autotransformer rating" — always derive carefully and show units.
- [x] **Pitfall alert:** Never write $I_L = a I_{DOL}$ — it is always $a^2 I_{DOL}$ for the autotransformer.
- [x] **Torque unit:** N·m; current unit: A; kVA: kVA. Always include.
- [x] **Square-law loads:** $T_{load} \propto N^2$ (pumps, fans) → autotransformer suitable. Constant-torque loads (conveyors, compressors) → check 80% tap gives enough torque.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Star-Delta Starter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Star-Delta Starter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Star-Delta Starter** is a *reduced-voltage starting method* used for three-phase squirrel-cage induction motors, in which the stator winding of the motor is initially connected in **star (Y) configuration** during the starting period to limit the high inrush current, and subsequently reconfigured to **delta (Δ) configuration** for normal running operation. The transition from star to delta is executed automatically by a set of contactors controlled by a timer relay once the motor accelerates to approximately **70%–80%** of its rated synchronous speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A star-delta starter is permissible **only for induction motors designed to operate in delta connection at rated line voltage** under normal running conditions (i.e., motors whose nameplate rated voltage equals the line voltage in delta). This is because in star, the phase voltage is reduced by a factor of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$, and this reduced voltage must correspond to the rated phase voltage of the motor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the star-delta starter like **shifting gears on a motorbike while climbing a steep hill**:

- When you start climbing, you shift to **first gear (star mode)**. The bike moves slowly, but the engine is not strained, and there is no violent jerking.
- Once you have gathered enough momentum (≈ 75% speed), you **shift to second/third gear (delta mode)**. Now you can cruise smoothly at full speed with full power.

In the electrical world, if you directly connect an induction motor to the supply (Direct-On-Line, DOL), it is like starting the bike in top gear — the motor draws a **massive inrush current (typically 5 to 7 times the rated current)** from the supply, causing voltage dips, heating, and mechanical shock. The star connection acts as the "first gear," reducing both voltage and current to safe limits.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Starting current with DOL** | **5 to 7 times** full-load current ($I_{FL}$) |
| **Starting current in star** | $\frac{1}{3}$ of DOL starting current |
| **Starting torque in star** | $\frac{1}{3}$ of DOL starting torque |
| **Transition time (star to delta)** | **3 to 10 seconds** (timer-set) |
| **Transition speed** | **70%–80%** of synchronous speed |
| **Suitable motor rating** | Generally **> 5 HP (3.7 kW)** for industrial use |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The star-delta starter reduces the **starting current by a factor of 3** and the **starting torque also by a factor of 3**.
