Hence, it is suitable only for applications where the load does not require a high starting torque — such as fans, blowers, centrifugal pumps, and compressors. It is **NOT** suitable for heavy loads like conveyors or crushers that demand high starting torque.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic: Step-by-Step Breakdown

The star-delta starter operates on a precise sequence orchestrated by three contactors and a timer:

**Step 1 — Initial Power ON (Star Mode):**
- The **main contactor (K1)** closes, connecting all three supply lines (R, Y, B) to the motor terminals (U, V, W).
- The **star contactor (K2)** closes, short-circuiting the three "tail" ends of the stator winding (typically labeled $W_2$, $U_2$, $V_2$) together. This forms the star point, connecting the motor in star.
- The motor accelerates from standstill with reduced phase voltage = $\frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$.

**Step 2 — Acceleration Phase:**
- As the motor gains speed, the back-EMF in the rotor increases, naturally reducing the stator current.
- The **timer relay** is pre-set (typically 3–10 seconds) and begins counting.

**Step 3 — Transition (Star → Delta):**
- When the timer expires, **K2 (star contactor) opens first**, breaking the star connection.
- After a brief time delay (to ensure no short-circuit during transition), the **delta contactor (K3) closes**, connecting the tail of one phase to the head of the next phase, forming the delta loop.
- The motor is now in full delta connection, with full rated line voltage across each phase.

> [!WARNING]
> **Interlock Criticality:** K2 (star) and K3 (delta) must be **mechanically and electrically interlocked**. If both close simultaneously, a direct **three-phase short circuit** occurs at the supply terminals, which is catastrophic. The timer ensures K2 opens fully before K3 closes.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates all derivable relationships essential for KTU 2024 examinations.

| # | Quantity | Star (Starting) | Delta (Running) | Ratio (Star / Delta) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Phase Voltage $V_{ph}$ | $\dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $V_L$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ |
| 2 | Line Voltage $V_L$ | $V_L$ (supply) | $V_L$ (supply) | $1$ |
| 3 | Phase Current $I_{ph}$ | $I_{L,Y}$ | $\dfrac{I_{L,\Delta}}{\sqrt{3}}$ | — |
| 4 | Line Current $I_L$ | $I_{L,Y} = \dfrac{I_{ph,Y}}{1}$ | $\sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph,\Delta}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3}$ |
| 5 | Starting Current (vs DOL) | $\dfrac{1}{3} I_{st,DOL}$ | $I_{st,DOL}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3}$ |
| 6 | Starting Torque (vs DOL) | $\dfrac{1}{3} T_{st,DOL}$ | $T_{st,DOL}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3}$ |
| 7 | Running Torque $T$ | — | $\dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2 \cdot R_r/s}{(R_r/s)^2 + (X_s+X_r)^2}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** "Voltage reduces by $\sqrt{3}$, but **Current and Torque** both reduce by **3**."
> $$V_{ph,star} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad I_{L,star} = \frac{I_{L,DOL}}{3}, \quad T_{star} = \frac{T_{DOL}}{3}$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Star-delta starters are the **workhorse of industrial motor starting**, deployed extensively in:

- **HVAC systems** (large chillers, cooling tower fans)
- **Water treatment plants** (centrifugal pumps)
- **Cement and steel industries** (fans, blowers, compressors)
- **Paper mills** (pulp agitators, dryer fans)
- **Building services** (large lift pumps, fire-fighting pumps)

Their popularity stems from **low cost**, **simple construction** (no autotransformer or reactor needed), **high reliability**, and **low maintenance** compared to electronic soft starters or VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Starting Current in Star vs Direct-On-Line (DOL)

Let us derive the fundamental relationship $I_{L,star} = \frac{1}{3} I_{L,DOL}$.

**Given:**
- $V_L$ = Supply line voltage (constant)
- $Z_{ph}$ = Per-phase stator impedance (constant during starting, as rotor is at standstill)
- $I_{st,DOL}$ = Starting line current with Direct-On-Line (delta) connection

**Step 1: Calculate starting current in DOL (delta) connection.**

In delta, the phase voltage equals the line voltage. So the phase current at starting is:

$$I_{ph,DOL} = \frac{V_{ph,\Delta}}{Z_{ph}} = \frac{V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

The line current in delta is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase current:

$$I_{st,DOL} = I_{L,DOL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph,DOL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate starting current in star connection.**

In star, the phase voltage is reduced to $\frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$. The phase current is:

$$I_{ph,star} = \frac{V_{ph,Y}}{Z_{ph}} = \frac{V_L / \sqrt{3}}{Z_{ph}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{ph}}$$

In star connection, the line current equals the phase current:

$$I_{st,star} = I_{L,star} = I_{ph,star} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{ph}}$$

**Step 3: Take the ratio of star to DOL starting current.**

$$\frac{I_{st,star}}{I_{st,DOL}} = \frac{\dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{ph}}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{V_L}{Z_{ph}}}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the ratio.**

$$\frac{I_{st,star}}{I_{st,DOL}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{ph}} \cdot \frac{Z_{ph}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 5: Final Result.**

$$\boxed{I_{st,star} = \frac{1}{3} I_{st,DOL}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Interpretation:** Although the phase voltage is reduced by a factor of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ (≈ 0.577), the line current in star is reduced by an additional factor of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ due to the connection geometry (line current = phase current in star, but = $\sqrt{3} \cdot$ phase current in delta). These two factors compound: $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{1}{3}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Starting Torque Ratio in Star vs DOL

The starting torque of an induction motor is given by:

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot R_r}{(R_s + R_r)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$$

Where:
- $V_{ph}$ = Per-phase voltage applied to the stator
- $R_s, X_s$ = Stator resistance and reactance
- $R_r, X_r$ = Rotor resistance and reactance (referred to stator)
- $\omega_s$ = Synchronous speed in rad/s

**Step 1: Identify that torque is proportional to $V_{ph}^2$.**

At starting (slip $s = 1$), the denominator is a constant (depends on motor parameters only). Therefore:

$$T_{st} \propto V_{ph}^2$$

**Step 2: Write the DOL starting torque.**

In delta (DOL), $V_{ph,\Delta} = V_L$:

$$T_{st,DOL} = k \cdot V_L^2$$

where $k = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{R_r}{(R_s + R_r)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$ is a constant.

**Step 3: Write the star starting torque.**

In star, $V_{ph,Y} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$:

$$T_{st,star} = k \cdot \left(\frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = k \cdot \frac{V_L^2}{3}$$

**Step 4: Take the ratio.**

$$\frac{T_{st,star}}{T_{st,DOL}} = \frac{k \cdot \dfrac{V_L^2}{3}}{k \cdot V_L^2} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 5: Final Result.**

$$\boxed{T_{st,star} = \frac{1}{3} T_{st,DOL}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Consequence:** The starting torque is reduced to **one-third** of its DOL value. If the load torque at standstill exceeds this reduced starting torque, **the motor will fail to start**. Therefore, star-delta starting is strictly limited to **light-load, low-torque** applications where $T_L \le \frac{1}{3} T_{st,DOL}$ at standstill.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Metric | DOL Starter | Star-Delta Starter | Autotransformer (65% tap) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Starting Current (line)** | $I_{st}$ (100%) | $\frac{I_{st}}{3}$ (33.3%) | $0.65^2 \cdot I_{st}$ (42.3%) |
| **Starting Torque** | $T_{st}$ (100%) | $\frac{T_{st}}{3}$ (33.3%) | $0.65^2 \cdot T_{st}$ (42.3%) |
| **Voltage at motor** | $V_L$ (100%) | $\frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ (57.7%) | $0.65 V_L$ (65%) |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| **Smoothness of start** | Jerky | Two-step (transient) | Smooth (tapped) |
| **Torque per ampere** | Lowest | **Highest** (1:1 ratio) | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Among reduced-voltage starters, the star-delta starter gives the **best "torque per ampere" efficiency** — every unit of current reduction yields an equal unit of torque reduction. The autotransformer wastes more current relative to the torque it preserves.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Circuit Topology (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a star-delta starter's power circuit, showing the three contactors, the motor winding terminations, and the interlock logic.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PWR[3-Phase Supply R Y B]:::source
    
    K1A[Main Contactor K1 Pole 1]:::contactor
    K1B[Main Contactor K1 Pole 2]:::contactor
    K1C[Main Contactor K1 Pole 3]:::contactor
    
    K2A[Star Contactor K2 Pole 1]:::contactor
    K2B[Star Contactor K2 Pole 2]:::contactor
    K2C[Star Contactor K2 Pole 3]:::contactor
    
    K3A[Delta Contactor K3 Pole 1]:::contactor
    K3B[Delta Contactor K3 Pole 2]:::contactor
    K3C[Delta Contactor K3 Pole 3]:::contactor
    
    U[Motor Terminal U1]:::motor
    V[Motor Terminal V1]:::motor
    W[Motor Terminal W1]:::motor
    
    SP[Star Point W2 U2 V2 Shorted]:::junction
    MTR[3-Phase Induction Motor Stator Windings]:::motor
    
    PWR --> K1A
    PWR --> K1B
    PWR --> K1C
    
    K1A --> U
    K1B --> V
    K1C --> W
    
    U --> K2A
    V --> K2B
    W --> K2C
    
    K2A --> SP
    K2B --> SP
    K2C --> SP
    
    U --> K3A
    K3A --> K3B
    K3B --> V
    K3B --> K3C
    K3C --> W
    
    U --> MTR
    V --> MTR
    W --> MTR
    
    classDef source fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef contactor fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef motor fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef junction fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Control Sequence Flowchart

The operational sequence is governed by a timer relay, pushbuttons, and auxiliary contacts. The flow is as follows:

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST[Start] --> PB[Press START Pushbutton]:::action
    PB --> CK1[Energize Main Contactor K1]:::contactor
    CK1 --> CK2[Energize Star Contactor K2]:::contactor
    CK2 --> TR[Timer Starts Counting 3-10 sec]:::timer
    TR --> TC{Timer Elapsed?}:::decision
    TC -->|No| WAIT[Motor Accelerates in Star Mode]:::state
    WAIT --> TC
    TC -->|Yes| DK2[De-energize K2 Open Star Point]:::contactor
    DK2 --> DD[Dwell Delay 50-100 ms]:::timer
    DD --> CK3[Energize Delta Contactor K3]:::contactor
    CK3 --> RUN[Motor Runs in Full Delta Mode]:::state
    RUN --> SP2{Press STOP?}:::decision
    SP2 -->|Yes| DK1[De-energize All Contactors]:::contactor
    DK1 --> END[Motor Stops]:::endstate
    SP2 -->|No| RUN
    
    classDef action fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef contactor fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef timer fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef state fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef endstate fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Contact Logic Truth Table

| K1 (Main) | K2 (Star) | K3 (Delta) | Motor State | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Stopped | All off |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Starting in Star | Reduced voltage, current = 33% |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Running in Delta | Full voltage, normal operation |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | **FORBIDDEN** | **Three-phase short circuit!** |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | Invalid (K1 off) | K2 must drop with K1 |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | Invalid (K1 off) | K3 must drop with K1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Row 4 is the most dangerous state.** Mechanical and electrical interlocks between K2 and K3 are mandatory in any industrial star-delta starter to prevent catastrophic short circuits. KTU examiners frequently test this safety logic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a star-delta starter. Why is it classified as a reduced-voltage starting method?**

**Model Answer:**

A star-delta starter is a starting method for three-phase squirrel-cage induction motors in which the stator winding is initially connected in **star (Y)** during starting and then reconnected in **delta (Δ)** for normal running operation. 

It is classified as a reduced-voltage starter because, in star mode, the per-phase voltage applied to the motor is reduced to $\frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ (approximately 57.7% of line voltage) instead of the full $V_L$ as in Direct-On-Line starting. This reduction limits the starting current to about **one-third** of the DOL starting current, and correspondingly reduces the starting torque to **one-third** of its DOL value.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three applications where star-delta starters are preferred and justify your choice.**

**Model Answer:**

Star-delta starters are preferred for **light-load, low-starting-torque** applications, including:

1. **Centrifugal pumps** — The load torque at standstill is nearly zero and increases with the square of speed, so a low starting torque is sufficient.
2. **Fans and blowers** — These are also low-torque-at-startup loads, making them ideal candidates.
3. **Air compressors (unloaded)** — When started with the discharge valve closed, the load torque is minimal.

**Justification:** In all these applications, the load torque at starting is well below $\frac{1}{3}$ of the motor's DOL starting torque, so the motor can successfully accelerate in star mode. Additionally, the reduced starting current protects the supply network from severe voltage dips in industrial installations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by forgetting to explicitly state that the motor must be **delta-rated for the line voltage** (i.e., a 415 V motor must have its nameplate delta voltage = 415 V).
> - Another frequent mistake is **confusing phase current with line current** in star vs delta derivations. Always write the connection first, then state $I_L = I_{ph}$ (star) or $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph}$ (delta).
> - Examiners specifically look for the **interlock mechanism** between K2 and K3 contactors. Omitting this loses easy marks.
> - Numerical problems: Always **assume the motor's per-phase impedance is the same** in star and delta — only the applied voltage changes.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A 3-phase, 10 HP, 400 V, 50 Hz, 6-pole squirrel-cage induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator: $R_s = 0.5\ \Omega$, $R_r' = 0.6\ \Omega$, $X_s = 1.0\ \Omega$, $X_r' = 1.2\ \Omega$. The motor is started using a star-delta starter.**

**(a) [7 Marks] Calculate the starting current and starting torque with the star-delta starter. Compare these with the corresponding DOL values.**

**(b) [7 Marks] If the motor is running at 4% slip on full load in delta, calculate the full-load torque, full-load speed, and the rotor copper loss.**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Given Data:**
- Rated Power = 10 HP ≈ 7.46 kW
- Line Voltage $V_L = 400$ V
- Frequency $f = 50$ Hz
- Poles $P = 6$
- $R_s = 0.5\ \Omega$, $R_r' = 0.6\ \Omega$, $X_s = 1.0\ \Omega$, $X_r' = 1.2\ \Omega$

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate synchronous speed.**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate per-phase impedance in DOL (delta) at starting ($s = 1$).**

In DOL delta connection, $V_{ph} = V_L = 400$ V.

$$Z_{ph} = \sqrt{(R_s + R_r')^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}$$

$$Z_{ph} = \sqrt{(0.5 + 0.6)^2 + (1.0 + 1.2)^2} = \sqrt{1.1^2 + 2.2^2}$$

$$Z_{ph} = \sqrt{1.21 + 4.84} = \sqrt{6.05} = 2.46\ \Omega$$

**Step 3: DOL starting phase current and line current.**

$$I_{ph,DOL} = \frac{V_L}{Z_{ph}} = \frac{400}{2.46} = 162.6 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{st,DOL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph,DOL} = \sqrt{3} \times 162.6 = 281.6 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: DOL starting torque.**

$$T_{st,DOL} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot R_r'}{(R_s + R_r')^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}$$

$$T_{st,DOL} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{400^2 \times 0.6}{6.05} = \frac{3 \times 160000 \times 0.6}{104.72 \times 6.05}$$

$$T_{st,DOL} = \frac{288000}{633.55} = 454.7 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Star starting current.**

$$I_{st,star} = \frac{1}{3} I_{st,DOL} = \frac{281.6}{3} = 93.87 \text{ A}$$

**Step 6: Star starting torque.**

$$T_{st,star} = \frac{1}{3} T_{st,DOL} = \frac{454.7}{3} = 151.6 \text{ N·m}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Quantity | DOL (Delta) | Star-Delta (Star) | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| Starting Current (line) | 281.6 A | 93.87 A | 1/3 |
| Starting Torque | 454.7 N·m | 151.6 N·m | 1/3 |

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Given:** Slip $s = 0.04$ (full load)

**Step 1: Full-load speed.**

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1000 \times (1 - 0.04) = 960 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2: Full-load torque using exact expression.**

At full load in delta, $V_{ph} = 400$ V.

$$T_{FL} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_s + R_r'/s)^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}$$

Calculate $R_r'/s = 0.6/0.04 = 15\ \Omega$.

$$R_s + R_r'/s = 0.5 + 15 = 15.5\ \Omega$$

$$(R_s + R_r'/s)^2 = 15.5^2 = 240.25\ \Omega^2$$

$$(X_s + X_r')^2 = 2.2^2 = 4.84\ \Omega^2$$

$$T_{FL} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{400^2 \times 15}{240.25 + 4.84} = \frac{3 \times 160000 \times 15}{104.72 \times 245.09}$$

$$T_{FL} = \frac{7,200,000}{25,665} = 280.5 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Rotor copper loss at full load.**

Using the relation $\dfrac{P_{cu,r}}{P_{ag}} = s$, where $P_{ag}$ is the air-gap power:

$$P_{ag} = T_{FL} \cdot \omega_s = 280.5 \times 104.72 = 29,374 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{cu,r} = s \cdot P_{ag} = 0.04 \times 29,374 = 1175 \text{ W} \approx 1.175 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Verification using mechanical power developed.**

$$P_{m,dev} = T_{FL} \cdot \omega_r = 280.5 \times \frac{2\pi \times 960}{60} = 280.5 \times 100.53 = 28,200 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{cu,r} = P_{ag} - P_{m,dev} = 29,374 - 28,200 = 1174 \text{ W} \approx 1.175 \text{ kW} \ \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **— INTERNAL CHOICE**

**(a) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a star-delta starter. Why is an interlock required between the star and delta contactors?**

**(b) [7 Marks] A 3-phase induction motor draws 6 times its full-load current and develops 1.8 times its full-load torque at starting. Calculate the starting current and starting torque when started by a star-delta starter. Comment on the suitability of this starter for a load requiring 1.2 times full-load torque at starting.**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) Solution:**

A star-delta starter consists of **three contactors** (K1 — Main, K2 — Star, K3 — Delta), a **timer relay**, pushbuttons (START/STOP), and **overload relays**.

**Working Sequence:**

1. **Pressing START** energizes the main contactor **K1**, connecting the three-phase supply to motor terminals U1, V1, W1.
2. Simultaneously, the **star contactor K2** closes, shorting the motor terminals W2, U2, V2 together to form a star point.
3. The motor runs in star mode with reduced phase voltage $\dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$, drawing reduced current.
4. The **timer relay** begins counting from the moment of start.
5. When the timer expires (typically 3–10 seconds), K2 opens first, breaking the star point.
6. After a brief interlock delay (50–100 ms), the **delta contactor K3** closes, connecting the motor in delta.
7. The motor now runs at full voltage, full torque in delta mode.

**Interlock Justification:**

The interlock between K2 and K3 is **absolutely essential** for safety. If both K2 and K3 close simultaneously:

- K2 shorts the motor's tail terminals together.
- K3 connects those very same terminals in a delta loop across the supply lines.

This creates a **direct three-phase short circuit** at the supply terminals, which would trip the upstream circuit breaker, blow fuses, and potentially cause an arc-flash hazard or fire. The interlock (mechanical and/or electrical using normally-closed auxiliary contacts) ensures K2 and K3 can **never be closed at the same time**.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Given:**
- DOL starting current $I_{st,DOL} = 6 I_{FL}$
- DOL starting torque $T_{st,DOL} = 1.8 T_{FL}$
- Load torque required at starting $T_L = 1.2 T_{FL}$

**Step 1: Starting current with star-delta.**

$$I_{st,star} = \frac{1}{3} I_{st,DOL} = \frac{1}{3} \times 6 I_{FL} = 2 I_{FL}$$

**Step 2: Starting torque with star-delta.**

$$T_{st,star} = \frac{1}{3} T_{st,DOL} = \frac{1}{3} \times 1.8 T_{FL} = 0.6 T_{FL}$$

**Step 3: Suitability analysis.**

The starting torque available in star mode is $0.6 T_{FL}$, but the load requires $1.2 T_{FL}$ to start.

$$\text{Since } T_{st,star} = 0.6 T_{FL} < T_L = 1.2 T_{FL}$$

The motor **will not start** under this load condition. The star-delta starter is **NOT suitable** for this application.

**Conclusion / Recommendation:**

A different starting method must be used. Suitable alternatives include:

- **Autotransformer starter** (e.g., 75% tap gives starting torque = $0.75^2 \times 1.8 T_{FL} = 1.0125 T_{FL}$ — still insufficient, try 80% tap → $0.64 \times 1.8 = 1.152 T_{FL}$ — marginal).
- **Slip-ring induction motor** with external rotor resistance (gives high starting torque).
- **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** for precise soft starting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write "the starter is suitable" simply because the starting current is reduced. The decisive criterion is whether the **starting torque exceeds the load torque**, not the current reduction. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this oversight.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Star-Delta Starter (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

### Core Concept Bullets

- **Purpose:** Reduced-voltage starting of 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motors to limit inrush current.
- **Connection Sequence:** Star (Y) at start → Delta (Δ) at run, transitioned automatically by a timer.
- **Voltage Reduction:** Phase voltage in star = $\dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ ≈ 57.7% of line voltage.
- **Current Reduction:** Starting current in star = $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of DOL starting current.
- **Torque Reduction:** Starting torque in star = $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of DOL starting torque (since $T \propto V^2$).
- **Motor Suitability:** Only motors rated for **delta at line voltage** (e.g., 415 V, Δ-connected).
- **Transition Speed:** 70–80% of $N_s$ (i.e., after 3–10 s).
- **Components:** 3 contactors (K1, K2, K3) + timer + overload relay + pushbuttons.
- **Mandatory Interlock:** K2 and K3 must be interlocked (mechanical + electrical) — never both closed.

### Critical KTU "Must-Remember" Formulas

$$I_{st,star} = \frac{1}{3} I_{st,DOL} \qquad T_{st,star} = \frac{1}{3} T_{st,DOL}$$

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \qquad T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_s + R_r'/s)^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}$$

### Suitable vs. Unsuitable Applications

| ✅ Suitable (Low starting torque load) | ❌ Not Suitable (High starting torque load) |
|---|---|
| Centrifugal pumps | Belt conveyors (loaded) |
| Fans and blowers | Crushers and mills |
| Air compressors (unloaded) | Elevators and hoists |
| Light woodworking machines | Compressors (loaded/pressurized) |
| HVAC blower motors | Reciprocating pumps |

### Common KTU Exam Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Mixing up phase and line current** in star vs delta — always state the connection before computing.
2. **Forgetting to mention the interlock** between K2 and K3 — it's a guaranteed 1–2 mark question.
3. **Stating that voltage reduces by 1/3** — it actually reduces by $1/\sqrt{3}$. Only current and torque reduce by 1/3.
4. **Recommending star-delta for high-torque loads** without checking $T_{st,star} \ge T_{load}$.
5. **Omitting the motor's nameplate rating criterion** — must be delta-rated at line voltage.
6. **Confusing transition time with slip** — transition happens at high speed (low slip, $s \approx 0.2$–0.3), not at standstill.

### One-Line Memory Hooks for the Exam Hall

- **"Voltage 1/√3, Current 1/3, Torque 1/3"** — the trinity of star-delta reductions.
- **"Interlock saves the day — K2 and K3 never play."**
- **"Light loads, light starters; heavy loads, heavy starters."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotor Resistance Starter (No Design)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rotor Resistance Starter (No Design)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rotor Resistance Starter (RRS)** is an electromechanical starting device used exclusively for **slip-ring (wound-rotor) induction motors**, in which a graded three-phase **external rheostat** is connected in series with the rotor winding through slip rings during the starting period. The resistance is progressively **cut out in discrete steps** as the motor accelerates, until it is completely removed and the slip rings are short-circuited for normal running operation.

The principal objectives of this starter are:
- **To limit the high inrush starting current** drawn by the induction motor at slip $s = 1$ (standstill).
- **To improve the starting torque** by modifying the rotor circuit's effective impedance.
- **To obtain a smooth, controlled acceleration** from zero speed to rated speed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Mountain Bicycle**
> Imagine you are starting to pedal a bicycle from a complete stop on a steep uphill climb. If you were stuck in the **highest gear** (a small rear sprocket), you would have to push the pedal with enormous force, and the chain might even snap — analogous to drawing a very high starting current from the supply. However, if you **start in the lowest gear** (a large rear sprocket), each push of the pedal produces a small forward movement with much less effort. As the bicycle picks up speed, you progressively **shift to higher gears**, reducing the mechanical "resistance" felt at the pedal until you reach the cruising gear.
>
> In this analogy, the **rotor resistance starter** acts exactly like the bicycle's gear-shifting mechanism. The **external rotor resistance** is the "low gear" — it limits current and produces useful starting torque. As the motor speeds up, the **resistance is gradually cut out** (like shifting to higher gears), until at full speed, the rotor is short-circuited (the cruising gear). The slip rings and brushes are the mechanical "linkage" that allows this resistance to be inserted into the spinning rotor.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standstill rotor frequency:** $f_{r} = s \cdot f_s = 1 \cdot f_s = f_s$ (full supply frequency) at the moment of starting.
- **At full load running (s ≈ 0.03 to 0.05):** $f_{r} \approx 1.5$ to $2.5\,\text{Hz}$.
- **Typical external resistance range:** **0.5 Ω to 5 Ω per phase**, depending on motor rating.
- **Number of starter steps (typical):** **3 to 7 steps** for industrial-grade starters.
- **Starting current reduction factor:** can limit starting current to **1.5 to 2.5 times full-load current** (versus 5 to 7 times for DOL).
- **Starting torque enhancement:** Can produce starting torque of **1.5 to 2.5 times full-load torque**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly states "No Design" for this topic, which means students are **NOT** required to calculate the ohmic values of each starter step. Focus is on the **principle of operation**, the **effect on the torque-slip curve**, the **equivalent circuit interpretation**, and **qualitative reasoning**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why a Rotor Resistance Starter is Needed

When a three-phase induction motor is started **direct-on-line (DOL)**, the following problems arise at $s = 1$:

1. **The rotor induced EMF is at its maximum** ($E_{2,\text{standstill}} = s \cdot E_2 / s \to E_2$ at $s=1$).
2. **The rotor frequency is at its maximum** ($f_r = f_s$, typically 50 Hz).
3. **The rotor reactance is at its maximum** ($X_2 = 2\pi f_s L_2$), while the rotor resistance $R_2$ is fixed and small.
4. **The rotor impedance angle is large** ($\tan^{-1}(X_2/R_2) \approx 80°$ to $85°$).
5. **The power factor is very poor** ($\cos\phi_2 \approx 0.1$ to $0.2$).

The combined effect is a **very high starting current** (5 to 7 times full-load) but a **poor starting torque** (often only 0.5 to 1.5 times full-load torque). The high current causes:
- Voltage dip in the supply system, affecting other connected loads.
- Excessive $I^2R$ heating in the stator and rotor windings.
- High mechanical stress on the shaft, couplings, and driven load.

### 2.2 The Core Idea: Adding External Rotor Resistance

By inserting an **external resistance $R_{\text{ext}}$** in series with each rotor phase, the total rotor circuit resistance per phase becomes:

$$R_2' = R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}$$

This single change produces two simultaneous, highly beneficial effects:

- **Rotor impedance increases**, so starting current is reduced.
- **Rotor power factor improves** because the impedance angle $\phi_2 = \tan^{-1}(X_2 / R_2')$ becomes smaller, so $\cos\phi_2$ becomes larger.
- **Starting torque increases** because the torque expression is proportional to $\cos\phi_2$ divided by impedance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Unlike a **stator-side** resistance starter (which is a pure loss-making device because it wastes power in the stator supply lines), a **rotor-side** resistance starter is **NOT** a wasteful device. The slip-ring construction allows the external resistance to be placed in the **rotor circuit**, which is **electrically isolated** from the supply and which carries only the induced (not the supply) current. Furthermore, as the motor speeds up and the external resistance is cut out, the losses in $R_{\text{ext}}$ drop to zero.

### 2.3 Operational Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic)

The starter consists of a **star-connected, three-phase, liquid or wire-wound rheostat** with a manually or automatically operated wiper arm. The steps proceed as follows:

1. **At standstill:** All external resistance is **IN**. Rotor current is limited and torque is high. The motor begins to accelerate.
2. **At first preset speed (say, 25% of synchronous speed):** A portion of resistance is **cut out** (Step 1 → Step 2). Torque momentarily rises again, pushing the motor to higher speed.
3. **At each subsequent speed threshold:** More resistance is cut out.
4. **At near-rated speed:** All external resistance is **fully cut out**. The slip rings are **short-circuited** through a metal collar, and the brushes may be lifted to reduce wear.
5. **Normal running:** Rotor operates like a squirrel-cage with $R_2$ alone.

### 2.4 Effect on the Torque-Slip Characteristic

This is the **most important concept for KTU examinations**. Adding external rotor resistance produces a family of torque-slip curves, all having the same maximum torque $T_{\max}$ (independent of rotor resistance) but with the **slip at maximum torque** shifting to the right.

| Quantity | Formula | Effect of Increasing $R_{\text{ext}}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Starting torque at $s=1$ | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2 (R_2 + R_{\text{ext}})}{(R_2+R_{\text{ext}})^2 + (sX_2)^2} \bigg\vert_{s=1}$ | **Increases** (up to $T_{\max}$ at $s_{mT}$) |
| Maximum torque $T_{\max}$ | $T_{\max} = \dfrac{3V_1^2}{2\omega_s X_2}$ | **Independent** of $R_{\text{ext}}$ |
| Slip at max torque $s_{mT}$ | $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}}{X_2}$ | **Increases linearly** |
| Starting current $I_{st}$ | $I_{st} = \dfrac{sE_2}{\sqrt{(R_2+R_{\text{ext}})^2 + (sX_2)^2}} \bigg\vert_{s=1}$ | **Decreases** |
| Rotor power factor $\cos\phi_2$ | $\cos\phi_2 = \dfrac{R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}}{\sqrt{(R_2+R_{\text{ext}})^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$ | **Improves** at start |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU:** "$R_{\text{ext}}$ does NOT change the maximum torque, but it shifts the slip at which maximum torque occurs and increases the starting torque." This statement is worth 3 to 4 marks on its own in university exams.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (No Design)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **explicitly in syllabus scope**. "No Design" means you do NOT need to compute $R_{\text{ext}}$ values, but you MUST know the qualitative and quantitative relations.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_2' = R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}$ | Total effective rotor resistance per phase |
| 2 | $f_r = s \cdot f_s$ | Rotor frequency at slip $s$ |
| 3 | $X_2 = 2\pi f_s L_2$ | Standstill rotor reactance per phase (fixed, depends on $f_s$) |
| 4 | $X_{2,\text{eff}} = sX_2$ | Effective rotor reactance at slip $s$ |
| 5 | $E_{2s} = sE_2$ | Rotor induced EMF per phase at slip $s$ |
| 6 | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2 \cdot (R_2+R_{\text{ext}}) \cdot s}{s^2 X_2^2 + (R_2+R_{\text{ext}})^2}$ | Torque-slip equation (approximate, with $R_1$ neglected) |
| 7 | $T_{\max} = \dfrac{3 V_1^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$ | Maximum torque, **independent of $R_2$ and $R_{\text{ext}}$** |
| 8 | $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}}{X_2}$ | Slip at maximum torque |
| 9 | $I_{2,\text{start}} = \dfrac{E_2}{\sqrt{(R_2+R_{\text{ext}})^2 + X_2^2}}$ | Rotor starting current per phase |
| 10 | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2 (R_2+R_{\text{ext}})}{(R_2+R_{\text{ext}})^2 + X_2^2}$ | Starting torque (evaluated at $s=1$) |

> Symbols: $V_1$ = stator phase voltage, $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s / p$ (synchronous speed in rad/s, $p$ = pairs of poles), $R_2$ = rotor winding resistance per phase, $L_2$ = rotor leakage inductance per phase.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cranes, hoists, and elevators:** Require very high starting torque to lift heavy loads from rest. RRS is the **industry-standard starter** for these applications.
- **Large blowers, fans, and compressors:** Need controlled acceleration to avoid mechanical shock to the driven equipment.
- **Conveyor belts and mills:** Slip-ring induction motors with RRS are common in cement plants, paper mills, and mining.
- **Punch presses and stamping machines:** High starting torque under load is critical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Note:** Although modern Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) are replacing RRS in many new installations, the **rotor resistance starter is still the most economical and rugged solution** for high-torque, high-inertia loads in harsh industrial environments, particularly for motors above 100 kW.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effect of $R_{\text{ext}}$ on Starting Torque

We begin from the per-phase equivalent circuit of an induction motor referred to the stator, neglecting $R_1$ and $X_1$ for simplicity (valid for the rotor-side analysis required in this topic). The Thevenin voltage $V_{th} \approx V_1$ under this approximation.

The air-gap power transferred to the rotor circuit per phase is:

$$P_{g,\text{phase}} = \dfrac{V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}) / s}{\left[ (R_2 + R_{\text{ext}})/s \right]^2 + X_2^2}$$

Multiplying by 3 for three phases, the electromagnetic torque developed by the motor is:

$$T = \dfrac{P_g}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}) / s}{\left[ (R_2 + R_{\text{ext}})/s \right]^2 + X_2^2}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s^2$ to simplify:

$$T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^2 \cdot s \cdot (R_2 + R_{\text{ext}})}{s^2 X_2^2 + (R_2 + R_{\text{ext}})^2}$$

This is the **general torque-slip equation** with external rotor resistance. At starting, $s = 1$:

$$T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_2 + R_{\text{ext}})}{X_2^2 + (R_2 + R_{\text{ext}})^2}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Torque is Independent of Rotor Resistance

To find the slip at maximum torque, differentiate $T$ with respect to $s$ and set to zero. Let $R_2' = R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}$ for brevity.

$$T = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s R_2'}{s^2 X_2^2 + (R_2')^2}$$

Let $k = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2}{\omega_s}$ (a constant). Then:

$$T = k \cdot \dfrac{s R_2'}{s^2 X_2^2 + (R_2')^2}$$

Apply the quotient rule $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$:

$$\dfrac{dT}{ds} = k \cdot \dfrac{(s^2 X_2^2 + (R_2')^2) \cdot R_2' - s R_2' \cdot (2s X_2^2)}{(s^2 X_2^2 + (R_2')^2)^2} = 0$$

The numerator must equal zero (denominator is non-zero):

$$R_2' (s^2 X_2^2 + (R_2')^2) - 2 s^2 X_2^2 R_2' = 0$$

Dividing both sides by $R_2'$ (non-zero):

$$s^2 X_2^2 + (R_2')^2 - 2 s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$

$$(R_2')^2 - s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$

$$s^2 X_2^2 = (R_2')^2$$

Taking the positive square root (slip is positive for motoring):

$$s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2'}{X_2} = \dfrac{R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}}{X_2}$$

This is the **slip at maximum torque**. Now substitute $s_{mT}$ back into the torque equation to find $T_{\max}$:

$$T_{\max} = k \cdot \dfrac{s_{mT} R_2'}{s_{mT}^2 X_2^2 + (R_2')^2}$$

Since $s_{mT}^2 X_2^2 = (R_2')^2$, the denominator becomes $(R_2')^2 + (R_2')^2 = 2(R_2')^2$. Also, $s_{mT} R_2' = \dfrac{R_2'}{X_2} \cdot R_2' = \dfrac{(R_2')^2}{X_2}$.

$$T_{\max} = k \cdot \dfrac{(R_2')^2 / X_2}{2(R_2')^2} = \dfrac{k}{2 X_2}$$

Substituting back $k = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2}{\omega_s}$:

$$T_{\max} = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $R_{\text{ext}}$ has completely vanished from the expression for $T_{\max}$!** This is the rigorous proof that maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance. Only the **slip at which it occurs** is changed by $R_{\text{ext}}$. This derivation is **exam-favorite** and worth 7 marks in KTU 2024 pattern.

### 3.3 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Starting Torque

A particularly valuable practical question is: "What value of $R_{\text{ext}}$ will make the starting torque equal to the maximum torque?" This is the condition for **optimum starting performance**.

Setting $T_{st} = T_{\max}$, the starting torque equation at $s = 1$ must equal the maximum torque expression. By inspection of the previous derivations, $T_{st} = T_{\max}$ occurs when $s = s_{mT}$ and $s_{mT} = 1$:

$$s_{mT} = 1 = \dfrac{R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}}{X_2}$$

Solving for $R_{\text{ext}}$:

$$R_{\text{ext,opt}} = X_2 - R_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practice Insight:** When asked "If the motor is to develop maximum torque at starting, what should be the total rotor circuit resistance?", the answer is **$R_2' = X_2$** (i.e., total rotor resistance equals standstill rotor reactance). Although we are not designing values, this qualitative relation is **routinely asked**.

### 3.4 Derivation: Ratio of Starting Torque to Maximum Torque

A common KTU 2-mark or 3-mark question asks: "Express $T_{st}/T_{\max}$ in terms of $s_{mT}$."

We use the two expressions:

$$T_{st} = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{X_2^2 + (R_2')^2}, \quad T_{\max} = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{\max}} = \dfrac{2 X_2 R_2'}{X_2^2 + (R_2')^2}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $1/X_2^2$:

$$\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{\max}} = \dfrac{2 (R_2'/X_2)}{1 + (R_2'/X_2)^2} = \dfrac{2 s_{mT}}{1 + s_{mT}^2}$$

This compact formula is widely used in KTU problems:

$$\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{\max}} = \dfrac{2 s_{mT}}{1 + s_{mT}^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is a **double-duty formula**: it lets you compute $T_{st}$ given $T_{\max}$ and $s_{mT}$, and it can be inverted to find $s_{mT}$ given the torque ratio. Memorize it.

### 3.5 Illustrative Numerical Example (No Design, Conceptual)

**Given:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, slip-ring induction motor with rotor resistance per phase $R_2 = 0.1 \, \Omega$ and standstill rotor reactance per phase $X_2 = 0.5 \, \Omega$. The motor is started with a rotor resistance starter.

**Find (conceptually):**
1. Synchronous speed: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \,\text{rpm}$.
2. Synchronous angular speed: $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \,\text{rad/s}$.
3. Slip at maximum torque **without** $R_{\text{ext}}$: $s_{mT,\text{natural}} = \dfrac{0.1}{0.5} = 0.2$ (i.e., 20%).
4. Slip at maximum torque **with** $R_{\text{ext}} = 0.4 \, \Omega$ (i.e., total $R_2' = 0.5 \, \Omega$): $s_{mT,\text{new}} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.5} = 1.0$ (i.e., 100%, which is at standstill — meaning starting torque is now equal to maximum torque).
5. The rotor starting current is reduced because impedance has increased.

This illustrates the **principle** (without computing exact numerical starter step values — which is the "No Design" mandate).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Rotor Resistance Starter


### 4.2 Torque-Slip Family of Curves (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[No External Resistance R-ext = 0]
        A1[Smax T-max at low slip ~0.05 to 0.2]

    end

        B1[Smax T-max shifts to higher slip]
        B2[Improved starting torque]
    end

        C2[Starting torque equals maximum torque]
    end

    end

    A --> B --> C --> D

    B1 -.-> B2
    C1 -.-> C2
    D1 -.-> D2

    style A fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c

    style D fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
```

### 4.3 Equivalent Circuit (Rotor-Side, with $R_{\text{ext}}$)

The per-phase rotor equivalent circuit, referred to the stator, with external resistance inserted, can be represented schematically as:

```
  +-------+          +-------+         +-------+
  | E2s   |          | R2    |         | R-ext |   <-- External
  | sE2   |--[R2]---| (rotor|--[R-ext]-| (star |-- per phase
  | (EMF) |          | res.) |         | conn.) |
  +-------+          +-------+         +-------+
       |                                  |
       +-----[X2 (sX2 at slip s)]--------+
                  |
                  v
             (To load / mechanical output)
```

Key relationships in this equivalent circuit:

- The **EMF source** $E_{2s} = s E_2$ varies from $E_2$ at $s=1$ to nearly zero at $s \to 0$.
- The **reactance** $X_2$ is the standstill rotor reactance, fixed in value; at any slip $s$, the effective reactance is $s X_2$.
- The **external resistance** $R_{\text{ext}}$ is added in series with $R_2$, effectively increasing the total series resistance and the circuit's $R/X$ ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Aid:** When the external resistance is added, the rotor circuit becomes a more **resistive** (less reactive) circuit. The phasor of the rotor current $I_2$ rotates **closer to the in-phase (resistive) axis**, increasing $\cos\phi_2$. This is the geometric origin of the improved power factor and hence the improved starting torque.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*"Why is a rotor resistance starter preferred over a stator resistance starter for starting slip-ring induction motors?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A rotor resistance starter is preferred because:

1. **(1 mark)** The external resistance is inserted in the **rotor circuit**, which carries only the **induced (slip-frequency) current** — not the supply current. The power dissipated in this resistance is $s$ times the air-gap power, so at high slip during starting, although losses are real, they are confined to the **rotor side** and are NOT reflected back to the supply as a voltage drop.

2. **(1 mark)** In contrast, a **stator resistance starter** wastes power continuously in the supply lines, causes a **persistent supply voltage drop** affecting other consumers, and **does not improve starting torque** (only reduces current).

3. **(1 mark)** Furthermore, the rotor resistance starter **simultaneously increases starting torque** (by improving rotor power factor) while **limiting starting current** — a dual benefit that stator resistance starters cannot provide.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*"State and explain the effect of adding external resistance to the rotor circuit on the starting torque and maximum torque of an induction motor."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** **Effect on Maximum Torque:** The maximum torque $T_{\max} = \dfrac{3V_1^2}{2\omega_s X_2}$ is **independent** of rotor resistance. Hence, adding $R_{\text{ext}}$ does NOT change $T_{\max}$.

2. **(1 mark)** **Effect on Slip at Maximum Torque:** The slip at which maximum torque occurs shifts to a higher value: $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}}{X_2}$. By choosing $R_{\text{ext}}$ appropriately, $s_{mT}$ can be made equal to 1 (standstill), so the motor develops **maximum torque right from the start**.

3. **(1 mark)** **Effect on Starting Torque:** Starting torque (at $s = 1$) increases with $R_{\text{ext}}$ up to a point — specifically, $T_{st}$ increases as $R_{\text{ext}}$ is added, reaches $T_{\max}$ when $R_2 + R_{\text{ext}} = X_2$, and then decreases if more resistance is added. Thus there is an **optimum** value of $R_{\text{ext}}$ for maximum $T_{st}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 4 Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and principle of operation of a rotor resistance starter used for slip-ring induction motors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of torque-slip characteristics, explain how the addition of external rotor resistance improves the starting performance of an induction motor. Show that the maximum torque developed by an induction motor is independent of rotor circuit resistance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Operation (7 marks):**

A rotor resistance starter consists of the following key components: **(1 mark)**
- **Three slip rings** mounted on the rotor shaft, insulated from each other and from the shaft.
- **Three sets of carbon brushes** riding on the slip rings, connected via flexible cables to the starter.
- A **three-phase, star-connected rheostat** with a graded resistance per phase, with a common movable wiper arm.
- A **step-controlling mechanism** (manual handle, contactor-based, or liquid electrolyte level sensor).

**Operation (Step-by-Step) — (4 marks):**
1. At standstill, the **full resistance** of the rheostat is in series with the rotor winding through the slip rings. The rotor circuit impedance is high, so starting current is limited.
2. The high resistance improves the rotor power factor, producing a high starting torque.
3. As the motor accelerates, the operator (or automatic timer) progressively **short-circuits sections** of the rheostat, moving from Step 1 → Step 2 → ... → final step.
4. At the last step, all external resistance is **fully cut out**. A **short-circuiting device** (a metal collar operated by a lever) directly short-circuits the slip rings. The brushes may then be **lifted** to reduce wear.
5. The motor now runs in the **normal short-circuited rotor mode**, identical in performance to a squirrel-cage motor.

**Sketch description (2 marks):** A neat diagram showing the three slip rings connected to the three rotor phases, the brushes leading to a star-connected rheostat with stepped tappings, and the short-circuiting collar. *(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for the functional flow.)*

---

**(b) Torque-Slip Characteristics and Proof of $T_{\max}$ Independence (7 marks):**

**Torque-slip family of curves (3 marks):**

For a given motor, the natural torque-slip curve (with $R_2$ alone) has its peak at $s_{mT} = R_2/X_2 \approx 0.05$ to $0.2$, which is far from the starting point $s=1$. The starting torque $T_{st}$ on this natural curve is typically only 0.5 to 1.5 times the full-load torque.

When $R_{\text{ext},1}$ is added, a new torque-slip curve is obtained with the **same peak torque** $T_{\max}$ but occurring at a **higher slip** $s_{mT,1} = (R_2 + R_{\text{ext},1})/X_2$. The starting torque on this new curve is higher than on the natural curve.

Adding progressively more resistance ($R_{\text{ext},1} < R_{\text{ext},2} < R_{\text{ext},3} < \ldots$), the family of curves shifts rightward, with $T_{st}$ increasing until it equals $T_{\max}$ when $R_2 + R_{\text{ext}} = X_2$.

**Proof that $T_{\max}$ is independent of rotor resistance (4 marks):**

Starting from the per-phase torque equation:

$$T = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s R_2'}{s^2 X_2^2 + (R_2')^2} \quad \text{where} \quad R_2' = R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}$$

Differentiating with respect to $s$ and setting $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$:

$$\dfrac{d}{ds}\left[\dfrac{s R_2'}{s^2 X_2^2 + (R_2')^2}\right] = 0$$

Applying the quotient rule:

$$R_2'(s^2 X_2^2 + (R_2')^2) - s R_2' (2s X_2^2) = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$(R_2')^2 - s^2 X_2^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2'}{X_2}$$

Substituting $s_{mT}$ back into the torque expression:

$$T_{\max} = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{(R_2'/X_2) \cdot R_2'}{2(R_2')^2} = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$$

The **rotor resistance terms $R_2$ and $R_{\text{ext}}$ have completely cancelled out**, proving that $T_{\max}$ depends only on $V_1$, $\omega_s$, and $X_2$, and not on the rotor circuit resistance. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module 4 Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam]**

**(a)** Explain why a slip-ring induction motor requires a starting arrangement. Discuss the limitations of direct-on-line starting and explain how a rotor resistance starter overcomes these limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz slip-ring induction motor, the rotor resistance per phase is $R_2 = 0.2 \, \Omega$ and the standstill rotor reactance per phase is $X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$. Determine the value of external resistance to be added per phase so that the motor develops maximum torque at starting. Also calculate the ratio of starting torque to maximum torque without external resistance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Starting Arrangements for Slip-Ring IM (7 marks):**

1. **Why starting arrangement is needed (2 marks):**
   At standstill, the rotor induced EMF is at its maximum ($E_{2s} = E_2$), and the rotor frequency equals the supply frequency. The rotor impedance is dominated by the leakage reactance $X_2$ because $X_2 \gg R_2$. This leads to:
   - **Large starting current** (5 to 7 times full-load) due to low impedance.
   - **Poor starting power factor** (rotor current lags rotor EMF by a large angle, $\cos\phi_2 \approx 0.1$).
   - **Low starting torque** (torque $\propto \cos\phi_2$).

2. **Limitations of DOL starting (2 marks):**
   - Severe **voltage dip** in the supply system, affecting other connected loads.
   - **Heating** in stator and rotor windings from $I^2R$ losses.
   - **Mechanical stress** on shaft, bearings, and driven equipment (especially high-inertia loads).
   - **Tripping** of protective devices (overcurrent relays, circuit breakers).

3. **How rotor resistance starter overcomes these (3 marks):**
   - Inserting $R_{\text{ext}}$ increases the total rotor circuit impedance, **reducing starting current**.
   - Increasing $R_2'$ improves the rotor **power factor** $\cos\phi_2 = R_2'/\sqrt{(R_2')^2 + X_2^2}$, which **increases starting torque**.
   - The starter provides **smooth, step-wise acceleration** rather than an abrupt DOL jerk.
   - Unlike stator-side resistance, the rotor-side external resistance is **not continuously dissipative**; it is cut out as the motor speeds up.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P = 6$ poles, $f_s = 50 \, \text{Hz}$, $R_2 = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$.

**Step 1: Slip at maximum torque with natural rotor resistance (1 mark):**
$$s_{mT,\text{natural}} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2} = \dfrac{0.2}{1.0} = 0.2$$

**Step 2: Condition for maximum torque at starting (2 marks):**
For maximum torque to occur at $s = 1$ (i.e., at standstill), we require:
$$s_{mT} = 1 = \dfrac{R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}}{X_2}$$
$$R_2 + R_{\text{ext}} = X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$$
$$\boxed{R_{\text{ext}} = X_2 - R_2 = 1.0 - 0.2 = 0.8 \, \Omega \text{ per phase}}$$

**Step 3: Synchronous speed (1 mark):**
$$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \, \text{rpm}$$
$$\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} \approx 104.72 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Maximum torque (1 mark):** Using the Thevenin approximation $V_{th} \approx V_1$ (line-to-neutral):
$$T_{\max} = \dfrac{3 V_1^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$$

**Step 5: Starting torque without external resistance (1 mark):** At $s = 1$ with $R_{\text{ext}} = 0$:
$$T_{st,\text{natural}} = \dfrac{3 V_1^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{X_2^2 + R_2^2} = \dfrac{3 V_1^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{0.2}{1.0 + 0.04} = \dfrac{3 V_1^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{0.2}{1.04}$$

**Step 6: Ratio $T_{st}/T_{\max}$ (1 mark):**
$$\dfrac{T_{st,\text{natural}}}{T_{\max}} = \dfrac{2 s_{mT,\text{natural}}}{1 + s_{mT,\text{natural}}^2} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.2}{1 + 0.04} = \dfrac{0.4}{1.04} \approx 0.3846$$

**Interpretation:** Without external resistance, the starting torque is only about **38.5%** of the maximum torque available from the motor. With $R_{\text{ext}} = 0.8 \, \Omega$ added, the starting torque becomes **equal to $T_{\max}$** — a major performance improvement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the role of $R_{\text{ext}}$:** Many students write that $R_{\text{ext}}$ *increases maximum torque*. **This is wrong.** $R_{\text{ext}}$ only *shifts the slip* at which $T_{\max}$ occurs and *increases the starting torque*. The value of $T_{\max}$ itself does not change. Loss of 2 to 3 marks if this is misstated.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $s_{mT}$ shift:** The torque-slip curve shifts to the **right** (toward higher slip) as $R_{\text{ext}}$ increases. Some students draw the curves shifting left or shifting upward — both are incorrect. The **peak height stays the same**; only its horizontal position moves.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the two formulas:** $T_{st}$ and $T_{\max}$ have different denominators. $T_{st}$ has $(R_2')^2 + X_2^2$ in the denominator (because $s = 1$ in the $s^2 X_2^2$ term). $T_{\max}$ has $2(R_2')^2$ in the denominator (because the optimum-slip substitution yields a different form). Writing $T_{st}$ as if it were $T_{\max}$ loses 2 marks.
>
> 4. **"No Design" trap:** Some students attempt to compute the **ohmic values of each starter step** using complex arithmetic. The 2024 scheme explicitly says **"No Design"** — focus on the principle, the curves, and the qualitative relationships, not the step-by-step ohmic calculations.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the power factor improvement:** A complete answer must explain that $R_{\text{ext}}$ improves $\cos\phi_2$ and this improvement is the **fundamental reason** for higher starting torque. Simply stating "$T_{st}$ increases" without explaining the power factor angle change is incomplete and may lose 1 to 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Stator vs Rotor confusion:** Stator resistance starters waste power and reduce torque; rotor resistance starters improve torque and limit current. Conflating the two is a serious conceptual error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rotor Resistance Starter (No Design)**

- **Motor type:** Used **only** for **slip-ring (wound-rotor) induction motors**; not applicable to squirrel-cage motors.
- **Core principle:** Add external resistance $R_{\text{ext}}$ in series with the rotor winding through slip rings, and progressively cut it out in steps as the motor accelerates.
- **Dual benefit:** **(i)** Starting current is reduced; **(ii)** Starting torque is increased — both simultaneously.
- **Maximum torque is INDEPENDENT of $R_{\text{ext}}$:** $T_{\max} = \dfrac{3V_1^2}{2\omega_s X_2}$ — only $V_1$, $\omega_s$, and $X_2$ matter.
- **Slip at $T_{\max}$ SHIFTS with $R_{\text{ext}}$:** $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}}{X_2}$ — increases linearly with $R_{\text{ext}}$.
- **Optimum starting condition:** When $R_2 + R_{\text{ext}} = X_2$, the motor develops **$T_{\max}$ at starting** ($s_{mT} = 1$).
- **Power factor improvement:** $R_{\text{ext}}$ reduces the rotor impedance angle $\phi_2$, improving $\cos\phi_2$ at start.
- **Power loss:** Power dissipated in $R_{\text{ext}}$ is $s \times P_{ag}$ (slip times air-gap power). It is **not** reflected as a continuous supply voltage drop because it is on the rotor side.
- **Final running state:** All $R_{\text{ext}}$ cut out, slip rings **short-circuited** (and brushes may be lifted).
- **Key ratio formula:** $\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{\max}} = \dfrac{2 s_{mT}}{1 + s_{mT}^2}$ — **memorize** for numerical problems.
- **Industrial applications:** Cranes, hoists, elevators, large fans, conveyors, mills — anywhere high starting torque is needed.
- **No-Design mandate:** KTU 2024 scheme **does not require** computing the ohmic values of starter steps; focus on the operating principle, the torque-slip curves, and the qualitative + quantitative relationships above.
- **Advantage over stator-side resistance starter:** Rotor-side resistance improves torque (not just limits current) and is not a permanent power drain on the supply.
- **Number of starter steps (typical):** 3 to 7 steps, chosen to keep the acceleration torque within acceptable bounds.
- **Common exam errors to avoid:** (1) Stating that $R_{\text{ext}}$ changes $T_{\max}$ — it does not. (2) Drawing torque curves shifting up instead of right. (3) Forgetting the $s=1$ condition in $T_{st}$. (4) Confusing rotor and stator resistance starter behavior.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🔷 1.1 What is a Three-Phase Induction Motor?

A **Three-Phase Induction Motor (TIM)** is a self-starting, robust, and maintenance-free AC machine that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy based on the principle of **electromagnetic induction** between a stationary stator and a rotating rotor. It is the **workhorse of modern industry**, driving pumps, fans, compressors, conveyors, and machine tools in approximately **80% of all industrial applications**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A three-phase induction motor is a singly-excited AC machine in which the stator winding is connected to a 3-phase supply and the rotor derives its power through electromagnetic induction from the stator field at a speed less than the synchronous speed."*

### 🔹 1.2 The "Why" Behind Module 4 Numerical Problems

In Module 4, you are required to **analyze the steady-state performance** of a three-phase induction motor using:
- The **exact per-phase equivalent circuit**
- The **approximate equivalent circuit** (with magnetizing branch shifted to stator terminals)
- The **power flow stages** (Stator Input → Air-Gap Power → Mechanical Power Developed → Shaft Output)
- The **torque-slip characteristic**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight (KTU Examiner Focus):**
> Every numerical problem in this module ultimately traces back to **two master equations**:
> 1. **Power Division:** $P_{gap} : P_{cu,rotor} : P_{m,dev} = 1 : s : (1-s)$
> 2. **Torque Equation:** $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$
>
> Master these two, and you master Module 4 numericals.

## 🔷 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Slip Conveyor Belt"

Imagine a **rotating magnetic field** as a giant **circular conveyor belt** moving at synchronous speed $N_s$. The **rotor conductors** are like **fitness enthusiasts running on this belt**.

- If the runner matches the belt's speed perfectly → **No relative motion** → **No induced EMF** → **No torque** (useless!)
- If the runner stops completely → **Maximum relative motion** → **High induced EMF, but rotor is inefficient** (high losses)
- The **sweet spot** is somewhere in between, where the runner falls slightly behind the belt by a small percentage called the **slip ($s$)** — this is exactly when **maximum useful mechanical work** is done.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world reference:** A ceiling fan runs at ~1440 rpm when the supply is 50 Hz, 4-pole ($N_s = 1500$ rpm). The slip is 4%, which is the **typical full-load operating zone** for industrial motors.

## 🔷 1.4 Standard Assumptions in KTU Numerical Problems

| Assumption | Why It Is Made |
|------------|----------------|
| Stator and rotor windings are **symmetrical and balanced** | Permits use of a **single per-phase equivalent circuit** |
| Magnetizing current is **purely reactive** | Core loss lumped into a separate shunt resistance $R_c$ (when given) |
| Rotor EMF $E_2$ and reactance $X_2$ are **referred to the stator side** ($E_2'$, $X_2'$) | Enables single-loop circuit analysis |
| Mechanical losses = Friction + Windage only (if not specified) | Simplifies the power flow diagram |
| Synchronous speed is **constant** for a given supply | Decouples electrical and mechanical sub-problems |

# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔷 2.1 The Power Flow — Stage by Stage Logic

A three-phase induction motor's power flow follows a strict **cascaded deduction chain**. The key is that **rotor copper loss is the only "tax" paid between air-gap power and mechanical power developed**.

$$\boxed{P_{input} \to P_{stator\,losses} \to P_{gap} \to P_{cu,rotor} \to P_{m,dev} \to P_{mech\,losses} \to P_{shaft}}$$

Where:
- $P_{stator\,losses} = P_{cu,stator} + P_{core}$ (stator copper + iron loss)
- $P_{gap} = $ Power crossing the air-gap from stator to rotor
- $P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{gap}$ (rotor copper loss is the fraction $s$ of air-gap power)
- $P_{m,dev} = (1-s) \cdot P_{gap}$ (mechanical power developed)
- $P_{shaft} = P_{m,dev} - P_{mech\,losses}$ (after friction, windage, stray load loss)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Induction Motor Power Stages:**
> The **air-gap power** is the parent quantity, and it splits between the **rotor copper loss** ($s$ share) and the **developed mechanical power** ($(1-s)$ share). The ratio $P_{cu,rotor} : P_{m,dev} = s : (1-s)$ is the **single most useful fact** in any KTU power flow problem.

## 🔷 2.2 The Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit (Thevenin & Exact)

In the **exact equivalent circuit**, the rotor branch is modelled as $R_2'/s$, which is split for analysis convenience as:
- $R_2'$ → actual rotor resistance (carries $P_{cu,rotor}$)
- $R_2'(1-s)/s$ → mechanical load equivalent resistance (carries $P_{m,dev}$)

### 🔹 Thevenin Equivalent (Used for Torque Problems)

To simplify torque calculations, the circuit is reduced to a **single-series Thevenin equivalent** seen from the rotor terminals:

$$V_{th} = V_1 \cdot \frac{jX_m}{(R_1 + jX_1) + jX_m}, \quad Z_{th} = \frac{(R_1 + jX_1)(jX_m)}{R_1 + jX_1 + jX_m}$$

The Thevenin form for **total rotor-side impedance at slip $s$**:
$$Z_{rotor} = \frac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2'$$

## 🔷 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **The table below is the ONLY reference you need during exam preparation for Module 4 numericals. Print it. Memorize it.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | $P$ = number of poles (even integer) |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | pu (0 to 1) | $s = 0$ at synchronous, $s = 1$ at standstill |
| 3 | Slip in rpm | $N_{slip} = N_s - N_r$ | rpm | The "missing rpm" relative to field |
| 4 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s f$ | Hz | At standstill: $f_r = f$ |
| 5 | Rotor EMF at slip | $E_{2s} = s E_{2(\text{standstill})}$ | V/phase | Linear with slip |
| 6 | Rotor reactance at slip | $X_{2s} = s X_{2(\text{standstill})}$ | Ω/phase | Linear with slip |
| 7 | Synchronous angular velocity | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | For torque equations |
| 8 | Gross (developed) torque | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | N·m | Uses **referred** $E_2$, $R_2$, $X_2$ if $V_{th}$ used |
| 9 | Slip at max torque | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | pu | Independent of load! |
| 10 | Maximum torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2}{X_2}$ | N·m | **Independent of $R_2$** (a remarkable result) |
| 11 | Starting torque ($s=1$) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ | N·m | Often less than full-load torque in cage motors |
| 12 | Air-gap power | $P_{gap} = \dfrac{P_{cu,rotor}}{s} = \dfrac{P_{m,dev}}{1-s}$ | W | The "parent" power |
| 13 | Developed mechanical power | $P_{m,dev} = (1-s) P_{gap} = \dfrac{3 I_2'^2 R_2' (1-s)}{s}$ | W | Across the air gap |
| 14 | Shaft output power | $P_{shaft} = T_{shaft} \cdot \omega_r$ | W | Where $\omega_r = (1-s)\omega_s$ |
| 15 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{input}} \times 100\%$ | % | Total efficiency |
| 16 | Power factor | $\cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{input}}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_L}$ | pu | For 3-phase line values |
| 17 | Torque ratio to max | $\dfrac{T}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2 a s}{a^2 + s^2}$ where $a = \dfrac{R_2}{s_{max} X_2}$ | pu | Useful for torque-speed curve plotting |

## 🔷 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry / Equipment | Why Induction Motor is the Only Choice |
|----------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Pumps & Compressors** (water treatment, HVAC, petrochemical) | Constant-speed, high reliability, no sparking (explosion-safe) |
| **Conveyor Belts & Mixers** (food, mining, cement) | High starting torque, rugged construction |
| **Elevators & Cranes** (with VFD control) | V/f control gives variable speed with constant torque |
| **Electric Vehicles (Tesla, Tata, Hyundai EVs)** | Squirrel-cage induction motor with vector control (Field-Oriented Control) |
| **Domestic Appliances** (washing machines, refrigerators) | Single-phase capacitor-start or shaded-pole variants |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection:** Modern Tesla Model S/X uses a **3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor** with a copper rotor bar design, controlled by a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** that adjusts the stator frequency from near-DC (during start) to ~200 Hz at high speed. The KTU module gives you the **theoretical foundation** for this very real engineering system.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Numerical Solutions (Solved Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following problems are **modeled on the KTU 2024 Scheme question patterns**, including 2-mark sub-questions, 7-mark derivations, and the classic 14-mark two-part questions. **Each step is fully written out** so you can follow the logic in real-time during exam preparation.
