## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

> **Why is a single-phase induction motor not self-starting? Explain using the double revolving field theory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A single-phase induction motor has only one stator winding, which, when energised by single-phase AC, produces an **alternating (pulsating) flux** along the axis of the winding. According to the **double revolving field theory**, this pulsating flux can be resolved into **two equal counter-rotating flux components**, each of magnitude $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$ and rotating at synchronous speed $N_s$ in opposite directions.

Each of these two rotating fields induces currents in the rotor and produces a torque in its own direction of rotation. At standstill (slip $s = 1$ for both fields), the forward torque and the backward torque are **equal in magnitude but opposite in direction**, so the net torque on the rotor is **zero**. Hence, the rotor cannot start rotating on its own, and an auxiliary starting mechanism is required to give the rotor an initial push in the desired direction.

> **Marking Key:** Definition of double revolving field (1 mark), explanation of $T_f = T_b$ at standstill (1 mark), conclusion — net torque is zero — hence not self-starting (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Expected Pattern]**

> **List any three starting methods of single-phase induction motors and state the application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Starting Method | Application |
| :-: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Split-phase (Resistance Start)** | Small fans, blowers, centrifugal pumps, washing machines (light-duty). |
| 2 | **Capacitor-Start** | Compressors, conveyors, reciprocating pumps (medium-duty where high starting torque is required). |
| 3 | **Shaded-Pole** | Small fans, hair dryers, clocks, record players, toys (very low starting torque, low-cost applications). |

> **Marking Key:** Any three correct methods with valid applications (1 mark each = 3 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **forget the factor of 2** in the rotor resistance expressions $\dfrac{R_2}{2s}$ and $\dfrac{R_2}{2(2-s)}$. This is the most common reason for losing **2 to 3 marks** in Part B derivations. Always draw the equivalent circuit explicitly and label all impedances.
> 2. In numerical problems, students **mix up the slip values** $s$ and $(2 - s)$. Remember: $s$ corresponds to the **forward** field, $(2 - s)$ corresponds to the **backward** field.
> 3. For the shaded-pole motor, students often wrongly state that the shading ring is made of iron. **It is a single-turn copper ring** — lose 1 mark if you say iron.
> 4. **Comparison questions** (e.g., capacitor-start vs. capacitor-run) must include a **circuit diagram or table** — drawing fetches full marks; a purely textual answer is penalised by 1 to 2 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified Pattern]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the forward and backward torques in a single-phase induction motor using the double revolving field theory. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Step 1 — State the theorem:**
A pulsating flux $\phi(t) = \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$ can be resolved into two counter-rotating components:

$$\phi_f = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t - \theta), \quad \phi_b = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t + \theta)$$

**Step 2 — Slip relations:**

Let the rotor rotate at $(1 - s) \omega$ in the forward direction. Then:
- Slip with respect to forward field: $s_f = s$
- Slip with respect to backward field: $s_b = 2 - s$

**Step 3 — Rotor induced EMFs and impedances:**

$$E_{2f} = s E_2 \quad ; \quad Z_{2f} = R_2 + j s X_2$$
$$E_{2b} = (2 - s) E_2 \quad ; \quad Z_{2b} = R_2 + j (2 - s) X_2$$

**Step 4 — Air-gap powers:**

$$P_{gf} = 2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}\right)^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2 / s}{(R_2 / s)^2 + X_2^2} = \dfrac{\phi_m^2}{2} \cdot \dfrac{R_2 s}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$

$$P_{gb} = \dfrac{\phi_m^2}{2} \cdot \dfrac{R_2 (2 - s)}{R_2^2 + ((2 - s) X_2)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Torques:**

$$T_f = \dfrac{P_{gf}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{K \phi_m^2 R_2}{2} \cdot \dfrac{s}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$

$$T_b = \dfrac{P_{gb}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{K \phi_m^2 R_2}{2} \cdot \dfrac{2 - s}{R_2^2 + ((2 - s) X_2)^2}$$

This is the required derivation. **(7 marks distributed: 1 mark for theorem, 1 mark for slip, 2 marks for impedance/power, 2 marks for final torques, 1 mark for units and net torque conclusion.)**

---

**(b) Explain the working of a capacitor-start single-phase induction motor with a neat sketch. Why is a centrifugal switch used? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Construction:**
- The stator carries two windings: **main (running) winding** and **auxiliary (starting) winding**, displaced spatially by 90 electrical degrees.
- The **auxiliary winding** is connected in series with a **capacitor $C$** and a **centrifugal switch**.
- The **main winding** is connected directly across the single-phase supply.

**Working:**
- When the supply is switched on, the current in the main winding lags the voltage by a small angle (high inductance), while the current in the auxiliary winding leads the voltage due to the series capacitor.
- The two currents are thus **phase-displaced by approximately 80° to 90°**, which produces a **rotating magnetic field** in the air gap (similar to a 2-phase motor).
- This rotating field cuts the squirrel-cage rotor, inducing currents, and the motor starts with a high starting torque (3 to 4.5 times full-load torque).
- As the motor accelerates to about **70 % to 80 % of synchronous speed**, the **centrifugal switch** opens, disconnecting the auxiliary winding and the capacitor from the supply.
- The motor then continues to run on the main winding alone as a single-phase induction motor.

**Why a centrifugal switch is needed:**
- The auxiliary winding and capacitor are designed for **short-time duty (intermittent operation)** only — typically a few seconds during starting.
- If the auxiliary winding were to remain in the circuit, the capacitor would cause excessive current and heating.
- The centrifugal switch **automatically disconnects the auxiliary winding** once the motor reaches sufficient speed, protecting the winding and improving efficiency.

**Sketch (textual, since drawing in text is approximate):**
```
     AC SUPPLY (230 V, 50 Hz)
            |
       +----+----+
       |         |
       |         |
   [MAIN WND]  [AUX WND]
       |         |
       |      [CAPACITOR C]
       |         |
       |      [CENTRIFUGAL SWITCH]
       |         |
       +----+----+
            |
          ROTOR (SQUIRREL CAGE)
```

> **Marking Key (Part b):** Construction details (2 marks), working principle with phase-displacement (2 marks), role of centrifugal switch (2 marks), neat sketch (1 mark).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified Pattern]**

**(a) With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the construction and working of a capacitor-start capacitor-run (CSCR) single-phase induction motor. List its advantages over the capacitor-start motor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Construction:**
- The stator has two windings: **main winding** and **auxiliary winding**, displaced by 90° electrical.
- The auxiliary winding is connected in series with **two capacitors in parallel**:
  - A **start capacitor $C_s$** (large value, typically 50 to 300 µF, electrolytic type) in series with a **centrifugal switch**.
  - A **run capacitor $C_r$** (smaller value, typically 5 to 50 µF, oil-filled paper type) connected permanently.
- Both capacitors are in parallel with each other but $C_s$ is in series with the switch.

**Working:**
- At starting, both $C_s$ and $C_r$ are in the circuit, giving a very high starting torque (3 to 4.5 × full-load torque) and a smooth, quiet start.
- When the motor reaches about 70 to 80 % of synchronous speed, the centrifugal switch opens, disconnecting $C_s$.
- The auxiliary winding and $C_r$ remain in the circuit permanently, providing improved running performance — higher power factor, higher efficiency, and smoother torque.

**Circuit Diagram (textual):**
```
              SUPPLY
                |
        +-------+--------+
        |                |
        |                |
     [MAIN]         [AUX WND]
        |                |
        |         +------+------+
        |         |             |
        |       [C_s]      [C_r]
        |         |             |
        |     [SWITCH]          |
        |         |             |
        +---------+-------------+
                  |
                ROTOR
```

**Advantages of CSCR over Capacitor-Start:**
1. **Higher starting torque** (3 to 4.5 × full-load torque, compared to 3 to 3.5 × in capacitor-start).
2. **Higher running power factor** (up to 0.95 to 1.0, compared to 0.6 to 0.8 in capacitor-start).
3. **Higher running efficiency** (5 % to 10 % improvement).
4. **Smoother and quieter operation** — the permanent run capacitor cancels the backward rotating field partially, reducing hum and vibration.
5. **Higher pull-out torque** — better overload capacity.

> **Marking Key (Part a):** Circuit diagram (2 marks), working principle (2 marks), starting and running sequence (2 marks), at least three advantages (1 mark).

---

**(b) A 4-pole, 50 Hz single-phase induction motor has rotor resistance and standstill reactance of 2 Ω and 3 Ω per phase respectively. Calculate the slip at maximum torque and the ratio of maximum torque to starting torque. (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz
- $R_2 = 2 \; \Omega$, $X_2 = 3 \; \Omega$

**Step 1 — Slip at maximum torque:**

For the forward field, the slip at which $T_{f,max}$ occurs is:
$$s_{f, max} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2} = \dfrac{2}{3} = 0.6667$$

**Step 2 — Maximum torque expression (per unit, using $K \phi^2 / (2 X_2)$ as base):**

The normalised torque-slip relation per field is:
$$T = \dfrac{2 \alpha s}{1 + (\alpha s)^2}, \quad \text{where } \alpha = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$$

At $s = s_{max} = R_2 / X_2 = 1/\alpha$, we have $\alpha s = 1$, hence:
$$T_{max} = \dfrac{2 \alpha \cdot (1/\alpha)}{1 + 1^2} = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 3 — Starting torque (at $s = 1$):**

$$T_{start} = \dfrac{2 \alpha \cdot 1}{1 + \alpha^2} = \dfrac{2 \alpha}{1 + \alpha^2}$$

With $\alpha = R_2 / X_2 = 2/3$:
$$T_{start} = \dfrac{2 \times (2/3)}{1 + (2/3)^2} = \dfrac{4/3}{1 + 4/9} = \dfrac{4/3}{13/9} = \dfrac{4}{3} \times \dfrac{9}{13} = \dfrac{36}{39} = \dfrac{12}{13} \approx 0.923 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 4 — Ratio of maximum torque to starting torque:**

$$\dfrac{T_{max}}{T_{start}} = \dfrac{1}{12/13} = \dfrac{13}{12} \approx 1.083$$

> **Final Answers:**
> - $s_{max} = 0.6667$ (or **66.67 %**).
> - $\dfrac{T_{max}}{T_{start}} = \dfrac{13}{12} \approx 1.083$.

> **Marking Key (Part b):** Formula for $s_{max}$ (2 marks), correct calculation of starting torque (2 marks), correct ratio (2 marks), units and final answer (1 mark).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A SPIM runs on single-phase AC but is **not self-starting**; it requires an auxiliary mechanism.
- **Double Revolving Field Theory** splits the pulsating stator flux into **two counter-rotating fluxes of half amplitude**, each at synchronous speed.
- **Net starting torque is zero** because $T_f = T_b$ at $s = 1$ (standstill).
- Once pushed, the motor develops a net torque in the direction of the push.
- **Cross-field theory** is an alternative view; both theories give the same equivalent circuit.
- **Equivalent circuit** has magnetising branch $X_m$ in parallel with two rotor branches: $\dfrac{R_2}{2s}$ (forward) and $\dfrac{R_2}{2(2-s)}$ (backward).
- **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$.
- **Slip:** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$.
- **Slip for maximum torque:** $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$.
- **Maximum torque:** $T_{max} = \dfrac{K \phi^2}{4 X_2}$ (per forward field).
- **Starting methods and their typical applications:**
  - **Split-phase** → fans, blowers (low cost, moderate starting torque).
  - **Capacitor-start** → compressors, pumps (high starting torque).
  - **Capacitor-start capacitor-run (CSCR)** → refrigerators, A.C. compressors (best performance).
  - **Permanent capacitor (capacitor-run)** → ceiling fans, small blowers (smooth, quiet).
  - **Shaded-pole** → hair dryers, small fans, clocks (lowest cost, very low starting torque).
  - **Reluctance-start** → low-power clocks, toys.
  - **Repulsion-start** → legacy high-starting-torque applications (now obsolete).
- **Direction reversal** in a split-phase, capacitor-start, or CSCR motor is achieved by **reversing the connections of the auxiliary winding** while keeping the main winding connection unchanged.
- **Shaded-pole motor direction** is fixed by the position of the shading ring and cannot be easily reversed.
- **Centrifugal switch** is a speed-activated mechanical switch that disconnects the starting winding at about 70 to 80 % of $N_s$.
- **Capacitor value** for optimal phase-split is $C \approx \dfrac{I_m \sin \theta}{2 \pi f V}$ where $I_m$ is the auxiliary winding current magnitude and $\theta$ is the desired phase angle between currents.
- **Power factor** of SPIMs is generally **lower** than three-phase motors of the same rating.
- **Efficiency** of SPIMs ranges from **50 % to 70 %** in fractional kW ratings.
- **Universal motors** (series-wound AC motors) are NOT induction motors and are used where very high speed and high starting torque are required (mixers, hand tools).
- **Common exam traps:**
  - Confusing slip $s$ with $(2 - s)$.
  - Forgetting the factor of 2 in $\dfrac{R_2}{2s}$.
  - Stating the shading ring is made of iron (it is **copper**).
  - Recommending shaded-pole for compressor applications.
  - Drawing the equivalent circuit with only one rotor branch.

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** Always draw the **equivalent circuit**, **state the assumptions clearly**, and **carry the units** in numerical problems. The valuation key rewards stepwise working — never skip algebraic steps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Double Revolving Field Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Double Revolving Field Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Double Revolving Field Theory (DRFT):** An analytical concept used to resolve a **stationary, sinusoidally pulsating magnetic field** (produced by a single-phase AC winding) into **two equal-magnitude rotating magnetic fields** revolving in **opposite directions** at **synchronous speed** in the air-gap of an AC machine.

The theory was formally proposed by **Boris Lapiendo (early 1900s)** and is the cornerstone for explaining the **starting behaviour** of **single-phase induction motors** — which, unlike three-phase motors, are **not inherently self-starting**.

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Supply Frequency | $f$ | **Hz** (typically **50 Hz** in India) |
| Synchronous Speed | $N_s$ | **RPM** |
| Pole Pairs | $P$ | **integer** |
| Maximum Pulsating Flux | $\Phi_{max}$ | **Wb** |
| Electrical Angular Velocity | $\omega$ | **rad/s** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are standing at the centre of a merry-go-round, holding a powerful flashlight that you switch **ON** and **OFF** 50 times every second. A child sitting on the rim of the merry-go-round would only see a **flashing light** — never a sense of rotation.
>
> Now imagine **two identical flashlights**, both flashing at 50 Hz, but mounted on the merry-go-round — one spinning **clockwise**, the other spinning **anticlockwise** at exactly the same speed. To the child sitting at any point on the rim, the **combined brightness** of both flashlights would produce exactly the **same flashing pattern** as your single stationary flashlight!
>
> This is precisely what Double Revolving Field Theory states: **a stationary pulsating field is mathematically indistinguishable from two equal counter-rotating fields.**

**Physical interpretation in an AC machine:**
- A single-phase winding fed from a single-phase AC supply produces a flux that **rises, falls, and reverses** along a fixed axis in space — it does **not rotate**.
- This non-rotating, alternating flux can be **mathematically decomposed** into two rotating fluxes, each with **half the peak amplitude**, rotating in opposite directions at synchronous speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this theory critical for KTU?**
> Without DRFT, you cannot mathematically justify **why a single-phase induction motor fails to start on its own** and **why auxiliary winding / capacitor-start arrangements are mandatory.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Analytical Logic — Step-by-Step

### Step 1: Flux produced by a Single-Phase Winding

When a single-phase distributed winding is energised by $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$, the resulting air-gap flux density (assumed sinusoidally distributed in space) is:

$$B(\theta, t) = B_{max} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta)$$

where:
- $\theta$ = electrical space angle measured from the winding axis (in electrical radians / mechanical degrees)
- $\omega$ = angular supply frequency in rad/s
- $B_{max}$ = peak value of the alternating air-gap flux density

> **Note:** This is a **standing wave** — a **pulsating field**, not a travelling wave. At any instant, it has a fixed spatial distribution $\cos(\theta)$, but the amplitude scales with $\sin(\omega t)$.

### Step 2: Application of the Trigonometric Identity

Apply the **product-to-sum identity**:

$$\cos(A)\cos(B) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\bigr]$$

Let $A = \omega t$ and $B = \theta$. Substituting:

$$B(\theta, t) = \frac{B_{max}}{2}\cos(\omega t - \theta) + \frac{B_{max}}{2}\cos(\omega t + \theta)$$

### Step 3: Interpretation of the Two Components

| Component | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward (F)** | $\dfrac{B_{max}}{2}\cos(\omega t - \theta)$ | Rotates in **+θ direction** (counter-clockwise) |
| **Backward (B)** | $\dfrac{B_{max}}{2}\cos(\omega t + \theta)$ | Rotates in **–θ direction** (clockwise) |

- Both components have **equal amplitude** $B_{max}/2$ = **half** of the peak pulsating field.
- Both rotate at **synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ RPM.

### Step 4: Why the Motor is Not Self-Starting

At **standstill** (rotor speed $N_r = 0$):
- The forward component induces a rotor current that produces a torque $T_f$ in the forward direction.
- The backward component induces a rotor current that produces a torque $T_b$ in the backward direction.
- By symmetry, $\vert T_f \vert = \vert T_b \vert$, so the **net starting torque is zero**.

$$T_{net,\,standstill} = T_f + T_b = 0$$

### Step 5: Operation After the Rotor is Spun Manually

Suppose the rotor is given a small push in the **forward** direction, so it runs at speed $N_r > 0$.
- Slip with respect to forward field: $s_f = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ (small, forward torque dominates).
- Slip with respect to backward field: $s_b = \dfrac{N_s - (-N_r)}{N_s} = \dfrac{N_s + N_r}{N_s} = 2 - s_f$ (large, backward torque weak).

$$T_{net} = T_f(s_f) - T_b(s_b)$$

This produces a **net forward torque**, and the motor accelerates to near-synchronous speed.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it appears in nearly every KTU university question on DRFT.**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $B(\theta,t) = B_{max}\sin(\omega t)\cos(\theta)$ | Pulsating field of a single-phase winding |
| 2 | $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$ | The key trigonometric identity used |
| 3 | $B_{f} = B_{max}/2$ | Amplitude of forward rotating component |
| 4 | $B_{b} = B_{max}/2$ | Amplitude of backward rotating component |
| 5 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed in RPM |
| 6 | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P}$ rad/s | Synchronous speed in electrical rad/s |
| 7 | $s_f = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip w.r.t. forward field |
| 8 | $s_b = 2 - s_f$ | Slip w.r.t. backward field |
| 9 | $T_{net} = T_f(s_f) - T_b(s_b)$ | Net rotor torque |
| 10 | $T_{start} = 0$ | Net starting torque at standstill |

> **Units used throughout:** $f$ in Hz, $N$ in RPM, $P$ in poles, $T$ in N·m, $\Phi$ in Wb, $B$ in Tesla.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Design of single-phase induction motors** — DRFT explains *why* capacitor-start, capacitor-run, shaded-pole, and split-phase constructions are needed.
2. **Performance prediction** — Once the forward and backward torque-slip characteristics are known, the **net torque curve** of any single-phase induction motor can be plotted by simple algebraic superposition.
3. **Servo and instrumentation motors** — Two-phase servomotors rely on **asymmetry** in the double revolving field to produce a controlled unidirectional torque.
4. **Fault diagnosis** — A single phasing-out in a 3-phase motor (loss of one phase) reduces it to single-phase operation, where DRFT accurately predicts the eventual loss of starting torque.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Field Decomposition

### Step 1: Setup the expression for the air-gap flux

Consider a single-phase winding placed in 2 slots separated by 180 electrical degrees on the stator. When energised by $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$, by the **Biot–Savart law** and assuming a sinusoidal MMF distribution, the air-gap flux density at any point with angular coordinate $\theta$ and time $t$ is:

$$B(\theta, t) = B_{max} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta)$$

### Step 2: Identify the standalone variables

- The function is a **product** of two cosines (treating $\sin\omega t$ as $\cos(\omega t - \pi/2)$ for symmetry of notation).
- Re-express $\sin(\omega t) = \cos(\omega t - \pi/2)$ — this is optional; we keep it in sine form for the next step.

### Step 3: Apply the product-to-sum identity

Using:

$$\sin(\omega t)\cos(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[\sin(\omega t + \theta) + \sin(\omega t - \theta)\bigr]$$

> Note: The identity used here is $\sin A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)]$ — distinct from the $\cos A \cos B$ form used in SECTION 2 but yielding an equivalent physical decomposition.

### Step 4: Rewrite the air-gap flux

$$B(\theta, t) = \frac{B_{max}}{2}\sin(\omega t + \theta) + \frac{B_{max}}{2}\sin(\omega t - \theta)$$

### Step 5: Interpret the components

**Forward component** (rotating in the **–θ** spatial direction because the argument $\omega t - \theta = \text{const}$ requires $\theta$ to increase with $t$):

$$B_f(\theta, t) = \frac{B_{max}}{2}\sin(\omega t - \theta)$$

**Backward component** (rotating in the **+θ** spatial direction because $\omega t + \theta = \text{const}$ requires $\theta$ to decrease with $t$):

$$B_b(\theta, t) = \frac{B_{max}}{2}\sin(\omega t + \theta)$$

### Step 6: Compute the synchronous speed

For the forward field, set the argument constant:

$$\omega t - \theta = K \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega = 2\pi f \;\text{(electrical rad/s)}$$

Mechanical synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \frac{60 \cdot f}{P/2} \times \text{conversion} = \frac{120 f}{P} \quad \text{RPM}$$

### Step 7: Show the standstill torque is zero

At standstill ($N_r = 0$, so slip w.r.t. both fields = 1):
- Forward torque: $T_f(1) = K \cdot \dfrac{s_f \cdot R_2 / s_f}{R_2^2/s_f^2 + X_2^2} = K \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ (positive).
- Backward torque: $T_b(1) = K \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ (equal magnitude, opposite sign).

$$T_{start} = T_f(1) - T_b(1) = 0$$

### Step 8: Show the running torque is non-zero

Suppose the rotor runs at slip $s_f$ w.r.t. forward field. Then:

$$s_b = 2 - s_f$$

For typical running condition $s_f \approx 0.05$, $s_b \approx 1.95$. Reading from a standard induction-motor torque-slip curve, $T_b$ at $s \approx 2$ is much smaller than $T_f$ at $s \approx 0.05$:

$$T_{net} = T_f(s_f) - T_b(s_b) > 0$$

Hence the motor develops a **forward net torque** and continues to run.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A 4-pole, 50 Hz single-phase induction motor runs at 1440 RPM at full load. Compute:
> (a) Synchronous speed
> (b) Forward slip $s_f$
> (c) Backward slip $s_b$
> (d) Comment on net torque direction.

### Solution:

**(a)** Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \;\text{RPM}$$

**(b)** Forward slip:

$$s_f = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \;\;(4\%)$$

**(c)** Backward slip:

$$s_b = 2 - s_f = 2 - 0.04 = 1.96$$

**(d)** Since $s_f = 0.04$ is small (high forward torque region) and $s_b = 1.96$ is near 2 (very low backward torque region), the **net torque is positive in the forward direction**, sustaining rotation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Supply: single phase, f Hz] --> B[Stator Winding: single-phase distributed coil]
    B --> C[Pulsating MMF: Bmax sinwt along fixed axis]
    C --> D[Apply product-to-sum identity: sin A cos B = half sin A+B plus sin A-B]
    D --> E1[Forward Revolving Field Bf equals Bmax over 2, rotating CCW at Ns]
    D --> E2[Backward Revolving Field Bb equals Bmax over 2, rotating CW at Ns]
    E1 --> F1[Rotor Sees Slip sf equals Ns minus Nr over Ns]
    E2 --> F2[Rotor Sees Slip sb equals 2 minus sf]
    F1 --> G1[Forward Torque Tf at sf]
    F2 --> G2[Backward Torque Tb at sb]
    G1 --> H[Superposition: Tnet equals Tf minus Tb]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I{Standstill Condition}
    I -->|Yes, Nr equals 0| J[Tf equals Tb, Tnet equals 0, Motor Cannot Self Start]
    I -->|No, Nr greater than 0| K[At Running Slip sf near 0.04, Tb small, Tnet positive forward]
    K --> L[Motor Accelerates and Runs at Near Synchronous Speed]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Vector Diagram (Textual Description)

```
            (+Y axis)
                 |
                 |    Φ_max (peak at t = π/2ω)
                 |       ↑
        Φ_b      |       |
       (CW)     [STATOR AXIS]
       ←—•       |       •——→  Φ_f (CCW rotation)
                 |
                 |
       (–Y axis)

        • Φ_f  → travels counter-clockwise
        • Φ_b  → travels clockwise
        • Both have magnitude Φ_max / 2
        • Their vector sum at any instant = Φ_max cos(ωt) along the stator axis
```

## 4.3 Torque-Slip Characteristic (Conceptual Plot)

```
   Torque (T)
     ↑
     |          T_f  (forward torque-slip curve)
     |         /\
     |        /  \
     |       /    \
     |      /      \____________________
     |     /                       ______ T_running (net)
     |    /                ________/
     |   /         _______/
     |  /  _______/
     | / _/        <-- T_b (backward torque, mirror image)
     |/_/___________________________________→ Slip (s)
     0        0.5       1.0       1.5      2.0
              ↑                  ↑
          T_start = 0       s_b region
        (no starting torque)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The forward and backward torque-slip curves are **mirror images** of each other about the vertical line $s = 1$. Their algebraic sum (superposition) gives the **net torque-slip curve** of a single-phase induction motor, which is **zero at $s = 1$** (standstill).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
**State the Double Revolving Field Theory. Show that a single-phase winding produces a pulsating field and not a rotating field.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):**
> The Double Revolving Field Theory states that an alternating (pulsating) magnetic field produced by a single-phase winding can be resolved into **two rotating magnetic fields of equal magnitude** ($B_{max}/2$) rotating in **opposite directions** at **synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f / P$.

> **Mathematical Proof (2 marks):**
> The air-gap flux density of a single-phase winding is:
> $$B(\theta, t) = B_{max}\sin(\omega t)\cos(\theta)$$
> This is a **product of a time function** $\sin(\omega t)$ and a **space function** $\cos(\theta)$. Since the space function is fixed in space and only the magnitude varies sinusoidally with time, the resultant is a **pulsating field** along the winding axis — it does not travel around the air-gap. Hence a single-phase winding alone cannot produce a rotating magnetic field.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
**Why is a single-phase induction motor not self-starting? Justify with the help of Double Revolving Field Theory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> (1 mark) At standstill, the rotor is at rest ($N_r = 0$). The forward and backward rotating fields both have slip $s_f = s_b = 1$.
> (1 mark) The forward component produces a torque $T_f$ in one direction, while the backward component produces an equal and opposite torque $T_b$ such that $T_f = -T_b$.
> (1 mark) Hence the **net starting torque** $T_{net} = T_f + T_b = 0$, and the motor **fails to start** without auxiliary means (capacitor-start, split-phase, or shaded-pole).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Writing $B(\theta,t) = B_{max}\sin(\omega t)\sin(\theta)$ instead of $\cos(\theta)$. The space function **must** be $\cos(\theta)$ when the reference axis is taken along the winding.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to divide the amplitude by **2** after applying the trigonometric identity. Both rotating components have amplitude $B_{max}/2$, **not** $B_{max}$.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Stating slip of backward field as $\dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ instead of $\dfrac{N_s + N_r}{N_s} = 2 - s_f$.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Drawing the torque-slip curves as **identical** instead of as **mirror images** about $s = 1$.
> - **Always** mention the key identity $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$ explicitly for full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat mathematical derivation, explain the **Double Revolving Field Theory**. Show that the two rotating fields have **equal amplitude** and rotate in **opposite directions** at **synchronous speed**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A **4-pole, 50 Hz single-phase induction motor** has a rotor running at **1425 RPM**. Compute:
- (i) Synchronous speed
- (ii) Forward slip and backward slip
- (iii) Comment on the relative magnitudes of forward and backward torques and the direction of net torque.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### (a) Derivation of Double Revolving Field Theory [7 marks]

The air-gap flux density produced by a single-phase stator winding carrying current $i = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ is:

$$B(\theta, t) = B_{max}\sin(\omega t)\cos(\theta)$$

Applying the product-to-sum identity:

$$\sin(\omega t)\cos(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[\sin(\omega t + \theta) + \sin(\omega t - \theta)\bigr]$$

$$\therefore \quad B(\theta, t) = \underbrace{\frac{B_{max}}{2}\sin(\omega t - \theta)}_{\text{Forward field } B_f} + \underbrace{\frac{B_{max}}{2}\sin(\omega t + \theta)}_{\text{Backward field } B_b}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $B_f$ rotates in the **forward (+θ) direction** at angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi f / P$ electrical rad/s.
- $B_b$ rotates in the **backward (–θ) direction** at the same angular velocity.
- Both have amplitude $B_{max}/2$.

For $B_f = \text{constant}$, set $\omega t - \theta = K$, giving $d\theta/dt = \omega$.
Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \text{ RPM}$$

For a 4-pole, 50 Hz supply: $N_s = 120 \times 50 / 4 = 1500$ RPM.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A pulsating field is mathematically equivalent to two counter-rotating fields of half amplitude each at synchronous speed.

#### (b) Numerical Part [7 marks]

**(i) Synchronous speed [2 marks]:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

**(ii) Slips [3 marks]:**

$$s_f = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1425}{1500} = \frac{75}{1500} = 0.05 \quad (5\%)$$

$$s_b = 2 - s_f = 2 - 0.05 = 1.95$$

**(iii) Torque comment [2 marks]:**
- At $s_f = 0.05$, the rotor operates in the **stable running region** where forward torque $T_f$ is large (close to maximum).
- At $s_b = 1.95$, the backward torque $T_b$ is small (operating near the negative tail of the torque-slip curve).
- $T_{net} = T_f - T_b > 0$, hence the motor produces a **net forward torque** and continues to run in the forward direction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw and explain the **equivalent circuit** of a single-phase induction motor referred to the **stator**, clearly showing the forward and backward rotor branches. Derive the expression for the **net mechanical power** developed.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a sketch, explain the **forward and backward torque-slip characteristics** of a single-phase induction motor. Show that the **starting torque is zero** and the motor is **not self-starting**.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### (a) Equivalent Circuit & Mechanical Power [7 marks]

The equivalent circuit referred to the stator contains:
- Stator resistance $R_1$ and leakage reactance $X_1$.
- Magnetising reactance $jX_m$.
- **Two rotor branches** in parallel:
  - **Forward branch** with impedance $\dfrac{R_2}{2s_f} + j\dfrac{X_2}{2}$ representing the forward field.
  - **Backward branch** with impedance $\dfrac{R_2}{2(2-s_f)} + j\dfrac{X_2}{2}$ representing the backward field.

The factor of 2 appears because each field carries only half the air-gap power.

**Mechanical power developed (per phase):**

$$P_{m,\,f} = I_{2f}^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{2s_f}\left(1 - s_f\right) \quad \text{(forward)}$$

$$P_{m,\,b} = I_{2b}^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{2(2-s_f)}\left[1 - (2-s_f)\right] = I_{2b}^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{2(2-s_f)}(s_f - 1)$$

**Net mechanical power developed:**

$$P_{m,\,net} = P_{m,\,f} - P_{m,\,b} = \frac{1}{2}I_{2f}^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s_f}(1-s_f) - \frac{1}{2}I_{2b}^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{(2-s_f)}(1-s_f)$$

#### (b) Torque-Slip Curves & Non-Self-Starting Nature [7 marks]

**Sketch (textual description):**

```
   T ↑    T_f(s)
         /\
        /  \____
   T_net
        ______/    T_b(s) (mirror image)
   _____/        /
   0 ───────────●────→ s
              s = 1   s_b = 2
         ↑
      T_start = 0
```

- The forward torque-slip curve $T_f(s)$ rises from zero at $s = 0$, peaks near $s \approx 0.2$, then falls.
- The backward torque-slip curve $T_b(s)$ is the **mirror image** of $T_f$ about $s = 1$.
- The net torque curve is $T_{net}(s) = T_f(s) - T_b(s)$, which is **zero at $s = 1$** (standstill).

**Mathematical proof of zero starting torque (1 mark):**

At $s_f = s_b = 1$, by symmetry $T_f(1) = T_b(1)$, hence:

$$T_{net,\,start} = T_f(1) - T_b(1) = 0$$

Hence the **motor cannot start by itself** — it requires an auxiliary starting mechanism (capacitor, split-phase, or shaded pole).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Double Revolving Field Theory**

- [x] A **single-phase winding** produces a **pulsating (alternating) field**, **not a rotating** field.
- [x] DRFT **resolves** the pulsating field into **two counter-rotating** fields, each of **amplitude $B_{max}/2$**.
- [x] Both rotating fields revolve at **synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f / P$.
- [x] The trigonometric identity used is: $\sin A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)]$.
- [x] **Starting torque of a single-phase induction motor is zero** — this is the central consequence of DRFT.
- [x] **Slip of backward field:** $s_b = 2 - s_f$ (not $s_f$).
- [x] Forward and backward torque-slip curves are **mirror images** about $s = 1$.
- [x] At standstill ($s = 1$), $T_f = T_b$, hence $T_{net} = 0$.
- [x] Once started, the motor develops **positive net torque** because $T_f(s_f)$ dominates over $T_b(s_b)$.
- [x] Practical single-phase motors (capacitor-start, split-phase, shaded-pole) use **auxiliary windings** to create a **phase-shifted flux**, breaking the symmetry of the two counter-rotating fields.
- [x] DRFT applies to **single-phase induction motors only**; three-phase machines produce a true rotating field by spatial 120° winding displacement.
- [x] Always remember: **"$B_{max}/2$ for each rotating component"** — half amplitude is the most-tested numerical fact.
- [x] For KTU 2024 Scheme: expect 3-mark definition/numerical in **Part A** and full 14-mark derivation + numerical in **Part B**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque-Slip Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Topic:** Torque-Slip Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — TORQUE-SLIP CHARACTERISTICS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torque-Slip Characteristic** of a three-phase induction motor is the graphical (and analytical) relationship between the **electromagnetic torque developed by the rotor (T)** and the **per-unit slip (s)** of the motor, evaluated across the entire operating range from standstill (s = 1) to synchronous speed (s = 0).

The **slip (s)** is defined as the relative difference between the synchronous speed ($N_s$) of the rotating magnetic field and the actual rotor speed ($N_r$):

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

The torque-slip curve is one of the most important performance plots in AC machine analysis, as it determines the **starting behaviour**, **acceleration capability**, **steady-state operating point**, and **pull-out (breakdown) limit** of the induction motor.

### 1.2 Key Operating Speeds (Reference Values)

- **Synchronous Speed** ($N_s$): $\frac{120 f}{P}$ rpm, where **f** is supply frequency and **P** is the number of poles.
- **Full-Load Speed** ($N_{fl}$): Typically 2–5% below $N_s$.
- **No-Load Speed**: Very close to $N_s$ (slip < 0.1%).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Cyclist on a Hilly Track 🚴

Imagine a cyclist on a circular track:

- The **cyclist's pedalling force** is analogous to the **motor torque (T)**.
- The **gap between the cyclist's speed and the track's apparent "wind speed" (synchronous field)** is the **slip (s)**.
- When the cyclist is just starting, they need a **huge push (high starting torque)** — that is, slip is at its maximum (**s = 1**).
- Once the cyclist is cruising at near-track speed, only a small pedalling force is needed to overcome wind drag — this is the **low-slip running region**.
- If the track suddenly tilts upward (a sudden mechanical overload), the cyclist slows down (slip increases) and must apply more force. If the slope is too steep, the cyclist may stall (motor pulls out).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The induction motor is *self-protecting* — if mechanical load increases beyond the breakdown torque, the motor cannot accelerate further, slip increases rapidly, and the motor either stalls or draws huge current (and trips on overcurrent protection).

### 1.4 Why is this Topic Critical for KTU 2024?

- It is the **foundation for all subsequent induction motor problems** (Module 4 onward).
- Numerical questions on **maximum torque, starting torque, slip at maximum torque, and the effect of rotor resistance** are **high-frequency items** in KTU End Semester Examinations.
- It is directly mapped to **CO2 / CO3** of PCEOT402 (Analyse the performance characteristics of AC electrical machines).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Torque Equation (First Principles)

The electromagnetic torque developed by a three-phase induction motor (per-phase equivalent circuit model) is given by:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^{2} \, R_2}{R_2^{2} + (sX_2)^{2}} \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

where:
- **T** = Electromagnetic torque developed (N·m)
- **$E_2$** = Rotor induced EMF per phase at standstill (V)
- **$R_2$** = Rotor resistance per phase (Ω)
- **$X_2$** = Rotor reactance per phase at standstill (Ω)
- **s** = Per-unit slip
- **$\omega_s$** = Synchronous angular speed in rad/s = $\frac{2\pi N_s}{60}$

### 2.2 Logical Steps to Reach the Torque Equation

1. **The rotating magnetic field** (produced by the 3-phase stator winding) cuts the stationary rotor conductors at synchronous speed.
2. A **rotor EMF ($E_{2s} = sE_2$)** is induced at slip frequency $sf$.
3. Rotor current flows: $I_2 = \frac{sE_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$.
4. **Gross mechanical power** developed: $P_m = 3 I_2^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}(1-s)$.
5. Since $P_m = T \cdot \omega_r = T \cdot (1-s)\omega_s$, dividing yields the torque equation above.

## 2.3 Critical Torque Quantities (Extremum Analysis)

### (a) **Starting Torque ($T_{st}$)** — at $s = 1$

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + X_2^{2}}$$

### (b) **Slip for Maximum Torque ($s_{mT}$)** — derived by setting $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$$

### (c) **Maximum (Pull-Out / Breakdown) Torque ($T_{max}$)** — independent of $R_2$!

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2}$$

### (d) **Condition for Maximum Torque at Starting (i.e., $s_{mT} = 1$)**

$$R_2 = X_2$$

> [!TIP]
> **This is the principle behind slip-ring (wound rotor) induction motors** — external rotor resistance is added so that $R_2 = X_2$, making $T_{st} = T_{max}$. The motor then starts with **maximum possible torque**!

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Slip $s$ | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | $0 \le s \le 1$ for motoring |
| 2 | Synchronous speed $N_s$ | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | In rpm |
| 3 | Angular sync. speed $\omega_s$ | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | In rad/s |
| 4 | Torque $T$ | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + (s X_2)^{2}}$ | General expression |
| 5 | Starting torque $T_{st}$ | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + X_2^{2}}$ | At $s = 1$ |
| 6 | Slip for max. torque $s_{mT}$ | $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | From $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$ |
| 7 | Maximum torque $T_{max}$ | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2}$ | **Independent of $R_2$** |
| 8 | Condition $T_{st} = T_{max}$ | $R_2 = X_2$ | Used in slip-ring motors |
| 9 | Torque ratio $\dfrac{T}{T_{max}}$ | $\dfrac{T}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{s}{s_{mT}} + \dfrac{s_{mT}}{s}}$ | Universal torque ratio |
| 10 | Starting-to-max ratio $\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{max}}$ | $\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2 \, s_{mT}}{1 + s_{mT}^{2}}$ | Useful for design checks |
| 11 | Full-load slip (typical) | $s_{fl} \approx 0.02$ to $0.05$ | For standard cage motors |
| 12 | Mechanical power $P_m$ | $P_m = 3 I_2^{2} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{s}(1-s)$ | Gross developed power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These equations are used daily in industries for:
> - **Motor selection** (matching starting torque to load).
> - **Soft-starter / VFD design** (controlling starting current and torque).
> - **Hoist and elevator applications** (requiring high $T_{st}$).
> - **Slip-ring motor control** (varying external rotor resistance to alter $s_{mT}$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Full Torque Equation of a Three-Phase Induction Motor

We will derive this from the **per-phase equivalent circuit** of the induction motor referred to the stator.

### Step 1 — Define the equivalent circuit rotor branch

The rotor branch (referred to stator) has:
- Resistance: $\dfrac{R_2}{s}$
- Reactance: $X_2$ (constant, evaluated at standstill frequency)

The total rotor impedance is:

$$Z_2 = \frac{R_2}{s} + jX_2$$

### Step 2 — Rotor current per phase

$$I_2 = \frac{E_2}{Z_2} = \frac{E_2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}}}$$

The magnitude is:

$$\vert I_2 \vert = \frac{E_2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}}}$$

### Step 3 — Power input to the rotor (air-gap power) per phase

$$P_{g,1\phi} = I_2^{2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

Total air-gap power for 3 phases:

$$P_g = 3 \, I_2^{2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

### Step 4 — Gross mechanical power developed

The fraction $(1-s)$ of the air-gap power is converted to mechanical form:

$$P_m = (1 - s) \, P_g = 3 \, I_2^{2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} \cdot (1 - s)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $I_2$ expression

$$P_m = 3 \cdot \frac{E_2^{2}}{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} \cdot (1 - s)$$

### Step 6 — Express torque as $P_m / \omega_r$

Since the actual rotor angular speed is $\omega_r = (1 - s) \omega_s$:

$$T = \frac{P_m}{\omega_r} = \frac{P_m}{(1 - s) \, \omega_s}$$

The $(1-s)$ terms cancel:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^{2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}}{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}}$$

### Step 7 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $s^{2}$ to simplify

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^{2} \, R_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}}$$

> ✅ **This is the canonical KTU-board torque equation.**

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Condition for Maximum Torque

Starting from the torque equation:

$$T = \frac{3 E_2^{2}}{\omega_s} \cdot \underbrace{\left[\frac{s \, R_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}}\right]}_{f(s)}$$

Since $\dfrac{3 E_2^{2}}{\omega_s}$ is a positive constant (independent of $s$), the maximum of $T$ coincides with the maximum of $f(s)$.

### Step 1 — Differentiate $f(s)$ with respect to $s$ and set to zero

Let $f(s) = \dfrac{s R_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}}$. Applying the quotient rule:

$$\frac{df}{ds} = \frac{R_2 \left( R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2} \right) - s R_2 \left( 2 s X_2^{2} \right)}{\left( R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2} \right)^{2}}$$

### Step 2 — Numerator simplification

$$\text{Numerator} = R_2 \left[ R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2} - 2 s^{2} X_2^{2} \right] = R_2 \left[ R_2^{2} - s^{2} X_2^{2} \right]$$

### Step 3 — Equate numerator to zero

$$R_2^{2} - s^{2} X_2^{2} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s^{2} X_2^{2} = R_2^{2}$$

$$\boxed{s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}}$$

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Maximum Torque Value

Substitute $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ back into the torque equation:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{\left(\frac{R_2}{X_2}\right) E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + \left(\frac{R_2}{X_2}\right)^{2} X_2^{2}}$$

### Step 1 — Simplify the denominator

$$R_2^{2} + \left(\frac{R_2}{X_2}\right)^{2} X_2^{2} = R_2^{2} + R_2^{2} = 2 R_2^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Simplify the numerator

$$\left(\frac{R_2}{X_2}\right) \cdot E_2^{2} \cdot R_2 = \frac{R_2^{2} E_2^{2}}{X_2}$$

### Step 3 — Combine

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{R_2^{2} E_2^{2}}{X_2}}{2 R_2^{2}} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^{2}}{2 X_2}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $R_2$ terms cancel out** — proving that **maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance**, although the slip at which it occurs is directly proportional to $R_2$.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Universal Torque Ratio

We derive the expression $\dfrac{T}{T_{max}}$ which is useful when the slip is varied:

$$\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{ \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}} }{ \dfrac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2} }$$

### Step 1 — Cancel common factors $\dfrac{3 E_2^{2}}{\omega_s}$

$$\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{ \dfrac{2 s R_2 X_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}} }{ 1 } = \frac{2 s R_2 X_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}}$$

### Step 2 — Divide numerator and denominator by $R_2 X_2$ and introduce $s_{mT} = R_2/X_2$

Divide top and bottom by $R_2 X_2$:

$$\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{2 s}{\dfrac{R_2}{X_2} + \dfrac{s^{2} X_2}{R_2}} = \frac{2 s}{s_{mT} + \dfrac{s^{2}}{s_{mT}}}$$

### Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $s_{mT}$

$$\boxed{\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{2}{\dfrac{s}{s_{mT}} + \dfrac{s_{mT}}{s}}}$$

> **Engineering use:** This is the *master equation* used in numerical problems to compute torque at *any* slip, given only $T_{max}$ and $s_{mT}$.

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 5 — Ratio of Starting Torque to Maximum Torque

At $s = 1$:

$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \frac{2}{\dfrac{1}{s_{mT}} + \dfrac{s_{mT}}{1}} = \frac{2 \, s_{mT}}{1 + s_{mT}^{2}}$$

This ratio is always $\le 1$, with equality only when $s_{mT} = 1$ (i.e., $R_2 = X_2$).

---

## 3.6 Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor has rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.05\;\Omega$ and standstill reactance $X_2 = 0.5\;\Omega$ per phase. At a slip of 0.04, the rotor EMF is 100 V per phase. Compute the developed torque.

### Step 1 — Synchronous speed

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 2 — Plug into torque equation with $s = 0.04$

$$T = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{0.04 \times 100^{2} \times 0.05}{0.05^{2} + (0.04 \times 0.5)^{2}}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate denominator

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.0025 + (0.02)^{2} = 0.0025 + 0.0004 = 0.0029\;\Omega^{2}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate numerator

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.04 \times 10000 \times 0.05 = 20$$

### Step 5 — Final calculation

$$T = \frac{3 \times 20}{157.08 \times 0.0029} = \frac{60}{0.4555} \approx 131.7 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 131.7 \text{ N·m}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Logic Tree of Torque Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stator 3-phase Supply] --> B[Rotating Magnetic Field at Ns]
    B --> C[Rotor EMF E2s = sE2 induced]
    C --> D[Rotor Current I2 computed from Z2 = R2/s + jX2]
    D --> E[Air-Gap Power Pg = 3 I2² R2/s]
    E --> F[Gross Mechanical Power Pm = 1-s times Pg]
    F --> G[Torque T = Pm divided by omega r = 1-s times omega s]
    G --> H[Final Equation T = 3 by omega s times s E2² R2 over R2² + sX2²]
    H --> I{Analyse Extremum}
    I --> J[dT by ds = 0]
    J --> K[Slip at Max Torque smT = R2 by X2]
    J --> L[Max Torque Tmax = 3 E2² by 2 omega s X2]
    H --> M[At s = 1]
    M --> N[Starting Torque Tst = 3 E2² R2 by omega s R2² + X2²]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Operating Regions of Torque-Slip Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REGION_LOW_SLIP[s near 0 — Normal Running Region]
        RS1[Slip small, s less than 0.05] --> RT1[Stable operation] --> RP1[Torque approximately proportional to s]
    end

    subgraph REGION_MID[s around smT — Pull-out Region]
        RM1[Slip equals R2 by X2] --> RMT[Maximum Torque Tmax occurs] --> RCOM[Beyond Tmax, motor cannot sustain load]
    end

    subgraph REGION_HIGH[s near 1 — Starting Region]
        RH1[Slip equals 1, rotor at standstill] --> RST[Starting Torque Tst] --> RNOTE[Stable only if Tst greater than load torque at start]
    end

    REGION_LOW_SLIP --> REGION_MID
    REGION_MID --> REGION_HIGH
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Effect of Rotor Resistance on Torque-Slip Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RES[Increase Rotor Resistance R2] --> EFF1[SmT = R2 by X2 increases, curve shifts right]
    RES --> EFF2[Tmax unchanged because Tmax depends only on E2 and X2]
    RES --> EFF3[Tst increases until R2 equals X2, then decreases]
    RES --> APP[Application: Slip-ring motors use external R2 to start heavy loads]

    subgraph CASE_A[R2 small — Cage Motor]
        CA1[SmT less than 1] --> CA2[Low starting torque] --> CA3[High starting current]
    end

    subgraph CASE_B[R2 equal X2 — Ideal Start]
        CB1[SmT equals 1] --> CB2[Tst equals Tmax] --> CB3[Best starting performance]
    end

    subgraph CASE_C[R2 very large]
        CC1[SmT much greater than 1] --> CC2[Tst falls below Tmax] --> CC3[Poor starting, useful only for low-load soft start]
    end

    CASE_A --> CASE_B
    CASE_B --> CASE_C
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Process Flow — Numerical Problem Solving Pattern (KTU Exam Trick)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Read problem: Given R2, X2, E2, f, P, s] --> P2[Compute Ns = 120f by P]
    P2 --> P3[Compute omega s = 2 pi Ns by 60]
    P3 --> P4{What is asked?}
    P4 -- Torque at slip s --> Q1[Use general T equation]
    P4 -- Max torque --> Q2[Compute Tmax = 3 E2² by 2 omega s X2]
    P4 -- Slip for max torque --> Q3[Compute smT = R2 by X2]
    P4 -- Starting torque --> Q4[Put s = 1 in T equation]
    Q1 --> OUT[Show full step-by-step substitution]
    Q2 --> OUT
    Q3 --> OUT
    Q4 --> OUT
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this flowchart for KTU exams** — examiners expect students to write the formula, substitute values in the *same order* as the formula, and then simplify step-by-step. Skipping the substitution step is the most common reason for mark deduction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the term "slip" in a three-phase induction motor. Why is it impossible for an induction motor to run at synchronous speed?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Slip (s)** is defined as the difference between the synchronous speed ($N_s$) of the rotating magnetic field and the actual rotor speed ($N_r$), expressed as a fraction (or per-unit) of $N_s$:
>
> $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$
>
> The induction motor **cannot run at synchronous speed** because rotor torque is produced *only* due to the **relative motion** between the rotating field and the rotor conductors. If $N_r = N_s$ (i.e., $s = 0$), the rotor conductors would be **stationary with respect to the field**, no EMF would be induced, no rotor current would flow, and hence **no torque** would be developed. The motor would decelerate due to load torque and slip would re-establish. (3 Marks)

---

### **Q2. State the condition for maximum torque in a 3-phase induction motor. What is the value of slip at maximum torque?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> The condition for maximum torque is obtained by setting $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$ in the torque-slip equation, which yields:
>
> $$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$$
>
> At this slip, the rotor circuit impedance angle equals $45°$, i.e., $\tan\phi = \dfrac{s_{mT} X_2}{R_2} = 1$.
>
> The corresponding maximum torque is:
>
> $$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2}$$
>
> Notably, **$T_{max}$ is independent of rotor resistance $R_2$**, although the slip at which it occurs is directly proportional to $R_2$. (3 Marks)

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 End Semester Examination pattern, Part B questions of Module 4 offer internal choice. Below are **Question A** and its alternative **Question B**. Attempt ANY ONE.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the electromagnetic torque developed by a three-phase induction motor. Hence, deduce the condition for maximum torque and the value of maximum torque.
> **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor has rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.3\;\Omega$ and standstill rotor reactance $X_2 = 1.5\;\Omega$ per phase. The rotor induced EMF at standstill is 110 V per phase. Compute:
> - (i) Starting torque
> - (ii) Slip at maximum torque
> - (iii) Maximum torque
> - (iv) Full-load torque at slip $s = 0.04$
> **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Per-phase rotor branch impedance:**

$$Z_2 = \frac{R_2}{s} + jX_2$$

**Step 2 — Rotor current magnitude:**

$$\vert I_2 \vert = \frac{E_2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}}}$$

**Step 3 — Air-gap power (3-phase):**

$$P_g = 3 \, I_2^{2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} = \frac{3 E_2^{2} \cdot R_2 / s}{\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical power developed:**

$$P_m = (1 - s) P_g$$

**Step 5 — Torque:**

$$T = \frac{P_m}{(1 - s) \omega_s} = \frac{3 E_2^{2} \cdot R_2 / s}{\omega_s \left[\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^{2} + X_2^{2}\right]}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s^{2}$:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum torque condition:**

Differentiating $T$ w.r.t. $s$ and equating to zero gives:

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum torque value:**

Substituting $s_{mT}$ in equation and simplifying:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** $T_{max}$ is independent of $R_2$. Higher $R_2$ shifts $s_{mT}$ to the right but does not change the peak torque value.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 6$, $R_2 = 0.3\;\Omega$, $X_2 = 1.5\;\Omega$, $E_2 = 110$ V.

**Preliminary Calculations:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = \frac{100\pi}{3} \approx 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(i) Starting Torque ($s = 1$):**

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{1 \times 110^{2} \times 0.3}{0.3^{2} + 1.5^{2}}$$

Denominator: $0.09 + 2.25 = 2.34$

Numerator: $1 \times 12100 \times 0.3 = 3630$

$$T_{st} = \frac{3 \times 3630}{104.72 \times 2.34} = \frac{10890}{245.05} \approx 44.44 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{st} \approx 44.4 \text{ N·m}} \quad \text{(1.5 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Slip at Maximum Torque:**

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.3}{1.5} = 0.2 \; (20\%)$$

$$\boxed{s_{mT} = 0.2} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**(iii) Maximum Torque:**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 \times 110^{2}}{2 \times 104.72 \times 1.5} = \frac{36300}{314.16} \approx 115.55 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} \approx 115.5 \text{ N·m}} \quad \text{(1.5 Marks)}$$

**(iv) Full-Load Torque ($s = 0.04$):**

$$T_{fl} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{0.04 \times 12100 \times 0.3}{0.3^{2} + (0.04 \times 1.5)^{2}}$$

Denominator: $0.09 + 0.0036 = 0.0936$

Numerator: $0.04 \times 12100 \times 0.3 = 145.2$

$$T_{fl} = \frac{3 \times 145.2}{104.72 \times 0.0936} = \frac{435.6}{9.802} \approx 44.44 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{fl} \approx 44.4 \text{ N·m}} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

> **Observation (0.5 Marks):** In this motor, starting torque equals full-load torque (both ~44.4 N·m). The motor is suitable for light starting loads but **not** for heavy starting loads such as hoists.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **torque-slip characteristics** of a three-phase induction motor. Identify and justify the **stable** and **unstable** operating regions.
> **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A 3-phase induction motor has rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.04\;\Omega$ and standstill reactance $X_2 = 0.4\;\Omega$ per phase. The slip at maximum torque is **0.1**. A new rotor design requires the starting torque to be **equal to the maximum torque**. Determine:
> - (i) The new rotor resistance required.
> - (ii) The new slip at maximum torque.
> - (iii) Comment on the effect of this change on the full-load speed of the motor.
> **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 Marks]

**Sketch of Torque-Slip Curve (Descriptive):**

A typical torque-slip curve is shown below. The x-axis represents slip $s$ (0 to 1.2) and the y-axis represents torque $T$. The curve rises from a value $T_{st}$ at $s = 1$, reaches a peak $T_{max}$ at $s = s_{mT}$, and then falls back towards zero as $s \to 0$.

```
T │            ★ Tmax
  │           /│\
  │          / │ \
  │   Tst   /  │  \
  │────────/   │   \─────────── (stable)
  │       /    │    \
  │      / UNSTABLE\   \  STABLE
  │     /     │     \     \
  │____/______│______\_____\____ s
  0    smT    │      1.0
              │
```

**Key Features of the Curve (1.5 Marks each):**

1. **At $s = 0$ (Synchronous speed):** Torque $T = 0$ because there is no relative motion.
2. **At $s = s_{mT}$ (Maximum torque point):** $T = T_{max}$ — this is the *breakdown torque*. Beyond this point, the motor cannot sustain a higher load.
3. **At $s = 1$ (Standstill):** $T = T_{st}$ — the starting torque.
4. **Normal operating region:** $0 < s < s_{mT}$ — the **stable region**.
5. **Pull-out region:** $s_{mT} < s < 1$ — the **unstable region** (in practice, motor won't settle here).

**Stable vs Unstable Region Justification (3.5 Marks):**

- In the **stable region** ($0 < s < s_{mT}$), the slope $\dfrac{dT}{ds} > 0$. This means: if load suddenly increases, the motor slows down (slip increases) and the motor torque *also increases* to match — equilibrium is restored.
- In the **unstable region** ($s_{mT} < s < 1$), the slope $\dfrac{dT}{ds} < 0$. If load increases, slip increases, but motor torque *decreases* further — the motor decelerates rapidly and **stalls**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $R_2 = 0.04\;\Omega$, $X_2 = 0.4\;\Omega$, $s_{mT} = 0.1$ (original).

**Step 1 — Verify original $s_{mT}$:**

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.04}{0.4} = 0.1 \;\; \checkmark$$

**(i) New rotor resistance for $T_{st} = T_{max}$:**

For $T_{st} = T_{max}$, we need $s_{mT} = 1$, which gives:

$$R_2' = X_2 = 0.4\;\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_2' = 0.4\;\Omega} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**(ii) New slip at maximum torque:**

$$\boxed{s_{mT}' = \frac{R_2'}{X_2} = \frac{0.4}{0.4} = 1.0} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**(iii) Effect on Full-Load Speed (3 Marks):**

- The maximum torque value is **unchanged** (since $T_{max}$ is independent of $R_2$).
- The new slip at maximum torque is now at $s = 1$ (standstill). This means in the new design, the motor can deliver its **peak torque right from starting**, which is ideal for **high-inertia, heavy-starting loads** (crushers, conveyors, hoists).
- However, the **full-load speed drops** because, for the same load torque, the operating slip $s_{fl}$ is now **larger**. A typical full-load slip might increase from $\sim 4\%$ to $\sim 6$–$8\%$, reducing the full-load speed.
- This is the trade-off — **better starting performance** but **poorer speed regulation** under load.

> This is precisely why **slip-ring induction motors with external rotor resistance controllers** are used in industrial applications requiring both high starting torque and reasonable running efficiency.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong use of $\omega_s$ vs $N_s$:** Students frequently substitute $N_s$ (in rpm) directly into the formula instead of converting to rad/s using $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60}$. This yields wrong units (N·m vs N·m/min). **Always convert to rad/s.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 3:** For a three-phase motor, the total torque is **three times** the per-phase value. Omitting the factor of 3 is a **2-mark deduction** trap.
>
> 3. **Confusing rotor EMF with supply voltage:** The induced rotor EMF $E_2$ is **not** equal to the stator supply voltage $V$. Use the transformer relation $\dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ (or stator/rotor turns ratio) if the problem gives $V$ and not $E_2$.
>
> 4. **Sign error in $s_{mT}$ derivation:** The slip at maximum torque is always **positive** for motoring mode. Taking $s_{mT} = -\dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ is a critical sign error — check direction of motion.
>
> 5. **Showing final answer without intermediate steps:** KTU examiners award marks for **substitution and simplification steps**, not just the final numerical value. A 7-mark problem with only the answer gets at most **2 marks**.
>
> 6. **Mixing up starting torque formula with maximum torque formula:** $T_{st} \ne T_{max}$ in general. $T_{st} = T_{max}$ only when $R_2 = X_2$.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Slip** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$; always between **0 and 1** for motoring.
- ✅ **Synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ rpm.
- ✅ **Torque equation:** $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + s^{2} X_2^{2}}$ — this is the *single most important* formula of Module 4.
- ✅ **Slip at maximum torque:** $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ — directly proportional to rotor resistance.
- ✅ **Maximum torque:** $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 E_2^{2}}{2 \omega_s X_2}$ — **independent of $R_2$**.
- ✅ **Starting torque:** $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^{2} R_2}{R_2^{2} + X_2^{2}}$ — evaluated at $s = 1$.
- ✅ **Condition for $T_{st} = T_{max}$:** $R_2 = X_2$ — used in **slip-ring (wound rotor) motors**.
- ✅ **Stable operating region:** $0 < s < s_{mT}$ (positive slope of T-s curve).
- ✅ **Unstable operating region:** $s_{mT} < s < 1$ (negative slope — motor will stall).
- ✅ **Effect of increasing rotor resistance:** $s_{mT}$ shifts right, $T_{max}$ unchanged, $T_{st}$ rises initially then falls, speed regulation worsens.
- ✅ **Universal torque ratio:** $\dfrac{T}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{s}{s_{mT}} + \dfrac{s_{mT}}{s}}$ — use this when only $T_{max}$ and $s_{mT}$ are given.
- ✅ **Always convert $N_s$ to $\omega_s$** in rad/s before substituting in torque formula.
- ✅ **Three-phase factor of 3** must always appear in the numerator for total torque.
- ✅ **Power input to rotor** = air-gap power $P_g = \dfrac{3 I_2^{2} R_2}{s}$.
- ✅ **Mechanical power developed** $P_m = (1 - s) P_g$.
- ✅ **Rotor copper loss** $= s \cdot P_g$ — the **fundamental energy loss mechanism** in induction motors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types

# TYPES OF TRANSFORMERS — A Comprehensive KTU 2024 Engineering Treatise

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transformer (as per KTU 2024 PCEOT402 Syllabus):** A *static electrical machine* that transfers electrical energy from one alternating-current circuit to one or more other circuits, operating on the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction** between two or more magnetically coupled windings, without any change in frequency. The transformation is achieved through a common magnetic core that links the primary and secondary windings.

A transformer, by its design and application, is classified into **several types** based on:
1. **Construction (Core geometry)** → Core-type, Shell-type, Berry-type
2. **Number of phases** → Single-phase, Three-phase
3. **Application / Power rating** → Power, Distribution, Instrument transformers
4. **Winding configuration** → Two-winding, Autotransformers
5. **Cooling method** → Oil-immersed, Dry-type, Air-cooled
6. **Three-phase connection** → Star-Star, Star-Delta, Delta-Delta, Delta-Star, Open-Delta, Scott-T (T-T), V-V

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Pressure Tap":**
> Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline (Primary) carrying water at, say, 11 kV equivalent. You want to safely use it at home (230 V). A transformer acts like a **pressure-reduction tap**: it does NOT pump water; it only changes pressure (voltage) by clever magnetic coupling. The water (power) still flows, but at a safer pressure. The "tap mechanism" is the **magnetic core**, while the two pressure gauges are the **primary and secondary windings**.

The various *types* of transformers are simply **different mechanical and electrical designs** of this "tap mechanism," each suited for a different job — from tiny mobile-phone chargers (**dry-type, low-frequency**) to massive 500 MVA grid transformers (**oil-immersed, shell-type**).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Frequency of operation (India):** **50 Hz**
- **Standard residential voltage (India):** **230 V** (single-phase), **415 V** (three-phase)
- **Standard transmission voltages:** **11 kV, 22 kV, 66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV**
- **Permeability of silicon steel core (CRGO):** μᵣ ≈ **10,000 to 30,000**
- **Maximum flux density (Bₘ):** **1.1 T to 1.4 T** for CRGO steel

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Logic — The "Why" and "How"

#### A. **Construction-Based Types**

**1. Core-Type Transformer**
- **How it works:** Windings surround a significant portion of the laminated core.
- **Structure:** The core is rectangular with two limbs; half of the low-voltage (LV) and high-voltage (HV) windings are placed concentrically on each limb.
- **Why this design:** Provides **natural cooling paths**, simpler construction, easier maintenance, and lower cost. Ideal for **high-voltage** applications.
- **Used in:** Distribution transformers, power transformers up to medium ratings.

**2. Shell-Type Transformer**
- **How it works:** Core surrounds a major portion of the windings.
- **Structure:** The core has three limbs; the central limb carries the entire winding, and the outer limbs provide the return flux path.
- **Why this design:** **Shorter magnetic path length**, lower leakage reactance, better mechanical bracing for short-circuit forces, superior for **low-voltage, high-current** applications.
- **Used in:** Small distribution transformers, welding transformers.

**3. Berry-Type Transformer (Distribution)**
- **How it works:** A unique **distributed shell-type** design where the core is divided into multiple sections (berries) interleaved with windings.
- **Why this design:** Provides **better cooling**, lower magnetizing current, and reduced leakage reactance for **distribution-class transformers**.

#### B. **Phase-Based Types**

**1. Single-Phase Transformer** — Operates on single-phase AC supply. Used in residential and light commercial applications.

**2. Three-Phase Transformer** — Either:
- **Three separate single-phase units** (bank of three), OR
- **A single 3-phase unit** with three sets of windings on a common core (compact, efficient).

#### C. **Application-Based Types**

| Type | Purpose | Standard VA Rating |
|------|---------|---------------------|
| **Power Transformer** | Step-up at generating station / Step-down at grid substations | **> 200 kVA**, typically 5–500 MVA |
| **Distribution Transformer** | Final step-down to consumer voltage | **≤ 200 kVA**, typically 11 kV/415 V |
| **Current Transformer (CT)** | Steps down high current for measurement | Burden in VA |
| **Potential Transformer (PT/VT)** | Steps down high voltage for measurement | Burden in VA |

#### D. **Autotransformer**
- **How it works:** A single continuous winding acts as both primary and secondary, with a **common portion** shared.
- **Key advantage:** Smaller size, lower cost, higher efficiency for small transformation ratios (close to 1:1).
- **Disadvantage:** **No isolation** between primary and secondary — a critical safety concern.

#### E. **Cooling-Based Types (As per IS 1180 & IEC 60076)**

| Cooling Code | Description |
|--------------|-------------|
| **ONAN** | Oil Natural, Air Natural |
| **ONAF** | Oil Natural, Air Forced |
| **OFAF** | Oil Forced, Air Forced |
| **OFWF** | Oil Forced, Water Forced |
| **AN** | Dry-type, Air Natural |
| **AF** | Dry-type, Air Forced |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$ | EMF per turn | V |
| 2 | $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{I_2}{I_1} = a$ | Transformation ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\Phi_m = B_m \times A_i$ | Flux per limb | Wb |
| 4 | $A_i = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times V_1}{4.44 \, f \, B_m \, N_1 \, k}$ | KTU-std EMF form (3-φ) | m² |
| 5 | $kVA = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L}{1000}$ | 3-φ rating | kVA |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi + P_i + P_{cu}}$ | Efficiency | % |
| 7 | $P_{cu} = I_1^2 R_{01}$ | Copper loss (referred) | W |
| 8 | $\text{Condition for max }\eta:\; P_{cu} = P_i$ | Max efficiency | — |
| 9 | $V_{sc} = I_{fl} \times Z_{01}$ | Short-circuit voltage | V |
| 10 | $\%Z = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | Percentage impedance | % |
| 11 | $\Delta V = I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | Voltage regulation | V |
| 12 | $\%VR = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | Voltage regulation | % |
| 13 | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{I_{fl}}{\%Z/100}$ | Short-circuit current | A |
| 14 | $N_t = \dfrac{V}{4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m}$ | Turns per phase | turns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid — "FNBV":** **F**lux × **N**umber of turns × **B**max × **A**rea = Voltage. Master this and EMF equation derivations become trivial.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Power Transformers** form the backbone of the **national grid (PGCIL, Kerala State Electricity Board — KSEB)**, enabling 400 kV/220 kV/110 kV transmission.
- **Distribution Transformers** (11 kV/415 V) are mounted on **KSEB poles** throughout Kerala.
- **CTs and PTs** feed protective relays and energy meters, enabling **smart grid metering**.
- **Autotransformers** are used as **booster transformers** in rural feeders and as **induction motor starters** (e.g., in factories in Kochi).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — EMF Equation (Foundation for All Transformer Calculations)

**Statement:** To derive the RMS value of EMF induced in a transformer winding having $N$ turns, operating at supply frequency $f$ Hz, with maximum core flux $\Phi_m$ Wb.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } \phi(t) &= \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{[Flux varies sinusoidally]} \\[6pt]
\text{EMF per turn, } e_t &= -N \cdot \dfrac{d\phi}{dt} \quad \text{[Faraday's Law]} \\[6pt]
e_t &= -N \cdot \dfrac{d}{dt}\bigl[\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)\bigr] \\[6pt]
e_t &= -N \cdot \Phi_m \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t) \\[6pt]
e_t,\text{ max} &= N \cdot \Phi_m \cdot \omega \\[6pt]
\text{Mean value: } E_{\text{mean}} &= \dfrac{2}{\pi} \cdot e_{t,\text{max}} \quad \text{[Form factor for sine wave]} \\[6pt]
\text{RMS value: } E_{rms} &= 1.11 \cdot E_{\text{mean}} \\[6pt]
E_{rms} &= 1.11 \cdot \dfrac{2}{\pi} \cdot N \cdot \Phi_m \cdot \omega \\[6pt]
\text{Substituting } \omega &= 2\pi f: \\[6pt]
E_{rms} &= 1.11 \cdot \dfrac{2}{\pi} \cdot N \cdot \Phi_m \cdot 2\pi f \\[6pt]
E_{rms} &= 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This is the **single most important equation** for a transformer. Every KTU problem — from "find number of turns" to "find cross-sectional area of core" — starts here.

### 3.2 Derivation — Transformation Ratio & Voltage/Current Relationship

**For an ideal transformer** (no losses, zero leakage flux):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} &= \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = a \quad \text{[Voltage ratio]} \\[6pt]
\text{By energy conservation: } V_1 I_1 \cos\phi_1 &= V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2 \\[6pt]
\text{For ideal: } \phi_1 &= \phi_2 = 0 \text{ and } \cos\phi = 1 \\[6pt]
\therefore V_1 I_1 &= V_2 I_2 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{I_1}{I_2} &= \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{1}{a} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Core-Type vs Shell-Type

| Parameter | **Core-Type** | **Shell-Type** |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| Magnetic path length | **Longer** (2 limbs in series) | **Shorter** (3 limbs in parallel) |
| Leakage reactance | Higher | Lower |
| Cooling | Better (more surface area) | Poorer (windings buried) |
| Mechanical strength | Lower | Higher (sandwiched core) |
| Best suited for | **HV applications** | **LV, high-current** applications |
| Number of limbs carrying windings | 2 | 1 (central) |
| Typical application | Distribution transformers, large power units | Welding transformers, low-voltage heavy-current units |

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Autotransformer vs Two-Winding Transformer

| Parameter | **Two-Winding** | **Autotransformer** |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Electrical isolation** | Yes | **No** |
| **Size & cost** | Larger, costlier | Smaller, cheaper (for small ratios) |
| **Power transferred conductively** | None | $(1 - 1/a) \times S$ |
| **Power transferred inductively** | All | $S/a$ |
| **Efficiency** | High | **Higher** (less copper) |
| **Used as booster** | No | **Yes** (most common) |
| **Safety** | Safer | **Less safe** (no isolation) |

### 3.5 Three-Phase Transformer Connection Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** "Compare Star-Star and Delta-Delta connections of three-phase transformers."

| Connection | Advantages | Disadvantages | Application |
|------------|------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Y-Y (Star-Star)** | Neutral available, less insulation | Third-harmonic issues, neutrals shift | Small distribution, autotransformers |
| **Δ-Δ (Delta-Delta)** | No phase shift, no 3rd-harmonic issue | No neutral, higher insulation cost | Large industrial loads |
| **Y-Δ (Star-Delta)** | Neutral on primary for grounding, secondary delta handles unbalance | Phase shift of **30°**, no neutral on secondary | **Step-down at sub-stations** |
| **Δ-Y (Delta-Star)** | Neutral on secondary for single-phase loads, phase shift 30° | Phase shift present | **Generating station step-up** |
| **V-V (Open Delta)** | Continuity even if one transformer fails (capacity drops to **58%**) | Capacity reduces to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Emergency operation |
| **Scott-T (T-T)** | Converts 3-φ to 2-φ | Special design required | **Traction supply, electric locomotives** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Core-Type vs Shell-Type Transformer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CT["CORE-TYPE TRANSFORMER"]
        A1["HV Winding - Upper Half"] --> C1["Laminated Core Limb L1"]
        A2["LV Winding - Upper Half"] --> C1
        B1["HV Winding - Lower Half"] --> C2["Laminated Core Limb L2"]
        B2["LV Winding - Lower Half"] --> C2
        C1 --- Y1["Yoke - Top"]
        C2 --- Y1
        C1 --- Y2["Yoke - Bottom"]
        C2 --- Y2
    end

    subgraph ST["SHELL-TYPE TRANSFORMER"]
        D1["Complete HV Winding"] --> E1["Central Core Limb"]
        D2["Complete LV Winding"] --> E1
        E1 --- F1["Outer Core Path - Left"]
        E1 --- F2["Outer Core Path - Right"]
        F1 --- G1["Top Yoke Return"]
        F2 --- G1
    end
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Transformer Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["AC INPUT SUPPLY"] --> B{TRANSFORMER TYPE}
    B -->|Single Phase| C["SINGLE-PHASE TYPES"]
    B -->|Three Phase| D["THREE-PHASE TYPES"]

    C --> C1["Core Type - LV"]
    C --> C2["Shell Type - LV"]
    C --> C3["Berry Type - Distribution"]
    C --> C4["Autotransformer - Booster"]

    D --> D1["Y-Y - Small Dist"]
    D --> D2["Delta-Delta - Industrial"]
    D --> D3["Star-Delta - Substation Step-Down"]
    D --> D4["Delta-Star - Generator Step-Up"]
```
