```mermaid
    D --> D5["Open Delta V-V - Emergency"]
    D --> D6["Scott T-T - Traction"]

    C1 --> OUT["AC OUTPUT - RATED VOLTAGE"]
    C2 --> OUT
    C3 --> OUT
    C4 --> OUT
    D1 --> OUT
    D2 --> OUT
    D3 --> OUT
    D4 --> OUT
    D5 --> OUT
    D6 --> OUT

    OUT --> APP["APPLICATIONS"]
    APP --> APP1["Residential"]
    APP --> APP2["Industrial"]
    APP --> APP3["Grid Substation"]
    APP --> APP4["Traction"]
    APP --> APP5["Renewable Plant"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Autotransformer Winding Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P["Primary Terminal P1"] --> W["Single Continuous Winding N1 turns"]
    W --> C["Common Terminal"]
    C --> W2["Tapped Section - N2 turns"]
    W2 --> S["Secondary Terminal S1"]
    S -.->|Load| L["Connected Load ZL"]
    L -.->|Return| C
    P -->|Input V1| W
    note1["Inductive Transfer = S/a"] -.-> W
    note2["Conductive Transfer = S(1 - 1/a)"] -.-> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for Students:** In an autotransformer, the power flows through *two* paths — inductively (via the magnetic core) and conductively (via the direct electrical connection). This is why autotransformers are more efficient but **less safe**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1]**  
> **Q1.** Distinguish between **core-type** and **shell-type** transformers with neat diagrams. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
- **Core-Type:** The windings surround the core. Two limbs carry concentric HV and LV windings. Magnetic path is **longer** (two limbs in series). Better cooling, used in **high-voltage** applications.
- **Shell-Type:** The core surrounds the windings. A single central limb carries the windings, and the magnetic flux returns through two outer limbs. Magnetic path is **shorter** (three limbs in parallel). Better mechanical bracing, used in **low-voltage, high-current** applications like welding.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2]**  
> **Q2.** Why is an **autotransformer** not used for isolating a load from the source? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
In an autotransformer, the primary and secondary windings are **electrically connected** through a common winding portion. Hence, any disturbance (like a high-voltage surge or a ground fault on the primary side) is **directly transferred** to the secondary side, defeating the purpose of isolation. Additionally, the secondary side is at line potential relative to ground, posing a **severe safety hazard** to operating personnel.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice — Set A]**

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain, with the help of a neat sketch, the construction and working of a **three-phase core-type transformer**. Compare it with a **shell-type** three-phase transformer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase transformer has 500 turns on the primary and 1000 turns on the secondary. The primary is connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz supply. Calculate: **(i)** Transformation ratio, (ii) Secondary EMF on no-load, (iii) Maximum value of flux in the core, (iv) Cross-sectional area of the core if $B_m = 1.2$ T. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

A three-phase core-type transformer has **three limbs** in a single plane, each carrying one phase winding (primary and secondary). The core is built from **CRGO laminations** of thickness 0.35 mm, insulated from each other by a thin varnish coating to reduce eddy-current losses. The LV and HV windings are wound concentrically (LV near the core, HV outside) and are usually of the **helical type**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Core-Type (3-φ) | Shell-Type (3-φ) |
|---------|-----------------|------------------|
| Core limbs | 3 (in one plane) | 5 (or 4 with central) |
| Cooling | **Better** | Poorer |
| Used for | High voltage, **large ratings** | Low voltage, high current |
| Mechanical strength | Lower | **Higher** |

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $N_1 = 500$ turns, $N_2 = 1000$ turns, $V_1 = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $B_m = 1.2$ T.

**(i) Transformation ratio:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{1000}{500} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Secondary EMF on no-load:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_2 &= a \times V_1 = 2 \times 230 = 460 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Maximum flux $\Phi_m$** — using $E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_m &= \dfrac{E_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_1} = \dfrac{230}{4.44 \times 50 \times 500} \\[6pt]
\Phi_m &= \dfrac{230}{111{,}000} = 2.072 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb} = 2.072 \text{ mWb}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Cross-sectional area $A_i$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_i &= \dfrac{\Phi_m}{B_m} = \dfrac{2.072 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2} = 1.726 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\[6pt]
A_i &\approx 17.27 \text{ cm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **star-star** and **delta-delta** connections of three-phase transformers. State any **two advantages and two disadvantages** of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 100 kVA, 11000 V/433 V, 50 Hz, single-phase transformer has the following test results:
- **OC test** (LV side): 433 V, 9.5 A, 500 W
- **SC test** (HV side): 318 V, 9.09 A, 1670 W

Calculate: **(i)** Core loss and copper loss at full load, **(ii)** Efficiency at 0.8 power factor, **(iii)** Maximum efficiency and the load at which it occurs. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

In **Y-Y (Star-Star)** connection, both primary and secondary windings are connected in star with the neutral point available. The line voltage is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase voltage.

**Advantages of Y-Y:** (1) Neutral is available on both sides for grounding and single-phase loads. (2) Insulation level is minimum (phase voltage is $1/\sqrt{3}$ of line voltage).

**Disadvantages of Y-Y:** (1) Third-harmonic currents cause severe neutral shifting and resonance issues. (2) If the load is unbalanced, neutral point shifts, leading to unequal phase voltages.

In **Δ-Δ (Delta-Delta)** connection, both windings are connected in delta. Line voltage equals phase voltage.

**Advantages of Δ-Δ:** (1) Suitable for unbalanced and non-linear loads. (2) Third-harmonic currents circulate in the delta, eliminating neutral shifting.

**Disadvantages of Δ-Δ:** (1) No neutral is available for grounding or single-phase loads. (2) Higher insulation required (line voltage appears across each winding).

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $S = 100$ kVA, $V_1/V_2 = 11000/433$ V, $f = 50$ Hz.

**From OC test (LV side, instruments on primary):**
- Core (iron) loss: $P_i = 500$ W
- (No-load current gives magnetizing branch info — not required for efficiency)

**From SC test (HV side):**
- Full-load copper loss: $P_{cu, FL} = 1670$ W (since SC test is performed at rated current)

**(i) At full load:**
- $P_i = 500$ W
- $P_{cu} = 1670$ W
- Total losses = 500 + 1670 = **2170 W**

**(ii) Efficiency at 0.8 p.f. (full load):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{S \cdot \cos\phi}{S \cdot \cos\phi + P_i + P_{cu}} \\[6pt]
\eta &= \dfrac{100 \times 10^3 \times 0.8}{100 \times 10^3 \times 0.8 + 500 + 1670} \\[6pt]
\eta &= \dfrac{80{,}000}{80{,}000 + 2170} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{82{,}170} \\[6pt]
\eta &= 0.9736 = \mathbf{97.36\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Maximum efficiency condition:** $P_{cu,\,x} = P_i$
$$
\begin{aligned}
x^2 \cdot P_{cu,FL} &= P_i \\[6pt]
x^2 &= \dfrac{P_i}{P_{cu,FL}} = \dfrac{500}{1670} = 0.2994 \\[6pt]
x &= 0.547 \quad \text{(fraction of full load)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Load at max efficiency:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max\eta} &= 0.547 \times 100 = \mathbf{54.7 \text{ kVA}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum efficiency:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\max} &= \dfrac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + 2 P_i} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.547 \times 100 \times 10^3 \times 0.8}{0.547 \times 100 \times 10^3 \times 0.8 + 2 \times 500} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{43{,}760}{43{,}760 + 1000} = \dfrac{43{,}760}{44{,}760} \\[6pt]
\eta_{\max} &= 0.9777 = \mathbf{97.77\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** Always use the SC test copper loss as **full-load copper loss** only if the SC test was conducted at **rated current** (often the case). Students confuse LV and HV side readings.
> 2. **Unit Conversion Pitfall:** Flux in Wb, area in m², $B_m$ in Tesla. Mixing cm² and m² causes a **massive numerical error** (factor of $10^4$).
> 3. **Autotransformer Question Trap:** Examiner frequently asks for "the power transferred conductively" — students forget the $(1 - 1/a)$ factor.
> 4. **Open-Delta (V-V) Trap:** Available capacity is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = 57.7\%$, NOT 66.7%. This is a common error.
> 5. **3-φ EMF equation:** Use $\sqrt{3}$ factor in the EMF equation for **line voltage**; omit it for **phase voltage**. Read the question carefully.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ⭐ **EMF Equation** $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$ — Memorize the **4.44 constant** and the derivation steps.
- ⭐ **Core-Type vs Shell-Type** — Core: windings surround core (HV); Shell: core surrounds windings (LV, high current).
- ⭐ **Berry-type** — Distributed shell design for distribution transformers.
- ⭐ **Autotransformer** — Single winding, **no isolation**, used as booster. Power splits into **conductive + inductive** parts.
- ⭐ **CT / PT** — CT is connected in **series** with the line; PT is connected in **parallel**.
- ⭐ **Delta-Delta** — Most reliable; can operate as **V-V (open-delta)** with 57.7% capacity.
- ⭐ **Star-Delta** — Used for **step-down at distribution sub-stations**; introduces 30° phase shift.
- ⭐ **Delta-Star** — Used for **step-up at generating stations**; provides neutral for single-phase loads.
- ⭐ **Scott-T (T-T)** — Converts 3-φ to 2-φ; used in **electric traction (Indian Railways)**.
- ⭐ **Cooling codes** — ONAN is the default for distribution transformers; ONAF for power transformers.
- ⭐ **% Z** — Determines **short-circuit current level**; standard range 5%–12% for power transformers.
- ⭐ **Voltage regulation** — Better for lower $R$ and $X$; core-type has higher regulation than shell-type.
- ⭐ **Max efficiency** occurs when $P_{cu} = P_i$ (variable loss = constant loss).
- ⭐ **All-day efficiency** — Used for distribution transformers (depends on the **load cycle**, not peak load).
- ⭐ **Buchholz relay** — Used in oil-immersed transformers; detects **incipient faults**.
- ⭐ **Conservator tank** — Maintains oil level; allows for thermal expansion.
- ⭐ **Tap changer** — OLTC (On-Load Tap Changer) for power transformers; Off-load for distribution.

> **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE examinations! 🚀**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Split-Phase
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Split-Phase # MODULE 4 — Split-Phase Induction Motor ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) A **Split-Phase Induction Motor** is a single-phase AC induction motor that employs **two stator windings** — a **main (running) winding** and an **auxiliary (starting) winding** — spatially displaced by **90 electrical degrees** in space, and supplied by the **same single-phase AC source**. The auxiliary winding is designed with **higher resistance-to-reactance (R/X) ratio** than the main winding, so that the currents in the two windings are **temporally phase-displaced** by a small angle (typically **25° to 30° electrical**). This phase-split produces a weak rotating magnetic field at start, generating sufficient **starting torque** to accelerate the rotor, after which the centrifugal switch disconnects the auxiliary winding. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** The "split" in Split-Phase refers to the **temporal phase-split** of the single-phase supply into two quasi-two-phase currents — NOT a split of the supply voltage. The motor still runs from a **single-phase supply**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Person Swing" Imagine pushing a child on a swing: - If **only one person** pushes the swing from one side, the swing rocks back and forth but cannot start rotating on its own — this is the classic single-phase problem (no starting torque). - Now imagine **two people pushing the swing from opposite ends**, but **not perfectly synchronized** — one pushes slightly earlier than the other (say, by 30° of the cycle). The swing now receives a **rotating push** and gradually begins to rotate. This "two-pushers-not-in-sync" is exactly how the split-phase motor works: - **Pusher 1 (Main Winding):** Highly inductive, current lags voltage by a large angle (~70°). - **Pusher 2 (Auxiliary Winding):** More resistive, current lags voltage by a smaller angle (~40°). - **Result:** Currents in the two windings are out of phase by ~30°, producing a weak elliptical rotating field. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Supply Frequency | **50 Hz** (India) / **60 Hz** (US) | | Phase Displacement (α) | **25° to 30° electrical** | | Starting Torque | **1.5 to 2.0 times** full-load torque | | Power Rating Range | **60 W to 250 W** (fractional kW) | | Starting Current | **6 to 8 times** full-load current | | Speed | Just below synchronous speed (e.g., ~1440 rpm for 4-pole, 50 Hz) | > [!NOTE] > **Why "Split-Phase" and not "Two-Phase"?** Although the currents are phase-shifted, both windings are fed from the **same single-phase mains**. A true two-phase system would require two separate sources displaced by 90°. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructional Details ### 2.1.1 Stator The stator is laminated silicon steel with **two embedded windings** placed **90 electrical degrees apart** in space: **A. Main (Running) Winding:** - Large conductor diameter (low resistance) - Many turns of thick copper wire - High inductive reactance (X_LM is large) - Low R/X ratio → current lags voltage by a large angle (≈ 70°) **B. Auxiliary (Starting) Winding:** - Small conductor diameter (high resistance) - Fewer turns of thin wire - Lower inductive reactance (X_LA is small) - High R/X ratio → current lags voltage by a smaller angle (≈ 40°) - Connected in **parallel** with the main winding ### 2.1.2 Rotor - **Squirrel-cage rotor** (same as three-phase induction motor) - Aluminum or copper bars short-circuited at both ends by end rings - No brushes, no slip rings — rugged and maintenance-free ### 2.1.3 Centrifugal Switch - Mounted on the rotor shaft - **Closed** at standstill (auxiliary winding is connected) - **Opens** when rotor reaches **70–80% of synchronous speed** - Disconnects the auxiliary winding from the supply > [!IMPORTANT] > The auxiliary winding is **only for starting**, NOT for running. If left connected, it would overheat and burn out because it is designed for short-duration duty. ## 2.2 Working Principle — Why It Starts ### 2.2.1 The Single-Phase Problem (Recap) A pure single-phase winding produces only a **pulsating (alternating) magnetic field**, NOT a rotating one. By **double revolving field theory**, this pulsating field can be resolved into two counter-rotating fields of equal magnitude. The net torque at standstill is **ZERO** — hence a single-phase motor is **not self-starting**. ### 2.2.2 How Split-Phase Solves the Problem **Step 1: Spatial Displacement (90° in space)** The two windings are placed 90 electrical degrees apart on the stator periphery. **Step 2: Temporal Phase Displacement (α ≈ 30° in time)** Because the auxiliary winding has higher R/X ratio: - Current in main winding: $I_m$ lags V by angle $\phi_m$ (large, ≈ 70°) - Current in auxiliary winding: $I_a$ lags V by angle $\phi_a$ (small, ≈ 40°) - Phase difference between currents: $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a$ (typically 25° to 30°) **Step 3: Resultant Rotating Field** The two currents, displaced in both space AND time, produce a **weak elliptical rotating magnetic field** instead of a pure alternating field. This field has a net forward component that drags the rotor in one direction. **Step 4: Acceleration & Switch-Off** As rotor accelerates, torque from main winding alone becomes sufficient. The centrifugal switch opens at ~75% of synchronous speed, leaving only the main winding to operate the motor. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm; f = supply freq, P = poles | | 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | per unit (0 to 1) | | 3 | Main Winding Current Angle | $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_{m}}{R_{m}}\right)$ | degrees | | 4 | Auxiliary Winding Current Angle | $\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_{a}}{R_{a}}\right)$ | degrees | | 5 | Phase Split Angle | $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a$ | ≈ 25° to 30° | | 6 | Rotor Speed at Running | $N_r = N_s(1 - s)$ | rpm | | 7 | Forward Field Flux | $\Phi_f = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}$ | Wb | | 8 | Backward Field Flux | $\Phi_b = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}$ | Wb | | 9 | Starting Torque (qualitative) | $T_{st} \propto I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin\alpha$ | Nm | | 10 | Rotor Copper Loss | $P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ | Watts | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL CONSTRAINT FOR TABLES:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` (pipe) inside any cell to avoid breaking the markdown parser. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Split-phase motors are the **workhorses of low-power single-phase applications**: - **Domestic:** Washing machines (without capacitor), ceiling fans (older designs), small pumps, refrigerators (hermetic compressor in some designs) - **Industrial:** Small machine tools, blowers, woodworking machines, printing presses (fractional HP) - **Agricultural:** Seed drills, small irrigation pumps Their dominance comes from: **simple construction, no capacitor required, low cost, and reliable starting**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Phase Displacement Between Winding Currents ### Setup Let the supply voltage be: $$V = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$ ### Main Winding Impedance The main winding is highly inductive with low resistance: $$Z_m = R_m + jX_m$$ Impedance magnitude: $$\vert Z_m \vert = \sqrt{R_m^2 + X_m^2}$$ Current phase angle in main winding: $$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$$ Main winding current equation: $$i_m = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi_m)$$ ### Auxiliary Winding Impedance The auxiliary winding has high resistance and low reactance: $$Z_a = R_a + jX_a \quad \text{where} \quad R_a \gg X_a$$ Impedance magnitude: $$\vert Z_a \vert = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_a^2}$$ Current phase angle in auxiliary winding: $$\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_a}{R_a}\right)$$ Auxiliary winding current equation: $$i_a = I_a \sin(\omega t - \phi_a)$$ ### Phase Split Angle (α) $$\boxed{\;\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_a}{R_a}\right)\;}$$ **Numerical Example:** If $R_m = 4\ \Omega$, $X_m = 11\ \Omega$, $R_a = 20\ \Omega$, $X_a = 11\ \Omega$: $$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(11/4) = 70.0^\circ$$ $$\phi_a = \tan^{-1}(11/20) = 28.8^\circ$$ $$\alpha = 70.0^\circ - 28.8^\circ = 41.2^\circ$$ This is an idealized value; practical split-phase motors achieve $\alpha \approx 25^\circ$–$30^\circ$. ## 3.2 Derivation — Resultant Magnetic Field ### Field from Main Winding (along axis M) $$\Phi_m(t) = \Phi_M \sin(\omega t - \phi_m) \cdot \cos(\theta_s)$$ where $\theta_s$ is the spatial angle along the stator periphery. ### Field from Auxiliary Winding (along axis A, 90° displaced) $$\Phi_a(t) = \Phi_A \sin(\omega t - \phi_a) \cdot \cos(\theta_s - 90^\circ)$$ ### Net Field $$\Phi_{net}(\theta_s, t) = \Phi_m(t) + \Phi_a(t)$$ After algebraic expansion using product-to-sum identities: $$\Phi_{net} = \Phi_f \cos(\theta_s - \omega t) + \Phi_b \cos(\theta_s + \omega t)$$ where: $$\Phi_f = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\Phi_M^2 + \Phi_A^2 + 2\Phi_M \Phi_A \cos\alpha}$$ $$\Phi_b = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\Phi_M^2 + \Phi_A^2 - 2\Phi_M \Phi_A \cos\alpha}$$ The forward field $\Phi_f$ exceeds the backward field $\Phi_b$, creating an **elliptical rotating field** that drags the rotor forward. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Forward vs. Backward Field | Condition | Forward Flux $\Phi_f$ | Backward Flux $\Phi_b$ | Field Nature | Result | |---|---|---|---|---| | $\alpha = 0^\circ$ | $\Phi_f = \Phi_b$ | Equal | Pure alternating (pulsating) | **No starting torque** | | $\alpha = 90^\circ$ | $\Phi_f = \Phi_M$ | $\Phi_b = 0$ | Pure rotating | **Maximum starting torque** | | $\alpha = 30^\circ$ (typical split-phase) | $\Phi_f > \Phi_b$ | $\Phi_b < \Phi_f$ | Elliptical | **Moderate starting torque** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** To maximize starting torque, we want $\alpha$ as close to 90° as possible. Split-phase achieves only ~30° — hence its starting torque is low (1.5–2× FLT). **Capacitor-start motors** achieve ~80°–90° by adding a capacitor in series with the auxiliary winding. ## 3.4 Derivation — Starting Torque Equation Starting torque in a split-phase motor is given by: $$T_{st} = K \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot \Phi$$ where: - $K$ = machine constant - $I_m$ = main winding current magnitude - $I_a$ = auxiliary winding current magnitude - $\alpha$ = phase split angle - $\Phi$ = average air-gap flux For maximum starting torque, $\sin\alpha$ must be maximum, which requires $\alpha = 90^\circ$. Since split-phase motors have $\alpha \approx 25^\circ$–$30°$: $$\sin(25^\circ) \approx 0.42$$ $$\sin(30^\circ) \approx 0.50$$ This explains why starting torque is limited. **To increase $T_{st}$, one MUST increase $\alpha$.** ## 3.5 Numerical Problem — Worked Example **Problem:** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, single-phase split-phase induction motor has main winding impedance $Z_m = 4 + j11\ \Omega$ and auxiliary winding impedance $Z_a = 20 + j11\ \Omega$. Both windings are connected across 230 V, 50 Hz supply. Determine: 1. Main and auxiliary winding currents 2. Phase split angle α 3. Synchronous speed **Solution:** **Part 1 — Currents:** $$\vert Z_m \vert = \sqrt{4^2 + 11^2} = \sqrt{16 + 121} = \sqrt{137} \approx 11.70\ \Omega$$ $$I_m = \frac{V}{\vert Z_m \vert} = \frac{230}{11.70} \approx 19.66\ \text{A}$$ $$\vert Z_a \vert = \sqrt{20^2 + 11^2} = \sqrt{400 + 121} = \sqrt{521} \approx 22.83\ \Omega$$ $$I_a = \frac{V}{\vert Z_a \vert} = \frac{230}{22.83} \approx 10.08\ \text{A}$$ **Part 2 — Phase Split Angle:** $$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(11/4) = \tan^{-1}(2.75) \approx 70.02^\circ$$ $$\phi_a = \tan^{-1}(11/20) = \tan^{-1}(0.55) \approx 28.81^\circ$$ $$\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = 70.02^\circ - 28.81^\circ \approx 41.21^\circ$$ **Part 3 — Synchronous Speed:** $$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P} = \frac{120 \cdot 50}{4} = 1500\ \text{rpm}$$ **Rotor running speed** (assuming slip s = 0.05): $$N_r = N_s(1 - s) = 1500(1 - 0.05) = 1425\ \text{rpm}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Constructional Architecture of Split-Phase Motor ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ST["STATOR ASSEMBLY"] A1["MAIN WINDING
Low R, High X_L
Many turns thick wire
High inductance"] A2["AUXILIARY WINDING
High R, Low X_L
Few turns thin wire
High resistance"] A3["LAMINATED STATOR CORE
Silicon steel stamping"] A1 --- A3 A2 --- A3 end subgraph SP["SPATIAL PLACEMENT 90 DEG"] B1["Main Winding Axis: 0 deg"] B2["Aux Winding Axis: 90 deg"] end subgraph ROT["ROTOR ASSEMBLY"] C1["SQUIRGREL CAGE ROTOR
Aluminum bars
End rings shorted"] C2["SHAFT"] C3["CENTRIFUGAL SWITCH
Mounted on shaft"] C1 --- C2 C2 --- C3 end subgraph SUP["SUPPLY AND PROTECTION"] D1["SINGLE PHASE AC MAINS
230 V 50 Hz"] D2["CENTRIFUGAL SWITCH CONTACT
Normally closed at standstill"] D3["TERMINAL BOX"] end ST --> SP SP --> ROT SUP --> ST D1 --> D3 D3 --> A1 D3 --> D2 D2 --> A2 ROT -.rotates.-> C3 C3 -.opens above 75 percent Ns.-> D2 style A1 fill:#FFD580 style A2 fill:#FFB6C1 style C1 fill:#90EE90 style C3 fill:#DDA0DD style D1 fill:#87CEEB ``` ## 4.2 Functional Flow — Working Sequence ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Step 1: Apply Single Phase AC Supply"] --> S2["Step 2: Main winding current lags V by large angle phi_m"] S1 --> S3["Step 3: Auxiliary winding current lags V by small angle phi_a"] S2 --> S4["Step 4: Phase difference alpha between I_m and I_a about 30 deg"] S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5["Step 5: Spatially 90 deg apart currents produce elliptical rotating field"] S5 --> S6["Step 6: Net forward torque accelerates rotor"] S6 --> S7{"Step 7: Rotor speed exceeds 75 percent Ns?"} S7 -- No --> S6 S7 -- Yes --> S8["Step 8: Centrifugal switch OPENS"] S8 --> S9["Step 9: Auxiliary winding DISCONNECTED"] S9 --> S10["Step 10: Motor runs on main winding only as single phase motor"] style S1 fill:#FFE4B5 style S5 fill:#98FB98 style S8 fill:#FFA07A style S10 fill:#87CEFA ``` ## 4.3 Phasor Representation — Voltage and Currents ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PH["PHASOR DIAGRAM AT STANDSTILL"] V["V reference horizontal axis"] IA["I_a: lags V by phi_a about 30 deg small angle"] IM["I_m: lags V by phi_m about 70 deg large angle"] ALPHA["alpha = phi_m minus phi_a about 40 deg PHASE SPLIT"] end V --- IA V --- IM IA --- ALPHA IM --- ALPHA style V fill:#FFFACD style IA fill:#FFB6C1 style IM fill:#ADD8E6 style ALPHA fill:#90EE90 ``` ## 4.4 Torque-Speed Characteristic (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["At standstill N_r equals 0"] --> B["Starting Torque T_st about 1.5 to 2 times FLT"] B --> C["Acceleration region: Torque rises to peak at s equals 0.2 to 0.3"] C --> D["Pull-up torque region"] D --> E["Pull-out torque at s about 0.1 to 0.2"] E --> F["Normal running region: s about 0.03 to 0.05"] F --> G["Full load torque at rated N_r"] A -.aux winding ON.-> B G -.aux winding OFF centrifugal switch open.-> H["Continues running on main winding only"] style B fill:#FFD700 style E fill:#FF6347 style G fill:#90EE90 style H fill:#87CEEB ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Explain why a single-phase induction motor is not self-starting and how a split-phase motor overcomes this limitation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A single-phase induction motor fed from a single-phase supply produces only a **pulsating (alternating) magnetic field**, which can be resolved into two counter-rotating fields of equal magnitude (by Double Revolving Field Theory). At standstill, the two torques are equal and opposite, giving **zero net starting torque**. Hence the motor is not self-starting. The **split-phase motor** overcomes this by using **two stator windings** (main and auxiliary) placed **90 electrical degrees apart in space** and supplied from the **same single-phase source**. The auxiliary winding has a **high R/X ratio** so that its current lags the supply voltage by a small angle, while the main winding current lags by a large angle. This produces a **phase split of ~25°–30°** between the two currents. The spatially and temporally displaced currents create an **elliptical rotating magnetic field** with a net forward component, generating sufficient starting torque (1.5–2 × FLT) to accelerate the rotor. *(Mark distribution: 1 mark for single-phase non-self-starting reason + 1 mark for construction of split-phase + 1 mark for phase split mechanism)* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Why is the centrifugal switch provided in a split-phase induction motor? What happens if it fails to open?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The centrifugal switch is mounted on the rotor shaft and is **closed at standstill**, connecting the auxiliary winding to the supply. When the rotor reaches **70–80% of synchronous speed**, the centrifugal force causes the switch to **open automatically**, disconnecting the auxiliary winding. This is necessary because: 1. The auxiliary winding is designed for **short-duration duty only** (high resistance, thin wire — would overheat on continuous operation). 2. The motor can run efficiently on the main winding alone once sufficient speed is reached. **If the centrifugal switch fails to open:** the auxiliary winding remains energized continuously, leading to **excessive current draw, overheating, insulation failure, and eventual burning out of the auxiliary winding**. The motor may also draw unbalanced currents causing supply disturbances. *(Mark distribution: 1 mark purpose + 1 mark speed threshold + 1 mark consequence of failure)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Choice A]` **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **single-phase split-phase induction motor**. Discuss the role of the centrifugal switch in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-pole, 50 Hz split-phase induction motor has main winding impedance $Z_m = (4 + j11)\ \Omega$ and auxiliary winding impedance $Z_a = (20 + j11)\ \Omega$. Both windings are connected across 230 V, 50 Hz supply. Calculate: 1. Main winding and auxiliary winding currents. 2. Their phase angles with respect to supply voltage. 3. Phase split angle α. 4. Synchronous speed. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Construction (3 marks):** The split-phase induction motor consists of: **1. Stator:** - Laminated silicon steel stator core with two distinct windings embedded in the slots: - **Main (Running) Winding:** Made of thick copper wire with many turns. Has **low resistance** and **high inductive reactance** (X_LM large). Placed along a reference axis. - **Auxiliary (Starting) Winding:** Made of thin copper wire with fewer turns. Has **high resistance** and **low inductive reactance** (X_LA small). Placed **90 electrical degrees apart** from the main winding in space. **2. Rotor:** - Squirrel-cage type: aluminum or copper bars embedded in rotor slots, short-circuited at both ends by conducting end rings. Robust, maintenance-free. **3. Centrifugal Switch:** - Mechanical switch mounted on the rotor shaft. - Consists of a spring-loaded weight and contact points. - **Closed at standstill** (centrifugal force is insufficient to overcome spring tension). - **Opens at ~75% of synchronous speed** (centrifugal force overcomes spring, throwing the weight outward and breaking contact). **Working Principle (4 marks):** When single-phase AC supply is applied: - Current in main winding $I_m$ lags voltage by large angle $\phi_m$ (since X_LM ≫ R_M). - Current in auxiliary winding $I_a$ lags voltage by small angle $\phi_a$ (since R_a is comparable to or larger than X_LA). - The phase difference between $I_m$ and $I_a$ is $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a \approx 25°$–$30°$. The two windings are **spatially 90° apart** and **temporally α apart**, producing an **elliptical rotating magnetic field** with a net forward component. This exerts a starting torque of 1.5 to 2 times full-load torque, accelerating the rotor. Once the rotor reaches ~75% of synchronous speed, the **centrifugal switch opens**, disconnecting the auxiliary winding. The motor then continues to run on the main winding alone, operating as a pure single-phase motor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Role of Centrifugal Switch (separate from construction marks):** > - Protects auxiliary winding from thermal damage (designed for intermittent duty). > - Improves running efficiency by removing the lossy auxiliary winding from the circuit. > - Reduces running current and improves power factor during normal operation. --- ### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Given:** - Poles $P = 4$ - Frequency $f = 50$ Hz - $Z_m = (4 + j11)\ \Omega$ - $Z_a = (20 + j11)\ \Omega$ - $V = 230$ V **(i) Main Winding Current (2 marks):** $$\vert Z_m \vert = \sqrt{R_m^2 + X_m^2} = \sqrt{4^2 + 11^2} = \sqrt{16 + 121} = \sqrt{137}$$ $$\vert Z_m \vert \approx 11.70\ \Omega$$ $$I_m = \frac{V}{\vert Z_m \vert} = \frac{230}{11.70} \approx 19.66\ \text{A}$$ **(ii) Auxiliary Winding Current (2 marks):** $$\vert Z_a \vert = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_a^2} = \sqrt{20^2 + 11^2} = \sqrt{400 + 121} = \sqrt{521}$$ $$\vert Z_a \vert \approx 22.83\ \Omega$$ $$I_a = \frac{V}{\vert Z_a \vert} = \frac{230}{22.83} \approx 10.08\ \text{A}$$ **(iii) Phase Angles & Phase Split (2 marks):** $$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{11}{4}\right) = \tan^{-1}(2.75) \approx 70.02^\circ$$ $$\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_a}{R_a}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{11}{20}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.55) \approx 28.81^\circ$$ $$\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = 70.02^\circ - 28.81^\circ \approx 41.21^\circ$$ **(iv) Synchronous Speed (1 mark):** $$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\ \text{rpm}$$ **Summary Table:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | $I_m$ | 19.66 A | | $I_a$ | 10.08 A | | $\phi_m$ | 70.02° | | $\phi_a$ | 28.81° | | $\alpha$ | 41.21° | | $N_s$ | 1500 rpm | --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice B]` **(a)** Explain the **Double Revolving Field Theory** as applied to single-phase induction motors. How does the split-phase motor use this theory to produce starting torque? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare split-phase motor with **capacitor-start motor** and **capacitor-run motor** in terms of construction, starting torque, power factor, and applications. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Double Revolving Field Theory (5 marks):** Proposed by **Ferraris**, this theory states that: > *Any alternating (pulsating) magnetic field can be resolved into two rotating magnetic fields of equal magnitude rotating in opposite directions at synchronous speed.* **Mathematical Proof:** Consider a pulsating field along axis XX': $$\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$ This can be written as the sum of two counter-rotating vectors: $$\Phi(t) = \underbrace{\frac{\Phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t - \theta)}_{\text{Forward field}} + \underbrace{\frac{\Phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t + \theta)}_{\text{Backward field}}$$ where $\theta$ is the spatial angle. **Significance:** - Each rotating field induces EMF and current in the rotor. - The **forward field** produces forward torque $T_f$ (in direction of rotation). - The **backward field** produces backward torque $T_b$ (opposing rotation). - **At standstill (s = 1):** Both slip values are equal (slip = 1 for both fields), so $T_f = T_b$. Net torque = **0**. Motor cannot start. **Application to Split-Phase Motor (2 marks):** In a split-phase motor, due to the **phase-split current** ($\alpha \approx 25°$–$30°$), the two counter-rotating fields are **no longer equal**. The forward field magnitude is: $$\Phi_f = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\Phi_M^2 + \Phi_A^2 + 2\Phi_M \Phi_A \cos\alpha}$$ The backward field magnitude is: $$\Phi_b = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\Phi_M^2 + \Phi_A^2 - 2\Phi_M \Phi_A \cos\alpha}$$ Since $\cos\alpha > 0$ for $\alpha < 90°$, $\Phi_f > \Phi_b$. This **unbalanced field pair** produces a net forward starting torque, allowing the motor to self-start. --- ### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Feature | Split-Phase Motor | Capacitor-Start Motor | Capacitor-Run Motor | |---|---|---|---| | **Auxiliary Winding** | High R, low X (no capacitor) | In series with **starting capacitor** (large value, electrolytic) | In series with **running capacitor** (small value, oil-filled) | | **Phase Split Angle (α)** | 25°–30° | 80°–90° | ~90° | | **Starting Torque** | 1.5–2 × FLT (low) | 3–4.5 × FLT (high) | 1.5–2 × FLT | | **Power Factor at Start** | Poor (~0.4 lagging) | Better (~0.7) | Good | | **Centrifugal Switch** | Yes (must disconnect aux) | Yes (must disconnect aux + capacitor) | **Not required** (capacitor stays in circuit) | | **Running Performance** | Moderate | Moderate (no aux during run) | Excellent (smooth, quiet, high PF) | | **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | | **Applications** | Fans, blowers, small pumps, washing machines (older), machine tools | Compressors, conveyors, large pumps, air conditioners, refrigerator compressors | Ceiling fans (modern), blowers requiring quiet operation, oil burners | | **Typical Ratings** | 60 W to 250 W | 100 W to 7.5 kW | 50 W to 1.5 kW | **Explanation (2 marks):** The **capacitor-start motor** achieves a much higher starting torque (3–4.5 × FLT) because the capacitor in series with the auxiliary winding produces a phase split of nearly 90°. The **capacitor-run motor** sacrifices some starting torque for superior running performance: higher power factor, smoother torque, and quieter operation. The split-phase motor is the simplest and cheapest but offers the lowest starting torque, limiting it to low-starting-load applications. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "split-phase" with "two-phase"** — Examiners specifically deduct marks if students claim split-phase motors use two-phase supply. Always clarify: **both windings are fed from the SAME single-phase source**. > 2. **Forgetting the spatial 90° placement** — Many students only mention temporal phase displacement, missing that the windings MUST also be 90 electrical degrees apart in space. Both conditions are mandatory. > 3. **Incorrect unit for starting torque** — Always express as a **multiple of full-load torque** (e.g., 1.5–2 × FLT), not in absolute Nm (which depends on motor rating). > 4. **Omitting the centrifugal switch explanation** — In 7-mark construction questions, failure to explain the **switch operating mechanism (centrifugal force vs. spring tension)** loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Wrong synchronous speed formula** — Using $N_s = 60f/P$ instead of $N_s = 120f/P$ is a frequent error. **Both formulas exist; KTU uses 120f/P.** > 6. **Sign error in phase split angle** — $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a$ (NOT the reverse). Since $\phi_m > \phi_a$, α is positive. > 7. **Treating the motor as three-phase** — In numerical problems, students sometimes incorrectly apply three-phase formulas (e.g., $V_L$ instead of $V_{ph}$). For single-phase, $V_{ph} = V_L$. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - ⭐ **Definition:** Single-phase motor with two windings (main + auxiliary) on same supply, 90° spatial separation, ~30° temporal phase split. - ⭐ **Why not self-starting:** Single-phase produces pulsating field (Double Revolving Field Theory) → equal forward & backward torques → net zero starting torque. - ⭐ **Solution:** Phase split creates elliptical rotating field with $\Phi_f > \Phi_b$ → net forward torque. - ⭐ **Key equations to memorize:** - $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = \tan^{-1}(X_m/R_m) - \tan^{-1}(X_a/R_a)$ - $N_s = 120f/P$ - $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ - $T_{st} \propto I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin\alpha$ - ⭐ **Starting torque:** 1.5–2 × FLT (LOW — main limitation). - ⭐ **Centrifugal switch:** Opens at 70–80% of $N_s$ to protect auxiliary winding. - ⭐ **Construction memory hook:** Main winding = "thick & many turns" (low R, high X). Auxiliary winding = "thin & few turns" (high R, low X). - ⭐ **Direction reversal:** Swap ONLY the auxiliary winding terminals (NOT the main winding). - ⭐ **Typical applications:** Fans, blowers, washing machines (without capacitor start), small pumps, low-cost fractional HP drives. - ⭐ **Comparison memory:** Split-phase = no capacitor, low cost, low starting torque; Capacitor-start = high starting torque but switch needed; Capacitor-run = no switch, best running performance. - ⭐ **Power rating range:** 60 W to 250 W (fractional horsepower). - ⭐ **Phasor angle memory:** $\phi_m \approx 70°$ (inductive), $\phi_a \approx 40°$ (resistive), $\alpha \approx 25°$–$30°$. - ⭐ **Examiner trap:** "Can split-phase motor be used for high-inertia loads?" → **NO**, because starting torque is only 1.5–2 × FLT, insufficient for compressors or conveyors.

topics.title: Capacitor-Start Induction-Run
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capacitor-Start Induction-Run

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacitor-Start Induction-Run Motor:** A single-phase induction motor that employs a **capacitor in series with an auxiliary (starting) winding** to create a large phase displacement (ideally **90°**) between the currents of the main (running) winding and the auxiliary winding during starting. The auxiliary winding and capacitor are **disconnected from the supply by a centrifugal switch** when the motor reaches approximately **70% to 80% of synchronous speed**, after which the motor continues to run on the main winding alone.

### Key Identifier Parameters

| Parameter | Standard KTU Value |
|---|---|
| Supply | Single-phase AC, **50 Hz** (India) |
| Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ |
| Starting Phase Angle Target | $\alpha \approx 90^\circ$ (ideal) |
| Centrifugal Switch Cut-out | **70% – 80%** of $N_s$ |
| Direction | Reversible (by swapping auxiliary winding) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Two-Person Swing:**
> Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If **one person pushes at just the right moment** (say, at the top of the swing's motion) and **another person pulls the rope slightly later**, the swing will start moving with great force. The **time gap** (phase shift) between their actions is what makes the swing start smoothly and powerfully.
>
> In the **Capacitor-Start motor**, the **main winding** is one pusher, and the **auxiliary winding (in series with a capacitor)** is the second pusher. The **capacitor delays** the current in the auxiliary winding so that the magnetic "push" from the two windings is **90° apart in time**, creating a powerful **rotating magnetic field** that spins the rotor from rest. Once the rotor gains speed, the second pusher (auxiliary winding + capacitor) is **automatically disconnected** by a centrifugal switch — just like the second person walking away once the swing is moving on its own.

## 1.3 Why Capacitor-Start Motors Are Needed

> [!TIP]
> A plain **single-phase induction motor is NOT self-starting** because a single-phase supply produces a **pulsating magnetic field** (not rotating). To make it self-starting, we need a **second phase**, which is artificially created using an auxiliary winding + capacitor. The capacitor is the most efficient way to achieve this artificial phase splitting because it provides a **leading current**, which produces a much **larger phase angle difference** than a simple resistor.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of Capacitor-Start Induction-Run Motor

The motor consists of the following key components:

### A. Stator
- **Laminated silicon-steel core** with uniformly distributed slots.
- Carries **two physically displaced windings** (typically 90° electrical apart in space):
  - **Main (Running) Winding (M):** Heavy gauge copper wire, low resistance, high inductance, placed at the **bottom of the slots**. Designed to carry current continuously.
  - **Auxiliary (Starting) Winding (A):** Thin gauge copper wire, high resistance (in some designs), placed at the **top of the slots**. Connected in series with a **capacitor $C$** and a **centrifugal switch**.

### B. Rotor
- **Squirrel-cage rotor** — identical to that of a three-phase induction motor.
- No external electrical connection; currents are induced by transformer action.

### C. Capacitor
- **Electrolytic type (AC-rated)** — chosen because:
  - High capacitance per unit volume.
  - Low cost.
  - Used ONLY for short-duration starting (typically < 3 seconds).
- Connected in series with the auxiliary winding and centrifugal switch.

### D. Centrifugal Switch
- Mounted on the **rotor shaft** (mechanically coupled to the rotor).
- **At rest:** Spring-held in **closed** position → auxiliary circuit is connected.
- **At ~75% of $N_s$:** Centrifugal force overcomes spring force → **switch opens** → auxiliary winding + capacitor disconnected.
- **After power off:** Spring returns the switch to the closed position for the next start.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — At Starting (Rotor at Rest):**
> The centrifugal switch is **closed**. Both main winding (M) and auxiliary winding (A, in series with capacitor C) are connected to the single-phase AC supply.
>
> **Step 2 — Phase Splitting via Capacitor:**
> - Current in main winding $I_m$ lags the supply voltage $V$ by a large angle $\theta_m$ (highly inductive).
> - Current in auxiliary winding $I_a$ **leads** the supply voltage $V$ by an angle $\theta_a$ (capacitive).
> - The **phase angle between $I_m$ and $I_a$** is:
>
> $$\alpha = \theta_m + \theta_a \approx 90^\circ$$
>
> **Step 3 — Rotating Magnetic Field Produced:**
> Two windings carrying currents that are **90° apart in time** and **90° apart in space** produce a **uniform rotating magnetic field** (similar to a two-phase system), which exerts a strong starting torque on the rotor.
>
> **Step 4 — Acceleration:**
> Rotor accelerates in the direction of the rotating magnetic field.
>
> **Step 5 — Centrifugal Switch Operation:**
> When rotor reaches **70% – 80%** of synchronous speed, the centrifugal switch **opens**, disconnecting the auxiliary winding and capacitor.
>
> **Step 6 — Steady-State Running:**
> The motor now runs as a **single-phase induction motor** on the main winding alone. The rotor's induced currents sustain the rotation through cross-field theory.

## 2.3 Starting Torque — The "Why" and "How"

The starting torque of a single-phase induction motor with an auxiliary winding is given by:

$$T_{st} = K \cdot \phi_m \cdot \phi_a \cdot \sin\alpha$$

Where:
- $\phi_m$ = flux produced by main winding
- $\phi_a$ = flux produced by auxiliary winding
- $\alpha$ = phase angle between the two currents (and hence fluxes)
- $K$ = constant depending on machine construction

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" behind maximum starting torque:** Starting torque is proportional to $\sin\alpha$. Since the **maximum value of $\sin\alpha$ is 1** (when $\alpha = 90^\circ$), the **ideal capacitor value** is chosen so that the two currents are exactly **90° apart in time**. This gives the **maximum possible starting torque** — typically **2.5 to 4.5 times** the full-load torque, which is much higher than a split-phase motor (which has $\alpha \approx 25°-30°$).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **master reference** for solving KTU 2024 numerical problems on this topic.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | RPM |
| 2 | $T_{st} = K \cdot \phi_m \cdot \phi_a \cdot \sin\alpha$ | Starting torque (general) | N·m |
| 3 | $T_{st(max)}$ when $\alpha = 90^\circ$ | Condition for max starting torque | — |
| 4 | $X_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Capacitive reactance of starting capacitor | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{I_m \sin\theta_m}{2\pi f V}$ | Capacitance for $90°$ phase shift | Farads (F) |
| 6 | $C = \dfrac{\sin\theta_m}{2\pi f Z_m} \cdot \cos\phi_m$ | Alternate form using $Z_m$ and $\cos\phi_m$ | Farads (F) |
| 7 | $I_m = \dfrac{V}{Z_m} = \dfrac{V}{R_m + jX_m}$ | Main winding current (phasor) | Amperes |
| 8 | $I_a = \dfrac{V}{R_a - jX_c}$ | Auxiliary winding current (phasor) | Amperes |
| 9 | $\alpha = \theta_m + \theta_a$ | Phase angle between $I_m$ and $I_a$ | degrees/radians |
| 10 | $\cos\phi_m = \dfrac{R_m}{Z_m}$ | Main winding power factor | dimensionless |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> Capacitor-start induction-run motors are the **workhorse** of small-to-medium horsepower single-phase applications. They are used in:
> - **Air compressors** (high starting torque required)
> - **Refrigerators and air-conditioner compressors**
> - **Water pumps** (jet pumps, submersible pumps)
> - **Industrial fans and blowers**
> - **Machine tools, conveyor systems**
> - **Washing machines, woodworking machinery**

Their key advantage is a **high starting torque** (3 to 4.5 × full-load torque) at **low starting current**, making them ideal for **frequent start-stop** industrial and domestic applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Condition for Maximum Starting Torque

**Given:** A single-phase induction motor with two windings displaced $90°$ in space, carrying currents $I_m$ and $I_a$ with a phase angle $\alpha$ between them.

**Starting torque equation:**

$$T_{st} = K \cdot \phi_m \cdot \phi_a \cdot \sin\alpha$$

Assume the fluxes $\phi_m$ and $\phi_a$ are proportional to the respective currents (for unsaturated magnetic circuit):

$$\phi_m \propto I_m, \quad \phi_a \propto I_a$$

Substituting:

$$T_{st} = K' \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin\alpha$$

For **maximum starting torque**, we take the derivative with respect to $\alpha$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{dT_{st}}{d\alpha} = K' \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \cos\alpha = 0$$

This gives:

$$\cos\alpha = 0 \implies \alpha = 90^\circ$$

**Therefore, the starting torque is maximum when the two currents are $90°$ apart in time.**

$$\boxed{T_{st(max)} = K' \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \quad \text{at} \quad \alpha = 90^\circ}$$

## 3.2 Derivation II: Capacitance Required for 90° Phase Shift (KTU Most Asked)

**Objective:** Find the value of capacitance $C$ in series with the auxiliary winding so that the phase angle $\alpha$ between $I_m$ and $I_a$ is exactly **90°**.

**Phasor setup:**

Let supply voltage $V$ be the reference phasor: $\vec{V} = V \angle 0°$.

**Main winding (highly inductive):**

$$\vec{I_m} = \frac{V}{Z_m} = \frac{V}{R_m + jX_m}$$

Let $Z_m = \sqrt{R_m^2 + X_m^2}$ and $\cos\phi_m = \dfrac{R_m}{Z_m}$

Therefore:

$$\vec{I_m} = \frac{V}{Z_m} \angle (-\phi_m)$$

So $I_m$ **lags** $V$ by angle $\phi_m = \theta_m$ (using the convention that $\theta_m$ is the lag angle of main winding current).

**Auxiliary winding (resistance + capacitance in series):**

$$\vec{I_a} = \frac{V}{R_a - jX_c} = \frac{V}{Z_a} \angle (+\phi_a)$$

where $Z_a = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_c^2}$ and $I_a$ **leads** $V$ by angle $\phi_a = \theta_a$.

**Phase angle between the two currents:**

$$\alpha = \theta_m + \theta_a$$

**For 90° phase displacement:**

$$\alpha = \theta_m + \theta_a = 90°$$

Therefore:

$$\theta_a = 90° - \theta_m$$

**Now, $\tan\theta_a$ from the auxiliary circuit:**

$$\tan\theta_a = \frac{X_c}{R_a} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C \cdot R_a}$$

**Substitute $\theta_a = 90° - \theta_m$:**

$$\tan(90° - \theta_m) = \frac{1}{2\pi f C \cdot R_a}$$

Using the identity $\tan(90° - \theta_m) = \cot\theta_m = \dfrac{\cos\theta_m}{\sin\theta_m}$:

$$\frac{\cos\theta_m}{\sin\theta_m} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C \cdot R_a}$$

Solving for $C$:

$$\boxed{C = \frac{\sin\theta_m}{2\pi f \cdot R_a \cdot \cos\theta_m} = \frac{\tan\theta_m}{2\pi f \cdot R_a}}$$

**Alternative form using the main winding impedance $Z_m$:**

Since $\sin\theta_m = \dfrac{X_m}{Z_m}$ and $R_m = Z_m \cos\theta_m$:

$$C = \frac{X_m / Z_m}{2\pi f \cdot R_a \cdot (R_m / Z_m)} = \frac{X_m}{2\pi f \cdot R_a \cdot R_m}$$

**If $R_a \approx R_m = R$ (a common simplification in textbooks):**

$$\boxed{C = \frac{X_m}{2\pi f \cdot R^2} = \frac{\omega L_m}{2\pi f \cdot R^2} = \frac{L_m}{R^2}}$$

This is the **exact formula** to be used in KTU board exam numerical problems.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A $230\text{ V}$, $50\text{ Hz}$ single-phase capacitor-start induction motor has the following winding parameters:
- Main winding: $R_m = 4\ \Omega$, $X_m = 7\ \Omega$
- Auxiliary winding: $R_a = 7.5\ \Omega$, $X_a = 4\ \Omega$ (inductive)

**Find:**
1. The capacitance $C$ required in series with the auxiliary winding for $90°$ phase displacement.
2. The starting torque in terms of a constant $K'$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute main winding impedance and phase angle:**

$$Z_m = \sqrt{R_m^2 + X_m^2} = \sqrt{4^2 + 7^2} = \sqrt{16 + 49} = \sqrt{65} \approx 8.06\ \Omega$$

$$\theta_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{7}{4}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.75) \approx 60.26°$$

**Step 2 — Main winding current:**

$$I_m = \frac{V}{Z_m} = \frac{230}{8.06} \approx 28.54\text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Auxiliary winding current (after adding capacitor):**

The auxiliary winding now has effective reactance: $X_{a,eff} = X_a - X_c$

We need the new total phase angle such that the new $I_a$ leads $V$ by:

$$\theta_a = 90° - \theta_m = 90° - 60.26° = 29.74°$$

**Step 4 — Effective impedance of auxiliary branch:**

$$\tan\theta_a = \frac{X_{a,eff}}{R_a} \implies X_{a,eff} = R_a \cdot \tan\theta_a$$

$$X_{a,eff} = 7.5 \cdot \tan(29.74°) = 7.5 \cdot 0.5710 \approx 4.28\ \Omega$$

**Step 5 — Required capacitive reactance:**

$$X_c = X_a - X_{a,eff} = 4 - 4.28 = -0.28\ \Omega$$

This gives a **negative** value, meaning the required phase shift cannot be achieved without a **larger capacitor that overcompensates** the inductive reactance. In practice, the auxiliary winding is designed such that the formula yields a positive $X_c$.

**Step 6 — Recompute using the alternative KTU formula:**

Using the direct capacitance formula:

$$C = \frac{\sin\theta_m}{2\pi f R_a \cos\theta_m} = \frac{\tan\theta_m}{2\pi f R_a}$$

$$C = \frac{\tan(60.26°)}{2\pi \times 50 \times 7.5} = \frac{1.75}{2356.19} \approx 7.43 \times 10^{-4}\text{ F} = 743\ \mu\text{F}$$

**Step 7 — Starting Torque:**

$$I_a = \frac{V}{Z_a} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_a^2 + X_{a,eff}^2}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{7.5^2 + 4.28^2}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{56.25 + 18.32}} = \frac{230}{8.64} \approx 26.62\text{ A}$$

$$T_{st} = K' \cdot I_m \cdot I_a = K' \cdot 28.54 \cdot 26.62 \approx K' \cdot 759.7$$

$$\boxed{T_{st} \approx 759.7 \cdot K' \quad \text{(maximum, since } \alpha = 90°)}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Feature | Split-Phase Motor | Capacitor-Start Motor | Capacitor-Start Capacitor-Run |
|---|---|---|---|
| Auxiliary winding element | Resistor | **Capacitor** | Capacitor (stays in circuit) |
| Phase angle $\alpha$ | 25° – 30° | **75° – 90°** | 90° (ideal) |
| Starting torque (× FLT) | 1.5 – 2 | **3 – 4.5** | 1.5 – 2.5 |
| Starting current | Moderate | **Low** | Low |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Higher |
| Capacitor type | None | Electrolytic (short duty) | Oil-filled (continuous duty) |
| Power factor at start | Poor (~0.5) | **Good (~0.8 – 0.9)** | Excellent (~0.95) |
| Applications | Fans, small pumps | Compressors, conveyors | Fans, blowers (high run PF) |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Capacitor-Start Induction-Run Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Single Phase AC Supply 230V 50Hz] --> B[Common Terminal]
    B --> C[Main Winding M]
    B --> D[Centrifugal Switch CS]
    D --> E[Auxiliary Winding A]
    E --> F[Starting Capacitor C]
    F --> G[Return to Supply]
    C --> G
    H[Squirrel Cage Rotor on Shaft] --> I[Mechanical Load Output]
    J[Centrifugal Mechanism on Rotor Shaft] --> K{At 75 percent of Ns}
    K -->|Yes Switch Opens| L[Aux Circuit Disconnected]
    K -->|No Switch Closed| D
    M[Stator Laminations] --> C
    M --> E
    H --> J
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Circuit Schematic — Winding Interconnection

```mermaid
graph LR
    SUP[AC Supply V] --> N[Node A]
    N --> M1[Main Winding Rm jXm]
    M1 --> N2[Node B]
    N2 --> RT[Return to Supply]
    N --> CS1[Centrifugal Switch NC Contact]
    CS1 --> AX1[Auxiliary Winding Ra jXa]
    AX1 --> CAP[Capacitor jXc]
    CAP --> N2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Phasor Diagram — Phase Relationship Between Currents

```mermaid
graph LR
    VREF[Reference Voltage V along 0 degrees] --> IML[Im lags V by angle theta m around 60 degrees]
    VREF --> IAL[Ia leads V by angle theta a around 30 degrees]
    IML --> ALPHA[Phase Angle alpha = theta m plus theta a equals 90 degrees]
    IAL --> ALPHA
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Torque-Speed Characteristic Curve — Functional Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ST[Starting Torque at N=0: 3 to 4.5 times FLT] --> ACC[Rotor Accelerates]
    ACC --> SW[At 75 percent Ns: Centrifugal Switch Opens]
    SW --> AUXOFF[Auxiliary Winding and Capacitor Disconnected]
    AUXOFF --> RUN[Motor Runs on Main Winding Alone]
    RUN --> SS[Steady State at Near Synchronous Speed]
    ST --> ACC
```

## 4.5 Physical Layout Description (For Student Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Layout of the Motor (Cross-Sectional View):**
>
> 1. **Outer Shell:** Cast-iron or die-cast aluminum frame.
> 2. **Stator Core:** Laminated silicon steel ring with **inner slots** distributed uniformly around the bore.
> 3. **Main Winding Coils:** Wound with **thick enameled copper wire** (low resistance, high current capacity), placed in the **bottom of slots**.
> 4. **Auxiliary Winding Coils:** Wound with **thinner wire**, placed in the **top of slots** (90° electrical displacement from main winding).
> 5. **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage bars (aluminum or copper) embedded in rotor laminations, mounted on shaft.
> 6. **Bearings:** At both ends of the shaft.
> 7. **Centrifugal Switch:** Mounted inside the motor, on the shaft, near one bearing.
> 8. **Capacitor:** Mounted externally on top of the motor frame (in a protective casing).
> 9. **Cooling Fan:** Often attached to the non-driving end of the shaft.

---
