# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Why is a capacitor connected in series with the auxiliary winding of a capacitor-start single-phase induction motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A single-phase induction motor is not self-starting because a single-phase supply produces only a **pulsating magnetic field** and not a rotating one. To make it self-starting, we need a **second phase** displaced in time and space. The capacitor connected in series with the auxiliary winding causes the current in the auxiliary winding to **lead** the supply voltage, while the current in the main winding **lags** the supply voltage. This produces a **large phase difference** of nearly **90°** between the two currents, generating a strong **rotating magnetic field** that produces a **high starting torque** (3 to 4.5 times full-load torque). The capacitor is disconnected by a centrifugal switch once the motor reaches ~75% of synchronous speed.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: State the function of the centrifugal switch in a capacitor-start induction-run motor. At what speed does it operate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **centrifugal switch** is a speed-sensitive mechanical switch mounted on the rotor shaft. Its function is to **automatically disconnect the auxiliary winding and the starting capacitor from the supply** once the motor has accelerated to a sufficient speed. At rest, the switch is **closed** (spring-held) so that the auxiliary winding and capacitor are in the circuit. As the rotor speed increases, the **centrifugal force on a flyweight mechanism** increases. When the rotor reaches approximately **70% to 80% of the synchronous speed**, the centrifugal force overcomes the spring tension and the switch **opens**, breaking the auxiliary circuit. This protects the electrolytic capacitor from damage due to prolonged operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write the capacitance formula as $C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f X_c}$ without deriving it from the phase angle condition. **Always show the full derivation** from $\alpha = 90°$ → $\tan\theta_a$ → capacitance. (Loses 2 marks)
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **synchronous speed** ($N_s$) with **actual rotor speed** ($N$). Remember: $N = N_s(1 - s)$ where $s$ is slip.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Using the wrong capacitor type (oil-filled vs electrolytic) in the answer. **Electrolytic** is for short-duty starting; **oil-filled** is for continuous duty.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to specify that the centrifugal switch opens at **75% of $N_s$**, not 100%. (Loses 1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Not drawing a **phasor diagram** in the starting torque derivation. The phasor diagram showing $I_m$ lagging and $I_a$ leading is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram the construction and working of a **capacitor-start induction-run motor**. Discuss the role of the starting capacitor and the centrifugal switch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $250\text{ V}$, $50\text{ Hz}$ single-phase capacitor-start induction motor has the following parameters:
- Main winding: $R_m = 5\ \Omega$, $X_m = 8\ \Omega$
- Auxiliary winding: $R_a = 10\ \Omega$, $X_a = 5\ \Omega$

Calculate:
1. The capacitance required in the auxiliary winding circuit for $90°$ phase displacement between the currents.
2. The currents in the main and auxiliary windings at starting. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A capacitor-start induction-run motor is a single-phase induction motor designed to provide **high starting torque** for applications such as compressors, pumps, and conveyors.

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** Laminated core with two windings placed **90° electrical apart in space**:
  - **Main winding (M):** Thick wire, low resistance, high inductance, in the bottom of the slots.
  - **Auxiliary winding (A):** Thinner wire, in the top of the slots, in series with a **capacitor $C$** and a **centrifugal switch**.
- **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage type, identical to three-phase motors.
- **Capacitor:** Electrolytic type, AC-rated, mounted on the motor frame.
- **Centrifugal switch:** Mounted on the rotor shaft, normally closed.

**Working:**
1. At start, the centrifugal switch is closed, connecting both windings to the supply.
2. The main winding current $I_m$ lags the voltage by a large angle $\theta_m$ (inductive).
3. The auxiliary winding current $I_a$ leads the voltage by angle $\theta_a$ (capacitive).
4. The phase angle between $I_m$ and $I_a$ is: $\alpha = \theta_m + \theta_a \approx 90°$.
5. The two currents, displaced 90° in time and 90° in space, produce a **uniform rotating magnetic field**, exerting a strong starting torque (3–4.5 × FLT).
6. At ~75% of synchronous speed, the centrifugal switch **opens**, disconnecting the auxiliary winding and capacitor.
7. The motor continues to run on the main winding alone as a single-phase induction motor.

**Role of Capacitor:** Provides a leading current in the auxiliary winding, achieving a near-90° phase shift for maximum starting torque.

**Role of Centrifugal Switch:** Protects the electrolytic capacitor (which is rated for short-duration starting only) and improves running efficiency by removing the auxiliary winding from the circuit.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $V = 250\text{ V}$, $f = 50\text{ Hz}$, $R_m = 5\ \Omega$, $X_m = 8\ \Omega$, $R_a = 10\ \Omega$, $X_a = 5\ \Omega$

**Step 1: Main winding impedance and phase angle:**

$$Z_m = \sqrt{R_m^2 + X_m^2} = \sqrt{25 + 64} = \sqrt{89} \approx 9.43\ \Omega$$

$$\theta_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{8}{5}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.6) \approx 57.99° \approx 58°$$

**Step 2: Main winding current at starting:**

$$I_m = \frac{V}{Z_m} = \frac{250}{9.43} \approx 26.51\text{ A (lagging)}$$

**Step 3: For 90° phase shift, $\theta_a$ must be:**

$$\theta_a = 90° - \theta_m = 90° - 58° = 32°$$

**Step 4: Auxiliary winding phase angle (without capacitor):**

The auxiliary winding alone has: $\theta_{a,inductive} = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_a}{R_a}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{5}{10}\right) = 26.57°$ (current lagging).

With the capacitor in series, the current leads. The total effective reactance must satisfy:

$$\tan\theta_a = \frac{X_a - X_c}{R_a} = \tan(32°)$$

$$\frac{5 - X_c}{10} = 0.6249$$

$$5 - X_c = 6.249 \implies X_c = -1.249\ \Omega$$

The **negative sign** indicates that the capacitor's reactance must exceed the auxiliary winding's inductive reactance, which is physically valid.

$$\vert X_c \vert = 1.249\ \Omega$$

**Step 5: Required capacitance:**

$$C = \frac{1}{2\pi f \cdot \vert X_c \vert} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 1.249}$$

$$C = \frac{1}{392.36} \approx 2.548 \times 10^{-3}\text{ F} = 2548\ \mu\text{F}$$

**Step 6: Auxiliary winding current (with capacitor):**

$$Z_a = \sqrt{R_a^2 + (X_a - X_c)^2} = \sqrt{10^2 + (-1.249)^2} = \sqrt{100 + 1.56} = \sqrt{101.56} \approx 10.08\ \Omega$$

$$I_a = \frac{V}{Z_a} = \frac{250}{10.08} \approx 24.80\text{ A (leading)}$$

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{C \approx 2548\ \mu\text{F}, \quad I_m \approx 26.51\text{ A}, \quad I_a \approx 24.80\text{ A}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model] — Alternative Choice`

**(a)** Derive the condition for **maximum starting torque** in a single-phase induction motor with an auxiliary winding. Show that this requires the angle between the main and auxiliary winding currents to be **90°**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **construction and working** of a capacitor-start induction-run motor with a **neat phasor diagram** showing the phase relationship between the two winding currents. List **four applications**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Setup:**
Consider a single-phase induction motor with two windings:
- Main winding carrying current $\vec{I_m}$ producing flux $\phi_m$.
- Auxiliary winding carrying current $\vec{I_a}$ producing flux $\phi_a$.

The two windings are displaced **90° in space**. Let the time-phase angle between $\vec{I_m}$ and $\vec{I_a}$ be $\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Starting Torque Expression:**
The starting torque in such a motor is analogous to that of a two-phase motor:

$$T_{st} = K \cdot \phi_m \cdot \phi_a \cdot \sin\alpha$$

Assuming the iron is unsaturated, $\phi_m \propto I_m$ and $\phi_a \propto I_a$:

$$T_{st} = K' \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Maximization:**
Differentiate $T_{st}$ with respect to $\alpha$:

$$\frac{dT_{st}}{d\alpha} = K' \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \cos\alpha$$

Set to zero for extremum:

$$\cos\alpha = 0 \implies \alpha = 90°\ (\text{or}\ 270°)$$

**Step 4 — Confirm Maximum:**

$$\frac{d^2 T_{st}}{d\alpha^2} = -K' \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin\alpha$$

At $\alpha = 90°$: $\dfrac{d^2 T_{st}}{d\alpha^2} = -K' \cdot I_m \cdot I_a < 0$ (since $I_m, I_a, K' > 0$), confirming a **maximum**.

**Conclusion:** The starting torque is maximum when $\alpha = 90°$.

$$\boxed{T_{st(max)} = K' \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \quad \text{at} \quad \alpha = 90°}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Construction:** (Refer to Section 2.1 and 4.5 for full description.)

**Phasor Diagram Description:**

Let $\vec{V} = V \angle 0°$ be the reference.
- $\vec{I_m}$ **lags** $\vec{V}$ by $\theta_m$ (e.g., 60°) — main winding current.
- $\vec{I_a}$ **leads** $\vec{V}$ by $\theta_a$ (e.g., 30°) — auxiliary winding current (capacitor effect).
- Phase angle between $\vec{I_m}$ and $\vec{I_a}$: $\alpha = \theta_m + \theta_a = 90°$.

```
         I_a
          ↗  (leads V by θ_a)
         /
        /
       /_________ V (reference)
        \
         \
          ↘  (lags V by θ_m)
         I_m
```

**Four Applications:**
1. **Air compressors and refrigerator compressors** — require high starting torque.
2. **Water pumps (jet pumps, centrifugal pumps)** — need strong starting push.
3. **Conveyor belts and machine tools** — loaded starts demand high torque.
4. **Washing machines and large blowers** — frequently started under load.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- [x] **Capacitor-Start Induction-Run Motor** uses a **capacitor in series with the auxiliary winding** to create a near-90° phase shift for high starting torque.
- [x] The **starting torque is proportional to** $\sin\alpha$ where $\alpha$ is the phase angle between $I_m$ and $I_a$. **Maximum at $\alpha = 90°$**.
- [x] Typical **starting torque = 3 to 4.5 × full-load torque**.
- [x] **Centrifugal switch** disconnects the auxiliary circuit at **70%–80% of $N_s$**.
- [x] **Electrolytic capacitor** (AC-rated) is used because of short duty cycle and high capacitance per unit volume.
- [x] **Two windings are 90° apart in space**; their currents are **90° apart in time** → rotating magnetic field.
- [x] **Key formula:** $C = \dfrac{\sin\theta_m}{2\pi f R_a \cos\theta_m} = \dfrac{\tan\theta_m}{2\pi f R_a}$
- [x] **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ RPM.
- [x] **Direction reversal:** Swap the auxiliary winding terminals (NOT the main winding).
- [x] **Applications:** Compressors, pumps, conveyors, machine tools, washing machines.
- [x] **Advantages over split-phase motor:** Higher starting torque, lower starting current, better power factor at start.
- [x] **Disadvantage:** Capacitor has limited life; centrifugal switch may fail; not suitable for frequent start-stop (use capacitor-start capacitor-run instead).
- [x] **Cannot run on auxiliary winding alone** at full load — the auxiliary winding is designed for short-duration operation only.
- [x] **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always draw the **phasor diagram** and show the **90° angle** between $I_m$ and $I_a$ in any starting torque question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Permanent Capacitor Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Permanent Capacitor Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Permanent Capacitor Single-Phase Induction Motor (PSC Motor)**, also known as a **Capacitor-Run Motor**, is a type of split-phase alternating current induction motor in which a suitably rated **AC electrolytic or oil-filled paper capacitor is permanently connected in series with the auxiliary (starting) winding** throughout the entire operation of the motor — both at start and during running conditions.

Unlike Capacitor-Start or Capacitor-Start Capacitor-Run motors, the permanent capacitor motor **does not employ a centrifugal switch** to disconnect the auxiliary winding or capacitor after the rotor accelerates. The capacitor remains continuously in the auxiliary branch, providing a near-quadrature phase displacement (ideally 90°) between the main and auxiliary winding currents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCEOT402 Module 4):**
> A permanent capacitor single-phase induction motor uses a permanently connected capacitor in the auxiliary winding circuit. The capacitor value is selected to optimize the **running performance** (high power factor, smooth torque, low noise) rather than starting performance. It is widely employed in ceiling fans, table fans, blowers, and small office machinery.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a heavy merry-go-round alone. You can only push it from one side, and it gets stuck (this is the **single-phase problem** — no rotating field). Now, suppose you have a friend who can push it simultaneously, but a little *earlier* in time (i.e., **out of phase** with you). If your friend pushes from a different direction with the right timing, the merry-go-round will start rotating smoothly. The capacitor's job in a permanent capacitor motor is exactly this — to create a **"friend that pushes at the right time"** by making the current in the auxiliary winding *lead* the current in the main winding. The capacitor never leaves; it keeps helping the motor push at the right moment forever.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Board Exam:**
> - **Capacitor-Start Motor** → Capacitor is used **only at starting**; disconnected by centrifugal switch.
> - **Capacitor-Run / Permanent Capacitor Motor** → Capacitor stays **permanently in circuit** for both start and run.
> - **Capacitor-Start Capacitor-Run Motor** → Uses **two capacitors** — large one for starting, small one permanently for running.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Performance Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Supply Frequency ($f$) | **50 Hz** (India) |
| Supply Voltage ($V$) | **230 V (single-phase AC)** |
| Permanent Capacitance ($C$) | **2 μF to 20 μF** |
| Phase Displacement Achieved | **80° to 90°** (near-quadrature) |
| Power Factor Improvement | **0.8 to 0.95 (lagging → near unity)** |
| Starting Torque | **50% to 100% of full-load torque** (low) |
| Efficiency | **60% to 75%** |
| Typical Power Rating | **40 W to 400 W** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy

The permanent capacitor motor consists of the following key parts:

- **Stator Core:** Laminated silicon steel stamping stack with uniformly distributed slots.
- **Main Winding (Running Winding):** Occupies approximately two-thirds of the stator slots. Has high inductance and resistance, designed for continuous operation.
- **Auxiliary Winding (Starting Winding):** Occupies the remaining one-third of stator slots, physically displaced by **90 electrical degrees** in space from the main winding.
- **Permanent Capacitor:** An oil-filled or metallized polypropylene AC capacitor connected in series with the auxiliary winding. It is **permanently wired** and operates continuously.
- **Squirrel-Cage Rotor:** Identical to that of a three-phase induction motor — bars and end rings cast in aluminum.
- **No Centrifugal Switch:** A major constructional simplification that enhances reliability.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Phase-Splitting Mechanism

The operation of a permanent capacitor motor hinges on the principle of **two-phase rotating magnetic field production from a single-phase supply**.

1. The single-phase AC supply is fed to both windings simultaneously.
2. The capacitor in series with the auxiliary winding causes the **auxiliary current $\vec{I}_a$ to lead the supply voltage** by a large angle (since the capacitor's reactance $X_C$ dominates).
3. The main winding current $\vec{I}_m$ **lags the supply voltage** (inductive behavior).
4. The net result: $\vec{I}_a$ and $\vec{I}_m$ are displaced in time phase by approximately **90 electrical degrees**.
5. Since the windings are also physically displaced by 90° in space, this **space + time quadrature** produces a **rotating magnetic field** of nearly constant magnitude.
6. This rotating field cuts the squirrel-cage rotor bars, induces EMF, drives rotor current, and produces torque.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Capacitor

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Capacitor and Not a Resistor?**
> A pure resistor gives at best 30° phase shift, producing an **elliptical** rotating field → poor starting. A capacitor can easily provide **80°–90°** shift, producing a near-**circular** rotating field → strong, smooth starting and running.

The capacitive reactance is given by:

$$X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C}$$

For $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ and $C = 4\,\mu\text{F}$:

$$X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 795.77\,\Omega$$

This large reactance dominates the auxiliary branch, forcing the current $\vec{I}_a$ to lead the voltage by a substantial angle.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C}$ | Capacitive reactance of the permanent capacitor |
| 2 | $T = k \cdot \phi_m \cdot I_r \cdot \cos(\psi)$ | General electromagnetic torque equation |
| 3 | $T = T_f - T_b$ | Net torque = Forward torque − Backward torque |
| 4 | $T_f = \frac{k \cdot s \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s \cdot X_2)^2}$ | Forward field torque at slip $s$ |
| 5 | $T_b = \frac{k \cdot (2-s) \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + ((2-s) \cdot X_2)^2}$ | Backward field torque at slip $(2-s)$ |
| 6 | $\vec{I}_a = \frac{\vec{V}}{R_a + j(X_a - X_C)}$ | Auxiliary branch current (capacitive) |
| 7 | $\vec{I}_m = \frac{\vec{V}}{R_m + j X_m}$ | Main branch current (inductive) |
| 8 | $\alpha = \angle (\vec{I}_a) - \angle (\vec{I}_m)$ | Phase angle between winding currents; ideally $\approx 90°$ |
| 9 | $\phi = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \phi_{max} \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Pulsating MMF resolved into two counter-rotating fluxes |
| 10 | $n_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed (RPM) |
| 11 | $s = \frac{n_s - n_r}{n_s}$ | Slip (fraction) |
| 12 | $\cos\phi_{motor} = \frac{P_{input}}{V \cdot I_{line}}$ | Overall power factor (improved to near unity) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for Board Exam:** When writing $X_a - X_C$ in the auxiliary branch, remember the sign. The capacitor's reactance **subtracts** from the inductive reactance of the auxiliary winding. This is the heart of the design.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Permanent capacitor motors dominate the **consumer appliance industry**:
- **Ceiling fans and table fans** (the largest single application globally).
- **Refrigerator compressors** (hermetic sealed units).
- **Air conditioner indoor blower units.**
- **Office equipment**: photocopying machines, paper shredders, small printers.
- **Water pumps** (small domestic booster pumps).
- **Hospital ventilators** (silent operation is critical).

The reasons: **no switch means no switching noise, no sparking, longer life, and improved power factor** — all crucial for domestic and medical environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resolution of Pulsating Flux into Two Counter-Rotating Fields

A single-phase alternating current produces a **pulsating magnetic field**, not a rotating one. However, by **double revolving field theory** (proposed by Leblanc), this pulsating field can be mathematically decomposed into two equal, oppositely rotating fields.

**Let** the main winding produce a sinusoidally varying flux along the horizontal axis:

$$\phi_m(t) = \Phi_{max} \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

We wish to represent this as the sum of two rotating flux vectors of magnitude $\frac{\Phi_{max}}{2}$ rotating in opposite directions at synchronous angular velocity $\omega$.

**Forward rotating flux component** (counter-clockwise):

$$\phi_f = \frac{\Phi_{max}}{2} \cdot \cos(\omega t - \theta)$$

**Backward rotating flux component** (clockwise):

$$\phi_b = \frac{\Phi_{max}}{2} \cdot \cos(\omega t + \theta)$$

**Step 1 — Sum the two components:**

$$\phi_f + \phi_b = \frac{\Phi_{max}}{2} \cdot [\cos(\omega t - \theta) + \cos(\omega t + \theta)]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B) = 2 \cos A \cos B$:**

$$\phi_f + \phi_b = \frac{\Phi_{max}}{2} \cdot 2 \cdot \cos(\omega t) \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

$$\phi_f + \phi_b = \Phi_{max} \cdot \cos(\omega t) \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluating along the axis of the main winding ($\theta = 0$):**

$$\phi(\theta = 0) = \Phi_{max} \cdot \cos(\omega t) \cdot \cos(0) = \Phi_{max} \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

This is exactly the original pulsating flux. **Q.E.D.** — the pulsating field is rigorously equivalent to two counter-rotating fields of half magnitude.

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque Expression in Terms of Slip

The two counter-rotating fields induce EMFs in the rotor. The **forward field** operates at slip $s$, and the **backward field** operates at slip $(2-s)$ relative to the rotor.

**For the forward field:**

- Slip with respect to forward synchronous speed $= s$
- Rotor frequency $= s \cdot f$
- Rotor resistance (referred) $= R_2$
- Rotor reactance at slip frequency $= s \cdot X_2$

The forward torque using the standard induction motor torque formula is:

$$T_f = \frac{k \cdot s \cdot E_{2f}^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s \cdot X_2)^2}$$

**For the backward field:**

- Slip with respect to backward synchronous speed $= 2 - s$
- Rotor frequency $= (2-s) \cdot f$
- Rotor reactance at this slip frequency $= (2-s) \cdot X_2$

The backward torque is:

$$T_b = \frac{k \cdot (2-s) \cdot E_{2b}^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + ((2-s) \cdot X_2)^2$$

**Step 4 — Net electromagnetic torque produced by the motor:**

$$T_{net} = T_f - T_b$$

$$\boxed{T_{net} = \frac{k \cdot s \cdot E_{2f}^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s \cdot X_2)^2} - \frac{k \cdot (2-s) \cdot E_{2b}^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + ((2-s) \cdot X_2)^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Special case at starting ($s = 1$):**

At starting, $s = 1$ and $(2-s) = 1$. Substituting:

$$T_{start} = \frac{k \cdot E_{2f}^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} - \frac{k \cdot E_{2b}^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$$

**Step 6 — For a permanent capacitor motor, the two EMFs are related by the turns ratio and phase shift. If we assume the auxiliary winding produces a flux of comparable magnitude to the main winding (due to near-90° phase shift), then $E_{2f} \approx E_{2b}$, and:**

$$T_{start} \approx 0 \quad \text{(in the pure single-phase case)}$$

However, the **quadrature time phase** between $\vec{I}_m$ and $\vec{I}_a$ ensures that the *net* rotating field is dominant in one direction, giving a non-zero starting torque — typically **50% to 100% of full-load torque**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Auxiliary Branch Current and Phase Angle

**Step 1 — Auxiliary winding impedance:**

The auxiliary branch consists of resistance $R_a$, inductive reactance $X_a$, and a series capacitor of reactance $X_C$.

$$Z_a = R_a + j(X_a - X_C)$$

**Step 2 — Auxiliary current magnitude:**

$$I_a = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_a^2 + (X_a - X_C)^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Auxiliary current phase angle (leading the voltage by):**

$$\beta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_C - X_a}{R_a}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Main winding current phase angle (lagging the voltage by):**

$$\gamma = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Total phase angle between the two currents:**

$$\alpha = \beta + \gamma$$

**Step 6 — For perfect circular rotating field, we need $\alpha = 90°$. Hence, the capacitor sizing must satisfy:**

$$X_C = X_a + \frac{R_a}{\tan(\gamma)} \approx X_a + R_a \cdot \cot(\gamma)$$

This is the **design equation for the permanent capacitor** in a permanent capacitor motor.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Capacitor-Start | Permanent Capacitor | Capacitor-Start Capacitor-Run |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitor in circuit at start | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Capacitor in circuit at run | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (smaller one) |
| Centrifugal switch | ✅ Required | ❌ Not required | ✅ Required |
| Starting Torque | **High (200–400% FLT)** | **Low (50–100% FLT)** | **Very High (300–450% FLT)** |
| Power Factor at Run | Poor (0.6–0.7) | **High (0.85–0.95)** | **High (0.85–0.95)** |
| Efficiency | Moderate | **Good (continuous capacitor)** | Good |
| Cost & Complexity | Moderate | **Low (simplest)** | High (two capacitors) |
| Reliability | Lower (switch issues) | **Highest (no switch)** | Moderate |
| Typical Application | Compressors, pumps | **Fans, blowers** | Compressors, large pumps |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define a permanent capacitor single-phase induction motor. List its two main advantages over a capacitor-start motor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A permanent capacitor single-phase induction motor is a type of split-phase induction motor in which a capacitor is **permanently connected in series with the auxiliary winding** without using a centrifugal switch. The capacitor remains in the circuit during both starting and running conditions.

**Two Main Advantages over Capacitor-Start Motor:**
1. **Higher Power Factor at Running:** The permanent capacitor provides continuous reactive power compensation, improving the running power factor to 0.85–0.95 (lagging).
2. **Higher Reliability and Longer Life:** Absence of a centrifugal switch eliminates mechanical switching failures, sparking, and switch contact wear, making the motor more reliable and silent in operation.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional Exam]`
**Why is the capacitor in a permanent capacitor motor permanently connected, and how does it improve the running performance?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The capacitor in a permanent capacitor motor is permanently connected to **continuously maintain a near-quadrature phase displacement (≈90°)** between the main and auxiliary winding currents. This produces a nearly **circular rotating magnetic field** during running, which:

1. **Reduces the backward rotating field component**, minimizing negative sequence torque and rotor copper losses.
2. **Improves the running power factor** to near unity by supplying leading reactive volt-amperes.
3. **Ensures smooth, silent, vibration-free running** with higher efficiency.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each. The student attempts **either Question A OR Question B** in full.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`

**Question A:** With the help of a neat circuit diagram and phasor diagram, explain the construction, working principle, and torque-speed characteristics of a permanent capacitor single-phase induction motor. Also derive the expression for the auxiliary winding current and the phase angle between the two winding currents.

**Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Phasor Diagram [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Construction [2 Marks]:**
The permanent capacitor motor has the following parts:
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel core with two windings — main winding (2/3 of slots) and auxiliary winding (1/3 of slots), physically displaced by 90 electrical degrees.
- **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage type, identical to three-phase induction motor.
- **Capacitor:** Oil-filled AC capacitor of value 2–20 μF connected permanently in series with the auxiliary winding.
- **No centrifugal switch.**

**2. Working Principle [3 Marks]:**
When a single-phase AC supply is given, the main winding draws current $\vec{I}_m$ which lags the voltage by a large angle (inductive). The auxiliary winding, in series with the capacitor, draws current $\vec{I}_a$ which **leads** the voltage by a substantial angle (capacitive). The two currents are displaced in time by an angle $\alpha = 80°\text{–}90°$. Since the windings are also displaced by 90° in space, a **rotating magnetic field of nearly constant magnitude** is produced. This field cuts the rotor bars, inducing EMF, current, and producing torque in the direction of the rotating field.

**3. Phasor Diagram [2 Marks]:**
- $\vec{V}$ → reference supply voltage.
- $\vec{I}_m$ → lags $\vec{V}$ by angle $\gamma = \tan^{-1}(X_m/R_m)$.
- $\vec{I}_a$ → leads $\vec{V}$ by angle $\beta = \tan^{-1}[(X_C - X_a)/R_a]$.
- Phase displacement $\alpha = \beta + \gamma \approx 90°$ (ideal for circular rotating field).

---

**Part (b) — Derivation of Auxiliary Current, Phase Angle, and Torque-Speed Characteristic [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Auxiliary Winding Current [3 Marks]:**

The auxiliary branch impedance:

$$Z_a = R_a + j(X_a - X_C)$$

Magnitude:

$$I_a = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_a^2 + (X_a - X_C)^2}}$$

Phase angle of $\vec{I}_a$ w.r.t. $\vec{V}$ (leading):

$$\beta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_C - X_a}{R_a}\right)$$

**2. Phase Angle Between Currents [2 Marks]:**

Main current phase (lagging):

$$\gamma = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$$

Total displacement between $\vec{I}_a$ and $\vec{I}_m$:

$$\alpha = \beta + \gamma = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_C - X_a}{R_a}\right) + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$$

For circular rotating field: $\alpha = 90°$, giving the design condition:

$$X_C = X_a + R_a \cdot \cot(\gamma)$$

**3. Torque-Speed Characteristic [2 Marks]:**

The net torque is given by:

$$T_{net} = \frac{k \cdot s \cdot E_{2f}^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s \cdot X_2)^2} - \frac{k \cdot (2-s) \cdot E_{2b}^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + ((2-s) \cdot X_2)^2}$$

The characteristic shows:
- Starting torque = 50%–100% of full-load torque.
- Smooth, gradual rise to maximum torque near synchronous speed.
- No double-cage dips, and reduced backward torque due to the permanent capacitor.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Supplementary Exam]`

**Question B:** Compare the permanent capacitor motor with the capacitor-start and capacitor-start capacitor-run motors. Discuss the role of the permanent capacitor in producing a rotating magnetic field, and explain why this motor is preferred for ceiling fan applications.

**Part (a) — Comparison Table and Role of Capacitor [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Comparison Table [3 Marks]:**

| Feature | Capacitor-Start | Permanent Capacitor | Cap-Start Cap-Run |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitor at start | Yes | Yes | Yes (large) |
| Capacitor at run | No | Yes | Yes (small) |
| Centrifugal switch | Yes | No | Yes |
| Starting torque | High (3-4× FLT) | Low (0.5-1× FLT) | Very high (4-5× FLT) |
| Running PF | 0.6-0.7 | 0.85-0.95 | 0.85-0.95 |
| Cost | Moderate | Low | High |
| Reliability | Moderate | High | Moderate |

**2. Role of Permanent Capacitor [4 Marks]:**
- The capacitor introduces a **leading phase shift** in the auxiliary current.
- This compensates the inductive lag in the main current, achieving a **near-90° time-phase displacement**.
- Combined with the 90° space displacement of the windings, this generates a **near-circular rotating magnetic field**.
- The capacitor's reactive volt-amperes **compensate the inductive VARs** of the motor, **improving the power factor** to near unity.
- It also **suppresses the backward rotating field component**, reducing stray losses and acoustic noise.

---

**Part (b) — Suitability for Ceiling Fans [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Fan Load Characteristics [2 Marks]:**
A ceiling fan load requires **low starting torque** (only 0.5–1.0 times full-load torque) but demands **smooth, vibration-free, and silent operation** at constant speed over long durations (8–16 hours/day).

**2. Why Permanent Capacitor Motor Suits Fans [5 Marks]:**

(i) **No centrifugal switch** → no switching noise, no sparking, and no mechanical wear — critical for household ceiling fans where silent operation is mandatory.

(ii) **Improved power factor** (0.85–0.95) → reduces line current, saves electricity bills, and avoids penalty from electricity boards.

(iii) **Higher efficiency and cooler running** → the capacitor compensates reactive power, reducing $I^2R$ losses in windings and supply lines.

(iv) **Smoother torque-speed curve** → less vibration, longer bearing life, and reduced acoustic humming.

(v) **Compact, lightweight, and inexpensive** → ideal for domestic fan housings and aesthetic designs.

(vi) **Maintenance-free** → no switch contacts to clean or replace; ideal for ceiling-mounted applications where maintenance is difficult.

(vii) **Self-starting** with adequate starting torque for fan-blade inertia.

Hence, the permanent capacitor motor is the **standard, almost universal, choice for ceiling and table fans** worldwide.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Capacitor-Start" with "Permanent Capacitor"** — The capacitor-start motor disconnects the capacitor after starting via a centrifugal switch. The permanent capacitor motor NEVER disconnects it. This distinction is worth 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Writing $X_a + X_C$ instead of $X_a - X_C$** — In the auxiliary branch, the capacitor's reactance **subtracts** from the inductive reactance. Writing the wrong sign will lose a full mark in derivation questions.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the difference between space and time phase** — A perfect rotating field requires BOTH 90° space displacement (winding position) AND 90° time displacement (current phase). Many students mention only one.
>
> 4. **Wrong unit of capacitance** — Always write in microfarads (μF) for permanent capacitor motors. Farads will be marked wrong.
>
> 5. **Saying "Permanent Capacitor Motor has high starting torque"** — This is FALSE. It has **low starting torque** (50–100% FLT), which is why it cannot be used for compressor loads.
>
> 6. **Skipping the phasor diagram in 14-mark questions** — Always draw a labeled phasor diagram. Examiners allot 2–3 marks specifically for the diagram.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning applications** — Always end with at least 2 real-world applications (e.g., ceiling fan, blower). This is worth 1 mark in most valuation schemes.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Permanent Capacitor Motor (PSC Motor)** uses a capacitor **permanently in series with the auxiliary winding**; no centrifugal switch.
- ✅ The capacitor provides a **time-phase displacement of ~90°** between main and auxiliary currents.
- ✅ Combined with the **90° space displacement** of windings, this produces a **near-circular rotating magnetic field**.
- ✅ The auxiliary branch impedance is $Z_a = R_a + j(X_a - X_C)$ — the **minus sign is critical**.
- ✅ Capacitor reactance: $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$, where $C$ is typically 2–20 μF.
- ✅ Starting torque is **low (50–100% of full-load torque)** — not suitable for high-inertia loads.
- ✅ Running power factor is **high (0.85–0.95)** — major advantage.
- ✅ Net torque: $T_{net} = T_f - T_b$, where each is derived from double-revolving-field theory.
- ✅ Design condition for circular field: $X_C = X_a + R_a \cot(\gamma)$, where $\gamma$ is the main winding phase angle.
- ✅ The motor is **self-starting**, reliable, silent, and maintenance-free.
- ✅ **Primary application: ceiling fans, table fans, blowers, small domestic appliances.**
- ✅ NOT suitable for refrigerator compressors or air conditioner compressors (these need high starting torque → use capacitor-start or CSR motors).
- ✅ Forward field torque: $T_f = \dfrac{k \cdot s \cdot E_{2f}^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s \cdot X_2)^2}$
- ✅ Backward field torque: $T_b = \dfrac{k \cdot (2-s) \cdot E_{2b}^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + ((2-s) \cdot X_2)^2}$
- ✅ Synchronous speed: $n_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ (RPM).

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Permanent Capacitor Types**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, PCEOT402 — Electrical Machines*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT402 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **PCEOT402 – Electrical Machines (Module 4)**, the topic *"Applications"* refers to the **practical deployment scenarios of single-phase induction motors (SPIMs)** and related fractional-horsepower AC machines across domestic, commercial, and light-industrial sectors. A **single-phase induction motor** is a self-starting (with auxiliary means) AC machine that operates on a **single-phase AC supply**, converting electrical energy into mechanical energy through electromagnetic induction. Unlike three-phase induction motors, SPIMs are **not inherently self-starting** because a single-phase stator winding produces only a **pulsating, non-rotating magnetic field**, which can be resolved (via the *double-revolving field theory*) into two equal and opposite rotating fields that produce zero net starting torque. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 typically covers Single-Phase Induction Motors — their construction, double-revolving field theory, starting methods, and **applications**. The "Applications" segment requires students to map each motor type to its real-world use case, justifying the choice based on starting torque, running performance, cost, and duty cycle. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are pushing a heavy merry-go-round. If you push from one side only, it just rocks back and forth — it does not spin. But if you give it a slight twist from one side while pushing from the other, it starts rotating smoothly. A **single-phase induction motor** behaves the same way: it needs an *auxiliary push* (a starting winding, capacitor, or shading ring) to start rotating in one direction. ## 1.3 Why Applications Matter in KTU Examinations - **Direct theory questions** of 3 marks frequently ask: *"List the applications of shaded-pole motors"* or *"Where are capacitor-start motors used?"* - **Application-based 14-mark questions** may present a real-world scenario (e.g., *"A washing machine requires high starting torque… which motor is suitable? Justify."*) and test the student's ability to select and justify the appropriate motor type. > [!NOTE] > **Key Performance Metrics for Motor Selection:** > - **Starting Torque (T_st)** — typically **1.5 to 3 times** rated torque for capacitor-start motors > - **Rated Power** — fractional range **50 W to 1.5 kW** > - **Supply Standard** — **230 V, 50 Hz** in India > - **Speed** — typically **1440 rpm (4-pole)** or **2880 rpm (2-pole)** synchronous at 50 Hz # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Single-Phase Induction Motors Single-phase induction motors are classified based on **starting mechanism** and **auxiliary winding design**. Each type has a distinct torque-speed characteristic and thus a distinct application domain. ### 2.1.1 Split-Phase Motor **Construction:** Two windings on the stator — a **main (running) winding** with high inductance (low resistance, many turns of thin wire) and an **auxiliary (starting) winding** with low inductance (high resistance, few turns of thick wire). The two windings are displaced by **90° electrical** in space. **Operating Principle:** The high resistance of the starting winding shifts its current closer in phase with the supply voltage, while the main winding current lags significantly. This produces a phase displacement of roughly **25°–30°** between the two currents, generating a weak rotating magnetic field that produces just enough starting torque. **Characteristic Values:** - Starting Torque: **1.5 to 2 times** full-load torque - Starting Current: **6 to 8 times** full-load current ### 2.1.2 Capacitor-Start Motor **Construction:** Identical to split-phase, but a **capacitor C_s** (electrolytic, AC-rated, typically **80–300 μF**) is connected in series with the auxiliary winding. **Operating Principle:** The capacitor causes the auxiliary winding current to **lead** the main winding current by approximately **80°–90°**, producing a near-circular rotating field at start. **Characteristic Values:** - Starting Torque: **3 to 4.5 times** full-load torque - Centrifugal switch disconnects the auxiliary winding at **~75% of synchronous speed**. ### 2.1.3 Capacitor-Run Motor (Permanent Capacitor) **Construction:** A **low-value oil-paper or metallized polypropylene capacitor** (typically **3–16 μF**) remains permanently in the auxiliary winding circuit — no centrifugal switch. **Operating Principle:** Provides better running performance, higher power factor, and quieter operation at the cost of lower starting torque. **Characteristic Values:** - Starting Torque: **0.5 to 1.5 times** full-load torque - Running Power Factor: significantly improved (close to unity possible) ### 2.1.4 Capacitor-Start Capacitor-Run Motor (Two-Value Capacitor) **Construction:** Combines both an **electrolytic start capacitor** and a **permanent run capacitor**. The start capacitor is disconnected via centrifugal switch once motor reaches running speed. **Operating Principle:** Optimized for both **high starting torque** AND **excellent running performance**. **Characteristic Values:** - Starting Torque: **2.5 to 4.5 times** full-load torque - Best all-round performance among single-phase motors. ### 2.1.5 Shaded-Pole Motor **Construction:** Has salient poles with a **copper shading ring (shading coil)** embedded across a portion of each pole face (~30–40% of pole arc). **Operating Principle:** The shading ring delays the flux in its portion of the pole, creating a moving flux wave from un-shaded to shaded region — producing a weak rotating field. **Characteristic Values:** - Starting Torque: **0.5 to 1 times** full-load torque (very low) - Efficiency: **30–40%** (lowest among SPIMs) - No centrifugal switch, no capacitor, no auxiliary winding — simplest construction. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes | |-----------|---------|---------------| | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | $f$ in Hz, $P$ = poles | | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless, **0.03–0.06** typical | | Rotor Frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz | | Torque (qualitative) | $T \propto \dfrac{s \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2 / s}{R_2^2/s^2 + X_2^2}$ | N·m | | Forward Field Speed | $N_f = +N_s$ | rpm | | Backward Field Speed | $N_b = -N_s$ | rpm (relative to rotor) | | Double Revolving Field | $\phi_m \cos\omega t = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}(\cos(\omega t - \theta) + \cos(\omega t + \theta))$ | Tesla | | Starting Capacitor (typical) | $C_s = 80$ to $300$ $\mu F$ | electrolytic, short duty | | Run Capacitor (typical) | $C_r = 3$ to $16$ $\mu F$ | oil-filled, continuous duty | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Reminder:** Never confuse the *direction* of rotation logic in double-revolving field theory. A single-phase winding produces **two equal, opposite rotating fields** that sum to zero starting torque — this is *why* auxiliary starting methods are mandatory. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Single-phase induction motors form the **backbone of the global fractional-horsepower motor market** (>70% of all motors manufactured by unit volume). They power everything from ceiling fans to refrigerator compressors. Understanding their applications enables: 1. **Industrial product design** — selecting the right motor for HVAC, pumps, and appliances. 2. **Energy efficiency engineering** — capacitor-run designs comply with IS 996 and IEC 60034-30 IE-class efficiency standards. 3. **Maintenance and troubleshooting** — identifying motor type from nameplate enables correct repair procedures (e.g., centrifugal switch inspection for capacitor-start motors). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Double Revolving Field Theory The double revolving field theory is foundational to explaining **why single-phase induction motors need auxiliary starting methods**, and frequently appears in KTU derivations. **Step 1:** A single-phase stator winding carrying alternating current $i = I_m \cos(\omega t)$ produces a pulsating (not rotating) magnetic flux along the axis of the winding: $$\phi(t) = \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 2:** Express this pulsating flux as the sum of two counter-rotating vectors using the trigonometric identity: $$\phi_m \cos(\omega t) = \phi_m \cos\theta \cdot \cos(\omega t - \theta)$$ This identity holds for any arbitrary angle $\theta$ — choose $\theta = \omega t$ for the spatial reference. **Step 3:** Apply the product-to-sum identity: $$ \begin{aligned} \cos A \cdot \cos B &= \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)] \\ \phi_m \cos\theta \cos(\omega t - \theta) &= \tfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t - 2\theta) + \tfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Now resolve into two rotating components. Define: - Forward flux: $\phi_f = \tfrac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t - \theta)$ rotating at $+\omega$ - Backward flux: $\phi_b = \tfrac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t + \theta)$ rotating at $-\omega$ **Step 5:** Sum of the two rotating vectors: $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_f + \phi_b &= \tfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t - \theta) + \tfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t + \theta) \\ &= \phi_m \cos\theta \cdot \cos(\omega t) \\ \end{aligned} $$ which, when observed at a fixed point ($\theta = $ constant), reproduces the original pulsating flux. **Step 6:** At standstill ($\omega_r = 0$), the rotor sees both forward and backward fields of **equal magnitude** rotating at $+\omega$ and $-\omega$ respectively. The rotor responds identically (equal but opposite torque). Hence: $$T_{net,\,start} = T_f + T_b = 0$$ This is the **mathematical proof** of why single-phase motors cannot self-start without auxiliary means. ## 3.2 Derivation: Starting Capacitor Selection (Approximate Method) For a **capacitor-start motor**, the desired phase angle between main and auxiliary winding currents should ideally be **90°** for maximum starting torque. **Step 1:** The main winding impedance (approx. inductive): $$Z_m = R_m + jX_m$$ **Step 2:** The auxiliary winding impedance with capacitor $C_s$ in series: $$Z_a = R_a + jX_a - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_s}$$ **Step 3:** The current in the main winding: $$I_m = \dfrac{V}{Z_m}$$ **Step 4:** The current in the auxiliary winding: $$I_a = \dfrac{V}{Z_a}$$ **Step 5:** For 90° phase displacement between $I_m$ and $I_a$, the imaginary part of the auxiliary current must align with the real part of the main current. Solving: $$ \begin{aligned} \omega C_s &= \dfrac{R_a R_m + X_a X_m}{R_a X_m - X_a R_m} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R_m^2 + X_m^2} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** A simpler practical approximation used in industry: $$C_s \approx \dfrac{I_m \sin\phi_m}{\omega V \cdot k}$$ where $\phi_m$ is the phase angle of the main winding and $k$ is a design factor (typically 0.5–0.7). ## 3.3 Application Selection Matrix | Motor Type | Starting Torque (× FLT) | Cost | Maintenance | Typical Applications | |-----------|------------------------|------|-------------|---------------------| | **Split-Phase** | 1.5 – 2.0 | Low | Low (centrifugal switch) | Fans, blowers, small pumps, washing machines (older designs) | | **Capacitor-Start** | 3.0 – 4.5 | Medium | Medium | Refrigerators, air conditioners, compressors, conveyors, machine tools | | **Capacitor-Run** | 0.5 – 1.5 | Medium-High | Very Low | Ceiling fans, table fans, exhaust fans, blowers requiring quiet operation | | **Capacitor-Start Capacitor-Run** | 2.5 – 4.5 | High | Medium | Large refrigerators, air conditioners, commercial pumps, woodworking machinery | | **Shaded-Pole** | 0.5 – 1.0 | Very Low | Very Low (no moving parts in start circuit) | Small fans, hair dryers, toy motors, record players, clocks, exhaust hoods | | **Repulsion (briefly)** | 3.0 – 5.0 | High | High | Compressors, high-start applications (now mostly obsolete, replaced by capacitor-start) | > [!NOTE] > **Practical Reading:** Notice the trade-off — *higher starting torque comes with higher cost and complexity*. This is the central design dilemma in selecting single-phase motors for applications. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A 230 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole single-phase induction motor runs at 1425 rpm at full load. Calculate: 1. Synchronous speed 2. Slip 3. Rotor frequency **Solution:** **Step 1:** Synchronous speed: $$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 2:** Slip: $$ \begin{aligned} s &= \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \\ &= \dfrac{1500 - 1425}{1500} \\ &= \dfrac{75}{1500} = 0.05 \text{ (or 5\%)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Rotor frequency: $$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.05 \times 50 = 2.5 \text{ Hz}$$ These values are **typical for fractional-horsepower industrial motors**. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **"List any six applications of capacitor-start single-phase induction motors."** **Model Answer:** Capacitor-start motors are used where **high starting torque** is essential: 1. **Refrigerators and freezers** — to start the compressor against refrigerant back-pressure. 2. **Air conditioners and window AC units** — compressor starting under high load. 3. **Water pumps (mono-block and centrifugal)** — overcoming static water column. 4. **Air compressors** — starting against tank pressure. 5. **Conveyor belts and small machine tools** — high-inertia loads. 6. **Washing machines (top-loading, older designs)** — agitator start under full load of clothes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Justification Point (for full marks):** A complete answer must explain *why* — because these applications involve **high starting torque requirements (3 to 4.5 times FLT)** that the capacitor-start design uniquely satisfies. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **"Why are shaded-pole motors used only in low-power applications? Give four examples."** **Model Answer:** Shaded-pole motors have: - **Very low starting torque** (0.5 to 1.0 times full-load torque) - **Low efficiency** (30–40%) - **Low power factor** (around 0.4–0.6) These limitations make them unsuitable for heavy-load applications, restricting use to **low-power, low-torque** scenarios. Examples: 1. **Table fans and small exhaust fans** 2. **Hair dryers and hand dryers** 3. **Toy motors and record players** 4. **Clocks, timers, and small domestic appliances** ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and principle of operation of a **capacitor-start single-phase induction motor**. Discuss the role of the centrifugal switch. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Compare the **performance characteristics** (starting torque, starting current, power factor, efficiency, applications) of split-phase, capacitor-start, and capacitor-run single-phase induction motors. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Construction:** A capacitor-start motor consists of: 1. **Stator** with laminated silicon steel core, carrying two windings displaced 90° electrical: - **Main winding (running winding)** — high inductance, low resistance. - **Auxiliary winding (starting winding)** — low inductance, high resistance. 2. **Rotor** — squirrel-cage type, identical to three-phase motor rotor. 3. **Capacitor $C_s$** — electrolytic AC type (80–300 μF), connected in series with the auxiliary winding. 4. **Centrifugal switch** — mounted on the rotor shaft, normally closed at standstill. **Principle of Operation:** At standstill, the centrifugal switch is **closed**. With both windings energized: - Main winding current $I_m$ lags the supply voltage by a large angle (highly inductive). - Auxiliary winding current $I_a$ **leads** the supply voltage by a smaller angle due to capacitive reactance. The net phase displacement between $I_m$ and $I_a$ approaches **80°–90°**, producing a strong rotating magnetic field. The rotating field cuts the squirrel-cage rotor, inducing currents and generating torque in the direction of rotation. As rotor accelerates to **~75% of synchronous speed**, centrifugal force overcomes spring tension in the centrifugal switch, **opening the contact** and disconnecting the auxiliary winding + capacitor from the circuit. The motor then continues to run on the main winding alone, sustained by the forward-rotating field component. **Role of Centrifugal Switch:** 1. **Disconnects auxiliary winding** once motor reaches running speed. 2. **Protects the electrolytic capacitor** from prolonged current flow (which would cause overheating and explosion). 3. **Improves running efficiency** by eliminating auxiliary winding losses. --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Split-Phase | Capacitor-Start | Capacitor-Run | |-----------|-------------|-----------------|---------------| | Starting Torque | 1.5 – 2 × FLT | 3.0 – 4.5 × FLT | 0.5 – 1.5 × FLT | | Starting Current | 6 – 8 × FLC | 5 – 7 × FLC | 3 – 5 × FLC | | Power Factor (Running) | 0.6 – 0.7 | 0.7 – 0.8 | 0.85 – 0.95 | | Efficiency | Moderate | Moderate | Higher (due to improved PF) | | Cost | Lowest | Medium | Medium-High | | Auxiliary Winding Operation | Disconnected at start | Disconnected at start | Permanently in circuit | | Applications | Fans, small pumps | Compressors, AC units, conveyors | Ceiling fans, exhaust fans | **Analysis:** - Split-phase is the **simplest and cheapest** but provides only moderate starting torque. - Capacitor-start provides the **highest starting torque** at moderate cost — ideal for hard-start loads. - Capacitor-run sacrifices starting torque for **superior running performance, quietness, and high power factor** — ideal for continuously running applications like fans. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks [Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Explain the **double revolving field theory** as applied to single-phase induction motors. Using this theory, derive the expression for the **net torque at starting condition**. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A **230 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole** capacitor-start single-phase induction motor has the following nameplate data: full-load speed = **1420 rpm**, full-load current = **8 A**, starting current = **40 A**. Calculate the **[7 Marks]**: 1. Synchronous speed 2. Full-load slip 3. Rotor copper frequency 4. Starting-to-full-load current ratio --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Double Revolving Field Theory — Step-by-Step:** A single-phase stator winding carrying AC current $i = I_m \cos(\omega t)$ produces an alternating (pulsating) flux: $$\phi(t) = \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$ This pulsating flux can be mathematically resolved into **two counter-rotating flux components** of equal magnitude $\phi_m/2$: **Step 1:** Use the trigonometric identity: $$\phi_m \cos(\omega t) = \phi_m \cos\theta \cdot \cos(\omega t - \theta)$$ **Step 2:** Apply product-to-sum formula: $$ \begin{aligned} \cos A \cos B &= \tfrac{1}{2}\cos(A-B) + \tfrac{1}{2}\cos(A+B) \\ \phi_m \cos\theta \cos(\omega t - \theta) &= \tfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t - 2\theta) + \tfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Rearranging with substitution $\omega t = \theta$: $$\phi_m \cos(\omega t) = \underbrace{\tfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t - \theta)}_{\phi_f \text{ (forward)}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t + \theta)}_{\phi_b \text{ (backward)}}$$ **Step 4:** Interpretation: - $\phi_f$ rotates at angular velocity $+\omega$ (forward) - $\phi_b$ rotates at angular velocity $-\omega$ (backward) **Step 5:** Net starting torque derivation: At standstill ($\omega_r = 0$), the rotor slips with respect to both fields equally: - Slip with respect to forward field: $s_f = 1$ - Slip with respect to backward field: $s_b = 1$ The forward field produces torque $T_f$ in the forward direction. The backward field produces torque $T_b$ in the reverse direction. For an induction motor, torque is approximately proportional to slip divided by rotor impedance squared. At $s = 1$, both fields produce equal-magnitude opposing torques: $$T_f = T_b \implies T_{net} = T_f - T_b = 0$$ This proves a single-phase induction motor has **zero starting torque** without auxiliary means. --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Given Data:** - $V = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$ poles - $N_r = 1420$ rpm (full load) - $I_{FL} = 8$ A - $I_{start} = 40$ A **1. Synchronous Speed:** $$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ **2. Full-Load Slip:** $$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \dfrac{1500 - 1420}{1500} = \dfrac{80}{1500} = 0.0533 \text{ (or 5.33\%)}$$ **3. Rotor Copper Frequency:** $$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.0533 \times 50 = 2.667 \text{ Hz} \approx 2.67 \text{ Hz}$$ **4. Starting-to-Full-Load Current Ratio:** $$\dfrac{I_{start}}{I_{FL}} = \dfrac{40}{8} = 5.0$$ This ratio of 5 is within the typical range (5–7) for capacitor-start single-phase induction motors. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > Students frequently lose marks in the following ways: > > 1. **Confusing rotor frequency with stator supply frequency.** The rotor frequency is **NOT** 50 Hz during running — it is $s \cdot f$. Always compute slip first. > > 2. **Forgetting the centrifugal switch role in capacitor-start motors.** Any explanation without mention of the centrifugal switch is **incomplete** and loses 2 marks. > > 3. **Mixing up forward and backward field directions** in double-revolving field derivations. Always use +ω for forward and −ω for backward. Using opposite signs invalidates the entire derivation. > > 4. **In comparison questions, students list features but fail to provide justification.** A comparison without reasoning ("Capacitor-start has higher starting torque") gets only partial credit. Add *"because the capacitor produces near 90° phase displacement between currents, generating a near-circular rotating field at start."* > > 5. **Applications questions without explanation.** Writing *"fan, pump, compressor"* alone scores 1–2 marks; explaining *why* (e.g., "because shaded-pole motors have very low starting torque, they suit only small fans and toys") earns full 3 marks. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Applications of Single-Phase Induction Motors** > > - **Single-phase motors are NOT inherently self-starting** because single-phase windings produce only a pulsating (non-rotating) magnetic field — proved by **double revolving field theory**. > - **The five main types** of single-phase induction motors are: Split-phase, Capacitor-start, Capacitor-run, Capacitor-start capacitor-run, and Shaded-pole. > - **Synchronous Speed Formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ — memorize without confusion (this is identical to three-phase). > - **Typical Slip Range:** **3–6%** under full load. > - **Rotor Frequency:** $f_r = s \cdot f$ — typically **1.5 to 3 Hz** during running. > - **Capacitor-start** → **highest starting torque (3–4.5 × FLT)** → compressors, refrigerators, AC units. > - **Capacitor-run** → **best running PF and quietness** → ceiling fans, exhaust fans. > - **Shaded-pole** → **simplest, cheapest, but lowest torque** → small fans, hair dryers, toys. > - **Split-phase** → **moderate performance at low cost** → small pumps, blowers, washing machines. > - **Capacitor-start capacitor-run** → **best of both worlds** (high start torque + excellent running) → commercial AC, large refrigerators, industrial pumps. > - **Centrifugal switch** disconnects the auxiliary circuit at approximately **75% of synchronous speed**. > - **Start capacitors are electrolytic** (short duty); **run capacitors are oil-paper** (continuous duty). > - In **KTU examinations**, always **justify your motor choice** with quantitative reasoning (starting torque requirement) — don't just list applications. > - Remember the **standard Indian domestic supply**: **230 V, 50 Hz, single-phase** (with three-phase also available for commercial/industrial loads). > - The **double revolving field theory** mathematically proves that starting torque is zero: $T_{start} = T_f - T_b = 0$ when $s_f = s_b = 1$ at standstill.
